Yao Yuan's Trade in Feelings
(English translation — source folder: 姚媛的情感買賣)

============================================================

Chapter 1 — Gold You'll Get Tomorrow Is Worth Less Than Copper in Hand
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640248.html

Gold you'll get tomorrow is worth less than copper in hand.
Friday, eleven o'clock in the morning, on the dot.
The ring light flared to life, its cold white glow like the shadowless lamp over an operating table, wrapping Yao Yuan in its beam. Behind her was a wall-to-wall window. Beyond the glass there were no buildings, no neon — only a vast, churning expanse of muddy yellow stretched out below: the Yellow River. From this height it didn't roar and surge the way it did in promotional films. Instead it looked like some great, weary beast tamed by the enormous window frame, grinding silently past the edge of the city, countless grains of unseen silt sunk in its turbid sheen.
At the edge of the frame, a gold-thin-bordered diagram of "the Energy Pyramid" hung on the wall, its lines cold and heavy with implication.
Yao Yuan was dressed all in red. A flowing dark-red silk cape set off skin white as snow. Beneath the cape, the neckline of her crimson slip dress was cut with restraint, yet the soft drape of the fabric traced a heart-stopping rise and fall as she moved. The diamonds on her collarbone chain caught the light with every motion, throwing off sharp little points of light. She faced the camera, unhurriedly reapplying Armani lipstick No. 400 — a red that was aggressive, that announced its own presence.
On the iPad beside the phone stand, the live viewer count ticked upward: 82,156.
The light stretched the shadows of her lashes long, casting them beneath her eyes like two silent, watchful wings. This carefully built space — the precise lighting, the symbolic backdrop, the eternal flowing chaos beyond the window, and the woman in red within it — together formed an instrument, a precision instrument for dissecting the human heart, taking apart desire, and measuring the exchange rate between feeling and gain. Stripped of every excess emotion, she was the clear-eyed observer, and also the surgeon who, without a flicker of expression, cut open every truth and lie.
She blotted her lips even, pressed a fingertip lightly to the peak of her mouth, finishing the last step. Then she lifted her eyes, her gaze a probe driving straight into the lens.
"You're here?" Her voice wasn't loud, but it had a strange pull to it, instantly pinning down the greetings scrolling across the public screen.
"Welcome to my 'Clear-Eyed Operating Table.' Here we don't talk romance, we talk rules; we don't watch tears, we watch leverage."
(The scrolling comments started to speed up: [Here! Here!] [Sis is radiating two-point-eight meters of aura today!] [Red battle robe! Is she about to go all-in?])
She turned slightly to the side, letting the camera capture the Yellow River beyond the window more clearly, and the gold pyramid diagram.
"See this light? Cold, white, bright. It hits my face not so you'll think 'Yao Yuan looks gorgeous today.' It's a measuring stick, a reminder — at the negotiating table, clarity is the first virtue. You have to let the other side see exactly what your hand is made of, and you have to see, even more clearly, whether what they're handing you is an olive branch or a dull knife wrapped in silk. Soft light? Dreamy filters? Those are for people still asleep. Here, we face only reality, even when it's coarse and harsh on the eyes."
"This river behind me — you see it? How many years has it flowed, how much joy and sorrow has it watched? In front of it, those tangled little love troubles of yours are as small as a single grain of sand. But it says nothing. It only takes everything in, takes in all the mud and silt, and keeps flowing east. Sitting here, facing it, can let those useless grievances and panics of yours settle a little. Only when a person calms down can they hear what their heart truly wants, and only then can they take in the truth."
"This diagram," she pointed a fingertip vaguely at the Energy Pyramid on the wall, "don't get it? Doesn't matter. All you need to know is that the world of feelings obeys conservation of energy too. Stuck at the bottom, you can only have your energy plundered; if you want to climb, you have to understand the rules of energy exchange. Feelings will bankrupt you. Logic is what holds your value — and even grows it."
(Comments: [Don't understand a word but deeply shaken] [Conservation of energy, sure, why not] [So love is a transaction?] [Crude words, solid truth])
She glanced at the iPad, the comments scrolling across it like a racing river.
"Comments coming in fast? Let me tell you, in my eyes there are only three kinds of people: the ones who need to be woken up, the ones here for the show, and — the ones ready to pay the price and looking for a solution. My time, my insight, is my most valuable asset. So every sentence I speak has to have information density, has to be like a needle, lancing the blister of your emotions with precision so you can see the pus underneath, or the blood. Here, there's no room for filler to survive."
"Quiet?" She smiled, the smile faint, without much warmth. "Of course we keep it quiet. That love-song background music — what does it do besides interfere with your judgment, sink you into a self-pitying soundtrack of your own making? This is an operating room. You need to hear the blade slice through the skin, separate the tissue, find the lesion. All that 'does he even love me,' 'am I just not good enough' muttering — in here, I take it apart on the spot into: What cards are in your hand? What do you want to win? And what are you willing to pay to win it?"
"So," she leaned her body slightly forward, those too-clear eyes seeming, through the lens, to scorch a person, "don't talk to me about feelings. If you want to talk feelings, you shouldn't have clicked into this room. Once you're in, get your position straight. You're not a pitiful little wretch here for comfort. You're an apprentice here to learn how to convert the empty emotions of the feelings-world into real, solid leverage in your life."
"This light, this river, this diagram, me included — they're all teaching aids."
"Now, the call-in line is open. Who has the courage to be the first to lie down on this operating table and pay the first installment of 'tuition for clear sight'?"
[Line Connected]
After a faint crackle of static, a young voice came through — fighting to stay composed but still tight with strain: "Hi, Teacher Yuanyuan, I… I waited in line a long time…"
(Comments: [Oho, here we go!] [First brave soul] [Got my little stool and snacks ready])
Yao Yuan offered no small talk, her face set in an almost detached focus. After the caller, in a trembling voice, rushed through her tangled mess of a situation — boyfriend's verbal promise of a five-million childbirth bonus, living expenses cut in half after they moved in together, an unplanned pregnancy, the man's family proposing they register the marriage first in exchange for a million in bride-price — Yao Yuan was silent for two seconds. Then the questions probed in, precise as cold surgical forceps.
"What do you do for work?"
"…I'm a university teacher."
"What does the gentleman do?"
"He… he's the dean of our department, and he holds shares in a few companies of his own."
"How did you two meet?"
"At work, at the school…"
"What's his annual income?"
"Mine is around two hundred thousand or so. His — when the company dividends are good, a few million, but he says cash flow's been tight lately."
"How old are you?"
"Twenty-six."
"How old is he?"
"Thirty-six."
(The comments started to explode: [Holy crap! Ten years!] [The dean? Teacher-student?] [That's a lot of information] [Five million! A verbal promise?] [Smells like a trap])
When the girl mentioned she'd been a fan for three years, and choked up saying that early in his pursuit the man had spent close to a million on her, a faint, almost razor-sharp gleam crossed Yao Yuan's eyes, like a hunter who has finally confirmed the trail of her prey.
"Good." She let out the single word, her voice level, yet weighted as if it could crush. "First — before you moved in, he was able to put real resources into you. On that point, you didn't handle it badly. Remember: a man's emotional depth and loyalty tend to follow the cost he actually invests. Where the money is, that's where his attention, his sense of responsibility, and his sunk cost are likely to be too."
(Comments: [Truth!] [Where a man's money is, his heart is] [Provided the money's actually spent on you])
She leaned slightly forward, the red silk flowing with her, her body's lines drawn taut into an elegant, loaded bow. Under the camera's close-up, her extra-long crimson nails were like ten tempered miniature daggers, reflecting the cold light.
"But, girl," her pace suddenly quickened, every word like a nail trying to drive through the wall of the other woman's thinking, "ears up, mind sharp, listen carefully!"
(She raised a hand and clapped twice, crisply, the sound startling in the dead-quiet room — even the comments stalled for a beat.)
"First — your 'being so understanding' is a cheap, self-flattering gift, a knife you handed over with your own hands!"
"He says the monthly living allowance of twenty thousand is now ten, you took it, and you even 'understood' that his business is bad? With one stack of bills cut to half value, he bought out your bargaining power outright, and threw in a 'sensible girl' plaque for good measure! Is his bad business your doing? Does he have receivables out there he hasn't collected that you need to share the risk for? In his eyes, and his family's, your 'understanding' is no virtue at all — it's consent. Consent that from now on he can put a fifty-percent discount, or worse, on every promise, every agreement! And that little 'kindness' he later showed your family — what is that? It's the change handed back after the discount! It's the maintenance fee to keep this 'closed deal, appreciating asset' — you — stable! That first step of yours, your whole posture went down on its knees. Understand?"
(Comments: [Total wake-up call!] [Self-flattery is a disease, treat it!] [Begging on your knees and still saying thank you])
"Second — the only ace in your hand has an expiration date, and the hourglass is draining fast!"
"Twenty-six, university teacher, pregnant, and right now he wants this child — that's your one and only bargaining chip at this moment, and it's depreciating fast. His dean's title, his company shares, his few million in dividends — until they flow into your pocket in cold hard cash through a legally binding channel, they have nothing to do with you, not one cent! That's just a carrot dangling in front of your eyes, a mirage! Your belly is the negotiating table right now, and the months are the hourglass on it. Once the hourglass runs out and the baby is born, you won't even have the right to sit at the table! By then you're meat on the chopping block, there for anyone to carve up!"
(Comments: [Brutal but true] [The child is the ace and the weak spot] [Pregnancy is the golden negotiation window, sis!])
"Third — the most dangerous, most foolish notion in your head right now is letting him throw off your whole rhythm with a 'marriage' shell!"
"The moment he brings up marriage, offers a million in bride-price, you panic and forget all about that five-million childbirth bonus? This is a textbook 'break the big order, swap in a small one' negotiation tactic! Use a 'marriage' shell that sounds glamorous and carries huge social pressure to swallow up, blur out, and stall your five-million 'childbirth bonus' — clearly pointed, spelled out in black and white (even if only verbally) — into some vague, unprovable 'marital common property'! Let me ask you: once you're married, what is that five million? A pre-marriage gift? Marital common property? Or 'compensation you voluntarily waived'? How are you supposed to demand it then, openly, as its own separate item? 'Gold you'll get tomorrow is worth less than copper in hand' — take a branding iron and sear that line into the folds of your brain! Five million is gold, one million is copper. Don't hand over a gold mine just to hear a little copper bell jingle!"
(Comments: [She's going off!] [Break the big order, swap the small one! That's business thinking!] [Marriage is the shell, feelings are the wrapping])
Yao Yuan's pace grew faster and faster, her gaze sharp as a blade, as if she meant to perform psychic surgery on that girl right through the screen.
"Fourth — now, right now, immediately, stop burning yourself out and go execute!"
"1. Stop all wedding fantasies! The gown, the diamond ring, the vows, the marriage certificate — that's bonus animation for the happily-ever-after of a fairy tale, not the strategy guide you should be studying at this life-or-death moment! Your first goal, your only goal, is to dig out the dungeon's biggest treasure — that five million — before you get the bonus content, and get it safely, with legal recognition, into your own pocket! Everything else, step aside!"
"2. Learn to dangle the carrot for him, but only after you've swallowed your own steadying pill first! Go talk to him — posture soft, core made of steel. Say it like this: 'Honey, now that we have a baby, I feel our lives are more complete. I'm so looking forward to a future with the three of us. Before I marry you in style, with nothing left to worry about, I want to lock in the biggest security and blessing we can give our baby. For example, that five million — part of it set up as an irrevocable growth trust for the baby, part of it to settle my parents so they can fully relax and also see your dedication and your devotion. That way, when I marry you, it's truly the icing on the cake. Everyone will only envy me for marrying so well, marrying with such security. That makes me feel steadier, more dignified, than any bride-price.' — Understood? Use the carrot of 'a happy future marriage' to trade for the solid fact of 'cash in pocket now'! Package 'asking for money' as 'planning for the family's future, building you up as a good-man image'!"
"3. Concrete numbers, an ironclad path, a locked deadline! At the negotiating table, nothing is more taboo than 'roughly,' 'I'll try,' 'as soon as possible'! When you negotiate, negotiate down to concrete figures: how much into the trust (it must be irrevocable, the beneficiary clearly you or the child), how much is a pre-marriage cash gift to you, by what latest date and through what method it lands. While he most wants the child, while he may feel a touch of guilt over that earlier 'discount,' and while he's showing 'sincerity about marriage' — lock these terms down in a legally recognized form (agreement, notarization, transfer memos)! No money in the account, nothing else is on the table! Your pregnancy is the golden negotiation window, not his stalling tactic!"
(Comments: [Learned the script!] [Concrete path!] [Pregnancy is not a stalling tactic!] [This is the real substance!])
She drew a deep breath, as if that dense burst of output had drained no small amount of her energy too, but her eyes still blazed, piercing through the lens as if to burn away every wishful illusion:
"Girl, you keep saying you've been my fan for three years, that you've studied 'disenchantment,' that you know to grab the cold hard cash. But just now, two glossy-sounding words — 'feelings' and 'marriage' — nearly knocked you straight back to square one! My last gift to you, and to every sister in this room with your ears up: every marriage that avoids talking about how money is divided is a reservist for 'precision poverty relief'; every promise that can't be cashed out in full, on the spot, is a suspended sentence for emotional fraud!"
"Keep your head clear! The call-in time is up. For the specific operations, how to draft the agreement, how to build the script step by step — contact my assistant after the stream ends. We have a detailed 'pre-marriage asset negotiation' manual. Now, hang up. Go think. Go do it!"
[Line Disconnected]
"Beep—"
The tone signaling the call had cut off was short and clean. For a few seconds an eerie, vacuum-like silence filled the room, only the eternal low murmur of the Yellow River beyond the window. Then the comments and gift effects burst out like a lit powder keg, utterly drowning the screen.
Holy crap! I can't breathe! That logic just steamrolled everything!
This isn't an emotional streamer, this is the lead surgeon of the Clear-Eyed Operating Table!
Sis, you're killing me! Brutal, but every word hits the mark!
Already recorded, memorizing the whole thing, going to write it out ten times from memory before I marry!
That sister better wake up! Do what Teacher Yao said!
Sending a rocket! For Teacher's throat, give it a rest!
Still though… if you calculate like this, what's the point of feelings? (This one was quickly buried)
To the one ahead of me — feelings are fun, but being calculated out the door with nothing left is even more fun.
Yao Yuan didn't immediately look at the tumbling praise, the arguments, or the tips. She leaned slowly back into that expensive ergonomic chair, lifted a hand, and with one bright-red, sharp nail pressed lightly at her own temple. The small gesture leaked a trace of the fatigue that came after high-intensity, high-density output, but only for an instant before her habitual cold, penetrating expression covered that flicker of weariness over again.
Beyond the window the Yellow River still ran on in silence, the muddy-yellow water glinting with a heavy sheen in the afternoon sun. The studio lights lit her and her little corner of the world bright and crystalline, like a fine, dazzling, yet utterly lonely transparent square suspended above an endless chaos of dust.
She picked up the insulated cup on the desk and took a slow sip of water, the faint sound of her swallow caught clearly by the high-sensitivity microphone. Then she looked at the camera, and that smile resurfaced on her face — the one that controlled everything, with its faint professional distance — only deep in her eyes lay the same pool of calm, still water.
"Babies in the room," her voice was a touch gentler than when she'd handled the call, but the core was still hard as iron, "in the world of feelings, the deepest traps are usually carpeted with the flowers of 'warmth,' 'understanding,' and 'for your own good.' What you must train isn't to weep at the edge of the trap, but to learn, even as the flowers bloom, to calmly make out the trip-wire beneath the path, count how much leverage you still hold, and weigh the cost of leaping across."
"Kindness without teeth is an invitation for others to come and gnaw at you. A soft heart without boundaries is the breeding ground that feeds people who push for more and more."
"Remember, your worth has never been proven by 'how much pain you're willing to endure.' It's priced by 'how much respect and real, solid benefit you dare to demand.'"

========================================

Chapter 2 — Never Walk Away From a Table Empty-Handed, Least of All One You've Already Bet Expensive Chips On
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640249.html

One live call after another, Yao Yuan worked like a surgeon — fast, precise, ruthless — lancing one emotional abscess after the next.
……
[Stream status] Yao Yuan's gaze drifted, casual and unhurried, over the viewer count still climbing in the top-right corner. It had already broken a hundred thousand. She shifted slightly in her seat, letting the hem of her dark-red silk dress fall in a more elegant line. She had just taken a sip of water, and her full, freshly moistened red lips, under the cold white light, looked like plum blossoms bursting open against snow — a jolt to the eye. Her face wore a faint, almost weary kind of focus, as if through the cold lens and the surge of scrolling comments she could see the minute trembles and silent screams across countless spectrums of feeling. Outside the window, the Yellow River sent up its heavy, low, never-ceasing sound of flowing water, like the vast, silent heartbeat behind this glittering human world.
"All right," her voice rose again, steady and penetrating, pulling everyone back from the lingering aftermath of that intense surgery. "Let's connect to tonight's final caller. I hope your question is worth the time I'm about to give it."
[Call connected]
After a faint crackle of static, a young woman's voice came through, carrying an obvious sob she was struggling — and failing — to hold back, the tremble leaking out at the ends of her words like a string stretched to its breaking point:
"Teacher Yuanyuan…" (a clear sniffle) "I… I waited a long time, and I finally got through… I'm twenty-three, just graduated. My boyfriend… no, ex-boyfriend, we got together freshman year, four years ago. We… it was love at first sight, it really was, so, so beautiful. He's my first love, he's my… my whole youth."
(The comments began to shift: [A campus romance…][Four years of youth…][This hurts just to hear][Another mismatched-backgrounds story?])
Yao Yuan (offering no comfort, only a barely audible "Mm" to signal her to go on, her eyes calm and unmoved, as if listening to the opening of a story that had nothing to do with her): "And then? Graduation season is breakup season. Time to wake up from the dream?"
The caller (seeming stung by the blunt, almost cold phrasing, choking up harder): "Yes… His family is well-off, very well-off. His father is… a person of standing, and his mother's family has a strong background too. My family… we're just an ordinary rural household, my parents are honest, simple farmers. Compared to his family, the gap is enormous. We always knew that, but we kept thinking… love conquers everything. Then we graduated, and reality just… His family openly objected. They lined up a proper match for him, froze his money, even… put enormous pressure on him. He… he started to waver. He says he loves me, but he can't ignore his parents, can't… let his family down. He says he needs time, that his parents will come around slowly."
Yao Yuan (that vivid splash of red on her fingertips tapping the desk, lightly, on and off, with a steady, almost hypnotic "tap, tap"): "So we're mid-breakup right now? Did he say it outright, or is he just dragging it out, keeping it vague?"
The caller (finally breaking, the suppressed crying spilling out): "He hasn't said breakup… but he isn't fighting it either. He says he needs time, that his parents will come around. But his mother came to me privately, and she made herself very clear, very ugly about it… said I'm not good enough for him, that she hopes I'll be sensible and leave on my own. Teacher Yuanyuan, I don't understand… four years together, all those shared memories, all those plans… is it really worth nothing just because my family doesn't have his money and power? I'm in so much pain, I can't eat, I can't sleep, I feel like my whole world has collapsed. I… what should I do? Should I wait for him? Or… should I really be 'sensible,' and slink away?"
(The comments turned tangled, an argument flaring up: [Class snobbery really does cut deep][The boy isn't firm enough, what does four years even mean][Wake up, girl, his family flat-out looks down on you][Four years of youth thrown to the dogs?][Love really is fragile in the face of reality][Maybe the boy has his own hardships too?])
Yao Yuan listened quietly to the girl's broken sobbing and her story, saying nothing right away. She even tilted her head slightly, turning her gaze to the muddy, surging yellow beyond the window, as if listening to that eternal sound of water, or perhaps just giving herself a moment's buffer. Only when the crying eased into hiccupping sniffles did she slowly turn back, her eyes returning to the lens. There was no flood of pity in that gaze, only the utterly composed scrutiny of a scalpel about to come down.
"Girl, wipe your tears first." Her voice wasn't loud, but it carried a strange power that could press down on panic. "If tears could wash away class barriers, there'd be no more tragedies in this world. Now, sit up straight, blow away those romantic bubbles in your head, and listen to me."
"First — stop moving yourself with that fairy-tale line, 'love conquers everything.'"
"That line is the best-selling and most poisonous bit of comfort-food wisdom in the sterile environment of campus romance. Inside the vacuum bell of the ivory tower, 'love' is the only subject of the sentence, and it looks all-powerful. But the moment you step out the gate, 'everything' becomes the subject. And that 'everything' includes the wealth, connections, social standing, and face his family has stacked up over decades — generations, even; it includes his parents' design for his life and their grip on it; it includes the 'family enterprise' he'll have to inherit, maintain, and even expand over the coming decades. Your love, your four years, you yourself, and that family behind you that needs his 'poverty relief' — set against all of that 'everything' he has to face, the weight classes just aren't close. It isn't that he doesn't love you; maybe he's in pain right now too. But when the weight called 'love' is too light to budge the colossus called 'reality,' his choice — given the family upbringing he absorbed and the web of interests he sits inside — is all but a foregone conclusion. He isn't the male lead of the story. There's no script in him about fighting the whole world for you."
(Comments: [Right in the heart, fam][Reality really is a colossus][Love is just an egg against an interest bloc])
The caller (sobbing, arguing back, unwilling): "But our four years together, that depth of feeling, all those little moments…"
Yao Yuan (cutting in coolly, in a tone that brooked no argument): "Second — those four years of 'deep feeling' are a stretch of youth the two of you invested in together. They are not collateral you deposited with him alone, to be redeemed with principal and interest on demand."
"Your pain comes from believing that your four-year investment — time, emotion, your body, your plans for the future — ought to yield a return, ought to deliver the ending of 'happily ever after.' But the most basic principle in economics tells us: investment doesn't guarantee a return, least of all emotional investment. Youth, especially, is a sunk cost — it can't hold its value, and it can't be recovered. Over those four years you gave him your purest stirrings of the heart, your companionship, your emotional value, and he gave you the same in return. It was a voluntary exchange on both sides, a span of time spent together. It's just that, four years on, when this 'love joint venture' faces liquidation, the split of returns has come out wildly unequal. The belief that he 'owes' you a future — that line of thinking does nothing but keep you marinating in victim's pain and torturing yourself. For your actual situation right now, it offers zero constructive help."
Yao Yuan (leaning slightly forward, her tone hardening, every word landing like a hammer on the heart): "Third — and this is where it hurts most, the root you can't accept right now — it isn't your family's poverty, and it isn't even his family's objection. It's that in this relationship, you've completely lost your 'right to interpret' and your 'sense of control.'"
"In the closed environment of campus, your relationship was defined by 'love,' and you were equal lovers. But now his family has picked up the measuring stick called 'class' and redefined you — you've become the one who's 'reaching above her station,' an 'object' to be examined, picked apart, weighed for value, and finally decided over: stay or go. And your boyfriend's silence, his wavering, his refusal to fight — that is him handing you over with his own hands, laying you before his family as a sacrifice in the name of 'love.' He has tacitly endorsed his family's definition of you and of your relationship. That is the sharpest knife of all: you've not only been rejected by a family you valued, you've also, in a sense, had that rejection tacitly endorsed — in front of his family's values — by the very person you loved for four years and treated as the faith of your youth. You've lost the right to define your own worth, and you've lost any sense of control over where this relationship is headed."
(Comments: [Right to interpret! Sense of control! Devastating!][So the pain is in being defined, being judged][The boy's silence makes him an accomplice])
The caller (seeming stunned by this cold, precise analysis, the crying stopping, leaving only a dazed murmur): "Then… then what can I even do? I… I can't just accept this…"
Yao Yuan (her tone resolute, with an almost cruel clarity): "Stop asking 'will he choose me.' Swap out the subject, and start thinking: 'Do I want him — and is he, and the family he represents, even worth wanting anymore.'"
"Now, bundle him and that family of his together for the moment, and see them as a single whole. Then strip away all the resentment and the filters, and ask yourself a few questions. Even if he ultimately defies his family and chooses you, could you step calmly into an environment that belittled you and looked down on your origins from the very start? Could you bear the decades of possible hidden discrimination, the imbalance of resources, the clashes of worldview, and the 'original sin' his family could dredge up at any time to pressure you — or your future children? When that initial passion of 'fighting the world together' has been ground flat by the rice-and-salt of a long marriage and the endless trivia of family life, could you still keep your dignity, your joy, and your sense of self-worth, always, inside this 'unequal' relationship? If the answer to all of those is no, or even hesitation — then does it really matter what he chooses?"
She paused, her voice slowing, which only made the cruelty of someone who saw through the world more apparent:
"What's more, judging by his current stalling stance — 'doesn't initiate, doesn't refuse, doesn't take responsibility' — he doesn't even have the will or the concrete action to openly resist, to fight for you. He's just waiting. Waiting for you to burn through your patience and leave on your own, or waiting for his family to arrange everything so he can 'reluctantly' accept it. Either way, girl, you are the option being weighed, compared, and finally discarded. You're still waiting for him to give you an answer — and his silence is already the answer."
(Comments: [Silence is already the answer][Doesn't initiate, doesn't refuse, doesn't take responsibility — the classic trash-guy three-nots][Run, girl!])
The caller (her voice hollow, edged with despair): "So… there's nothing I can do but resign myself and leave?"
Yao Yuan (suddenly letting out a soft little laugh, a laugh with no warmth in it, only a sharpness that cut through the fog): "Resign yourself and leave? And then what? Go back to your starting line — further back, even — with a broken heart, two empty hands, and four years of 'sunk cost'? Girl, you're a listener in my stream. Even if you were only passing through, you should have heard my basic rule — never walk away from a table empty-handed, least of all one you've already bet expensive chips on."
"What you need to do now is not cry and resign yourself. It's to calm down, and like the shrewdest of merchants, take stock of your 'investment' — and then find a way, before you leave the table, to 'cash out' as much as possible of the 'core technology' and 'potential returns' you can take with you. That is the most basic responsibility you owe to your four years of youth, and the most effective painkiller for the pain you're in right now."
(Comments: [???][Cash out?][Damn, this angle!][Teacher Yao is about to unleash it!])
The caller (completely stunned): "Cash… cash out? But we… we're talking about feelings…"
Yao Yuan (eyes sharp as a blade, her tone resolute): "Yes. Cash out. You think his family simply looks down on you and wants to break you two up? Wrong. They're using the high-sounding excuse of 'class' to carry out a full-spectrum 'denial of your value' and 'emotional plunder' against you. They want you to feel ashamed of yourself, to be 'sensible' and exit for free — and ideally to feel grateful for their son's 'no choice in the matter.' And you — you're actually still tangled up in romance, thinking about how to leave gracefully and with nothing? Your four best years, your purest emotional investment, just thrown away to feed the… you're planning to write it all off like that, with a possible years-long bonus of heartbreak and self-doubt thrown in? That is the greatest betrayal of my stream's principle: 'real money, never empty-handed!'"
(Comments: [Denial of value! Emotional plunder!][Right on the nail!][So you can't be sacrificed for nothing!])
Yao Yuan (speaking faster now, her logic cold and crystal-clear, as if laying out a precise military campaign): "Listen carefully. What you need to do now is not to win him back, and not to sit there crying and pitying yourself. It's to use this last window of time and whatever guilt he may have left, and convert as much of your 'sunk cost' as possible — legally and reasonably — into 'hard capital' that can support your future life. This is the 'youth depreciation compensation' you're owed. It's the 'consideration' that he, and that noble family of his, ought to pay you for these four years of 'emotional companionship' and 'lost future opportunity.'"
"Step one — a total shift in mindset: from 'abandoned, pitiable lover' to 'business partner whose project has ended, here to settle the books.'"
"Stop all the self-moving and emotional drain immediately. Redefine your four-year relationship as a 'joint venture' about to be wound down. You invested technology — your 'youth,' your 'emotional value,' your 'future possibilities.' He invested capital — his 'family background,' his 'future potential.' Now the capital side, citing a 'strategic adjustment' (class), wants to unilaterally pull its funds and tries to deny the value of your tech equity. Your task is not to kneel and beg to keep the partnership going. It's to calmly calculate the depreciated value of your technology, and demand reasonable compensation for your exit."
"Step two — negotiation prep: draw up your 'list of convertible resources and losses.'"
"1. Discounting your emotional value: over four years, you provided intimate companionship, emotional support, academic encouragement, care in daily life — possibly even hidden support for him socially and psychologically. When things were going his way, that was 'priceless love'; at the end of the relationship, it's 'emotional labor' with market value. Reference the hourly rate of high-end psychological counseling or private emotional companionship, multiply by a reasonable number of hours, apply a reasonable discount. This is the 'service compensation' you're owed."
"2. Settling opportunity cost: because you were deeply tied to him for these four years, did you turn down other possible paths of growth? A better choice of city for work (stay in your hometown or go to a big city), potential — perhaps more suitable — suitors, even time to develop your own skills? Especially now, fresh out of school, you're in the golden launch phase of your career and your life; the loss of this 'window of opportunity' must be counted in. Your youth has a price; your right to choose has an even higher one."
"3. The premium on a 'graceful exit': this is your most important bargaining chip. You leave 'sensibly,' quietly, no fuss, no scene, preserving the 'face' and 'reputation' that he and his family prize above all — especially for a man of standing like his father — sparing them any potential PR risk or family upheaval. To people who care about appearances, this 'service' is worth a great deal. You need to make him and his family understand that your 'sensibleness' and your 'silence' have a price — and not a cheap one."
(Comments: [The list! Opportunity cost!][A graceful exit is a bargaining chip!][Learned it, going to draft my list right now])
The caller (her voice shaking a little, but seeming drawn in by this new, cold-blooded logic): "But… how do I even negotiate this? It's so… so transactional, like making a deal…"
Yao Yuan (a humorless curve lifting the corner of her lips): "Transactional? When his family was beating you with the reality-stick of 'class,' did they talk feelings with you? His wavering right now — isn't that exactly him subconsciously calculating which is bigger, the 'real cost of defying his family' or the 'emotional loss of losing you'? He, and his family, have long been thinking and acting in the logic of reality. You're still answering with the logic of campus romance. If you don't lose, who does?"
"Step three — the concrete move: hand him a 'settlement plan' for the 'debt of feeling and opportunity,' instead of weeping at him."
"Pick a suitable moment, and go see him calmly — even with a trace of weariness and distance (but not resentment). Don't cry, don't make a scene, don't pour out your grievances. In a calm, matter-of-fact tone, say something like this: 'XX, I'm sorry it's come to this after our four years together. I gave all the sincerity and time I had to give — that's a fact. I accept this outcome now, and I understand your pressure and your difficulty. But what I gave, and the losses I've taken on because of it — emotional and in terms of future opportunity — are real. I don't want us to end as a bitter couple, and I don't want to make things hard for you. But I think a clean parting needs more than emotional closure; it needs a clear accounting in practical terms too — to help me transition smoothly, and to give these four years of ours a responsible ending.'"
"Then, lay out your 'plan' — note, a 'plan,' not a 'plea':"
"Option A (best — resource swap): isn't he the one with the comfortable background, the abundant resources? Have him use real resources and connections to compensate your intangible 'losses' and your future uncertainty. For example: 'I know your family's situation, and I know this is hard on you. I don't want to drag it out and embarrass us both. If you sincerely feel you owe me, and you genuinely want me to be able to start a new life, could you — or your family — do me a favor at the right time? For instance, introduce me to XX (a platform within his family's sphere of influence that would meaningfully elevate your career)? Or write a recommendation letter with real weight for me to pursue further study at XX school, and provide a guarantee if needed? This matters enormously to my future, and it would let you set your mind at ease — call it the most practical accounting you do for me, and for these four years of ours.' — Package your 'leaving' as a 'career leap' or 'credential upgrade' that requires his family's help to pull off, neatly transforming the emotional debt into an investment of favor or resources. For them it's a small effort; for you it's a shot at changing your fate."
"Option B (second best — financial compensation): 'If pulling family strings is inconvenient, then could you give me some real support through the transition while I look for work and settle into a new city? For instance, a sum to cover my living, study, or a small business attempt for a while — startup money (with a specific amount, reasonably estimated against his means and your "losses," say three to five hundred thousand) — or help me with the down payment on a small apartment? Give me a small foothold in this city to stand on, so I have the confidence to start over. That would let me truly let go, and call it a clean, clear ending between us.' — Put a clear price on your 'youth loss' and 'opportunity cost' (it doesn't sound pretty, but it's direct and effective), and turn it into a 'resettlement fee' or 'fresh-start fund.'"
(Comments: [Specific scripts!][Resource swap! This angle is genius!][Financial comp needs a concrete number too])
"Core point: your tone and posture must be 'seeking a clear ending and help that's good for both sides,' 'for the sake of us each being truly all right' — not 'emotional blackmail' or 'burning it all down.' Fully leverage whatever guilt he may have left, and his family's mindset of 'spend money to avoid trouble,' 'pay for peace and quiet,' and protect their face. The calmer, the more graceful, the more 'considerate of him' you are in raising these practical demands, the likelier he is to agree — and even to persuade his family to help. Because for him and his family, this is using a controllable, one-time cost (a sum of money or a favor) to resolve a potential headache that could otherwise keep generating emotional turbulence — and might even spiral into an uncontrollable risk (you, turning love to hate and doing something reckless). For them, that's a very cost-effective 'risk-buyout' deal."
Yao Yuan (finally, fixing her eyes on the lens, word by word, as if to carve these lines into everyone's heart): "Girl, my stream doesn't teach people to daydream, and it doesn't hand out illusory emotional anesthetics. I only teach people to stay clear-headed amid reality's wreckage — to crouch down, dig the still-usable bricks and tiles out of the rubble, and build themselves the next place to shelter, even if it's small at first. Love can die suddenly. But the person you loved should become a ladder or fuel for your growth — not a lingering poison. Either let him be a springboard to a higher platform, a wider world (resources); or let him provide real, tangible startup fuel for the next leg of your life (money). That is the fullest possible responsibility you can take for those four years of youth you gave without reserve. And it's the most elegant — and loudest — slap to that lofty 'class' worldview of his family: they used class to deny your worth, so you use the very rules and logic they know to secure yourself real, tangible 'future capital' they can never strip away again."
"Done crying? Wipe the tears. Then rein in the flood of romance in your head too. Go do it. Go negotiate. Go fight for it. If you walk away with nothing real in hand, then your tears, your pain, your four years of sincerity — those are the things that are truly worth nothing, thrown away to feed the…"
She paused, leaving the word unsaid, though the meaning was already clear.
[Call time nearly up]
Yao Yuan looked into the lens, her gaze seeming to pass through the screen and land on that heartbroken, lost young woman. For an instant, she thought she saw her own twenty-four-year-old self in the girl's face — those old pains about the gap, the betrayal, the unwillingness, the ones that had once seared her heart in the dead of night, long since tempered by the years into the cold reason and sharp weapon now in her eyes.
"Last, a word for you — and for every girl in this stream who might face a 'gap.'" Her voice dropped lower, carrying a strange echo, as if from somewhere far away. "Some bridges were never built for everyone to walk across side by side. Rather than stand at the bridgehead begging for a permit to cross, reading someone's face, turn around and build a boat of your own. Or, more thoroughly still — go find a plain, open and broad, where you can run as you please without crossing any bridge at all. Your world hasn't collapsed. It just needs to be rebuilt — and this time, the cornerstone must be you: complete, independent, and strong."
"Use these four years of sweetness and pain to give yourself that expensive lesson on 'the reality of love.' Then, carrying the understanding you bought with blood and tears, take leave of this station and walk into a more complex, broader, real life. The meaning of youth was never to bind yourself to one person. It's to throw yourself fully into the experience — and then, with the strength, wisdom, and clarity that experience gives you, head toward the next stretch of mountains and rivers, more resolute and more at ease."
[Call disconnected]
"Beep—"
The disconnect tone sounded. This time, the comments didn't explode at once; they seemed still immersed in that cold, naked, yet immensely forceful 'lesson in cashing out.' Only after a few seconds did they suddenly surge.
[…I'm dumbstruck.]
[It sounds a little… but somehow it makes so much sense, what's going on?]
[This is what real female independence looks like! Never walk away from any table empty-handed!]
[Quantifying emotional harm and turning it into growth capital… brutal, but so practical]
[To the people up top calling it scheming — so getting four years of youth leeched for free is noble?]
[Is Teacher Yao teaching people to go dark? But I love it!]
[Recorded it, memorizing the whole thing, just in case.]
[This lesson is worth a million…]
Yao Yuan didn't look at the churning comments anymore. She calmly announced: "All right, tonight's 'surgery' ends here. I hope every darling struggling in the river of love finally climbs ashore clear-eyed, holding chips of real gold and silver in your hands — not a fistful of useless mud, swollen and waterlogged with tears."
Then she leaned back into her chair, a trace of weariness surfacing on her face, faint, almost invisible — only to be covered over in an instant by her professional shine. She arched an eyebrow at the main camera. It was her signature move, carrying a touch of disdain and command.
"That whole stretch just now on 'cutting losses and cashing out' — the complete logic and the scripts — every woman in this stream between twenty-two and twenty-eight, screenshot it and send it to the backend of the fan group. My assistant will tidy it up later into a detailed 'emotional-project loss-cutting checklist' and 'analysis of key negotiation points,' as this week's perk for fans."
"All right, that's the end of today's stream. Anyone who needs deeper case analysis or specific strategic guidance, contact my little assistant to book a paid consultation. Next Monday at eleven a.m., we'll…"
She paused deliberately, her red lips curving into a precise, flawless smile fit to be frozen on a promotional poster:
"Be there. Have a great weekend, my darlings."
A light tap of her finger on the screen, and the live signal cut out.
"Click."
After the faint electronic sound, the world seemed to have a layer of clamor drained out of it, falling abruptly quiet. All that remained was the low, eternal flow of the Yellow River outside the window, and the faint hum of the cooling fans in the equipment.
The carefully calibrated expression on Yao Yuan's face — sharp, striking, forever 'in state' — went dark in an instant, like a screen losing power, leaving only a blank slackness. She raised a hand and rubbed hard at both temples, her fingertips icy, pressing at the dull, throbbing ache pulsing there beat by beat.
"That last bit about the 'checklist' and the 'resource swap,' the concrete moves — cut it out, flag the key points, and put it in the paid course 'Breaking the Impasse, Chapter Three: How to Extract Residual Value From a Draining Relationship,' as the core case study." She didn't turn around, her voice carrying the particular dryness and rasp of high-intensity output, giving the order to the air.
"Got it, Sister Yuan." The little assistant's voice came from behind the console, fingers tapping the keyboard, taking it down. "That girl… can she really do what you said? She seems pretty soft…"
Yao Yuan picked up the half-glass of water on the desk, gone cold now, and took a sip, soothing her burning throat. Then, at her own blurry reflection on the darkened phone screen, she gave the faintest tug at the corner of her mouth — a smile with no warmth in it, only the detachment of someone who sees through everything.
"Anyone who can make this call, wait in this queue, pass through all those streams full of 'Teacher, you're so gentle' and 'Sister, let me hug you,' and find her way to me…" she said slowly and deliberately, her voice flat and unrippled, "what she wants has never been cheap comfort, or a good cry together. What she wants is a weapon, a map, a blade to hack a way out of a dead end. I gave her the blade, sharpened it, pointed the way. As for whether she has the nerve to pick it up, the strength to swing it…"
She paused, and downed the rest of the water in one go.
"That's her business."
"Numbers." She set the glass down, not turning around, her voice back to its everyday brevity.
"Peak viewers broke the record again, Sister — holding steady above a hundred and ten thousand. The top five on the tip board are all old fans, but new sign-ups and DM-adds shot up; the backend froze several times. The topic's already gaining traction." The little assistant's voice carried a suppressed excitement, her fingers switching between several screens, monitoring the data streams.
Yao Yuan let out an "Mm" and asked nothing more. With one hand bracing the small of her back — sore and swollen to near numbness — and the other on the cold desktop, she pushed herself up, slowly and with some effort, from that pricey, ergonomic chair that still left her spine rigid as an iron plate after four hours. For four hours she'd been like a nail pinned in front of the lens, every inch of muscle, from the levators of her cheekbones to her ankles, held taut in 'best state.' Shoulders and back straight, neckline elegant — even the angle her lips lifted when she smiled was precisely controlled by muscle memory. Only she knew the needle-prick soreness running up from the base of her spine, and the dull, swelling pain behind her temples that ballooned with every heartbeat.
She walked unsteadily, half-drained of strength, over to the broad sofa on the other side of the stream room, draped in a soft cream cashmere throw. Then, like a sand tower with its frame suddenly pulled out, like a beast that had spent every ounce of strength out on the open plain and finally trudged back to its den, she collapsed — utterly, without a shred of poise — into the soft fabric.
The instant her body sank into the sofa, she nearly let out a satisfied, faint little sigh. The expensive cashmere gently wrapped every inch of muscle and bone that had been clenched tight and was now completely loose. She closed her eyes; the world did not go still at once. Her ears still held the faint electric hum left behind by the high-fidelity headphones, and the fragments of countless voices — the caller's tearful hesitation and despair, the words tearing across the public screen in fervent agreement or savage curses, her own voice, dressed up by a top-grade sound card into something magnetic and undeniably penetrating… the afterimages of these sounds still echoed inside her skull, colliding, slowly dissolving.
Outsiders only saw the 'Yao Yuan' in front of the lens — makeup exquisite and flawless, red lips razor-edged, words like knives, logic tight as a precision instrument, lancing one emotional abscess after another fast and precise and ruthless, dispensing cold and seemingly effective 'solutions.' They either envied her traffic and influence, or debated her sharpness and controversy, or copied her 'clear-eyed' one-liners, or cursed her for 'objectifying feelings' and 'peddling anxiety.' They thought she merely flapped her lips, peddling some 'love success-ology' or 'emotional dark arts,' and raked it in — living in a mansion, driving a good car, basking in adoration.
No one saw that before every stream she needed at least two hours of absolute silence and solitude, emptying all the tangled thoughts of daily life, like a warrior about to take the field wiping down armor and blade — honing and tuning her chain of logic, her stock of scripts, her emotional valves and mental state to the sharpest, steadiest, 'drawn-from-the-sheath' condition. Through the four hours of the stream, her brain had to run like a top-tier processor in overclock, multithreaded and parallel: instantly catching the real demands and deep fears behind a caller's words; monitoring in real time the latent drift of opinion on the public screen, ready at any moment to steer or suppress it; precisely controlling the rhythm of her every line, its emotional density, and the various readings and legal-moral risks it might trigger; and at the same time weaving every shard of information seamlessly into her self-consistent, forceful, and highly shareable value framework of 'quantifying emotional interest.' She had to keep absolute reason and control at all times, yet at the crucial moment release, with precision, a thread of almost cruel tenderness that was 'for your own good'; she had to give advice that was resolute and seemingly irrefutable, while leaving herself, in every single line, enough room to maneuver and reinterpret on the legal and moral plane. Every minute was a high-intensity, high-density burning of mental force.
For four hours she had to hold that 'breath' the entire time — the 'professional breath' that made the eyes blaze, made the voice cohere, gave every line an undeniable weight and incitement. That breath could not scatter. If it scattered, the whole thing collapsed, and that invincible, forever-clear-eyed virtual image called 'Yao Yuan' would show its cracks.
The little assistant came over softly and set a cup of honey water at just the right temperature on the low table beside the sofa. "Sister, you worked hard. That last bit on 'cashing out' — the comments are going wild, calling you the 'ceiling of clear-headedness.' There are some curses too… but the heat is exploding."
Yao Yuan didn't open her eyes, only let out a barely audible "Mm" from her nose by way of reply. The ceiling? That was nothing but onlookers, standing at a safe distance, voicing their bloodthirsty, excited admiration at a brilliant and brutal 'emotional surgery.' She rubbed at her still-tight brow, fingertips icy. Success? Glamour? Yes — these days she had a luxury car with a driver waiting when she went out, lived in a penthouse overlooking half the city's river view, and could send tens of thousands of people into thought or argument with a single line. But beneath this seemingly 'effortless' success was her day-after-day chewing over and dissecting the most subtle, most unbearable side of human nature; her sustained tolerance and digestion of a flood of negative emotion and extreme cases; and, once the stream was off — like now — a soul-deep exhaustion that made lifting even a single finger feel like spending the last of her strength, along with an unseen, sediment-laden emptiness she had to swallow down alone.
The little assistant thoughtfully shut off all the harsh photography lights in the stream room, leaving only a soft-lit floor lamp in the corner. The afternoon sun, filtered through the large glass window, turned tame and languid, slanting into the room and warmly enveloping the body on the sofa — that body that had, for the moment, laid down all its armor and its halo, looking unusually soft and worn.
Glamour was her armor — and her cocoon.
And that Yellow River outside the window, forever rushing on, muddy and silent; that sediment that clung to her soles with every upward step she climbed, and had perhaps, by now, quietly seeped into her very blood and bones — that was the real, heavy ground tone beneath this dazzling stage.

========================================

Chapter 3 — A Tiger Brought Low Is Bullied by Dogs
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640250.html

In the living room, a woman's voice drifted out of a phone—thin and cold, like silk soaked in ice water. It was wistful, resentful, faintly mocking, and carried a weary, deathly chill:
"This upside-down world, how absurd it is
A kind soul never comes to a good end
This profit-chasing world is hard to fathom
You can pluck a sky full of stars and not trade them for a single candy
Different mouths telling the same lies
Different beasts trapped in the same skins
One hand doing every evil deed, smug and proud
The other pressed in prayer, begging the gods for pardon
□□ dress up in gold, all righteousness as they sit in high halls
The pheasant calls itself a phoenix, appearing only after a thousand summons
The fox turns merchant for a day, and the ants lose home and life
The lynx trusts the jackal—one heart traded for a body of wounds
The camel carries its pack, wandering lifetime after lifetime
The old dog dares not bark, sleepless and tireless for a mouthful of broth
The migrant bird through cold and heat can find no wall to block the wind
The bee, neither grasping nor fighting, stores up grain for others in the end
Different mouths telling the same lies
Different beasts trapped in the same skins
One hand doing every evil deed, smug and proud
The other pressed in prayer, begging the gods for pardon"
It was inside this song that Shuai Hongqiang jolted awake.
The afternoon sun, filtered through double-paned glass, had gone lukewarm and feeble, sprawling lazily across the living-room floor. He lay on the sofa, a thin cashmere throw over him. Shuai Hongqiang hadn't gone back to the bedroom; the master bed was too big, too quiet—quiet enough to make a man's heart race. The sofa was better. The space was cramped, but it gave him the illusion of being wrapped up, held.
He'd thought he couldn't fall asleep. His eyelids were heavy, but his mind was strangely clear, his nerves drawn taut every second, like a frightened stray dog with its ears always pricked, watching for the slightest stir outside. Inside, the sunlight carried visible motes of dust, turning slowly before his eyes, rising and falling. He stared until his eyes went dry, and at last his consciousness was dragged into a gray-white murk of fog.
He dreamed he was running across a vast, unfinished construction site. It was the sports-and-culture center he had taken on a few years back, its blueprints grand and sweeping. But there were no workers on the site, no machinery—only countless sheets of white A4 paper, sent whirling by the wind across the sky, like a silent blizzard. He fought desperately to grab a few, to make out the words on them, but the moment his fingertips touched them, they crumbled to ash. In the distance, where the building should have risen, there was only an enormous, bottomless foundation pit, like a jeering mouth.
He cried out and snapped his eyes open. A dream. Once awake, his temple throbbed with a stabbing, pulsing pain.
The song was still going in the living room, line after line, each one pricking into his eardrums like a needle:
"……□□ dress up in gold, all righteousness as they sit in high halls……The old dog dares not bark, sleepless and tireless for a mouthful of broth……"
"Damn, that's vicious." Shuai Hongqiang stared at the minimalist ceiling lamp overhead and gave a soundless, twisted little grin, his throat dry and aching. These lyrics—not a single line mentioned the human heart, yet every line was the human heart. Vicious as a boning knife, scraping away every last scrap of what lay beneath a person's skin. Back in his glory days, when he listened to music he listened only to the fun of it, never really caring what the words said. Now the lyrics had sunk into him, and every word felt like a little hammer striking his heart, dull and echoing.
Shuai Hongqiang steadied himself, got up, and poured a cup of cold tea, draining it in one go. Only then did the heavy, muddled exhaustion in his body start to ebb away.
"Ring-a-ling—ring-a-ling—"
The phone rang right then. Not his usual ringtone, but the most basic, most piercing default alert, jarring in the silent living room, like an alarm.
Shuai Hongqiang's hand jerked, and he nearly dropped the purple-clay teacup. He stared at the string of numbers on the screen—no contact name, but the location read "Langfang, Hebei." The one before had been "Foshan, Guangdong"; the one before that, "Chengdu, Sichuan." The collection agency's trick: virtual numbers, calling from every corner of the country, running you ragged, keeping your nerves on edge every second.
He drew in a deep breath. The breath went in and settled, heavy, in his lungs. He answered.
"Hello." His voice was a little hoarse, but steady enough.
"Good afternoon. Am I speaking with Mr. Shuai Hongqiang?" It was a young man's voice, standard Mandarin, the enunciation so crisp it sounded trained—almost too clear. In the background were the faint clatter of keyboards and other voices speaking just as fast, just as flat, like some cold-blooded chorus.
"Speaking. Who's this?" Shuai Hongqiang walked to the window and looked down at the street below.
"Good afternoon, Mr. Shuai. I'm a collections specialist with Zhongxin Asset Management Co., Ltd., employee number K307, surname Wu." The man sounded like he was reading from a notice. "Acting under commission from Bank of Communications Co., Ltd., I'm contacting you regarding the eight million yuan in principal owed by your affiliated enterprise 'Hongchang Real Estate Co., Ltd.,' along with the overdue penalty interest and breach penalties accrued to date. May I ask whether you have a concrete repayment plan today?"
Here we go again. Shuai Hongqiang shut his eyes, his fingers unconsciously tightening on the window frame. "Specialist Wu, I've said it many times—it's not that I won't pay. It's that the city government still owes me fifteen million yuan in project payments, and they keep dragging it out! If that money comes back, I can settle your debt immediately!"
"Mr. Shuai, your dispute with a third party—the city government—has nothing to do with the debt my company has been commissioned to collect." The man's tone was utterly flat, even carrying a touch of programmed impatience. "According to our system records, you have used this excuse to put us off many times. The bank's patience, and ours, is limited. You are currently in serious default."
"Put you off?" A surge of anger shot up, and Shuai Hongqiang raised his voice. "That's a fact! There's a contract in black and white! They won't give me my money—what am I supposed to pay with? If you're so capable, go get the government's debt back for me! Get it back and I'll settle the whole thing, principal and interest, in one shot!"
The other end fell silent for two seconds. When the young man spoke again, his voice had dropped a little, the background noise seemingly deliberately muted, every word landing like a bead of ice, crisp and clear: "Mr. Shuai, please stay calm. My company operates lawfully and within the rules. You have two choices right now. First: within three business days, come to our office and sign an installment repayment commitment, with a first payment of no less than five percent of the total debt—that is, four hundred thousand yuan. Second: if you refuse or are unable to comply, tomorrow we will formally submit materials to the people's court with jurisdiction, applying for measures to restrict your high consumption, and applying to freeze all bank accounts in the names of you, your spouse, and your children, along with WeChat, Alipay, and other third-party payment channels."
Shuai Hongqiang felt a rush of hot blood slam into the top of his skull. "So what do you want? Hound me to death—will that make money appear?"
The other end was silent again for two seconds, and then the young man's voice dropped a little, the keyboard clatter in the background seeming to recede as well, and he went on in that tone, steady to the point of cruelty: "Mr. Shuai, we're only following procedure. Also, a friendly reminder—" he paused, as if weighing his words, but the threat in them spilled out, naked. "We've noticed you have a son and a daughter. Your daughter, Shuai Wenrui, will sit the college entrance exam next year, and her grades are excellent. Certain special institutions—military, public security academies, some classified majors at key universities—are extremely strict in the political-review stage about the credit records and enforcement status of immediate family members. For your child's future, you'd do well to consider this carefully."
"Don't you dare!" Shuai Hongqiang growled, the veins at his temple jumping.
The blood in his whole body seemed to rush to his head in an instant, then freeze the next second. Wenrui… his daughter with his ex-wife, Qiu Ying. Shuai Wenrui's quiet, stubborn eyes flashed through his mind; even as a child she'd never given him much to worry about. The college exam… the political review…
"You… you're threatening me!" He forced a few more words out through clenched teeth.
"Mr. Shuai, this is a risk advisory, not a threat." The man's voice returned to that professional calm.
"Fine, fine… you're really something," he heard his own voice shaking, whether from rage or fear he couldn't tell. "Go get the fifteen million in project payments the city government owes me! Once that's back, I'll pay off everything in one shot, principal and interest! And I'll personally throw in fifty thousand for your trouble! How about that? Huh?!"
From the other end came a very faint, almost inaudible snort of a laugh—or maybe he only imagined it. "I'm sorry, Mr. Shuai. A debt is a debt; a dispute is a dispute. We all go by the rules, and we handle only the lawful claims we're commissioned to collect. Please give me a clear answer today."
"I'll answer your mother!" Months of pent-up rage, humiliation, and despair detonated in that instant. Shuai Hongqiang roared into the receiver, then jabbed the call dead.
The phone screen went dark, reflecting his own face, slightly distorted with anger. His chest heaved violently, like a fish out of water.
A few seconds later, the ringtone exploded again, another unfamiliar number flashing on the screen, location "Liuzhou, Guangxi."
Shuai Hongqiang didn't even look. His finger swiped across the screen, blocking it, the motion so practiced it was heartbreaking. In the past six months, he'd blocked more numbers than the contacts he'd saved up over the past few decades.
The living room sank back into dead silence. Only that long-finished song lingered, leaving behind an empty stretch of background sound. "……This upside-down world, how absurd it is……A kind soul never comes to a good end……This profit-chasing world is hard to fathom……"
Outside, the sunlight blazed on the glass curtain wall of a distant high-rise, glittering gold, sharp enough to make his eyes sting.
A tiger brought low is bullied by dogs.
The phrase leaped into his head without warning. What welled up after it was something more tangled, more viscous—rage, humiliation, and a thread of icy panic.
Shuai Hongqiang thought, with a self-mocking edge: A tiger? Was he even worthy of that? A real tiger, even brought low onto the plain, kept some of its old menace, claws that could still tear off a few hunks of flesh. And him? What was he? A creature with its teeth pulled out and its legs broken, able only to hide in a corner licking its wounds, hounded and bitten by the very stray dogs that once fawned and wagged their tails at him—a homeless cur.
His ruin was plain to the eye. Compared with the old "Boss Shuai" who drove a Mercedes S-Class, frequented the clubs, and was waited on by crowds, it was heaven and earth. The fifteen million bad debt from the city was like a yoke around his neck; the eight million overdue at the bank was a sword hanging over his head. His other businesses and assets—everything that could be liquidated, everything that could pay down debt—had mostly been poured in over the past two years, just barely plugging the other holes, holding on to this apartment and a basic standard of living. Lin Wan's income from livestreaming was steady, even higher than many white-collar workers', and it carried the household's daily expenses and the children's tuition, letting him still maintain a bare minimum of dignity. Only he knew how fragile this "dignity" was—a thin sheet of ice, with bone-chilling cold water and man-eating debt right beneath it. The people who used to fawn on him now either avoided him or, like this collections specialist, jabbed at his sorest spots in the most "compliant" way possible.
He thought back to those years. That account manager surnamed Wang from the Bank of Communications, who'd dropped by his "Hongchang Real Estate" office every other day, pouring tea and offering cigarettes, smiling so wide his eyes vanished. "Boss Shuai, try this, the Jinjunmei tea just came in!" "Boss Shuai, our bank's got a new credit offer for quality enterprises—high limits, low rates. Why not consider it? Think of it as helping a little brother hit his targets!" Back then he was a fairly well-known private entrepreneur in the city, with two well-regarded residential developments under his belt, smooth relations between business and government, and banks chasing after him to hand over money. He really hadn't been short of cash, but he couldn't withstand little Manager Wang's pestering, and figured a bit more working capital never hurt—and that was how the eight million came to be.
How many years was that?
The pandemic had swept in like a sudden tsunami, carrying everything away. Construction halted, sales stalled, and the cash flow snapped with a single crack. The bank that had once begged him to take its loans turned on him faster than flipping a page. "Little Manager Wang," now promoted to branch president, was nowhere to be seen, replaced by these cold "specialists" scattered across the country. The "friends" who'd called him brother dodged him wherever they could—not answering calls, not replying on WeChat. The fifteen million in project payments from the city government he'd been chasing for three years, running his legs off and talking himself hoarse, only ever got him the same reply: "Fiscal difficulties—we're coordinating."
"Thirty years on this side of the river, thirty years on the other…" he murmured to himself, his voice dry. Who needed thirty years? Three or four was enough to drop a man from the clouds into the mud, smashing him to pieces.
He was alone in the house. Wenyao had long since left for school. Lin Wan… Lin Wan wasn't home either. He didn't know whether she'd gone for a run or off to some library or café again. She had more and more of her own things to do, more and more of her own plans. She'd never again be like she was just after they married, giving up her doctoral spot because of a pregnancy, never again revolving around him and the children in everything.
The phone rang again—a local, unfamiliar number. He hesitated two seconds, then answered.
"Hey, Boss Shuai!" The other voice was loud, the background noisy. "It's me, Old Liu! Old Liu from Tongda Building Materials! Remember?"
Shuai Hongqiang frowned and kept his tone as level as he could. "Boss Liu, hello."
"Aiya, Boss Shuai, sorry to bother you. Just wanted to ask… that payment the city government owes you—any word on it lately?" Old Liu's voice carried a shrewd warmth. "See, that batch of exterior-wall stone you took from me back then—the balance has been dragging quite a while now… I run a small operation, I really…"
Shuai Hongqiang shut his eyes and pinched the bridge of his nose. "Boss Liu, the moment the money comes back, you'll be the first one I settle with. Things really are hard right now—you know the situation."
"I know, I know. Aiya, it's not easy for anyone." Old Liu laughed it off, but his tone took a turn. "Still, Boss Shuai, I hear your wife's doing livestreaming now—they say livestreaming makes good money these days… so I was wondering whether maybe…"
"Boss Liu," Shuai Hongqiang cut in, his voice going colder, "I'll pay the money back. The moment there's news, I'll let you know. I've got something to deal with here, so I'll hang up now."
Without waiting for a reply, he ended the call. There was a tightness in his chest. Old Liu used to bow lower than anyone when he saw him.
He walked back to the tea table, poured another cup of the cold-through tea, and gulped it down. The water slid down his throat, leaving him a little clearer-headed, pressing down that floating, unsteady feeling a bit.
He couldn't just sit here. He had to move—even if it was only aimless wandering.
He went to the bathroom and freshened up. The man in the mirror had faint blue-black shadows under his eyes, the mark of long anxiety and lack of sleep. Shuai Hongqiang splashed his face with cold water, droplets rolling along his jawline into his collar—his clothes were always pressed neat by Lin Wan, kept tidy and impeccable. He changed, choosing a fine-quality crew-neck cashmere sweater, a style from several years back, but the cut still crisp.
In the underground garage, the Mercedes S-Class and the sports car he used to park there were long gone—sold to fill the holes. Only in the corner stood his old BMW X6. Deep black, a thin layer of dust on the body, but the silhouette still commanding. It was the first good car he'd bought after he'd made his name, and it had run with him to construction sites, met clients, and carried the whole family on trips. He couldn't bear to sell it, and it wouldn't fetch a good price anyway, so he'd kept it—like a stubborn medal from days gone by.
He pulled open the door and got in. The interior was well kept, the leather seats still soft, though the mileage was high by now. He turned the key, and the engine roared low to life, the sound no longer as full-bodied as in its prime, carrying a wheezing, raspy edge that echoed in the silent garage. Like an old beast caged too long behind iron bars, scarred and weary yet still refusing to lie all the way down, letting out a whimper of defiance. The car rolled slowly out of the garage and out of the compound. The gatekeeper recognized the car and saluted. Shuai Hongqiang gave a slight nod. The sun fell just right across the windshield, a little dazzling.
He gripped the wheel and merged into the gathering early rush-hour traffic. The string inside him was still drawn taut, but life had to go on like this. A tiger brought low onto the plain was still, in the end, a tiger. As long as he could still stand, this breath in him couldn't be allowed to scatter completely. It was just that this barely-held-together "ordinary middle-class" life felt, at every step, like walking on thin ice, listening to the faint, ceaseless "crack" of the layer beneath his feet.

========================================

Chapter 4 — Once Within Reach, Now Beyond His Grasp
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640251.html

Once within reach, now beyond his grasp.
The car slid into Jinshi's new CBD on one of those rare northern winter days—clear skies, brilliant sun spilling across the whole city. The glass curtain wall of that skyscraping office tower was cold and sharp as a blade, mirroring back an entire sky's worth of blue, and farther off, that ancient, silent ribbon of the Yellow River. He parked, but didn't get out right away. He rolled the window up, and in the cramped space there was nothing left but the sound of his own breathing.
Three years. Only three years.
He had once been one of the players in this golden district too. Sixteenth floor. Three hundred and sixty square meters, facing southeast, floor-to-ceiling windows the whole way around. In the morning he could watch the sun rise from the edge of the city, gold light flooding half the district; in the evening, that silent Yellow River in the distance would turn into a band of molten metal. Back then he'd thought life was like this skyline that kept setting new records for height—no ceiling. Money came too easily. Real estate, guesthouses, a farm outside the city—he bought up land and projects the way other men collect medals, certain he could hold on to something permanent.
Then the tide went out, and you find out who's been swimming naked.
Heavy assets became the chains that bound his hands and feet. Liquidating them was less a business operation than a slow self-dismemberment, a sentence served on his own body. He sold off house after house at fire-sale prices, each one breaking through the floor of the last, like carving off flesh that was still healthy—agony at first, then nearly numb, until all he wanted was something "clean," some "closure." The guesthouses closed; the farm changed hands; those investments that had once stood for vision and feeling were now nothing but rows of staggering loss figures on a financial statement. He poured most of the flesh and blood he'd accumulated over the past decade and more into a pit that had suddenly caved in.
In the end he was like a rich man who'd bankrupted himself paying for treatment: he kept his life and the clothes on his back, but inside he'd been hollowed out, his vital force badly drained.
And Yao Yuan—the woman who had always been calmer and sharper than he was—had placed her bet against the grain just as the whole world was singing of recession. Paid in full. Seven hundred and thirty square meters, thirty-eighth floor. A number and a height he now had to look up to reach.
Life is always this dramatic. He thought, with a bitter twist, of that internet line: "The me you can't be bothered with now—the me of the future is more than you can ever afford." Fitting. And true.
He pushed open the car door, and the city wind swept into his face, carrying all the restlessness of the lunchtime rush. He stood still and looked up. In the reflection on the glass curtain wall, white clouds drifted slowly past, and for one instant he had the illusion that the moving play of light and shadow had settled on a window pane somewhere up on the thirty-eighth floor. She who had once been within reach up there was now beyond his grasp. "We" had long since become "he" and "she." He stood below; she was up in the clouds. Between them lay a past of bankruptcy and liquidation, and a glass cliff—invisible, yet utterly solid—called fortune.
He straightened the cuff of a jacket that had no wrinkle in it at all, and walked toward that vast revolving door that seemed capable of swallowing the whole of his past. Every step landed on the wreckage of his old ambitions, soundless.
The mirrored surface of the elevator reflected Shuai Hongqiang's smooth collar. As the numbers climbed from 1 to 38, he reached up to fix his cuff again without thinking. The elevator doors slid open with a "ding," and when he stepped onto the pale gray carpet of the thirty-eighth-floor corridor, his footsteps were swallowed nearly to silence. The receptionist met him with a professional smile: "Hello. Do you have an appointment?"
"I'm here to see Yao Yuan." When Shuai Hongqiang gave the name, his face stayed mild and unbothered, but his fingertips drew in despite himself.
He looked at the enormous abstract art installation hanging down from the ceiling behind the front desk, at the white-collar staff in fitted skirt suits and immaculate makeup hurrying past, the smell of coffee mixed with perfume. He'd once been one of the masters of this place. Now he was a visitor.
The receptionist's smile paused for half a second. She picked up the internal line, murmured a few words, hung up, and made a "please" gesture: "Boss Yao is in a meeting. She asks that you wait in the reception area first."
Crossing the open-plan office, the clatter of keyboards and low discussion rolled over him like a tide. The staff's eyes brushed over him, here and gone, touched with curiosity.
The glass wall of the reception area faced the CEO's office, the blinds drawn; he could only make out figures moving faintly inside. Shuai Hongqiang sat on the sofa, his gaze falling on the corporate brochure on the coffee table.
