The cover bore a photo of Yao Yuan in a stylish business outfit, plunging neckline, a black leather waist cincher accentuating a graceful line. Her big, loose waves were full and shapely, her brows and eyes sharp, her look aimed straight at the camera, radiating confidence and an ambition she made no effort to hide—the magnetism and presence of a mature woman. It gradually overlapped with the girl in his memory who used to curl up in his arms, smiling like a flower.
He'd lost track of how long it had been when the office door opened. A cluster of people came out around Yao Yuan; she had her head turned, listening intently to a deputy's report. Catching sight of Shuai Hongqiang in the reception area, her step faltered slightly, and then she said to the group, "Work the rest out among yourselves and bring me the result."
The crowd dispersed. Yao Yuan came over with an unhurried grace, still in the same red dress she'd worn an hour earlier on her livestream. At 168 centimeters, paired with a pair of five-centimeter pointed heels glinting with fine gold, she set off her figure all the more—curves front and back, like a peach ripened to perfection, tempting a man to lean closer and breathe in that intoxicating, heady scent of woman. The crisp, forceful click of her heels on the floor recalled Shuai Hongqiang from his moment of dazed admiration. She really did suit red—or maybe her beauty was just that flagrant, or even more than that.
Yao Yuan sat down in the single armchair across from him. She didn't call him by his name the way she used to, only said lightly: "Did Boss Shuai need something from me?"
"Mm." Shuai Hongqiang took a document from his briefcase and pushed it across. "I've got a project in hand. I was hoping we could work together."
Yao Yuan didn't look at the document. Her fingertips tapped lightly on the armrest, her gaze resting on his face. Shuai Hongqiang's 178-centimeter frame was wrapped in a well-cut dark gray cashmere suit, the shoulder line still firm—only, with his body sunk into the sofa, the fabric had left a few creases at his waist that wouldn't smooth out. His left hand rested on the armrest; the wrist that used to wear a Rolex Submariner Green was bare, while his right hand held a phone with a faint awkwardness. Most of his hair was still jet-black, but right near the crown on the right side, a few strands of silver had pushed through, stark and gleaming. His brows grew thick and black, almost without a curve from start to finish, the tails sloping gently down to either side like two relaxed arcs, carrying a touch of careless ease. There was no high rise and fall at the brow's peak the way ordinary eyebrows have—they simply lay steady above his eyes, with a hard, upright spirit. Beneath the brows was a pair of clearly defined eyes, broad double lids, the creases deep and clean, running smoothly from inner corner to outer. The eyes themselves were round and large, the whites and the dark sharply distinct, the whites carrying a clarity rare in a middle-aged man; the deep amber irises turned a warm, gentle luster under the light. His long, dense lashes had real presence; when he lowered his eyelids, they cast a small, soft fan of shadow over his lower lid. Just now, too stiff to know what to say, he pretended to look out the window, and as those long lashes lifted slightly to reveal the full, bright eyes, what they held was the shrewd, situation-reading wit deposited by years of rising and sinking in the sea of business—and a barely perceptible trace of the merchant's calculating self-interest. His nose bridge wasn't very high; the tip was full without being blunt, with a settled, certain roundness. His lips were on the thin side, his jaw line clear but not gaunt, a fine match for his upright, square-round face, giving an impression of trustworthy steadiness and approachability. His skin was on the fair side, with fine lines at the corners of his eyes and across his brow worn in by time—a mature man whom the years and the world had blended into something richer and more complex.
Now, at forty-six, Shuai Hongqiang sat before her. This man, nearing fifty, his bearing settled now, was the man who, in the year she was twenty-eight, had once made her offer up a true heart, ready to hand over everything and spend her whole life with him.
Yao Yuan picked up the document and flipped through it quickly. The pages rustled, especially clear in the silence of the office. Suddenly she lifted her eyes and fixed her gaze on him: "Shuai Hongqiang, we haven't seen each other in eight years."
Shuai Hongqiang's throat bobbed. The weight of time sometimes presses down on a single form of address, a single meeting of eyes. "You're a big internet celebrity now." His voice was a little tight, as if afraid of breaking something. "I keep coming across you in videos. I left messages for your assistant a few times before I got this contact."
Yao Yuan closed the document and set it back on the mirror-bright glass coffee table. Her tone was flat, unruffled, as though appraising the most ordinary of projects: "Still the same old line of business. Traditional manufacturing—the profit margins were squeezed dry ages ago."
The fingers gripping his phone tightened a fraction without his noticing. In the past she had often said such things at his side too—those forward-looking, incisive business insights, like flickering stars. But back then he'd only taken her for a pretty girl with a little cleverness who liked to look good. When she spoke, he'd listen with a smile, thinking it was just her cute, impractical fantasy; he hadn't taken it seriously, let alone acted on it. Later, reality bore out her predictions again and again, accurate to the point of cruelty. Only by then, she was no longer at his side.
"The profit is thin," Shuai Hongqiang's voice dropped, carrying a resigned sort of weariness, "but this project is the steadiest. It's a field I know inside out—I know every dip and rut in it. It's hard work, hard work, but at least… it's guaranteed not to lose money."
"Leave the document with me. I'll give you an answer once I've read it." Yao Yuan stood. The advantage of her height naturally lent her a scrutinizing air. The diamond pendant at her collarbone swayed with the motion, refracting a cold, sharp light.
"All right. I'll wait to hear from you." Shuai Hongqiang stood as well, took an elegant paper bag from beside the armrest, and held it out. "Eight years apart—a small token."
"What is it?" Yao Yuan took it and drew out a dark brown rectangular wooden box. She opened it, and on the velvet lining lay a bronze mirror the size of a palm.
It was cool to the touch, heavy—a weight that seemed able to sink straight to the bottom of one's heart. The mirror itself was no more than seven or eight centimeters wide, its edges worn jade-smooth by long ages, glowing with a dark blue-brown patina. At the center of the back was a small bridge-shaped knob, and around it coiled intricate cloud-and-thunder patterns and a relief of a Kui-dragon; though the lines had been blurred somewhat by time's erosion, they still breathed an ancient, vigorous air.
Strangest of all was the mirror's face. An ordinary old bronze mirror should long since have gone dull and clouded, yet this little mirror dimly reflected her face. Only the image was hazy, blurred, as if behind a layer of misty Jiangnan rain that would never lift. She ran a fingertip across the surface—icy and slick, with not the slightest roughness or rust.
It was a beautiful, peculiar object. Yao Yuan turned it over in her hands, but a question stirred in her: why give her a mirror that couldn't show a face clearly?
Shuai Hongqiang read the look in her eyes. "I remembered you liked these fine little things," he explained. "This mirror was bought at that auction on the winter solstice in '17. They called it an antique—not all that old. The moment I saw it, I felt it should be yours. I wanted to give it to you, called you… but you were already in Hangzhou by then." He paused, his voice sinking further. "And so it sat for eight years. Now it's gone back to its rightful owner."
The year seventeen. The winter solstice.
The needle of memory jabbed hard at Yao Yuan. It was that very winter she had severed every last hesitation and flown south to Hangzhou alone.
"I'm surprised you still remember." For an instant Yao Yuan's expression softened almost imperceptibly, her tone turning gentle. "Thank you. I'll keep the gift."
Without warning, a sour warmth welled up in her chest, then was covered over by a deeper bewilderment. She was no longer the girl who was so easily moved. As today's cold-hearted "Godmother of Feelings," known for her decisive ruthlessness in both feeling and business, how could she be so easily stirred by an old mirror and a few old words?
"Bzzz, bzzz, bzzz—"
The phone on the coffee table buzzed right on cue, the hum short and dense, instantly breaking that subtle stillness in the air.
It was a call confirming the departure time for a tech-world dinner Wang Qian had invited her to.
"Yao Yuan…" The word "Yuan'er" turned over a few times on Shuai Hongqiang's tongue but in the end wouldn't come out. "You go ahead and get to your work. I won't keep you. When there's time… let's have a meal together."
Shuai Hongqiang took his leave and turned toward the office door.
Just as he was about to step out, Yao Yuan's voice came from behind him—not loud, but clear:
"Shuai Hongqiang."
He turned back.
Yao Yuan stood at the office doorway, the vast floor-to-ceiling windows and the city's daylight at her back. Against the light, her fine silhouette blurred; he couldn't make out her exact expression.
"I'll get back to you as soon as I can," she said.
Then she turned, and the office door closed without a sound, sealing the two of them back into their separate worlds.
The elevator descended at a steady pace, the numbers ticking down floor by floor. Shuai Hongqiang stared at the flickering red digits, as if silently counting all the old days that had slipped away along the way, never to be retrieved.
The elevator mirror was cold, reflecting a middle-aged man's face that couldn't hide its weariness. He drew a deep breath and straightened his spine.
On the other side of the door, Yao Yuan didn't go straight back behind her desk. She stood where she was, her fingertips brushing absently along the edge of the wooden box. The bronze mirror lay quietly inside, its misty, rain-blurred face seeming to have quietly absorbed that brief softness of a moment ago as well.
She returned to her desk and tossed the project document onto the top of a stack of materials waiting to be read. But her gaze drifted, against her will, to the bronze mirror.
The office held nothing but the low hum of the air conditioning. Yao Yuan picked up the bronze mirror again, the pad of her finger rubbing the cold back. The intricate cloud-and-thunder patterns pressed against her skin like some ancient code.
"The winter solstice of '17…" she repeated the year under her breath.
That had been the most strained moment of their relationship. Yao Yuan remembered fine snow drifting outside the window that day; she'd stood in a hospital corridor, asking Shuai Hongqiang over the phone for the last time when they were going to register their marriage, and he'd only laughed it off, saying he'd take her out that evening to a newly opened French restaurant. It was on that snowy day, too, that she had gone through an abortion alone in the hospital, recovered for three days, and booked a one-way ticket to Hangzhou.
The bronze mirror lay quietly in her palm, its face still hazy, like a thin fog that could never be wiped clean. On impulse she raised the mirror up, angling it toward the ceiling light. In the instant the light struck the surface, something seemed to flash, very fast, through the depths of that dim, blurred face—not the reflected light of the lamp, but more like a knot of chaotic shadow, twisting, contracting, then vanishing. An instant later, a chill more piercing than the metal itself shot up her fingertips into her arm, jolting her into letting go.
"Clang—"
The bronze mirror fell onto the thick wool carpet with a muffled thud, face down.
Yao Yuan's heart jumped. She bent to pick it up. The moment her fingertips touched the mirror's back, a sudden sting—she picked the mirror up, and in its face her own outline, blurred, was still reflected. The mirror's edge was smudged with red. Looking closely, she found she'd somehow cut her finger, and the blood had touched the surface. Just then, again, a flash of light seemed to come and go in an instant.
Yao Yuan froze for a moment, her hand still pressed to the bronze mirror, her breathing a little ragged. A hallucination? The fatigue of running flat out at high intensity these past stretches?
"Knock, knock, knock."
A knock came, and her assistant poked her head in: "Boss Yao, the car's ready. President Wang's side just asked when you'll be setting off."
"…Got it." Yao Yuan gathered up every trace of emotion and recovered her habitual cool composure. She pulled open a drawer, put the bronze mirror back in its wooden box, and tossed it in; the sound of metal striking wood was crisp and clean.
"File the document, mark it 'pending assessment.'" She rose and grabbed her coat. Passing the coffee table, she glanced at the now-empty gift bag without breaking stride. "Also—look into 'Shuai Hongqiang's' financial situation and litigation records over the past five years. I want a detailed report."
"Yes, Boss Yao."
The sound of her heels faded away. The office sank back into silence, with only the closed drawer seeming to hold a faint chill that didn't belong to this era.
(Meanwhile)
Shuai Hongqiang walked out of that mirror-bright glass curtain-wall tower standing at the city's heart. The early-winter wind, carrying a biting edge, swept the city's dust and clamor against his face. He didn't get into his car right away, only walked along the sidewalk, aimless.
The phone in his pocket buzzed once. He took it out—an unsigned message:
"The thing—did she take it?"
He stared at that line for a long while, then pressed his thumb hard against the screen and deleted the message. He looked up at the towering glass curtain-wall building rising into the clouds; sunlight reflected off a window on the top floor stung his eyes.
Shuai Hongqiang had, in fact, concealed a few things.
This mirror—bidding for it back then hadn't been only because he'd thought she would like it. The white-haired old appraiser, at the handover, had hesitated as if about to speak, then in the end only murmured a vague warning: "This mirror… the period's hard to say, the aura is a little 'heavy.' There's something 'uncanny' about it. They say it's a piece from the late Ming or early Qing, but no exact year can be tested. If you mean to keep it yourself, sir, no harm done; but if you mean to give it away—especially to someone close to you—you'd best… think it over."
At the time he'd taken it for mystification. Until, in recent years, his cash flow broke several times over. In the thick of his troubles, as if led by some unseen hand, he'd thought of this mirror—thought of the "Great Fortune of the Nine-Purple Fire" that the feng shui master who once read her fate had spoken of, and of the appraiser's tight-lipped, evasive expression.
Perhaps this was not merely a gift eight years late, a clumsy bit of nostalgia—it was even, in a way, a… cry for help. But in the end he hadn't managed to say it out loud. Facing the Yao Yuan of today, blazing with light, her eyes sharp, that line—"help me"—had stuck in his throat and turned into a pale, feeble "guaranteed profit, no loss."
Shuai Hongqiang drew a deep breath of the cold air, pressing his sigh down into his heart, got into the car, and pulled slowly into the surging current of traffic, soon lost beyond recognition. He didn't know that the ancient mirror—spanning eight years' time, wound about with tangled feelings and an unexplained strangeness—lay at this moment in a dark drawer high above the city, like a riddle lightly tapped, waiting for the next chance to be opened, quietly rewriting the ruts that fate would run in.

Chapter 5 — The Red Rose and the Binary: Zhao Yiming, Founder of "Western World"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640252.html

The private room was heated to the point of being stuffy, the air thick with the expensive blend of aloeswood incense and cigars. The glow of the crystal chandelier spilled down onto the rosewood card table, throwing a hard, cold metallic sheen across the chips.

Zhao Yiming was not seated at the head of the table.

He was, in name, tonight's guest of honor — this private club called "Songyun" was itself one of Western World Technology's discreet venues for entertaining top-tier LPs (limited partners) and strategic shareholders. But at the moment he was reclining in the corner, sunk into a huge leather sofa, pinching a small white porcelain teacup between his fingers. The tea had long gone cold, the amber liquid untouched. He wore a dark-gray cashmere sweater, the cuffs rolled back slightly to reveal lean wrists. Amid a room full of Armani suits and Hermès belts, the outfit looked out of place, almost shabby. Yet no one dared underestimate him. The laughter at the card table was loud — three other capital heavyweights feeling one another out — while Zhao Yiming simply listened, those eyes a little unfocused from years spent staring at screens, drifting idly behind his rimless glasses toward the door of the room.

When the heavy solid-wood door was pushed open by a server and a cold draft swept in carrying a crisp note of perfume, the clamor inside seemed to hit pause.

Yao Yuan stood in the doorway like a rose that had been bathed in fire. Her beauty carried an edge of aggression — scorching, blazing — and yet within those burning embers shimmered an allure beyond words. It was the bearing of a woman tempered again and again by the mortal world: she had once shattered, then gathered herself up piece by piece and made herself whole again; she had long since seen through it all, and still chose, of her own free will, to turn back and return to this clamorous battlefield. She gave off a kind of solitary courage, a sharpness that had been mended, a quality impossible to sketch and yet enough to seize the heart.

She wore a white tiger-mink coat, the fur so pure it held not a single off shade, setting off skin whiter than snow. The hem swung with her steps, hinting at a length of red gown beneath; her high heels struck the carpet with a muffled, certain sound.

Wang Qian — the current president of the Jinshi branch of the Bank of Communications, in the middle of shuffling — paused, then broke into a wide smile. "Aiyo, Miss Yao's finally here! This game's been short a God of Wealth — now we're complete!"

Zhao Yiming did not rise.

In the instant he saw Yao Yuan, his pupils contracted, almost imperceptibly. It wasn't dazzlement — it was more like a top-tier programmer, deep in a vast ocean of code, suddenly stumbling on a core function that ran flawlessly, logically self-consistent, and yet was entirely unfamiliar, of unknown origin. An instinctive, highly alert signal.

Yao Yuan's gaze swept the room like radar and finally settled on the one man who hadn't stood to greet her, there in the corner.

Zhao Yiming, founder of Western World Technology, thirty-nine, young and accomplished. His background hid a kind of "cross-disciplinary gene" rare in tech circles. He'd done his undergraduate degree in physics, switched to computer science for his doctorate, and during his postdoc had immersed himself in a Silicon Valley biology lab, studying the intersection of neural networks and computational models. At thirty he'd come home with three top-journal papers and one bare-bones algorithmic model, rented a ten-square-meter office in a high-tech park, and — on three million in angel funding — keyed out the core code for the first generation of intelligent interactive chips.

Yao Yuan peeled off her leather gloves, handed them off to the assistant behind her without looking, and walked straight toward the card table — but stopped as she passed the sofa. She looked down at Zhao Yiming, the corner of her mouth curving into an arc that gave nothing away.

"They all say Director Zhao of Western World is the hardest man to land. Thanks to President Wang here, I finally get to see you in the flesh." Her voice was clear, with a particular trace of languor and edge. "What's this, Director Zhao — not going to play a hand? I hear tech people are the best at calculating odds."

Zhao Yiming set down the teacup his lips had never touched; its base clinked lightly against the marble side table with a crisp "tak." He finally lifted his head, meeting that appraising look with a level gaze.

"I don't gamble, Miss Yao." His tone was flat, without the slightest ripple of feeling. "I only do things that are certain."

"Oh?" Yao Yuan raised a brow and, taking the cue, sat down in the single armchair across from him, crossing her legs. The coat slid off one shoulder, baring the smooth line of it. "But President Wang tells me you're in a rush to find money to plug a hole this time. That's quite a gamble too."

The moment the words were out, the mood in the room froze a degree. The murmuring over by the tea table stopped; the other two at the card table set down their hands as well, watching Zhao Yiming warily.

It was naked provocation, and a probe. She was testing exactly how badly Western World Technology's cash flow had ruptured after the "circuit-breaker incident."

Zhao Yiming's face still showed nothing. He reached up and pushed the glasses up the bridge of his nose — his signature gesture for entering combat mode.

"Western World doesn't need to plug holes. We're rebuilding the foundation." He said it lightly, his voice low but every word distinct. "As for gambling… Miss Yao, you dressed this grandly to come see me — isn't that because you mean to place a bet here far bigger than anything on that card table?"

He didn't dodge the crisis; he threw the question back, precisely.

Yao Yuan blinked, then laughed. She leaned forward slightly, closing the distance. "Director Zhao, what kind of clueless-straight-guy talk is that? This is just how I dress. I plain enjoy dressing up, plain love looking good. I read people and I read myself. What's wrong with that?"

"Since you've decided I'm here to place a bet, why don't I show my hand? That second-gen model of your 'Genesis Engine' — can it actually run all the way through? Don't let it be like last time: burn a few hundred million and end up blowing up your own server room." She deliberately slowed down on those last few words.

"Blowing up the server room" — that was the insiders' nickname for that accident. Wang Qian coughed awkwardly beside them, trying to smooth things over.

Zhao Yiming's expression darkened a shade, but he kept his breathing under control. He looked Yao Yuan straight in the eye, his gaze as sharp as a scalpel.

"Technology never lies. Only people do. My code, my models, all my experimental data — it's right there, ready to face any level, any form of audit at any time. The question is, Miss Yao…"

He stood and walked to the huge floor-to-ceiling window, his back to the gaudy room, looking down at the city lights surging outside, brilliant as a streaming flow of data. The enormous LED screen flickered silently behind him, blue light sliding across his straight spine.

"Do you actually understand it?" He turned, the cold glow of the screen streaming behind him, setting him off as though he stood in another dimension. "Or do you just want to buy the hottest 'ticket' to the future — even if… you have no idea where this ship is headed, and don't even care whether it's seaworthy?"

He walked back toward the sofa, didn't sit, only bent slightly to close the physical distance between himself and Yao Yuan, bringing an intangible pressure with it.

"This is a setup," Zhao Yiming said, looking at her, biting off each word. "But I set it to filter out the speculators whose vision is too short. If you want in, you first have to prove you're not the 'outsider' the algorithm will scrub away."

You could have heard a pin drop in the room — only the low hum of the heating system. Yao Yuan let the smile fade from her face, her eyes turning serious. She knew that this was the true purpose of tonight's dinner.

Just then, the heavy solid-wood door was pushed open by a server again, and another beautiful woman came in. As she opened it, the hallway light fell across her just so — a crisply tailored black velvet suit, the shoulder pads not wide but enough to command the room, a moon-white silk shirt beneath, the collar left casually undone at the top two buttons to reveal clean collarbones: formal, yet carrying a touch of careless ease.

Below she wore matching high-waisted cigarette trousers, the cuffs just reaching the edge of slim-heeled black ankle boots, soundless on the carpet, yet every step steady. Her hair was loosely gathered at the back with an ebony hairpin, a few stray strands falling along her neck, softening the suit's severity. She wore little jewelry — only a single minimalist platinum hoop in her left ear, swaying gently with her movements, glinting with an understated shine in the warm light.

There was not a trace of unease about her. Her eyes swept over everyone present — neither servile nor superior — a faint smile resting at the corner of her mouth, with none of the deliberate warmth of someone trying to please, and none of the stiffness of a stranger either. It was a steadiness that came from within. Like a hibiscus grown wild in open country, both branch and leaf having weathered wind and rain, and so quiet, and so tough, its roots long since driven deep into the unseen.

She was Jin Lan, the helmswoman of Jinshi's dining empire, "Yellow River Tower." The story went that at eighteen she'd left her southwestern hometown alone, plunged headlong into the unfamiliar northwest, and from then on, single-handedly, won this stretch of sky for herself.

"What kept you?" Yao Yuan rose, her tone familiar, walking over to link arms affectionately with Jin Lan and introduce this new arrival to the watching eyes, open and hidden, between the card table and the sofa.

With everyone present, they sorted themselves into host and guests and took their seats.

The round table had been laid. Zhao Yiming took the head without ceremony. To his right was Yao Yuan; beside her was Director Zhang, who worked in new energy and had just closed a deal with Western World on automotive-grade chips. To Zhao Yiming's left was Wang Qian, and beside Wang Qian sat the newly arrived Jin Lan, then in turn Director Liu from a sensor company, Gao Jieyuan — a young partner from the venture-capital world — and two professors from a top university's artificial-intelligence lab.

The cold dishes arrived, crystalline and exquisitely plated. Zhao Yiming didn't pick up his chopsticks. He first lifted the warmed decanter, stood, and personally poured half a cup of amber rice wine for each of the two senior professors.

"Professor Li, Professor Wang, you two are older — drink something mild, it's easy on the stomach." His tone was respectful, the courtesy of a junior toward scholars. Then he scanned the table, raised the cup of clear liquor before him — limpid as water yet hiding a fierce strength. "The rest of you, suit yourselves. No pressing drinks tonight; we're here mainly to talk business. But the first cup — to welcome our two lady bosses, Director Yao and Boss Jin, and to thank everyone for honoring us."

With that he tipped his head back and downed the fiery liquor in one go. The motion was clean and decisive, carrying that stubborn sense of ritual peculiar to tech people. The others raised their cups in answer; the bright clink of glasses rang out, and the first cup slid down warm and burning.

After three rounds, the mood at the table loosened and warmed, visibly.

"Pretty Yao," said Wang Qian, clearly an old acquaintance, teasing with a smile, using the nickname from years ago. "I watched that livestream of yours today. Good grief — your tongue gets sharper by the day. That 'value exchange' theory of feelings of yours has more and more devotees. Even my wife is a die-hard fan. These days I both love and resent her — love that coming home is genuinely cozy, resent that she can't save up a cent of pocket money. With a fan base like yours, you must be raking it in?"

Yao Yuan and Wang Qian went way back — they'd known each other since she was a young girl, back when Wang Qian had only just started work at the Bank of Communications and used to drop by Shuai Hongqiang's office to cadge tea. Through one thing and another they'd gotten acquainted, and since they were close in age, they'd quickly grown familiar. "Pretty Yao" was the nickname he'd given her then.

Yao Yuan's eyes flashed; she pretended to shoot him a sidelong glare, that one look full of flirtation and a familiar edge. "President Wang, you've got the bargain and you're still showing off. Spend a little money, the household's harmonious, the emotional value maxed out, the feelings warming up — as long as you're not getting other ideas, isn't that a good deal? If you want life sweeter still, send your wife to my 'Sisters' Private Class.'" She finished, laughed first herself, then turned to the others to explain. "Actually my fan base isn't as broad as everyone imagines — nothing compared to the huge followings of the top sales-livestreamers. My clients are mostly urban women aged twenty-five to forty, highly educated, high-income. Money, brains, minds of their own — but maybe lacking security in their relationships. What they need isn't fantasy; it's strategy and method. Like our Boss Jin here," she said with a smile, gesturing to Jin Lan beside her. "Wisdom and real strength — that's the best security there is."

"Impressive, impressive!" Gao Jieyuan, sitting opposite, jumped right in, his smile radiant, showing two sharp little canine teeth. That young face, caught between boy and man, carried an unworn-away sincerity. "Sure enough, serving the high-net-worth crowd is the most efficient way to make money. The old saying's right — women's and children's money is the easiest to earn. Better yet, rich women's money."

Yao Yuan lifted her eyes, let her gaze rest on Gao Jieyuan's face for a beat, half a smile, and raised her cup in a slight toast. "Thank you for the appreciation, young Boss Gao." Then, elegantly, she drained what was left in her glass.

At this point Zhao Yiming, at the head of the table, let his gaze travel slowly around the round table and come to rest again on the two professors. "Professor Li, Professor Wang," he said, steering the small talk back on course, "the joint brain-computer interface lab we discussed last week — the site approval came through. Next week I'll have my assistant send over the design drawings; take a look and tell me what needs adjusting."

Professor Li nodded with a smile. "Director Zhao, your efficiency really is beyond reproach. On our end we've done a preliminary screening of the first batch of researchers — all top-notch PhDs in the relevant fields, very clean backgrounds."

The servers began bringing out the hot dishes soundlessly, the fragrance of fine delicacies drifting through the room.

Director Liu of the sensor company seized the chance to speak, his tone carrying a merchant's smoothness and a probing note. "Director Zhao, about… our next-generation smart-home chip — could the power-consumption standard be relaxed, maybe just a touch, two points? The test data on our new materials is honestly running a little tight…"

"Can't relax it." Zhao Yiming cut him off, his tone even but carrying an unshakable certainty, like stating a law of physics. "Users want battery life, not an excuse for us to compromise." He lifted a piece — the most tender part of the steamed reeves shad — and set it naturally onto Professor Wang's plate, while picking up the thread. "Here's what we'll do, Director Liu. Next week, have your CTO bring the core data over for a closed-door meeting with our chip team. Find the room in the underlying algorithm optimization — that's more solid than changing the standard."

He turned to Professor Wang, a different kind of light in his eyes now, the focus particular to talk of technology. "Professor Wang, that neural-signal denoising algorithm you mentioned last time — I had the team reproduce it. The data came out thirty percent better than expected. When are you publishing? Our company would like to sponsor it under our name."

Professor Wang's eyes lit up, color rising in his face. "Final round of revisions — definitely submitting next month! If Director Zhao's willing to support us, the upgrade cost for those few old machines in our lab…"

"Send the budget over." Zhao Yiming answered crisply, then turned the conversation with a negotiator's shrewdness. "But I have one condition: the patent for this technology, we hold jointly. For any later commercialization, Western World gets priority licensing."

Director Zhang of the new-energy firm laughed out loud beside them, pointing at Zhao Yiming. "Director Zhao, oh Director Zhao — that brain of yours is calculating the industry-academia-research conversion rate even at dinner. Doesn't miss a step!"

Zhao Yiming laughed too, and the laugh softened the habitual coldness of his face, showing a glimpse of genuine pleasure. He raised his cup to the table. "It's not calculating — it's responsibility. For those of us in tech, isn't the ultimate goal to turn the formulas and code in the lab into real, durable, usable things in ordinary people's hands? Come — don't just talk, try this matsutake-and-chicken stew. I had the kitchen add bamboo fungus specially. You researchers burn the most mental energy; you need to replenish."

The neon outside slanted through the floor-to-ceiling glass, casting interlaced light and shadow across the side of his face. Those eyes, always mirroring code and data streams, now — amid the rising steam of the food and the faint glints of clinking cups — held a little less of the machine's chill and a little more of a rare, vivid certainty. As if, even in the most worldly bustle of this dinner, he could still see, with perfect clarity, that solid road to the future paved by technology.

By the time the dinner was half over, the mood held a delicate balance in the alcohol and the talk of people each nursing their own designs. They discussed markets, technology, policy, edges hidden in their words, and yet in the round of pouring and drinking it all seemed convivial. Jin Lan spoke little; only when Wang Qian or Director Liu steered the topic her way did she respond briefly, with a fitting smile, on the digitalization of the dining industry or supply-chain finance — precise, watertight. But her gaze drifted now and then, thoughtful, over the laughing, talkative Yao Yuan, and over Zhao Yiming at the head of the table, who took part in the conversation while his eyes stayed clear throughout, as though forever modeling and analyzing in his head.

In the gap before another main course came out, Yao Yuan picked up a hot towel, wiped her hands unhurriedly, then lifted her cup — not toasting the whole table, but turning to Zhao Yiming beside her.

"Director Zhao," she said, not loudly, yet the talk of those nearby naturally dropped lower, "what I said at the door earlier was too harsh. I'll fine myself a cup and apologize." With that, without waiting for him to react, she drank the liquor down. The motion was clean, with the bracing readiness of someone street-smart.

Zhao Yiming looked at the empty cup, his expression unmoved, and only lifted his own to take a small sip. "You speak too gravely, Miss Yao. A technical discussion — about the matter, not the person."

Yao Yuan set down her cup and turned her body slightly toward him, a posture that read as closer, and that also walled off some of the others' eyes. "Then I won't beat around the bush either. Director Zhao, that 'Genesis Engine' of yours aims at the Holy Grail of artificial general intelligence. The scale of the vision, the scope of the ambition — I admire it. But the road to the Grail burns money, burns time, and burns investors' patience even more."

Zhao Yiming's fingers unconsciously rubbed the rim of his cup as he waited for what came next.

"I'm a businessperson. I care about return on investment, and I care about… booking the gains while I can." Yao Yuan tapped a fingertip lightly on the table. "Your large model is the 'operating system' of the future — limitless potential. But what I want to talk about is an 'application' that can run all the way through right now, can be monetized, can immediately serve my existing million high-net-worth female users — an emotional-counseling AI built on your engine's core capabilities."

The table went quiet in an instant. Even the two professors, who'd been talking in low voices, set down their chopsticks and looked over with keen interest. Jin Lan lifted her teacup, lashes lowered as if savoring the aroma, while in fact she was taking in every sound.

Zhao Yiming finally raised his eyes and looked Yao Yuan full in the face, the gaze behind his lenses sharp as a probe. "An emotional-counseling AI? There are plenty of chatbots on the market, plenty of algorithms dressed in psychology's clothing. Which kind does Miss Yao's 'Pretty Big One' want to be? A higher-grade script library, or…"

"Neither." Yao Yuan cut him off, her eyes blazing, with the near-fanatical light of someone speaking of her true field. "What I want isn't a chat companion, isn't a placebo of soothing scripts. What I want is a 'super consultant' that can genuinely 'understand' complex emotional relationships, that can run deep analysis based on a vast body of real cases and psychological models, and deliver executable strategies. It has to understand the subtleties of human nature, the games of self-interest, the power dynamics within intimate relationships — and it has to understand my users: those smart, wealthy, but equally fragile and bewildered modern women. The advice it gives can't be 'drink more hot water.' It has to be a deliverable, personalized 'solution.'"

She paused, leaned forward, dropped her voice lower still, but with more force. "Director Zhao, your engine's strengths are understanding, reasoning, and generation. My 'Pretty Big One' has accumulated five years' worth — over a hundred thousand hours of real counseling recordings (de-identified), several million pieces of user-interaction data, and an analytical framework we've iterated ourselves that actually works. What we lack is the 'brain' that fuses this framework deeply with your 'understanding and generation' power — an AI core that can keep learning and evolving, that truly possesses 'emotional intelligence.'"

Zhao Yiming fell silent. He leaned back against the chair, fingers tapping lightly on the table — his habitual gesture when sinking into deep thought. All that was left in the room was the low, lingering guqin of the background music and the faint clamor of the street far off.

"A very interesting direction." After a moment he spoke, his tone back to the calm of a technical discussion. "Affective computing is the deep water of AI ethics, and a technical hard problem. What you're asking for isn't pattern recognition — it's value judgment, predictive strategy generation. That involves high-dimensional modeling of unstructured emotional data, dynamic simulation of complex social relationships, and…" He looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze like fire. "How do you define a 'correct' or 'effective' strategy? Does your data framework itself carry biases you haven't noticed? And if the AI's 'advice' produces a bad outcome, how is responsibility defined?"

Every question struck straight at the core.

Yao Yuan didn't dodge. She met his gaze, the corner of her mouth even curving into an arc of appreciation. "That's exactly why I say this is a challenge only you and your team could possibly take on. On data bias and the ethics framework, my team has a preliminary plan, but it needs a top-tier technical mind like yours to refine and 'sterilize' it. On responsibility, we can design a layered-interaction and risk-warning system — the AI is a 'consultant,' not a 'decision-maker'; the final choice always stays in the user's hands. Most important of all…"

She leaned in, her voice carrying an almost bewitching certainty. "Director Zhao, this isn't merely a business project. It's a superb 'real-world testing ground.' Your 'Genesis Engine' needs to be forged, validated, and evolved on concrete, complex, uncertainty-filled real-world tasks. What could test an AI's 'intelligence' better than 'emotion' and 'relationships' — the oldest, most subtle, most elusive things humanity has? If your engine can run through here, that means it's taken a key step in understanding human nature and complex systems. Isn't that more valuable than simply burning money to pass generic benchmarks — and… sexier, too?"

The word "sexy," spoken from her lips and applied to a technical discussion, brought a strange jolt of impact. Zhao Yiming's fingertips stopped tapping.

At this Jin Lan set her teacup down lightly, the porcelain meeting the saucer with a crisp "ding" that broke the taut magnetic field between the two of them. She looked at Zhao Yiming, her voice calm as water yet carrying a quiet strength. "Director Zhao, I'm a layperson — I don't understand the technology. But I understand business, and I understand people. This idea of Yao Yuan's sounds like a flight of fancy, but its roots run deep. She has precise clientele in hand, accumulated data, a mature product logic — and more crucially, she has clear demand and a market willing to pay for high-quality solutions. You have the sharpest technical weapon. If the two can be combined, it might not just make a successful AI product — it might open a new window into understanding 'people.' That might… be closer to the essence of technology than many a seemingly grand project."

Zhao Yiming's gaze moved between Yao Yuan's burning expectation and Jin Lan's cool analysis. He picked up the tea that had gone stone-cold and took a sip; the chill of the liquid sharpened his thoughts further.

"I'll need to see all your data protocols, de-identification process, your existing analytical framework, and a preliminary ethics-review report." He finally spoke, his tone the rigor of strict business. "My team will do a technical-feasibility assessment and risk simulation. If the first-stage assessment passes, we can set up a joint project group. As for the form of investment — I won't use traditional financial investment. I want technical control of this joint venture and the corresponding share of intellectual property. If the project succeeds, Western World gets right of first refusal on the core technology modules of 'Pretty Big One Emotional AI,' plus licensing for subsequent development. At the same time, the technical insights and data dimensions this project generates — the ones that help the general model evolve — need to be fed back to the 'Genesis Engine' master model in a manner we agree on."

His terms were clear and hard, carrying the confidence and sense of boundary peculiar to one who commands a technology.

The smile on Yao Yuan's face widened — the thrill of a hunter watching coveted prey finally step to the edge of the trap, even if she herself might be prey at the same time. She didn't agree at once, but mused for a moment, a shrewd calculation flickering in her eyes.

"Technical control is negotiable, but product definition and user experience must be led by my team. Intellectual property shared — but the 'Pretty Big One' brand and customer data belong to our side. The mechanism and boundaries of the technical feedback need a detailed agreement. As for the investment amount, the equity structure, and the milestone bet-on-performance clauses…" She stood and held out her right hand, exaggeratedly long nails set off by vivid red polish, as if announcing to the world her singular personality and untamed soul. "Director Zhao, it seems we'll need a few long meetings on our own, to 'spar' over the details. Welcome to… the laboratory of human nature."

Zhao Yiming looked at that extended hand — well-tended, representing a vast female business empire — and slowly rose to his feet as well. He didn't take it at once; instead he picked up the decanter and refilled both his and Yao Yuan's cups with clear liquor.

"The laboratory welcomes all serious researchers," he said, handing her one cup and raising the other, its rim held slightly lower than hers. "But the first rule of my laboratory is: respect the data, respect the logic, respect the unknown. Cheers, Miss Yao."

"To verifiable solutions," Yao Yuan said, clinking her cup against his, her smile bright and sharp, "and to a feelings market about to be redefined."

The two cups met in the air with a crisp ring. The clear liquid swayed, reflecting the fine, scattered light of the crystal chandelier overhead — and reflecting, too, the ambition and anticipation in each pair of eyes: utterly different, and yet equally ablaze.

By the time the dinner broke up, the night had grown deep. Yao Yuan and Jin Lan walked side by side toward the club's entrance, talking in low voices. Zhao Yiming lagged behind with the two professors, still discussing some algorithmic detail. Wang Qian, Director Liu, and the others were each collected by their drivers.

Before they parted, Zhao Yiming said to Yao Yuan: "Tuesday next week, ten in the morning, Western World headquarters. Bring your core team and your data clearances. We begin, officially."

Yao Yuan nodded, her eyes startlingly bright in the dark. "It's a date, Director Zhao."

The car door closed, and the sedan carrying Yao Yuan and Jin Lan pulled into the neon-streaming streets. Zhao Yiming stood where he was, letting the night wind blow over him, and lifted his eyes toward the blurred sky above the city, its stars masked by light pollution — as if, within that chaos, he could see a corner of a clear, brand-new picture of the future, built of zeros and ones.

And in that picture, for the first time, two non-standardized variables had appeared so vividly: "emotion" and "relationships." The challenge was unprecedented — but this was exactly what he had always been searching for. Only the hardest problems are worth cracking.

========================================

Chapter 6 — The Bathroom Mirror Devours Her Soul: Traveling Back to Twenty-Two and Her First Love, Jiang Hai
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640253.html

The Bathroom Mirror Devours Her Soul: Traveling Back to Twenty-Two and Her First Love, Jiang Hai
It was nearly midnight by the time Yao Yuan got home.
The huge apartment was silent. Only the smart system, sensing its owner's return, had quietly lit a few warm-yellow floor lamps, tracing the cold, empty outline of the vast living room. She kicked off the high heels that had tormented her all evening. Her stocking-clad feet met the icy marble floor, and that faint sting actually cleared away some of the tipsy haze left over from the dinner, a haze tangled with everyone's hidden agendas.
The bathtub was already full of hot water, with a few drops of nerve-soothing aromatherapy oil added. She lowered herself into the warmth, closed her eyes, and tried to let her tight muscles and her tangled thoughts unwind together. Fine bubbles floated on the surface; the rising steam blurred her vision. Zhao Yiming's eyes, calm to the point of cruelty; Jin Lan's face, still as water; Wang Qian's smooth, oily smile; and all those probing thrusts and counter-thrusts moving like undercurrents beneath the clinking of glasses... they spun through her mind like images on a revolving lantern. Western World, the Genesis Engine, emotional AI, vast ambition, intricate calculation... The hot water rose past her collarbone, yet somewhere in her chest she still felt a chill she couldn't shake.
A long while later, when the water had cooled, she finally rose. She wrapped herself in a soft silk robe and walked to the wide vanity mirror. The glass was spotless, reflecting her face with perfect clarity. With her exquisite makeup removed and the polish washed away, that face had lost some of the flawless sharpness it wore by day. Under the light her skin looked too pale, almost translucent, betraying a faint weariness that long years of tension couldn't quite hide. At thirty-six, the marks of age had been carefully held back by expensive skincare and strict self-discipline. But at the corners of her eyes and brows there had finally settled something only time and experience could give, something hard to put into words. It wasn't wrinkles. It was a kind of presence, a clarity that had sunk into her bones after watching the rise and fall of things and tasting both warmth and cold, and behind that clarity, a certain emptiness.
She picked up her serum and, out of habit, began her nightly skincare routine. Her fingertips slid across her cheeks, cool and slick. The lingering effect of the alcohol left her mind sluggish and blank. The sharp-edged conversations of the day, the precise calculations of profit and loss, had receded for now, leaving behind a hollow exhaustion.
Just then, the tip of her right index finger, where that afternoon the edge of the antique bronze mirror Shuai Hongqiang had given her had carelessly cut a tiny wound, one that had long since stopped bleeding, suddenly and without any warning flared with a clear, burning, stabbing pain!
The pain was so vivid it could not be imagined. Yao Yuan froze and frowned down at her fingertip. The skin around the cut was slightly swollen from soaking in the water, and the searing sensation crept upward along the nerves of her finger, thread by thread, carrying a strange, heart-pounding throb.
Instinctively she lifted her head and looked at herself in the mirror.
In the glass, her face was the same, her eyes dazed with pain and surprise. But in the very next instant, her pupils contracted sharply!
The surface of the mirror... was rippling.
Not a physical crack or warp, but like the surface of water struck by a pebble. Centered on her reflection, soundless, transparent ripples spread outward in rings. The "her" in the mirror blurred, stretched, and distorted within the ripples, and those eyes, wide with shock, seemed to become two bottomless whirlpools.
An irresistible, immense pull, not from physical space but acting directly on the depths of her consciousness! As if an invisible hand had suddenly seized her soul and yanked it hard!
"Ngh..."
A short, stifled groan caught in her throat. The world spun. Everything before her eyes, the warm bathroom light, the gleaming tiles, the neatly arranged bottles of skincare, was instantly stretched, twisted, shattered into countless dazzling, blinding blocks of color, then swept by some brutal force into a fast-spinning, formless vortex! In her ears was a piercing, eardrum-shredding drone, mixed with countless broken, tuneless murmurs and meaningless noise drifting in from distant times and places. The feeling of her body vanished, leaving only her consciousness tumbling and plummeting through an endless chaos...
She had no idea how long it lasted. Perhaps an instant, perhaps an eternity.
Her consciousness, like something struggling up from the bottom of the deepest, thickest mire of chaos, fought its way to the surface bit by painful bit. The first thing to return was her hearing.
A... an absolute silence.
No, not entirely soundless. It was a "white noise," a foundational kind, stripped of every other sound, pure to the point of panic. There was no faint hum of an air conditioner, no distant traffic from the city outside the window, no pulse of blood flowing past her eardrums, not even the sound of her own breathing and heartbeat. Everything had been filtered out, swallowed up, leaving only this vast, even, boundless "stillness," pressing down on every nerve.
Then came touch.
Beneath her was a hard, faintly cold, flat surface, smooth, without texture, without warmth. She lay on top of it like a specimen laid out on an impossibly huge, pure-white laboratory table.
Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open.
White. Endless, all-consuming white that devoured every sense of direction and distance.
The ceiling was white, the floor beneath her was white. Front, back, left, right, everywhere she looked, all of it the same even, soft, yet lifelessly cold white. There was no light source, but the light filled the space evenly. She tried to sit up. Her movements were a little slow, but her body was fine, and the searing pain in her fingertip was gone, as if everything just now had been a hallucination. She touched her own face. It was warm, the sensation real. And her body... she was still wearing the silk nightgown she had changed into after coming home, the soft fabric clinging to her skin.
This was not a dream. At least, not any kind of dream she knew. Dreams did not possess this kind of clear, unsettling, internally consistent absurdity of sensation.
"Hello? Hello, is anyone there?" she tried calling out. Her voice carried into this pure white with no echo and no response of any kind, as if instantly absorbed by this strange space.
She stood up and looked around. Apart from white, there was only more white.
She forced down the urge in her throat to scream and made her nearly frozen brain start working again.
The burning in her fingertip, the strange change in the mirror... the bronze mirror Shuai Hongqiang had given her? That ancient, exquisite, beautiful mirror she had tucked into her office drawer, the one with her blood on it? Could it be that I've somehow gotten blood-bonded to that bronze mirror and traveled through time? Impossible, I've probably just been watching too many time-travel mini-dramas.
The absurd thoughts surged up beyond her control. But this surreal scene before her left no room for ordinary logic to explain it. Not long ago she had been at a dinner table discussing the cutting edge of artificial intelligence, quantum computing, and virtual reality with top scientists and investors, and in the blink of an eye she was trapped in this pure-white cage that looked like the set of a cheap sci-fi film. The enormous contrast brought on a dizziness that bordered on the absurd.
"Calm down, Yao Yuan, calm down," she said quietly to herself, her voice pitifully faint in this eerie silence. "Analyze the environment, find the patterns, find the exit..." She began to walk through the white, barefoot on the smooth, cold floor. With no point of reference, she couldn't even tell whether she was moving in a straight line or simply turning in place. But no matter how far she walked, the scene around her didn't change in the slightest. It was as if she were trapped in a white prison without borders.
Just as that icy terror was about to drown her, the white "space" not far ahead suddenly began to ripple.
Like a drop of thick ink falling into clear water, except what spread was not black. First came an extremely faint gray, almost imperceptible, and then colors and lines appeared out of nowhere, swiftly sketching, filling, growing solid and vivid... as if an invisible, masterful brush were painting in the void before her at an astonishing speed.
In a few seconds, perhaps less, a complete, vivid, richly detailed scene hung suspended in the boundless white.
It was a... slightly worn-out balcony, full of the texture of everyday life. By its style, it looked like the kind common in old residential buildings many years ago. Rusted green iron railings; a few plastic storage bins printed with supermarket logos piled in the corner, stuffed with out-of-season shoes and odds and ends. A few potted plants, probably aloe or spider plants, sat thriving on the concrete ledge. The afternoon sun was lovely, golden, spilling across most of the balcony, so clear she could even see fine dust motes drifting and swimming in the light. On the drying pole hung a few pieces of clothing, a white cotton T-shirt and a pale purple dress, swaying gently in the breeze. The background was a blurred apartment-building exterior wall, equally dated, with peeling paint and rows upon rows of windows.
Everything was so real. The light and shadow, the texture, the detail of the breeze stirring the hems of the clothes, even the warm, dry smell of sun-baked cotton that seemed to drift through the air, mixed with the faint dustiness of an old house. And yet this lifelike scene, like a three-dimensional scroll carefully cut out, hung suspended in this boundless white void, its edges drawn sharply apart from the white space, strange and inexplicable.
Yao Yuan's heart began to pound violently. Cautiously, step by step, she approached that "picture." About two or three meters from it, she stopped. She could see a corner of the living room inside the balcony: simple furniture, pale yellow wood flooring, a television set in the center. And then she saw a "person."
A young man, his back to her, stood by the sink on the balcony, head lowered, seeming to be washing something. He wore an ordinary white T-shirt and dark sweatpants, his frame tall and straight, his hair a little tousled. A few strands of black hair stuck up unruly, their edges glowing a faint gold in the afternoon sun. Just the sight of his back carried an indescribable... familiarity.
As if he'd heard the movement, or sensed he was being watched, the man stopped what he was doing and slowly turned around.
The sunlight fell unobstructed onto his face, lighting up its clear contours.
It was a very young face, the eyes clear, carrying the cleanness particular to the young and a trace of not-yet-faded boyish rawness. The bridge of his nose was high, his lips well-defined and pressed together. His gaze was very still, a quiet that ran deeper than his years, settled too early, and in the depths of that quiet there was a barely perceptible scrutiny of his surroundings.
This face... Yao Yuan's breath seemed to stop on the spot.
Time, at this strange boundary between pure white and vivid life, seemed stretched and frozen without end.
Yao Yuan stood rooted in place. In that instant her blood seemed to stop flowing, then surged madly again, pounding against her eardrums with a thunderous roar. She stared fixedly at that face. Every detail slammed against her brain, fitting seamlessly over an image buried deep in her memory, one she had deliberately sealed away yet that had never truly faded.
Jiang Hai.
It was Jiang Hai. Jiang Hai at twenty-five.
This place... was that little room, the small, hope-filled "love nest" she had rented near his workplace right after graduating from the Beijing Dance Academy. That year, she had been twenty-two.
Countless images, sounds, smells, sensations... were brutally pried out from behind their seal by this single simple recognition, surging up all at once, almost drowning her.
Yao Yuan had studied dance since childhood. The practice rooms were forever thick with the mingled smell of sweat, rosin, and old wooden floors. The girls in the mirrors were each slender of waist, long of limb, lovely of face. They were the seedlings of art and the focus of every gaze. From a clueless child to a graceful young woman, Yao Yuan had grown used to the eager attention that swirled around the beautiful girls beside her. There were callow classmates, there were high-spirited senior students, and there were the hot-eyed "successful men" from outside the school who came with all sorts of intentions. She had understood early on that beauty was a gift, and also a kind of leverage. Love, in her eyes, had never been merely the flutter of romance and moonlight. It was a transaction that could be carefully calculated, something to hold out for the best price. She meant to use her beauty, her talent, and this carefully cultivated "love" to trade for a richer, more secure, more admired future. In four years at the academy she had watched too many similar stories with cold detachment. The suitors around her classmates changed like images on a revolving lantern: young masters of the Beijing circle, sons of southern entrepreneurs, advertising directors, art-world investors... Lively, yes, but in the end the ones who actually saw it through were vanishingly few. Most played the role of passing strangers, longer or shorter, in that gorgeous drama called "love," and when the curtain fell and the crowd dispersed, each rushed off toward the next bit of excitement. She, Yao Yuan, refused to be such a passerby.
Her professional grades were excellent, her flexibility in waist and legs outstanding, her sense of rhythm and stage presence praised by teacher after teacher. In her sophomore year, a teacher recommended her for a fairly famous televised dance competition. She danced for three full months, refining every movement to the extreme, and in the end took only fourth place, just 0.5 points shy of the top three. It seemed to always go that way: her technique was clearly outstanding, but she always lacked a bit of luck or a bit of the audience's favor. On the awards stage, the spotlight fell on the gold, silver, and bronze winners. She stood in the dimmer corner, keeping a fitting smile on her face while her nails dug deep into her palms. People only remembered the top three. Fourth place? Who cared. And yet, even so, she remained one of the most striking girls in her year at the academy, never without suitors. There were brilliant but ordinary-born senior students who wrote beautiful love poems, and there were rich heirs who drove sports cars to wait at the school gate, generous with their spending, tossing off sweet words without effort. She turned them all down with a smile, polite but distant. Her love could not be only romance and moonlight; it had to carry more solid value.
Meeting Jiang Hai happened in the winter of her senior year, during an important off-campus collaboration with a symphony orchestra. The school took it seriously and put her in charge of liaison work and part of the choreography. Jiang Hai was the contact person from the other organization, come to coordinate the details.
When he pushed open the door, he was wearing a well-made dark gray overcoat over a crisp white shirt, no tie, his posture upright. At a height of one hundred eighty centimeters, he held his own even among a crowd of artsy young men. What struck people most was his face. Not the delicate, fashionable kind of handsome, but a very "upright" handsomeness. His brow and eyes were open and broad, his nose high and straight, his jawline clear. When he wasn't speaking he looked somewhat severe, but his gaze was very clean, focused and earnest when he looked at people, never drifting or shifting. He reached out to shake hands, his fingers long and strong, his palm dry and warm. "Hello, I'm Jiang Hai. I'm handling the coordination for this performance."
His voice wasn't loud, but it was steady and clear.
The whole coordination process was efficient and smooth. He didn't say much, but every sentence hit the mark; he considered problems thoroughly and carefully, and for the requirements raised by the dance side he could always offer practical yet flexible solutions, with none of the buck-passing or bureaucratic air common among people in the system. Professional, steady, reliable. That was Yao Yuan's first impression of him.
The performance was a great success. At the celebration banquet, the crowd was noisy. Jiang Hai came over with a glass of wine and toasted her. "Classmate Yao, you worked hard. You danced beautifully." His praise was simple, but his eyes were bright and sincere.
Later, from classmates who had taken part in that performance, Yao Yuan "happened" to hear some news. Jiang Hai's family was no ordinary one. Three generations had been in government, with deep roots in the Beijing circle, a true "old establishment family." The classmates were vague about the details, speaking with a touch of mystery and envy.
Hearing it, the precise scale in Yao Yuan's heart tilted ever so slightly.
And Jiang Hai's pursuit of her was ardent and direct, yet not off-putting. He had none of those overblown romantic gestures, but he would wait at her door with a thermos of late-night supper when she rehearsed deep into the night; he would go to great lengths through other people to track down some out-of-print professional book she had casually mentioned wanting; before she went onstage he would send a brief "Good luck, you're the best," and then quietly watch her entire performance from the most inconspicuous corner below the stage.
His goodness was solid and real, lodged in the details, carrying a near-clumsy sincerity that didn't quite match his family background.
Yao Yuan was not made of stone. On the contrary, beneath all those days of disciplined, goal-driven training and cold-eyed observation, didn't she too, deep down, hide an ordinary girl's faint yearning for pure love? It was only that this yearning had been tightly wrapped and pressed down by thick layers of reason and reality.
Jiang Hai's appearance was like a beam of sunlight, shining without permission into the defenses she had so carefully built, brick by brick, out of calculations of profit. His family was the ladder she wanted, and the man himself... seemed, as it happened, to be exactly the type that could make her heart move. Clear-eyed calculation and unconscious attraction, subtly intertwined.
They got together as a matter of course, on Jiang Hai's explicit promise that marriage was the goal. After graduation, on the strength of her excellent grades and the fame she'd built up through several performances, Yao Yuan smoothly joined a fairly prestigious dance troupe in Beijing. Jiang Hai's job was stable but often required night shifts. They rented a one-bedroom apartment near his workplace, not large, but Yao Yuan made it warm and tidy. It was the first time she had a little home that was completely "her own," full of the warmth of everyday living.
The early days were as sweet as if soaked in honey. When she went out of town with the troupe and they were apart for ten days or half a month, Jiang Hai would always have the little place spotless by the time she came back, with her favorite flowers on the table. A short parting was sweeter than a wedding night; the brief separations only made their reunions more tender and clinging. She sank into a steady happiness she had never known before, and even began to think that maybe love won through calculation could be real too.
In the second year, because of his outstanding ability, Jiang Hai was transferred to the Ministry of Commerce. His prospects were bright, but his business trips suddenly became frequent and longer. Sometimes he'd be gone for two or three months at a stretch. In the days of guarding an empty room alone, Yao Yuan would sometimes drift off while practicing in front of the mirror. The longing was real; the comfort his voice brought over the phone was real; and when she received the small gifts he had gone to such trouble to have someone carry back from far away, gifts perhaps not very valuable but full of feeling, the leap of joy was real too.
She began to feel a faint flicker of panic. She found herself caring more and more about his comfort, aching over the toil of his travels. Once, while out shopping, she saw a wristwatch she liked very much, expensive. Sun Feifei, who performed alongside her, had an identical one, said to be a gift from her rich-heir boyfriend, worth several months of his salary. In the past, she might have hinted demurely that she liked it and waited for him to give it to her. But that time she hesitated for a long while and in the end said nothing. She even started learning to make soup, and on the rare days he was home, she fumbled together a table of not-quite-tasty dishes.
"A woman who aches for a man is unlucky for life, miserable to the end." In the depths of a midnight dream, that cold, almost cruel voice from the bottom of her heart would leap out and issue its sharp warning. She would jolt awake in alarm, look at the sleeping profile of the man beside her, and feel a chill run through her. She knew she was straying from her original purpose, sinking into a dangerous descent called "love." Yet the next time, when he came home travel-worn, dropping with exhaustion but still pulling her tightly into his arms, that flicker of clear-eyed warning was easily shattered by the warmth of his body and the familiar scent of him. She analyzed herself clearly even as she sank helplessly into it, like someone dancing at the edge of a cliff, knowing the danger yet hypnotized by the scenery beneath their feet.
And yet every story that seems beautiful seems doomed to a sudden, graceless ending. And that ending is often unworthy of the sincere, passionate opening it began with.
After two years of living together, Yao Yuan felt the time was about right. Once, after a tender moment, she nestled in Jiang Hai's arms, her fingertip tracing absent circles on his chest, and mentioned, as if in passing, that a colleague in the troupe had just registered her marriage and taken very beautiful wedding photos. Jiang Hai was silent for a while, then kissed her forehead and said, "It's about time I took my wife home to meet my mom and dad."
The meeting took place at a Jiang family residence that wasn't lived in often, yet still managed to look understated and refined. Jiang Hai's parents had fine bearing, dressed impeccably, smiled politely, and yet carried a natural, casual distance. Throughout the meal, Yao Yuan's back stayed stiff. They asked her in detail about her work, how often she performed, what sort of people she came into contact with. Learning that she had lost her father young, that her mother had remarried, and that she had a ten-year-old half-brother by the same mother, they only nodded slightly, betraying no emotion. Then, as if chatting idly, they mentioned that dancers lived on their youth, that being out in the public eye all the time was never quite steady, and that a family like theirs needed a daughter-in-law who could sit firmly in the background, not draw so much attention. At last, as if suddenly remembering, Jiang Hai's mother said to him with a smile, "Oh, right, Uncle Zhou's daughter is coming back from England next week, isn't she? That girl used to love running after you when she was little. Uncle Zhou called just the other day, said the two families haven't gotten together in ages, and the children are all grown now..."
As her words fell, an eerie silence settled over the dining room for a moment. Jiang Hai frowned and was about to speak, but a faint glance from his father stopped him.
After that meal, a thorn lodged itself in Yao Yuan's heart. A cold, sharp thorn marked with an unmistakable sign of class. She knew Jiang Hai's parents were not satisfied, not with her family background, not with her profession, and perhaps not even with her as a person. Her old calculation now revealed its naive and cruel side: she had only seen the ladder behind Jiang Hai, and forgotten to weigh whether she was even qualified to set foot on it.
On the way home, Jiang Hai held her hand tightly and said over and over, "Don't overthink it, Yuanyuan. That's just how my parents are, old-fashioned. Who I love, who I marry, is my own business. I only love you. I'll only marry you."
His palm was hot, his tone firm. Yao Yuan leaned against his shoulder and said nothing. But the thorn in her heart only sank deeper. Did she believe him? Perhaps she believed the sincerity of that moment. But reality?
In the days that followed, as if to prove something, Jiang Hai refused the meeting his family had arranged with that "Uncle Zhou's daughter." The consequences showed up quickly. He began to run into inexplicable obstacles at work; a project that had been a sure thing fell through; the promotion he'd been expecting never came up again. The calls from home grew colder with each one. Jiang Hai came home later and later, his face darker and darker. The steady, reliable young man he had once been now carried a stubborn gloom and irritation in his brow.
Their first fierce quarrel happened on a rainy night. The cause was small, nothing more than Yao Yuan asking a few extra questions because he had once again broken his promise and failed to come to an important performance of hers. The accumulated pressure, frustration, grievance, and that deeply buried thorn were all set off in that instant. She accused him of being dismissive, of not caring about her feelings; he roared that she was being unreasonable, that she had no idea how much pressure he was under. Words flew toward each other's most fragile places like poison-dipped knives, beyond all control. He said she "knew nothing but dancing, only knew how to take," and she sneered back, "Right, I'm beneath you, so go find your well-matched Miss Zhou!"
By the end of the fight, both of them were utterly spent, staring at each other with red eyes, like strangers, like enemies. In that moment, Yao Yuan saw clearly that something had shattered between them.
Later, Jiang Hai apologized, reeking of alcohol and heavy exhaustion. He held her, buried his head in the crook of her neck, and said hoarsely, "Yuanyuan, I'm sorry... I'm just so tired... Let's not fight anymore, all right?"
Life seemed to return to a surface calm. He still brought her gifts; she still left a night light on for him. But something, in the end, was different. That unreserved closeness and sweetness was gone. When they were together, there was now a careful, probing wariness and an unspeakable distance. In bed, the fierce motions were more like a release than a communion.
Two more months passed. On an ordinary weekend afternoon, Yao Yuan met a close friend for afternoon tea at a newly opened mall in Wangfujing. Just as they were chatting happily, her friend suddenly nudged her with an elbow and gestured with her eyes toward something off to the front.
Yao Yuan followed her gaze, and in an instant her whole body went rigid, her blood running cold.
Not far away, at the counter of a brand-name jewelry store, Jiang Hai stood slightly turned to one side, and beside him was a young girl in a Chanel skirt-suit. The girl was holding a necklace up to her neck, tilting her head back and laughing as she asked him something. Jiang Hai looked down at the necklace in her hand, his profile the one she knew so well, but his expression one she hadn't seen in a long time: relaxed, even gentle. The girl was lovely, the kind of beauty that came obviously from a well-off family and careful pampering, with a refined air and a bright smile.
An hour earlier, Jiang Hai had been on the phone, telling her in that familiar voice, faintly tired but gentle, "I have to work overtime tonight, there's an urgent job, I can't come back to have dinner with you. Remember to eat well on your own."
The voice from the receiver still lingered in her ears, yet the scene before her stabbed into her eyes like the sharpest icicle, piercing straight through her heart. A sense of absurdity, cold and sharp, swept over her in an instant. So this was the answer. So clear, so blunt, so... unbearable.
She didn't move, and she didn't rush over right away. A faint curve even slowly rose at the corners of her mouth, one she herself wasn't aware of. That smile was cold, hollow, carrying a near-masochistic clarity. She watched them, watched the girl naturally loop her arm through Jiang Hai's, watched Jiang Hai seem to freeze for a moment, yet not push her away at once.
Then she drew her gaze back and said to her friend, "Let's go."
She rose, picked up her bag, and the route toward the mall exit happened to pass right by the door of that jewelry store. She didn't slow her steps. Only in the instant she passed parallel to Jiang Hai, she turned her head slightly, let her calm gaze fall on his face, frozen in shock and then drained white, and in a voice neither loud nor soft, just clear enough for the few people nearby to hear, she greeted him lightly:
"Hi, Director Jiang, what a coincidence. Out shopping with your girlfriend?"
Her tone was as flat as if she'd asked, "Have you eaten?" With that, without even waiting for his reaction, without sparing the girl a single glance, she linked arms with her equally dumbstruck friend and walked steadily, straight out, into the clamoring crowds and the blinding sunlight outside the mall.

========================================

Chapter 7 — Traveling Back to Tell My 22-Year-Old Self to Break Up
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640254.html

It wasn't until she was sitting in the taxi, had given the address of that little nest, and had shut the door, sealing out the whole world, that she let her ramrod-straight spine slowly collapse. She didn't cry. She only felt tired — a cold exhaustion seeping out from the marrow of her bones.
She went back to that little apartment, once full of sweetness, now offering nothing but suffocation. She closed the door and bolted it. With her back against the cold panel, she slid slowly down to sit on the floor. The room still carried the smell of the aftershave he'd used that morning; the shirt he'd changed out of last night lay draped over the sofa; the snacks they'd bought together at the supermarket the week before, half-eaten, still sat on the coffee table.
Silence — boundless silence — surged in like a tide and drowned her.
Then the tears came, without any warning, pouring out. Not soft sobbing, but the broken, hoarse weeping that bursts from deep in the throat after being held back to the breaking point. She clamped a hand hard over her mouth, but the tears ran wild, blurring her vision in an instant, dripping onto the cold floor and spreading into dark stains.
She cried until her whole body shook, until she couldn't stop. Reason screamed in her head: What good is crying? Does it solve anything? Will it change his mind? Beyond showing your weakness and helplessness, what use is it? But emotion was a flood that had breached the dam, sweeping away every levee reason had built. All that past sweetness, those warm little moments, those late-night embraces, those careful, hopeful glimpses of a future together — all of it turned into sharp shards, churning madly inside her heart. She'd thought she was a clear-eyed player, counting her chips, when in truth, at some moment she couldn't even pinpoint, she had already wagered her whole heart. That heart was now the biggest joke of all, and the deepest pain.
She didn't know how long she cried. The tears seemed to run dry, leaving only a physical hiccuping and a dull, throbbing ache at her temples. Slowly the crying stopped. In the dim daylight she looked at the puddle of tears on the floor. Her gaze, blank with pain, gradually gathered a cold light again.
It was over. It had to be over.
But — how to end it?
Tear off the mask, make a scene like a woman cast aside? No. That would be ugly, and it would change nothing; it would only make him and his family despise her more, and make her even more pitiful.
Pretend not to know, keep up this hollow relationship, and wait for him to pass sentence? No. Her pride wouldn't allow it.
That left only one path: to leave with dignity. But before she left, she needed compensation.
