Not for the money itself, but for the two years of youth she had given, the sincerity she had given, and the dignity that had been so cheaply trampled. A "settlement" large enough to let her leave the capital and start over — or a job opportunity somewhere far from here, where she could find her footing.
Jiang Hai surely felt some guilt toward her, and some residue of feeling. But that guilt and that feeling had to yield before his career. You only had to look at how his career had stalled the moment he defied his family's authority, and how, for the sake of that career, he had chosen to compromise and go meet Miss Zhou, to understand. Men, where their own interests are concerned, are always more practical than women.
Use that guilt to win herself the greatest advantage, and then turn and leave, cleanly. No clinging, no lingering — close this chapter for good.
That was what she, Yao Yuan, should do.
The only question was how to broach it, so as to get what she wanted. Negotiate calmly, or play the victim and plead through tears? How much to ask for, so that he would feel it "worth it" to pay and put the matter to rest, yet not so much that it provoked a backlash from him and his family?
She needed to think it through. Carefully, coldly, think it through.
It was deep into the night by the time Jiang Hai pushed the door open.
Only a single dim floor lamp was on in the room, its light barely tracing the curled-up figure on the sofa. Yao Yuan hugged her knees, her face buried in the crook of her arm, her long hair fallen loose and disheveled, hiding most of her face. She was still wearing the outfit she'd gone out in that afternoon, only badly creased now, as if she had held this pose for a very long time — long enough to almost melt into the gloomy play of light and shadow.
At the sound of the door, her shoulders trembled almost imperceptibly, but she didn't lift her head.
The air was thick with a silence close to solid, and with… a very faint, lingering trace of the salt of dried tears. Jiang Hai's steps halted in the entryway, his fingers tightening reflexively around the cold metal door handle. Everything familiar in the living room — the beige sofa cover she had chosen with such care, the spider plant on the windowsill that she always forgot to water yet that stubbornly lived on, the photo-booth strip on the TV cabinet from their trip to the amusement park last year — all of it now seemed coated in frosted glass, gone blurry and far away. His throat tightened. The serene, almost empty face she'd worn at the mall that afternoon overlapped with the figure now huddled in the shadows, so fragile it might shatter at a touch, and it struck him with a dull, heavy blow.
He changed his shoes, his movements soft, as if afraid of disturbing something. He walked to the sofa and stopped at a distance — not too close, not too far. The lamplight threw a long, silent shadow at his feet.
"Yuanyuan," he said. His voice was badly hoarse, as if he hadn't spoken in a long time, or as if something were lodged in his throat.
The figure on the sofa didn't move.
He stepped closer, crouched down, brought his eyes level with hers. From this angle he could see the sliver of profile that showed past the crook of her arm. Her skin was bloodless and pale, her eyelids red and swollen, her thick lashes wet and clumped together, still trembling ever so faintly. She hadn't cried out loud, hadn't even hiccuped, but that silent posture of utter, abandoned fragility hit harder than any wailing. This was not a performance — Jiang Hai knew it. Yao Yuan might scheme, might weigh her options, might put on all kinds of masks, but this kind of instinctive pain, completely drained of strength, could not be faked.
His heart felt as if an invisible hand had wrung it hard, and a sour ache spread instantly to every limb. The panic, the embarrassment, even the flicker of irritation at being questioned that he'd felt the moment he was caught that afternoon — all of it had long since vanished, leaving only an overwhelming guilt and… a deep helplessness. He reached out, wanting to touch her shoulder, but his fingertips froze in midair just before they met the thin fabric.
"This afternoon…" He struggled to assemble the words, each one like grit scraping his throat. "That was Uncle Zhou's daughter. She just got back from England, and the family insisted I keep her company — have a meal, walk around a bit. I… I didn't tell you because I was afraid you'd read too much into it." The explanation was pale and powerless; even he couldn't convince himself.
At last Yao Yuan responded. Very, very slowly, she lifted her head. Tear tracks crisscrossed her face, her eyes were red and swollen, but her gaze was blank, a calm that bordered on lifeless. She looked at him with those eyes as if looking at a stranger, or as if looking through him at some empty point beyond.
"Afraid I'd read too much into it?" she said. Her voice was hoarse and broken from long silence and from crying, yet strangely flat, with little emotional rise or fall — she simply repeated those words evenly, as if savoring some utterly absurd joke. Then she tugged ever so faintly at the corner of her mouth, a curve uglier than crying. "Jiang Hai, I look… pretty stupid, don't I?"
"No! Yuanyuan, I…" Jiang Hai tried desperately to defend himself, tried to grab her hand, but she dodged it lightly and yet with absolute firmness. The motion was small, but it fell between them like a cold chasm, suddenly and starkly.
"Don't bother," Yao Yuan cut him off, burying her face back in the crook of her arm, leaving only the crown of her dark hair showing. Her voice came out muffled, carrying a hollowness exhausted to its limit. "I'm tired, Jiang Hai. Really tired."
That night, for the first time, they lay in the same bed, but back to back, with a gap between them wide enough to fit another person. The silence was heavy as something solid, pressing on the chest, making it hard to breathe. Jiang Hai could hear the small, suppressed tremor of her breathing, could feel her body held rigid, perfectly still. He knew she wasn't asleep — maybe crying, maybe just lying with her eyes open, staring into the thick darkness. He wanted to turn and hold her, to melt that cold barrier with his body heat, to say something more, even the most useless apology. But his body felt nailed in place. The shadow of his family, his parents' icy words, the Zhou daughter's tasteful, sure-of-herself smile, and Yao Yuan's hollow gaze that afternoon… countless images wove and tangled together, binding him so he couldn't move. In the end, he too just lay with his eyes open, staring at the blurred outline of the ceiling, until the sky outside the window slowly turned a cold pale blue.
The days that followed became a silent, drawn-out torture.
Yao Yuan no longer brought up that day, no longer pressed him, no longer even showed any clear emotion. She went to rehearsal with her dance troupe as usual, came home, cooked, tidied the apartment. On the surface, life seemed to return to some kind of "normal" track. But everything was different. She spoke very little now, her gaze often vacant, able to stare quietly at some spot for a long time. She no longer smiled at him, no longer clung to him to share the funny things from the troupe, no longer left that warm lamp on to wait up when he came home late. She still prepared his meals, but no longer troubled herself over the flavors he liked — just simple food, enough to keep a body alive. At night she always washed up early, lay down with her back to him, her breathing shallow as if already asleep. But Jiang Hai knew she wasn't.
A refined, icy politeness hung between them. Like two people forced to live together — the most familiar of strangers.
Jiang Hai tried to make amends. He turned down unnecessary social engagements and came home on time whenever he could. He bought expensive skincare, bought the dress she'd once glanced at twice but couldn't bring herself to buy, booked restaurants that were hard to get into. She accepted everything, only saying a flat "thank you." The dress went into the back of the closet with its tag still on; the restaurant reservations were always canceled in the end because of "an extra rehearsal called at the troupe" or "not feeling well." He fumbled for things to talk about, mentioning matters at work, mentioning a recent movie. She only answered with "mm" and "oh," her gaze drifting, plainly somewhere else.
This silent, all-encompassing distance suffocated Jiang Hai more than any quarrel. A quarrel at least vented emotion, carried a desire to communicate. But the Yao Yuan of now was like a plant withering fast, drawing in all its leaves, sealing herself inside a glass dome he could not reach. Her pain was turned inward, yet it was everywhere, filling every corner of the place they had once called "home," silently condemning him.
He watched her cheeks grow gaunt so quickly, watched the dark circles under her eyes deepen, watched the profound exhaustion and sorrow in her profile when she sometimes stared blankly out the window. The guilt and helplessness inside him grew by the day, nearly drowning him. He knew she was in pain, and he was the one who had caused that pain yet could offer no cure.
Finally, after a dinner that was just as maddeningly silent, Yao Yuan put down her chopsticks, her bowl of rice barely touched. She raised her eyes and looked at him, her gaze calm and unrippled, yet it made Jiang Hai's heart clench hard.
"Jiang Hai," she said softly, every word clear, "there's no point in us going on like this."
The hand holding his chopsticks jerked. His throat went dry. "Yuanyuan…"
"I'm not making a scene with you." Yao Yuan cut him off, even smiling faintly — a smile thin as a sliver of early-winter ice, breaking at a touch. "I just think we both know some things can't go back to how they were. And I'm tired. I can't keep performing."
She used the word "performing." Jiang Hai's heart felt as if a needle had pricked it.
"I'm sorry…" Of a thousand things he might say, what finally rose to his lips were those three pale, powerless words.
Yao Yuan shook her head, her gaze sweeping across this little apartment full of memories. For an instant her eyes turned dazed, then settled back into that heart-stopping calm. "Sorry doesn't solve anything. Jiang Hai, we're not children anymore. Reality is right here in front of us." She paused, as if gathering her courage, then looked straight into his eyes, with a resolve like burning one's boats. "I started dating you at twenty-two. Half a year in, you asked me to move in together, and we agreed it was on the premise of marriage. And now my best years have all gone into this. I'm about to turn twenty-five. Four of the girlfriends my age are already married, and you still can't decide your own marriage. I never wanted anything from your family, but now…" She drew a breath. Her voice stayed steady, but carried a barely perceptible tremble. "I want some account settled. Not with you — with myself, for these years."
There it was. Jiang Hai closed his eyes. He'd known this moment would come. Guilt, pain, the residue of love, and that family yoke he couldn't break free of all collided fiercely inside his chest. He couldn't give her marriage, couldn't give her the future she wanted, couldn't even give her a clear promise. All he seemed able to give was something… practical, to patch over the debt he'd created, to ease his own guilt a little, and perhaps — humbly — in the hope that it might make her road ahead a little less hard, might let her be… a little better off.
He opened his eyes, the whites laced with red, his voice dry. "I understand. I'm the one who wronged you." He paused for a long time, long enough that the air seemed to congeal, before forcing out a string of numbers. "Is this amount enough? I know it can't make up for anything, but at least… it can give you some breathing room for a while, let you live well."
He named a fairly considerable sum. For him at the time, it wasn't easy, but he could manage it. It was more than just money — it was like an ugly, one-sided compensation agreement, putting a clear price tag on the feelings they had once shared.
Yao Yuan looked at him quietly, no expression on her face. No anticipated anger, no humiliation, no joy. Just that — looking at this man she had once loved with all her heart, with whom she had planned a future, now pricing their relationship in the most practical of terms. A sharp pain stabbed where her heart was, but it was quickly covered over by a colder numbness. So be it, she thought. This way was fine. Bare and naked was always better than dragging on through hypocrisy and entanglement.
She didn't answer at once, only lowered her eyes, her long lashes casting faint shadows on her pale face. After a long while, she said, barely audible: "All right."
No haggling, no crying or accusations — just a brief "all right." That was even harder for Jiang Hai to bear than a violent reaction. He would rather she curse him, hit him, throw the money in his face. But she simply accepted it, with the resigned calm of someone accepting an established fact.
The money soon arrived in Yao Yuan's account. The figure sat cold in the text notification. She didn't touch it, as if guarding some pitiful boundary. The days went on in that suffocating silence and politeness, only now that enormous sum standing between them was an invisible yet heavy wall, pushing them further apart. They still lived under one roof, yet were colder than flatmates. The occasional physical contact — unavoidable, like fingertips brushing when handing something over — sprang apart as if from an electric shock, leaving an awkward blank.
Both of them grew haggard at a visible pace. Jiang Hai smoked harder and harder, his eyes often bloodshot. His work seemed to hit even greater resistance, and he came home wrapped in a low pressure so thick it wouldn't disperse. Yao Yuan grew even more silent. Sometimes, cooking in the kitchen, she would suddenly stop, staring blankly into space until whatever was in the pot burned and the smell of scorching drifted up.
It was a slow, mutual torment, a wearing-away. Each bore the consequences of their choice, and the pain of losing the other. Only, in Yao Yuan's pain there was tempered a clear-eyed hatred and a resolute self-preservation; while in Jiang Hai's pain there twined more helplessness, more guilt, and a surrender to his family's fate.
Another month passed in the long ordeal, and the weather had turned from hot summer toward cool autumn.
One weekend, early in the morning, the sky was overcast, low leaden clouds pressing down. Yao Yuan got up very early and, rarely, put on light makeup to cover the worst of her haggard color. She changed into a crisp shirt and trousers and walked up to Jiang Hai, who was sitting at the dining table staring blankly at his coffee.
"Jiang Hai," she called him, her voice clear and calm, as if rehearsed countless times. "Let's break up."
The fingers holding his coffee cup tightened suddenly, knuckles going white. He raised his head and looked at her. She stood at the window, backlit, her features a little blurred, but her spine straight. He opened his mouth to say something, only to find every word pale and powerless. Beg her to stay? With what would he hold her? Promises? He couldn't afford them. Delay? That would only prolong the pain.
In the end he heard his own dry voice come out, carrying a resigned exhaustion: "…All right."
No question, no struggle — just an equally brief "all right." It set the final seal on this slow death that had dragged on for nearly months.
Yao Yuan nodded, as if she'd long foreseen this answer. She turned, took a folded document from her bag, set it gently on the table, and pushed it toward him.
"This is my job-transfer application," she said, looking at him, her gaze clear to the bottom, holding no resentment and no longing — only a calm desolation. "I want to transfer out of the capital, go back to my hometown, leave here. I'll need you… or your family's connections, to put in a word."
At last, in the most dignified and most thorough way, she demanded the final "compensation" for this love she had calculated over, sunk into, and finally lost so badly: a chance to leave behind every sorrowful piece of the past and start completely over. Not a plea — a cold transaction, trading the last scrap of feeling and guilt left between them for a clean exit.
Jiang Hai looked at that thin application, then at Yao Yuan's resolute, calm face. He knew that this time, she really was leaving. Leaving this apartment, leaving his life, leaving this city full of memory and pain. And he didn't even have the right to ask her to stay.
He reached out and took the application. The paper was light, but in his hand it weighed more than a thousand pounds.
"…All right." He said the word a third time, his voice low and hoarse, as if he'd spent every ounce of his strength. "I'll handle it."
Yao Yuan took one last look around this small "home," her gaze resting briefly on each familiar object, then turned without hesitation and walked toward the door. She didn't look back, and she didn't say goodbye.
She opened the door, and the gloomy wind outside rushed in.
She straightened her spine, walked out, and gently pulled the door shut behind her.
A soft "click."
Shutting two people, and that old time tangled with sweetness, scheming, sinking, and betrayal, forever behind her.
Over the next two months, time flowed with an almost eerie efficiency — fast enough to make her dizzy, yet slow enough to grind certain scars into rough calluses.
Yao Yuan never went back to that apartment. She moved into the temporary dormitory the troupe provided, a narrow but tidy single room with nothing in it but a bed, a desk, and a crude wardrobe. Most of the things that belonged to the "past" she gave away if she could donate them, threw out if she couldn't, keeping only a few necessary clothes and the smallest handful of personal items, packed into one not-very-large suitcase. That case stood in the corner like a silent footnote, ready to set off at any moment.
She and Jiang Hai never met again. All further communication was handled through cold, efficient texts. The money in her account she didn't spend a cent of — a sleeping stone of shame, settled at the bottom of the bank account. The transfer seemed to move along on Jiang Hai's end with unusual smoothness — or perhaps some force, eager to smooth over the "trouble," was quietly pushing it through. She only had to fill out the forms as required, submit her materials, and wait.
In the waiting days she went to the troupe as usual. Rehearsing, performing, smiling and chatting with colleagues, joining group activities. She acted perfectly normal, even more "part of the group" than before. Only she no longer danced lead, stepping back of her own accord to an inconspicuous spot in the rear; and during breaks she often sat alone in a corner, watching the young figures spinning and leaping in the mirror, her gaze vast and empty, as if through them she were watching herself in another time and place, equally full of longing, equally sure she had grasped her destiny. The troupe was not without its whispers — about her sudden quietness, about how her "distinguished" boyfriend seemed to have stopped showing up. But Yao Yuan wrapped herself in a flawless, polite, distant calm that sealed off every probing gaze. She was like a plant abruptly transplanted to barren ground, drawing in all its showy branches and leaves, maintaining only the most basic signs of life.
The notice came on an overcast afternoon. The text alert sounded, from an unfamiliar internal-system number. In terse official wording it informed her that her transfer application had been approved: she was to report to the Jinshi Provincial Department of Culture and Tourism, posted as a choreographer and grassroots arts instructor, with the term to be announced separately. No congratulations, no welcome — just a cool personnel order.
She stared at the phone screen for a long time, until the black characters blurred and bled together before her eyes. There was none of the relief she'd imagined, nor any deeper pain. The place where her heart was felt hollow, only a faint, numb chill spreading along her limbs. At last, it was settled. This way out, bought with nearly three years of youth, a heartbreak, and a cold sum of "compensation," now lay clearly before her.
She didn't tell Jiang Hai. He seemed to learn of it through other channels anyway. The day after she got the notice, that long-silent number sent a text — just four characters and a single mark:
"Safe travels."
Yao Yuan didn't reply. She deleted the message, and the number along with it, wiping it from her contacts completely. Like brushing away the last speck of dust on a table.
The day she left the capital, the year's first scattered snowflakes drifted down, melting into a cold, wet sleet before they reached the ground, dampening the shoulders of passersby and the surface of suitcases. No send-off, no farewells. The troupe leaders said a few perfunctory words of encouragement, some colleagues showed a flicker of regret, but most were busy with their own schedules. Pulling her not-very-large suitcase, a simple backpack on her shoulders, Yao Yuan stood at the troupe's entrance and took one last look back at the gray building from which she had once thought she would leap into a brilliant future. A snowflake landed on her bare neck, ice-cold.
She turned, ducked into a waiting taxi, and headed straight for the train station. She hadn't chosen the faster option of flying, as if she needed a slow, tangible journey over land to confirm the widening distance, to leave behind, bit by bit, that vast city that had carried too much dream and too much pain.
The train rumbled out of the platform, the city's high-rise skyline gradually receding under the leaden sky, shrinking, finally vanishing at the horizon. The scenery beyond the window shifted from the bustle of city streets to the bare winter fields, the gray hazy villages, the bleak woods dusted with thin snow. Yao Yuan sat by the window, her face pressed to the cold glass, her eyes unfocused on the monotonous landscape rushing past outside. The noisy chatter in the car, a child crying, the rattling wheels of the snack cart… all of it seemed to be behind a thick pane of glass, distant and unreal.
She remembered many years ago, also winter, when she had carried a big suitcase, her acceptance letter and a leaping heart tucked close, traveling from her third-tier hometown to the capital, that political center where dragons crouch and tigers hide. Back then the world beyond the window had been brand new, glittering, full of endless possibility. And now, the same journey, run in reverse, carried away only a suitcase emptied of its contents and a weary, patched-up shell of a body.
In the pure white void, Yao Yuan and the young Jiang Hai on the balcony looked at each other in silence across that invisible yet unmistakable boundary.
Time seemed stretched and frozen by this eerie scene. Yao Yuan could make out the fine down on his lashes lit by the sun, could see his Adam's apple roll faintly, could even see a faint, not-quite-washed-out stain at the collar of his white T-shirt. After she asked, "What year is it this year," her heart hung suspended, waiting for the foregone answer, and waiting too for… some verification or shock she herself couldn't quite name.
And yet the young Jiang Hai's expression did not change at all after she spoke. The sharp scrutiny didn't disappear, but it mixed with a thicker confusion, even… a barely perceptible doubt about his own senses. His gaze didn't land precisely on her eyes, but drifted slightly off, as if staring at the blank white emptiness behind her, or, through her, at some point he couldn't bring into focus.
He… couldn't hear her? No — perhaps it wasn't only that he couldn't hear. Yao Yuan raised her voice again, even taking a step forward, her hand almost touching the edge of the scene. "Jiang Hai! Look at me! Can you hear me? I'm right here!"
This time Jiang Hai seemed to sense something, tilting his head slightly. Not the sound — more like a faint change in the air current, or simply instinct.
He frowned, lifted his arm to wipe the sweat from his forehead, and turned, as if to examine every inch of the balcony — his gaze sweeping over the rusted railing, the thriving green plants, the cluttered corner, then settling back on the "empty spot" where Yao Yuan stood, where the scene met the pure white. In his eyes, the clear confusion was replaced by an almost instinctive wariness, as if he had sensed some discordant "presence" he couldn't capture with his senses.
"Strange…" he muttered to himself, shaking his head, then turned back, twisted on the faucet, and went on focusing on the work in his hands, water rushing loudly.
Yao Yuan stood frozen, cold all over. She understood now. In this time and place, in this "mirror" world, to Jiang Hai she did not exist — or was at least a "ghost" he could not perceive. She could see and hear everything about him, while he had no awareness of her at all. An immense sense of absurdity and an even deeper loneliness drowned her. She was like a prisoner forced to watch footage of bygone days — able to see, but unable to touch, unable to change, unable even to be seen.
Just as Yao Yuan felt a cold helplessness at this utter "being ignored" and "untouchability," the glass door connecting the balcony to the living room slid open with a sharp rasp.
A wave of heat, wrapped in the breath of youth, rushed at her.
"Jiang Hai, Jiang Hai! Quick, quick, quick! Catch it! It's melting!"
A figure came barreling onto the balcony, bringing a roomful of liveliness and commotion. It was the twenty-two-year-old Yao Yuan. Her hair was in a high ponytail, and from running and the heat a few loose strands clung wetly to her smooth forehead and slender neck. She wore a goose-yellow sleeveless dress, the hem just reaching her knees, baring straight, fair calves, simple white sandals on her feet. Her face flushed with healthy color from running and excitement, her eyes bright, she held up two cream popsicles dripping fast with sugary water, almost too slippery to hold.
"Slow down, you scatterbrain." Jiang Hai's words were scolding, but his tone was full of laughter and indulgence. He quickly set down what was in his hands, reached out to take the two "imperiled" popsicles, and handed one back to her. "Here, yours. Why'd you buy these? Half of it's melted on the way home — don't we have some in the fridge?"
"Couldn't wait till we got home! And these are the best!" The young Yao Yuan eagerly licked the cream about to drip off, narrowing her eyes in satisfaction, like a cat that had stolen a treat. She sidled up to Jiang Hai and, naturally, rested her free hand on his sweat-damp arm, not minding the stickiness at all, craning to look into the sink. "What're you washing there?"
"A circuit board." Jiang Hai turned his head to look at her, the sun scattering fine light at the smiling corners of his eyes. Easily he lifted a hand and, with the clean back of it, wiped a bit of nearly melted cream off the tip of her nose. "Look at you, sweating buckets, like you've been fished out of the water. Go inside and sit under the fan."
"Don't want to, there's a breeze on the balcony." Yao Yuan refused to budge, taking another bite of the popsicle off Jiang Hai's hand, cheeks puffed full, mumbling indistinctly, "So hurry up and wash. There's the singing contest on 'Tonight's Great Voice' tonight — you're watching it with me!"
"Fine, I'll watch it with you." Jiang Hai agreed readily, his gaze falling on her lips, gone glossy from the popsicle, his eyes deepening.
Yao Yuan seemed to notice, her face going a little redder, but boldly she met his gaze, licked her lips, and smiled like a sly little fox. The air was thick with the sweet, cloying scent of the popsicles, the dry heat of the summer afternoon, and the sweet, sticky pull between young lovers that needed no words.
Standing in the pure white void, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan watched this scene in silence. That girl in the goose-yellow dress, vivid as a summer sunflower — every expression, every small gesture, radiated unreserved love and joy. This direct, blazing sweetness, without a trace of shadow, was like a blunt instrument warmed by the sun striking her chest, bringing a dull ache and a powerful, time-displaced dizziness. This was the summer she was twenty-two, the most passionate and purest period of her love with Jiang Hai, not yet eroded by the frost and storms of reality — when her heart and eyes were full of nothing but the other person and a future within reach, when her biggest worry was probably what gift to choose for the coming Qixi.
She watched her young self rise on her toes and quickly kiss Jiang Hai on the cheek, then run off giggling, the goose-yellow hem skimming the old floor like a butterfly. Jiang Hai touched the spot where he'd been kissed, shaking his head with a helpless laugh, his gaze following that figure the whole way until she vanished inside. Only then did he draw his eyes back and pick up the circuit board again, the smile lingering long at the corners of his mouth, even his washing motions turned lighter.
That evening they squeezed onto the small sofa, excitedly watching 'The Voice of China,' kissing at the dull parts, embracing, planning how they would decorate the bigger house they'd have someday, bickering and making up over what to name their child…
All of this, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan remembered. More clearly, even, than what she now saw with her own eyes — because it was a faded memory she had turned over and over with years and pain. And now this memory replayed before her, so bare, so vivid, while she was nothing but a ghost shut out from it. She watched her young self, and in her eyes were complicated tremors, a cold scrutiny, and a distant nostalgia she herself was unwilling to admit. That naive girl, who thought she had grasped the whole world, whose eyes and heart held only love and the future — how strange she was, and how… pitiable.
The night grew late. Jiang Hai washed up in the bathroom, water running loudly. The young Yao Yuan finished her shower first and, in a cotton nightdress printed with cartoon characters, her wet long hair wrapped in a towel, hummed a song and sat down at the dressing table. That oval dressing mirror set into the cheap particle-board desk reflected, under the energy-saving lamp, her cheeks flushed pink from the steam and her clear, bright eyes. Her skin was smooth and firm, untouched by the marks of time, the corners of her mouth turned up naturally, holding a baseless expectation for tomorrow. She picked up a bottle of some then-popular Korean-brand aloe gel in flashy packaging, about to apply it.
The mirror's surface suddenly rippled, ever so faintly, like water touched by a breeze.
The young Yao Yuan's hand stopped. She leaned closer, puzzled. The image of herself in the mirror began to blur and warp, as if a film of bathroom steam had settled over it. But the steam didn't disperse; instead, within it, another face slowly took shape — a face more mature, more refined, yet also colder and lonelier, its gaze so complicated it made her heart lurch in an instant. That face had outlines startlingly like her own, but the skin was the carefully cared-for fairness of someone who tended it, the brows trimmed clean and crisp, the lips a vivid red, the eyes deep as a cold pool with no visible bottom, settled with things she couldn't begin to understand — complicated pain, a cold clarity, and a trace of… almost pitying scrutiny? She wore what looked like a very fine silk nightgown.
The young Yao Yuan gasped in fright and jerked back, her spine slamming into the chair back with a dull thud. Her heart hammered wildly in her chest, and the jar of aloe gel in her hand clattered onto the desk. "Who's there?!" Her voice was tight, with an obvious tremor. Instinctively she glanced back at the closed bathroom door, behind which came the sound of Jiang Hai washing up, then whipped back around to stare hard at the mirror.
The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror looked at her young self's terrified eyes, a cold calm in her heart. She knew that perhaps only through this mirror, in this eerie space, could a conversation across time become possible. Slowly, she opened her mouth to the frightened girl in the mirror. Her voice didn't travel through the air, but sounded directly and clearly in the depths of the young Yao Yuan's mind, carrying the particular, unquestionable penetration of someone who has been through the world, each word like a bead of ice falling on a jade plate:
"Don't be afraid."
"Look at me."
"Listen to me."
"Leave Jiang Hai."
"What?!" The young Yao Yuan's pupils shrank suddenly, shock overtaking her initial fear in an instant, replaced by a flame of anger at being violated by this absurd prophecy. "What nonsense are you talking? Who are you? What right do you have to tell me to leave Jiang Hai? We're together and we're fine, and then we'll definitely get marr—!" Her hand instinctively clenched the soft fabric of her nightdress, fingertips going white with the force.
"There's no future in it." The Yao Yuan in the mirror cut her off, her tone flat and absolute, without the slightest room for turning back, as if stating a theorem already proven countless times. "Between the two of you, there is no future. His family will not accept you, and in the end he will compromise. You'll be hurt, you'll be in great pain, you'll lose everything you now hold dear, including your confidence in yourself and your hopes. In the end, you'll only walk away in disgrace with a pitiful bit of compensation, and it'll take you years just to barely piece your broken self back together. The arcs you two trace are destined from the start to cross, and then to part. Family, class, the realities you'll have to face in the future… the sweetness now is only a summer cloudburst — it looks gorgeous and freeing, but in the blink of an eye it leaves nothing but damp and stifling heat. Keep walking this road and you'll be soaked through, you'll catch cold, you'll need a long time to dry yourself out — you might even be left with a chronic ailment."
"No! You're lying!" The young Yao Yuan shook all over with rage, tears rushing uncontrollably to her eyes — not from fear, but from extreme anger and a refusal at being dragged forcibly into a nightmare. "Jiang Hai loves me, we're going to get married! He promised me! What on earth are you? Why have you come to tell me all this? A hacker? Or am I hallucinating from too much stress lately? Are you the future…?" She seemed to catch onto something, yet didn't dare follow the thought, for it was too terrifying. She even reached up and pinched her own arm hard. A clear pain came through, but the image in the mirror did not vanish.
In the eyes of the Yao Yuan in the mirror there seemed to flicker a very faint, almost pitying glimmer, but it was gone so fast it might have been an illusion, immediately covered by a deeper, ice-locked calm — half pity, half mockery. "Who am I — I'm you, thirty-six years old, inexplicably seeing myself from fourteen years ago. I think it's an illusion too. But what I'm telling you matters. You have to think it over seriously, treat it seriously. This is destined to happen. The sweetness you see now is all a soap bubble — one poke and it's gone. While you haven't sunk in deeper, while you can still pull yourself out relatively whole, leave him. Go pursue your career, your dancing, your self. Don't stake your future on a man who will betray you in the end — or rather, who isn't his own master. I'm the far shore you're sailing toward. While there's still time, before the boat has left the harbor too far, you can still turn around. Rely on yourself, and swim out to wider waters. Don't drop your anchor at a dock that will never be yours."
"I don't believe you!" The young Yao Yuan's tears spilled over, but she stubbornly wiped them away and glared at the "self" in the mirror. "Jiang Hai isn't that kind of person! We'll prove it to everyone! You… if you're really the future me, then why are you even here? Why are you so… so cold? What on earth did you go through?"
"What I went through is what you're about to go through." The voice of the Yao Yuan in the mirror grew colder still, as if to freeze every possible ripple of feeling. "Pain, betrayal, self-doubt, a long repair. Believe me, it's not worth it. What you now feel you can't bear to give up will look, to the future you, like just one… expensive mistake. Leaving him now, so that when you look back you can still remember it as a romantic, sweet love — that is the greatest mercy you can show yourself."
"No! I won't listen!" The young Yao Yuan suddenly thrust out her hand, wanting to knock the mirror over, but her fingers only passed through the cold glass and met the solid wall behind it. The image in the mirror swayed, but did not vanish.
"You need to calm down. What I'm telling you is the optimal choice for your life right now — the shortcut that skips the detours and goes straight there. Don't forget why you got together with Jiang Hai in the first place. Rather than relying on someone else to vault you across class lines, make better use of every useful resource in your own hands and turn yourself into someone more excellent, more formidable. Stand on your own, and let the higher class invite you up."
"Of course, you can also choose not to listen. As you can see, the thirty-six-year-old me is doing fine too — only my body and heart have been through breaking, and that feeling of patching yourself back together with everything you have is not pleasant. I trust you don't want that kind of suffering. We're the same person, with the same cool reason. Leaving him is cutting your losses, saving yourself. I trust you'll come around."
With that, the faint light on the mirror's surface receded like an ebbing tide. The Yao Yuan in the mirror gave her young self one last deep look, and that face belonging to the future Yao Yuan quickly blurred and faded…
The pure white space began to ripple faintly, the scene before her like a poorly received television picture, starting to flicker and pull out bars of static. The image of that warm, softly lit little apartment was growing unstable, as if it might dissolve at any moment.
Yao Yuan knew this eerie "mirror" journey was perhaps about to end. But the seed of doubt and unease planted tonight in her young self's heart — would it break the soil at some future moment, speeding up or changing certain trajectories?
Or perhaps some roads are meant to be walked once, oneself, battered and bloodied, before one learns to turn back. Some pains are meant to be tasted firsthand before they can be carved into the bone.
She didn't know.
Before the scene vanished completely, she took one last look at that small home that had held her earliest love and pain. Then she closed her eyes.
The endless, all-devouring white settled over her once more.

========================================

Chapter 8 — Back to Reality: Yu Hao, the One-A.M. "Companion"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640255.html

The endless, all-devouring white pulled back like a receding tide. The sensation of falling stopped abruptly, replaced by a heavy, all-too-real feeling of "landing"—not a physical impact, but the dull thud of consciousness slamming back into its shell.

Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open and she gasped violently, like a drowning woman finally breaking the surface.

Her vision sharpened from a blur, and the first thing she saw was the familiar, finely textured off-white ceiling of her apartment bathroom, and the recessed overhead light spilling its soft glow. Beneath her was the cold, hard tile floor; a dull ache ran through her back and elbows. She was lying flat on the bathroom floor, the hem of her silk robe in disarray, the soles of her bare feet feeling the chill of the tiles seep into her bones.

She was back.

The midnight silence was total, broken only by the faint, low-frequency hum of a city that never sleeps, present like background noise. The water in the tub had long gone cold, its surface flat and still. The vanity mirror was spotless and bright, reflecting her exactly as she was now: face pale as paper, the hair at her forehead soaked with cold sweat and plastered to her skin, her eyes still holding traces of a fright that hadn't fully faded, a daze, and an exhaustion that seemed to cut across time and space. The searing sting in her fingertips was long gone, leaving only smooth skin.

Bracing herself against the cold floor, she slowly sat up; every joint felt rusted. When she gripped the edge of the basin to stand, her legs went weak and she had to lean there a moment. The woman in the mirror slowly pulled her gaze back into focus. The clarity, the composure, and the certain hardness at the core of thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan settled back over her, inch by inch, forcing the shock and emotional aftershock of that bizarre journey through time down to the very deepest part of her eyes.

She reached up and turned off the bathtub tap—it had, astonishingly, been trickling the whole time—then looked around. Everything was exactly as it had been when she "left." Nothing was out of place.

Was it a dream?

No. The details were too vivid—the smell of sweat on a young Jianghai, the dry, dusty heat of the summer air in 2012, the angry tears in the eyes of her twenty-two-year-old self, and finally those bone-chillingly cold words "she" had said to herself... Every single moment was terrifyingly real, branded into her mind, carrying a heavy weight.

She walked into the living room and picked up the phone she'd tossed onto the sofa. The screen lit up, its cold white light making her squint.

The time read: 00:47.

It felt like no more than an hour since she'd stepped into the bathroom. But in that strange space where pure white had interwoven with the past, it had seemed an impossibly long stretch of time.

A vast, cold loneliness, riding the silence of the late night, wrapped around her without a sound. The loneliness didn't come from being alone—it came from the eerie conversation she'd just had with her own past, one she could tell no one, one no one would believe. She was like a traveler who had just tumbled back from a parallel world, road-worn and weathered, with no one to know where she'd been.

She needed a little sound—a living, breathing trace of the "now," of "reality"—to confirm her own existence, to drive away the too-vivid ghosts of the past in her mind. She needed someone... who wouldn't press for details, yet could offer some rational anchor.

Her fingers slid unconsciously across the cold screen, skimming past a string of names both familiar and distant, until at last they stopped on a contact saved as "Yu Hao."

"Boyfriend companion"—that was how she'd positioned Yu Hao in her mind. Thirty years old, a University of Hong Kong graduate, from a family that married media and business. His résumé was the stuff of legend in their circle: he'd made his first bucket of gold in his sophomore year, weathered several setbacks during university—even facing expulsion—yet, on the strength of formidable persuasiveness and resilience, not only graduated smoothly but had already amassed a startling fortune by the time he did. Now he was an independent star financing advisor, specializing in cutting-edge fields like healthcare and tech going global, renowned for his ruthlessly sharp eye, his extreme efficiency, and his knack for linking up top-tier resources. People in the industry described him as "bold and confident, deeply versed in human nature, a man who sets the rules himself."

They'd met a year earlier at a private cocktail reception after a top-tier tech forum. At the time, Yao Yuan had just led her team into exploring AI, seeking strategic resources for the next phase; Yu Hao was one of the youngest invited keynote speakers at that forum, his topic "Efficiency Above All: The Misallocation of Resources in the New Consumer Era." The two of them had slipped away from the noise to the terrace and talked for half an hour over champagne—from choosing the right track to the weaknesses of human nature, from business models to the marginal utility of romantic relationships. No flirtatious probing, only the satisfaction of meeting a worthy opponent and a clear-eyed appraisal of each other's value. Soon they reached an unspoken understanding: for occasions that called for a presentable partner, they were each other's best "face" and conversational extension; in private, they were companions who could sample the city's hottest new restaurants together, talk industry trends and observations on human nature, and occasionally take in a niche art exhibition with a high barrier to entry; and when there was a brief physical or emotional need, they could satisfy each other with the same easy understanding, leaving no loose ends afterward. No talk of profound love, no talk of long-term binding, no intrusion into each other's core social circle or career decisions—keeping a comfortable distance and absolute respect. It was a highly modern "strategic ██ relationship," stripped of romantic illusion, built on powerful rational awareness and the principle of mutual benefit.

Yu Hao met every "practical" requirement she had for a partner at this stage of her life: he was accomplished enough, with a dazzling background, so taking him out was a plus; he was smart enough and on the same wavelength, so the cost of communicating with him was minimal; he was clear-headed enough and clear about his goals, so he wouldn't sink into pointless emotional drain; and most importantly, he was busy enough—and understood the boundaries of this kind of relationship well enough—that he wouldn't over-encroach on her time and attention. As he so often said: "Pick the wrong rules and all your effort is wasted. Only within the right relationship framework can efficiency be maximized."

It was now nearly one in the morning. Making this call was a bit of a breach—it broke their unspoken rule of "no late-night contact unless necessary." But Yao Yuan couldn't be bothered with that now. She needed to hear a calm voice that belonged to the present, a voice that could haul her out of the surreal mire and back onto the solid ground of reality.

The phone rang three times before it was picked up. The background held some kind of very low-volume, slow-paced electronic music, which was promptly switched off, leaving an absolute, focused quiet.

"Yao Yuan?" Yu Hao's voice came through, low, with the slight rasp and clarity unique to someone who had just finished intense mental work—not the faintest trace of irritation at being disturbed, only a precise note of inquiry. His ability to manage his background was first-rate; even taking her call at this hour, his first instinct was to assess priority and potential value—the instinct of a top financing advisor.

"Mm, it's me." Only as she spoke did Yao Yuan realize her voice was a little dry. She cleared her throat and walked to the floor-to-ceiling window, looking out at the sleeping city and its scattered lights. "Did I wake you?"

"Just wrapped up a video call with a Silicon Valley fund partner—jet lag." Yu Hao's tone was steady as ever, carrying a reassuring looseness. "A call at this hour isn't your style. Run into something thorny?"

He didn't ask "what's wrong," he asked "did you run into something thorny," steering the conversation straight to the problem-solving level. That was very Yu Hao.

Yao Yuan was silent for a few seconds. Everything from the world in the mirror churned in her mind, but in the end she chose the safest entry point—the one closest to a part of "reality." She needed to unburden herself, but not about traveling through time; about that cognitive shockwave and sense of emptiness the present had taken on after the past ambushed her without warning.

"I had a very long, very clear dream." Looking at her own blurred reflection in the glass, she spoke slowly, her voice carrying a rare fragility even she hadn't noticed. "I dreamed of years ago, right after I graduated, back when I was in Jingshi—living in a rented room, no air conditioning in summer, treating a router repair like a major event, ready to stamp my feet if my phone froze... The details were terrifyingly real, like... like plugging the hard drive of my memory straight into my brain and playing it back."

The line went quiet for a moment, only Yu Hao's steady breathing. Then he spoke, his voice as calm as if he were analyzing a project due-diligence report. "Retrospective scenario reconstruction. A break in high-intensity work, layered with recent exposure to past-associated information carrying a heavy emotional load—say, Zhao Yiming and Jin Lan, whom you saw this evening, with a conversation touching on emotional AI and memory data. In a relaxed state, the brain's prefrontal cortex is prone to launching a deep memory-sorting program, dramatically recombining fragments of experience that haven't been fully 'archived' or that carry strong emotional markers. It's a psychological defense mechanism, and also a signal of cognitive iteration."

His analysis was calm, rational, proceeding entirely from neuropsychology and real-world associative logic, instantly hauling that bizarre, kaleidoscopic "journey through the mirror" back into the realm of the explicable and the manageable. He'd even pinpointed the likely trigger—the meeting with Zhao Yiming and Jin Lan. This was exactly what Yao Yuan needed right now: a powerful "dimensional reduction strike" grounded in real-world logic and cognitive science, to "normalize" and "technicalize" the surreal experience.

"Maybe." Yao Yuan went along with him, feeling her taut nerves loosen a fraction. "It's just that when I woke up I felt... a little empty. Like I was standing outside of time, watching a film whose ending was already written, but I still had to act it out all over again. It felt a little... absurd. I started to wonder whether the path I've walked these past ten-plus years really had to be walked at all."

"Detachment and existential questioning. A common response after a high-intensity cognitive or emotional shock." Yu Hao's voice came through the receiver, steady as ballast, carrying his particular habit of converting every experience into a cognitive model. "From an evolutionary-psychology standpoint, this ability to 'examine the self from outside of time' is a uniquely human, higher-order cognitive function—the basis for strategic planning and path optimization. Feeling it's 'absurd' means your cognitive system is attempting to reassess and reassign value to the path you've taken. That's a good thing."

He paused, seeming to take a sip of water, and went on: "As for whether it 'had to be walked'... my view is that path dependence is real, but the 'optimal solution' usually only reveals itself in hindsight. The key isn't agonizing over sunk costs already incurred—it's extracting reusable 'algorithms' and a 'guide to avoiding pitfalls' from your existing experience, to optimize your future decision tree. As I always say: pick the wrong rules and all your effort is wasted. But once the rules are validated, you have to squeeze every bit of efficiency out of them."

His words were like a precise scalpel, cutting open the chaos of what she felt right now to reveal the rational veins inside. She thought of the things she—in the mirror—had said to her younger self about "cutting losses," "shortcuts," "saving yourself." Her twenty-two-year-old self couldn't take them in, because back then her "mental model" and "cognitive processing power" weren't yet enough to run such a complex early-warning program built on future pain feedback. And her thirty-six-year-old self, having lived through all of it, had finally "compiled" that pain into runnable "warning code."

"So," she said in a low voice, more as if sorting out her own thoughts, "some roads maybe really do have to be walked yourself—you have to run the 'error code' out before you can fully understand why it was wrong, and then write a more robust 'program.'"

"Exactly right." Yu Hao replied, his tone carrying a rare, almost appreciative note. "Personal trial and error is one of the most efficient ways to learn—provided the cost of the trial is controllable and you can review and iterate in time. The thing most to be feared is looping inside the same error pattern without upgrading your cognitive framework. That 'dream' of yours just now was actually a deep, high-fidelity 'stress-test replay.' Your brain was running a war-game simulation for you. The process is painful, but the result of the simulation—that 'sense of the absurd' and that 'emptiness'—is itself extremely valuable output data. It's telling you that certain underlying emotional operating logic or dependency paths may need to be patched, or even rebuilt."

War-game simulation... stress-test replay... Yao Yuan repeated the words silently. Yu Hao could always use the language of the field he knew best to deconstruct the most chaotic emotional experience into clear operating instructions. It gave her a strange sense of reassurance—no matter how bizarre the experience, in his hands it could seemingly be brought into some rational framework for analysis, dissolving its uncontrollable terror.

"Do you need me to come over?" Yu Hao changed tack, offering a very practical set of options. "My driver can be there within twenty minutes. Or, if you think digesting this 'output data' on your own is more efficient, we can stay on the line—you can keep talking, or just listen, until your system stabilizes. As it happens, I have to deal with some key points from the minutes of that meeting."

He didn't say "don't overthink it," offered no hollow comfort, but laid out two solutions based on different "efficiency models." Coming over meant physical company and possibly more direct "system debugging" (physical comfort included); staying on the line meant remote technical support and "system-log analysis" on the mental level. He handed the decision back to her, while making clear he had a parallel task on hand, so she wouldn't feel any "guilt over occupying his resources."

Yao Yuan closed her eyes for a moment. The city outside the window seemed to breathe slowly along with her gradually steadying heartbeat. Yu Hao's calm was like a potent, efficient sedative, pulling her fully free of that panic of dislocated time and space.

"Mm—I miss you a lot tonight." she said, her voice recovering its usual strength and clarity, even with a touch of coquetry. "What about your minutes? Will it set back your work?"

"No effect—my pleasure! I can finish them on the way over." Yu Hao seemed to smile, almost imperceptibly, very faint, but through the current it carried a slight, relaxing vibration.

The call didn't end; she could hear, on his end, the sounds of packing up a laptop, grabbing clothes, heading out the door.

Yao Yuan opened a saved track of Alpha-wave background music while walking to the sofa, sinking into the soft cushions and pulling a thin blanket over her knees. An exceedingly faint, soothing electronic sound—like the surge of the deep sea or wind passing through a pine forest—rose gently.

She put the phone on speaker and set it beside her. Soon the receiver carried the sounds of Yu Hao getting into the car and starting the engine.

"Has your system stabilized on your end? Should I read you the few points of consensus from the talk just now with the Silicon Valley side about the 'emotional-computing investment track'? I guarantee the information density is high enough to help take your mind off things."

His rare, faintly teasing offer curved the corner of Yao Yuan's mouth, almost imperceptibly. The tension dissolved completely.

On Yu Hao's end, the evenly paced clatter of a keyboard, the rustle of pages being turned, and the soft, soothing background music wove together into a reassuring white noise of order that belonged to the real world—steady, continuous, full of purpose.

Those scenes of the summer of 2012, of young Jianghai, of the cold conversation in the mirror, seemed, against this steady and "productive" backdrop, to be loaded into a background process—still occupying memory, but no longer frantically seizing the foreground of her attention, no longer holding the power to tear at the boundary of reality.

She looked at the ceiling, listening to that regular sound that belonged to another high-efficiency brain at work, and suddenly spoke, her voice very soft, less a question than an act of cognitive calibration: "Yu Hao, do you believe in 'prophecy'? Or rather, warnings from the future that try to help you avoid pain?"

The keyboard barely paused; Yu Hao's answer came almost instantly, with his characteristic, categorical clarity: "No. I only believe in probabilistic judgments based on sufficient data and logical deduction. So-called 'prophecy' or 'warning' is, at its core, a cognitive gap under information asymmetry. The side with more information—even information from a hypothetical future—is trying to intervene in the behavior of the side that lacks it. But the problem is, first, the truth and completeness of the information can't be verified; second, even if the information is true, the recipient's cognitive level, decision model, and execution environment may differ enormously from the provider's, causing the intervention to fail or even backfire."

He tapped the keyboard a few times and went on: "It's like if I told a nineteen-year-old version of myself—one who firmly believes love conquers all, whose cognitive framework hasn't yet been thoroughly 'stress-tested' by reality: 'Don't start a company, go get a job, it's more stable.' Would he listen? No. Because he doesn't have my current cognitive model; he can't understand the real weight of 'stability' at a particular life stage, nor can he grasp the specific pains and gains of the startup process. He has to go run into it himself, to trial-and-error, to accumulate his own 'training data,' before he can iterate into the cognitive version capable of understanding that sentence of mine. So I'm highly skeptical of 'prophecy.' What's more effective is probably providing methodology and thinking frameworks, not specific path instructions."

Yao Yuan listened in silence. His words once again struck the core with precision. Wasn't her present thirty-six-year-old self doing exactly that—trying to "intervene in the behavior" of her twenty-two-year-old self? And the result was fierce resistance. Because the cognitive versions were incompatible. The twenty-two-year-old Yao Yuan didn't yet have the "system environment" required to run that "pain code."

"So," she summed up softly, "some roads maybe really do have to be walked yourself. You only learn how hard the wall is by hitting it—and how hard your own head is, too, or where to go to find the right tools."

"But when you walk it, you can carry more precise measuring tools and a richer library of maps." Yu Hao replied. The keyboard sounds stopped, as if he'd finished a paragraph. "Even if it turns out to be a load-bearing wall in the end, at least you'll know its material parameters and stress distribution, so next time you can calculate the demolition charge more precisely, or simply take a detour. It's purely emotional, repeated head-on collisions that are the greatest waste of cognitive resources and time capital. Our goal is always to raise the expected value—the EV—of every decision."

Expected value... EV... Yao Yuan thought: did her thirty-six-year-old self, in the mirror, raise the future decision EV of her younger self? Not necessarily. But perhaps it had planted, in her thirty-six-year-old self's heart, a seed about "reassessing the path." And this companion at her side—calm, efficient, supplying steady "system white noise" and "cognitive frameworks"—he himself was a very-high-EV "strategic asset" in this stage of her life's decisions.

"Thank you." she said suddenly, her voice very soft but clear, no longer carrying the earlier dryness and daze.

The line went quiet for a beat, and then Yu Hao's voice came through, steady as ever, but seemingly a touch gentler than before, like a precision instrument completing a successful calibration: "You're welcome. The minutes are done—I'm at your door."

The lock gave a soft electronic click—Yu Hao letting himself in with the temporary code she'd given him before. His footsteps were light, carrying the faint chill of the night air.

Yao Yuan didn't get up; she stayed curled on the sofa, only turning her head toward the entryway. Yu Hao appeared at the edge of the living room's warm amber glow. He wore a well-tailored dark-gray cashmere coat over a simple black turtleneck, a slim laptop bag in hand. At thirty, his figure was tall and straight, his bearing clean; his face showed none of the fatigue of a late-night drive—instead a kind of clear-headed sharpness that came after handling something thorny. Only those always-too-calm eyes showed, in the lamplight, a rare softness now.

"Very efficient." Yao Yuan said, her voice still carrying a little of her curled-up languor.

"Just minutes—practice makes perfect." Yu Hao set the laptop bag on the entryway cabinet, took off his coat and hung it up offhandedly, the movements smooth and natural. He walked over to the sofa, didn't sit down right away, but first bent down and lightly pressed his palm to her forehead—a touch free of desire, confirming her status. "Temperature's normal. CPU not fried?"

His fingertips carried the cool of the outdoors, and it felt good against her skin. Yao Yuan closed her eyes and, almost imperceptibly, nuzzled into his palm. "Not fried. Just ran a few high-power-consumption memory processes—a bit of lag."

"A restart will fix it." Yu Hao withdrew his hand and sat down in the single armchair beside her, legs casually crossed. He glanced at the empty wine glass on the coffee table, his gaze lingering less than half a second, and asked nothing. "The Silicon Valley side's consensus on the 'emotional computing' track has a few key points—want to hear them? Or do you need more direct 'system maintenance'?"

He offered a choice, like presenting different solutions. Yao Yuan opened her eyes and looked at him. Yu Hao's face was especially clear in the soft light—straight-bridged nose, a clean, crisp jawline, the kind of handsomeness charged with modernity and intellectual appeal. He looked at her, his gaze calm, waiting for instructions, like the most reliable operating system.

"The consensus first." Yao Yuan said, pulling the thin blanket up a little. "I need a high-density stream of real-world data to overwrite the garbled code in the background."

"All right." Yu Hao leaned back slightly and began to lay it out, his pace steady, his wording precise, instantly drawing the room into another dimension of thought. "First, pure emotional companionship or chatbots have limited value—an obvious ceiling. Second, the real barrier lies in a 'personalized adaptation engine' and 'cross-scenario continuity,' and the difficulty is data acquisition and privacy compliance. Third, medical-grade applications—like assisted intervention for depression and anxiety—are currently the most favored path to commercialization, but the regulatory bar is extremely high. Fourth, and most important," he paused and looked at Yao Yuan, "almost all top-tier institutions believe this track will ultimately produce a few platform-type companies, and a platform's core competitiveness isn't just technology—it's a 'product philosophy' grounded in a profound understanding of complex human needs and ethical boundaries. Zhao Yiming's 'Genesis Engine' has technical ambition, but lacks the latter. Your 'Pretty Big One' has the embryo of the latter, but needs the former to empower it. A union of the two is logically sound, but the execution difficulty is hell-tier."

In a few sentences he placed her tense, undercurrent-laden negotiation with Zhao Yiming that afternoon into a grander, crueler coordinate system. Yao Yuan listened quietly, and the daze about the world in the mirror, about 2012, ebbed further. The hard, clear rules of the real world's game reasserted their dominance.

"So, what's the EV—the expected value—of cooperating with Zhao Yiming, in your view?" she asked, using the term he'd just raised.

"High risk, potentially super-high return, but a failure probability greater than seventy percent." Yu Hao answered without hesitation. "Zhao Yiming is a tech genius, and also a control freak. His 'Genesis Engine' is his life. For you to build an 'emotional application' on top of it is like operating on his core code. He won't hand over the scalpel easily. Your cooperation is, at its core, a fight for control. Your contact this afternoon only earned you the qualification to stand in and observe the surgery—you're a long way from holding the scalpel yourself."

"But if the surgery succeeds, the payoff is monopolizing the application gateway to a future hundred-billion-scale market." Yao Yuan picked up, her gaze turning sharp.

"Exactly. That's why it's worth the bet." Yu Hao nodded. "But the stakes have to be clear. Your stake is your 'Pretty Big One' data, your user ecosystem, your brand. His stake is partial technical openness and future platform earnings. You need to think clearly about the maximum loss you can accept, and where your stop-loss point is. And," he looked at her with a deep gaze, "whether you're ready to work, even spar, at such close range with a man like Zhao Yiming. He's not like any opponent you've met before."

His words were like ice water, sobering her completely. The mood that had been thrown into disorder that afternoon by Zhao Yiming's technical blueprint and the world in the mirror was now combed perfectly clear by Yu Hao's calm investment logic. Yes—control, stop-loss point, opponent assessment. This was the real-world game.

"I need a more detailed opponent-assessment report." Yao Yuan said, her voice recovering its cold clarity from working hours. "On Zhao Yiming, Western World, and every known and potential risk of the 'Genesis Engine.'"

"In your encrypted inbox before ten tomorrow morning." Yu Hao agreed, as if he'd long anticipated she'd need it. This was their understanding: he always supplied the most precise "ammunition" or "map" the moment she needed it.

With business settled, the room fell quiet for a while, only the Alpha-wave music echoing gently like a deep-sea undertow.

Yu Hao suddenly stood up, walked over to stand before her, then crouched on one knee, bringing his eyes level with hers. The posture broke the safe distance they usually kept, carrying a trace of invasiveness that brooked no refusal, yet his gaze remained calm, even gentle.

"Now," he said, his voice dropping a few degrees, "the high-density data stream has finished loading. According to the system-log analysis, your core anomaly tonight, beyond work stress, also involves a high-load retrospective of emotional memory. Pure rational overwriting may treat the symptom, not the root. Do you need to launch the auxiliary... physical cache-clearing program?"

He put it in extremely technical terms, but Yao Yuan understood. He was asking whether she needed physical contact, whether she needed a more direct way to soothe those awakened emotional ghosts of the past.

He didn't simply embrace or comfort her the way an ordinary boyfriend would; instead he gave a "program launch request" that required her confirmation. It preserved her absolute control, and fit the setup of their relationship—intimate yet boundary-keeping.

Yao Yuan looked into his eyes, right there before her, where her own face was clearly reflected.

"Launch approved." She heard herself say, her voice very soft, languid and coquettish.

The corner of Yu Hao's lips curved into a fine arc. He reached out—not to embrace, but his palm steadily cradling the back of her neck—and drew her gently toward himself, then kissed her.

This kiss was the opposite of his calm, restrained surface: ardent, eager, carrying an undeniable predatory force and skill. The two of them explored and soothed without pause in the narrow space of the sofa. His breath was clean and crisp, carrying a touch of the night's chill, quickly warmed by their shared body heat. Yao Yuan closed her eyes and let herself sink into the kiss, let his familiar, steady scent cover every sense. The Alpha-wave music still flowed, but the noisy clamor of memory, the afterimages of the world in the mirror, the dry heat and tears of the summer of 2012—all of it was finally, in this solid, rational kiss, temporarily driven off, overwritten, suppressed.

This was not love—at least not the kind of love she had understood at twenty-two. This was a more complex, more modern emotional contract: built on mutual appreciation, rational choice, clear boundaries, and efficient comfort. It promised no eternity, wove no fantasy, but in this moment it so truly and effectively fished her out of that lonely dislocation of time and space, anchoring her to the solid ground of the present.

She didn't know how long it lasted before Yu Hao drew back slightly, the tip of his nose brushing hers, his breathing a little ragged, but his eyes still clear. "Cache-clearing progress: thirty percent." he said in a low voice, using their code.

Yao Yuan couldn't help a soft laugh; that knot of gloom dissolved entirely. "Efficiency could use some improvement, Boss Yu."

"Feedback received—optimizing the program." Yu Hao stood and pulled her up from the sofa in the same motion. "I suggest we relocate the field of operations for deep system maintenance. This place," he glanced at the sofa, "isn't conducive to peak performance."

Yao Yuan rose on the strength of his pull, her legs still a little weak, and leaned against him. Yu Hao's arm circled her waist steadily, holding her up.

"I'll follow your lead." she said, burying her face in the cool collar of his sweater and drawing a deep breath—that clean, reassuring scent that belonged to this moment, to reality, to Yu Hao.

He held her and walked toward the bedroom.

In the bedroom only a single bedside reading lamp was on, dimmed to its lowest, blooming a patch of warm amber, blurred glow that just barely outlined the quiet contours of the furniture, leaving most of the space to a perfectly judged dimness. Drifting in the air was the faint, lingering scent of Yao Yuan's post-bath freshness, now mingled with the crisp aftershave and faint night chill Yu Hao had brought in, forming a distinctive, private atmosphere.

Yu Hao walked to the bedside and sat down beside her, the mattress dipping slightly. He didn't rush to touch her; instead, in the dim light, he gazed at her quietly for a few seconds, his eyes like the most precise scanner, carrying the aggression of desire, as if assessing the truest status parameters of her in this moment.

Yao Yuan lay on her side facing him, the sash of her silk robe already loosened, the collar fallen open to reveal a stretch of fair neck and a clear line of collarbone. In the warm dim light her gaze looked a little misted—no longer the alarm before the mirror, and stripped of the sharpness of talking shop—but a kind of slight bewilderment and weariness, all defenses laid down, and a faint, hard-to-detect dependence on his presence in this moment.

He reached out, his fingertips first lightly touching the still-damp ends of her hair spread across the pillow, then sliding slowly up along their curve, finally settling on her temple, where, with exceedingly steady pressure, he began to massage slowly in small circles. Less an easing of taut nerves than a tease.

"Detecting excessively high residual-thread occupancy." he said in a low voice, his voice especially clear in the silent room, yet softened by the dimness. "Attempting to force-quit non-critical processes."

The pads of his fingers were warm, the pressure perfectly judged, carrying a reassuring sense of control. Yao Yuan closed her eyes, her long, dense lashes casting faint shadows beneath them. She let his fingers roam over her temple, her forehead, and even the pressure-gathering points behind her ears, as if he really could, through this contact, clear away the stubborn residual "garbled code" in her mind from another time and space.

The massage went on a while, the silence holding only their light, shallow breaths and the faint friction of fingertips on skin. Then those steadily pressing fingers slid slowly downward, grazing the sensitive curve of her ear, brushing past her jawline, finally coming to rest at the collar of her robe. His fingertips, faint as a whisper, touched the skin at her collarbone, bringing a cool, electric-current-like shiver.

"Running a surface-level system scan." he said softly, more as if explaining the "program's" purpose to her. His kiss came down—first on the temple he'd just massaged, then along her brow bone, her eyelids, the bridge of her nose, drifting gently downward, finally covering her lips. This kiss was deeper than the one in the living room, and more patient, like a slow, rigorous data-synchronization protocol, free of impatient demand, trying instead, through close contact, to transmit a steady "confirmation signal" that belonged to this moment.

Yao Yuan's response was at first a little passive, as if she were still adjusting to this switch of channels from rational analysis to intimate contact. But gradually, led by his rhythm, she began to keep pace with this slow, firm "synchronization." Her arms wound around his neck, her fingertips sinking unconsciously into the taut muscle of his shoulders and back. The sash of her silk robe was fully undone, the slick fabric rubbing against her skin with a faint rustle, magnified in the silence, then swiftly swallowed by a deeper kiss.

Yu Hao's touch maintained a strange calm throughout, even as he explored more secret territory. His palm stroked over her back, his clearly defined fingers tracing down the hollow of her spine vertebra by vertebra, as if reading some encrypted sequence; his kisses landing on her shoulder and neck, her collarbone, even the curves further down, all carried a quality of study and soothing in equal measure—like debugging a precise but momentarily mis-calibrated instrument, the goal not to destroy or conquer but to restore its optimal operating state.

The two of them's interwoven breaths gradually grew heavy, their body heat climbing steadily in the close press of skin, fabric and bedding rubbing out a continuous, private sound. The dim light cast their overlapping silhouettes on the wall—stretched, swaying, distorted, vivid and alive.

At a certain moment, when the connection was finally established, a deeply suppressed, brief whimper spilled from Yao Yuan's throat, like the uncontrollable aftershock produced when a taut string is at last plucked. She bit down hard on Yu Hao's left shoulder, whimpering "Yu Hao, Yu Hao~," her fingertips digging deeper into his skin. Yu Hao's movements stalled for an instant, as if receiving this sudden burst of feedback, and then he lowered his head, buried his face in the crook of her neck, his breath scorching against her skin. His rhythm, so well controlled before, slipped into an almost imperceptible disorder, and an unrestrained, panting breath rose abruptly~

This "deep system maintenance" lasted a not-inconsiderable stretch of time, like a meticulous concerto demanding the closest cooperation of both sides. There weren't many frills, but every link was precise and on point, striking straight at the core. In the end, when all the accumulated pressure and disorder seemed to find an outlet for release—like a program reaching a certain node, completing a crucial data exchange and status update—the violent surges gradually subsided, leaving only a slow, lingering tremor, and breath and heartbeat returning, little by little, to steadiness.

Yu Hao didn't leave at once; his weight rested partly on her, his body heat blazing, a faint dampness of sweat. He steadied his breathing at her neck, and after a moment propped himself up on his arms, drawing back a little, studying her face in the warm dim light.

Yao Yuan kept her eyes closed, a post-exertion flush on her face, her lashes wetted by fine beads of sweat and clinging together. She looked exhausted, but that hollow of alarm and emptiness was gone, replaced by a deep relaxation and a near-blunted calm that came after desire had been properly satisfied.

"Cache-clearing progress: one hundred percent." he said in a low voice, his tone roughened in the aftermath, yet already restored to an almost infuriating calm, as if that fierce "data exchange" just now had been merely another successfully executed task. "System operating-status assessment: stable. Core process priority restored."

Yao Yuan didn't open her eyes, only tugged at the corner of her mouth almost imperceptibly by way of reply. She was too tired to want to move even a finger.

Yu Hao watched her quietly for a few more seconds, then got up and went into the bathroom. Soon came the sound of warm running water. He wrung out a hot towel and came back, his movements not especially tender but careful enough as he helped wipe her down. The faintly scalding towel passed over her skin, bringing comfortable relief. Then he pulled over the disheveled covers, tucked her in properly, and only then lay down beside her and switched off that single bedside lamp.

The room sank into complete darkness and quiet. Only their gradually synchronizing, long, steady breaths.

The memory of the body is stubborn. Those tremors covered over both tenderly and forcefully, that clearly perceived body heat and heartbeat belonging to another person, those moments at the peak that had nearly broken through the line of reason... all of these deep "data writes" at the physical level, more powerfully than any rational verbal comfort, anchored her firmly out of that dizzying dislocation of time and space and into this moment—this warm, real night with another person solidly present.

"The report will be in by ten tomorrow morning, right on time." In the instant before Yao Yuan was about to sink into sleep, Yu Hao's voice rose in the darkness, steady as always, as if nothing just now had happened—or as if it had merely been another item smoothly checked off the schedule.

"Mm." Yao Yuan answered vaguely, her consciousness already sliding to the edge of deep sleep.

Before she lost consciousness entirely, the last thought to cross her mind was: this "strategic companion," in certain specific situations, had his "utility" seriously underestimated. And that cold logic of his, which "programmatized" and "task-ified" everything, had, on some level, become the most effective sedative and shelter in this very moment.

The sky outside the window, in the unwatched dark, was inching unstoppably, bit by bit, toward dawn.

========================================

Chapter 9 — Stepfather Zhou Lufei and His "Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640256.html

Yao Yuan was woken by sunlight that had slipped through the gap in the curtains, far too bright. She opened her eyes with a frown, let them adjust to the light, and only then realized it was already late.
