The space beside her was empty, but the sheets still held their creases and a lingering warmth, and the air was still thick with the mingled scents of the night before. Lit now by the sun, it all took on a lazy, drowsy intimacy she didn't usually feel.
She grabbed the phone off the nightstand and tapped the screen awake.
10:27.
She'd slept well, at least. As she woke, her body's memory came drifting back—the sounds she'd swallowed and held in, the heat where skin met skin, and the deep sleep she'd dropped into afterward, spent and exhausted... It all washed slowly over her mind like a rising tide. She propped herself up on her aching, languid body and sat up; the silk quilt slid away, bringing a wave of cold.
Just then the bathroom door slid open and Yu Hao came out. He had already washed up, dressed in a simple white cotton T-shirt and gray lounge pants, his hair slightly damp, his expression its usual crisp and tidy self—not a trace of fatigue or indulgence on him, as if he'd done nothing but get a good night's sleep. Seeing she was awake, his steps paused for a beat, then he walked straight to the wardrobe—where the shirt and slacks he'd changed out of the night before hung, waiting to be pressed, along with a few changes of clothes he kept here.
"Morning," he said, his voice carrying that particular morning rasp, but clear. "I made coffee. It's in the kitchen."
"Morning," Yao Yuan answered, her voice still rough with sleep. She threw off the covers, climbed out of bed, and stepped barefoot onto the cool wooden floor, a secret ache spreading from the tops of her thighs. She said nothing more and headed straight for the bathroom.
By the time she finished her shower and came out trailing steam and the fresh scent of body wash, Yu Hao was no longer in the bedroom. She changed into comfortable loungewear and walked to the open kitchen. The coffee machine was giving off a faint warming hum, and the air carried a rich, full aroma. Two glasses of water sat poured on the island, along with Yu Hao's laptop, its screen lit, showing what looked like a chart of data.
Yu Hao stood beside the island, one hand holding his phone as he tapped out a quick reply to something, the other resting idly on the counter, his fingers drumming without thinking. Hearing her footsteps, he looked up.
"You don't have much in the fridge, so I just made a sandwich to tide you over. We'll go out for lunch." He kept it brief, his gaze lingering for a moment on her still-dripping hair. "You're not going to blow-dry it?"
"No need, it'll dry soon." Yao Yuan poured herself a cup of black coffee and leaned against the other side of the island, sipping it in small mouthfuls. The scalding, bitter liquid slid down her throat, bringing a clear, sharp alertness that chased off the last of her drowsiness. With the island between them, the air floated with the smell of coffee and a strange, after-the-fact stillness. No awkwardness, no excessive tenderness—more like two people who had just finished a demanding joint project and were now in a brief phase of rest and resupply.
Yu Hao finished his message, set down his phone, and picked up his own coffee. For a moment neither spoke, each drinking, their eyes occasionally meeting in the air before drifting apart again naturally. Sunlight poured in great sheets into the living room, falling on the polished floor, dust motes dancing quietly in the columns of light.
What broke the stillness was a clear, audible growl from Yao Yuan's stomach.
Yu Hao raised an eyebrow and looked at her.
Yao Yuan didn't bat an eye, and took another sip of coffee. "Where's that sandwich you made?"
Yu Hao set down his cup, walked over to the microwave, picked up a plate, and handed it to her.
"Try it." He sat down across from her and passed her a fork.
The sandwich had a rich sauce, the egg fried tender and smooth, the luncheon meat seared crisp and fragrant—a perfectly decent, home-style taste that could quickly soothe an empty stomach. Yao Yuan ate quietly, her stomach filling with warm food, her mood lifting along with it. With a teasing lilt she praised, "Boss Yu, delicious~" She drew out the word "delicious" with all sorts of winding curves, the meaning anything but innocent.
Yu Hao was caught off guard by the tease. The tips of his ears flushed crimson in an instant. He feigned composure, but his throat bobbed lightly, his gaze instinctively dodging away, even as the corners of his mouth couldn't help curving up.
He came around the island and pulled the giggling, doubled-over Yao Yuan into his arms, biting lightly at the tip of her nose.
The bite tickled the tip of her nose, and she laughed and burrowed closer into his chest, her hair brushing the side of his neck, carrying the steam of a fresh shower. "What are you, part dog?" Her voice was soft and fond, but her fingertips toyed lightly at the third button of his shirt—the one whose thread had come loose, the one she'd yanked the night before.
Yu Hao lowered his head, his breath falling on the crown of her hair, carrying the faint bitterness of coffee and the sweet of her body wash. "And whose mind was running somewhere it shouldn't?" His arm tightened, hemming her in between himself and the island. "Are you still eating the sandwich?"
"No more," Yao Yuan tilted her head up to look at him, a trace of steam still clinging to her lashes. "You've stirred me up till I've lost my appetite."
"Then how about a different thing to eat?" His eyebrow arched, his gaze sliding from her reddened earlobe to her slightly parted lips, his throat moving.
Yao Yuan's heart skipped half a beat. She deliberately turned her head to look out the window, where the sun fell warm on her bare ankle. "My hair's still wet." She grabbed for an excuse, but her voice held little conviction.
Yu Hao said nothing, just turned and went into the bathroom. When he came back, he had a hair towel in his hand. He stood behind her, lifting the loose strands scattered over her shoulders and gathering them together, pressing them gently with the towel. His movements were slow, the pads of his fingers occasionally brushing the back of her neck like a feather's sweep, making her back tense slightly.
His breath was right at the side of her neck. The friction of the towel, the birdsong outside the window, the hum of the coffee machine—all of it blurred into background noise, leaving only the faint tremor where skin touched skin.
He bent lower, his lips almost against her ear. Yao Yuan turned around and crashed into the smile in his eyes. Yu Hao's hand was still resting on her shoulder; the hair towel slipped to the floor, and he took the chance to clasp her wrist, his fingertips stroking the fine, delicate skin over the bone.
Sunlight leaked in through the gap between them, casting overlapping shadows on the floor. Yao Yuan looked at his face, so close to hers, and suddenly rose up on her toes to peck him quickly on the chin.
Like a water droplet falling into a pot of hot oil, it burst instantly into a fine, prickling itch. Yu Hao's eyes darkened, and before she could pull back, he lowered his head and kissed her. The sunlight outside was just right, the scent of coffee mingling with body wash, and in this small kitchen it brewed into something thicker, warmer.
After a good while of fooling around, it was nearly noon. The two of them washed up quickly and headed out for lunch.
Time slid toward midday. They left the apartment, and Yu Hao's driver already had the car waiting downstairs—a black Audi A8, steady and understated. The car drove across nearly half the city before finally stopping on a bustling but well-laid-out restaurant street. There were no shortage of famous establishments lining both sides, but one building stood out in particular: a three-story structure in an unmistakable new-Chinese style, with a wide-open front. A massive plaque hung high, gold characters on black, bearing five forceful, vigorous words—"Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot"—signed by some contemporary master calligrapher. Even at noon on a weekday, quite a few nice cars were already parked out front, and uniformed doormen directed them with practiced ease.
"Right here." Yao Yuan signaled the driver to stop. She was clearly familiar with the place, though her expression stayed flat.
Lifting aside the heavy cotton curtain embroidered with an interlocking-lotus pattern, a rush of warmth and noise instantly shut out the chill of the winter day. The first floor was the open dining hall for walk-in guests, with ceilings a good five or six meters high, spacious and bright, with none of the cramped feel of an old shop. The decor was a thoughtfully done new-Chinese style: dark walnut tables and chairs, partitions of carved lattice windows, and walls decorated with artfully rendered diagrams of mutton cuts and black-and-white photos of old Jinshi life. The tables were spaced just right, keeping the lively dine-in atmosphere without feeling crowded. Because it was a Saturday, every seat was already taken, voices clamoring, copper pots bubbling, white steam curling up. The waitstaff, dressed in matching navy-blue Chinese-style jackets, threaded through the room like flying with huge wooden trays, their full-throated calls weaving together with the chatter, laughter, and clinking glasses of the diners into a symphony brimming with life.
"Miss Yuan is here!" A capable-looking woman of about forty-odd, in a dark silk cheongsam, had sharp eyes; she stepped briskly out from behind the cashier's counter deep inside, her smile warm and pitched to exactly the right degree. "Boss Zhou just called to ask. And this would be... Mr. Yu? Boss Zhou gave special instructions—the private room's been kept for the two of you. This way, please." Her gaze swept over Yu Hao very quickly, carrying a professional deference and a barely perceptible note of appraisal.
"Manager Li, thank you for the trouble." Yao Yuan gave a slight nod and gestured to Yu Hao. "My stepfather's place. He usually works upstairs in the office."
"You're too kind, Miss Yao." Manager Li turned to lead the way, guiding them around the noisy first-floor hall and up a staircase on one side to the second floor. The second floor was the higher-grade private-room area; the corridor was laid with thick carpet, and the surroundings turned instantly quiet and elegant. She led them to the door of a private room named "Listening to Snow" at the end of the hall and pushed it gently open.
The room wasn't large, but it was exquisite. The street-facing wall was a single floor-to-ceiling window, hung with bamboo blinds that softened the light. In the center stood a six-seat rosewood table, with a purple-copper charcoal pot polished to a gleam in the middle, the charcoal glowing red, a clear spring-water broth simmering gently with a pure aroma. Four dishes and eight bowls of classic accompaniments were already arranged around it. The mutton was top-grade, hand-cut and fresh, marbled with fat and lean, sliced thin enough to let light through, laid out neatly on blue-and-white porcelain plates like works of art. The condiments—sugar-pickled garlic, chive-flower paste, fermented bean curd, sesame sauce—were even more delicately presented in small dishes.
"Boss Zhou says, please go ahead and start. He's handling something and will be right down." Manager Li set out their bowls and chopsticks with her own hands, asked about any dietary restrictions, then withdrew without a sound, closing the door behind her.
"Quite a setup." Yu Hao took off his coat, draped it over the rack to the side, and looked around the room, his tone flat, neither praise nor criticism.
"He's got a taste for this kind of pomp—thinks it's the only way to do justice to a time-honored name and the business as it stands now." Yao Yuan sat down at the table, picked up the long chopsticks, lifted a slice of rosy mutton, and swished it casually through the clear broth. "But the meat and the craft really are the heart of this place. The food is good."
The two had barely started eating when there was a knock at the door, and it swung open. A man of about sixty-five or sixty-six walked in, his figure kept in excellent shape, dressed in a finely tailored dark-gray Mao suit—this was Zhou Lufei. His face was lean and clear, his temples slightly gray, but his gaze was sharp and his stride brisk, carrying the shrewdness of a successful businessman and the easy composure of someone long seasoned in the world. Seeing Yao Yuan, a smile broke over his face—one that held concern, and also the satisfaction of seeing an accomplished junior of one's own.
"Yuanyuan! You're finally here! And this must be Mr. Yu Hao? I've heard so much about you!" Zhou Lufei's voice was loud and full of vigor as he reached out his hand to Yu Hao, the handshake firm and brief. "This girl Yao Yuan has high standards. Anyone she'd bring to eat with me as a friend must be a fine young talent! Don't stand on ceremony—once you're here, treat it like your own home. Today's meat, I guarantee, you won't forget once you've tasted it!"
"Boss Zhou, a pleasure. Yao Yuan flatters me. Forgive the intrusion today—I came because of the place's reputation." Yu Hao rose, shook hands, his manner neither obsequious nor aloof, his courtesy complete, carrying a steadiness beyond his years. "A time-honored name in Jinshi—it lives up to the reputation."
"Hahaha, never mind all this 'Boss' business—just call me Uncle Zhou!" Zhou Lufei laughed heartily and settled naturally into the head seat, waving for them all to sit. "Just a small business, putting food on the table—nothing next to the big ventures you young people are building. Come, dig in. This mutton came in this morning from the Huyang pastures, still carrying the chill of the grasslands. You've got to eat it fresh!"
He took up the chopsticks himself, lifted a few of the prettiest slices of mutton shoulder, swished them quickly apart in the rolling clear broth, and fished them out the moment the red faded, placing them into Yao Yuan's and Yu Hao's dishes. "Try it—just a touch of salt, that's how you taste it at its purest."
Yu Hao went along readily and tried it as told. The mutton was exquisitely tender, sweet beyond compare, with a milky note—genuinely first-rate. He nodded and praised it sincerely: "Good meat. The heat and the knife work both show real skill."
"You know your stuff!" Zhou Lufei's eyes lit up, clearly delighted that Yu Hao knew the trade, and the conversation opened right up—from the selection, slaughter, and aging of the mutton, to the blending of the secret-recipe sesame sauce, the choice of charcoal, and even how the shop maintained its quality by building long-term partnerships with the pastures and training its meat-cutters... He had it all at his fingertips, his words carrying both pride in traditional craft and the thinking of modern business management—not the kind of stubbornly old-fashioned businessman who clung to the past.
For most of it, Yao Yuan quietly cooked her meat, picked at dishes, and listened to them talk. Her relationship with Zhou Lufei, ground smooth over these years, had long since settled into a stable pattern: courteous, respectful, keeping a moderate distance, but never lacking in due care and decorum. Materially, Zhou Lufei had never shortchanged her, and he was willing to lend support in connections and resources, but he rarely pried into the details of her love life, giving her a good deal of freedom and respect. For Yao Yuan, this kind of relationship felt far more comfortable than the overly intimate sort of family bond that tried to intervene in all her choices.
Over the meal, Zhou Lufei steered the topic naturally toward Yao Yuan's work.
"I hear you've been talking about a partnership with some high-tech company lately? Something to do with that... emotional-counseling AI?" Zhou Lufei took a sip of the warm yellow wine and looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes full of concern but mostly the posture of a listener. "Uncle Zhou's an outsider to all this—can't give you any advice. But, Yuanyuan, with business matters, it's worth hearing different views, especially from a professional friend like Yu Hao." He turned to Yu Hao and raised his cup. "Yu Hao, you're the expert—you've got to help her keep an eye on things. This girl has a mind of her own, but sometimes she pushes herself too hard. I worry she'll come out the worse for it."
It was a deftly put remark—it expressed concern, while cleverly handing the responsibility for assessment and advice over to Yu Hao, and slipping in a bit of flattery for him besides.
"Don't worry, Uncle Zhou." Yu Hao set down his chopsticks, picked up the damp towel to wipe his hands, then spoke slowly, his tone steady and objective. "Yao Yuan has a clear strategic positioning and risk assessment for the project. Western World's technology really does have its unique strengths, and the logic of the partnership holds. As for the specific terms and risk hedging, we'll work through them carefully. I have a lot of confidence in Yao Yuan's decision-making."
His answer both affirmed Yao Yuan's agency and made clear he would provide professional support, while hinting that the partnership was still under evaluation and the risk was manageable. The balance was struck just right—it reassured Zhou Lufei while fully respecting Yao Yuan's independence.
Hearing this, Zhou Lufei's smile grew wider, and he nodded again and again. "Good, good! You young people talk things through, professional and bold both—better than us old fellows! Come, let's have another round!"
The meal went along unhurried, the mood far more relaxed than Yao Yuan had expected. Zhou Lufei was talkative but not long-winded, knowing when to stop; Yu Hao, though a man of few words, hit the key point every time he spoke, and the two of them found plenty to discuss. As they left, Zhou Lufei saw them all the way to the front door on the first floor, said plenty of polite things to Yu Hao about "coming often" and "treating this as your own canteen," and personally watched as the doorman directed Yu Hao's car out of the lot.
"I've got an online roadshow to listen to at three, and some things to handle after that, so I'll head off first." Standing on the wide steps at the entrance, Yu Hao said to Yao Yuan. The winter afternoon sun fell on his crisp dark coat, tracing a clean silhouette.
"All right." Yao Yuan nodded.
"The preliminary assessment of Zhao Yiming and Western World you asked for last night—I've already sent it to your encrypted inbox. The more detailed follow-up analysis I'll send later." He added it in the tone of a work handoff.
"Very efficient." Yao Yuan said, and she meant it. Dealing with him was easy precisely because of this—clear instructions, timely delivery, never any dragging or mess.
"As it should be. Maximize efficiency within the rules."
With no superfluous goodbye, Yu Hao nodded once more to her and Zhou Lufei, then turned and walked down the steps, getting into the waiting car. It pulled away smoothly.
"That young man—he really is something." Zhou Lufei watched the direction the car had disappeared in, and suddenly said to Yao Yuan, his tone carrying the certainty of a man who had read countless people. "Steady, not flighty, with a plan in his head. Not the kind who's got a little cleverness and looks down on everyone. As for your mom—I'll talk to her later, tell her to stop fretting over nothing."
Yao Yuan didn't answer, only smiled faintly. Coming from Zhou Lufei, this counted as a fairly high assessment of Yu Hao, and an indirect sign of his approval of her current social choices.
"Let's go, head home. Your mom's been going on about it since this morning, just waiting for you." Zhou Lufei patted her shoulder, his tone back to its usual briskness, and gestured toward his own Mercedes S-Class parked alongside.
"All right, home." Yao Yuan answered, opening the car door and getting in.

Chapter 10 — A Mother's Thorns, a Daughter's Armor
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640257.html

A Mother's Thorns, a Daughter's Armor
Yao Yuan's mother, Zhang Fengxia—a mother's thorns, a daughter's armor.
The car pulled away from the bustling restaurant street and headed toward an upscale residential complex in Jinshi's new district. The sweet richness of the lamb and the smooth depth of the sesame paste still lingered in her mouth, and the good food and the touch of alcohol had left her body warm and content. Deep down there was also a faint, hard-to-name fatigue and a kind of satisfaction left over from the fierce tangle of last night and this morning. Her stepfather's words, and that thoroughly pleasant lunch where host and guest alike had enjoyed themselves, were like a soft yet sturdy cushion. They eased some of her usual tension about facing the mother who waited ahead, and that "home" that was always thick with a subtle atmosphere.
Home was just up ahead—the "home" her mother had built after remarrying, the one that always felt a little distant and quietly awkward to her. In it was the mother she was tied to by blood yet bound to by a complicated relationship; a half-brother fourteen years younger, sharing a mother but not a father; a lavishly decorated space that never quite let her relax; old knots in the heart still waiting to be untied; and perhaps, too, some attempt to mend them. Over the years, distance and politeness had become the way they got along.
She was no longer the twenty-two-year-old girl with nothing, who had only the fantasy of love to lean on. Nor was she the lone woman startled by the chaos in time the night before. She was coming back to face all of it carrying her own career, her own calculations, her own armor—and a "strategic partnership" that, while unconventional, was solid enough.
The car glided smoothly through the complex gate. The winter afternoon sun was just right, and the city showed a kind of transparent, faintly cold splendor in the cold season. Through the car window, light and shadow flickered across her calm profile.
Stepping out, she checked her outfit one more time. She had put some thought into what to wear home today. Not so casual that it seemed disrespectful, not so formal that it seemed deliberate or distant—ideally a tasteful, understated signal of "excellence" and "independence."
In the end she had chosen a soft cream cashmere sweater, paired with well-cut charcoal-gray wool straight-leg trousers, over which she wore a long camel cashmere coat, with low-heeled ankle boots on her feet. Her hair was loosely pinned up with a simple ebony hairpin, and her makeup was so light it was almost invisible, accenting only the shape of her brows and the color of her lips to brighten her complexion. At her ears were that pair of pale lavender jade earrings; on her wrist, the square leather-strap watch she wore every day. Overall she looked gentle and tasteful, calm in bearing, with a self-possessed ease and certainty—the kind that comes after weathering storms, and that no one could take lightly.
At thirty-six, Yao Yuan was no longer the one who passively endured. She was a traveler who had crossed through the fog, a player who knew how to calculate EV in tangled relationships. She had her battlefield, her calculations, her armor.
What had to be faced always had to be faced. Right now, she needed to face another concrete, real part of where her own life had begun. And this time, she seemed better prepared for this "going home" than she had ever been before.
The elevator rose smoothly, the numbers ticking upward. Yao Yuan looked at her reflection in the metal doors—that flawless image she had so carefully crafted. But when the elevator stopped with a "ding" at her floor, some corner of her heart still curled up despite her.
Behind that heavy solid-wood door was Zhang Fengxia's world.
When Old Zhou opened the door, she paused for half a second, drew in a deep breath, and slowly let it out. She closed her eyes, then opened them again, her gaze clear and calm.
The apartment was very quiet. The three o'clock afternoon sun slanted in through the balcony's glass door. The spacious entryway floor was polished to a mirror shine, and in the passage leading to the living room, a huge peony embroidery hung dead center, its gold thread glinting under the light. This was Zhang Fengxia's taste—warm, direct, making no effort to hide her longing for "the good life." The light cast diamond-shaped patches across the living room floor. A faint scent of tea drifted in the air, along with the lingering trace of burned sandalwood incense—the incense her mother always used, said to calm the mind.
"Mom?" Yao Yuan called into the apartment.
"In here!" The voice from the balcony came strong and clear. There was a rustling, and Zhang Fengxia pushed open the glass door and stepped out, a small spray bottle still in her hand, misting the few pots of golden pothos. She wore a deep purple house outfit with a charcoal-gray cardigan thrown over it, her dark-brown dyed hair pulled into a neat bun at the back of her head. The thin winter sunlight on her hair made it look almost soft.
Zhang Fengxia straightened up and turned her head. Time had carved deep marks into this woman's face, but those eyes hadn't changed—bright as a fire-tempered blade, able to see right through a person at a glance.
"Yao Yuan's back?" She glanced up at Yao Yuan, then went back to what she was doing. She didn't say "Yuanyuan"; she used the full name, "Yao Yuan." That form of address had run through Yao Yuan's whole childhood—when she slacked off at dance practice, when her exam scores fell short, or simply when Zhang Fengxia was in a bad mood.
Yao Yuan took off her coat and hung it up, then walked toward the balcony. She stopped a few steps short of the balcony door—just the right distance.
"Have you eaten with your Uncle Zhou?" In all the years since Zhang Fengxia had remarried Zhou Lufei, she had never required Yao Yuan to switch to calling him "Dad." She had always called him Uncle Zhou, and Zhou Lufei didn't mind.
"Yeah, I had hotpot at the shop with Yuanyuan and her good friend Yu Hao," Zhou Lufei said with a smile, having changed into comfortable clothes and come forward.
"Listen to you, currying favor." Zhang Fengxia gave Old Zhou a soft snort, set down the spray bottle, wiped her hands on the towel beside her, and walked into the living room.
Yao Yuan didn't answer. She knew there was a subtle way her mother and Uncle Zhou had with each other—the kind unique to couples of their age: dismissive on the surface, leaning on each other underneath.
Yao Yuan set down her bag and changed into slippers. Walking over to the coffee table in the living room, she saw a plate of washed winter jujubes on it, along with a few oranges—all the things she'd loved as a child. Her mother was always like this: never saying anything out loud, yet every detail full of remembering.
Zhang Fengxia's gaze swept over Yao Yuan from head to toe, from her hair to the tips of her shoes. There was a touch of criticism in it, and even more, a hard-to-detect pride.
"Sit. What are you standing around for—I'm not about to lecture you." Zhang Fengxia sat down on the sofa and patted the spot beside her.
Yao Yuan chose the single armchair off to the side, keeping a not-too-far, not-too-close distance from her mother. It was a distance they were both used to—close enough, yet leaving each other room. Zhou Lufei tactfully went off to the study, leaving the space to the two of them, mother and daughter.
The afternoon sun was warm, and the living room was very quiet. Zhang Fengxia picked up an orange and peeled it slowly, the orange-yellow rind splitting open between her rough fingers to reveal the plump flesh.
"Where's Xiaofan?" Yao Yuan asked.
"Still in Shanghai. Says he's working on some project with his advisor—won't be back until almost Little New Year." Zhang Fengxia broke off a segment of orange and handed it to Yao Yuan. "That boy, he's like your Uncle Zhou—can sit still, studying medicine." She paused, then looked up at her daughter. "Not like you. Couldn't sit still from the time you were little, always hopping and skipping around."
There was no telling whether it was praise or a dig. Yao Yuan took the orange, put it in her mouth. It was very sweet.
"Busy lately?" Zhang Fengxia asked, breaking off another segment for herself.
"It's year-end. The company's got a lot going on—a few important things I have to oversee myself," Yao Yuan said.
"You're getting more and more famous online, and more and more people know who you are. Those relatives and friends who watched your livestreams at the start, sneaking a look just for the show—they all come around to me now to fish for information. Some want to land their kid a job at your company, some want you to pull strings or find resources, some just want to meet you. I turned them all away. I don't want you held hostage by all this so-called family obligation. Those people have thick enough skin, too—not stopping to think how they laughed at you behind your back back then." Even now, remembering the faces those people used to make, Zhang Fengxia couldn't help feeling indignant.
She still remembered: five years ago, after Yao Yuan's first video blew up online, she'd started livestreaming all over the internet. Because of her bold, blunt, sharp-edged style, full of street-smart wisdom—but also because she objectified feelings and preached utilitarianism—she'd drawn fierce controversy across the whole internet. At the same time, amid those polarized, praise-and-blame-in-equal-measure arguments, she'd steadily become one of the top streamers in the relationship-and-emotional-advice space. Instead of sinking, she'd only grown more popular, with more and more people liking and agreeing with her views. Some gave her the title "the Godmother of Feelings"; others thought "the McKinsey of Feelings" fit her better.
But while going viral online brought Yao Yuan enormous traffic, it also disrupted the quiet life of her mother and Uncle Zhou, bringing them a certain amount of trouble.
Zhang Fengxia still clearly remembered those strange looks and the whispering from five years ago.
The first to "choke on the water" was Zhang Fengxia's own younger sister, Yao Yuan's aunt. The phone rang right on schedule at dinnertime, carrying a deliberately suppressed excitement: "Sis, that video of Yuanyuan's... aiya, it's really something! My Tingting watched it and admires her to death—says Sister Yuanyuan really sees through everything!" But the way the tone curved, Zhang Fengxia clearly heard another layer of meaning: your daughter's "brilliant theories" about how "marriage is starting a company together, feelings have to come with a clear price tag"—just don't let them lead our Tingting "astray." Zhang Fengxia mumbled vague replies, the pineapple in her mouth tasting like chewed wax.
Next came the subtle silence in the family WeChat group. In years past, during the Spring Festival rounds of relatives showing off their kids, there had always been the obligatory compliment: "Yuanyuan's doing so well at the Cultural Center, real prospects." Now the group was just as lively, sharing wellness articles and Pinduoduo group-buy links, yet no one mentioned Yao Yuan's name anymore. That figure who had swept the short-video platforms in the name of "the Beauty's Worry-Cure Shop"—sharp-tongued, stylishly dressed—had become an unspoken taboo. Now and then a cousin's wife would share one of Yuanyuan's hit videos about "female independence," followed immediately by a face-covering laughing emoji, and the group would fall into a vacuum of several minutes where no one picked up the thread. That vacuum sat heavy and uncomfortable in Zhang Fengxia's chest.
Grocery shopping, she ran into Auntie Zhang in the complex, who chased after her clutching her phone: "I watched that video of your Yao Yuan's, saying girls these days shouldn't always count on a man. There's nothing wrong with that, but does she have to put it so harshly? Like she's picking a fight with somebody."
At the cash register of the lamb hotpot shop, Zhou Lufei ran into a former coworker: "Old Zhou, your daughter's teaching people how to find a partner online? My girl watches her every day, says she 'sees right through it all.' I think the way she talks is too blunt myself—isn't she afraid of offending people?"
During that stretch, scenes like these were many.
The real "raging storm" came at a family gathering one weekend. In the not-so-spacious living room of an uncle in his seventies, the crystal chandelier shone bright over a table laden with dishes. Three cups of wine in, Zhou Lufei's eldest uncle—who had always praised Yuanyuan's schooling to the skies—tapped the edge of a plate with his chopsticks and said, as if just in passing: "Lufei, ah, Yuanyuan's a big internet celebrity now. What she says... has no small influence. Yao Yuan may not be your own flesh and blood, but she's family all the same, and we Zhous have always been decent, proper folk. For a young woman, wouldn't it be better to rein some of that talk in a bit? She's not even married yet, after all." As the words landed, the sound of chewing around the table grew softer. Zhou Lufei felt his face burn, as if he were being lashed in public by everyone's eyes. He opened his mouth, wanting to say "the child has her own ideas," but in the end could only squeeze out a dry: "Eh, eat, eat."
It wasn't that they'd never seen this "daughter's" "battlefield." In those finely edited short videos, Yao Yuan had immaculate makeup and bright eyes, holding forth eloquently against a perfectly lit backdrop. She talked about "the shackles of the family of origin," about "bride price as the objectification of reproductive value," about "refusing emotional blackmail from family." Every line was like a stone tossed into a lake, sending tens of thousands of ripples through the comment section—some fervent support, some fierce abuse. The praise or curses of those strangers, the old couple could force themselves to ignore. But when those words were chewed over, digested, and spat back out by the relatives—turning into pointed probing at the dinner table, into roundabout "concern" over the phone, even into a kind of silent questioning of how they'd raised their child—then that all-pervading pressure truly left them nowhere to hide.
They had become the strangers who knew "internet-celebrity Yao Yuan" best. In her videos their daughter lived in dazzling glory, brazenly unrestrained—a side of her they'd never seen and could barely understand. And on the phone, Yao Yuan's voice was still crisp, carrying the fatigue of stolen moments between busy stretches: "Mom, I'm fine, don't listen to the nonsense online. Did the massage device I bought you two arrive?"
Late at night, the old couple lay in the dark. The streetlight outside slipped through the gap in the not-quite-drawn curtains, cutting a dim yellow patch onto the wall.
"Her aunt asked again today, whether the things Yuanyuan says... mean she's got a grievance against the family." Zhang Fengxia's voice was muffled in her pillow.
Zhou Lufei didn't answer right away. After a long while, he let out a long sigh, the breath of it especially heavy in the silence.
"Those people online, praising her, cursing her—it's all at a remove. But the people around us, they're really watching us, watching how we 'raised' a daughter like this." He paused, as if chewing over some hard-to-name bitterness. "She does live pretty 'big-woman.' But us... it's like overnight we turned into the old backdrop in her persona, the part that most needs to be 'broken down' and 'examined.'"
"Ah, let her be. We're old, we can't keep up with how young people think, can't get into their world anymore. Those things she says online, she figured them out from taking her own falls. If people listen, if people relate, that means people agree, people need advice like that. As long as it helps young people, that's enough." The moonlight outside fell on the nightstand, where the family photo Yuanyuan had taken when she came home last year stood. In it she grinned wide and careless, and the image merged with that sharp figure from the videos—which, oddly, settled the old couple's hearts quite a bit.
The patch of light on the wall trembled faintly, probably from a car passing outside. This world of familiar faces—woven from relatives and neighbors, where they'd lived most of their lives—was, because of the daughter they were proudest of, growing full of scrutiny, the air gone thick and sticky. They felt a complicated flicker of pride at their daughter's achievements and courage, and at the same time a kind of embarrassment and loneliness at being flung into the center of a whirlpool of public opinion, forced to stand stripped bare.
Yao Yuan was breaking her shackles, charging toward a wider world. And some invisible shards came falling down, quietly poking dense, painful little holes into the once-peaceful world that had been theirs.
Thinking of how those people's cold mockery had turned into today's fawning toadying, a hidden, secret satisfaction flickered through Zhang Fengxia's heart.
By the third year after Yao Yuan started going viral, the direction the air flowed had quietly shifted.
At first it was tentative ripples. A few distant relatives who usually only sent greetings at holidays suddenly turned active in the family group, eagerly tagging Zhang Fengxia: "Sister-in-law, that Yuanyuan really brings glory to our Yao family! I watched her video about workplace negotiation—she talks about it so well! My niece here is just looking for work; could you have Yuanyuan give her a pointer or two? Not for free—we'll treat Yuanyuan to a meal afterward!"
Zhang Fengxia stared at the phone screen, fingers hovering over the keyboard, thinking, how do you have the nerve? She thought again of those flat, decisive lines from her daughter's videos—"The essence of connections is value exchange. When you have no value yourself, so-called 'relationships' are the most fragile thing there is." Hadn't these very relatives just proved that line themselves? Their cold-eyed looking-on, even their cold mockery, before Yao Yuan made it big—and now this scramble to flock to her after she'd gotten results and succeeded. Zhang Fengxia curled her lip in disdain and typed a perfunctory reply, saying Yuanyuan was busy with work, it probably wasn't convenient. The other side quickly sent back an "I understand" sticker, and after that the group went quiet again—a silence that seemed to carry an added layer of something hard to name.
Then came a more direct wave. Yao Yuan's second aunt-in-law showed up in person, carrying a basket of expensive imported fruit. After only a few pleasantries she got to the point: "Fengxia, ah, look, Yuanyuan knows so many people now, people of standing. That deputy section-chief position at your brother-in-law's unit has been empty for nearly half a year. I hear the final pick is just one word from the higher-ups... Could you have Yuanyuan ask those capable friends of hers, put in a word? We don't even ask that it works out—just put in a word, and whether it works or not, we'll be grateful to Yuanyuan!" Second aunt-in-law clutched Zhang Fengxia's hand, so eager it made her palms sweat. Before her eyes flashed her daughter's sharp, bright eyes mocking "the unspoken rules of a society run on personal favors," and she only replied perfunctorily: "She just does self-media—how would she know any officials... Children's affairs, there's really nothing we parents can say."
On her husband Zhou Lufei's side, the son of an old coworker wanted to get into a well-known internet tech giant. Having found out, through a roundabout chain, that Yao Yuan seemed to have had some business dealings with an executive at that company, he came begging to Zhou Lufei. The old coworker's tone on the phone was earnest, even carrying a careful, ingratiating note he'd never had before: "Lufei, we go back so many years—just help me ask, see if he can pass along a résumé? If it doesn't work out, I won't blame the kid at all!" Zhou Lufei was a man who cared about face and valued loyalty. Faced with a decades-old friend's request, the word "no" rolled and rolled in his throat but never came out. He looked at Zhang Fengxia, his eyes full of difficulty.
Before, these people had used the names of "personal favors," "face," and "we're all family" to pressure them into making Yao Yuan less of a maverick. Now these same people used the names of "personal favors," "face," and "we're all family" to pressure them into helping—and after being refused, would even sour-grape behind their backs: see, the daughter makes it big and the parents put on airs, won't even do this little favor. People, ah—drawn to profit, fleeing from harm; human nature is always this real, yet can't stand having that real human nature dissected and laid out.
Sometimes Zhang Fengxia wavered too, thinking that now that her daughter was capable, and they were relatives after all, she should help where she could—while also satisfying a bit of her vanity as a mother, letting them see just how excellent her daughter was. But the thought was quickly dismissed.
On this day, Yao Yuan's eldest aunt-by-marriage came around again. This time the reason was that her son-in-law's e-commerce company was opening for business, and she wanted Yao Yuan to come cut the ribbon. "Yuanyuan doesn't have to prepare anything special—just show her face, say a few auspicious words, that's all! It's publicity for Yuanyuan too!" Eldest aunt-by-marriage smiled brightly, as if offering a magnificent opportunity.
Zhang Fengxia was in the kitchen trimming vegetables. The water ran loudly. Looking at the bright green leaves, she suddenly felt a wave of speechlessness, and a strange calm. She dried her hands, walked into the living room, and didn't take the hot tea eldest aunt-by-marriage held out. She just stood there, a gentle but somewhat distant smile on her face, and said, clear and slow:
"Sister-in-law, all of Yuanyuan's time and schedule now are strictly planned by her team. She has to handle all sorts of business collaborations and content creation—she just can't get away. And that child, she's stubborn by nature, strong on principle; she never takes on this kind of pure-favor appearance. As her own relatives, we ought to be the first to respect the child's rules and her work, don't you think? Otherwise, if I agree on my end and she flatly refuses on hers, then as her mother I'm not just putting the child in a bind—I'm making myself out to be wrong on every side, aren't I?"
Her tone was mild, even carrying a touch of homey helplessness, but the meaning was like a nail wrapped in cotton—soft yet firm. For the first time she hadn't reached for excuses like "the child's busy" or "I can't get hold of her." Instead she set her daughter's principle plainly out there, and drew her own stance clearly as well.
The smile on eldest aunt-by-marriage's face stiffened. She muttered a few more "I understand, I understand"s, sat for less than ten minutes, then made an excuse about having something to do and left.
The moment the door closed, the apartment went quiet. Zhou Lufei came out from the inner room, looked at his wife, and teased: "That was... maybe a little too blunt?"
Zhang Fengxia walked back into the kitchen and turned the tap on again. The sound of water striking the basin wall drowned out her slightly quickened heartbeat. She looked out at the familiar street scene through the window and said softly: "Out there, in front of hundreds of thousands, millions of people she doesn't know, Yuanyuan dares to tell the truth and set rules. If we, as her parents, can't even hold off these few familiar faces, how could we have the nerve to say... we support her?"
The water ran on. She knew the phone would ring again, that personal favors would keep poking their heads in through every kind of channel, that the words to turn them away might still need careful weighing. But she would block these unnecessary burdens from her daughter's path forward. Perhaps this was the most clumsy, and the most solid, "support" that they—this pair of ordinary parents—could offer, in this rapidly changing, brand-new world their daughter had split open with sheer traffic.

========================================

Chapter 11 — Zhang Fengxia's Love Is a Tenderness With Barbs
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640258.html

Yao Yuan watched, half-amused, as her mother's face turned suddenly indignant at the mention of the past. "All right, all right, stop being angry. It's all behind us now. Don't let unimportant people get to you."
Zhang Fengxia nodded and said no more. She didn't really understand the specifics of how her daughter's company ran—only that it made money, and that was enough. The way she saw it, a woman who could stand on her own two feet beat anything else.
"Your Uncle Zhou said," Zhang Fengxia began suddenly, her tone offhand, though Yao Yuan could hear the probe in it, "that there was someone else at lunch today?"
Here it comes. Yao Yuan's chest tightened, though her face stayed calm. "Mm. A friend."
"What kind of friend?" Zhang Fengxia fixed her with a stare, those eyes still sharp.
Yao Yuan knew there was no hiding it, and she had no intention of hiding it. "Just a close male friend. We have a few business ventures together."
The air in the living room seemed to freeze for a moment. Zhang Fengxia set down the orange in her hand and leaned slightly forward. "What's his name? How old? What does he do?"
"Yu Hao. He's in investments. A few years younger than me." Yao Yuan answered each question, her voice steady.
"What's his family like?"
"Both parents are abroad. He has a sister who's abroad too."
"How long have you known each other?"
"A little over a year."
The questions came out one after another like bullets, each one taking precise aim at the most essential parts of reality. This was Zhang Fengxia—straight to the point, always. She didn't care for roundabout things, and she couldn't do them anyway.
Zhang Fengxia settled back against the sofa and studied her daughter—from the calm of her face, to the straightness of her spine, to those hands resting on her knees, drawn slightly tight.
"Yao Yuan," Zhang Fengxia's voice dropped, "you're thirty-six this year."
"I know." Yao Yuan met her mother's gaze.
"Then you should also remember—the year you turned twenty-two, fresh out of school, you told me you'd fallen in love, that in a couple of years, once work settled down, you'd get married." Zhang Fengxia's tone was very calm, calm in a way that made the heart clench. "You said that man understood you, loved you, that the two of you were soulmates. And then? Two years later you threw away your job in Jingshi without a second thought and came home crying. Such a bright future in Jingshi—given up for one man. If back then you'd chosen to stay in Jingshi and seized that chance to study abroad, you might have done just as well as you have now, and you wouldn't have had to put up with all that gossip."
And then? And then, when it came down to interests, I was the one they threw away. Yao Yuan finished the thought silently, her nails digging into her palm.
"Mom," she said, her voice steadier than she'd expected, "I'm thirty-six, not twenty-four. Yu Hao and I aren't in a relationship headed toward marriage."
"Then what kind of relationship is it?" Zhang Fengxia pressed. "Tell me. What kind of relationship leaves a change of clothes at my daughter's place?"
Yao Yuan fell silent. How was she supposed to describe Yu Hao? Say he was steady and reliable? That he could see the real her? That they'd met on that chaotic night and yet fit together so strangely well? That between them was an understanding and a rapport beyond love? That they were an unconventional but solid enough pair of "strategic partners" and "bed buddies"?
In the end, all she said was: "He makes me feel safe."
Zhang Fengxia stared at her daughter for a long, long while—long enough for the clock on the wall to tick through a full five minutes. Then she stood up. "I'll boil some water, make some tea."
That was the signal that the conversation had been put on pause. Yao Yuan let out a breath. Not knowing how to explain things to her mother, this unresolved, hanging feeling left her even more on edge.
From the tea table came the sound of water heating, along with the clink of a tea canister opening and closing. Yao Yuan walked out onto the balcony and looked at the few pots of pothos. The leaves were a glossy deep green, thriving, the vines trailing down and swaying gently in the afternoon breeze. She remembered her mother had brought these pothos from the old apartment; she'd been growing them for over a decade.
"Your father, back then," Zhang Fengxia's voice came suddenly from behind her. Yao Yuan turned and saw her mother standing in the balcony doorway with a tea tray. "Said the very same thing to me."
Yao Yuan froze.
Zhang Fengxia came over and set the tray on the little round table on the balcony. It was excellent jasmine tea, steam curling up from it, carrying a familiar fragrance.
"He said, Fengxia, you make me feel safe." Zhang Fengxia sat down in the rattan chair and motioned for her daughter to sit too. "I was only twenty then, working at the textile mill, and he drove for the transport crew. Other people set me up with matches, all of them better off than him, but I just saw that he was a solid man, someone you could count on."
Yao Yuan slowly sat down. This was the first time her mother had ever brought up her father unprompted. In her memory, her father was a forbidden subject—her mother never spoke of him on her own, and she had never dared to ask.
"Later he passed," Zhang Fengxia lifted her teacup and blew on the steam, "and only then did I learn that the least reliable thing in this world is those two words: 'feel safe.' Gone just like that, leaving you alone with a seven-year-old child. What were you supposed to do?"
Her voice was very flat, as if she were talking about someone else's affairs, but Yao Yuan saw that the hand holding the cup was trembling slightly.
"Mom…" Yao Yuan wanted to say something, but didn't know where to begin.
"The year you turned eleven," Zhang Fengxia cut her off, raising her eyes to look out the window, "I married your Uncle Zhou—half of it was for you. Dance lessons burned through money. My little salary couldn't cover it. Lufei was your father's best friend in life. A solid man. He brought it up himself. He said, Old Yao's child is my child."
Yao Yuan's throat tightened. She had always known that part of the reason her mother married Uncle Zhou was so she could keep dancing. But hearing her mother say it out loud for the first time felt completely different.
"I know you resent me," Zhang Fengxia took a sip of tea, her gaze still fixed on the distance, "resent that I remarried so quickly, resent that I later had Xiaofan. You think I forgot your father, that I wronged him."
"I don't…" Yao Yuan wanted to argue, but her mother raised a hand to stop her.
"You do. I know." Zhang Fengxia turned her head to look at her daughter, the rims of her eyes faintly red. "The way you looked at me back then—I remember all of it. But Yao Yuan, your mom is a person too. Your mom gets tired too, gets scared too. The year your father passed, I worked at the factory by day and took in sewing at night, sleeping less than four hours a day. Once I nearly toppled off the sewing machine, and right then I thought—if I went too, what would happen to you?"
Tears welled up without warning. Yao Yuan fought desperately to hold them back, her fingertips driving deep into her palm.
"Marrying Lufei was the most selfish decision I made in my life, and also the one I regret the least." Zhang Fengxia's voice was very soft, yet every word landed hard on Yao Yuan's heart. "Selfish, because I really did want someone to lean on. No regrets, because he let you keep dancing, let the two of us go on living—living like proper human beings."
She set down her cup, the ceramic base clinking crisply against the glass tabletop.
"Later, when you got into Beijing Dance Academy, I was so happy I couldn't sleep all night. But then you went off to Jingshi, lived on campus, came home twice a year, and every time you came back, I felt like you were drifting further and further from me." Zhang Fengxia gave a bitter little laugh. "I know—you found me crude, found me uneducated, found me too blunt, no good at all those fancy words. But Yao Yuan, your mom is just this kind of person. I grew up rolling in the mud, then scraped by in a hotpot shop. I can't learn gentleness, can't learn how to be considerate. I can only be good to you my own way."
"Mom, I never found you crude…" Yao Yuan's voice broke. "I remember it so clearly," she went on, her voice a little unmoored. "The year I got into Beijing Dance Academy, the tuition was steep, and you sold the gold bracelet Grandma left you. The day I left for Jingshi, you and Uncle Zhou saw me off onto the train, and when the train started moving, I saw you wiping your eyes on the platform—that was the first time, and the only time, I ever saw you cry."
"You did. It's all right, I don't blame you." Zhang Fengxia waved a hand. "The winter you were twenty-four, you came back from Jingshi looking like you'd lost your soul. It broke my heart, but it made me even angrier—angry that you wouldn't pull yourself together, angry that you'd let a man wreck you like that. So I scolded you, said you'd let down everything I'd given, said I'd raised you for nothing. I said those things because I was afraid you'd never get back on your feet, afraid you'd just throw yourself away."
She paused and drew a deep breath. "But do you know—every night when you cried in your room, I sat right outside your door and cried too. I was afraid you'd hear me, didn't dare make a sound, so I bit down on my sleeve and cried. I, Zhang Fengxia, have never been afraid of anything in my life, but in that stretch of time I really was afraid—afraid my daughter would never be able to smile again."
"Mom…" Yao Yuan's voice caught, but she held it back. "I know you love me—in your own way, loving me with everything you have. But sometimes your love is too heavy, so heavy I can't breathe. When I was twenty-four, heartbroken, and transferred my job back to Jinshi, you scolded me for being useless, said I'd let down everything you'd given. Yes, I disappointed you. But back then I also needed someone to tell me it was okay to have my heart broken, okay to be dumped, that I'd still meet someone better, that the road was still long… and that there were other roads too." Yao Yuan could hold back no longer, and the tears spilled over.
"Later you got better, went into business, started a company, did better and better." Zhang Fengxia wiped the corners of her eyes with her sleeve, the motion coarse and clumsy. "I was glad, but I was worried too. Afraid you'd run into another bad person, afraid you'd get hurt again. Yao Yuan, your mom's getting old. I can't protect you for the rest of your life."
She reached out and took her daughter's hand. That hand was rough, dry, covered in calluses—yet so warm it made the heart tremble.
"Just now you said that this Yu Hao makes you feel safe." Zhang Fengxia looked at her daughter, her eyes softer than they'd ever been. "Then bring him home, let your mom take a look at him. I might not be a good judge of people in this life, but I've got to vet him for you—got to know whether he can really make you feel safe."
Yao Yuan turned her hand over to clasp her mother's, and said gently, "Mom, Yu Hao and I… we're just keeping company like this. We don't plan on registering a marriage." She paused, then decided to be completely open with Zhang Fengxia. "I'm telling you the truth up front, so you and Uncle Zhou don't put too many hopes on him."
"What do you mean, 'just keeping company like this'?" Zhang Fengxia's eyes went wide, then narrowed again. "Neither of you is young anymore, and you're a few years older than him on top of that. Not getting married—what kind of thing is that? It's all well and good now, but in a few more years you'll be forty, and if you split up… A man in his forties or fifties can still find someone younger, can still have kids. It's different for a woman. Older, and you're hard to match, and having children gets dangerous too…"
"It's just… having each other for company, but without being tied together."
Zhang Fengxia was silent for a while, then suddenly let go of her hand and stood up. She had returned to that usual stiff expression of hers, but her eyes weren't quite so tense anymore.
"Fine, I get it." She pursed her lips. "It's just that thing you young people call being 'buddies' these days, isn't it… Your mom goes online too, you know. I'm not some old fossil." She shot Yao Yuan an exasperated look. "Watching all those men and women in your livestream, all those marriages of every shape, all those strange and wondrous relationships, I've come around too. What does it matter whether you marry or not? Money in hand, a life lived in peace—that's what's real."
Yao Yuan was taken aback. She'd braced herself for a long explanation, even an argument, and never imagined her mother would accept it just like that, so plainly and quietly. The string that had been pulled tight inside her suddenly went slack, and her eyes welled up again.
"What's there to cry about—thirty-six years old, how embarrassing." Zhang Fengxia turned her head and headed for the kitchen, her voice carrying back from over there. "I'm going to make dinner. What do you want to eat?"
"Anything's fine." Yao Yuan wiped the corners of her eyes, but she was smiling.
"Braised pork, then—your favorite when you were little." As Zhang Fengxia said it, her footsteps paused at the kitchen doorway. "Don't just sit out here. Go to the study and keep your Uncle Zhou company, have a chat with him."
Soon the sounds of washing and chopping drifted from the kitchen, a clatter of pots and knives, full of the warm, lively bustle of everyday life.
Yao Yuan sat on the balcony, watching the sun slowly sinking in the west, and somewhere inside her that had been pulled taut for so long suddenly let go.
Yao Yuan leaned back in the rattan chair and closed her eyes. The last of the sunset shone through the glass onto her face, warm. She suddenly thought of many years ago—an evening just like this one, when she'd been practicing dance at home, her mother cooking in the kitchen, her father not yet back from work. The girl she'd been then didn't yet know that some stability is so brief, that some partings are so sudden.
But now, on this winter afternoon, in the rhythm of her mother chopping vegetables, she seemed to touch once more a long-missed warmth that belonged to the word "home."
This home wasn't perfect, and there was still so much left unsaid between her and her mother, so many knots untied—but at least in this moment, they were trying to draw closer.
That was enough, Yao Yuan thought. For a stubborn pair of mother and daughter like them, just being able to sit together like this, drink a cup of tea, and say a few honest words was already great progress.
From the kitchen drifted the fragrance of scallion and ginger sizzling in the pan—the smell of home.
Dinner was a feast. The braised pork was glossy and meltingly tender, the rich dark-soy-and-oil dish of memory, simmered to just the right point; the steamed fish was laid with fine threads of scallion and ginger, its soy dressing balanced fresh and sweet; a plate of stir-fried greens was jade-bright and crisp, and there was also Uncle Zhou's specialty yam-and-pork-rib soup, stewed to a milky white, its steam rising thick. At the table, Zhang Fengxia didn't bring up the earlier subject at all, only kept piling pork into Yao Yuan's bowl, muttering, "Dieting should be done in moderation—eat more." Uncle Zhou smoothed things over with a chuckle, asking Yao Yuan harmless little questions about her company. The atmosphere had a deliberately maintained yet reassuring ordinariness, as if that earlier baring of hearts had been nothing more than a wisp of steam rising from the soup pot and dissolving away.
Yao Yuan ate a lot, her stomach full and warm. It was a long-missed feeling, of being steadily caught by the most ordinary home-cooked food.
After dinner, she kept Zhang Fengxia company washing dishes in the kitchen, helping to slowly wipe down the stove with a cloth, glancing at her now and then. Under the kitchen's amber light, the line of her mother's profile seemed a little softer than usual.
With everything tidied up, Yao Yuan picked up her bag. "Mom, Uncle Zhou, I'm heading back."
"Mm. Has the car you called arrived?" Zhang Fengxia knew she hadn't driven today. "Send a message when you get there."
"Okay."
Uncle Zhou saw her to the door. "Come home for dinner often."
Night had grown thick, the evening wind carrying a chill. Yao Yuan got into the car. The city's neon lights came on one after another, the traffic weaving like threads. The cabin was quiet, with only the low hum of the engine. The warmth in her stomach was still there, but the taut, battle-ready strength in her heart had quietly dispersed, turning into a trace of weariness, and more than that, into a calm and settled peace. Her mother's last words, and that solid, hearty meal, were like a weighty ballast stone, finally steadying a heart that had drifted for so long.
Back at her apartment in the city center, she switched on the lights to a cold, empty room. But that chill, for now, didn't seem so hard to face. She poured herself a glass of water and stood by the window, looking down at the dazzling, soundless river of cars below.
This day had been like one long emotional trek. Arriving at its end now, weary as she was, the scenery had already changed.

========================================

Chapter 12 — Borrowed Fortune
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640259.html

Borrowed Fortune
Ever since he'd seen Yao Yuan the day before and handed over that mysterious bronze mirror, Shuai Hongqiang had gone home in a daze, his mind restless and uneasy.

Winter mornings came late. A fuzzy bloom of frost had crystallized on the glass. It was only half past seven when Shuai Hongqiang, asleep in the guest room, was woken by the thump-thump of running footsteps in the living room. He listened with his eyes shut for a couple of seconds — it was his six-year-old son, Shuai Wenyao. The boy always had more energy on weekends than on school days. It baffled Shuai Hongqiang: on mornings he had to get up for school, the kid would burrow under the covers and refuse to move, but on weekends, when he was supposed to rest, he'd wake up bright and early to play. He was at that age, brimming with energy, never still for a moment.

Shuai Hongqiang pushed open the bedroom door and was met with a wave of warmth and the rice-sweet smell of congee. In the living room, Shuai Wenyao was racing around with a toy airplane held high, his little face flushed red. "Daddy! My plane can fly over that mountain!" The boy pointed out the window at the distant, mist-shrouded cluster of buildings. Shuai Hongqiang looked out. The winter sky was a pale gray, and the old scholar tree in the yard, its branches bare, swayed gently in the cold wind.

From the kitchen came the sizzle of frying. Lin Wan, in a checkered apron, was lifting steaming buns out of a bamboo steamer. In the frying pan beside her, a few eggs had crisped golden-brown at the edges, their yolks still trembling at the center. On the stove, white congee bubbled in a small clay pot, fine bubbles rising. "Awake?" She turned and smiled, fine beads of sweat on the tip of her nose. "Wenyao crawled out of bed at seven, insisting he had to help beat the eggs."

By the time breakfast was on the table, the sky outside had brightened a little more. Shuai Wenyao knelt on his chair, his small hand fumbling with the chopsticks as he tried to pick up a piece of pickled cucumber. "Mommy, it's so slippery!"

"Slow down — you can use a spoon," Lin Wan said with a laugh as she ladled out his congee. In the little blue-and-white porcelain bowl in front of him, the white congee steamed; on a small dish beside it sat half a cube of fermented bean curd and a few strips of pickled cucumber. In front of Shuai Hongqiang was a bowl of golden cornmeal congee — the way they ate it back in his hometown, which Lin Wan had remembered.

The family of three sat down around the table. A smear of sauce clung to the corner of Shuai Wenyao's mouth as he concentrated on "building" an imaginary dam in his bowl of congee with his chopsticks. Lin Wan placed a perfectly fried egg into Shuai Hongqiang's bowl. "It's really cold today. Put a scarf on Wenyao before you head out."

Shuai Hongqiang gave a small nod. His chopsticks had just lifted that golden egg when the phone in his pocket buzzed softly. He pulled it out on instinct — a bank text. "Your account ending 8810 received a transfer of RMB 500,000.00 at 08:02…" The string of figures stood out starkly on the screen, and his chopsticks froze in midair.

"What is it?" Lin Wan asked with concern, still holding the wooden ladle, her eyes full of question.

Before Shuai Hongqiang could answer, the phone rang sharply — and it was Priscilla Chan's "Thousand Songs," its familiar, faintly weathered melody oddly out of place in the calm of the morning. He glanced down at the screen: an unfamiliar overseas number flashing there. He was instantly on guard — almost certainly another scam call. He'd meant to hang up, but somehow his finger slipped and hit Answer instead.

"Brother Qiang! It's me, Ji Wenshan." The voice on the other end was hoarse but smiling, and behind it he could faintly hear the drone of a chainsaw and men shouting in a foreign language.

Shuai Hongqiang couldn't help freezing. He really hadn't expected it to be him — counting it out, they hadn't seen each other in eleven years. He hurried to his feet and walked toward the balcony. As he slid the glass door open, a blast of icy air rushed in, swallowing him whole.

"Brother — I really didn't think it'd be you. All these years, how have you been?" There was surprise and warmth in Shuai Hongqiang's voice.

"I'm doing all right now. You saw the text, didn't you? Five hundred thousand! Three hundred thousand principal, and the rest is interest — don't say anything yet!" Ji Wenshan spoke fast, as if words he'd stored up for years were spilling out all at once.

Shuai Hongqiang gripped the phone tightly, his fingers stiffening of their own accord — and not from the cold.

"Eleven years, Brother Qiang," Ji Wenshan went on, his voice thick with feeling. "The winter of 2014, my factory went under completely. I was buried in debt. I was in despair, ready to give up on everything. I crouched alone at the edge of the Yellow River, honestly thinking about ending it all, jumping in. And right then, you showed up with three hundred thousand in cash. You found me and said, 'Go lie low for a while. Being alive beats everything else.'"

Shuai Hongqiang remembered it clearly. That winter had been bitterly cold, the howling river wind cutting like a knife. Ji Wenshan had been huddled in a tattered cotton coat, clutching a half-empty bottle of baijiu, his eyes full of despair and helplessness.

"I went to Laos — Luang Prabang. The first three years…" Ji Wenshan's voice suddenly caught, and after a moment he said quietly, "I slept in the workers' shed, learned to cut timber from the locals. Got cheated twice along the way. At my lowest, I got lost in the forest for two whole days and only pulled through by drinking from a stream. Later I met an old overseas Chinese man, a master craftsman in rosewood. He took pity on me and made me his apprentice."

Beyond the balcony, the bare branches of the scholar tree shivered in the cold wind. On the street in the distance, early risers hurried along, coats pulled tight, faces tense.

"Bit by bit, I finally figured out the trade — from grading timber for other people at first, to buying it myself later, and eventually opening a small workshop," Ji Wenshan said, a smile creeping into his voice. "A few years back I bought a patch of forest. Not big, but the timber quality is excellent. I caught those good years when the rosewood furniture market was booming, and I finally made a little money… I put some aside every year, always thinking I had to pay you back — principal and interest both."

Something seemed to lodge in Shuai Hongqiang's throat. He forced the words out: "The interest is too much."

"Too much, nothing!" Ji Wenshan raised his voice, firm. "If it hadn't been for your three hundred thousand back then, I'd have been gone long ago — the grass on my grave would've gone through who knows how many seasons by now! I heard the economy hasn't been great at home these years, and it might've hit your business. Think of this little bit of money as a brother's small show of support. Oh — and I've shipped a tea table back to you. Lao red sandalwood. It should arrive when spring comes. Same address, your old place, right? My workers are calling me — Brother Qiang, thank you. Really."

The moment he finished, the call cut off, the busy tone going dut-dut-dut four or five times. Shuai Hongqiang still gripped the phone tightly, unable to lower it for a long while. He slowly turned his head and looked back inside — at the table, Lin Wan was gently wiping the sauce from their son's mouth with a tissue. Then she lifted her head, her eyes meeting his, full of question. Shuai Wenyao, meanwhile, held up a half-eaten bun, his big curious eyes fixed unblinking on his father standing on the balcony, taking the call.

Shuai Hongqiang sat back down at the table and turned the phone screen toward his wife. Lin Wan looked down at it for a long moment, her lashes trembling faintly in the morning light.

"Ji Wenshan? The brother you told me about in your startup story, the one you did the building-materials business with?" she asked softly.

"He went to Laos, makes rosewood furniture." Shuai Hongqiang bent his head to drink his congee, the steam warm against his face. "That was him just now, paying me back. Principal and interest."

"That's wonderful." Lin Wan smiled gently and asked nothing more.

Five hundred thousand had once meant nothing to Shuai Hongqiang, but to the Shuai Hongqiang of today, it was no small sum.

He picked up the now-lukewarm fried egg and chewed slowly. Shuai Wenyao clamored for another half bowl of congee, and Lin Wan got up to fetch it. As she passed behind him, her hand came to rest lightly on his shoulder, lingering for two seconds.

"Daddy!" Shuai Wenyao held up his soup spoon, a grain of rice stuck to the corner of his mouth. "Are we having cornmeal congee again tomorrow?"

"We are." Shuai Hongqiang took the congee Lin Wan had ladled and set it in front of his son, then looked over at her. His wife was taking small sips of her own congee, and behind the rising steam there were very faint smile lines at the corners of her eyes.

In front of his wife and son, Shuai Hongqiang held tightly to his outward calm. He slowly finished the last mouthful of cornmeal congee in his bowl and even managed a smile for Lin Wan. "These pickled cucumbers get better every time."

Lin Wan gave him a tender look. "Eat more if you like them — there's plenty."

Shuai Wenyao had already hopped down from his chair and was flying his toy airplane on patrol around the living room. The child's laughter was clear and innocent, but listening to it, Shuai Hongqiang felt as though it reached him through some barrier, not quite real.

Beneath his calm surface, his heart slammed against his ribs like a war drum. Borrowed fortune? Could there really be something so uncanny in this world? That bronze mirror he'd won at auction eight years ago — which he'd only thought of as elegant and antique, maybe enough to win Yao Yuan a smile — did it truly hold some unbelievable power, just as the auctioneer's vague hints had suggested back then?

He thought of that white-haired old appraiser. Eight years ago, at the winter auction at "Yaji Studio," when the old man had displayed the mirror, his fingers had paused for a moment on the cloud-and-dragon pattern on its back, a meaningful look in his eyes, as if there were something he wanted to say but wouldn't. In the end, all he'd said was, "This mirror is said to carry an ancient resonance; its keeper must treat it with a sincere heart." There had been something equivocal and mysterious in his tone.

And more than that — there was Yao Yuan.

This sudden "return on his gift" had thrown open the floodgates of memory. During the years he and Yao Yuan had been together, they'd once gone wandering through the antique market. Yao Yuan, her arm linked through his, had stopped in front of a jade shop. An old man in a gray Daoist robe, with white hair and a white beard, had suddenly stepped out from a side alley, stared at Yao Yuan's face for a full half minute, and then strode up to them.

"Young lady, might I have a word in private?"

Shuai Hongqiang had instinctively pulled Yao Yuan behind him. "Can I help you?"

The old man didn't answer, only kept his eyes on Yao Yuan, a strange light in them. "This young lady's fate is unusual — very like an account in a certain damaged scroll I once read. I'll take not a single coin; I only want to confirm it."

Yao Yuan found it amusing; Shuai Hongqiang thought the man a street charlatan. But the old man persisted, trailing them for half a block, until Yao Yuan, worn down, finally gave him her birth date and hour.

The old man worked the count on his fingers for a long time, his expression growing graver with every calculation.

"You lost a parent young, and your mother remarried — is that so?"

Yao Yuan's smile faded a little. She nodded.

"Your career line is excellent. There's a small setback or two before thirty, but the overall trend is upward." The old man quickened his pace. "After thirty, a great fortune is coming. If this poor Daoist hasn't miscalculated, you are entering the Ninth Purple Fire Cycle. This cycle turns once every twenty years and governs change, innovation, and fire-natured industries. This fortune will push you to heights in your career you've never seen before."

He paused, looking into Yao Yuan's eyes. "You are a person who carries great destiny. One day you may be able to influence — even change — the rules of certain fields. But great fortune comes with great peril. Remember, above all, to hold fast to your true heart."

Before leaving, the old man took from his robe an exquisitely carved fire-phoenix gourd, no bigger than a thumb, strung on a red cord. "This is for you — to forge a good karmic tie."

Yao Yuan didn't let him read her fortune for nothing; smiling, she pressed six hundred yuan into his hand. The episode later became a topic over tea and meals, with friends laughing that the old man had a head for business — trading a gourd worth a few yuan for six hundred. They'd treated it as nothing more than an amusing interlude, and gradually forgot it.

It was only later, after they'd gone their separate ways and his core businesses had been battered by the pandemic — his cash flow on the brink of snapping more than once — while Yao Yuan, just as the prophecy had foretold, rode the wind to soaring heights, her career blazing at its peak, that on some difficult, sleepless night he'd suddenly remembered this old story, and a complicated feeling beyond words had welled up in him.

Three months ago, after yet another fruitless attempt to chase a client for payment, Shuai Hongqiang had been drinking alone late at night when he dug out that bronze mirror from the back of a drawer. Eight years on, the patina seemed a shade deeper, and the lines of the Kui-dragon's eyes had wound like something alive under the desk lamp. He'd thought of the auctioneer's vague "its keeper must treat it with a sincere heart," of the geomancer's "Ninth Purple Fire Cycle," of Yao Yuan's present glory and his own straits.

An absurd notion had quietly taken root that hazy, drunken night.

What if… what if that mirror really did have something special about it? What if the auctioneer's words weren't just mystification?

Once the thought appeared, it grew and twined like a vine. He began looking up all sorts of occult talk online about "borrowing fortune" and "turning one's luck" — most of it preposterous, yet he couldn't stop himself from wondering: what if?

Perhaps… it was precisely in that hard-to-name state of mind — a mix of nostalgia, resentment, and a secret urge to "just try it" — that yesterday he had taken that mirror, sealed away for eight years, and gone to see Yao Yuan.

The mirror had been handed over. And just one night later, Ji Wenshan, gone for ten years, had paid back five hundred thousand. The timing was such a coincidence it made his heart pound with dread.

"I'm going out for a bit." Shuai Hongqiang set down his chopsticks and said to Lin Wan. There was still a little congee left in his bowl. He pulled out a tissue and wiped his mouth, keeping his movements as natural as he could. "Something I need to do — over by the antique market." His own voice came out steadier than he expected.

Lin Wan never pressed him. She only nodded. "Be careful on the road. Coming back for lunch?"

"Hard to say. Eat without me, don't wait." He bent down and kissed his son's forehead, grabbed his coat, and went out the door.

========================================

Chapter 13 — The Secret of the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror: Reversing Time, Turning Fortune
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640260.html

Cold wind hit him in the face as he stepped out of the building. Shuai Hongqiang started the car and pulled into the thin Saturday-morning traffic of a winter weekend. He switched on the car radio, then switched it off again. It was too quiet—quiet enough that he could hear his own heartbeat.
He had bought that bronze mirror eight years ago, at the winter auction held by "Yaji Studio." Back then the auction house's white-haired old appraiser had presented the lot himself, but when it came to introducing it, he had been oddly vague.
"This is a Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror. The patterning is clear, the patina natural, though the edge is damaged." The old appraiser adjusted his glasses and paused for a moment. "This piece has years on it. Its collector must be sincere of heart."
Someone had laughed and asked, "Old Chen, is there some story behind this mirror?"
The old appraiser had given the man a long, deep look. "Old things have souls of their own. They only ask to be treated well."
At the time, Shuai Hongqiang had taken it for the auction house's usual sales patter. Now, thinking back, it seemed there had been something hidden in that look on the old man's face—words he had started to say and swallowed.
If… if the mirror really worked, then that old man surely knew something.
The antiques district was in the western part of the city, and on a weekend morning it was still quiet. Shuai Hongqiang parked and walked straight to "Guangu Hall." Beneath a wooden plaque, warm yellow light glowed behind the glass door. He pushed it open, and a wind chime sounded softly.
The shop was very still, only a young assistant wiping down a curio shelf. Seeing a customer, the assistant looked up. "Is there something you'd like to see, sir?"
"I'm looking for Old Chen—is Master Chen in?"
The assistant sized him up. "Master Chen is in the back tidying the storeroom. One moment, please."
