Shuai Hongqiang stood at the counter, his gaze drifting over the porcelain vases and jade pieces on the shelves. Everything here carried the weight of accumulated time; even the air was thick with the smell of old wood and paper.
About ten minutes later, the curtain at the back parted, and a white-haired old man came out. It was the old appraiser, Master Chen. He wore a dark grey Chinese-style jacket and a pair of reading glasses, and when he saw Shuai Hongqiang, he gave a slight start.
"It's you." Master Chen's memory was excellent. "The gentleman who bought that Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror eight years ago."
"You have a good memory, Master Chen." Shuai Hongqiang stepped forward. "I've come today to ask about that mirror."
Master Chen gave him a long, deep look, said nothing, and turned to gesture him inside. Beyond the curtain was a small tea room, with a purple-clay tea set arranged on a rosewood table. Master Chen sat down behind the table and began heating water and rinsing the cups.
"Sit."
Shuai Hongqiang sat down across from him. The water came to a boil, and Master Chen warmed the cups, took out the leaves, and brewed the tea, his movements unhurried. The fragrance of the tea slowly filled the room—the rich, mellow scent particular to aged pu'er.
"That mirror," Master Chen said at last, pushing a cup in front of Shuai Hongqiang. "You gave it away?"
Shuai Hongqiang's hand jerked, and the tea nearly spilled. "How did you know?"
"A guess." Master Chen lifted his own cup and breathed in its scent. "That mirror is no ordinary thing. When you won it that year, I wanted to warn you, but every trade has its rules—some things can't be spelled out. Over the years, I've wondered now and then where that mirror had ended up."
Shuai Hongqiang gripped his cup. "Master Chen, that mirror… what exactly is special about it?"
Master Chen raised his eyes to him, the gaze behind his lenses sharp as a hawk's. "After you gave it away—did something happen?"
Silence spread through the tea room. The din from outside the window drifted in faintly, only making the room feel quieter still.
"This morning," Shuai Hongqiang heard his own voice come out dry, "I received a debt that had gone eleven years unpaid—five hundred thousand." He went on to mention the mirror's original owner—Yao Yuan—and the prophecy a feng shui master had made years ago.
"And then you thought of this mirror." Master Chen nodded slowly when he had finished. "Eight years ago, when you won it, I saw that your eyes were clear—you only wanted to win a beautiful woman's smile—so I said nothing more. Now that you've come to me, you must have a suspicion."
With that he let out a long sigh, his fingers tapping idly on the rosewood tabletop. "So it's true… that mirror has, after all, passed into the hands of the 'one destined to bear it.'"
Shuai Hongqiang's throat bobbed. "Master Chen, please tell me plainly. What exactly is strange about this mirror? This talk of borrowed fortune—can it really be true?"
Master Chen didn't answer directly. He rose and went to the depths of a locked bookcase behind him, taking out a plain, antique wooden box. He opened it; inside was a parchment booklet carefully bound with silk thread, the pages scorched yellow and brittle, the corners badly worn.
"This is a damaged scroll handed down through generations of my school," Master Chen said, opening the booklet carefully. "It records certain… extraordinary old objects. The bronze mirror you won is among them." He turned the pages gently and pointed to one of them.
Shuai Hongqiang leaned in to look. On the yellowed page, a bronze mirror had been rendered in fine, meticulous brushwork—almost identical to the one he had bought. Beside the image ran dense columns of small regular script, the ink faded but still faintly legible.
"In the Tianbao years of the Tang dynasty, there lived a magus named Lingxuzi, deeply versed in astrology and the principles of yin and yang, and master of the art of reading the heavens. On the Kunlun Ruins he once glimpsed the breath of the cosmos at its first opening—that wisp of primordial chaos-energy, condensed yet undispersed, hidden within the marrow of stone, which he gathered and stored by secret art. Reading the omens of disorder in the stars, he knew the golden age was about to fall. Having also chanced upon a strange bronze fallen from beyond the heavens, he took that mystic bronze for its frame and, expending the strength of his whole life, smelted that wisp of primordial energy into it, casting three 'Time-Reversal Mirrors,' each concealing the art of 'turning time and changing fortune.'" Master Chen's aged fingers traced over the characters, his voice low. "The mystic bronze locks the energy in; the talismanic array channels the fortune. The roots of this mirror were sunk, from the very start, into the primordial vein of energy from before heaven and earth divided. The power of this mirror lies not in reflecting form, but in… reflecting fortune, reflecting time."
Master Chen went on, his gaze sweeping over the faint ring of cloud patterns on the mirror's back in the diagram. "Look at this outermost band of patterning. It seems like ordinary cloud ornament, but in truth it is the 'Primal-Drawing Array,' designed solely to lock in the primordial energy. That energy is by nature without fixed form or fixed course—the very root of chaos—yet it can transform, following the human heart and the turning of worldly affairs, into ten thousand possibilities. This is the foundation of 'turning time and changing fortune.'"
"Reflecting time?" Shuai Hongqiang's heart lurched.
"Just so." Master Chen nodded, pointing to several deeply hidden nodes within the patterning on the mirror's back. "Look here, and here… The lines of the pattern secretly accord with the constellations of the celestial round, and within them are embedded two ancient talismanic arrays that generate and draw upon each other. One is the 'Lure of Time's Reversal,' the other the 'Pivot of Fortune's Shift.' Both the 'Lure of Time's Reversal' and the 'Pivot of Fortune's Shift' draw their source from the primordial energy. When the 'Lure of Time's Reversal' meets the intense, regret-laden longing of one born under the 'Nine-Purple Departing-Fire' fate, it can stir the property of 'time-reversal' contained within the primordial energy, drawing that person's spirit briefly back to some important moment in their life. It might be the moment before a major choice, or the moment a regret took place."
"Briefly drawn back? Sent back?" Shuai Hongqiang licked his dry lips.
"It is not the whole body crossing over, nor a lasting stay," Master Chen explained. "According to the scroll, the mirror-bearer's primary consciousness—or 'spirit-mind'—temporarily returns into their past self. Not for long: perhaps only a few hours, or a day or two. During that time, the returned 'spirit-mind' knows the future, and can guide and warn the past self, telling them the 'correct path.'"
Shuai Hongqiang felt his throat go dry. "Then… does the past self listen? Can fate be changed?"
"Hard to say." Master Chen shook his head. "The scroll is vague on this. The 'spirit-mind' returns like an onlooker entering a dream to guide their own self, but whether the past self grasps it, whether it obeys, and how the track of fate truly shifts afterward… these are all variables. Perhaps small pits and bumps can be avoided; perhaps the larger tide cannot, in the end, be turned. But whatever the case, once this 'reversal and guidance' takes place…"
He raised his eyes and looked at Shuai Hongqiang, his gaze deep and dark. "According to the record—" Master Chen turned to the next page, which held a more intricate analysis of the talismanic script, the channels of energy traced in cinnabar like a coiling dragon. He slowly spoke the most crucial part: "The instant the 'Lure of Time's Reversal' is triggered, the 'Pivot of Fortune's Shift' is set off as well. Every attempt the mirror-bearer makes, in the past, to intervene in their own fate—whether it succeeds in the end or not—is regarded by this array as a 'disturbance and refinement of an already-fixed fortune.' And this purer 'fortune-energy,' produced by the disturbance, is drawn by the 'Pivot of Fortune's Shift' into the mirror, to fuse and be refined together with the primordial energy—like melting scattered scraps of silver foil into a single solid ingot…"
"The primordial energy can take in all things, and can transform all things. The fortune-energy it refines becomes purer still. And you—you were the one who gave the mirror, and at the moment you gave it, your heart held the thought of borrowing its fortune to change your own straits—even if that thought was weak at first, only a fleeting 'let's see if it works.' The moment that thought arose, it became a 'pact' linking you to the primordial energy within the mirror, making you the receiving end of this chain of 'fortune-shifting.' Each time a person of that particular fate reverses time and guides their past self once more, the refined fortune-energy peels away from the primordial energy along this pact and flows instead to you—and your fortune in the present improves by a measure. Wealth, gain, opportunity—all become possible."
"Then what about Yao Yuan? What happens to her? If she keeps 'going back' like this, does it harm her?" Shuai Hongqiang asked urgently, guilt surging up in him like a tide. When he first gave the mirror, his feelings had been complicated, but he had never imagined placing her in so strange and dangerous a position.
"The scroll gives a warning: 'Let the spirit cross too often, and its root is easily damaged; fortune's shift is fickle—bear its fruit with care.'" Master Chen's expression was grave. "It means that crossing the barrier of time and space too frequently wears down the very mind and soul of the mirror-bearer; do it too many times, and the consequences are unforeseeable. And the one who borrows the fortune seems to reap without sowing, but the fortune they receive comes from disturbing and refining another's fate—it is unstable by nature, and entangled in cause and effect. Take the benefit, and you must bear the matching fruit. When that retribution will come, and in what form, no one can say."
At last Shuai Hongqiang understood where that five hundred thousand had come from—this money was the return on Ji Wenshan's ten years of struggle, and it was the "fortune" the bronze mirror had drawn off and converted when Yao Yuan "reversed time" in an attempt to change her own course. Without the fortune the mirror had "borrowed," there was no telling when Ji Wenshan would ever have repaid the money.
This was the first "fruit" he had received.
"Master Chen, what should I do now? Take the mirror back? Destroy it?" Shuai Hongqiang's voice was tight.
"The thought of borrowing fortune has already taken root in you, and the mirror-pact is formed. Even if you take it back, the borrowing of fortune will go on—only perhaps more slowly." Master Chen closed the booklet. "The pact is made; forcing it back or destroying it may bring disaster. Especially for the mirror-bearer: if her 'spirit-mind' happens to be in the midst of a reversal, moving the mirror rashly could keep her consciousness from returning to its place properly—and then there would be real trouble."
He looked at Shuai Hongqiang, a trace of pity in his eyes. "For now, you might first watch and wait, but remember two things. First: every stroke of good fortune you gain comes from disturbing the track of her fate. It is not rightfully yours; to use it you must feel shame, and you cannot enjoy it in peace. As the mirror keeps turning and you gain more, the greater the backlash if you cannot keep the energy in balance—everything you've borrowed will be lost in ways you cannot foresee. You can only pray that heaven and earth keep their balance, and the backlash is not too severe. Second: watch her condition closely. If she mentions 'reversals' more and more often, or shows signs of mental disturbance, you must find her at once and bring her to me. Perhaps… there is some other remedy, but it will take careful thought."
"Master Chen, since you knew how dangerous this mirror was, why didn't you stop me back then?"
"I dropped a hint." The old man smiled bitterly. "That line—'its collector must be sincere of heart'—was already breaking the rules. The auction house's code forbids us from openly stating whether a piece is good or ill; it all rests on the buyer's eye and fate. And besides…" He paused. "Back then I read your face, and you were no greedy or wicked sort. I thought you might only be collecting it, so I didn't press to stop you. Who knew that, eight years on, this thought would stir in you."
The clamor of the antiques district drifted in through the window—all those people haggling, appraising, turning pieces over in their hands. In Shuai Hongqiang's ears now, it sounded so distant, so unreal.
As he left "Yaji Studio," Shuai Hongqiang's steps felt unsteady. The winter sun fell on him, but carried not the slightest warmth.
He climbed into the car and didn't start it right away, just staring blankly ahead. His phone screen was lit, showing his WeChat conversation with Yao Yuan. His fingers hovered over the keyboard, typing words out and deleting them again.
In the end, he sent only a single line: "Yao Yuan, about the mirror I gave you yesterday—if you feel anything is off, or… if you have any very strange dreams, tell me anytime."
He waited five minutes, but no reply came from her.
Shuai Hongqiang looked at the WeChat conversation, his heart a tangle of mixed feelings. She still had no idea that she had become the heart of a strange "fortune-shifting." And he, the one who had first given the mirror, was torn between conscience and greed within, even as he watched, helpless, the bronze mirror raise a dangerous, alluring bridge between his fate and hers.
At the far end of the bridge lay her past, and a track that might be rewritten.
At this end of the bridge lay his present, and this endless, scalding "good fortune."
And this, perhaps, was only the beginning.
Just then his phone buzzed—a WeChat message from Lin Wan: "Wenyao says he misses you and wants to know when you're coming home. I've kept lunch for you."
He typed back: "Soon."
Shuai Hongqiang gathered himself, and drove home.

========================================

Chapter 14 — Lin Wan, "The Wife with the Face of a Virtuous Lady"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640261.html

When Shuai Hongqiang pushed open the front door, the grandfather clock in the living room was just striking the second chime of one-thirty in the afternoon. The tone was deep and heavy — an antique he'd had shipped up from Guangzhou specially when they married. Lin Wan had said at the time that it was "a bit too grand," but in the end she'd let it take up residence in the living room anyway.

The house was very quiet. Their son, Shuai Wenyao, had eaten lunch and was down for his nap; the door of the children's room stood ajar, leaking the faint white noise of a humidifier. On the kitchen counter, a few white porcelain bowls and dishes sat covered — his lunch, kept for him. Braised pork ribs, stir-fried baby bok choy, tomato and egg soup, all warming on a heating mat, kept at exactly the right temperature for the first bite.

Everything was perfectly seen to. Lin Wan fulfilled every duty of a "wife" flawlessly — even more flawlessly than before.

In the blink of an eye, they'd been married more than seven years. People talk about the "seven-year itch"; whether such an itch existed, Shuai Hongqiang couldn't quite say. He only knew, plainly, that Lin Wan was no longer the way she'd been as a newlywed — when everything had revolved around him, when she'd poured all her care and love onto him without holding anything back.

The Lin Wan who had once made him feel "seen," the Lin Wan who treated him as a living, complicated person worth exploring rather than merely a "husband" —

the Lin Wan whose eyes and brows used to light up with tenderness whenever she mentioned him — was quietly making her exit, day by day, inside all this perfection.

And yet, in every small detail of daily life, Lin Wan still arranged everything beautifully, with not a single fault to find. What they shared now was only a carefully maintained, constant "appropriateness," and a calm blueprint concerning their child's future.

Shuai Hongqiang walked to the study door. It wasn't shut all the way, left open a hand's width.

Lin Wan was inside.

She had her back to the door, seated at the broad solid-wood desk. The afternoon sun sliced in at an angle through the gaps in the blinds, plating a fuzzy gold edge onto her crisp short hair and her pale-gray cashmere cardigan. Wearing those thin-rimmed blue-light glasses, she was studying three monitors lined up side by side. The left one held a dense table of data; the middle, a mind map of a livestream script; the right, the page of some women's personal-growth forum.

She wasn't speaking, and she wasn't operating anything — just looking, quietly. Now and then she'd tap the trackpad lightly with a fingertip, scrolling the page, or make a small mark on one line of text. The whole motion was smooth, precise, without a trace of excess hesitation or emotional ripple.

In that moment she looked exactly like the educated woman from years ago at the redevelopment symposium, sitting upright in the audience, calmly recording and dissecting everything.

Shuai Hongqiang was a grassroots man who had clawed his way out of a deep mountain gully with his life.

The first half of his life was written full of the weight of reality. The year he was in middle school, his mother fell gravely ill, and it crushed the family flat — crushed his schoolbooks along with it. He'd ground himself down into the dust, fist and foot, and built — with nothing but a wild, scrappy ferocity — the thing other people called "success." Maybe that was exactly why those halls he'd never entered, those books he'd never finished, became a stubborn point of brightness deep in his heart. He had an almost instinctive admiration and longing for women who were highly educated, cultured. At the dinner table, in the world of business, the shrewdness he'd forged was enough to handle most situations. But when such women happened to be talking — when the name of some unfamiliar author, a stretch of jargon, a viewpoint beyond the range of his experience drifted past — though he couldn't catch it, his heart would stir faintly. That bit of not understanding, far from diminishing anything, instead wrapped them in a halo that both soothed and fascinated him — that was art, that was civilization, that was the other world he'd missed along the way. Yao Yuan was that light, and so was Lin Wan.

Yao Yuan's beauty was a beauty that flaunted itself and knew it.

It was concrete, with an aggressive edge. Like a bared blade — bright, dazzling, gleaming hot — cleaving open all the stale air to come straight at you. Hers was a wanton beauty, a swaggering, triumphant beauty, and she knew full well the power of that beauty. She wasn't just beautiful; she had the intelligence to match it. Standing beside her satisfied more than a man's vanity — it was an inflated masculine pride, the kind that came from conquering the most brilliant quarry of all. Shuai Hongqiang had loved her — perhaps, in his meager emotional history, the one burning that came closest to that thing called "love." But the flame was too fierce; it scorched him into panic. Marry her? That took an enormous kind of courage. He'd been too young then; his fists had earned him money, but not enough nerve — they hadn't polished away the dread of being "uncultured" that lived in his very marrow. He was afraid — afraid this fire was something he'd never be able to contain, afraid that his nouveau-riche foundation couldn't hold up under the dazzle of her beauty and the sharp life-force in her. So he hesitated, he shrank back, he stayed silent, and watched her cool down inside all that expectation. In the end, heart gone cold, she left — taking with her a whole bank of fire-clouds, like a sunset burning out at the edge of the sky.

Lin Wan walked into his world in that cold winter, the very winter Yao Yuan left.

Lin Wan's beauty was a beauty unaware of itself.

Not dazzling, not arresting — like a smooth, warm piece of jade, like a pool of still water, utterly unconscious of itself. She had a face naturally drawn by traditional taste into the very picture of "virtue and grace." A full forehead, smooth brow-bone, a nose-bridge straight but never sharp, lips whose curve carried a faint, gentle smile by nature. Without any makeup, she was a well-bred young lady stepped out of an old magazine illustration; with a light touch of powder, she gave off the composed dignity of the lady of the house. Her fans jokingly called this Lin Wan's classic "matriarch face" — not meaning she looked old, but that warm quality, settled by years, able to take in all hardship and all stories.

Lin Wan's eyes were very clear, like an autumn lake, the irises a pale brown, holding a strange, compassionate insight when she looked at people. Crisp short hair, three centimeters above the ear, not a strand out of place. Her figure was tall and straight — none of the frailty of deliberate dieting, but a suppleness and strength sculpted by years of disciplined exercise.

This beauty didn't flaunt itself, yet it carried its own weight.

She'd been sitting just like this the first time he saw her too, back straight as a young poplar. On stage the experts held forth at length; down below she took rapid notes, occasionally lifting her eyes — and in those pale-brown eyes there was not a trace of worship or excitement, only a calm scrutiny and analysis. It was that very gaze, in a hall full of slickness and anxiety and calculation, that stood out fresh and self-possessed.

Late winter, 2017: a symposium hosted by the municipal planning bureau, "Organic Renewal and Community Revitalization of Old Industrial Districts." The venue chosen was a creative park converted from an old textile mill, where rough concrete pillars stood in jarring contrast with sleek spotlights.

Twenty-eight-year-old Lin Wan attended as a young-scholar delegate, in her capacity as a researcher and doctoral candidate at a top university's Center for Urban and Social Development, prepared to present a report based on big-data analysis: "Class Mobility and Emotional Cost in Spatial Restructuring — A Study of Three Redevelopment Zones."

That day she wore a well-tailored pale-gray skirt suit, her short hair crisp, her makeup light. Sitting in the third row, a notebook stamped with the university crest open on her lap, an extra-fine black pen in her hand, she jotted down keywords in the gaps between speakers. Her posture was upright, her expression focused — a sharp contrast to some of the attendees around her who whispered to one another and scrolled their phones. In the interval while she waited to go up, she even took an English monograph dotted with sticky notes from her bag and read it, undistracted.

Thirty-eight-year-old Shuai Hongqiang, meanwhile, had been "invited" by the organizers under the double billing of "local entrepreneur" and "potential investor" — to fill out the room and to hear the policy talk. He wore an expensive but slightly dated dark-blue suit, the top button of his shirt undone, his tie a little crooked. He sat in the back row by the door, handy for taking calls and slipping out early. He had a notebook open in front of him too, but on it was no text — it was a crude floor plan of a warehouse scrawled in thick marker, with a few rows of figures. He was calculating how much compensation floor-area his logistics warehouse — about to fall within the redevelopment zone — could be traded for, or what loopholes in the policy he might exploit.

Past the halfway mark, the symposium moved into the Q&A. A planning-institute expert on stage was holding forth on "humanistic care" and "community warmth," his language abstract and lovely.

Shuai Hongqiang had grown impatient. The instant the expert finished, he was the first to raise his hand. He didn't wait for a staffer to pass him a microphone; he just stood up, his voice booming, carrying the blunt, looming pressure peculiar to a grassroots businessman:

"You speak very well, sir, but I want to ask something concrete. I'm the owner of that old freight-station plot in such-and-such district. You talk about 'revitalization,' you talk about 'sentiment' — well, what happens to my warehouse? There are thirty-odd workers in there counting on it to eat. The government's compensation plan only counts building floor-area; it doesn't count the paved ground and the security systems I've put in over the years. Is that reasonable? Can all that 'warmth' of yours help me pay my workers' wages?"

The hall went quiet for a moment. The expert, interrupted mid-lyric, was a little embarrassed, and tried to answer with "the macro view" and "long-term interests."

That was when Lin Wan raised her hand. She took the microphone, her voice clear and steady — not loud, but enough for the whole room to hear:

"This gentleman's question is very specific, and very representative. As it happens, I've studied the very district you mention." She turned to a particular page of her notebook, her pace even, yet brimming with solid data to back it. "Cross-referencing business-registration and social-insurance records from 2014 to 2017, the logistics cluster where your warehouse sits actually contains seventeen active small-and-medium logistics firms, employing roughly two hundred and eighty people directly — not the thirty-odd you mentioned. The average survival cycle of these firms is 2.7 years, with an annual staff-turnover rate of 35 percent. Which means that even if the warehouse stayed untouched, the employment of these workers would itself be extremely unstable."

Shuai Hongqiang was startled. He narrowed his eyes and, for the first time, looked carefully at the young woman who had risen in the front rows.

Lin Wan continued, turning her calm gaze toward Shuai Hongqiang, then to the expert on stage: "So the problem may need to be broken down. First, there's the technical question of property-rights compensation, which involves valuation standards. I've noticed that Article 13 of the latest 'Implementation Rules (Draft for Comment)' leaves a gray area on the compensation of special structures — that might serve as an opening for negotiation." She stated the article number precisely.

"Second, there's the question of the social cost of resettling the workers. This is exactly where the 'humanistic care' in the plan runs into difficulty on the ground. My team has run the numbers: if you adopt a package of 'compensation payment + targeted skills training + priority entry into the newly built industrial park,' the government's short-term cost rises by about 8 percent, but it lowers the potential unemployment-relief outlays and social-instability risk over the next five years. In the long run, it's economical. There's a detailed model later in my report."

When she finished, she gave a slight nod and sat down. No emotion — only analysis and a plan.

At the tea break, Shuai Hongqiang, carrying a cup of instant coffee, walked over of his own accord to Lin Wan, who stood alone by the window going over her documents.

"Dr. Lin?" he said, his tone a little softer than on stage, but still with an appraising edge.

"You're the gentleman who asked the question just now?" Lin Wan turned, her expression composed.

"Name's Shuai. Shuai Hongqiang." He handed over a business card, the title "General Manager, such-and-such Logistics Co., Ltd.," exquisitely designed. "Those figures you gave just now — where'd they come from? Are they accurate?"

"My research requires going through a great deal of policy text, local archives, and case precedents. Cross-validation of public data, plus a limited number of sample interviews. The accuracy rate is around 92 percent." Lin Wan took the card, glanced at it, and drew an understated card of her own from an inside jacket pocket to hand back. "My research needs field verification. If you don't mind, Boss Shuai, perhaps I could come look at your warehouse — some of the data may need checking."

Shuai Hongqiang laughed, the amused laugh of a man with a streetwise edge. "Research? Research how we get our buildings torn down? And after the research is done? Write a paper, win some award? Not a damn bit of use to us. Aren't you afraid I'll think you're meddling?" The words were blunt, full of the grassroots distrust — and faint hostility — toward intellectuals who fought their battles on paper.

Lin Wan's tone stayed calm, as if she were stating a theorem. "Research is for understanding. Understanding why things turn out this way, understanding where your predicament sits within the whole system, understanding which strategies of resistance are effective and which are useless or even harmful. For instance —" she shifted, pointing to the paving Shuai Hongqiang had mentioned earlier, "the heavy-truck load-bearing pavement you spoke of does have corresponding provisions in the classification standards for urban infrastructure and in the compensation rules. But in the district baseline land-price system updated in 2013, its weighting was lowered. If you can produce the special construction-acceptance certificate from that year, you might be able to invoke an earlier local-regulation clause as grounds in the negotiation."

Her pace was even, her terms professional, every sentence landing squarely at the core of the practical problems Shuai Hongqiang cared about. The contempt on his face gradually withdrew, replaced by a look of surprise and reassessment.

Lin Wan then asked back, "Are you the owners' representative for this district? I've noticed that the success rate of scattered, individual resistance is very low, but once a small alliance forms, the negotiating leverage increases exponentially. Does your current alliance have a shared bottom-line plan? Or is everyone out for themselves?"

Shuai Hongqiang didn't answer the question; he only studied the young woman before him again, carefully. She wasn't spouting empty theory — she really seemed to "get it," and to get it to a level that could hold a conversation with his streetwise experience. He looked again at the thick monograph in her hands, full of charts and English annotations. A strange feeling welled up in him. He'd seen many women — beautiful ones, gentle ones, shrewd ones — but never one like this: like a precise, beautiful instrument whose operating manual he couldn't read at all. She even... seemed to be treating him as an interesting research sample.

"There's people at the warehouse on weekends," Shuai Hongqiang said, draining his coffee and crushing the paper cup flat. "Come by — call ahead. Let me see for myself how you top-rank scholars 'research' rough types like us."

Leaving the venue that day, Lin Wan wrote in her notebook:

"New investigative contact: grassroots entrepreneur, Shuai Hongqiang. Typical sample, sensitive to policy, pragmatism above all, possesses a strategy of resistance, emotional expression direct and rough. Worth observing in depth: 1. risk-decision pattern; 2. mode of interaction with grassroots bureaucracy; 3. complex attitude toward 'knowledge' (both dismissive and curious). Potential research value."

Shuai Hongqiang, meanwhile, sat back in his Land Rover but didn't start it right away. He took out Lin Wan's card and looked at it again. The sunlight fell through the car window onto those words — "Doctoral Candidate in Sociology."

"Doctor..." he said aloud, and shook his head — yet he tucked the card carefully into a sleeve of his wallet, rather than tossing it casually into the glove compartment.

He thought of the clear-eyed look she'd worn when she spoke, and that calm expression that said "everything can be analyzed." Interesting. Completely different from the women in his world, who either clung or calculated. She seemed to live by another set of rules — a set made of numbers, models, and "logic."

And Shuai Hongqiang's creed in life was this: all rules could, in the end, be exploited, negotiated, or broken.

Maybe this was a new domain of "rules" worth getting to know a little.

Outside the car window, the chimney of the old factory stood silent in the setting sun.

Their meeting began in a stretch of old factory grounds about to be torn down and remade — the analytical framework of a sociologist colliding, in that moment, with the survival wisdom of a grassroots entrepreneur, striking the first sparks. Neither of them imagined that those sparks would also open a long contest — over rules, power, and self-definition — that would in time spread to every corner of their lives.

Maybe this is the most interesting thing between a man and a woman. Shuai Hongqiang was drawn to Lin Wan — the highly educated, cerebral woman he himself had never possessed — entranced by that calm, clear light she carried from another order of the world. And Lin Wan had been raised, from childhood, disciplined by the rules and rituals of propriety and academia. She admired the survival wisdom Shuai Hongqiang had honed in the wilds, that free-and-easy, streetwise loyalty of a man unbound by rules. They say love is finding your own reflection in another person — those familiar traits, those shared interests, like finding another self in the vast sea of people, fitting together, drawing each other in; but really it's more like finding in the other the parts of yourself that are missing — the qualities, the courage, or the dreams we long for but have never had.

"You're back?"

At some point Lin Wan had already turned her head, looking at him through the crack in the door. She'd taken off her glasses, and on her face was the gentle, gracious smile he knew so well — the corners of her mouth curved to perfection, her eyes holding exactly the right measure of inquiry.

"Your food's in the kitchen, the temperature should be just right. Wenyao has art class at four; I'll wake him at two-fifty. If you've got nothing on this afternoon, you take him to class."

Her voice, like her smile, was gentle, clear, methodical.

"All right." Shuai Hongqiang heard himself answer, his own voice as usual.

He turned and walked to the kitchen, lifting that bowl of soup at exactly the right temperature. The soup was savory, the ribs stewed tender to the bone, the greens a vivid green.

He took a sip, then another.

His stomach was warm, and it settled a little of the alarm of having learned the secret of the bronze mirror.

But some place in his heart still felt wrong, and uneasy. He knew that some things were like this bowl of soup at exactly the right temperature — you couldn't find a single fault with it. And yet you also knew, clearly, that the first boiling, churning, scalding heat had been left behind forever in some moment of the past.

He'd married her, certainly, because she carried everything "respectable" and "high-class" that he longed for, giving him — a middle-school dropout from the grassroots — a secret sense of conquest and of making up for what he lacked. Her cerebral nature, her composure, that warm temperament of hers that seemed able to take in everything and dissolve everything, made him feel safe, made him feel a satisfaction of being "elevated." But even more, it was because he'd thought that light could be his own, could illuminate the dark corners of his world — the ones left from all the years he'd fought on by instinct and fists, corners even he couldn't quite name. That it could let him truly say goodbye to that Shuai Hongqiang who had walked barefoot out of the mountain gully, covered in dust and dread.

The indicator light on the kitchen heating mat went from red to green and from green to red again, cycling soundlessly.

At four in the afternoon, after dropping off the boy, Shuai Hongqiang didn't go home. He drove two aimless loops around the area, then came back to the foot of the art-school building, sat in the car, and watched the daylight slowly slant west through the window, until five o'clock. He picked up Wenyao, the boy chattering away about the fun things from class, and he answered with vague mm-hmms.

Back home, the house was filled with the smell of cooking. Lin Wan was just carrying out the last dish from the kitchen, steamed sea bass. She'd changed into loungewear, but her hair was still neat, and on her face was the usual, comforting smile.

"Mama! Look at my starry sky! Teacher said the purple I painted looks really good!" Wenyao swooped toward the dining table like a little bird, his small hands still smudged with unwashed watercolor, holding up his painting in high spirits.

Smiling, Lin Wan brought out egg tarts from the oven, baked to just the right point with edges lightly browned, golden and glistening. "Wow, that's beautiful! Go wash your hands quick, then come taste the Portuguese tarts Mama just made — a reward for our little artist." She turned and gave Shuai Hongqiang a natural little wink. "He's been going on about it all afternoon — said the smell drifting over from the bakery next to the art classroom was just too tempting."

Two dishes and a soup were already laid out on the table. Hot oil had been poured sizzling over the steamed sea bass, garnished prettily with slivers of scallion and red chili. The garlic broccoli was a crisp, vivid green, and the soup was a milky-white fish-head-and-tofu soup, its steam curling up wreathed in fragrance.

"The sea bass turned out well today — taste it, see if it's soaked up more flavor than last time?" Lin Wan said, tying a napkin around Wenyao while nodding her chin toward the fish, a light note of fishing-for-praise in her voice.

Shuai Hongqiang picked up a piece — fresh and juicy, the timing indeed just right. "Mm, delicious." He nodded, then picked up another piece and set it in Wenyao's bowl. "Eat plenty of meat, son, so you grow tall."

Wenyao took a bite of egg tart, flakes of pastry crumbling down, and squinted in satisfaction, then rushed to show off his painting again: "Papa, look, this is the Milky Way! And here — I drew the three of us, our whole family, lying on the grass watching the stars! It's just... it's just that Mama's face came out a little round..."

Lin Wan burst out laughing and pulled a tissue to wipe the crumbs from her son's mouth: "How is Mama that chubby? Tomorrow I'll be your model so you can paint it again."

"Papa's face is square too!" Wenyao "told on" him.

Shuai Hongqiang deliberately put on a stern face and pretended to jab his chopsticks at his son: "Eat! Look at your painting after you've finished."

The lights were warm, the food was good, the boy chattered away in his childish prattle, and his wife wore an easy smile, now and then exchanging a glance with him about their child. The air was filled with the smell of food, a faint whiff of acrylic paint, and the homey, reassuring sound of conversation.

Shuai Hongqiang ate slowly, watching his son's waving chopsticks, feeling that everything in this moment was good. Warm lights, good food, wife and child at his side. Life was showing its plainest and most joyful face.

He knew that in a little while, when the hands of the clock reached seven, this woman who was now picking out food for him and asking whether there was enough soup would walk into that already-prepared study, and under the carefully arranged lighting, would tell countless strangers about a life of another temperature. She would face the livestream camera and, in this same gentle, clear, methodical voice — only with an added professional penetration — dissect, for countless unfamiliar usernames, a specimen called "marriage." Inside that specimen was their life, his shadow, refined and expounded, turned into "content" to be watched, consumed, even used to heal others. Though he knew perfectly well that the words in the livestream, for the sake of traffic and effect, inevitably involved performance and exaggeration. But the skeleton, the texture of it, were after all dismantled out of their real life.

Lin Wan had started doing livestreams four years ago. At first it was just sharing book reflections; somehow the topic slid toward marriage and managing a relationship, toward the dynamics between men and women, and she began to talk about how the two of them lived together — those details, warm or frictional. He'd objected, thinking how could private matters be laid out for outsiders to see, especially when they weren't short of money at the time. But Lin Wan was dead set on it, and so the livestream carried on. To his surprise, it really took off; the fans grew, and in those two or three years when his business was struggling and they were most strapped, it was the income from that livestream room that steadily held up the sinking foundation of this family. He could no longer bring himself to voice his objections — only the feeling stayed, complicated as a wad of water-soaked cotton, lodged heavy in his chest.

By ten to seven in the evening, dinner had been eaten in quiet, the bowls and chopsticks washed and put back in place. Wenyao was doing his homework in his own room. Shuai Hongqiang sat on the living-room sofa, the TV screen dark, his phone in his hand though he hadn't opened a single app.

The study door opened. Lin Wan came out and went straight to the bathroom. A few minutes later she appeared again, already changed. Not loungewear, and not the formal wear for going out, but a soft-textured oatmeal-colored knit that set off the warmth of her complexion, her hair falling smoothly over her shoulders, her face done in light, refined makeup — makeup that cleverly brought out the softness of her features and the clarity of her gaze, making her look both approachable and trustworthy, impeccable.

She gave him a faint smile, no different from the one in the daytime — gentle, gracious. "Qiang-ge, I start at seven, about two and a half hours." Her voice was steady as ever, only with a barely perceptible focus, the concentration of being in work mode.

From the time Lin Wan and Shuai Hongqiang first met, she had at first dutifully called him "Boss Shuai." Later, as the two of them dated and then stepped into marriage, her way of addressing him settled permanently into "Qiang-ge," and never changed. Perhaps it was because Shuai Hongqiang was ten years her senior, carrying the appeal of a mature man and the warmth of an elder brother; or perhaps, in this relationship, such a form of address conveyed her dependence and love just right. And Shuai Hongqiang quite relished the name "Qiang-ge." After all, in this world, there is probably no man who can resist the sweetly called "ge" from the woman he loves — that simple syllable, brimming with deep feeling and admiration, like a warm current that flows straight into a man's heart.

"All right." Shuai Hongqiang heard his own reply, equally steady.

She turned and walked into the study, gently shutting the door. A soft click — the faint sound of the lock turning: during the livestream, absolute quiet was needed, with no disturbances.

Chapter 15 — The "Gentle Awakening" Livestream — Host Lin Wan
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640262.html

The "Gentle Awakening" Livestream — Host Lin Wan
Seven o'clock on the dot.
Amber light leaked through the gaps of the ring fill-lamp and wrapped softly around half of Lin Wan's face. The other half sank into a carefully arranged haze, so that her outline looked both sharp and a little unreally soft. The space beyond the frame wasn't large — a spare bedroom remade to look like a study.
Behind her was a plain off-white wall, two minimalist decorative prints hanging on it. Below the prints stood a walnut-colored bookcase that nearly filled the whole wall, the spines lined up in neat rows. But if you looked closely, the gilded titles were mostly psychology, Rebuilding Intimacy, Emotional Value and the Rational Partner — they were more set dressing than reading, a silent declaration that the conversation here would rest on a certain kind of "reason" and "wisdom."
On the desk, just at the corner the camera could catch, stood a retro lamp with a brass base and a green glass shade, unlit for now. A scented candle burned quietly at the edge of the frame, the white tea and cedar scent so faint it was almost overwhelmed by the cool perfume on Lin Wan herself. The flame swayed slightly, throwing a small dancing patch of warm light onto the glass wall of her water cup. Beside the cup lay an open leather notebook and an elegant fountain pen, but the page was blank, as if waiting to be filled by the story about to be confided tonight.
The most prominent things on the desktop, besides the camera pointed at her, were a tablet standing upright beside it, fan comments scrolling fast across the screen. The colored danmu flowed like a restless, garish river, carrying lines like "let me call in," "Teacher's right," "what should I do." Her gaze swept across it now and then, sharp as a radar hunting for a specific signal — but the moment she fixed her eyes back on the main camera, that sharpness sank away into pure focus.
There was a special "quiet" in the air. Not utter silence, but the cleanness of all background noise filtered out by technology. Behind the microphone's pop filter, every faint breath she took, every brush of lips and teeth as a word left her mouth, was clearly captured, amplified, and tapped straight onto the listeners' eardrums through countless earphones. That absolute "presence" made this small space, circled out by light, feel peeled away from the real world — a floating, transparent consultation room, an emotional vacuum chamber.
On one shelf behind her sat a slender-leafed pachira plant. She adjusted her posture slightly, leaned forward a little, and curved her lips into a ready, precisely measured smile. When she spoke, her voice — modulated through a precise sound card — came out warm, magnetic, and unmistakably firm:
"Good evening, everyone, and welcome to 'Gentle Awakening.' I'm Lin Wan." She opened with a tone of comfortable familiarity and sincerity. "Tonight, let's talk about marriage — the longest and most intimate relationship of our lives — and how, inside that relationship, we can keep from losing ourselves in someone else's script for the rest of our days."
The danmu came alive:
[User 54321]: Wanwan, good evening! I've waited a whole week!
[Stay Clear-Eyed]: Tonight's topic hits home, pulling up my little stool.
[Marriage Is a Grave]: Here for it. Love watching Wanwan tear off the mask of tenderness.
"Now, first let me tell you sisters a little about myself, and my marriage." Lin Wan's tone slowed, like opening a long-sealed box.
"My husband is ten years older than me. I always called him Brother Qiang. We've been married seven years and have an adorable six-year-old son. I loved him very much, and respected him. When I met Brother Qiang, he was rich — a fairly well-known entrepreneur in our area — bold, loyal, unlike any man I'd dealt with before. I admired him, so when he proposed dating, then marriage, I agreed quickly, because I felt that flutter of falling in love. During that time, Brother Qiang respected me and took care of me. He told me the story of how he built his business, showed me his commercial empire, let me look at his company's annual balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement, the balance in his personal bank account. He bought property in my name, bought me a car, gave me large bank certificates of deposit in my name alone — playing the part of the perfect good husband in every way."
The danmu drifted past:
[Lemon's Not Sour]: That opening — textbook domineering CEO falls for me.
[Clear-Eyed in This World]: Jealous, jealous, this is what marrying the right man looks like.
[Wait and See]: Why do I catch a whiff of something off? Usually a perfect opening like this...
"Half a year after the wedding, I got pregnant. The morning sickness was severe — I'd throw up almost anything I ate, to the point where I could only get nutrition through IV drips. Because of it, I gave up my doctoral research. My physical misery made me more and more psychologically dependent on Brother Qiang. At that point he suggested hiring a professional nanny to take care of me, and I refused. I felt that home was a very private space; I didn't like strangers coming in, and on top of that we were newlyweds, so I was even less willing to let a stranger into our private life. So Brother Qiang, the perfect good husband, arranged his work and personally looked after me for a while."
Lin Wan paused, a faint ripple of memory crossing her eyes.
"After a week, just as I'd finished another bout of vomiting, Brother Qiang looked at me strangely and said: 'Lin Wan, this can't go on. I can't leave a whole pile of company business sitting there and take care of you every day. It's better to hire a nanny.'"
The danmu reacted:
[Being a Mom Is Hard]: Just one week???
[Bolt From the Blue]: That line sounds so cold.
[Motherhood Makes You Strong]: Morning sickness can take half your life — shouldn't your husband be by your side?
"Having just been knocked flat by morning sickness, I heard Brother Qiang's words and immediately felt wronged — felt wronged to death." Lin Wan's voice carried a trace of the feeling from that time. "'I'm this miserable because of you. It's because I'm carrying your child that my body is going through this much pain; it's because I'm carrying your child that I gave up my doctorate. I made this huge sacrifice for you, and now that I'm suffering like this, you've taken care of me for only a few days and you're already calling me a burden, not wanting to bother?' I was heartbroken, clutching the trash can, crying as I confronted him."
"Brother Qiang stood not far from me, watching calmly, then said flatly: 'Lin Wan, first — having the child was your own choice. I didn't force you. You have to take responsibility for the choices you make and bear the consequences. Second — it's not that I don't want to look after you personally all the time. For one, I'm not very good at caring for a pregnant woman, and for another, I genuinely don't have the time. There's a whole pile of business at the company waiting on my decisions, hundreds of workers waiting for me to pay their wages, and important clients and friends outside the company who need maintaining. There's no way I can be at your side every moment.'"
The danmu exploded instantly:
[Logic Genius]: ??? This script — graduated from an advanced PUA course, right?
[My Heart Went Cold]: "You chose this yourself" — that line cuts too deep.
[Business Logic]: Cold-blooded, sure, but... it kind of makes sense? (Don't hit me.)
[Wanwan Don't Cry]: Sense my ass! Aren't your wife and child more important than a client?
"'Don't you love me anymore, Brother Qiang? Can't you just put work aside for a while, for me, and stay by my side?' I cried, unable to understand back then."
"Want to guess how Brother Qiang answered?" Lin Wan looked into the camera, as if meeting the eyes of every listener. "He said, 'Lin Wan, I love you, and I love the child in your belly, and I love this home of ours. But — can love be eaten as food? If I don't run the company, don't go out to entertain clients, where do the clients come from, how does the company make a profit? If the company doesn't profit, how do we live in a mansion this big in this rich neighborhood, drive a car this nice, enjoy material comforts this good? I've given you the best material life I can provide — isn't that proof enough of my love?'"
The danmu scrolled fast:
[Material = Love?]: Equating material giving directly with love — this is textbook objectification of feelings.
[Suffocating]: Love is companionship, is tenderness, not a bank-card balance!
[The Realist Faction]: Ugly to hear, but very real. Without money you truly can't take a single step.
[What Happened Next, Wanwan]: And then? Wanwan, were you persuaded?
"Sisters, on first hearing, doesn't this sound fine, almost airtight?" Lin Wan's eyes turned sharp. "But chew on it carefully and the problem shows. The sacrifices I made for the family weren't met with an equal return from Brother Qiang's contribution to it. And Brother Qiang working and making money wasn't for me — at its core it was for himself. Without me, he'd still work just as hard and make money all the same. But once he framed it that way, it sounded as if all his hard work earning money was for me — which is pure emotional blackmail and a swapping out of logic. At the time, though, I didn't realize it. I was even persuaded by this theory of his. Because I wasn't used to a stranger caring for me, in the end Brother Qiang brought my mother over to look after me at home, and he went right back to his old rhythm of life."
"Later, things like this happened often. Like suddenly having something come up and being unable to come to my prenatal checkup; an important client showing up on my birthday so he couldn't spend it with me; not being able to rush back from out of town in time when our son was sick to take me to the hospital... Every time, I felt especially wronged. I'd think, what's the point of earning all that money when he can't be by my side at the moments I need him. And Brother Qiang would use that same line — 'it's all for you, for this family, you have to understand me' — to persuade me over and over. So under his logic, I grew more and more obedient, taking care of him, taking care of the child, putting all my attention on this home."
The danmu sighed:
[Common Ground]: A portrait of so many sisters' married lives...
[Losing Yourself]: Bit by bit you lose yourself.
[Boiling a Frog Slowly]: This is the scariest kind — little by little making you feel it's all just natural.
"Until the year my son turned two, when I suddenly realized this was wrong — very wrong." Lin Wan's pitch rose, carrying the force of an awakening. "I'd follow Brother Qiang to meet his friends, enter his social circle, work to fit into their stratum, get along with his friends and their families, talk about their topics. Brother Qiang's friends were usually business partners or important, worth-cultivating friends or 'big brothers.' Sometimes, listening to the men talk business while the women talked luxury goods and gossip... a question would suddenly rise in me: why am I here? Where are my own friends, my own social circle, those sociology studies of mine? In these few years I'd run this home beautifully — but what about me? Where had I gone? I was lost..."
The danmu resonated strongly:
[Same Question]: I often zone out at dinners too — who am I, where am I?
[Find Yourself Again]: Wanwan has woken up!
[Social Burnout]: For his circle, you lose your own.
"So, after I got home, I reconnected with my old advisor and classmates. We talked about our field, about topics we cared about, and now and then we'd go out and gather. Being with them made me genuinely, deeply happy every time — at ease."
"Then Brother Qiang started giving me 'suggestions.'" A trace of mockery entered Lin Wan's tone. "Saying, 'Wanwan, that girlfriend of yours talks too bluntly, see less of her.' 'Isn't this outfit a little too figure-hugging?' He acted more 'caring' — checking my phone's location from time to time, his reason being 'worried about my safety.' Demanding I be home by ten at night, his reason being 'our son is too young and needs his mom, and staying up late is bad for your health.'"
"One day, I found three missed calls on my phone." Lin Wan slowly turned the water cup in her hand. "They were from my best male friend from university, wanting to meet up before he emigrated. I called back, talked for ten minutes. After I hung up, Brother Qiang asked, 'Whose call?'"
"I answered honestly. He nodded and went on watching the news. But half an hour later, he said he wanted to borrow my phone to look something up. Five minutes later, he handed it back to me — on the screen was that friend's contact page. He said, 'Delete it. No need to stay in touch.'"
Lin Wan lifted her head, a complicated clarity in her eyes.
"I didn't argue. I deleted it. That night I lay awake until three in the morning, turning over one question: why didn't I resist?"
The danmu erupted in an uproar:
[Control Freak!]: This has gone past caring — this is control!
[Why Not Resist?]: Right, why?
[I Understand Wanwan]: Because there's still love, because it's habit, because it's fear.
[Suffocating]: I feel suffocated even through the screen.
"The answer is complicated." She paused. "Because he really was very good to me most of the time — good enough to offset that moment of cold. Because he'd arrange a hospital the instant my parents fell ill, warm milk for me in the dead of night, remember every tiny preference of mine. And also because," she drew a deep breath, "deep down a voice told me: 'He just cares too much about you.' 'He loves you too much.'"
The danmu began to split:
[Classic PUA]: Textbook — slap you, then hand you a sweet date!
[So Contradictory]: But those good things were real too...
[Blackmail by Love]: Control in the name of love is the most terrifying.
"It's a contradiction." Lin Wan nodded gently, as if she could see the danmu. "The truest marriage is a synthesis of contradictions. One person can give you both the greatest sense of security and the greatest sense of suffocation. That year I learned two phrases: sweet control, and the gentle cage."
I began to learn "marital tactics": how to keep a small patch of my own mental ground without alerting the other person. I registered an anonymous account and kept a diary in some unknown corner of the internet. I discovered that when I acted "obedient" and "dependent" enough, Brother Qiang's control would loosen slightly, shifting to material compensation instead — a new bag, a limited-edition lipstick, or a large transfer of money. He'd say: "My wife deserves the best."
I was briefly confused too: was this love, or a performance of love? But my son's pleasant laughter, my parents' contentment at seeing a "perfect marriage," and society's approval of "marrying well" — these sealed over my doubts like layer upon layer of beeswax.
The danmu lamented:
[Tactics Master]: She's already started using tactics...
[Performative Marriage]: How many people live inside a performance.
[Beeswax]: That word is so precise — sealed over, unable to see the truth.
"Until that winter."
Lin Wan's voice sank, and the air in the livestream seemed to congeal too.
"That night, close to eleven, Brother Qiang came home drunk from an engagement. He happily shared with me a big deal he'd just signed. I was reading a sociology paper online that I found very interesting, so I gave him a perfunctory 'congratulations.' Then Brother Qiang suddenly flew into a rage, kicked over the solid-wood dining table in front of me, the laptop I'd been reading on went flying out of my sight — crack, the screen shattered — and our two-year-old son was startled into crying."
The danmu flooded the screen in shock:
[Domestic Violence!]: This is domestic violence!
[Violent Tendencies]: Throwing things is a warning sign!
[The Baby's Scared]: The child! How's the child!
[Scum!]: Leave him! Wanwan, run!
"I froze for a minute, as if suddenly waking up." Lin Wan's tone was unusually calm, a cruel contrast with what she described. "I felt no anger, didn't confront the maybe-drunk, maybe-not, just-furious Brother Qiang, and didn't tend to the shattered computer. I got up, embraced the still-raging Brother Qiang, and in my steadiest voice said: 'Husband, I'm sorry, it's my fault. I'm too tired, I wasn't really listening to you... I love you, you're all that's in my heart, I'm trying to give you a especially warm, especially good home.'"
The danmu was full of bewilderment and fury:
[Why Apologize?!]: He's the one in the wrong!
[My Heart Aches for Wanwan]: How many times did it take to be able to soothe him this "skillfully"?
[Tragic]: So tragic, that you'd have to soothe the abuser.
"Brother Qiang was soothed, calmed, even patted my head. Then he went to the study. I cleared away the wreckage and coaxed our son to sleep. Early the next morning, the doorbell rang — Brother Qiang had someone deliver the latest model of laptop. As if nothing from the night before had ever happened. But I knew some things could never go back to the way they were."
After that day, I understood: in this relationship, I wasn't just Brother Qiang's wife, I was his "emotional-container manager." My job was to receive, digest, and transform all his emotional garbage, keeping the system stable. And his emotions were a privilege, a test, a right to flip the table at any moment.
I began to record — to record like running an experiment: Brother Qiang's anger triggers, the effective scripts that could soothe him, the material compensation after each "rage event," like a set of jewelry, a new bag, a trip. I was often lost too, asking myself, "Does Brother Qiang really love me?" "And me — do I love this control-hungry Brother Qiang who treats me this way?" Sisters, I don't know, I really don't. Love is complicated. "The opposite of love isn't hate — it's a cost-benefit calculation."
The danmu, in its anger, was laced with deep thought:
[Emotional Container]: That phrase is killer — and it's the reality for many wives.
[Cost-Benefit Calculation]: An epiphany! When you're calculating the cost of enduring against the benefit of gaining, love is already no longer pure.
[Recording]: She actually started keeping experimental records — Wanwan, you're ruthless.
"Then one night, he came home drunk, and I heard him on the phone with someone, mentioning his ex-girlfriend, mentioning me. I heard him tell the person on the other end that marrying me back then wasn't because he loved me much, but because I looked suited to being a 'virtuous wife and good mother,' and my higher education, averaged against his own schooling — he hadn't finished middle school — would improve the genes of their old Shuai family."
The danmu was all cursing and heartache:
[Tool, a Human Tool!]: So you were only his tool for improving genes!
[Utterly Disgusting]: Calculated down to the last detail, not a shred of feeling.
[Wanwan Run]: Still not leaving? Waiting until New Year's?
"The next day, I asked Brother Qiang once he'd sobered up, and he denied it. Then, from his close friends, I felt out indirectly some of his emotional history and his real thoughts about our marriage. After learning all of it, I was in great pain — it turned out the man I loved had never loved me from the start."
"I thought it through clearly: I would be an independent woman. With all my skills, leaving a man who didn't love me, a marriage that drained me — the world would surely be vast and wide."
"I decided to start with going cold-turkey off the money Brother Qiang gave me. I stopped using his supplementary card, moved out of the house he'd bought, and rented a small apartment. I brought my mother over to help look after our son for the time being. I had my own job, managed my own stock and fund accounts. Most of my savings I put in my mother's name. I practiced the 'production principle of the matrilineal household': myself, my mother, my son, forming a stable economic and emotional triangle. The man became a replaceable peripheral part."
The danmu began to rally:
[Beautifully Done!]: Financial independence is the first step!
[Matrilineal Household]: This line of thinking is brilliant! Taking notes!
[Queen Wanwan]: This is exactly how it should be!
By then I'd already been trying out livestreaming for a while, but that day I changed the stream's name to "Lin Has Her Own Path," and swapped the background music for some cool post-rock. The content shifted from "managing a marriage" to "women's financial independence," "building psychological boundaries," "how to build an intimacy free of illusions."
Brother Qiang sensed it. I no longer had any need of him — emotionally, financially, or sexually. I became calm, efficient, beyond reproach, and beyond reach. He tried to draw my attention the old ways — nitpicking, cold wars, even brief "returns to tenderness." Sisters, honestly, there were moments I wavered too. But in the end I held firm, and I thought I'd won. With absolute reason, I'd built absolute safety.
The danmu encouraged and supported:
[Never Look Back!]: A gentle knife — every cut takes a life!
[Long Live Reason]: Supporting Wanwan!
[You Won]: Yes, you won!
"Then something happened that made me reexamine those two words, 'marriage,' all over again." Lin Wan's tone turned deep once more, foreshadowing a turn.
"That Spring Festival, when I took my mother and son away on vacation.
My father paid a sudden visit, and along with Brother Qiang — who happened to come home to fetch some things — the two of them were alone together in Jinshi for seven days.
For those seven days, I sat in the resort hotel unable to settle. I feared two possibilities: either the two men would disagree and clash; or, more frightening — they'd agree, form an alliance, and jointly pass judgment on my 'rebellion.'
The result was the latter, but in a way I'd completely failed to anticipate.
Seven days later, I came back to Jinshi. At the airport, my father and my husband stood shoulder to shoulder, the mood harmonious. My father patted Brother Qiang's shoulder and said to me: 'Xiaowan, Qiangzi these past few days took me to walk in the park, to listen to opera, out to restaurants — more thoughtful than you, his own daughter. This son-in-law, he's reliable.'
Brother Qiang naturally took my luggage and opened his arms to our son. The sun was lovely, the scene as warm as an advertisement."
Confusion and unease began to appear in the danmu:
[What's Going On?]: They made up?
[Won Over?]: Did your dad get bought by him?
[Hair-Raising]: This scene... the more you think, the more terrifying.
"In that very instant," Lin Wan's voice was clear and calm, carrying a piercing force, "I suddenly saw it clearly: the 'war of independence' I'd imagined — my opponent had never been Brother Qiang alone, but the entire pervasive system I depended on to survive."
"This system praises the independent woman, but praises even more the 'independent woman who can keep the family harmonious, her parents content, her husband's face intact.' This system lets me earn money, but encourages me even more to 'put the money toward building the family.' This system grants me strength, but hopes even more that I'll use that strength to 'maintain a surface of wholeness and harmony.'
My father's smile, my son's delight, Brother Qiang's 'cooperation' in that moment, the glances of 'what a happy family' from passersby at the airport... all of these together formed a gentle, powerful, hard-to-escape net."
"I realized I could beat Brother Qiang, but I couldn't beat the roles of 'father's son-in-law,' 'son's father,' 'good husband in outsiders' eyes.' That role is needed and upheld by the entire social system. And when Brother Qiang played that role perfectly, all my resistance, in the system's eyes, became 'not understanding,' 'immature,' 'destroying harmony.'"
The danmu fell into fierce thought and debate:
[The System!]: That word is so precise! We're all inside the system!
[Despair]: Then what do we do? Isn't there no solution?
[Reality Is Like This]: This is reality — divorce isn't just a matter of two people.
[What Now, Wanwan]: So... you compromised?
"That night, I didn't lie awake — instead I was unusually clear-headed. I thought it all the way through," Lin Wan's gaze blazed as she looked into the camera, as if she meant to ignite something through the screen.
"In marriage, 'love' versus 'no love,' 'true heart' versus 'false intent,' are beginner-level questions. Tangling over them keeps you forever spinning in an emotional swamp.
The truly advanced question is: how, inside an imperfect, even inherently oppressive system, do you maximize your own survival space, freedom to develop, and inner order?"
"If this marriage has made you suffer, don't first think about 'leaving the marriage' — think instead about 'how to define my marriage.'"
"Only the weak need to flee. The strong simply stand there."
"I found that once I was truly financially independent and mentally strong, the nature of the marriage changed. It went from a 'cage I had to depend on to survive' to a 'project I could manage on my own terms.'
Brother Qiang went from a 'man who had to love me' to a 'project partner I needed to cooperate with.'
This partner has his functions: providing a degree of convenience to the social image, sharing part of the childcare responsibility, playing his role well in front of my parents so I can save myself the trouble. This partner also has his costs: he requires you to spend a certain amount of emotional-management energy, requires you to tolerate certain limits in his thinking.
And you are the CEO of this project. You hold final decision-making power, resource-allocation power, the power to set the terms of cooperation.
I no longer ask whether he loves me; I only assess whether he's competent as a cooperative partner. I no longer suffer over his emotional outbursts; I simply treat them as 'a partner's lack of emotional-management ability' and book it as a cost of cooperation. I no longer crave a pure emotional connection, but build clear boundaries of rights and obligations."
"This way I won real composure: not the finality of fleeing, but the confidence of 'I can leave at any time, but for now I choose to stay, and to rebuild this place according to my own will.'"
The danmu boiled over completely, viewpoints clashing fiercely:
[CEO Mindset]: Awesome! A dimensional-reduction strike!
[Too Cold-Blooded]: This... is this still marriage? How is it different from running a company?
[Pragmatism]: I think it's great. Tangible benefits you can hold are more reliable than illusory love.
[Pitiful]: Running a marriage like project management — is that clarity, or is it pitiful?
[Suits Me]: I get it now! Stable core, relationship defined on my terms!
"So I livestream." Lin Wan's voice regained its strength, a cold and resolute strength. "I don't teach people to 'manage a marriage' — I share 'survival strategies for the marriage system':
How to build a personal economic foundation no one can shake;
How to recognize the power games within feelings, and devise countermeasures;
How to turn emotional problems into negotiable, manageable technical problems;
Most importantly, how to find that dynamic, self-serving balance point among social rules, family expectations, and personal freedom.
Following this 'survival strategy for the marriage system,' from then on, my relationship with Brother Qiang entered a subtle, stable new phase. We were like two top players who knew the rules of the game cold, conducting calm cooperation and contest on the chessboard of marriage. There were tender moments, but it was a tenderness with measure, grounded in rational choice. There were conflicts, but they'd quickly be folded into a 'problem — negotiation — resolution' process.
Toward him, I no longer had disillusioned hatred, nor false love. Only a kind of highly rational scrutiny and cooperation, grounded in deep understanding."
"Toward a man with no blood ties to me, I never let down my guard." Lin Wan said with a very faint, knowing smile at the corner of her mouth.
The danmu carried the last of the reflections and questions:
[Real Life]: That last line is the truth.
[Teacher Wanwan]: I've learned something, though it's a bit cruel.
[Do You Still Love Him?]: So Wanwan, do you still love him now?
"But guarding doesn't mean closing off. When marriage is no longer a shackle, I still have the courage to build a relationship. Only this time, the blueprint is in my hands, I choose the materials, and I decide the timeline."
"I walked the most winding road before I understood one truth: a woman's ultimate strength isn't not needing relationships — it's having the ability to build any relationship she wants. Including one that is clear-eyed, calm, and even, in some people's eyes, 'not romantic enough.'"
"This — this is my vast, wide world. Sisters, having heard my story, my marriage — I nearly lost myself accompanying Brother Qiang through that marriage — what about you? What does your marriage look like? Sisters, feel free to leave comments. When I see them, I'll give the best advice I can.
All right, that's it for today's stream. Next time I'll keep sharing and discussing. Remember to follow me."
The screen went dark. Outside the study came the laughter of Wenyao and Brother Qiang. Lin Wan didn't go out right away. She sat in the quiet livestream room, looking out the window at the thousands of lights of the city.
Under each of those lamps, perhaps, lay a story, a silent war, a choice of either compromise or awakening. And her story was this: she had once fallen into the deepest illusion, lived through the most painful clarity, and in the end, with her own hands, dug a road to freedom out of the rubble of her marriage — a road that lay not in the direction of fleeing, but within the mind that redefined everything.
The study door lock gave a soft "click." Lin Wan walked out. She'd already changed out of the oatmeal-colored knit she'd worn for the stream, into ordinary loungewear, her makeup washed off, revealing a touch of weariness — but her eyes were very still. She saw the father and son playing in the living room, and walked over with a faint smile, sitting down on the sofa beside them. Shuai Hongqiang handed her a cup of warm water: "Tactics master, how'd tonight's battle go?"
Lin Wan took the cup and gave a sly smile: "Total victory. The enemy forces have been completely conquered by our side's wisdom and charm."
Both of them played their roles, clear-eyed and flawless.
Shuai Wenyao bounced excitedly on the sofa beside them. Across his son, Shuai Hongqiang looked toward Lin Wan.
He wanted to find some trace of the old days in her face — that woman whose eyes would light up looking into his, who would tilt her head back slightly while listening to him talk. But there was none. Only a familiar calm. She didn't ask whether he'd listened to the stream, nor did she explain anything about its content. As if it were just an ordinary job of hers that required focus to complete, and now the job was done. This woman he'd shared a bed with for seven years, this woman who had borne him a child, kept the household running, held the family up in his low moments — at this moment she seemed both utterly familiar and utterly strange.
Shuai Wenyao played a while longer. Lin Wan set down her cup, looked at him, her gaze placid and unrippled: "Wenyao has taekwondo practice at ten-thirty tomorrow. Get to bed early."
With that, she led her son toward the bedroom, paused at the doorway, and without turning back, said in a soft but clearly carrying voice:
"By the way, it's on the fifth floor of RT-Mart. You go drop it off. Tomorrow I... have a brand-side meeting."
She used a declarative sentence — no consultation, just notice. Like a CEO assigning the next stage's tasks to a project partner.
Shuai Hongqiang held the cup of warm water, the heat at his fingertips a stark contrast to the emptiness in his heart. He watched her walk into the bedroom and close the door, sealing the room's warm light away inside.
He knew that some things had been thoroughly, calmly laid bare on the negotiating table called "marriage." Now, it was this woman — whom he'd married with money and a "yearning for culture" — who, with terminology he found tongue-twisting and with real, tangible spending, was holding up the daily running of this household and redefining the relationship between them. The veil of tender feeling had been torn off, and beneath it was a naked balance of power and exchange of resources. Calmly, she had measured out the clear line called "cooperation" that ran between them.
He slowly drank the last mouthful of water in the cup. The water was warm; it flowed down his throat, but stirred not the slightest warmth.
Outside the window it was pitch black. He suddenly felt that the life the two of them led was like that river back in his hometown — looking on, it was still the same river, but underneath, the channel had long since shifted, silt and sand churning down toward a place he no longer recognized and could not find the bottom of. And that moon that had once shone on the river's surface, making him feel like quite the big shot — that, too, had sunk, damn it, at some point he couldn't name.

========================================

Chapter 16 — Crossing Paths, Interwoven Fates
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640263.html

Sunday morning, 9:30.
The clamor of the city flooded in like a rising tide, yet it was deftly sealed out by a carefully maintained quiet. Shuai Hongqiang held the steering wheel steady with both hands, his gaze fixed straight ahead on the snarl of traffic, as if it pierced through the press of cars and looked off toward some unknown distance. In the passenger seat, Lin Wan was bent over her phone, firing off replies to work messages, her nails tapping the screen with a steady click-click, like the monotonous beat of a drum keeping time alone in the silent car. In the back seat, their son Shuai Wenyao had his headphones on, completely lost in his own cartoon world. The cheerful images danced in his innocent eyes, and he was utterly oblivious to the faint pressure of conflict hidden beneath the harmony between his parents up front.
Last night's livestream, those heart-baring words of Lin Wan's, seemed sealed away in the past twelve hours. There was no sharp interrogation, no eruption of argument, not even a single glance to hint at the matter. Like well-trained actors, they played the part of a "loving couple" in perfect, unspoken coordination: he handled drop-offs and pickups, she rushed off to work. Only, beneath this seemingly affectionate surface, things were like a riverbed just scoured by ice water—bare and hard—and every step was taken with care, each one keenly aware of the wariness hidden beneath the calm.
The car stopped in front of a glass-curtained office building near the RT-Mart. "Right here, thanks." Lin Wan's tone was flat as she unbuckled her seatbelt, picked up her handbag, her movements smooth. "Wenyao will call me when class lets out. If I'm in a meeting, just take him straight home."
"Got it." Shuai Hongqiang answered without looking at her. Only after she shut the car door and strode crisply toward the revolving door in her low-heeled ankle boots did he glance at her retreating figure. The oatmeal-colored coat was tailored to fit, making her back look straight and poised. She didn't look back.
He stepped on the gas and merged into traffic. That nameless, stifled feeling welled up in his chest again. She really was busy now—negotiating partnerships, meeting with brands, propping up the "everyday" of this household in real, tangible ways, by the very methods he had once dismissed. And he, who had once been the absolute provider, was now more like a... driver, and a business partner who needed to be informed of the schedule.
Meanwhile, elsewhere.
Yao Yuan drifted awake from a deep, dreamless sleep. Sunlight slipped through the thin gauze curtains, scattering softly over the bed like golden threads, spreading a warm glow across half of it.
