That afternoon yesterday at her mother's house, the two of them talking honestly, had felt like lifting a great stone that had pressed on her heart for so long; even her breathing had turned light and easy. First she ran a comfortable hot bath, letting the warm water soothe every inch of her skin and chase away the lingering fatigue. Then came a simple but cozy breakfast, followed by a calming yoga session along with a video—as if every crease pent up in her body and mind had been ironed smooth, one by one.
Only in this thoroughly relaxed state did she pick up her phone and begin methodically working through the messages that had piled up. Replies from business partners needed careful weighing, invitations from her girlfriends made her smile, and her colleagues' requests awaited her sign-off... As she scrolled through them one by one, that text from Shuai Hongqiang leapt into view.
The message had been sent at noon the day before, brief but oddly worded: "Yao Yuan, that mirror I delivered yesterday—if you feel anything's off about it, or... if you have any strange dreams, tell me anytime."
Her fingertip hovered above the screen for a few seconds. The chaos and terror she had lived through on the day she traveled through time seeped out again from deep in her memory, thread by thread, bringing a wave of cold vertigo. That plain old bronze mirror... could it really be more than a coincidence? But the night she traveled through time, the mirror had been in a drawer in her office, clearly not with her. So then—was the thing that triggered all these strange events not the physical mirror itself, but some invisible, intangible... mysterious connection?
She shook her head hard, trying to drive these unsettling associations out of her mind. Outside the window, the sun was perfect, the road thick with traffic, a scene of bustling prosperity. On the tablet at her side was the latest data-analysis report on the emotional AI model that Yu Hao had just sent over; the complex charts and code gave off a cold, rational light, as if to remind her that she lived in a world full of science and logic. She was someone exploring the frontier of that solid scientific field—how could she let herself be so easily seized by these groundless, vaporous metaphysical guesses? The idea was simply too absurd.
Right—it had to be a hallucination, surely just too much stress lately causing a brief cognitive scramble. She thought it with conviction; her current vigorous, well-rested state gave her plenty of backbone to trust her own judgment. As for Shuai Hongqiang... this text was probably just a pretext to show concern, when really it was a roundabout reminder, nudging her not to forget to look at the project proposal he'd given her. She didn't reply, and set the phone aside, like brushing away a speck of dust.
At noon she kept a lunch date with a close girlfriend from the "Sisters' League," heading to a newly opened Yunnan restaurant on the sixth floor of the RT-Mart. She drove herself, savoring the freedom of being alone.
After reaching the mall, she parked and took the elevator straight to the sixth floor. The dining floor buzzed with voices, the aromas of food all mingling together. On her way toward the appointed restaurant, her gaze happened to skim past the floor-to-ceiling window of a family restaurant next door, one built around kids' meals.
Her steps faltered, almost imperceptibly.
Inside, at a spot near the aisle, sat Shuai Hongqiang and his family of three. Lin Wan was leaning slightly forward, head bowed as she fastened a bib on their son Shuai Wenyao, her profile gentle and serene. Shuai Hongqiang sat across from them, carefully nudging a cup of juice toward the child, a look of... a doting father's tenderness on his face—an expression Yao Yuan had never seen on him before. It looked a little clumsy, yet was tender beyond measure and utterly focused, as if the whole world had narrowed to this lively, adorable child in front of him. The little guy held up a dinosaur toy, waving it around in excitement, gesturing animatedly as he said something to his dad, his innocent smile as brilliant as sunshine.
It was the most ordinary of scenes, yet an utterly harmonious one—a family of three eating out on the weekend.
Shuai Hongqiang didn't see her, and Yao Yuan made no move to greet him. Her steps didn't stop; she walked calmly past. Only, deep down, that picture had stirred her, ever so slightly. The man who in business was by turns forceful, slick, or mercenary—so this was how tender he became as a father. And that child... was indeed adorable.
Without thinking, she touched her own lower belly. There, too, a child had once been—if only... A very faint, indescribable bitterness spread out, only to be quickly pressed back down into her heart. What was past had to be set down completely, buried away. As for other people's lives, they had nothing to do with her.
Over lunch, Yao Yuan and her friend chatted happily; the amusing little moments of daily life and their mutual gossip let her set aside, for a while, all her earlier tangled thoughts. A little over an hour later, they said goodbye at the elevator landing by the mall's parking garage and went their separate ways to fetch their cars.
Yao Yuan's red Bentley sat quietly in a corner off to the side, like an elegant scarlet beast. She got in, started the engine, and the car eased out of its spot, its bright headlights slicing instantly through the garage's dim light. Just as she turned toward the exit lane, an old black BMW X6 was rolling slowly across the intersection ahead, the driver's window half down.
It was Shuai Hongqiang's car. Lin Wan sat in the passenger seat, and in the back the faint outline of a child's safety seat could be made out.
Yao Yuan's eyes weren't fixed on that car in particular; she was focused on watching for traffic behind her, waiting for the right moment to accelerate. Her Bentley, like a nimble red sprite, glided smoothly and steadily—almost soundlessly—past the front of Shuai Hongqiang's car and straight on toward the exit, without pausing for an instant. Naturally, she didn't notice the pair of eyes in the driver's seat that had suddenly turned to look her way.
But Shuai Hongqiang saw her clearly.
That streak of elegant red, carrying an air utterly unlike the sedan he was now driving—remote yet swift, like a phantom from another world—flashed past before his eyes. He even saw clearly Yao Yuan's profile in the driver's seat: calm and refined, her gaze fixed firmly ahead, as if the car beside her carrying a family of three simply didn't exist.
The fingers gripping the steering wheel clenched suddenly, of their own accord, the knuckles whitening faintly with the force.
Lin Wan, in the passenger seat, seemed to sense his brief, subtle stiffening. She turned her head, following his gaze toward the exit, catching only a red taillight just about to vanish from sight. She glanced at Shuai Hongqiang's expressionless profile; a flicker of barely perceptible puzzlement crossed her eyes, but in the end she asked nothing, only pressing her lips together lightly and lowering her head back to her phone.
In the garage, the low growl of the Bentley's engine quickly faded into the distance, leaving only a faint smell of exhaust. Shuai Hongqiang slowly loosened his grip on the wheel, drew a deep breath, stepped on the gas, and followed the flow of traffic toward the exit, easing out into the slightly languid sunlight of a weekend afternoon. That glance just now was like a small stone dropped into an already restless lake, sending out a ring of faint ripples only he could perceive. In the rearview mirror, his son's smile was still bright, his wife still absorbed in her phone—everything exactly as it had been a few minutes ago.
And yet, somehow, something felt different. That red Bentley brushing past was like a brief confirmation of another track of life, reminding him of certain things he was losing, and certain others he had never truly possessed. The quiet inside the car felt heavier now than before.

========================================

Chapter 17 — A Must-Read for Women: A Guide to Emotional "Mergers & Acquisitions"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640264.html

Weekend rest is always short. When Monday's dawn cracks the night sky, countless "beasts of burden" are swept up against their will by the surging crowd, carried along, and thrown back into the grind of every trade and profession—like cogs in a vast, precise social machine, turning round and round, a millstone that never stops.

In the gaps between the busyness, some people might pause for a moment, a flicker of confusion rising in their hearts: why work so hard? What, exactly, is all this toil for? Is it only so you can scrape together a little money and, in some brief vacation, rush off to a scenic spot that capital carefully built for you, leave snowy-mountain and beach photos on your social feed, splurge freely, and chase a fleeting taste of pleasure? Trading long stretches of labor for a flash of relaxation and joy, then crawling back to your post to grind again—just to save up enough capital for the next splurge?

For others, work means things like the satisfaction of personal achievement, rising above mediocrity, the pursuit of excellence—or earning social recognition and realizing social value, though the measuring stick for that value is held by an invisible hand. Aristotle held that man is a political animal, born to take part in social life; so people, willing or not, labor away in the city's cubicles or out in the fields, never stopping.

More people are simply breathing on repeat, drifting through life. They have no time at all to ponder the meaning of work, because from the moment they're born, their parents say, "Study hard and find a good job," and their teachers say, "Be a useful member of society"—all passing down the same idea: when you grow up you should work hard, and only by working hard can you live a better life. But what does this so-called "better life" actually look like? The parents never spelled it out, and school never cleared it up. The "better life" is like the carrot dangled in front of the donkey: always one step ahead, always fresh and tempting, never defined. We learned how to run, but no one taught us to question the direction we were running in.

Standing on the thirty-eighth floor, she watched the crowds below, dense and teeming, rushing along the gullies of the streets, pouring into subway gates, vanishing into the glass grid. In the distance, the Yellow River rolled on as it had for thousands of years, surging without pause, gleaming dull gold in the sunlight—tireless, indifferent to meaning, simply flowing.

Yao Yuan couldn't help reflecting on what, after all these years of hard struggle, she had really been chasing.

There was no doubt that for her, the hunger for more and better—for the most lavish material life—had been planted deep in her heart since childhood. After all, growing up in an art world saturated with fame and vanity, the pursuit of material things had become a kind of instinct. She had outrun the childhood version of herself who once stood at the edge of the art world's fame-and-fortune circus, peering in. Mansions, luxury cars, the freedom of top-tier luxury goods—she had chased down by her own effort the wealth she'd craved since she was small, and that achievement made her proud.

But where would her road go from here? Apart from more money, what else did she want? What should she want? She had no answer.

Her livestream business looked to be at its peak now, blazing hot. But the company's blueprints, the team's expectations, the tide of the industry—all of them, like invisible yet powerful forces, were shoving her, no argument allowed, toward the "next milestone": building her own AI emotional model. This journey that looked so much like initiative, like forward drive—wasn't it just another, more hidden, harder-to-escape form of being swept along?

Heidegger said, "Man is thrown into the world." Yao Yuan felt that she had been thrown, from childhood, into the hunger and scramble for fame and fortune. After a long, hard run, she seemed to have reached the summit. But standing at this seemingly glorious height, she couldn't help asking herself: what, in the end, lies at the finish line of this chase?

"Yuan-jie, the studio's all set up. About twenty more minutes till eleven—we go live right on time, okay?" The crisp reminder from her young assistant cut through Yao Yuan's drifting thoughts.

Right. Living in the present is the truest reality there is. The matters waiting to be handled were piled up like a mountain; she couldn't afford any more time to brood over an unknown future. They say pessimists are right and optimists move forward—she gathered her wits and gently pressed those scattered thoughts back down into her heart. The lights were set, the camera ready. A livestream was about to begin.

Monday, eleven in the morning. The Beauty's Worry-Cure Shop livestream opened right on time.

Behind the host's chair, the backdrop was still that vast, silent Yellow River. It flowed on, unchanging since the dawn of time, its water murky and heavy, weighed down by countless grains of sand in shapes too blurred to make out. The corner of wall the camera could catch still held that same gold-framed "Energy Pyramid" diagram.

"This view—I picked it on purpose."

Yao Yuan slowly extended her hand, showing the fans behind the camera the window and the river behind her. That hand seemed to carry its own spotlight, instantly seizing everyone's attention. Her fingers were long and slender, each fingertip like a finely carved work of art—the dramatic long nails like ornate miniature sculptures. The nails extended elegantly past her fingertips, far longer than most people would think possible, lending her fingers a distinct edge and allure.

That vivid red polish was like a blazing flame, flamboyant and fierce. Pure red, dazzling red, like the deep luster given off by congealed blood, carrying an inborn seduction and mystery. When the light spilled over it, the surface of the polish threw back a brilliant glow, like a flowing lake of red gemstones—each tilt refracting a bewitching halo, calling to mind burning desire and endless temptation.

This hand looked utterly different from anyone else's, as if announcing to the world her singular character and untamed soul. The dramatic long nails paired with the bright red polish had become her own unmistakable mark, impossible to forget once seen.

"My darling fans, do you know why I chose this backdrop?

Let me tell you. Every livestream, I sit right here, my back to it. I let my face show every detail under the ring light—every inch of my expression broadcasting 'control'—while behind me lies the most uncontrollable force of nature, thousands of kilometers long, mud and sand pouring along together. It's telling you this: all your tangled feelings, all those messes you can't untie—in my eyes, they're just like that river outside the window. Vast and chaotic on the surface, but there's a riverbed underneath. And my job is to dig out that riverbed for you, and scoop up the little bit of real gold inside."

When the afternoon light burned strongest, the river reflected a heavy, metallic glow, like molten copper congealed and splashed across the window. That light fell on Yao Yuan's profile, glazing her in a cold, hard, otherworldly enamel. One darling fan said, Teacher Yuanyuan, your backdrop has such presence.

She smiled. "Presence? No. That's the price tag. It's a reminder to everyone who comes in: every gift was long ago marked with a murky price in the ledger of fate—just like this river, which can carry boats and also swallow silt."

"Sometimes, between call-ins, I turn my head to glance at it. I watch that muddy yellow water flow on, never looking back; I watch the scenery on the far bank blur and warp in the rising mist. That's good. Chaos means possibility; blur means it can be defined—by me. What I want is exactly this: to sit before this ultimate, primal chaos, and with every word that leaves my mouth, build for you a new order—cold, but clear.

At dusk, the river darkens to a deeper shade, an ochre-brown close to clotted blood. That's when my studio lights look brighter, warmer, more like a refuge. Countless girls dial in, projecting their emotional troubles onto this ancient river that stays utterly unmoved by all their love and hate. They ask, 'Does he love me,' while outside the window the Yellow River silently demonstrates what 'existence' itself means—nothing to do with love or hate, just moving forward, carrying off what it will carry off, leaving behind what it will leave behind."

"See it, my darlings? Sometimes I tap the screen lightly with my pen, the way you'd knock on the glass that separates me from the Yellow River. Feelings are like the water in this river. You see it surging mighty and grand, but ninety percent of it is useless silt. My studio doesn't teach you to admire the scenery—it teaches you how to sift the gold out of the silt. Don't talk to me about the volume of feeling. Let's talk about how many grams of gold you've actually got in your hand."

"Remember: your eyes can't tolerate a grain of sand, and only then can your hands hold on to gold."

"Now—call-ins begin."

The caller (voice soft and thin, on the edge of tears): "Yuanyuan-jie… I'm twenty-eight this year, and I've been with a married man for three years. He's spent maybe eight hundred thousand and more on me, but lately he's getting colder and colder, and I…"

Yao Yuan (suddenly setting down her lipstick, snapping the cap shut with a click, cutting in): "Stop. This question of yours is worth a thousand yuan—the call-in fee—so I'll say it only once. Listen up."

(Leaning forward, pupils gleaming jet-black behind her colored contacts.)

"First: you're not in a relationship. You're running a business. The buyer in this deal is him, the seller is you, the goods are your youth and emotional value, and the contract value is eight hundred thousand."

(The scrolling comments exploded: [???] [Damn, no warm-up] [Crude words, sound logic])

The caller (stunned): "But we have feelings for each other…"

Yao Yuan (a cold laugh): "Feelings are the wrapping paper. Tear it open and look—why did he pick you? Because you're young and good-looking, presentable in public, and you can play the understanding little sister when he gripes about his wife. Why did you pick him? Because his money and connections let you skip ten years of struggle in Beijing. (Tapping the iPad screen.) If you ask me, you ran this deal at a loss."

The caller (crying in a panic): "But I can't leave him now, I…"

Yao Yuan (suddenly raising her volume, words firing like bullets): "Then turn the losing deal into financing! Listen carefully: next Monday morning, go 'run into' him at the gym he always uses. Wear the Max Mara coat he bought you, but underneath it put on that white shirt your programmer ex gave you. When he asks, you answer, 'I've been meeting new people lately. I want to live a normal life.'"

(Scrolling comments: [???] [Textbook-level move] [That's ruthless])

The caller (trembling): "Won't he think I'm forcing his hand…"

Yao Yuan (corner of her mouth lifting): "Forcing his hand? No—this is a risk alert. Number one on a married man's fear ranking: the girl suddenly wising up. He'll instantly start calculating—the money it'd take to buy you an apartment, the risk of you making a scene at his company, the shame of 'being compared' after you marry someone else. (Pause, sips water.) That's when you softly add, 'My mom's been sick lately. I'm so tired.'"

The assistant's voice off-camera (low): "Yuan-jie, the studio's getting a warning…"

Yao Yuan (a glance past the camera, talking even faster): "Last step: after he sends you money to smooth things over, take that money and immediately enroll in an MBA—and find your next one among the classmates. A woman's ladder has to be built one rung at a time, by herself, but every rung has to be paid for by someone."

[30 seconds left on the call]

The caller (breathing fast): "But isn't this a bit too…"

Yao Yuan (dead certain): "Too what? Remember this: kindness is a memorial arch, not a weapon. Your tears at twenty-six are pearls; your tears after thirty are rainwater—worth nothing except ruining your makeup. (Checking her watch.) Time's up. Next."

[Call cut off]

Yao Yuan leaned back into her chair and arched a brow at the main camera with a smile: "Remember—"

(Suddenly leaning close to the camera, red lips almost touching the screen.)

"In any relationship, whoever's in pain is the one who changes; whoever changes is the one who benefits. You are your own CEO, not anyone's charity project."

"The rest of you sisters, listen too: your mercy is cruelty to yourself. Your hesitation is the gap others use to get a grip on you. When interests are on the table—feelings, please step aside!"

========================================

Chapter 18 — Yao Yuan Coaches a Cheated-On Housewife to Reclaim Her Bargaining Power in Three Steps
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640265.html

Yao Yuan Coaches a Cheated-On Housewife to Reclaim Her Bargaining Power in Three Steps
The livestream rolled on. A brass-colored light slanted in through the window, casting one half of Yao Yuan's face in the glow of a deity while the other half stayed hidden in the cold white of the ring light. She glanced at the live viewer count, and the corner of her mouth twitched almost imperceptibly — the curve of a hunter watching prey step into the trap.
Yao Yuan (to the camera, her voice smooth as silk): "Feelings are the most extravagant garbage in the world. Your ability to deal with garbage decides how pure your life is. Next."
[Call connected]
Caller Two (a middle-aged woman, her voice dry, her words slow): "Teacher Yuanyuan... I'm forty-five, married twenty years. I found out he's been cheating, with a woman who works under him, for three years now. I... I don't have a job, my kid just started university. I checked — he's quietly transferred two properties out of reach, and I don't know about the cash. I can't sleep at all anymore. I look at the man beside me and he feels like a stranger. It makes me want to throw up."
(The comments scrolled: [Poor sister][The tragedy of the housewife][How do you even survive a divorce])
Yao Yuan (she didn't answer right away; instead she leaned slowly back into her chair, those long red nails tapping lightly on the desk — tap, tap, tap, in a steady rhythm. Outside the window, the muddy river churned over an unseen whirlpool.)
"First," she said at last, her voice holding no sympathy, only a calm that bordered on cruelty, "stop the 'want to throw up' reflex. It's useless. Your stomach won't win the houses back for you."
The woman (choking up): "I just... I can't take being deceived. Twenty years..."
Yao Yuan (cutting in, leaning forward, her pupils locking onto the camera): "Your 'can't take it' — how much is it worth? One property? Two? Let me tell you: emotion is the cheapest currency in the world, especially at the negotiating table. What you need to do right now isn't to process your feelings. It's to freeze them — turn them into fuel for later."
(She picked up a gold pen and tapped it once on the blank iPad, as if tapping it straight into the heart of every viewer.)
"I'll give you three steps. Listen carefully. I'll only say this once."
"Step one: 'illness.' Starting tomorrow, go to the best hospital. Register for neurology and traditional Chinese medicine. Get a full workup, and walk out with a stack of diagnoses — 'severe anxiety, insomnia, depressive tendencies.' Keep the receipts. This isn't faking it. This is converting your psychological trauma into a 'fact of harm' that he can understand and that the law may recognize."
"Step two: 'study.' Sign up for the most expensive three-month course in town — 'high-end home-organization specialist' or 'private nutritionist.' Pay with the supplementary card on his account. On your social feed, post only study notes and sunny profile shots, captioned: 'Life reset to zero. Starting fresh.' Remember, the posture has to look good. The goal is to leave behind proof of 'active self-rescue' while you keep spending his money."
"Step three, and this is the crucial one—" Yao Yuan paused. The light reflecting off the river outside happened to graze her eyes, startlingly bright. "'Talk.' But not you doing the talking. Find the uncle or brother on your side of the family who carries the most weight, who shows the least mercy, but who truly has your back. Have them 'pay a visit' to your husband, on the pretext of 'we heard our sister's unwell and came to check in.' Let them do the talking — about the family's face, about the children's future, about 'this family is not one to be pushed around.' Your job is to lie in the bedroom looking 'frail,' shedding tears, but not saying a single word."
The woman (seeming stunned by the clarity of the steps, hesitating): "This... won't it look too ugly? Burning the bridge..."
Yao Yuan (with a short, cold laugh): "Look good? The lining of the marriage rotted away long ago, and you still want to paste a pretty face over it? Right now he's a gambler who cheats, and you're a player with one last chip in hand. Your chips are your twenty years of giving, the bond of the children, and that little leftover fear he has of 'trouble.' What you have to do is drag him back to the card table and tell him: either we redivide the chips, or we flip the whole table together — and nobody walks away with their dignity intact."
(The comments had gone wild: [Holy crap this is the hardcore practical school][Terrifyingly calm][Sister woke up sharp!])
The woman (breathing harder, as if drawing courage): "And... after that? What if he still..."
Yao Yuan (flat and final): "There's no 'what if.' Once you finish these three steps — especially the third — he'll come to you to 'negotiate.' That's when you take out the records you found on those two properties and ask just one question: 'Is your future with her worth this price?' Remember: don't bring up feelings, don't bring up betrayal. Just talk about this. It's business. It's an asset restructuring."
[15 seconds left on the call]
Yao Yuan (speeding up, every word a driven nail): "One last line for you, and for every sister struggling in the swamp of a marriage in this room: 'Mercy needs teeth. Kindness needs an edge. You offered yourself up as a sacrifice for twenty years. Now it's time to taste what it's like to be the hunter.' Your battlefield isn't in the bedroom. It's in the property deeds and the bank statements. Now — go."
(The call cut off. A brief silence fell over the room, with only the low, ceaseless flow of the Yellow River as background sound.)
Yao Yuan (leaning slowly back into her chair, her gaze sweeping over the frantically scrolling gift effects and comments, a very faint, almost weary smile surfacing — but it vanished in an instant, replaced once more by a professional sharpness). She reached out and gently adjusted the angle of the ring light beside her, focusing the beam more tightly on her face. Under the strong light those bright red nails looked like congealed blood, and also like a flag of victory.
"See," she said to the camera, her voice suddenly turning soft, yet carrying an even deeper power to sink in, "the Yellow River out there never asks the silt whether it wants to be carried off. It simply flows, and then deposits new land. A woman's rebirth often begins with 'settling the account' — owning every loss, and then, entry by entry, tallying it all up clean."
"Next." She sipped from her thermos, her eyes refocused, cold and intent, waiting for the next soul swept up in the silt of feeling to sink willingly into these waters of hers, so full of temptation and danger.
And outside the window, the Yellow River ran muddy as ever, surging silently eastward, mirroring the countless small and endless wars of the human world — over love and desire, over giving up and getting back.
...
The livestream ran until three in the afternoon.
Yao Yuan rubbed her stiff neck and shoulders, gave her assistant a few quick instructions about what came next, then shuffled back to her office, kneading her aching lower back. The door clicked shut behind her. She unloaded herself into the sofa, too tired to keep up even a shred of composure. In the darkness behind her closed eyes, what flickered was the light and shadow of the livestream, the comments, and all the subtext she'd left unspoken. A single thought rose up, clear and cold as iron: when, exactly, would women finally learn to think like the winners of history? In the face of absolute stakes, to strip away that nagging softness and fantasy, and lock their eyes only on the bullseye of the target.
She thought of the similar shadows in history.
At Changban Slope, to flee fast and light, Liu Bei abandoned his wife and children and galloped off with only a few dozen riders. In the rout at Pengcheng, to shed weight and gain speed, Liu Bang kicked his own children off the carriage three times. Three times Xiahou Ying scooped them back up — and more than ten times the urge to kill rose in Liu Bang's heart.
You see, in a man's dream of conquering rivers and mountains, love and attachment are an ornament in good times and a burden in bad. Later generations are happy to drape them in the cloak of "seeing the bigger picture," but the so-called bigger picture is just another name for their own ambitions. The common people are the foundation, the people's hearts are the capital, and wife and children — once the great enterprise is nearly won, why worry about finding new ones?
They are permitted to be utterly self-serving, and crowned with the title of "heroic sovereign."
And women? Always wrapped in the sacred robes of "maternal love" and "the virtuous wife," expected to become the silent cornerstone, the sacrificial offering. History writes wisdom of trade-offs for men, and for women annotates only the virtue of devotion.
Yao Yuan buried her face in a throw pillow and laughed without a sound. The smile was thin and cool, like a sliver of sharp porcelain.
So what has never changed across the ages was never the human heart, but the ruler by which the human heart is measured.
This may be cruel, but it is the rule written by countless stories.
True clear-sightedness begins with disenchantment. Peel away the dreamy powder the world dusts over relationships, and the essence beneath is mostly the same: value exchange. If you are in full bloom, the breeze and the butterflies come on their own; if you are dim, even tenderness scatters easily into drifting clouds.
History bears witness: which of the women who climbed to the clouds of power didn't understand this deeply? Wu Zetian, Mi Yue, Xiaozhuang — wasn't the creed of their lives, all of them, "everything to be used by me, everything to empower me"? They saw through it early: useless lofty pride buys not an inch of progress; ambiguity can be a passage, connections can be a staircase. The key is this — to be within feeling yet unmoved by it, to own nothing yet to use everything.
This isn't cynicism. It's a profound realism. It doesn't teach you coldness; it teaches you discernment. It doesn't make you a plunderer; it makes you a magnet. When you yourself become an irreplaceable source of value, all that "networking upward" finally transforms into truly "walking side by side."
In her bright office, Yao Yuan let out a long breath. Light filtered through the glass and fell on her clasped fingertips, warm and quiet. She suddenly felt like someone lighting a single lamp in a deep river.
What it could illuminate, in the end, was only the few willing to open their eyes.
She could dissect the rules, could puncture the illusions, could spread that script — the one that has run silently for thousands of years — open under the light. But in the end, whether to pick it up, whether to dare to live by it: that was each viewer's own war. However clear-sighted she was, she could not wake a soul that chose to sleep; however sharp she was, she could not cut through the cocoon it had spent thousands of years winding around itself.
But the lamp stays lit, and someone will follow the light. At that thought, the thread of helplessness deep in her heart was suddenly replaced by a calm clarity.
One person cannot ferry everyone across. But a road of awakening begins the moment the first person opens their eyes.
At least, someone has woken up.

========================================

Chapter 19 — Traveling Through Time Again, Back to Twenty-Six and the Height of Her Romance with Shuai Hongqiang
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640266.html

Yao Yuan got up and sat back down at her desk, her gaze landing on the file at the top of the pile — Shuai Hongqiang's project proposal. Until she had a clear picture of his finances and credit record over the past few years, she would give him no business answer at all. But her thoughts drifted, floating back to that "dream" that had felt at once real and unreal. Could it be... that it really had something to do with that mirror?
She pulled open the drawer and took out the bronze mirror once more.
Yao Yuan examined it closely, turning it over in her hands. Apart from its age, she could find nothing unusual. The body of the mirror was seven or eight centimeters wide, its edges not sharp but warm and smooth as old jade, glazed with a dark blue-brown patina of copper rust. At the center of the back was only a small bridge-shaped knob, ringed by coiling cloud-and-thunder patterns, with a Kui-dragon carved in low relief lying quietly among them.
In the sunlight spilling through the floor-to-ceiling glass, it took on a kind of muted refinement. She studied it again and again, and the pad of her finger, without thinking, brushed across the faintly concave surface of the mirror —
And in that instant, the light in the room seemed to give a soundless twist.
A faint, thin dragon's cry, like something rising from the bottom of a very deep well of years, swept past her ear. Yao Yuan's breath caught, and she flipped the bronze mirror over in a flash. The Kui-dragon on the back came suddenly alive in the shadow, and those carved eyes seemed to glint with a ghostly light —
Before she could even feel afraid, a vast, irresistible pull — eerily familiar — seized her soul and wrenched it hard.
"Ngh..."
A stifled groan caught in her throat. Everything in her field of vision — the desk, the screen, the daylight outside the window — was instantly stretched into warped streams of color, then in the next second shattered into glittering fragments, sucked into a silently howling vortex. Her body lost all weight; her consciousness kept plunging and tumbling through the chaos...
She had no idea how long it lasted.
Stillness.
A stillness absolute and complete.
Beneath her was a hard flat surface, smooth, icy, without a trace of warmth.
Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open.
White. The ceiling was white, the ground beneath her was white, and on every side, endlessly, was the same even, soft white — yet a white so cold it held no life at all. There was no source of light, yet light was everywhere. She slowly sat up, the feathers on her black knit dress trembling faintly — still the outfit she'd worn for the livestream.
This was not a dream.
This was not a dream. If it were a dream, then she had been here once before, in her last dream.
"Hello? Hello? Is anyone there?" Still, she tried calling out a few times. The sound came from her, but there was no echo and no answer, as though it had been swallowed whole by this pure white.
She stood and looked around. As she had last time, she ventured forward, testing. After a few steps, the calm white "air" ahead of her suddenly rippled outward —
A complete, vivid scene rose soundlessly out of the void.
It was a street of antique shops. The afternoon sun, sliding toward the west, lay warm over the gray flagstones, and the air carried that particular faint warmth and languor of the turn from spring to summer. A man stood with his back to the "camera," before an antique shop hung with an old plaque, his head turned slightly as he talked with the owner behind the counter. His bearing was tall and straight. He wore a pure cotton green shirt, crisp with a touch of American ruggedness, the sleeves casually rolled up to his forearms, baring a length of firm wrist. Around that wrist, loosely wound, was a pale yellow bracelet.
The strand he'd carelessly wound around his wrist was a star-and-moon bodhi mala of superb quality. The beads were not the harsh white of fresh seeds, but had been worked to a warm, creamy honey base — even and mellow in tone, with a soft matte sheen. On each bead, the "moon" eye was clear and well placed, the "star" points fine as scattered ink, sparse and well spaced — exactly the "bright moon, sparse stars" quality that collectors prize. The surface had already formed a thin, glossy, glass-like patina that flowed with a restrained luminescence in the warm afternoon sun, looking at once elegant and full of life.
What drew the eye most were the accent beads worked in among them. A round bead of high-porcelain, high-blue turquoise served as the head bead, its color a deep blue like a frozen lakeheart, its texture pure and dense; against the honey-colored star-and-moon beads it looked all the more cool and noble, like a fleck of icy soul dropped into a warm current. On either side were spacer slices of persimmon-red nanhong agate to set it off — a red full and lustrous, vivid without being garish, like ripe cherries or refined cinnabar, brimming with a warm, auspicious quality. The "cold blue" of the turquoise and the "warm red" of the nanhong agate, against the mellow base of the star-and-moon bodhi, struck a clever clash and balance. They held each other in check and yet completed each other — the cold of the turquoise quietly tempered by the warmth of the nanhong, the ardor of the nanhong gently pressed down by the calm of the turquoise — together stringing up a visual rhythm like a "song of ice and fire," striking and arresting yet never losing its overall harmony and high taste. It showed that its owner had spent not only on the materials but also a great deal of thought on the aesthetics of the pairing.
The man seemed quite pleased with the bodhi beads, his fingertips absently rolling one bead, though his gaze had already turned to another pair of objects on the counter. "These walnuts... the grain's solid enough, and the matching's passable, but I have my doubts about your 'Mentougou muffled-tip lionhead' story." His voice was low, carrying the unhurried fussiness only an insider has. "The base navel isn't quite straight, and the tips are muffled in a way that feels a bit deliberate. Old-tree fruit? Looks more to me like a later, man-doctored 'official-cap' lineage. The skin's a touch stiff — it doesn't have that natural, aged richness."
The shop owner was a lean, middle-aged man. He took no offense at this, just gave a "heh heh" laugh, baring teeth stained yellow by smoke. "You've got a sharp eye. But these days, where are you going to find old-tree muffled-tips that are so 'flawless'? This pair — the body shape, the grain, the weight — they're all rare for this price range. If you're a serious player, I'll give you an honest price..."
The man neither agreed nor refused. He gave the pair of deep-red, oily-bright walnuts a light tap with his fingertips, drawing out a crisp "click," as though still weighing it.
Just then, the brass bell hung on the antique shop's carved door — patterned with interlocking lotus vines — gave a light "ding-a-ling."
A young woman walked out from inside the shop, and the warm golden afternoon sun wrapped around her in an instant, as if plating her in a flowing honeyed light. The moment she appeared, even the somewhat worn street scene around her seemed to brighten a few shades. She wore a true-red silk diagonal-collared off-shoulder top — that red, an utterly pure, utterly intense cinnabar red, set off the bare skin of her shoulders, neck, and arms, white as frost and outshining snow. The top was cut to fit perfectly, tracing the slender, supple line of her waist and her full curves; the collar and front were embroidered in matching silk thread with intricate hidden patterns of interlocking vines that shimmered faintly in the light, classical yet possessed of an aggressively striking beauty.
Below she wore black high-waisted, slightly flared suit trousers with a superb drape, flowing smoothly from her waist down to the tops of her shoes, lengthening her figure to perfection; as she walked, her steps stirred the air with an unwitting, swaying allure. On her feet was a pair of simple black thin-strap heels, her bare ankles slender and fair. Her long hair was not carelessly tied up but coiled into a low bun, slightly loose yet brimming with charm, a few lazy curls falling at her neck. At her temple was pinned a pearl-tassel hairpin of vintage design that swayed with her movements, all glimmer and shine. Her makeup was exquisite, her brows and eyes drawn especially clear, her lips a vintage true-red echoing her top. The whole of her was like a moving flame, bright and scorching, in stark contrast to the plain, old-fashioned surroundings — and yet strangely drawing every eye.
In the pure white void, Yao Yuan's breath suddenly stopped.
That face, those brows and eyes, that bearing...
It was unmistakably herself at twenty-seven.
Younger, the face flush with the fullness of youth, the brows and eyes still holding a trace of green softness not yet tempered by the dust of the world, interwoven with that powerfully striking beauty — a beauty uniquely bold and dazzling.
She held a small brocade pouch in her hand and looked up naturally toward the man at the door, the smile spreading at the corners of her mouth bright and bold, alive with the cherished, fearless ease of someone who is loved — plainly intimate and at ease with him.
And now, the Yao Yuan in the pure white space also saw clearly the profile of the man standing at the door — it was Shuai Hongqiang. Shuai Hongqiang at thirty-seven, carved by time to just the right point: the brashness and rawness of a young man shed, his brows and eyes settled into the composure and certainty of a man whose career had taken hold; and yet still far from the age when worldliness soaks through and shows as weariness or oiliness. His features were distinct, his jawline clean and crisp, the bridge of his nose high, a faint not-quite-smile curving at his lips. It was the charm of someone at the full, rising peak of life's arc — a blend of the steadiness experience confers, the confidence success nourishes, and a near-genuine vigor not yet worn down by the grind of living. The star-and-moon bodhi mala on his wrist caught and threw back a warm glow with his movements.
But back then, his "taste" and "hobbies" tended to carry distinct marks of imitation and social climbing. That superb-quality star-and-moon bodhi on his wrist, his fussiness over walnut grain, skin, and provenance, even the slightly fussy, relaxed insider's posture he struck now, talking walnuts with the owner at the antique shop door — the source of it all was perhaps some "business big brother" whose circle he was desperately trying to attach himself to, or at least to enter.
Yao Yuan dimly remembered that, during that period, he had seemed especially keen on attending private tea gatherings and small auctions, and would come back carrying a mingled scent of agarwood and Pu'er tea, dropping words like "patina," "crackle glaze," and "old-age feel" into conversation without thinking. He was clumsily and eagerly tracing the language and symbols of that circle.
Obsession? Perhaps there was a little. But for the Shuai Hongqiang of that time, what he was obsessed with was perhaps not the settled time within the antiques themselves, but what owning, appraising, and discussing them symbolized — a higher-grade badge of identity, closer to the circle of "the successful," a "refined hobby" that served as a knock at the door, setting him apart from the nouveau riche. He was obsessed with anything that could swiftly proclaim "I have style": the oily, glossy strand on his wrist, the walnuts he turned clicking in his palm, that pricey Tao inkstone in his study... All of it, together with his rapidly accumulated wealth and his just-right gentlemanly manner, had successfully "equipped" him into a man of substance, taste, and worldliness. Back then Yao Yuan had wandered with him through every antique market — local and nearby, and even in the cities they traveled to.
When the brass bell rang "ding-a-ling," in the instant he saw Yao Yuan step out the door, an undisguised flash of dazzlement and possessiveness crossed his eyes, and the fussy, appraising look on his face melted at once, replaced by a fervent, proud tenderness. He reached out his hand, utterly naturally, his fingertips lingering as they brushed a strand of hair fallen at her shoulder — a gesture intimate and full of admiration. "Done looking? Find anything you like?" His gaze lingered over her, plainly very pleased with this eye-catching outfit of hers. As if this bold, beautiful woman were one of the "collections" he found most worth showing off, and most proud of, at that moment.
Twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's smile grew, and with a little flush of pride she gave the pouch in her hand a shake. "Just picked up a few cheap little things. You two finished talking?" Her voice was a touch clearer and brighter than it would later become, carrying the vivid spirit particular to that age.
"Just about." Shuai Hongqiang lifted the pair of walnuts in his hand and glanced at the owner again. "I'll think the price over." With that, he reached out, utterly natural, his palm settling steadily against the bare, smooth side of her waist, drawing her gently to his side — a gesture full of possessiveness and protectiveness, and faintly of display too. Yao Yuan leaned into it, her posture relaxed and trusting, even carrying a touch of taking-it-for-granted enjoyment.
And in that very moment, the twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan, held in Shuai Hongqiang's arm, let her gaze sweep absently toward some direction down the antique street. Then, as if sensing some far-off, indistinct presence, she suddenly lifted her head, her line of sight passing over Shuai Hongqiang's shoulder, over the clamorous backdrop of the marketplace, even over the barrier of time — and in those eyes drawn so exquisitely and seductively flickered a barely perceptible flash of sharp, dreamlike bewilderment, utterly out of place with the bright mood around her, like someone just waking from a dream — looking, straight and unobstructed, into the pure white void, at the future self who was watching all of this!
The eyes of the two met, in the turbulent current of time!
Though she knew full well the other could not possibly truly see her, that instant of meeting eyes sent a sudden jolt through the very depths of Yao Yuan's (present) soul!
"Boom——!"
The mind of the Yao Yuan in the pure white space went blank. That red dress, that bold beauty, that intimate posture of being held in a lover's arms... all of it had once been a vivid, warm part of her memory.
"In my last dream... I clearly warned her — the twenty-two-year-old her..."
Thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan stood in the void, murmuring to herself, her voice sounding hollow in the absolute silence. In an earlier, more chaotic and blurred "dream" — one she had once taken for nothing more than a dream — she had, in a vague, frantic form, screamed with all her might at that twenty-two-year-old self deep in her romance with Jiang Hai, trying to turn fate aside — "Leave Jiang Hai, his family will make him give you up! Stay in the capital! Or go abroad to study! Don't go back to Jinshi!"
If the twenty-two-year-old Yao Yuan had heeded her warning, then the track of her life should have changed. At twenty-seven she should have been in the capital or abroad — so how had she still ended up back in Jinshi? Still wearing the red dress Shuai Hongqiang loved best, smiling like a flower in the crook of his arm?
Destiny — could it really be a net already woven, impossible to break free of? No matter how the threads in between trembled, would it all in the end be drawn together toward the same single knot?
If every struggle — even a struggle from the "future" — was futile, then this eerie thing that sent her, the thirty-six-year-old, back into the past, that dragged her time and again into this pure white cage to watch some node of her own life — what was its purpose? Was it the malicious mockery of some god, or some crueler punishment, forcing her to tear open her own healed scars with her own hands?
No, that wasn't right. A cold voice rose from the depths of her mind. If it were only repetition, why would there be a "warning"? And if the "warning" was doomed to be useless, then what meaning did the very existence of the "warning" have?
Carrying that doubt, the black dress thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan had prepared for the livestream felt heavy now as a suit of armor. Powerlessly she watched everything before her, like someone who already knew the entire script, watching a film she had starred in herself yet could not switch off.
She watched them stroll, talk, and laugh on that antique street at the turn of spring into summer in 2016, the very picture of a couple in love. Twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan, bright and bold before thirty-seven-year-old Shuai Hongqiang, like the most dazzling of flowers. As if rewinding a tape, she watched the feng shui master who had read her a fortune of "Nine-Purple Fire Great Luck" appear, as expected, from the other end of the street, doggedly chasing after the twenty-seven-year-old her to tell her fortune; watched her make a fuss, and watched him — the one holding her safe in his arms — laugh. In those days, each of them mirrored the other in their eyes, and they too had once given each other what they believed was their "true heart."
Even if, beneath that true heart, fate had long since planted the foreshadowing that flowed toward different ends.

========================================

Chapter 20 — The Secret History of Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang's Entangled Fates
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640267.html

The Secret History of Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang's Entangled Fates
The starting point of all this entanglement traces back to a dinner held even earlier — a banquet thrown to win the furniture-bidding project for Jinshi's provincial theater.
That year, Yao Yuan was twenty-six.
It was 2015, the sixteenth day of the first lunar month, a Friday — the day of "Awakening of Insects."
The flavor of the New Year still lingered. Red lanterns still hung along the streets of Jinshi, and the air carried the sulfur tang of leftover firecrackers along with the lazy ease that only holidays bring. But on the top floor of "Sky-River Tower" — the most lavishly gilded club along the banks of the Yellow River — a grand banquet had just begun, the kind held after the New Year's first day back to work, meant to grease the right palms and open up new ground.
The private room was named "Guanlan," and it lived up to its imposing name. One wall was a single seamless sheet of floor-to-ceiling glass. Beyond it, the muddy, surging waters of the Yellow River churned, carrying chunks of unmelted ice, looking — under the night sky and the city's brilliant lights — like a roaring dragon of dark gold rushing past below. Sitting inside, raising and exchanging cups, one felt a fleeting, illusory grandeur, as if perched above the eternal river itself. The opposite wall held an enormous Suzhou-embroidery screen, stitched with a panorama of mountains and rivers across ten thousand miles, its gold thread faintly shimmering in the light. The room was large enough to easily seat twenty at a revolving table, yet right now held only nine — which made it feel all the more spacious and extravagant.
Shuai Hongqiang was, beyond question, tonight's host. He wore a finely tailored navy cashmere suit with a subtle weave, no tie, the top button of his shirt left open. A green-dialed Rolex flashed at his wrist whenever he carelessly lifted his hand. His smile was hearty, his voice booming, his command of the room assured — he embodied the word "magnanimous" to the fullest.
"Brother Wang, Director Li, Section Chief Zhang... gentlemen, leaders, Happy New Year! New year, new beginnings — our first drink back at work has to be here, looking out over our Mother River!" He raised his glass to open the evening, his tone warm yet respectful. "Tonight there's nothing else on the agenda — just good cheer! Drink as you please, talk as you please!"
The waiters filed in one after another, and the dishes were so extravagant they left people speechless. None of the usual hotel fare — every plate was pushed to the limit of regional specialty and seasonal rarity. The Yellow River carp had to be a "Golden-Scale General" over three feet long, with only the two liveliest crescent-shaped cuts beneath the gills taken, blanched in clear chicken broth, the flesh tender as tofu. There was wild Alashan lamb, the most tender cut of the ribs, slow-roasted over fruitwood until the skin was crisp and the inside ran with juice. From the south came butter crabs, their roe rich and golden, steamed in Huadiao wine, the fragrance filling the whole room. And there was ganba mushroom — hailed as "the king of mushrooms" — stir-fried with Xuanwei ham, its aroma overpowering. With every dish that arrived, Shuai Hongqiang could smile and explain its story: the difficulty of sourcing this ingredient, the pedigree of that chef. It showed his care while, without a trace of effort, broadcasting his means and his taste.
The wine was the real headline act. "I know you leaders have tasted every fine liquor there is. Tonight we won't play those empty games — we'll drink the real thing!" Shuai Hongqiang waved a hand, and the waiters carried in two cases of eighth-generation Wuliangye, then at the very peak of its fame and its price. "This stuff is rich and smooth, it won't go to your head — perfect for the bold spirit of our Yellow River!" He opened the bottles himself, poured every glass himself, and went a full round without his expression changing. His toasts were artful too — never putting anyone on the spot, yet always making you lift your cup willingly. "Director Li, I'll drain mine, you drink as you like — this one's to your steady rise in the new year!" "Brother Wang, this one I have to offer to you — last year's project owed everything to your guidance!"
The mood at the table soon warmed under his coaxing. Just as the wine had gone three rounds and the dishes had been savored, with everyone deep in lively talk, there came a soft knock at the door, which then swung open.
Director Ren of the Culture and Tourism Bureau walked in smiling, apologizing all the while: "Sorry, sorry I'm late — just got out of a meeting. Hongqiang, everyone, my apologies!" But the figure who followed in behind him made the noisy, animated room go still for an almost imperceptible instant.
It was Yao Yuan.
She was dressed all in red — not a gaudy, vulgar bright red, but a darker shade, a deep crimson like aged wine or fine velvet. It was a crimson off-the-shoulder midi dress in subtly lustrous satin, cut with extreme simplicity and a close fit that perfectly traced her curves. The hem fell to the knee, leaving a stretch of fair, straight calf, and on her feet were a pair of black velvet pointed-toe stilettos. She wore little jewelry — just two faint sparks of diamond at her earlobes, and at her throat a very fine platinum chain whose pendant was a small ruby that echoed the color of her lips. Her long hair flowed smoothly over her shoulders, rippling gently with each step.
Against the room's lavish but somewhat sober palette, that red was like a walking flame — restrained yet scorching — and it seized everyone's gaze at once. The smile on her face was pitch-perfect, neither meek nor proud. It blended into the festive remnants of the New Year, yet strangely she commanded the room without seeming the least bit flippant. Her beauty was aggressive, almost weaponized, yet steadied through and through by that calm temperament.
Director Ren, taking the seat of honor, introduced her to Shuai Hongqiang: "Hongqiang, this is the leading star of our provincial song-and-dance troupe, Yao Yuan. Not only does she dance beautifully, she's one of the young backbone talents in our bureau — capable, and sharp as a tack. I brought her along to add a little artistic flavor to our gathering, and to let her see the world a bit." His words, in and out of the lines, made clear her "status" and her value, while leaving ample room for imagination.
The moment Yao Yuan stepped through the door, something undisguised flashed in Shuai Hongqiang's eyes — pure dazzlement. He rose at once, came around half the table, and stretched out his hand, his smile even broader than before — a smile mixing admiration, warmth, and the bearing of a host: "Teacher Yao! I've long heard your name — today I finally meet you in person! Truly... radiant! Happy New Year, Happy New Year!"
As they shook hands, he felt her fingertips slightly cool, her grip just right. Her voice was clear and bright, edged with a smile: "Boss Shuai, Happy New Year. Sorry to intrude." Her manner was easy and gracious.
Shuai Hongqiang personally led her to her seat, placing her right beside Director Ren. Returning to his own chair, he raised his glass and announced to the whole table in his booming voice: "Come, let's welcome Teacher Yao! This cup — to beauty, to art, and to a new year that blazes red-hot for us all!" He laid deliberate stress on "blazes red-hot," his gaze sweeping over Yao Yuan's deep crimson dress, the implication clear. It drew a knowing chuckle from everyone, and they all chimed in.
Yao Yuan lifted her glass too — the Wuliangye a waiter had just poured for her. She stood, her posture tall and graceful, her smile bright: "Thank you, Boss Shuai, thank you, leaders. I can't really drink, but I'm happy today, and it's the new year, so let me offer everyone a toast — may you all have everything you wish for this year, and thank you, Director Ren, for bringing me along." With that, under the slightly surprised and admiring gazes around her, she drained the clear liquor in one go, the motion crisp and clean. A faint flush rose to her cheeks, lending her even more color.
"Well done! Teacher Yao doesn't hold back!" Shuai Hongqiang was the first to cheer, leading the applause. The look he gave her now held, beyond the initial dazzlement, a good deal more probing and intense interest. This woman wasn't just stunning to look at — in a setting like this she was poised and proper, even a little bold.
Through the dinner Yao Yuan didn't volunteer much, but whenever the talk turned to artistic performances or marketing, or whenever she was asked about the troupe, she always had something substantial and clear to say. Now and then she could even field a harmless joke, smoothing the mood at just the right moment. When someone, emboldened by drink, half-jokingly asked whether "she had connections up in the capital," she only looked over with those clear, bright eyes, gave a slight smile, and swirled her empty glass: "Leader, now you're teasing me. If I really had such a powerful backer, would I still be sitting here learning from all of you? I'd have ascended to heaven long ago. Me — I'm just someone raised by the soil and water of Jinshi. Right now all I want is to dance well, to polish our province's cultural calling card a little brighter, and for that I'll still need all you leaders to lend your support." A few words, and she'd turned aside a thousand pounds with four ounces. She neither denied the vague halo that the rumor lent her, nor handed over any handle that could be used against her. The mystery surrounding her supposed transfer tied to the Jiang and Hai families was one of her best suits of armor.
When she finished, she again drew laughter from everyone present, and the leader who'd asked just pointed at her with a grin and let the matter drop.
Shuai Hongqiang held his glass and watched her through the mingled fragrance of food and wine. When she leaned in to listen to a bureau chief beside her, her lashes lowered, casting a small shadow on her eyelids, her bearing intent; when she turned to murmur for the waiter to refill her tea, she seemed utterly serene. She was beautiful — but beautiful in a way that was very "useful," very "safe," even very "professional." It made him set aside his initial casualness and study her with more seriousness.
While Shuai Hongqiang worked the room among the various leaders, he took in everything at the table.
As the dinner wound down, he waved his hand grandly: "Let's go — next stop! 'Jinding' Club. I've booked the biggest private room. Tonight we have to enjoy ourselves to the fullest!" "Jinding" was then Jinshi's most exclusive private club — members-only, with excellent privacy.
After dinner, as they moved on to the club, Director Ren patted Shuai Hongqiang on the shoulder and said with a low laugh: "Little Yao's something, isn't she? She makes her living on real ability, even has a little business of her own — very sharp. You young people should talk more." The words served to clear up a few things, hold out a little possibility, and dispel Shuai Hongqiang's suspicion that Director Ren and Yao Yuan might have some hidden relationship.
The light in the club's private room was hazy and ambiguous. There was a huge ring-shaped leather sofa, and mellow blues music drifted through a top-grade sound system as background. Fruit platters, snacks, foreign spirits, and red wine had long been laid out. Some of the leaders began to sing, some gathered in twos and threes to chat, some started up a game of dice.
Yao Yuan sat in a corner of the sofa, quietly sipping the warm lemon water a waiter had brought. In the shifting colored lights, her deep crimson gown took on different sheens, and she was like a silently burning flower, keeping a subtle distance from the faint din around her.
After making his rounds, Shuai Hongqiang finally came over with a square-cut glass holding amber liquor (very likely Hennessy XO) and sat down on the single armchair beside her — not too close, not too far, a distance that allowed conversation without being overly intimate.
"That outfit suits the occasion today, Teacher Yao — and it looks especially good on you." His tone now carried a touch more ease and a lower register than at the table, his gaze settling on her sincerely, full of appreciation.
Yao Yuan turned her head and gave him a smile, one that in the hazy light shed some of its official polish and gained a bit more truth: "Thank you, Boss Shuai. It's the new year, after all — red is auspicious. But it's you who went all out today, arranging everything so thoughtfully."
"As it should be — everyone having a good time is what matters most." Shuai Hongqiang swirled his glass and asked, as if in passing, "From the way you spoke at dinner, Teacher Yao, you know your way around marketing? And you have a company of your own?"
"Just a small company I run for fun with a friend — mainly taking on some performance-related promotional design, earning a bit of pocket money, and it makes my work with the troupe more convenient too." Yao Yuan answered modestly, but there was a barely perceptible thread of confidence in her tone.
"Impressive." Shuai Hongqiang praised her sincerely and leaned slightly forward — a posture signaling focus and a closing of psychological distance. "I'll be honest with you, I've been mulling over a few cultural-investment projects lately. I think there's a lot to be done in that space. Down the road, there may well be a place where Teacher Yao and I could work together."
"You're joking, Boss Shuai — you're a man who does big things. But if there's ever anything I can be useful for, I'll certainly do my best." Yao Yuan lifted her water glass and lightly touched it to the glass in his hand, drawing a soft "clink," her smile perfectly judged.
Just then, someone on the screen queued up Faye Wong's "Legend," the melody gentle. Shuai Hongqiang suddenly asked, "Would you like to sing, Teacher Yao? Pick one? Whatever you like, I'll cue it up for you."
Yao Yuan shook her head and declined with a smile: "I'm all right at dancing, but I'll pass on singing."
"What a shame." Shuai Hongqiang laughed, not pressing it, and instead began talking about a concert he'd been to recently — the conversation flowing on naturally.
After a while, Director Ren came over to call Shuai Hongqiang away to drink with another bureau chief. Shuai Hongqiang rose and gave Yao Yuan an apologetic smile: "Sit tight a moment, Teacher Yao, I'll just be over there."
Yao Yuan nodded. As he turned to leave, her gaze seemed to drift, as if by chance, over his retreating back — the well-cut suit, the upright bearing, and that air of control and bold confidence that belongs to a successful man, evident in his every word and move. Then she lowered her lashes, the long fringe casting a small shadow beneath her eyes, her feelings unreadable.
Halfway across the room, Shuai Hongqiang glanced back. He saw Yao Yuan still sitting there quietly, her profile tracing a graceful curve in the hazy light, the cup of lemon water cradled in her hands, the rising steam seeming to blur her face. In that moment, the calm — even slightly aloof — beauty about her formed a strange contrast with the lively poise she'd shown at the table. She was like a vivid flame he couldn't quite touch, and some desire to conquer began to quietly take root in his heart.
He knew this "meeting" would by no means stop here. This "koi carp" — he'd already silently given her a code name in his mind — he was set on reeling in.
That night, they exchanged contact information. Shuai Hongqiang's pretext was: "In case the troupe needs anything on the promotion side down the line, or there's a chance for your own company to collaborate — let's keep in touch." Lofty, unassailable.
After Yao Yuan left, Shuai Hongqiang stood at the club's entrance and lit a cigarette. The chill of the spring night carried a lingering bite that scattered some of the wine's haze. He recalled the feel of her red velvet hem brushing his knee as she came around to sit beside him, remembered the faint light in her eyes when she spoke of her work, and remembered the composure beyond her years with which she'd parried every probe.
This woman was interesting. There was a contradiction about her: beneath the stunning beauty lay a clear head and a definite drive for her career; she seemed attached to the system, yet kept her own independent boundaries and livelihood; rumor wrapped her in a fog, while she herself displayed solid, genuine ability. To conquer her seemed not merely to conquer a beautiful woman, but more like conquering a stretch of uncharted, challenging territory not easily mapped. It stirred a powerful interest in him — an interest mixing male instinct, commercial appraisal, and the urge to conquer.
So it turned out everything had been foreshadowed. When Shuai Hongqiang first looked at Yao Yuan, there may have been dazzlement, but more of it was scrutiny and assessment. Her "usefulness," her resistance to easy control — these were precisely the bait that first drew him in.
A month later, Shuai Hongqiang won, with no suspense at all, the provincial theater's entire fixed-furniture-and-fittings project for every zone, totaling 15.8 million — from the seats in the auditorium to the furnishings of the conference rooms and VIP lounges, all of it swept into his pocket. With the project secured, the dealings grew frequent. His contact with the theater, the Culture and Tourism Bureau, and related units became ever closer — and that naturally included Yao Yuan.
In the course of all this back-and-forth, his gaze gradually shifted from "business partner" to the woman herself.
Over half a year, Shuai Hongqiang's commercial map quietly expanded. His company image badly needed an upgrade, and almost without a second thought, he handed the entire commission for the corporate visuals and promotional materials over to Yao Yuan's still-growing advertising company. From promotional brochures and custom gifts to a full set of business cards — everything her company could take on, he gave her. That steady, substantial order earned Yao Yuan's company a very real first bucket of gold, and let it gain a firm foothold in the industry fast.
Everything seemed to fall into place of its own accord.
He kept displaying the deep resources, bold style, and mature charm of a successful man, precisely delivering care and resources. Nor did she shrink from the stage — whether in professional insight or in the deft maneuvering of social settings, she handled it all with ease, showing the radiance and value of a woman who could hold her own ground.
Mutual appreciation, mutual designs — plus those subtle flutters that passed between their brows and eyes outside the bounds of professional exchange — a tacit, unspoken attraction had long since quietly grown.
And so, the date arrived: February 14, 2016.
That evening, Shuai Hongqiang sat on the observation deck of Jinshi's highest revolving restaurant at the cloud-top. Beyond the glass curtain wall, the city's lights merged with the sunset glow on the horizon into one glittering river of stars. Tender live jazz floated through the air, and in the center of the long table sat a large bouquet of deep-red roses, every petal beaded with water — a delicate contrast to the still-chilly spring night outside.
He himself wore a well-fitted bespoke suit, looking more formal than usual. Yao Yuan arrived as promised, led in by an attendant, wrapped in a calf-length black cashmere coat of superb texture. The coat's cut was crisp and flowing, cinched only by a narrow belt at the waist, tracing her slender waist. She raised her hands and slipped the coat off, handing it to the attendant at her side — the gesture itself like a small, elegant ritual.
Beneath the coat was the true showpiece of the evening: a dark-red satin slip gown. That red was like aged port — deep, rich — and under the restaurant's carefully designed lighting it flowed with a fine sheen, as if velvet and silk were woven together. The thin straps perfectly displayed the lines of her graceful shoulders, neck, and collarbones. The dress clung as it fell, drew in slightly at the waist, then spread out in a slow fishtail curve, so that with each step the light rippled across it, setting off skin white as snow. She was like a velvet rose suddenly breaking free of the night's embrace, burning hot — a beauty of extreme tension and aggression that seized all the air and every gaze in the room at once.
Undisguised admiration and a sense of conquest flashed in Shuai Hongqiang's eyes. He strode forward, personally pulling out her chair, his fingertips brushing — barely there — against her bare, faintly cool upper arm. "Cold out there?" he asked in a low voice, his tone an intimate concern.
"It was fine." Yao Yuan sat down and revealed a smile to him, a smile that, set off by the dazzling light and her red dress, was bright beyond compare. The attendant carefully stored away her coat — that quiet black cashmere, like the last shred of cool armor she'd carried in from the real world, now laid aside.
Dinner was a carefully composed Chef's Menu, paired with vintage champagne. The atmosphere gradually warmed amid the fine food, the fine wine, and the ever-deepening, ever-more-brilliant night view beyond the window. At just the right moment, he took out a deep-blue velvet jewelry box. Opening it revealed not an ordinary ring but a diamond necklace. The center stone was a fancy-color yellow diamond of about three carats, surrounded by sparkling white diamonds like stars attending the moon, refracting blazing fire in the light — precious and dazzling, yet distinctive, never falling into cliché.
"Yuan'er," he called her name, his voice lower than usual, his gaze focused and deep. "This necklace is called 'Toward the Sun.'" He paused, then went on, "From the first time I saw you, I felt you were like a flame — bright, radiant, full of life. I don't want to bind that flame. I only hope I can stand beside you — and perhaps, give you something to lean on."
His words deftly avoided any frivolous promise, yet expressed his admiration and his wish to protect. There was no dramatic scene of going down on one knee, yet he set his posture sincere enough. He picked up the necklace, stepped behind her, and gently fastened it on. The cold diamonds met her warm skin, and the hanging yellow diamond settled exactly at the center of her collarbone, glittering, reflecting the light in his eyes.
Yao Yuan touched the diamonds at her throat — hard and brilliant against her fingers. She lifted her eyes to their reflection in the glass: he stood behind her, his hand still resting lightly at her shoulder, his posture intimate and full of possession. The restaurant's lilting music, the boundless night view beyond the window, the heavy, dark-piercing brilliance at her throat, and before her this man laying his resources, his thoughts, and his desire bare — all of it composed an irresistible, grand, and exquisite temptation.
In that moment, the heart of twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan was flooded with a mix of vanity, emotion, a vague longing for the future, and a genuine attraction. She turned her head and gave him a smile beyond bright, even touched with a little sweet shyness, a smile that against the diamond's glow was beautiful beyond compare.
She didn't say "yes," but that smile — and the fingertips she quietly laid over the back of his hand — were already her silent consent.
On this Valentine's evening, at the highest point of the city, between diamonds and lights, the relationship between them crossed past the push and pull of ambiguity and formally began.
The restaurant's brilliant lights, the cold glint of diamonds, and the bubbles of champagne all finally settled into a private stillness high above the city. In the presidential suite on the top floor of the cloud-top hotel, the heavy door closed soundlessly behind them, sealing out the whole city's clamor. The lights in the room were dimmed to their lowest, leaving only a few wall lamps in the corners casting warm yellow halos, the air filled with the scent of high-end perfume. Beyond the floor-to-ceiling windows lay the boundless, silent sea of city lights, interwoven with the moonlight of the sixteenth night — dreamlike and ambiguous.
The elegant waltz that had begun at the restaurant, the moment it reached the room, turned fierce and exhilarating in rhythm and step — exactly like a tango between the two of them.
Words became superfluous ornament, replaced by the language of bodies, the cadence of breath, the meeting of gazes. The expensive suit and that dark-red satin gown lay heaped on the thick carpet — black and red, a perfectly matched pairing of colors.
Shuai Hongqiang's breath was scorching, carrying an unquestionable desire to dominate, sweeping over frozen ground like a warm current, set on surveying every inch of territory and leaving his own mark and warmth upon it. Yao Yuan's response was like waterweed beneath a hidden tide — seeming to sway with the current, to lean and yield, yet possessed of its own supple roots and a will of its own in how it twined, quietly drawing the life-force and a certain confirmation the warm current brought, through every winding closeness.
Shuai Hongqiang was conquering a rich and mysterious land he had long desired, while Yao Yuan was experiencing the thrill of being wholly enveloped by a powerful force, briefly surrendering to dizziness and loss of control — and at the same time, with all her sensitivity and responsiveness, appraising this "conqueror's" sincerity and strength.
Beyond the window, the city's river of light flowed soundlessly. Within, the temperature rose, and the most primal force and beauty played out their ageless drama against a backdrop of extreme luxury and privacy.
In the end, all the exploration, negotiation, tides, and storms came to rest in a deep silence carried with exhaustion and a strange kind of satisfaction. Only the not-yet-settled heartbeats of the two of them tapped out the lingering echo between their pressed-together chests.
Shuai Hongqiang's arm circled her into his embrace — a posture full of possession. His chin rested against the crown of her hair, and his voice came hoarse and satisfied in the aftermath: "You're mine now, Yuan'er." The words were like a stamp sealing the verdict on everything tonight.
Yao Yuan didn't answer right away. She only pressed her cheek to his sweat-damp chest, listening to that steady, powerful heartbeat. In her eyes were reflected the distant lights beyond the window — bright, yet somewhat unfocused. The pleasure of the body was real; a certain emotional dependence and the satisfaction of vanity were real too. But beneath this real, lingering warmth, a very faint clarity, thin as a thread of ice, was quietly stirring. She had gained the attention she craved, the proof of material things, and a seemingly reliable support to lean on — but she had also handed over a part of herself. And not only her body.

========================================

Chapter 21 — The Prophecy in the Mirror
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640268.html

In the pure white space, thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan closed her eyes for a moment. The memory came to an end.

The memory of the body and the embers of feeling, across all those long years, still seemed to carry back a faint, complicated warmth she could hardly put into words. The flutter of the heart, the giving of herself, the vague unease and the enormous longing of that time, all mixed together, had once again brewed a strong liquor called "love" — and she, half resisting and half giving in, had drained it in a single swallow.

Now, looking back in absolute clarity, what she saw was an exchange whose price had long since been marked, a mutual choice that began in passion and calculation. Every detail of that night, every touch, every whispered word, was now stripped of its romantic sugar coating, baring the more essential shape beneath: a contest of desire, a swapping of resources, the comfort of the lonely, and a hazy investment in future gain.

The time-travel had set her here in this pure white space to relive the scene — perhaps not to humiliate her, but to let her see clearly at last: the road that had led her to where she was today was not paved entirely with flowers at its starting point. Some of it was made of the chips she herself had handed over, and the gorgeous, heavy necklace she had been all too willing to put on.

It had all been written down long ago. She had simply, at the time, been unable to read the opening chapter of her own fate.

When Yao Yuan decided to be with Shuai Hongqiang, she knew he was divorced and single, with a daughter living with his ex-wife. But she only truly understood the story behind it all after they had been together for more than half a year.

Shuai Hongqiang's ex-wife was named Qiu Ying, three years his senior, a native of Jinshi born and raised.
