Their first meeting came in 2003, beside the clattering lathes of a factory in the western district — back then, twenty-four-year-old Shuai Hongqiang became a coworker of Qiu Ying's stepfather. The old man liked the honesty and quick wits of this young man from the mountains, and a year later he brought his stepdaughter to meet him. Another year, and the wedding character was pasted to the window. Shuai Hongqiang followed his father-in-law back to Jinshi and set his foot on the path of starting a business.

In 2006 their daughter Shuai Wenrui was born. The real turning point for this family came in 2010 — Jinshi pushed forward with urban planning, and demolition of the old town began. Qiu Ying's family was inside the zone. Overnight, the bulldozers of urban renewal also bulldozed in fate's generous gift. At the time Shuai Hongqiang's household registration was still in his home village, so to ladle out an extra bowl of soup through the cracks in the policy, they calmly signed a divorce agreement, "folding away" the marriage for the time being — legally, the daughter, the house, and the savings all went to Qiu Ying.

With the three properties he was allotted and the capital he had built up earlier, Shuai Hongqiang caught the wave of urban construction over the following years. He swiftly transformed from a young dropout out of the mountains into the well-known "Boss Shuai" of the business world.

As his enterprise grew larger and his social circle wider, he gradually lost any common language with Qiu Ying, who had stayed home tending to the family ever since the marriage. Even so, Shuai Hongqiang was never stingy with money. The living expenses, the daughter's education, even the care of Qiu Ying's parents — he shouldered all of it generously and without complaint.

It was only when Yao Yuan walked into his life that this "fake divorce" finally turned into a real separation. Shuai Hongqiang gave Qiu Ying a one-time compensation generous enough to guarantee her a comfortable, want-free life from then on. Their daughter Shuai Wenrui stayed with her mother, while all of her education costs were borne by her father.

Later, word had it that not long after Shuai Hongqiang married Lin Wan, Qiu Ying too remarried — to a fellow demolition resettler from the same district, an old classmate of hers from primary school.

Back then, once Yao Yuan knew the full story, the thought did flash through her mind — that the men of this world generally tire of the old and crave the new, even though she herself happened to be that "new." But in private she did not dwell on it too much. For one thing, Shuai Hongqiang cherished her with the utmost care, his doting tenderness plain in every gesture. For another, she did not believe she was the main reason Shuai Hongqiang and Qiu Ying had parted ways.

If thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan looked back now, she would see straight through to the core at a glance: in a relationship, when one person races ahead while the other stays rooted where they were — and this "racing ahead" is not necessarily the ability to make money, but rather a matter of vision, insight, and the territory of the spirit — then parting is all but inevitable.

There was something else in Shuai Hongqiang, an almost primal yearning passed down through a thousand years: he wanted a son. After they got together, Yao Yuan came to truly know how vigorous his appetites were, sometimes to the point of being insatiable, leaving her hardly able to keep up. Each time, he would gather her into his arms and coax her in a breathy, honeyed voice: "Let's have a son, all right? With the two of us for parents, the kid will be unbelievably good-looking, unbelievably smart… I love making money, so our son's nickname will be 'Little Shuai Money.' A jaunty cap worn at an angle, riding a kick scooter — he'll definitely be the cutest little guy in the whole neighborhood." He said this far too many times, half true and half in jest, and bit by bit it seeped into Yao Yuan's heart.

Yao Yuan had lost her father at seven. Although her stepfather, Uncle Zhou, treated her well enough, emotionally she could never quite reach the level of utter closeness and complete reliance. But thirty-seven-year-old Shuai Hongqiang gave her endless doting and indulgence, letting her feel both the warmth of being loved like a daughter and the sweetness between lovers. Steeped for so long in this kind of tenderness, and with Shuai Hongqiang bringing up the idea of a son more and more often, the thought of stepping into marriage took root in Yao Yuan's heart for the second time. She thought: judging from the way Shuai Hongqiang had handled things with his ex-wife, this man's character couldn't be too bad — perhaps he was worth entrusting her whole life to. On top of that, her coworkers of the same age were marrying one after another, her mother Zhang Fengxia often nagged in her ear, and Shuai Hongqiang was successful in business and well-off in every respect. He truly was an ideal candidate for marriage.

And yet, who could ever truly fathom what Shuai Hongqiang really thought in his heart? His love for her was real; his tenderness was real; he was never stingy with material things. But every time the conversation drifted toward the threshold of marriage, he would skirt around it without leaving a trace. In business they could advance hand in hand — Shuai Hongqiang was smooth in handling affairs, Yao Yuan moved deftly through human relationships, and the two worked together so seamlessly they were nearly unstoppable. But to actually marry Yao Yuan home, Shuai Hongqiang lost his nerve. He was afraid he could never truly "catch" a Yao Yuan who shone so brightly.

Yao Yuan kept waiting for Shuai Hongqiang to bring up a proposal on his own, but all she ever got was vague, evasive answers. Two years later, Yao Yuan unexpectedly fell pregnant, but she did not tell Shuai Hongqiang. She only asked him one last time when they would register the marriage, and once again failed to get a straight answer. So the child was gone, and she left far away for Hangcheng.

At this point in her thoughts, thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan unconsciously laid her hand gently over her lower belly again. If that child had been born, it would have been seven this year.

What was even more ironic was that the man who had once clung to her, begging for a "Little Shuai Money," turned around and married Lin Wan, who looked every bit the "virtuous wife and loving mother," a woman fit to keep a home.

Perhaps this was simply human nature: full of self-contradictory desires, finely calculated trade-offs, and a dim complexity that one cannot even face head-on. In human nature there is no absolute good, and no absolute bad.

Thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan stood in the pure white space, helplessly reliving the past on one hand, and on the other watching the twenty-seven-year-old version of herself still strolling with Shuai Hongqiang through the antiques street. How could her twenty-seven-year-old self have resisted that feng shui master's relentless cajoling? In the end she let the man read in her a destiny of the "Great Fortune of Nine-Purple Departing Fire," and even accepted the fire-phoenix gourd the old Daoist gave her. After that she walked into a somewhat shabby antique shop. The store was small, the light a dim yellow, and old wooden cabinets held a scattering of relics whose authenticity was hard to tell. Shuai Hongqiang gave it a single glance, lost interest, and ambled out the door to chat with the neighboring shopkeeper about the quality of phoenix-eye bodhi beads. So the only one left in the shop was twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan, her gaze drifting idly over those dust-covered old things.

Then, in the instant she looked up, she caught sight of a faint, dark glint of bronze amid a tangled heap in the corner of the counter.

She reached out and worked it free of the clutter. It was a small bronze mirror, about seven or eight centimeters across, its surface a dull yellow that reflected a person only faintly. But the back was carved with fine craftsmanship — a cloud-and-thunder pattern as a ground, with a single Kui-dragon coiled within, plain and antique yet carrying a touch of the mysterious. "Quite exquisite," she murmured to herself, and was just about to raise her voice and ask the price —

The air around her abruptly sank, as though frozen by an unseen force. That dull yellow mirror surface rippled like water, and slowly there surfaced a blurred yet utterly familiar face. It was her thirty-six-year-old self, gazing back at her across the void of time.

Two Yao Yuans, in dislocated time and space, met each other's eyes once again.

"You—" they spoke at the same moment, then fell silent at the same moment, each startled by the other's voice.

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan steadied her emotions first and broke the silence, her voice calm but carrying an all-knowing chill: "The twenty-two-year-old you still didn't believe what I said, did you? Otherwise, how would the twenty-seven-year-old you now have crossed paths with Shuai Hongqiang again?"

A flicker of awkwardness crossed the twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's face, and her fingers tightened slightly around the bronze mirror. "At first I really did find it hard to believe. You appeared without any warning, saying all those bizarre things that ran completely against my wonderful life at the time. Anyone in my place wouldn't have believed it so easily. After you vanished, I even thought it had only been a hallucination. Then that Spring Festival, I didn't go back to Jinshi — I went to Jianghai's family with him instead. His family had a lot of relatives, and their words were laced through and through with gossip. I even met that Miss Zhou. After I got back, I thought it over for a long time, analyzed everything carefully, and finally decided to believe what you'd said. Later I got a spot for advanced study at the Amsterdam dance academy in the Netherlands and studied abroad for two years. Not long after I came home, Mom suddenly coughed up blood. Her condition kept flaring up; several times she was at death's door, and in the end she had a lung resection. To make it easier to care for her, I decided to move my work back to Jinshi. After that, in the course of work, I slowly came together with Shuai Hongqiang. I imagine you know all of this already — after all, you're the future me, aren't you?"

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan listened quietly, her face neither sad nor glad, as though all of these ripples were within her expectations, and as though every deviation would, in the end, return to its origin. Was a fixed fate truly impossible to shake? The fine details might be altered — like her twenty-two-year-old self, who got to part from Jianghai more gracefully and to glimpse a wider world, so that the wound ran much shallower. But the crucial junctures, as if drawn by an invisible magnetic pull, stubbornly returned to their original track. And yet — wasn't that different "experience" itself a kind of change?

She drew back the deep musings surging in her eyes and refocused on the twenty-seven-year-old self's eyes, which still held reliance and hope, and spoke word by word, clear and cold:

"You've already proven my prophecy. So listen well to my warning right now: leave Shuai Hongqiang. Now."

The twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's pupils contracted slightly. Having only just entered a formal relationship with Shuai Hongqiang, wrapped in doting and sweetness, she instinctively recoiled. "Why?"

"Because he does love you, and he treats you well — but he won't marry you." The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan's words were like a blade tempered in ice, cleaving precisely through the tender surface. "In his heart, you're clever and dazzling, a partner who can stand shoulder to shoulder with him in business." Thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan went on, her words terse and cruel: "But you're not suited to be married home to keep a husband and raise children — simply because you don't have that so-called virtuous, demure face. If you don't leave, a year from now you'll fall pregnant by accident, you'll break your heart in his silence, and in the end you'll go far away alone. That taste — I don't want you to swallow it a second time."

"How… could it be like this?" the twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan murmured, the bronze mirror in her hand seeming to grow heavy and scalding. "Then what should I do?"

"Apply for unpaid leave and go to Shanghai," the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan said, picking up speed, with a kind of urgency that raced against time. "That's the financial and internet center of the future — a vast world, full of promise. You absolutely can—"

Before the words were finished, the scene before her suddenly convulsed violently. The twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's astonished face, the dim-yellow antique shop, and the entire pure white space all shook like a reflection in water stirred by an unseen giant hand. They surged, twisted, and shattered into hundreds of millions of flecks of light, then were swallowed by an absolute void.

Right after came the familiar, dizzying, whirling sense of weightlessness.

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open, her heart slamming heavily in her chest. A hard, cold sensation pressed into her palm. She looked down — the cloud-and-thunder Kui-dragon bronze mirror was clutched tightly in her hand, its antique patterns pressed deep into her skin, bringing a distinct ache. She slowly loosened her fingers; the outline of the Kui-dragon on the mirror's back stood out clearly.

So it turned out that this bronze mirror, which Shuai Hongqiang had obtained from an auction, had already quietly broken into her life at such an early time.

The office was very quiet, the light bright and clear, the bustle and clamor of the real world outside the window.

This time, she was utterly certain.

It was not a dream, nor a hallucination. She had truly, in some bizarre way that transcended time and space, returned to a certain juncture of the past. She had tried to help her past self take fewer detours, to cut straight to a shortcut — and yet she had still arrived at the predetermined juncture.

Her past self had not gone through that heart-rending pain of parting with Jianghai, but had instead suffered the anxious heartache of a mother gravely ill. Truly, if fate offers a gift, it must secretly draw something away.

And the next time — where would she travel to? Or rather, could she really change the ending behind that bronze mirror, the one that seemed already written?

Yao Yuan steadied herself and glanced at the corner of her computer screen.

16:10.

From the moment she clutched the bronze mirror and fell into that pure white expanse of time and space, to this moment of jolting awake in the silence of the office, only a single hour had passed in the real world.

She rubbed her temple, pressing down for now the exhaustion and daze welling up from the depths of her consciousness. Right now there were matters in the realm of real-world science, far more pressing than any uncanny mysticism, that demanded handling. She pressed the intercom, her voice already restored to its usual crisp decisiveness: "Tell the project team — number one conference room in fifteen minutes. We'll do a final confirmation of every detail for tomorrow's negotiation with Western World Technology on the 'Pretty Big One Emotional AI model.'"

At 17:30, she politely declined a business partner's dinner invitation and brought home a simple takeout meal. The drain of the livestream and the mental shock of the time-travel layered together into a heavy fatigue, and tomorrow's meeting with Zhao Yiming's company could not afford a single misstep. She needed plenty of rest. After a hot bath, drowsiness surged in like a tide, and she sank into deep sleep almost the instant her head touched the pillow. The Kui-dragon bronze mirror lying quietly in her bag, together with all the tangled riddles tied to Shuai Hongqiang behind it, were, in the priorities of this one night, set aside for the time being by reason and weariness.

========================================

Chapter 22 — The First Fusion of the Genesis Engine and the Pretty Big One's Core Emotional Data
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640269.html

Tuesday, nine-fifty in the morning.

The Western World Technology headquarters tower stood at the heart of Jinshi's high-tech district. Its skin of matte-black smart-glass curtain wall absorbed and transformed the crisp winter sunlight, giving off a cold, restrained, intensely futuristic quality. The building's lines were clean and sharp, free of any superfluous ornament, yet it looked like a great beast lying in wait — silent, but charged with the power to wake at any moment and rewrite some set of rules.

Yao Yuan's black Maybach slid soundlessly into the underground garage. She pushed the door open and stepped out; the cold winter air sharpened her senses. Today she wore a dark-gray cashmere skirt suit, exquisitely tailored. The jacket's shoulder line was straight and crisp, the waist nipped in at just the right curve; the skirt fell to the knee, taut and smooth. In the light, the fine cashmere shimmered with a subdued, pearl-like luster. Beneath it she wore a silk-satin blouse in the same tone but a shade lighter, its collar folded neatly out over the jacket's lapels — the soft sheen of silk playing against the matte texture of cashmere in a delicate layered contrast. Her long hair was drawn back into a low ponytail, not a strand out of place, the tie blending seamlessly with her hair color; only a few deliberately loosened wisps curled softly at her forehead and temples, gentling the lines of her face and lending an effortless touch of life. At her ears, a pair of classic Chanel double-C metal drop earrings traced clean, decisive lines, catching a cold gleam with every movement, a clever accent against the overall palette. On her wrist sat a square-faced watch with a firm red leather strap. The whole look was understated yet rich in skill, simple yet full of hidden detail — a perfect statement of exactly what she needed to project right now: professional authority, an unquestionable calm, and a quiet but absolutely present edge of fashion.

Behind her came two members of her core team. One was Bai Wei, the Pretty Big One's chief product officer and resident psychology expert — thirty-five or thirty-six, with a quiet, intellectual poise. The other was Su Hang, the young technical director, in black-framed glasses, gripping an encrypted tablet, his expression taut.

The elevator took them straight to the top floor. The corridor was empty and silent — the floor a dark-gray micro-cement, the walls pure white and undecorated, lit only by linear strips embedded in the surfaces that gave off an even, soft, cold glow. It looked nothing like the headquarters of a tech company pulling in nearly ten billion a year; more like a giant laboratory or sterile cleanroom that hadn't yet been put to use.

An assistant in a white lab coat, face expressionless, was already waiting at the elevator. After verifying their identities, the assistant led them in silence toward the depths. Their footsteps were swallowed by the empty corridor, the echoes almost inaudible.

At last they stopped before an unmarked metal-gray door. The assistant pressed a palm to the sensor panel beside it, and the heavy door slid open without a sound.

A wave of mixed scents rolled out to meet them — ozone, the heat venting off precision instruments, and something harder to name, like wet earth and metal after rain. Beyond the door was no ordinary conference room but an enormous spherical space, the ceiling at least eight meters high. The walls, the ceiling, even parts of the floor, were assembled from countless fine, nearly invisible hexagonal screens, currently displaying a deep, slowly drifting starscape that gave one a vertiginous sense of floating in the cosmos. At the center floated a glowing ring-shaped console. Zhao Yiming stood at it with his back to the door, gazing at a cascade of blue data streaming through the empty air before him. Today he wore a simple black turtleneck and dark trousers; against the vast backdrop of data his frame looked lean and intent.

Hearing the footsteps, he didn't turn. He merely raised his right hand in a small gesture. The flowing data instantly froze, contracted, and reorganized itself into a clean three-dimensional architecture diagram suspended above the console.

"Right on time." Zhao Yiming finally turned. His gaze rested on Yao Yuan for a moment, taking in her crisp attire, then moved to Bai Wei and Su Hang behind her. "And these two are?"

"Bai Wei, my chief product officer, a doctor of psychology, in charge of the Pretty Big One's content system and counseling framework. Su Hang, technical director, responsible for the data platform and algorithm deployment." Yao Yuan made the introductions briefly, her eyes already drawn to this strange "conference room." "Boss Zhao, your setup here is — quite something."

"This is the Mirror Room, one of the immersive development and testing environments for the Genesis Engine." Zhao Yiming walked back to the console and gestured for them to come closer. "Here, we can interact directly with the model's 'consciousness layer' at a high dimension — orders of magnitude more efficient than a conventional interface. The data interfaces and preliminary framework you've brought can be imported straight in."

Su Hang drew a deep breath, stepped forward, and connected the tablet to a port on the console with a dedicated cable. Bai Wei, meanwhile, took a heavy encrypted hard drive from her briefcase.

"All data has undergone deep cleaning and multiple rounds of anonymization, meeting the strictest privacy and security standards. The original recordings have been converted into feature vectors, and the text-interaction data has been annotated across multiple dimensions. The tagging system is based on our proprietary 'Relationship Dynamics' and 'Emotional Energy Map' models. This is the framework white paper." Bai Wei's voice was calm and professional as she handed over a bound document.

Zhao Yiming took it but didn't open it right away. Instead he gave the assistant an order: "Initialize the security sandbox. Isolation level: Black Moon."

The assistant tapped rapidly on the console. The surrounding starscape dimmed, replaced by a soft, semi-transparent silver film of light that rose from all sides and completely enclosed the central area where they stood. Across its surface, countless tiny silver points of light, like runes, flowed and flickered in some hidden pattern.

"Now we're entirely cut off from the outside. Under the Black Moon protocol, everything that happens in here — data exchanges, conversation, even shifts in thought — will not be recorded or perceived by any external device." Zhao Yiming explained, then looked at Su Hang. "Begin the import. Start with the smallest sample set."

The data began to flow in. The 3D architecture diagram above the console immediately changed: the pale-gold points and lines representing the Pretty Big One's data system began to embed themselves into the original azure structure that formed the Genesis Engine's main trunk. At first it was halting — the gold points probing and colliding at the edges of the blue tracery — but soon, under Zhao Yiming's deft adjustments and the "lubrication" of a few key algorithms, the gold began to merge slowly but surely into the blue, forming a strange, interwoven blue-and-gold blend, flowing as one.

"Compatibility's better than expected." Zhao Yiming watched the fusing data streams, his fingers sliding and tapping rapidly in the empty air, adjusting parameters. "Your annotation system has its own ingenuity — especially these two dimensions, 'power dynamics' and 'latent needs.' They make up for the data sparsity in our model's modeling of social-relationship gamesmanship. But —"

He frowned slightly. "The underlying logic of your emotional-value assessment module is built on an assumed 'rational optimal solution.' That may be efficient in most cases, but human emotional decision-making — especially under extreme stress or intense attachment — is often irrational, even self-contradictory. Your model flags that portion of the data as 'noise' or 'to be corrected,' and that risks losing the most subtle, and most essential, part of understanding human feeling."

Bai Wei responded at once: "Boss Zhao is right. That's a current limitation of our framework, and one of the key reasons we're seeking this partnership. We've come to recognize the boundaries of a purely rational framework. We're hoping to use the Genesis Engine's stronger contextual understanding, empathy simulation, and creative reasoning to process that 'noise' — to extract deeper patterns from it, and even to feed back and refine and enrich our own assessment logic."

Yao Yuan had been watching the fusion of the data and the exchange between the two in silence. Now she spoke. "Boss Zhao, this is exactly why I say we don't just need a smarter chatbot. What we need is a partner that can handle 'contradiction,' understand 'the irrational,' and even generate 'creative strategies' that go beyond conventional thinking within emotional dilemmas that look unsolvable. That asks an AI to have not only logic but — in some sense — 'intuition' or 'inspiration.'"

Zhao Yiming glanced at Yao Yuan, a very faint light, almost like appreciation, crossing his eyes. "Well put. In computational terms, 'intuition' and 'inspiration' can be understood as the 'emergent' capabilities a model produces on the foundation of massive-scale training and complex pattern recognition. Let's give it a try."

He swept both hands across the console. The blue-gold blended data stream rapidly collapsed and reorganized, condensing before them into an extremely intricate, constantly shifting three-dimensional network diagram. The network was built of countless nodes and connecting lines: the nodes were points of light in different colors and sizes, representing different emotional states, personality traits, and relationship roles, while the lines represented the dynamic associations, influences, and flows of energy between them. This was the dynamic simulation map of "modern urban intimate relationships" generated by the preliminary fusion of the Pretty Big One's core framework with the Genesis Engine.

"This is the current fused model's overall cognitive map of the ten thousand high-conflict case samples you provided." Zhao Yiming's voice carried a researcher's particular excitement. "We can select a single node and explore it in depth. Su Hang — pull a high-difficulty, high-ambiguity live consultation case at random from your test set, anonymize it, and import it."

Su Hang worked quickly. Soon an encrypted case stream was loaded. In the 3D network diagram, a pale-purple node representing the "consulting user" lit up, and the nodes connected to it — representing the "partner," "family of origin," "work pressure," "self-perception," and more — flickered bright and dim in turn, data flowing like blood through the tangle of lines.

"Case background: female user, thirty-two, high income, highly educated, who has recently discovered long-term covert emotional manipulation and financial deception by her partner. But the partner's image in their social circle is impeccable, the user still feels a strong emotional attachment to the partner, and there are complex shared financial interests binding them together. The user's request: to find the 'optimal' solution while protecting her own interests — but inside she's full of contradiction, self-doubt, and emotional rupture." Bai Wei recited the key points of the case quickly.

"A classic case of multi-objective conflict and cognitive dissonance." Zhao Yiming murmured, his fingers flicking in the air to magnify the structure around that pale-purple node. "Engage the 'high-dimensional empathy inference module.' Try to generate strategy paths."

The console gave off a low hum. The 3D network diagram began to spin and shift at high speed; countless possible strategy paths radiated outward from the central node as beams of light, extending and interweaving, then colliding, annihilating, and recombining at certain nodes. This was no simple decision tree but the inference of an extraordinarily complex dynamic system — one that simulated emotion, reason, social relationships, interest calculations, and even random perturbations (representing uncertainty).

Yao Yuan watched the dazzling dance of light with bated breath. She could follow part of it — the paths labeled "gather evidence," "legal consultation," "financial separation" were clear and direct. But more of the paths were blurred, roundabout, even seemingly self-contradictory. Some, after extending for a while, would loop back, curve away, and link to nodes you'd never have expected — things like "provoke the partner's guilt but control the degree," "maintain appearances for now to win outside allies," "use social pressure to apply reverse leverage," and so on.

"The model is trying to understand and simulate the user's true, possibly unspoken, multiple objectives: she wants to protect her interests but also longs for some form of relationship 'correction' or her partner's 'repentance'; she wants to punish him yet fears loneliness and the collapse of their social image; she wants to act decisively yet is held back by lingering feeling." Zhao Yiming explained, his eyes tracking the shifting paths. "It's exploring the combined 'emotion-interest' utility value of every possible combination. Interesting."

Suddenly, a faint, almost transparent pale-gold path lit up at the edge of the intricate network. It led toward none of the usual "resolution" nodes but wound instead toward a dark node that seemed to have nothing to do with the core conflict — one labeled "user's unfulfilled childhood wishes / latent self." The moment it appeared, the energy flow of the entire network seemed to bend subtly.

"What's that?" Yao Yuan asked instinctively.

Zhao Yiming's gaze sharpened. He moved quickly to highlight and magnify the pale-gold path. At its end lay not a concrete strategic recommendation but a series of abstract symbols and constantly shifting imagery, seeming to point toward a deeper possibility — about reshaping the self and awakening an inner strength.

"The model has — emerged a direction of advice that lies outside the framework we set." Zhao Yiming's voice carried a trace of uncertain wonder. "It didn't directly resolve the surface conflict. Instead it tried to guide the user to shift her attention and energy, in part, away from the dimension of 'how do I change the other person / handle the relationship' and toward the more fundamental dimension of 'how do I rebuild a stronger, more complete sense of self.' It inferred that when the user's inner core undergoes a kind of qualitative change, the path to resolving the external predicament will surface on its own — and may even 'dissolve' in unexpected ways."

Bai Wei drew a sharp breath. "This — this is a bit like the approach in deep psychotherapy to working with 'repetition compulsion' and 'internal object relations.' But our framework never explicitly coded that logic! How did it —"

At that very moment, the anomaly struck.

The pale-gold path — the very embodiment of the model's "emergent" capability — reached a critical point and suddenly blazed with light, as if it had tripped some hidden switch. The entire Mirror Room shuddered violently. It was not a physical tremor but a distortion of the space itself, every screen, every play of light and shadow rippling eerily like the surface of water.

Inside the spherical space, the hexagonal screens that had been displaying stars and data all turned in an instant into flickering, jittering static, swarming with garbled code. Then the static spun and collapsed wildly, as if drawn by an invisible black hole, rushing to condense and implode toward the center of the space — toward the end of that glowing pale-gold path.

"Warning! Unknown data disturbance! High-dimensional link unstable! Logic-layer entropy surging!" On the console, glaring red warning text scrolled madly alongside a piercing alarm, setting the heart pounding.

Zhao Yiming's face changed in an instant, the eyes behind his lenses sharp as knives. His hands flew across the console fast enough to leave afterimages, commands falling like a downpour. "Su Hang! Force-disconnect the external high-dimensional data feed, now! Physical cutoff! Bai Wei — initiate the 'Bulwark' emergency protocol, triple-lock the core emotional-logic unit! Boss Yao, please step back at once, get to the safe zone!"

His voice stayed calm, but the speed of his speech betrayed how grave the situation was. And yet the data's collapse and backlash came too fast, too uncannily. A low hum rose — *bzzz* — seeming to pierce straight through the eardrums, yet it came from no speaker; it acted directly on the nerve endings. The orb of light, where the garbled code and pale-gold glow had condensed to a peak, did not explode. Instead, like a punctured bubble, it silently collapsed and dimmed, finally fading to an empty pitch-black that scattered across a portion of the screens. The pale-gold path broke off entirely and vanished.

The first deliberate attempt at compatibility and deep activation was declared a failure.

The lights in the Mirror Room returned to normal, but the air carried the faint scorched smell of overloaded data (perhaps only in the mind) and a brief dead silence. Zhao Yiming pressed his lips tight, his gaze locked on the disordered waveforms and error logs lingering on the main screen. Behind him his technical team had already sprung into motion, faces grim as they began checking the various raw-data interfaces and tracing the possible damage from that abnormal high-frequency surge — low-level logic corruption, physical-circuit overload, and the like. The clatter of keyboards and the low, rapid exchange of commands rose again, the atmosphere tense.

Yao Yuan had retreated cleanly to the safe zone by the wall the moment Zhao Yiming spoke. There was little panic on her face now — more a focused observation and thought. Her team members had gathered around her with practiced discipline, not disturbing the others, simply recording every anomaly they had observed.

"My apologies, Boss Yao." Zhao Yiming drew a deep breath, turning away from the console for the moment and rubbing his brow as he spoke to her. "We hit an unexpected data turbulence — the underlying compatibility is even thornier than we anticipated. We need to troubleshoot and repair it urgently. I'm afraid we'll have to suspend the demonstration for now."

Yao Yuan nodded to show she fully understood, her tone even. "Safety first; data integrity takes priority. Boss Zhao, you handle it — we can wait."

"Thank you for understanding. Xiao Chen —" Zhao Yiming signaled to the assistant beside him. "Take Boss Yao and her team to Conference Room Two to rest, and provide any support they need."

"Of course, Boss Zhao. This way, everyone, please."

Yao Yuan and her group were politely shown to another spare conference room to wait. Over the next hour, hurried footsteps and faint technical discussion drifted in from the corridor outside, but on the whole things stayed orderly. Yao Yuan used the time to review everything they'd just seen with her team in low voices, noting down the key questions and observations.

About an hour later, Zhao Yiming walked into Conference Room Two, looking somewhat tired but with eyes still clear. He gave a brief account of the situation: the preliminary judgment was that some segment of "non-standard emotional mapping" in the external high-dimensional data feed had, in attempting to couple deeply with the Pretty Big One's core model, triggered a chain reaction of underlying logic conflicts — something like "immune rejection," but far more violent at the data level. The physical circuits and base architecture had checked out fine, but some of the core model's emotional weighting parameters needed recalibration and reinforcement, and a stricter "emotional firewall" protocol had to be established.

"Forgive the spectacle," Zhao Yiming said, tugging up the corner of his mouth in a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "On the frontier, accidents are the norm. But we've captured crucial failure data — and that's worth more than a hundred smooth demonstrations."

Yao Yuan agreed. "Indeed. Failure defines the boundary. So perhaps we set the deep demonstration aside for now and, working from the current stable version, have a more practical conversation about the Pretty Big One Emotional AI model — the specific investment share in our plan, a phased implementation and rollout path, a data-sharing and privacy-compliance framework, and a detailed scheme for adapting it to commercial application scenarios?"

Zhao Yiming readily agreed. "That's exactly what I had in mind."

The meeting that followed moved at a tight, practical pace. Both teams set aside the earlier incident and focused on concrete terms of cooperation, technical metrics, timelines, resource commitments, and contingency plans. Yao Yuan was thoroughly prepared, with a deep grasp of both business and technology; her questions cut to the heart of the matter, and in negotiation she held firm on core interests while staying flexible. Zhao Yiming, for his part, showed a commercial acuity beyond his role as a technical leader — yielding nothing on the key points, yet willing to seek the optimal solution wherever there was room to maneuver.

By the time the meeting ended, it was past noon. Zhao Yiming offered to host Yao Yuan's team for a simple lunch in the company cafeteria. The space was modern and bright, the food healthy and abundant, the mood far more relaxed than in the conference room.

Over the meal, the conversation drifted by chance to the company's name. Yao Yuan dabbed at the corner of her mouth with a napkin and smiled. "Boss Zhao, I've had a small curiosity all along. Your company is named 'Western World' — was the inspiration the American series of the same name? What made you choose that name back then? It's memorable, but also — thought-provoking."

Zhao Yiming set down his spoon, a sharp yet keenly interested light crossing his eyes; he seemed glad to talk about it. "That's right, the inspiration did come from that show. But not out of worship or full agreement with the world it depicts. Naming it 'Western World' is more a warning and a form of self-examination. One of that show's central themes is what happens when artificial beings grow complex enough to possess memory, emotion, even the experience of pain — how their relationship with their 'creators' will evolve. Tools? Partners? Or potential 'others,' even threats?"

He paused, then went on. "When we study AI — especially when we explore affective computing and higher cognition — we can't dodge these ultimate ethical questions. The name 'Western World' is a constant reminder of where the boundaries of technology lie, and how heavy the ethical responsibility of creation is. We're not trying to create an 'awakened mainframe' that turns on humanity. We're trying to explore the possibility of AI assisting, augmenting, even understanding human emotion — but only on the premise that we fully understand it and can set absolute safety boundaries. It's like walking on thin ice, but it's worth exploring."

Yao Yuan nodded thoughtfully and picked up the thread. "I watched that show. What stayed with me most was how the 'hosts' — through countless loops, with their memories wiped and yet leaving fragments behind — gradually developed a sense of 'unease,' and then began to ask after the 'truth.' That actually touches on something core: if we give an AI the capacity for memory and emotional simulation — even just simulation — could the accumulation of that 'experience' produce, deep in the algorithm, some 'trace' or 'tendency' that can't simply be reset to zero? Just like a moment ago — when your model produced that violent rejection while trying to take on high-dimensional data, was that, in some sense, also a kind of brush against the boundary of 'unease,' a reaction to it?"

Zhao Yiming looked at Yao Yuan with still more appreciation in his eyes. "A very incisive read, Boss Yao. That's precisely one of the core challenges of our research. The 'trace' theory is a frontier debate in the field right now. Part of our work is the attempt to define, measure, and control that very 'trace.' Today's accident, setback though it was, also confirmed the existence of certain model boundaries. We need a more precise 'scalpel' and 'safety valve.'" He smiled, with the frankness and ambition particular to a technical mind. "Fear comes from the unknown. And we are trying to turn the unknown into the known and the controllable. That may be harder than the story on the screen — but also more interesting, don't you think?"

The lunch drew to a close amid this faintly philosophical and technical exchange. Yao Yuan glanced at the time and politely took her leave. Zhao Yiming saw her to the elevator himself.

"Today's exchange was extremely valuable — both the successful parts and the — accidental ones." Yao Yuan shook hands with Zhao Yiming in farewell. "I look forward to the detailed plan and test data to come."

"And I look forward to a deeper partnership with your side," Zhao Yiming said, gripping her hand firmly. "I made a spectacle of myself today, Boss Yao. Next time, we'll be better prepared."

The elevator doors slid slowly shut, sealing off the futuristic, faintly cold space of "Western World." Yao Yuan leaned against the inner wall of the car and let out a soft breath. In her mind, the heart-stopping scene of the morning's data collapse interwove with the lunchtime conversation about AI awakening and ethics, giving her a more three-dimensional — and more challenging — picture of Zhao Yiming and the field he pursued.

And in her handbag, the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror from another dimension seemed, on this day saturated with cutting-edge technology and speculation about the future, all the more mysterious and out of place. This afternoon, she had to arrange to meet Shuai Hongqiang. The mirror he had given her, the "data turbulence" she'd witnessed today, the "trace" and "boundary" Zhao Yiming spoke of — could there be some unimaginable connection among them all?

The elevator descended, the numbers ticking by, carrying her back to the solid ground of reality. But her thoughts seemed to hover in some more complex dimension — a place where the ancient bronze mirror and the code of the future were quietly entering into some unknown resonance.

========================================

Chapter 23 — Shuai Hongqiang Unravels the Riddle of the Bronze Mirror for Yao Yuan — The Mirror Pact
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640270.html

Shuai Hongqiang Unravels the Riddle of the Bronze Mirror for Yao Yuan — The Mirror Pact
On Shuai Hongqiang's side, the air had a different texture altogether.
The eight-million bank loan hung over his head like a ticking clock, every second piling on more weight. The fifteen million in project payments the city government still owed him was, right now, the only thing that could turn the situation around. Last Saturday Ji Wenshan had brought in an unexpected windfall of five hundred thousand, but against an eight-million bank loan, that was a drop in the bucket.
For this lifeline of cash, he had set up a lunch first thing this morning. The other party was a middleman who supposedly "had some connections," someone who could grease the wheels with the government and chase down that bad debt.
Right now he was driving to the meeting spot.
His phone buzzed once on the passenger seat. WeChat. He grabbed it at a red light and glanced down.
It was from Lin Wan, brief and to the point: "Wenyao's parent-teacher meeting tonight, six thirty, don't forget." A classroom number followed.
His thumb moved, and he sent back a single word: "Got it."
He scrolled up. There were a few more unread messages. One was a repayment reminder from the bank app. One was from the boss of a materials supplier he'd worked with before, fishing in a roundabout way: "Any movement on those project payments the government owes you?" And one was a debt-collection form letter, "politely" reminding him that his debt was overdue, along with the relevant legal consequences.
He swiped them all away without opening a single one.
He pushed the car door open and walked into the elevator. The mirror in the car gleamed bright enough to see himself in: a navy cashmere overcoat over a crisp, well-pressed shirt, hair neatly trimmed, chin cleanly shaved. This was the "basic image management" Lin Wan demanded. She'd told him, "However hard things get, a man can't let his appearance fall apart first." He'd gone along with it, even if sometimes it felt like painting the outer walls of a house already hollowed out by termites inside.
The elevator opened straight onto the ground-floor lobby. The ceiling was nearly ten meters high, the cold marble floor polished smooth as a mirror, a huge abstract art installation hanging down from above. They'd booked a private room at a Japanese restaurant on the second floor. A place like this was quiet, private, convenient for talking business — and a single meal cost a small fortune.
A waiter led him into the private room. The other party hadn't arrived yet. He took off his coat, sat down, and looked out the window. From this angle he could just see the office tower across the Yellow River where Yao Yuan worked. In the winter afternoon sun, its glass curtain wall reflected a cold, sharp light, like a vast, unfeeling slab of blue ice. The Yellow River, winding through the city, was on this clear afternoon nothing but a quiet, gray-yellow ribbon, without a glimmer.
He thought of the song he'd heard while parking just now. The lyrics surfaced like a ghost: "...the old dog dares not bark, sleepless and tireless, working for a mouthful of broth..."
He gave a self-mocking tug at the corner of his mouth. Wasn't he, right now, running himself ragged for a mouthful of "broth"? Begging people, forcing smiles, cashing in the last of his connections and his pride for a sliver of hope at getting that debt back. That fifteen million had become the lifeline he pinned his entire comeback on — and also the boulder pinning him down in this swamp.
While he waited, the numbers churned through his head unbidden:
Owed to the bank: 8 million (the penalty interest snowballing every month)
Owed to him: 15 million (overdue nearly three years, with no end in sight)
Fixed assets (what's left): one apartment he lived in (worth about 4 million when bought, now down by half, in Lin Wan's name, unmortgaged), one old BMW X6.
Household monthly cash flow: mainly from Lin Wan's livestreaming and product-sales income (about thirty to fifty thousand, unstable), covering household expenses, the child's education, basic socializing. His own income had all but dried up.
Personal account: usable funds kept at a few tens of thousands year-round, a hedge against the risk of being frozen at any moment.
This was the whole of his economic map. A dangerous, extremely fragile balance. The bank's collection was the open spear, the government's arrears the hidden arrow. Lin Wan's income was the cloth that kept this household looking calm on the surface, covering the riddled, holey truth beneath.
He had it far better than someone truly at the bottom — a roof, hot meals, his child's future not yet ruined. But he had also lost forever the bold sweep he once had, waking every day to calculation and anxiety, falling asleep with his dignity worn raw again and again. Like an insect trapped in amber, frozen in the posture of flight, yet long since unable to move.
"Boss Shuai! So sorry to keep you waiting, so sorry!"
The private-room door slid open and a middle-aged man in a suit, his face heaped with smiles, walked in, his booming voice shattering Shuai Hongqiang's thoughts.
Shuai Hongqiang stood up at once, his face already shifting in an instant into a flawless, faintly warm smile, hand extended.
"Director Li, don't mention it, I just got here myself. Please, sit."
Pleasantries, sitting down, ordering. The sake was warmed, the sashimi platter arranged exquisite as a painting. The man talked at length, thumping his chest, spinning the same old loops about "people up top," "knowing the process," "just needing some funds to smooth things over."
Shuai Hongqiang smiled, played along, raised his cup in toast. But the ledger in his heart coldly turned to another page: this round of "smoothing things over" would be another sizable outlay, and whether it would come to anything was still anyone's guess. Right now he was like a gambler nearly out of chips — even at the glimpse of the faintest flicker of light, he had to push in what little remained, betting on a comeback as insubstantial as smoke. Sunlight came through the fine-latticed wooden window of the Japanese restaurant, casting crisscrossing patterns of light over the back of his hand. Half in the bright warm sun, half in deep shadow.
He raised the small sake cup, gestured to the "Director Li" across from him, and drank it down in one. The liquor was warm and smooth as it slid down his throat, yet he couldn't taste a thing.
Just as Shuai Hongqiang was propping up his composure, carefully maneuvering around "Director Li," his phone chimed. His heart sank; he instinctively assumed it was another debt-collection message or some other junk text. Still, he set down his chopsticks, picked up the phone, and ran his eyes across the screen —
His movement froze in an instant.
It was a deposit notice from the bank. The Municipal Finance Budget Settlement Center had transferred it in at 11 a.m., amount: 10 million. Because it was a large transaction, the credit had been delayed, and only now had it appeared before his eyes.
A barely perceptible tremor ran through Shuai Hongqiang. A tangle of feelings he couldn't name surged up — absurdity, irony, an unbelievable lightness, and a deeper bewilderment. Here he was, treading carefully, burning money, trying to pry open that tightly shut door — while behind that door, the money had already leaked out, more than half of it, without a sound.
Then a thought slammed into his mind: the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror. Could this be no accident, but rather Yao Yuan once again traveling back through time, the good fortune transformed from the Primordial Purple Energy she'd brought back?
The ringing of the phone pulled him back from his churning thoughts. The name "Yao Yuan" flashed on the screen. He answered, and her voice came through, asking to meet him at her office this afternoon, to talk about the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror he'd given her.
After hanging up, Shuai Hongqiang looked back at the still-gushing Director Li, his frame of mind quietly altered. He no longer eagerly echoed and pushed things along; his manner stayed polite but turned noticeably cooler. The meal was eaten, the wine drunk, and this thread couldn't be cut off entirely — after all, there were still five million to go. He kept up the basic appearances and finally, with a string of empty, going-through-the-motions courtesies, brought this costly lunch to a close.
Having seen Director Li and his group off, Shuai Hongqiang didn't go home. He got straight into the car, started it, and drove across the bridge spanning the Yellow River. Outside the window the murky river water rolled eastward, while in his mind that plain, ancient bronze mirror seemed to cast a faint, elusive light up from the riverbed.
The car came to a stop below Yao Yuan's company building, tires crunching over the withered yellow plane-tree leaves with a fine, brittle sound. Shuai Hongqiang pushed the door open and got out; a cold wind carrying the dry, biting chill peculiar to a northern November rushed at his face. He looked up at the towering glass-curtain-walled building. The gray-white sky spread across the sharp-edged façade, the shadows of a few bare branches slicing over the cold-toned glass surface.
The elevator rose smoothly to the 38th floor. Unlike last time's inquiries and waiting, Yao Yuan seemed to have given instructions ahead. This time, the moment the receptionist heard "here to see Boss Yao," she put on a professional smile and led him deep down the corridor — to Yao Yuan's office. He pushed open the well-soundproofed, light-gray grained wood door — Yao Yuan stood with her back to the door, before the full wall of floor-to-ceiling windows. The winter afternoon sun poured in, gilding her silhouette with a faint gold. Hearing the sound, she turned around.
Her gaze first landed on his face, lingered a moment, then moved away — drifting naturally to the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror at the center of the desk.
The office was modern minimalist, in tones of black, white, and gray, the heating running soundlessly, the room as warm as spring. And that bronze mirror sat quietly on the matte black tempered-glass desktop, its dark blue-green patina dim, the Kui-dragon cloud pattern winding under the ceiling light, as if carrying its own veil of ancient, unshakeable mist. Two eras stood facing off here, out of joint.
Yao Yuan looked at him for a good while.
The memories brought back by yesterday's time-travel hadn't yet receded; those emotions — fierce, tender, aching, scalding — surged anew through her veins. She looked at him, the forty-six-year-old Shuai Hongqiang, in his navy overcoat, the shirt collar at the neckline of his sweater clean and flat. For a hazy moment she couldn't tell whether the man before her was the one who, years ago, had unwound his scarf in the snow to wrap her up, cherishing and doting on her — or the one who, just four days ago, had reappeared after eight years seeking a business partnership.
Time was clearly the present, but her heart was sunk in the past.
"Yuan—" Shuai Hongqiang spoke first. A "Yuan'er" nearly slipped out; he caught himself, paused, and changed it. "Yao Yuan. These past few days — have you been having very strange dreams?"
Yao Yuan gathered herself, her gaze returning to scrutinize his face.
"Shuai Hongqiang," she said, her voice calm yet thin as a blade's edge, "the years we were together, I can honestly say I never did you wrong. After we broke up, I never clung on either. Eight years later you came to me to talk business — that was work, and I took it. Now—"
She walked to the desk, her fingers lightly brushing the mirror's cold edge.
"Be honest. What exactly is strange about this mirror? Why did you give it to me?" She raised her eyes, looking straight at him. "To harm me?"
"No! Absolutely not!" Shuai Hongqiang stepped quickly forward, then forced himself to a hard stop two paces from her, his voice edged with agitation. "How could I possibly want to harm you? I... didn't even know the mirror's secret myself, at first."
He drew a deep breath, his gaze also falling on the bronze mirror.
"After I gave you the mirror that day, I went home and didn't sleep all night. Suddenly I remembered something the old appraiser at the auction house had said back then — at the time I just thought he was being deliberately mysterious, and didn't take it to heart." Shuai Hongqiang knit his brows, sinking into memory. "He said the mirror's age was hard to pin down, its aura was 'heavy,' a little uncanny. They couldn't test out a specific year. If you kept it for yourself, no harm; but if you gave it to someone, especially someone close to you, you'd have to... think it over. Something about 'the collector must be sincere of heart; all things have a spirit.'"
Shuai Hongqiang did not mention the faint, opportunistic hope of changing his luck that had stirred in him when he gave her the mirror.
Yao Yuan listened quietly, her fingertip sliding unconsciously along the rim.
"The more I thought about it, the more wrong it felt. I was afraid there really was some taboo, something bad for you. First thing the next morning I went to the antiques market to find that old master." Shuai Hongqiang looked at her, his eyes complicated. "And that's when I got the mirror's origin out of him."
"What origin?" Yao Yuan pressed.
"The master said this mirror was forged by a Yin-Yang adept of the Tang dynasty, who refined a single strand of Primordial Purple Energy from the moment heaven and earth first opened, using a secret method. Hidden within the Kui-dragon cloud-and-thunder pattern on the back is a 'Time-Reversal Talisman.'" Shuai Hongqiang spoke each word distinctly. "And so, it can let a person — travel back through time."
The air went abruptly still.
The sound of the wind outside was sealed off by the double glazing into a vague background. Yao Yuan's fingers stopped on the bronze mirror, tightening slightly.
"...Time-travel?" she repeated, her voice very soft.
"Yes. But there are conditions." Shuai Hongqiang paused. "Not just anyone holding the mirror can use it. Take me — I kept it in hand for eight years, and to me it was never anything but an ordinary old mirror."
"What conditions?"
"Do you remember?" Shuai Hongqiang looked at her. "Many years ago, at the antiques market, there was an old Daoist who chased after you, insisting on reading your fate, saying you carried the great destiny of the 'Nine Purple Departing Fire'?"
A flicker stirred in Yao Yuan's eyes. How could she not remember — hadn't she, just yesterday, watched the whole thing again with her own eyes: the twenty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan chased down by an old Daoist for a fortune-telling? At the thought, she couldn't help a low laugh.
"What he said was true." Shuai Hongqiang lowered his voice, as if afraid of disturbing something. "The one who can activate this bronze mirror must be a person carrying great fortune. Only such a person can forge a 'Mirror Pact' with the mirror. And at certain specific times... trigger the time-travel."
He got this far, then suddenly stopped.
There was a flash of hesitation. Extremely brief, so brief it was nearly impossible to notice. But Yao Yuan saw it — his eyes wavered for a moment, his throat moved slightly, as if he had swallowed back some words.
(He's hiding something.)
The thought surfaced, clear and cold. Yao Yuan didn't press right away; she only lowered her eyes to the bronze mirror. Its surface was dim and clouded, casting no clear reflection, only the faint blur of her own outline, and behind it, the face of Shuai Hongqiang on the verge of speaking and stopping.
The office was so still you could hear the faint sound of the heating vents.
"So," Yao Yuan slowly raised her eyes, her gaze placid and unrippled, "I wasn't dreaming. I really did... go back?"
"Yes." Shuai Hongqiang nodded, then added, "But the master also said time-travel isn't without a price. Reversing time stirs up one's fate, and all the more so for someone carrying great fortune. Each trip drains the spirit — you need to be careful to rest and recover."
He said it earnestly, with genuine concern in his eyes.
Yet that flicker of wrongness in Yao Yuan's heart wouldn't go away.
(If it's only draining the spirit, what was he hesitating over just now?)
(What is it he doesn't dare to say?)
She walked to the window, her back to him, looking down at the pedestrians on the street below, bundled tight in their coats. Withered leaves spun in the wind.
"Why did you give me this mirror?" she asked, her voice floating in the glass's reflection.
Behind her, silence for a moment.
"The first time I saw it, all those years ago, I just felt you'd like it." Shuai Hongqiang's voice came rough. "Later... we parted, and I kept it put away. When we met again, giving it to you felt like returning it to its rightful owner. I just never expected—"
He stopped.
Yao Yuan turned around. Backlit by the cold, indifferent winter light outside the window, her expression couldn't quite be made out.
"Shuai Hongqiang," she said, "some things — once they're past, they're past. I'll keep the mirror, since it's fated to be with me. As for the time-travel..."
She paused, a faint curve, of an indefinable meaning, tugging at the corner of her lips.
"Let's just call it a very vivid dream. From now on, I won't touch it again."
Shuai Hongqiang opened his mouth, as if he wanted to say something, but in the end only nodded. "...All right. As long as you're safe."
He stood there a while longer, his gaze lingering on her face for a moment, sweeping over the bronze mirror, before he said in a low voice, "Then I'll go now. You... take good care of yourself."
The door closed softly, the latch giving a faint click.
Yao Yuan still stood at the window, watching the distant Yellow River flow soundlessly by.
She turned, walked back to the desk, and bent her head to gaze at the bronze mirror.
Her fingertip brushed across the cold surface, the Kui-dragon cloud-and-thunder pattern winding and rising under her touch. For a hazy moment, she seemed to be back at the mouth of that little Jiangnan alley she'd once passed through on a trip — the plum-rain season, the damp air, the bluestone-paved road, that man standing beneath the camphor tree, smiling and beckoning to her.
(Shuai Hongqiang, what exactly were you about to say just now that you didn't say?)
(Behind this "Time-Reversal Talisman" — is it really as simple as just "draining the spirit"?)
(The part you hid... what is it?)
She pulled open the drawer, took out the wooden box, and carefully placed the bronze mirror inside, face down, the ancient patterns on its back looking all the deeper against the dark velvet.
Just as she was about to close the lid, her hand suddenly stopped.
(That's not right.)
(He said "only one carrying great fortune can forge a Mirror Pact.")
(So if I've already forged a Mirror Pact...)
(How is this pact undone?)
(Why didn't he mention it?)
Yao Yuan's fingers hovered above the box, her fingertips going faintly cold. She looked at the bronze mirror again, and those winding patterns no longer seemed mere quaint ornament — more like some silent incantation, some pact binding her together with the unknown.
The heating in the office was strong, yet she suddenly felt a little cold.
Downstairs, Shuai Hongqiang got into the driver's seat and shut the car door, but didn't start the engine right away.
He gripped the steering wheel with both hands, the backs of his hands going white with the force, veins faintly bulging. The warmth from the air-conditioning still lingered in the car, yet he felt a chill crawl up his spine.
(I didn't tell her everything.)
(The master said what's hidden in the Kui-dragon cloud-and-thunder pattern isn't only the "Time-Reversal Talisman.")
(There's also a "Fortune-Turning Talisman" — that's the second talisman, hidden in the reverse-scale pattern. Each time one carrying great fortune travels back and stirs up their own fate, a portion of that fortune flows through the Mirror Pact, silently... blessing the one who gave the mirror.)
(The giver of the mirror bears its fortune; the traveler drains their spirit. One waxes as the other wanes — it's called "borrowing fortune.")
Shuai Hongqiang squeezed his eyes shut and pressed his forehead against the steering wheel.
That day in the dim back room of the antiques city, the old master's voice had been aged and heavy: "This mirror is uncanny precisely because it recognizes both its owner and its pact. You gave it to her — that forged a pact of giving. If she uses the mirror to reverse time, that forges a pact of fortune-turning. The former is a bond of fate; the latter is a debt. She loses spirit and lifespan; you gain fortune and opportunity. This debt... can never be repaid."
"Is there a way to undo it?" he'd asked then, his voice dry.
The old master shook his head. "The Mirror Pact is already forged. To forcibly take it back or destroy it may bring disaster. Especially for the one holding the mirror — if her 'spirit-mind' is in the midst of a reversal, rashly moving the mirror could leave her consciousness unable to return properly to its place. Unless she perceives it herself and finds a way to break the pact — but that method was lost to time long ago. For now, you might first watch quietly how things unfold, keep a close eye on her state. If she mentions 'reversal' more and more often, or shows any mental abnormality, you must find her at once and bring her to see me. Perhaps... there may be some other remedy, but it will need long and careful thought."
Shuai Hongqiang opened his eyes, the rims red.
(I didn't mean to harm her.)
(I just... didn't tell her everything.)
(Maybe she won't use the mirror again. The old master said, as long as the reversal isn't triggered, the Fortune-Turning Talisman won't activate, and she won't keep draining herself...)
The phone suddenly buzzed; his wife's name lit up on the screen. He stared at those two characters for a few seconds, and in the end pressed the call away and started the car.
The sound of the engine was especially jarring in the quiet winter parking lot.
He pulled out of the garage. In the rearview mirror, that glass tower grew smaller and smaller. Behind some window on the 38th floor, Yao Yuan was probably still there, with that mirror.
And he chose to drive the car in another direction — to the Bank of Communications, where there was still an eight-million loan to repay.
Human nature can't withstand the test. Especially for a man who has once grabbed a vine at the cliff's edge — it's very hard to talk himself into letting go.
In the office on the 38th floor, Yao Yuan had not closed that quaint old wooden box.
She took the bronze mirror out again, lifted it before her eyes, and under the ceiling light studied the Kui-dragon cloud-and-thunder pattern closely. The cloud-and-thunder pattern coiled, the dragon's body wound, and at one turning point the pattern seemed especially dense — was that the position of the reverse scale?
Her fingertip brushed across that area; the texture seemed a touch warmer and smoother than the rest, as if it had been rubbed by a hand over many years.
(If there really is a "Time-Reversal Talisman"... where is it hidden?)
(And this inexplicable exhaustion and palpitation these past two days — is it really just the normal aftereffect of time-travel?)
She set the mirror down, walked to the bookshelf, and pulled out a copy of A Study of Tang Dynasty Patterns. She turned to the chapter on the Kui-dragon pattern, those ancient designs stretching across the page, the annotation reading: "The Kui-dragon, a divine beast of antiquity, shaped like an ox, gray-bodied and hornless, with a single foot. Wherever it enters or leaves the water, great wind and rain arise; its light is like the sun and moon, its voice like thunder. It governs time and space, and fortune..."
The phone rang.
It was her assistant. "Boss Yao, the four o'clock meeting with Mingke Capital — would you like to head over now?"
Yao Yuan glanced at the clock on the wall. Three forty-five in the afternoon.
"Push it back half an hour for me," she said. "Also, look someone up for me — eight years ago, the old master in charge of appraising the bronze-mirror category at Jinshi's winter auction. Surname... possibly Chen, very old. Find his contact details and send them to me."
She hung up, took one last look at the bronze mirror, placed it back into the box, and locked it in the drawer.
But the moment the drawer closed, she suddenly felt an inexplicable palpitation, as if something had been tugged at, ever so lightly.
The mirror lay in its box, silent as a riddle.
And the pact was already forged; the threads of fate, quietly winding tight.

========================================

Chapter 24 — Yao Yuan and Yu Hao: Algorithms and a Sincere Heart
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640271.html

The meeting wrapped up just as the sky outside took on that gray-blue tint unique to a winter evening. Yao Yuan rubbed the space between her brows, putting an end to a day of intense mental work. That strange, mystical conversation with Shuai Hongqiang about the bronze mirror still hung over her like a thin haze. She gathered up her files and was getting ready to leave when her phone rang. It was Yu Hao.
"I just got back from Shanghai. I'm right downstairs from your company — wondering if Boss Yao would do me the honor of dinner." The voice on the other end carried a smile and a road-weary, magnetic warmth that chased away a little of her fatigue.
Hearing his voice, she found the corners of her mouth curving up before she realized it. "Sure. I was just thinking I wanted to talk to you. Where to?"
"The usual place — Yellow River Garden. I've already booked a table."
"Great. I'll be right down."
Yu Hao's car was parked at the curb not far from the company exit, and he was standing beside it. When he saw Yao Yuan come out, he took a few quick steps forward to meet her. "Hungry?" he asked with a grin.
At thirty, Yu Hao was a man in his prime. Six feet tall, he carried himself straight as a pine, each step steady and self-assured, with a presence that was impossible to ignore.
His single-lidded eyes were long, narrow, and deep, and beneath that depth ran a keen sharpness. A single glance from him could take in details most people would miss, the light in his gaze seeming to cut through fog straight to the heart of a thing. A high, straight nose lent his face a strong, sculpted line, adding a touch of resolve and decisiveness. His clean, crisp jawline carried an unmistakable firmness that matched the polished air he gave off overall — a handsomeness that was thoroughly modern and intelligent.
Today he wore a dark gray wool overcoat that fell just above the knee, its simple straight cut making him look even taller and leaner. The collar was a folded-lapel design; underneath he had on a white turtleneck cashmere sweater, with black straight-leg jeans below, the lines perfectly straight and the cuffs tucked into black Martin boots. A light gray wool scarf was looped casually twice around his neck, one end left to hang naturally. On his wrist sat a silver-gray mechanical watch with a black crocodile band. He looked every bit the stylish elite — sharp and put-together.
Yao Yuan was caught off guard by how handsome he looked for a moment. She walked toward him with a smile and teased, "I was pretty hungry, actually, but the second I see our dashing Boss Yu my appetite just disappears. Truly a feast for the eyes." Then she burst out laughing, mischievous and loud.
The sudden flirting flustered Yu Hao into a blush, and the tips of his ears stayed red all the way through dinner.
Yellow River Garden sat along the city's riverside scenic belt, famous for its refined fusion cuisine and its up-close view of the Yellow River at night. They walked to their reserved table, and Yu Hao naturally pulled out Yao Yuan's chair, took her coat, and handed it to the waiter — smooth, thoughtful motions. He shrugged off his own coat and draped it over the back of his chair, the turtleneck underneath making the flat line of his shoulders stand out all the more.
Yu Hao sat down, his gaze lingering on her face for a moment, sharp enough to catch a deeper thread of preoccupation in Yao Yuan, something beyond her usual work fatigue. "What's wrong? From the look on your face, did something thorny come up on the project today? Is it the 'Pretty Big One Emotional AI model' progress?"
He could always read the center of gravity of her state at a glance. Yao Yuan picked up the warm barley tea and took a sip, the warmth sliding down her throat. "Yes — and no. A lot happened today, actually. I've been wanting to talk it all through with you."
Yu Hao didn't push. He simply topped off her tea and ordered the dishes to both their tastes. Only once the waiter had gone did he lean forward slightly, elbows on the white tablecloth, hands clasped, settling into the posture of a listener — his gaze gentle and focused.
Outside the window, the winter Yellow River lacked the surging force of summer. Under the dusk and the lights along both banks, it looked like a quiet ribbon of ink-dark silk, flowing slowly eastward. The lights on the far shore scattered like stars, blending into the deep-purple line of the horizon.
The dishes arrived one after another. Yu Hao naturally served her some food and chatted about what he'd seen on his Shanghai trip and the latest industry trends. After a few bites of hot food, the mood loosened, and Yao Yuan finally got to the point.
"This morning, my team and I went to Zhao Yiming's Western World. We imported one of the cases from the 'Pretty Big One AI' emotional core database into the Genesis Engine." Yao Yuan set down her chopsticks, her tone turning serious.
Yu Hao dropped the small talk too, slipping into focused listening mode. "Mm — as I recall, the core is the dynamic emotional map and the multimodal feedback engine. Hit a bottleneck?"
"The 'Pretty Big One' core framework and the 'Genesis Engine' tried to be compatible and deeply activate each other for the first time — and it failed. The biggest challenge right now isn't actually the model itself." Yao Yuan frowned slightly. "It's the boundaries and the ethical presets."
"Oh?" Yu Hao leaned in even further, showing real interest. "Tell me more."
"The higher the model's learning ability and realism, the harder its 'emotional responses' are to tell apart from a real person's. In internal testing, a tiny handful of senior testers have already reported that in certain deep, situational conversations they get an intense feeling of being 'understood,' even 'empathized with.' That's the successful side of it, of course." She paused, lowering her voice. "But the problem that comes with it is this: are the ethical guardrails we've set up actually solid and smart enough? When the model 'understands' a person's loneliness, longing, even pain, and uses that as a basis to 'soothe' or 'guide' them — where's the boundary on that 'guiding'? Its 'empathy' is, at bottom, an algorithm optimizing for patterns, but the user may be investing genuine emotion. There's an asymmetry there, and a potential risk of emotional dependence."
Yu Hao listened in silence, his fingers tapping unconsciously on the tabletop — a habit of his when he was thinking deeply. When Yao Yuan finished, he was quiet for a moment, as if rapidly digesting and integrating the information.
"That's a crucial question, and one that was bound to come up," Yu Hao said, his voice steady and clear, carrying his trademark analytical penetration. "It means 'Pretty Big One AI' has already taken half a step into the blurry zone between a tool and a 'quasi-subject.' Traditional ethical guardrails based on rule-driven prohibition lists definitely won't cut it anymore."
He thought for a moment, then went on. "My suggestion is that before the official release, you have to add an independent 'dynamic ethics assessment module.' This module wouldn't interfere with how the model runs; instead it would monitor metrics in real time — the emotional depth of an interaction, topic sensitivity, user investment level — and build a risk early-warning mechanism. At the same time, the user agreement has to be overhauled. It needs to tell users in the clearest possible way that they're interacting with a highly realistic emotional simulation model, that all of its responses are based on algorithms and data patterns, and that it has no genuine emotion or consciousness. That's not just legal protection — it's a necessary reminder for the user."
Yao Yuan nodded. "Zhao Yiming feels the same way, but the technical implementation and the performance overhead still need to be assessed. There's also the commercial side: over-stressing the 'non-human' attribute might hurt how some users — the ones chasing the ultimate experience — accept the product."
"It's a balance," Yu Hao said, his gaze sharp. "But the bottom line can't be compromised. The commercial value of emotional simulation is enormous — and precisely because of that, its ethical risks and responsibilities are equally enormous. We can make 'safety' and 'transparency' one of the core selling points, drawing in the users who genuinely need 'Pretty Big One AI' and who value their own mental health and sustainable interaction. Short term, it might filter out some extreme demand. Long term, it's the cornerstone of building brand trust and avoiding systemic risk."
He paused and looked at her. "Also — how's the team holding up? Zhao Yiming is a technical genius, but a project like this is a huge test of a core leader's mindset and values. He needs support, and he needs regular ethical review too."
"You could consider bringing in an external advisory committee," Yu Hao suggested, "made up of experts in psychology, ethics, sociology, even philosophy, to periodically review the project's direction. That gives you authoritative backing and forms an institutionalized check and balance."
Their conversation went deep and ran efficiently, from technical details to business strategy, then stretching out to team management and long-term planning. Yu Hao's insights always struck right at the heart of things — strategic in altitude, yet practical in execution. Yao Yuan felt some of the knots she'd been wrestling with alone during the day gradually loosening in an exchange like this, and new lines of thinking beginning to surface.
With the business settled, the mood relaxed. Yu Hao naturally served Yao Yuan another piece of a dish she liked, his tone softening too. "Sounds like you were under a lot of pressure today. Besides the project, is there something else?" He remembered her saying earlier that "a lot happened."
Yao Yuan hesitated. The bronze mirror and all of Shuai Hongqiang's bizarre talk about "time-travel" and "the Mirror Pact" turned over once on the tip of her tongue — and in the end she swallowed it back down. It was too personal, and too… hard to put into the rational language of technology and ethics they'd just been discussing. She didn't want to ruin the clear train of thought that had formed after their professional discussion, and for now she didn't want to bring that vague, unsettling daze into her time with Yu Hao.
"It's nothing — just all of it piling up at once, it's a bit draining." She smiled and changed the subject. "It's good the Shanghai project went smoothly. You must have gotten a lot out of this trip?"
Yu Hao noticed her brief hesitation and the way she'd steered the conversation away, but he didn't press. He simply picked up her thread and talked about what he'd seen in Shanghai, his gaze resting gently on her face, full of understanding and tolerance.
Dinner ended on a warm, easy note. As they paid and left, the wind by the river was bitterly cold, carrying the dry chill unique to the Yellow River in winter. Yu Hao carefully tugged the collar of her coat up snug and slipped an arm naturally around her shoulders to shield her from the wind. The thoughtful gesture sent a wave of warmth through her.
Settling into the warm car, Yao Yuan leaned back against the seat and watched the city neon flash backward outside the window, the Yellow River dark and dim in the distance. The conversation with Yu Hao had helped her sort out several key threads on the "Pretty Big One AI" project, but the riddle that Shuai Hongqiang and that bronze mirror had brought still lingered, like the deep night beyond the glass, hovering in some corner of her heart.
