She turned her head to look at Yu Hao, focused on driving, the lines of his profile shifting between light and shadow under the streetlamps — steady and dependable. At least on the level of work and reality, she had a partner she could trust, a strategic teammate like this. As for that more private, more bizarre puzzle… she needed time, alone, to think it through.
The car merged smoothly into the night traffic and headed for her apartment. Outside the windows the city blazed with light, and in Yao Yuan's heart the reasoning about tech ethics and the doubts about the ancient bronze mirror wove together — like the waters of the Yellow River and the lights along its banks — into an inner landscape of uneven light and dark.

========================================

Chapter 25 — The Right to Narrate a Marriage
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640272.html

The Right to Narrate a Marriage
Once Shuai Hongqiang had finished settling the repayment terms with the bank, he hurried off to his son Wenyao's parent-teacher conference. Afterward he took the boy out for dinner, and by the time they got home, Lin Wan had already started tonight's livestream.

The set hadn't changed much. The ring light still glowed amber, falling soft and hazy over Lin Wan. Behind her, two simple decorative paintings hung on a plain cream-colored wall. Below them, a walnut-toned bookshelf that took up nearly the whole wall held neat rows of gilt-titled spines. Beyond the ones from last time — psychology, the rebuilding of intimacy, emotional value and the rational partner — there was now a new addition: a copy of *Game Theory*. They were a stage set, but also a silent declaration: the conversation here would rest on a certain kind of "reason" and "wisdom."

On the desk, in the corner the camera could catch, sat a retro lamp with a brass base and a green glass shade, a scented candle burning quietly, a glass of water, an open leather notebook, and an elegant fountain pen. The livestream camera faced Lin Wan head-on; beside it, a propped-up tablet streamed a fast scroll of fan comments and colorful danmu across the public screen. Lin Wan looked steadily into the camera, her shoulders dropped two centimeters lower, which made her whole presence seem calm and gentle. Her lips curved into a smile of precise proportions — warm enough to feel close, never cloyingly sweet. Her voice came down half a tone, her pace slowed, like a knowing older sister on a late-night radio show, carrying a warm magnetism and an unshakable certainty:

"Good evening, everyone, and welcome to 'A Gentle Crossing.' I'm Lin Wan." She opened with a comfortable familiarity and sincerity. "Tonight, let's go on talking about marriage — the longest and most intimate relationship of our lives."

The danmu started pouring in:
[Wanwan's on time]: Here, here! Right on schedule!
[Can't wait for tonight's topic]: I couldn't sleep after last time. What are we talking about today?
[Came with a question]: Wanwan, how do you tell whether a partner is doing PUA on you?

"When you talk about marriage, you can't avoid talking about love. They say marriage is the grave of love — but without the grave that is marriage, love would have nowhere to be buried at all." Lin Wan paused slightly, her gaze sweeping over the camera. "So, sisters — is there still a dead love buried somewhere in your marriage? But after several decades of living, do we actually understand love? Is the love we know real love? And if it isn't, then what does real love look like?"

"My answer is this," she said, leaning forward a little, her voice clear and firm, "love is a limited-edition firework — one that takes enormous wisdom to experience safely."

The danmu lit up instantly:
[Limited-edition firework]: That metaphor is so beautiful, and so sad.
[Takes wisdom]: So true. Love without brains dies fast.
[Fireworks cool quickly]: So most people's love is just an illusion?

"Sisters, what's your answer? Tell me in the comments." Lin Wan took a sip of water, her expression turning gentle and thoughtful.

"The old regulars in my room already know my marriage story, but let me give a quick recap for the friends who've just joined. My husband is ten years older than me; I've always called him Brother Qiang. We've been married seven years and have an adorable six-year-old son. When we got married, everyone thought we were a perfect match — a husband ten years older, mature and steady, and me, gentle and clever. I used to be a woman who truly believed in love, in promises, in 'forever.' I even 'succeeded' for a few years — until my son was two, when I discovered the problems in my marriage. After a string of painful spiritual growth and transformation, I worked out a method for dealing with the power game inside a marriage and an emotional relationship, and I built a 'systematic survival strategy for marriage.' Of course, I've kept refining and improving it the whole way. Following that strategy, my relationship with Brother Qiang has worked through constant adjustment and correction to reach where it is today."

The danmu scrolled by:
[New fan checking in]: A friend talked me into coming. Excited!
[Old fan in tears]: I've followed Wanwan's whole journey. It's like binging a real-life drama.
[Systematic survival strategy]: Can someone summarize this for the class?

"Friends who caught my last stream should remember: Brother Qiang once got drunk and called me, and I heard him say that he hadn't married me out of love back then — or not entirely out of love — but because he thought I was 'suitable.'" Lin Wan's tone stayed calm, as if she were telling someone else's story. "Learning the truth hurt me badly, and I ran away from home. A lot happened afterward, and after many inner upheavals, I worked out that 'systematic survival strategy for marriage.' Once things between us had slowly settled, Brother Qiang and I had one frank, no-holds-barred talk. He admitted that marrying me had been more about practical considerations, but there had definitely been fondness and liking too — otherwise, given his standing, he would never have married a woman he didn't care for at all. He said that at his age, the family-bond you build from living together matters more than love. He even said, 'Lin Wan, look around — how many couples still have love after years of life together? Most of them turn into family, into comrades-in-arms.'"

"And once again I fell into confusion." A genuine bewilderment surfaced in Lin Wan's eyes. "Is love the heat of love at first sight, or the calm of feelings that grow over time? Or is it both, and neither? Human emotions really are complicated. So, sisters, you tell me — does what Brother Qiang feels for me count as love?"

The danmu clashed fiercely:
[Family love is love too]: After living together a long time, love turning into family-bond is totally normal. It's more stable.
[Self-deception]: This is obviously just an excuse! A marriage without love is just running a company together.
[Love comes in many forms]: Maybe it's not the kind of love we imagine, but it's still a form of love, isn't it?
[Wanwan, don't buy it]: He's switching definitions on you! Using "family love" to cover up the fact that he doesn't love you enough!

"I see a lot of you asking whether a ten-year age gap leads to an unequal, subordinate relationship." Lin Wan shifted her posture and answered earnestly. "My answer is: it does, and it doesn't." She paused briefly. "A traditional, rigid subordinate relationship is fixed, but in a healthy marriage, power and leadership can flow, and they can be reallocated according to the actual situation."

"For example, when we renovated our home, he led, because he had the experience. But when it came to choosing the home decor style, I led, because I'm more sensitive to aesthetics and comfort. He's better at investing and managing money, but when it comes to picking tech products or planning travel routes, I pick it up faster."

Lin Wan leaned forward, eyes bright. "The key isn't who always has the final say — it's a kind of unspoken understanding: leading the other in the areas each of you is good at, and negotiating as equals in areas that need joint decisions. This flowing, ability-based form of subordination is actually more real and more efficient than rigidly chasing a surface-level 'equality.' What do you think, sisters?"

The danmu agreed in a rush:
[Ability-driven]: Well said! Whoever's better takes the lead — that's real equality.
[Dynamic balance]: Learned something. Marriage isn't static; it's the art of dynamic balance.
[No moral blackmail]: The worst is the type who can't do it but insists on calling the shots anyway!

"And this healthy flow of power has to rest on mutual respect and trust — not on age, gender, or any external condition." Lin Wan summed up. "The real wisdom between us isn't how to defeat or suppress each other; it's how to recognize when to lead and when to follow. Sometimes an age gap actually makes that recognition easier — we naturally hold different experience and accumulated knowledge in different areas."

"So the wisdom of getting along in a marriage," she said with a knowing smile, "isn't fighting over winning or losing in the moment. It's about how to jointly set rules both of you are comfortable with, how to share limited space and time, how to let power flow naturally on a foundation of trust. The best 'tactic' is sometimes carefully planned communication, sometimes a well-considered concession. And the finest 'victory' is when both sides feel understood and respected, both grow together, and both get what they wanted."

"Remember — don't turn your home into a battlefield thick with gun smoke. Treat it as a negotiating table where you can actually talk things through. A good marriage isn't one without disagreements; it's one where every disagreement teaches you both more about how to live with each other. What we need to do, sisters, is stay clear-eyed and gentle at the same time, and steadily take back the respect and the say we deserve in our marriages."

"That's why I do this livestream." Lin Wan's voice was full of clear strength. "Not just to share my experiences, but to rebuild an order. In front of the camera, in the telling of my story, I am the absolute lead, and Brother Qiang is an important character in my story. In my narrative, I'm the one actively managing this relationship, I'm the one working out the rules, I'm the one striving to bend this 'partnership' toward the balance I have in mind."

"And the reason you love watching may be that you see in it a certain possibility — a woman breaking out of passivity in her marriage and fighting for the upper hand."

The danmu flooded past with rows of "respect" and "thank you":
[You're my light]: Wanwan, you're the one who made me start rethinking my own marriage.
[Narrative is power]: Yes! Whoever tells the story holds the power to define it!
[From victim to lead]: From complaining to managing — change your mindset, and the world changes.

========================================

Chapter 26 — The "Emotional Home" at Home and the "Feelings Relationship" on the Phone
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640273.html

"Next, we're going to set aside the specific relationship tactics for a moment, and skip the things that can be dressed up as 'sweet little games.'" Lin Wan's voice suddenly dropped lower, her expression turning grave. "Let's talk about the things in a marriage that are truly cruel — the things that can quietly eat away at a woman's entire dignity and sense of belief. Let's talk about what kind of foundation a marriage order that looks so refined, so stable, so reassuring is actually built on."
The scrolling comments thinned out in an instant. The audience seemed to hold its breath, sensing that what came next would not be light.
"The year my son turned four, I happened to attend an afternoon tea," Lin Wan said, her fingers absentmindedly tracing the warm rim of her cup. "It was a few friends from my husband's circle, all so-called 'successful men' with thriving careers. None of their wives were there — at a pure 'men's gathering' like that, the wives usually don't take part."
She paused for a long time, long enough that a few "And then?" and "Did the stream freeze?" questions drifted across the screen.
"They started talking about social apps, about the all kinds of girls they'd met on them. Young dance teachers, fresh-faced college students, university lecturers, even girls working on their master's or doctorate… those girls who'd been so well 'disciplined' by society — usually kind and simple, sometimes even a little proud and aloof, with an intense hunger for emotional connection." Lin Wan said it word by word, slowly, as if reading out a cold clinical report on a social pathology.
"They were swapping 'tips.' They said breaking through a girl's defenses was easy — maybe just two cups of coffee, one small gift that looked thoughtful, a few well-placed tender words that showed off their 'mature charm.' They said some of them have offices with an outer room for work and an inner room with a bed, very convenient. They even concluded that the girls who'd been disciplined the hardest, the ones who looked the most proper and self-possessed on the outside, were actually the easiest to… stir up in private, that there was a 'contrast' to it that made it more thrilling." Lin Wan's voice was very calm, but the knuckles gripping her cup were going slightly white.
"They said —" For the first time Lin Wan's voice carried the faintest tremor, but she quickly brought it under control. "'Getting them to take their clothes off is actually very easy.'"
The livestream fell into dead silence. No comments, no gift effects — only the barely audible sound of her breathing through the high-quality microphone. That silence was deafening.
A few seconds later, the comments erupted:
[Sick]: Scum! Reducing women to objects!
[I threw up]: So this is what some "successful men" are really like behind closed doors?!
[Chills down my spine]: So what exactly are we, in their eyes?
[Wanwan…]: Hugs, Wanwan. How awful that must have been for you.
"I was so disgusted I left early that day," Lin Wan went on, her voice back to calm. "When I got home, I asked my husband: Don't you develop feelings for any of them, doing things like that? Faced with so many different, vivid young women, don't you start comparing? Doesn't it affect the family?"
Lin Wan lifted her head and looked into the camera, but her gaze was somehow hollow, as if it pierced through the lens and back to that scene.
"I still remember every word of the answer he gave me." Lin Wan tilted her head slightly, as if recalling a complicated, cold mathematical formula. "He said: There are emotions, yes. That kind of 'emotion' makes a man feel excited, gives him highs and lows, pushes him to pay more attention to his image, gives him more drive at work. But feeling — real feeling — there is none. Because real feeling involves moral responsibility, social judgment, family stability. They draw the line very clearly — with the ones outside, there's only 'emotion'; with the one at home, that's where the 'feeling' is."
"He also said," Lin Wan's mouth curved into an arc with no humor in it at all, almost a sneer, "since it's a 'relationship on the phone,' it's a relationship in the dark, one that never sees daylight. A relationship in the dark satisfies your own 'dark side.' It can never, and will never be allowed to, be brought up into the open."
Suddenly she let out a very faint, very cold laugh.
"Do you understand?" Lin Wan's gaze turned abruptly sharp. "What a 'perfect' division of labor. The wife is the 'feeling,' the 'home to return to,' the cornerstone of 'social judgment.' And those girls are the 'emotion,' the 'dark side,' the 'relationship on the phone.' A complete, internally consistent ethical system, serving certain elite men. In this system, everything is rationalized, everything finds its 'place.'"
The comments were drowned in fury and grief:
[Clear division of labor]: Objectification, through and through! The wife and the other woman are both tools!
[Systemic violence]: This isn't a personal morality problem — it's an oppressive system!
[My heart is dead]: So our sincerity is just one "module" in their system?
[Despair]: Then what meaning does marriage even have?
"That night, I lay in bed, looking at the man sleeping beside me." Lin Wan's voice dropped to almost a whisper, yet it carried the chill of a blade. "Many fragments that had once been blurry suddenly snapped together into a clear picture."
"I understood why he later seemed to stop controlling my social life as strictly as before — because he'd perhaps come to realize that a higher form of control is granting you a certain 'freedom.' The net he'd woven from social status, economic ties, family responsibility, and our child was already everywhere, leaving no need to keep pulling it tight."
"Why he'd learned a more 'mature' way to apologize and show tenderness — because it made the container called 'feeling' more solid and comfortable, made the 'home to return to' more worth maintaining and investing in. 'Emotion' can be outsourced on the cheap, but the soil of 'feeling' must be cultivated with care."
The comments finally began to scroll, dense and packed, full of the cries of women suddenly seeing it clearly:
[The more you think the scarier it gets]: So those "good things" might have been part of the system running too?
[Cultivating feeling]: What a cold phrase… are we projects being managed?
[Nowhere to run]: That net… just thinking about it is suffocating.
"Why our marriage later looked calm, even 'better and better' — because in his value system, the 'one at home' has to stay respectable, stable, perfect. A stable marriage is a component of social identity, an invisible endorsement of business credibility, even proof that he's a cut above the lower, messier players."
Lin Wan's tears slid down without a sound, but she didn't wipe them away, letting them run across her calm face.
"In their secretly running elite-male ethical system, we wives are no longer simply their partners. We've become the professional managers of their 'feeling' division, responsible for maintaining its stable operation and growing its assets. And those girls have become disposable, single-use 'consumables' on their 'emotion' supply chain, replaceable at any moment. We and those girls seem to be on opposite sides, but in truth we're both trapped in the same cruel order that upholds the appearance of a 'perfect marriage' — fighting on different battlefields, in the same war that can never be won by either side."
"Remember what I said on a past stream, about how he made me delete the phone number of a male classmate from college?" Lin Wan's voice turned suddenly, unusually sharp. "Back then I thought it was possessiveness, insecurity. But now I understand completely — that was him doing everything he could to protect the exclusivity and purity of his 'feeling' division. His 'feeling' container has to look and feel absolutely loyal and clean, because that is his 'moral certificate' in society's eyes and in his own! The cleaner and more unimpeachable that certificate, the more guilt-free and righteous he can feel when he 'outsources' his 'emotion' elsewhere!"
The comments scrolled wildly:
[Moral certificate]: Holy hell! That phrase is unreal! Right to the bone!
[Double-standard logic]: He demands absolute loyalty from his wife, but gets to outsource his emotion?
[Maintenance cost]: The loyalty we maintain becomes the nerve behind their indulgence?!
[Don't cry, Wanwan]: It hurts too much, Wanwan, it really hurts too much.
"Some friends who just joined the stream are asking — if that's how it is, why not leave?" Lin Wan shook her head, her tears already dry, leaving only a deep exhaustion and clarity. "Because this order is too powerful, its force is everywhere. It wraps you in comfortable material security, soothes you with the social approval of a 'complete family,' persuades you with 'at least he takes responsibility for the family,' 'compared to others he's already pretty good.' It can even manufacture an exquisite illusion — making you feel that you're special, that you're the one finally chosen, that you're the precious 'feeling' in his world, not the cheap 'emotion.'"
"Do you know?" Lin Wan leaned forward, her eyes still red, but her gaze like a cold star. "The most terrifying, most helpless part is that, after years of steady soaking, a piece of this order has quietly become internalized in my own thinking. I'll instinctively protect the dignity of the 'rightful wife' and the posture of the 'winner,' I'll unconsciously weigh the cost and benefit of 'blowing it open,' I'll shrewdly calculate the total loss after 'tearing off the mask.' And on countless nights, I've even used this system's own talking points to comfort myself — 'at least he knows what matters, he only plays with 'emotion' and doesn't touch 'feeling'; at least this family is still whole.'"
She raised a hand, lightly covered her face, and her shoulders trembled almost imperceptibly — the enormous swell of emotion she was forcing down.
After a long while she lowered her hand and lifted her head. The tear-tracks were still there, but her eyes were now a sheet of cold, clear clarity.
"After that, Hongqiang and I had another long talk that lasted all night," Lin Wan said, her voice rough with exhaustion yet utterly resolute. "I didn't cry or make a scene, didn't threaten, didn't use any emotional approach. I just calmly asked him: In this order you've built, what exactly am I? The manager of your 'feeling' division, or a flesh-and-blood partner who deserves to be treated as an equal?"
"He was silent for a long time, long enough that I thought there'd be no answer. Then he said: You're my choice. Not out of pure morality, not just out of duty — it's because… I can only imagine growing old with you. With anyone else, it wouldn't work."
"I looked into his eyes and said: Good. Then from today, I'm going to redefine this 'choice' you speak of."
"First, those friends of yours who practice and flaunt this 'emotion-feeling dichotomy' — I don't want to see them at our private gatherings anymore. My social circle needs a level of cleanliness. Second, from today our family finances must be completely transparent, and I want real joint management and decision-making power, not just the right to be informed. Third, and most important," Lin Wan said, word by deliberate word, perfectly clear, "if in the future I discover you've got any 'relationship on the phone,' I won't cry, I won't make a scene, I won't give you any trouble at all. I'll simply make you pay the price in the way you care about most — you'll lose a sizable portion of the wealth you treasure, and the social reputation you've worked so hard to build. Not because I'm more noble than you, but because I want you to understand completely: I haven't just seen through the rules of your game — I'm also capable of playing this game by your own rules."
Facing the camera, Lin Wan suddenly smiled. This time it was a real smile, glistening with tears, yet full of the strength of something breaking out of its cocoon.
"He said: Good."
"So now, after that reshaping a few years ago, our marriage has entered a new, even stranger phase of 'stability.'" Lin Wan's tone carried a complicated sarcasm. "I'm no longer just the clueless head of the 'feeling' division. I've become Hongqiang's partner — a partner who knows clearly all his rules and dark sides and still chooses to cooperate. Hongqiang knows I've seen through everything; I know Hongqiang knows I've seen through it. On that basis, we've rebuilt a dangerous, fragile balance."
"It sounds pretty pathetic, doesn't it? But this is the most real reality I'm facing right now." Lin Wan's voice was soft, yet it landed heavily on every heart. "When a marriage reaches a certain point, maybe it stops being a fairy tale and becomes two adults who, after seeing through every one of each other's calculations, selfishness, darkness, and limitations, still decide — within this order that isn't beautiful, that's even cruel, to use the chips each of them holds and renegotiate a set of 'terms of cooperation' most favorable to themselves."
"Sisters, if you too feel suffocated and confused in a marriage like this, I want to tell you: seeing the order is the first step to breaking it — or at least to negotiating with it. If, after weighing it, you're willing to keep serving as the manager of the 'feeling' division, then you must know that you have the right to demand corresponding 'equity' and 'dividends,' not just a fixed 'salary.' If you're not willing, you always have the right to 'cash out and walk away.' But whatever you do, don't be the player who walks in clueless, not even knowing the rules of the game, and ends up losing everything."
The comments fell into deep reflection and debate:
[Negotiation mindset]: Switching from an emotion mindset to a negotiation mindset is a higher-dimensional strike.
[So cruel, but real]: Is this the adult world? Not a shred of warmth?
[Don't endorse it but understand]: I probably couldn't be this calm, but I understand Wanwan's choice.
[Another kind of independence]: Financial independence is the foundation; this cognitive independence is the ultimate weapon.
Lin Wan sat up straight, brushing away the last bit of wetness at the corner of her eye with a fingertip. Her expression returned to the cool professionalism of the stream's early minutes, even carrying a faint, almost imperceptible sharp smile, like release.
"I also want to break down this 'dualism of emotion and feeling' for every girl who meets a so-called 'gentle older man' or 'mature entrepreneur' on a dating app," she said, her tone turning serious and earnest. "Girl, you need to know — the tenderness, patience, and emotional skill at reading a woman's heart that he practices on you will very likely come back doubled, poured into his wife, into building up his family. Your brief doubt about your own worth, your own objectification, may invisibly be reinforcing the seemingly solid marriage another woman — his wife — is living inside. Without knowing it, you've very possibly become the designed, expendable 'emotion supply' link in this oppressive system."
"And once again, I want to say this solemnly to every wife," Lin Wan said, her gaze blazing. "When you feel grateful that 'at least he still knows to come home,' that 'at least he brings the money back,' please stop and think: in this logic, what have you quietly been shaped into? You are the 'gatekeeper' of his social reputation, the 'custodian' of his moral certificate, the 'professional manager' of his 'feeling' division. The 'stability' you've gained may have come at the cost of surrendering part of your dignity and tacitly accepting a certain unequal order."
After the phrase "professional manager" fell, Lin Wan's voice left a long blank. In the livestream there was only the faint crackle of the scented candle's flame, and the tsunami of scrolling comments and gift effects on the screen. Those virtual "rockets" and "castles" kept bursting open, their gorgeous light playing across her profile, calm to the point of being cold. She wasn't looking at the costly tips; her gaze seemed to pierce through the lens and the screen, landing somewhere farther, more empty.
"So, this stream, 'Gentle Awakening' — who exactly is it awakening?" Lin Wan asked and answered herself, slow and heavy. "It's for the girls who think that, by sheer simplicity, kindness, and a naive fantasy about love, they can trade their way to equal sincerity in return — to make them see clearly the real 'price' of 'emotion supply' in a certain game. And it's for myself too — when I sit here and calmly dissect, precisely name, and ruthlessly deconstruct the pain, humiliation, calculation, and gamesmanship I've lived through, I'm no longer just the 'wife' or 'manager' defined by the marriage system. I've taken back a part of the power to define. I've turned my experiences, my marriage, even my husband's 'dark side,' into my material, my content, the cornerstone of my economic and spiritual independence."
"He uses money, social resources, and that secret elite-male ethical order to build his kingdom; I use words, emotional resonance, and a clear-eyed deconstruction of this order — an order that has always been disordered yet has endured for hundreds, thousands of years — to build my own fortress. What we've reached between us is a new balance, an unspoken one, built on mutual insight and mutual check. That's the truest undertone of how we live together, at this stage."
She leaned back slightly against her chair, her posture giving off the weariness of someone who has been through a hard-fought war — and, beneath that weariness, an unmistakable sharpness. "Dear friends, whether the long river of marriage, like the Yellow River, also has nine thousand nine hundred and ninety-nine bends, I don't know. But I do know that every time it flows past a treacherous shoal, every time it passes through a hidden current, the two people in the marriage have to sit down again, review the voyage so far, and negotiate which way to turn the rudder next. So modern marriage may look more and more like a joint venture. There's a clear evaluation of inputs and outputs, a strict risk-control mechanism, shared profit goals — and there may also… be each party's quietly understood 'side circulation.' The surface warmth may be the pleasing decorative figures on a financial report, while what really keeps things running underneath is, more often than not, cold logic and precise calculation."
"Let's stop here for tonight." Lin Wan abruptly ended her outpouring, her tone returning to the gentle steadiness of the stream's early minutes, even carrying a trace of relieved calm. "I'm saying all this not to make you fear marriage or fall into despair. Quite the opposite — I hope you can enter or stay in any relationship clear-eyed, prepared, and with strength. See the rules and you can use them; master the rules and you might even… take part in making new ones. Goodnight. See you next time."
The black "stream ended" notice popped up. In the study, that bright ring of fill light went dark, leaving only the retro desk lamp spreading a small warm-yellow glow. Lin Wan sat quietly in her chair, not moving right away. She watched the last few comments drift across the screen — "Stay strong, Sister Wan!" "Thank you for telling us the truth," "Striving while staying clear-eyed" — her face showing no expression at all. She raised her hand and, slowly and carefully, removed the delicate headset, the motion as gentle as ending a solemn ritual, or like a warrior taking off her armor.
Outside the study door, in the shadows of the living room, Shuai Hongqiang sat on the sofa, having watched the entire stream from start to finish in silence.
He picked up the tea at his side, already gone cold, and took a sip. It was very bitter.
Shuai Hongqiang's feelings about Lin Wan's streaming had always been complicated and conflicted. From thinking at first that it was "fooling around" and "a waste of time," to later feeling it was "embarrassing," "airing the family's business in public," to finally witnessing — and being forced to admit — that Lin Wan's streaming income genuinely supported the family's ever-growing expenses, their son's steep tuition, even part of the capital his business urgently needed for cash flow. When Lin Wan calmly transferred a sum of money to him and said "use this for now," he breathed a sigh of relief, but something inside him twisted awkwardly, a feeling that something wasn't quite right. The gratitude was real, and there was guilt too, but more than that was a hard-to-voice awkwardness at being helped by a woman he'd once looked down on, and a panic at the loss of his sense of control.
Back when Shuai Hongqiang married Lin Wan, it was like bringing a Guanyin into the house. Not only was she pleasant and bright to look at, it felt as if even the Shuai family "bloodline" would be improved by her, lifted up a notch. He was glad to keep her, to give her the best material life, to watch her tend flowers and read the classics with hands that were meant to hold a pen and turn pages — and his heart filled with a strange steadiness and satisfaction, feeling that the money he'd earned, the assets he'd built, the life he led had finally landed somewhere solid, had gained "taste" and "class."
This rule of "the man works outside, the woman runs the home; the man provides, the woman looks up to him" was one Shuai Hongqiang ran very smoothly, and very much enjoyed. He put up the money; she put up "that kind of atmosphere," along with her dependence on him and her admiration. He considered this a perfectly natural balance. Lin Wan loved him — wasn't that how it should be? Maybe he hadn't poured all of a young man's burning love into her the way she had into him, but he'd given her the best material life within his means. Wasn't that enough?
Until tonight, when he heard with his own ears how she took the boasting, vanity-laced "small talk" and "bragging" the men in their circle shared in their idle hours, and with a cold, sharp scalpel dissected it into "a complete, internally consistent ethical system serving certain men." How she pried apart and crushed the thinking he took for granted, the unspeakable "methods" of his business and social dealings, the self-righteous "authority" and "provision" he prided himself on as a husband — and added those terms he found awkward on the tongue yet horrifyingly precise, "the dualism of emotion and feeling," "professional manager," "side circulation," "moral certificate" — turning it all into "material for analysis" and "teaching content," feeding it to tens of thousands of strangers on the other side of the screen.
When he first heard it, Shuai Hongqiang's mind went "buzz" and blanked out. Then came a sharp shame, the feeling of being skinned in public, burning across his whole body. After the shame passed, a nameless rage shot up out of nowhere. He suddenly understood, as if struck by enlightenment, that what he could least stand was perhaps not her complaining about him, accusing him, not even her making those "ugly things" public. It was the detached, specimen-studying calm with which she narrated it all. That clear-eyed, increasingly removed clarity with which she saw straight through him, through the life, the feelings, the gamesmanship they'd built together over so many years.
Shuai Hongqiang could admit to himself that when he first married Lin Wan, "suitability" and "value for money" had weighed more than the burning "love" of a young man (although over the long years of living together, a great deal of warmth and habit he couldn't bear to give up had grown too). But he couldn't accept that Lin Wan seemed to no longer "love" him either, at least not in the familiar way, the love laced with dependence and admiration. And that she could, so calmly, even with a cool scrutiny and a sense of putting it to use, take this shift in feeling, take the power structure and cold calculation inside the marriage, and lay it all out, item by item, in public, as a lecture. It made him feel like a complete fool, seen through entirely and still acting out his own little play. The "good life" he'd once given, the "provision" and "control" he'd believed in without question, had long been clearly converted, in her hands, into "capital" — into the chips that gave her a voice in this "marriage joint venture," into the nerve that let her openly dissect his "dark side" under the spotlight. She no longer needed the light he "gave"; she could generate her own power now, and use that power to light him up, inside and out.
Shuai Hongqiang knew that a new round of negotiation had begun. And the chips in this negotiation were the now-opened eyes of a woman.
Shuai Hongqiang stood up and walked to the closed study door. He paused for a moment before raising his hand to knock, then pushed it open. Lin Wan was tidying up her equipment, her profile serene in the glow of the desk lamp. He walked over and handed her a cup of warm honey water, his face nonchalant, his tone feigning ease: "Tonight's 'tactical analysis' on the stream was pretty deep. How's the 'battle result' assessment? What score do you give our side's performance?"
Lin Wan took the cup, her fingertips brushing the warm back of his hand. She lowered her head and took a sip — the temperature just right, the sweetness just so. Then she lifted her head and looked at him, the same flawless, breezy smile on her face: "The enemy was too strong, the system too complete, the foundation too deep. To have fought to this point, to have won a seat at an equal dialogue, is already no small feat. Tonight, I'd grudgingly call it a draw."
Shuai Hongqiang looked at her and managed a smile: "Admitting it's a draw — that's the most masterful tactic of all."
Lin Wan answered with a faint smile, saying not a word about the heart-stopping dissections from the stream, the cold terminology, the content that had exposed the order inside him to the world. By unspoken agreement, they both stepped around that real "battlefield" tens of thousands of people had just watched live, as if it were nothing more than an ordinary work report from her. The woman who had wept and laughed coldly before the camera, who had torn open the marriage's gorgeous robe with her own hands to reveal the precise, cold gears inside, and this wife now gently tidying her things, were like two completely different people.
Shuai Hongqiang suddenly realized with absolute clarity: she no longer pressed him, no longer argued, no longer even showed anger or sorrow — not because of forgiveness, tolerance, or numbness, but because… she had stepped off the chessboard of their old games. She had become an observer and analyst standing on higher ground — and the architect using all of this to build her own new world.

========================================

Chapter 27 — Nature Is Without Stain; It's the Heart That Sorts Clean from Foul
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640274.html

Nature is without stain; it's the heart that sorts clean from foul. A necessary act for human reproduction, yet treated as "dirty and unmentionable," something not fit for polite company.
Wednesday, two o'clock in the afternoon.
Yao Yuan started her livestream right on time.
Behind the host's chair the great Yellow River flowed on in silence, ancient and unchanging, its muddy yellow waves rolling a thousand years of silt over banks creased by time. The wind drifted past the windowsill, mingling with the low sob of the river. Beyond the noise and the studio lights lay the silence and vastness of mountains and rivers that had stood for ages.
On the slice of wall the camera could catch hung the gold-framed "Energy Pyramid" diagram, its edges hard and cold, its tiers sharply divided, glowing in the warm white light with a restrained yet showy matte gold. The structure narrowed as it climbed, layer by layer, stacking up an order and a desire that someone had defined — on one side the clamor of human voices and traffic inside the screen, on the other the silent hierarchy of rules hung high on the wall.
The moment Yao Yuan sat down, the picture in the frame was wrapped in a fine, shimmering glow.
The silver-wire crystal crown in her hair, its openwork flower-and-leaf design lit by the spotlights, scattered shards of light across her deep-brown waves like a handful of crushed stars. As her hair brushed her ears, the pair of dangling amethyst earrings swayed gently, the glass petals rippling out a hazy violet light that softened her naturally cool features.
She wore a silver-grey sequined cocktail gown — understated luxury — its semi-sheer mesh studded with fine diamonds and silver-thread embroidery, the low neckline cut just low enough to reveal the line of her collarbone, then veiled again in a soft haze of tulle, carrying a wealth that seemed to hold something back. On the long mesh sleeves, the silver-thread patterns brightened and dimmed in the frame as she raised her arm, like flowing moonlight.
When the camera moved in close, the fans could see the tiny diamond flecks on her lashes blend with the pearl sheen of the gauze, making Yao Yuan look like a princess carved from crystal. Just then, lashes lowered, she was adjusting her equipment, the curve of her lightly pursed lips exactly right, the line of her neck drawn taut in a cool arc — and the whole silver-grey ensemble gave off an aloofness that said, look but don't touch.
The comments scrolled by fast. Someone said that tonight, in her blingbling dress, she looked like a princess kept in a crystal box. Only she knew that every time she went live, the lavish — even slightly over-the-top — clothes, jewelry, and makeup were the armor she wrapped around herself. They were her battle robes.
"Good afternoon, my beautiful darlings! Welcome to my studio. It's time again to clear up some confusion for the lovely ladies. All right, let's connect our first call-in friend of the day." After a brief adjustment, Yao Yuan's voice came through with a smile — steady and carrying.
[Call connected]
The caller (female, voice clear, pace even, with a restrained rationality, but a barely perceptible thread of confusion underneath): "Teacher Yuanyuan, good evening. I've followed you a long time — this is my first time calling in. Here's my baseline: I'm thirty-five, I run a small company of my own, my annual income is fairly stable, around two hundred thousand after tax. All these years I've thrown myself into my career and figured relationships could wait. But now my parents are pushing, the social clock is ticking, and I myself feel… maybe it's time to move into the next stage of life."
(Comments: [Sis is amazing!] [Thirty-five with two hundred K, I'm jealous] [The social clock really is a pain])
Yao Yuan (without interrupting, just lifting her chin slightly to signal her to go on): "Mm, and then?"
The caller: "Through an introduction, I met a man. He's thirty-seven, middle management at a large company, his income about the same as mine. Family background, education, values — we match on every front. You could call it a perfect pairing. We've been seeing each other for half a year. It's calm, almost no arguments. He understands my work and respects me. Both sets of parents are very pleased and are pushing us to settle things."
Yao Yuan (catching the key words sharply): "'Almost no arguments,' 'very calm'… it sounds like you're describing an ideal business partner. So — where's the problem?" Her gaze turned keen in front of the camera.
The caller (silent for two seconds, that thread of confusion finally surfacing in her voice): "The problem is… I don't feel any spark for him. His looks are… pretty ordinary, a little below average, to be objective. I've tried, but there's genuinely no physical attraction. In fact… I'm a little put off by intimate contact. I keep telling myself that at this age, meeting someone who matches my conditions so well and is easy to be around is rare, and that I shouldn't chase some vague, illusory feeling. But I'm also afraid — if there isn't even the most basic attraction, can a marriage like that last? Am I overthinking it, being too greedy?"
(The comments exploded: [This is the dilemma of so many older elite women] [Great on paper but no feelings] [Why marry without love] [Can feelings put food on the table?])
Yao Yuan didn't answer right away. She leaned back into her chair, her long crimson nails tapping lightly on the desk — tap, tap, tap — a steady rhythm, as if she were calculating something. The sound of the Yellow River outside the window seemed to swell, becoming the backdrop to her thinking.
A few seconds later, she leaned forward, her pupils — deepened by the colored contacts — as black as a bottomless pool, locked firmly on the camera.
"All right. I heard you clearly." Her voice wasn't loud, but it carried a cold, cutting clarity that sliced straight through the tangle.
"First, stop using 'greedy' to gaslight yourself."
"You're not greedy. You're half clear-eyed and half muddled. The clear-eyed part: you know that 'matching conditions' is an important foundation for a stable marriage. The muddled part: you're trying to use that single answer, 'matching conditions,' to cover the whole complex, multiple-choice question that is marriage. Marriage isn't a consolidated balance sheet — a balanced asset column alone isn't enough. Physical attraction, emotional attachment, shared language, life rhythm… these are all scoring items with different weights. Your situation right now is that you score very high on the other items, but on 'physical attraction' you've handed in a blank sheet — maybe even a negative score."
The caller (her voice a little dry): "But feelings change. Affection can be cultivated…"
Yao Yuan (cutting in directly, her tone almost severe): "Second, don't gamble the back half of your life on 'affection can be cultivated'!"
"Affection might be cultivated, but biological attraction is very hard to 'cultivate.' That's hormones, that's dopamine, that's the most primal code in the body. You're thirty-five, not twenty-five. At twenty-five you might have the patience and the hormonal surplus to 'cultivate' feelings for someone who doesn't really attract you. But at thirty-five, your career has drained huge amounts of your emotion and energy. What you need is a partner who recharges you — not a business partner who requires you to spend extra effort just to 'boot up' the feeling. You're put off even by intimate contact — so tell me, how do you plan to 'cultivate' anything? Through willpower? Through repeatedly auto-suggesting to yourself that the 'conditions match'? That's burning through the psychological resources you barely have left to deal with real life!"
(Comments: [So true…] [When you're exhausted you really don't have the energy to cultivate feelings] [The body is the most honest])
Yao Yuan (speeding up, pressing harder with every step): "Third, you're using the 'social clock' and 'parents are pleased' to dodge being honest about your own desire."
"You say your parents are pushing, the social clock is ringing. Honestly, that's the most high-sounding excuse you've found for yourself! Because you're afraid — afraid that if you give up this 'condition-matched' option, you might never meet a 'better' one (better in your eyes meaning equally good on paper). You're afraid of facing the brutal judgment the dating market hands to women over thirty-five. You're afraid of bearing the risk of 'waiting' or 'choosing wrong.' So you'd rather ignore the clear alarm your body is sounding, try to talk yourself into it with logic, and numb yourself with 'it's time to get married.' That isn't maturity. That's cowardice. It's disrespect for your own true feelings."
The caller (seemingly struck, her breathing growing a little heavier): "Then… Teacher Yuanyuan, what do you suggest?"
Yao Yuan (easing her tone a touch, though the content stayed razor-sharp): "My advice comes in three steps. Carry them out immediately."
"Step one: face it honestly. Run a test. Stop all the self-persuasion. Find a quiet moment and seriously ask yourself: setting aside his income, his background, your parents' approval, society's gaze… purely as a woman, do you have any longing to embrace this man? Any urge to kiss him? When you imagine decades of intimate life ahead, do you look forward to it, or does it feel like a burden? Your body's reaction knows the answer earlier than your brain does."
"Step two: reassess 'matching conditions.' What you call being 'evenly matched' is static. But marriage is dynamic, stretching across decades. Do his 'conditions' include emotional stability, a high tolerance, the ability to give you the emotional value you need (even if not passion)? When you face stress, illness, low points in the future, can this 'calm' turn into solid support? If 'matching conditions' is his only core advantage, while you have a hard flaw at the level of core feeling — intimate attraction — then the long-term risk on this 'deal' is extremely high."
"Step three, and the most important: expand your options, but don't settle. Your biggest misconception is thinking it's 'either him, or someone worse, or growing old alone.' Who decided that? You're thirty-five, earning two hundred thousand a year, with a career, experience, and capital. Your power to choose a partner is far greater than you think. What you need to do isn't to lower your standard on 'feeling' to accommodate the 'conditions.' It's to use your 'conditions' to attract and filter for people who can both make your heart race and stand at your side. You could also choose someone whose 'conditions' don't match yours but for whom you feel genuine physical attraction — someone who'll take good care of you and your family. That takes time, takes initiative, takes breaking out of the passive mindset of 'waiting to be arranged.' But that's what it means to take real responsibility for your own life."
Yao Yuan paused, looking into the camera as if to pierce through the screen and gaze straight at the woman on the line — and at everyone with a similar confusion.
"Last, let me leave you with a line — and leave it with everyone caught struggling between 'suitable' and 'in love': when our own conditions are already good enough, the 'conditions' of the person we marry are the threshold of the marriage. They decide whether the two of you can walk through that door together. But 'feeling' is the light inside the house. It decides whether you'll be willing to stay under the same roof for the long haul. You can choose someone whose threshold isn't so high, but whose house is bright and warm. Just never force yourself to spend a lifetime with someone whose house always feels dim and cold to you — not even if that threshold is rimmed with gold."
"Your worth doesn't need to be proven by a marriage that 'looks perfect' but is cold at the core. You fought your way to where you are today not to win a 'condition-matched' trophy and then go home every day to a roommate you have no desire to be close to."
[Call time is almost up]
Yao Yuan (her tone firm and final): "So the answer is clear. If, after the most honest examination, you still can't feel any attraction, then cut your losses in time. Thank him for showing up — he confirmed what you require in a partner's 'conditions.' Then let go politely and move on. Your life's blueprint should include a partner, but it shouldn't hold only one 'roughly suitable' option. You deserve both the 'threshold' and the 'light.'"
"Time's up. I hope you understood."
[Call disconnected]
The studio was once again drowned in a surge of comments. Yao Yuan watched the countless reactions scroll past the screen and didn't speak right away. She just turned her head and gazed once more out the window at that all-devouring, ceaselessly rushing darkness. The sound of the Yellow River was like an eternal background noise, reminding everyone: the strongest force often springs from the deepest, most primal drive. A choice that betrays your true heart is like swimming upstream against the current — in the end, it drains you until there's nothing left.
She turned back to face the camera and gave a faint, meaningful smile.
"Darlings, the question from the lovely caller just now is also a common dilemma facing many accomplished career women today — our incomes have climbed a step up, but our thinking still lingers in the era of bound feet. That's the lingering poison of several thousand years of patriarchal society, but we ourselves have to wake up to it."
"We talk about independence. Independent in what? Not just economically, not just intellectually, but also in having a say over our own bodies. For body and mind to be content — note that 'body' comes first. If two people marry without any physical attraction, the 'body' is already uncomfortable, so how can the 'heart' be content? You work yourself to the bone, you earn money — surely it isn't so you can find someone who looks like a match on paper but whom you don't even want to touch behind closed doors?"
"'The appetite for food and sex is human nature.' In our cultural tradition, we avoid talking about sex, treating it as something hidden, unmentionable, not fit for the table. So many women lack any real understanding of sex education, are strangers to their own bodily sensations, and end up overlooked in marriage. On this point, I owe special thanks to the sociologist and sexology scholar Teacher Li Yinhe — she pulled 'sex' out of what we considered a 'filthy mire' and restored to it the dignity it deserves."
"Human reproduction depends on sex, yet for thousands of years it could never be discussed openly and honestly. And on top of that, sex is one of humanity's most primal, most direct sources of joy. Suppressing it, ignoring it — both are steps backward."
"I am firmly against any independent, accomplished woman choosing a partner she feels no physical attraction to — whether boyfriend or husband. Make yourself happy first, and only then can you bring happiness to someone else. From there, your life and your career will unfold smoothly in a state of contentment. If you don't know how to make your body happy, you can come to our 'Hum-and-Hah' class and learn the body's code."
"Remember," she looked into the camera, her gaze blazing, "whether you're facing the silt of emotion or the choices of life — being clear-eyed is forever your sexiest quality."
(Call-in segment ends)
The calls were still coming through one after another. Each caller walked away with the path to a solution they wanted, or didn't want. As for whether they later followed what Yao Yuan said, and how they followed it — that remained unknown.

Chapter 28 — Pre-Marriage Acquisition, Post-Marriage Restructuring
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640275.html

Pre-Marriage Acquisition, Post-Marriage Restructuring
[Livestream Status]
Viewers online: 103,427
The comments scrolled by at a furious pace, gift effects flashing across the screen now and then. Yao Yuan had just finished with the last caller and lifted her bone-china cup to take a sip of coffee. Outside the window, the Yellow River caught the winter dusk in heavy, dark-gold ripples, its murky current flowing slow, twilight creeping over the banks, a cold wind sighing through it all, wrapped in the vast, still desolation of a northern deep winter. She set the cup down, the red nails of her fingertips tapping the desk twice.
"All right, connect the next caller." There was a faint, almost imperceptible weariness in her voice, but her eyes were as sharp as ever.
[Call Connected]
After a brief crackle of static, a woman's voice came through. It was steady, the word choices careful, carrying a certain trained politeness — but underneath ran a thread of barely concealed irritation and... a sense of superiority.
"Hello, Teacher Yuanyuan. I've followed you for half a year now, I watch every livestream." The opening was textbook, almost deliberately courteous. "My situation is probably a little different from the sisters you've talked to before. I'm thirty this year. My family is in traditional manufacturing — the scale is... decent. When I was twenty-four, an elder in the family introduced me to my husband."
(Comments: [A family business!] [Another well-matched union?] [A blind date at twenty-four, very traditional])
Yao Yuan simply listened, leaning back slightly, the red nail on her right index finger tracing unconscious circles around the rim of her cup — her habitual gesture when she was listening closely.
The caller went on, her pace even, like someone delivering a business report. "His family's circumstances are on par with ours, also in real industry. As a person, he's... how do I put it — very solid, very reliable. In these six years of marriage, household matters, and later helping me manage part of the company's business too, he's handled all of it in perfect order, never a single slip-up. My parents, his parents, they're all extremely satisfied."
She paused, as if weighing her words, and that thread of irritation finally rose to the surface. "But, Teacher Yuanyuan, that's exactly where the problem is. He's... too 'mediocre.' I don't mean his ability — there's nothing wrong with how he gets things done. I mean his looks, his build, and... that aura. He's just a very ordinary person. Drop him in a crowd and you couldn't pick him out. The first two years of marriage, I thought a steady, solid life together was nice. We got along... well enough, I suppose. But these past few years, our business has been transforming, and I'm often out negotiating partnerships, attending summits, looking at projects. The caliber of people I deal with keeps climbing. The more I see, the more I'm exposed to, the more I come home and look at him..."
(Her voice dropped, but the distaste in it was unmistakable.) "...and the more I feel he's not someone I can take out in public. At an important reception, standing next to those founders and investors who really have presence, he looks like... like a senior assistant. When I talk to him about the latest industry trends, about certain cutting-edge concepts, he can follow, but he gives me nothing beyond what I'd expect — he just brings it all back down to the level of execution, very practical. And in daily life... there's no romance to it at all. The anniversary dinner is always at the same few restaurants, the gift is always jewelry or a bag — expensive, sure, but with zero imagination."
(The comments began to split: [??? So she finds her husband ugly? No romance?] [Is a solid, reliable man not enough?] [Sis, this is a bit of a humblebrag] [He can run your business without a single slip-up, and that's still not good?] [But having no spiritual connection between spouses really is painful])
"Now I get a little annoyed just looking at him, and even... I'd rather he didn't touch me." The caller finally spoke the core of the conflict, a kind of self-justified candor in her voice. "I know this probably sounds very 'spoiled,' very ungrateful. My parents both say I've had it too good and I'm stirring up trouble for nothing. But this feeling is real, I can't lie to myself. I'm only thirty. For the next several decades, am I really supposed to spend my life facing this kind of... this kind of person? Teacher Yuanyuan, I want to hear how you see it. Is there any need to continue this marriage? If I continue, how do I adjust my mindset? And if... I mean, if... I want to end it, what do I need to consider?"
When she finished, the livestream went quiet for a moment. There was only the low background murmur of the Yellow River.
Yao Yuan finally moved. She set down her cup and folded both hands on the desk — the posture of someone preparing for deep analysis. There was nothing on her face, neither judgment nor sympathy, only a near-cold focus.
"First, thank you for your honesty." Yao Yuan began, her voice level, as if analyzing a business case. "The fact that you can state your 'distaste' this plainly means, at the very least, you haven't dressed it up with empty phrases like 'incompatible personalities' or 'no common language,' and you haven't deceived yourself. That's one small step ahead of the women who enjoy the material benefits of a partner on one hand while morally blackmailing themselves into faking devotion on the other."
(Comments: [??? Is this supposed to be praise?] [Teacher Yao never plays by the rules, as expected])
"All right, let's break down your problem." Yao Yuan leaned slightly forward, her gaze landing on the camera like a surgical lamp. "Your core demand is actually this: in the 'joint venture' that is your marriage, you, as the majority shareholder — assuming your family leads — have discovered that the 'managing partner' you originally chose, your husband, now that the enterprise has entered a new stage of development, falls seriously short on the soft metrics: his 'image value,' his 'emotional value,' his 'strategic foresight value' no longer meet your current expectations for the enterprise's brand, or your personal aesthetic needs. You're questioning whether he still deserves the 'partner' seat, even considering a 'divestment' — divorce."
(Comments: [Joint venture! Managing partner!] [The business metaphor, late but it arrived] [Put that way it actually does seem clearer])
The caller seemed jolted by the cold metaphor, and hesitated. "But... we're husband and wife, after all. There are feelings involved..."
"Feelings?" Yao Yuan cut in gently, a faint, barely perceptible mockery in her tone. "In everything you just described, I didn't hear much that was actually about 'feelings.' I only heard 'solid and reliable,' 'never a slip-up,' 'parents satisfied' — these are all evaluations of a 'partner's' job performance. And, 'can't be taken out in public,' 'gives no feedback beyond expectations,' 'no romance at all' — these are complaints about a 'partner's' added value. Your pain, in essence, isn't the pain of a broken relationship. It's the psychological discomfort caused by a 'valuation gap.'"
She gave the other woman no time to defend herself, picking up her pace, the chain of logic clear and ice-cold.
"Let's use my framework now and put you through a thorough 'marital asset audit':
1. Your inputs and returns (the balance sheet):
Your inputs: the prime mate-selection years from twenty-four to thirty (opportunity cost), being locked into the identity of 'wife' (which may have limited other romantic possibilities), and the 'distaste' you now feel toward him (a negative emotional cost).
Your returns: a 'partner' who is reliably capable, who puts your parents at ease, who can genuinely share the management burden of the family business, and who keeps the household running on a stable footing. He ensures that while you pioneer on the front lines, your rear base doesn't fall into chaos. This has provided steady logistical support for your 'broadened horizons' and 'career development' over the past few years. Please don't underestimate this value. How many capable women have been dragged down by unreliable partners and household trivia?
2. The shift in his 'valuation' (market revaluation):
Pre-marriage / early-marriage valuation: comparable family background (risk hedging), solid and reliable (stable fundamentals), parents satisfied (lowering internal friction costs within the family). Within your scope of awareness and tier of needs at the time, he was 'quality stock.'
Current-stage valuation: your own 'valuation' has skyrocketed thanks to your expanded horizons, resources, and exposure. Your yardstick has changed, from 'seeking stability' to 'seeking excellence,' 'seeking resonance,' 'seeking brand premium.' Meanwhile his 'fundamentals' haven't changed — and may even be depreciating over time (appearance, passion). In your new valuation system, he's become a 'mediocre asset.' But note: his 'practical value' (running the business, stabilizing the rear) may not have declined at all. You've simply stopped valuing that part the way you used to.
3. The nature and cost of 'distaste':
Your 'distaste' stems from the 'cognitive leap' brought on by your own growth. You've seen the possibility of 'better,' and you've started using that standard of 'better' to find fault with the 'good enough' partner in front of you. This isn't his fault — it may not even be your fault. It's the inevitable friction of uneven development.
But 'distaste' has costs. First, the cost of emotional drain: when he grates on you, you suffer too. Second, the cost of relationship risk: once your distaste is perceived by him or those around you, it shakes the foundation of the partnership. Third, opportunity cost — and this is the most critical — because you're immersed in this distaste, you may be neglecting the issues that truly matter."
Yao Yuan paused, her gaze sharp as a blade.
"You asked just now: if you continue, how to adjust your mindset; if you end it, what to consider. I'm telling you now, both of those questions are wrong. The correct question is: what exactly do you want to get out of marriage? And, what price are you willing to pay for the thing you want?"
"You say you're only thirty and you don't want to settle for the next several decades. Fine, then let's run the scenarios:
Path A: Ending the partnership (divorce).
What you can get: a free body, the chance to choose a partner again, the short-term emotional relief of escaping that 'sense of distaste.'
What you'd have to pay:
1. Actual division costs: the splitting of assets (family-business equity, marital assets possibly halved). Given how deeply he's involved in the business, this could get very complicated, even bone-breaking.
Family-upheaval costs: the fierce opposition of both sets of parents (especially the very satisfied ones), the rupture of internal family relationships, and possibly even an impact on the stability and reputation of the enterprise.
2. Replacement-search costs: at thirty, twice-married, with a family-business background, re-entering the marriage market — can you find a man whose 'practical value' is higher than your current husband's (someone equally able to stabilize your rear and handle practical matters) and whose 'soft value' (looks, romance, spiritual resonance) is also outstanding? How likely is that? How much time, energy, and trial-and-error cost would you need to invest? Would the new 'perfect partner' even be willing to step into a complicated situation — a remarriage that comes with a family business? What's he after? Your money, or you as a person?
3. Personal risk costs: once you lose your current stable partner, you'll have to face all the pressure of the rear alone. Or, before you find someone new, you'll go through a fairly long empty stretch and chaos. Will your business expansion suffer?
(Comments: [Damn, a divorce cost itemization] [Put that way, divorce really doesn't seem worth it] [Feels like her husband is the quality asset here])
Path B: Continue the partnership, but adjust the model.
What you need to do:
1. Reposition him: take him down from the 'soul mate + star partner' pedestal of your fantasy and put him back in his true, original place — the reliable chief of strategic logistics and the stabilizer of the family alliance. Stop expecting him to give you moonlight and roses and spiritual highs. His core functions are 'backstop' and 'execution.'
2. Outsource value: the emotional value, aesthetic pleasure, and spiritual resonance you lack — don't demand them from your 'logistics chief.' That's a mismatch of roles. You can 'outsource' them by other means: make friends who can discuss cutting-edge topics, join high-quality social circles, hire tasteful consultants, even... (she paused meaningfully) develop a few safe, controllable 'extracurricular relationships' that provide pure emotional or aesthetic value. But remember, the precondition is that they're safe, controllable, and don't destabilize the core partnership. Your marriage is an 'enterprise.' What you need is to protect the enterprise's core interests, not to force your CFO to double as brand spokesperson and creative director.
3. Raise your own premium: part of your distaste comes from feeling you've 'lost out.' But marriage isn't static. You can use his 'stability' as the bedrock and springboard for your own value and career leap. Use him well, let him hold the home base rock-solid, and you can fly higher with a clear mind, reaching the most elite circles and resources. When your personal value and energy field grow strong enough, wherever you stand becomes the center stage. By then, does it really matter who's beside you? He may be mediocre, but if a woman standing at the summit has a mediocre but reliable partner at her side, people will only praise her for being loyal and not forgetting her roots. Whereas a half-baked person who looks down on a mediocre partner only becomes a laughingstock.
4. Try a limited upgrade: if you still have the bandwidth, you can try to 'train' your partner. But keep the direction practical. Take him to a better gym, hire an image consultant, encourage him to learn some macro-level knowledge. But this is like corporate training — invest in moderation, manage your expectations. If he goes from a sixty to a seventy, that's success. Don't expect a ninety.
(Comments: [Outsource value! Extracurricular relationships?! (warning)] [Is this something you can just say out loud?] [But it does seem very real...] [Using his stability as a springboard, that's brilliant])
Yao Yuan looked at the camera as if she could see the complicated expression on the caller's face right now, and she delivered her final conclusion, her voice calm and merciless.
"So, miss, back to your question. Is there any need to continue this marriage? From the pure standpoint of interest and risk control — there's every need. What you want now isn't the 'optimal solution' based on rational calculation. It's an emotional 'intolerance.' It's like a company where the CEO, because she thinks the CTO isn't trendy enough and his slides aren't pretty enough, wants to kick out the company's core technical talent — a co-founder holding a large block of shares. That's a foolish gamble."
"How do you adjust your mindset? Three steps. First, gratitude. Be grateful that this 'mediocre' man, with his mediocrity and reliability, has provided a stable platform for your rise these past few years. Without that platform, your 'broadened horizons' might never have come into being. Second, positioning. Treat him as your 'house-guarding treasure,' your 'anchor of the seas' — not as a soul mate. Third, outsourcing and self-sufficiency. Go elsewhere for moonlight and roses; use his 'steadiness' to achieve your 'flight.'"
"As for what you need to consider if you end it?" Yao Yuan almost gave a soft laugh, a smile with no warmth in it. "Consider whether you have the nerve, the ability, and the luck — after paying a sky-high 'breakup fee' and weathering enormous family upheaval — to still find a new partner whose 'practical value' is no worse than his while his 'soft value' far exceeds his. And, to make sure that new partner won't, when you're forty, fifty, when your own 'valuation' may be fluctuating with time, look at you with that same gaze of 'distaste.'"
"In the partnership of marriage, once it reaches a certain stage, the greatest wisdom sometimes isn't searching for a better partner. It's how to carry out a 'functional restructuring' and 'value reallocation' with your current partner, so that this deeply bound community of interest continues to serve the main quest of your life, rather than becoming a shackle on your emotions."
"Distaste is the most useless of emotions. It can't raise his value; it only lowers your scope and the quality of your decisions. Whether you let this 'distaste' destroy your existing home base, or harness this 'reluctance' to drive yourself toward a higher peak — the choice is yours."
The comments boiled over completely, viewpoints clashing fiercely.

Yao Yuan didn't look at the comments again. She glanced out the window at the eternally flowing Yellow River, now only a blurred, deep, low roar. Beneath that murky surface lay who knew how much sand and stone worn smooth by time, and against the night it made the livestream studio look all the more like a floating, glowing, transparent box. She lifted the now-cold coffee and drained it in one go. The icy touch sliding down her throat brought a brief sting and clarity to her vocal cords, scorched from so much talking.
