After hearing the addition, a deeper sharpness flickered through Yao Yuan's eyes, as if she'd glimpsed a far more complicated game board. She fell silent for a few seconds, the rhythm of her red nails tapping the desk picking up slightly — her habit when facing a high-complexity case.
"The situation has escalated." She spoke, her voice colder than before. "From a simple 'partner valuation imbalance,' it's escalated into a complex shareholder dispute involving the disposal of a 'core joint asset' (the child) and 'second-round financing pressure' (the second child). Good. This comes closer to the real-world marital dilemma."
(Comments: [Core joint asset! Second-round financing! Metaphor genius!])
"Now, let's re-audit." Yao Yuan leaned forward, as if facing a thorny acquisition case. "The basis of the earlier analysis stays the same, but we must add two new variables: Variable A, your four-year-old son. Variable B, the pressure from both sets of parents to have a second child."
"First, let's analyze the 'core joint asset' — your son."
"1. Emotional and ethical binding: the child is the toughest tie between you and also the greatest shackle of morality and responsibility. Divorce costs therefore rise exponentially, involving custody, visitation, joint education, and an endless string of entanglements — and it inflicts irreversible harm on the child. Your personal 'distaste' for him, when set against the child, will bring enormous internal (maternal love) and external (social judgment) pressure to bear.
2. The fusing agent of family interests: the child, especially a grandson, is the 'living contract' that binds your two families' strategic alliance. The intense preciousness with which your in-laws regard their grandson means that if you unilaterally tear up the marriage contract (divorce), it isn't merely a war between you and your husband. It's a war between you and your in-laws, and possibly an indirect impact on your own family's relationships too. The child turns a simple 'partner dissolution' into a major event that could shake the very foundations of the 'group alliance.'
3. Your personal bargaining chip and weak spot: the child is your weak spot, because of a mother's nature. But it's also a major bargaining chip. Part of your negotiating power within the family comes precisely from being the mother of his only child. The in-laws' push for more children also confirms, from the side, how important your value as a 'childbearing vessel' and 'continuer of the family line' is in their eyes. But note: this chip must be used with extreme caution."
"Next, let's analyze the 'second-round financing pressure' — the push for a second child."
"1. The essential source of the pressure: this isn't merely the traditional notion that 'more children mean more blessings.' It's a strategic move by both families to further consolidate the alliance, increase sunk costs, and lock in the future. One more shared child adds an even firmer lock to your marriage contract, and the difficulty and cost of divorce climb higher. It's also a potential counterbalance and pacifier for your 'unstable mindset' (distaste) — using a new childbearing responsibility to bind you more tightly into the existing arrangement.
2. A double pressure on you: this worsens your predicament. On one hand, you physically and psychologically resist intimacy with your husband and resist childbearing. On the other hand, refusing to bear a child may provoke the displeasure of both sets of parents (especially the in-laws who may provide crucial support), weakening your 'virtuous wife' image and your standing in the family, and possibly even affecting your voice in the family business (where inheritance and the like are involved).
3. The possibility of re-bargaining 'childbearing value': note that this is also a potential bargaining opportunity. They (your husband and both families) have a strong 'second-child demand,' while you hold the power to 'refuse or delay.' That in itself is negotiating capital."
Yao Yuan paused, her gaze blazing, as if to pierce the screen and see the other woman's most secret calculations.
"Now, let's reassess your two paths, after adding variables A and B:
The cost of Path A (divorce) becomes extremely steep:
1. The child becomes a battlefield: the custody fight will be exceptionally brutal (especially when the other side is well-off and has strong parental backing). Even if you win custody, the future tug-of-war with your ex and your former in-laws over education and visitation will drag on for ages.
2. The risk of the family alliance rupturing: it could evolve from a couple's divorce into discord or even outright opposition between two families, directly affecting business cooperation and family reputation.
3. The 'heartless mother' label: in social circles and on the moral plane, you may bear greater pressure (unfair as it is, that's the reality).
4. The impact on your remarriage-market value: bringing a son, with complicated entanglements tied to the ex-husband's family, will narrow her range of options in the high-end marriage market.
The strategy for Path B (continuing) must be adjusted and upgraded:
"It can no longer be a simple 'adjust your mindset, outsource value.' You must conduct an internal negotiation and strategic restructuring based on the new variables."
Yao Yuan's pace grew faster, more resolute, like issuing orders.
"1. Immediately turn the 'second child' issue into your core bargaining chip. Don't passively absorb the pressure — proactively use it. Convey a clear message to your husband (and skillfully relay it to both sets of parents): 'Having a second child isn't out of the question, but the precondition is that the quality of our marriage needs to be raised to a new level. I need to see change and sincerity.'
2. Concretize your 'demand list' (your asking price): personal space and value enhancement. Under the banner of 'creating better physical and mental conditions for carrying a second child,' demand more personal time and resource investment. For example, require the company to take on more of the concrete tasks, so you have more time to pursue advanced study (an EMBA), expand high-end networks, and improve yourself. This both temporarily avoids physical intimacy and substantively raises your personal value and bargaining power.
3. A 'limited upgrade' plan for your husband: explicitly state that you want him to make 'targeted improvements' in image, knowledge (at least enough to keep up with your topics), and the romance of daily life, and that you're willing to provide resources (a personal trainer, an image consultant, attending high-end courses together). Frame this as a necessary investment in 'setting a better father figure for the children' and 'creating a higher-quality family atmosphere.' This transforms your distaste into a 'remodeling requirement' for him and gives it family legitimacy.
4. Fine-tune the family power structure: use their expectation of you bearing a child to win greater say and decision-making space in family matters (including possibly business matters). For example, if the second child happens as they wish, your decision-making power in certain areas needs to be strengthened.
5. Reinforce your irreplaceability as 'the child's mother': put more care into your relationship with your son, while maintaining good interaction with your in-laws (especially around the grandson). Let your image as an 'excellent mother' and 'dutiful daughter-in-law' take root deeply. This effectively hedges against the negative judgment your 'distaste for your husband' might bring, and adds to your moral capital and the weight of your voice within the family.
6. The upgraded version of 'value outsourcing': on the precondition of guaranteed safety, secrecy, and absolutely not destabilizing the core (marriage, child, family alliance), you may expand your external social circle more strategically, seeking emotional and intellectual supplements. But you must remember: you are no longer single. You are now the steward burdened with a 'core asset' and a 'family alliance.' Any 'outsourcing' carries extremely high risk and must be done as if treading on thin ice. It's best to channel this need toward safe domains — pure business collaboration, high-end clubs, women's growth communities.
Finally, Yao Yuan delivered a conclusion both colder and more realistic.
"Miss, with a child, your battlefield has changed. You're no longer merely a 'wife who finds her husband distasteful.' You are a 'mother,' a 'key node in the chain of family interests.' Every decision you make must treat the interests of your 'son' and the stability of the 'family alliance' as the foremost considerations."
"The cost of divorce is now so high you very likely can't bear it. The most rational choice isn't to flee, but to manage this unsatisfying marriage into a 'platform' and 'springboard' that's more favorable to your personal development and your child's future."
"Use their craving for you to 'bear a second child' to trade for room for your personal growth, the 'limited remodeling rights' over your husband, and more power and resources within the family. Turn the pressure of 'forced childbearing' into the bargaining opportunity of 'conditional childbearing.'"
"When, through this round of the game, you make yourself stronger (richer, more knowledgeable, more powerful), gain more real power within the family, and successfully 'remodel' your husband into someone a little easier on the eyes (even if only in appearance and speech), your circumstances will improve a great deal. By then, when you look back, you may find that this 'mediocre' but reliable partner, and the 'core asset' you share (the child), are precisely your most solid bedrock. And the choices you'll hold then will be far more real and far broader than the fantasy you have now of 'finding someone better after divorce.'"
"Remember, the wisdom of a high-level player isn't always thinking about swapping cards. It's how, with the hand you hold, to play for the highest possible return through precise maneuvering and gamesmanship. You now hold the trump card of the child, and the other side's urgent 'second-child' demand card. How to play it depends on you, not on your emotions."
"If you find him distasteful, then use that reluctance to push yourself to grow, to negotiate with him, to change the lay of the board — rather than letting distaste be merely an emotion that drains you."
"Time's up. My final advice: when your personal value holds no crushing advantage and the bond of your joint asset (the child) runs deep, divorce is the worst of options. Turning your marriage into your advanced training camp and resource extractor — that's the best policy. As for exactly how to negotiate and how to execute, we can discuss it in detail after the stream."
"Of course, if you insist on experiencing the business risk lesson of 'losing your core partner,' I won't stop you. The tuition will be expensive — but perhaps for you, it's another required course in 'growth.'"
"All right, the call is over."
[Call Disconnected]
"Beep—"
After a moment of shock, the comments exploded again:
[Treating the child as a 'core joint asset'... cold-blooded but irrefutable]
[Using the push for a second child to negotiate for resources and space! This line of thinking is wild!]
[This is treating marriage as a commercial acquisition and an equity game]
[Hard as it is to hear, it really does seem like the optimal solution... in a divorce she'd never out-fight the in-laws]
[Yao Yuan is teaching people 'strategic marriage' here]
[But if you calculate everything like this, what meaning does marriage even have?]
[To the one above: elite marriage was never purely about meaning. It's about interests.]
[I feel like I just took a higher-dimensional marriage-management course]
After cutting the call, Yao Yuan didn't speak right away. She leaned back and gazed out the window. The Yellow River flowed in silence, carrying away the silt, shaping a new riverbed. Marriage, family, interests, children, desire, weariness... all these heavy things, in her eyes, seemed to break down into cold chips and variables.
Then she turned back to the camera, and the calm, almost indifferent expression on her face changed; smiling, she said to the lens, "All right, my dears, that's it for today's livestream. Any sisters who need help, consult our little assistant — whether it's about feelings or about the body." She drew out those last two words, "the body," with a sliver of mischief.
Then she raised her hand and pressed the end button cleanly. The instant the screen went dark, the meticulously maintained, flawless sharpness on her face receded like a tide, leaving only weariness, and a faint, barely perceptible understanding of all the entanglements of the human world.
She rubbed the space between her brows and said quietly in the direction of her assistant, "The last caller's case — flag it as 'marital gamesmanship involving children and childbearing pressure,' and file it under the advanced course 'Personal Strategy Under the Binding of Family Interests.'"
The ring of fill lights surrounding her went dark one by one, until only the dim, yellowish desk lamp remained. Yao Yuan's expression returned to calm — a deep calm, even... a trace of emptiness. It was as if she could hear the low, eternal flow of the Yellow River, that sound thick, steady, devoid of any emotion, simply existing, simply moving forward.
Yao Yuan didn't move at once. She sat quietly in her chair, looking at her own blurred silhouette in the pitch-black glass window across from her. That shimmering silver-gray dress was, in the darkness, nothing but a dense shadow.
After a long while, she laughed — very softly, almost soundlessly. In that smile there was the weariness of dust settling, and a near-cold clarity.
She stood, bare feet on the slightly cool floor, and walked to the window. The huge glass pane became, in an instant, a pitch-black mirror, reflecting her clearly along with this small space full of precise instruments. Outside, the thick night that swallowed everything, and that ancient, endlessly rushing great river — now no longer truly visible — seemed deep and void. She and her livestream studio were like something suspended above this ancient, flowing chaos, a calm and incongruous modern footnote.
She knew the lights would come on again, the camera would open again, and once more countless souls swept along by the silt of their feelings would yearn to receive from her a filter — perhaps sharp, perhaps painful.
And she would still sit here, with that silent, silt-laden Yellow River behind her.
Using the most clear-eyed and most merciless language, teaching them how, in this turbid river of life, to sift out their own small, solid measure of "gold."

========================================

Chapter 29 — The "King's Game" at the Crimson Night Palace
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640276.html

The "King's Game" at the Crimson Night Palace
Today's livestream had run half an hour over. When Yao Yuan stepped out through the company's front doors, a bitter wind cut into her, and the city's neon was already steeped in the thick, ink-black wash of a northern winter night.
She had a quick dinner with a colleague at a nearby mall, then drove over to the "Crimson Night Palace," which Jin Lan, Wang Qian, and the others had booked well in advance. After their back-to-back grind, the plan was to wring a few moments of looseness and emptiness out of the night with a few drinks and one good buzz.
At eighteen minutes past ten, Yao Yuan's figure slipped through the shimmering, light-washed doors of the "Crimson Night Palace." She crossed the glittering but clamorous lobby, and a waiter in formal dress with a textbook smile led her toward a deeper, quieter inner corridor. The floor of the long passage was laid with a Persian carpet woven in ink-black and dark-gold thread; her steps fell on it without a sound. The walls on either side were pieced together from countless dark laser-glass panels of varying sizes and dark velvet padding. Under the spotlights, as her angle of approach shifted, they gave off a deep, shifting glow like the Milky Way. From the ceiling, enormous irregularly shaped flower-shaped chandeliers cast dreamlike, ever-moving patches of light across the walls and carpet, as if one were threading through a world of crystal. The air carried a custom-blended fragrance: a top note of cold, green galbanum, a heart note that gradually gave way to expensive oud and a faint whisper of leather, and a base note that lingered with the faintest trace of amber and musk, thread by thread. Carried along by the slow, ethereal background music of the corridor, Yao Yuan—draped in a silver-mist frost-gray Arctic-fox fur—moved through this shimmering, fragrance-soaked passage like the lead of a film stepping through a veil of light and shadow toward a stage made for her alone.
At a wall ground from a single block of dark crystal, a waiter in a tailored butler's uniform stood quietly beside the heavy door of a private room marked "999." Seeing her arrive, he bowed slightly, a trained, perfectly curved smile of deference on his face, and pushed open the heavy soundproof door.
A space utterly different from the public, hallucinatory haze of the lobby outside—more private, yet lavish to the extreme—rushed at her, along with the suddenly sharp surge of sound and light, the clamor and intoxication that belonged to this one night.
The room was enormously spacious, like a miniature luxury private club. At its center sat a giant ring-shaped velvet sofa, a deep crimson-purple that flowed with a satin sheen under the shifting lights; sinking into it felt like being swallowed and held. Facing it were three walls of seamlessly joined giant laser-projection screens, now playing high-definition, visually striking abstract images of light, cleverly dissolved into the visuals of a music video so that the two blended into an immersive wraparound of sight. A top-tier surround-sound system delivered every drumbeat and every vocal cleanly to the eardrum, the volume held at a perfect threshold that could ignite the mood without ever becoming uncomfortable.
The lighting system was extraordinarily intricate. Beyond the light from the screens themselves, programmable LED strips were hidden in the ceiling, now flowing in aurora-like shifting tones of blue and violet; concealed light troughs set into the walls gave off a soft, warm yellow that lit the abstract modern art pieces hanging there. Above the coffee table, a dazzling crystal chandelier—its main light unswitched—still caught and refracted the faint glow around it through its countless facets, scattering fine glints like starlight. The air was thick with the faint mellow scent of cigars, the sweet freshness of fruit platters, and the rich, indistinguishable blend of several high-end perfumes.
As she crossed the heavy soundproof door of the "Crimson Night Palace" Room 999, a faint, almost imperceptible smile played at the corner of Yao Yuan's lips. She had long understood, in her bones, that inside and outside that door were always two different worlds—outside was the howling north wind and the order of reason, the propriety and clear-headedness she had to keep up at all times; inside was a garden of desire, carefully cultivated at constant temperature and humidity, a playground where she could release her charm in measured doses and control the rhythm. The cool-toned, silver-mist frost-gray full-pelt Arctic-fox long coat she wore underwent a curious chemical reaction with the room's over-saturated warm light the moment she stepped in: the fur was fluffy as cloud, its tips stained with the hazy tones of where moonlight meets polar night—a pale silver-white base, with smoke-frost gray and faint mist-blue interwoven in fragments and shaded together, and a soft pearl-gray luster pooled at the roots. The expensive fur caught and refracted every stray beam of light that brushed past it, as if it were giving off a cool halo of its own, instantly raising an invisible barrier between her and the boiling, worldly clamor around her. It was her protective coloring, and her ground to stand on—it kept her from looking out of place, while letting her hold on to a bystander's clarity, reading the mind of everyone in the room.
She slipped off the fur and handed it to the waiter, and the silver-gray sequined gown she'd worn for the livestream spilled out from beneath it. The cut was razor-sharp, the fabric draping soft and supple, tracing the smooth lines of her figure—elegant with a thread of sexiness running through it, exactly mirroring her state right now: stepping clear-eyed into intoxication, melting cool-headed into abandon. She knew very well what kind of looks this image of her would draw—the dazzlement, the covetousness, the probing—and she had foreseen all of them, had even deliberately engineered the effect. As the "Godmother of Feelings," who spent her years dismantling emotional riddles and teaching others to master the human heart, she understood all too well how to seize the whole room's attention with one outfit, one glance—without ever seeming to try too hard.
Right now she was like a cold-toned star that had drifted by chance into a dreamlike galaxy, walking step by step into this tender, intoxicating haze born for the night.
A fair number of people had already gathered on the ring-shaped sofa. Wang Qian sat near the song-selection console, holding a microphone and singing along to the screen, his mood running high. Beside him sat a man in a black shirt, looking not yet thirty, singing along with a bottle in hand. Yao Yuan had never seen him before. Jin Lan was tucked into the other side of the sofa, whispering head-to-head with a girlfriend she'd brought along. The girlfriend's makeup was equally exquisite; in a gold sequined cocktail dress, she glittered in the dimness—a bright, striking beauty.
In the relatively open space at the center of the room stood a man in a faintly garish printed shirt, collar wide open, flanked by two shapely, good-looking young women—by the look of it, the room's "hostess girls." Yao Yuan had seen him before; last time, at a dinner with Zhao Yiming, his name had seemed to be Gao Jieyuan, a second-generation type from the venture-capital circle. Gao Jieyuan had one hand draped casually over the shoulder of one of the beauties, the other gripping a microphone, singing along to a love song in a slightly exaggerated tone—though his eyes kept flicking toward the door, until Yao Yuan's figure came into view.
"Hey! Our gorgeous Miss Yao has finally arrived!" Gao Jieyuan's eyes lit up. Without breaking off his singing, he shouted the line into the microphone; amplified by the speakers, his voice came back with a teasing echo and instantly drew part of the room's attention to Yao Yuan, who had just walked in. His gaze roamed over her, the undisguised desire all but spilling out. Yao Yuan gave it a faint inward glance—men like Gao Jieyuan she had seen too many of: blunt, flashy, carrying the conceit of a spoiled rich kid. His interest in her sprang mostly from the conqueror's itch of "can't have her," not from any genuine appreciation.
Hearing him, Jin Lan looked up and at once waved with a smile. "Yuanyuan, over here! I saved you a seat!" She patted the soft, empty spot on the sofa beside her.
Wang Qian paused his howling too, raising his glass with a smile in greeting. His friend beside him and the woman in the sequined dress also turned their eyes her way—sizing her up, or simply friendly.
Yao Yuan paused briefly at the door, letting herself adjust to the more concentrated, more fervent atmosphere of the room.
Under the ever-shifting light of a KTV room, the silver-gray sequined gown beneath flowed with brilliance. She walked unhurriedly toward the seat Jin Lan had saved, answering Gao Jieyuan's appraising, dazzled gaze and the looks from the two beauties beside him with a calm smile, then gracefully took her seat. That composure was no act—it came from a certainty deep within her: she knew her own worth, and she knew the meanings hidden behind those looks.
"Bad traffic on the way?" Jin Lan leaned in affectionately and handed her a poured glass of clear, light-colored champagne.
"Not too bad. Nice room." Yao Yuan took the glass, her fingertips feeling the chill of the rim; that trace of cool was just enough to press down the faint restlessness rising in her at all the noise and bustle. Her gaze swept over the room's lavish fittings and the already lively crowd.
"Of course—Triple-Nine is one of the best rooms in this place." Wang Qian leaned over, his face flushed faintly with drink. "We've just been waiting for you to warm things up! Young Boss Gao's already sung a bunch!"
Gao Jieyuan set down the microphone now, swirled his glass, and ambled over, settling naturally into the seat diagonally in front of Yao Yuan. The two beauties dutifully topped up the drinks of the guests seated there. "The second Boss Yao walks in, the whole room feels brighter. Come, come—latecomers fine themselves three drinks first, that's the rule!" A slightly flippant smile sat on his face, his gaze roving over her.
Jin Lan laughed and gave Gao Jieyuan a light shove. "To hell with your rule—Yuanyuan just got here, she hasn't even caught her breath." The words sounded like a block, but there was little real intent to stop him in her tone; if anything, it was tossing another log onto this tipsy overture. The room was warm and cozy, the music restless, and on every face floated a layer of eager, loosened readiness—the fuse of indulgence had long since been lit.
Yao Yuan only curved her lips in a smile, neither accepting nor refusing the "three forfeit drinks" jest. She calmly raised the crystal-clear champagne flute and offered a vague toast toward the blurred figures in the booth. "Sorry to keep everyone waiting. Let me toast you all first." Her tone was proper, the measure pitched just right—conveying her apology without losing her poise, without a trace of flattery or stiffness.
With that, she lifted her wrist slightly, the rim of the glass meeting her lips, the golden liquid resting a moment at her mouth and leaving a small, moist gleam. She didn't gulp; she only sipped and stopped. Alcohol was a messenger of socializing, but it must never become an obstacle to controlling the room. She had to stay clear-headed even as she relaxed—only then could she read everyone's reactions, only then could she keep a firm grip on the initiative in this social game.
She set the glass down, and then came the never-ending round of introductions that no social occasion is without. Yao Yuan sat upright, her expression calm, while her mind raced beneath, making precise judgments about each person being introduced.
She set the glass down, and then came the round where acquaintances introduce new friends. Names, identities, and brief backgrounds floated through the air like pieces of a puzzle, swiftly fitting together into tonight's temporary social map.
"Liu Qingzhuo, my girlfriend." Jin Lan affectionately hooked an arm around the bright, striking woman's shoulder. "Her family's in the liquor business—all the drinks at my 'Yellow River Tower' are supplied exclusively by her family." Then she leaned to Yao Yuan's ear and added in a breath of a whisper, "Just got back to being single, the certificate's still warm." There was a touch of that subtle empathy between women in her tone.
Yao Yuan raised her eyes to Liu Qingzhuo. Those especially bright eyes held a bold, open beauty in the way their light shifted—such a vivid, brisk temperament was a rare thing. She lifted her glass in return, already clear in her own mind. "Girlfriend"—business partner, out together for some fun: deepening the bond of the alliance while keeping each other company, loosening up, and dispelling boredom.
Wang Qian clapped the shoulder of the man beside him. "Chen Ye, my buddy—he and Young Boss Gao have been close since they were running around bare-bottomed as kids."
The man wore a black shirt of crisp, well-cut fabric, the cuffs casually rolled up to reveal a watch on his wrist—simple in style but far from cheap. He raised his eyes and nodded to Yao Yuan; his features were refined and handsome, his complexion on the fair side, and he wore rimless glasses, his gaze as calm and gentle as a young professor who teaches and writes at a university. "This guy's family really does sit on a mine—a gold mine," Wang Qian said, waggling his eyebrows. Chen Ye pushed his glasses up, a slightly helpless smile surfacing, and raised his glass to Yao Yuan in greeting—an easy, unhurried motion, without the swagger so common in third-generation mining heirs. Something stirred in Yao Yuan's mind: Chen Ye's gentleness and calm were out of place in this clamorous, lavish room. He was more like a bystander, quietly watching everything in the room. What sort of thoughts lay hidden beneath that reserve? She returned the toast without a flicker, her smile proper—neither deliberately drawing close nor deliberately keeping distance.
Yao Yuan responded to each in turn, her smile gracious.
Several rounds of introductions, several rounds of clinking glasses—the crisp ring of glass against glass rose and fell, mingling with laughter and off-key singing, swiftly filling the room. Alcohol became the most capable messenger, quietly melting away the distance of first meetings and the walls of status. An eager, loosened ease had already floated up in the room; the fuse of indulgence had long since been lit. Someone picked songs, someone sang along; Wang Qian gripped the microphone and howled golden oldies, while Gao Jieyuan, arm around his companion, belted love songs in an exaggerated tone, his eyes still flicking toward her now and then. Yao Yuan simply reclined into the sofa, fingertips circling the stem of her glass, her toes tapping lightly to the beat now and then, her mind perfectly clear—she kept this distance on purpose, for the more detached she seemed, the more it stirred the conqueror's itch in men like Gao Jieyuan. It was a little trick of hers for controlling the room: effortless, yet keeping her always in the driver's seat.
The differently colored liquor bottles on the coffee table gradually emptied.
"Just singing is dull—let's play a game!" Gao Jieyuan suddenly proposed, an eager light flashing in his eyes. Yao Yuan saw through his intent at a glance—nothing more than using the game to close the distance with her, maybe even to manufacture some suggestive physical contact. She laughed inwardly but didn't refuse; it was only a game. She rather wanted to see what tricks Gao Jieyuan could pull off—and she had only to counter each move as it came, keeping the initiative all along.
At some point a clear patch had been cleared on the coffee table, and a deck of strikingly designed black playing cards with gilded edges was slapped down "snap" in the center. Gao Jieyuan was clearly well-versed in this; he shuffled the cards expertly, the corners of his mouth curling into a playful smile. "Just drinking and singing is so dull. Come, let's play something more direct—'the King's Command.' Everyone knows the rules, right? Whoever draws the King card draws one from the command cards, and whatever it says, you do. Don't do it—" he paused, his gaze sweeping over everyone, lingering a moment on Yao Yuan's flushed face, "—and it's three forfeit drinks, neat."

========================================

Chapter 30 — Alcohol Numbs the Brain but Releases Dopamine to Please the Body
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640277.html

Alcohol Numbs the Brain but Releases Dopamine to Please the Body
The game was simple, charged with a thrill of risk and the urge to pry into each other's lives, and it got going quickly amid the cheering. What was surprising, yet somehow felt inevitable, was that tonight the "King" seemed to favor Yao Yuan in particular. In the very first round, the card her slender fingers turned over was a black card with gold lettering: the "King."
"Whoa!" the others jeered.
Gao Jieyuan, smiling, pushed a stack of command cards with intricate patterns on their backs toward her. "Boss Yao, please. Let's see what fate hands you as your first command."
A flicker passed through Yao Yuan's eyes. She drew one at random and flipped it over. The card read, plainly: Hug the person to your left.
To her left sat Wang Qian. He blinked, then split into a grin and threw his arms wide. "Yuanyuan, the card says so. Come here, let Brother Qian give you a warm hug!"
Whistles and laughter filled the room. Yao Yuan's expression didn't change. She only arched an eyebrow lightly, then leaned over with easy grace and gave Wang Qian a loose, perfunctory hug, breaking apart the instant they touched.
It was just a hug, nothing more than a trivial bit of social etiquette. Refusing would have looked petty, given people something to gossip about, and made Gao Jieyuan think he had an opening.
"There," she said, sitting back up, calm as if she'd just finished a handshake, not a ripple in her heart. To her, this kind of physical contact was merely one small step in the social game—about measure, not emotion.
But the King's "favor" wasn't over. In the next two rounds, as if by some unseen hand, the King card landed in Yao Yuan's grasp again and again. The second time, the command she drew was: Down a drink yourself. That one was easy. Without a word, she picked up the gin and tonic in front of her—still more than half full—and, under everyone's gaze, tilted her head back and drained it. The cold, sharp liquid scraped down her throat; she frowned almost imperceptibly, then composed herself. She knew this drink was for show—proof that she was a good sport, that she didn't sulk, that she could blend in better. It was also a reminder to her own clear head: she could drink, but her measure couldn't slip, and her presence couldn't lose ground.
The third time, when Yao Yuan's fingers flipped over that card symbolizing "power" for a third time, even Jin Lan couldn't help clicking her tongue. "Yuanyuan, with luck like that, you'd better hurry to the lottery shop and buy a ticket!"
The interest in Gao Jieyuan's eyes grew thicker. He pushed the command cards toward Yao Yuan once more, his voice cutting clear through the noisy music. "Third time's the charm, Boss Yao. What'll it be this round?"
A faint premonition stirred in her—luck this good was too deliberate. Perhaps Gao Jieyuan had rigged it? But she didn't call it out. Her fingertips paused, then drew a card and turned it over.
The words on the card made the air in the room freeze for an instant, then erupt into even louder jeering—
Kiss one member of the opposite sex present.
"Whoa—!!!"
"Going big, huh?!"
"Boss Yao, pick one! Pick one!"
The command card was passed around to be read and confirmed. Wang Qian let out a piercing whistle, and Jin Lan covered her mouth and laughed, her eyes roaming over Yao Yuan and the several men in the room. Gao Jieyuan leaned back into the sofa, unhurried, arms spread along the backrest, his posture relaxed but his gaze locked tight on Yao Yuan, that spectator's smile at the corner of his mouth. The certainty in his eyes was as if he'd already decided she would choose him. Even Chen Ye, who had shown little expression all night, raised his eyes and looked quietly through his glasses at Yao Yuan, who held the command card and fell into a brief silence.
Three penalty drinks, neat. The alternative was laid out plainly. But Yao Yuan—caught one round after another and now pinned at the center of everyone's attention—only lowered her eyes to the card, her cheeks flushed, whether from the alcohol or from this sudden, suggestive command. The hazy light slid across the side of her face, grazing her lightly pressed lips, yet her mind was unusually clear. On the first draw, she would choose no one. A kiss was an intimate, ambiguous act—even in a game, she wouldn't yield so easily, and she certainly wouldn't let herself fall into a passive position. If Gao Jieyuan thought he could use the game to corner her, that was far too naive. She would rather take the penalty drinks than let her rhythm be disrupted, and she would give no one an opening to misread her.
She didn't touch the three glasses of liquor already poured. She only lifted her eyes and swept a calm gaze across the room—past the smiling, ambiguous Gao Jieyuan, with a cold laugh inside: his little scheme had backfired; past Wang Qian, enjoying the show, and her old friends watching the fun; past Jin Lan, who looked faintly concerned, and a touch of warmth rose in her—the one person in this whole gathering who truly had her interests at heart; and past Chen Ye, whose expression was hard to read. Beneath that calm, what exactly was he thinking?
She was choosing.
The wave of jeering in the room peaked during Yao Yuan's few seconds of silence, then ebbed as her composed gaze swept across them, settling into a low buzz of expectant whispers. Every eye was fixed on her, waiting for her choice—would she follow this teasing, ambiguous command, or take on the punishment of three neat drinks?
Yao Yuan's fingertips brushed once along the edge of that gilded command card, and then, under everyone's held breath, she set it gently back in the center of the tea table. Without looking at anyone, without a word more, she simply turned slightly, reached out, and picked up the three glasses of liquor someone had already filled in front of her. The liquid inside was clear, refracting a cold glint under the trippy lights—obviously high-proof at a glance.
"Yuanyuan!" Jin Lan murmured, trying to stop her. Yao Yuan shook her head lightly and curved her lips into the faintest, almost soothing arc—she knew Jin Lan worried she was drinking too fast, that she'd get drunk easily. But she'd decided to do it; this was her way of controlling the room, and she would never compromise her principles for any person or any game.
She tipped her head back, her throat moving, and drained the first glass in one go. The liquid rolled down her throat, burning and stinging. Her cheeks reddened visibly, the sheen in her eyes thickening, the alcohol's heat spreading instantly to every limb—but her reason stayed clear. She paused for less than a second, giving herself little time to recover, and picked up the second glass, her movements still steady. Only as she closed her eyes and drank did her long lashes tremble faintly. Setting down the empty glass, she drew a soft breath. The stubbornness in her hardened further—this much wine couldn't trap her, this small scene couldn't faze her.
"I'll take one for her." A crisp female voice rang out—it was Liu Qingzhuo. At some point she'd already picked up the third glass, her eyes bright, carrying the brisk frankness of a woman of the world. The way she looked at Yao Yuan held the understanding and loyalty of a fellow player in the game. Without waiting for Yao Yuan's reply, she too tipped her head back and downed her glass. She drank fast and choked a little, but her manner was clean and decisive.
Yao Yuan looked at her, real warmth flickering in her eyes—Liu Qingzhuo's show of loyalty came out of nowhere, yet it was especially touching. That clarity and frankness loosened the taut strings in her chest just a little.
Seeing this, Jin Lan immediately picked up her own glass too. "Then I'll take one as well! This wretched card keeps targeting our Yuanyuan!" And she took a big gulp. Yao Yuan didn't refuse the kindness; she knew this bond was genuine. But once they had drunk, she picked up the last full glass on the table—her third penalty drink. She raised it to Jin Lan and Liu Qingzhuo, and to the whole room, then steadily drained the last mouthful of the burning liquid. She couldn't let others bear her punishment for her—this was her pride and her principle. Having chosen the penalty, she would carry it through to the end herself. Only that way could she hold her presence and her measure in front of everyone.
Three glasses of liquor down, and even with Yao Yuan's decent tolerance, she now felt a strong wave of heat surge from her stomach straight to the top of her head. The music in her ears seemed to drift further away, and the light and shadow before her wavered and blurred. She set the glass down lightly; its base met the glass tea table with a soft "tap." She lifted a hand and pressed the back of it gently against her burning cheek, saying nothing, simply settling quietly back into the sofa. Her spine stayed straight, but the air about her brows and eyes—steamed up by the alcohol and her recent choice, a mix of restraint and faint allure—grew only more pronounced.
Her mind was still clear; only her senses had been amplified by the alcohol, and the gazes fixed on her grew sharper—admiration, tenderness, frustration, curiosity. She enjoyed the feeling of being the center of attention, but that enjoyment always came with a clear boundary; she never sank into it.
The room went quiet for a moment. Gao Jieyuan arched an eyebrow, his smile thinning, his eyes complicated—perhaps with a trace of defeated disappointment that finally dissolved into a soft laugh of unclear meaning. He began to clap. "Boss Yao, what nerve." Yao Yuan nodded faintly, saying nothing more. She knew that after this round, Gao Jieyuan's interest in her would only deepen—but she was also more certain that she could keep a firm grip on this distance.
Wang Qian put away his teasing and gave a thumbs-up. Chen Ye's gaze fell on the faint rise and fall of Yao Yuan's chest and her flushed cheeks, lingered a moment, then moved calmly away as he refilled himself half a glass of ice water.
The game went on. Maybe it was a case of fortune turning after misfortune, but Yao Yuan didn't draw the King card again after that. The new "King" fell to Liu Qingzhuo. The command card she drew was, sure enough, once more: Kiss one member of the opposite sex present.
"Whoa! Are we stuck on kissing tonight?" Wang Qian yelped.
Liu Qingzhuo held the card, a radiant smile on her bright face, her gaze sweeping openly over the men present—Gao Jieyuan's smile was playful, Wang Qian wore a clowning "don't pick me" face, and Chen Ye sat in a single armchair a little further off, seemingly detached from the commotion on this side, his head bowed over his phone screen, his profile especially refined and handsome in the dim light.
"I choose…" Liu Qingzhuo drew out the syllables, her finger pointing vaguely in the air, then settled, steadily, on the corner. "Chen Ye, Mr. Chen."
"I choose… Chen Ye, Mr. Chen." As Liu Qingzhuo's voice fell, Yao Yuan arched an eyebrow slightly, understanding in her heart—Liu Qingzhuo was perceptive after all. Gao Jieyuan was flashy, Wang Qian was flighty, Chen Ye was reserved. Choosing Chen Ye was the safest option: it wouldn't drag her into Gao Jieyuan's entanglements, nor would Wang Qian's antics spoil the mood. Chen Ye's gentle reserve meant he'd most likely play along without creating any extra ambiguity.
Chen Ye, in the corner, seemed to freeze for a beat. He looked up, and a clear flash of surprise crossed the eyes behind his glasses, as if he hadn't expected to be named at all. He glanced at Liu Qingzhuo, then instinctively looked toward Yao Yuan—who was reclining against the sofa, propping her temple on one hand, with the drowsy languor of someone tipsy, paying no particular attention to this side. Yao Yuan caught his gaze and smiled faintly inside. That reflex of Chen Ye's was rather interesting; it seemed he had a barely perceptible attentiveness toward her. But it was just attentiveness, nothing romantic—perhaps merely simple curiosity.
"Qingzhuo, this…" Jin Lan tried to smooth things over.
But Liu Qingzhuo had already risen with a smile and walked over to Chen Ye with easy grace.
Under everyone's eyes and jeers, Chen Ye had no choice but to set down his phone and stand, his expression resigned but still composed. Liu Qingzhuo came up to him, her height reaching only to his chin. She rose slightly onto her toes and pressed a quick, light kiss to his cheek, breaking away the instant they touched. "There!" Liu Qingzhuo retreated to her seat, her smile brilliant, as if she'd merely completed an ordinary social courtesy. Chen Ye lifted a hand a little awkwardly and touched his knuckle to the spot that had just been kissed, smiled at everyone, and sat back down—only the tips of his ears tinged with a faint, barely noticeable red. Watching this, a touch of warmth rose in Yao Yuan's heart. This was how the game should be—light, decent, no crossing of lines, no entanglement.
This little interlude was soon overshadowed by the next round. This time, the one who drew the King card was Wang Qian.
"Hahaha! The wheel turns at last—finally my turn!" Wang Qian waved the King card, that symbol of "power," in excitement, his slightly tipsy face flushed red; the alcohol and the game had him completely loosened up. Without even looking, he drew a card from the pile, flipped it over for a glance, and cracked up first himself, slapping his thigh and showing the card to everyone. "Hahaha! This is good! This is good! I need to let loose right now!"
The card read, plain as day: Go out the door to the corridor and shout three times—I'm an idiot!
"Whoa! Brother Qian, what luck!" "Go on then, Brother Qian, we're all waiting to hear it!" The crowd jeered at once, dissolving into laughter. Jin Lan pointed at Wang Qian, laughing too hard to speak; Liu Qingzhuo covered her lips and giggled; Gao Jieyuan whistled; even Yao Yuan curved her lips at the ridiculous command, the laughter spreading wide across her alcohol-misted eyes. Chen Ye pushed up his glasses, revealing a faint, helpless, amused smile.
"Fine, I'll go! A bet's a bet—I can take it!" Wang Qian loved a lively scene to begin with and felt no embarrassment at all, only amusement. He yanked open the heavy soundproof door and strode out, valiantly, even leaving the door cracked on purpose so the laughter inside could "see him off." Watching his retreating back, Yao Yuan laughed softly to herself. Wang Qian's flighty, easygoing slickness added a touch of lightness to this slightly tense gathering. A man like that could cover all the bases in any setting—the easiest sort of company.
The corridor was laid with thick, sound-absorbing carpet, the laser-lit walls flowing with quiet, dreamlike light, that lavish cold fragrance drifting silently through it. Wang Qian walked to the middle of the corridor, a few steps from the door where it opened a little wider, planted his hands on his hips, cleared his throat, and—without the slightest hesitation—threw open his voice and shouted into the grand, empty hallway:
"I'm an idiot—!"
Full-throated and laced with laughter, his voice rang especially clear in the superb soundproofing, echoing back with a hint of loneliness.
"I'm an idiot—!"
The second shout he delivered with even more flourish, as if performing, adding some exaggerated gestures of his own, mugging at the empty air.
And just as he drew a deep breath, ready to bellow the third—the most "rousing"—shout, a group of people happened to round the corner of the corridor. The man leading them was tall and upright, dressed in a dark, finely tailored casual suit, no tie, his shirt collar left carelessly open, a coat draped over one arm. He was turned toward a friend beside him, speaking, that habitual, unhurried smile at the corner of his mouth.
It was Yu Hao.
The moment she saw Yu Hao's figure, Yao Yuan's heart gave a slight jolt. She and Yu Hao were compatible "buddies," reliable business partners. When they got together, they'd agreed—not to interfere with each other's feelings, not to meddle in each other's social lives. Yet deep down, she knew that at some point Yu Hao had become more than just a "buddy" to her; beneath his composure lay a barely perceptible possessiveness. His appearance might break the calm, controlled situation she held right now.
Wang Qian's "I'm an—" had already burst from his throat, but the words "idiot" jammed hard the instant Yu Hao turned at the sound and his gaze landed precisely on him, twisting into a strange, choked-off little noise.
Their eyes met. A clear flash of surprise crossed Yu Hao's face, and then that surprise quickly turned to comprehension, and from there into an undisguised, almost overflowing mischief and amusement. He arched an eyebrow, looking at Wang Qian, who was still frozen mid-shout in the middle of the corridor, the curve of his mouth stretching wider and wider.
"Pfft…" One of the friends behind Yu Hao couldn't hold back and laughed out loud.
Wang Qian's face went red, but he was a man used to such scenes; the awkwardness lasted only a tenth of a second. He swiftly dropped his hands from his hips, gave a dry cough, and forced out the third command in a slightly rushed but still "booming" tone: "…idiot!" Then he instantly changed his face, piling on a friendly grin and striding toward Yu Hao. "Aiyo! Boss Yu! How is it you?! Now this is just… what a damn coincidence, the coincidence of coincidences!"
Yu Hao finally laughed out loud, reached out, and gave Wang Qian's shoulder a light punch with his fist. "I was wondering why the corridor was so lively—turns out it's you out here doing… performance art?" He deliberately drew out his words, his gaze sweeping mockingly over Wang Qian. "What, President Wang's into this lately? A new way to relieve stress?"
"Get lost!" Wang Qian chuckled, slinging an arm over Yu Hao's shoulder and lowering his voice—though still loud enough for those nearby to hear. "Lost a round of the King game, so it's a penalty! A bet's a bet, your boy's just being honest! How come you're here, Boss Yu? Out with friends too?"
"Mm, brought a couple of clients over to sit a while; just saw them off." Yu Hao explained with a smile, his gaze drifting, seemingly by chance, over the "999" room door behind Wang Qian, cracked open and leaking faint music and laughter. "Your room? Sounds pretty lively—who's in there?"
"Just our usual crowd! Jin Lan, Jieyuan and the rest—and Yao Yuan's here too!" Wang Qian blurted it out and warmly extended the invitation. "Boss Yu, running into each other is fate. You done over there? Why not come in and play a while? More people, more fun! Yao Yuan's here too!" He pointedly mentioned Yao Yuan's name a second time, with a hint of unspoken understanding between men. Sitting inside the room, Yao Yuan heard Wang Qian's words clearly and thought to herself: hearing her name, would Yu Hao come in, or just leave as usual? If he came in, would it make this gathering more interesting?
Hearing Yao Yuan's name, Yu Hao's eyes shifted almost imperceptibly, though his smile turned even more casual and natural. "Yao Yuan's here too? What a coincidence, then. I won't be intruding, will I?"
"Intruding, my foot! We'd be glad to have you! Come on, come on—Jieyuan was just grumbling that we're short a real drinker!" Without giving him a chance to argue, Wang Qian half-pulled, half-pushed Yu Hao toward the room.
The heavy soundproof door was thrown fully open, and the room's dazzling lights and roaring music came rushing out. Wang Qian's booming voice rose above the music: "Everyone! Look who I caught!"
All eyes swung toward the doorway at once. Yu Hao's figure appeared at the entrance amid the interplay of light and shadow. He shrugged off his suit jacket, handing it with elegant ease to a waiter who had quietly stepped forward, a smile on his face pitched just right to blend into the atmosphere—but his gaze, like precise radar, instantly locked onto Yao Yuan, seated deep in the sofa and looking up at that very moment. Her cheeks were flushed, her eyes drowsy, the silver-gray sequined gown shimmering with seductive luster under the hazy lights. Yu Hao's gaze lingered on her for a beat, an unreadable undercurrent passing through his deep-set eyes, before he turned, unhurried, to the others and greeted them with a smile. "Boss Jin, young Gao, young Chen—long time no see. Looks like I came at just the right time, with things this lively." Moving in the same venture-capital circles, Yu Hao knew both Gao Jieyuan and Chen Ye.
She made brief eye contact with him. She caught that flash of deeper meaning in his eyes—a tangle of possessiveness and concern, with a trace of barely perceptible scrutiny. He was checking: was she "at ease" in this room? She also saw the flawless social smile he unfurled right after, sealing that hidden emotion away completely. Yao Yuan said nothing; she only lowered her lashes ever so slightly, picked up the glass before her, and drank down the last drops. The cold liquid slid into her burning throat, pressing down that faint ripple inside her. They were buddies, partners, but not lovers—at least not lovers in the standard sense. She wouldn't change her rhythm because he appeared, and she certainly wouldn't let his possessiveness sway her choices.
"Boss Yu!" "Brother Hao!" Jin Lan, Gao Jieyuan, and the others called out their greetings one after another. A flash of surprise crossed Gao Jieyuan's face, but he switched to a smile at once—only the warmth in that smile had cooled a few degrees, with an added trace of appraisal. "Brother Hao, what a rare guest! Come, sit!"
Yu Hao settled naturally into an empty seat, the spot cleverly chosen—close enough to talk with everyone, yet neither too near nor too far from Yao Yuan, his gaze able to brush over her with ease. Wang Qian loudly recounted the "chance encounter" with Yu Hao in the corridor, omitting the specific content of his shouting and saying only that it was a game penalty, drawing another round of laughter.
The atmosphere seemed even warmer now, but an undercurrent was faintly stirring. Yu Hao's joining was like a stone dropped into a seemingly calm lake. He took part in the game, the chatter, the drinking with effortless ease—but anyone observant would notice that his attention, or a considerable part of his senses, hovered around Yao Yuan, faint but constant. He noticed Gao Jieyuan's overly attentive toasts and suggestive flattery toward Yao Yuan, and he noticed the languor and allure gradually surfacing in Yao Yuan under the influence of the alcohol.
Likewise, Gao Jieyuan keenly sensed Yu Hao's unusual attentiveness toward Yao Yuan. That seemingly offhand yet appraising, faintly possessive gaze set off a quiet alarm in Gao Jieyuan's mind. He didn't know the relationship between Yu Hao and Yao Yuan, but at this moment, with Yu Hao's attitude, a male animal's instinctive competitiveness and a quiet sense of rivalry began to rise in him.
The game went on. This time, the one to draw the King card was Jin Lan. "Oh, my turn!" Jin Lan's eyes lit up. She drew a card from the pile, read it, and laughed, sweeping her gaze around the room before settling on Gao Jieyuan. "Young Gao, come on, play along." The card read: Drink a cross-cupped toast with a member of the opposite sex. "Sister Lan choosing me—what an honor." Gao Jieyuan arched an eyebrow with a smile and obligingly picked up his glass and stood.
Jin Lan rose with a smile too, and amid the room's jeering the two crossed arms and tipped their heads back, draining their glasses. The motion was clean and crisp; finishing, they exchanged a smile and sat back down, as if it were nothing more than a harmless little joke. Yao Yuan leaned against the sofa, watching quietly, a faint smile at the corner of her lips. She understood: Jin Lan choosing Gao Jieyuan was deliberate—first, to give Gao Jieyuan a graceful way out, and second, to ease the room's delicate atmosphere and keep Yu Hao and Gao Jieyuan from clashing head-on too soon. Yao Yuan saw that thoughtfulness, and she kept it in mind.
Yao Yuan enjoyed this atmosphere—the feeling of being surrounded by quality men, their gazes following her. As a professional who "analyzed men to enlighten women," she knew all too well where her charm lay, and exactly how to navigate such occasions with ease. The alcohol amplified her senses and made that subtle sense of control even clearer. She liked being watched, admired, even quietly competed over—this wasn't shallow vanity, but a double confirmation of both her own charm and her social value. She was like a chess player commanding the whole board, watching everyone in the room advance, step by step, to the rhythm she'd set in advance. The feeling left her utterly at ease.
