Chapter 31 — The Clear-Eyed Hunting Ground: A Dance of Golden Wind and Jade Dew
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640278.html

The Clear-Eyed Hunting Ground: A Dance of Golden Wind and Jade Dew
Next round, the King card landed in Yao Yuan's hands again. "Boss Yao again!" "The King card's stuck to you tonight, huh!" Yao Yuan only curved her lips into a smile and drew a card from the command deck. She flipped it over. It read: Perform a talent. "Oh, this one's good!" "Yuanyuan, give us a dance! You have to dance!" "Yeah! Dance—Yuanyuan's a pro at it!"
Amid the cheering, Yao Yuan's gaze drifted across the smiling faces around her. She didn't put on a coy show of refusal. With a faint smile, she set her wine glass down lightly on the tea table, the crystal base meeting the glass top with a crisp little chime. She rose, bent down, hooked her slender fingers under the thin strap of a silver high heel, and slipped it off carelessly, planting her bare feet on the thick, soft carpet. The cool, fine plush sent its touch up through her soles, a subtle contrast to the warm air of the room. She'd decided to dance—not to please anyone, but to take the room more firmly in hand, to use her own allure to lock every gaze onto herself, and to make Yu Hao and Gao Jieyuan understand that her charm was hers to command, no one else's to steer.
She didn't bother walking to the center of the private room. She simply found her footing in that small patch of open space between the sofa and the tea table. Someone—she didn't know who—queued up "Golden Wind and Jade Dew." As the lingering melody of the intro poured from the top-tier speakers, she closed her eyes, drew a deep breath, and when she opened them again, the hazy languor in them had cleared, replaced by an absorbed, tender glow.
Evening wind, oh, plucking the strings of a lover's heart / Play me a tune and bring you to my side.
White dew, oh, soaking a lover's gentle face / Unlocking the thousand words of love I've hidden in my heart.
Golden wind and jade dew, oh, in a single meeting / It is you and I, two hearts in mutual delight.
Softly we whisper our trickling longing / White dew nestles into the evening wind.
Golden wind and jade dew, oh, in a single meeting / It is you and I, walking the mortal world together.
Gently we link hands, slowly moving forward / The evening wind sings the dusk down, sleeping beside the white dew.
Evening wind, oh, you can't dispel the longing in a lover's heart / One gust, and I gather you into my arms.
White dew, oh, you can't rinse away a lover's stirred eyes / We gaze at each other, and before we know it, tears have soaked them.
Golden wind and jade dew, oh, in a single meeting / It is you and I, two hearts in mutual delight / Softly we whisper our trickling longing.
The music was like water, and she was a lotus slowly opening within it.
Yao Yuan made no large movements. She only stretched her shoulders and neck back in the faintest degree, lifted her chin a touch, and let an elegant curve run from collarbone to jaw. The wrist of her right hand—holding the imaginary "evening wind"—turned lightly over, her fingers like orchids, as if she really were plucking unseen strings. A fine tremor ran from her shoulder to her wrist and on to her slender fingertips, every joint speaking of softness. Her left arm hung naturally at her side, palm turned slightly up, as if cradling the "white dew."
With "Play me a tune and bring you to my side," her waist began to sway slowly with the rhythm. It was not the hot, unbridled writhing of a nightclub, but a swaying like ripples on water, like willow branches—pivoting on the hips, drawing the upper body into soft, winding arcs. The silver-gray sequined gown streamed scattered light with every movement, like moonlight scattered over a faintly rippling lake. Her core control was superb: stillness within motion, motion within stillness. The range was small, yet it captured that restrained invitation and that long, drawn-out longing to the fullest. She sank into her own world, setting aside all her calculation and control for the moment, simply savoring the looseness the dance gave her. That oblivious focus, that disregard for everyone around her, was itself the deadliest pull.
Her bare feet moved across the carpet, steps light and full of rhythm. Each one landed in the snuggest spot in the beat. Forward, back, turn—she used the small space to the utmost. One light spin, and her hem swung out a ring of flowing light. She angled her body slightly, her arm extending, her gaze trailing her fingertips as if looking toward a distant lover. "Unlocking the thousand words of love I've hidden in my heart"—in that instant of pause and gazing, her eyes were dazed yet intent, as if she truly had endless words of love trembling on the verge of being spoken.
She knew that, in this moment, she had to be beautiful—beautiful enough to set every man in the room aching.
"Golden wind and jade dew, oh, in a single meeting, it is you and I, two hearts in mutual delight…" Now the movements clearly opened up. The lines of her arms stretched and reached further, like two lovers twining neck to neck. The backward lean and forward tilt of her body, paired with the circling of her arms, made it seem she was dancing with an invisible beloved. The beginnings of a back bend—her supple waist curved into a heart-stopping arc, then rebounded lightly just as it neared its limit, like a reed brushed by the wind. Her head and neck swayed gently with the melody, strands of hair grazing her faintly flushed cheeks. The shifting KTV lights swept now across her closed lashes, now lit her dewy red lips—that mood poised between clear-eyed and intoxicated, magnified to its peak.
Every turn of her wrist, every opening and closing of her fingers, carried the flavor of an opera gesture. The line of her neck lengthened when she turned her head, looking fragile and lovely. The rise and fall of her chest moved faintly with her breath and motion, the sequined gown tracing alluring shadows. As her bare feet touched down and her insteps arched taut, even her slender anklebones and graceful arches, in the interplay of light and shadow, gave off a kind of silent temptation.
She could feel clearly how the gazes fixed on her were growing more scorching, more rapt—Gao Jieyuan's infatuation, Wang Qian's astonishment, Jin Lan's pride, Chen Ye's appreciation, and Yu Hao's depth. Yu Hao most of all: his gaze was like a sea under the night, calm on the surface but with undercurrents surging silently in its depths—and within them, possessiveness, appreciation, and a faint, hard-to-catch tension.
When the song reached "Softly we whisper our trickling longing," her lashes lowered, casting a small shadow beneath her eyes, yet at the corner of her lips hung a barely-there curve, sweet and wistful. At "Gently we link hands, slowly moving forward," she lifted her eyes toward some point in empty space, her gaze tender and lingering, as if she truly saw the one walking beside her. That focus created a strange sense of isolation between her and the noisy room around her. She sank into the world built of her own dance and music, and that oblivious focus, that disregard for everyone there, was itself the deadliest pull.
Gao Jieyuan had long forgotten to sing. He held the microphone and stared, fixated, his throat bobbing once without his meaning it to. The two "club princesses" beside him had stopped their giggling too, their eyes carrying that sizing-up between women and a faint, hard-to-catch envy. Wang Qian swayed his head gently to the beat, humming along soundlessly. Jin Lan wore a look of pure pride, as if showing off a treasure of her own. Chen Ye, the third-generation mining heir, let a look of intent appreciation surface on his usually refined, calm face. He leaned slightly forward, his fingers unconsciously tapping the beat on his knee, his gaze trailing Yao Yuan's dancing form—especially that absorbed, moving expression on her face. Behind his glasses, his eyes deepened a shade. And Yu Hao set down his glass, a look of appreciation on his face, his gaze deep and dim, flickering with a light that came and went.
"The evening wind sings the dusk down, sleeping beside the white dew…"
The last line sung, its echo curled in the air. Yao Yuan's movements came to rest with the music.
It was the foundation of a professional, yet without any of a performer's stiffness; it was free, improvised swaying, yet everywhere it showed a beauty carefully honed. There in that small patch of ground, in the midst of the gazes of friends and half-strangers, she had danced an entire dance through.
As the last note fell, she gathered herself in slowly, in a wholly natural pose, her arms folding softly before her like wings drawing in. She bowed her head a little, her chest rising and falling lightly from the brief exertion. Then she lifted her face, a thin flush of post-exercise color on her cheeks. The dazed, intoxicated expression faded; her eyes recovered their clarity. Only a trace of watery sheen lingered at their depths, as though that tender, alluring woman who had just turned into a wisp of wind and a drop of dew with the music was nothing but a dazed dream at the dim edge of the lamplight.
The room fell silent for an instant, with only the last echo of the music's accompaniment. Then—
"Bravo!" Wang Qian was the first to clap hard, and he whistled.
"So beautiful, Yuanyuan! The old blade hasn't dulled!" Jin Lan clapped excitedly.
Only now did Gao Jieyuan snap out of it. He set down the microphone and applauded, his gaze even more scorching. "Eye-opening, truly eye-opening! Boss Yao, that dance was incredible—no worse than those dancers on TV! I have to toast you again!"
Chen Ye clapped softly too, a mild, appreciative smile at the corner of his mouth. He said nothing, only picked up his glass and tipped it toward Yao Yuan. Yu Hao applauded as well, a look of appreciation on his face—but that look didn't fully reach his eyes. His gaze settled on her cheeks, faintly flushed from the dance, and on the fine beads of sweat at her neck, lingering a moment, the meaning in his eyes growing only heavier.
Yao Yuan merely gave a faint smile, as if that soul-stirring dance had been nothing but a casual whim. She walked back to her seat, slipped her high heels on again, picked up her glass of champagne, raised it to the room's cheers, and took a shallow sip. The cool liquid slid down her throat, easing the slight heat the dance had stirred up. She knew how the dance had landed, and she knew that, after it, her grip on this gathering had grown a few degrees firmer. But her expression stayed calm. Only in the flow of her gaze did that allure—coaxed up by the music and the dance, not yet fully dissolved—still ripple gently under the KTV's hazy light and shadow.
The dance done, before the applause and cheers had fully faded, a new round of toasts came one after another. Maybe that dance had unwittingly lit something; maybe the alcohol had long since quietly loosened the locks of restraint. The mood grew visibly livelier, even reckless. Gao Jieyuan raised his glass first, his eyes bright, his words carrying undisguised admiration. Wang Qian joined the clamor; Jin Lan smilingly drank one along too. Even the refined, quiet Chen Ye lifted his glass again, tipping it gently toward Yao Yuan before drinking it down in silence. And Yu Hao only held his glass and looked at her, deeply. Yao Yuan turned no one away. That faint, unhurried smile stayed at her lips as glass after glass of champagne and crafted cocktails slid down her throat, turning into the crimson slowly blooming on her cheeks and the ever-mistier sheen in her eyes.
Alcohol really is one of humanity's great inventions—a brief numbing of your brain, a quick release of dopamine to please your body. In this moment, catalyzed by the dance's slight tipsiness and the dazzled gazes of the crowd, it plucked at certain heartstrings, making the night subtle and unpredictable.
A new round of the game began. Yao Yuan leaned back into the sofa, taking in the gazes fixed on her—appreciative, scorching, playful, deep. She liked this feeling, liked this sense of control that came from being surrounded, from being the focus. As a woman who "understood" men, she knew all too well how to move with ease through a setting like this—how to release her charm offhandedly, and how to keep just the right distance. But at the same time, a faint weariness welled up at the bottom of her heart. This endless game of maneuvering, this being gazed at like a "trophy," sometimes left her feeling jaded.
Another draw. This time, the one who drew the King card was Gao Jieyuan. His eyes lit up. He pulled a card from the command deck, read it, and a meaningful smile curved his lips as his gaze went straight to Yao Yuan. "Kiss one member of the opposite sex present," he read out, then showed the card to everyone—though his eyes stayed locked on Yao Yuan's face. "Boss Yao, earlier when you drew this card, you chose the penalty drink. Now it's my turn to draw it—" He paused, his smile deepening. "I choose to exercise the right."
The room fell silent in an instant, every gaze flicking back and forth between Gao Jieyuan and Yao Yuan. Yao Yuan lifted her eyes to look at him, that faint, composed smile still on her face, as if waiting for him to go on. But in her heart she'd long since guessed it: Gao Jieyuan wouldn't let this chance slip. He wanted to use the game to force her into yielding, to break through her boundaries. His bluntness, carrying a streak of childish stubbornness, stirred a faint, hard-to-catch disgust in her.
"The member of the opposite sex I want to kiss," Gao Jieyuan said, slowly and clearly, "is Yao Yuan. Boss Yao."
The air froze. Jin Lan's brow knit faintly; Wang Qian raised an eyebrow; a playful glint crossed Liu Qingzhuo's eyes; Chen Ye pushed up his glasses. And Yu Hao—the habitual, composed smile faded from his face. His gaze settled calmly on Gao Jieyuan, then slid slowly to Yao Yuan's face, waiting for her reaction. Within that gaze were suppressed anger, possessiveness, and a faint, hard-to-catch anticipation—he was waiting to see how she would choose, whether she would yield to Gao Jieyuan.
Yao Yuan gave the glass in her hand a light swirl, the ice clinking crisply against the sides. She lifted her eyes and met Gao Jieyuan's, and in those eyes—misted with watery sheen from the wine and the dance—was now reflected, clearly, his face and its confident, this-is-mine smile. "Young Boss Gao is sure he's choosing me?" Her voice was a little husky, carrying the languor of drink, yet every word was crisp, her tone laced with a barely perceptible distance and amusement.
"Of course." Gao Jieyuan smiled and leaned forward slightly. "What, Boss Yao doesn't dare?"
Yao Yuan curved the corner of her mouth, and that smile held a heart-stopping beauty under the hazy lights. She set down her glass and rose slowly. The silver-gray sequined gown streamed scattered light with her movement. Barefoot on the carpet, she walked toward Gao Jieyuan, one step at a time. The room was quiet enough to hear the faint drumbeat behind the music. Every gaze focused on her. Yu Hao's body tensed slightly, his fingers unconsciously tightening around his glass.
She stopped in front of Gao Jieyuan. He stood up too. They were very close—close enough to feel each other's breath. A confident, this-is-mine light glittered in his eyes as he lowered his head slightly and waited. Yao Yuan lifted her hand, but she didn't touch him. She only tapped her own cheek lightly with a finger. "Here," she said, her voice calm, yet her gaze carried a commanding, playful amusement.
Gao Jieyuan stalled for a beat, then laughed. "Boss Yao, you mean—"
"Didn't Young Boss Gao say he'd kiss one member of the opposite sex present?" Yao Yuan tilted her head, her long hair sliding off her shoulder. "I agreed. But—" she paused, her smile deepening, "since it's a game, let's go by the game's rules. A kiss on the cheek should fit the rules, shouldn't it?" She said it deliberately—both giving Gao Jieyuan a way to save face and making her own boundary clear: she could play along with the game, but never let it cross the line. Her body, her feelings, had always been hers to command, and no one could force them.
Gao Jieyuan looked at her, a tangle of emotions flickering through his eyes—surprise, amusement, and the chafing of having been outmaneuvered, but more than anything, a thicker, more intense interest rising up at having been challenged. He laughed, the sound carrying clearly through the quiet room. "Boss Yao's right—the game's rules." He nodded, then leaned in slightly and pressed a light, fleeting kiss to the cheek she'd designated. It was very soft, very quick—it barely even touched her skin, just a symbolic gesture. But it was enough. He'd kept his face, and he hadn't truly offended Yao Yuan. And Yao Yuan had guarded her boundary and kept the situation in hand.
After he drew back, Yao Yuan still stood where she was, her smile unchanged, only with a victor's composure added to her eyes now. She turned to walk back to her seat.
"Wait." Yu Hao's voice rang out suddenly. Yao Yuan stopped and turned to look at him. In her heart she knew: Yu Hao couldn't hold back after all. His possessiveness wouldn't allow anyone—even under the cover of a game—to get close to her.
At some point Yu Hao had already risen. The smile was still on his face, but it didn't reach his eyes. He took the bottle from the table and poured a glass each for himself and Gao Jieyuan, the liquor swaying in the glasses, refracting the hazy light. "Jieyuan," Yu Hao said, pushing one glass toward him, his voice level yet carrying a force that brooked no argument, "since the game's been so much fun, let me toast you."
Gao Jieyuan raised an eyebrow at him, an ambiguous half-smile curving his lips. "Brother Hao, this is—" He knew it too: this drink Yu Hao was offering wasn't a toast. It was a declaration of ownership, a warning—stay away from Yao Yuan.
"Nothing. I just feel like toasting you." Yu Hao raised his own glass, looking at him calmly. "What, won't you give me face?"
The air tightened in an instant. Everyone felt the silent standoff between the two men. Wang Qian and Jin Lan exchanged a glance; Liu Qingzhuo watched with relish; Chen Ye pushed up his glasses, his gaze behind the lenses moving back and forth between Yu Hao and Gao Jieyuan. Yao Yuan stood where she was, watching the two men, that composed, playful smile still on her face—as if watching a play that had everything and nothing to do with her.
She enjoyed this feeling of being fought over, of being seen as a target by quality men. But at the same time, a faint weariness rose from deep within—weariness at this beast-instinct contest between males, at this posture that treated her as a trophy. What she wanted was appreciation, pursuit, a willing surrender—not this childish, possessive scramble. Yu Hao's possessiveness, Gao Jieyuan's lust to conquer—both made her feel as if she'd become an object, snatched back and forth between them, rather than a person with her own will and her own rhythm.
Gao Jieyuan looked at Yu Hao, then at Yao Yuan standing off to the side, and suddenly laughed. He picked up the glass Yu Hao had pushed over and raised it. "Brother Hao's offering a toast—of course I'll give you face. Come on, bottoms up." The two glasses met in the air with a crisp clink. They tipped their heads back at the same time and drained them.
Setting his glass down, Yu Hao filled two more. "This one's to tonight's gathering," he said, his gaze still calm. Gao Jieyuan raised an eyebrow but didn't refuse, lifting his glass again. "To the gathering." Another drained. Yu Hao kept pouring. "This one's to…" he paused, his gaze sweeping past Yao Yuan and returning to Gao Jieyuan's face, "to Boss Yao's dance just now. It was beautiful." Gao Jieyuan's gaze darkened, but the smile at his lips deepened. "It really was. This one's worth drinking to."
The third went down. The drinking contest had, just like that, formally begun beneath all the seemingly polite small talk and toasting. From then on, the game gradually faded into the background. Yu Hao and Gao Jieyuan seemed to have found a contest more interesting than the game. They no longer needed the command deck; reasons to toast each other came in endless supply—to the reunion, to the collaboration, to the weather, even to a song that had just finished playing. One glass for you, one for me, drinking fast and hard, as if what was in the cups wasn't liquor but some kind of will that had to overpower the other.
Yao Yuan walked back to her seat, sat down again, and watched in silence. She watched Yu Hao's steady yet relentless press of an offensive, and Gao Jieyuan's face going gradually red while he refused to concede. The alcohol burned inside her, sending her thoughts drifting one moment and turning them oddly clear the next. She knew exactly what these two men were contesting—her, and that laughable possessiveness. Yu Hao—even though they'd agreed not to restrict each other's social lives, it seemed that the moment a man got involved with a woman, he was born with a kind of possessiveness, feeling that as long as he was present, she was by default his property. And Gao Jieyuan—she, a person who made her living analyzing men to untangle other women's troubles, had, at their very first meeting, effortlessly sensed his curiosity and his liking for her. That liking carried a wastrel heir's arrogance, more like a desire to conquer: "If I want it, I have to have it."
Did she like this feeling? Yes. As a woman, she knew her own charm too well, and understood too well how to enjoy this sense of being surrounded, being fought over. But at the same time, she felt a subtle disgust—disgust at this beast-instinct contest between males, at this posture that treated her as a trophy. What she wanted was appreciation, pursuit, a willing surrender—not this childish, possessive scramble. They both wanted to control her, yet neither had ever asked her what she wanted, or how she wanted to be together.
Another round, and Gao Jieyuan's tongue had clearly gone stiff, his eyes unfocused, yet he still forced himself to pour Yu Hao another glass. Yu Hao's face was flushed with drink too, but his eyes stayed deep and clear. He pressed down the hand pouring the liquor and said evenly, "Jieyuan, that's really about enough." "Enough—enough of what! Brother Hao, you look down on me?" Gao Jieyuan flung his hand off, splashing some of the liquor.
Yao Yuan watched the scene and suddenly found it a little funny, and a little dull. This contest was getting more and more childish, drifting further and further from the elegant script she'd set out in her mind. She stood, her figure swaying slightly, and picked up her clutch and the silver-fox fur that had been draped over the armrest the whole time. Her voice husky with the heavy weight of the drink, she said to Jin Lan, who sat closest to her, "Sister Lan, my head's a little dizzy. I'm going out for some air—and to touch up my makeup."
With that, in her high heels, her steps somewhat unsteady yet stubbornly held straight, she walked directly to the door of the private room, pulled it open, and stepped out. She shut that roomful of luxury, of noise, and of the invisible war those two men had kindled over her behind her, for the moment. She needed air, needed to sort out her thoughts, needed to find that sense of control again—away from this childish scramble.
In the corridor, the cool, lavish laser-glass wall pressed against her back, bringing a touch of sobering chill. Yao Yuan drew a few deep breaths of air laced with crisp fragrance, then turned and headed toward the restroom. The woman in the mirror still wore exquisite makeup—only the sheen in her eyes was fuller now, the flush on her cheeks like the finest rouge had been brushed across them, carrying a heart-stopping allure steamed up by the drink and emotion. But more striking was the sharpness, clear and faintly playful, that had gathered again at the bottom of her eyes.
She touched up a little lipstick, pressed her lips together, and looked at her own alluring reflection—a "spoil of war," you might say—a faint, knowing curve rising at the corner of her mouth. Good. Yu Hao's appearance, the instant tensing of Gao Jieyuan's nerves, and those invisible sparks colliding in the air… all of it was within expectation—or rather, a situation she'd permitted and even unwittingly nudged along. The alcohol magnified her senses, and magnified this subtle sense of control too. She enjoyed this feeling of being noticed by quality men, of having them quietly compete over her—not out of shallow vanity, but as a double confirmation of her own charm, her social value, and her judgment of feelings. See, they're bidding for me. That recognition, carrying the warmth of tipsiness, pressed soothingly against certain places deep in her heart.
She was just… a little tired of Gao Jieyuan's overly blunt, even clumsy advances, and felt a slight, subtle displeasure at Yu Hao's "protective" posture and its air of entitlement. Contesting was fine, but the posture had to look good, and the rules had to be set by her. That was her inner standard. Clearly, those two in the room had both drifted somewhat from the elegant script she'd set. She needed to recalibrate the situation, to make them understand who was really in control of this game.
With that recalibrating mindset, she turned to walk back. The corridor was still long, the lighting dreamlike, the doors on both sides shut tight, only faint music seeping out. The alcohol had, after all, affected her judgment, and with her thoughts more sunk in savoring her own charm and assessing the situation, she walked back along memory and similar decor, her gaze searching for that "999" door plate. From a distance she saw the metal numbers set into one door seem a little loose; under the shifting light, the shapes looked somewhat blurred—the top "9," its lower round metal piece seeming to droop slightly, looked, at a certain angle, oddly like a "6."
Yao Yuan's steps faltered, and she narrowed her eyes. Was this it? She didn't remember walking this far on the way out… but maybe she'd misremembered. The alcohol had dulled her sense of time and space, weakening her usual absolute precision. She didn't dwell on it. All she wanted now was to get back to the room quickly, retake control, and end that childish contest. She glanced at her own reflection in the smooth mirrored surface beside the door, reached up to grip the cold handle, and pushed it open.
The deafening music and familiar din she'd expected didn't rush out to meet her.
The room was dark—not pitch black, but the deep dark of all the equipment on standby, with the faint breathing glow of electronics' standby lights. There was no smell of alcohol, fruit, or perfume in the air. The full-length mirror on the wall facing the door seemed startled awake by her intrusion, suddenly rippling out rings of eerie, water-like ripples, their center pointed straight at her. An instant later, a familiar, powerful pull appeared out of nowhere. Her soul was yanked hard, and dizziness washed over her.
"?!!" Yao Yuan's heart seized, and all her leisurely thoughts of emotional gamesmanship froze in an instant. One high heel turned under her foot, and her whole body lurched forward out of control, tumbling into the sofa in front of her. The silver-fox fur and clutch in her hands slipped free, falling soundlessly onto the cold floor. She stretched out a hand in vain, trying to grab hold of something, but her vision filled in an instant with twisted light and shadow, and she lost consciousness.
At the bottom of her heart there was only one thought left—she had, uncontrollably, traveled through time again.

========================================

Chapter 32 — Crimson Night, Palace Walls, the Mirror's Crossing: An Old Dream of High Summer — Zhuge Jinye
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640279.html

Crimson Night, Palace Walls, the Mirror's Crossing: An Old Dream of High Summer — Zhuge Jinye

The third time Yao Yuan woke in that pure-white, soundless space, a strange sense of familiarity rose in her chest.

She waited calmly. Sure enough, before long, a ripple stirred at one spot in the white expanse.

The ripple sharpened, resolving into a professional dance studio.

A tall, open, square room without a single pillar. A floor-to-ceiling wall of dance mirrors, spotless; the tempered glass glowed with a soft, warm sheen, and a hidden light strip along the mirror's edge gathered the glow into a still, gentle wash. Overhead, square ceiling mirrors were set neatly into the matte drop ceiling, mirroring the floor-length glass below so that the space seemed to extend upward without end. The pale-gray dance flooring was laid flat and smooth, and the lower half of the cream-colored walls was wrapped in matching crash padding, every corner rounded off. The solid-wood barre had the fine, mellow gleam of natural timber, and beside it the built-in storage cabinets were shut without a seam.

Beyond the double-paned soundproof glass, every sound from the outside world was cut off. No fussy ornament, no loud color — the whole room was clean, orderly, and held a kind of ultimate professionalism.

It was as quiet here as a dream waiting to be woken.

Just then the door opened gently, and a young man and woman stepped in together, instantly breaking the room's stillness.

The young man wore a fitted black practice suit; the hard lines of his arm muscles swelled faintly beneath the fabric, every fiber radiating power. The young woman wore a white spaghetti-strap dance dress, her tall figure making her waist look slender as a willow; in the fall of her skirt, before a single move began, there was already grace in the air.

The two stood at the center of the room, and the instant their eyes met, the air seemed to freeze. Before the music came, the dance broke loose.

The man's hand rested lightly at the woman's waist, and with a guiding pull he drew her toward him. She arched back with the motion, her spine bending into a lovely curve, her long neck stretching like a swan's. They closed the distance between them in a breath, then parted in an instant — like two clouds weaving together and drifting apart in the wind.

Their movements were fluid and full of tension. He lifted her up, and in the air she unfurled her body, her skirt flying. In the ceiling mirrors countless overlapping figures appeared, light and shadow swaying, dreamlike and unreal.

Then came turns and approaches full of effortless understanding. She spun toward the wall and pressed her back lightly to the mirror. He drew near, his breath close enough to hear. She slid down along the force of him, and in the mirror their shadows nearly merged into one. Her fingertips touched lightly at his shoulder and back, moving as the force passed through them, as if silently answering the same melody.

The music suddenly poured out, the drumbeat like a heartbeat. The two figures crossed and wove — no longer a simple partnering, but a resonance from the depths of the soul. Lifts, turns, leaning, parting… every move fit seamlessly, every glance laced with unspoken feeling. In the ceiling mirrors, light and shadow shifted and flowed with their dancing, as if the whole space had come alive.

At last the music faded. The man drew the woman gently back against him, and her forehead rested lightly on his shoulder. The two leaned quietly together, only the sound of their still-uneven breathing echoing softly in the empty room. The soft light of the ceiling mirrors wrapped around them, as if to freeze this moment of understanding and connection forever in flowing time.

When their breathing eased a little, the pair slowly lifted their heads, eyes meeting, a thousand words seeming to pass between them. The next moment, as if by silent agreement, they leaned forward, stopping at a breath's distance, letting a soundless tremor flow in that small space between them.

Standing in the pure-white space, thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan froze on the spot, caught off guard, her heart suddenly clenching. The next second, memories sealed away for years came roaring back, surging over her. Yes — that entwined pair in the mirror was her at twenty-nine, and Zhuge Jinye.

That past lay deep in the years, glazed over with a faded sugar frosting. In the deep winter of her twenty-eighth year, the north wind swept cold snow across all of Jinshi. Heartbroken and disillusioned with Shuai Hongqiang, Yao Yuan left, with bitter resolve, the city that had trapped her youth and her love, heading all the way south to the soft, humid city of Hangcheng. She went there for one reason only: to seek out the person she had been closest to all her life — An Nanchan.

An Nanchan was the dearest friend etched into her years. From grade school through middle school the two were inseparable, walking the road together; as girls they soaked in the children's dance class together, stretching their legs, arching their backs, polishing the basics over and over, their sweat falling on the same studio floor. They shared all the joys and grievances of those green young years, and all the secrets of girlhood too. An Nanchan's background had always been special — she was, in others' veiled words, an illegitimate child. She never lacked money; her birth father's family was wealthy, and materially she was never shorted a single thing — fine clothes, rich food, comfort without worry. But that abundance could never fill the hollow in her heart. The father's absence from her childhood, the veiled appraising stares of others, a birth without proper standing — all of it pressed long on her, leaving her hard-pressed to find any real happiness. Perhaps it was for this reason that Yao Yuan later especially supported those financially capable, independent women who chose to "keep the child, leave the father" — though she herself had never thought to walk that road. She had seen the hollow in An Nanchan as she grew up; so when Shuai Hongqiang refused to marry her, she gave up that child with bitter resolve. She did not want her own child, one day, to suffer the same kind of spiritual torment.

An Nanchan was by nature meek and solitary, always quiet and reserved. Yao Yuan was the opposite — willful and competitive, vivid and fierce. Two people so utterly different in temperament became each other's best friends, perhaps out of some destined kinship of spirit. Each had a flawed childhood: An Nanchan had a birth father, yet one absent year after year, a father in name only; Yao Yuan lost her own father painfully at seven, and lost her support early. The year she finished middle school, her birth father, far off in Hangcheng, formally came to take her away. She left her homeland and moved to that bustling, indifferent city of the Jiangnan region for high school. But fate's thread was never cut: later the two worked side by side and tested into Beijing Dance Academy together, becoming college classmates once more — in the same city, the same school, carrying on a bond that had grown with them from childhood. They walked four years of college side by side, and after graduation, their life paths quietly forked. An Nanchan grew weary of the clamor of Jingshi and, following her heart, returned to settle in Hangcheng, while Yao Yuan stayed alone to make her way in the capital. Apart in two places, they never grew distant; they kept in touch year after year, each the other's most steadfast refuge.

So when Yao Yuan truly could no longer hold on, the first place she rushed to was Hangcheng, to seek out this one true confidante. But the An Nanchan of that time was trapped in an exquisite cage of her own. Her intensely controlling birth father had forcibly arranged her life, pressing her step by step, ordering her to accept a family marriage alliance — trading her marriage for commercial gain, binding her life firmly onto a chessboard of interests. Beneath the glossy family wealth lay shackles she could not escape; An Nanchan was stifled and oppressed day after day, wrapped all over in a gloom that would not lift.

Yao Yuan's arrival became the one bright spot in her gray life. The two crowded into An Nanchan's waterside apartment, keeping each other company morning and night, comforting one another. At twenty-eight, a dancer's golden years were nearly past. To re-plan her life and find a new way out, Yao Yuan decided to step into the rising internet track, enrolling in a six-month systematic course run by a local professional e-commerce training academy, learning new-media operations, content planning, and on-camera expression from scratch. And it was in that course that she met Zhuge Jinye.

He was three years younger than her, also a formally trained professional dance student — upright in build, fierce in strength, carrying the unique aura and wildness of a dancer. The Yao Yuan of that time had just said goodbye to her past in Jinshi, her heart adrift, her whole self weary; and the young, ardent Zhuge Jinye was like a reckless, scorching beam of light, bursting unguarded into the dull, dry transition of her life. From then on fate entwined them — and laid the groundwork for the love, hate, and parting to come.

And the scene now before her in the mirror — the pair locked in an embrace and a kiss — was the hottest, most ardent page of that old time.

Yao Yuan had craved that sudden warmth that sent her heartbeat out of control. Rather than say they were like dry kindling and raging fire, catching at a single touch, it was truer to say they were two souls of the same frequency, two flames — the instant they met, they merged into a fiercer blaze. They shared the same field, kindred interests, close values, and above all the same identical ideal: to rise above others, to become someone who stood at the top. So alike, and yet so drawn to each other — it was as if she had fallen in love with another self in this world.

Half a year passed, slow as the flowing water of the Jiangnan region, and fast as wind through one's fingers. Yao Yuan and Zhuge Jinye spent their days soaked in the training institute — from livestream scripting and on-camera presence to account operations and content directing, they sat through every class together, their notes crammed full. After class they huddled together to work through practice scripts, correcting each other's expressions and movements in front of the camera. Zhuge Jinye had a professional dancer's command of his body and could always pinpoint exactly what was off in her posture on camera; Yao Yuan, fine and careful in thought, could always help him untangle the complex logic of operations. The two were in perfect sync, becoming the best-matched pair in the class.

Their off-class hours belonged entirely to Hangcheng's everyday warmth and romance. They went out in the morning light to eat scalding soup dumplings and fresh, sweet pian'er chuan noodles from the alleys; in the afternoons they hid in dessert shops gnawing on ice-cold osmanthus cake; at dusk they followed the scents into old lanes for soy-braised duck and Dongpo pork, eating their way through every corner of Hangcheng's food. In their free time they wandered every corner of the city — to Lingyin Temple to watch the curling incense smoke, to Xiaohe Straight Street to browse the lazy little shops, to Prince Bay Park to watch the wind blow over the sea of flowers — and the place they went most often was West Lake.

The West Lake of late spring, its waters rippling bright, its willows trailing low. The two walked slowly along the Su Causeway, the evening wind carrying the lake's damp, brushing tenderly against their faces. Looking up at Leifeng Pagoda in the dusk, dotted with lamplight, Yao Yuan suddenly laughed softly and turned her head toward the tall, upright Zhuge Jinye beside her. "Tell me — Lady White, beneath this Leifeng Pagoda, what do you think she's doing right now?"

Laughter rippled in Zhuge Jinye's eyes, his gaze fixed firmly on her face, and he picked up her words to talk about the Legend of the White Snake. After a moment's silence, he suddenly said, "If it were you, which one of them would you want to be?"

"I'd want to be Xiao Qing." Yao Yuan watched the ripples on the lake, her voice slow and soft. "Bold to love and bold to hate, clear-eyed and clean about it. Not like Lady White, trapped in a pagoda for love, wearing away a whole lifetime."

Zhuge Jinye's fingertips paused. He reached out and gently brushed aside the loose strands the wind had blown across her face, his tone earnest and tender. "Then I'll be Zhang Yutang."

Yao Yuan started and lifted her eyes to him.

"I know," he said, looking into her eyes, his voice low, "Xiao Qing and Zhang Yutang loved each other to the very limit and still couldn't stay together. In the end all they could do was drink the waters of Forgetfulness and forget each other completely. But if it's fated that two can't be together, then to forget utterly, to suffer no more the torment of longing — for both of them, perhaps that too is a kind of perfection."

In that instant a nameless ache rode the lake wind up into Yao Yuan's heart. She wanted to say something, but in the end it became only a soft sigh, buried in the sky-wide dusk. At the time she took it for one of his offhand musings — she never imagined those words would become a prophecy of what came after.

Time slipped by, and the last month of the half-year course arrived; Hangcheng plunged fully into high summer. The blazing sun scorched the whole city, the air thick with a parched, hot wind — and the feeling between Yao Yuan and Zhuge Jinye, like the weather of that high summer, burned into a sky-wide blaze that could no longer be held back.

Young hearts in love need no words. An Nanchan's apartment, the rented dance studio, the night-time shore of West Lake, the small guesthouse where they stayed a while… many places held the traces of their time together. His gaze was always intent and scorching, and in those moments she let down her guard, letting herself sink into that warmth that asked nothing of tomorrow.

Every approach was careful, every locked gaze set her heart pounding like a drum. They craved each other's company, and in that brief summer they gave over their most heartfelt feelings. They knew the parting was at hand, yet neither would say it aloud; they only wanted to seize every moment of the present.

That day after class, they were in Zhuge Jinye's apartment practicing on-camera posture. It was her turn to lead. The air warmed gradually in the focused adjustments, and a soundless tension spread through the stillness.

Something struck some chord in him; Zhuge Jinye's gaze suddenly changed — he gripped her wrist, turned, and in an instant the two were inches apart.

The sofa sank slightly. In his eyes Yao Yuan saw a surging emotion, familiar yet strange. No words, only breaths suddenly crossing, clearer than ever before, carrying an almost desperate resolve, and hiding within it a barely perceptible tremble.

His hand brushed over the hair at her forehead, fingertips slipping into the strands. Yao Yuan closed her eyes, taking in everything around her, and the reassuring scent that came from him — and that stubbornness the two of them shared.

Summer was about to end. They both knew it well.

But in this moment, neither was willing to step back.

A thunderstorm was coming, the air sultry and thick. Beyond the window dark clouds pressed against the city, and the wind drove damp air into the room.

Yao Yuan leaned back into the sofa, her fingertips unconsciously twisting the collar of his practice suit. Zhuge Jinye knelt on one knee before her, close enough to feel the faint heat off each other. Outside the window the muffled thunder rolled, throwing the silence of the room into sharper relief.

He lowered his head, his breath drifting over her lips. The next instant a cool touch came down, gentle but firm. It was a kiss carrying summer's damp and a tangle of feeling, nothing to do with technique — only the most direct passing of emotion. Her fingertips curled slightly, and in the end caught lightly at a corner of his shirt.

This kiss was very light, like a feather brushing the tip of her heart; and very heavy too, carrying too much that went unsaid. A flash of lightning streaked past the window, lighting up for an instant the close-pressed outlines in the room before they sank back into the dim. Raindrops began tapping the glass, sparse then thick, gradually merging into one noisy curtain of rain.

After a long while he drew back a little, his forehead resting against hers, their breaths weaving together, warm and moist. In the sound of the rain the two heartbeats were clearly audible, resonating at the same frequency.

His eyes were damp, reflecting the warm glow of the floor lamp, brimming with deep feeling and reluctance.

"Don't go, all right?" His voice carried a barely perceptible tremor.

Yao Yuan opened her eyes, reached up to wind her arms around his neck, her fingertips gently stroking the sweat-damp skin of his back. She parted her lips, but in the end only shook her head and buried her face in the curve of his neck.

Some answers don't need to be spoken aloud.

That night, they held each other tightly just like that, and in the sound of high summer's downpour they pressed all their love and reluctance into every lingering breath. Beyond the window the lightning still tore the night sky apart, while the warm glow in the bedroom wrapped tenderly around the young man and woman, locking their secret forever in that sweltering summer.

Chapter 33 — The Mirror's Fire, Not Yet Spent
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640280.html

It was in the very thick of that intoxicating, tender stretch of time that a fork in the road appeared. On the strength of her outstanding study and her solid foundation in dance, Yao Yuan was noticed by a top MCN agency in Shanghai. They extended an olive branch, laid out generous terms, and pressed her to come work for them. Looking at the contract, Yao Yuan hesitated—but not for long. As her pen touched the page, she knew exactly what she was doing: she and Zhuge Jinye were a magnificent garnish on each other's lives, a finishing flourish, but not a final home. Maybe one day in the future their flames would meet again and burn into something even fiercer. But not now.

And Zhuge Jinye had long since decided to stay in Hangzhou. He refused to give up his profession. He ran around making preparations, getting ready to open a professional dance theater of his own, dedicated to staging boy-group dance performances and building his own dance brand. His future was rooted in Hangzhou, while Yao Yuan's road ahead lay in Shanghai.

Two cities, not far apart on a map, yet the distance between them was one they could never cross.

At the same time, with Yao Yuan's patient counsel and careful strategizing, An Nanchan finally untangled the trap of her arranged marriage. She didn't openly defy her domineering father—that would only bring harsher restraints down on her. Instead she calmly went through the marriage candidates her father had screened and picked one: a young man from a good family, decent in character, whom she genuinely liked. That way she satisfied her father without shortchanging herself. Then she played her cards one step at a time. On the grounds of living independently and securing her own livelihood for the years ahead, she asked her father for a generous dowry and assets of her own, winning herself lasting security and a firm footing—so that even once she married, she would hold the high ground and never have to live at anyone's mercy.

You see, all those years ago, Yao Yuan had already shown that clear-eyed wisdom, that gift for reading people and laying her plans in silence.

With everything settled, the time came for Yao Yuan to leave.

The night before she left Hangzhou for Shanghai, she and Zhuge Jinye held each other through the whole night, in that dance studio so full of their memories. There were no extra words—only quickened breaths, scalding tears, and a tenderness drawn out to its very end. The summer wind outside the window couldn't carry off the ache in their hearts. They both understood: once tonight was over, it would be goodbye.

No quarrel, no plea to stay. In the world of adults, in the end you each have to set off down your own road.

The next day Yao Yuan dragged her suitcase, said farewell to An Nanchan, who had come to see her off, and boarded the high-speed train to Shanghai alone. Beyond the window, Hangzhou's scenery slowly slid away. The heat of that summer, the love, the tenderness, and the man named Zhuge Jinye all became a sweet, fleeting old dream in the depths of her heart.

And Zhuge Jinye, standing in the dance studio, gazed up at the scattered mirror panels on the ceiling. In them was only his own lonely figure. It was a long while before he slowly closed his eyes.

That high summer, that fierce, all-consuming love, in the end turned out exactly as they'd said it would—leaving behind nothing but a past that was destined to be forgotten.

Forced to stand and watch from within the pure-white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan felt her faded memories stir. The vivid sight of the young man and woman kissing before her was like a key, and in an instant it unlocked the sealed box of her memory, sending those forgotten flutters and that buried love surging hot through her again. The sweet days with Zhuge Jinye flickered through her mind like scene after scene of a film. And she realized what was about to happen next. They would lose themselves in each other in this professional dance studio, watching frame after living, scalding frame play out in the mirrors, the shame and the secret thrill churning all the more violently, her chest as though scorched by open flame—because that, too, was her. That was the woman who had once merged souls with Zhuge Jinye and held nothing back. Those poses caught frozen in the mirrors, that flawless attunement hidden in their eyes—all of it was her own past, faded long ago and now wrenched fiercely awake, a past carved to the bone.

The twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan had already let go of every tension. Her fingertips were the first to wind around Zhuge Jinye's wrist; the heat of his palm seared through her skin until her whole body trembled. Zhuge Jinye bent slightly, his kiss deepening, his arm sweeping around to pin her firmly against the mirrored wall of the studio, his warm breath falling all over the curve of her neck.

Mirror-light poured down from all four sides, weaving this pure-white studio into an airtight cage. The twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan was held fast against the mirrored wall by Zhuge Jinye, the cold glass pressing against her sweat-damp back, jolting a shiver through her—even as the burning heat of his body left every bone in her soft and yielding.

She was forced to look up, and her gaze ran straight into the mirror before her. In it, his head was bowed very low, his lips grazing the curve of her ear, every breath carrying a scorching heat that flushed the skin of her neck red. His hand was startlingly large, cradling her waist with perfect steadiness; a slight press of his fingers, and she surrendered entirely into his arms. The twenty-nine-year-old her tipped her head back to take it, strands of hair clinging in disarray to her cheeks, her eyes half-shut, infatuation and abandon roiling in their depths. Every faint expression was magnified, crisp and clear, in the mirror—even his eyes, reflecting the image of her, became the most unguarded brand.

Before she could pull her gaze from the picture in that mirror, the mirror on the ceiling overhead broke into view without warning. From that god's-eye view looking down, his broad back covered her small frame completely, their entwined bodies tracing lines of taut tension in the glass—her legs wound around his waist, the arch of her foot drawn into a beautiful curve, a dancer's suppleness and strength fusing without reserve in that moment; his arms locked across her back, his knuckles pale, every measure of force as precise as an attunement rehearsed a thousand times. The two mirrors, from the vertical and the horizontal, refracted and layered every tender motion over and over, leaving her nowhere to escape—she could only watch, wide-eyed, as she and he became one inseparable whole in the glass.

Every pose in the mirror carried temptation taken to its extreme: the flushed tips of her ears, her slightly parted lips, the trembling line of her shoulders; his taut jaw, his absorbed gaze, the controlling force of his hands—even the faint flush rising where their bodies touched glowed in the mirror-light. Shame shot through her limbs like an electric current, yet that secret thrill set her blood boiling. A dancer's acute awareness of the body was magnified without limit; in the mirror she could see exactly how much force he put behind each motion, and see every inch of her own surrender. That feeling of being watched twice over, possessed twice over, made her tremble more than the closeness alone ever could.

Zhuge Jinye had clearly noticed the mirrors too. Far from reining himself in, the obsession in his eyes only deepened. He tilted his head a little, his lips brushing the corner of hers, his voice low and rough, deliberately enticing: "Watch."

She was forced to look back from the mirror, and crashed into his ink-black eyes. The mirror-light flowed between them, making the reality before her and the reflection in the glass overlap perfectly, until she couldn't tell which was real and which was only image. His kiss landed harder, carrying a force that refused to be refused, his arm tightening abruptly to lift her up so that she hung entirely against him. The dancers' attunement reached its peak in that instant. Her body fit itself to his on instinct, every motion carrying the scalding resonance of two souls, every breath woven together with his. In the mirror-light on all four sides, they were each other's reflection, and each other's home.

In another corner of the pure-white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan, forced to watch this whole living tableau of her own past play out, looked at that pair of figures in the mirror—holding nothing back, fitted to each other completely. She watched her twenty-nine-year-old self bloom recklessly in Zhuge Jinye's arms, watched their every embrace, every caress, in the mirror-light, and the shame and the thrill rose into a tide that drowned her. That was her youth, the most scalding past in the depths of her soul—those wild, burning poses in the mirror, charged with a dancer's particular tension. So it had never been that the memory faded; it was that she had never truly let it go. The scene before her was the fusion of her and Zhuge Jinye, carved into blood and bone—the fierce passion and deep tenderness that belonged to them alone, blooming in the mirror-light.

The lingering warmth slowly dispersed, leaving only the two of them breathing themselves calm in the studio. They tidied their disordered clothes, and Zhuge Jinye lowered his eyes to ruffle the burning-hot crown of her head, murmuring that he'd go downstairs to buy some iced drinks. Then he turned and slipped out, easing the door shut behind him.

In an instant the vast white space held only the twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan. Her body still soft and aching, the feeling not yet faded, she had no strength to get up. She just lay sprawled on the cold floor, languid and dazed, her gaze fixed on the whole expanse of mirror above her head. In the ceiling glass, her reflection looked back—hair in disarray, cheeks faintly flushed, a misty haze of moisture still lingering in her eyes after that extreme tenderness, her face full of a blank, drifting daze.

Just then the once-clear, gleaming mirror suddenly clouded over with a faint white mist, like ripples spreading across water. And then a face, utterly unfamiliar yet utterly familiar, slowly surfaced from the depths of the glass.

It was the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan.

There she was, emerging in the mirror, looking down from on high at herself on the floor. Her features carried the steady calm settled by the passing years, and a trace of something hard to name—an indifference. Looking at the still-young, still-vivid twenty-nine-year-old self lying on the ground, for a moment she was choked into silence. There was helplessness, there was rue, there was the vexation of someone furious at another for not fighting harder—a thousand emotions jammed in her chest. In the end she forced out a hoarse voice and was the first to break this eerie, hushed standoff across time.

"When you were twenty-six, didn't I warn you, plain as day—leave Shuai Hongqiang at once, go straight to Shanghai? You clearly took it in. So why did you have to take the long way round to Hangzhou? Why, of all things, did you have to meet Zhuge Jinye there in Hangzhou?"

Word by word, weighted with the bitterness and reproach of the years gone by, it struck against the heart of the twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan.

Lying on the floor, she gave a violent start. The blankness in her eyes snapped into focus, fixing straight on the thirty-six-year-old self in the mirror. From below, the face in the glass carried a sharpness honed by the years, yet also a bitterness that wouldn't dissolve. That was her future self—the her who had been ground down by everything the world could throw at her.

The defenses in her heart collapsed all at once. The eyes of the twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan reddened faintly, her fingertips gripping the rubber dance mat beneath her. Her voice was light enough to shake, but still, word by word, she poured out the whole of the past she had never told another soul.

"After you vanished, I thought about it a long time. Shuai Hongqiang was so good to me back then—how could he not want to marry me? So I went straight at it and gave him two choices: marry me now, or break up now. He chose to marry me. We got our marriage license soon after and held a grand wedding. We spent six sweet months as newlyweds, and then I got pregnant."

She paused, a heavy bitterness rising in her throat. Remembering that agonizing stretch of the past, she couldn't help trembling all over.

"In March of our second year of marriage, the baby was just six months along, my belly already obvious. That day we were on a business trip together, and on the road we got into a fight over some trivial little thing. He lost his temper, lost his head completely, couldn't keep the wheel steady, and there was a car accident… He broke his shin. I hemorrhaged in a miscarriage and lost the baby. It was a boy who had already taken shape…"

The tears finally slid down from the corners of her eyes, dripping onto the floor and blooming into a small wet stain.

"The doctor said I was too badly hurt, that it would be very hard for me to conceive again. I hated him—hated that one moment of impulse that destroyed my child, that destroyed my right to be a mother. And he resented me—resented that I'd quarreled with him and brought the whole disaster down on us. There was no going back for us. We tormented each other, hated each other, couldn't go on living together at all. In the autumn of my twenty-seventh year, we filed for divorce, and Shuai Hongqiang split half his fortune with me."

"After the divorce I went to Shanghai, just as you said. I started in sales, worked myself to the bone, and in two years I made department head. This March the boss sent me to Hangzhou to study for half a year, with a promise that I'd be made assistant to the president when I went back."

"Zhuge and I… were classmates. There was a kind of fatal attraction between us. I couldn't resist it, and I didn't want to."

When she'd finished, she looked at herself in the mirror, her eyes full of confusion and pain. "Should I just have done exactly what you told me, every time? But I couldn't accept that… I wanted to walk my own way, to see whether the road was passable. What happened with Shuai Hongqiang taught me that fate can be changed at the small junctures—but there's a price to pay. The first warning you ever gave me played out the same way: after I went abroad to study and came back, it was because Mother fell ill and needed surgery that I couldn't stay on in Beijing, and ended up back in Jinshi, on the track fate had already set. Maybe, if fate hands you a gift, it quietly takes something away in the dark."

Hearing all of this, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror went bloodless in an instant, ashen as though every spark of life had been drained out of her. Her lips trembled beyond her control, and even her fingertips were clenched white inside her sleeves. Looking at the young self in the mirror—tear-streaked, eyes full of bewilderment—every question lodged in her throat, all the speechlessness and unwillingness brimming in her heart turned into a heart-rending, dull ache that spread thick and dense through all her limbs. She opened her mouth, but for the longest time no sound would come. Time and again she had crossed through time to warn and remind herself, wanting only to let her young self escape those tangled, unbreakable knots, to avoid that bone-eating pain. Yet fate kept circling back, like an invisible net, and in the end dragged her viciously back onto the track it had set. The torment she was meant to endure was not lessened by even half; instead, knowing the ending, she bore double the despair and double the suffering.

So then—what, in the end, was the meaning of crossing through time again and again? Giving it everything she had, what could she actually change? In the heart of the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan a boundless confusion spread, like falling into a fog with no bottom, until she could no longer find even a direction.

"…" She opened her mouth in vain. A thousand words jammed in her chest—bitterness, pain, helplessness all tangled together—and in the end not a single word made it out.

The twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan saw through the complicated emotions churning in her eyes at a glance; that hesitation, that almost-saying, was far too plain. She took a step forward of her own accord and asked, her voice trembling faintly: "So—this time you've appeared, is there some advice you have for me?"

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan was silent a long while, her throat parched and aching, each word as though squeezed out from between her teeth, hoarse past all recognition: "This time, I have no warning. Cherish your days with Zhuge Jinye. They'll be the one thing you can hold up in the long years ahead, the most beautiful and the sweetest memory you have. Don't go out of your way to hurt him, and don't be a fool and let him hurt you. When you love, be happy with everything you've got; once you part, each of you keep well, and leave the other alone. If I have to add one reminder—within your means, put aside some funds and invest in him. The men's dance troupe he founds later will catch fire across the whole country and be adored by countless women. And one more thing—whoever your boss in Shanghai turns out to be, whatever you do, don't ever toward him…"

The words broke off without warning. With no sign at all, a familiar force, cold and powerful, wrenched the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan into a churning torrent of time. The familiar studio before her twisted, stretched, and blurred at speed. In the spinning of heaven and earth a vast dizziness swept over her, and she lost consciousness entirely, sinking into boundless darkness.

In the studio, only the twenty-nine-year-old Yao Yuan was left lying alone on the floor, gazing up at the empty mirror overhead.

In the glass, there was no one left.

AD

========================================

Chapter 34 — Yao Yuan Reflects on Fate: Who Steers Our Destiny?
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640281.html

Yao Yuan's Reflections on Fate: Who Steers Our Destiny?
Yao Yuan struggled back up to the surface of consciousness through a distant chorus of voices.
"Yao Yuan… Yao Yuan! Wake up, can you hear me?"
The voice drifted near and far, as if filtered through a thick layer of water. Her head throbbed as though it might split open, her whole body limp and weak, without even the strength to lift an eyelid. Scenes from this life and the last whipped through her mind in a frenzy of overlapping images—the glint of the dance-studio mirrors, Zhuge Jinye's scalding breath, the bewildered face of her twenty-nine-year-old self—all the memories churned into a single tangled mass, until she could no longer tell what day or year it was.
It took an enormous effort just to pry her heavy eyelids open.
Her vision blurred, then sharpened. Jin Lan's face was the first thing she saw. Jin Lan was crouched by the side of the sofa, both hands gripping Yao Yuan's wrist so tightly her nails nearly dug into the flesh. Her eyes were red-rimmed, with unfallen tears still clinging to her lashes.
"Yuanyuan…" Jin Lan's voice cracked with the threat of tears, and also with relief.
Yao Yuan rolled her eyes around blankly, staring dazed at the scene before her. Yu Hao, Wang Qian, and Gao Jieyuan were all gathered at the side of the sofa, every face etched with lingering fear and worry.
"I…" She opened her mouth, throat dry and tight, her voice hoarse and disoriented as if just startled from sleep. "Where am I?"
"You scared us half to death!" Jin Lan swiped at her eyes, equal parts angry and frantic. "You were lying here all alone, out cold, and no matter how we shouted you wouldn't respond! We almost called an ambulance!"
Yao Yuan braced herself against the sofa and slowly sat up. Her temples pounded, and the heart-wrenching pain from before still lingered in her chest, leaving even her breaths slightly labored. She forced herself to stay calm, swiftly weaving together a cover story in her mind.
"It's nothing…" She pressed a hand to her temple, her voice still weak. "I just came out of the restroom, the alcohol hit me, and I walked into the wrong room… I thought I'd rest for a bit, and didn't expect to fall asleep."
She said it lightly, brushing it off, but only she knew that a film of cold sweat had broken out across her palms.
Time-travel—say a thing like that out loud and people would only take you for a lunatic.
"You'd been gone more than half an hour, and you weren't picking up your phone!" Yu Hao stood behind Jin Lan, brow tightly furrowed, his voice low. "We searched the whole corridor."
Yao Yuan lifted her eyes to him. Yu Hao's gaze was very deep, something roiling within it—it looked like worry, and like something else.
She looked away. Seeing the concern on everyone's faces, a warmth welled up in her heart, yet that warmth couldn't smother the bitterness and confusion underneath. She quickly murmured an apology, soothing them with a few words, forcing herself not to let anything strange show. Seeing that aside from her pale face and weary expression she seemed fine, everyone finally set their dangling hearts at ease. Jin Lan fussed and nagged for a good while longer, telling her never again to wander off alone after drinking, before they all gathered their things and filed out of the room one by one.
By the time they stepped out of the KTV, the night had grown deep. The north wind carried a chill, and it bit at her face, sobering her in an instant—though it couldn't blow away the chaotic images in her mind.
Yu Hao insisted on driving her home. Yao Yuan didn't refuse—she genuinely had no strength left.
The car was eerily quiet. Yao Yuan leaned back against the seat, eyes closed, pretending to be asleep. But beneath her lids her eyeballs trembled faintly. All the fragments churned in her mind—the despair of thirty-six, the bewilderment of twenty-nine, Zhuge Jinye's future, a fate that could not be changed…
The car came to a steady stop beneath her building. Yao Yuan slowly opened her eyes and unbuckled her seatbelt, but didn't get out right away.
Everything that had happened that night was too chaotic—the exhaustion of time-travel, the helplessness of witnessing the past, the lost and overwhelmed feeling about the future, plus the alcohol—her thoughts were jumbled to the breaking point, body and mind both pushed to their limits. Right now all she wanted was to hide away alone, to quietly digest every emotion, to face no one and say not one more word.
She raised her eyes to Yu Hao, the exhaustion and the weight on her mind no longer hideable, her voice as light as a feather. "I'm all right now. You head back. I won't ask you up."
Yu Hao turned to look at her. His profile was half-lit, half-shadowed in the glow of the streetlamp outside the window, his gaze deep, as if it could see straight through all of her feigned calm. He was silent for a few minutes, but he didn't press, didn't insist—he only reached out and very gently tousled her hair, an almost doting gesture, so tender it stung the bridge of her nose.
"All right." That was the only word he said.
With that, he withdrew his hand without the slightest hesitation, lingered not a moment longer, and turned to walk toward his own car. His back was straight and upright, yet it carried a quiet, understanding kind of retreat, gradually vanishing into the night.
Yao Yuan sat in the car, gazing in the direction he'd gone, unmoving for a long time. The north wind slipped in through the gap in the window, carrying its chill. She leaned back against the seat and finally let every disguise fall away, her eyes filled with bewilderment and pain.
She only felt tired. An exhaustion that seeped out from the marrow of her bones, as though someone had drained her of all her strength.
She didn't know how long she sat in the car, until the cold had soaked all the way through her, before she finally pushed the door open and dragged her heavy steps upstairs.
She opened the door to her home, and a wave of cold emptiness met her. Without turning on the lights, she groped her way to the side of the living-room sofa and sank into it completely, as if sinking into the deep sea.
Moonlight leaked in through the gap in the curtains, casting a pale streak of light across the floor.
She just curled up there in the darkness, letting those chaotic thoughts careen wildly through her mind. The little moments with Zhuge Jinye when she was twenty-nine, the weariness and pain in her twenty-nine-year-old eyes…
So the past she'd fought with everything to change had, in the end, been in vain. So the pain she'd thought she could sidestep, the endings she'd thought she could reverse, had been written in stone by fate long ago. Each time she traveled through time, she had merely glimpsed the ending in advance, yet still had to walk through every hardship with her own two feet—worse, she had to bear the double torment of foreseeing everything and being powerless to change it.
What was the point?
She asked herself over and over, but there was no answer—only the ceaseless dull ache in her chest, reminding her how real that conversation across time had been, and reminding her that she could never, in the end, escape a life already laid out.
The alcohol still worked faintly in her veins, and exhaustion surged over her whole body like a tide, yet she felt not the slightest urge to sleep. She raised a hand to cover her face, and from between her fingers a suppressed sob slowly seeped out. She didn't cry aloud, but she was more grief-stricken than at any other moment.
She thought of her younger self asking, "Do you have any advice for me?"—thought of how she'd never managed to give all the warnings she meant to—thought of that line, "Together we were ecstatically happy; apart, we each found our own peace"—and her heart ached all the more.
That was the sweetest memory of all the rest of her life, and yet, knowing the ending full well, she could only watch helplessly as her younger self rushed headlong in, watch that love begin, burn white-hot, and then move toward its parting.
The moonlight outside shone in through the gap in the curtains, casting a cold, clear stripe of light across the floor. Yao Yuan stayed curled on the sofa like that, letting the confusion, the regret, the pain in her heart spread unchecked. She didn't know when the next time-travel would come, didn't know whether she'd ever see her younger self again, and least of all knew with what frame of mind she should face the days ahead.
Only in this moment did she finally understand: some fates simply cannot be fought; some meetings are destined only to be remembered.
Not until the sky outside began to pale did she sink, in utter exhaustion, into a heavy sleep. In her dreams there was still the abyss into which she endlessly fell, and her own face in countless mirrors.
When Yao Yuan woke, the daylight was already bright.
She was lying on the bed in her own bedroom, the morning light filtering through the gauze curtains and scattering soft patches across the teak floor. Everything from last night—the faces of her friends in the [Crimson Night Palace] room, Yu Hao's silent gaze, and the eyes of her twenty-nine-year-old self in the mirror, brimming with tears—all of it was now frighteningly clear.
Thirty-six years old.
Yao Yuan sat up and walked barefoot to the full-length mirror. The woman in the mirror wore a silk robe, her long hair slightly tousled, a faint line at the corner of her eye that you wouldn't notice unless you looked closely—a mark left by the years. But her eyes were calm. She had been through betrayal, loss, the hardship of building a company; she had also known white-hot love and resolute parting. She was the "Godmother of Feelings" her fans spoke of, a company partner, the Teacher Yuanyuan who could make a breaking-down woman stop crying with just three sentences in the livestream room.
Yet in this moment, looking at herself in the mirror, she suddenly felt like a stranger.
"Godmother of Feelings." She murmured the title under her breath, the corner of her mouth tugging into a very faint, almost self-mocking curve.
What an irony. She could dissect feelings for countless others, point them out of their confusion, yet she couldn't untangle this snarled mess of her own life. Time and again she had traveled through time, trying to change the past, only to discover in the end that not an ounce of the pain she was meant to suffer had been spared, and not a single step of the road she was meant to walk had been avoided.
Fate was like a meticulously woven net. She'd thought she'd leapt clear of one knot, only to land on another, more tightly bound, intersection of its threads.
Yao Yuan walked into the bathroom and turned on the tap. The cold water splashed against her face, stinging her skin and bringing her fully awake.
She had no consultations scheduled today, and no livestream. The morning was time set aside for herself.
She brewed a pot of hand-drip coffee and carried the cup to the floor-to-ceiling window of her study. From the height of the twenty-seventh floor, she could see half the city's skyline waking in the morning light. Traffic streamed like a woven cloth, pedestrians moved like ants, each person rushing along their own track.
But how many of them had ever wondered: who laid down these tracks?
Yao Yuan took a sip of coffee, the bitter liquid sliding down her throat.
She thought of last night, in that pure-white space, when her twenty-nine-year-old self had said, "I want to walk it myself, and see whether the road goes through."
The stubbornness of youth, with the naivety of a moth flinging itself at the flame. Yet it was precisely that naivety that had led her through everything that came after—that fierce, fruitless love with Zhuge Jinye, the ups and downs of her career in Shanghai, being betrayed by people she trusted, and then gritting her teeth and climbing back up, founding her own brand, becoming the Yao Yuan of today.
If there were also a "future self" from beyond thirty-six who came to tell the present me not to make the "Pretty Big One AI Emotional Model," would I obey outright?
And if the twenty-six-year-old Yao Yuan back then had truly listened to the "thirty-six-year-old me" who came back from the future—her own future self's warning—and left Shuai Hongqiang immediately, gone straight to Shanghai, would her life have been better?
Maybe she wouldn't have gone through that heartbreak. But maybe, too, she wouldn't have ended up at that MCN agency in Shanghai, pushed into an even deeper abyss by the boss she had once trusted with her whole heart.
That unfinished warning—"Whatever you do, do not, with him…"
Yao Yuan's fingertips tightened, the warmth of the cup pressing against her palm.
It was Ding Shitong. Her Shanghai boss—the man who had once taught her, hands-on, how to invest, how to operate a business, who had brought her into the industry, and who, in the end, just as her wings were growing strong, had used the filthiest means to kick her out of the game.
If her twenty-nine-year-old self had known in advance, would she have steered clear? Maybe. But if she'd steered clear of Ding Shitong, would she still have founded her own brand? Would she still have become today's "Godmother of Feelings"?
Fate was never a simple game of "dodging the pitfalls." Every choice, right or wrong, was tied to flesh and blood, and had grown into the bones she carried now. Pull out any one piece, and she might no longer be who she was today.
Yao Yuan set down her coffee cup and walked to the bookshelf. An entire wall of books—psychology, sociology, relationships, personal growth, and quite a few volumes of philosophy and religion. After becoming a feelings blogger, her reading had soared; she was always trying to dredge up, from the wisdom of others, a few lines that could soothe the human heart.
But this time, what she pulled out was a very thin booklet—a modern annotated edition of "Zhuangzi: On the Equality of Things." The page that fell open read: "In being born there is dying; in dying there is being born. In the possible there is the impossible; in the impossible there is the possible."
All things are in flux; right and wrong have no absolute boundary.
She closed the book, her gaze settling on a sandalwood box in the corner of the shelf. She walked over and opened it. Inside were the mirrors she had collected, bit by bit, over the years. There was a handmade Venetian mirror she'd picked up while traveling, a cloisonné enamel vanity mirror a friend had given her, and a very small vintage hand mirror set with dark-red agate.
But not one of them could send her back into the past the way the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror did.
Why mirrors?
Yao Yuan sat back down at the desk and opened her laptop. She didn't search keywords aimlessly the way she had when she was young. First she sent her assistant a message: "That background research I had you look into last time, on the 'Winter Auction' held in Jinshi eight years ago—did you find it? Especially the roster of resident antique appraisers, the master surnamed Chen who specializes in authenticating antique bronze mirrors—did you locate him? I want it detailed: age, credentials, lineage, contact information. Also, look into whether there's any literature or folk record about the term 'Mirror Pact.' Compile every lead you find and send it to me."
After sending the message, she leaned back in her chair and closed her eyes.
