She had heard plenty of bizarre, otherworldly stories: people who claimed to have dreamed of a past life, people convinced they were "cursed," people who always felt their lives were controlled by some invisible hand. In the past, she had mostly explained these with psychology—trauma flashbacks, the workings of the subconscious, compulsive repetition.
But when this kind of thing happened to her, every theory seemed pale and useless.
Three time-travels, all connected to mirrors. The first was the makeup mirror in her bathroom at home, the second the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror, the third the wall mirror in the KTV room.
According to Shuai Hongqiang, ever since she had sealed the Mirror Pact with the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror, any mirror could serve as the medium for all three of her time-travels—but the trigger was a violent surge of emotion in her, stirring up the buried feelings she had never properly dealt with.
The first time-travel may have happened because of that day's livestream call-in: that girl, freshly graduated from college and "dumped," whose situation was so much like hers and Jiang Hai's, triggering the past she'd buried and the hidden ache that had never fully vanished from her heart.
The second time-travel may have happened because she'd gone to eat at a mall and seen Shuai Hongqiang with his family—that adorable little boy stirring up the regret she pretended not to care about but had buried deep inside.
And the third time-travel? Yao Yuan sorted through everything from the day before and reached a conclusion: yesterday's livestream call-in had several women who, in seeking a partner or in their married lives, had overlooked the importance of ██—failing to grasp the importance of "physical attraction" between men and women. Later, catalyzed by the alcohol, it had triggered her own "sweet frosting."
And there was one more thing Shuai Hongqiang hadn't said: how to undo the Mirror Pact.
What she needed to do now was start from a professional appraisal channel and trace the origins of the "Mirror Pact" and similar objects.
After sending the message, Yao Yuan turned back to the computer screen. She opened an encrypted folder containing a "case library" she had built as a psychology-and-feelings blogger—all personal information had, of course, been anonymized. But it recorded many "sensations," "dreams," or moments of déjà vu described by those involved, things hard to explain by common sense.
She typed in the keywords "mirror," "recurring dreams," "temporal illusion."
A dozen or so records popped up. She read through them one by one.
One woman kept dreaming of a shattered mirror, and each time she woke, she could find some unlucky real-life event that corresponded to the dream. Later this blogger had guided her back through her memories, and discovered that as a child she had once broken her mother's most treasured vanity mirror and been severely punished for it. That mirror had become a symbol of fear in her subconscious.
There was also a boy who always said he could see another "self" in the mirror doing different things. A psychological evaluation ruled out mental illness, and in the end it was found to be a severe dissociative tendency, rooted in childhood trauma.
These could all be explained by psychology.
But her situation was different. That was no dream, and no hallucination. It was a real, conversable "time-travel" in which she could perceive emotion and the body. And what's more, what she saw was her "past self," and she could speak with her.
This touched on a more fundamental question: self-identity.
Who is "I"? Is it this present conscious subject, here and now, or an existence formed by countless "past-moment selves" strung together in continuity? If the "past me" can hold an independent conversation, is she still "me"? Or is every "momentary me" an independent, parallel existence?
Yao Yuan thought of the "many-worlds interpretation" in physics—every choice splitting off a parallel universe. So in countless parallel universes, were there not countless Yao Yuans, having made different choices, living utterly different lives?
In one universe, she might never have left Beijing, tangled with Jiang Hai for a lifetime.
In another universe, she might have steered clear of Shuai Hongqiang and gone straight to Shanghai, only to meet some other setback.
In yet another universe, she might have ended up happily ever after with Zhuge Jinye…
Then how had this universe of hers—this universe in which she became "Godmother of Feelings" Yao Yuan—been the one chosen? Would there be another "future Yao Yuan," based on the present her, who would come to help her correct her present?
And what role did mirrors play in all of it? A passage? A trigger? Or… the proof of some kind of pact?
"Mirror Pact."
Yao Yuan softly read the two words aloud. She opened a new document and began to record:
Hypothesis One: The mirror is a special medium that, when a person's emotions swing violently or their consciousness is weakened, can connect the "self-consciousness" across different times and spaces.
Hypothesis Two: This connection is not unlimited; it seems to follow "rules." So far, it appears she can only meet her self at specific ages (22, 26, 29), and the time for communication is limited, forcibly cut off.
Hypothesis Three: The content of the communication may influence the future, but to a limited degree, and may incur an unforeseeable "cost" (as in the Shuai Hongqiang incident—the process changed, but the ending was similar, or even worse).
Hypothesis Four: The mirror itself may have a "binding" or "contract" attribute. The term "Mirror Pact" hints at some kind of agreement or constraint. The exact meaning needs to be uncovered.
Having written all this, Yao Yuan leaned back in her chair and let out a long breath.
All these years, she had used reason to take apart emotions, used logic to analyze relationships. But when the problem landed on herself—especially a problem that defied common sense—she found that she still panicked, still feared, still didn't know what to do.
But she had also learned one other thing: rather than passively endure, better to actively seek answers.
If this was her fate, then she would know clearly, too—just who had decided this fate, and how it could be broken.
The phone on the desk buzzed. Her assistant had sent over the preliminary materials.
"Boss Yao, on the Winter Auction side, there are three veteran masters skilled at authenticating antique bronze mirrors. One of them, a master named Chen Yanqing, is seventy-three this year. He studied under the late grandmaster of antiques, Old Master Zhou, and is said to have a particular expertise in obscure, out-of-the-way old objects. He commands great respect in the circle, but his temperament is rather reclusive and he rarely appears in public. His contact information has been sent to your email. As for the 'Mirror Pact,' public material is extremely scarce. I only found someone mentioning the term on a very small folklore-research forum, saying it has to do with some ancient folk notion of an object 'recognizing its master' or a 'spatial medium,' but the account was vague. I've tried to contact the person who posted it, but they haven't replied yet."
Yao Yuan replied: "Set up an appointment for me with Master Chen Yanqing, as soon as possible. Tell him I have an old family-heirloom mirror I'd like him to take a look at. Be courteous about it."
"Will do, Boss Yao."
Next, then, was to go see that Master Chen Yanqing.
Yao Yuan shut off the computer and walked to the window. The sun was just right, the city's skyline clear and resolute in the light.
She suddenly recalled something her dance teacher had said many years ago: "When you dance, don't always be thinking about whether the next move is right. You have to trust your body—it remembers all the rhythms. Just dance. Wrong is right, and right is wrong; only when you've finished is the dance complete."
Perhaps life was the same.
Always thinking about correcting the past, always worrying about choosing the wrong future, you only ended up trapped in the present, unable to move.
Since fate had given her this mirror, given her this power to travel through time, then she would use it—to see clearly, to understand, and then, to choose.
Even if, after the choosing, it was still the track laid out for her.
But this time she would walk it with her own eyes open, step by step.
Yao Yuan turned and walked into her dressing room.
She picked out a fitted leather jumpsuit-dress, tailored close to the body but never tight, the waistline cinched to just the right degree—simple and striking, with a cold, alluring air all its own. On her feet, a pair of black high-shafted boots, crisp and icy. Paired with a luxurious fur coat, it was at once regal and seductive.
She did her makeup carefully before the mirror, drawing her brows and setting her face, finishing with a stroke of rich, vintage true-red lipstick, bright and dazzling.
The woman in the mirror—calm of gaze, composed of bearing.
She was Yao Yuan, the Godmother of Feelings whom everyone looked up to. She was the Yao Yuan who had been through three time-travels and seen her past selves. She was the Yao Yuan who now carried a riddle in her heart, about to set out and probe the hidden truth behind the mirror.
And she was, too, the Yao Yuan who would keep walking forward, no matter what the truth turned out to be.
Picking up her handbag and car keys, she took one last look at herself in the mirror, then turned and pushed the door open and out.
Beyond the door was the world she had to face, and the answers waiting for her.

========================================

Chapter 35 — Shuai Hongqiang's Stroke of Luck
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640282.html

Shuai Hongqiang's Stroke of Luck
Meanwhile, on the other side.
Shuai Hongqiang was jolted awake by the frantic ringing of his phone.
He had slept deeply the night before, so deeply it was like sinking into a pile of cotton, without a single dream. In the last three years of his life, that was a rarity. Ever since his project payments started getting held up — with material suppliers showing up at his door every few days and the bank calling nonstop to chase its debts — he hadn't slept a full night in a long time.
Then, two days ago, the ten million in city-government project funds that had been owed to him unexpectedly landed in his account. The first thing he did was pay off eight million in bank loans and settle the largest material debts. The thousand-pound boulder that had been pressing on his chest finally hit the ground.
And because that weight had lifted from his heart, last night he'd fallen asleep the moment his head touched the pillow — and slept unusually sweetly.
Now the name flashing on his phone screen made him bolt upright with a jolt — "Director Zhang, Municipal Housing and Construction Bureau."
His heart lurched. He knew this number all too well. Over the past three years he'd dialed it no fewer than a hundred times — mostly to be brushed off, occasionally to be scolded, never once with good news. He took a deep breath, cleared his throat, and answered, his tone carrying the habitual deference with a barely perceptible note of exhaustion. "Hello, Director Zhang. Good morning."
To his surprise, the voice on the other end was no longer coldly official. It even carried a rare warmth. "Boss Shuai. I didn't disturb your rest, did I?"
Shuai Hongqiang froze and instinctively glanced out the window — the sky was only just turning gray with dawn. "No, no. Go ahead, Director Zhang."
"It's like this. Do you have time this morning? Nine o'clock, come to the conference room on the third floor of city hall. The preparatory team for that public fitness center project has a few things they'd like to talk over with you."
The public fitness center? Shuai Hongqiang's mind raced. That was a flagship public-welfare project the city had put on its agenda last year. The investment was said to be considerable. But a small-time contractor like him — owed over ten million, his reputation in the trade nearly in tatters — couldn't even get near something like that. Why would they come to him?
"Director Zhang, do you mean to say…"
"It's hard to explain over the phone. You'll see when you get here. Oh, and," Director Zhang paused, lowering his voice a little, "that outstanding balance on your old Culture Plaza job — five million, right? I've already had a word with the finance department. The paperwork should go through today; the money should reach your company's account by this afternoon at the latest. It dragged on this long because our office didn't do its job properly. Don't take it to heart."
Shuai Hongqiang gripped the phone, his fingertips going ice-cold in an instant, then a scalding heat surged up after. He opened his mouth, but his throat felt stuffed with cotton, and he couldn't get out a single word.
"Hello? Boss Shuai? Did you hear me?"
"I — I heard you!" Shuai Hongqiang snapped out of it, his voice trembling slightly. "Thank you, Director Zhang! Thank you, sir! I — I'll be there right on time!"
After hanging up, he sat on the edge of the bed and didn't move for a long while. The faint light of early morning slipped through the gap in the not-quite-drawn curtains and fell on his face, reflecting the blankness in his eyes and an overwhelming sense of unreality.
Five million… that was settled too?
And the public fitness center project… they wanted to talk to him about it?
His heart pounded in his chest like a drum — not from pure joy, but from a violent mix of elation, suspicion, and a deep unease.
The third unexpected "stroke of luck."
She must have triggered the Time-Reversal Talisman again.
This was the third time.
And each time, the luck that came to him was more direct than the last.
Shuai Hongqiang raised a hand and scrubbed hard at his face. His palm came away damp — he couldn't tell if it was sweat or something else. He walked to the window and yanked the curtains open. The morning light stabbed at his eyes; the distant honking of cars on the road below drifted up. Everything was vivid and real.
But how had all of this come about?
Was it because of that mirror, because of the so-called Mirror Pact? Because he had unwittingly become the bearer — while Yao Yuan, that seemingly calm, powerful, all-capable Godmother of Feelings, was paying some price he couldn't comprehend, a price that crossed time and space? And those costs were converted by the Primordial Purple Energy sealed inside the bronze mirror into his good fortune?
The thought churned in his stomach. He couldn't say whether it was fear or shame.
He, Shuai Hongqiang, was no saint. After more than a decade scrabbling around in the construction trade, he'd seen plenty of gray areas, and he'd gotten his own hands muddy along the way too. He craved success. He craved a turnaround. He craved the chance to pay the workers the blood-and-sweat wages he owed them, to stand tall in front of his parents back home. He needed this luck — craved it, even.
But… what if this luck was built on another person's pain, even on some unimaginable overdraft against "fate" itself?
The phone buzzed again — a text from the bank confirming the deposit. He stared at that long string of numbers, the kind of figure he'd once only seen in his dreams, and now it felt like it was scalding his hand.
At nine-fifty that morning, Shuai Hongqiang was already standing in front of the imposing city hall building. He'd put on his best suit and shaved clean. He took a deep breath and walked inside.
The meeting went far more smoothly than he'd imagined — you could even call it "friendly."
Director Zhang, who chaired the meeting, and a few other officials were warm and gracious. They praised Shuai Hongqiang's diligence and sense of responsibility on the old Culture Plaza project (despite three years of withheld payments), the solid quality of his work, and the fact that he'd never once shortchanged his laborers' wages (the last line he'd held). Then the conversation pivoted to the public fitness center project, set to officially launch after the New Year. They said they needed a partner like him — "experienced, with a strong reputation" — to take part in building some of the venues.
"Consider it making up a little for the trouble the withheld payments caused you," Director Zhang said with a smile, his tone as light as casual small talk. "But Boss Shuai, the project has high standards. You'll need to put together your best crew — no slipping up."
Shuai Hongqiang nodded over and over, made his promises, voiced his resolve. He drew on years of practiced caution and deference toward clients and handled it all without a single misstep. But only he knew that the back of his shirt was already soaked through with a small patch of cold sweat.
It wasn't nerves. It was a deeper unease.
This was all too smooth — smooth like a carefully staged play. And he was like a lucky man suddenly shoved to center stage, the spotlight on him so warm it burned, yet he couldn't see where the light came from, much less know what the next scene in the script would be.
When the meeting ended, he politely declined the officials' invitation to "grab a quick bite together" and all but fled the city hall building. Once he was seated in his old-model BMW X6, hands on the wheel, they were still trembling faintly.
Sunlight poured in through the car window, dazzlingly bright. The phone in his pocket felt heavy, holding the electronic copy of the letter of intent they'd just settled on, and that text about the five million landing in his account.
He should have been happy. He could pay off the suppliers; his company could survive; and now he had an even more promising project. He should have laughed out loud, should have immediately called the brothers who'd toughed it out alongside him, called his wife at home to share the good news.
But he couldn't laugh.
What kept surfacing in his mind were Yao Yuan's eyes.
He pulled out his phone, his fingertip hovering over Yao Yuan's number for a long moment before he finally pressed it.
The phone rang several times before she picked up.
"Hello?" Yao Yuan's voice came through, calm, even carrying the brisk efficiency she usually had when handling business. Nothing seemed off. But Shuai Hongqiang still keenly caught the thread of exhaustion underneath it — heavier than before.
"Yuan — Yao Yuan," Shuai Hongqiang licked his slightly dry lips, trying to make his voice sound natural. "It's me, Shuai Hongqiang. Have you… been all right lately? Have you been time-trav — having any strange dreams again?" Though they both tacitly understood about the time-travel, when the words reached his lips, he still couldn't bring himself to ask outright.
The line went silent for two seconds. Those two seconds sent Shuai Hongqiang's heart up into his throat.
"I'm fine, just a little tired. Thanks for asking." Yao Yuan's tone didn't waver, and she cut straight to the point. "Actually, there's something I wanted to ask you. That old master you mentioned before — the one at the winter auction who specializes in appraising bronze mirrors — surname Chen, full name Chen Yanqing, right? When you brought him up before, did you mean he knows more about the Mirror Pact? I need to contact him immediately."
Shuai Hongqiang's heart gave a thud. Just as he'd feared — she'd asked, and gone straight for the heart of it.
"Old Chen… yes, Old Chen, that's his name." His pace slowed without his meaning it to, his mind spinning fast. "But Yao Yuan, you want to find him… is it about that bronze mirror? Or…"
"I have my reasons." Yao Yuan's voice stayed calm, but it carried an insistence that brooked no argument. "Do you have his contact information, or know how to find him?"
Shuai Hongqiang's throat tightened. Of course he knew. That old master Chen Yanqing was one of the few old scholars in the trade who might genuinely know about something as mysterious as the Mirror Pact — and he was the one who had once hinted to him, "The mirror opens a way to the hidden; the pact, once made, carries fortune." If Yao Yuan went looking for him, her purpose was obvious — she wanted to uncover the truth of the Mirror Pact, and maybe even unravel it.
She can't be allowed to find him. A voice shrieked in the depths of Shuai Hongqiang's heart. Find Old Chen, ask him everything, undo the Mirror Pact — and your luck is cut off! That five million, that new project, everything within reach — it could all vanish!
"Yao Yuan," he weighed his words, feeling his palms start to sweat again, "Old Chen, he… really is an authority on this sort of thing. But the old man's getting on in years, his temper's a bit odd, and he doesn't much like being disturbed. And I just heard a couple of days ago that he seems to have gone down south — Hainan, or Yunnan, somewhere like that. Said he wouldn't come back until winter was over and things warmed up after the New Year. So right now… you probably can't reach him."
Having said all that, Shuai Hongqiang held his breath. What he'd said was half true. Old Chen's odd temper was real; so was his dislike of being disturbed. But going south for the winter — that was something he'd made up on the spot. He just needed time. He needed to keep Yao Yuan steady until the new project was fully locked in and his company stood firmly on its feet again.
The line went silent once more. This silence stretched longer, so long that Shuai Hongqiang could almost hear his own heart slamming against his ribs.
Just as he was about to crack and say something to ease the mood, Yao Yuan's voice came again, betraying no emotion, only a touch more detached. "Is that so. Gone south… won't be back until after the New Year."
"Yes, yes. The south's warmer, better for an old man to winter in." Shuai Hongqiang chimed in stiffly.
"All right. I understand." After saying that, Yao Yuan paused, then suddenly asked something that seemed unrelated. "Boss Shuai, today… did something good happen to you?"
Shuai Hongqiang's scalp prickled, and the words all but blurted out of him. "No — no! What good thing could there be? Same as always — running errands, chasing down debts…" The moment the words were out, he regretted them. Too deliberate. Too guilty.
Sure enough, Yao Yuan let out a soft "Hmph." The sound was faint, but it was like a fine needle pricking Shuai Hongqiang's eardrum.
"Is that so." She repeated those words, then said, "Then I'll leave it there. I've got things to do — I'll hang up now."
"Yao Yuan…" Shuai Hongqiang still had more he wanted to say, but the dial tone was already buzzing in the receiver.
He sat frozen in the driver's seat, gripping the phone. Outside the window the sunlight was still brilliant, the city hall building grand and solemn, the phone in his pocket carrying the hope of a turning point in his life.
And yet he felt cold all over.
That soft laugh of Yao Yuan's at the end, that "is that so" — they were like a cold key, opening the door in his heart labeled "dread." Did she know? Had she guessed? Had she long since sensed the unsettling link between her "time-travel" and his "good luck"?
And his evasions, his lies just now — in front of those eyes of hers that seemed to see straight through people, didn't they come off as clumsy and laughable?
Shuai Hongqiang buried his face deep in his hands and rubbed hard.
The phone buzzed again — a message from his project manager. "Brother Qiang, the suppliers heard the money's coming and they're over the moon! Asking when they can settle up. Everyone's counting on this money to get through the New Year!"
He leaned heavily back against the seat and closed his eyes.
The sunlight came through his eyelids as a field of blood-red, throbbing spots of light.
After a long while, he picked up the phone and sent the project manager a voice message, his voice hoarse but struggling to stay steady. "Tell everyone: this afternoon, by this afternoon at the latest, the money will definitely be in. Have everyone… hold on a little longer. It's almost done."
After sending the message, he lifted his head and started the car.
The roar of the engine filled the cabin. The car pulled slowly away from city hall and merged into the flow of traffic.
In the rearview mirror, that building — the symbol of where today's "good luck" had begun — grew ever more distant. And the road ahead stretched, under the bright winter sun, toward some far-off place he couldn't yet see.
He knew that ever since he had given that Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror to Yao Yuan, something had already changed. The taste of this "luck" was sweet at first; but once swallowed, it gave off more and more bitterness and dread.
But an arrow loosed from the bow doesn't come back.
Did he — did he even have a choice anymore?

========================================

Chapter 36 — (Side Story) Lin Wan's Marriage Advice for Newlywed Women
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640283.html

Lin Wan's Marriage Advice for Newlywed Women
"Good evening, everyone, and welcome to 'A Gentle Awakening.' I'm Lin Wan."
Under the ring light, Lin Wan's figure was wrapped in a soft amber glow. The corners of her lips held their habitual, precisely measured smile, and her voice came through the high-end sound card warm and assured.
"Tonight, we're going to talk about marriage again — the longest relationship of our lives, and the one that takes the most wisdom to manage."
The comments began to scroll:
[Newlywed Newbie]: Hi, Sister Wan! Married three months, here to take notes!
[Lost Wife]: +1. I always feel something's off after the wedding, but I can't put my finger on it.
[Old Fan Reporting In]: Here for Wan Wan to break down a new case!
"Old friends who've followed me for a while know that my marriage to Qiang has been through plenty of ups and downs, and now it's entered a new, relatively stable phase." Lin Wan's tone was calm, as if she were stating an accomplished fact. "And 'stable' doesn't mean smooth sailing. It means we drew up new rules for the relationship, redivided each other's rights and boundaries. And those rules usually hide in the smallest, most trivial moments of daily life."
She leaned forward slightly and lowered her voice, as if sharing a secret between close friends.
"For example, a couple of years ago, Qiang and I held a formal 'household-chores treaty' negotiation." A flicker of reminiscent laughter passed through her eyes. "It started over something tiny — washing the dishes."
The comments instantly lit up in agreement:
[Hahaha]: Dishwashing! The eternal war!
[Same At My House]: My husband wants to cram literally everything into the dishwasher!
[Team Hand-Wash]: Some things just have to be hand-washed! Even the feel is different!
"He's convinced the dishwasher is the greatest invention of the twentieth century — liberating the human hand." Lin Wan mimicked a certain unshakable tone, then pivoted. "But I believe precious porcelain, sharp knives, and his beloved tea sets all have to be hand-washed to show proper care. We argued over this 'matter of principle' on and off for three whole years."
She let out a soft laugh and shook her head.
"Until last week. I got up in the middle of the night for a drink of water and noticed the kitchen light was on. I walked over and found him secretly putting the glasses I'd hand-washed and polished to a shine that evening back into the dishwasher, one by one." Lin Wan paused, her gaze sweeping the camera. "In that moment, it suddenly clicked. This was never about 'clean or not.' It wasn't even about 'lazy or not.' It was about who, in the end, holds the power to set the rules inside the household."
Realization drifted across the comments:
[Mind Blown]: Right! You're not fighting over the thing — you're fighting over who has the say!
[Rule-Making Power]: Noted. New lesson learned!
[Wan Wan, hurry, tell us what to do!]
"I didn't blow up on the spot." Lin Wan's tone was even, edged with a sense of control. "The next day, I launched our 'rule-making procedure.' I took out a small whiteboard and listed every piece of tableware and cookware in the house, then sorted them into three categories: A, must be hand-washed; B, fine for the dishwasher; C, either way works. Then I handed him the marker and asked him to draw up his own list, independently."
Someone in the comments sighed: "She's brought project management into her marriage."
"The result was fascinating." Analytical light glinted in Lin Wan's eyes. "On my list, the 'must hand-wash' column had eighteen items. On his, only three. But here's the key: in that gray zone of 'either way works,' a full twelve items overlapped. See? There — the room to negotiate appears."
"I made my proposal. The twelve overlapping items would all go in the dishwasher from now on — easy and efficient. For the non-overlapping ones, each of us would pick three items from the other's 'must' list and respect their habit by hand-washing those. And as for whatever was left . . ." She winked slyly. "We'd settle it with rock-paper-scissors. Best two out of three. Win or lose, you honor the bet."
"He agreed. Partly because it felt like a game, which was fun. And partly," Lin Wan smiled, "because he was rather confident in his own luck at rock-paper-scissors."
The comments boiled over:
[Rock-paper-scissors?!]
[This is way too cute!]
[Real-world marriage psychology in action!]
"But what he didn't know," Lin Wan's smile carried a hint of the pleasure of outsmarting someone, "was that I'd done a little research on 'the psychology of rock-paper-scissors' beforehand. The data shows men are far more likely to throw 'rock' on the first round than anything else. So in the first game, I threw 'paper' and won. In the following rounds, I deliberately lost one or two to make the overall score look tense and fair. In the end, the agreement we reached was this: sixty percent of items follow the efficiency principle (dishwasher), twenty percent respect personal habit (hand-wash), and the remaining twenty percent are left to luck and play (decided by rock-paper-scissors)."
"So, ladies, this really isn't just about washing dishes." Lin Wan summed up, her voice clear and firm. "It's about how to get two grown adults with independent wills to willingly accept and follow the same set of rules for living — without hurting feelings, without sparking confrontation. The differences in age and gender showed themselves here: he cares more about results and efficiency, while I tried to wrap emotional value and a sense of fun into the rules. My 'tactic' was to package seemingly opposing emotional needs into a 'game' he could understand and even enjoy, so that, without realizing it, he stepped into the negotiation framework I'd set up in advance."
The comments filled with "learned something" and "can't take notes fast enough."
"Here's another one — the battle over household space." Lin Wan shifted in her seat, as if about to launch into another story. "Qiang is ten years older than me, which means he has many habits that are more entrenched, harder to budge. For instance, he insists the living room sofa must face the TV wall directly, and at a distance of exactly 3.5 meters. His reasoning: this is the 'optimal viewing-and-listening distance' he's researched."
"But I think the sofa should be placed by the window. Daytime natural light, the greenery and scenery outside — they matter far more than a TV screen, and they nourish the atmosphere of a home." She spread her hands. "On the surface, this is a dispute over aesthetics or function. Underneath, it's still a contest over who holds the say on household space."
"I tried just moving the sofa over while he was at work. He came home, said nothing, and quietly moved it back. I also tried reasoning with him — and he'd trot out a pile of acoustic and visual theory and tell me this was 'science.'"
A clever glint flashed in Lin Wan's eyes.
"Until one day, inspiration struck. I stopped trying to move that heavy sofa, and instead moved something else — his TV."
A string of question marks drifted across the comments.
"While he was away on a business trip, I had a technician come out and install a wall-mounted TV that could swivel through a wide angle. The sofa's position stayed exactly where it was, but this TV could rotate nearly sixty degrees to the left." She gestured with her hands. "The evening he got back, I deliberately sat reading in the single armchair by the window. He sat down as usual, turned on the TV, and then discovered — he had to twist awkwardly to one side just to see the whole screen."
"I didn't even look up. I just said, very casually, 'Looks like the TV can swivel. Want to give it a try?'"
Lin Wan mimicked her husband's stunned expression at that moment, to a tee.
"He picked up the remote, puzzled, and tried turning the TV. The screen rotated smoothly around and lined up perfectly with the sofa. Then, almost reflexively, he turned it a bit more toward me by the window — and found it lined up perfectly there too." She smiled. "I said nothing extra and went back to my book. But that weekend, he brought it up himself: 'Actually . . . maybe moving the sofa over to the window now and then, to catch some sun and look at the view, isn't bad?'"
The comments instantly flooded with "Brilliant!", "Elegant!", "Won without spilling a drop of blood!"
"But I didn't actually 'win.'" Lin Wan shook her head, her smile turning a little rueful. "His condition for agreeing to move the sofa was that I had to retire my cute little Bluetooth speakers and buy a professional-grade home audio system he'd long had his eye on. Because in his view, my old equipment was 'a grave insult to the art of film.'"
"So I 'shelled out' half a month of his salary and brought home that sound system. Now our sofa has the freedom to move, but the price is that every 'movie night' requires firing up that colossal contraption, and the volume and audio effects must meet the 'cinema-grade acoustic standards' he laid down."
"See — this is the real bargaining and compromise inside a marriage." Lin Wan summed up, her tone calm and clear-sighted. "You can rarely hold every piece of high ground. Every time you successfully push through one change, you usually have to give ground somewhere else. The differences in age and experience showed their playful side here — he's good at building defenses out of 'science,' 'data,' and 'professional standards,' while I tried to outflank him with psychology, indirect 'breakthrough points,' and creative solutions. In the end, what we got wasn't a living room that perfectly suited one person's wishes, but a compromise space — a movable sofa and a very professional sound system. And, more importantly, an unspoken understanding: every change has a cost, and every right comes with a trade."
The comments were full of feeling:
[Crying, it's so true]
[Marriage is one long negotiation]
[Learned it — not head-on collisions, but finding trades]
"So, some people say a good marriage requires you to 'kill' your old self. But the way I understand it, what it kills isn't your true nature or your sense of self. It's that overly naive, sentimental 'old self' carried away by unrealistic fantasies." Lin Wan's expression grew serious and profound. "A good partnership is never about binding each other down and wearing each other out. It's about growing together through friction, becoming a 'new self' that's clearer, more whole, and more powerful. It's the bad marriage that keeps devouring your dignity, your independence, and the light of your character."
"A home is a society in miniature. Spouses living together often advance step by careful step, weighing pros and cons, full of mental tug-of-war and testing. After a while, you really do sometimes feel like you're acting in a spy thriller." She joked, and the comments drifted by with "hahaha, so accurate."
"Of course, sisters, I'm absolutely not here to preach that marriage is some monstrous flood." Lin Wan pivoted, her tone gentle but firm. "Quite the opposite. Precisely because marriage is so complex, so close to the depths of human nature, we need all the more to set rules early and consciously. With clear, fair rules that both sides accept, this long 'partnership' of marriage can carry on more stably, more healthily."
"Recently, I received a long letter from a newlywed follower. She said she'd given so much for her husband, for their little family — attentive in every detail, thoughtful down to the smallest thing. But far from being grateful, her husband often picked at her and blamed her. She felt her heart had been thrown on the floor and trampled, her love betrayed. She asked me what to do; she felt utterly hopeless."
Lin Wan's voice dropped, carrying the force of empathy.
"My answer is this: your giving must be 'seen' by him. Your love needs to be properly 'received.' This isn't about claiming credit, and it isn't scheming — it's the positive feedback loop every healthy relationship needs. Let me tell you a story of my own."
The livestream room went quiet, the flame of the scented candle flickering faintly.
"This was back when we were still newlyweds. One morning, I woke at five-thirty." Lin Wan's voice was very soft, drawing everyone into that cool, early hour. "When the kitchen light came on, it was still deep blue outside; the day hadn't fully broken. I kneaded the dough quietly, stir-roasted black sesame, carefully ground the red-bean paste, wanting to make him a bowl of handmade glutinous rice balls. I knew he was under heavy pressure at work then, and his stomach wasn't great, so I made the dough especially soft and prepared two fillings — black sesame and red-bean paste — both gentle on the stomach."
"I tossed a crisp, light cold dish; the lettuce leaves soaked in cold water for ten minutes to make them crunchier and more tender. When I fried the egg, I controlled the heat with great care — I wanted a runny yolk, but the white had to be fully set, because he'd once mentioned offhand that he was a little squeamish about seeing liquid raw yolk."
Lin Wan smiled faintly, a smile holding some distant nostalgia and a trace of complicated understanding.
"At six-thirty, breakfast was on the table. The jade-white rice balls bobbed in the dark-red brown-sugar ginger soup, the lettuce a fresh green, the fried egg golden, all arranged on a fine white porcelain plate. Full of anticipation, I woke him, imagining the light that would come into his eyes at the first bite."
Her tone gradually lowered, taking on a faint, barely perceptible chill.
"He sat down, took one look, and picked up his chopsticks. His first words were: 'Aren't these rice balls cooked a bit too soft? Look at the skin — it seems kind of see-through.' Then he poked at the cold dish with his chopsticks: 'This lettuce — has it been sitting too long? The edges are a little wilted.'"
The comments slowed, the messages on screen thinning out, as if everyone could feel the air freeze in that moment and that silent, fine-grained chill seeping into their hearts bit by bit.
"In that moment," Lin Wan said softly, as if afraid of disturbing the memory, "I think I heard something inside me break — softly, crisply. Not the dishes. Some corner of my heart I'd been carefully cradling and keeping warm. I just stood there, listening to him go on about the soup being a bit too sweet, the egg's edges seeming a little overcooked . . . and all at once, I felt an exhaustion like nothing before. Not physical tiredness — my heart simply sank to the bottom, and I no longer had the strength to explain a single word, let alone argue."
"I didn't eat a single bite. I turned around, went back to the bedroom, and shut the door."
Lin Wan drew a deep breath, the sound of it clearly audible through the microphone.
"I sat on the edge of the bed, my mind blank and yet somehow crammed full. I thought back to our dating days, when I could cook the most ordinary bowl of instant noodles and he'd eat it with relish, praising it endlessly. I thought of when we were just married, the first time I tried to make a dish from his hometown, fumbling and making a complete mess of it — and he ate every bit of it, head down, and said it tasted like home. I thought of these past few months of marriage: no matter how early, as long as he was home, I wanted him to have a decent, hot breakfast."
"And then I asked myself: Lin Wan, what are you so hurt about? Is it really just because of those few critical remarks? Because the rice balls were cooked too soft, or the lettuce wasn't crunchy?"
She slowly shook her head.
"Not entirely. What hurt me was that the man who once would have run across three streets in the dead of a freezing winter morning just to buy me a cup of hot soy milk now couldn't see the heart I'd put into lighting up that kitchen for him in the cold of five-thirty a.m. What saddened me was that, somewhere along the way, marriage had turned some acts of giving into things 'taken for granted,' and turned some gratitude into 'nitpicking.' What chills you most is often not how much you do, but the other person feeling that you were simply supposed to."
The comments surged, full of resonance and ache:
[Bawling! Exactly the same!]
[Truly — it's not that the food was bad, it's that the heart behind it was ignored]
['Taken for granted' is the most poisonous phrase in a marriage]
[Wan Wan, hugs to you, and hugs to my old self too]
"But it was in that very moment that I suddenly understood something." Lin Wan sat up straight, her gaze clear and strong again. "The most terrifying thing in a marriage may not be the fierce quarrels, but this day-after-day, silent 'habit.' Growing used to the other person's giving, used to being loved, used to it until you go numb — to the point where you can no longer see how much sleepy early-morning effort, how much late-night thought, how many careful adjustments of the heat are hidden behind that love."
"And the most precious thing in a marriage is precisely when someone can suddenly wake from that 'habit,' look you in the eye, and earnestly say: 'I see you. I see everything you've done for me.'"
Her voice was gentle and firm, carrying a healing power.
"So I want to say, to that newlywed sister who wrote to me, and to all of you in front of your screens who give in love and yet sometimes feel let down: in a marriage, never let your giving fall into silence. Never let your love become the other person's blind spot."
"If you're the one giving, learn to say it. Not 'look how hard I work' to claim credit, but 'I got up early to make this specially for you,' 'I seasoned this to match your taste.' It's a gentle reminder — a reminder to each other that love needs to be 'seen,' and that the heart behind it needs to be 'acknowledged.'"
"If you're the one being loved, remember to say it. Not a perfunctory 'thanks,' but 'I saw it — you got up early and worked hard for me,' 'This tastes wonderful, I love it.' It isn't mere courtesy. It's an active response to love, the starlight that makes the giver feel it was all worth it."
"The person willing to be confined to the kitchen for you, studying the rice and salt and oil of daily life — the person afraid you'll go hungry, afraid you'll be cold, afraid you'll frown — was never born obligated to be that way. It's love that turns the eye-stinging cooking smoke into poetry, that turns trivial everyday life into the deepest attachment."
"And the one who is gently tended to, carefully favored," Lin Wan's voice grew light as a sigh, yet every word stayed clear, "you can keep your preferences, and you can even have a few harmless little nitpicks. But please, never, ever fail to see the love behind it all — the earliest morning light, the deepest dark of night, and one person's wholehearted, utterly unreserved care."
The livestream room was flooded with "tearing up," "thank you, Sister Wan," "I'm going to go tell my husband," the gift effects pouring in without pause.
"So, if you asked me for one key secret to keeping a marriage healthy?" Lin Wan asked and answered her own question, her gaze clear. "I think it's 'seeing.' Seeing the other person's giving, and seeing what you yourself receive; seeing the way love first made your heart race, and seeing the faint glow that still stubbornly flickers after it's been worn down day after day. And then, on some ordinary morning or night, by hand, gently, polishing it bright again."
The warm, embracing smile returned to her face, as if all the turbulence had settled into deep wisdom.
"May you all be gently 'seen' in your marriages. And may you always keep the ability to 'see' the other person, and to 'see' love. To see the one who keeps a lamp lit for you, keeps a bowl of soup warm, and waits for you to come home."

========================================

Chapter 37 — Killing the Old Self Inside a Marriage
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640284.html

Killing the Old Self Inside a Marriage
"Finally, let me share a little personal insight. Anyone who truly sees through the nature of marriage will understand this: a lasting marriage is never just the home that romantic love comes to rest in. It's more like a long collaboration, one that requires both sides to keep their dignity and to stand on equal footing. There's care in it, but also gamesmanship; there's tenderness, but also cool, rational calculation."
"But the woman I am now will never burn herself out again over some vague, intangible 'emotional value.' A good marriage is a journey two people take toward a new life together. At first I thought 'killing the old self' sounded too brutal a phrase, but later I understood. What gets 'killed' is never your true heart or your soul. It's the self that was too naive, too fragile, too full of unrealistic fantasies inside love; it's the obsessive, self-consuming, easily-lost old version of you from that relationship."
"It was the deep practice of marriage that let me break myself apart and rebuild myself with my own hands. I set down the infant's expectation of omnipotence, kicked the habit of endless emotional churn, shed my childish impulses of raw feeling, and learned to weigh things clearly and run things steadily. I threw away my fragile glass heart, gave up the dependent posture of pinning my whole life on another person, and through the polishing of the years and the friction of the relationship, I grew sturdier bones. I became a more independent, more clear-eyed self, with a steadier core."
"This was never compromise or surrender. It was a grand rebirth."
"And a genuinely healthy marriage is never a solo dance for one. It's a shared practice for two. Not draining each other, not dragging each other down, but you understanding how hard things are for me, and me understanding what you struggle with; you spurring me to become a better version of myself, and me walking with you through the fog that won't last. Inside this deepest of relationships, we sand down the edges that wound, correct the flaws in our characters, set aside stubbornness and self-interest, and toward the future we both long for, transform into more mature, more compatible, more complete partners."
"This so-called 'killing yourself' kills the old self that didn't know how to love itself, wasn't strong enough, lost itself too easily in a relationship. What it builds is a brand-new self that knows how to treasure itself, knows how to manage a relationship, and is full of inner strength. A good partner is a mirror that shows you your shortcomings; even more, a partner is a light that lights the road ahead, letting two independent lives reflect one another across the long years, growing together, both of them living out a freer, fuller shape."
"Know what the core need at the center of your heart truly is. Then let every word and every action revolve around that goal, clearly and firmly. Pursue it in the most efficient way, the way that spares the most of your energy and consumes the least of yourself. Once you can do that, you'll never fall into a passive position in any relationship again."
"It's like the 'plan' I later tailored for how Hongqiang and I get along. I take the lead in presenting myself as gentle, easy to talk to, even occasionally willing to be 'controlled' — a low posture that makes it look like he's the one in charge. But in truth, I'm the one quietly holding the overall rhythm of the relationship, the one who nudges the needle ever so lightly at the crucial moments. Only when I completely let go of greedily demanding 'emotional value,' and could instead supply it on demand — giving emotional support freely, dialing it up or down at will — did I truly become strong. The thoughtfulness, the tolerance, the empathy I offer are no longer lowly people-pleasing or helpless surrender. They're the clear-eyed choices of someone strong, an effective strategy and a controllable chip for protecting the relationship and reaching the goal."
"Human nature is often like this: the more desperately you crave something, the more easily that very thing has you pinned down, stuck in place."
The scrolling comments filled with deep reflection and praise:
[The mindset of the strong!]
[From 'asking' to 'giving' — the whole level changed]
[Stable core, the relationship follows me]
[Sister Wanwan, you're my internet mentor!]
"All right, sisters, that's where we'll end tonight's sharing." Lin Wan smiled into the camera, a warm and soothing smile. "If you have any confusion about marriage or about your feelings, leave a comment below and I'll try to read them. May you all keep your gentleness in a relationship, and also hold the strength of a clear head. Good night, see you next time."
She gave a light wave, the livestream image slowly dimmed, and the last frame froze on her calm, powerful smile.

Outside the study, the living room was lit by a single floor lamp. Shuai Hongqiang sat on the sofa at the border of light and shadow, the tablet screen in his hands long gone dark. He didn't get up right away. In the dim yellow light, he slowly finished the last sip of sobering tea at the bottom of his cup.
Everything about today felt like an unreal dream.
From the moment he got the invitation for the city's "National Fitness Center" project, that dazed, walking-on-clouds feeling hadn't stopped. Then there was the construction payment that had been owed for more than three full years, the one that had been eating him alive — ten million yuan, and it had actually landed in the account a few days ago. Just when he thought that was the whole of his luck, the remaining five million in the balance had, with no warning at all, hit the company account this afternoon too.
Fifteen million.
When that number flickered on the mobile banking page, Shuai Hongqiang stared at it for three solid minutes. Little Wang from finance was on the other end of the line, so excited he could barely string words together, while all he managed was a single "Mm." After hanging up, he realized his palms were soaked with sweat.
The money was in. The deadlock was broken. The boulder that had pressed on his back for three years crashed to the ground.
But what came with it was a deeper unease.
He thought of Yao Yuan, of those eyes of hers that seemed to see through everything, and of the warning from Old Chen, the antiques appraiser: "Everything has its cause and effect. The fruit you reap now is the seed you planted before; the seed you plant now is the fruit you'll have to repay in the future." This fifteen million that had finally been settled, this red-hot new project — they were sweet, fresh fruit, and yet his heart fluttered with dread. In the future, what kind of price would he have to pay for this "good luck"?
That evening, he did something he should have done long ago: he invited all the material suppliers he'd worked with, and owed money to, to the best restaurant in Jinshi. The private room was wreathed in smoke, wine cups clinking.
He stood up and raised his glass. The first toast was to their partnership, thanking everyone for their support over the years. For the second, he bent down and bowed deeply — for the goods he'd failed to pay for, and for those humiliating moments when they'd chased him all the way home, blocked his office door, and all he could do was say, red-faced, "Just a little longer." He apologized in earnest.
"I'm sorry, brothers. The material payments dragged on so long, and it put you all through hell. Thank you again for your loyalty and your patience these past stretches."
For the third toast he drained the cup in one go, then set it down, his voice clear and firm: "Every last material payment will be settled in full by tomorrow afternoon at the latest. Not a cent owed."
The room went quiet for an instant, then erupted into even louder commotion. Those faces that had once flushed with anger were now stacked with smiles, gripping his hand and calling him "Boss Shuai," saying "We understand, we understand," "It's hard for everyone," "We'll be counting on you to lead us to fortune from here on."
Amid the back-and-forth of toasts, he tossed out the olive branch of the "National Fitness Center" project too. The mood was pushed to its peak. It was as if all the squabbles, the complaints, even the curses behind his back during the debt-collecting days had never existed. He had turned back into that "Boss Shuai" everyone crowded around, riding high.
But only he himself knew that something was different. Beneath that "high" lay a layer of something hollow he couldn't name, and a faint worry over that "debt of gratitude" he owed Yao Yuan.
But whatever the case, the deadlock at the level of reality was finally untied. Coming out of the restaurant, with the night wind blowing, that long-lost confidence — the kind that belongs to a man — rose bit by bit from the soles of his feet, mixed with the buzz of the alcohol. He couldn't wait to get home, couldn't wait to see Lin Wan.
Most of all, he wanted to tell her: I've pulled through. This home can be carried by me again, the way it used to be.
He could finally put an end to the bind of the past half-year and more — an end to the humiliation of leaning on his wife's livestream income to supplement the household, pay their son's tuition, even cover his cash flow. That secret shame of being propped up, that unease and faint inferiority from feeling his role as "provider" teetering — it could finally be brought to a close. He was going to take back the place in this relationship that was most familiar to him, the place where he stood most firmly.
By the time he got home, Lin Wan's livestream was almost over. He didn't go into the study; he just sat down in the living room and watched silently on the tablet. On the screen her voice was calm, her analysis incisive, dissecting the daily gamesmanship between them like a sand-table war game. Listening, the corners of his mouth lifted on their own, and the restless heat of wanting to rush in and share his good news slowly settled.
The chime signaling the end of the broadcast sounded. He put down the tablet, got up, and went to the kitchen. From the temperature-controlled kettle he poured water at just the right warmth, scooped in a spoonful of the locust-flower honey Lin Wan liked, and stirred it slowly.
When had this habit started? It seemed to be right after Lin Wan's livestream income grew steady and truly began to carry part of the household's expenses. At first it had perhaps been an unconscious kind of compensation, a clumsy bit of thoughtfulness. Later it became a fixed, unspoken ritual at the end of every night's broadcast.
A faint sound came from the study door. Cup in hand, he turned and saw Lin Wan come out, a faint tiredness on her face after the high-intensity session, but her eyes still clear and bright under the warm yellow light.
"Master tactician of marriage," Shuai Hongqiang walked over and handed the honey water into her hands, asking with a smile, a lightness and anticipation in his tone that he himself hadn't noticed, "how was tonight's battle record?"
Lin Wan took the cup, her fingertips brushing lightly against his warm palm. She lowered her head and took a sip, the gentle sweetness sliding down her throat, then lifted her head, met his gaze, and gave a sly little smile: "A total victory. The enemy forces have been completely conquered by our side's wit and charm."
The same familiar lines, dressed up in pretend-joking. Shuai Hongqiang shook his head with a soft laugh and reached out to ruffle her neat, soft short hair — even this small affectionate gesture seemed long overdue. "Go wash up first," his voice dropped a little, carrying a flutter of excitement he couldn't hold back. "In a bit, I've got good news to tell you."
Lin Wan held the warm cup and took another sip, then raised her eyes and looked at him quietly. Their gazes met in the dim, hushed light of the living room, silently exchanging certain pieces of information only the two of them could understand.
On the wall, the clock's hands ticked quietly toward half past ten.
In the living room, their son Wen Yao's toys were tidied neatly into the woven basket; the warm aroma of the home-cooked dinner still lingered in the air; at the doorway of the children's room, a little night-light gave off a soft, fuzzy halo.
Another small "battle" with no smoke of gunpowder had drawn to a close. There was no declaration of who won or lost, but some new, subtle balance — or you could say the "terms of cooperation" for the next stage — seemed to have been silently confirmed and renewed in this cup of honey water at just the right temperature.
Tomorrow, life would turn a new page on its own, and a new game of chess, a new negotiation, might already be quietly taking shape. But at least in this moment, on this night, in this small patch of space wrapped in warmth and quiet, they shared this hard-won, unspoken "peace."
— It was the deepest understanding of an "opponent" worth taking seriously in the game, and even more, of a "partner" bound to her in fate; a complicated, hard-to-name respect.
Tonight, they walked side by side down the hallway toward the bedroom, their figures merging into one under the light.
The new tomorrow really did seem to be slowly unfolding. And the "good news" he was bringing might well be the very key to restarting some cycle. So Shuai Hongqiang thought, and for now the faint unease that Yao Yuan had stirred in him was pressed down beneath the solid warmth and anticipation right in front of his eyes.

========================================

Chapter 38 — Human Nature Is a Balance Sheet That Never Stops Fluctuating
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640285.html

The car headed toward the antiques market. Yao Yuan kept her hands on the wheel, but her thoughts stayed fixed on that Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror—the secret only she and Shuai Hongqiang knew, the three dislocations in space and time that no ordinary logic could explain. This was something she could tell no one. In the world of adults, the value of certain secrets lies precisely in the fact that they are kept airtight.
Her phone rang. It was Shuai Hongqiang. His voice carried its usual note of concern, but Yao Yuan's ear worked like the most precise voiceprint analyzer, and in an instant it caught the faint, unnatural frequency beneath that concern—he was probing.
"Old Chen?" Her eyes stayed on the traffic ahead, her tone as calm as if she were discussing the weather. "Have you heard from him lately?"
On the other end there was a pause, almost too slight to notice. "Old Chen, huh... I think I heard someone mention it—he went south for the winter, didn't he? Right, it's warmer down south. Old folks all like to go."
Yao Yuan said nothing. A lie. She knew the rhythm of a man lying all too well—that deliberately manufactured smoothness only exposed the haste and hesitation underneath. She just hadn't expected Shuai Hongqiang to lie to her, at least not about this.
"Is that so," she said, her tone unchanged. "What a shame, then."
She hung up and set the phone on the passenger seat. The car was quiet, only the faint sound of tires rolling over the road. She didn't immediately dwell on the lie itself; instead, out of habit, she began dismantling the chain of logic behind it:
Behavior: providing false information at a key node concerning Old Chen's whereabouts.
Surface motive: to delay or block her contact with Old Chen.
Underlying assumption: Shuai Hongqiang takes for granted that her contact with Old Chen would be bad for him, or at least would interfere with his plans.
Risk level: provisionally medium. This falls under information isolation and defensive maneuvering. It hasn't yet touched a core conflict of interest, but it marks the point where the trust threshold begins to wobble.
She quickly filed and tagged this assessment in the temporary case folder in her mind, the same way she would handle any instance of concealment between partners in a consulting case. The difference was that this time, she was the party involved.
The GuanguTang storefront was small, heavy with the musty smell of old things. The owner was a middle-aged man, head bent, polishing a jade thumb ring. When Yao Yuan asked after Master Chen Yanqing, he didn't even look up. "Master Chen? His daughter took him south for the New Year. He won't be back until spring."
Yao Yuan stood there without moving.
A sense of absurdity dripped into the deep pool of her consciousness like a single drop of cold water, sending out one faint ring of ripples. To stop her, Shuai Hongqiang had thrown out a casual lie—"gone south for the winter." And that lie had, by sheer coincidence, collided with the truth.
Sometimes life is just like that: the truths you carefully prepare go unbelieved, while the fig leaf you snatch up in a hurry happens to cover the sharp edges of the truth perfectly. An ironic, almost theatrical kind of "correctness."
She turned and left. There was no anger, only a colder clarity—when a coincidence is precise enough to raise suspicion, it usually stops being a coincidence and becomes the surface symptom of some deeper linkage.
Back at the office, her assistant had already left the organized files on her desk. Shuai Hongqiang's credit reports from the past five years, his company's cash flow, his debt breakdown. Yao Yuan put on the "human-nature audit glasses" she used for analyzing complex cases and turned the pages one by one.
The numbers were honest—more honest than people.
Three years ago, the pandemic became the turning point in many stories. Misjudged investments, large sums of withheld project payments—the lifelines of the several projects under his name had lit up red one after another. His credit record was clean, but the company's performance had nearly stalled. Eight million in bank debt, with litigation risk hanging over his head. Scattered unpaid bills to material suppliers and construction crews lay like dust along the edges of the balance sheet.
And then, just a few days ago—at a timing precise enough to make her eyelid twitch—the crucial municipal project payment that had been withheld for years was settled in full, in two installments.
Yao Yuan leaned back into her chair, her gaze drifting out to the jungle of steel and concrete. The setting sun plated the glass curtain walls with an illusory gold rim.
This money had arrived too "punctually." Punctual enough to look like a carefully staged rescue—and the moment that rescue signal was sent overlapped, subtly, with the point at which she had returned for the second time from the turbulence of the bronze mirror's space-time current.
She had no proof, only intuition. But in her framework of understanding, intuition was never mysticism. It was the high-speed computation the brain performed beneath the surface of awareness, drawing on a vast pool of latent information—a result not yet describable in precise words. Her intuition was sounding the alarm.
She needed to see Old Chen, had to see him. But Old Chen wouldn't be back until spring.
She had to wait.
In the days when waiting became the only option, Yao Yuan's thoughts would inevitably drift back to the man, Shuai Hongqiang. To think of him was to be unable to skirt around "human nature"—the most complex and unstable variable of all.
He was not a "good man" in the conventional sense, nor was he simply a "bad man." In fact, across the thousands of cases she had handled, she had long discarded that naive black-and-white binary. The purely good probably exist only in the lives of saints, while the purely evil can usually construct, even for themselves, a logically self-consistent "narrative of hardship" to justify their wrongdoing.
She had long judged people by a different set of standards: their base color, their demands, their behavioral patterns, and the choices they tended to make under specific pressures.
Shuai Hongqiang's base color was the earthy, raw "loyalty" and "substance" forged out on the streets. That was his fundamental ground. The good in him was real: the overbearing tenderness of draping his whole coat over her head in the rain while he himself got soaked through; the sense of responsibility that made him settle his ex-wife properly even at his own expense during the divorce (the twenty-six-year-old "her" inside the bronze mirror had even walked away with half his assets); the bottom-up survival wisdom of never being stingy about sharing resources with "his own people." These were the solid pieces of his personality puzzle, the ones that couldn't be sanded away.
But his limits were just as conspicuous. The ceiling shaped by his educational background and early experiences meant that on certain boards requiring higher-dimensional cognition, his processing power fell short. He might have the nerve for big moves, yet on small matters he could betray a petty, small-trader's calculation. And his concealment and lying in this bronze mirror affair clearly marked out his behavioral pattern under the pressure of a dead end: defensive concealment, a tendency to monopolize resources, and an instinct to keep even his closest allies walled off from information.
Why?
Yao Yuan's gaze fell back on the financial report. Eight million in debt, a cash flow on the brink of collapse, a life-saving sum that suddenly landed.
The answer all but spoke itself.
A person at the edge of a cliff grabs at anything as if it's the straw that will save him. To him, the bronze mirror was no longer a strange encounter or a love token—it was the one and only uncontrollable "variable" that could turn his desperate situation around. He didn't dare let her know everything. He feared she would fight him for the initiative, feared that after a rational weighing she would choose to stop it, feared that this last sliver of hope would slip through his fingers too.
This wasn't evil. This was the biological instinct of a creature in a dead end. Selfish, but real to the point of cruelty.
Yao Yuan understood that kind of reality. She could even calmly take apart the chain of logic within it: survival anxiety > perception of resource scarcity > intense desire to control the only variable > defensive strategy against potential competitors.
She only felt a deep, icy familiarity. For him, and for some past version of herself.
She hadn't been without resentment over their separation when she was young. She had resented that his calculations were never frank, resented that he wouldn't give a clear promise. Looking back now, how much of that resentment had come from a naive misreading of the complexity of human nature? And how much from a stubborn attachment to the fairy-tale premise that "love must be sterile"?
She didn't think that way anymore.
In the world of adults, everything is a mixture. Emotion inevitably carries calculations of interest; calculation may wrap a few strands of loyalty and feeling inside it; truth needs the lubricant of lies to move forward; and deep within certain lies may hide a truth no one dares to name. Just like Shuai Hongqiang—he was deceiving her right now, yet he was also genuinely struggling at the edge of the abyss. He chose concealment, but the real, tangible sacrifices he had made in the past couldn't be erased from the ledger of his life either.
Human nature is just this kind of chaotic asset, woven through with both longs and shorts. You can't strip them apart cleanly; you can only keep assessing their weights and risk coefficients across different situations.
That was why she could keep from clinging when they parted, and still sit at the same negotiating table when they met again. It wasn't that she had let go—it was a cognitive upgrade. She had seen the rules of the game clearly and set aside unrealistic illusions. Love and hate, grudges and passion, were too extravagant, the cost too high. Better to focus on the executable zone of cooperation where consensus could be reached in the present.
For now, her goal was to unlock the physical and space-time secrets of the bronze mirror. His goal was to use the mirror's mystery to get through his financial crisis. Different purposes, but converging paths in the short term.
So she would keep walking that converging path. Maintain cooperation, maintain observation, keep up the quiet retrieval of key information. Until the intersection appeared naturally, or until one party decided to unilaterally change course.
The assistant knocked and came in. "President Yao, we've confirmed Old Chen's daughter's exact address and contact information in Hainan. Should I start laying the groundwork to reach out now?"
Yao Yuan drew her gaze back from the window. "No need. Spring is still a long way off. Going on the offensive now would only expose our need and raise the other side's psychological price. We wait."
"Then on Boss Shuai's end..."
"Everything as usual." Yao Yuan closed the folder, her voice without a ripple. "The cooperation that should move forward keeps moving forward; the information that should be exchanged keeps being exchanged. Some cards, played too early, cost you the initiative at the table. Some truths can't be rushed."
The assistant left. The office sank back into silence, only the city's light dimming inch by inch beyond the window, like a tide pulling back.
Yao Yuan sat alone. Night seeped in through the glass, staining the outline of her form.
Life, perhaps, was a dynamic system built of a series of lies—some careful, some hasty—and countless coincidences, some inevitable, some accidental, all nested within and catalyzing one another.
Shuai Hongqiang had set up a roadblock with a lie, and the roadblock had unexpectedly become a signpost. The mist of time she had crossed through with the bronze mirror might itself have become his floating bridge out of a dead end. True and false, cause and effect, tangled in this moment into a chaos she couldn't immediately sort out.
The one thing she was certain of was that she needed the truth. The physical truth about the bronze mirror, the logical truth about the time-travel, and—most subtle and most crucial of all—the truth about the unfathomable thing called "the human heart" under extreme circumstances.
Until then, waiting was the only strategy. Wait for spring, wait for the ice and snow to melt and all things to revive, wait until the people who should be seen could be seen, the questions that should be asked could be asked, and the cards that should be shown had to be shown.
And until then, she would guard this secret as tightly as he did. Because some transcendent experiences become hysteria the moment they're spoken aloud; some key leverage depreciates the instant it's exposed.
She opened the drawer and took out the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror.
The surface was cold and hard, reflecting her face in a blur. The same brows and eyes, the same exquisite makeup. But something, beneath the mirror's surface, deep in her pupils, had undergone an irreversible chemical change.
She gazed at it for a moment, then returned it to the depths of the drawer. With a soft "click," the bolt slid home.
Wait for spring, then.
When spring comes, the frozen earth loosens; what lies dormant will wake, what is hidden will surface. Many unresolved questions might then find an answer—not a perfect one, but one real enough.
And before that answer was revealed, life still had to go on. With the secret, with the wariness, with the cool calculation, taking step after step through the net woven of lies and coincidences, toward the future she had chosen—or had no choice but to accept.
The afternoon's work proceeded in order. She handled several business partnerships that needed urgent decisions, turned down afternoon-tea invitations from two girlfriends, and signed off on the next phase of the content marketing plan the assistant had drawn up. Her efficiency was high, but her attention was as if behind a pane of frosted glass—the cold touch of the bronze mirror and that faint pause in Shuai Hongqiang's phone call kept flickering past at unguarded moments.
Just after four, she closed her laptop. Today was no day for tasks that demanded sharp rational focus. She picked up her car keys and decided to go to her mother, Zhang Fengxia.
When she reached the building, Zhang Fengxia was already waiting at the entrance, and handed her a thermos. "Just stewed some pear soup. You use your throat so much—soothe it a little." She pulled open the car door and got in, brisk in her movements, keeping between herself and Yao Yuan just the right measure of quiet distance, the kind that belongs to grown mother and daughter.
The sign for "Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot" glowed warm yellow against the deepening dusk. Inside, steam rose and voices clamored—another kind of reality. When Zhou Lufei saw them, his smile crinkled the corners of his eyes; he came hurrying out of the back kitchen rubbing his hands, the smell of beef tallow and spices on him.
"Yuanyuan, you came! Quick, help me take a look at this livestreaming—it's worrying me sick. Look here," Zhou Lufei rubbed his hands a little sheepishly. "I've gone live a few times. People come and go, but they just won't stay, and not many place orders. This mouth of mine—chatting with customers in the shop is fine, but the second I face this camera, I don't know what to say. Have a look, will you? Where's the problem?"
He didn't bother with small talk and led her straight to the "livestream corner" set up temporarily in the corner of the shop. Phone, fill light, microphone—the equipment was all there, but it gave off an air of clumsy effort.
Yao Yuan glanced over his livestream backend data: average viewing time under a minute, conversion rate pitifully low. She opened a replay clip. On screen, Zhou Lufei was a bit tense, reciting the set menu like he was reading off a recipe, his movements stiff. When he introduced the "hand-cut fresh lamb," it was like reciting a product manual—the merits laid out clearly, but missing the "soul" that hooks people.
"Uncle Zhou," she dragged over a stool and sat down, her tone the calm, assured one she used at work. "We're not 'selling' meat. We're 'treating guests.'"
She began breaking it down, speaking unhurriedly, every point landing solid. "Don't recite a script. Just imagine the person on the other side of the camera is an old neighbor you haven't seen in years. Talk like a human. Take this lamb—'stands up on the plate without falling' is trade jargon; the audience doesn't get it. You say: 'Look, this meat sticks to the plate—stand it up on its edge and it still won't slide down. Why? It's fresh, no added water, the fibers are tight. This is hind-leg meat from a Inner Mongolian lamb, from the parts that move the most, so it comes out tender with a good chew when you cook it.' Say it and do it at the same time—stand the plate up and show them. That's 'demonstrating value.' It works better than 'describing value.'"
"Camera presence is simple. Don't keep staring at yourself in the frame. Look at the lens like you're looking into someone's eyes. When you're cutting meat, look at the meat. When the soup boils, look at the soup. Be natural, like you're chatting with a regular in the shop. If you get nervous, just think of it as teaching an old friend the best way to cook hotpot."
She adjusted the angle of the fill light so the warm glow fell more directly on the vivid grain of the lamb and the milky white rolling of the pot. "Sight comes before taste. Make the light a little warmer, and the food looks more appetizing, more like the taste of 'home'—not 'merchandise.'"
Zhou Lufei took notes in a little book, nodding nonstop, his brow gradually smoothing out. Zhang Fengxia silently passed over two cups of warm water, one for her daughter, one for her husband.
With the coaching at a pause, the steaming copper pot was brought out. A clear broth base, with goji berries and red dates bobbing up and down. The hand-cut lamb was a tidy arrangement of red and white; the vegetables were fresh and crisp. The sesame dipping sauce was mixed by Zhou Lufei himself, rich and fragrant.
Cooking meat, picking up vegetables, idle chatter. In the warmth of the food's steam, the conversation grew loose. Somehow, the talk turned to Yao Yuan's childhood.
"You were stubborn from the start, always had your own mind." Zhang Fengxia picked up a slice of cabbage and let it swirl slowly in the pot, her tone flat, as if she were talking about someone else.
Yao Yuan gave a small "mm" and waited for her to go on.
"Remember that time in third grade? You were class monitor, collecting homework." Zhang Fengxia raised her eyes, her gaze passing through the curling steam to land on Yao Yuan's face.
The floodgate of memory was pushed open by one flat sentence. The hand holding Yao Yuan's chopsticks paused, almost imperceptibly. Of course she remembered.
It was third grade of primary school, and she was class monitor. The teacher had told her to collect the whole class's exercise books. Everyone else had turned theirs in—only one tall, sturdy boy, Wang Hu, sat tilting his chair lazily and said he hadn't done it and wouldn't hand it in.
A near-obsessive sense of duty had gripped little Yao Yuan. She felt it was a task that had to be completed. She stood in front of Wang Hu's desk, her voice quiet but clear. "You have to hand it in, or I can't account for it to the teacher."
"Not handing it in. None of your business," Wang Hu said impatiently, feeling humiliated at being pressed by a girl.
After a few rounds of back-and-forth, Wang Hu suddenly stood up and shoved her hard. The force was great. Caught off guard, she fell back onto the floor, her elbow and tailbone striking the cold, hard cement with a dull ache. But before the pain came the embarrassment and humiliation of being the focus of the whole classroom's stares. Tears rushed into her eyes, hot and stinging, and she bit down hard on her lip.
At the very instant when that mixture of pain, grievance, and the instinct to cry for help reached its peak, she instinctively turned her head and looked at the back door of the classroom.
Zhang Fengxia was standing there. No telling how long she'd been there—backlit, her figure a little blurred. But Yao Yuan knew her mother had seen the whole thing.
She looked at her mother, tears welling, a silent plea and accusation. She thought her mom would rush in, help her up, scold Wang Hu, maybe even go argue it out with the teacher.
But Zhang Fengxia didn't move. She just stood there, watched for a few seconds, then spoke, her voice without the slightest ripple, even a little cold and hard:
"What are you still lying there for? Aren't you getting up to go home?"
