Yao Yuan was stunned, forgetting to cry. She pushed herself up off the floor, stood on her own, patted the dust off her pants, lowered her head, silently picked up the scattered books (including Wang Hu's blank one), and walked to the door.
The walk home wasn't long, but the silence pressed down hard. As they neared home, Yao Yuan finally couldn't hold it back and asked, heavy with a stuffy nose, "Mom, why didn't you help me up just now? Why didn't you scold him?"
Zhang Fengxia stopped walking and turned around. The halo of the streetlamp traced the expressionless side of her face.
"I help you up, and then what?" She looked at her daughter, her gaze direct. "I scold him, and then what? Next time? The time after that?"
"Yao Yuan, you remember this," her voice wasn't loud, but every word was like a little hammer striking the heart that was still innocent back then. "The road of your life ahead is so long you can't see the end of it, and most of the time, you'll have to walk it alone. No one can be by your side every second, ready to lift you up the moment you fall, to stand up for you the moment you're wronged."
"If just now you felt wronged, felt he was in the wrong, then find your own way to take the round back. Use your own methods. Tell the teacher, tell the whole class he's a bully, or wait until you're bigger and stronger than him and hit him back—any of those is fine. But that's your business."
"If you don't have the ability to claim it back on the spot yourself, then don't count on someone else being sure to help you. Someone can help you once; they can't help you a whole lifetime. If you don't have the means today, then you can only endure it first, hold it in, remember it. When one day you have the ability, the means, then you can think about taking it back."
"When you fall, you climb up yourself—that's the first thing you have to learn. Always counting on someone else to lift you, and you'll never learn to watch the road, never learn to use your own strength."
That night's dinner, Yao Yuan ate in heavy silence. Her elbow still hurt, and her heart was even more clogged. But Zhang Fengxia's words, like a rough but hard file, carved themselves stroke by stroke into the understanding she still only half-grasped back then.
Now, sitting in the warm hotpot shop, listening to the gurgle of the boiling broth, smelling the familiar fragrance of sesame sauce, Yao Yuan slowly chewed that slice of sauce-dipped lamb. The meat was fresh and tender, the flavor rich.
"I remember." She nodded, her tone calm and unruffled. "Back then I resented you in my heart, thought you were cruel."
Zhang Fengxia gave a light snort through her nose and put a piece of frozen tofu in her bowl. "Resentment is right. If you hadn't resented it, would you have remembered it so firmly?"
Zhou Lufei, listening from the side, kept shaking his head. "Fengxia, the child was so small, and you—"
"Small?" Zhang Fengxia cut him off with a glance. "Small is exactly why you teach. Wait until the bones harden and the temperament is set, and you couldn't bend it back even if you tried." Her gaze fell again on Yao Yuan's face, no tenderness in it, but a strange certainty. "Look at her now. Does she look like someone who falls down at one shove and lies there waiting for others to lift her up?"
Yao Yuan didn't answer. Slowly, mouthful by mouthful, she ate the food in her bowl.
Maybe it really was so. Zhang Fengxia's near-cold "bystander" style of education, that way of carving the three words "rely on yourself" into her bones in the most painful manner, had invisibly shaped the hardest core of her character.
Not to show weakness easily, not to depend on outside help, accustomed to analyzing the situation, assessing the cost, and searching for solutions on her own after a fall. Grievance and pain could be felt, but she couldn't let herself be drowned by them. If she was powerless to resist for the moment, then endure calmly, store up strength, wait for the right time—rather than useless weeping or impulsive resistance. Lower her expectations of the outside world to the minimum, raise her demands on herself to the maximum.
This had forged her later independence, and also forged the clear sense of boundaries and near-instinctive rational weighing she brought to dealing with people. There were advantages and disadvantages. But in a complex world, this "survival logic," born of a single fall onto childhood cement, had indeed tempered her clarity and independence—and quietly sanctioned a certain degree of loneliness.
"The meat's about to overcook—eat up." Zhang Fengxia's voice broke into her thoughts, and another chopstickful of fresh, tender lamb shoulder dropped into her bubbling little pot.
"Mm." Yao Yuan lowered her eyes and focused on the food in her bowl.
The broth in the copper pot kept boiling, and the hazy white vapor blurred her mother's face across from her. Those distant, sharply painful lessons and the warm, tangible care before her merged in a strange way, forming the bond between them—both distant and tenacious.
Perhaps everyone's base color of character is a pattern, set at last after the imprint of one or several decisive moments in childhood has been painted over, tested, and revised again and again by the years. It was just that her base color had been laid down by Zhang Fengxia with an especially coarse brushstroke.
Yao Yuan thought: if one day she needed to sum up Zhang Fengxia's whole philosophy of education into a single "Godmother of Feelings" aphorism, it would probably be this:
"To recognize as early as possible the reality that 'in the end you have to rely on yourself' is the most hardcore, and the most precious, gift parents can give a child. It doesn't guarantee you'll never fall, but it guarantees that every time you do, you'll know how to climb back up—and which way to go."
Come to think of it, this bitter dose of clarity suited her livestream room rather well. One line, cutting straight to the essence.
She quietly finished the meat her mother had placed in her bowl. A solid texture, warm and comforting.
Outside the window, night had settled, and the road blazed with lights. Inside, voices, the clink of bowls and dishes, the bubbling of the soup wove together into a steady, homely market din. In this moment, there was no space-time riddle to crack, no human-nature leverage to weigh, no emotional tangle to dissect. Only one solid meal, a memory worn smooth of its sharp edges by time, and an unspoken, blood-deep accord of understanding.
For her, this was already a precious enough breathing space that life had granted.
Dinner ended close to eight. Zhou Lufei insisted on packing up an order of the shop's homemade sesame flatbread for her to take. On the drive back, Zhang Fengxia sat in the passenger seat, watching the streetscape flow past the window, and suddenly spoke, her voice clear in the quiet of the car.
"That Shuai Hongqiang—he's been looking for you again lately?"
The fingers on Yao Yuan's steering wheel tightened for an almost imperceptible instant, then relaxed. Her mother's information network was, on occasion, sharp enough to surprise her.
"Mm, there's some cooperation under discussion." Her answer was brief, free of emotion.
Zhang Fengxia was silent for a moment before she said, "That man—his heart isn't bad. But at the root, he's not the same kind of person as us. You keep that straight in your mind."
"I know." Yao Yuan watched the red light ahead turn green and slowly pressed the accelerator.
She knew what her mother meant. Zhang Fengxia read people with a near-instinctive sharpness honed by life at the bottom—she paid no heed to the halos or words floating on the surface, going straight to the bone. She hadn't said Shuai Hongqiang was bad; she'd only said "not the same kind of person." Within that lay an insight into his origins, his way of thinking, his logic of handling matters, even his ordering of values.
"As long as you know." Zhang Fengxia said no more, and changed the subject. "Remember to drink the pear soup."
After dropping her mother home, Yao Yuan drove alone back to her own apartment. The city's neon flowed past the car windows into blurred bands of light. Inside the car it was quiet, and the calm on her face slowly faded, revealing the deep deliberation beneath.
Zhang Fengxia's words were like a pebble dropped into the lake of her heart. She knew what her mother was reminding her of. Not a simple "be careful of men," but a reminder of the fundamental reason they had once parted.
She was used to rational calculation, risk control, clear boundaries. He, at his core, carried the gambler's streak of a street rover, an instinctive division between "his own people" and "outsiders," and a capacity, in a dead end, for action that broke all ordinary rules.
Cooperation could be based on short-term interest, but trust... Yao Yuan thought of that lie about Old Chen. The foundation of trust had already begun to show fine cracks.
Back at the apartment, she didn't switch on the main light, only twisted on a reading lamp by the sofa. She took out the brocade pouch holding the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror—the one she'd locked in the office drawer, then taken out again and tucked into her everyday bag—but she didn't open it. She just held it in her palm, feeling its heavy weight.
The truth. She needed the truth. The truth about this mirror's secret beyond the laws of physics, the truth about what Shuai Hongqiang had actually done with it, or tried to do, and the truth behind all of it—whether this was a contest of human hearts, or whether some unknowable force was truly pulling the strings.
But the truth often has to be bought at a price, sometimes a steep one.
She gripped the pouch tight. The cold metal contour pressed into her palm.
Spring. She drew that as her final deadline. When the ice and snow melted and Old Chen returned, no matter what, she had to get some answers.
Until then, she needed to position her pieces more carefully, wait more patiently—just as Zhang Fengxia had taught her years ago: when strength isn't on your side or the information is unclear, steady yourself first, see clearly, store up your power.
Beyond the window the city still blazed with light, countless stories breeding, entwining, and dying out within it. Yao Yuan sat in that small pool of warm yellow glow, her form still and quiet, like a chess piece sunk into deep water, silently calculating where to place her next move amid currents no one knew of.
Human nature is a balance sheet that never stops fluctuating, and in this moment, she was checking and rechecking the chips in her own hand, assessing those unseen liabilities and risks, waiting for the moment that could tip the scales in her favor.

========================================

Chapter 39 — Do the Wives of Big Shots Always Come to a Bad End?
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640286.html

Friday, one o'clock in the afternoon. Yao Yuan's livestream began right on schedule.
The studio lights were tuned to a cold white, throwing her brows and eyes into sharper, keener relief. The crimson polish on her fingertips trailed a faint afterimage across the screen, like some kind of warning. Outside the window, the rushing of the Yellow River was the only background sound — deep, brutal, impossible to ignore.
Yao Yuan (speaking first, after the faint crackle of the connecting line, her voice carrying a weary certainty): "Hello? Go ahead. Your kid is about three, right? And you've found out your husband either paid for sex, or was sexting, or was tipping some female streamer. You can't decide whether to leave, and you feel like all those years of effort are about to go down the drain, and you can't stomach it. Am I close?"
(A brief silence. The caller's breathing suddenly grew heavier.)
Caller (a woman's voice, fighting to stay composed, but the tremor leaking out at the ends of her words): "…You are. Teacher Yuanyuan. My husband and I, we dated in college, started a company together right after graduation. In the worst times the two of us split one cup of instant noodles. The company's on solid ground now, getting ready to go public. In a good year, the profit is this much (she names a substantial figure). We're both run off our feet, flying all over. After I got pregnant, my health was bad and the baby was fussy, so we started sleeping in separate rooms. In three years, I can count the number of times we've shared a bed on one hand. That one rare time… he asked me how it felt, and I… maybe I was tired, maybe I was just being difficult, and I said 'not great.' I never thought those words would hurt him… Later I took my parents and the baby to the Maldives, and looking at his phone bills, I found that in half a year he'd tipped over four hundred thousand to one entertainment streamer… plus two charges for in-home SPA sessions, the very expensive kind. I pressed him and he admitted it… he'd booked a massage, and with one of them, it happened once."
(Her voice began to break, the sobs she was forcing down bleeding through): "He knelt and begged me. Said it was just the stress, a moment of stupidity, said it would never happen again, said in his heart there's only me and this family… Teacher Yuanyuan, should I believe him? Leave? The company shares, the child, everything we've fought for all these years — how do you tear all that apart? Stay? It disgusts me! Just thinking about it makes my whole body shake! Everyone says men go bad when they get money. Do the wives of big shots… really always come to a bad end? In this world, is there just no such thing as a love that lasts from beginning to end? I don't understand… we came up together out of the hard days, we did…"
(The comments surged: [Holy crap, four hundred grand in tips!] [Once? Who believes "only once"] [The tragedy of a big shot's wife] [What happens to the company, what happens to the kid])
Yao Yuan (did not answer right away. She leaned slowly back, her neck tilting into a fragile arc, her gaze fixed on some empty point on the ceiling. The "tap, tap" of her fingertips started up again, forming a cold duet with the water outside the window. After a few seconds, she sat bolt upright, her eyes spearing toward the camera like an icicle.)
"Good. Done crying? Now, those silly questions of yours — 'is there no love that lasts,' 'do we always come to a bad end' — take them out of your head and throw them away."
"Those questions are a swamp. The more you think about them, the deeper you sink, the faster you die."
"We're going to take this apart one. Piece. At. A. Time."
She raised a single finger, the crimson nail flashing a cold light.
"First. Stop asking 'will he do it again,' and start thinking 'what cards do I still hold.'"
"Your account is full of a 'victim's' grievance and resentment — 'we suffered together,' 'I bore his child,' 'he betrayed me.' Those emotions are one hundred percent valid. But apart from making you suffer and making him feel guilty — and that guilt will burn out fast — what good are they?"
"Your problem right now isn't an emotional problem. It's a major operating crisis caused by a partner's defection. The two of you co-founded a limited liability company called 'marriage.' You're the CEO and majority shareholder, and you produced an important 'new product' (the child). Now your CTO (your husband) has embezzled a huge chunk of the company's cash flow (the tips, the prostitution fees) and tried to use company resources — his body, and possibly your family time — to subsidize external competitors (the streamer, the masseuse). He's damaged the company's core asset, trust, and left you, the CEO, with your management authority and emotional investment wiped out."
"So stop thinking like some heartbroken woman in the inner chambers abandoned by a faithless man. Use the brain you'd use to negotiate a merger, read a financial report, assess investment risk — and use it to assess your marriage!"
(The comments: [The company metaphor is killer!] [Instantly clear-headed!] [Business thinking is the GOAT])
She raised a second finger.
"Second. Settle your 'balance sheet' and your 'negotiating leverage.'"
"1. The asset side: your shared company shares, real estate, investments, cash. Do you know the percentage and source of every single fen? Marital property? Pre-marital contributions? Gifts from parents? Bring out your auditing skills and figure it out, immediately, right now. This is your economic foundation.
2. The liability/risk side: the facts of his cheating (the tip records, the transfer receipts, the possible chat logs, the recording of his admission — if you have one). This is the sword hanging over his head. Then there's your own emotions and health (the cost of continuing to drain yourself), and the child's custody and growing-up environment. This is your core risk.
3. The negotiating leverage: your actual control over the company and how indispensable you are (without you, does it collapse? does the business break down?). The cheating evidence in your hands (is it enough to give you an absolute edge in a divorce suit, or to extract excess gains in a negotiation?). Your ability and willingness to live alone and raise the child without him. This is the source of your power."
Yao Yuan leaned forward, her face enlarged and frigid on the screen.
"Based on all that, I'll give you three plans — upper, middle, lower. Not so you can choose, but so you can see the situation clearly, and then choose only the upper one."
"The lower plan: cry, beg, forgive, and keep limping along."
"You believe his tears and act like nothing happened. The result? Your trust gets a price tag stamped on it — four hundred grand in tips plus one round of paid sex is enough to buy it out. Your bottom line is trampled flat. From now on you'll live in endless suspicion; he'll live walking on eggshells, or grow even bolder. Your marriage becomes a gorgeous coffin, with your dignity and your feelings lying inside it. The company suffers (a feuding boss-couple is a commercial disaster), and the child grows up in a cold, eerie atmosphere. This is slow suicide. The stupidest choice. Rule it out at once."
"The middle plan: driven by emotion, mutual destruction."
"You can't take the disgust, so you divorce, no question. But in the divorce you only think about venting your feelings — the property division is a muddle, the fight over company control wounds you both, and you turn it into a citywide spectacle, letting employees, clients, and the child watch the whole farce. In the end you walk off the battlefield with a sum that looks fair but is actually shrunken, and a self that's exhausted in body and mind. You punished him, but you crippled yourself too. Kill a thousand of the enemy, lose eight hundred of your own. Not smart enough."
"The upper plan: interests first, reshape the whole structure."
"Step one: emotional isolation. Stop asking whether he loves you, whether he regrets it. Treat him as a partner who has developed a serious professional-ethics problem. Your pain, your disgust — digest it behind closed doors on your own, or take it to the best therapist you can find, but do not show him another ounce of it. Your composure is your strongest armor."
"Step two: lock down the evidence and display your strength. Quietly collect and back up everything. At the same time, return to the company in a more engaged, sharper state, making sure the key clients, the core team, and the financial processes are all under your control or clearly understood by you. Let him — and let everyone — see that this family might not work without him, but this company definitely doesn't work without you."
"Step three: lay out your 'terms of reconciliation' (or call them the 'atonement clauses'). Pick a moment when both of you are relatively calm. State it flat and plain, no emotion:
'XX, I know what you did. My feelings about it, I don't want to dwell on. Right now, we're only going to talk about how to solve it. Divorce — for the child, for the company, for our parents, for everything we've built over the years — is a nuclear-level blow. I don't want to go there, unless you force me to.'
'The condition for not divorcing is that our marital relationship, our property relationship, and our family power structure must be completely rebuilt.'
'1. Property restructuring: the money you lost on tips and paid sex gets deducted from your personal share and compensated to me. On top of that, we sign a new marital property agreement that registers XX% of the company shares / XX properties / XX in funds clearly under my name alone — as the penalty for any future wrongdoing on your part, and as security for me and the child. At the same time, all of your financial movements become completely transparent to me.'
'2. Behavioral constraints: sign a fidelity agreement that spells out a punitive payout for cheating again (an amount big enough to hurt). All non-essential socializing gets reported in advance. Family time takes priority — at least X nights a week, you're home before X o'clock. We can try to find a way both of us accept to improve our intimate life, but it has to be built on respect and honesty, not going through the motions.'
'3. Reset of family responsibility: I hold the main decision-making power over the child's education and the family's important decisions. You have to put in real, concrete time on parenting and housework, not just write checks.'
'Accept it, and we go to a lawyer and a notary, then try to repair things. Refuse, or pay lip service while doing otherwise, and we start divorce proceedings immediately — and I will use every piece of evidence I hold to make you pay the biggest price you can imagine, in property and in reputation.'"
Yao Yuan's gaze was sharp as a blade, her voice low but every word weighed a ton:
"Remember, this is not a request. It's a notification. You're not giving him a chance to turn back from the brink. You're giving him a choice: pay an enormous price for the possibility of keeping the marriage alive. If he signs, it means he'll live forever in the shadow of 'cheating costs are sky-high,' while you hold the economic lifeline and the household sovereignty. If he won't sign, you launch the divorce immediately, and on the strength of your evidence and your control, you take an absolute advantage in the property division."
"Finally, to answer those two childish questions of yours."
"Do the wives of big shots come to a bad end? No — people with no independent will, only a dependent mindset, man or woman, all come to a bad end. The wife of a big shot has to first be the 'big shot' of her own life. Your end doesn't depend on whether he goes bad. It depends on whether, when he does go bad, you have the ability to make him pay a price he can't bear, and the nerve to turn and walk away and live better."
"Is there such a thing in this world as a love that lasts from beginning to end? Yes. But it's not a fairy tale. It's a rational choice that two constantly growing, mutually awed adults — who both know full well the devastating cost of losing each other — make together and sustain for a lifetime. Its foundation isn't fragile love; it's a deep entanglement of interests, shared growth in value, and a high cost of breach that neither dares to cross lightly."
"So stop fantasizing about going back to the past. You can't go back."
"What you need to do is take all the composure, nerve, and tactics you had when you built your company, and turn this crisis into a thorough 'marital restructuring' that works in your favor."
"Either you end up with a new 'company' where the shares are reorganized, you hold the lead, and he doesn't dare step out of line."
"Or you walk away with enough 'severance compensation' and 'non-compete damages' to leave with dignity, and use that capital to open the second track of your life."
"Tears and accusations are the most useless things in this world. Only real power, real shares, real agreements, and the ability to turn and leave at any moment — those are your eternal amulet."
After delivering this cold, interest-based analysis, Yao Yuan paused for a moment. The crimson polish on her fingertip rapped lightly against the desktop, giving a crisp sound. Her gaze toward the camera shifted from pure sharpness, taking on a more complex layer — an insight close to compassion.
"Finally, to answer that childish question of yours about a love that 'lasts from beginning to end.'" Her voice slowed a little, yet seemed sharper for it, like a thin, cold scalpel cutting through to deeper tissue.
"Whether such a love truly exists in this world, I can't say for certain. But I do know that marriages which last from beginning to end really do exist. Do you really believe that two living, breathing people — with flesh and blood, flaws and desires — can, over decades of long years, resist every temptation, friction, weariness, and swelling of ego, on nothing but an extremely unstable emotion called 'love'?"
"What fairy tales and idol dramas don't dare tell you is that every marriage that manages to keep going has had problems along the way — has even had affairs (of the mind, or the ⬛⬛). Even the marriage of Yang Jiang and Qian Zhongshu, universally praised as a match made in heaven, that lucid and restrained, that perfectly matched in soul — even they had their bumps and turns, their friction and wavering, let alone us. The difference is that in some cases the problem festers into a sore and finally rots away; while in others, both parties cut it out with enormous wisdom, patience, weighing of interests, even luck — leaving a scar, but the person survives, and the relationship survives too, in a different form."
(She leaned forward slightly, her gaze blazing, as if to burn through every unrealistic illusion.)
"And you — and a lot of women in this stream who are suffering just like you — what's your biggest problem? It's being 'rich, but with no real eyes.'"
"Your 'no real eyes' isn't that you don't understand business. It's that you don't understand real human nature, you don't understand actual marriage. You imagine love as a tower of flawless glass untouched by dust, and treat marriage as a one-time perfect contract. What you love is that symbol of a 'husband who lasts from beginning to end' in your fantasy, the concept of 'fidelity' beautified and purified in novels and films. But you don't actually love — can't love — the concrete, living man who gets tired, gets fragile, runs away, has low base urges, makes mistakes, and may even feel ignored and lonely while you're pregnant."
"You think your 'building a company together, suffering together' is romance. But over the long years, that 'suffering' can curdle into resentment, that 'busyness' can curdle into estrangement, and those nights with no communication can curdle into ice. And you — with 'busy,' with 'tired,' with stubborn words — shut the door on communication and repair. You think you're guarding some kind of 'purity'; in fact you're avoiding the messy, unflattering work of running a real relationship."
"Love the concrete person, not the abstract feeling. The concrete person eats, drinks, and shits, gets bad breath, goes bald, puts on a middle-aged paunch, and under pressure goes looking for crude ways to vent. The concrete person needs to be seen, needed, soothed — not enshrined on the altar of 'meritorious contributor' or 'the kid's dad.' Your husband going to streamers and masseuses is of course his own baseness, his breach, his one hundred percent of the blame. But your 'no real eyes' is that you never truly saw his needs and frailties as a 'concrete man.' You only enjoyed the halo and convenience that came from him as the abstract symbol of a 'successful partner.'"
"So stop dumping all the blame on a shallow excuse like 'men go bad when they get money.' Tragedy is often a conspiracy. His part in the conspiracy was cheating, basely. Your part was being absent, naively. You were absent from the small, concrete work of building feeling between husband and wife; absent from recognizing and guarding against the weaknesses of human nature — until he gave you this bloody lesson in the ugliest way possible."
"Now, what you have to do isn't only what I said earlier — fight for shares, sign agreements, seize the initiative. That's the 'technique' you must master. You also have to learn the lesson of the 'way': let go of your obsession with 'perfect love' and 'absolute fidelity,' and learn how to build, with a concrete and complicated person, a real relationship — one with both solid entanglement of interests and real flow of feeling, one that can share the glory and also have compassion for the frailty."
"If, after all this, all you've learned is how to leash a man with an agreement and how to punish a single mistake with money, then you've only jumped from one kind of 'no real eyes' to another, shrewder kind. True sight is this: after seeing through all the gray and uncertainty of human nature, still having the ability to build a little warmth and certainty; after mastering every weapon to punish the other person, still knowing when not to pull the trigger."
"Whether you choose to use the 'technique' I gave you to balance and negotiate, or choose to leave — that's your road. But whichever you choose, please remember: from now on, love concrete people, handle concrete matters, bear concrete consequences. Stop living in that tower of glass you built out of money and fantasy, the one that shatters at a touch."
"You should grow up — after a price this painful."
(Having said this, Yao Yuan stopped looking at the comments scrolling madly across the screen. She turned her head, gazing out the window at that never-resting river. Her profile, in the cold light of the screen, looked utterly clear and utterly weary. It was the clarity of someone who has seen through all the rules of the game and yet finds no joy in it — left only with a deep fatigue.)
(After delivering her dissection of the "concrete person," Yao Yuan did not stop. She set down the pen in her hand, and the "click" of it rang out strikingly in the silence. Her gaze toward the camera was no longer pure scrutiny now; it was more like a mentor about to lift the final veil on the answer, wearing a complex expression that mixed pity and edge.)
"…You say you love him, that you can't bear to leave behind so many years of effort. But do you know? Your biggest problem, besides the 'rich with no real eyes' I just mentioned, is one even more fatal: you've seen too few men."
(She paused deliberately, letting the line sink into the caller and into every listener.)
"Your life script sounds very inspiring: campus sweethearts, building a company together, going from dirt-poor to financially free. But have you ever thought that this also means your emotional sample size is pitifully thin? In your world, apart from your father, he may be the only 'man' who has ever occupied all of your attention and feeling. You took the love template you carried out of school — untested by the world — and forced it onto a grown man who's been through the cut-and-thrust of business and has seen every kind of desire and temptation. You used the purity of your student days to demand things of a middle-aged man's complexity. That, in itself, is a kind of cognitive mismatch and naivety."
"You don't understand the nature of men." Yao Yuan's voice was very calm, but it was as if she were stating a law of physics. "Or rather, you're unwilling to understand that broader, more real male world beyond your love fairy tale. The nature of men isn't only responsibility, duty, and devotion to family. Written into the nature of men, just as much, are hunting, conquest, possession, vanity, laziness, a craving for fresh stimulation, a need to be worshipped, and the use of the most direct methods (including sex) to relieve pressure and confirm their own existence. These aren't 'evil.' This is a dark part of human nature, code written into the male animal's genes. The only difference is that men with cultivation, with reverence, with higher pursuits, use reason, responsibility, and love to constrain and elevate them; while others let them run wild, and even get devoured by them."
"Your husband is clearly the latter. But that doesn't mean the first man you met represents all of them. You've been trapped inside this 'company' with him too long — so long that you think the world is only this big and men come in only this one kind. You think every problem is 'he doesn't love me anymore' or 'he's gone bad.' You can't see that behind it lies the inevitable collapse of a universal human predicament inside a relationship that lacked effective constraint and nourishment."
"So, back to your question about 'lasting from beginning to end.' What matters isn't whether it exists. It's whether you have the ability — after seeing a person's nature in full, his light and his darkness, his responsibility and his weakness — to still choose to stand at his side, and to build together a set of rules that constrain the darkness and draw out the light. That set of rules includes economic interests, includes emotional communication, and includes a clear-eyed recognition and prevention of human weakness. Before this, you two only had a community of interests; you never built this 'flood-control levee' against human nature."
"When I tell you to love the concrete person, I mean: first go and 'see' this concrete person. See the mediocrity beneath his halo, the anxiety behind his success, the fragility inside his strength, and… the desires and needs he has as an ordinary man — the ones that can't be brought to the table but truly exist. This isn't an excuse for his cheating. It's so you understand what you're facing. If you don't even know the full shape of your 'enemy' (the weaknesses of human nature), how are you going to fight?"
"You feel the sky has fallen right now, because your world has only this one pillar — him. Let me tell you, the world is very big, and there are very many men. Some are worse and more rotten than him; some are more responsible and more restrained than him. Part of your pain comes from his betrayal; the other part comes from your poverty of imagination and the narrowness of your choices. You can't imagine what life could be like without him, because you've never truly broadened your own emotional map and life possibilities."
"So my advice, on top of all the 'technique' (the shares, the agreements), adds one most fundamental 'way': go out, see a broader world, meet a wider variety of people. Not to make you go on dates immediately — to make you first move your eyes off of him. Read books, read history, read psychology, see how human nature repeats its light and its darkness across the long ages. Make friends, befriend men and women of different ages, different classes, different values, and listen to their stories, their desires, their predicaments. Go observe your husband again — not as a 'lover,' but as a 'human specimen' — and analyze the logic of his behavior coldly."
"When you've seen enough samples, you'll know that your husband's cheating is just an ordinary one among countless similar stories. It isn't unique, it isn't forgivable, but it can be analyzed and it can be dealt with. You'll walk out of the 'why me' victim's emotion and into the 'what do I do now' problem-solving mode. You'll understand that running a long-term relationship isn't guarding a fragile dream; it's managing a complex cooperative project in human nature. What you need is wisdom, strength, and maneuvering — not just love and trust."
"Whether you stay and rebuild, or leave and restart, the choice is yours. But whichever you choose, please first free yourself from the narrow world of 'only one man.' Your sight should not be limited by the wealth you possess, and still less defined by one man's betrayal. Only after you've seen the vast ocean will you stop grieving too much over a pond drying up; only after you understand human nature will you stop being caught off guard by a concrete betrayal."
"Now, hang up the phone. Beside the computer where you audit the accounts and draft the agreement, set down a copy of Sapiens or a copy of Intimate Relationships. Treat the symptom, but treat the root too. Your illness is half in him, and half in that field of your own heart that has never truly been opened up. Also, I'd suggest you enroll in our 'Heng-Ha' course. Sex matters a great deal in married life — it's the catalyst of a couple's feelings."
Having finished, Yao Yuan did not wait for any response. She cleanly cut the line. She leaned back against the chair and let out a long, soundless breath, as if those words had cost her enormous strength too. On the screen, her clear and weary face was reflected, along with the Yellow River outside the window — the one that swallows everything, and also gives birth to everything.

========================================

Chapter 40 — Trading "Tolerance" for Your Child's Future "Silver Spoon" and "Iron Armor" — the Wisdom of a Mother Beast in the Jungle, Cruel but Effective
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640287.html

Trading "tolerance" for the "silver spoon" and "iron armor" of your own and your child's future — this is the wisdom of a mother beast in the jungle, a cruel but effective survival strategy.
[Call connected]
Under the studio lights, Yao Yuan's red nails looked like flames frozen mid-flicker. She listened to the call-in girl's account — accented, the girl fighting to keep her composure — and showed not a flicker of feeling, only the faint glow of her fingertip sliding slowly across her tablet. Outside the window, the Yellow River roared low in the dead of winter, like a beast that could never be satisfied.
Caller (female voice, 26, plain accent, words tumbling out fast): "Teacher Yuanyuan, I... I'm a girl from the countryside, went to an ordinary university. Two years ago I married my husband. He's 29, his family runs a factory, so he's sort of a rich second-generation kid. Half a year into the marriage I found out he... he sees prostitutes. The first time I caught him, he admitted he was wrong, bought me a small apartment, paid the 200,000 down payment, put it in my name. The second time was last year — I found out again, and he transferred me 100,000, said it would never happen again. But this month, I found out he went to one of those clubs again!"
(Her voice began to shake, tangled with shame and bewilderment.)
"I don't know what to do now... and on top of that, I... I'm pregnant. Just past three months. Divorce? But I get nothing from my own family. My salary's only five or six thousand, that apartment hasn't even been handed over yet, and I've already spent some of the 100,000... and I'm carrying a child... Not divorce? It disgusts me! Really, Teacher Yuanyuan, the moment I picture him with those women I go cold all over. My parents think I married up, they tell me to put up with it. But I'm only 26. Am I supposed to put up with it my whole life?"
The scrolling comments exploded in an instant:
[Pregnant?! This plot just got complicated.]
[With PUA it's zero times or infinite times — run, sister, run!]
[Apartment + 100k + pregnant: more chips, but more trapped.]
[A kid is a bond and a chain both.]
[Divorce now — single mom plus rural background? Brutal.]
Yao Yuan (laughed softly, the laugh short and cold, like a sliver of ice cracking): "Girl, just from the way you talk, I can tell you're an honest soul. An honest soul marries into a wolf's den, gets bitten three times, and only then thinks to ask, 'Why does it eat people?'"
(She leaned forward, blood-red nail tapping the camera as if tapping the girl right on the forehead.)
"Your biggest mistake right now is that you're still thinking in words like 'marriage,' 'loyalty,' 'disgust.' Your husband stopped speaking that language long ago — he's been talking to you in the language of 'business.' First time, one apartment. Second time, 200,000. Clearly priced, no haggling, no cheating the customer. He's telling you: my whoring is my business; your silence is your price. The two of you have already built a deformed but smoothly functioning 'compensation-transaction system.'"
Caller (voice cracking): "But I don't want this kind of deal! I want a normal marriage... and I'm carrying a child right now..."
Yao Yuan (cutting in, tone suddenly severe): "Shut up. Stop dreaming."
"When you married in, didn't you know the risks? A rural girl, ordinary degree, marrying up across class lines. You think what carried you was true love conquering all? No. What carried you was your youth, your simplicity, your 'good for bearing children,' and the 'easy to control' his family probably wanted. Now that you're pregnant, you've cemented that 'good for bearing children' value. His marrying you, in a sense, follows the same logic as you getting the apartment and the money now — each takes what they need. It's just that what you wanted was 'marriage,' 'a step up,' and 'a child,' while what he wanted was 'convenience,' 'peace of mind,' and 'an heir.' Now he's spent extra, and he's willing to pay for it. From a cold-blooded transactional standpoint, the way he's honored the 'breach-of-contract clause' in this marriage is, if anything, pretty good faith."
(Comments: [Pregnancy makes her tool-status even more obvious.] [Marriage economics.] [She just ripped off the fig leaf.])
"So stop asking that stupid 'divorce or not' question." Yao Yuan slowed her pace, every word a hammer driving a nail.
"Ask yourself this: if you leave him, pregnant, on your tiny salary, how long would it take you to earn an apartment plus a few hundred thousand in cash? And on top of that, cover your pregnancy expenses, prenatal checkups, the birth, raising the child? Your in-laws' resources, connections, standard of living — those are things your birth family could never give you in this lifetime, and they're the starting line you can give your child. For you and your child, divorce means a double drop in class."
Caller (seemingly stunned, in a small voice): "So... I just keep putting up with it? Wait until he catches a disease and passes it to me and the baby? Or wait until I'm old and worn out and get dumped?"
Yao Yuan (revealing the first almost "pleased" cold smile of the night): "Put up with it? Who told you to just sit there and take it? Pregnancy is your crisis — but more than that, it's a strategic window you've never had before. Your 'tolerance' has to have strategy, has to have a purpose. It has to become a process of 'bleeding him dry,' 'arming yourself,' and 'laying the foundation for your child.'"
"Listen carefully, I'll only say it once. This is the road you should take:"
"Step one — a mindset revolution: from 'pregnant wife' to 'project director carrying the heir.'"
"In your belly is his legal heir. That's the heaviest card in your hand, and the one with the shortest shelf life. Stop treating him as a husband; treat him as a long-term, stable investor with a moral defect. Your core task is to use this stage — pregnancy and nursing, the period his family values most, when his guilt may run thickest (if he has any left), when he's most willing to invest — to extract resources to the maximum. Complete your own primitive accumulation, your upgrade and iteration, and secure unshakeable guarantees for your child."
"Step two — escalate the negotiation: build a 'compensation-and-security formula' centered on 'the child.'"
"Next time, no crying, no scenes. Calmly lay out the evidence in front of him and his parents. Then say: 'Dad, Mom, my husband went to one of those clubs again. I'm heartbroken, but I'm more worried. I'm carrying a child now, and my emotions and health directly affect the baby. The earlier incidents — the apartment and the cash were my compensation. But this time, for the child's sake, I need more security. I want to set up a family trust for the baby, an initial fund of X million, jointly overseen by me and professionals, so that no matter what the future holds, the child's education, healthcare, and living are absolutely guaranteed. At the same time, I want a titular position at the company, with social insurance and an income. I need to set an example as an independent mother for the baby, and I need a steady source of income to keep my spirits up.'"
"Key points: 1. Frame every demand under the banner of 'for the child' — politically correct, hard to argue against. 2. Upgrade the form of compensation from personal spending to irrevocable trust assets (giving it to the child also gives you the initiative). 3. Win yourself a continuous income and a social identity (a job, social insurance). 4. Pull his parents to your side by exploiting how much they value the grandchild."
"Step three, and the core: extract resources, complete a double accumulation (for you, and for your child)."
"Use this period — pregnancy and the time after birth — to do the following things, and keep your priorities straight:"
"1. Security first: the trust is the top priority. It's the greatest leverage you leave yourself for future negotiation, or for going it alone, and the greatest protection for your child. The legal documents must be airtight.
2. Degree/skill upgrade: under the name of 'prenatal study and fetal education, and self-improvement after birth so you can educate the child,' make him pay for an online MBA, child nutrition courses, family-education counselor certification, and so on. Keep the receipts; pad your résumé.
3. Infiltrate the networks and manage assets: under the pretext of 'understanding the family business for the child's future' or 'doing light work during pregnancy to lift your mood,' enter his family's company or circle. Meet the key contacts, learn asset management, convert the compensation money into solid investments.
4. Manage your body and image: use the best medical resources for prenatal care, postpartum recovery, a personal trainer, a nutritionist. Stay healthy, get your form back. The healthier, the more beautiful, the more vibrant you are, the higher your value as the child's mother and as a potential partner."
Yao Yuan's gaze blazed, her voice low but intensely persuasive:
"Picture this: when the child is three, you're 29. In your hands you might hold a healthy child, a legally protected trust for your offspring, an apartment, a body of appreciating assets, a high-value degree or skill, a quality network, a social-insurance record, and a figure that's recovered well — maybe grown even more graceful."
"At that point, look back at today's question again. Is 'leave him or not' even a question anymore?"
"Leave, and your wings are full — you have assets, ability, a way out, a child and the security that comes with it as a single mother. You might even, through the network you've built over these years, find a more reliable partner or business ally."
"Stay, and you're no longer that pitiful creature who could only collect a bit of hush money. You're the mother of this family's bloodline, you hold control of the trust, you have your own career as a partner. You've become a part of this community of interests that can't be cut loose. If he wants to act out again, the cost will touch his own child's direct interests and his parents' thunderous fury — it'll hurt enough that he does the math. You can live in comfort, run your own little business, and treat him as a 'jumbo ATM' and 'resource platform' with a bad habit, who simply needs managing."
"Grab the big things, let the small ones go. What's big? Your own growth, your assets, your child's absolute security, your future possibilities. What's small? The disgust from his inability to control what's below the waist. While you're still weak and carrying a new life, trade 'tolerance' for the 'silver spoon' and 'iron armor' of your own and your child's future. That's the wisdom of a mother beast in the jungle — a cruel but effective survival strategy."
"Love? That's a pure luxury only the poor can afford to discuss. In your position, loving yourself, arming yourself, securing your child — that is the greatest justice and the greatest maternal love. Once you're strong, everything that turns your stomach today will become a footnote in your success story, a passage that made you tougher."
"You're lucky — you crossed class lines, you're carrying the heir. But luck takes wisdom and skill to cash in. If you need it, you can contact my assistant after the broadcast for more specific one-on-one consultation and planning — especially the parts on the in-marriage agreement, setting up the trust, and pregnancy-stage negotiation strategy."
"Now, dry your eyes. Swallow the disgust. Fuse your ambition with your motherhood. Your battlefield is not in his belt — it's in the trust documents, your skill certificates, and the empire you build for your child. Turn every piece of evidence of his cheating into a new source of funding on your growth plan. The moment you can't bear it anymore isn't the moment you break down — it's the moment your wings are full, the moment you can renegotiate the terms with him, or walk away with grace."
With that, Yao Yuan cut the connection without waiting for a response. She leaned back, rubbed the space between her brows, and a very faint flicker of weariness crossed her face. The glow of the screen lit her exquisite profile, and behind her, beyond the window, that boundless black river water that seemed able to swallow every filth and ambition. The comments were full of remarks like clear-eyed to the point of cruelty — this is real comeback science and maternal love can be strategic too. She knew that another batch of people would be stung by her words, and then, perhaps, would begin to change.
"All right, my dears, that's where today's broadcast ends. Have a lovely weekend, and I'll see you next Monday. Any of you who have questions and want a consultation, contact our assistant. Bye-bye." With that, Yao Yuan extended her index finger and pressed the "End Broadcast" button, crisp and clean.
The screen went black in an instant.
Another brilliant, cruel "emotional surgery" was over. Every minute of the surgery was a high-intensity, high-concentration burning of her mental energy. Every piece of advice to a caller was her day-after-day chewing over and dissecting the most obscure, most unbearable side of human nature. And then, once the broadcast ended — like now — there was the relentless tolerance and digestion, alone, of a flood of negative emotion and extreme cases, a soul-deep exhaustion that made even lifting a single finger feel like spending the last of her strength, and a silt-laden emptiness that no one could see, that she had to swallow down entirely on her own.

========================================

Chapter 41 — A New Year's Eve Banquet
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640288.html

Time slipped by fast, steeped in city neon and alcohol. The calendar turned page after page, and before she knew it deep winter had arrived. November's cold winds stripped the last withered leaf from the treetops, and December's first snow quietly blanketed the whole city one predawn morning. Through those two months, Yao Yuan's life moved along its fixed rhythm—livestreams, business, socializing—and the time-travel incidents had not recurred since the last one, the strangeness now feeling like nothing more than a dream. She met Yu Hao now and then, keeping up that easy, neither-deep-nor-shallow "buddy" arrangement. As for Zhao Yiming's "Genesis Engine," in mid-December it had once again attempted a deep fusion with "the Pretty Big One's core framework," and once again the attempt ended in failure. The technical team's diagnostic report was calm and obscure: "Our assessment remains that some segment of 'non-standard emotional mapping' in the external high-dimensional data stream, when attempting deep coupling with the 'Pretty Big One' core model, triggered a chain reaction of conflict in the underlying logic—similar to 'immune rejection,' but expressing itself with particular violence at the data layer." The physical circuitry and base architecture checked out fine, but some of the emotional weight parameters in the core model needed recalibration and reinforcement, and a stricter "emotional firewall" protocol had to be established.

Zhao Yiming's voice on the phone gave nothing away emotionally—only the focus peculiar to a technical man facing an unsolved problem. "Something in your emotional data is making my model 'allergic.'" Yao Yuan listened and only returned a flat line: "Then it means it isn't 'robust' enough yet."

Not robust enough. The phrase circled in Yao Yuan's mind for a few days, finally dissolving into a barely audible little laugh. People or AI, it made no difference—not robust enough, and you deserved to come out behind in the game.

In the blink of an eye it was New Year's Day.

On the first day of the new year, as dusk gathered, Jin Lan's "Yellow River Tower" blazed with light. The building, known as "the First Tower of the Ten-Thousand-Mile Yellow River," stood at the highest point of the city's riverside scenic belt: 93.39 meters tall, sixteen floors above ground and one below, a tower structure of seven visible tiers and six hidden ones that, in the night, looked like a glowing pagoda of glazed tile. Its mock-antique design in the Han and Tang styles layered flying eaves and bracket sets one atop another, while vermilion railings and jade-green tiles, lit up, glowed with a lustre where the weight of history fused with modern luxury. Standing before the floor-to-ceiling windows of the top-floor private room, you could look down on the entire winding course of the Yellow River through the city, the skyline on the far bank as brilliant as a galaxy hung upside down.

Yao Yuan was the third to arrive. Tonight she wore a breathtaking gold gown. Not a gaudy, glaring gold, but an expensive silk somewhere between champagne and molten sunset, woven with a faintly visible dark pattern that, when the light swept across it, rippled with a flowing, water-like sheen. The dress was a one-shoulder mermaid cut, tracing the smooth line from collarbone to ankle to perfection, cinched very tight at the waist, the hem unfurling slowly from below the knee and swaying gently with her steps—like a lazy, dazzling goldfish drifting through the night. Her jewelry was just as striking: from her ears hung two gold tassel earrings, minimal in design but heavy in presence, each segment linked by tiny diamonds that threw off scattered starlight when they swung; at her throat lay a matching collarbone chain, set at its center with a teardrop yellow diamond. Under the room's warm amber light, that gold was deep and rich, as if a small sliver of sunlight had been imprisoned forever against her skin. Her long hair was loosely coiled at the back of her head, a few carefully arranged loose strands falling at the side of her neck, making that neck look all the longer and fairer. She wore no fur, only a sharply tailored black cashmere coat—now handed to the attendant—and she leaned against the window, watching the lights reflected on the river's surface, her whole figure like a golden work of art that had been meticulously forged and then granted life.

"Yuanyuan!" Jin Lan came in from the door, in a sapphire-blue embroidered qipao with a white fur stole over it, makeup exquisite, beaming. "Happy New Year! We were just waiting on you—oh wait, no, there are still a few who haven't shown." Her gaze lingered on Yao Yuan for a few seconds, and she said with genuine admiration, "God, this outfit of yours… tonight you've clearly made up your mind to make sure no one can take their eyes off you."

Yao Yuan turned and embraced her with a smile, the tassels at her ears tapping together with a very faint, crisp little sound. "Happy New Year, Jin Lan. You chose this place well—the view is incredible."

"Of course. My own turf—the best private room naturally goes to my own people." Jin Lan took her by the arm and led her to her seat. The room was enormous, with a round redwood table at its center big enough for fifteen, already set with delicate blue-and-white porcelain and a glass lazy Susan. All around stood mock-antique screens and curio shelves displaying porcelain pieces, the overall style classical and refined with an understated luxury running through it.

People arrived one after another.

Wang Qian came in with his wife. Mrs. Wang was thirty-five or thirty-six, round-faced, with gentle features, dressed in a cream knit dress under a light-gray coat, her makeup soft and understated—a curious contrast to Wang Qian's smooth, all-things-to-all-people air. The moment she stepped in, her eyes locked onto Yao Yuan, and they instantly lit up; almost reflexively she pressed a hand to her mouth.

Zhao Yiming came alone, in a dark-gray suit with no tie, his usual faintly distant little smile on his face. He greeted Jin Lan first, then turned his gaze to Yao Yuan, pausing on her half a second longer than on anyone else. Behind the lenses his eyes showed no ripple; he only gave an almost imperceptible nod, which counted as a greeting.

Chen Ye and Liu Qingzhuo arrived together. This made Yao Yuan raise an eyebrow slightly. Chen Ye still wore a shirt of fine material, though this time light gray, with a navy cashmere cardigan over it that made him look more cultured. Liu Qingzhuo wore a wine-red velvet jumpsuit, her makeup vivid, her hand resting quite naturally in the crook of Chen Ye's arm. As they came through the door they were murmuring something to each other, Chen Ye tilting his head slightly to listen, a gentle smile at the corner of his mouth, nodding now and then—an expression utterly different from the startled helplessness when he'd been passively kissed in the KTV last time, now carrying a few degrees more of natural intimacy.

So that kiss in the game last time really had become the spark, Yao Yuan thought, the corners of her lips curving into a faintly amused arc. Good. Liu Qingzhuo was decisive, Chen Ye steady, their family backgrounds well matched—a decent trade, all things considered.

Gao Jieyuan was the second-to-last to arrive, in a flashy wine-red velvet suit, his hair gelled into perfect order. He flung his arms open dramatically the moment he came in. "Happy New Year, everyone! Ah, sorry I'm late, the roads were jammed like crazy!" His gaze swept the whole room like radar and jolted to a stop on Yao Yuan; his throat seemed to bob, his smile deepened, and his voice rose a few notches. "President Yao, tonight you've… put the sun on? You're so dazzling I can barely keep my eyes open."

Yao Yuan only smiled faintly and raised her glass in acknowledgment, the yellow diamond at her throat flashing a sharp glint as she moved.

The last to arrive was an unfamiliar woman. About twenty-seven or twenty-eight, tall, in a minimally tailored black suit over a white silk blouse, her long hair tied into a low ponytail at the back of her head, her makeup light, wearing fine-rimmed glasses—an altogether intellectual, crisp air about her. She carried a dark-brown briefcase. Coming in, she first nodded in greeting to Jin Lan, then her gaze settled on Yao Yuan, carrying a clear curiosity and scrutiny—and within that scrutiny was mixed the focus of a researcher facing a precious specimen.

"Let me introduce everyone," Jin Lan said, taking the woman's hand. "Jiang Tiantian, a friend I've recently met. Her father is Jiang Shuhai—Teacher Jiang—yes, that well-known author. Tiantian herself mainly works as a director and in film investment, focusing on women's subject matter. She's seen Yuanyuan's livestreams and the audience comments, found her especially interesting, and kept saying she wanted to meet her."

Jiang Tiantian stepped forward, gave the group a slight nod, her bearing composed, then fixed her gaze firmly on Yao Yuan and extended her hand. "Teacher Yao, I've long admired you. I'm a deep consumer of your content, and a researcher of your methodology."

Yao Yuan rose and shook her hand, her gold skirt sweeping a graceful arc, a fine gold bracelet sliding out from her sleeve, its pendant a tiny, abstractly shaped lock. "Miss Jiang, you're too kind. I've read Teacher Jiang's books as well—penetrating, powerful writing." Her hand was dry and warm, her grip well measured, neither overly eager nor coldly distant.

"My father would be delighted to know you've read his books." Jiang Tiantian's tone was sincere, the eyes behind her lenses bright and sparkling, that glow rational yet ardent. "Actually, my coming uninvited today, aside from wanting to meet you, has a very specific purpose—I've recently been preparing a documentary series about contemporary urban women's survival strategies, and I'm also drafting a novel on a related theme. As 'the McKinsey of Feelings,' your entire logical system—how you analyze men and women, teach women to gain precisely in love and marriage, with everything centered on real interest—I find tremendously valuable for research and highly representative of our era. I wonder if there might be a chance later to have a systematic, in-depth conversation with you?"

Yao Yuan looked at the undisguised inquisitiveness in her eyes, and that sense of kinship in placing emotion, too, under a rational analytical framework, and the little pleasure of being understood by one of her own kind rippled gently open inside her. She liked being treated this way—as a "methodology supplier," a "strategist," rather than simply an "emotional mentor" or a "beautiful woman." "Of course," she smiled, that smile a precisely calculated blend of propriety and openness. "I look forward to talking with you too, Miss Jiang. After all, helping women live with more clarity and lose out less—that's a good thing."

Everyone took their seats. As host, Jin Lan took the head of the table, Yao Yuan to her right, and Jiang Tiantian sat down quite naturally beside Yao Yuan. Zhao Yiming sat to Jin Lan's left, followed by the Wang couple and Gao Jieyuan; Chen Ye and Liu Qingzhuo sat on the window side, their seats very close together.

The cold dishes were already on the table. On the delicate platters were brine-cured duck liver, drunken shrimp, osmanthus-sweet lotus root, cold-dressed jellyfish head, and four-happiness baked gluten—elegantly plated, enticing in color. Attendants came forward to pour the drinks; tonight Moutai had been laid out, along with a white Burgundy, and freshly squeezed juice.

Jin Lan raised her glass and stood. "Come, everyone—on the first day of the new year, the fact that we can gather here is fate itself. I drink a toast to you all. Happy New Year, and may everything go your way!"

Everyone rose, glasses clinking, the crisp sounds running together into one. "Happy New Year!" "Wishing Sister Lan's business roaring success!" "Good health to all!"

The first glass down, the mood at the table came alive at once.

Wang Qian was forever the best at warming a room. He filled his second glass, stood, and toasted Jin Lan. "Boss Jin, this Yellow River Tower just gets grander and grander! I hear last month you hosted several foreign delegations? Amazing! I drink to you—may the new year bring you wealth rolling in, and may you turn this 'First Tower of the Ten-Thousand-Mile Yellow River' into the 'First Tower of China'!"

Jin Lan laughed and drained her glass. "No one talks like you!"

Wang Qian turned to Yao Yuan, his smile carrying a few degrees more mischief, but a genuine admiration deep in his eyes. "Yuanyuan, this glass I have to drink to you alone. That dance you did last time at the Crimson Night Palace—after my wife saw the little clip I secretly filmed, she went on about it for days, said she absolutely had to meet you in person. Today she's finally got her chance." He turned to pull his wife alongside him. "Come on, dear, aren't you Teacher Yao's diehard fan? Quick, say something."

Mrs. Wang's face went slightly red. A little embarrassed, she stood, holding a glass of juice—she'd said earlier she was driving, so no alcohol. She looked at Yao Yuan, eyes bright and shining, her tone sincere to the point of being endearing, even carrying a touch of the nervousness of meeting an idol. "Teacher Yao, I really am an old fan of yours. From your very first livestreams, I've followed every episode, and I've bought all your courses. I—I especially love your whole system about the 'marriage-partner mindset' and 'emotional cost-benefit accounting'—it simply opened the door to a new world for me! I always used to think talking feelings hurts your wallet and talking money hurts your feelings, but after listening to you I finally understood: feelings that don't account for interest are castles in the air!" The more she spoke the more worked up she got, cheeks flushing. "And there's what you said—'a woman should be a shareholder in her marriage, not an employee.' I got so many of my girlfriends to come listen, and now we've got a group where we discuss every day how to apply your methods…"

Everyone at the table laughed, the laughter carrying different shades of meaning. Wang Qian clutched his chest theatrically. "So, dear, you've been treating Teacher Yao's words as a war manual—taking them home to use against me?"

"That's called jointly building a happy home and optimizing our cooperation model," Mrs. Wang shot back, glaring at him, righteous, then turned to Yao Yuan with worshipful eyes. "Teacher Yao, I really think you're brilliant. You're not the kind who teaches women to give foolishly, or to be relentlessly independent—you teach how to win smartly within a relationship, so both people benefit, while making sure you don't lose out yourself. That's what's truly high-level. This glass is for you! I'll use tea in place of wine—please, as you like!"

Yao Yuan was amused by this sincere yet precise "fan confession," a flicker of warmth at being understood rising in her heart. She lifted her glass and looked earnestly at Mrs. Wang. "Thank you for understanding it so thoroughly. That you can apply the theory in your own life and form a healthy dynamic with your partner—that in itself proves the method works. This glass is for you—and for every smart woman who knows how to plan shrewdly for herself, and how to run a win-win relationship too."

The two drank to each other. As Mrs. Wang sat down, an excited flush still on her face, she secretly tugged Wang Qian's sleeve and whispered, "I drank with my idol! And she even praised me!"

Wang Qian patted her hand indulgently, leaned to her ear and said something. Mrs. Wang gave him a light punch, and the two exchanged a smile. The scene was warm and natural, and watching it, a faint, almost imperceptible satisfaction crossed the depths of Yao Yuan's eyes. See—her "methodology" was flowering and bearing fruit in the real world, creating a template of "happiness" that looked traditional but was brand-new at its core.

The hot dishes began to come out. Yellow River Tower's signature dishes were brought up one by one: steamed Yellow River carp, braised pork knuckle, crabmeat lion's-head meatballs, Wensi tofu soup, "boiled-water" cabbage… each one exquisite and meticulous, the aromas spreading everywhere.

The toasting and exchanging of cups went on around the table, lively beyond measure. Gao Jieyuan made an energetic round of toasts, and when he reached Yao Yuan he deliberately poured himself a full glass of Moutai, eyes blazing as they fixed on the tassels swaying at her ears. "President Yao, new year, new beginnings—this glass I'll drain, and you, as you like. I wish you a new year of growing ever more beautiful, your career climbing another level, and you carrying on as the… guiding light in all our hearts!" With that he tipped his head back and drank it dry, showing off the empty bottom of his glass.

Yao Yuan smiled and took a small sip. "Thank you, Young Boss Gao. 'Guiding light' is too much—I just share a few takeaways, that's all."

"Hey, that won't do—I drained mine, how can you go as you like?" Gao Jieyuan wouldn't let it slide and moved to pour her more.

"Jieyuan, Yuanyuan doesn't hold her liquor well—the gesture's enough." Jin Lan spoke up, her tone smiling, though it sounded more like fanning the flames.

But Yao Yuan picked up the decanter herself, the gold bracelet sliding down, and filled her own glass to seven-tenths. She raised it toward Gao Jieyuan, the yellow diamond glittering at her throat. "Since Young Boss Gao is giving me such face, of course I'll keep you company for one. But 'guiding light' is too heavy—I prefer the position of 'strategy supplier.'" With that, under everyone's watching eyes, she drank the white liquor down steadily. From throat to stomach ran a line of heat, but her expression was beautifully controlled—only the flush on her cheeks deepened a little, the gleam in her eyes growing more limpid, reflecting the room's lights and the sheen of her gold gown, with a kind of heart-stopping splendor.

"Bravo!" Wang Qian applauded.

Gao Jieyuan's eyes lit up further, and he was about to say something more when Yao Yuan had already turned to Jiang Tiantian beside her, raising her glass. "Miss Jiang, this is for you. Thank you, and thank your father for his work, and thank you for your attention to this field of 'emotional strategy.'"

Jiang Tiantian hurriedly raised her glass, the eyes behind her lenses flashing a sharp light. "Teacher Yao, this is exactly what I want to ask you about in depth. You encourage marriage, you encourage having children—in the current climate of a certain 'radical singlehood' or 'fear of marriage and childbearing,' that seems like a 'clear stream' running against the current. And yet your core is entirely rational calculation and the safeguarding of interest. How do you grasp the balance point between these? Or rather, how do you explain this 'positive pragmatism' to your audience?"

The question thrown out, the table went quiet for an instant. Even Chen Ye, who was ladling soup for Liu Qingzhuo, stopped moving, and Zhao Yiming set down his chopsticks too, turning a calm gaze toward Yao Yuan, as if he too were waiting for an answer outside any algorithm.

Yao Yuan gently swirled the amber liquor in her glass, the gold polish on her fingertips mirrored against it. She mused a moment before speaking, her voice clear and steady, carrying that tone peculiar to her livestreams—soothing and intensely persuasive at once.

"I encourage marriage and childbearing because, under the existing social structure and biological reality, for the vast majority of ordinary women, this is still a path of relatively lower resistance, with potentially higher overall returns—emotional support, mutual economic aid, shared risk, the realization of reproductive value, and so on. But that absolutely does not mean letting women walk into it blindly, sacrificing themselves." She paused, her gaze sweeping over those at the table before landing back on Jiang Tiantian's intent face. "My core is this: you should go in, but not go in blind; you should give, but not give for nothing; you should pursue a long-term relationship, but you must make sure the terms of that relationship favor you. Marriage is a special kind of partnership project; childbearing is a major joint investment. What a woman needs to do is run that project with a partner's clear eyes, and evaluate that investment with an investor's caution. Use feeling as the lubricant; use law and practical means as the safeguard. Not losing out in the middle is the precondition for this game being playable at all. If a relationship is destined to leave you worse off, then it should never have begun in the first place."

Jiang Tiantian was recording rapidly in her phone's memo, fingers flying, and asked without lifting her head, "And love? Pure emotional attraction—how much weight does it carry in your model?"

"Emotional attraction is the ticket in, the start-up capital." Yao Yuan smiled, that smile rational to the point of coldness. "But it isn't the capital for ongoing operations. A long-term partnership relies on matched value, clear rules, and the steady accumulation of shared interest. Love matters very much—it makes all the hardship sweet to swallow—but it can't be used to pay the bills, and it can't serve as compensation when the other party breaches. So I teach women to cherish love, but not to be superstitious about it; to wield love, but not to be held hostage by it."

The table fell silent for a few seconds, only the soft bubbling of the lamb hotpot base.

"Brilliant." Jin Lan was the first to applaud, her eyes full of admiration. "Couldn't be clearer! I used to be far too ruled by my feelings!"

Mrs. Wang nodded over and over too, looking deeply moved.

Gao Jieyuan stroked his chin, his smile a little playful. "President Yao, this theory of yours sends a chill down us men's backs. So from now on, to start a relationship, we have to pass your 'risk assessment' first?"

"A quality candidate like Young Boss Gao would naturally pass the highest standard of assessment." Yao Yuan deflected the force with ease, raising her glass toward him, her smile flawless.

Jiang Tiantian let out a long breath, lifted her head, her eyes ardent. "Teacher Yao, this system of yours is more… real, more powerful, than any women's narrative I've encountered before. It doesn't shout empty slogans—it provides an actionable action map. I absolutely must find time to do an in-depth interview with you and lay it all out systematically."

"Welcome anytime." Yao Yuan smiled and lightly touched her glass to Jiang Tiantian's, the gold earring tassels swaying.

The topic was ignited. The women began discussing specific "strategies": how to negotiate a prenuptial agreement effectively without hurting feelings; how to structure family assets to safeguard women's interests; how to secure a partner's substantive support rather than verbal concern before and after childbirth; even how to identify a "high cost-performance" partner. The men joined in too—Wang Qian shared his takeaways as an "object of assessment," Chen Ye spoke gently about "building an equal, contractual intimate relationship," and Gao Jieyuan half-jokingly complained that "these days you have to hand over a business plan just to court a girl."

Amid this conversation woven of reason and revelry, Chen Ye and Liu Qingzhuo's interactions looked especially natural and sweet, yet faintly carried a "deal closed" understanding. Chen Ye would attentively spin around the dish Liu Qingzhuo wanted to eat, listen seriously when she spoke, occasionally adding a quiet line or two that often cut right to the point. When Liu Qingzhuo got animated, she'd unconsciously pat Chen Ye's arm, and Chen Ye would only smile and let her, his eyes holding the indulgence of someone holding the winning hand. Once, when Liu Qingzhuo's glass was nearly empty, Chen Ye quite naturally took up the bottle and filled it for her, his fingers lightly brushing the back of her hand; Liu Qingzhuo lifted her eyes to him, and the two exchanged a smile—a smile that, beyond affection, seemed to hold a mutual, knowing grasp of each other's terms, pace, and progress.

"That's enough out of you two," Gao Jieyuan said sourly. "How long have you even been together, and you're already this in sync? Could you spare a thought for those of us still single, stuck at the 'due diligence' stage?"

Liu Qingzhuo arched a brow, threw an arm around Chen Ye's, lifted her chin, her smile vivid and crisp. "What, jealous? If you're jealous, go find someone yourself—just like Teacher Yao says, assess properly and strike precisely."

Held by her, the tips of Chen Ye's ears went slightly red, but his smile stayed gentle and tolerant. He said nothing, only patting the back of her hand lightly—everything understood without a word.

"Find someone how? Haven't all the good women already evolved by your standards?" Gao Jieyuan said, his gaze drifting toward Yao Yuan, faintly, with a challenging edge.

Yao Yuan was sipping soup in small mouthfuls with a silver spoon, as if she hadn't heard. Only a very faint curve at the corner of her lips showed she'd taken it all in.

After three rounds of wine, the mood at the table grew warmer still. Zhao Yiming, rarely, took the initiative to raise his glass, toasting Jin Lan and Wang Qian, then turning to Yao Yuan. His voice stayed steady and clear amid the noise. "President Yao, I'll drink to you too. Thank you for the data you provided, and… the line of thinking."

Yao Yuan knew he meant the AI model and their last conversation. She raised her glass and touched it to his, the rims meeting with a crisp ring. "You're welcome. Any progress?"

"Sort of, and sort of not." Zhao Yiming drained his glass, set it down without changing expression, and pushed up his glasses. "On the technical side, we rebuilt the emotional firewall and adjusted the weight parameters, trying to frame emotional responses with a stricter 'contract logic.' But the problem persists." He paused, seeming to search for a way a layperson could understand. "Your data—or rather, the kind of highly rational, interest-driven emotional decision-making mode you represent—produces a strong rejection reaction when fused with the more 'humanized' empathy model we're trying to build. It's like…" He thought for a moment. "Like an extremely precise business logic trying to understand a poem. It can analyze the poem's meter, word frequency, the probability of an image appearing—but it can't understand why the combination of these elements moves people. Your data lacks that 'overflow that can't be computed.'"

Yao Yuan swirled her glass, the ice bobbing in the golden liquid. She turned it over thoughtfully. "Maybe that 'overflow' is unnecessary noise to begin with—even harmful. At least in an emotional model that pursues stable returns. A person is moved by a poem, and then what? After the emotion, life still has to go on, the bills still have to be paid, the relationship still has to be run. If your AI truly wants to simulate 'successful' emotional interaction, perhaps it should focus on learning how to efficiently reach cooperative goals and create positive experiences—not on simulating those useless 'moments of being moved' that lower decision-making efficiency."

Zhao Yiming looked at her, the eyes behind his lenses deep, as if observing some complex algorithm through her. "But your livestream data shows that the cases you consider 'successful'—those women who gained stable relationships and satisfying lives—weren't entirely without that 'overflow' along the way. They too had irrational impulses, uneconomical concessions, even foolish trust."

"That's part of the strategy." Yao Yuan smiled, the smile against the foil of her gold gown taking on a metallic quality. "Precise calculation needs a little smoke screen of sentiment. Complete rationality puts people on guard; a measured dose of 'irrationality' is what lowers the other party's defenses and brings the cooperation about. It's called the 'emotional lubricant'—the dosage and the timing matter. What your AI needs to learn isn't to 'possess' emotion, but to 'simulate' and 'wield' it, just like wielding any other tool."

Zhao Yiming was silent for a few seconds, then suddenly gave a very faint laugh, and that smile looked oddly out of place on his usually calm face. "Interesting. To instrumentalize and parameterize emotion as well. That actually happens to align with my latest cross-disciplinary line of thinking. I've noted it down."
