Their conversation was cut off by Wang Qian's booming voice. "What are you two on about, so deep? AI this, emotion that—today's New Year's Day, no talking work allowed! Drink! President Zhao, I drink to you—thank you for leaving us men a way to survive, for not turning us into AIs too!"

Everyone burst out laughing. Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming exchanged a glance and raised their glasses to join the next lively round of toasts. No one noticed that, as Zhao Yiming tipped his head back to drink, his gaze again flicked swiftly across the glittering yellow diamond at Yao Yuan's throat, a deeply focused light in his eyes—the kind a scientist holds when facing an unsolved riddle—flashing past in an instant.

This meal went on for nearly three hours. By the end the table was a wreck of cups and plates, several wine bottles emptied. Everyone had drunk a fair amount, faces flushed with the heat of alcohol, laughter loud, eyes hazy, the carefully maintained social masks of ordinary days loosening before the alcohol and familiar friends.

When they stepped out of Yellow River Tower, it was already past nine. The winter night's cold wind was knife-keen, striking their faces and instantly sweeping away the stifling heat pent up in the room, jolting their dazed heads alert. Yellow River Tower glowed from top to bottom in the night, the strings of red lanterns hung from its upturned eaves swinging violently in the wind, casting a warm, shifting halo that, in everyone's tipsy eyes, ran together into a flowing river of light. The distant river surface was ink-black, reflecting the cold bands of light from the high-rises on the far bank, glittering and rippling like a handful of crushed diamonds scattered into a bottomless abyss.

Suddenly, a thunderous "boom" tore through the cold night sky.

"It's the municipal New Year's fireworks show," Jin Lan explained with a smile, pulling her stole tighter, her face especially vivid amid the flickering brilliance. "Every year, set off right across the river. This spot of ours is the perfect viewing position."

Everyone stopped, lifted their heads, and fell silent. Even the noisiest one, Gao Jieyuan, shut his mouth for the moment. Riotous colors burst, dissolved, and burst again in everyone's pupils. Yao Yuan tilted her neck back slightly, that beautiful line fully exposed to the shifting sky-light. The tassels at her ears and the yellow diamond at her throat flared with each explosion's flash into one sharp, fleeting glitter, then sank back into darkness, waiting for the next illumination. A corner of her gold skirt was lifted by the river wind, then settled. The fireworks' splendor flowed across her face, dyeing her meticulously drawn makeup with dreamlike colors, and those eyes—always clear, always calculating, always scrutinizing—now reflected a sky full of fleeting, magnificent nothingness, showing for once a rare, almost bewildered absorption. She was watching the fireworks; and what Gao Jieyuan, quietly lifting his phone, froze in his frame was her radiance set off against the fireworks—a luxurious, fragile beauty.

Zhao Yiming stood a little apart, looking neither at the fireworks nor at Yao Yuan. He looked at the place across the river where the fireworks rose, at those points of light that turned to ash the instant after lighting the sky, pushed up his glasses, and no one knew what he was thinking. Chen Ye lightly held Liu Qingzhuo's shoulder, and Liu Qingzhuo leaned into his arms, head tipped back to the sky, a smile of pure appreciation on her face. Jiang Tiantian narrowed her eyes slightly, her lenses catching the light; what she watched seemed to be not the fireworks themselves but this crowd watching them, this scene, this atmosphere—like a cool-headed field researcher.

Ten minutes later, the last enormous, all-color umbrella-shaped firework blazed at the highest point, then wilted soundlessly. The night sky returned to a settled black, leaving only a faint, somewhat acrid smell of gunpowder smoke drifting in thin wisps through the bitter wind—proof that the splendid dream just past had not been a collective hallucination.

Wang Qian glanced at his watch, drew his wife's shoulder close, his voice carrying the hoarseness and warmth of drink. "Well, we have to head off first. The little ancestor at home is still waiting—they've called several times already."

Mrs. Wang was reluctant to part, glancing again at Yao Yuan, her cheeks ruddy from the wind. "Teacher Yao, I'm so sorry—let's gather again when there's a chance. C-can I take another photo with you? Just one!"

Yao Yuan nodded with a smile. "Of course."

Mrs. Wang immediately stood at Yao Yuan's side, and Wang Qian took out his phone, its screen light illuminating two differently smiling faces—one the excitement and contentment of meeting an idol, the other the usual, perfect, well-mannered smile. The gold skirt and the cream knit dress pressed together, like two utterly different worlds curiously sharing one frame.

"All right, all right, off you go—the child comes first," Jin Lan urged.

The Wang couple said their goodbyes several times over, then walked off arm in arm toward the parking lot, their figures soon vanishing into the night.

Chen Ye and Liu Qingzhuo came over too. Liu Qingzhuo still had the excited afterglow of the fireworks on her face, the tip of her nose a little red from the cold, which only added to her charm. Clutching Chen Ye's arm tightly, she said to the group with a smile, "We're off too. Chen Ye says he'll take me to that new warm-pavilion lounge by the river for a cup of mulled wine, clear off the drink, and along the way… talk over some plans for next year." As she spoke she lifted her eyes to Chen Ye, who returned a gentle smile carrying the ease of someone with everything in hand.

"Look at you two, moving fast," Gao Jieyuan teased.

"Efficiency is the primary productive force." Liu Qingzhuo arched a brow, her smile crisp—a double meaning in every word.

Chen Ye nodded to the group. "Happy New Year. We'll go on ahead."

The two left leaning close together, their figures stretched long in the mingled light of the streetlamps and distant neon—sweet, intimate, and carrying the solidity of a strong-meets-strong alliance.

Five remained: Yao Yuan, Jin Lan, Zhao Yiming, Gao Jieyuan, and Jiang Tiantian.

The cold wind seemed stronger now, scraping across their burning faces, bringing a stinging kind of clarity. The alcohol still burned inside them, driving off the chill and stirring a restlessness that wanted to prolong the revelry, to resist the lonely cold of being alone after the party broke up. Jin Lan rubbed her hands, pulled her fur stole tighter, and her eyes, amid the lingering smoke of the fireworks and the buzz of drink, turned and lit up. "Breaking up now is no fun. It's only just past nine—nightlife is just getting started. I know a new place, Liuyue Palace, supposedly fantastic—atmosphere, music, drinks, all top-tier. How about it, move the party there?"

Gao Jieyuan was the first to respond, almost impatiently. "We absolutely have to go! Anything Sister Lan recommends is sure to be good! On a freezing night like this, we've got to find somewhere lively to keep warm!" His gaze drifted to Yao Yuan again, that heat more scorching than the drink.

Zhao Yiming said nothing, only turning his gaze to Yao Yuan, as if waiting for her decision—or perhaps it made no difference to him where they went or what they did; he was simply going with the flow.

Jiang Tiantian looked at Yao Yuan too, pushing up her glasses, a clear research drive in her eyes—she had little interest in the club itself, but she was deeply interested in the subject of "the behavioral patterns of Yao Yuan, the Godmother of Feelings, in a nightclub social setting." It was an excellent field site for close observation.

Yao Yuan felt the night wind passing through her hair, brushing across the cold metal at her ears; the slightly tipsy warmth of the alcohol inside her and the restlessness of blood racing faster; that complex, brimming feeling in her chest of having been excited, praised, watched, briefly shaken by the fireworks, then jolted awake by the cold wind. She liked control, liked being surrounded, liked to observe, and also liked, now and then, to throw herself into a sensory current that required no excessive thought. She curved her red lips, and that smile, in the city night settling over them once more, was as brilliant as the diamond at her throat.

"Let's go."

========================================

Chapter 42 — The Hunting Ground of Liuyue Palace
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640289.html

Liuyue Palace sat at the far end of the city, a club that had been hailed as top-tier the moment it opened. As the car pulled up to the entrance, Yao Yuan looked through the window at the building's exterior — a minimalist, futuristic black geometric mass, its outer walls clad in some new kind of smart material that reflected and refracted light. At that moment, in time with the muffled waves of sound seeping out from inside, it streamed with shifting, dazzling, dizzying colored light — like mercury, like lava, like the aurora — as if the building itself were alive, breathing, pulsing. The entrance was a narrow, towering black doorway that seemed to swallow light whole, and above it glowed three Chinese characters in seal script — "Liuyue Palace." The forms were ancient, but the light was a cold, ghostly blue: heavy on design, carrying an air of mystery and seduction.

The instant she stepped out of the car, the dull, massive bass — still perfectly audible even after passing through those thick walls and doors — rose up through the soles of her feet, like a heartbeat from deep within the earth, hammering at everyone's chest. A long line had already formed at the door, kept in order by tall, stone-faced security guards in black uniforms. The men and women waiting were all fashionably dressed, their makeup immaculate; even in the biting wind of a winter night, they held their backs straight, showing off their best selves, their eyes a mix of anticipation, anxiety, and a faint, barely perceptible vanity. Jin Lan was clearly a regular. She led them straight to the VIP entrance on the side, where waiters in black suits with earpieces were already waiting respectfully, silently pulling open that heavy, glossy black door that seemed to absorb every sound.

Sound, heat, light and shadow, and a thick smell that mixed expensive perfume, alcohol, hormones, and some kind of hallucinogenic fragrance came crashing into their faces like a tangible tide, swallowing them whole in an instant.

Unlike the lavish, dreamlike KTV atmosphere of the "Crimson Night Pavilion," Liuyue Palace was a pure, contemporary nightclub space, full of futurism and sensory impact. In the center of the vast hall, whose ceiling rose nearly ten meters, stood a huge floating DJ booth made of multi-tiered metal. On it, a DJ in a fluorescent skintight outfit and enormous headphones worked the turntables in a frenzy, body convulsing to the heavy beats he was creating, like a priest performing some mysterious rite. From the ceiling, hundreds of programmable LED light spheres and laser emitters formed a complex web of light, which now — with every drumbeat, every scream of the music — shifted into dizzying geometric patterns, explosive trails of light, and sharp beams that sliced through space. All around were split-level booth areas, divided by semi-transparent glass with light patterns flowing inside. Every table held a glowing ice bucket, oddly shaped drinkware, and softly burning mood lamps. The air was so thick it seemed to have texture; every breath carried a cloying, intoxicating scent that quickened the pulse.

The dance floor was already a churning, writhing sea of people. Young bodies swayed, collided, and pressed together under the strobing, eerily colored lights, faces drunk or frenzied, every one of them written over with an unreserved chase after tonight, after this very moment, after sensory thrill. The music was industrial, dark electronic house, the drums heavy and dense as machine-gun fire, the high-frequency synths shrieking and tearing at the eardrums, as if striking the nerve endings directly.

The booth Jin Lan had reserved was in the best vantage spot on the second floor, directly facing the DJ booth and looking down over the whole dance floor. A waiter showed them to their seats and quickly brought out the drink package they'd ordered in advance — a full bottle of Ace of Spades champagne, single malt whisky, and several specialty cocktails that changed color with temperature, served in bizarrely shaped glassware, dry ice bobbing in the liquid and hissing up wisps of white mist like some witch's brew.

Yao Yuan slipped off her black cashmere coat and handed it to a waiter, leaving that brilliant golden gown once again fully exposed to the club's kaleidoscopic light. Under the strobes, the lasers, and the self-lit tabletops, the dark patterns woven into the silk seemed to come alive, the golden sheen shifting unpredictably — now like flowing molten gold, now like cold metal — forming a striking visual contrast with her snow-white skin, her crimson lips, and her glittering jewelry. She sank into the soft deep-purple sofa of the booth, long legs elegantly crossed, the diamond-studded toe of her silver high heel tapping lightly at the floor in time with the heavy beat. Her gaze swept calmly over the seething, self-forgetting sea of people below, then over her companions with their various expressions, finally settling on the cold-misting, blue-violet gradient cocktail in her own hand, the corner of her mouth curving into a barely perceptible arc — the arc of an observer.

Jiang Tiantian sat beside her, body slightly stiff, clearly not used to this direct, intense sensory bombardment. But her eyes were busy, darting around like a high-precision camera, recording every subtle expression and movement of Yao Yuan's, recording the environment, recording Gao Jieyuan eagerly pouring drinks, recording the profile of Zhao Yiming calmly sipping his whisky. Jin Lan had already begun swaying to the music, laughing as she clinked glasses with Gao Jieyuan. Zhao Yiming sat in the shadows across from them, whisky in hand, his gaze seeming to rest on the DJ booth — or perhaps to pierce through all the noise and land somewhere in the void.

Gao Jieyuan had quickly gotten into the mood. A few drinks in, with the environment as a catalyst, he was thoroughly worked up, shoulders rocking to the music. He poured himself a full glass of champagne and tipped his head back to down it, his throat bobbing. Then he turned, leaned forward, and over the roar of the music shouted at Yao Yuan: "Boss Yao! This place is electric, right?! Want to dance?"

Yao Yuan raised her eyes to him. The man's eyes blazed with a naked light kindled by alcohol, desire, and the hormones in the air, his smile fervent, carrying an air that brooked no refusal. The deafening music and flashing lights blurred the everyday courtesies of the social scene and amplified a kind of primal aggression. She didn't move. She only lifted the cocktail glass slowly to her lips and took a sip, the cold liquid sliding over her tongue with its complex fruity notes and the sharp burn of spirits. Then she set the glass down, curving her red lips at Gao Jieyuan, the smile heart-stoppingly beautiful in the dreamlike light, her voice carrying through the music — low, but clear:

"I'm tired. I'll sit a while. Go enjoy yourself, Young Boss Gao."

Gao Jieyuan's smile and his movements both froze for a beat, a clear flash of frustration crossing his eyes, followed by an even fiercer flare. But he quickly shrugged it off, dragging out a bigger, devil-may-care grin and raising his voice: "Fine! Then I'll go have fun on my own! Music this good, it'd be a waste not to dance!" With that, he turned and squeezed into the passage that led down to the dance floor, and soon the figure in his wine-red velvet suit vanished into the writhing sea of people and treacherous light below.

Jin Lan watched his back disappear at the stairwell, then leaned in to Yao Yuan's ear, breath warm with perfume against the curve of it, voice carrying a laugh and a meaning only the two of them would catch: "So Jieyuan still hasn't passed your 'risk assessment'?"

Yao Yuan neither confirmed nor denied it. She swirled her glass lightly, watching the blue-violet liquid blend with the dry-ice mist, her voice flat and even: "All investment carries risk; enter the market with caution. Especially an asset whose emotional value swings too wildly."

Jin Lan burst out laughing and clapped her on the shoulder, picking up her own glass: "Brilliant! Come on, forget about them — let's drink to ourselves!"

Jiang Tiantian took in every word and gesture of their exchange, quickly jotting a few keywords into the notes app on her phone. Then she picked up her own glass of juice, which she'd barely touched, and asked Yao Yuan in a low voice — a voice that took real effort to hear over the music: "Teacher Yuanyuan, in a setting like this, how do you usually... position yourself? As an observer, a participant, or... something else?"

Yao Yuan turned her head, and a strobe flash swept across her face just then, lighting the yellow diamond up like a small sun. She looked into Jiang Tiantian's knowledge-hungry eyes behind the lenses and smiled faintly, the smile in the shifting light somehow both genuine and remote:

"All of them, and none of them. Here, you can be anyone, or you can be no one. What matters is knowing exactly why you're here, what you want to get — and what price you're willing to pay." She paused, gaze drifting to the dance floor below, where Gao Jieyuan seemed to have found a partner and was dancing close with a girl in a glittering short dress. "Take him, for instance. He wants a brief sensory thrill and the rush of conquest, and he pays in money, time, and possible aftermath. Perfectly fair."

Jiang Tiantian fell into thought.

Down on the dance floor, the music shifted abruptly, the drums growing denser and heavier, as if pounding directly on the heart. The crowd let out a frenzied roar of cheers and screams. The lights went black, leaving only a few ghostly pale spotlights sweeping the floor. Then a single, blinding white pillar of light slammed down onto a small round stage rising in the center of the dance floor —

A figure in near-transparent fabric covered in silver sequins and tassels writhed up onto the stage like a water sprite, moving to the exotic drumbeat that suddenly exploded into life. Every motion was full of wild provocation, the tassels flying, the body shimmering under the spotlight, igniting the whole room's emotion to boiling point in an instant.

Cheers, whistles, screams pitched high with excitement surged up like a tsunami, almost lifting the roof off.

In this deafening flood of pure sensory clamor, Yao Yuan sat quietly at the border of shadow and flash in the second-floor booth, the golden hem of her gown streaming with a ghostly glow, like some deity standing utterly apart yet impossibly radiant. She watched, listened, felt the tremor shared by her chest and her eardrums, the alcohol burning slowly and steadily inside her.

Zhao Yiming, seated across from her, had drawn his gaze back at some point too. Through the swaying light, the drifting dry-ice mist, the writhing bodies and the rims of glasses, he looked toward Yao Yuan. His gaze was calm, deep, without any trace of admiration or desire — more like he was observing an extremely complex dynamic model, one driven jointly by emotional data, social strategy, biological instinct, and environmental variables.

Yao Yuan seemed to sense his attention. She drew her gaze back from the dance floor and met his across the space.

The music thundered; no one could hear anyone speak.

But, looking at him, Yao Yuan suddenly raised her glass — so faintly it was almost impossible to see.

Then she tilted her head slightly, her gaze turning back to the seething sea below, the symbol of all the uncalculated, chaotic, vivid desire. Her red lips parted and closed; she said something without a sound.

Zhao Yiming read it clearly.

She was saying:

"Happy New Year."

Then she tipped her head back and drank down the blue-violet, ice-cold liquid in her glass in a single swallow. The cold drink collided thunderously with the searing blood inside her, with this seething place, with this brand-new year that had only just begun — a year destined, like every other, to thread its way through precise calculation and uncontrolled risk.

The deafening music soaked every inch of Liuyue Palace like a viscous fluid. The second-floor booth offered a little distance from the crowd, but the low-frequency drumbeat still struck at the spine, setting off waves of resonance. Yao Yuan had drunk down that cold specialty cocktail, and the blue-violet liquor seemed to carry a current, burning all the way from her throat to her stomach, mixing quickly with the alcohol already in her and boiling up into an even fiercer heat. The flush on her cheeks deepened and faded in the shifting light, and as her eyes moved, that clear, scrutinizing gaze softened at the edges into a misty glow, revealing a languid, dazed quality rarely seen in her.

Gao Jieyuan, after going wild on the dance floor for a while, squeezed back to the booth carrying a fresh wave of heat and the scent of someone else's perfume. His hair was damp at the brow, his eyes startlingly bright. He moved straight to Yao Yuan's side again, this time sitting even closer — close enough that she could almost feel the heat radiating off his body. "Sis," he said, switching to a more intimate address, his voice in the gaps of the music deliberately sweet and clingy, holding out a small dish of fruit as if by magic, delicate silver forks stuck in it, "tired from dancing? Have some fruit, sober up a little."

The word "Sis" he drew out with endless twists, both pointing to the fact that Yao Yuan was a few years older than him and wrapping it in a sugar coating of both coquetry and challenge. Yao Yuan turned her head to look at him. In the young man's eyes was an undisguised, conquest-tinged affection, along with a certain self-assurance of "I know I'm young and I have the capital." She smiled and didn't take the dish of fruit, only lifting her champagne glass for a sip, her voice low but clear: "As long as you're enjoying yourself, Young Boss Gao. No need to look after me."

"How could I not," Gao Jieyuan said, setting the fruit dish on the table in front of her and seating himself on the armrest of the sofa beside her. The spot was half-height, letting him look down at her while seeming less overbearing. "Of course I should look after you, Sis. The way you look tonight, you're the center of attention wherever you sit. I have to keep off all the wandering bees and butterflies who don't know any better." As he spoke, his gaze swept pointedly over the others in the booth, lingering half a second on Zhao Yiming, drinking alone a little way off.

Zhao Yiming acted as if he hadn't heard, hadn't seen. He leaned deep into the sofa, long legs crossed, still holding that glass of whisky, its ice nearly melted away. His gaze rested on the shifting light and tide of people on the dance floor below, the line of his profile looking somewhat cold and hard in the flickering light. Only when Yao Yuan gently dodged Gao Jieyuan's hand offering the fruit, and when Gao Jieyuan called her "Sis," did the fingers around his glass tighten almost imperceptibly for an instant, then loosen again, his gaze still resting calmly on the distance, as if none of it had anything to do with him. But a kind of invisible, cold barrier seemed to quietly congeal around him, forming a silent standoff with the searing aggression radiating off Gao Jieyuan.

Yao Yuan took in every silent subtlety between the two men. Gao Jieyuan's attack was blunt and fervent, like a fire burning recklessly with no care for anything; Zhao Yiming's silence was like the deep sea — calm on the surface, but hiding beneath it an immeasurable, perhaps more dangerous power. She was genuinely curious about Zhao Yiming — a curiosity drawn to a top-tier mind and an explorer of unknown territory. Occasionally — watching his profile as he focused on tuning a model under the lab's cold white lights, or catching that flash of near-pure excitement in his eyes after they'd talked through an idea late at night — she would even feel a flicker of something beyond their working relationship, a physical, man-and-woman appreciation. He was clever, calm, possessed of a focus stripped of worldly restlessness, and to Yao Yuan, who had seen every mask of desire there was, that held a distinct appeal. But she had Yu Hao — an easy, well-matched, no-trouble bed partner. More importantly, she knew full well that her collaboration with Zhao Yiming involved enormous interests, and introducing a romantic relationship was the highest-risk variable of all, one that would wreck every carefully calculated balance. So she kept that subtle attraction and curiosity carefully confined within the bounds of "appreciation" and "the flirtation game." She enjoyed that faint, intangible pull, enjoyed the gaze he occasionally turned on her that went beyond data observation — but she never took a single step further. Just like now: she was happy to see the tension formed between Gao Jieyuan's attentiveness and Zhao Yiming's silence, which made her feel she stood at the center of the eye of the storm, safely in command of every flow of emotion.

"Wandering bees and butterflies don't worry me," Yao Yuan said, swirling her glass and giving Gao Jieyuan a radiant smile, a smile of devastating force in Liuyue Palace's hazy light. "What worries me are the little butterflies who think their young wings are strong enough to go crashing about everywhere."

Gao Jieyuan's heart swayed at her smile, and the soft thorn buried in her words pricked him lightly — yet far from annoyed, he found it all the more thrilling. He leaned in closer, almost breathing the words against her ear: "Then teach me, Sis — how should I fly so it doesn't count as crashing about?"

Just then the music changed to another track. No longer the heavy industrial pounding from before, but a rhythm blending retro funk with modern electronics, the melody sexy yet lively, the beat full of bounce, instantly kindling a different mood on the dance floor.

Yao Yuan didn't answer Gao Jieyuan. She suddenly drained the rest of the champagne in her glass, set it down, and stood up. The golden gown flowed like water with her movement, refracting the light sweeping in from every direction; she was like an ornate candelabra suddenly lit in the dim booth.

"This song's not bad," she said, her voice carrying a slight post-drink huskiness and a faint, eager lightness. She didn't look at Gao Jieyuan, didn't look at Zhao Yiming, her gaze cast toward the dance floor below, now writhing all the more merrily to the new music.

"You want to dance, Sis?" Gao Jieyuan's eyes lit up, and he immediately stood too.

Yao Yuan ignored him. She was already swaying her shoulders lightly to the beat, stepping in time, heading toward the stairs down to the dance floor. The ground trembled, and every step she took seemed to land on the music's pulse. Jin Lan let out a whistle, and Jiang Tiantian dropped her observer's posture too, watching with startled, rapt attention. Zhao Yiming's gaze finally drew back from the distance and settled on that golden figure walking step by step into the whirlpool of light below.

Yao Yuan didn't rush eagerly into the densest part of the crowd like the young girls. She found a relatively open spot at the edge of the dance floor and planted herself there. The waves of music wrapped completely around her, the flickering beams sweeping over her whole body now and then. She closed her eyes and drew a deep breath, as if soaking in this baptism of sound and light, and as if shutting out, for now, all the subtle calculation, probing, and rational analysis of the booth.

Then she began to move.

No deliberately choreographed steps, not even a fixed pattern. She simply surrendered herself completely to the music. Her neck and shoulders, her waist and hips, her arms, her legs and feet — every joint, every inch of muscle, seemed to understand that retro-yet-fresh rhythm. Her swaying was gentle at first, like water plants drifting in a deep current, her waist and hips the axis carrying her upper body through smooth, rounded arcs, the golden hem of her gown rippling out in tender waves. Her arms moved naturally and expressively, now stretching like wings, now curling as if to embrace herself, even the faint tremor of her fingertips seeming to speak the breath of the melody.

As the music moved into a fiercer passage, her movements opened up and intensified too. A crisp toss of her head sent a few strands of long hair tumbling loose from their soft knot, brushing across her flushed cheeks and throat. Her footwork turned nimble and springy — forward, back, turning — pivoting, stamping, tapping on the smooth floor, every step steady and precise. With each spin, the golden hem bloomed into a brilliant flower, leaving a dazzling afterimage under the strobes. Her waist was unbelievably supple, swaying to the side, arching back, rising and falling in waves, displaying the beauty of a woman's curves to the fullest — yet without the faintest hint of vulgarity, only a confident sensuality born of powerful control over her own body.

Sweat gradually beaded on her clear forehead and collarbones, glittering in fine flecks under the lights. Her cheeks grew redder and redder, her lips parting slightly with her breathing, moist and crimson. Her eyes were misty, their focus dissolved, no longer carrying their usual clear-eyed scrutiny, replaced by a wholly immersed intoxication captured by the music and the pleasure of her own body. She was no longer the Godmother of Feelings weighing gains and losses, no longer the queen of the social scene navigating men's gazes — she was simply a woman who had utterly released her body's instinct in the music, beautiful, vivid, full of a primal, irresistible allure.

Many on the dance floor noticed her and instinctively cleared a little space for her, their eyes admiring, or envious, or simply struck by her beauty. Gao Jieyuan had long since squeezed in near her, twisting clumsily to the music, his gaze glued to her like adhesive, full of undisguised craving and certainty of victory. He even tried to move in close and dance with her, but Yao Yuan seemed immersed in another dimension; a light pivot turned her naturally away from his approach, and she carried on with her own dance. That self-possessed focus, oblivious to everyone around her, only made her more mysterious and seductive.

Zhao Yiming was still seated in the second-floor booth. From this angle, he could see that shining figure on the dance floor clearly. He watched her spin and sway in the light and shadow, watched the rare look of intoxication on her face with every mask stripped away, watched the sweat-damp hair at her temples and her chest heaving violently with each breath. The hand holding his glass was steady, his expression still calm — only the eyes behind the lenses were deep as a bottomless icy pool, with something settling slowly, coldly, inside them. He didn't show the searing interest Gao Jieyuan did, but that long, unblinking stare carried, in itself, a presence stronger than any words. He watched her as if assessing the running of a most complex dynamic model — and as if simply... watching.

One song ended, and another, slower and stickier, followed at once. Yao Yuan's movements eased with it into a more casual sway, breathing hard, lifting a hand to tuck the damp hair at her cheek behind her ear — a simple gesture that, in her current state, was full of allure. Gao Jieyuan seized the chance and tried to move in close again.

========================================

Chapter 43 — Yao Yuan Uses Godmother-of-Feelings Logic to Dismantle Jin Lan's Ex
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640290.html

It was right then that a small commotion broke out at the edge of the dance floor. A young man — fashionably dressed, rather handsome — was struggling to push past the security guards, shouting something frantically toward the second-floor booths. You could just make out the words "Lanlan," "let me through," "let me explain." His eyes were locked hard on Jin Lan's spot upstairs.

It was Jin Lan's ex, the one she'd dumped not long ago. Yao Yuan remembered him vaguely — some small-time model and influencer, nearly ten years younger than Jin Lan. For the whole length of the relationship, everything from his clothes and shoes to his car and rent had been bankrolled by Jin Lan. Then she'd found out he was secretly using her money to court a younger college girl. Jin Lan cut him off on the spot, severed every cent of support, and tossed him out.

So losing his "sugar mama" had made life hard, and now he'd come crawling back, hungry for the pasture again. Yao Yuan stopped dancing, her breath not yet steady, her chest still rising and falling, and watched the farce downstairs with cold eyes. Up on the second floor Jin Lan had seen it too. Her face darkened in an instant, and she made a hard, cutting gesture at the guards.

The guards immediately blocked him harder. Seeing no way through, the ex actually dropped to his knees with a thud at the edge of the dance floor. Indifferent to the startled stares and the phones filming him, he started wailing at the top of his lungs: "Lanlan! I was wrong! I really know I was wrong! I can't live without you! Please, give me one more chance! I love you!"

The music played on, but his sobbing cut through part of the noise, drawing more sidelong glances and murmurs. Jin Lan went white with fury and rose to her feet to come down.

But Yao Yuan got there first. She parted the crowd and walked over. Her golden figure stood out sharply in the chaos, like a moving beam of judgment that demanded to be noticed. She stopped a few steps from the man kneeling and howling on the ground and looked down at him. Her face still carried a fading flush and a sheen of sweat, but her eyes had returned to that calm, dissecting sharpness.

"Save your energy. Stop the act." Her voice wasn't loud, yet it carried a strange clarity and force that pierced the din, making the man's wailing catch in his throat. She sized him up, her gaze like a scalpel, sweeping from the obviously last-season designer clothes on his body to his face — gaunt from agitation and probably hard living, yet still carefully groomed — as if she were analyzing the most ordinary case study. "You say you love her? Let's break down this so-called 'love' of yours."

She tilted her head slightly, as if genuinely thinking it over, her gold earring tracing a cool arc with the movement.

"In this relationship, what you ate, wore, used, and lived in — even that designer coat on your back, out of season but still designer — your social spending, all the capital that let you keep up 'appearances,' it all came from her. She was your client, your investor, the hand that fed and clothed you. And you, as the contractor, your core value and job description was to provide emotional value, to look after the client's feelings and needs, to keep this partnership pleasant and stable. It was a perfectly clear value exchange."

Her tone was even, her words precise — no insults, only calm statement, yet more humiliating and corrosive than any abuse. The people close enough to hear let out murmurs of sudden understanding and snickers. The man's face shifted from agitated red to ghastly pale.

Her words were cold and naked, like a boning knife, stripping the man's "love" costume clean off. The people who heard let out low jeers and laughter. The man's face went from red to white, then from white to green, fury and shame mixing as he jabbed a finger at Yao Yuan. "You — what are you talking about! Lanlan and I are truly in love!"

"Truly?" Yao Yuan gave a soft laugh, the sound cutting clearly through the loud music. "Your 'truly' should mean flawless performance of the contract. So how did you perform?" She went on, her tone even carrying a note of professional regret for a failed case. "During the contract term, holding the budget and resources the client provided, you not only failed your core duty as the contractor — that is, making the client feel respected, satisfied, and treasured — you used your position to go behind the client's back and develop your so-called 'personal business,' trying to use the client's investment to court another potential target. That's a serious breach of professional ethics. It's a breach of contract. It's a betrayal of the spirit of the agreement."

She paused, giving him — and everyone around with ears pricked — a moment to digest the cold logic.

"So when the client discovered your serious dereliction and your breach, and in keeping with the spirit of the contract — there was no paper contract, but social rules and basic decency are a contract by default — terminated the partnership and cut off all resources, that was a perfectly reasonable business decision. And your behavior now —" her gaze swept over his kneeling posture and the tear tracks on his face, without the slightest ripple of feeling, "— this isn't a plea for love. It's that, having lost your job, you've found you can't land another 'position' with the same pay and terms, so you're trying to use emotional blackmail and the pressure of public opinion to force your former client to overlook your major misconduct and rehire you. It's very unprofessional. And very… ugly."

Every sentence was like a cold nail, pinning his carefully groomed costume of "love" and his pose of "repentance" firmly to the pillory of "exchange of interests" and "breach of contract." The man opened and closed his mouth, wanting to argue back, only to find that any defense of "feelings" looked pale, powerless, even laughable in front of her seamless, de-emotionalized business analysis. He reached out a futile hand, pointing at Yao Yuan, his finger trembling, but he couldn't get out a single word — left with nothing but the fury and despair of someone seen through and stripped bare.

"Know your position." Yao Yuan gave him one last look, the kind you'd give the summary of a failed investment case. "A contractor has to have a contractor's self-awareness. The partnership is over, so exit with dignity, raise your own value, and go find your next suitable client. Clinging and groveling is the cheapest and most useless thing you can do."

She wasted no more time on him and turned to Jin Lan, who had hurried over and stood off to the side with a complicated look on her face. Her tone slipped back to its usual calm: "Jin Lanlan, the best way to handle a business breach is to cut clean and stop the loss in time. There's no need to pour any more emotion or time into a write-off asset. Security — please see this gentleman out. Don't let him spoil the other guests' experience."

The guards moved in at once. This time their handling had less of the roughness reserved for a troublemaker and more of the businesslike manner of "removing an unqualified supplier," hauling out the man, who had gone completely limp, his face the color of ash.

A farce wrapped up abruptly, in this extremely rational, even cold-blooded way. The music on the dance floor took over again, but the way many people looked at Yao Yuan had changed. It was no longer simple admiration for a beautiful woman, but a mix of awe, wariness, and deep curiosity — this woman could actually use the language of business to so thoroughly deconstruct and end an emotional dispute.

Yao Yuan walked back toward the booth area, her steps steady. Jin Lan followed behind her and let out a heavy breath, as if expelling a long-pent-up stuffiness from her chest, looking at Yao Yuan with a complicated expression. "Yuanyuan, I… I really don't know what to say. What you said back there was crueler than slapping him, but it worked better than any comfort."

"I just helped you sort out the facts." Yao Yuan sat back down, took the ice water Gao Jieyuan eagerly handed over, and drank a sip to wet a throat gone a little dry from all the talking and analyzing. The intense flush from her exertion was slowly fading, replaced by a lazy calm that came from spending both body and mind.

Jiang Tiantian was recording on her phone's notepad at an almost feverish speed, fingers flying, the eyes behind her lenses startlingly bright, glancing up at Yao Yuan now and then with a look full of admiration for a "master-class case study." Gao Jieyuan had put away his earlier clingy teasing too. The look in his eyes toward Yao Yuan now held, beyond desire, a clear layer of wariness and… an even stronger urge to conquer — a woman like this was too hard to control, but also too challenging, too alluring.

Zhao Yiming's gaze fell on Yao Yuan's calm profile. She had just completed a brilliant piece of "live teaching" and "crisis PR" built on her own methodology — airtight logic, a sly angle, fully weaponizing an emotional problem to striking effect. He held his wine glass, fingertips brushing lightly against the cold wall of it, the light behind his lenses deep and unreadable. He suddenly found himself wanting to know: within her "emotional business model," how would she define himself — or rather, the complex relationship between them, built on AI model development and tangled with technical discussion, mutual interest, and a subtle attraction? Client and contractor? Partners? Or… some new kind of experimental project not yet classified?

"A very efficient solution." Zhao Yiming finally spoke, his voice still even, betraying little. "Converting a nonstandard emotional problem into a standard case of contract and risk management cuts down on unnecessary emotional drain. Your 'emotional firewall' logic applies just as thoroughly to real-world relationships." His words were both an analysis of what had just happened and, it seemed, a faint echo of their earlier discussion about an AI emotional model's "immune rejection."

Yao Yuan raised her eyes to him, and their gazes met briefly in the air again. She could feel the unspoken probing beneath his words. She smiled, the smile carrying a trace of after-the-fact weariness and a subtle interest, like meeting a worthy opponent. "Thank you for the recognition, Mr. Zhao. The underlying logic is simply the same. Protect core interests, define the boundaries of rights and responsibilities, build a firewall — whether in the world of code or in human relationships, those are the foundations for lowering system risk and keeping things running stably. Only, the variable of the human heart is sometimes harder to predict than data." Her words were part answer, part a new riddle tossed back, about "variables."

Gao Jieyuan watched the subtle atmosphere flowing between them once more — the kind built on some high-dimensional understanding that left outsiders no room to enter — and his brow furrowed almost imperceptibly. Then it smoothed into an even brighter grin, carrying a touch of boyish, unwilling-to-lose spirit, as he leaned back in, his voice going softer, full of deliberate dependence and flattery: "Sis~ you were amazing back there! I was completely stunned! You're basically my idol! From now on, when I'm out there, I've got to learn from you so I don't get played. Sis, you have to teach me…" He was trying to use this low-posture flattery and plea for help to pull Yao Yuan's attention back into a more ordinary mode of interaction that he led — to break the unsettling, "above-it-all" rapport between her and Zhao Yiming.

The night was still deep, and the waves of sound and shifting lights of Liuyue Palace surged on. The contest of desire, the duel of reason, the testing of identities, and that subtle, never-fully-calculable variable called "attraction" — against this intoxicating backdrop, they kept sketching out a complex and seductive scene. Yao Yuan sat serenely within the fortress she had built — reason as her client, charm as her blade — enjoying control, analysis, and the occasional indulgence. Only, when Zhao Yiming's calm eyes — eyes that seemed able to see through every surface — turned her way, somewhere inside her solid "emotional firewall," in some deeply hidden protocol layer, there seemed to ripple a faint, barely detectable need to reassess risk.

The clamor of Liuyue Palace hit some saturation point in the small hours, then began to slide slowly toward a slack ending. The music still thundered, but the dance-floor crowd had thinned considerably, leaving behind a floor of empty glasses, flickering shards of light, and a sense of post-revelry exhaustion. The few people in the booth all showed varying degrees of drunkenness.

Jin Lan leaned back into the sofa, her eyes a little unfocused, but a habitual host's smile still hung at the corner of her mouth — only beneath it showed a deep fatigue. Clearly the aftershock of her ex's farce was far greater than she let on. Zhao Yiming still sat upright, only he had taken off his glasses and was slowly pressing the bridge of his nose with his fingertips. Under the cold white light, the usual layer of detachment on his face had been softened a little by the alcohol, revealing a rare, human kind of weariness.

Gao Jieyuan was the most visibly drunk. He talked more than before, his gestures bigger, sitting against Yao Yuan with nearly half his body leaning into her, calling her "Sis" in an ever sweeter, stickier tone, his eyes fixed on her, burning with alcohol and an unsatisfied, intensifying desire. Yao Yuan felt light-headed too, her temples throbbing, as if a warm, lazy fire were slow-cooking inside her, making her thoughts drift and her reactions lag a beat. But the alertness in her bones remained. When Gao Jieyuan tried once more to wrap an arm around her shoulders, she used the motion of reaching for her glass to slip away without a trace, leaving him only a golden-sheened profile, close at hand yet untouchable.

Jiang Tiantian was still relatively sober, though she had six or seven parts of a buzz on. She insisted on calling a designated driver herself, and before leaving she confirmed once more with Yao Yuan her intent for the follow-up interview. Then, swaying but thoroughly satisfied, she left hugging her briefcase.

"That's about it. Let's break it up." Jin Lan pushed off the sofa's armrest and stood, swaying once. Zhao Yiming reached out to steady her lightly.

"I'll take you." Zhao Yiming's voice was a little low and rough, but clear. He and Jin Lan lived in roughly the same direction.

Jin Lan didn't refuse. She nodded, then looked at Yao Yuan, her eyes carrying concern and a trace of understanding. "Yuanyuan, you…"

"I'll take Sis!" Gao Jieyuan cut in at once, springing up so suddenly that his body swayed before he found his footing. He thumped his chest, working hard to look reliable. "Sis Lan, don't worry, I promise I'll get her home safe! My driver's waiting right outside, and the car's nice and roomy!"

Yao Yuan raised her eyes, her gaze pausing for a second on Gao Jieyuan's eager (or rather, anxious) face, then skimming over Zhao Yiming, who stood quietly to the side. Zhao Yiming was looking at her too, the eyes behind his lenses unreadable in the dim light, only the gaze calm, carrying a clarity that cut through the alcohol, somehow steadying her drifting mind. But then she thought: let him take her? No — that would introduce more complicated variables. Gao Jieyuan was a nuisance, but he was straightforward; the upside was he was easy to manage and easy to get rid of.

"Mm, then I'll trouble Young Boss Gao." Yao Yuan smiled at Jin Lan by way of reply, then braced herself on the edge of the coffee table and slowly stood. The hem of her long golden dress dragged a little, and she stumbled slightly. Gao Jieyuan immediately reached out to support her, but Yao Yuan had already steadied herself. She only rested her hand lightly on his offered arm, borrowing a bit of his strength, her bearing still graceful, still keeping its distance. "Let's go."

Stepping out through the heavy doors of Liuyue Palace, the predawn chill poured over them like ice water dumped on their heads, jolting everyone. Gao Jieyuan's flashily styled sports car was already parked at the entrance, and the driver respectfully opened the door. Yao Yuan waved at Jin Lan and Zhao Yiming and bent to slide into the back seat. Gao Jieyuan wanted to squeeze in after her, but Yao Yuan had already naturally scooted inward, setting her clutch on the empty seat between them, and said with a smile, "I get a little carsick. I want to lean against the window for some air."

Gao Jieyuan's "I'll sit in the back too," already at the tip of his tongue, had to be swallowed. He rubbed his nose sheepishly, turned, and went to the front passenger seat.

The car slid soundlessly into the empty predawn streets. Outside the window the city's neon still glittered, but it had shed the peak-hour clamor of the night, looking quiet and remote. Yao Yuan leaned against the cold window and closed her eyes. The drink and the fatigue surged up like a tide. The lights and sounds of the club, the tear-streaked face of Jin Lan's ex, Gao Jieyuan's scorching stare, Zhao Yiming's silent gaze… all of it spun and wove through her mind in fragments, then slowly settled and blurred.

Gao Jieyuan kept turning back from the front seat to find topics, his voice buzzing a little in the sealed cabin. Yao Yuan mostly just put him off with an "mm" or an "oh," answering once or twice, her eyelids growing heavier and heavier.

She didn't know how long it had been when the car stopped. "Sis, we're here." Gao Jieyuan's voice pulled her back from the edge of half-sleep.

Yao Yuan opened her eyes. Outside the window stood her familiar upscale apartment building, looming silently in the faint predawn light. "Thank you." Her voice was slightly hoarse as she pushed open the door. Gao Jieyuan immediately hopped out too, wanting to help her, to walk her up.

"No need, Young Boss Gao." Yao Yuan straightened up, and a gust of night wind cleared her head a good deal. Her tone was gentle but firm. "It's very late. You should head back and rest soon too. Thank you for today." She smiled at him, and even in the cold predawn light that smile was still perfect — yet like a pane of transparent glass, it kept every possibility of closeness shut outside.

Gao Jieyuan looked at her and opened his mouth, as if he still wanted to say something, but meeting the unquestionable clarity in her eyes, he finally swallowed his words and forced out a somewhat reluctant smile. "Then… get some rest soon, Sis. Good night."

"Good night."

Watching the taillights vanish around the corner, Yao Yuan finally turned, pulled her black cashmere coat tighter around her, and walked toward the apartment lobby on still-unsteady steps. The night-shift guard nodded to her in greeting, and she dipped her head slightly in return.

The elevator car was as bright as a mirror, polished to a shine. Yao Yuan stepped in, pressed her floor, and then out of habit looked at herself in the mirror. The woman in the glass — her makeup, washed by a night of revelry and sweat, had grown a little patchy, with clear traces of weariness under her eyes. But those eyes, even now with the drink not yet faded, still kept a sliver of sharp core. Under the elevator's ceiling light, the long golden dress still shimmered with a luxurious yet faintly tired luster, like a costume after the curtain falls on a magnificent play.

She looked at herself in the mirror, at those features at once familiar and somehow strange, at the yellow diamond at her throat that even now glittered with a deep glow. The alcohol made her thoughts slow and viscous, and some distant, deeply buried fragments of memory seemed to surface unbidden — Shanghai at thirty, that ambition that felt as if it could own the whole world…

The elevator rose smoothly.

Suddenly, the mirror's surface, like a lake into which a stone had been dropped, rippled out a circle of strange, soundless waves. The center of those ripples was aimed straight at her face.

Yao Yuan froze for a moment, thinking it was the drink blurring her eyes. She blinked.

The ripples didn't vanish — instead they spread, faster and faster, more and more violently. The image in the mirror began to twist, stretch, deform. Her face, the hem of her golden dress, the elevator wall behind her, all flowed and intertwined like a melting wax figure. A familiar pull — stronger and more irresistible than the time at "Crimson Night Palace" — surged suddenly from deep within the mirror!

The world spun and reeled. She sank into unconsciousness.

========================================

Chapter 44 — The Apartment Elevator Mirror: Traveling Back to Hushi, the Origin of a Boss's Betrayal
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640291.html

The apartment elevator mirror: traveling back to Hushi, the origin of a boss's betrayal.
Consciousness rose out of chaos, the way a diver drifts slowly back to the surface.
The first thing to return was her body's awareness of "existing"—she was standing, with something solid beneath her feet. Yao Yuan opened her eyes. Before her stretched an endless, flawless field of pure white. There was no light source, yet the light was even and soft; there was no direction, yet gravity still held her. She was back here again, in this "transitional space" that lay between her several journeys through time—impossible to understand, impossible to control.
She steadied herself and wasted no time probing the physical makeup of all this white. Her first three trips had taught her that doing so was pointless. She focused her mind on her own state and ran a quick review. A moment ago, she had still been in the elevator on the way up to her penthouse with its view of the Jinshi night, staring blankly into the mirrored wall. Then the familiar dizziness and sense of being peeled loose had washed over her. When she opened her eyes again, she was here.
Which meant another "rewind" was about to begin, or was already underway. The bronze mirror's "rules" remained unpredictable, but they seemed to trigger whenever her thoughts converged on some pivotal stretch of the past or some node of intense emotion. This time… something stirred in her mind, and she felt a faint premonition of what she was about to face.
Sure enough, in the very instant that thought flickered past, the pure white space ahead began to ripple in a familiar way. Like a stone dropped into a still lake, the ripples spread outward in rings, and the scene at the center gradually warped, sharpened, and finally settled into a moving image—carrying with it the low murmur, the soft clink of glasses, and the dazzling, almost unreal glow refracted from crystal chandeliers, all unmistakably the sounds of a gala that even passing through space had not dulled.
The image rushed at her. The moment she made it out clearly, Yao Yuan's breath caught for one fleeting instant.
The Bund. The banquet hall of a top-tier hotel.
Beyond the floor-to-ceiling windows lay a textbook view of Lujiazui by night: the silhouettes of the Oriental Pearl Tower and the Jin Mao Tower glowing against the dark, the waters of the Huangpu River mirroring the neon, rippling like scattered gold. Inside, the room glittered with perfume and elegance, a gathering of the elite.
Her gaze locked, almost at once, onto two people in the scene.
One was a young woman in a white gown, standing half a step behind and to the side of a composed, dignified middle-aged man. That gown… in the white space, Yao Yuan's fingertips curled in unconsciously. A single-shoulder strap studded with tiny crystals, a high slit on the left running all the way up the thigh, so that with every step her bare white legs made a silent declaration. The cut hugged her perfectly, tracing a slender yet ample figure, and the back plunged into a deep V that bared a broad expanse of smooth skin. Her hair was swept up without a strand out of place, baring a long neck and a pair of dazzling diamond earrings. Standing there, she was like a white rose blooming to its absolute peak at midnight, beautiful to the point of aggression—vivid, scorching, instantly the secret focus of many converging glances in the room.
That was Yao Yuan at thirty. A look that belonged to that age, that stage—flaunting ambition and allure without the slightest restraint. Even as an "assistant," it had never once occurred to her to dim her own light.
The middle-aged man beside her was Ding Shitong. A navy bespoke suit of no discernible brand but superb cut, no tie, the top button of his shirt collar left casually undone, a understated Patek Philippe at his wrist. There was nothing extra on him; he simply stood there, leaning in slightly to listen to another magnate, a faint, disarming smile at the corner of his mouth, nodding now and then, his gaze calm and sharp, able to seize the heart of whatever the other man said in an instant. That aura—deep as a still pool, towering as a mountain, in control of everything—came through undiminished even across the screen of time and space.
In the pure white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan was like the sole, and cruelest, audience seated before a giant IMAX screen. She watched that moving image set into the void, watched the "self" in it whose brows and eyes still held a flicker of vitality and hope not yet ground away by reality, watched the man she had once looked up to, been infatuated with, and who had, in the end, pushed her into the abyss.
There was no violent surge of emotion. Only a cold, detached scrutiny, like a doctor examining an imaging file labeled "textbook case."
The image flowed on.
Ding Shitong always seemed able to sense Yao Yuan's slightest unease. When yet another unfamiliar executive came over with a glass in hand to make small talk, his gaze lingering half a second too long on the overly dazzling gown, Ding Shitong had already stepped forward half a pace without a trace of effort, using half his body to shield her more naturally behind him. He raised his glass in greeting, and in two or three sentences steered the topic toward recent macroeconomic trends—defusing the possible scrutiny and awkwardness while completing the necessary social courtesy. His hand would occasionally rest, very lightly, against her back to signal her—guiding her on whom to greet next, or when to hand over a business card. That touch, through the thin fabric of the gown, was brief and restrained, yet carried an unmistakable warmth and a note of command.
Yao Yuan watched, and she understood. Back then, that sense of being protected and guided, mixed with the powerful aura and the charm of his experience, had been a fatal catalyst for her. He was her boss, a heavyweight in the Hushi business world, eighteen years her senior, at the very peak where a man's bearing, experience, and energy fused just right. That age gap naturally lent him a complex authority—mentor, father, and friend all at once—and cloaked all his attentions in a layer that seemed reasonable, even worthy of gratitude.
She remembered the details: at drinking sessions, he would quietly fend off ill-meant toasts on her behalf, his tone gentle but brooking no argument: "Little Yao has a bad stomach, I'll take this one for her." When he came back from business trips, he would "casually" hand her a velvet box holding a limited-edition Cartier bracelet or a Tiffany diamond brooch, brushing it off lightly: "A client gave it to me, I have no use for it, you girls can wear it for fun." Or, even more "thoughtfully": "I was picking out a gift for my wife and thought this one suited you too, so I grabbed it." Those gifts were expensive, exquisite, and quietly matched her taste. The ambiguity was like a carefully blended perfume, drifting through every exchanged glance, every "casual" act of care. He never crossed the line, but that ever-present control of detail stirred her heart far more than any blunt confession could, and made her mistake it for a singular favor meant only for her.
In the image, Ding Shitong was talking with someone, his gaze drifting now and then to Yao Yuan at his side. Seeing her lift her chin slightly, trading easy pleasantries with a well-known producer, something seemed to flicker deep in his eyes—a very faint, hard-to-catch glint, a mix of appreciation and some deeper satisfaction that belonged to a man in control.
"Little Yao, come." Ding Shitong's voice was not loud, but in the noisy background it came through clear and steady, like a safe anchor point. He led her toward a relatively quiet lounge area on one side of the banquet hall, where a middle-aged man stood whose bearing was a touch unruly but whose eyes were unusually bright and keen.
"Boss Duan, long time no see. This is my special assistant, Yao Yuan." Ding Shitong introduced her, his tone familiar yet carrying a barely perceptible note of gravity.
Duan Yanqing. In the pure white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan silently mouthed the name. Her heart gave no extra flutter—only a cold, dead stillness.
In the image, Duan Yanqing looked Yao Yuan over, his gaze resting on her for several seconds. It was not an ordinary man's wonder or greed, but the focused appraisal of a top-tier predator sighting rare prey—deep as the sea, every trace of desire and certainty perfectly tucked behind a calm, even faintly admiring surface. He raised his glass toward Ding Shitong, his smile full of meaning: "Boss Ding really does have hidden talents around him. Miss Yao, a pleasure."
"Hello, Boss Duan, I've long admired your name." The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan smiled with composure and handed over her card. She could feel that this man was different, could feel the weight Ding Shitong placed on him.
What followed was a conversation that left a very deep impression in Yao Yuan's memory. Ding and Duan seemed to share a long-standing rapport; the topic skipped straight past any feeling-out and cut into a vast, cutting-edge blueprint.
"The age of content as king hasn't changed, but the medium and the way we consume it have been turned upside down," Duan Yanqing said, swirling the red wine in his glass. "Short dramas, interactive dramas, AI-generated scripts, virtual-idol livestreams… a gold mine buried in fragments of spare time. But what we need is a platform that doesn't just make content—one that can intelligently distribute it, even intelligently co-create it."
Ding Shitong nodded and picked up the thread: "Exactly. After the traffic gateway comes ecosystem stickiness. Smart homes, in-car systems, even the personal terminals of the next era—they're all living scenarios where content can seamlessly plug in. Imagine telling your smart screen in the kitchen, 'Give me a short drama to go with dinner,' and the AI recommends, or even generates, a clip based on your history, your mood at that moment, even what's in your fridge; the background sound while your robot vacuum works could be a customized knowledge podcast or a finance breakdown."
The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan listened quietly off to the side, inwardly stunned. She had followed Ding Shitong for a year and witnessed his command of strategy, his deep mastery of manufacturing and traditional investment, but the sparks struck now between him and Duan Yanqing were about the future—a far more ambitious building of an ecosystem. From AI screenwriting algorithms, vertical short-drama brand matrices, and immersive podcast experiences, to smart appliances (ambient lighting that could sync with a plot, scene-based voice interaction for robot vacuums), the exclusive content ecosystem of next-generation smart cockpits, even discussions of investing in or developing lightweight AR devices as the medium for future immersive short dramas… the breadth of coverage and depth of integration left her dazzled.
And as Ding Shitong laid all this out, he would occasionally turn to her and explain a little using analogies she could grasp: "It's like that community-retail model we analyzed before—at its core it's still a closed loop of terminal and service, only now we've swapped in 'content plus AI plus smart hardware.'" In moments like these, he always made her feel that she was not just a bystander but someone he was deliberately drawing into this grand narrative. That sense of being needed, of being led, of touching the future—mixed with her admiration for his intelligence and vision—fermented wildly in her young heart.
Duan Yanqing was clearly moved too by the clarity of Ding Shitong's vision. The two clinked glasses, and a preliminary intent had already landed amid the easy talk and laughter. "Let the teams gnaw on the details; with the direction set, all that's left is execution. Boss Ding, I can see Miss Yao here is very capable—we can have her coordinate more when the time comes."
The gala went on, and Ding Shitong took Yao Yuan to meet several more key figures. He always found a clever way to create opportunities for her to shine—having her briefly lay out the data on some project she had been tracking, or supplement an industry update at just the right moment. When she finished, he would cast her a faint, approving glance, a look that quickened her heartbeat half a beat more than any praise could.
There had been too many such moments over the past year. He brought her into private clubs known only to the topmost circles and taught her the lore behind appreciating wine and cigars (though she rarely indulged); when she was a little nervous at her first massive financing negotiation, he gave the back of her hand a light pat under the table and then, with perfect steadiness, took command of the whole room; he even, on her birthday, under the name of "rewarding an outstanding assistant," gave her a small sum of seed capital, guided her through her first stock-market investment, and patiently explained market logic and risk control—an investment that, under her careful handling, turned a decent profit. What he taught her was not just business skills but a poised way of facing a complicated world.
Toward him she felt the respect owed a teacher, the trust owed a friend, but deep down there was also a woman's longing and attraction toward an utterly superior man. She was captivated by the slight knit of his brow when he thought, the unhurried confidence with which he negotiated, the occasional flashes of singular insight into art or literature, even the faint, steady woody scent on him. It was a refined halo tempered together by experience, success, and rigorous self-discipline, and it held a fatal pull for her.
And yet Ding Shitong's boundaries were always clear. His care was the care of an elder, his protection that of a superior. He would remember her bad stomach and remind her to go easy on cold drinks, yet when he draped a coat over her he never overstepped; he would let his aura turn cold in an instant over the slightest slight against her, yet in private he never spoke or acted toward her with any intimacy. He was like a warm, solid fortress, allowing her to rest and grow within it, yet never once opening, of his own accord, that inner door leading into the realm of private feeling. That care, present yet never declared, that distance held with perfect measure, made the feelings in Yao Yuan's heart rise and fall quietly like the tide under moonlight—never finding a shore to break against.
By now the gala was thinning out. Ding Shitong had the driver take Yao Yuan home first. The car was hushed inside, soft classical music flowing through it.
"How did today feel?" He loosened his collar and leaned back against the seat, looking a little tired, the side of his face especially deep-set in the light and shadow streaming past the window.
"There was so much to take in—I learned a great deal. Especially the ecosystem vision you and Boss Duan discussed; it showed me a completely different scale of things." Yao Yuan spoke from the heart, her fingertips absently stroking her necklace.
Ding Shitong gave a small smile, a faint relief in it. "You've grown fast. If this project gets off the ground, I'll hand you some very important coordination work. The pressure won't be small, but I believe you can do it."
His words were trust, a charge entrusted, still strictly framed within the working relationship. Yet that trust itself felt somehow like a singular intimacy.
"I'll give it everything—I won't let you down." Yao Yuan said softly. The thread of tenderness born from the weariness he occasionally let slip wove together with that suppressed longing, a tangle of mixed feelings.
The car stopped at the foot of Yao Yuan's apartment building. As always, Ding Shitong instructed the driver to confirm she had safely entered the building before leaving.
"Boss Ding," Yao Yuan said before getting out, unable to help looking back, "you should rest early too."
Ding Shitong nodded, his gaze lingering on her face for a moment, that look especially deep and unreadable in the dim light, until at last it became only a gentle: "Goodnight, little Yuan."
The car door closed, and the black sedan eased into the night.
The black sedan drove into the night. The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan stood where she was, the night wind brushing past, the diamonds at her ears swaying faintly, her heart still steeped in that special "little Yuan," secretly delighted.
In the pure white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan watched, with absolute calm, the thirty-year-old self in her memory—the one thrown into turmoil by a single "little Yuan," standing alone in the night wind, secretly pleased.
Foolish?
No, not quite foolish. It was the inevitable result of limited understanding. The thirty-year-old self was like a carefully trained experimental subject who had begun to master a complex instrument, preening over the occasional approving glance from her creator, utterly unaware that she herself was part of the experiment—perhaps even a control group. The golden cage Ding Shitong had built, out of a halo blending a mentor's authority, a man's charm, and the shelter of top-tier resources, was too gorgeous; the "knowledge bait" and "emotional value" he fed her were too precise. For a young woman in whom ambition and loneliness coexisted, who hungered to be recognized on a higher plane, it was an almost impossible formula to resist. Everything he gave—exposure, opportunity, meticulous care, an affirmation kept just out of reach—landed precisely on her points of need and her vanity. He did not love her; this was high-order domestication. He was savoring the sense of omnipotence and the subtle satisfaction of raising and taming a superior, intelligent creature. And the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan had mistaken domestication for favor, the bait for a feast meant for her alone.
She knew what would come next.
The "pursuit" Duan Yanqing would unfold, aimed at the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan—if that precisely calculated torrent of resources and access, that dimension-flattening strike, could even be called a pursuit.
It was not the floral and jewelry bombardment of an ordinary rich heir, nor the romantic verse of a literary youth. Duan Yanqing's "methods" were built on a precise psychological profile of women like Yao Yuan. He saw straight through the vanity beneath her gorgeous gown—the near-starvation for "the high-end" and "recognition" that came from clawing her way up from the bottom—and saw through, too, the yearning and unease, hidden beneath her clever ambition, about truly stepping into "that class." What he meant to do was not to satisfy her, but to redefine the threshold of her desire—to make even the air she was used to feel cheap, to make the world of Ding Shitong she had once looked up to seem narrow and small.
The first wave: class access and the illusion of identity.
On the third day after the gala, Yao Yuan received an elegantly worded invitation, sealed in wax and stamped with the crest of a top Swiss private cigar club. The venue was a non-public old mansion in Bund Source. Duan Yanqing's assistant called, his tone deferential yet leaving no room for refusal: "Mr. Duan is hosting a small cigar tasting next week—everyone coming is a friend. He remembers Miss Yao has a knowledge of Cuban coffee and thought you might be interested in the cigar culture of the same growing region, so he asked me specially to invite you. Dress casually."
The result of "dress casually" was that, when Yao Yuan stepped into that hidden chamber thick with aged cedar and leather, she found the so-called "friends" were two internet new-money tycoons who often graced the covers of finance magazines, a director who had just brought home a Golden Lion from the Venice Film Festival, and an heir whose surname carried the heavy flavor of an old Hong Kong family. They smoked limited-edition Cohibas said to be worth more than gold, yet what they discussed was not business—it was the starry skies of some undisclosed Himalayan trekking route, the latest findings at an Antarctic emperor-penguin observation station, a tribal secret rite about to be held in some small African nation, open to only twenty guests. The way Duan Yanqing drew her into the conversation was utterly natural: "Yao Yuan has real insight into shifts in coffee-bean growing regions—we were just talking about how climate affects a crop's flavor, and it has a curious parallel with cigars." With a single sentence, he lifted her from "Ding Shitong's pretty assistant" to a position where she could hold some kind of "equal" conversation with these figures. He personally selected a mild cigar for her and demonstrated cutting, toasting, and lighting it, his fingers never once touching her—yet that atmosphere of drawing her into a private ritual was more invasive than any physical contact. That night she smoked only a few puffs, but every breath carried the mellow scent of money and power settled into something rich. As she left, Duan Yanqing merely gave a faint nod: "Try the Davidoff annual limited edition next time—you'll like it." As if they already had many a "next time."
The second wave: scarce experiences and sensory conquest.
Then came a "friends' gathering"—aboard a seventy-eight-meter superyacht in the Mediterranean. A helicopter ferried her over from a helipad in Monaco. Apart from a crew so well-trained and good-looking they rivaled models, there was only Duan Yanqing and two of his "childhood buddies" (only later did Yao Yuan learn that one was a silent shareholder in some old-line European luxury house, and the other's family controlled several key shipping lanes). There were no bikini-clad beauties or champagne revelry as she had imagined; by day they went deep-sea fishing, and the bluefin tuna they caught were prepared on the spot by a three-Michelin-star chef sailing with them; at dusk they watched the sunset on deck while the speakers played an unreleased private recording of some jazz master, transferred from vinyl; deep in the night, they lay under the starry sky on deck and talked of the scent of locust blossoms at the mouth of a childhood alley, the cold of sleeping in a basement after a first failed venture, the lifelong regrets of some elder now passed. Duan Yanqing rarely let slip a little "realness": pointing at the stars, he spoke of the loneliness of surveying the Gobi desert in his youth, of how back then the stars had seemed like cold diamonds, and only now seemed like "distant campfires." Then, very naturally, he draped a cashmere shawl gently over the shoulders of Yao Yuan, who wore only a silk slip dress: "The wind's harsh at night—don't catch a chill." That care broke free of Ding Shitong's elder-style fussing; it felt more like the consideration between equals, between two of a kind. In that moment, Yao Yuan vaguely felt she had touched a thread of genuine warmth beneath this man's hard shell. This "privileged sharing of something real" was more lethal than the yacht and the stars combined.
The third wave: resource empowerment and value recognition.
Duan Yanqing began to "use" her—not the way Ding Shitong assigned concrete tasks, but treating her as an "insider" who could take part in certain core affairs. He would have his assistant copy her on business briefings that were non-core but meaningful, then "offhandedly" ask her opinion. Once, it was even a letter of investment intent regarding an emerging consumer brand; after hearing her analysis based on user data, he nodded and told his assistant: "Note down the community-spread risk point Miss Yao raised—have the post-investment department run a stress test." Her opinion was actually adopted. He would also take her to view some extremely private collections—not to show off, but to discuss: "What do you think—this Northern Song Ru-ware piece, is there any way to translate its aesthetic value into contemporary commercial brand-building?" He was earnestly seeking her view, as if her taste and commercial instinct were assets worth treasuring. Once she happened to mention an interest in a certain niche avant-garde designer, and the very next week he arranged a private dinner with only her, the designer, and the brand's chief—over the course of which a preliminary cooperation intent for that brand's entry into the Chinese market was reached. Duan Yanqing said to her lightly: "You've got good taste—this label has potential. If you're interested, you could put in a little pocket money, call it a seed round." This was not gift-giving; this was granting her the power to invest and to turn stone into gold. He made her feel she was not a vase clinging to him, but a partner who could create value alongside him. This "value recognition" and "resource empowerment" was a fatal temptation for a woman like Yao Yuan—proud and hungry to prove her own ability. Ding Shitong cultivated her, but it was always "superior to subordinate"; Duan Yanqing, within a seemingly equal partnership, let her taste what it was to be a "ruler."
The fourth wave: erosion of detail and the colonization of life.
His infiltration was seamless yet never made her uncomfortable. She offhandedly mentioned liking some niche fragrance brand's scent of earth after rain, and before long the hotel suite where she usually stayed, even the inside of her own car, had quietly been switched to the same fragrance. She mentioned she had been sleeping poorly lately, and the next time they met he "happened" to bring a small tin of an ancient-recipe herbal tea said to come from some hidden Himalayan village, produced in tiny quantities each year: "A friend gave it to me—good for calming the nerves, give it a try." She found that he remembered all her dietary preferences (no scallion or ginger, a liking for sour and spicy), and once, at a business lunch, he naturally swapped onto her plate the restaurant's signature dessert that she had glanced at twice—the motion so smooth it was as if he had done it a thousand times. He never sent roses or jewelry that obviously symbolized romantic love; the things he gave were either out-of-print art books that "happened" to relate to her professional interests, or abstract prints he had "bid one extra copy of at a friend's auction," or a lifetime membership at some top equestrian club for her to "experience."
