None of these things touched on lust, yet they slotted into her life and her taste with uncanny precision, accustoming her to his presence, to his "understanding," to the feeling of being "cared for" at such a high concentration and such high taste. Her vanity was not merely a vanity for material things, but a deep addiction to being "treated so attentively by someone of such a level." It made her feel she really was "special"—special enough that this man standing at the summit was willing to put in such thought.
In the pure white space, the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan calmly dissected the thirty-year-old self's wavering, which had deepened by the day. She watched that young self study, over and over in the dead of night, the messages Duan Yanqing sent—worded never to overstep, yet heavy with meaning; watched her practice an ever more composed bearing before the mirror after each "high-end experience"; watched her begin, without realizing it, to compare the "guidance" and "gifts" Ding Shitong had given her—once treasured beyond price—with the "platform" and "possibilities" Duan Yanqing offered. Ding Shitong was good, was steady, was a reliable ladder—but what Duan Yanqing showed her was the vast sky above the ladder, another way of living she had never imagined, where freedom and power existed side by side.
Her vanity was fed until it flourished. She relished the faintly probing, courteous looks now cast at her by people she had once had to look up to; relished her invisible status in Duan Yanqing's circle, where no one dared overlook her even in her silence; relished that thrill—mingling danger with utter satisfaction—of "being pursued with full force by the most cunning hunter in the world."
Of course she was wary. But the high art of Duan Yanqing's hunting lay in this: he never truly forced her, always granted her the (seeming) power of choice, always kept a distance pitched just right. His "pursuit" was more like a lavish cultivation game tailored to her alone—letting her watch with her own eyes as her vision, her scale, her quality of life shot upward, accustoming her to this "high-end" until she could no longer endure the "ordinary." What he domesticated was not her body, but her taste, her thresholds, her desires.
The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan knew that, had it not been for the betrayal that came later, the thirty-year-old self might very well have truly fallen—fallen not for love, but for that vast illusion of being wrapped in top-tier resources, affirmed by overwhelming power, her own worth seemingly magnified without limit. That vain satisfaction was, to the Yao Yuan of that time—who longed to reach the summit, longed to prove she deserved it all—a kind of ▢▢.
And it was precisely because she had once truly wavered like that, come so close to being seduced, that the betrayal represented by Ding Shitong's later soft "Mm" was rendered all the more cruel and absolute. It shattered not merely the fantasy of one man, but the illusory understanding of her own allure and worth that she had built up through Duan Yanqing. She woke with a jolt: so in Ding Shitong's eyes, all this worth that Duan Yanqing had carefully "cultivated" and "courted" was, in the end, nothing more than a chip with a clear price tag, to be traded away! So the "specialness" and "being chosen" she had believed in were, on the higher chessboard of interests, nothing at all!
It was exactly this agony—of being slammed from the clouds of vanity into the mire of reality—that made the later Yao Yuan strip away every shred of emotional naivety. She saw clearly that in the world of Duan Yanqing and Ding Shitong, everything was appraisal and exchange. The so-called appreciation, pursuit, painstaking cultivation—all of it revolved around the core of "value." She no longer believed in any "kindness" without cause, no longer held any fantasy of "singular favor." She tempered the experience of this betrayal and seduction into a new law of survival: since everything is value exchange, then she would make herself the party with the highest value—and the one who firmly held the power to set the price. Depend on no one's "giving," fantasize about no "exception," believe only in quantifiable chips and controllable bindings of interest. Feeling is redundancy, softheartedness is weakness; only by ceaselessly raising her own value, calculating gain and loss with precision, and executing transactions coldly is there any way to survive.
This, then, was the starting point of all the logic of the later "Godmother of Feelings," Yao Yuan—and the original, and most painful, furnace in which that cold, hard bedrock of her character was forged.

========================================

Chapter 45 — Yao Yuan Warns Her Thirty-Year-Old Self; the Truth of the Chess Game
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640292.html

At first Ding Shitong paid no attention to these gestures from Duan Yanqing. He even took them as Duan Yanqing showing him respect, grooming him. But before long he sensed the sharp intent beneath that gentle surface.

After one event at the equestrian club, Ding Shitong did something rare: he had his driver take Yao Yuan home early. In the car he stayed silent for a long while, then offered a reminder, as if in passing. "Boss Duan… has a wide circle. But at some functions, if you feel uncomfortable, you don't have to force yourself. Your work, to me, is already more than good enough." He was trying to draw a line, to protect his own territory.

Duan Yanqing's patience, however, had limits, and his chips were heavy enough. When their vast collaboration plan—one massive enough to overturn several industries—entered its most critical phase, Duan Yanqing finally tore away that veneer of courtesy. It happened in a closed-door meeting with just the two of them, a cigar turning between his fingers.

"Shitong, let's not talk in riddles." Duan Yanqing blew out a smoke ring, his smile thinning. "Miss Yao—I'm very interested. It's the first time I've taken such a liking to anyone."

The hand holding Ding Shitong's teacup paused almost imperceptibly, his face betraying nothing. "Xiao Yao is a capable subordinate of mine, the East China regional head I'm grooming for the next stage. Your fondness, Boss Duan, is an honor for her. But she is not a project to be traded."

"Oh?" Duan Yanqing raised an eyebrow and leaned slightly forward, bringing an intangible pressure. "She doesn't seem to be your 'private property' either, does she, Boss Ding? An unmarried man, an unmarried woman—I court her in the normal way, what's wrong with that? Or… does Boss Ding harbor some thoughts of his own, which is why you won't part with her?" Having his thoughts laid bare, Ding Shitong felt a pang of guilt.

At the start, what he felt for Yao Yuan was genuinely the admiration a mentor feels for an outstanding junior. She was smart, decisive, with a startling capacity to learn, and—rarer still—a natural keenness amid the clink of glasses. He poured himself into grooming her, treating her as a future backbone of the company. He took her into every high-end venue, taught her hands-on how to read financial statements, analyze trends, even guided her personal investments. He could feel Yao Yuan's feelings for him: from the initial gratitude and reverence, fermenting day by day through their close contact—nearly sixteen hours a day. But he said nothing, and did nothing. He pressure-free enjoyed the worship and admiration of a beautiful young woman, which greatly flattered the vanity of a middle-aged man and seemed to spur him toward a better display of himself, granting him a new vitality.

And he, too, in the process, seemed to develop an attention toward her that went beyond the scope of "grooming." Perhaps it was the fatigue under her eyes after stretches of overtime, perhaps the slight furrow of her brow when she was made to drink at functions, perhaps simply the new pair of earrings today that better suited her complexion… That protective urge, that faint sense of possessiveness, which a man naturally breeds toward a beautiful, capable woman who "belongs" within his jurisdiction—it quietly spread.

Duan Yanqing deliberately leaned on the phrase "part with her," then softened his tone and threw out the real bait. "Our deal is at the very last step. I know you're fighting for the exclusive content-integration port on that 'Smart Drive Future' new-energy car network system. The approvals and channels—I happen to be able to speed up by at least half a year. My condition is simple." He looked Ding Shitong straight in the eye, spelling it out word by word. "I want Yao Yuan. One dinner, one 'accidental' night, that's all. What happens after, each of us by our own skill. For you, for the deal, this is the optimal solution."

The air froze. Ding Shitong looked at the tea leaves rising and sinking in his cup. Across his mind flashed Yao Yuan's trusting, dependent gaze; flashed the light that flared and was instantly hidden when she received a gift he'd given "casually"; flashed, too, the vast territory and supreme power this deal's success could bring. The struggle was violent. But the scale of his weighing, set against real interests on one side and his own feelings—feelings that could never see the light, that he could never promise—on the other, ultimately tipped. He thought of his wife and children far away in America, thought of the standing and reputation he'd built with bitter effort over years, thought of the higher peaks he could reach once bound to Duan Yanqing. A complicated mix of guilt, pain, and even a trace of relief—at last he need no longer be tormented by this uncontrollable feeling—drowned him.

In the end, he did not look at Duan Yanqing. He only let out an "Mm," so light it was nearly inaudible, as though it were just a small cough at an itch in his throat.

Then came the dinner that changed everything.

Yao Yuan was seated beside Duan Yanqing. Ding Shitong was there too, but seated across from her, no longer looking her way. The drinks came one after another, the reasons for toasting all high-sounding—celebrating the deal, wishing it success. For the first time, Ding Shitong did not block the drinks for her. He even smiled and personally raised a glass to her, saying, "Xiao Yao, you've worked hard these past stretches. Boss Duan thinks very highly of you—give Boss Duan a few more toasts." His gaze was calm, untroubled, even carrying an encouraging smile. In that smile, Yao Yuan's heart sank, bit by bit, turning cold.

She drank a great deal. Before her consciousness blurred, all she remembered was being "supported" out by Duan Yanqing's assistant, while Ding Shitong, his back to her, was chatting and laughing with someone else, never once turning around.

Yao Yuan, in the pure white space, recalled the helplessly struggling self who had been delivered into the presidential suite that night. There was no expression on her face.

The world she woke to was turned upside down. The physical discomfort and humiliation were secondary; when she pieced together, from various channels, the deal that Ding Shitong's feather-light "Mm" had represented, the sense of betrayal—of being pushed into the abyss by her own hand, by the man she most trusted and admired—pierced through every once-warm, once-ambiguous dream like an icicle.

Was she heartbroken? Disappointed? Yes. But just as her present-day theory as "the Godmother of Feelings" dissects it: wallowing in the emotions of a victim is worthless. She lay on the hotel's disheveled bed, gazing at the lavish crystal chandelier on the ceiling, and after her tears ran dry, all that remained in her eyes was a cold clarity and a nearly cruel rationality.

Ding Shitong had chosen profit and betrayed their bond—or perhaps that bond had never existed. Duan Yanqing saw her as a top-grade prey to be traded, determined to have her. Well then. Since the rules were like this, she would play by their rules.

She quickly "accepted" reality and let the current carry her to stay at Duan Yanqing's side. No longer forced, but a deliberate, shrewd choice. She used Duan Yanqing's resources and connections, learning and seizing as if starved. She was no longer the "Xiao Yao" who waited for rewards, but "Miss Yao," the most beautiful—and most clever—woman at Duan Yanqing's side. She knew how to display her charm with just the right touch, and knew better how to whisper in his ear, securing real interests for herself: an independent studio, startup capital, key project opportunities.

She founded her own company, its business cutting precisely into the gaps in the original Ding-Duan collaboration blueprint. She was more nimble than they were, better attuned to emerging markets, better at leveraging her identity as a woman and every tactic she'd learned from these two men. When, with thunderous force, she snatched a core project that Ding Shitong was set on winning—wresting away a key partnership from the tiger's jaws—Ding Shitong stopped her in the corridor after the signing ceremony.

He looked far more worn. With a complicated gaze he regarded the transformed, fiercely commanding woman before him, trying to find some shadow of the past in her eyes. "Xiao Yao… do you—do you hate me?"

Yao Yuan stopped, turned around, an impeccable, commercial smile on her face, but a desert in her eyes, no longer stirred. "You're joking, Boss Ding. The marketplace is a battlefield; each by their own means. I should even thank you and Boss Duan for that year's… 'devoted grooming.'" She deliberately paused a beat on "devoted grooming," and seeing Ding Shitong's pupils contract sharply, felt not a ripple within.

She had already wrung from these two men every usable resource, connection, and experience—down to the lessons they'd given her. Her company's wings were filling out, her capital robust.

Three years later, Yao Yuan stood in the top-floor office of her new company, looking down on the bustling yet cold city of Shanghai at her feet. This place had made her, and had also buried a part of her.

She pressed the intercom key, her voice clear and calm. "Notify management: the final resolution is in. Effective today, the company's headquarters begins the process of relocating to Jinshi. Everything here," she paused, her gaze sweeping the sky outside the window that had borne so much memory and calculation, "should end."

And just like that, she left Shanghai with resolve. A Yao Yuan washed clean of all glamour, stripped of all naivety, believing only in power and chips, carried her capital and ambition off to the next battlefield.

These later memories, like a fast-forwarded film, ran coldly and clearly through the mind of the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan as she watched the closing scene of the banquet. No pain of reliving—only a thorough re-confirmation about human nature and self-interest.

The scene shifted.

In it, the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan finally turned and walked into the apartment lobby. The click of her high heels on the marble floor sounded clear and lonely in the empty night. She pressed the elevator button; the metal doors slid open without a sound.

The elevator's interior was polished like a mirror. She stepped in, turned, and met her own eyes in the reflection. Perhaps it was the lingering warmth of the banquet, perhaps the afterglow of that "Little Yuan"—the woman in the mirror still had, at the corners of her eyes and brows, a trace of un-dissipated softness, touched with a hint of self-satisfaction. Looking at herself like that, she actually tugged gently at her lips and let out a slightly silly, contented smile.

Just as that smile froze on the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan's face—

The elevator's polished mirror, like a pool struck by a pebble, rippled out a silent ring. At the center of the ripples, the figure of another woman emerged from the void into solidity, slowly surfacing.

It was the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan. She appeared in the mirror in a gold gown and a black cashmere coat, her makeup somewhat smudged, with a tempered calm—appearing beside the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan with her exquisite makeup and spring in the corners of her eyes, like a harder reflection dredged up from the depths of time.

The silly smile on the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan's face froze abruptly, her pupils contracting in shock. Even though this was already the "fourth time" she'd seen her future self, each sudden appearance—materializing in this way that defied physical common sense and the rules of cognition—lost not a fraction of its impact and uncanniness.

Inside the mirror and out, the eyes of the two Yao Yuans collided in the void.

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan watched in silence for a few seconds that "past" self with embers still in her eyes. Within her there was neither grief nor joy, only a deep, almost academic understanding. She spoke slowly, her voice penetrating the barrier of mirror and time, calm and unrippled.

"See, you still couldn't hold out. That halo on Ding Shitong—mixing settled power, the crush of experience, and a carefully calibrated 'understanding'—is a targeted drug, precision-delivered to a woman at your stage, of your mental model. You'll get addicted to it. It's not your fault."

The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan snapped back to herself, hastily wiped away the lingering smile, replaced by wariness and urgency. "You… last time you only said 'be careful'! Is it Boss Ding? He means to do me harm?" There was a last struggle in her tone—defending Ding Shitong was almost instinct—but that warning had already lodged like a poison barb into the fabric of her trust.

"Boss Ding?" The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan repeated the title between her teeth, as if chewing some candy long since gone rancid, a faint, temperatureless arc curving at the corner of her mouth. "That title will soon stop meaning protection."

She took half a step forward, and her image in the mirror seemed to press closer too, bringing an intangible pressure.

"Do you know what it meant, that last look Duan Yanqing gave you tonight?" Not waiting for an answer, she went on, her pace steady, each word an icicle. "That was a hunter appraising a prey he's determined to take. And his appraisal was: worth the move, and with a very high certainty of success."

The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan paled slightly, but her reason still struggled. "Duan Yanqing… he does have a strong presence, but I'm just a little assistant. A figure like him…"

"A figure?" The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan cut her off, a cold mockery flashing in her eyes. "In the face of large enough interests, any 'figure' can be quantified and traded. And you, my dear past self, are unlucky enough to have been marked by Duan Yanqing as one of his 'desired attached conditions'—one of the terms he wants thrown in—in that vast collaboration about to unfold, one big enough to reshape the landscape of several industries. And Ding Shitong…"

She paused, her gaze sharp as it pierced the mirror, looking straight into the last tottering trace of trust in the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan's eyes.

"Your Boss Ding, after a brief struggle—perhaps with a sliver of genuine feeling—will make a choice entirely true to his merchant's nature, weighing the territory he prizes, the profit, and his own 'fondness' that can never see the light and can never be promised. He will acquiesce, even… personally bring about this trade. Using you, to exchange for an unobstructed channel of cooperation, for the key chips in Duan Yanqing's hand. At a dinner not long from now, you'll be made drunk, then delivered to Duan Yanqing's suite. And Ding Shitong—he'll sit at the head of the table, smiling, personally toasting you, thanking you for your 'contribution.'"

"No… that's impossible!" The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan blurted out, her body trembling slightly, whether from rage or fear she couldn't tell. "Boss Ding isn't that kind of person! With his own strength and standing, he has no need to use such a base means to win a deal! And the way he treats me…" She choked. Those ambiguous details became, in this moment, unbearably fragile.

"How does he treat you? Grooming you with care? Shielding you at every turn? Giving you gifts that are precious and to your taste? Appearing just when you need him?" The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan's tone was a nearly cruel calm. "That's investment, and it's domestication. Investing in your ability, to create value for him; domesticating your loyalty and fondness, to make sure you—this useful chess piece—stay willingly on his board, and can even be given away as a gift when he needs it. When he finds his domesticated 'pet' has caught the eye of a more powerful hunter, and that handing the 'pet' over can buy him the territory he wants more—what do you think his merchant's nature will choose?"

She looked at the young self in the mirror, face ashen, faith beginning to collapse in her eyes, and slowed her pace, each word like a heavy hammer.

"You overestimated the weight of 'sentiment' on his scale of value, and underestimated the power of interest and ambition to warp human nature. What you took as special care was only part of his controlling the whole board, satisfying his own subtle psychological needs. To him, you were never the irreplaceable 'one and only,' but a quality asset combining appreciation value, use value, and a certain emotional premium. When the 'transfer' of this asset can be exchanged for a higher return, transferring is the most rational business decision."

The air in the elevator seemed to congeal. The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan leaned against the cold mirror, digesting this horrifying "future." The roar of collapsing trust seemed to echo through her mind. It was a long time before she found her voice again, hoarse and taut. "Then I… what should I do? What's your advice?" She stared hard at her future self in the mirror, afraid she would suddenly vanish as in the previous times.

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan seemed to see through her fear, and this time did not vanish at once. Her gaze steady, she gave clear, cold instructions.

"First, immediately stop all unrealistic emotional projection onto Ding Shitong and any fantasy of depending on him. Restore him fully to the pure identities of 'boss,' 'resource provider,' 'a partner to be wary of.' Your emotions and attention are precious resources—don't waste them on an 'investment' destined to run a loss."

"Second, Duan Yanqing's 'interest' is both a crisis and leverage. Don't passively wait to be schemed against. Appraise the resources he can offer—connections, capital, information, a springboard. Then take the initiative to think: how do you convert his 'interest' in you into an 'opportunity' you can control and use? Remember, the initiative must be held in your own hands. To go with him, or not—that must be your proactive choice based on a clear-eyed weighing of pros and cons, not an 'ending' they've arranged for you and forced you to bear."

"Third, raise your hard strength and irreplaceability at top speed. Learn everything in the game's rules at their level like a madwoman—business, law, interpersonal networks, resource integration. Use your current position to draw nutrients with everything you've got, and accumulate your own capital: money, connections, cognition, results. You have to be fast—fast enough that when they want to move against you, they'll find you're no longer the little assistant they can manipulate at will, but a potential rival or partner who already has her own claws and teeth, who makes them weigh the cost."

The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan breathed rapidly, straining to absorb it all. Her future self's words were like a cold scalpel, cutting open the tender, sentimental surface to expose the naked logic of power and exchange beneath. Painful—but a nearly self-flagellating clarity came with it.

"So," she asked hoarsely, "the later me, today's you… you were schemed against, then chose Duan Yanqing, and that's how you got to where you are now—right?" She wanted to verify her guess.

"It was a choice, and it was using him." The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan did not deny it, her tone flat. "After being pushed into the abyss, crying is useless. Either rot in the abyss, or use the abyss as a springboard. I chose the latter. I stayed at his side, wrung from him every usable resource, completed the cruelest and most effective 'primitive accumulation of capital.' Then, when neither he nor Ding Shitong expected it, I struck back with a single blow, seized the key projects, and set up on my own."

She paused, looking at the mix of shock, understanding, and a trace of yearning in her young self's eyes, and added:

"Those three years were three years of shedding a layer of skin. But worth it."

The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan was silent for a long while, then suddenly asked a seemingly unrelated question. "Then… the you now, are you living well? Are you happy? Is this really the life you wanted?"

The question gave the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan a moment's stall. Her image in the mirror even seemed to blur for a tenth of a second. Then she smiled faintly, a smile carrying a complicated, ineffable weariness, though her voice remained steady.

"By worldly, quantifiable standards, very well. Financially free, successful in my career, holding considerable say and influence in a certain vertical field—in some sense, one of the 'people above people.'"

"As for whether it's what I wanted…" She lifted her eyes, her gaze seeming to pierce the mirror and time, looking toward some distant place. "I got the power to choose. To choose how to live, whom to associate with, what career to pursue. That alone is already luckier than too many people. Happiness is a momentary feeling, but a sense of control is the source of lasting security. Now, at least, I can control most of my own life's direction."

"I just didn't expect I'd end up the 'Godmother of Feelings.'" The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan tried to lighten the mood, with a touch of self-mockery.

"Yeah—an influencer, a top-tier streamer, praised and damned in equal measure." The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan picked up, no great emotion audible in her tone. "Selling clarity, puncturing illusions, laying out the gamesmanship, the value exchange, the true colors of human nature in emotional relationships. Plenty of people curse me, say I objectify everything, manufacture anxiety. But the ones who curse hardest are usually the ones most afraid of women understanding this logic. Because once you puncture the packaging of the 'love myth,' a lot of traditional relationship models built on exploitation and self-sacrifice become hard to sustain."

She looked at her young self and, rarely, said a few words more, like a warning, and like an explanation.

"This road stands in the path of the wind, and also on the edge of a blade. You'll be scrutinized, judged, attacked by countless people. You'll be very tired—tired in the heart. If I could, I'd honestly hope you could find another road, not so extreme, not so much under the spotlight and the magnifying glass. But…" She shook her head. "With your character, and the path I've walked, it seems very hard. All we can do is make sure we always hold chips—the backbone to turn and walk away, or to strike back head-on."

The thirty-year-old Yao Yuan fell into thought. The picture her future self painted was glamorous, yet heavy too. But strangely, that fear of fate's trajectory was replaced by a clear sense of "at least I can choose how to fight."

"I understand." She drew a deep breath, her gaze sharpening again. "I'll think carefully about the road ahead. You… do you have any advice on my current direction for starting up? Like, the future's next big thing?"

The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan seemed to consider for a moment, then said, "AI. Especially applications deeply combined with vertical industries—that'll be the key to the next cycle. I'm watching and positioning for it now myself…"

Before she could finish, that familiar, overwhelming, irresistible pull surged in again without warning. The thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan's image in the mirror suddenly twisted and faded. She had only time to cast her thirty-year-old self a complicated look, her lips moving slightly as if she still wanted to say something—

The next second, the mirror's ripples surged violently, and her figure was wiped away like a smudge of an eraser, vanishing in an instant.

In the elevator, silence returned. Only the thirty-year-old Yao Yuan remained, standing alone before the polished, person-reflecting mirror. In it, only her pale yet utterly changed face was left.

Even with the experience of the previous three times, watching another "self" vanish before her eyes in this way that defied common sense still set her heart pounding wildly with alarm. Uncanny, absurd—yet it had truly changed something.

Facing the empty mirror, she slowly, ever so slowly, exhaled the stale breath she'd been holding in her chest. Her fingertips were cold, but deep in her eyes, something had already thoroughly settled, cooled, and hardened.

========================================

Chapter 46 — Back From the Other Side, a Deal on the Table
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640293.html

Like being yanked up to the surface from deep water, a violent rush of weightlessness and vertigo seized her. The last words she'd left unfinished still seemed to echo in her ears, mixed with the low hum of an elevator in motion.
Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open.
Her vision was a blur at first, then sharpened fast. Smooth metal walls, and in the mirrored surface, her own thirty-six-year-old face, a little pale. She was lying on the floor of an elevator, and Yu Hao was crouched beside her, one arm out, as if about to gather her up.
She was back.
From that mirror conversation with her thirty-year-old self, her soul had settled back into place.
Her throat was dry and tight. She opened her mouth, her voice hoarse. "Yu Hao..."
His gaze fixed on her unusually pale face. There was still a scattered, unfocused quality in his eyes, the aftermath of some violent emotional blow. His brow furrowed slightly, and he looked down at her with a natural, genuine concern. "Yao Yuan? Are you all right? Why are you on the floor of the elevator?" As he spoke, his hand was already reaching out of habit, about to help her up.
In the instant before his fingertips touched her arm, Yao Yuan flinched back, barely perceptibly but with startling quickness.
She'd dodged him.
Yu Hao's hand froze in midair.
Yao Yuan lifted her eyes. In that split second, she had already forced down all the daze and tremor the time-travel had left behind, restoring herself to her usual clear-eyed calm. Only beneath that calm there now seemed to lie a sheet of unbreakable thin ice. The conversation in the mirror with the "past," the rehearsal of betrayal, those naked rules of the game — all of it had been filed away in her mind at high speed.
She looked at Yu Hao, this man she had chosen to partner with, the one she currently rated as a "reliable ally" and a "no-strings companion." The worry in his eyes was real. But having just been through a "high-intensity simulation" about the limits of human nature and the exchange of interests, any approach or contact she hadn't actively granted instantly tripped her sharply tuned defenses.
"I'm fine," she said, her voice still slightly slurred from the drinking. "I had a few drinks with Jin Lan and the others at the Gilded Palace tonight. It's cold outside, and the moment I stepped into the elevator it got stuffy. The alcohol hit me and I blacked out for a bit."
"Nobody saw you home?" Yu Hao's eyes stayed on her face. "It's too dangerous for you to be like this on your own. Don't forget what happened last time, passing out at the Crimson Night Palace. Drink less from now on."
Yao Yuan braced herself against the elevator wall and slowly got to her feet. The chill of the metal seeped through her clothes into her palm. Without quite meaning to, she sidestepped the fact that Gao Jieyuan had been the one to bring her home. "Weren't you in Hong Kong? Why are you suddenly back?"
The elevator gave a soft "ding" as it reached the floor. The doors slid apart, and the warm yellow light of the corridor poured in.
"First day of the new year," Yu Hao's voice came from behind her, a softness hidden beneath the calm that was easy to miss. "I suddenly missed you. I had dinner with my parents and then rebooked onto a night flight back."
They stepped out of the elevator one after the other. Yao Yuan's steps were still a little unsteady, yet without seeming to try, she kept a distance from him that was cooler than usual.
Yu Hao drew back the hand he'd meant to offer her, his gaze lingering on the side of her face. He'd caught that flinch, and the faint, almost imperceptible barrier in the air now. This wasn't the Yao Yuan he knew — even exhausted, she'd always carried a certain sharp sense of control.
"You're really okay?" He kept pace with her, watching as she unlocked the apartment door with her fingerprint.
"Mm." Her answer was clipped.
Inside, Yao Yuan shrugged off her coat, draped it over the back of a chair, and curled into the sofa. Yu Hao headed for the kitchen as a matter of course, poured a glass of warm water, and brought it over. "Drink some warm water, it'll help."
She tucked herself tighter into the sofa and took the glass. The warmth seeped through the wall of the cup into her palm, but she didn't drink, just held it.
Then Yu Hao sat down beside her. Not too close, but his presence was unmistakable. After a moment of silence, he reached out and gently slipped an arm around her shoulders.
"Yao Yuan." He said her name, his voice lower than usual. "This time in Hong Kong, watching the fireworks over Victoria Harbour, I suddenly missed you."
She didn't move, didn't push him away, just listened quietly.
"I know what we agreed on at the start — 'companions,' no feelings, no tying each other down." He paused, as if choosing his words. "But some things don't seem to stay under control."
Yao Yuan lowered her eyes, watching the faint ripples on the surface of the water in the cup.
"I've started to care," Yu Hao said, his voice steady but carrying a rare, clumsy honesty. "Like last time at the Crimson Night Palace, that ridiculous drinking contest with Gao Jieyuan. That wasn't just possessiveness, I think. But I'm not sure what it was either. We're both too rational. You take feelings apart for a living, I take numbers apart. The word 'love'... for people like us, it's a little foreign. A little extravagant, even."
The air went quiet. Only the faint trickle of the humidifier broke it.
It was a long while before Yao Yuan spoke, her voice tinged with weariness. "Yu Hao, I've been drinking tonight. My head's spinning and I'm tired. Now isn't the time to talk about this."
The tiredness was real. Not just the residue of the noisy club, but the drain of the time-travel, the shock of facing down her past self, the crushing weight of those memories of betrayal storming through her mind.
She gently freed herself from his arm and stood. "Tomorrow. We'll talk it through properly tomorrow."
Yu Hao looked at her, and in the end, he nodded. "Okay."
That night they washed up as usual and lay down in the same bed as usual, a polite distance between them. Yao Yuan lay on her side with her back to him, eyes open in the dark, and it was a long time before she fell asleep.
The next day was the second day of the new year, a day off.
Morning light filtered through the sheer curtains, spilling pale gold across the teak floor. The winter sun at half past ten had no warmth in it, but it was bright enough. Yao Yuan woke first and lay where she was in silence for a moment, letting her mind rise fully out of the deep water of sleep. Last night's conversation, the rare emotion Yu Hao had let show, and her own mood still not fully settled after coming back from the other side — in the morning light it all felt both vivid and somehow distant.
She got up and went into the bathroom. When the water started running, Yu Hao leaned back against the headboard, looking out at the pale blue winter sky, and let out a very soft, almost soundless sigh.
He knew that some things, once said out loud, could never go back to that "just right" distance they'd had before.
And today, in the end, they had to talk it through.
While Yu Hao made coffee in the kitchen, Yao Yuan came out in her loungewear, her hair still slightly damp. Their eyes met. The barrier was still there, but the ordinariness of the morning had worn it down a little.
"Order in?" Yu Hao asked, holding two cups of pour-over.
"Sure." Yao Yuan took one of them. The nutty aroma of Colombian beans spread through the air.
Breakfast came quickly: Cantonese dim sum, crystal-clear shrimp dumplings, soft and tender chicken feet. They sat at opposite ends of the table and ate in silence, like so many mornings they'd woken up together before. Only today, the silence held a heaviness, a waiting to be broken.
After they cleared away the takeout boxes, Yu Hao brewed a fresh pot of Yirgacheffe. Cups in hand, they walked into the study. It was the quietest room in the apartment — a whole wall of bookshelves, a large desk facing the city skyline beyond the window. Sunlight slanted in, picking out fine motes of dust in the air.
Yao Yuan settled into the single armchair, drew her legs up, and cradled the warm porcelain cup. Yu Hao leaned against the edge of the desk, not sitting. He knew that some things were easier to say standing up.
"What I said last night, I didn't finish." He spoke calmly, the tone of a man talking business. "This trip to Hong Kong, my parents sat me down for a real talk."
Yao Yuan lifted her eyes and waited for him to go on.
"I'm thirty." Yu Hao's gaze settled on something out the window. "The family business needs someone to take it over, and more than that, it needs a clear heir. They want me to settle down, as soon as possible."
He paused, then turned his eyes back to her. "I told them I'm seriously seeing someone."
The air went still for a beat. Yao Yuan's fingers ran lightly over the wall of the cup.
"But my father looked into you." There was a barely perceptible difficulty in Yu Hao's tone. "Your livestream clips, the public controversies, those 'Godmother of Feelings' labels... they can't accept it."
He took two steps forward and sat down in the armchair across from her, leaning in, elbows on his knees. It was a posture of candor.
"Yao Yuan, I admire you. I like being with you. These past six months or so have been the most..." he searched for the word, "the least lonely stretch of my life in years. But marriage won't work. Your public image is too high a risk for our family's listed company. If we got married, the day it went public, the share price would swing at least five percent. And that's before the ongoing fallout."
He said it plainly, the bluntness of a businessman weighing risk. Yao Yuan listened in silence, no flicker of expression on her face, only the frozen lake at the bottom of her eyes seeming to go a few degrees colder.
"So," Yu Hao drew a deep breath and laid out the core of his proposal, "we don't have to get married. But I need an heir. I want you to have a child for me."
The study went quiet enough to hear the trickle of the humidifier. Beyond the window the city ran on under the winter sun, remote and indistinct.
Yao Yuan set down her cup. The base met the glass tea table with a crisp little "tap." She leaned back into the sofa, hands folded in her lap. The posture was exactly how she looked dissecting a case in her livestream — detached, rational, ready to take something apart.
"Yu Hao," she said, her voice as level as if she were discussing someone else's affairs, "let's go through this point by point."
"One: the child's status. A child born out of wedlock — legally you'd have a duty to support it and it would have inheritance rights, but in practice you'd run into plenty of problems. Will your parents acknowledge this grandchild? Will the family trust list him as a beneficiary? And if you marry in the future and have a child within that marriage, how is this child's standing protected?"
"Two: my cost. Pregnancy, delivery, nursing — at least two years, during which my career takes a massive hit. Does the livestream stop or not? Do I run the company or not? Through the postpartum recovery period, my public image could shift from 'sharp-tongued godmother' to 'single mother.' Have you thought about the reputational risk in that?"
"Three: our relationship. Once there's a child, we can't possibly stay simple 'companions' anymore. Will you be involved in raising it? How often? Who does the child live with? And if we each find new relationships down the line, where does the child fit in?"
With every point she made, Yu Hao's expression grew a shade graver. She was too clear-eyed — clear-eyed enough that all the calculation he'd hidden under "feelings" had nowhere to hide.
"Four," Yao Yuan went on, her gaze sharp as a blade, "and this is the crux — what makes you think I'd be willing?"
The question landed with weight.
Yu Hao was silent for a long time. The sunlight moved across his face, from his jaw to his cheekbone.
"Because I thought," he said at last, his voice a little rough, "that you cared about me, at least a little. And I know you've always been searching for a kind of... security. A child gives you legal protection. I'd provide enough financial support to keep you and the child free of worry for life."
Yao Yuan gave a faint laugh. There was no warmth in it, only a weariness that saw straight through everything.
"Yu Hao, you just said we're both too rational." She spoke slowly. "Then let's be rational all the way to the end. The plan you've handed me is, at its core, trading my body and the risk to my career for a legitimate source of a family heir for you, while insulating yourselves from the share-price risk my public image might bring. And what you put in is just some 'sufficient financial support' — which, to you, is nothing more than a number."
She rose and walked to the window, her back to him. The outline of the city unfolded beyond the glass — cold, vast, full of rules.
"If I agreed," she said to her own reflection in the glass, "then one day in the future, when you need to marry for family interests — take a wife of matching background and a clean record — my child and I would become a 'legacy problem' for you to handle. By then, what would the 'caring' and 'liking' you talk about be worth?"
Yu Hao didn't answer. He couldn't.
"You said your father looked into me," Yao Yuan said, turning to lean against the window. "Then he must also know that at twenty-four I was rejected by my first love's family on the grounds of class, that at twenty-eight a wealthy boyfriend gave up on marriage because I was 'too hard to handle,' that at thirty I was used as a bargaining chip by the boss I trusted most. I know this script all too well — a woman, because she's 'not suitable enough,' only ever gets the discounted treatment, only ever gets slotted onto the unofficial track."
She walked back toward the sofa but didn't sit, just stood there looking down at him.
"Yu Hao, I spent more than ten years climbing out of those scripts. I built my own career, took hold of my own voice, made myself something other than the one who gets assessed, picked over, compromised on. And now you want me to go back? Back, for your so-called 'caring' and 'financial security'?"
Her voice was soft, but every word was like an icicle.
"I won't have the child," she said. "It's not spite. It's the optimal solution after I've calculated the risk-reward ratio. My womb, my time, my professional reputation — they're valued very high. The price you're offering isn't enough."
The study fell silent again. But this time, something in that silence had shattered for good.
Yu Hao slowly got to his feet. He looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes complicated — the awkwardness of being refused, the embarrassment of having his calculation seen through, and perhaps a thread of genuine loss he hadn't quite registered himself.
"I understand," he said, his voice back to its usual composure, though there were small cracks beneath it.
He walked to the door and stopped at the threshold, not turning around.
"Yao Yuan," he said, "you're the most clear-eyed, and the most... unapproachable person I've ever met."
The door opened, then closed.
Yao Yuan stood alone in the sunlight at the center of the study. She walked slowly back to the window and watched Yu Hao's figure appear on the street below, cross to the car parked at the curb, get in, and drive off.
Her face in the glass reflection was calm, unrippled. Only the hands clasped in front of her, knuckles slightly white from the pressure.
Her phone buzzed on the tea table. She went over to look — a message from her assistant: "Yuan-jie, the call-in applications for next week's livestream are blowing up. There's one really classic case — the woman got pregnant before marriage, and the man's family is proposing a prenup that cuts the five-million bride price down to one million..."
Yao Yuan finished reading and replied: "Send me the case file. Also, set up a meeting with Lawyer Deng for me — I want to consult on inheritance rights for children born out of wedlock."
Message sent, she put the phone down, picked up the cup of Yirgacheffe that had gone stone cold, and took a sip. Bitter, but clarifying.
Beyond the window the city ran on. And on this winter morning, certain possibilities had been closed off for good — while certain new calculations were only just beginning.
She knew that from today on, the "just right" balance between her and Yu Hao was broken. But that was fine. She was good at rebuilding order out of a wrecked board.
It was only in some very deep corner of her heart that there was a faint, almost imperceptible trace of melancholy. Not for Yu Hao, but for a kind of confirmation — that no matter how high she climbed, in certain people's eyes she was still something that could be assessed, traded away, filed under the "risk" column.
But soon that thread of melancholy was covered over by a harder resolve. She went to the desk, opened her laptop, and pulled up next quarter's business plan.
Feelings are a variable. Interests are a constant. And she chose to build her undefeatable ground in the world of constants.
The sunlight moved slowly across the desk, lighting up the dense financial models and growth curves on the screen. Those numbers were cold, certain, loyal — they never abandoned her on grounds of "class" or "image."
That was enough, she thought.
For her, at least, that was enough.

The car door shut, sealing out the winter chill along with the heavy glass door of Yao Yuan's apartment building. Yu Hao didn't start the car right away, just sat in the driver's seat, hands resting on the wheel, his gaze passing through the windshield to the endless stream of headlights on the overpass in the distance.
The low hum of the engine was especially clear in the enclosed space. He needed that sound, needed that bit of mechanical, controllable vibration to press down a rare, near-chaotic disorder inside him.
He'd been refused.
The realization sat before him, clear and cold. Not a refusal out of spite, not an emotional pushback — a Yao Yuan-style rejection: methodical, dissected, a rational "no" based on an assessment of risk and return. He could even picture the evaluation model spinning at high speed inside her head — variables, weights, probabilities, discount rates — the same kind of thing he used when making investment decisions himself.
That was exactly what left him with such a complicated sense of defeat. They spoke the same language, ran the same kind of math, and yet arrived at opposite conclusions.
Yu Hao undid a button at his collar, leaned back against the seat, and closed his eyes. The city's blurred clamor was filtered out beyond the car, but in his mind some long-ago scenes replayed with unusual clarity.
He wasn't a born playboy. Quite the opposite — from childhood he'd seemed out of step with the kids around him. While other boys were still shouting over game rankings and wins and losses on the court, the twelve-year-old Yu Hao could already sit reading the newspaper, frowning over the international news pages and the financial channel. He remembered the first time he "made money": in his first year of middle school, by analyzing a few unremarkable policy shifts and tender announcements for infrastructure projects, he'd talked his mother into stockpiling, cheap and early, a batch of saplings whose prices later shot up. The "pocket money" he made wasn't much, but the thrill of getting a return through an information gap and good prediction was branded into his early sense of the world.
Precocious. Everyone said so. He'd seen through the workings of the adult world too early — the rules about resources, exchange, leverage, and timing. It made it hard for him to take any real, deep interest in his peers. Their joys and sorrows, their loves and hatreds, often struck him as blunt, shallow, even... a bit of a waste of energy. What he needed was conversation that could keep up with the speed of his thought, resonance that could grasp his complex calculations, a kindred mind that could read the state of the board shoulder to shoulder with him.
Until he met Yao Yuan.
She'd appeared at exactly the right time — just when he'd grown thoroughly tired of all those beautifully made-up but empty-headed women in the world of glamour. Yao Yuan was different. She was beautiful, no question, an aggressive, commanding kind of beauty, but she never relied on beauty alone. The sharpness with which she discussed traffic algorithms, the precision with which she took apart business cases, even the candor with which she admitted she "loved money" and "admired strength" — all of it gave him a rare sense of a match on the level of the mind.
With her, body and mind were satisfied in sync. His body drawn to her, his mind lit up by her. He could go with her from Merkel's political legacy to TikTok's recommendation engine, from Schrödinger's cat to the next likely wave of consumer downgrading. All the things he could never say to anyone else — his hidden worries about the family business, his cold detachment toward the capital markets, the moments of emptiness about the meaning of life — in front of her all seemed to find a kind of unspoken understanding. She was like a mirror polished razor-sharp, reflecting him, and letting him see clearly many of his own blurry zones.
Proposing a child wasn't an impulse. It was the optimal solution after a full assessment.
Biologically, their gene combination had a high probability of producing superior offspring — intelligence, looks, resilience. Practically, he needed an heir to ease the family pressure, and she could provide that "vessel." Emotionally, his care and possessiveness toward her had gone beyond the bounds of "companion," and a child would bind that connection in the most unbreakable way. From a risk-control standpoint, not marrying would maximally isolate the family from the potential negative impact of her public image.
As for what Yao Yuan herself might need? He'd thought it through, he believed. She loved money — he'd give her ample financial security, ensure she and the child leapt up a class and lived in comfort for life. She valued her career — he could promise to put resources to work, helping her career rise even higher after she gave birth. She needed security — a legal agreement could protect her and the child's rights to the fullest. He'd even considered that if she minded the out-of-wedlock status, they might in the future arrange a surrogate, keeping the disruption to her career to a minimum.
This was a plan he'd run through over and over, one that seemed to him logically self-consistent, mutually beneficial, win-win. He'd assumed that someone as realistic and as good at calculating as Yao Yuan would see the value in it.
But she hadn't.
"Aren't the weights enough?"
He opened his eyes, looked at his own blurred reflection in the car window, and asked it without a sound.
Did he need to raise the bid? By how much? Equity? Real estate? Set up an independent trust fund just for her and the child, large enough to make her completely financially free? Or... promise some form of "going official" down the line?
But those last words of Yao Yuan's came down like ice water.
"My womb, my time, my professional reputation — they're valued very high. The price you're offering isn't enough."
What she refused, it seemed, wasn't merely a specific price. What she refused was being placed on this scale and weighed at all. What she refused was the underlying logic of his proposal, which looked fair but in fact reduced her to a "high-quality reproductive vessel" and a "risk-insulation vessel."
Yu Hao's fingertips tapped lightly on the steering wheel. It was a habit when his mind was racing.
He suddenly realized he might have made a critical error. He'd always taken Yao Yuan's "realism" and "calculation" to be purely functional, something a higher bid could satisfy. But he'd overlooked the fact that her realism was born precisely from being objectified, traded, assessed, and then discarded, time after time. The reason she'd become so good at calculating was exactly to defend against the fate of being calculated.
And what he'd just thrown at her was precisely another "trade contract" that put a clear price tag on her core value — fertility, time, reputation.
How could she possibly accept it?
She'd climbed so long, bled so much, just to struggle out of that "priced" quagmire and reach a place where she could define the rules herself, even set prices for others. How could he still expect her, for a seemingly generous offer, to willingly jump back into the very rules of the game she despised?
This wasn't a question of how heavy the weights were.
It was a fundamental conflict in the rules of the game.
Yu Hao felt a deep wave of exhaustion, and something deeper still — interest. Yao Yuan was tougher than he'd imagined, and more complex. She wasn't a variable in his algorithmic model whose output one input could predict; she was an outlier that kept iterating, that could even turn back and bite the algorithm itself.
He started the car and pulled slowly into the lane. The city's neon flowed past the window in blurred ribbons of light.
The awkwardness of being refused was still there, but another, stronger emotion was taking root — the thrill of meeting a worthy opponent, and the drive to rise to a challenge that meant recalibrating his whole model.
He needed to reassess Yao Yuan. Not as a "suitable reproductive partner" or a "quality gene provider," but as a complete person with an independent will and a complex core, a worthy adversary in the game.
Maybe what he wanted had never been just a child, or an easy, low-maintenance relationship.
What he wanted was the one who made him feel constantly challenged, who forced him to run his mind at full power just to barely keep up, who could even now and then overload his processing — Yao Yuan herself.
And to get her — or rather, to build some more solid bond with her in a way he could accept without betraying his own core interests — seemed to require a whole new, more complex algorithm.
In the unseen interior of the car, the corner of Yu Hao's mouth twitched ever so faintly.
It wasn't a smile. It was more like the look of a hunter who has spotted unexpected, but far more worthy prey — that instinctive expression, a mix of gravity and excitement.
The car merged into the night traffic. His mind was already racing, casting off the old plan, building a new model. More variables, more complex constraints, and the objective function would need to be redefined too.
This game was far from over.
It had only entered the next round.

========================================

Chapter 47 — Shuai Hongqiang's "Good Luck" Dodges a Deadly Disaster at the Ski Resort
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640294.html

Meanwhile, on the second day of the New Year holiday.

Early that morning, Shuai Wenyao was bouncing around in excitement, pestering Shuai Hongqiang and Lin Wan to take him to the newly opened ski resort. "Dad! Mom! All my classmates have been there. They say it's super fun! Tons of kids from my class went! Let's go too — I want to go today!" The boy's eyes brimmed with anticipation; his holiday craving for fun overrode everything else.

Worn down by his son, and genuinely wanting to take the family out for a change of scenery himself, Shuai Hongqiang agreed. Smiling, Lin Wan began packing the snacks, hot water, and heavy clothes they'd need. The family's destination was a large new ski resort in the outskirts of Jinshi — the facilities were said to be brand new and suited to skiers of every level.

The resort had been built on a sheltered, gentle slope in the rolling mountains at the edge of the city. The heavy snowfall just before New Year's had blanketed the near and far peaks generously. In the city the snow had already melted, but up in the mountains the temperature stayed low, so the white expanse held — perfect timing for skiing. For that very reason, the road to the resort was clogged with traffic, mostly families like theirs out enjoying the holiday.

The road wasn't wide — a two-lane track winding up the mountain. On many shaded stretches, the snow that had melted over the past few days had frozen again, forming patches of slick "ice slides" across the road, glinting dangerously in the sunlight. Traffic crawled along, forcing frequent braking. Shuai Hongqiang drove with extra care, both hands gripping the wheel, fully focused. Beside him, Lin Wan kept saying "slow down." Shuai Wenyao, who'd started out pressed eagerly to the window taking in the silver-wrapped forest, gradually wilted under the long, grinding congestion.

The accident came without the slightest warning.

Right where a long downhill fed into a curve, the cars ahead all flared their brake lights at once, glaring and harsh — followed by a teeth-grating screech of skidding tires and the dull crunch of metal crumpling together! Shuai Hongqiang's heart clenched and he slammed on the brakes, but the car simply couldn't stop on the road's thin coat of clear ice. The tires locked, yet the car slid on like a runaway sled, sliding straight down the slope toward the tangled pileup of bumpers ahead!

"Ahh—!" Lin Wan let out a sharp little scream and instinctively threw an arm across Shuai Wenyao beside her. Shuai Wenyao went white with fear.

Cold sweat sprang out on Shuai Hongqiang's forehead. Less than twenty meters ahead, he could see clearly — the fronts and backs of several cars already twisted and crumpled, debris flying. His own car was sliding unstoppably toward the very heart of that "crash vortex"! In that instant, time seemed to stretch out; countless thoughts flashed through his mind, and he could grasp not a single one.

In that razor's-edge moment, the corner of his eye suddenly caught something to the right — behind the snow-covered brush at the roadside, there seemed to be an extremely narrow side turning! It didn't look like a proper road, more like a dirt-and-gravel track worn in by local villagers heading up the mountain to work — barely wide enough for one car, almost buried in snow, with a few dead branches bowed under the weight of it at the mouth.

Without a moment's hesitation, the survival instinct and years of driving experience drove Shuai Hongqiang into a reaction that was almost pure muscle memory! He yanked the wheel hard to the right, feathering the brake to match, and the capable SUV let out a sharp screech of rubber grinding against ice. The front end shaved past the snowbank heaped at the roadside by a hair's breadth and lurched onto that hidden little track! The car jolted violently, and the sound of the underbody scraping over gravel and snow came up from below.

Almost the very instant his rear bumper cleared the main road — "BANG—!!!" — an even heavier crash boomed out behind them. Back in the lane they'd just left, a seven-seater that couldn't stop slammed solidly into the tail of the car ahead, shoving it forward into the next. The chain of collisions kept spreading wider.

Shuai Hongqiang steadied the car on the narrow track and stopped. The whole family was still shaken, the cabin dead silent — nothing but their heavy breathing and pounding, drum-like heartbeats. He looked back at the main road through the rearview mirror and side window — a scene of carnage. At least a dozen cars were piled into one another nose to tail, some with crumpled fronts, some flipped on their sides at the roadside, shattered glass and parts scattered everywhere. Crying, cursing, and blaring horns all blended together. And the spot they had just left sat right in the middle of that "steel centipede."

If… if his reaction had been a tenth of a second slower, if he hadn't seen that track, if he'd turned the wheel a fraction too late… they would already be part of that heap of scrap metal right now.

"Hong… we…" Lin Wan's voice trembled as she clutched the stunned Shuai Wenyao tightly.

"It's okay, it's okay, we got out of it." Shuai Hongqiang drew a few deep breaths, forcing himself calm, and turned to comfort his wife and son — but his own hands on the wheel were still faintly shaking. That was the body's reaction to surviving a brush with death.

He inched the car carefully forward along the narrow road for a stretch, getting well clear of the accident scene, until he found a slightly wider spot where he could turn around. With some difficulty he swung the car about and drove slowly back the way they'd come, planning to take a detour or just head straight home. The mood for skiing had long since been frightened clean away.

On the drive back, the cabin was silent. Shuai Wenyao sat quietly, no longer making a fuss. Lin Wan's face was still pale. Shuai Hongqiang replayed those harrowing dozen-odd seconds over and over in his mind.

That little track… it had appeared too conveniently. As if it had been prepared just for him. It was so hidden, half-buried under snow and dead branches, yet in that tense moment he had spotted it at a single glance. And the timing had been perfect — five or six cars had crashed up ahead, creating a brief "impact gap" and a stall in speed, while the cars close behind hadn't fully reacted yet. In that lightning-fast window, he'd had both the ability and the room to make the turn. A car earlier, and he'd have been dragged straight into it; a car later, and the space would have been completely sealed off.

This could no longer be explained away as simple good luck. The coincidence was so precise it sent a chill crawling up his spine.

A single thought, like a dark current beneath the ice, rose irrepressibly into Shuai Hongqiang's mind — could this… could this be the work of Yao Yuan's "time-travel" again?

Ever since the day he had "returned" the bronze mirror to Yao Yuan, and then — after bearing his "good luck" a second time — told her she had formed a "Mirror Pact" with the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror and was tied to its time-travel, his heart had been uneasy, and harbored a secret kind of expectation too. He couldn't be sure what exactly had happened to Yao Yuan after the mirror carried her through time, but according to the arcane logic he knew (or thought he knew), the person who bore the great fortune of the "Mirror Pact" would, through the fluctuations of their fate and luck, channel the good fortune and blessings transmuted from the Primordial Purple Energy back — in some indescribable way — to the bearer who had given the mirror.

Was this narrow escape from death a manifestation of that "blessing"? Because Yao Yuan had once again triggered the bronze mirror's "regression" or "resonance," had the formless "good luck" spread out like ripples, granting him, the "bearer," a thread of survival at the critical moment?

The thought sent a chill across his back — yet mixed in with it was a faint, hard-to-name relief and… secret delight. If it really was so, then this mirror, this so-called "Mirror Pact," was even more mysterious than he had imagined, and also… more dangerous. It could bring "good luck," but the price of that "good luck"…? And what state was Yao Yuan in right now?

He glanced at his still-shaken wife and son beside him, his feelings a tangled mix. They had dodged it this time. But what about the next? Was this "good luck" sustainable, or a one-time expenditure? And what, because of it, was Yao Yuan bearing on her end?

The ski trip clearly couldn't go on. Shuai Hongqiang drove his family silently back into the city. Beyond the windows, the desolate winter mountainscape slipped past behind them. On the main road, the sirens and the wail of rescue vehicles could still be heard far off in the direction of the accident. The disaster that had brushed past them became an utterly abrupt and heavy footnote to this New Year holiday.

And the turmoil in Shuai Hongqiang's heart surged far more fiercely and tangled than the scenery outside. His attention to Yao Yuan's situation had grown into something he couldn't quite name, along with a deeper layer of worry. The price of this "good luck," he feared, would sooner or later show itself.

The car slowly left the mountains, the chaos and sirens outside gradually falling away behind them, while the cabin sank into the silence of having survived. Shuai Wenyao must have been frightened, or perhaps sensed his parents' heavy mood; he leaned obediently against Lin Wan and soon, surprisingly, fell asleep, though his brow was still faintly furrowed. Lin Wan patted her son's back gently, her gaze somewhat glazed as she stared at the bare trees flashing past the window, her face still pale.

Shuai Hongqiang drove with concentration, trying to steady his heartbeat and breathing, but that harrowing scene and that "conveniently" appearing track kept flashing back, impossible to shake. Unlike the previous two bouts of "good luck," this time the feeling was so intense that he could no longer convince himself with words like "coincidence" or "a turn in fortune."

The first two times — Ji Wenshan repaying his debt with interest, the city government's overdue payment unexpectedly clearing — had been delightful, even a little uncanny, but deep down Shuai Hongqiang could still find a "reasonable" anchor: that money had been his to begin with. Ji Wenshan's debt — the man's conscience had stirred, or he'd finally come into funds. The municipal project payment — maybe it had simply coincided with some special allocation or a push from above. Their "return" had been sudden, but it hadn't fully departed from how the real world worked — the creditor-debtor relationship was clear, the money was right there; only the timing of its return had been full of unexpected "luck." That kind of good fortune felt more like a justice arriving late, or fate's compensation for his earlier streak of bad luck — even if the timing was suspiciously convenient.

But today was completely different.

This was not the settling of a debt, not wealth lost and found again. This was, on an ice-covered downhill, at the edge of a death-vortex of more than a dozen cars piling into one another, a snow-buried farmer's track that should never have entered his awareness or his line of sight — appearing like a precisely deployed escape exit, at the very instant he most needed it and was the only instant he could possibly use it, "seen" by him and driven into successfully. This was not "taking back what was his." This was, at the brink of life and death, being forcibly "yanked" back by some invisible force from the very claws the reaper had already reached out.

The nature of it was worlds apart.

If the earlier "good luck" still carried a measure of the solid feeling of "things returning to their rightful owner," then this time it was pure, undeniable, beyond-all-reason "intervention" and "gift." And the gift was not money — it was the safety of him and Lin Wan and Shuai Wenyao, all three of them. It was the wholeness of the entire family.

And that gripped Shuai Hongqiang's heart with an enormous unease.

The appraiser Master Chen's aged, grave voice sounded in his ears once more, clearer than ever before: "The giver of the mirror bears its fortune; the time-traveler loses their spirit. As one waxes, the other wanes — this is called 'borrowing fortune.'" "What she loses is her spirit and her lifespan; what you gain is luck and opportunity. This debt… can never be repaid."

"Loses their spirit"… "spirit and lifespan"…

Before, his understanding of those words had been vague — perhaps just mental exhaustion, insomnia, restless dreams, that sort of thing. But today, when this "luck and opportunity" had manifested in such a violent, such a crucial way — directly averting a disaster that could have wrecked the car and killed them all — Shuai Hongqiang suddenly realized just how heavy this "fortune" weighed. And so the "price" Yao Yuan had to pay was surely far beyond anything that could be measured by mere "fatigue"!

This time it had saved their lives. So what was Yao Yuan going through right now? A splitting headache? A sudden fainting spell? Or some deeper, more terrifying drain? The two words Old Chen had spoken — "lifespan" — were like two cold needles driven into his heart. He didn't dare think too hard about it, yet he couldn't stop himself from imagining the very worst.

Cold sweat seeped out again — this time not from fear of what might have happened, but from a complex tangle of relief, guilt, dread, and intense unease. He was relieved his family was safe; that was the most genuine feeling. But hard on its heels came the realization that this safety might have been built on another person's — Yao Yuan's — unknown sacrifice, and that left him sitting as if on pins and needles. He had used (or rather, passively accepted) the "good luck" she might have brought, possibly even at the cost of harming her health, and that awareness bred a powerful moral guilt. The dread, for its part, came from his failure to understand the unknown mechanism and his worry over Yao Yuan's condition — and from the fear of whether he would, in the future, come to depend on, even "crave," this kind of luck.

Human nature is greedy, especially after a taste of something sweet. He was afraid he would gradually grow used to it, even start to look forward to this "good luck" — and that would undoubtedly push Yao Yuan into ever greater danger.

He glanced at his son sleeping soundly beside him and his still-shaken wife. Protecting them was his responsibility; he could not bring himself to regret the narrow escape just now. But the "price" of this safety pressed down, heavy, on his conscience.

The car pulled into the city, and the familiar streets and traffic brought back some sense of reality — yet the shadow over Shuai Hongqiang's heart only grew heavier. He took out his phone, his finger hovering for a long while over the name "Yao Yuan" in his contacts. He wanted to ask how she was, whether anything felt wrong. But on what pretext? Was he going to say, "I nearly had a car crash today, saved only by the good luck you maybe paid for with your own spirit — are you all right?" It was too absurd, and too cruel. He couldn't explain why he was linking two seemingly unrelated events, and he didn't dare say it either.

In the end, he set the phone down, dejected. A sense of helplessness and a deeper worry spread through him. The riddle of the bronze mirror, the truth of "borrowing fortune," Yao Yuan's present state — they tangled in his heart like a knot of thread. He realized this could no longer go on in such murky uncertainty. He couldn't keep enjoying the "good luck" that (very likely) Yao Yuan was trading some price for, while pretending he knew nothing of her plight, even secretly counting himself fortunate.

He needed to find Old Chen and get clear on what this "Mirror Pact" really was — how it worked, what exactly the price was, and most important of all, how to end it, or at least how to stop her from continuing to be harmed.

The ski trip had ended in a fright, but Shuai Hongqiang knew that another ordeal — one bound up with conscience and unknown risk — was only just beginning. He drove the car into the underground garage of his own residential complex and parked. Watching his wife and son get out, he told himself silently: he had to do something. For his family's safety, and for… that ever-heavier unease in his heart.

Shuai Hongqiang went straight to the Guangu Hall to find Old Chen, only to be told by the shop assistant that Old Chen had indeed gone south for the winter. He'd be back when spring came. He hadn't expected it himself — that the lie he'd casually made up at the time, about not wanting Yao Yuan to come into contact with Old Chen so soon, would actually come true.

========================================

Chapter 48 — The Price of the "Good Luck" Bought with the Mirror Pact
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640295.html

On the first weekend after the New Year's holiday, Shuai Hongqiang asked Yao Yuan to meet at "South Lake Cottage," a place the two of them used to frequent. The restaurant was tucked deep inside a park—quiet amid the bustle, elegantly decorated, wrapped in greenery, its specialty the light, refined local cuisine. This had once been Yao Yuan's favorite spot.

Shuai Hongqiang arrived early on purpose and chose a booth by the window. Outside lay a winter courtyard, a few stalks of withered bamboo swaying gently in the wind, sparse and still. Inside, though, it was another world entirely: a man-made eco-garden lush and green, warm and snug, holding onto a small patch of springtime life in the dry, cold northern season.

He ordered a pot of Tieguanyin tea and watched it pour into the cups, steam curling upward, his thoughts drifting. The terrifying scene of the car crash and the heavy unease that came with "borrowing fortune" had haunted him these past days. He had turned it over and over, and in the end he'd decided to ask Yao Yuan out. For one thing, he needed a reasonable, unremarkable excuse to probe into how she'd been doing lately—her health, above all. For another, there was indeed a piece of real business to discuss.

Yao Yuan arrived right on time. She wore a cream high-necked cashmere sweater under a long light-gray coat, her makeup immaculate, her manner its usual composed and capable self—nothing seemed off. Even so, Shuai Hongqiang instinctively studied her face carefully. She looked slightly paler than before, a faint shadow under her eyes, whether from poor rest or… something else, he couldn't tell.

"Been waiting long?" Yao Yuan sat down across from him and slipped off her coat; a waiter took it at once.

"No, I just got here too." Shuai Hongqiang pulled himself together and poured her tea. "This place is just like it always was."

Yao Yuan glanced around and smiled. "It is. The food hasn't changed either." Her tone was easy, as if they were just two old friends catching up, but deep in her eyes lay a hint of caution—she was clearly trying to gauge the purpose of this meeting.

The dishes came out one after another, all light and fresh. The two exchanged a few words about how they'd been, asked after each other's year-end busyness at their companies. The mood was neither warm nor cold, holding the proper distance of acquaintances.

Choosing his words, Shuai Hongqiang finally steered the conversation toward concern. "You look a little tired. Work been too busy lately? Or… not resting well?" He asked as casually as he could, though the fingers gripping his teacup tightened without his noticing.

Yao Yuan's chopsticks paused, almost imperceptibly, as she picked at the food. She raised her eyes to him, her gaze calm. "It's like this every year-end—all the wrap-ups and planning. What about you? You took the family out over the New Year, didn't you?" She deftly turned the topic back on him while watching his reaction.

"We went out to the suburbs, wanted to ski. Didn't expect a pileup on the road—we nearly got caught in it." Shuai Hongqiang sighed and shook his head, his voice carrying the aftershock of fear. "Lucky for us, there happened to be a small side road right beside us, so we slipped past disaster. But it scared us half to death—even Wenyao was wilted afterward." As he spoke, he watched Yao Yuan's expression out of the corner of his eye. He'd mentioned being "lucky," mentioned "slipping past disaster"—he wanted to see whether she'd connect it to anything, whether she'd react in any particular way.

Sure enough, Yao Yuan stopped, brows drawing together, and looked at him in earnest. "A crash? Is everyone all right? Was it bad?" Her concern was genuine, but as Shuai Hongqiang parsed it carefully, it seemed to be only the ordinary concern one shows for a routine traffic accident—nothing deeper, nothing that hinted at "this has to do with me."

"Everyone's fine, just a few scratches on the car—thank goodness." Shuai Hongqiang relaxed a little, then immediately tensed again. She looked entirely unaware. Did that mean the "drain" was invisible, something even she hadn't sensed? That worried him more, not less.

"That's good. You have to be careful driving from now on, especially in weather like this." Yao Yuan offered the reminder and went back to her meal, seeming to set the topic aside.

Shuai Hongqiang knew he couldn't push any further, or it would seem strange. He cleared his throat and steered the conversation toward the day's second purpose—the one out in the open, the main one.

"Actually, I asked you here today for more than just dinner. There's something I wanted to talk over with you." He set down his chopsticks, his expression turning a touch more formal. "Remember the letter of intent I brought you a few months back—the commercial complex tied to that old-town redevelopment district?"

Yao Yuan nodded. "I do. I read it. The overall margins were thin, and it got tangled up with leftover demolition issues in that district, which gave me some reservations. Why—has something changed?"

"Yes." Shuai Hongqiang admitted it frankly, his tone complicated. "I won't hide it from you. When I first came to you, it really was because a long-overdue government works payment hadn't come through, and the new project had a funding gap. I wanted to lean on your capital and your operating muscle. But"—he paused—"a little while ago, that overdue payment was suddenly settled in full, principal and interest both."

Yao Yuan looked slightly surprised by his candor, but only raised an eyebrow. "Oh? That's good news, then—a big problem solved."

"It is." Shuai Hongqiang gave a wry smile; the reason behind this "good news" left him conflicted. "So that supporting commercial complex—the funding problem's solved, and I can handle it myself. I figure the margins and the difficulty of running it probably wouldn't catch your eye anymore."

Yao Yuan neither agreed nor disagreed, waiting quietly for the rest.

Shuai Hongqiang looked at her and went on. "What I wanted to talk to you about today is a different project. After the New Year, the city will most likely launch a new 'National Fitness Center' project. The site may be in the new district—the scale and specifications will both be high. It counts as a key livelihood project. The baseline investment will be enormous, demanding more of both capital and overall operating capacity.
