Of course, if we win the bid, the prospects and the prestige are beyond what an ordinary project can offer."

He leaned forward slightly, his tone sincere. "This project would be a strain for me to swallow on my own, and the risk would be concentrated. If you're interested, we could talk seriously about the possibility of working together. You have the edge in resource integration and running high-end projects; I have the foundation in local construction and on-the-ground execution. Strong joined with strong—better odds of winning."

These words were half business proposal, half… an olive branch born of some impulse to make amends. He knew Yao Yuan's company was on a strong upward trajectory and might well have no use for his earlier project. But this National Fitness Center really was a better platform. If she could take part, and the partnership went smoothly, perhaps… he could give her more of a boost in business, which would count as an indirect form of… compensation? Shuai Hongqiang couldn't untangle these complicated feelings himself; he only felt he ought to do something.

Yao Yuan didn't answer right away. She sipped her tea slowly, her eyes settling on the leaves floating in the cup, as though weighing it all carefully. The room was very quiet, only faint background music in the air.

After a long moment she looked up, her gaze clear and sharp, fixed on Shuai Hongqiang. "Boss Shuai, this project does sound good. But I'm a little curious." She tilted her head slightly, a faintly probing smile on her lips. "You just solved your funding problem. You could perfectly well take a crack at a bigger piece of the pie yourself, or find other partners. Why come to me again, specifically?"

Her question was direct, and it struck right at the heart of it. Had Shuai Hongqiang really asked her here only to discuss a possible partnership?

Shuai Hongqiang met her gaze and sighed inwardly. Yao Yuan was as sharp as ever—she could pick up on the slightest thing that was off. He couldn't mention the bronze mirror, couldn't mention "borrowing fortune," and certainly couldn't mention that absurd unease and the urge to make amends.

He smiled, the smile carrying some helplessness, and some sincerity. "There are a few reasons. First, this project demands strong government relations and the ability to land things locally—that's my strength—but high-end planning and later-stage operations are yours. We complement each other. Second"—he paused—"we've known each other so many years, we know each other inside out. Working together, the communication costs are low, and there's a foundation of trust. Third…"

He looked at her and said slowly, "When money was tight, the person I thought of was you—because I felt you could pull me out of the fire. Now the money's sorted, and there's a better chance, and still the person I think of is you. I suppose it's just… I feel that good things should be done with people worth sharing them with. Of course, this is only my proposal. The choice is entirely yours."

Yao Yuan listened quietly, her expression betraying little change. Only after a good while did she set down her teacup, the faint click of porcelain.

"If it's convenient, send me the project materials and a preliminary feasibility analysis." Her tone was level, all business. "I'll need my team to evaluate it. As for working together"—she looked up, meeting Shuai Hongqiang's eyes directly—"once I've seen something concrete, we'll talk it through in detail. How's that?"

"Of course." Shuai Hongqiang nodded, a weight lifting from his chest—at least she hadn't refused outright. But he understood, too, that Yao Yuan's "evaluation" would never be only on the business level.

"All right, I'll wait to hear from you." Yao Yuan smiled faintly and picked up her napkin to dab at the corner of her mouth, seeming ready to bring the meeting to a close.

Shuai Hongqiang took the cue and let the project drop. The two chatted a little more, the mood seeming to return to the polite calm of when they'd first sat down.

Yao Yuan picked up her coat, her movements smooth and natural, as if it were just an offhand courtesy before leaving. But her next words froze the quiet air in the room solid:

"By the way—you said you only got past that crash on New Year's by luck? Nothing else? No warning you saw in advance, say? Or some special… feeling?"

Her tone was light, her eyes not even fully lifting from the coat she was straightening. Yet that seemingly casual question pierced, like a fine needle, straight through the calm Shuai Hongqiang had been working so hard to maintain.

His heart skipped a beat, then began to pound wildly.

(She's sensed it? She's felt something wrong herself?)

The appraiser Old Chen's aged, raspy warning rang out in his ears, suddenly crystal clear: "…The Mirror Pact is already made. Unless she realizes it herself, senses in some deep way that her fate has gone awry and comes seeking a way to break it of her own accord, no outside force can sever it… That is the first step to undoing the pact, and the crucial one. If she remains wholly unaware, then even if you talk yourself blue in the face she may not believe you, and the pact will not budge an inch."

Shuai Hongqiang's eyes swept quickly over Yao Yuan's face. Pale though she was, with a faint blue-green beneath her eyes—a sign of drained vitality, true—her gaze was clear, her bearing unchanged, nothing like the badly eroded, listless image he'd dreaded. That let his racing heart drop back down a little, and a tangle of feelings welled up—relief and an even heavier guilt. Relief that she seemed, for now, in no real danger; guilt that this "for now, no danger" was very likely bought with her present exhaustion and with unknown costs still to come.

He had to resolve this as soon as possible. The moment Old Chen returned from the south, he'd take her to see him! The thought was firmer than ever before.

He pressed down the surging emotions and forced his face into an expression that just barely passed for relaxed, though his voice came out drier than he'd expected. "No warning. It was just… good fortune smiling on me, I guess. At the last possible second, that side road suddenly caught my eye—like Heaven had opened a private lane just for me." He paused, then decided to stop circling the point. Meeting Yao Yuan's calm, searching gaze, he laid out the decision he'd been turning over all night:

"Come spring, the moment Old Chen is back in Jinshi, I'll take you to see him. We'll see if there's some… safe way to undo that 'Mirror Pact.'" His words quickened, as if to persuade her—and himself. "Even though it looks like there's no real harm to you for now, just poor rest at worst, who knows about later? Something like 'time-travel' is bizarre enough on its own, and none of us has gotten to the bottom of that mirror. Better to undo it completely, clean and clear, so there's no strange thing hidden inside that we don't know about, no endless trouble down the line."

Having said it, he held his breath and watched Yao Yuan, waiting for her reaction. In those words, the worry was real, the guilt was real, and the secret, faint reluctance to lose his "good luck" had been pressed down beneath a deeper fear—the fear that he might be dragging her down.

Yao Yuan didn't respond at once. She stood there quietly, coat draped over her arm, her gaze deep, as if reappraising this familiar yet unfamiliar man before her. That gaze seemed to have weight as it passed over his forced composure, his slightly tightened fingers, the urgency and unease in his eyes that he couldn't quite hide.

(Shuai Hongqiang…)

A clear realization surfaced in her mind. At his core, this man was, in the end, decent—he had his lines he wouldn't cross and his sense of honor. This was perhaps the limit of his caliber, a lack of ruthless decisiveness in certain capital games, but in this moment it became a rare and precious virtue. Swap him for one of those who'd forged hearts of iron and stone amid the tides of business—who, on learning that sacrificing a negligible bit of an "old flame's" spirit could trade for their own fortune flowing smoothly and disaster turning to safety, would treat it as a heaven-sent piece of leverage and scheme by every means to cement this "contract." Where would they ever think, of their own accord, of "undoing" it? They might even plot to keep the other party from ever noticing.

It really did prove that old saying: it's the dog-butchers who are most loyal, and the scholars who most betray. To be ruthless enough, sometimes, you really do need those "cultured types" who understand too many principles and calculate too finely.

A faint, almost self-mocking curve crossed her eyes. At last she spoke, her voice low but landing clearly in the quiet room:

"All right."

Just that one word. No further questions, no doubt, no flicker of fear or curiosity about the "Mirror Pact" itself—as if she'd merely agreed to the most ordinary little thing.

Yet that single word made Shuai Hongqiang's heart suddenly ease, only to fill at once with more complicated feelings. She'd agreed, just like that. Was it trust—or had she long harbored doubts of her own?

"Then… I'll be in touch when the time comes." He nodded, suddenly at a loss for anything more to say.

"Mm." Yao Yuan answered and slipped briskly into her coat, wrapping her scarf. "I'll go first. Just send the project materials to my email."

"All right. Take care on the way."

The two parted at the restaurant door, one going left, one going right, merging into the bustling stream of people on the street. The winter wind rushed at their faces, bringing a biting clarity.

Shuai Hongqiang walked toward the parking lot, pulled open the car door and got in, but didn't start the engine right away. He gripped the steering wheel with both hands, staring blankly ahead. Yao Yuan's last, calm "All right," and her fathomless eyes, replayed in his mind again and again.

(Just how much does she know? And how much does she believe?)

But no matter what, the path was clear. Spring, Old Chen, undoing the pact. This was the road he had to walk—the only road he could walk.

He started the car and eased into the traffic. In the rearview mirror, the restaurant's sign slowly receded. What he didn't know was that, at a street corner in another direction, Yao Yuan had also stopped. She looked back once toward where his car had vanished, thoughts churning in her eyes, and only after a long while turned and walked off with steady steps into the scattered lights of the city.

The thread of the Mirror Pact seemed, on the surface, to have loosened with the chance of "undoing" raised by one side. But the cause-and-effect and the tangled feelings wound around it seemed only to have begun to draw tight.

========================================

Chapter 49 — The Power Spectrum in Marriage: The Art of Moving from Conflict to Symbiosis
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640296.html

The ring light gave off a soft white glow. Lin Wan sat in front of the camera, an ash-gray silk blouse setting off her jade-smooth skin. Her makeup tonight was barely there; only her eyes were startlingly bright, like a lighthouse on a sea at midnight. On the bookshelf behind her, beside that old copy of "Game Theory," a new addition stood quietly: "The Second Sex," its Chinese title clear along the spine.
"Good evening, everyone. Welcome to 'The Gentle Crossing.' I'm Lin Wan."
Her voice was as calm as ever, but it carried a different kind of force, as if it could reach through the screen and gently knock on the door each person kept closed over the subject of intimacy.
"In the past we've talked about dividing housework, emotional value, intimate communication. Tonight, I want to invite you all to look at something deeper underneath it all." She paused for a moment, her gaze sweeping the lens as if looking into the eyes of every single viewer. "The invisible 'power spectrum' inside a marriage."
The comments started to come alive:
[A new topic!]
[Power spectrum? Sounds academic.]
[Wanwan feels different tonight.]
"A spectrum," Lin Wan repeated the word, her fingertip tracing an invisible arc across the desk. "It isn't black or white. It isn't, if you have it then I don't. It's a band of light that shades from one tone to the next, from the red of conflict, to the orange-gold of blending, and on to the blue-green of symbiosis. And most of us are out there on that spectrum, looking for our own place."
She leaned forward slightly, and the motion made the viewers lean in too without realizing it.
The comments started to come alive:
[Power? Sounds a bit heavy.]
"Recently, I got a very long letter in my inbox." Lin Wan touched the desk lightly. "A young woman wrote: 'Sis, I still hope you'll keep encouraging independent women. Because what passes for equality these days is, very often, just patriarchy in a more hidden form. For a woman to win the same social standing and the same social resources as a man is like having to chisel out a window of her own from a wall thousands of years in the building.'"
Lin Wan read it slowly, every word like a stone dropped into the lake of the heart.
"I read this letter three times and thought about it for a long while. And then I remembered the words of another woman, a scholar." She lifted her eyes, and her gaze turned distant. "She said that when we cry out for women's rights, we are never preaching that women's interests reign supreme, and certainly not denying everything men contribute, nor stirring up conflict between the sexes. We are pursuing the purest equality: I can do it, I can take responsibility, I can fulfill myself, and I can rightfully receive the legal rights that belong to me."
The comments froze for an instant, then surged:
[So well said.]
[This is what real equality means.]
[More people need to hear this.]
[In tears.]
"She said," Lin Wan went on, her voice tender and firm, "when my strength runs out, I am glad to give a sincere round of applause to a man's strong muscles. When I'm overflowing with inspiration and my talent is bursting out, I also allow a man's train of thought to stall for a while, to not have it all worked out. When I set out to build something of my own, women and men can both walk beside me, and I, just the same, can be a man's guide ahead."
"I've read this passage over and over." She raised her eyes to the lens. "Because hidden inside it is the ultimate answer to the question of power in marriage: it isn't about who overpowers whom, but about seeing each other, completing each other, finding a path to symbiosis within our differences, and within that symbiosis, blossoming into something larger."
The comments began to resonate:
[This is a healthy intimate relationship.]
[Not conflict, but collaboration.]
[Wanwan reached a new level tonight.]
"But before we arrive at that state, we have to face reality honestly." Lin Wan's tone grew serious. "Many women in a marriage look, on the surface, like they 'have a man protecting them,' when in fact they're still fighting alone. They have to handle society's scrutiny, and on top of that absorb all sorts of 'psychological discomfort' that comes from the gap in gender attitudes inside the marriage itself. And the exhaustion of fighting on those two fronts, in a context dominated by patriarchal thinking, often goes unseen, even unacknowledged."
"This is why," she said, word by word, "talking about power in marriage is not about manufacturing conflict, but about establishing a truly fair starting point. Fairness is not splitting everything down the middle. It's letting women who are good at striking out on their own, who can command social resources, go after them freely, hold tight to the resources and the confidence that belong to them, and shine on the social stage. It's letting women born with artistic talent in their bones immerse themselves in their creating, be embraced by the world and praised by the crowd, stay loyal to what they love their whole lives, and brew inspiration into the nourishment of their years. It's letting women who carry their country in their hearts, who have strategy and vision, bravely chase their political ideals, set their sights on becoming great statesmen, and use their own strength to shoulder responsibility and carve out a piece of the world."
"Man or woman, everyone should have the right to fulfill themselves. Every gift and every passion deserves to be respected and supported."
A heated discussion broke out in the comments:
[But the reality is women are forced to do it all.]
[My husband thinks I should put family first.]
[How do you change this fixed mindset?]
"Let's come back to the concrete scenes of marriage." Lin Wan shifted her tone, like descending from the clouds of theory to the solid ground of daily life. "Do you remember the 'housework treaty' and the 'battle over space' I shared with you all? Those seemingly trivial games are, at heart, about redrawing the map of power inside a marriage."
"But today I want to say this: the most masterful exercise of power often happens in the moment the 'sense of power' disappears."
In front of the camera, her expression softened.
"The truth is, over these past three years, Qiang's company hit a serious rough patch. Debt, unpaid receivables, cash-flow pressure. He couldn't sleep, night after night, but he never said a word to me. And I didn't ask. I just quietly adjusted my livestream schedule, took on a few more partnerships, did what I could to ease things financially at home. When he was anxious, I didn't rush to give advice. I just stayed with him."
"Later, when I was preparing a new series and needed a solid block of time to write, he would say, naturally, 'Go to the study, I've got Wenyao.' And when I lifted my head out of the words, I'd find the two of them in the living room, father and son with their heads together building Lego, the setting sun pouring through the window over them both."
Lin Wan's voice was very soft. "That was when I suddenly realized that the heading of this household had, at some point I couldn't name, shifted over to me steering."
"This isn't seizing power. It's a natural handover. When he could finally relax at home, no longer needing to play the all-capable 'strong one,' when he could let his most essential self, even the rough edges of it, out into the sunlight, I took up the helm."
"And the real test starts exactly here." Her gaze sharpened. "Once you've gained the resources and taken the wheel, can you stay clear-headed? Not arrogant, not thrown off balance, still carrying the load steadily forward? That ability is the hardest skill marriage ultimately teaches you. It's a social skill at its core: out in this jungle of a city, learning to break the wind and hold the sand in place, learning to plant trees and grow a forest, opening up a sustainable oasis for yourself and for your family."
The comments slid past:
[This kind of understanding between two people is so precious.]
[But it takes both people staying clear-headed.]
[I'm still angry about who does the dishes.]
Lin Wan smiled. "There's no 'should' here, no keeping score, and certainly no 'look how much I sacrificed for you.' There's only a dynamic division of labor built on deep understanding. This division no longer follows the old script of 'men outside, women at home.' It follows 'who needs support more right now, who is better at handling it.' It flows, as natural as breathing."
The comments turned thoughtful:
[Dynamic assessment!]
[This is what a married couple should look like.]
[But only if both sides have woken up.]
"Yes, the premise is awakening." Lin Wan nodded. "And in today's context, that awakening usually requires women to take the first step. Because the structural inertia of thousands of years is so powerful, so powerful that many men don't even register that the privilege exists."
"So the reply I gave that young woman was this: encouraging independent women isn't about encouraging women to fight the whole world alone. It's about encouraging women first to 'found a nation' inside themselves, to build a complete sense of sovereignty. Only when you yourself clearly know 'what I can do, what I want, where my boundaries are' can you bring real, weighty leverage to the negotiating table of marriage."
"And that 'founding a nation' process," she paused, "is exactly where we owe thanks to those pioneers of women's rights and their hard-won fight. That we can now live, at ease, as the people we want to be is thanks to their struggle: it lets us set out and travel far on our own, no longer bound by convention. Their steadfastness lets us walk into universities and receive an education, arming our minds with knowledge. Their cries let us hold a bank card in our own names, grasping the confidence of financial independence. Their fight gives us the right to choose contraception, taking control of our own bodies and lives. Their tireless efforts give us the freedom to divorce, so we no longer have to spend a whole life inside an unhappy marriage. And their work gives us safer, more humane production standards for sanitary pads, protecting the most basic dignity of the body. None of this ever came easily. It is space for rights that those before us chiseled out, bit by bit."
"The room we have to negotiate in a marriage is always directly tied to the space society grants women."
Many people in the comments typed "Respect to the pioneers."
"But even having come this far, the road to equality is still long." Lin Wan's tone grew complicated. "Just as that young woman in my inbox said, the equality of today still carries an undertone of patriarchy. And the most painful part is that sometimes women themselves become the ones upholding that undertone."
She told a story, her voice calm but with currents moving beneath it.
"I've been livestreaming for four years. For four years I've relied on putting out my own views, dissecting marriage, sharing the logic of survival, doing a solid four-year one-woman show. Some sisters understand me, and some have cursed me as 'love-brained': 'If marriage wears you out this much, why don't you just get divorced?' My viewership grew slowly. Every step was hard."
"But recently I discovered," she shook her head with a bitter smile, "that the traffic could actually come this easily. I don't have to do anything on purpose. I just have to bring Qiang in front of the camera and have him sit there quietly. Now and then I lean over and give him a kiss, let a little intimacy show on screen, and the numbers instantly double."
"He doesn't have to do anything else. He hands me a glass of water, or mentions, offhand, the company's prospects, or the groundwork he laid for me. And the next second the whole screen fills up: 'Qiang is so thoughtful,' 'This man spoils his wife too much,' 'This is what marriage at its best looks like.'"
Lin Wan looked into the lens, her gaze sharp as a knife. "People are far too easily fooled by the surface. My four years of independent analysis, of clear-eyed speaking, of pouring out my whole heart, count for less than a few moments of a man appearing on camera." Her voice dropped, edged with pain. "This is the unspoken rule of the internet, and the reality of the world too: the moment a woman has a man beside her, is wanted, has a marriage to fall back on, people's attitude toward her changes instantly. When others want to judge you freely, look down on you, push you around, they think twice first. As long as you wear the label of 'settled down, loved by a husband,' everyone starts singing the praises of a harmonious marriage, painting the besieged life inside those walls in pretty colors."
"I have a group of longtime followers, true fans who've always stayed with me, who understand the weariness and the clarity inside me. And there are many sisters who've been deeply hurt by men, who tell me privately: 'Sis, your livestream room used to be my refuge, my harbor. But the moment Qiang appears, I think of my father, and something in me instinctively flinches, gets scared. I don't dare keep watching. I can only leave the room.'"
"I understand that pain so well. People who have faced their family of origin head-on, who have faced the scars of marriage head-on, live more deeply, see more clearly. But the deep ones are always the minority." Lin Wan paused, letting the line settle. "Most people don't have the patience to listen to a woman's fragility, her grievances, her struggles. At their core, the masses fawn over men. Too many people's values have long since hardened: handle the man, and you've handled life. The best ending for a woman who marries is not having to work, being provided for, hiding behind a man her whole life and sheltering from the storm."
"And the ones saying these things are very often women themselves." She spoke word by word, as if interrogating every heart. "So, sisters, how is it that we can feel aggrieved at being suppressed by our husbands in marriage, and at the same time, without meaning to, feel that 'having a husband to lean on makes me secure,' even when that husband may not be someone you can lean on at all?"
The comments sank into reflection, the scroll slowing down.
"Back to marriage itself." Lin Wan changed her tone, moving from the sharp interrogation to a warm, gentle sharing. "After Qiang's financial crisis was resolved, we made a 'five-year family plan.' Not led by him, not led by me. Each of us wrote down five directions, and then we looked for where they overlapped."
"I found that he wanted to expand into new lines of business, while I wanted to do deep research on women's growth. They look unrelated, but the overlap is this: both need stronger learning ability and a stronger network. So we agreed on a 'family study night' once a week. We read reports together, analyze cases, pick apart each other's plans."
Lin Wan laughed, fine lines at the corners of her eyes, the composure that the years give as a gift. "In this process, he teaches me how to read people and network, and I teach him how to build user profiles. We're no longer husband and wife. We're each other's strategic partners. And the strange thing is, once that professional respect was established, a lot of the small frictions in daily life dissolved on their own."
The comments were all envy:
[The ideal relationship!]
[This takes two people who are both mature enough.]
[We're in a cold war over who does the dishes tonight...]
"Don't worry. Every evolution takes a process." Lin Wan said gently. "Maybe you can start by changing one sentence. Next time there's a disagreement, instead of 'why do you always...,' try 'I noticed... I need...' A change in language will quietly drive a change in thinking."
"So, in concrete terms, how do you move from conflict to symbiosis in a marriage?" Lin Wan laid out three points, and on the whiteboard behind her, clean keywords appeared one by one.
"First, recognize the power patterns, but don't get trapped in them. You need to see clearly which things are structural injustice (like 'raising the kids is naturally the mother's job') and which are differences in personal habit (like squeezing the toothpaste from the middle or the end). For the former, negotiate gently but firmly. For the latter, you might as well dissolve it with humor."
"Second, build an 'emotional account,' but deposit and withdraw wisely. Marriage is like a joint account. You can't have only one side constantly depositing (giving) while the other withdraws without limit (taking). The healthy way is: I deposit my consideration, and I expect you to deposit your support. I respect your career ambitions, and you respect my personal growth. Love flows, but it needs a conscious balance."
"Third, and most important: shift from 'fighting over power' to 'co-creating value.' When the two of you stop wrestling over 'who gets the final say in this house,' and start thinking 'how do we make this home better together, make each other better,' the relationship enters a new stage."
"There are always people asking what the true meaning of women's rights is. I believe the true meaning of women's rights is that women hold infinite possibilities. In this world, there has never been any such thing as 'how a woman is supposed to be.'"
"But even having come this far, the road to equality remains a long and heavy one. Just as that young woman in my inbox said, the so-called equality of today still carries a deep-rooted undertone of patriarchy. For women to win the same social standing as men, equal social resources, to break the prejudices and shackles buried by thousands of years of patriarchal society, still requires countless women to stay clear-headed and walk forward shoulder to shoulder."
"When we encourage independent women, it has never been about denying marriage or denying love. It's that we hope every woman can first become herself, and only then play the other roles. No need to depend on others, no need to be disciplined by gender, no need to be bound by marriage. Boldly chase what you love, fulfill your own value, hold the same opportunities and respect as men. This is the most authentic meaning of women's rights, and the way every woman should be able to stand in her life."
"Finally, I want to close tonight's sharing with that scholar's words, which are also my ultimate understanding of power in marriage."
Lin Wan sat up straight, the light gathering into clear points in her eyes.
"True equality is not women becoming the same as men, nor men submitting to women. It's that together we create a broader 'spectrum of humanity,' a spectrum in which strength and tenderness, reason and emotion, ambition and devotion to home are no longer stamped with gender labels. We can freely become whole human beings, and we can also rest easy leaning into each other as intimate partners."
Her voice echoed through the quiet studio:
"Marriage can be a miniature of this spectrum. Here, we learn how to keep ourselves and make room for another at once, how to claim our rights and shoulder our responsibilities at once, how to see the differences and create what we share at once."
"May we all grow, within marriage, from players of a chess game into dance partners of life. Not playing chess to decide a winner and a loser, but dancing one piece of music together. Sometimes you lead, sometimes I take the lead, but the music and the rhythm, we create together."
She gave a faint smile, like the night wind passing over a lake.
"That's all for tonight. As for how to find the balance point between 'conflict and symbiosis' in your own marriage, you're welcome to share your stories or your doubts in the comments. Good night. See you next time."
The end screen of the livestream faded out. Outside the study, Shuai Hongqiang stood under the warm yellow wall lamp in the hallway, arms crossed, leaning against the wall, watching quietly as she came out.
"Tonight's session," he began, a complicated feeling in his voice, "didn't feel like a tactics lecture. More like..."
"Like what?" Lin Wan walked over and, easily, took the glass of warm water he had set out on the dining table at some point.
Shuai Hongqiang thought about it and found the word. "A philosophy lecture." He watched the side of her face as she drank. "And it's like, for the first time, I completely understood what you were trying to say."
Lin Wan lifted her eyes and saw in his a kind of clear, unprecedented seriousness. Not the scrutiny of a game, not the weariness of compromise, but real seeing and understanding.
"So," she blinked, letting a rare bit of playfulness show, the kind that belonged to "Lin Wan" and not "Lin Wan the host," "what score does the student give the teacher?"
Shuai Hongqiang didn't laugh. He just reached out and gently touched her cheek with the back of his finger.
"Full marks," he said. "But not for a student or a teacher. For my partner."
That word, tonight, carried a whole new weight.
They walked side by side toward the bedroom. At the end of the hallway, the night light in the child's room glowed like a gentle little star. And further out beyond the window, the city's lights flowed like a river of stars, and under every one of those lights lay an intimate history being rewritten.
Some high walls are having windows chiseled into them. Some spectrums are quietly widening. And all of it begins with one ordinary night after another, one sincere conversation after another, one quiet, deep-running change after another.
The story of power in marriage has never ended. It only keeps evolving, from the red of conflict, to the orange-gold of blending, and on, at last, to that wider, freer blue of symbiosis.
And on this road, every individual who has awakened is a light that illuminates the others.

========================================

Chapter 50 — Spring Festival: A Team-Building Reward in Phuket
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640297.html

Spring Festival: A Team-Building Reward in Phuket
The twenty-third day of the twelfth lunar month, Little New Year. The fine snow over Jinshi hadn't yet settled on the ground before the heat rising off the airport runway steamed it into a thin mist.
Zhou Yifan's flight back from Shanghai landed at dusk. While waiting for his luggage, he switched on his phone, and Yao Yuan's message had already popped up in the family group chat: "@everyone This Spring Festival we're relocating to Phuket — company team-building plus family trip, seven days, six nights. Ten a.m. on the 28th, meet at the airport."
Below it was attached a minimalist itinerary and a list of notes. The per-person budget was clearly marked: thirty thousand yuan. A chartered direct flight, five-star the whole way, the schedule paced with a good mix of tight and loose — the first two days for free exploration, then sailing trips, a Thai cooking class, and Thai massage woven through the middle, and a complete switch-off at the end.
The group chat was quickly buried under cheering emojis from the staff. Zhou Yifan looked at the screen, the corner of his mouth curving up. This was so very Yao Yuan — make the reward tangible, make the experience top-tier. She wanted everyone who followed her to see it: effort had a price, and the price was clear.
He wheeled his suitcase out into the arrivals hall and spotted Yao Yuan at a glance among the crowd waiting to greet passengers. A cream-white cashmere coat, her long hair loosely pinned up, she was looking down at her phone. In the cold white airport lights, her profile had a quiet sharpness to it. Two years apart, and the sense of control she carried had settled into something like an aura.
"Sis." Zhou Yifan walked over.
Yao Yuan looked up, her eyes brightening for a moment, and she naturally took a small bag from his hand. "You've lost weight. Is the lab wearing you down?"
"It's worth it." Zhou Yifan looked at her. At twenty-two, the young man already stood much taller than she did, and the rigorous training of medical school had given him an unconscious clinical eye when he watched people. "The dark circles under your eyes — foundation can't hide them. Up all night staring at data again?"
Yao Yuan raised an eyebrow. "You don't even have your medical license yet, and you've already learned to diagnose by looking?"
The two of them headed toward the parking lot. The car was warm inside, and as Yao Yuan drove out of the airport she said, "The company beat its targets by thirty-eight percent this year. Time to let the team see something of the world. Thirty-odd people plus our family — I chartered a business jet. Mom and Dad are all set; it's just Uncle Zhou—"
She paused, a little exasperated. "He's fretting over the shop's Spring Festival business and doesn't want to go."
"And then you talked him into it?" Zhou Yifan fastened his seatbelt.
"Of course." Yao Yuan flicked the turn signal. "I told him to hand the shop over to the manager and do a video reconciliation of the books every day. This year's team-building theme is 'how to spend the money once you've earned it.' You're going to be a doctor — all the more reason to see how this world actually runs. It's not just operating rooms and labs; there are far more complicated lesions and compensation mechanisms inside the human heart."
She said it calmly, the streaming lights outside the window sweeping across her face. Zhou Yifan suddenly felt that his sister was opening another window onto the human world for him, in her own particular way — not a window of pathology, but of the dynamics of human nature.
Talking Zhou Lufei around had indeed taken some doing.
"Seven days! The seven best days for business!" In the back kitchen of Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot, Zhou Lufei's knife flashed through the lamb almost too fast to see. "And Yuanyuan, how much is this going to cost? Thirty thousand a head — that's a hundred and twenty thousand for the four of us! How much does this shop of mine make, slaving away dawn to dusk for half a year?"
Yao Yuan leaned against the counter, unhurried. "Uncle Zhou, what was the shop's net profit last year?"
"Around a hundred and eighty thousand." His hands never stopped, and there was a solid pride in his tone, earned over the better part of a lifetime. From the little eight-table place it once was, to this three-story new-Chinese-style building today — "Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot" in gold letters on black was a name to reckon with along the whole restaurant street. It was the most tangible achievement of his sixty-five years.
"My company's after-tax net profit last year was this much." Yao Yuan made a gesture.
Zhou Lufei's knife froze. He turned to look at her, eyes wide, and even forgot to set down the handsome lamb hindquarter in his hand.
"Money can never all be earned." Yao Yuan's voice softened. "But you're sixty-five, Uncle Zhou. You and my mom have been married twenty-five years. You raised me, and then you had Yifan. What's the farthest place the two of you have ever been? Beijing? Or Hangzhou, the year I graduated and took you there?"
Zhou Lufei fell silent, the rhythm of his blade against the cutting board slowing.
"Money is only living water when it moves. Sitting in a bank card, it's just a number. Spent on experiences and on broadening your horizons, it becomes life." Yao Yuan went on. "This trip isn't only for fun. I had my assistant contact two high-end Thai restaurant groups. They have hotpot brands under them that are doing quite well, and we can go take a look at their back kitchens, have a chat. Don't you want to know how a Thai hotpot supply chain is managed, how the customer experience is designed?"
That hit Zhou Lufei right where it itched. He set down the knife and wiped his hands. "We can really see other people's kitchens?"
"We can." Yao Yuan smiled. "I've arranged it all. And besides, it's about time my mom got out and saw the world too. In her whole life, she's never once stepped outside the country."
That last line finally broke down Zhou Lufei's resistance. He rubbed his hands and gave a slightly sheepish laugh. "All right then, I'll go along with you. I'll keep your mom company too, get out and have a look around... Oh, right, your sister's side—" he meant the daughter left to him by his late first wife, five years older than Yao Yuan, now working at the provincial Commerce Department, long since married with a child of her own. "I've already told her. They'll spend their own holiday with their little family this year and not worry about us."
The twenty-eighth day of the twelfth lunar month, the VIP departure lounge at Jinshi Airport.
More than thirty core staff from Yao Yuan's company, plus her own family — a team of thirty-eight — had assembled. The younger employees talked in low voices, unable to hold back their excitement. Several managers gathered around Yao Yuan, confirming the last details.
Zhou Yifan sat quietly beside his parents. Zhang Fengxia wore a newly bought crimson wool sweater, her hair carefully permed, looking bright and lively. Zhou Lufei, by contrast, was a little stiff, forever straightening the jacket he so rarely wore.
"Nervous?" Zhou Yifan asked quietly.
"A bit." Zhou Lufei admitted it honestly. "Such a grand setup... your sister, these days — she really is something."
Zhang Fengxia shot him a glance. "Your daughter's done well for herself, and you still can't relax?"
"It's not that I can't relax..." Zhou Lufei scratched his head. "I just feel like I'm dreaming."
Yao Yuan came over and crouched in front of Zhang Fengxia, helping her arrange her scarf. "Mom, when the plane climbs and descends, if your ears bother you, chew on these." She handed over a box of mints.
"I know, go on, go do your thing." Zhang Fengxia patted her hand, pride she couldn't hide in her eyes.
The Thai Airways charter landed in Phuket in the early afternoon. The tropical wind, wrapped in a salty dampness, rushed at their faces, and in an instant the northern winter was thrown far behind them.
Three luxury coaches took the team straight to the five-star resort on Kata Beach. Yao Yuan had chosen the hotel herself — standalone villas and pool suites scattered just so, the balance between privacy and sociability struck perfectly. The staff stayed two to a small villa; Yao Yuan's family had the four-bedroom villa with a private pool, tucked away in the deepest part of the grounds.
After check-in, there was a brief gathering. Yao Yuan stood in the open-plan lobby, facing more than thirty young faces.
"Each room has an itinerary and an envelope. Inside the envelope is fifty thousand baht of spending money. For these seven days, there are only three rules. First, stay safe, travel in pairs. Second, put down your work and relax completely. Third—"
She paused, her gaze sweeping over every face. "Experience it, fully. Experience all of this that you earned with your own abilities. Remember how it feels to stay in a good hotel, eat fine ingredients, be surrounded by excellent service. Grow the numbers another thirty percent next year, and we'll go to Japan. The year after, Europe. I want you to know that following me, Yao Yuan, what you earn isn't just money — it's the standing to choose how you live."
The applause was warm. A few of the young women had reddened eyes.
Zhou Yifan stood at the edge of the crowd, watching his sister. In that moment she was a leader, a person who made dreams come true, telling her followers in the most direct way possible: the good things in this world — you deserve them, and you can have them.
The schedule was loose and lavish.
The first day was free time, and the staff went off in pairs to the beach or the spa. Yao Yuan strolled around the resort with her family. Zhou Lufei was especially keen on the hotel kitchen and the restaurant's design, pulling Yao Yuan aside to ask about this and that. Zhang Fengxia and Zhou Yifan fell behind, walking slowly along a path through the coconut palms.
"Your sister's gone all out this time." Zhang Fengxia looked at Yao Yuan's back up ahead and said softly.
"She deserves it," Zhou Yifan said. "And she's willing to give it."
Zhang Fengxia turned her head to look at him. At twenty-two her son had grown fully into a man — in his brows and eyes there was her own stubbornness from her younger days, Zhou Lufei's gentle warmth, and, more than either, a quiet keenness all his own.
"Things going smoothly in Shanghai?"
"Smoothly. My advisor thinks highly of me, and the research is making progress." Zhou Yifan answered, then added, "Just busy. I don't get back often."
"Busy is good." Zhang Fengxia paused. "When your sister was your age... she went through a lot of hardship."
The words were light, but Zhou Yifan understood them. He knew some of his sister's past — not the details, but enough to piece together the outline: those fragments of betrayal, struggle, and rebirth.
"She's doing well now," he said.
"Yes." Zhang Fengxia watched Yao Yuan's upright figure. "She's made herself into a beam of light."
In the evening, the whole family had dinner at a seaside restaurant. The sun set in molten gold, the surf rolling in wave after wave. Zhou Lufei had a little of the local beer and grew talkative.
"This place really is something — good air, and the food's so fresh. I just keep thinking about the shop... Spring Festival is the busiest time for business."
"Didn't you watch the video? Everything's running like clockwork." Zhang Fengxia placed some grilled fish in his bowl. "Just stay put and relax."
Yao Yuan watched them with a smile. Her phone buzzed. A message from Yu Hao: "Happy Spring Festival."
It had been more than a month since their last conversation about "children and marriage." She didn't reply, and locked the screen.
"Sis," Zhou Yifan asked, "the sailing trip tomorrow — did you bring seasickness pills?"
"I did." Yao Yuan put her thoughts away. "Relax, little Doctor Zhou."
After dinner, Zhou Lufei said he wanted a walk along the beach to settle his stomach. Zhang Fengxia hadn't meant to go, but Yao Yuan gave her a gentle nudge. "Mom, keep Uncle Zhou company. The night view is lovely."
The two of them walked slowly along the sand. The night sea was deep blue, the surf gentle. After a while, Zhou Lufei suddenly spoke.
"Fengxia, thank you."
Zhang Fengxia turned to look at him. The streetlamps were dim, his expression a little blurred.
"Thank me for what?"
"A lot of things." Zhou Lufei's voice was a little hoarse. "Thank you for not minding, back then, that I was a man whose wife had died and who came with a daughter. Thank you for being so good to my daughter — she isn't your own flesh and blood, but no one could fault how you've treated her. Thank you for being willing... to give me Yifan."
Zhang Fengxia's steps slowed.
"I'm clumsy with words, I can't say pretty things." Zhou Lufei went on, like words pent up a long time had finally found their way out. "I know I'm not good enough for you. You're beautiful, capable, fiery. When you married me, plenty of people said you'd gotten the short end of it. I just thought — I have to be good to you, good to your daughter, can't ever let you regret it."
The sea breeze drifted by, carrying its salty damp.
"That temper of yours is actually a good thing." Zhou Lufei suddenly laughed. "Straight to the point, nothing hidden, nothing held back. My first one... she was soft, kept everything bottled up inside, and in the end it made her sick. You're the kind who lets it out — it's a relief. When I do something wrong, you scold me straight out, and once you're done, we go on living the way we always did. You don't hold a grudge."
Zhang Fengxia stopped. In the moonlight, the corners of her eyes glistened.
"Old Zhou," she said, very softly, "I have to thank you too."
Zhou Lufei was taken aback.
"Thank you for not minding that I came with Yuanyuan, that 'extra baggage.' Thank you for loving her like your own real daughter — when she was little, if you'd ever raised a hand to her, I'd have come at you, but you never once struck her. The most you ever did was sigh. Thank you... for not abandoning the two of us, mother and daughter, in those hardest years."
She drew a deep breath. "We were both on our second marriage, both carrying people and things from before in our hearts. But for these twenty-five years, you've lived this life with me honestly, no crooked thoughts, no hidden selfishness. Every cent the shop earned, you handed to me. To Yuanyuan you never said a single harsh word. When she went to university, when she started her business, you cared even more than I did. I've kept all of this in mind."
Zhou Lufei's eyes reddened. He turned his face away and blinked hard.
"We're an old married couple — what's the point of saying all this..." he muttered.
"What ought to be said has to be said." Zhang Fengxia took his arm. The gesture made Zhou Lufei stiffen all over — in twenty-five years, she had rarely reached for him like this. "Come on, let's head back. The children will be worrying."
The two of them walked slowly back the way they'd come, their shadows stretching long on the sand, now and then overlapping.
Chinese couples who marry midway through life rarely speak of love. Their affection is tucked into the meals made each day, into the cups of tea carried over when one of them is ill, into answering when the other's child calls them "Dad" or "Mom," into the time earned cent by cent and saved up together. There is nothing grand about it, only the steady, long-flowing burden and companionship.
And this, perhaps, is the most solid kind of deep devotion there is in this ordinary, fire-and-smoke human world.

========================================

Chapter 51 — A Chance Meeting in Phuket
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640298.html

A Chance Meeting in Phuket
On the afternoon of the third day, the team had a Thai massage on the schedule. Yao Yuan had booked out the entire hotel spa for the whole afternoon.
After her massage, as she walked toward the lounge in her robe, a vaguely familiar voice came from behind her.
"Yao Yuan?"
She turned. It was Zhao Yiming.
He was a shade darker than the last time she'd seen him at the forum, dressed in a linen shirt and khakis, his hair loose and casual—he looked like he was on holiday. When he saw her, his eyes lit up and he came straight over.
"So it really is you." Zhao Yiming smiled, frank and direct. "I thought you looked familiar just now—someone with that kind of presence is hard to forget."
"Boss Zhao." Yao Yuan smiled too. "What a coincidence."
"Hiding out for some peace and quiet." Zhao Yiming shrugged. "Year-end is wall-to-wall meetings—they give me a headache. I came out here to sit in the sun and, while I'm at it, think over the next round of algorithm tuning."
The two of them sat down on a soft couch in the lounge. A waiter brought over chilled young coconuts. Beyond the window lay a dense tropical garden, with the distant sound of waves faintly carrying over.
"The founder of Western World Technology still has to run the algorithms himself?" Yao Yuan stuck in a straw.
"The more you're the founder, the less you can afford to drift away from the code and the models." Zhao Yiming tilted his head to look at her, a focused curiosity in his eyes. "Especially with the project we're collaborating on right now—the 'Pretty Big One Emotional AI.' Polishing the underlying logic is something I have to do hands-on."
Yao Yuan raised an eyebrow. She knew Zhao Yiming's background: a physics undergrad, a computer science PhD, a postdoc spent in a Silicon Valley biology lab working at the intersection of neuroscience and computational modeling. At thirty, he'd come home with three top-journal papers and one crude algorithm, and used three million in angel funding to hammer out the code for the first generation of intelligent interaction chips. Now the company he'd founded, Western World Technology, was already a leader in brain-computer interfaces and affective computing.
"So even on vacation you're still thinking about our model?" she asked.
"More precisely, I'm thinking about people." Zhao Yiming's gaze turned serious. "The biggest challenge in emotional AI isn't the algorithm—it's modeling the sheer complexity of human nature. Emotions warp judgment, memory comes with its own beauty filter, decisions get swayed by hormones—and yet human 'intelligence' just happens to run on this buggy hardware. What our model needs to do isn't to eliminate these bugs, but to understand that they're part of the function itself."
The words tugged at something in Yao Yuan. She thought of those rationally calculated "emotional algorithms" in her own livestream room. In a sense, wasn't she also trying to use logic to discipline the "irrationality" in human nature?
"So what's your conclusion?" she asked.
"No conclusion yet." Zhao Yiming smiled, showing white teeth. "The tragedy of a scientist is that the more you know, the more you realize how little you know. But then again—"
He paused at her words, looked at Yao Yuan, and suddenly let out a very faint laugh. On his usually placid face, that smile looked a little strange, as if he'd just caught some crucial data point. "Interesting. Turning emotion itself into a tool, into parameters. That actually lines up perfectly with one of my latest cross-disciplinary ideas… We're trying to use multimodal data to model the emotional decision pathways of personality types like yours under extreme circumstances. Your case archive is an extremely valuable training sample."
"My data doesn't come cheap." Yao Yuan was half joking, but her eyes were serious.
"A worthwhile investment." Zhao Yiming looked at her and took off his sunglasses. Those eyes that always coolly analyzed the world looked especially deep against the blazing backdrop of sea and sky. "Because clean data can never simulate the chaos of the real world. Your data holds the most vivid—and most brutal—samples of human nature. And we both know," he leaned in slightly, his voice dropping a little, carrying an almost scholarly, focused fervor, "only by understanding both the most extreme order (your algorithm) and the most authentic chaos (the samples of human nature) can you possibly create something truly 'intelligent.' This excites me more than any paper."
This wasn't flirtation, or even ordinary flattery. It was a top-tier scientist offering the highest-grade admiration and invitation to explore to another complex system. It rested on months of professional collaboration, several intellectual sparring matches, and a deep mutual understanding of each other's fields.
Yao Yuan met his gaze without flinching. She could feel something familiar igniting in her chest, the kind of excitement that belonged to games and challenges—but here it was tinted by the sun, the sea breeze, and the pure inquisitiveness in his eyes, a different temperature than the one in a conference room.
"So," she said slowly, the corner of her lips curving into an equally challenging arc, "Boss Zhao, are you treating me as your next most important research subject?"
"No." Zhao Yiming shook his head, his answer unusually clear. "A subject is cold. You're hot, complex, a variable full of uncertainty. Studying you might make my model collapse all over again—but it might also…" He paused, searching for the right word. "Make it reach a genuine 'epiphany.' The risk is extremely high, but the payoff is incalculable. And I'm a scientist who likes high-payoff experiments."
The two of them fell into easy conversation. From AI ethics to power dynamics in intimate relationships, from the frontiers of brain science to the survival strategies of contemporary women. Yao Yuan found that talking with Zhao Yiming was a pleasure—he was smart without showing off, opinionated without being stubborn, and, more importantly, he understood her insights rooted in business and human nature, and could respond to them precisely from the angles of science and technology.
It was an intellectual resonance on the same frequency, something she hadn't felt in a long time.
When the massage was over, Yao Yuan invited Zhao Yiming to dinner that evening—to thank him for "bailing her out" at the forum last time. Zhao Yiming readily agreed.
Dinner was booked at another well-known seafood restaurant in the hotel. By the time Yao Yuan arrived with her family, Zhao Yiming was already there, having changed into a light blue shirt, looking fresh and refined.
"Mom, Dad, Yifan—this is Boss Zhao, Zhao Yiming, founder of Western World Technology and a brain science expert. We're collaborating on an emotional AI project." Yao Yuan made the introductions. "Boss Zhao, this is my mother, my Uncle Zhou, and my younger brother Zhou Yifan, who's studying medicine in Shanghai—his research also touches on neuroscience."
Zhao Yiming rose politely to greet them. Zhou Lufei and Zhang Fengxia sized him up, courteous but with an elder's caution. Zhou Yifan's eyes, however, lit up.
"Boss Zhao," Zhou Yifan couldn't help saying once they were seated, "I read your team's paper in that sub-journal of Cell, the one on the dynamic encoding of the prefrontal cortex–amygdala circuit in risk-based decision-making. The optogenetics combined with in vivo electrophysiology recording method you used was really illuminating for our understanding of the neural mechanisms behind anxiety disorders."
Zhao Yiming looked a little surprised, then smiled. "That was work from two years ago. So what's your research direction?"
"Early biomarkers for neurodegenerative diseases—mainly focused on the specific exosome carriers of tau protein and beta-amyloid in cerebrospinal fluid." Zhou Yifan's tone was earnest. "Lately we've been building a CSF exosome proteomic map of early-stage Alzheimer's patients, trying to find specific indicators that are more sensitive than the existing markers."
Zhao Yiming leaned forward, his gaze intent. "What separation method are you using? Differential centrifugation plus size-exclusion chromatography?"
"Right, but we added immunomagnetic bead sorting after the size-exclusion chromatography—using anti-L1CAM antibodies to enrich neuron-derived exosomes and cut down the background noise." Zhou Yifan was speaking faster now. "But the recovery rate is only around thirty percent—we're still optimizing it."
"You could try microfluidic chips combined with acoustic separation. Our lab developed a new platform last year that can get the recovery rate for neuronal exosomes up to sixty-five percent, with higher purity too." Zhao Yiming took out his phone. "I'll have my assistant send you the preprint. By the way, what mass spectrometry platform are you using for proteomics? An Orbitrap Fusion Lumos?"
"We're using an Exploris 480, but we're in talks to collaborate with a lab over at Fudan—we want to use their newly installed timsTOF Pro for 4D proteomics, to improve coverage depth."
The two of them dug deeper and deeper, technical jargon and English abbreviations flying back and forth. Zhou Lufei and Zhang Fengxia were completely lost, but seeing their son so rarely animated and glowing, they listened with smiles.
Yao Yuan ordered the food while she watched. Zhao Yiming was patient in conversation, with none of the lofty airs of an expert; on the contrary, his eyes would light up at a particular point and he'd press for details. That was the state only someone who genuinely loved research could be in.
"So you're already trying to use multi-omics data to predict the risk of mild cognitive impairment converting to Alzheimer's?" Zhao Yiming said thoughtfully after hearing out Zhou Yifan's account. "Interesting… We've recently been building a multimodal database of cognitive baselines for healthy populations. Maybe we could collaborate. A model is only robust if the sample size is big enough."
"Really?" Zhou Yifan's eyes lit up even more. "We've been worried our clinical sample size isn't big enough…"
Over the course of one dinner, the two scientists talked away happily, while the three onlookers ate to their satisfaction. The dishes Yao Yuan had ordered were authentic: the tom yum soup sour and spicy, the curry crab rich, the steamed lemon fish fresh and light. Zhao Yiming couldn't stop praising them.
Over dessert, Zhou Lufei and Zhang Fengxia said they wanted to take a stroll along the beach and left first. Zhou Yifan looked hesitantly at Yao Yuan.
"Stay and talk with Boss Zhao a while longer?" Yao Yuan could see the reluctance in her brother's eyes—a chance to talk in depth with an expert of this caliber was rare.
"Is that all right, Boss Zhao?" Zhou Yifan was a little embarrassed.
"Of course." Zhao Yiming smiled. "As it happens, I'd like to pick your brain on some of the practical details of ethics review for clinical samples."
The three of them moved to the open-air tea terrace outside the restaurant. The night had grown deep, the sea breeze gentle, and in the distance a band was playing jazz.
Zhou Yifan and Zhao Yiming talked for another half hour, until Yao Yuan lightly touched her brother's arm. "It's getting late—let Boss Zhao rest. We've got the boat trip tomorrow."
Only then did Zhou Yifan stop, exchanging contact details with Zhao Yiming. Zhao Yiming promised to invite him to tour the lab once they were back in Beijing.
On the way back to the villa, Zhou Yifan was unusually talkative. "Sis, Boss Zhao is incredible. He led a project independently at MIT at twenty, and now Western World's brain-computer interface technology is in the global top tier. But he doesn't put on any airs at all—his mind is so open…"
Yao Yuan listened, the corners of her lips curving slightly. "Enjoy talking with him?"
"Yeah!" Zhou Yifan nodded, then paused and tilted his head to look at her. "Is he… is he chasing you?"
Yao Yuan's steps didn't slow. "Why do you ask?"
"The way he looks at you." Zhou Yifan said it with a medical student's powers of observation. "And, well—tonight, even though he mostly talked shop with me, his attention was on you the whole time. The moment you moved, his eyes would follow."
Yao Yuan smiled, neither confirming nor denying it.
Back at the villa, Zhang Fengxia and Zhou Lufei were already in the living room. Seeing them return, Zhang Fengxia turned off the TV and asked, with feigned casualness, "That Boss Zhao—he seems quite learned."
"Mm, very impressive." Zhou Yifan was still riding his excitement.
"It's just…" Zhang Fengxia weighed her words. "He looks pretty young, too."
"Mom," Yao Yuan laughed helplessly, "if you've got something to say, just say it."
Zhang Fengxia coughed lightly. "I mean, he seems like a good sort—capable, without that showy air rich men tend to have. How did the two of you meet?"
Yao Yuan briefly recounted the chance meeting at the forum and the project collaboration. Zhou Lufei listened off to the side and finally said, "Intellectuals are good—clean. Better than those slippery old hands in the business world."
There was an edge of meaning to that. Yao Yuan just smiled and let it pass.
After washing up, she returned to her room and stood on the balcony, gazing at the pitch-black sea in the distance. Her phone buzzed again—still Yu Hao: "In Thailand? Saw the photos you posted. Have fun."
She opened her social feed and only then remembered she'd posted a few group shots of the team at the beach that afternoon.
She stared at the message for a few seconds, didn't reply, and locked the screen.
The night wind blew past, carrying faint music from far away. She thought of the conversation with Zhao Yiming that evening, of his eyes glowing when he talked about his research, of how he'd said "chaos is the truest sample of human nature."
And she thought of the gaze he'd occasionally let fall on her—focused, curious, carrying a pure appreciation.
Maybe Zhou Yifan was right.
Maybe it was time to allow a little "chaos" into her overly ordered life.
Only this time, she needed to think it through—what was it she actually wanted?
Another relationship built on value exchange?
Or… something closer to "the human" itself?
The waves rolled on, with no answer. Only the moonlight spilling quietly over the sea, like a shimmering road laid open toward the unknown.
Yao Yuan turned and went back into her room, closing the door.
She knew this Spring Festival was destined to be a little different. Not only because of the foreign sun and beaches, but because some new possibility was quietly sprouting in the warm sea breeze. And she needed to think it through—what kind of chaos she was ready to welcome, and what price she was willing to pay for it.
The night had grown deep, and the resort's clamor gradually settled. Yao Yuan lay in bed, but felt not the slightest bit sleepy.
Her phone screen lit up in the dark—another message from Yu Hao: "Phuket must be nice and warm right now. I remember you always hated the cold."
She stared at that line, fingertip hovering above the screen. Three seconds later, she pressed the screen dark.
Some roads, once walked, don't need to be retraced. Some people, once missed, are simply missed.

========================================

Chapter 52 — Zhao Yiming's Fastball
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640299.html

Zhao Yiming's Fastball

The fourth day was their day out at sea.

A double-deck yacht carried the team toward Emperor Island. The water shifted from emerald green to a deep, fathomless blue, like a spilled paint palette. The staff changed into swimsuits, snapping photos and horsing around on deck, young laughter spilling across the water.

Yao Yuan wore a long white linen dress and a wide-brimmed straw hat, leaning against the railing on the upper deck. The sea breeze brushed her cheek, carrying a salty, briny scent of freedom.

"Not going for a swim?" Zhao Yiming had appeared at her side without her noticing, holding out a glass of iced lemonade.

Today he wore a plain white T-shirt and beach shorts, sunglasses perched on his nose. He looked less like a stern academic and more like a man on vacation, relaxed and easy.

"I burn easily." Yao Yuan took the drink and smiled. "Besides, someone has to keep an eye on these kids so they don't get too wild."

"You always put yourself in the role of caretaker." Zhao Yiming leaned against the railing and turned his head to look at her. "Doesn't it wear you out?"

It was a blunt question. Yao Yuan was caught off guard for a moment, then shook her head. "I'm used to it."

"Habits can be changed." Zhao Yiming took off his sunglasses, his gaze serious. "Like a neural network. Give it enough stimulus and feedback, and it'll rewire its own connection paths."

The yacht dropped anchor near a coral reef. The staff jumped into the water to snorkel, schools of bright fish darting through the clear sea.

Zhou Yifan swam over and clung to the side of the boat. "Sis, the coral down here is gorgeous. You're not coming in to see it?"

"You all enjoy yourselves. I'm fine right here."

"I'll keep you company." Zhao Yiming picked up the thread naturally, then said to Zhou Yifan, "Go on. Be careful."

Zhou Yifan glanced between the two of them, flashed a knowing little smile, and turned to swim off.

The deck fell quiet, leaving only the soft slap of waves against the hull.

"Your brother is impressive," Zhao Yiming said. "Sharp mind, a real gift for research. Our lab needs young people exactly like him."

"He's been that way since he was little—once he decides something matters, he burrows right into it." There was an older sister's pride in her tone. "Sometimes I think he's perfectly suited to be a scientist—pure, focused."

"And you?" Zhao Yiming asked. "What do you think you are?"

The question made Yao Yuan fall silent for a moment.

"A businesswoman? An entrepreneur? Or…" She paused. "A walking contradiction—someone who tries to deconstruct emotion with reason, then feeds that reason back with emotion?"

"That's good self-awareness," Zhao Yiming said with a nod. "But don't you think that very act of deconstruction is itself a kind of defense mechanism?"

Yao Yuan turned to look at him.

"Analyzing everything down to the bone, calculating it all out, putting a price tag on it." Zhao Yiming's voice was soft, yet it dropped like a pebble into the lake of her heart. "That way you can't get hurt, can't lose control, can't… be disappointed."

The sea breeze turned suddenly cool.

Yao Yuan tightened her grip on the glass, beads of condensation sliding down between her fingers.

"Mr. Zhao," she said, her voice level, "we're just business partners. We don't really know each other outside of work—"

Zhao Yiming corrected her. "But some people's presence isn't measured by how well you know them."

He paused, then went on. "I supervised a lot of doctoral students at MIT, and I've met countless brilliant people. But you're one of the rare few who make me feel—I can't crack the algorithm running inside this person's head, but I very much want to try to understand it."

It was a candid thing to say, almost academically romantic.

In the distance came a cheer from the staff—someone had reeled in a big fish.

Yao Yuan watched the bustle and said quietly, "It took me a long time to build the order I have in my life now. Work, family, relationships… everything runs on schedule, controllable and predictable."

"And then?" Zhao Yiming asked. "Are you content?"

Content?

Yao Yuan thought of the nights she was still revising proposals at three in the morning, of the nights she carried the company alone through a funding winter, of the social functions she had to face with a smile when all she wanted was to turn and walk away.

She thought of the white hairs multiplying in her mother's hair, of her brother's eyes gone red from late nights in the lab, of her empty apartment where only the chime of the smart-home system answered her.

"A person shouldn't be too greedy," she said at last.

"Greed is the engine of human evolution," Zhao Yiming countered. "If our ancestors hadn't been greedy, they'd never have walked out of Africa. If scientists weren't greedy, they'd never explore the universe. If you weren't greedy, you'd never have grown your company to the scale it is today."

He turned to face her directly. "Yao Yuan, you let your team be greedy—you say you'll take them to Japan, to Europe. You let your family be greedy—you bring them here to Phuket to experience a life they've never had. So what about you? Do you let yourself be greedy?"

A seagull skimmed across the sky, letting out a bright, clear cry.

Yao Yuan didn't answer.

Because she didn't know the answer.

On the way back at dusk, the yacht headed for the dock in the setting sun. The sky was dyed gold and crimson, the clouds like burning silk.

The staff had worn themselves out and lay sprawled asleep across the deck. Zhou Lufei and Zhang Fengxia sat on the lower level, heads resting against each other, eyes closed in rest. Zhou Yifan was still discussing something with a few young researchers, sketching experimental steps in the air with his hands.

Yao Yuan stood alone at the bow, watching the sun sink inch by inch into the horizon.

"The sunset is beautiful."

Zhao Yiming's voice came from behind her. He handed her a thin blanket. "The wind's picking up."

Yao Yuan took it and wrapped it around her shoulders. The blanket still held the warmth of the sun.

"Thank you."

"You're welcome." Zhao Yiming stood beside her, also gazing out at the sea. "You know, in neuroscience, our perception of 'beauty' is really the end result of processing a series of neural signals. The visual cortex analyzes shape and color, the limbic system generates an emotional response, the prefrontal lobe assigns meaning… But no matter how you deconstruct it, when you actually stand before a scene like this, all the theory yields to the most primal sense of awe."

He tilted his head to look at her. "Just like—no matter how I analyze your behavioral patterns, when you stand here, gilded gold by the setting sun, the only thing I feel is: this moment is worth remembering."

It was too direct, almost nothing a thirty-nine-year-old scientist would say out loud.

Yao Yuan turned to look at him. The last light of the setting sun danced in his eyes—those eyes that always coolly analyzed the world were now brimming with undisguised admiration.

"Zhao Yiming," she said, calling him by his full name for the first time, "have you ever been in love?"

The question came out of nowhere. Zhao Yiming blinked, then laughed. "I have. At MIT, with a German girl. Three years. Later she went back to Munich to take over her family's business, and I came home to start my company, so we split."

"Any regrets?"

"At the time, yes. Now I think it was inevitable." He spoke frankly. "She wanted a stable, predictable life. I wanted uncertain exploration, full of challenges. Our underlying needs didn't match—like two incompatible operating systems. Force them to run together and they just crash."

"So what do you want now?" Yao Yuan asked.

Zhao Yiming was silent for a few seconds.

"Someone who can explore the unknown with me," he said. "Someone who isn't afraid of chaos—who can even create order out of chaos. Someone smart enough to understand my work, and emotional enough to make me forget the algorithms."

He turned toward her, the rippling light reflected in the eyes behind his lenses. There was no glib flirtation in them, only the focus and fervor of a scientist who has found the key variable. "I think I've found her."

The sea breeze suddenly gusted strong, sending Yao Yuan's long hair whipping wildly, the hem of her dress snapping in the wind. She raised a hand to hold down her straw hat—and that slightly hurried gesture, set against the grand backdrop of sunset, sea, and wind, revealed something vivid and real, something that belonged to being human.

"I'm thirty-six," her voice carried through the wind, as calm as if she were stating the parameters of a test sample. "Never married, but I've been through plenty. I've started a business, carried heavy burdens, been betrayed, and schemed against people to protect myself. My life is a complex system I've spent more than a decade writing and patching over and over—a stable version, but also… relatively closed off."

"Thirty-six is wonderful," Zhao Yiming answered at once, his tone a fact-based affirmation. "It means you're mature enough to know what you want and what you don't. Your system has been through a long enough 'stress test'—the core architecture is stable, the risk resistance is strong. Never having married is wonderful too—it means you've avoided having a certain set of default settings overwrite you, and you've kept your operating system independent and adaptable. Having had relationships is wonderful—it means you've been through the breaking and rebuilding of intimacy, so you know better how to cherish it. Having started a business is wonderful—it means you have the courage to begin from zero and the ability to bear the consequences."

He stepped forward, closing the distance between them until they could feel each other's breath.

"The betrayal, the scheming, the self-protection, the entrepreneurship you mentioned… all of those experiences are the most precious data. They're the truest part of your system logs—a record of your decision paths, your bug fixes, and your performance evolution under extreme conditions. It's precisely these 'non-standard' experiences—not the smooth, idealized data—that make you, as a system, one of a kind. And they make it possible for the 'human-emotion-decision' model I study to come close to the real thing."

"As for the operating logic," he smiled, "rewriting logic is my specialty. And the best systems always leave an interface open for upgrades."

The yacht's docking horn sounded, startling the seabirds off the pier into flight.

The staff began gathering their things, getting ready to disembark. Zhou Yifan waved at them from the upper deck. "Sis, Mr. Zhao, time to get off!"

Yao Yuan looked at Zhao Yiming for a long, long moment.

Then she smiled—a smile that held release, resignation, and a certain decision.

"Zhao Yiming," she said, "you're a very dangerous man."

"Why?"

"Because you know too well how to persuade people." Yao Yuan turned and walked toward the gangway. "And what I hate most is being persuaded."

Zhao Yiming followed behind her, amusement in his voice. "Then let me put it another way—I'm not persuading you. I'm inviting you. Inviting you to take part in an experiment, on the question: when two highly ordered systems meet, what chaos and what new life will they produce?"

Yao Yuan didn't look back, but her voice drifted off into the sea breeze.

"An experiment needs a design proposal, ethics review, and risk assessment."

"I'll submit them," Zhao Yiming said. "With the most rigorous scientific attitude."

Dinner was a beach barbecue. The staff cooked it themselves, the charcoal fire reddening one young face after another.

Yao Yuan sat in a beach chair a little apart, watching the lively scene. Her phone screen was lit—Yu Hao's fourth message: "Yuanyuan, can we talk?"

She still didn't reply.

Zhou Yifan came over carrying a plate of grilled shrimp and sat down beside her. "Sis, you and Mr. Zhao…"

"We're talking about the partnership." Yao Yuan took the shrimp and smoothly changed the subject. "You and he seem to get along well?"

"Yeah!" Zhou Yifan's eyes lit up. "He promised to take me on a tour of their lab once we're back in Jinshi. Sis, do you know what their brain-computer interface can already do? The new flexible electrodes they've developed barely trigger any immune response after implantation, and the signal stability is three times better than current technology! If this matures, Parkinson's, epilepsy, conditions like that…"

He talked on and on, pure passion on his face.

Yao Yuan listened quietly, and suddenly remembered how, many years ago, when she'd just started her own business, she too had talked about business models and market prospects with her eyes shining just like that.

That state of burning for something you love—she hadn't felt it in a very long time.

Over these years she'd become more and more like a precise machine: input a problem, output a solution, endlessly optimizing the algorithm—and forgetting that what had driven all of it in the first place was something more primal, more alive.

"Yifan," she cut her brother off, "do you love doing research? Truly love it?"

Zhou Yifan was startled, then nodded earnestly. "I do. It's hard, I fail all the time, but every time I make even a little progress, that feeling… it's like finally finding the handle of a door in the dark."

"Then keep at it." Yao Yuan patted his shoulder. "Your big sister's behind you."

"And you?" Zhou Yifan asked. "Sis, do you love what you do now?"

Love it?

Yao Yuan thought of the flashbulbs the day the company broke a hundred million in revenue, of the thrill when her livestream following crossed a million, of the grateful eyes of the staff whose fates she had changed.

She also thought of the countless sleepless nights, of the hollow politeness of dinner-table functions, of the compromises and calculations she'd had no choice but to make.

"Sometimes I love it. Sometimes…" She paused. "It's just responsibility."

Zhou Yifan looked at her and said suddenly, "Sis, you're too tired."

The words made her nose sting.

She turned her face away and pretended the smoke had gotten to her. "What are you talking about. Go tend to your shrimp."

Zhou Yifan didn't move. Instead he said softly, "Mom told me in secret yesterday that you haven't been sleeping well lately—you keep waking up in the middle of the night."

Yao Yuan was silent.

"Sis, there's no end to making money." Zhou Yifan mimicked the tone she'd used to win over Zhou Lufei. "You should… experience life a little too."

Experience life.

What a luxurious phrase.

Yao Yuan thought of Zhao Yiming's words: "You let everyone else be greedy—so what about you?"

Maybe it was time.

The last day of the trip was free time.

Yao Yuan went alone to a temple near the hotel. The ancient pagoda glowed gold in the sunlight, the air thick with the mingled scent of incense and frangipani.

She knelt before the Buddha, but didn't know what to pray for.

Pray for her career? The company was already on a steady track.

Pray for health? Her family was all safe and well.

Pray for love and marriage? She no longer believed in any so-called match made in heaven.

In the end, she simply knelt there quietly, listening to the crisp ring of the wind chime as the breeze passed through the eaves.

"Does something trouble you, benefactor?"

An aged voice spoke. Yao Yuan looked up and saw an old monk standing nearby, his face kind.

She rose and pressed her palms together. "Master."
