"Confusion is like clouds—they come, they go." The old monk smiled. "There's no need to cling to driving them away. Just watch them drift past."

"And if the clouds block the road?" Yao Yuan asked.

"Then wait," the old monk said. "Wait for the wind, wait for the clouds to scatter. Or—take a different road."

He handed her a string of sandalwood prayer beads. "There's a knot of worry between your brows, benefactor—you think too much. In all things of this world, seven parts effort, three parts letting fate take its course. Cling too tightly to control, and you'll only be trapped by it."

Yao Yuan took the beads, the scent of sandalwood seeping into her.

"Thank you, Master."

When she walked out of the temple, the sunlight was just right. She raised her hand and looked at the beads on her wrist, and suddenly she smiled.

Wait for the wind, wait for the clouds to scatter.

Or—take a different road.

On the flight home, Yao Yuan leaned against the window, watching the clouds spread out beneath her feet.

Zhou Lufei and Zhang Fengxia were already asleep, holding hands. Zhou Yifan was reading a paper with his headphones on. Most of the staff were catching up on sleep, and the cabin was quiet.

Zhao Yiming's WeChat had come in just before takeoff: "Once we're back in Jinshi, may I buy you a coffee? About that experiment design proposal."

She replied: "Sure."

A single simple word—yet it was like pushing open a door.

The plane passed through a cloud layer and hit a jolt of turbulence. Yao Yuan gripped the armrest and suddenly remembered how nervous she'd been the first time she ever flew, many years ago.

Back then she'd clutched her mother's hand tight, and her mother had said, "Don't be afraid. We just have to fly across it."

Yes. We just have to fly across it.

Clear skies or thunderstorms ahead—you only find out once you've flown across.

She closed her eyes and felt the slight weightlessness as the plane climbed.

This Spring Festival, the sun was bright and the sea breeze was warm.

And some new possibilities were quietly taking root, thirty thousand feet in the air.

========================================

Chapter 53 — Don't Bet on a Man's Will; Be Your Own Backstop
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640300.html

Spring comes late to the north, but when it arrives, it arrives with a roar.

After a few gusts of sharp early-spring wind, the pear trees lining the avenues seemed to ignite overnight. First a shy dot or two of pink, hidden among the grey-brown branches, and then it bloomed in sheets, in whole trees at once. That pink was unusual — not the boldness of peach blossom, not the paleness of cherry, but somewhere in between: pink shot through with white, white flushed with pink, with a crisp, reckless brilliance under the clear sunlight of early spring. The flowers crowded the branches, pressing against one another. From a distance they looked like puffs of pink-and-white cloud settling beside city streets that had been dry all winter; up close, each five-petaled blossom was delicate and open, the tender yellow stamens trembling as they reached out. When the wind passed, down came a soft rain of pink-and-white petals.

Yao Yuan's car drove slowly along the main road, awash in pear blossom. Now and then a petal stuck to the windshield, only to be carried off by the airflow. She watched that gorgeous, fleeting cloud of flowers and remembered someone once saying that spring in the north was a "rebirth" — watching bare branches inch into green, then bloom, as if anything could be torn down and started over. Not like the south, evergreen all year, where spring arrives lukewarm and blurred. These pear blossoms, blooming to their utmost as if they would wither tomorrow, did have a kind of resolute beauty.

Her phone buzzed in her palm, pulling her back from the flowers outside the window. The screen lit up.

It was Shuai Hongqiang: "Old Chen got back to Jinshi the day before yesterday. I've set something for three tomorrow afternoon, at the Guangu Hall. Are you free?"

The short message was like a pebble dropped into a heart that only looked calm, sending out ring after ring of invisible ripples.

The Mirror Pact. Time-travel. Those mystifying threads of cause and effect, those "anomalies" she still couldn't fully explain by logic but had genuinely lived through. Her throat tightened a little. Her fingertip hovered above the screen, paused three seconds, then pressed:

"Okay."

The reply came almost instantly: "Want me to come pick you up?"

"No need, I'll go on my own."

"Then… see you tomorrow."

The conversation ended. Yao Yuan stared at the darkened screen, her expressionless face faintly mirrored in the window. She thought of the day of the car crash at the ski resort after New Year's, when Shuai Hongqiang had brought up that brush with death at the "South Lake Cottage" — the complicated emotion that had flashed through his eyes, a mix of relief, guilt, and unease. At the time she had only vaguely felt something was off. Now that thread seemed to be slowly coming clear.

Some debts are invisible, like roots tangled deep in the early-spring earth — you never know when they'll push up through the seemingly solid ground.

Friday, 11 a.m.

The ring light snapped on, its cold white beam falling with the precision of a surgical lamp.

Yao Yuan sat in front of the camera. Today she wore a wine-red silk blouse, fitted to the body, with a deep V neckline that bared a stretch of collarbone and the faint hint of a curve. Over it was a black leather vest, sharply cut, the waistline pulled in extremely narrow. Her long hair was permed into loose, lazy waves, one side tucked behind her ear to show off an exaggerated geometric metal earring that refracted cold light with every movement. Her makeup was meticulous — brows arched high, eyeliner crisp, lips a rich vintage true-red that hit hard under the cold white light.

She adjusted her headset. The midnight-purple polish on her fingertips tapped the desk twice, a crisp sound that formed an odd duet with the faint rush of the Yellow River outside the window. Behind her was a full wall of floor-to-ceiling glass, the blinds half-closed now.

"Hello, my dears." She smiled and lifted her eyes to the camera. Her voice came through the professional sound card — steady, clear, carrying an unquestionable calm. "I'm Yao Yuan. Feelings aren't mysticism; they're a contest of human nature. Today, we keep dissecting."

"Little assistant, please connect our first call-in of the day."

【Call connected】

Caller (a woman's voice, 36, struggling to stay level, but exhaustion and resentment leaking through underneath): "Teacher Yuanyuan, good evening. I've followed you for a long time, and today I finally worked up the courage to call in. My situation… might be a little complicated. I'm 36 this year, with a five-year-old son. The child's father — I'm used to calling him Old Deng — he's 55."

(She paused, took a deep breath.)

"We met at a business event, when I was 27. He had a lot of energy, an expert in a certain field, very well connected. In the first three years together he really did introduce me to a lot of business, helped me get my footing. I'll admit, back then there was gratitude, and also… a reliance on that mature, steady quality of his, on his ability to pull me up. Later I got pregnant and had my son. I thought… this would make our relationship more solid, that he'd give us — me and the child — a clear future."

Yao Yuan (not interrupting, just leaning her body slightly forward, eyes sharp): "Mm. 'Thought.' And then? What does 'a clear future' mean? Marriage? Or an arrangement of assets?"

Caller (her voice beginning to tremble): "It's… both, I suppose. But he never brought up marriage. When I pressed him, he finally admitted he was indeed divorced, but… divorced without leaving home. His ex-wife and their 28-year-old son live in the U.S. year-round, but the house in China, the main social ties — they're all still bound up together. He said it was for the child, for face, for a lot of things he couldn't help."

"I put up with all of it. I told myself, at least we have a child; he'll have to plan for the child after all. But no! Teacher Yuanyuan, nothing! My son is five, and he's never mentioned putting any property in my son's name, never bought any insurance, never set up any trust. When I raise it, he says, 'You're still young, what's the hurry,' 'Isn't all my future going to be yours,' 'The business needs cash flow right now.' But he pays his American son's tuition, buys him a house, without even blinking! My son and I are like… like a project he keeps on the side, given just enough resources to keep going when needed, but never allowed onto the main road, let alone a share of the estate!"

(Her emotion surged, a sob entering her voice.)

"I really don't know what to do anymore! My youth is sunk into it, I've had the child, my career… though it started out leaning on him, it's hit a ceiling now, and the money I earn independently barely keeps my son and me living with some dignity. Leave him? I can't bring myself to, and I'm afraid of the child growing up without a father. Stay? I can't see any hope at all! I'm like someone clutching a straw that's about to snap, drifting in the water! What do I do, Teacher Yuanyuan?"

【Danmu explodes:】

[55 and divorced-but-not-gone?? Classic eating from both ends!]

[Sis, you got used for free AND threw in a kid on top!]

[What's the angle? Just those few business intros?]

[Buys his American son a house but won't lift a finger for the son in China — cold-blooded enough.]

[Isn't it obvious he's using you as a breeding tool plus temporary companion?]

Yao Yuan (after listening, she didn't speak right away. She leaned back into her chair, hands folded, that midnight-purple polish like the scales of some cold-blooded animal under the cold light. She laughed softly, a short laugh with no warmth):

"Fine. Done crying? Done resenting? Now, take all that mush in your head about 'can't bring myself to,' 'can't see any hope,' 'what do I do,' and pour it out. We are going to do the accounting."

She bit off each word, every one falling like a bead of ice.

"First, get clear on your 'project positioning' and the true nature of your 'client.'"

"You, and your son, in Old Deng's eyes, are not a 'family project' worthy of long-term investment and future planning — not even an important 'subsidiary.' You're merely a 'short-term strategic cooperation project' struck during a particular phase (his domestic emptiness, your need for resources) — he put up the connections and resources (business intros), you put up your youth, companionship, and reproductive value. The project ran three years; the core objectives (helping him sustain his domestic emotional life, you gaining start-up resources for your career) have already been met. The follow-up 'project maintenance' — raising the child, keeping you and your son in basic dignity — is the 'minimum operating cost' he pays to protect his own reputation and avoid trouble. As for the project's 'future planning' or 'asset injection'? Sorry, that clause was never written into this cooperation contract. His core assets and inheritance plan were signed over long ago to his 'lifelong strategic partner' — the ex-wife and that American son. You were never on his list of heirs. You don't even rate a footnote."

【Danmu: [Damn, business breakdown, right to the bone!] [Short-term strategic cooperation project… brutal but so true!]】

"Second, and the point you most need to see clearly —" Yao Yuan leaned forward, her gaze blazing, "about your son. A child born out of wedlock — what, in law, can he actually 'get,' and what can he 'not get.'"

She paused, her voice turning unusually clear and cool, as if reading out a statute:

"Under Article 1071 of the Civil Code, children born out of wedlock enjoy the same rights as children born in wedlock, and no organization or individual may harm or discriminate against them. That sounds lovely, but when it hits reality, you need to break it down."

"What your son 'can get':"

"1. The right to claim child support. As the biological father, Old Deng has a statutory duty to bear your son's support — living costs, education, medical care, and so on — until he turns 18 (or until he can support himself). This is your son's most basic right, and the only thing you can currently demand outright, by legal force."

"2. The right of inheritance. If Old Deng dies without leaving a valid will, then your son, as a first-order statutory heir (a child), enjoys equal inheritance rights with his child born in wedlock (that son in America). Note — it's 'equal,' not 'split evenly'; the exact share depends on the other heirs."

"3. The right to confirm paternity. If Old Deng denies the parent-child relationship, you can sue to demand a paternity test to confirm it. Once confirmed, the rights above arise automatically."

【Danmu: [So a child out of wedlock really does have inheritance rights!] [But only if there's no will…]】

"Now, here's the key point —" Yao Yuan's gaze turned sharp. "What your son 'very likely can't get':"

"1. Exclusion through testamentary inheritance. Under the Civil Code, testamentary inheritance takes priority over statutory inheritance. That means Old Deng can fully, and very likely will, by drawing up a valid will, designate the vast majority or even all of his property to his child born in wedlock (the American son), to his ex-wife, or to anyone else, thereby legally stripping your son of his share. At that point, 'isn't all my future going to be yours' will be a thoroughly worthless IOU."

"2. The limited protection of the 'reserved share.' The law does provide a 'reserved share' system — a will must reserve a necessary share for an heir who lacks the ability to work and has no source of income. But as long as your son is an adult able to support himself, he doesn't fall into this category. He's five now; by the age Old Deng might die (say seventy or eighty), he'll long since be grown. This 'safety net' is basically useless to your son."

"3. The risk of asset transfer. From 55 to death — twenty or thirty years — is plenty of time for a shrewd man, through gifts, sales, setting up trusts, and all manner of legal means, to transfer his assets clean away. By then, even with no will, the 'estate' left may amount to next to nothing."

"4. Emotional and identity recognition. The law can grant rights, but it can't grant a father's love, can't grant open acknowledgment, can't grant a whole family environment. These are things your son will never 'get' — as long as Old Deng doesn't want to give them."

Yao Yuan looked at the camera, as if she could see through the screen to that woman's bloodless face:

"So you're counting on 'waiting till he's old and getting a share of the estate through your son'?" She let out a cold laugh. "That's like counting on a dealer who can change the rules at any moment — even pick up the whole board and walk off — to pay out dividends according to the rules you imagined. Naive, and stupid."

【Danmu: [So true! One will and it's all over!] [Transferring assets is way too easy!] [The rights the law gives and what you can actually collect are two different things!]】

"Third, stop tallying 'sunk costs' and start taking stock of your 'remaining assets' and 'claimable rights.'"

"What you put in was: the nine golden years of your youth, from 27 to 36; the physical wear and the career-interruption risk of one childbirth; and unrealistic emotional and future expectations of this relationship. These have sunk; you can't fish them back. No matter how unwilling you are, they won't turn into your son's education fund. What you need to do now is seize the rights the law currently, clearly grants your son — rights that are enforceable."

"What's left in your hands now?"

"1. A five-year-old son he is legally required to acknowledge. (This is your most powerful card right now, and also your most fragile.)"

"2. The basic foundation of a career you can barely stand on your own. (It started out leaning on him, but it's yours now.)"

"3. You're only 36. Unmarried, legally a single mother. (Which means your choices on the marriage market — complicated though they are — aren't locked dead by a marriage certificate.)"

"4. Your last, and most important, clear-eyed read on his human nature."

Yao Yuan's gaze blazed, fixed on the camera, as if she could see through the screen to that woman's bloodless face. Her words sped up, each one like a driven nail:

"Based on all that, what I'm giving you is not 'what to do,' but a 'three-step stop-loss and rights-securing plan' you must execute immediately:"

"Step one: completely give up the fantasy of 'angling for the estate.' Lock your target onto 'securing your current statutory rights,' and shift your mindset to that of a 'single mother.'"

"From now on, stop treating him as a 'husband,' a 'father,' a 'support.' Treat him as 'a blood relative who is required to pay child support on a regular basis — a stranger.' Your one and only lawful claim on him is the child's support. Expect nothing else. Pull back all your emotional energy, your time, your effort, and pour it into yourself, into your son. The less you expect, the less he can control you with 'IOUs for the future.' The more independent you are, the more he may have to pay in real terms if he still wants to keep up appearances."

"Step two: use the 'biological son' card to sign an 'irrevocable security contract.'"

"Go talk to him — but don't cry, don't make a scene, don't talk feelings, don't talk about the future. Talk only about the child, about the law. In the coolest, most professional tone, tell him: 'Old Deng, the boy is five. He needs long-term education planning and health protection. I want nothing else from you, but as his father, you must give your son a basic, written guarantee. Under Article 1071 of the Civil Code, you have a duty of support. We need to sign an agreement, and the contents are simple:"

"1. Set up a growth fund/trust dedicated to your son, into which you inject a sum — in one payment or in installments (the amount must be specific, enough to make him wince but not impossible for him to produce) — earmarked for your son's education, medical care, and living up to age 18. This money is jointly supervised by you and me, but any use requires both signatures, to ensure it goes to the son."

"2. Buy your son full coverage of critical-illness insurance and savings-type insurance, with an irrevocable beneficiary."

"3. A legally notarized child-support payment agreement with a clearly stated amount (a specific figure can reference 20–30% of his income), pegged to your income growth.'"

"Stress this above all: this is the most basic statutory duty of a father, not charity. If he refuses or stalls even on this, you can 'gently' remind him that a father who is irresponsible, who has money yet won't pay child support, is not only morally deficient — you can also sue him in court to compel him to perform his duty. Drag it into court, and the damage to his face and social reputation will likely outweigh this bit of money by far. Remember, you are not begging him; you are notifying him to perform a statutory duty, and using an agreement to set that duty in stone. He wants face — so you build the 'responsible father' face-project for him, brick by brick, in cold hard cash."

"Step three: restart the main line of your life. Turn your career into the true 'estate' for you and your son."

"The days of relying on him for business intros are over. From now on, run your career as the one and only, the true 'estate' for you and your son's future. Analyze your existing business: which parts truly hold up on your own ability? Cut the ones that depend entirely on his face and have built nothing. Hone your professional skills to the limit; expand a client base and network that belong wholly to you. Even if you start from scratch, even if it's slower, let every step be solid, every step carry your name."

"At the same time, reassess your own worth on the marriage market. A 36-year-old woman with a child, but economically independent and clear-headed, still has a market in the right circles. But next time, remember: read the legal documents before the sweet talk; discuss the security clauses before the moonlight and roses. Your goal is not to find another 'Old Deng,' but a 'partner' who can fight shoulder to shoulder with you and share the future."

Yao Yuan summed up at the end, her voice categorical:

"So, back to your original question: what do I do?"

"The answer is: stop being the failed supplier waiting on a client to toss you the final payment of a project. Convert, right now, into the defender and CEO of your own and your son's rights. Demote Old Deng to your son's 'biological father and child-support payer' — beyond that, he has nothing to do with your feelings or your future."

"Your task is no longer to beg him for a vague, intangible 'family estate,' but to use every legal means to secure for your son the specific protections he's currently entitled to. And then, without a backward glance, go build the real 'family enterprise' that belongs to you and your son — one no one can take away."

"Wasting your youth on a dog isn't the terrible part. The terrible part is spending the years you have left waiting for that dog to turn around and toss you a meaty bone. Your future and your son's are not in his will — they're in every clear-eyed decision you make, every solid action you take, and in the rights the law sets down for your son in black and white. Now, hang up the phone, take out pen and paper, and start drafting that 'support security agreement.' That is the thing you and your son should truly be 'angling for.'"

【Danmu: [Pick up the legal weapon!] [Child support is the hard truth!] [Yao Yuan ran a law class today!]

[Explained the rights of children out of wedlock so clearly!]

[The rights the law gives — you still have to dare to claim them!]

[Yao Yuan is my internet clear-eyed mind plus legal counsel!]】

Yao Yuan didn't look at the flashing comments. She leaned back in her chair and rubbed her brow. Outside the window, the Yellow River shimmered in the early-spring sun, and the pear blossoms along the main road were at their most brilliant. She thought of Yu Hao, who had likewise used "the future" and "feelings" as bait, trying to get her to accept a relationship with no security.

Luckily, she'd woken early, and learned fast enough.

The law is the bottom line, a weapon — but it can never be the only thing you count on. True security always comes from one's own strength and clarity.

【Call disconnected】

There was no ripple on Yao Yuan's face, as if she had just finished a precise risk-assessment report. She faced the camera and said, lightly:

"Counting on someone else's conscience and someone else's will — a man's, especially — to secure your future, is worse than making yourself an unshakable asset right now. All right, connect the next one."

Chapter 54 — The "Businessman" Boyfriend
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640301.html

The "Businessman" Boyfriend
[Call connected]
The caller (female voice, 34, brisk and capable, though listen closely and you can catch a faint, well-hidden note of confusion and tension): "Teacher Yuanyuan, hello. I've followed you for a long time, and today I'd like to ask about something… maybe it's common, but it really troubles me. I'm 34 this year, and I've worked at the same company for six years. My boss is 40. When I joined I was an ordinary salesperson who knew nothing—he trained me up himself, gave me resources, taught me the ropes. Now my numbers are the best in the whole company."
(She speaks at a steady pace, like she's delivering a report.)
"About three years ago, we got together. It's a secret relationship—no one at the company knows. At work, I'm still his subordinate, drawing my salary and performance commission. In our private life… he treats me pretty well, we're together, but we rarely talk about the future. He's well off financially, but apart from the occasional gift, he's never spent a large sum on me. He's never brought up marriage, either."
(Her tone turns puzzled.)
"Now the company wants to expand into a very promising new line of business, and plans to spin it off into a separate company to run it. He's proposed… that I become the legal representative of this new company. He says he trusts my ability, and it'll make the business easier to run. But the equity structure of this new company, my share of the profits, my decision-making power in the future… none of these specifics has he ever discussed clearly. He just says, 'Let's get it going first, I won't shortchange you,' and 'Talking about this stuff between us would only hurt our feelings.' Teacher Yuanyuan, should I agree? I keep feeling something's off, but I can't put my finger on exactly what… If I don't agree, how do I raise it? And if I do, how do I protect myself?"

[Open your eyes, sisters—this is textbook workplace PUA + emotional blackmail!]
Yao Yuan didn't respond right away. She lowered her eyelids slightly, the midnight-purple polish on her fingertips catching a dark sheen under the cold light, as if she were weighing a high-risk merger. After a few seconds she lifted her gaze, eyes like an ice pick aimed at the camera, the corner of her mouth pulling into a curve without any warmth:
"Six years. From 28 to 34—the golden climb of a woman's career, and the golden window in the dating market, too. You packaged up these two most precious things and 'invested' them in the same man: your boss, and your secret lover."
Her voice was steady, yet carried a cold, scalpel-like precision.
"Now let's run a thorough 'value assessment' on your boss-slash-secret-lover, and see his true rate of return."
"One—career returns. He trained you, gave you opportunities, you became the breadwinner of the team. Sounds like a win-win? No. Look closely: what he pays is 'salary plus commission'—the standard price of an employment relationship. The value you create, the part that far exceeds that price, he writes off with two unquantifiable things: 'the debt of being trained' and 'a romantic relationship.' Real 'training' means giving you equity, giving you a platform for your career, making you a partner—not a subordinate forever. He hasn't."
"Two—emotional returns. A three-year secret affair, no talk of the future, no mention of marriage, limited material spending. What he pays is 'occasional companionship' and 'the boss's favor'—a low-cost form of emotional value. What you pay is 'a stable intimate relationship,' 'emotional fidelity,' and the option on a 'potential marriage.' In this trade, your premium is badly overpriced."
"Conclusion: over the past six years, in both career and feelings, you've been a 'severely undervalued, extremely low cash-flow' asset attached to him as the host. He is the only—and the biggest—winner in this investment."
[Comments: [Severely undervalued! Extremely low cash flow! Said it perfectly!] [The boss's favor = low-cost emotional value—brilliant!]
"'He trusts you, so he wants you to be the legal rep.'" She repeated the phrase, slow and clear. "'He's giving you an opportunity, so he wants you to take on unlimited liability.' 'I won't shortchange you,' so right now he won't talk about a single concrete condition."
She paused, her gaze cutting toward the camera like a scalpel:
"Girl, the man you're dealing with isn't an ordinary man—he's a top-tier businessman. What people like this do best is to use the lowest emotional cost and the art of language to lock down the deepest real interests—even your entire life."
[Comments: [Top-tier businessman! Sharp!] [Lowest cost, deepest stake—this is a killing blow!]**
"Come, let's take apart his 'business model.'"
Yao Yuan leaned forward, her leather vest giving off a faint creak. "Phase one: low-cost investment. Spend three years using 'the debt of training' and 'a secret affair'—inputs of almost zero economic cost—to lock down a capable, loyal subordinate-slash-lover who's emotionally dependent on him. The value you create far exceeds your salary, but he writes it off with 'feelings' and 'the future.'"
"Phase two: risk transfer and deep binding. The new business is risky? Make you the legal rep.
What is a legal representative? It's the company's legal 'face,' and when something goes wrong, the first-in-line 'shield'—even the 'scapegoat.' Tax problems, debt disputes, lawsuits—the legal rep is the first party held responsible."
"And what price did he pay to make you the legal rep? One feather-light word, 'trust,' and a blank check called 'I won't shortchange you,' deferred indefinitely. Equity? The profit split? Decision-making power? An exit mechanism? Risk protection? All of these—the core terms that make up a 'partnership agreement'—are completely blank!"
"This isn't 'entrusting you with an important role' at all—this is an invitation to a 'contract of indenture' that carries enormous risk and grants zero rights. What he wants you to do is use your entire personal credit and future legal exposure to guarantee the development of his new business, for free. If the business succeeds, how much you get is whatever he decides; if the business fails, or anything goes wrong, you're the one standing at the very front taking the blow. And you don't even have a piece of paper that says 'how much you'll be compensated after taking the blow.'"
"Phase three—and this is the point to be most wary of," Yao Yuan's eyes turned suddenly sharp, "he may take the initiative to suggest that you have a child for him."
[Comments: [???] [Holy crap! The more you think about it, the scarier it gets!] [It really could happen!]
"Why?" Yao Yuan answered her own question, her voice cruelly calm. "Because for a 40-year-old, calculating businessman, what could bind a woman more firmly—one who already takes the fall for his business and has feelings for him—than a 'shared child'? Once there's a child, you're even less likely to leave, even more willing to run this 'legal-rep company' for him body and soul, and later he'll even say, 'It's all for our child.' By then you won't just be his employee, lover, and legal rep—you'll be the mother of his child, too. Multiple identities locked tight, and you still hold no equity, share none of the real profits. You're just a piece on his vast chessboard, bound layer upon layer by feeling, duty, and blood—a… pawn."
She looked at the camera, as if she could see the woman on the other end of the line going pale:
"That you sense something's wrong right now—that's your instinct and your intelligence sounding the alarm. From start to finish, this man has been dressing up a thoroughly commercial exploitation in 'feelings.' What he wants isn't a partner; it's a 'multi-function tool' that can be used to the maximum and produced at minimal cost: the best employee, the secret lover, the risk shield, and a possible future childbearing tool. And all he has to pay is emotional value and a blank check."

"My answer is: absolutely do not accept the current terms! This isn't a question of whether to cooperate—it's a question of being roped, with 'feelings' and 'trust,' and pushed toward the edge of a cliff."

Which also means that, for the first time, you hold the leverage and the timing to formally negotiate over 'your true value' and 'the price of the risk.'"
"Now, what I'm giving you isn't a 'what to do'—it's a 'three-step negotiation and self-rescue plan' you must execute:"
"Step one: a revolution in mindset—from 'a subordinate-and-lover waiting to be placed' to 'a potential partner entering with core assets.'
Stop all the inner churn of 'will he shortchange me or not,' immediately. Your value isn't decided by his conscience—it's decided by your irreplaceable business ability, the resources you may control, and the legal-rep risk you're about to take on for him. This negotiation isn't begging for charity; it's bargaining over the terms of a business partnership."
"Step two: prepare your 'partnership offer'—turn every vague promise into ironclad legal text.
Go and talk to him, but it must be in a formal, private setting, in a fully businesslike tone. You can put it like this:
'Boss [X], I've thought carefully about the matter of being the new company's legal rep. Thank you for your trust—this is both a responsibility and a major personal risk for me. So that we can build this business steadily over the long term, there are some foundational terms of cooperation that need to be settled before the company is registered. That's how we look out for each other, too.'
Then lay out your list of conditions directly (be sure to consult a lawyer on the framework first):
1. Equity and profit-sharing: nail down your shareholding percentage in the new company (set according to your business contribution and the risk you bear—don't be shy), and write it into the company's articles of association. Nail down your right to dividends and your decision-making power (at minimum, a veto on major matters).
2. Compensation and incentives: beyond a base salary, you must agree on a clear, transparent profit-sharing scheme tied to performance.
3. Risk isolation: for the extra legal liability that may arise from serving as legal rep, the company or he personally must provide a corresponding guarantee or compensation mechanism (for example, signing a compensation agreement, buying directors' liability insurance, and so on).
4. Going public with the relationship, and security (optional but important): if you want to resolve the uncertainty in the relationship, this is the best moment. You can propose: 'So we can strive together more steadily, could we consider making the relationship public and making some plans for the future?' Watch his reaction. If he dodges, you should give up entirely on the 'emotional partnership' part and focus on negotiating the business side well."
"Step three: set your bottom line, and be fully prepared to 'walk if it falls apart.'
During the negotiation, watch his reactions. If he flies into a rage at your list of terms, brushes it off, or keeps making empty promises, then the answer is crystal clear: he has never seen you as an equal partner. To him you're just a useful, cheap 'tool' he can keep squeezing.
If the talks break down, your choice isn't to stay on in gloom—it's to launch 'Plan B' immediately:
1. Inventory your core abilities and resources: the business experience, client relationships, and industry insight you've built up over these six years are your greatest wealth.
2. Assess the possibility of going independent or switching jobs: quietly start updating your résumé, reach out to headhunters. With your ability, you can absolutely find a position on the market that respects your value, with clear rights, duties, and rewards—or, when the time is right, start your own business.
3. Emotional clean break: if you cut the cord on the career side, you must cut it on the emotional side at the same time. A relationship that has consumed six years of your golden prime and in the end won't even give you basic fairness—staying one second longer is a desecration of your own life."
Yao Yuan summed up at the last, every word like a gavel coming down:
"Girl—34, capable, experienced—what you need to do now isn't to agonize over 'does he love me, does he trust me.' It's to bravely, calmly, put a clear price on your six years of giving and your future risk.
That he's willing to sit down and talk proves you still have value as a partner. If he turns hostile, congratulations—at the smallest cost, you've seen a person clearly and set yourself free. Either way, you must go from a canary kept caged by 'the debt of training' and a 'secret affair' back into an eagle soaring across the sky. Your wings were never meant to decorate his cage."
"Remember, you are the only legal representative of your own life. Your credit, your body, your future—they can only serve your own choices and interests, not foot the bill for any 'businessman' who uses 'feelings' as bait while scheming at every step."
[Call time is up; the screen cuts off cleanly]
There wasn't a ripple on Yao Yuan's face, as if the conversation just past—one that flayed skin, took apart bone, and remade a soul—were nothing more than a routine case review in a day's ordinary work. She looked at the camera, her tone flat yet brimming with force:
"The most frightening thing in love isn't running into a bad person—it's running into a calculating 'businessman.' They cut you to bloody ribbons with a gentle blade, and still make you feel it's because you weren't good enough. Remember: any relationship that uses 'feelings,' 'trust,' or 'training' to substitute for clear rights and interests—whether in the workplace or in romance—is plunder of the weaker party. Your value must be priced by you, and you alone. That's all for tonight's livestream, beautiful babies. See you next time."
[Livestream ended]
The instant the streaming room went black, the comments were still rolling madly:
[A rude awakening!]
[This is exactly how you negotiate!]
[My wings aren't here to decorate his cage!]
[I won't sleep tonight—I need to take another hard look at my boyfriend…]
[Yao Yuan is my internet voice of clarity!]
[This lesson is worth a million!]
[Stand tall, sisters!]
Once again, with her cold, clear logic, Yao Yuan carved a path toward clarity and self-reliance for countless women trapped in similar emotional and workplace exploitation.

========================================

Chapter 55 — Spring Ends, the Pear Blossoms Fall, and the Whirlpool of Fate Stirs Again
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640302.html

Spring Ends, the Pear Blossoms Fall, and the Whirlpool of Fate Stirs Again

It was three in the afternoon. The chime that signaled the end of the livestream had sounded a while ago.

Yao Yuan shut down all her equipment, and the ring light dimmed. After such a stretch of intense, nonstop output, the exhaustion rolled in like a tide. She leaned back in her chair and closed her eyes to rest for a moment. The studio was quiet, with only the faint hum of the computer's cooling fan.

She stood and walked to the window, pushing it open a crack. At once the spring breeze poured in, carrying damp air and a faint fragrance. Far off, lining both sides of the main road, banks of pink-and-white mountain pear blossoms were in full bloom, looking under the afternoon sun like one grand, fleeting dream. Petals were swept up by the wind and scattered everywhere — beautiful, yes, but the bloom was too short. Just like certain human relationships: a dazzling opening, a hurried close.

In the stillness, her phone buzzed and the screen lit with a pale glow.

"I'm downstairs at your company."

It was Yu Hao.

Yao Yuan looked at the line of text, her gaze calm and unrippled. Ever since their last conversation about "a child and marriage" had ended badly, she hadn't answered a single one of his messages — not from then through the Spring Festival, and not since. She guessed he was probably waiting: waiting for her to waver, to reconsider, or to raise the offer herself. But she had simply vanished from his world entirely.

Now, he couldn't hold out any longer.

Fine. Some things have to be brought to a close.

She picked up her coat and her bag and walked out at a steady pace. As she passed down the corridor, her eyes drifted over that sea of mountain pear blossoms outside, so brilliant it almost seemed unreal.

The sun at three or four on a spring afternoon was just right, spilling warmly over her and chasing off the chill and fatigue she'd carried out of the studio. Yao Yuan stepped out of the elevator, crossed the empty, quiet lobby, and the glass doors slid open to either side.

Yu Hao leaned against his car in a well-cut black trench coat, no tie, the collar of his shirt left carelessly open. A cigarette was pinched between his fingers, unsmoked, the smoke curling away into the flower-scented spring breeze. When he saw her come out, he raised his eyes to her — deep, weighing, complicated. Behind him bloomed that blaze of mountain pear blossoms, and the pink-and-white cloud of flowers made him look somehow forlorn, somehow out of place.

"Finished for the day?" he said. His voice was a little low and rough, with less of its usual easy command.

"Mm." Yao Yuan stopped a few steps in front of him, with no intention of coming closer. Her calm gaze swept past him and settled on the brilliant flowering trees at his back. "Something the matter?"

Yu Hao looked at her calm, unrippled face. Those eyes that were always lit with a smile or with shrewd calculation now reflected her distance clearly — and a trace of something that might have been his imagination: a cool, detached appreciation as she gazed at the spring blossoms. The certainty he'd felt about all this suddenly began to waver. He'd assumed that if he let her stew for a while and then laid down heavyweight chips like "a child" and "marriage," she'd have to give some kind of response. Even if she didn't agree right away, there'd be discussion, bargaining. But there was nothing. It was as if she hadn't received those signals at all — as if she'd cut the line completely.

This feeling of being totally beyond his control made him deeply uncomfortable, even — there was a faint, indistinct unease, and anger.

"Find somewhere to sit?" He flicked away the cigarette butt and ground it out with the toe of his shoe, the motion a little too forceful.

"Let's just talk here." Yao Yuan glanced around. The little garden at the foot of the building had a few benches, and at this hour of the afternoon there was hardly anyone about — only the rustle of wind through the pear trees and a faint scent of flowers. "I have something at four."

Yu Hao stared at her for a few seconds, then suddenly laughed, the smile tinged with self-mockery and a probing edge, and a tension that wasn't quite hidden. "Yao Yuan, you don't even have time for a cup of coffee with me anymore? Have things between us really gotten this distant?"

"You're joking, Boss Yu." Yao Yuan curved her lips too, though the smile didn't reach her eyes, and brought her gaze back to his face. "We're each just busy in our own way. If there's something on your mind, just say it."

"Each busy in our own way..." Yu Hao chewed over the words, his gaze deepening. He took a step forward, closing the distance between them, and lowered his voice. "Is it down to 'Boss Yu' and polite formalities between us now? That thing I brought up last time — have you thought it over?"

"Which thing?" Yao Yuan asked, and the puzzlement in her tone was genuine, as if that unpleasant conversation had long since been forgotten in some corner of her mind.

Yu Hao choked, nearly losing his composure, the suppressed anger flaring up again. "The child. And... us."

"Oh." Realization dawned, and she gave a slight shake of her head, her tone as flat as if she were discussing when the mountain pear blossoms outside would fade. "Yu Hao, I thought I'd already made my position perfectly clear last time. I don't want a child born out of wedlock, one who might well spend years with his father absent — at least not now, and on that I'm as certain as you are that you don't want marriage. As for us..."

She paused, lifted her eyes, and looked straight into his — clear all the way down, no resentment, no lingering attachment, only a level, rational calm. "We've always been very good 'partners' — pleasant to work with, each taking what we need. Isn't that good enough? Why does it have to change?"

"Each taking what we need..." Yu Hao repeated, his gaze sharpening, his tone turning a few degrees colder. "You think what we have now is still just 'each taking what we need'? Yao Yuan, you know perfectly well how I've treated you. I thought what was between us was at least more than 'partners.'"

"Then what is it?" Yao Yuan countered, her tone still calm, even carrying a note of probing curiosity. "Love? Or have you finally decided that I'm 'suitable' to take home and make into a respectable Mrs. Yu, who'll conveniently bear you an heir?"

It was too blunt, edged even with a sharp, all-seeing scorn. Yu Hao's expression shifted, his jaw tightening. "Yao Yuan! Do you really have to put it so harshly?"

"Is it harsh?" Yao Yuan tilted her head slightly, her face carrying a look of genuine, earnest thought. "I'm only stating one possibility. You never used to mention marriage, never mentioned children. Now you suddenly do — is it because you've reached a certain age? Because your family's been pressing you? Or because you figure Yao Yuan's career is solid now, that I'm 'worthy' of being Yu Hao's wife, and that the child we'd have would be good 'value for money' in genes, in educational resources, in future connections?"

With every sentence, Yu Hao's face sank a little further, and the last bit of warmth drained from his eyes. He found, to his surprise, that he couldn't fully refute it. Those hidden calculations, the ones he hadn't wanted to examine even himself, had been laid out so nakedly in the sunlight, amid the scent of mountain pear blossoms — and it was suffocating.

"So this is how you've seen me all along?" His voice had gone completely cold, with the resentment of someone whose cover has been blown.

"No," Yao Yuan said, shaking her head, speaking very sincerely, and even smiling a little. "I used to think we were the same kind of person. Clear-eyed, rational, knowing the rules of the game and keeping to them. Happy together, dignified apart, binding no one and owing no one. That was the thing I admired about you. But now..."

Her smile faded. Her gaze swept over his tense face and went to that sea of flowers behind him, blooming at its peak as if it might wither in the very next instant, and her voice was soft. "It seems you've started wanting 'a little more.' There's nothing wrong with that — only... it isn't what I want. Like these mountain pear blossoms: gorgeous while they bloom, but I know the season is short, and I won't expect them to stay on the branch forever. To force them off and stick them in a vase only hastens their withering."

"What is it you want?" Yu Hao pressed, with a barely perceptible agitation he wasn't even aware of himself. "Yao Yuan, don't tell me a 'McKinsey of Feelings' is still waiting for some misty, formless love. Neither of us is twenty anymore. Be realistic!"

"That's right — neither of us is twenty anymore." Yao Yuan agreed softly, drawing her gaze back from the flowers to his face, clear and firm. "Which is exactly why we should be all the clearer about what works and what doesn't. I'll admit it: a woman at thirty-seven really is close to missing the prime window for having children, so I'll consider freezing my eggs. But right now, I don't want a child born out of wedlock, and I don't want to change the rhythm and center of gravity of my life for one. I don't have the energy to handle all those tangled family ties and clashing interests, and even less do I want my child born into a 'partnership' full of scheming and calculation. The way I am now is good."

She paused, and spoke each word with perfect clarity. "Yu Hao, let's part on good terms. Stay friends, or business partners — either is fine. But the 'partners' arrangement ends here. Thank you for this past year of... company."

The spring breeze gusted suddenly, lifting a rain of pink-and-white petals that drifted down between them, onto Yu Hao's shoulder, brushing past the ends of Yao Yuan's hair. Sunlight filtered through the swaying branches, casting a flickering play of light and shadow between them.

Yu Hao stood there, looking at the woman before him. She was still beautiful, still exquisite, still perfectly composed. But those eyes that had once held his reflection, that had carried admiration and even a faint, suggestive warmth, were now calm and unrippled, like the surface of a frozen lake, with nothing left beneath that he recognized as "tenderness" or "expectation." She had even used the mountain pear blossoms to describe what they had — beautiful, but brief, and she accepted that brevity with open eyes.

He suddenly understood, with absolute clarity, that he might truly have lost her. Not because she'd fallen in love with someone else, but because she had finally seen entirely through, and grown entirely weary of, the kind of relationship they'd had — one built on murky ground, full of scheming and calculation. Her wings had grown strong, her core had steadied; she no longer needed anyone to "shore her up" or "add to her luster," and she was no longer willing to pay any "partnership" emotion and future beyond its limits.

A mix of intense loss, of unwillingness, of the anger of being underestimated — and a trace of something he wouldn't admit even to himself, a wistfulness at losing this "comfortable arrangement" — surged up in him. But he was Yu Hao, after all, and he soon crushed those churning emotions firmly down, hanging that composed, faintly playful smile back on his face. Only the smile didn't reach his eyes.

"Looks like I flattered myself, read the whole thing wrong." He shrugged, his tone turning light, as if the tense standoff a moment ago had never happened, and lifted a hand to brush a petal from his shoulder. "All right. Since Boss Yao's mind is made up, I respect your choice. If we can't be 'partners,' we can still be friends, right? When a good project comes along, remember to call me."

"Of course." Yao Yuan smiled too — a proper, impeccable smile, the standard expression of social courtesy. "Boss Yu will always be a preferred partner."

The two of them traded a smile, and the mood seemed to recover its old ease. But only they knew that something had snapped completely and could never be put back — like those mountain pear blossoms blown from the branch, which, once parted from it, could never be rejoined.

"Then I won't keep you from what's next." Yu Hao turned first and pulled open the car door, his movements as smooth and crisp as ever, only the line of his back, amid the petals swirling everywhere, showing a deliberately straightened, lonely austerity. "See you around."

"See you around." Yao Yuan stayed where she was and watched him get into the car. The black sedan pulled steadily out of the lot, rolling over the fallen blossoms, and merged into the stream of traffic beneath that brilliant sea of flowers along the main road, soon vanishing from sight.

The spring breeze was still mild and warm, thick with the scent of flowers. Yao Yuan let out a soft breath, as though she'd finally set down a long-carried, invisible burden, and all at once her whole body felt lighter. She looked down and saw that, at some point, her palm had caught a single whole petal — pink-and-white, soft and tender, still holding the warmth of the sun.

She loosened her hand gently, and the petal rose on the wind, spinning, and dissolved into that boundless rain of falling blossoms.

Her phone buzzed once in her pocket. She took it out and looked: it was a message from Zhao Yiming, just a single image — what looked like the cover page of a document, titled "A Preliminary Risk Assessment and Benefit Analysis White Paper on the 'System Interaction Protocol' (Draft)."

Beneath it ran a line of text: "The first draft is complete. I respectfully request your review, Boss Yao. Might I have the honor of reporting in person this coming Saturday at six in the evening, in the Bamboo-Rhyme room at 'Yunting'?"

Yao Yuan looked at her phone screen, then raised her eyes again to that sea of mountain pear blossoms outside, blooming at its peak as if it were burning up its own life in beauty. Without realizing it, the corners of her mouth lifted, and a very faint but genuine smile crossed her eyes, like a slight ripple over spring water.

She replied: "All right. See you Saturday."

Tomorrow she would go to see Old Chen and unravel the mystery of the Mirror Pact.

After that... perhaps she could go and hear out that "white paper," and see what kind of "interaction protocol" another supremely clever, highly ordered "system" would put on the table.

Spring had come, and the mountain pear blossoms had bloomed and faded.

But there is always something new that quietly sprouts amid the breaking and the farewells.

Then she turned and walked, light and resolute, toward the bench in the little garden before the office building. The spring sun spilled down just so, carrying the lingering faint scent of the mountain pear blossoms, mingled with the smell of new grass and waking earth. She sat down on the bench, tilted her face up slightly, and closed her eyes, letting the warmth seep through her eyelids and bloom an orange-red haze across her retinas.

A moment later she opened her eyes, and her gaze fell naturally on the office building's glass curtain wall before her, smooth and mirror-bright. The wall was clear as a lake's surface, reflecting her figure whole — a wine-red silk shirt, a black leather vest, slightly waved long hair, and a face that, in the spring sun, betrayed not the slightest hurry of the passing years.

She looked quietly at "her." Still young, still pretty, the sharpness at the corners of her eyes and brows softened by the sun, leaving only a refinement that could withstand close scrutiny. The medical technology of this age really could, so long as you had enough money and were willing to keep pouring it in, buy a stay of execution against time, stretching the state of youth into a smooth curve that declined ever so slowly. Wrinkle removal, fillers, lifts, whitening... the cosmetic procedures came in endless varieties, extending from the face down to the neck — she'd even heard of people who, to keep a "girlish hand," had hyaluronic acid injected into their hands at regular intervals. As for the more drastic surgeries — double-eyelid cuts, raised nose bridges, chin fillers, jaw-bone shaving to reshape the face — they had turned "trending beauty" into a template you could download and swap out at will, so long as you could pay the price and endure the pain and risk of the recovery period.

But Yao Yuan didn't follow that assembly line. She had her own aesthetic line in the sand: beauty had to be high-grade, had to keep a "self" at its base, and must never sink into a forgettable factory product. She did only the most top-tier procedures, the kind that maintained the status quo — like the upkeep of the most precise instrument, meant to fight gravity and natural metabolism rather than to remake her face. As for her figure, a height of one hundred sixty-eight centimeters and a weight kept year-round at fifty-four kilograms were the joint result of a scientific diet and regular light exercise — an absolute management of her own body, woven into the everyday. That wasn't thinness; it was a taut, sculpted uprightness.

Just as she gazed at that still-"pretty" reflection in the glass curtain wall, her thoughts drifting slightly —

The anomaly struck without warning.

Across the smooth, mirror-bright surface of the glass wall, with no sign at all, a ring of ripples spread out.

It was not optical distortion, not a trick of the reflected light. The ripples spread out from the center of her reflection — one ring, then another, faster and faster, more and more violently, as if it were not a solid physical surface but a churning sheet of water into which a stone had been dropped.

Then a familiar, irresistible pull surged out from the center of the ripples! The force was formless and without substance, yet it seized her consciousness brutally, like a giant hand clamping around her soul and yanking — hard!

"Ugh—!"

The world spun.

The warm spring sun before her, the glass curtain wall, her own reflection, the brilliant sea of mountain pear blossoms in the distance... every image was wrenched in an instant by that "hand" into chaotic, garish blocks and lines of color, whirling, dragging, deforming wildly. A violent vertigo, with a strong wave of nausea, rose in her throat, and her vision was swiftly swallowed by a churning darkness.

Here it comes again...

In the instant before her consciousness was dragged completely down into the abyss of darkness, that thought streaked clearly across Yao Yuan's mind.

No omen, no pattern — like a die cast carelessly by fate, and she, once more caught by that formless force, was flung into the unknown turbulence of time and space.

========================================

Chapter 56 — Time-Travel Back to Thirty-Three, in the Gobi, Where an Unfinished Destiny Hides Its Secrets
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640303.html

How long had passed, she couldn't say. Maybe an instant. Maybe a very long time.
Consciousness rose slowly out of the cold depths of darkness.
The first thing to return was the body's sense of "being there"—she was standing, something solid beneath her feet, and yet all around her there was nothing at all. Yao Yuan opened her eyes slowly.
Before her stretched an endless, flawless expanse of pure white.
There was no light source, yet the light was even and soft, filling every inch of space. There was no direction, yet gravity still held, letting her stand steady. She was back here again—this "transitional space" that sat between her several journeys through time, something she could neither understand nor control.
She steadied herself and wasted no time probing the physical makeup of all this white—experience from the previous times had taught her it was pointless. She simply stood there, quietly, and waited.
By the "rules," soon enough images out of the past would surface from within all this white. Those pictures, some sharp and some blurred, would drag her into a particular time and place, to face a particular moment—one she might have forgotten, or deliberately buried.
This time, what would it be?
The dazzling night skyline of the Bund in Shanghai, carrying the shame of being traded away and the cold death of her heart?
Something earlier—the scalding passion and pain of her tangled past with Shuai Hongqiang?
Or… something else?
She held her breath and fixed her gaze on the depths of that empty white.
As if answering her stare, the white space ahead began to ripple in that familiar way. Like a stone dropped into a still lake, rings of waves spread outward; the scene at the center twisted, then sharpened, and at last the empty white was replaced by an image utterly clear—yet completely unlike any personal, emotional scene Yao Yuan had been bracing for.
A boundless, desolate, vast northwestern Gobi.
The sky was that kind of blue scoured by wind and sand, so high and far it made the heart clench. The land was a parched ochre, jagged black stones scattered across it, and in the distance ran an unbroken line of hard-edged, bare mountains. And there, at the center of this seemingly unchanging wasteland, stood a colossal structure of striking futuristic design.
In the pure-white space, the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan narrowed her eyes slightly.
It was an enormous building of silver-gray, its form pared down to the absolute extreme of clean simplicity. It looked like no traditional data center—no extra ornament, no complicated structure, the whole of it a smooth, flattened oval, like a giant metal egg fallen from beyond the sky, half sunk into the solid earth, half exposed to the scorching air and the fierce ultraviolet light. Its surface was sheathed in some optical material that adapted itself to the environment, and under the harsh Gobi sun it refracted a calm, almost inorganic metallic sheen, striking a forceful contrast with the raw, rough surroundings. There was barely a human figure to be seen anywhere near it—only sparse conduits of special weather-resistant material that dipped faintly underground, and farther off, vast wind turbines and arrays of solar panels turning and glinting in silence under the sun, supplying this "metal egg" with a near-perpetual clean energy.
Yao Yuan's gaze swept over the power arrays deployed at scale in the distance, the pipelines built from special weather-resistant materials, and the building's own form—that "function above all cost" simplicity. This was no laboratory, no rough first draft of a startup. This was hardcore infrastructure already on the ground and ready to carry massive volumes of data and the running of complex models. Every inch of the design reeked of money being burned, and of the builder's unquestionable resolve.
Several black off-road vehicles, specially modified to suit Gobi terrain, were parked before the building's one conspicuous entrance corridor. The entrance was a long, narrow opening set into the side of the "metal egg," like a slit coolly appraising the world outside.
The car doors opened, and a group of people stepped out.
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's breath caught, almost imperceptibly.
The man at the front looked about thirty-five or thirty-six, dressed simply in khaki work trousers and a gray fleece jacket.
It was him—Zhao Yiming.
No glasses. His skin, after years of exposure to all kinds of light out in the field and in the lab, was a healthy wheat color, even carrying a touch of the roughness the Gobi sun leaves behind. His hair was shorter than it would later be, more careless. But those eyes hadn't changed—still, focused. Right now they were narrowed slightly, gazing out at this "fortress" he had built with his own hands, with the pure, absorbed attention of a builder examining his own work, and a trace of certainty in the face of the wilderness he had imposed order upon.
So this early, he had already begun to pour the foundations of his "Genesis Engine" in this wasteland. The realization sent a subtle ripple through Yao Yuan's mind.
Then a figure stepped down from the car behind, and the gaze of the Yao Yuan in the pure-white space froze completely.
It was herself—though she couldn't tell how old. Because the scene before her had never once appeared in anything she had lived through.
She wore a beautifully tailored cream-white trench coat over a light-gray cashmere sweater and matching trousers, with flat ankle boots easy to walk in, her long hair tied into a neat low ponytail. Her makeup was refined, but she looked a touch greener than the Yao Yuan of now. The merchant's sharpness and the bone-deep weariness of long battles had not yet fully settled into her brow; in their place was a clear-eyed purpose, a vivid hunger to know, and a… cautious, appraising look that belonged to an "investor."
She got out of the car, and the dry, hot Gobi wind instantly stirred the hem of her coat and her hair. She raised a hand to press down her hat, her eyes first sweeping quickly over the staggering structure before her—a flash of clear wonder in them—and then her gaze settled on the slightly younger Zhao Yiming up ahead.
"Mr. Zhao," the younger Yao Yuan said, stepping forward, hand outstretched, her voice still clear and steady against the empty Gobi and the wind. "I've heard a great deal about you. I'm Yao Yuan. I've come from Shanghai. The northwestern scenery the whole way here was… truly magnificent."
The young Zhao Yiming turned and shook her hand. His grip was dry and firm, his smile frank and direct, with the plainspokenness common to researchers who aren't much good at pleasantries. "You've had a hard trip, Ms. Yao. The conditions here are crude, but"—he half-turned, gesturing toward the silver-gray building—"the 'Genesis Engine' needs an environment like this. Absolute quiet, physical isolation, energy independence, and enough space and cooling capacity to process and train the massive parameters we're about to face. After you."
The group passed through rigorous identity verification and security checks and entered the building.
Yao Yuan—thirty-seven—watched in silence.
Unlike the desolate, sci-fi feel outside, the interior was the ultimate in "cleanliness" and "order." The vast main space rose to a startling height, the temperature held constant and low, and in her ears was a deep hum, as if from the depths of the earth—the foundational chord of countless server clusters at work. There were no tangled cables in sight; all the infrastructure was sealed into uniform modular units, blinking with faint blue or pale green indicator lights, like the breathing of countless sleeping giant beasts. Technicians in anti-static suits moved through it all without a sound.
Zhao Yiming gave the tour himself, his pace of speech even, no boasting, only statement:
"The Zone A we're standing in now is the core compute array of the current 'Genesis Engine' version 1.0, handling mainly the training of general artificial-intelligence models. Zone B, under construction next to it, is a dedicated cluster reserved for the next-generation 'large emotional-parameter model.' It needs higher parallel-compute efficiency and memory bandwidth. The initial hardware investment"—he paused, and named a figure measured in the tens of millions—"is only the base hardware."
"Our in-house liquid-cooling system improves energy efficiency by forty percent over traditional air cooling. We can get PUE—power usage effectiveness—below 1.1. But the cost of developing and deploying this system up front"—he named another figure—"is the price of that. The Gobi's dry, cold air is our free, natural cooling resource, but 'connecting' that resource into the system, the investment in the groundwork and the pipeline network"—he pointed to the conduits beneath their feet and faintly visible in the distance—"accounts for a considerable part of the total infrastructure cost."
"On data security, physical isolation is the first step. All data in and out passes through multiple layers of encryption and one-way gates. Core training data is generated, processed, and destroyed here on site—it never leaves. Beside us is an independent disaster-backup center, hidden inside the mountain. The cost of excavating and reinforcing that part"—he looked at the slightly younger Yao Yuan—"is no less than the aboveground building."
Yao Yuan—thirty-seven—listened to these figures laid out in that calm narration, running the numbers quickly in her mind. Infrastructure, custom hardware, in-house systems, energy support, security redundancy… every module was burning capital. Zhao Yiming never came out and said "flagship scale: starts at two hundred million," but the itemized numbers he kept tossing out, and the level of completion and singularity this building displayed—far beyond an ordinary data center—silently confirmed that threshold to entry. He wasn't pleading poverty. He was calmly displaying the weight of this project and its capacity to devour money—and he was screening, too. Screening for investors who truly understood its value and had the strength to walk the road alongside it.
In the image, the slightly younger Yao Yuan listened closely, and the questions she occasionally raised all cut to the heart of the technology and the cost. She no longer asked "how does an emotional model understand human nature." Instead she asked about "the expected return-on-investment cycle of the dedicated cluster," "the long-term maintenance cost and risk of the liquid-cooling system," "the response level of the disaster backup and the data-recovery time objective." Her questions showed she was assessing the project's feasibility, risk, and potential return through an investor's eyes.
During a break in the tour, in a relatively open curved corridor linking the different zones. One side of the corridor was a vast floor-to-ceiling window, the boundless Gobi beyond it; the other was a black wall, smooth as a mirror, dimly reflecting the figures of those who passed.
The slightly younger Yao Yuan's phone buzzed. She glanced at the caller ID, made an apologetic gesture to Zhao Yiming, and walked off toward the window to take the call. Her profile by the window—it seemed to be a phone call at a critical moment in some cross-border merger and acquisition. Her tone quickly turned decisive and incisive, her expression calm, nodding now and then, issuing a few crisp instructions. She was a different person entirely from the one who had stood listening quietly to the technical briefing moments before.
A few minutes later she hung up, the sharpness of business negotiation not yet fully faded from her face, and turned to head back to continue the tour.
And in the instant she turned—
Her gaze brushed, without meaning to, across the smooth mirror-black wall of the corridor.
The wall reflected her clearly: cream-white trench coat, neat ponytail, the face of a thirty-three-year-old woman in her prime, with a faint flush left by the Gobi sun.
But then, right beside that clear reflection—almost pressed against it—another figure surfaced and congealed without the slightest warning!
The same height and outline, the same brow and eyes familiar to the bone—but the air about her was different. Stiller, more transparent, as though all the fierceness and struggle had been internalized into bedrock at the foundation. In the depths of her eyes lay a profundity the present self could not yet fully understand, the depth that comes after living through far more stories, and a… faint weariness. She wore a wine-red silk blouse and a black leather vest, her long hair slightly waved—it was the future Yao Yuan! Watching her present self quietly, through the mirror, her gaze complicated.
"!!!"
The younger Yao Yuan jerked to a stop, her body going rigid in an instant, her pupils contracting sharply. A chill mingled with a violent jolt of alarm shot up her spine to the crown of her head; her blood seemed to roar in her ears. She stared, fixed, at that eerie pair of "twin reflections" side by side in the mirror, the knuckles gripping her phone white with force. The fifth time. Even though this was already the fifth time she had run into her "future self" in a mirror this way—abruptly, defying every law of physics—the shock and absurdity of that moment had lost not the slightest edge. Especially now, standing inside a data-center that stood for the very frontier of human reason and technology, this surreal dislocation made her scalp crawl.
In the mirror, that older "she" in the wine-red blouse had eyes calm and unrippled—no astonishment, only a deep, almost compassionate understanding. As if this scene had long been marked on her own timeline; or as if this were exactly the coordinate she had come across the chaos to confirm.
(It's you…)
A clear, soundless recognition slammed into the younger Yao Yuan's mind. Not a question—a confirmation. This was the "voice from the future" that, three years ago, at the most crucial fork in her life, had appeared like a cold prophecy, and with cruel truth and a clear accounting of pros and cons had wrenched her—by force—off the track of a "caged canary to be admired and traded" and onto another road entirely.
The "she" in the mirror stirred slightly, as if drawing a soft breath, and then that familiar voice, only a little hoarser for the years settled into it, seemed to sound directly inside the younger Yao Yuan's mind, and also to come through the mirror:
"We meet again." The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan spoke, her tone strangely steady, carrying the faint feeling of a long-parted reunion, yet restrained and distant. "My past self. How old are you this year… thirty-something?"
The younger Yao Yuan forced herself out of that enormous shock, swiftly pressing the churning emotions down into her heart. She met those stiller eyes in the mirror, her voice surprisingly steady, even carrying a trace of the investor's cool appraisal: "Thirty-three. It's been exactly three years since I last 'heard' your voice."
She paused, as though reporting a major strategic transformation to the highest decision-maker, her words clear and concise:
"Three years ago, your 'warning' was right on time. I saw through the game early and didn't end up someone else's chip. I made use of that last bit of 'appreciation' Ding Shitong had—the part he hadn't yet had time to turn into a bargaining chip—and seized the resources and connections he could offer earlier, and harder. Duan Yanqing's 'I will have it no matter what' became, in my hands, a chip for equal negotiation—I took on some of the high-profit but borderline projects he held, and followed their lead to make a few short-term plays in the financial markets. No high-mindedness, no illusions. I only calculated input against return."
Her gaze was sharp, reflecting the still face of that other self in the mirror:
"In three years, I completed the primitive accumulation. Now I have capital, and I have choices. I didn't go become the 'Godmother of Feelings' you mentioned—the one who has to step into the spotlight and dissect human hearts. That's too conspicuous, too easy to make a target. I chose to be the capital beneath the surface. Coming here today, to assess Zhao Yiming's 'Genesis Engine' and his emotional model—this is what I consider the next track worth a heavy bet, based on the 'AI direction' you brought up back then. What I want isn't just financial return. It's a ticket into the future."
She finished and watched the "future" in the mirror in silence. No showing off—only the statement of a fact, a starkly different "fact" that had been born of "her" intervention.
In the mirror, the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan listened quietly, no surprise on her face, only a far-reaching stillness, as if seeing through time itself. Not until the thirty-three-year-old Yao Yuan finished did she give a slow, almost imperceptible nod.
"What you've done," the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan said, a very faint, almost admiring sigh in her voice, "is far better, and far faster, than what the 'me' on the original timeline did."
She looked at the sharpness and vivid ambition in the eyes of this self three years younger, edges not yet worn down by too much of the world, and went on:
"It wasn't until last year, at thirty-six, that I began to follow the AI track systematically, with the intent to combine it with my emotional-case data. That was when I first formally got in contact with Zhao Yiming, to work together on developing the 'Pretty Big One' Emotional AI model. You've laid your groundwork three full years before me, and met him three years earlier too." She paused, then added, "In this field, three years is enough to decide whether you lead the wave or chase it."
The thirty-three-year-old Yao Yuan caught that point in time keenly: "So this is… the fifth time you've appeared before me. You're a year older than when you last told me all this." She suddenly remembered a question she had never had the chance to ask: "I don't think I've ever asked—future 'me,' how old, exactly, are you now?"
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror seemed to pause, then the corner of her mouth curved into a very faint, almost self-mocking arc: "Thirty-seven. The previous four… 'meetings,' I was thirty-six every time. They happened in dense succession, almost as if compressed into a very short span." She didn't go deeper into what that "density" of time-travel meant, but beneath that calm tone there seemed to hide an enormous weariness.
The thirty-three-year-old Yao Yuan nodded, filing the information away. She didn't dwell on those unspoken hardships—time was short. She asked the most crucial question: "Then, with this appearance, do you have any advice for me? About this place"—her gaze swept over the cold walls around her, walls that stood for the very frontier of technology—"about Zhao Yiming, about AI, or… something else?"
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan was silent for a moment, her eyes growing deeper still, as if rapidly weighing what could be revealed against the ripples it might send through time and space. She spoke slowly, her voice lower now, with a note of caution:
"Keep believing in AI—that direction is right. It's one of the core variables of the next ten years and beyond. The depth and breadth of its penetration will go beyond what most people now can imagine. As for how far it can develop…" She shook her head. "The 'Pretty Big One' Emotional AI model I'm building with Zhao Yiming is still in a difficult stage of 'fusion.' The clash between human nature and algorithm is more complicated than we expected. This is a blue ocean full of opportunity but riddled with hidden reefs."
She seemed about to say something more, her gaze flickering slightly: "Also, in the venture-capital circle, if you ever meet someone called Yu—"
The words went unfinished.
The mirror surface suddenly heaved violently! Like water struck by a boulder, the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's figure twisted, blurred, and shattered in the spreading ripples!
"Wait—" The thirty-three-year-old Yao Yuan instinctively took half a step forward, reaching out, but touched only the cold, smooth glass.
The ripples settled.
In the mirror, only her own face remained—eyes slightly widened, faintly pale, carrying an astonishment and a searching that had not yet faded. The "she" in the wine-red blouse, come from three years on, had vanished without a trace, as if she had never appeared.
"…"
She stood frozen in place for a few seconds, then drew a deep, hard breath of the cold air of the data-center—constant in temperature and humidity, carrying that special clean smell—and forced her eyes shut. Her lashes cast fine shadows on her pale skin. When she opened them again, all the violent emotion in their depths had been forced down to the deepest place, leaving only a tempered, cold clarity and sharpness.
Something had already changed. Not shock, but a deeper comprehension and resolve. She turned slowly, her back to the mirror that had just played out that surreal scene, and looked toward the far end of the corridor—Zhao Yiming was finishing his conversation with the technical lead, and seemed about to turn and come find her.
She watched his profile, focused, carrying the particular stubbornness of a scientist, and her gaze became immensely complicated. Within that complexity was an assessment grounded in business logic, an appreciation for top-tier intellect, a hunger for unknown technology—and now, quietly mixed in, a trace of something only she knew: a heavy confirmation and a sense of destiny, come from the far shore of time.
The eternal wind of the Gobi seemed to pass through the solid walls and leave a soundless sob in her ear.
(The view cuts back to the real timeline—the little garden below Yao Yuan's office.)
Yao Yuan's body gave a faint, almost imperceptible shiver. Her long, dense lashes trembled a few times, then slowly lifted.
The spring-afternoon sun still poured warm onto her shoulders, the cloying-sweet scent of mountain-pear blossoms wreathing the tip of her nose, the blurred sound of traffic drifting from the distant street. She was still in the same posture, leaning back against the long bench, an arm draped over its back, as if she'd only dozed off for a moment in a break from work.
Only she knew that in that just-past "moment," her consciousness had been dragged roughly across time and space, meeting—soundlessly, heart-shakingly—with her self of three years ago, in the Gobi wind and the cold mirror of a data-center.
The violent drain that time-travel brought, rising from the depths of consciousness, ebbed slowly away like a tide, leaving behind a familiar weariness and a kind of exhaustion, as if she'd just done several rounds of high-intensity mental sparring back to back. She frowned faintly and raised a hand to press gently at her temple.
She picked up her phone; the screen lit.
16:22.
