From the time she'd sat down here, felt the glass curtain wall stir strangely, and slipped into vertigo, only about… half an hour had passed. The flow of real time seemed to have been twisted and compressed in the moment the time-travel occurred.
She leaned back against the bench and didn't move at once, letting the sun smooth over her skin while she digested all she had just "witnessed."
She had seen the "fruit" of the change. Her thirty-three-year-old self—capable, sharp, clear in her aims—standing before Zhao Yiming as an investor, talking about infrastructure investment on the order of hundreds of millions and the technological waves of the future. That was no pawn pushed along by fate; that was a player moving her own pieces. Fate had indeed been pried loose at that tiny node at thirty, shifted off course by a single "warning" from the future. Career path, the way wealth was accumulated, the context and identity in which she met a key figure (Zhao Yiming)—all of it was different.
But she had also seen new "causes" being quietly sown in that altered soil. The thirty-three-year-old "she" was still searching for a powerful collaborator (Zhao Yiming), still laying out a future full of risk (AI), still standing in the complex, precise arena of human nature and the calculation of interests. The abrupt cutting-off of the conversation in the mirror, that unfinished syllable "Yu"… seemed to declare, without a sound: escaping one known trap may be only stepping into another maze laid out differently; changing the starting point of a path does not mean the scenery at its end will be wholly different. The price of growth, the contest of human nature, the eternal pursuit of power and safety—they had only changed their rules, changed their face, and still followed her like a shadow.
The desolate wind of the Gobi mirage, and the cloying-sweet floral scent of this spring day before her, at this moment formed a strange, profound intertextuality in the depths of her consciousness.
She knew that the elusive power of the bronze mirror, the riddle of the "Mirror Pact" that hung over her head, was far from solved. The next "reversal"—to where she didn't know, and for reasons she didn't know—might soon catch her again and drag her into another sealed-away entanglement.
But in this moment, sitting in the spring light, she was only the observer who had just locked eyes—silently—with the "achievement of the past." The stone she had cast with her own hand onto the timeline had stirred up unforeseen ripples, and what those ripples reflected was both comfort and a deeper contemplation of "freedom" and "the foreordained."
The change was real. She had proven that a person is not a wholly passive vessel.
But the road ahead remained thick with fog—and, because of this very "change," had gained new, unknown variables.
The spring mountain-pear blossoms, blooming to their utmost, would in the end wither and fall, scattering into mud. Just like those passages of life that had been rewritten, and those that had not.
And the wind of the Gobi never ceases, passing through all the timelines rewritten and unrewritten, coldly telling of some ancient truth.
The game of chess between a person and fate—the moves, perhaps, can be played differently, but the game itself seems never to truly end. Every step a new position; every step, the heart in its mouth.

========================================

Chapter 57 — Seeking a Way to Break the Mirror Pact — Old Chen
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640304.html

When Shuai Hongqiang woke, daylight had already slipped through the gap in the heavy curtains, cutting a sharp blade of light across the floor.

Out of habit, he checked his phone first. Two emails sat on the screen. One was from the tender office of the city construction commission: "Invitation to Tender for the Construction and Operation of the Jinshi Public Fitness Center Project (Phase One)" — an official notice from the commission. Shuai Hongqiang stared at that line of subject text for a good ten-odd seconds. He sat down and read through the whole thing slowly, carefully. This wasn't a feeler, wasn't an informal sounding-out. It was in black and white, stamped with an official seal — a formal invitation to bid. His company, Hongchang Properties, was named among the seven units invited. Ranked toward the bottom, sure, but in the door all the same.

The other email was a weekly construction report from his project manager, with a few photos of the site attached. That old-town redevelopment support project — the one he'd discussed with Yao Yuan before the New Year and then taken on independently once the funding came through — now bristled with tower cranes, pile foundations driven deep, everything advancing on schedule, even a little smoother than expected. A few approval processes that had been stuck for ages had, inexplicably, gone through recently. He leaned back against the headboard and stared at those two photos for a long while. The sun glared off the phone screen, a little harsh on the eyes.

The phone buzzed again. A message from Lin Wan — she was at the supermarket, asking what he wanted for dinner, mentioning that Wenyao's math had improved on this month's exam and the teacher had singled him out for praise. A cute little smiley face followed.

His career was back on track, even faintly trending upward; home was steady, wife and son both doing well. After those years of running himself ragged, hitting wall after wall, lying awake until dawn — all of this should be called "sweetness after long bitterness," or "a small fullness in life."

He should be content, and he did feel a steady warmth.

But beneath that warmth there was always one spot left hanging — cold, hollow, sinking a little now and then.

He thought of Yao Yuan again.

This afternoon at four, they'd arranged to see Old Chen at the Guanguta Hall. About that mirror. About all that mystifying business of "time-travel" and "borrowed fortune." About the exhaustion that flickered through her eyes now and then, the kind she covered so perfectly.

Was there any way at all to undo this uncanny "Mirror Pact"?

Two-fifty in the afternoon, outside the Guanguta Hall.

Shuai Hongqiang had arrived early. He didn't go in. He leaned against a corridor pillar by the door and lit a cigarette. The antiques street was quiet in the afternoon; the sun lit the old wooden plaque reading "Guanguta Hall" until it gleamed, the years legible in its grain. Halfway through the cigarette, he remembered that winter eight years ago.

That auction. He and Yao Yuan had not long parted, both of them carrying wounds, carrying a hardness. When the auction catalogue arrived in his hands, he'd only meant to leaf through it idly — until he saw that "Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror." The print was blurry, but the distinctive shape, the intricate cloud-and-thunder pattern, and the line in the description — "date uncertain, unusual ornamentation, possibly with an occult background" — somehow lodged in his eye, and on instinct he felt Yao Yuan would love it.

As if driven by something, he went. Raised his paddle, bid, and in the end took it for a price that was neither high nor low — but at the time still pocket money to him. When he had the thing in hand, he sat alone in his car for a long time. The mirror was packed in a brocade box; to the touch it was strangely, bone-deep cold — not like metal, more like gripping a piece of ice drawn from a deep pool. In the dim light of the car, the Kui-dragon pattern on its back seemed to circulate a faint, dark sheen. Those beast-eyes, inlaid with silver dots, looked coldly back at you from any angle — indifferent, hollow, as if taking the measure of your soul.

A strange tremor went through his heart then, and he nearly turned it over to someone else on the spot. But in the end he carried it home and locked it deep in a cabinet.

Later, when his business hit rock bottom, when he was at the end of his rope, a single thought rose up at that moment — soundless, like an undercurrent beneath the water. That "maybe" became a phantom straw to clutch at.

"Give it a try... what if?"

That thought, faint to the point of being negligible, was like a pebble dropped into stagnant water. He hadn't thought hard at the time about what "give it a try" meant — only had the vague sense that the mirror might bring a "different" kind of aura. He wrapped it carefully and decided to give it to the person who, as he saw it then, had the "most energy" of anyone around him, the one "most likely to change her circumstances": Yao Yuan.

Now that he thought back, that unnatural coldness at his fingertips, that eerie answering gaze of the beast-eyes — perhaps they had all been warnings from some unseen quarter. And he, his mind clouded by his troubles, had ignored them.

When he gave her the mirror, that secret, barely-admitted delusion he carried — "maybe she can turn things around" — that was the starting point of the "pact."

Now he understood it all.

"Shuai Hongqiang."

A clear, cool voice cut into his recollection. He looked up.

Yao Yuan was stepping out of that sleek-lined red Bentley of hers. The afternoon sun was fierce; she raised a hand to shield her eyes, and a platinum square watch set with diamonds flashed a sharp glint at her wrist. Today she wore a true-red silk shirt, the collar carelessly open at two buttons, baring slender collarbones and a very fine diamond chain whose pendant vanished beneath the placket. Below, sharply cut black wide-leg trousers set off her tall, commanding figure. Her long hair was permed into loose big curls, worn down without fuss; her makeup was exquisite, her red lips striking, and a large pair of sunglasses hid most of her face — the kind of aggressive beauty that turns heads on the street and dominates a boardroom.

But Shuai Hongqiang saw it plainly. When she pushed the sunglasses up and tucked them carelessly into her shirt pocket, the faint bluish shadow beneath her eyes — even the best concealer hadn't fully hidden it. Her spirits looked passable, even "good," you might say, but that bone-deep exhaustion, forced down by will, was like the hairline crackle on porcelain: it couldn't fool anyone who knew.

"Yao Yuan." He stubbed out the cigarette he hadn't even lit and nodded. "Let's go in. Master Chen is waiting."

Inside the Guanguta Hall, tea-scent still curled in the air, the light dim, carrying the particular smell of aged timber and old books — an atmosphere that settled the mind.

Old Chen seemed to have anticipated their coming. Tea sat clear in three purple-clay cups. He gestured for them to sit, his gaze lingering a moment on Yao Yuan's bright but visibly tired face, sweeping over the costly diamond collarbone chain at her throat, before meeting Shuai Hongqiang's faintly anxious eyes. He let out a barely perceptible sigh.

"Old Chen, sorry to trouble you." Yao Yuan settled into the seat across from him, composed, a measured faint smile at her lips. First she pushed a brocade box gently toward the center of the tea table. "When I reached out earlier, you said we'd talk in detail come spring, so we've been waiting for you to return to Jinshi. The Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror — I've brought it today." Her tone was gentle, a junior's respect for an elder, but with no excess pleasantries; she went straight to the heart of it.

She paused, her gaze passing over Shuai Hongqiang beside her, then settling back on Old Chen's face. Her smile dimmed a little, revealing the candor of a pragmatist after answers. "I'll be honest with you. Just yesterday afternoon, I traveled back through time again, against my will."

Her account was steady and clear, with no deliberate dramatizing — as if she were reporting a project bottleneck that urgently needed solving. "Counting this one, it's the fifth time. Every time it ends, it feels much the same — utterly drained, mind hollowed out, like I've pulled several all-nighters in a row. The good news, for now, is that rest brings me back, and physical exams have found nothing organically wrong."

She leaned forward slightly, eyes fixed intently on Old Chen, a barely detectable urgency entering her voice — the urgency of someone seeking a solution — though she kept her cool framework. "But this can't go on. A completely passive, unpredictable experience like this has too much uncertainty; it's no long-term plan. That feeling of having no control, of your soul suddenly being yanked away — it's eerie and deeply unpleasant. We came today hoping you could help us get to the bottom of this whole thing, see if there's any thread to follow."

She showed no fragility, but didn't force toughness either — just a pragmatic stance, seeking professional help out of a real predicament. Shuai Hongqiang noticed that, from the moment they walked in, she had addressed him as "Shuai Hongqiang" — full name, polite and distant, locating him precisely on the scale of "someone with a shared past, now a business partner, forced by a strange affair into the same boat." Not a degree closer.

Old Chen nodded slightly. His gaze didn't go at once to the brocade box; instead it turned first to Shuai Hongqiang, those eyes that had seen all the world's affairs carrying a question. "Mr. Shuai, the matters that should have been explained — have you made them all clear to Miss Yao?"

Shuai Hongqiang's throat tightened. Under the double scrutiny of Yao Yuan's gaze — which had turned questioning, and naturally wary, at his words — and Old Chen's knowing eyes, his face went stiff, and with difficulty he shook his head, his voice dry. "There are... still some key parts I haven't told her."

The measured smile on Yao Yuan's face didn't change, but those beautiful eyes narrowed for an instant, and the scrutiny in her gaze at Shuai Hongqiang sharpened all at once — like a precise scanner, trying to parse from his murky expression and vague words just what kind of problem these hidden "key parts" really were. She didn't press immediately, but that instant of change in her eyes was enough to freeze the air by half a degree.

Old Chen took a slow sip of tea and set the cup down, his aged gaze carrying a clarity that saw through everything. "Since Miss Yao traveled only yesterday, has Mr. Shuai had any good news happen today?"

Shuai Hongqiang nodded with difficulty. So that formal, sealed-and-stamped invitation to tender for the Jinshi Public Fitness Center Project (Phase One) construction and operation, received first thing this morning in black and white — that was the "good fortune" this trip of Yao Yuan's had brought him.

"Miss Yao." Only now did Old Chen turn to her, his voice slow and clear. "Lately, have you often felt run-down, easily tired, sleeping lightly and dreaming a great deal — bizarre, lurid dreams, sometimes hard to tell from reality? At certain moments without any warning — gazing at a mirror's surface, say, or in the flicker of lamplight, or just when your mind suddenly relaxes — do you feel a brief dizziness, a blanking-out, even... flashes before your eyes of images or scenes that aren't where you are now, abnormally real?"

Yao Yuan's spine straightened, almost imperceptibly. She was silent for two seconds before she spoke, her voice still steady, but the deliberately maintained ease was gone. "Yes. Especially the latter. These past few months... it's gotten much more frequent. I thought it was just overwork, frayed nerves." She didn't mention what specifically she'd seen — that was the deepest secret in her heart.

"That isn't frayed nerves." Old Chen shook his head, his expression grave. "That is your 'spirit-mind,' drawn by that mirror, frequently and passively crossing the barrier of time and space."

He paused, as if weighing how to make these mystifying words more credible. "Mr. Shuai has surely told you this mirror can make a person 'travel back through time.' But the principle behind it — I'm afraid neither of you understands. At this mirror's core is no ordinary bronze, but a sealed strand of an exceedingly rare thing, refined by ancient occultists: the 'Primordial Purple Energy.' This energy is the wellspring from before heaven and earth were parted; it holds the seed of creation itself, and is the purest form of 'fortune.' Using this energy as its source, two ancient talismanic arts were inscribed on the mirror, each completing the other."

"The first: the 'Time-Tracing Draw.'" Old Chen's gaze locked onto Yao Yuan. "This art does not work on everyone. It resonates only with one special kind of fate — what the old texts call the 'Nine-Purple Departing-Fire' destiny. Those of this destiny are strong-willed, unwilling to be mediocre; their life paths tend to swing wide and dramatic, sharply up and down, and deep within they often carry obsession and powerful regret. When such a person, triggered by real circumstances, gives rise to an intense thought — especially extreme remorse, unwillingness, or the 'if only I could do it over' impulse toward some major past choice, some deep regret — their violently fluctuating spirit-mind resonates with the mirror's 'Time-Tracing Draw,' rousing the Primordial Purple Energy, and a strand of their consciousness is briefly dragged back to that particular 'past moment.'"

The hand Yao Yuan had resting on her knee curled slightly, fingertips folding into her palm. Nine-Purple Departing-Fire destiny... powerful regret... unwillingness... these words pricked at her heart like needles. All those things in Shanghai, Ding Shitong, Duan Yanqing, those "what ifs" that gnawed at her heart on the deep of the night... so this was the key.

"So I can 'trigger' it because of my... destiny? And my 'regret'?" she asked, her voice betraying no emotion.

"Yes, and not entirely." Old Chen said. "The destiny is the fuse; powerful regret is the catalyst. But the deeper cause may lie in the 'intensity' and 'uncertainty' of your own life energy. Your character, your experiences, your choices have shaped a fate full of tension and variables. This 'high-energy,' 'many-possibility' state is precisely the best 'fuel' for the 'Time-Tracing Draw' to operate. Each time you go back, prompt your past self, make a different choice, you are in fact disturbing your original fate-line, producing a kind of 'variable energy.'"

He shifted, looking at the ever-paler Shuai Hongqiang. "And this leads to the second talismanic art — the 'Fortune-Shifting Pivot.' This art transfers and grafts. The Primordial Purple Energy has the power to refine: it can refine the 'variable energy' produced by your disturbed fate into a purer, guidable 'fortune.' And the direction of that guidance is decided by the 'pact.'"

"Mr. Shuai." Old Chen's gaze was like fire. "Eight years ago, at the winter auction, when you took this mirror — did you think of Miss Yao? And later, when you gave her the mirror, your business floundering, was there a single moment — however faint, however fleeting — when you thought 'this mirror is no ordinary thing,' or 'Yao Yuan's circumstances are special,' that perhaps it might... bring some kind of different turnaround?"

Cold sweat beaded at Shuai Hongqiang's temple. Under Yao Yuan's suddenly knife-sharp gaze, there was no avoiding it; he could only force the sound out of his throat. "...Yes. At the time... I did have that delusion."

"The moment that thought was born, the 'pact' was formed." Old Chen spoke each word distinctly. "Mr. Shuai gave the mirror harboring the thought of 'borrowing her fortune to mend my own straits,' and so he made a 'fortune-borrowing' pact with the Primordial Energy in the mirror — became the target locked on by the 'Fortune-Shifting Pivot.' From then on, each time Miss Yao's regret triggered the 'Time-Tracing Draw,' each time she traced back and changed something, the 'fortune' refined from her disturbed fate flowed steadily along this 'fortune-borrowing pact' — straight to him."

"Miss Yao, you traveled yesterday afternoon and came back exhausted; Mr. Shuai had good news first thing this morning." Old Chen looked at Yao Yuan. "The timing, the cause and effect — they all line up."

The inner hall fell dead silent.

Even the tea-scent seemed to congeal. Only the faint sounds of the street outside threw the air inside into heavier relief.

Slowly, very slowly, Yao Yuan turned her head and looked at Shuai Hongqiang. There was no expression on her face — no shock, no anger, only a fathomless, ice-cold calm. But beneath that calm, magma seemed to surge. Her red lips parted, her voice low but every word clear, edged with ice:

"So when you told me at 'South Lake Lodge' that you'd 'nearly had an accident' at the ski slope, the relief and guilt in your eyes weren't just worry for me — it was because you knew perfectly well that your 'good fortune' was bought with my 'discomfort'?"

"Yao Yuan, I..." Shuai Hongqiang's face was paper-white, his lips trembling, a vast shame and helplessness flooding over him like a tide. He wanted to say "I didn't know it would be like this," wanted to say he'd found out later but hadn't dared speak, wanted to say how many times he'd regretted it... but under her cold, all-seeing gaze, every word turned as pale as paper ash.

"Miss Yao." Old Chen spoke up at the right moment, his voice carrying a heavy sigh. "This affair is uncanny, beyond ordinary reason. When Mr. Shuai first gave the mirror, he could hardly have foreseen such consequences. And this fact of 'borrowed fortune' — I myself only confirmed it later, deducing it again and again from the phenomena, piecing it together with what the surviving fragments record. Later, when he knew part of the truth and kept it from you, yes, there was self-interest — but there was also my warning. I feared that if you learned the truth of the borrowed fortune, the violent shock to your mind would only intensify the mirror-art's pull on your soul and its drain on you, making things worse."

He looked at Shuai Hongqiang, his gaze complicated. "Still, concealment is in the end the lesser course. Cause and effect are linked; laying it open today, though it hurts, is a necessary pain."

Then he looked at Yao Yuan again, his eyes earnest and worried. "More crucially — the harm this art does to you may go far beyond exhaustion. The fragments record: 'When the spirit-mind crosses too often, the root is easily harmed; when fortune shifts without constancy, bear its fruit with care.' Meaning: a person's consciousness crossing time and space frequently is a fundamental drain on the soul. Right now you're only mentally tired, and rest can ease it, because you're young, your vitality is strong, and the intervals are not yet at the limit. But over the long run — especially if the triggers grow more frequent... at best, scrambled memory, a mind not your own; at worst, soul-damage, consciousness scattered — the consequences are unthinkable!"

Yao Yuan's spine tensed for an imperceptible instant. The hand on her knee gripped tighter, the dark-purple lacquered nails nearly digging into her palm. She wasn't afraid of challenges, wasn't afraid of being schemed against, wasn't even afraid of getting hurt — but she could never tolerate becoming "unclear-headed," "irrational." That was more terrifying to her than being killed. Still, she did not lose her composure.

"Then... then what do we do?" Shuai Hongqiang's voice was hoarse, his eyes bloodshot. "Old Chen, I don't want this 'fortune'! How do I give it back to her? Or — I'll take the mirror back! Destroy it! Won't that cut it off?"

"Absolutely not!" Old Chen stopped him sharply. "The Mirror Pact is formed; cause and effect are deeply rooted. To force the mirror back or destroy it — if it happened just as Miss Yao's spirit-mind was in mid-trace, it would be like severing the rope of one crossing a river: her consciousness would surely be lost in the chaotic currents of time and space, never able to return! That would be the calamity of a soul scattered to oblivion!"

Shuai Hongqiang flinched as if doused with ice water, staggering back a step, slamming into the chair-back, his face ashen.

"For now, the two of you must understand two things." Old Chen's tone was heavy as a mountain. "First — Mr. Shuai, every bit of good fortune you've received is not rightfully yours. Like a tree without roots, it comes by an improper road. You cannot enjoy it in peace; you must use it with a sense of shame. From now on, keep this in mind at all times: act so as to keep a clear conscience, accumulate good deeds and store up virtue — perhaps you can slightly ease the backlash that will one day surely come. Heaven and earth keep their own measure. To harm others for one's own gain is, in the end, not the proper way; everything you've gained may be repaid, doubled, in the way you'd least wish to see."

"Second." He looked at Yao Yuan, his expression grave. "We must find a way to fully end this pact as soon as possible. Otherwise, with your two fates so uncannily bound, her soul daily worn down, your fortune and misfortune impossible to foresee — there can be no resolution."

"Is there a way?" Yao Yuan spoke up at last, her voice colder than before, and steadier — like steel tempered in fire.

Old Chen rose and went to the deepest part of the bookshelf, crowded with mottled old volumes. After a moment's searching he drew out a very thin, very old thread-bound booklet, carefully wrapped in oilcloth. He undid it with slow, gentle movements; the pages were yellowed and brittle, the edges damaged, the binding-thread already rotted away.

"This is a surviving fragment my school acquired in early years; it happens to record this kind of 'Mirror Pact' art." He opened it carefully to one page.

Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang fixed their eyes on it. On the yellowed page, drawn in fine, meticulous brushwork, was a bronze mirror — its ornamentation and form identical in every detail to the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror on the table! Beside the figure ran several lines of small vertical script, the ink faded but still faintly legible:

"The mirror pact: begun in sincerity, swollen by greed, ended in regret. Should regret arise, seek the descendant of the mirror-master; with blood as the medium, break the art and return to the true."

"The descendant of the mirror-master?" Yao Yuan seized on the key point sharply.

"That is, the descendant of the occultist who made this mirror and set down the 'Time-Tracing' and 'Fortune-Shifting' arts back then, or the heir to his teachings." Old Chen closed the booklet, shaking his head with a sigh. "But this book is damaged; several key pages are already lost. How to find one, how to confirm a 'descendant of the mirror-master,' whether 'with blood as the medium' refers figuratively to the price of sincerity or to an actual blood-pact rite, the specific steps to 'break the art' — none of it is recorded. A thousand years have passed, seas turned to mulberry fields; whether such a lineage still has an heir alive in the world is truly... a remote hope."

Hope seemed both near at hand and far beyond reach.

The inner hall sank into silence again, with only despair spreading soundlessly.

After a long while, Old Chen seemed to make up his mind. He turned and took something from a locked, old boxwood case beneath the curio shelf.

It was a jade pendant.

Not large — a little wider than a thumb, carved to follow the stone's natural shape, not regular. The jade was the most lustrous mutton-fat white, but through that congealed-fat whiteness ran several natural strands of pale purple, like drifting mist and rosy cloud, like the purple energy coming from the east. The pendant's surface had a thick, rich patina, its luster restrained, warm to the touch — a soothing warmth seeping slowly into the skin.

"This jade is named 'Heart-Guarding.'" Old Chen pushed it gently toward Yao Yuan, his gaze earnest. "It was given to my founding master long ago by a hidden Daoist master while he sought the Way at Mount Zhongnan. It's said that, sustained by the recitation of true Daoist words, it can settle the soul and steady the spirit, guard the clarity of the mind-altar, and ward off the intrusion of outside evils. My teacher passed it to me with his own hands, charging me not to use it lightly except at the most critical hour."

He gazed at Yao Yuan, his tone graver than ever. "Miss Yao, your soul is unstable from frequent tracing. Worn against your body, this jade may guard a strand of your original clarity when your consciousness is drawn away, easing the soul's shock and drain after you return. It can't cure the root — it can't stop the traveling — but it may hold off the harm for a time, buying you more time to find the way to break this."

Yao Yuan looked at the soul-settling old jade, its warm, lustrous glow flowing in the dim light, and did not reach for it at once. She raised her eyes and looked straight at Old Chen. "Old Chen, this jade is a treasure of your school, a thing of its lineage. My disaster with this mirror has its own cause and effect; it shouldn't drag in your school's greatest treasure."

"A jade has its own spirit; it chooses its own master." Old Chen shook his head, his tone brooking no refusal. "Your situation right now is exactly what calls for this thing's protection. If you came to harm through the Mirror Pact, how could I rest easy? If this jade by your side can help you steady your mind, that is its merit fulfilled. Take it, and don't refuse. Breaking the Mirror Pact is likely no work of a single day; you must first keep yourself whole."

Shuai Hongqiang too looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes full of pleading and guilt.

Yao Yuan held Old Chen's gaze a moment, then finally reached out her long fingers, lacquered dark purple, and gently picked up the soul-settling old jade. The pendant was warm in her hand, and that warmth seemed almost alive, rising thread by thread along her meridians, gently wrapping and soothing the spirit she'd kept strung tight and burning these past days with traveling, livestreaming, and scheming. She felt clearly that her temple, which had been faintly aching all along, seemed to ease a little.

"Thank you." She closed the pendant in her palm and gave Old Chen a slight nod — brief in words, but in earnest.

"See that you keep it well, wear it against your body, never let it leave you." Old Chen counseled, his expression still grave. "Once you two are back, Mr. Shuai should keep my words in mind and act only so as to keep a clear conscience. Miss Yao must watch for bodily signs; if anything is amiss, come tell me at once. I will leaf through other old texts as well and ask after a few reclusive old friends, to see if I can find any trace of the 'mirror-master' lineage or any clue to breaking the pact. This matter... can't be rushed, but it can't be put off either."

When they left the Guanguta Hall, the sun had slanted west, drawing their shadows out long and thin.

Shuai Hongqiang and Yao Yuan walked side by side down the gradually noisier antiques street, silence lying between them — colder, heavier than when they'd parted eight years ago. The soul-settling old jade was clenched in Yao Yuan's palm, its warm, lustrous heat the only real thing in that moment.

"Yao Yuan, I..." Shuai Hongqiang spoke up with difficulty, his voice rough.

"Shuai Hongqiang." Yao Yuan cut him off, stopped, and turned around. The setting sun scattered gold across her dense lashes, but couldn't reach into her deep eyes. She looked at him, her gaze calm, even composed — but beneath that composure was a frost that kept all comers a thousand miles off. "It's come to this. Remorse is useless, blame is useless. Old Chen is right — the urgent thing now is to find a way to break it."

She paused, her tone pure business-negotiation. "I'm keeping the mirror, but I'll be careful. The jade — I've accepted it. Your career: do it well, but don't forget Old Chen's warning — what you've gained may not be a blessing. Any clue about the 'mirror-master' or anything else, however remote — notify me the moment you have it."

With that, she no longer looked at him. She turned, and on her fine high heels walked at a steady pace toward where she'd parked — as if all the earth-shaking secrets she'd just heard hadn't truly shaken her at all. The retreating figure in the red shirt was like a searing flame in the sunset, and like a resolute boundary marker.

Shuai Hongqiang stood frozen where he was, watching her briskly pull open the car door, slide into the driver's seat, start the engine. The red Bentley merged smoothly into traffic and vanished around the corner ahead.

Dusk closed in; the antiques street's lanterns came on. He stood alone on the street as it grew lively, his palm cold. That faint "small fullness in life" born of his career's smooth turn had long since been ground to nothing by the heavy, near-suffocating guilt, dread, and deep fear of an unknown future.

The Mirror Pact was like a lock, locking two people together — one end tied to the peace of her soul, the other to his uncertain fortune. And the key, hidden somewhere in the folds of time — how was it to be found?

The road ahead was thick with fog, and every step felt like treading at the edge of an abyss.

========================================

Chapter 58 — Marriage Washes Off the Gloss; The Rest of Your Life Takes Root in Yourself
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640305.html

Marriage Washes Off the Gloss; The Rest of Your Life Takes Root in Yourself
"Good evening, everyone. Welcome to 'The Gentle Crossing.' I'm Lin Wan."
Tonight's stream felt different. The ring light had been dimmed a touch. Lin Wan wasn't wearing her usual silk blouse or knit sweater, but a loose, off-white linen robe, her short hair soft and tousled. There was no delicate teacup in front of her, only a glass of plain water. On the bookshelf behind her, the potted paulownia tree had been moved to the edge of the frame. In its place stood a short sprig of tender bamboo in a plain white porcelain vase, and a single white banksia rose just beginning to wilt, its petals curling faintly at the edges.
The comments scrolled by gently, tinged with curiosity and a quiet calm:
[Wanwan looks so plain and elegant tonight]
[This mood feels like she's about to tell a really deep story]
[The bamboo and the flower — do they mean something?]
"Tonight, we won't talk tactics, and we won't analyze power structures." Lin Wan's voice was even calmer than usual, like a stream slipping over pebbles late at night — unhurried, yet carrying its own quiet strength. "I want to talk with you about what marriage, that long and arduous journey, ultimately leaves behind in us — about the shape life reworks us into."
She paused for a moment, her gaze drifting tenderly over that sprig of green bamboo, then settling on the wilting flower.
"Let me start with a story from my own life." Lin Wan's voice was very soft, as if she were sharing a secret. "Brother Qiang has a business partner he's known for many years — they're close, so our two families spend a lot of time together. His wife — I've always called her Sister Man — just turned forty this year. In Sister Man's dressing room, one whole wall is hung with haute couture gowns for every occasion. In her jewelry box lie diamonds and jade that many women only dream of. On her vanity, everything is top-tier skincare — the kind you have to book an appointment for, none of it cheap. Just last month, she finished the latest anti-aging facial treatment. Her skin is firm and luminous, in such good condition it makes you envious."
"Whenever there's an important dinner, Sister Man slips into the newest dress, spritzes on a limited-edition perfume, and wears that flawless, perfectly measured smile, gracefully working the room beside her husband. She's the acknowledged 'perfect wife' of that circle — elegant, polished, as if time and worry had never touched her."
"So many people tell her, 'Sister Man, you're a real winner at life. At forty you're living more vividly than you did at twenty. You married well, you'll never have a care in the world — how enviable.' Sister Man always smiles, raises her glass with grace, and answers in a light, teasing tone, 'Is that so? Take a look at me, then — am I still like a young girl?'"
At this point Lin Wan let out a soft sigh — a sigh that held no judgment, only deep understanding.
"But perhaps only those who've gotten close enough can glimpse, through the cracks in that perfect smile, a hint of something else. It's the kind of homeless panic that quietly wells up only in the dead of night, when she's taken off all her makeup and faces a bedroom that's gorgeous yet empty."
Her tone grew calmer still, as if she were tracing the tangled threads behind an exquisite piece of embroidery:
"This is the fifteenth year of Sister Man's marriage. She has gotten, by the world's standards, much of what a woman might dream of in a marriage: a life of comfort beyond most people's reach, the freedom of never having to scramble for a living, more luxury goods than she could ever wear, an endless supply of the most cutting-edge care. She lives in a vast floor-through apartment overlooking the city's night skyline, has a driver when she goes out and a housekeeper for the chores — she's become the very template of what so many people imagine 'marrying well' to be."
"But what she lost — or rather, what was traded away and worn down in the process of getting all this — is like a soft splinter buried in her heart that won't come out. Not fatal, but always there, a reminder of some emptiness."
"She lost the independence that lets a person stand firm on her own. After they married, her husband, aching at the thought that she 'shouldn't have to work so hard,' coaxed her — in the name of love — to quit the design career she'd already built some modest success in. That had been work she loved, work that gave her a real sense of worth and an income of her own. From then on, her world gradually shrank, its center shifting to her husband, her children, and all the socializing that kept up the family's public image. Her emotions, her sense of worth, wound themselves ever more tightly around her husband's moods and the family's 'respectability.' When her husband was in a good mood, the house was sunny; when he was glum, she softened even her breathing."
"She lost a certain footing for speaking as an equal. After fifteen years together, a pattern had hardened into place. Her husband was used to making the decisions, used to her gentle compliance. She couldn't hold too many 'different' opinions, especially about 'matters out in the world.' Even when she felt wronged, most of the time she could only swallow it, wrapping her feelings up in that flawless social etiquette, keeping the shell of the marriage bright and gleaming. She didn't dare truly lose her temper, didn't dare show 'unseemly' fragility — didn't even dare, anymore, to be the real self she once was: a little willful, with clear likes and dislikes. She had become exactly the kind of 'wife' her husband and their social web most needed, while losing track of where she'd dropped the girl called 'herself.'"
"Her deepest loss, perhaps, was that solid inner steadiness and sense of belonging. The house is huge, lavish, but often so quiet that the only sound is her own footsteps. Her husband is very busy; even when he's home, they're often each absorbed in a phone or a computer, exchanging only what's necessary about the chores and the children. She fights gravity with the most expensive skincare and wraps her body in the most splendid clothes, but deep down there always lingers a thread of fear — fear of youth slipping away day by day, fear that this seemingly solid life, in truth wholly dependent on someone else, might collapse, fear of the self growing ever more hollow beneath that polished shell."
Lin Wan's gaze turned distant. "She came to possess material abundance most people can't imagine, yet without realizing it, she pawned off her independent soul, her right to be respected as an equal, and that reassurance of 'no matter what, I can always stand on my own.' Late at night, when all is quiet, she might sit alone in the spacious living room, looking at a home ablaze with light, and feel nothing but a vast loneliness. Outward brilliance can't fill the hollow inside. No amount of jewelry can buy a single conversation in which the soul is truly seen."
"She slowly came to understand that the ledger of gains and losses in a marriage is never simple addition and subtraction. She gained an easily won comfort, but lost the strength of character that can only be forged through struggle and growth, the baseline of being respected, and the anchor of her soul. Surrounded by every splendor, her heart was like rootless duckweed — feeling, inside that exquisite golden cage, a hidden and unending pain and bewilderment."
She paused and looked into the camera, her eyes full of compassion. "This is, perhaps, a not-unfamiliar portrait of many women trapped in similar circumstances: they've attained the 'completeness' society defines, yet through day after day of adapting, compromising, even talking themselves around, they've gradually ceded the boundaries of their dignity, the wholeness of their character, and that vivid, outspoken, living true self. They wear an ever-thicker 'perfect' mask, but their hearts have nowhere to rest — left only with the panic and bitterness they can't speak of, beneath the polished makeup."
"So," Lin Wan asked softly, as if asking the audience, and also asking herself, "tell me — what has fifteen years of marriage shaped Sister Man into? An artificial flower, never to wilt, carefully kept in an expensive vase? Or a strand of dodder that looks lush and leafy but really lives off the tree it clings to?"
The comments fell into a brief, thoughtful silence, then began to scroll:
[So real, it's like I'm watching my own aunt...]
[Gain and loss have always come bundled together.]
[An independent character is the priceless treasure.]
[Wanwan, you're making me want to cry — am I slowly turning into this?]
"Sister Man's story," Lin Wan paused, her gaze sweeping gently across the camera as if meeting the eyes of every listener, "or rather, the state she's in, moves us — even quietly aches in us — because it touches the very core of the struggle so many women face in marriage: the dense thicket of social expectation against the inner self that longs to grow."
The comments started sliding past:
[Honestly, it's like I'm seeing myself ten years from now. So scary.]
[That suffocating feeling is too real.]
[It's not just marriage — it's what society as a whole demands of women.]
She leaned slightly forward, her tone growing calmer still, as if sharing notes she'd long been turning over:
"I came to think later that the way marriage shapes a woman is truly like a many-edged blade — extremely complex. It can offer a harbor, but it can also become, without anyone meaning it to, a gentle set of shackles."
The comments:
[A many-edged blade! That metaphor is perfect!]
[Gentle shackles... I'm crying.]
[Finally someone says marriage isn't all good.]
"The challenges and the toll it brings are sometimes silent." Lin Wan tapped the desk lightly with her fingertips. "For instance, it can lead us, without realizing it, to outsource our worth — pinning it on whether our looks stay young enough, whether we keep house perfectly enough, whether we can forever be the 'presentable' partner. We pour enormous effort into upholding these standards, just like the shell Sister Man so carefully maintains with cosmetic procedures and luxury goods. But where the real 'I' inside should live grows blurry instead — and so we feel we have 'nowhere to dock.'"
The comments:
[I got that word 'outsource' instantly! I've always lived for other people's eyes!]
[Living myself to death just to earn a 'your wife's so great.']
[With no home inside, the bigger the house outside, the emptier it is.]
"For another, there's the invisible emotional labor. In a relationship, the woman is often the one who first senses a shift in mood, who actively smooths the atmosphere, who looks after everyone's feelings. This work has no KPI, no salary — day after day, yet it genuinely drains the spirit. Over time, it's easy to feel a deep exhaustion, to feel that the smile on your face is like a mask worn too long: it won't come off, but you can't breathe under it either."
The comments surged in an instant:
[Sobbing! That's me! The whole family's emotional trash can!]
[The mask! Every day before I step in the door after work I have to take a deep breath and put on the mask!]
[The phrase 'emotional labor' is so good — finally there's language for my exhaustion!]
[No pay, no thanks, only 'it's your job.']
"And again, there's the quiet shrinking of life's center of gravity. To run the household well, we may retreat bit by bit within the boundaries of home. Our old circle of friends, our own hobbies, the room we might have to stretch in our careers — all of it gives way before we know it. The world grows smaller, and so all our emotional needs and our sense of security naturally tie themselves more deeply to this one relationship and this one person. That 'panic' of fearing loss — its root usually lies right here."
The comments: [That's me. After marriage I had fewer and fewer friends, and the only topics left were my husband and kids.]
[I used to paint. Now I can't even find a brush.]
[The world shrinks, the fear grows — it really does happen like this.]
[Without your own social support network, you're just too fragile.]
"There's also the fact that, even amid material abundance, if the roots of your own development slowly wither inside the relationship, a deep unease about your ability to stand on your own surfaces. Take Sister Man — she has so much, but she's afraid too. Afraid that if the foundation of it all isn't something she tamped down herself, is it really steady? That unease has nothing to do with how much jewelry you own. It's about the deepest part of the heart — that footing of 'can I stand firm on my own?'"
The comments: [So true! Even when I have every right to ask for money, holding out my hand still leaves me feeling hollow inside.]
[Without financial independence, it's hard to be independent in character.]
[That's why girls absolutely must have their own careers and their own money!]
[Security is something only you can give yourself.]
Then her tone shifted, and a brighter, more hopeful strength entered her voice:
"But let's not forget — marriage can just as well bring nourishment and growth. A good relationship should be a profound alliance. It can offer a kind of emotional support and understanding found nowhere else in the world, becoming the most solid bedrock in our hearts when we face the storms outside. Those frictions, those adjustments — painful as they are — are also a precious training ground where we come to know ourselves, practice love, and learn to make room for another. Two people pulling together should be able to share the pressure, lift each other up, and make one plus one greater than two."
The comments: [Tearing up — I still long for a healthy intimate relationship.]
[Yes, yes, you can't write everyone off at once!]
[A good marriage really does heal both people.]
[That phrase 'training ground' — painful, but you grow.]
"So you see, marriage itself is rarely a black-or-white answer. It's more like a complex mixture, where positive nourishment and negative depletion are often woven together." Lin Wan's eyes grew utterly clear and firm. "And the key to telling it all apart, the way I see it, usually isn't the form of 'married' or 'not married,' but the very nature of the relationship we find ourselves in."
She spoke each word clearly:
"Is this relationship built on equality and respect? Are both people's independent characters cherished together, or is one side silently compromising and sacrificing?"
The comments scrolled quickly:
[A question for the soul!]
[Equality is so hard, but we have to chase it.]
[Always compromising, until I got used to it...]
"In this relationship, is the woman an 'object' to be endlessly examined and demanded of, or a 'subject' who can help write the future together? Are her voice, her needs, her dreams as an independent individual seen and valued — or ignored and set aside?"
The comments:
[Subject! I want to be a subject!]
[Ignored for so long, I'd even forgotten I had dreams too.]
[Always meeting his needs — what about mine?]
"Finally, does this relationship widen each other's worlds, making both people freer and richer? Or does it compress each other's territory, leaving everyone feeling a kind of hemmed-in exhaustion?"
The comments:
[Widen or compress? I'm going to lose sleep tonight.]
[My husband and I widen each other — it feels so good.]
[I think I've been getting compressed all along...]
Lin Wan paused for a long while, letting these questions sink into her listeners' hearts.
"Sister Man's plight — and that of so many women who suffer as she does — its root may lie right here." She summed up softly at the end, with deep compassion and understanding. "The environment or the relationship they're in has failed to nourish their strength as 'subjects.' Their sense of worth has to be confirmed again and again through outside approval — 'like a twenty-year-old,' 'has so much' — yet they can't draw real peace and strength from a stable, self-sufficient, vigorously growing core within. This isn't just the problem of one marriage. What it reflects is a far broader, deeper gaze that reduces a woman's worth to a single thing and turns her into an object."
The comments welled up in that moment:
[I've got goosebumps.]
[Wanwan, you see right through it.]
[We need more clear-eyed voices like this!]
[Starting today, I'm learning to be the subject of my own life.]
"And our learning to be clear-eyed, our learning to grow — perhaps it's all so that we can recognize that gaze, and within it, no matter what, guard the roots of our own inner bamboo, and reach with all our might toward our own sky."
The comments flooded the screen:
[Guard your own roots!]
[Grow toward your own sky!]
[Good night, Wanwan — thank you.]
[Another night of being healed and empowered.]
The music in the stream rose up gently.

========================================

Chapter 59 — From Flower to Bamboo: Becoming New in the Cold Water of Marriage
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640306.html

"And then, as it happens, there was a coincidence." Lin Wan's tone lifted a little, steering the topic toward herself. "Some time ago, an old friend I've known for years came to see me. We were drinking tea, talking about these eight years of my marriage. She looked at me and said, with sudden feeling, 'Wanwan, I think the reason you can see things so clearly now, the reason you live so wide awake, is that you've been standing on Qiang's shoulders. That's how you got to see a farther, truer world.'"
The scrolling comments went quiet for a moment, and then a few people sent symbols of agreement.
"At the time, I just smiled. I didn't argue. But I turned that sentence over in my mind for a long while afterward." Lin Wan's fingertips unconsciously brushed the smooth glass of her cup, her eyes drifting into memory. "And later I understood it — she was right, but not entirely right."
"For a lot of women, marriage really is like standing on a higher platform. The person who brings you up there might be your partner, or it might be some perspective the marriage itself grants you. He uses his own experience, his knowledge, even his scars, to mark out the potholes on the road ahead, to open windows you might never have reached on your own. When you're afraid of how hard life is, he uses his experience to tell you, 'Don't be scared, there's a way.' When some sudden joy goes to your head, he reminds you, 'Steady — this is only one stop.' Maybe one of his greatest social values is exactly this: he can lift his partner to his own level of understanding, so she can see the complicated face of the world."
"But," she said, the conversation turning, a supple strength entering her voice, "very few people will also tell you the other truth at the same time — the scenery you see from someone else's shoulders, and the scenery you take in after climbing to the summit yourself, one step at a time, are two completely different experiences. The first is a gift, a guiding hand, something to be grateful for. The second is what it means to truly own it — a strength and a vision carved into your very bones."
More resonance and reflection began to appear in the comments:
[Two kinds of scenery! So well put!]
[Being carried up, and climbing up yourself — the confidence is completely different!]
[Wanwan, you're talking about your own changes over these years, aren't you?]
"Let me share something from the first few years of my marriage." Lin Wan leaned back slightly, gentle folds gathering in her linen robe, her gaze growing distant. "It was around the fourth year. Qiang took me to a very important industry gathering. It was my first time stepping into that circle — a room full of accomplished entrepreneurs and investors. I wore what I thought was my most appropriate dress, tried hard to keep smiling, and listened to all that unfamiliar jargon and verbal fencing, feeling like an outsider who'd wandered into a banquet by mistake. Lost, out of place."
"On the way home, I felt a little low. But Qiang was happy. He said, 'See? This is how the real world works. You have to adapt.'"
"That night I couldn't sleep. Not because of him, but because of a deep sense of weightlessness — I realized that the 'world' I'd understood and lived in all along, and the 'world' he moved through with such ease, were separated by a wide, deep canyon. And our marriage was quietly pushing me toward the edge of that canyon, demanding that I find some way to cross it."
She took a sip of water and went on, her tone calm but charged with strength:
"The crossing was not graceful. I was like a primary-school student, learning from scratch how to read a financial statement, how to grasp business logic, learning to catch the useful information buried in those seemingly ordinary dinner-table conversations, practicing how to respond without showing fear, without ruining things. Sometimes Qiang would offer me a pointer or two, but more often he taught me simply by existing — letting me witness with my own eyes how people calculate before their own interests, feel the cold mercilessness of real-world rules, see clearly the cruel, efficient way resources and networks of connection move."
"And in that process, I heard the one sentence from him that stung me the most — and yet, in the end, woke me up completely." Lin Wan lowered her voice, but every word was crystal clear, striking at the hearts of her audience. "It was once when he'd been drinking. We were arguing over some small thing, and he said, with irritation and a kind of 'why won't you live up to your potential' tone, 'Sometimes I think showing you all this is like handling a lotus root. If you want it to taste crisp, you have to dig it out of the mud that looks so comfortable and soak it in icy, bone-chilling water. That little bit of pride in you, that streak in your eyes that can't stand a speck of filth — if you don't grind it away, you'll never get a feel for the real workings of this world. You'll never make anything of yourself.'"
The comments exploded in an instant, full of anger and ache:
[That's so hurtful! What gives him the right to say that!]
[A lotus root?? What a terrible metaphor!]
[He's way too controlling! Wanwan, how could you stand it!]
[This is PUA! Naked belittling!]
Lin Wan watched the comments race up the screen in calm silence. There was no grievance on her face, and no anger — only the clarity and peace of someone who has been through it.
"At the time, I didn't argue with anything," she said slowly. "But that 'lotus root' metaphor was like a thin, tough thorn, driven into the deepest part of my heart. It took me a long time to digest that sentence, to sort out the emotion in it, the motive, and the cruel truth wrapped so crudely inside — a truth that maybe even he wasn't aware of."
"Later, I slowly came to understand two things."
"First, it wasn't that he was controlling me. It was that the 'highly sensitive' me, the one chasing a flawless purity, had placed far too much of her attention on his judgment. I wanted so badly to be the 'right person' in his eyes, I was so afraid of being dirtied by the mud, that I ended up trapping myself."
"Second," her gaze grew clear, even touched with a wise compassion, "I have to admit that, in that stage, from a certain extremely practical, even crude angle, he was right. At least in that stage, he was right. Only later did I read it correctly — that seemingly cold 'control' of his, that way of pushing me again and again into the 'cold water' and the 'mud,' was actually him trying, in the way he knew best — a way full of rough survival wisdom — to make me 'stronger.' As a child he'd clawed his way out of the mud and the cold water himself. In his understanding, that was the only effective 'path to growth' there was."
The anger in the comments gradually settled, replaced by deep thought and complicated feeling:
[My god... this angle...]
[It's still hard to accept, but I think I can understand that clumsy, cruel kind of 'for your own good' now.]
[Wanwan's reflection is so profound.]
[Marriage really is a discipline mixed with pain.]
"In these eight years with Qiang, I've had countless moments where I felt stifled, wronged, where I wanted to turn and run." Lin Wan's voice was soft, yet it carried enormous weight. "But it was also these eight years that let me see, more and more clearly: this walled city of marriage — for most women who choose to enter it, maybe it was never simply about coming in to enjoy shelter and sweetness. It's more like the most realistic, the least forgiving training ground there is."
"Here, you're forced to train a pair of eyes that can pierce through the glamour and the sweet talk, to see the interwoven light and dark at the bottom of human nature. You have to learn to stay clear-headed in the cold water called 'life,' to anchor your own roots in the complicated 'mud of reality' without being completely drowned or assimilated. The hardest lesson of all is this: you have to find your way, step by step, to walk out a path of your own inside rules other people made."
The comments surged with powerful emotional resonance:
[A training ground... that word is so precise.]
[The process of cultivation really does hurt.]
[But it seems like only this way can you truly grow up, truly have strength.]
"This long process of 'cultivation,' from unaware to aware, took me nearly eight years." There was no resentment in Lin Wan's voice — only a stillness and strength forged together by time and experience. "Eight years of quiet observation, endurance, adaptation, digestion; eight years of learning to stay clear-headed under the sting of all that 'cold water,' of practicing how to root myself inside the 'mud.' And then, I don't know which day it started, I suddenly realized that the course this family was steering — in so many small but crucial places — had quietly, without anyone noticing, passed to me at the helm."
She reached out and, with the lightest touch of her fingertip, brushed the green, vigorous stalk of bamboo standing in the porcelain vase.
"That old friend said something else later: 'Wanwan, you were meant to be such a beautiful flower, and now you've been forced into growing into a stalk of bamboo.'"
Lin Wan smiled, and in that smile there was release, a pride that comes after weathering storms, and a faint, mature woman's blend of worldly wear and wisdom.
"I answered her: 'Yes — but bamboo is good too. Its roots strike deeper; they can drive through hard rock. Its frame is more supple; when wind and rain come, it knows how to bend. Maybe its vision is higher, too, because what it's reaching for is the wider expanse of the sky.'"
"From a flower to a stalk of bamboo. That's probably the deepest shaping my marriage gave me." Her voice was gentle and firm, holding an inner strength. "I lost the flower's delicacy, its quickness to wilt, and that dependence on being kept in a greenhouse. But I gained the bamboo's toughness, its suppleness, its unbroken will to keep growing. I no longer need a constant warm room; my roots have learned to find water on their own. I no longer fear the unpredictable wind and rain, because every swaying, every burden borne, only adds to the strength and resilience of my life."
"So, back to what my friend said at the very start." Lin Wan's gaze turned clear toward the camera, her eyes glinting with points of light bright and steady as stars. "Yes — at a certain stage, I really did stand on his shoulders to look at the world. For that, I'll always hold a complicated gratitude. But later, I stepped down from those shoulders, steadily, on my own."
"With the clarity I kept in the cold water over those eight years, and the strength of the roots I clawed out of the mud, I laid my own higher foundation, brick by brick. Now, we stand side by side, and what we see is the scenery you can only take in together — at the place where two separate climbs, two separate journeys, meet at a certain height. In that scenery there's the vastness he brought, and the depth I opened up with my own hands."
The background music of the livestream rose slowly — a minimal, ethereal piano melody, like raindrops falling on bamboo leaves, like the far-off, clear echo of wind passing through a bamboo grove.
"When a marriage gets this far, maybe there's no better state than this." Lin Wan's voice melted into the music, like a final summing-up, and like a tender word of parting. "We were once a flight of stairs for each other on some stretch of the way, and in the end we became the steady horizon in each other's lives. In his world you learned strength and clarity; in your presence, perhaps he slowly came to know softness, and another kind of strength. From a flower that needed cherishing, and a tree that thought it could shield from wind and rain, the two of you grew together into this bamboo grove — each upright and independent, yet roots deeply joined beneath the ground, sending out a harmonious, resilient echo through the storms of the years."
"That's all for tonight. If you're also struggling in the 'cold water' and the 'mud' of marriage, if you also feel yourself turning from a 'flower' into some other kind of plant, you're welcome to leave how you feel in the comments. The process of transformation is always lonely, but knowing someone else is walking a similar road is, in itself, a kind of strength."
"Good night. May we all, in this most cruel training ground called life, master our own gentle and powerful kung fu. See you next time."
The livestream picture slowly dimmed. The study returned to quiet, with only the bamboo stalk casting its lean, slender shadow under the light. And Lin Wan's words, like the wind among bamboo leaves, lingered long in the heart of every listener.
In the living room, Shuai Hongqiang had been standing at the floor-to-ceiling window ever since he came back from the Guangu Hall, looking out at the city lights beyond the glass. The cigarette in his hand had burned halfway down, and for a long time he hadn't taken a drag.
When Lin Wan came out, this was the back she saw. She didn't speak. She walked over, wrapped her arms gently around him from behind, and pressed her face against his broad back.
Shuai Hongqiang's body stiffened slightly, then relaxed. He raised a hand and laid it over hers where it circled his waist. They stood like that in silence, neither of them saying a word.
On the windowpane, their leaning figures were reflected, and beyond them that vast, complicated, real grown-up world.
After a long while, Shuai Hongqiang spoke, his voice hoarse, so low it was almost inaudible:
"...That thing about the lotus root... I'm sorry."
Behind him, Lin Wan shook her head gently.
"You don't have to be sorry." Her voice was muffled in his clothes, but it was clear. "Without that pool of cold water, I might... have stayed nothing but a flower that needs a vase, forever."
She paused, then held him a little tighter.
"And besides — aren't you, now... finally willing to soak yourself in the sun, and loosen up your own soil a little?"
Shuai Hongqiang's throat worked, and in the end he only gripped her hand more tightly.
The bamboo stalk stood quietly in the porcelain vase in the study. It knew it came from a patch of mud, and that only after soaking in cold water did it come to have its cool, clear bones. And in this moment, toward the light, toward a wider imagining, it was growing unseen, upward-reaching joints.

========================================

Chapter 60 — Zhao Yiming's Love White Paper
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640307.html

Zhao Yiming's Love White Paper
At ten to six on Saturday evening, Yao Yuan's car rolled slowly into the private lot of "Yunting."
It was a members-only club tucked away in an old garden on the west side of the city — no sign out front, reservations only. On this late-spring evening, the sky hung in that not-quite-dark in-between, the air carrying the faint mingled scent of new leaves and tuberose, the wind still sharp with the last bite of the cold season.
Tonight she had chosen a lilac silk-crepe gown that, in the dim golden glow, took on a hazy, refined grayed-violet, as if she had gathered the lingering trace of the last sunset onto herself. The dress was cut close to the body, the V-neck open just right, tracing the elegant line of her collarbone and throat. Over it she wore a knee-length cream belted trench, its lines crisp, lending her a certain dashing edge. The trench wasn't fully buttoned, left casually open to reveal the outline of the lilac dress beneath, setting off her slender figure. At her ears, a pair of violet jadeite teardrop earrings swayed softly. The purple was richer than the dress's, crystal-clear, the light turning in them with a restrained, sumptuous sheen. With her smallest movements they cast a faint violet shadow against the side of her neck, forming an exquisite gradation with the soft lilac of her whole ensemble.
Her makeup was vivid, her brows sharply defined, her lips painted a deep retro true-red that struck a dazzling contrast against all that lilac, lifting her color just right — and masking some of the fatigue these past days of time-travel and the Mirror Pact had left on her.
At her throat, the "heart-guarding" old jade hung on a fine platinum chain, worn against the skin just below the collarbone. The luminous white jade looked all the more warm and pure between the lilac silk and the cream trench, and the few wisps of pale purple mist-grain naturally suffused through it answered, miraculously, the violets of her clothes in a quiet echo.
The night wind passed, stirring her loosely waved hair and the hem of the trench. She drew the front of the coat a little tighter, and her fingers brushed by chance against the old jade at her chest. A gentle warmth kept flowing up from beneath her fingertips, like a silent barrier, letting her hold on to a strange inner composure even after the successive jumps through time and the shock of the Mirror Pact's truth. That reassuring warmth seemed to draw a quiet layer between her and both the lingering spring chill and the tangle of thoughts crowding her mind.
The Bamboo Rhyme room lay at the garden's deepest point. She followed the attendant along a winding bamboo path; the evening wind whispered through the grove, and the lanterns under the eaves washed the scene in a hushed, warm gold.
Zhao Yiming was already there.
He sat at the tea table by the window, brewing tea. Hearing her footsteps, he raised his head. Tonight he wore a pale blue Oxford-cloth shirt, sleeves rolled casually to the elbow, no tie — a touch more at ease than how he looked in the lab. Yet the steadiness and certainty of a man steering a top-tier tech company had not dimmed in the slightest.
"Boss Yao, right on time." He came toward her, took the thin trench from her hands quite naturally, and passed it to the attendant beside him. His gaze lingered on her for a moment, then politely moved away as he pulled out her chair. There was clear appreciation in that single glance, perfectly pure, not the kind that puts one off.
"When Boss Zhao invites me, I wouldn't dare be late." Yao Yuan smiled and took her seat. The room was classic "Yunting" — a long solid-wood table, white walls, a whole wall of floor-to-ceiling glass overlooking a carefully tended dry-landscape garden, a paper lantern hung beneath the eaves glowing warm against the thickening night. Besides the tea set, the table held a deep-gray leather portfolio, not thick, but of fine quality.
"Try this — Mengding Ganlu, brought by a friend this year. The fragrance is a little mellower than Anji white tea. Good for the evening." Zhao Yiming sat down across from her, lifted the white porcelain pot, and poured for her. The motion was smooth and natural, his wrist steady, the stream of tea drawing a clean, shallow arc into the cup.
Yao Yuan raised it, breathed in lightly, then took a small sip. A clear, elegant fragrance bloomed on the tip of her tongue, settling the mind. "Good tea."
"The tea master here is excellent, but I wanted to do it myself." Zhao Yiming took a sip too, set down his cup, his eyes falling on the gray portfolio, then lifting to her, open and frank. "So — shall we get to the point?"
"Let's." Yao Yuan set down her cup as well, sat up a little straighter, the social softness fading from her face, replaced by the caution and focus that belonged to "Boss Yao."
Zhao Yiming picked up the portfolio but did not open it at once. He looked at her, and in his eyes was the earnestness and candor of a scientist facing an important experiment.
"In Phuket, I said I'd prepare a white paper on a 'system interaction protocol.'" He began, his voice level. "After I got back, I spent some time organizing my thoughts. This isn't merely a business plan for deep cooperation merging 'Pretty Big One's' core emotional data with the 'Genesis Engine' technology — though that part is its base framework."
He paused, as if weighing his words. "It's more like a systematic projection and tabletop simulation of every interaction mode, potential risk, expected return, and… sustainable path of joint development that I can foresee arising between our two 'systems.'"
Yao Yuan's fingertips traced lightly along the warm wall of her teacup. She said nothing, only signaled with her eyes for him to go on.
At last Zhao Yiming opened the portfolio. Inside was a neatly bound printed document, the cover the very image he had sent her earlier — "Preliminary Risk Assessment and Benefit Analysis White Paper on a 'System Interaction Protocol' (Draft)." Below the title was a smaller line: Proposed by: Zhao Yiming; Potential collaborating system: Yao Yuan.
"I'm used to thinking through problems this way, especially the important ones." Zhao Yiming turned the document toward her, but did not push it across, as if he wanted to walk her through it verbally first. "Breaking a complex system apart piece by piece — especially one involving people — to see its threads, its key points, its hidden risks. It helps me see the essence of things."
"Part One is an analysis of each system's current state and core assets." He turned to the first page, where a concise list ran down the sheet. "'The Yao Yuan System': core assets include — a mature database of emotional decision-making cases and a uniquely original analytical logic, seasoned commercial operating ability and resource-integration capacity, a deep personal network in Jinshi and certain specialized fields, and the public recognition and trust-momentum of the 'Pretty Big One' brand. Potential risk modules include: a business model heavily dependent on the personal IP, challenges to the compliance and long-term scalability of the data sources, and the possibility that the core algorithm could be imitated or surpassed."
He spoke in pure business terms — cool, objective. Yao Yuan gave a slight nod, no objection. It was all fact.
"The Zhao Yiming System: core assets are the 'Genesis Engine's' underlying architecture and computing power, a top-tier R&D team in brain science and affective computing, an ultra-large-scale dedicated data center already in operation, and my credit endorsement in academic and frontier-tech investment circles. The risks: long R&D cycles, an extremely fast burn rate, uncertainty in landing market applications, and a continual dependence on — and competition for — top talent."
"Part Two is a feasibility analysis of the interaction interfaces and the protocol." Zhao Yiming turned to the next page, where a simple system-connection diagram was drawn. "Our current cooperation rests on a primary, data-interface-level protocol — you license the emotional data, I provide algorithm-model optimization, the goal being to build that 'emotional super-advisor' AI you envision. But after Phuket and the exchanges since, I believe there's room to establish a deeper, more multidimensional interaction protocol."
Yao Yuan nodded. This was precisely her most central demand — an AI advisor that could deeply understand, analyze, and offer concrete strategy, not a high-end chat companion.
He raised his head, his gaze meeting hers, no ambiguity in it, only the pure clarity of discovering a new path and a restrained excitement. "For instance, a deep binding of capital and strategy. Western World Technology is already a commercially mature tech company; our smart chips and solutions have landed with leading clients across multiple industries. Cooperation with 'Pretty Big One' isn't just opening a new, hugely promising application scenario for us — affective computing — it's an important window into understanding 'people,' the most complex terminal of all. We could explore more flexible equity exchanges, set up a joint venture focused on the emotional-AI track, even co-plan the future product forms and business ecology of that track. That would bring long-term value and strategic initiative far beyond any technology-licensing fee." His tone stayed level, but the words revealed a vision that treated 'Pretty Big One' as a major strategic partner.
Something stirred in Yao Yuan. Zhao Yiming's Western World had long since stopped being a startup that needed round after round of funding; in proposing a deep binding, what he valued was the unique worth of 'Pretty Big One' and the space it held for the imagination. That recognition carried real weight.
"The closed-loop flywheel of R&D and data." He went on, his tone touched with a researcher's excitement. "Your livestream room is a 'rich vein' continuously producing high-quality, high-concentration emotional-interaction data. If we could build a safe, compliant, efficient mechanism for data and research collaboration — letting this real-time, living data, once anonymized, feed back into the training and iteration of the 'emotional super-advisor' model more directly and quickly — then we'd have a closed loop of 'real scenario — data feedback — model optimization — better service.' Which means your 'Pretty Big One' would have an AI core that grows with its users, evolves in real time, and gets smarter the more it's used, building a barrier of time and data that's almost impossible to imitate."
This was exactly what Yao Yuan had dreamed of — an AI brain that kept learning and evolving, not an algorithm frozen at some version.
"Mutual empowerment of insight and influence." Zhao Yiming looked at her, his eyes earnest. "You are a top expert in understanding the emotional dilemmas and growth needs of modern high-net-worth women; your insight and content influence are scarce resources. I work at the frontier of technology, especially brain science and artificial intelligence. Our deep cooperation could let us speak together, set standards, even shape the direction of the field, in this emerging space of 'making emotion scientific' and 'making growth measurable.' That would not only cement 'Pretty Big One's' authority in the industry, it would also add cases of genuine human warmth and real significance to Western World's work on 'AI for Good' and 'responsible AI.' It's a shared appreciation of both brand and influence."
He used no obscure metaphors; every point came through clear and penetrating, striking straight at the core demands and future bottlenecks of 'Pretty Big One's' development, and offering deeply attractive solutions and points of shared interest.
Yao Yuan listened quietly, ripples stirring within her. Zhao Yiming's thinking was clear, sharp, aimed straight at the heart of the matter. He saw not only the possibility of cooperation but the path to a fusion and upgrade. The first half of this "white paper" was already a highly attractive, sincere proposal for strategic cooperation.
"So then, Part Three," Yao Yuan said, her red lips parting slightly. "The risks and challenges. The deeper we go, the tighter we bind, the bigger the storms will be."
"Yes." Zhao Yiming's expression turned grave as he flipped to the latter part of the document. "Deep interaction necessarily comes with risk. I've laid out the few most important ones."
"One: technical-ethics and data-security risk. The boundaries of emotional AI matter enormously. Users' absolute trust is your bedrock, and a bottom line we must guard together. We need to build the industry's strictest data-security and privacy-protection system, and reach a high consensus on the AI's ethical principles and values alignment — even set up an independent ethics-review mechanism. On this point, there's no room for compromise."
"Two: commercial-integration and interest-coordination risk. The brand-association effects deep cooperation may bring, the definition of property rights over core data and knowledge, the disputes that could arise in dividing interests, and… the shock to each system should the cooperation fail. This part needs extremely rigorous legal documents and exit mechanisms — safeguarded by the most rigorous contracts and the most frequent, candid communication."
He paused, his tone softening, warmer but still clear.
"Three: system-compatibility risk — the risk of two people running themselves in and building trust." His gaze toward Yao Yuan was frank and direct. "This is the most central risk, and the least controllable. Two highly complex, highly independent systems, each with its own powerful self-driving logic, will inevitably generate friction, energy loss, even momentary 'crashes' in deep interaction. No deep cooperation can avoid it. We're both people with strong convictions, our own logic of action, our own rhythm. Our priorities, our risk tolerance, even our ways of communicating may differ. If these differences aren't handled well, they'll burn up precious cooperative energy and turn what should be a promising thing into a hard slog. So in my vision, beyond the black-and-white agreement, an efficient, candid, mutually tunable mechanism of communication and trust between us may matter just as much as the business terms themselves."
He spoke plainly. Yao Yuan thought of her own experiences with partners and past collaborators, and knew well how true his words were. The heart-guarding old jade lay warm against her skin, letting her keep a strange calm even as she weighed these very real challenges.
