"And finally, the 'far shore' we might reach if we can cross all this." Zhao Yiming turned to the last few pages — no complex charts here, only headline text. "The benefit analysis. The most direct is enormous commercial success and an unshakable position in the industry. Deeper than that, I believe this kind of high-quality, deep collaboration lets each of us break through some of our existing limits, reaching heights of insight and networks of resources we could never touch going it alone. You'd gain a constantly evolving, truly intelligent strategic-grade weapon, completely solving the over-dependence on the personal IP, upgrading 'Pretty Big One' from an excellent brand into a platform with a powerful technical core and ecosystem. And I — and Western World — through this most complex 'human nature' application scenario, could temper our technology, expand the boundary of our understanding of 'intelligence,' and leave behind genuinely influential work at the intersection of technology and humanity."
Yao Yuan lowered her lashes, watching the tea leaves rise and sink in her cup.
He closed the portfolio and gently pushed it once more toward Yao Yuan.
"All my thinking is in here. You can take it back and weigh it carefully against 'Pretty Big One's' actual situation." He leaned back in his chair, showing for the first time tonight a flicker of something almost like "uncertainty," though it was quickly covered by his habitual calm, giving way to a faint but genuine smile. "This is only a projection based on the present and on future possibilities. In actual interaction, the variables are endless. But I think, with the abilities and resources we both have, it's worth a try."
The room fell quiet, only the faint sound of wind outside and the rustle of bamboo leaves brushing together.
Yao Yuan did not reach for the document at once. She raised her head and looked at Zhao Yiming. The lamplight cast soft shadows on his face; he looked earnest to the point of reverence, like a scientist submitting his life's work to a review board.
"Very Zhao Yiming." She suddenly let out a soft laugh, and there was a touch of appreciative feeling in it. "You saw not just where 'Pretty Big One' stands now, but the bottleneck it may hit in the future — and the key to breaking through it. Especially the parts on the data closed-loop flywheel and on cognitive empowerment — those are exactly what I've been thinking about lately. Including… human relationships."
Zhao Yiming did not deny it, saying frankly, "It's the only thing I'm good at, and the way I consider most responsible. Define clearly, assess fully, then choose. It's more likely to reach a sustainable state of coordination than vague feelings and impulsive decisions."
"A sustainable state of coordination…" Yao Yuan repeated the phrase, her fingertips unconsciously brushing the heart-guarding old jade against her skin. The gentle warmth made her think of Old Chen's warning, of the Mirror Pact's eerie, un-modelable bond, of the fragments of fate that kept dragging her uncontrollably back into the "past."
The man before her was trying to use the most rational framework to define and plan a relationship full of uncertainty. While she had only just struggled free of the most irrational, fate-bound entanglement, still carrying against her chest a piece of old jade meant to "settle the soul."
The contrast was absurd, and yet, strangely… it made her feel grounded.
At least here, everything could be analyzed, discussed, defined. Even the risks, if there were any, were risks written down on paper.
She paused, then spoke slowly, meeting his waiting gaze. "Your 'white paper' draft has a clear framework, rigorous logic, and well-considered risks. As a proposal for commercial and strategic cooperation, it already holds high value for discussion. But, Zhao Yiming — in your blueprint, when our cooperation deepens to a certain point, say when we diverge on strategic priorities, or differ in philosophy on handling some thorny problem — what do we rely on to reach consensus? Do we look at equity ratios, at short-term data, or do we return to the shared 'North Star' goal we both agreed on when we first decided to join hands? And if even our understanding of that 'North Star' drifts as we grow, how do we recalibrate? This conflict-resolution mechanism — does your agreement have a preset plan for it?"
The question pointed sharply at the most central decision-making mechanism and values-alignment problem in deep cooperation — the most essential question of power and coordination in any relationship.
Zhao Yiming had clearly thought it through. He leaned forward slightly, hands clasped on the table, the posture of someone in earnest discussion.
"An excellent question, and the one I care about most." His pace was steady, his reasoning clear. "In my understanding, deep cooperation — especially the kind we're discussing, involving strategy and core resources — shouldn't rest on simple equity votes or one party's say-so. It should be built on co-governance."
"First, right at the start, we must clearly define our shared 'North Star' — not just commercial success, but what kind of product we want to create, what kind of users we want to serve, what ethical bottom line we hold. Write it down as the highest principle."
"Second, establish a regular, high-quality mechanism for strategic alignment. Not reporting — real brainstorming and synced understanding. Making sure we always stay on the same frequency about the big direction."
"Then, on specific decisions, we can design a tiered decision mechanism. Pure commercial and technical questions can go by professional division of labor and pre-agreed authority; anything involving strategic direction, core values, or major risk must reach deep consensus between both core decision-makers. And if we truly can't agree…" He looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes clear and firm. "The preset 'safety valve' is to pause, step back, or bring in a third-party advisor we both trust for perspective — but never to force something through at the cost of the 'North Star' goal or of damaging each other's trust."
"As for whether the 'North Star' itself needs recalibrating," he continued, "of course it does. Times change, we grow. But the process of recalibration must be one both sides take part in, debate fully, and finally, willingly, agree to. That in itself is the deepest test and tempering of the cooperative relationship."
He looked at her, his eyes clear and firm, and finished: "The foundation of all this requires highly efficient, candid communication and deep mutual trust. That can't be written into a contract, yet it's the soil in which every contract either thrives or fails. I think we at least both have the will to cultivate that soil."
Yao Yuan held his gaze. His answer was rational, thorough, and full of respect for a partner's independence. He hadn't dodged the difficulties; he'd tried to build mechanisms to solve them. That attitude was more reassuring than any flowery promise.
"I need time to assess this draft in detail with my core team." Yao Yuan said at last, her tone professionally cautious, yet the shadow in her eyes — from fatigue and the matter of the Mirror Pact — seemed lit a little by the other's clear, firm thinking. "But there's no doubt: the directions for deepening you've pointed to, especially building the data closed-loop and jointly defining industry standards, align closely with 'Pretty Big One's' strategic vision for the next stage. I'm personally very interested."
A relaxed smile spread across Zhao Yiming's face, a pleasure that came from the heart. "Wonderful. The value of this draft is exactly that it can open a conversation like this. Take your time assessing it. Any thoughts at all, and we can talk again whenever you like."

========================================

Chapter 61 — A Stirring of the Heart
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640308.html

A Stirring of the Heart
The business was, for now, settled. The dishes arrived right on cue—refined, light, exactly the sort of thing the Yunting kept to. The mood between them eased of its own accord, sliding from the sharp, focused strategy session into the looser, easier chatter of a shared meal.
Yao Yuan cut into the tender perch on her plate and, recalling an earlier thread of conversation, asked offhandedly, "By the way—you mentioned before that the model fusion had a kind of 'rejection response.' Any progress lately? Our 'Emotional Super-Advisor' prototype is still waiting on that stronger 'heart.'"
Zhao Yiming nodded. "I've had a conceptual breakthrough. The key may be that you can't just crudely stitch things together—you have to design dedicated reasoning modules at a deeper level, in the 'cognitive architecture' itself, for highly complex, multi-objective problems like emotional decision-making. It's like…" He thought for a moment, then found his metaphor. "It's not draping a general-purpose robot in a layer of psychology. It's building it, from the very first design choice, as a specialist in handling 'relationships' and 'choices.' That takes more data like the 'Pretty Big One' case library—full of contradictions, trade-offs, and real consequences—to feed it and to validate it. So," he raised his glass toward her, "our partnership matters enormously."
Yao Yuan caught his meaning and tapped her glass lightly against his. "To an AI that understands the human heart a little better." She took a sip, then said, as if in passing, "My brother, Yifan—since he got back from Phuket last time, he keeps talking about how well the two of you clicked. Says he got a lot out of those conversations. This year he applied to a joint-training program at MIT, focusing on early biomarkers for neurodegenerative disease—which I think overlaps with some of the foundational research you did?"
Zhao Yiming's eyes lit up. "Yifan is exceptional. His thinking really cuts through to the core. I know the person who runs that MIT program—if it would help, I can write him a recommendation. And if he ever wants to explore the cutting-edge intersections of brain-computer interfaces or computational neuroscience, the labs and clinical-collaboration network at Western World are open to him anytime, to come exchange ideas and learn."
That easy, genuine concern for Zhou Yifan's future—and the real, concrete help behind it—warmed something in Yao Yuan. He was always like this: steady, considerate, offering warmth in the places you least expected it.
Dessert was a delicate almond tofu, served in little crackle-glazed bowls. The conversation between them slipped, naturally, into more personal territory.
Yao Yuan stirred the gleaming white custard with a small silver spoon, then looked up, seemingly at random, at Zhao Yiming, whose expression had softened across the table. The room's lamplight was warm, catching the clean lines of his profile. She thought of the unfinished conversation on the yacht in Phuket, and something in her stirred.
"Zhao Yiming," she said, setting down the spoon—the first time all evening she had used his full name in such an informal moment. Her voice was low, carrying a pure, purely personal curiosity. "I remember in Phuket you mentioned that relationship of yours at MIT. Since then… have you ever met a 'system' you felt was 'compatible'?"
The question was a touch private, but her tone was candid and her gaze clear, as if she were merely continuing the high-caliber meeting of minds from over dinner—only shifting the topic from business strategy to human nature itself.
Zhao Yiming clearly hadn't expected the conversation to turn here. He blinked, briefly caught off guard, then relaxed and leaned back into his chair, the tip of one finger tapping unconsciously against the smooth tabletop, as if entering some command for thought. He didn't dodge the question; if anything, because it came from Yao Yuan, he treated it with a special seriousness.
"Since then…" He mulled it over for a moment, then shook his head, a trace of self-mockery in his smile. "The first few years of the startup, I was all in on the 'Genesis Engine.' The lab and the Gobi were my entire world—no time, and no inclination, to think about 'compatibility.' Later, once the company found its footing and I met more people, but…" He paused, searching for the precise words. "It seemed to get harder."
He looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze frank, carrying the cool detachment of a researcher dissecting a phenomenon—yet strangely mixed with a faint, hard-to-name wistfulness. "You start to realize that when your world turns more and more 'hardcore'—revolving around algorithms, neuroscience, hyperscale data centers—the people who can truly understand what you're doing, why it excites you, why it makes you anxious, are themselves very few. And among those few, to then find someone who not only understands but resonates, who can even… make you feel, beyond that cold rational code and those models, a completely different dimension of 'attraction' and 'challenge'…"
He stopped without finishing, but the unspoken meaning was already clear. It was the loneliness of standing where the air is too thin at the heights—and an utterly clear-eyed grasp of his own inner needs.
Yao Yuan listened quietly, not interrupting. She understood the feeling. In her own way, in her own field—dissecting emotion, the games of human nature, the workings of capital—she had reached a similar place. Many understood her; few resonated. And the people who could truly make her feel something was "interesting," "a challenge," who could even illuminate certain blind spots in her thinking—those were rarer still.
"So," she said softly, steering the conversation, "your standard now—or rather, what you're hoping for—is it still the same as what you described in Phuket? Someone who can explore the unknown alongside you, who isn't afraid of chaos, who can even create order within the chaos?"
Zhao Yiming's eyes met hers. The absolute rationality from their earlier business talk was gone now, replaced by something deeper, harder to parse. He nodded slowly.
"Yes. But maybe… a little more specific than back then, and a little more… greedy." He leaned slightly forward, and the distance between them seemed, in the soft light, to close invisibly. "After going through some things, seeing some of the world—including a few conversations with you—I'm more and more convinced that what I need may not be a 'compatible' system, but a 'catalyst' that sets off a beneficial mutation. Or a 'collaborator' who can help build out entirely new emergent properties."
His phrasing still carried the flavor of his field, but the emotional core was almost ready to break the surface.
"This person needs to be smart and resilient enough to understand—even take part in—the building of my world, someone who won't shrink back in the face of complexity and difficulty. But at the same time, she has to possess an inner world that's wholly foreign to me, maybe one I could never fully simulate with algorithms—rich, alive. A world with its own operating logic, its own emotional temperature, its own aesthetic system. One that can offer unexpected perspectives and inspiration when I'm too lost in rational deduction; that can remind me, when I'm surrounded by cold code and data, of the parts of human nature that can't be quantified and yet matter most—things like trust, like intuition, like a certain kind of… attraction that goes beyond any utilitarian calculation."
By the end his voice had dropped low, but his gaze stayed locked on Yao Yuan's face—as if, through her bright makeup, her dazzling jewels, even that suit of armor that stood for "Boss Yao," he were examining the soul beneath: complex, powerful, full of contradictory allure, and possessing an "inner world" he was endlessly curious about.
Yao Yuan felt the heart-guarding jade against her chest grow faintly warm for an instant. She held his gaze without flinching, and at the corner of her red lips a smile spread—very faint, but heavy with meaning.
"Sounds like your bar is high," she said, in a tone you couldn't tell was teasing or simply stating fact. "You want both a rational mind to fight shoulder to shoulder with you and a soul-deep emotional warmth that resonates; you want someone who can move with ease inside your rules, and yet possess an 'inner world' you can't fully control… It's almost a paradox."
"It is. A high-difficulty, multi-objective-optimization paradox." Zhao Yiming admitted it, but there wasn't the slightest retreat in his eyes—instead there was that light a person gets facing a top-tier problem, a mix of excitement and certainty. "Which is exactly why I say it's hard. But precisely because it's hard, if I ever truly meet that person, I shouldn't let her slip away lightly. She's worth the greatest sincerity and patience—worth trying to solve this 'paradox,' to see what finally 'emerges.'"
He never said "you are that person," but every word of his description was like a carefully ground prism, and the light it refracted fell, squarely and unmistakably, on Yao Yuan's own qualities. This kind of "expression"—no blunt confession, yet brimming with intellectual admiration and deep inquiry—was more penetrating than any sweet talk, and far better at striking the heartstrings of a woman at Yao Yuan's level.
The room fell quiet for a few seconds, with only the faint sound of wind beyond the window. The air was thick with words left unsaid and a tension growing quietly. It was a most refined ambiguity, built on the highest order of intellectual recognition and respect for character—not loud, not frivolous, yet pressing heavily into every breath, full of infinite possibility and dangerous appeal.
Yao Yuan lowered her lashes, looking at her own blurred reflection in the bowl, then glanced at the warm jade against her chest. On one side, an invitation full of challenge, standing for rational exploration and future possibility; on the other, a curse to be broken, a symbol of fated entanglement and the shadows of the past. And she stood in the middle, pulled both ways.
At last she raised her head and gave him a bright smile—one that put back on a little of the "Boss Yao" social mask, though the faint ripples deep in her eyes had not entirely stilled.
"A very impressive 'user-needs portrait,' Boss Zhao," she said, with a hint of playfulness, deftly using the language of business to pull the nearly overflowing private mood back into the safe zone. "It seems you're not only good at mapping out technical roadmaps—you've got your expectations for a 'partner' charted out just as clearly."
Zhao Yiming laughed too, accepting the line she had just drawn. He understood the measure of advance and retreat.
"Just a deduction grounded in facts." He picked the thread back up easily, lifting his teacup, as if that soul-deep exchange a moment ago had been nothing more than another high-caliber meeting of minds. "And I know the best 'partnership' is never one-sided need being met—it's mutual fit and mutual spark. My 'portrait' is clear, but whether a 'match' is finally possible takes both sides' 'data' to speak, and… time and shared experience to verify."
There was meaning beneath his words, and he left ample room for it, and for the imagination.
Yao Yuan didn't press the topic further. She turned instead to a recent hot-button event in the AI industry, and Zhao Yiming picked it up in perfect step, bringing up the latest energy-optimization figures from the Gobi data center.
And yet, once certain things are said, once certain hopes are laid bare, the air is already different. The emotional undercurrent surging beneath that rational "white paper," woven together with the tacit tension now between them, lent this meeting—originally one with a clear purpose—an entirely different, heart-quickening color.
By the time they left, the night had grown deep, bamboo shadows swaying.
Yao Yuan pulled her cream-white trench coat tighter, the heart-guarding jade at her throat appearing and vanishing at the collar, its warm touch unbroken.
Zhao Yiming walked her to the car and, quite naturally, opened the door for her. As she settled into the driver's seat, he rested his arm lightly against the roof, leaned down, and said gently, "Drive safe. Take your time with the draft—there's no rush."
"All right." Yao Yuan looked up and smiled at him. In the night her eyes shone especially bright under the makeup and the light. "We talked it through thoroughly tonight. Thank you."
Zhao Yiming nodded and stepped back, watching her. "Good night, Yao Yuan."
"Good night." Yao Yuan answered, and started the car.

As she pulled onto the main road, the city's neon flowed by like a river. On the passenger seat, the "Preliminary Risk Assessment and Benefit Analysis White Paper on the 'System Interaction Protocol' (Draft)" lay quietly.
A rational blueprint toward a future business empire and a place in the industry; a warm piece of old jade guarding the mind, holding off an uncanny fate.
Her world was still split between these two stark poles. But tonight, that blueprint was so clear and so tempting, the man who'd drafted it so steady and dependable, that even as she struggled against a chaotic destiny, she saw—more clearly than ever before—a bright road she could open up by her own wits and her own strength.
The shadow of the Mirror Pact was still there. But ahead, it seemed, a stronger light and a more solid ally were beginning to appear.
Her phone screen lit up—a message from Zhao Yiming: "Home now. Any thoughts on the draft, we can talk anytime." Brief, steady, just like the man himself.
Yao Yuan looked at that line of text, glanced again at the folder beside her, and a complicated but genuine smile broke across her bright face. She replied: "Will do. Talk soon."
At that moment, Zhao Yiming's last words about "mutual fit" and "verification over time," and the focused, burning gaze he'd worn while describing that "high-difficulty paradox" of a partner, were printed clearly in her mind.
Rationality and feeling, future and past, a clear protocol and a heart in turmoil… all the contradictions and pulls reached a new peak in this moment. And the direction of the story, because of this dinner—woven through with a top-tier clash of intellect and a deep probing of emotion—grew all the more elusive, and all the more compelling.
Spring was gone; high summer was on its way. Some entanglements may be hard to break free of, but a new chapter, along with this weighty "white paper," had now been solemnly lifted at one corner.

========================================

Chapter 62 — When the Roseleaf Raspberry Blooms, Spring Is Done
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640309.html

Spring in the north arrives in a vast, magnificent surge, but slips away leaving no trace at all. All it leaves behind is a city layered in greenery and shade, the warm breeze soaking in a deepening, mellow gentleness that spreads across the whole human world.

It seems that in the space of a single sweet dream, the pink-and-white clouds of pear blossoms woven across the city quietly finish falling. Then come the winter jasmine spreading its gold, the pink cherry blossoms drooping from the branches, the white apricot bursting into bud, the red peach blazing open, the plum blossoms gathered like snow — every kind of beauty rushing to one grand spring banquet. They take the stage one after another, blooming in turns, lush and clamorous, refusing to pause even for a moment.

When the peony at last arrives to close the show, carrying the bearing of a nation-toppling beauty, regal and unrivaled among all the season's flowers; and then the roseleaf raspberry, its plain white scattered petals unfurling at their leisure deep in the dense shade — this spectacle of spring at its very height has already come to its end.

When the roseleaf raspberry blooms, spring is done.

A grand yet hurried cycle of flowering, no more than two or three months in all, pours out an entire year's brilliance and lingering tenderness. The splendor falls suddenly from the stage, surrendering the whole human stage to high summer, which gathers its momentum and surges forth, its life force sprawling and magnificent.

Since the day she came back from the Hall of Antiquities, more than a month has now passed.

The matter of the Mirror Pact was like a stone dropped into a deep pool — at first it stirred heart-stopping ripples, then it sank to the bottom, and the surface seemed to return to calm. Yao Yuan called on every connection she could think of, asking openly and in secret after any trace of ancient-method occultists, of bronze-mirror craftsmanship, and above all of anything tied to the "Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern." But the harvest was meager. She had wrapped that mirror carefully in a length of black velvet and locked it in the deepest corner of a bank safe-deposit box, as if that alone could seal away the uncanny tie along with it.

And the strangest thing of all was that the frequent, uncontrollable bouts of "time-travel" had truly stopped recurring.

Was it the "heart-guarding" old jade that Old Chen had given her, finally doing its work? Or had something shifted on Shuai Hongqiang's side — remorse in his heart, his fortunes turning — temporarily cutting off the "pull of the time-reversal"? Or was it merely some brief calm before the storm? Yao Yuan had no way to know. She only wore that old jade against her skin every day, feeling its unbroken, gentle warmth press soothingly against her spirit, letting her hold an outwardly flawless calm and efficiency through the layered pressures of livestreaming, company affairs, and her secret search for the mirror-maker's descendants. Even her bodily fatigue seemed to ease a little — though deep down, that taut string never truly slackened.

Today was another livestream day.

Early in the morning her phone buzzed on the desk. It was Jin Lan.

"Yuanyuan, what time do you wrap up today?" Jin Lan's voice came through the receiver, steady and capable, carrying the dining queen's particular composure — the ease of someone who controls every affair in hand — but for once there was a thread of relaxed interest in her tone.

"It ends at three this afternoon, Jin Lan-lan." Yao Yuan picked up and leaned back against her chair, at ease. Between her and Jin Lan there were years of close friendship and an evenly matched business partnership; in private they had long since dropped the courtesies.

"Free tonight? A friend forced two tickets on me — there's an all-male dance troupe that's been pretty hot lately, on their national tour, the Jinshi stop, at the Provincial Grand Theatre. I asked around; the standard's not bad, pure artistic appreciation, a good way to clear the head too. Together?" Jin Lan was brief and to the point, but in those words "pure artistic appreciation" there was a knowing, mutual teasing that belongs to mature women. At their age and standing, they had long been able to admire all beautiful things openly and aboveboard — including excellent, strikingly handsome performers — and there was nothing carnal in it, only a refined aesthetic pleasure and a confidence in their own taste.

Yao Yuan was indeed a little worn out in spirit, but Jin Lan rarely took the initiative to suggest something leisurely, and it was a dance performance — an art she had steeped herself in for years and knew inside out. To weigh it with a professional eye would be both a relaxation and a kind of looking back on a passion from her past. She was still mulling it over for a moment when Jin Lan's smiling voice came again, with a touch of "don't miss the chance" urgency:

"Hey, sis, these tickets are hard to come by — my friend's a sponsor, so they're insider tickets." Jin Lan caught her hesitation and laughed as she added another weight to the scale, "In or out? Just treat it as putting your Beijing Dance Academy eye to the test — check the real grade of this male troupe, and give your eyes a holiday while you're at it. I've booked a table at 'Lanting' near the theatre; the dishes are light, just right for you. Six o'clock, meet at the theatre entrance?"

"All right." She no longer hesitated, and agreed. "See you at six."

After hanging up, Yao Yuan walked into the dressing room. The first thing to choose was her livestream battle-dress; what she wore on stream had always been part of her style manifesto. Her eyes swept across the dazzling wardrobe and finally settled on an extremely formal, extremely saturated peach-pink silk taffeta dress. The color was so intense it was almost blinding, but the fabric's own heavy, lustrous sheen reined in just enough of what might otherwise have spilled into frivolous gaudiness, drawing out instead a restrained splendor. The dress was a simple bateau-neck, short-sleeve design, but the cut was punishingly perfect, pinching out a slender waist with precision; knee-length, its lines clean and flowing. This was a color that judged its wearer mercilessly — worn badly, it was vulgar — but on Yao Yuan, set against her snow-white skin, thick black hair, and bright, commanding features, it carried a forceful, who-else-but-me magnificence and brilliance, not the least bit gaudy, leaving only the impression that color this intense had been born to belong to her.

She blew her long hair full and loose, gathered it low into a soft bun at the back, with a few stray strands trailing lazily by her neck, carrying the easy casualness of an early-summer morning. Today's makeup was a touch lighter than her usual livestream look, but it played up a clean eyeliner and a full peach-pink lip color, striking a high-impact echo with the dress. At her ears were a pair of minimally styled but generously sized platinum diamond hoop earrings, refracting cool, fine flecks of light with every movement. On her wrist was a Patek Philippe Nautilus in rose gold, lending this utterly feminine look a barely perceptible note of hardness and rarity.

The heart-guarding old jade was still worn against her skin, tucked neatly out of sight by the dress, leaving only its gentle warmth to press soothingly — the steadying anchor amid her turbulent moods. Over it all she threw a cream-white long silk crinkle-crepe shirt jacket, its fabric extremely light and flowing, glowing with a faint pearl sheen. It could ward off the slight chill of the studio's early-summer air conditioning and also cleverly temper the high-impact richness of the peach-pink dress beneath, adding a touch of ease, layering, and room to breathe, looking all the more relaxed and chic. She stepped into a pair of nude fine-strap high heels and picked up a small silver clutch.

The woman in the mirror was dazzling in peach-pink, her figure slender, half-hidden and half-revealed in the thin mist the cream-white jacket created, radiating all over a beauty and presence that years had settled into something more mellow, more self-aware, and brimming with life. She curved the corner of her lips, satisfied. Whether she was about to face the millions of viewers behind the lens, or the performance that night with its perhaps-stirring undercurrents, she, Yao Yuan, would always appear in her most dazzling, most commanding, and most unrestrained form.

========================================

Chapter 63 — A Circle Isn't Something You Wait For, It's Something You Fight Your Way Into; Tenderness Isn't Weakness, It's a Weapon
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640310.html

A circle isn't something you wait for, it's something you fight your way into; tenderness isn't weakness, it's a weapon.

Eleven o'clock sharp.

Yao Yuan started the livestream right on time.

【Livestream Status】

Bright, full early-summer morning light poured in through the entire wall of floor-to-ceiling windows, mingling and colliding with the cold white beams of the ring lights to form a clear, intensely modern field of brightness. Outside the windows lay Jinshi's lush summer greenery and a clean, open sky; the glass curtain walls of the surrounding buildings sliced the blue sky into geometric blocks that gave back a crystalline shine.

Yao Yuan sat composed within this play of light and shadow. Her dress of deep peach-pink silk and the crisp, eye-catching "Tiffany blue" polish on the fingers resting on her black mouse formed a clever, bold visual clash under the sunlight and the lamps. It lit up the whole frame in an instant — striking and commanding, yet carrying a lively, playful chic.

The scrolling comments rolled past quickly with the morning's topic tags: #HowToGetOutOfTheSingleZoneWhenYourCircleIsTooNarrow# #CantProvideEmotionalValue# #WednesdayWakeUpCall#.

Yao Yuan glanced at the steadily climbing viewer count in the top right and the comments flying past, her expression calm, carrying the particular professionalism and focus of someone who has slipped into work mode. She adjusted her headset, her sky-blue fingertips tapping unconsciously on the smooth desktop like fingers striking the keys of a soundless piano, and spoke, her voice clear and steady:

"Good morning, everyone. Welcome to my livestream. Let's get right to it and take our first call."

【Call Connected】

Caller (female voice, 25, clear but plainly lost and dejected): "Hello, Teacher Yuanyuan. I've followed you for a long time, and today I wanted to ask about something that's probably very common, but that I genuinely can't solve on my own… I'm 25 this year, I have a stable job, and my looks and figure are above average, I'd say. But I really don't know how to date, how to get married."

She sighed, the breath carrying real helplessness through the microphone: "Right out of school I went into admin at a state-owned enterprise. My coworkers are basically all women, or older uncles and aunties. Day to day it's just home and office, and on weekends, apart from shopping and movies with the same set of close girlfriends, I almost never meet anyone new and suitable of the opposite sex. My social circle is incredibly narrow. It feels like being trapped under a transparent dome."

"A few months ago, a relative kindly introduced me to a guy — pretty outstanding among people our age, runs his own business, good circumstances. We tried dating for less than three months. He was very generous with me — he'd give expensive gifts on holidays, and never let me pay when we ate out. But… our personalities are too alike, both stubborn. Once we argued over something small, where to go on the weekend, and neither of us would back down first. We gave each other the cold shoulder for days. In the end he messaged first, but it wasn't an apology — he said he felt he wasn't getting the 'emotional value' he wanted out of the relationship, that it was too exhausting, and then… he broke it off, cold and flat."

Her voice dropped, carrying both reluctance and a deeper confusion: "Teacher Yuanyuan, I really do want a serious relationship, but right now I don't know where to meet new, reliable men, and I don't know how to manage a relationship either. With a personality like mine, am I just not cut out for dating? I can't even find a chance to start."

【The scrolling comments came alive, flooding with people who related:

[Sis, you're literally me! I'm at a state enterprise too, my circle is cleaner than my face!]

[Two stubborn mules together really is hopeless… they just torture each other.]

[Are guys all this practical? Generous with gifts so they demand emotional value?]

["Emotional value" is almost a worn-out phrase by now, but it really does seem to be a sore spot.]

[Where do you even meet people? Same question! Feels like the moment you leave campus you become an isolated island.]】

Yao Yuan (after listening, she didn't comment right away. She leaned back gracefully, the delicate collarbones beneath the peach-pink off-shoulder neckline half-hidden, a faint, knowing half-smile curving her lips):

"Twenty-five, stable job, above-average looks, a short-term partner who was generous in material terms but clearly wanted emotional value — and then you broke up because 'you're both stubborn.'" She recapped, her pace even, as if sorting through a case file. "Your problem, broken open, is two separate layers. First, a channel problem — in your fish pond, either there are no fish, or the fish that swim in aren't the ones you want. Second, a problem of angling and rearing technique — a fish finally swam over, and you didn't land it, or you used the wrong methods and either drove the fish off in a huff or let it starve away."

【Comments: [The fish-pond theory is vulgar but the logic's solid!][Hits the nail on the head!]】

"Let's deal with the second one first — the technique problem." Yao Yuan leaned slightly forward, her gaze turning sharp, like a scalpel. "You said you're both stubborn, that when conflict comes up neither of you backs down, each waiting for the other to come smooth things over. Then the guy says he 'isn't getting emotional value.' Girl, sit with that — really sit with that sentence. A man who, early in the relationship, was already willing to be generous in material terms, who seemingly held a degree of the upper hand — why would he blurt out a relatively introspective complaint like 'I'm not getting emotional value'?"

She didn't wait for an answer, asking and answering herself, her tone calm and certain: "Because he was using the most direct, most 'weighty' method within his frame of understanding — material giving — to express that he valued you and was sincere, and he didn't feel any 'glad acceptance' or 'emotional reciprocation' from you toward that giving. Your 'stubbornness,' which to you might be standing firm in who you are, in his eyes, in that situation, very likely read as resistance, ingratitude, even cold confrontation. When he gave you a gift, what he subconsciously hoped to see was probably not your taking it as your due, but the smile it brought to your face, a warmer atmosphere between you, and the more intimate connection that grew out of that — not, at the next argument, the same stone-cold face that wouldn't yield an inch. His demand for 'emotional value,' translated into plain words, is: I hope my giving makes us more comfortable together, sweeter, not more exhausted and more rigid. He was looking for positive emotional feedback and a return on his investment, and he didn't find it."

【Some in the comments had a dawning realization:

[So that's what emotional value means! It's not just being good at talking.]

[Sounds like it makes sense — taking an expensive gift and then sulking through a cold war all day really is a turn-off.]

[But can't a girl have her own temper and principles? Why should she have to coax him?]】

"You can have a temper. You must have principles." Yao Yuan's voice was clear and forceful, carrying an affirmation that brooked no argument. "But it depends on timing, on the person, and even more on the means and wisdom you use to reach your goal. In relationships between men and women, especially when the other side has already shown sincerity about the long term and concrete material giving, a woman's smartest, most effortless strategy is usually not to butt heads in a contest of who's more stubborn, but to use softness to overcome hardness, skill to break force. This isn't pandering, it isn't losing yourself — it's using the smallest cost, based on a deep understanding of human nature, to resolve the biggest conflict and to bring harmony and progress to the relationship."

She paused, her eyes briefly lost in memory, then launched into a story full of that signature "Yao Yuan" flavor:

"Many years ago, when I'd just started my business — running a high-end private club — there was a time I brought along a young assistant fresh out of school to meet a client. Over the meal, the little assistant, wanting to talk me up in front of the guests, and maybe wanting to show off too, said in a deliberately exaggerated tone: 'Our Boss Yao is really something. Her ex tried to give her a place worth tens of millions by the Bund as a breakup-win-back fee, and she sent it back without a second glance. She just wanted the breakup, not the apartment.'"

"One of the guests at the table — a man of considerable status and weight — heard this and said to me, half-joking, half-serious: 'Boss Yao, this isn't right of you. Such a good man, such a heavy gift, and you turn it down just like that — that's not appreciating what you have. A woman like you is bound to meet a man one day who doesn't know how to cherish you, and you'll get hurt.'"

"That remark," Yao Yuan looked into the camera, her eyes clear and bright, "actually had a barb in it — that typical, condescending male gaze passing judgment, even a faint 'you'll get your comeuppance sooner or later' lecturing tone." She seemed to ask the girl on the call, and all her listeners too: "If it were you — fresh out of a not-so-pleasant relationship, hearing a fairly important business contact or elder appraise you that way — how would you react? Would you feel wronged and snap back on the spot? Or sit there in awkward silence, refusing to accept it inside but with no idea how to respond?"

【The comments guessed: [Probably annoyed but wouldn't dare say anything?][Feels like it just hit a sore spot?]】

"In that moment, I didn't push back at all." Yao Yuan smiled, and in that smile was a cunning, unhurried insight into the ways of the world, and absolute control over her own emotions. "I just raised my head, looked at the man with the most innocent, the clearest, even slightly bewildered eyes, and said, soft and gentle, with just the right touch of confusion and coquettish pout: 'How could that be? I think… no one could bear to hurt me…' I paused deliberately for half a second, and in the instant he was caught off guard, only then added the last half: '…unless that person were you.'"

【The comments exploded at once, boiling over:

[Holy crap! Brilliant! Absolutely brilliant!]

[Who could withstand that?! Anger turned to a racing heart in one second!]

[Retreat as advance, turn guest into host, with a touch of flirtation thrown in!]

[Learned something!! The point isn't the words, it's the eyes and the tone! Yao Yuan's a real actress!]】

"And then?" Yao Yuan asked and answered herself, her tone easy. "That man clearly froze for a moment, then caught on, and not only did that faint trace of lecturing vanish into thin air — he burst out laughing instead, saying over and over, 'Boss Yao's something else.' Afterward, far from falling out, we became good friends, and he introduced me to plenty of valuable resources. With one line that looked like showing weakness, with a hint of playful charm, I easily dissolved a possible confrontation, even turned the situation around, and won respect and future opportunities."

She put away the playful look and grew serious: "That is the power of using softness to overcome hardness. It's not that you're actually weak — it's that you know how, at the right moment, to use a woman's innate warmth and air of softness to dissolve conflict, to guide the other person's emotions, and to reach your core goal. This is especially useful in intimate relationships. When the two of you are at a standstill, swords drawn, a single coquettish 'come on, give in to me a little,' a wronged, pitiable look that melts the heart, can be a hundred times more effective than three days of cold war or a red-faced screaming match. Men need a sense of accomplishment, need the feeling of being needed and admired. It's something written into a lot of people's genes. Your occasional, well-judged 'softness' and 'showing weakness' is precisely what gives him that emotional value of being needed and relied on, which makes him more willing to give to you, more proactive in working for the harmony of the relationship."

【The comments split between doubt and agreement, the debate fierce:

[Sounds exhausting — you have to act just to date? You have to scheme?]

[Isn't this just calculation? Shouldn't feelings be sincere?]

[But honestly, most straight guys really do fall for this… it's human nature.]

[Managing a relationship wisely so both people are comfortable — that's not acting, is it? Do you have to hurt each other for it to count as sincere?]】

"Think this is 'acting'? 'Calculation'?" Yao Yuan raised an eyebrow slightly, the arch carrying a trace of cold wisdom. "Then let me give a 'grander' example. When Wu Zetian had just come to power, across the whole court, how many noble-house ministers fought to the death, fiercely opposing the idea of a woman 'crowing like a hen at dawn,' standing above the realm. So after court let out, what did Wu Zetian do? She changed into the plainest clothes, brought no retinue or ceremonial guard, and walked on foot herself to the homes of those few chancellors and ministers who opposed her most fiercely — and wept before them. She wept over the trembling, walking-on-thin-ice fear of a lone woman, over how crushingly heavy was the realm the late emperor had entrusted to her, over the difficulty and loneliness of governing a country. She used the most traditional tears and shows of weakness, those belonging to a 'frail woman,' and successfully stirred, in the depths of those iron-blooded statesmen and great Confucian scholars, a natural pity and protective instinct toward women. And the result? Quite a few of them became, from then on, her most steadfast, most capable supporters, steadying the court for her in those early days."

"A truth that top statesmen and emperors well versed in the arts of rule all understood," Yao Yuan concluded, her voice carrying a calm that pierced through history. "When the balance of power is hugely lopsided, or when a stalemate where both sides lose has already become inevitable, butting heads blindly is often the stupidest choice. Read the situation, show weakness at the right time, reveal the 'soft' side, and thereby rouse a stronger sense of responsibility and protectiveness in the other person, turning resistance into help, achieving your strategic goal. In the micro-world of feelings, this shares the same underlying logic as Wu Zetian facing her whole court — using the cleverest leverage to resolve the hardest conflict, steering the relationship in a direction favorable to you. This isn't hypocrisy; it's the wisdom of survival and growth."

【The comments fell into deep thought, the rhythm slowing:

[I actually can't argue with this…]

[That Wu Zetian example really broadened my perspective!]

[So it's not that you can't be tough — you need toughness and softness together, and proportion and timing are the keys.]

[Feels like a door to a new world just opened… dating is a game of interpersonal strategy too.]】

"All right, that's the inner method on the technical level for now. Let's come back to the first one — the channel problem. How does your fish pond get fish, and good fish?" Yao Yuan pulled the topic crisply back to reality.

"You said your social circle is narrow, that apart from work it's just those few close girlfriends. This isn't solely a problem of your environment — to a large degree it's a problem with your social initiative and the depth of your reach." She cut straight to the point, sparing no one. "This is the mobile internet age. Geography and profession stopped being the iron walls that trap you long ago. You have ten thousand ways to actively break through them."

"First, break the ice online, take the initiative to show yourself. All sorts of relatively reliable social apps, professional forums in vertical fields, interest-based Douban groups, even the quality communities on knowledge-payment platforms — these are all places where you can 'cast your net.' The key is, don't just be a submarine. Speak up. Share your real interests and hobbies, your insightful opinions, even the things you're confused about. Start with high-quality text or voice exchanges online, and gradually filter out the people whose values align with yours, who you click with, who make you feel comfortable. This is the lowest-cost first round of screening."

"Second, convert it offline, bravely break out of your circle. Many of the forums and communities you follow regularly organize offline themed gatherings — book-sharing sessions, film discussions, citywide hikes, board games and murder-mystery games, wine tastings, craft workshops… Even if you don't know a single person there, go anyway — bravely. Put yourself together, look presentable and bright, and bring an open, friendly attitude. Go once, and you'll very likely meet five to ten new friends from all different backgrounds and industries. Their perspectives and experiences may open a whole new world for you."

"Third — and this is the fastest, most effective method — find and 'lock onto' that natural 'social hub' type of friend around you." When Yao Yuan reached this point, her tone took on a faint amusement, as if sharing a secret that never failed. "Almost everyone's social network has one or two of those people who are naturally warm, love to organize things, have friends all over, and seem to know everyone. They're the key nodes in the interpersonal web, the junction where information and resources meet. As it happens, I myself, in most of the circles I belong to, often play exactly this role."

Her eyes were bright, carrying a frank confidence and a generous gladness to share: "I enjoy meeting interesting new people, and I'm genuinely happy to introduce my friends to one another and connect them. To me, sharing my social resources — letting the right people meet, spark off each other, even create greater value — is in itself something full of fun, accomplishment, and an energy return. If you have a friend like this around you, take part sincerely in the gatherings she organizes, and put your heart into talking with her and her friends. Very soon, your social network will expand like a rolling snowball, exponentially — a friend introduces you to their friend, and the friend's friend brings their own circle… After one round of it, the industry information, the ways of thinking, the life possibilities you come into contact with will be richer than you can imagine."

【The comments were full of envy and agreement:

[Where do I get a divine friend like this!]

[Yao Yuan herself has to be a top-tier social hub! So envious!]

[Honestly — the turning point of my life was meeting a close friend like this, who brought me into the circle I'm in now.]

[At the end of the day you still have to take the initiative yourself, and you have to provide value, not just be a taker.]】

"So, to sum up." Yao Yuan sat up straight and gave her clear, actionable final advice. "Inwardly, cultivate the wisdom of 'softness.' Learn to provide 'emotional value' in intimate relationships, use clever leverage to dissolve conflict, and bring the relationship into a virtuous cycle of mutual nourishment. Outwardly, take the initiative to widen your social boundaries, combine online and offline, and be good at borrowing the strength of 'social hub' friends to open up the situation quickly and build your network."

"Don't let yourself stay trapped long-term in the self-pity and complaints of 'my circle is so narrow,' 'my personality is so stubborn,' 'no one understands me.' Channels are found and fought for by yourself; relationships are managed and maintained with wisdom. When you first become a more open, wiser woman who better understands connection and value, that 'right person' will naturally cross paths with you, or be drawn to you, in a far wider world."

【Call time up; the feed cut off cleanly and decisively.】

The calm, unruffled professional look returned to Yao Yuan's face. She looked into the camera, her tone steady: "A circle isn't something you wait for, it's something you fight your way into. Tenderness isn't weakness, it's a weapon. All right, let's take the next caller."

After a moment of digesting and being stunned, the livestream's comments were once again flooded madly with 【So clear!】【Got the practical guide!】【Going to make plans with my super-social bestie right now!】. And that 25-year-old caller's predicament might not suddenly turn bright from this one speech, but at the very least, with her sharp, profound insight and vivid, living examples, Yao Yuan had cut open a visible path for her and pointed out a set of inner methods she could practice again and again.

========================================

Chapter 64 — Burn "Make Money" and "Raise Your Value" Into Your Soul
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640311.html

Burn "Make Money" and "Raise Your Value" Into Your Soul!
The livestream rolled on. One caller after another came in carrying their own confusion, and left again with new thoughts and new resolve.
"Put the next one through." She spoke up again, her voice already roughened a little after three straight hours on air, but somehow that rasp only added a worn, real texture and a deeper reach to her words. She rubbed the space between her brows without thinking, and the small gesture let slip just how much that level of mental output had cost her. Outside the window, the early-summer afternoon sun over Jinshi was bright to the point of dazzling, falling through the blinds to lay parallel bars of light and shadow across the floor beside her. And she, at the very center of this swirl of light and sound, stayed steady as bedrock.
[Call connected]
Caller (Xiaoya, female voice, 28, her tone hollowed out with exhaustion, a sob barely held down beneath it, like a string pulled tight to the breaking point): "Teacher Yuanyuan, hello. I really… I can't hold on much longer. It feels like my body and my heart have both been completely scooped out — not one drop of energy left. I'm twenty-eight this year, working in operations at a big internet company. The salary sounds decent, but the price is overtime till late into the night as the norm, and weekends I often have to watch the data and respond at any moment. What's worse, I feel like I've already hit the ceiling in this job — no hope of promotion any time soon, and at any moment I could be replaced by someone younger, someone who can pull more all-nighters, someone cheaper."
She sniffed, her words speeding up on their own, the anxiety almost spilling off the screen: "On top of all that, my parents have been nagging me to get married until I'm nearly losing my mind. Every sentence on the phone is, 'Once a girl's past thirty, nobody wants her,' or, 'What's the point of all your striving? Better to find a good family and settle down safe.' But every day I get home from work with barely enough breath left to shower, and I lie in bed with my mind gone numb. Where would I find the energy to meet new people, to nurture a relationship? I look at myself — barely any savings, no love, my career stuck at a wall — and the whole of me is like an old machine with a battery run bone-dry, caked in dust, grinding away numbly and mechanically, with no future in sight anywhere… I even bought your book, I watch your videos all the time. At the time I felt slapped awake, full of strength. But once that surge passed, faced with the chicken-feathers of real life everywhere, I'm still lost. I have the strength, but no direction. I don't know where to put it…"
[The scrolling comments erupted into a flood of recognition, as if a collective emotion had found its outlet:
[She's literally me! A true portrait of a 28-year-old internet drone! Soul scooped clean!]
[+1. That hollowed-out feeling — even crying feels like a waste of energy.]
[Parents nagging about marriage + career wall + self-doubt: a triple knockout, white-knuckling it every single day.]
[Strength but no direction — too real… You drink your fill of motivational soup and still don't know which way the road goes.]
Yao Yuan (she listened quietly through the whole long, suffocating outpouring, not cutting in. She leaned far back into her chair, her sky-blue fingertips tapping once lightly on the smooth desktop, a crisp, cool little sound. She let out a near-inaudible sigh — a sigh that held no cheap pity, but the almost ruthless calm of someone who has seen straight to the heart of a thing):
"Xiaoya, I hear you." Her voice was even, like a river flowing in the dead of night, carrying a force that smoothed over agitation — but cold, too. "Yet through your voice I can't feel the surging 'life force' a twenty-eight-year-old woman ought to have. All I hear is a deep, passively-borne exhaustion, and the panic and numbness of being driven and whipped forward by every external standard there is — your parents' expectations, society's clock, the workplace's KPIs."
She leaned slightly forward, her gaze blazing, as if to pierce the screen and look straight into the other woman's frightened eyes:
"All your pain right now, the confusion, the drained energy — its root is this: you're trying to crack a systemic problem that is multidimensional and exponentially complex, using the single-threaded logic of 'work hard at a job, accumulate in a straight line.' Career achievement, marriage pressure, self-worth, the first pile of capital, your parents' traditional expectations… these problems all tangle together and feed off each other, and the complexity climbs exponentially. And you're counting on more overtime, on forcing yourself onto the 'matchmaking market' to tick off a task, to find a one-and-done answer. It's like —" she paused, and reached for a precise image — "like you've got an eating spoon in your hand and you're trying to dig a tunnel with it. It's not that the spoon isn't trying hard enough. It's that your tools and your strategy were dead wrong from the very start."
[The comments were struck dead by the image, boiling over in an instant:
[Linear logic against an exponential problem! Digging a tunnel with a spoon! So sharp it's suffocating!]
[Got it in one second! It's not that we don't work hard — it's that the direction and the method are completely wrong!]
[So we've been doing useless work this whole time, and half killing ourselves doing it…]
"You feel tired, with no energy left for love, choked breathless by the marriage nagging, like your life has no direction." Yao Yuan turned the blade. She threw out an extremely sharp, even cruel hypothetical: "Then let me ask you, Xiaoya — if right now, this very instant, five million yuan after tax suddenly appeared in your bank account, entirely yours, with no debt against it. Would you still feel that this job grinding you to exhaustion is a weight you can't bear? Would you still spiral into anxiety and self-doubt over your parents' 'hurry up and marry someone'? Would you still feel like a headless fly, with no direction at all?"
Caller (Xiaoya was visibly stunned; the hypothetical was too abrupt and too concrete. She hesitated for several seconds before answering, not quite sure): "…I suppose, it'd be a lot better? At least… my heart would feel a lot steadier. I wouldn't be so afraid of the future. Maybe… I'd even have the nerve to try some different things?"
"See — you've already given the answer yourself, and very clearly." Yao Yuan's voice was even and firm, carrying the undeniable clarity of someone laying bare a cruel truth. "In the real adult world, money of course isn't all-powerful. But it is the most effective lens for shrinking problems and amplifying choices. When your own economic base is thin and your chips are few, any small disturbance — one unpleasant overtime shift, one sigh from your parents, the number of your age ticking up, even someone else's glossy life on social media — gets magnified without limit, turned into a disaster movie that crushes you. But when you hold a real, thick-enough cushion of wealth in your hands, most of that outside noise and pressure automatically downgrades into distant background hum. You gain the nerve to say no, and room to choose."
She paused, her tone now aimed straight at the core, peeling back the emotional surface to expose the kernel of interest and power beneath:
"You feel marriage is enormous pressure — fundamentally that's because, in your subconscious, you assume you're standing in the 'being chosen,' 'being appraised' position. Your sense of security, the quality of your future life, seem to hang on another person's 'choice' and 'ongoing supply.' But if you've laid your own solid economic base, if you have an independent ability to create wealth, then you go from being a 'player' to, in a sense, the 'house' — and the whole picture changes. You no longer have to anxiously go to the 'matchmaking market' to be looked over and priced. You hold the right to filter and to choose actively. Marriage even downgrades from a 'lifeline you can't survive without' to a 'nice-to-have, take-it-or-leave-it option.' If you meet someone and you fit each other — wonderful. If not, you can live a rich, vivid, secure life on your own. Your anxiety is rooted in 'fear of not having,' and wealth is the most solid medicine there is for curing that 'fear of not having.'"
[The comments argued fiercely, viewpoints colliding:
[Cruel, but it seems to be the truth… The economic base determines the superstructure.]
[Isn't she making money out to be too important? What about feelings, sincerity? Don't those matter?]
[But reality is, love without economic independence rarely has equality or dignity. Yuanyuan's teaching us to see this society's rules of the game clearly.]
[My parents are the proof — my mom isn't financially independent, and she's swallowed her grievances her whole life at home.]
Caller (Xiaoya's voice grew urgent, deeper helplessness in it): "Teacher, I understand money matters — I get that! But making money isn't that easy, is it? Right now I work myself half to death every day, trading my life for money, and I still see no breakthrough, no hope — I'm nearly depressed. I genuinely have no idea where to even start 'making money.' It feels like every road is blocked."
"There — the classic 'student mindset' trap, back again!" Yao Yuan's tone sharpened abruptly, edged with the keen, urgent frustration of someone watching iron refuse to become steel. "You think that, like in your school days, if you follow the steps, listen to the teacher (the boss), work hard to finish the homework (the KPIs), and earn good grades (promotion and a raise), you can just wait for wealth to steadily pile up and for life to feel secure? Xiaoya, I've seen too many women at twenty-eight, at thirty, grinding themselves into the ground at work, burning the midnight oil, depleting themselves, only to reap almost nothing — even to be cast aside. The era changed long ago, and the rules of the game changed with it! Working a job won't make you big money — it only lets you 'survive,' or barely even that. If you want to 'live well,' what you need is means of production, leverage to move resources, a profit model of your own."
Her voice softened, drawing in her own lived experience, which made the argument more convincing and more resonant:
"When I first pivoted to building my own media and personal brand, I went through a stretch that was bleak, lost, full of self-doubt too. Watching others seemingly hit their stride with no effort while I couldn't find the door, I was so anxious I lay awake whole nights, my hair falling out in clumps. How did I claw my way out? I made a decision that, at the time, looked cruel and lonely: I steeled myself and cut away every 'useless social connection' and every bout of 'self-congratulatory emotional drain' that couldn't directly bring me a gain in ability or a flow of cash. Every minute, every drop of energy I saved, I focused like a laser on two things. First: like doing academic research, I dug deep into the underlying logic of how different industries make money, the secrets of how traffic gets distributed, the weaknesses and needs of human nature. Second: I threw away my face and poured out value like crazy, connected with people above me, built my one-of-a-kind professional calling card and personal brand."
She shared a specific story, full of that 'Yao Yuan' style and sense of ritual:
"I remember it very clearly — when I truly earned my first ten million in net profit, the first thing I did was go put down a deposit on a Porsche Panamera I'd been eyeing for a long time but always told myself to 'wait a bit longer' on, that it 'wasn't necessary.' I chose my favorite color, with the configuration I most wanted. I did this not to show off, and certainly not out of vanity. I wanted to use that intensely ritual, tangible, weighty and fast material vessel to confirm my ability hard and concretely, to plant my value, to reward my own effort. When I held the wheel alone and truly drove it for the first time, threading through the dazzling river of city night traffic, that solid sense of achievement — built up inch by inch with my own hands — and that absolute sense of control over my life washed away every bit of my old lack of confidence, my fear of others' eyes, my confusion and uncertainty about the future, scrubbed it all clean. In that moment I finally understood, all the way through: money isn't just numbers jumping on a bank card. It's the weapon you use to win back the right to choose your own life, the bedrock on which you rebuild your inner order and reach an ultimate peace, the nerve that lets you freely say 'yes' or 'no.'"
[Comments: [First ten million and she buys a Panamera! That sense of ritual is unreal!]
[Using material things to confirm and reward your own value — what an honest, powerful angle!]
[Honestly, sometimes you just need a solid, real 'proof' like that, to tell yourself: you did it, you deserve it.]
"So, Xiaoya, take one piece of advice from me — the most solid, down-to-earth advice there is." Yao Yuan's tone turned grave, almost a stern warning. "Until the spendable balance in your bank account has stably broken into seven figures (you can set that number to your own situation), until the 'threshold' deep inside you for material security has been fully satisfied — your single most central, highest-priority task in life is one thing and one thing only: cast off every impractical false pride and every wild, idle thought, block out all the noise that drains your energy, and with your whole mind, by every means you can, make money."
"Don't think these words are too realistic, too harsh, too vulgar. The more concrete and quantifiable your goal, the more solid the point you push from, and the more direction your actions have. You love poetry, you long for far-off places, you crave the pure love of souls meeting? Of course you can — these are all beautiful adornments of life. But please first make sure you've built yourself an economic deck from which you can enjoy all of it with ease and a clear mind. Otherwise, all that romance and refinement is like a gorgeous castle built on quicksand: it looks beautiful, but any small wave of reality could collapse it in an instant and let you fall even harder."
She reached for an even more vivid, more piercing image:
"It's like this: every day, on your way to and from work, you pass that three-Michelin-star restaurant you have to book three months in advance. Through the glass you see the women inside, dressed in the season's haute couture, draped in dazzling jewels, savoring exquisite dishes you maybe can't even name, laughing and talking with ease. You can tell yourself the lamb skewers at the night market next door are delicious too, full of warm, smoky life. And yes, lamb skewers are of course tasty. But can you truly enjoy that skewer with a heart entirely untroubled, with no comparison, no longing — genuinely at peace, purely happy? It's hard. Because that 'wanting,' that 'curiosity' about the 'unknown deliciousness,' about 'another way of living,' has been suppressed by your reason and your circumstances. It's like a wooden float pressed under water: every time you push it down hard (telling yourself not to compare, to be content, to keep calm), it stubbornly bobs back up, because of the real unwillingness and longing inside you. This repeated self-suppression and struggle is itself violently draining your psychological energy."
"Real letting-go doesn't come from suppression and persuasion. It comes from having money, having enough nerve, being genuinely able to walk in anytime and unhurriedly order a full table and slowly taste that Michelin meal. And after you've eaten, you might suddenly realize, even be a little disappointed: 'Oh, so that's the taste — it's just this, nothing as magical as I imagined.' In that moment, you're truly free, truly released. After that, when you sit at the night-market stall again, eating your lamb skewers, that taste is pure and happy — because you know clearly you're not sitting there because you 'can't afford it' or 'don't deserve it,' but because 'I've experienced it, and I choose' to sit here, enjoying this plain, lively warmth. Money, at that point, becomes an utterly pure tool: to satisfy an experience, to dissolve an obsession, to widen the borders of your understanding, letting you see more clearly and more calmly what your heart actually wants, and what truly makes you feel happy and fulfilled."
[In the comments, countless people said the image had struck them hard to the core:
[The wooden-float image — I cried. This is exactly my daily mental drain!]
[Turns out I've been desperately suppressing my own desires and ambition, thinking I didn't deserve them…]
[Real freedom is 'I choose,' not 'I have no choice.' Said it straight into my heart.]
[So making money is to gain the freedom to choose, not to be enslaved by desire.]
"When, through your own wisdom, effort, and persistence, your material base reaches and even comfortably surpasses that inner 'threshold' of security and freedom," Yao Yuan concluded, her voice now charged with a powerful force that lifts a person up and forward, "you'll find the whole world's attitude toward you turns gentler. Your parents may stop nagging about marriage, because they've seen with their own eyes that you can live a rich, vivid life on your own, full of nerve. You'll stop being anxious about age, because time has become a friend that builds your experience, wisdom, and wealth — every wrinkle may hide a story and a kind of nerve. As for love and marriage, they'll no longer be the 'lifeline' that rescues you from disaster, but a pure gift that fate hands you on top of everything else. Have it, and it's icing on the cake, two people lighting each other up. Don't have it, and the scroll of your life lived alone is already vast and brilliant enough, full of endless possibility."
"So, Xiaoya — now, right now, stop all the meaningless inward draining!" Her tone was decisive, carrying the rallying force of a charge bugle. "Pull all your attention, all your heart's strength, hard out of those empty emotional vents — 'I'm so tired,' 'I'm so lost,' 'why does no one love me' — and shift it onto these three concrete questions: 'How can I make myself worth more?' 'What skill or interest do I already have that could be turned into cash flow?' 'Whom should I move closer to — which people or things that have already gotten results and can lead me to make money?' Take action, even if it starts from a tiny side hustle, from paying to connect with one industry role model you admire, from grinding away at one small skill the market needs. Getting moving is the cure for all confusion and inner drain. Only action produces real energy and real hope."
Caller (Xiaoya's voice changed markedly — the exhaustion was still audible, but the heavy numbness had been broken, infused now with a new, ship-burning resolve): "I understand, Teacher Yuanyuan! I really heard it! I can't keep spinning in the old thought-trap and the emotional swamp — I have to jump out myself! Making money, improving myself, building irreplaceable value — that's the only thing worth doing at this stage of my life, and the most worthwhile! I know how to adjust where I put my weight and my force now! Thank you!"
Yao Yuan finally let out the first truly relaxed smile of the whole broadcast, warm with encouragement and firm with trust: "That's right. Remember — making good money is the hardest nerve you have at this stage, and the shortest, most solid path to true independence of character and freedom in life. Now, hang up, take out paper and pen, and immediately list three concrete action plans you'll carry out over the next three months to increase your income and raise your value. Get moving — that's how you gather the energy you've let scatter back onto yourself."
[Call time up, line cut]
For a few seconds the studio fell into a strange quiet, as if every viewer were digesting this dose of strong medicine. Then the comments surged up like a volcano erupting:
[Thank you, Yuanyuan, for slapping me awake! No more lying down!]
[Twenty-eight — from now on, focus on making money! Improve myself!]
[Burn "make money" and "raise your value" into my soul!]
[This is real clear-eyed, rational, big-picture leading-lady thinking! Lesson learned!]
Yao Yuan watched the fighting-spirited comments roll across the screen, picked up the insulated cup beside her, and took a slow sip of perfectly warm aged-tangerine-peel white tea, soothing the dry ache in her throat from all the long, intense talking. Her sky-blue nails left a faint water mark on the pure white wall of the cup, like a patch of clear sky mirrored on snow. She let out a small, easing breath, and to the camera her tone returned to its usual cool calm — yet every word was clear and forceful:
"Be a little more clear-eyed, a little more pragmatic, a little harder on yourself. You'll find that the vast majority of the problems that bring you pain, anxiety, and sleepless nights will gradually shrink — even resolve themselves — as your personal value and the number in your account grow solidly."
Having said that last line of conclusion, Yao Yuan gave the camera a polished, perfectly curved smile. She didn't move right away, her gaze resting calmly on the comments still racing across the screen, unsatisfied, hungry for more. Those words — [Thank you, Yuanyuan!] [So good to be slapped awake!] [Going to list my plan right now!] — reflected in her pupils, still as a deep pool, as flowing, faint, but real points of light.

Chapter 65 — Only When You Have the Capacity for Happiness Does Life Have Meaning
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640312.html

Only when you have the capacity for happiness does life have meaning.
The livestream was still running, the viewer count holding steady at a high. In the center of the frame, Yao Yuan shifted slightly in her seat, pressed a fingertip lightly to the spot between her brows, then settled back into that flawless, razor-sharp expression. Behind her, beyond the vast floor-to-ceiling window, the Yellow River flowed on forever—silent, heavy, as if it carried every unspoken word and all the silt and grit that come with growing up. She looked into the camera, her gaze steady and clear as a scalpel.
"Alright. Let's connect with the last caller of the day."
[Call connected]
Caller (Su Jin, female, 32, her voice like a flower wrung dry of all its moisture—soft, withered, hollow with the exhaustion of a long, hard journey): "Teacher Yuanyuan, I... I can't find any meaning in life. I don't know what I'm living for."
Her very first sentence struck straight at the deepest black hole.
"My husband and I... I suppose, in other people's eyes, we're 'winners at life.' Both our families come from similar backgrounds, plenty of assets—house, cars, savings, we want for nothing. We'll probably never have to worry about money for the rest of our lives. I don't have to work. I live in an apartment with the best river view there is, and the children have a nanny and the best private school. To everyone looking in, my marriage is respectable, my life is comfortable, I lack nothing—I ought to be content."
She paused. The sound of her trembling breath came through the microphone, heavy with emptiness.
"But I've been depressed for a long time. I've seen doctors—I have depressive tendencies. I fall apart over the smallest things and feel like nothing matters. I clearly have everything, so why... why can I just not feel any happiness? My heart is always hollow, like I'm living in a lavishly decorated palace that never echoes. Every morning I wake to an endless, exquisite emptiness. I have no goal worth getting up early to fight for, no anticipation that makes my heart race, not even... not one single thing that makes me feel I'm 'useful.' I've tried shopping, travel, flower arranging, yoga—all the things people call 'enjoying life.' They only bring a moment's numbness, like giving a painkiller to a dying person. Once the drug wears off, that vast, all-devouring sense of nothingness comes back even stronger."
"I look at myself in the mirror—my makeup is perfect, my clothes are expensive, but I feel empty inside. I've never created a single thing of value that belongs only to 'me.' My life feels like nothing more than a respectable footnote to my husband's, a backdrop mother in my children's. I have so much—so why do I feel instead that my life is as weightless as a wisp of fog that could scatter at any moment, with no substance and no meaning at all?"
[After a brief stunned silence, the scrolling comments surged.]
[I get this so much. Material needs met, but the spirit left barren.]
[So money doesn't guarantee happiness after all. Feeling more balanced now.]
[Feels like she's been "kept" too well and lost her vitality.]
[She's describing a state of "weightlessness in life."]
[A lack of meaning—is this the terminal illness that comes after material saturation?]
Yao Yuan listened quietly, not responding right away until Su Jin's last trembling note faded. She leaned back into her chair, brought her ten fingertips together against her chin to form a steady triangle—her signature posture when thinking deeply.
"I hear your confusion," she said at last, her voice like a river flowing in the dead of night, calm yet carrying the power to cut through the surface of things. "But first, I need you to recognize one premise: the fact that you can sit here and calmly examine 'I can't find any meaning in living,' instead of frantically scrambling over tomorrow's grocery money or next month's mortgage—that, in itself, is a luxury granted to you by your material foundation."
Her eyes were bright, fixed on the camera as if to drive the point into every listener's mind.
"Because when a person is still struggling on the survival line, all their energy goes to weathering the storms of reality. They have nothing left to spare to tend the garden inside—to ask whether it's flourishing or barren. Material wealth is the ordinary person's ticket into the world of the spirit. You've long since secured a VIP seat. That's your good fortune—and your starting point."
[Scrolling comments]: [Hits the nail on the head! Searching for life's meaning is a luxury you get after you're fed and warm.]
[So it's not that money is useless—it's that money's function has changed.]
[Yuanyuan is always this clear-eyed. Affirm the material first, then talk about transcending it.]
"So, Su Jin, what I'm hearing is not a question about 'the meaning of life.'" She spoke, her voice like the first crack splitting through a sheet of ice—clear and cold. "What I'm hearing is the wail of a fully functional person's life instinct, after being placed for too long in a 'de-functionalized' environment. Your pain isn't because you have too much. It's that the few most core 'capacities' at the foundation of your life have, even as you came to possess all of this, been quietly uninstalled."
