She leaned forward slightly, her gaze like a searchlight aimed straight at the heart of the problem.
"You think meaning is a treasure hidden in some corner of the world, and that once you accumulate enough chips—wealth, marriage, status—you can trade them for the key. Wrong. Meaning has never been an object to be 'found.' It is the 'sense of happiness' that arises naturally when you, as a subject, act, feel, create, and connect in the world. When you stop acting, when your feelings dull, when you can no longer create, when you lose deep connection—you lose the capacity to create happiness. That is the true source of your emptiness: it's because you've lost the capacity to create happiness that you can't find any meaning in living."
[The scrolling comments seemed to hit pause—then exploded.]
[Lost the capacity to create happiness! My skull just got blown clean off!]
["De-functionalized" environment... so we've been kept until we lost our function?]
[So it's a problem with the state of one's life, not a philosophical problem!]
[Yao Yuan can always pull the most mysterious, abstract questions down to a workable level!]
"Which 'capacities,' specifically, have been uninstalled?" Yao Yuan asked and answered herself, raising three fingers, each one like a verdict. "First, the capacity to experience and process emotional tension. You live in absolute safety and ease, so even a tiny emotional ripple—the nanny taking a day off, a child throwing a tantrum—becomes an unbearable catastrophe for you. It's not that you're fragile. It's that your emotional muscles have atrophied from a long lack of 'weight training.' Real life-force exists in the complete process of facing pressure, digesting frustration, and adjusting your stance within it. You skipped that process and went straight to the result of 'calm'—and that calm is stagnant water."
"Second, the capacity to create value and receive feedback. The human brain needs the positive feedback loop of 'effort—overcoming—reward' to release dopamine and confirm 'I exist, I'm useful.' Your life is perfectly 'delivered'—no effort required, nothing to overcome, and the rewards have become things you simply take for granted. It's like being placed in a climate-controlled terrarium where the food arrives automatically: safe, but your instincts to hunt, to plant, to build all fall into disuse. You've lost the joy of the 'producer' and are left only with the emptiness of the 'consumer.'"
"Third—and this is the root—the capacity for self-definition and the construction of your own narrative." Yao Yuan's tone grew heavier, every word like a hammer driving a nail into your cognition. "The script of your life was written by others: rich man's daughter, good wife, good mother. You've played the parts well, but you've never asked yourself: if I tore up all these scripts and roles, who am I? What kind of story about 'me' do I want to tell? The meaning of your life is an external plug-in, running on someone else's server. The moment the main server—your family, society's judgment—fluctuates in any way, or the moment you yourself grow weary of the role, your whole world loses its commands and crashes to a black screen, and up pops a giant error dialog box reading 'no meaning.'"
On the other end of the line, Su Jin let out a very faint, sharp intake of breath, as if a wound that had been tightly bound in invisible bandages had suddenly been sliced open with precision.
"So, you're not happy because you've lost the capacity for 'happiness.' You feel life has no meaning because you've surrendered the right to 'create meaning.'" Yao Yuan's summary was cold as the glint of a scalpel. "These two are two faces of the same thing—different symptoms of the same paralyzed system."
[On the scrolling comments, countless people seemed to be looking into a mirror.]
["External plug-in system"... that's exactly me. Living inside my parents' expectations, and I crashed.]
[The emptiness of the "consumer"—from today on, I'm going to be a "producer"!]
[So we have to take back the right to narrate our own lives!]
"Then... what should I do?" Su Jin's voice carried the exhaustion of being thoroughly dissected, but also a trace of desperate longing to grab hold of something. "Can I still... reboot these functions?"
"Not reboot—rebuild. And you'll have to build with a will tougher than an ordinary person's, because your 'terrarium' is too comfortable. Breaking out of it takes far more strength." Yao Yuan's tone turned clear and practical, like an engineer laying out a repair plan.
"Step one: actively seek out 'beneficial discomfort' and retrain your emotional and value muscle groups. Go right now and do something with a clear threshold—something that requires sustained effort before you see any progress. Not 'elegant pastimes' like flower arranging or yoga, but something genuinely challenging. For example, study for a difficult professional certification related to your interests; spend a year quietly mastering a cuisine to the point where you could host friends with it; go anonymously into a real charity project and take on concrete responsibilities that will meet with setbacks. The goal isn't the certificate, the dinner, or the project's outcome. It's to experience again, in the process of going 'from can't to can,' 'from unable to able,' every ounce of struggle, anxiety, breakthrough, and joy. That is the most primal fuel for a sense of value."
"Step two: undertake a detailed 'rehabilitation of the senses and the mind.' Each day, deprive yourself of one hour of being 'fed'—binge-watching shows, scrolling through other people's glamorous lives. Use that hour for two things. One: deeply experience the most ordinary thing. For example, truly chew a single grain of rice through its whole process from hard to soft, releasing its sweetness; touch the textures of different materials in your home—wood, metal, wool—and feel their temperature and their character. Two: record undirected thought. Write down whatever drifts through your mind, without judging it, without chasing meaning, just observing the flow of your own thoughts. This reconnects you to the real world you've ignored and to your inner self. They are the soil in which a sense of meaning is nurtured."
"Step three—and this is the most crucial step—begin drafting your own 'life narrative.' Answer these three questions, without lying to yourself. One: when I'm completely alone, with no one watching, what makes me lose track of time? Two: setting aside all responsibilities and roles, what is the deepest, never-faced longing or fear inside me? Three: if my life were a novel, what kind of person do I want 'me,' the protagonist, to ultimately become? (Not what to 'have,' but what to 'become.') You don't need to have the answers—the questioning itself is construction. Start from the faintest clues in your answers and make the smallest of attempts. Even if it's just beginning to write a novel no one will read, researching some obscure historical mystery, learning to make a piece of leather goods. When you begin to act according to the draft inside you—however small—you stop being an actor in a script and become the author of your own life. An author never asks what meaning the story under their pen has, because the meaning is right there at the tip of the creating pen."
Yao Yuan paused, letting these instructions sink into the caller—and into the heart of every listener. Then she delivered her final, unifying closing words.
"Su Jin, your life until now has been like a well-equipped ship moored in a calm harbor—safe, but it has never set sail. You thought meaning was on some distant far shore. Now I'm telling you: meaning is not on the far shore. Meaning is in every motion of you raising the sails, trimming the sails, battling the wind and waves, and finally deciding your own heading. The capacity for happiness you've lost—that is this ship's sails, its rudder, its engine. What you need to do now is not gaze toward the empty distance, but repair these instruments on your ship with your own hands."
"There is no perfect template for life in this world. Agonizing over 'the meaning of life' will only trap you where you stand. Ten thousand idle thoughts are worth less than one action. In action, your feelings will revive, your sense of value will be rebuilt, and you will gradually see the outline of the story you long to tell. When you are the creator of your own life, 'happiness' and 'meaning' will no longer be two answers you must search for in pain. They will become the scenery that grows naturally on either side of your path of creation. Do it wrong, and you can adjust. Do it slow, and you can speed up. But standing still will only keep feeding your confusion and helplessness."
"You have an enviable material starting point—that is already enormous good fortune. Don't waste it. Let it be your safety net, then bravely go and become the person who gives her own life meaning and creates the experience of happiness with her own hands. When you begin to do this, you'll find that lavish palace gradually takes on your breath, your traces, your story. It will no longer be just a 'palace.' Slowly, it becomes a 'home.' Now—hang up the phone. Start with the first, smallest action you can think of, the one that takes 'effort' to complete. To move is the only spell that makes 'meaning,' that abstract ghost, take concrete shape as living experience."
[Call time up. The frame cut out amid an atmosphere of utter clarity and a charge to act.]
Yao Yuan leaned slowly back into her chair, her gaze drifting out the window to the Yellow River flowing on as it had since time began. The muddy water said nothing, yet it seemed to say everything—meaning is not in the still scenery on the far bank, but in the eternal, ceaseless flow itself, the flow that shapes mountains and rivers.
She turned her gaze back to the camera and left the day's livestream with its last and calmest words.
"Remember: we respect wealth, because it can buy the ship for exploration. But the oars on that ship, the fire in its hold, the map for the voyage, and the heart that ultimately decides what we sail for—these things, which make up happiness and meaning, can never be bought. They can only be forged by your own hands and honed, again and again, across the rest of your life. May you and I both become worthy helmsmen of our own vessels. Alright—that's where our livestream ends for today."
A few seconds later, she reached out and, with the index finger painted in a cool sky-blue polish, tapped crisply on the [End Stream] button on the control panel.
The screen went dark in an instant, switching to pure black, with only a small line of white text in the bottom right corner: "Stream ended."
The ring light went out with a snap, in sync.
The studio plunged abruptly into a relative dimness and silence, leaving only the bright—almost dazzling—natural daylight of an early-summer Jinshi afternoon flooding in through the window, washing the room in a warm, pale-gold glow. The central air conditioning hummed low and steady, the only background sound at that moment.
Yao Yuan held her upright posture, not moving right away. She leaned back a little, sinking fully into the soft, contoured ergonomic chair, closed her eyes, and let out a barely audible sigh of deep weariness from the depths of her chest. Four straight hours of high-intensity, high-density mental and emotional output—her brain felt as if it had weathered a precise and violent storm. Now that the storm had passed, what it left behind was a spiritual exhaustion sweeping through her whole body, along with a real burning dryness at the back of her throat.
She held that emptied-out posture for about a minute, just breathing evenly, letting her nerve fibers—strung to their absolute limit—slowly slacken and settle back into place. The heart-guarding jade against her chest pressed close to her skin, radiating its constant, unchanging warmth, like a gentle yet resilient current of energy quietly soaking into and soothing her overspent spirit.
Then she opened her eyes. The physical fatigue was still there, but that clear, calm core and sense of control that belonged to "Yao Yuan" had swiftly gathered itself back together. She stood and walked to the window, arms folded, looking down at the roadside trees and the flow of traffic glossy and green under the summer sun. A faintly warm breeze drifted in through the half-open window, stirring a loose strand of hair at her cheek and carrying with it the blurred clamor of the distant city.
The stories, tears, anxieties, despair, and newly risen sliver of hope from the callers during the stream now receded like a tide from the shore of her consciousness. What remained was a deeper observation of and compassion for the collective plight of women in this era, an almost instinctive, powerful sense of responsibility to "do something," and a faint trace of the helplessness of one who acts even knowing it cannot be fully done. She could split the fog, point to the root of the problem, and offer the method and the path—but to actually wade across that reef-strewn river and complete the hard transformation, each individual had, in the end, to walk it step by step themselves, to struggle, to see it through. She could light a stretch of the road, but she could not walk it for anyone.

========================================

Chapter 66 — Flame-Souls
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640313.html

Flame-Souls
Her phone buzzed once against the desktop, a dull droning sound. She walked back, picked it up: her assistant had sent over today's livestream data summary, along with a few business-partnership invitations flagged for her to review first. She skimmed them quickly and efficiently, tapping out a crisp reply on the cold screen with her sky-blue fingertips: "Read it. Numbers look good. Lay out the pros and cons of each partnership and we'll go through them together at the ten o'clock standup tomorrow."
Only after she'd handled these necessary loose ends did she finally step out of that high-energy, responsibility-laden persona of "Teacher Yuanyuan" and return to being plain Yao Yuan.
She dealt with more company emails and a few urgent matters in the study, and the time slipped quietly away. As evening drew near, she picked up the long cream silk shirt-jacket she'd tossed over the back of the sofa, ready to keep her appointment with Jin Lan.
She stepped into her private elevator. The metal doors slid smoothly shut, and the polished, mirror-bright walls of the car threw her reflection back at her with perfect clarity — still immaculate, though a day's fatigue lingered in her eyes that she couldn't quite shake. Only the clashing peach-pink and sky-blue of her outfit declared her unbowed brilliance and vitality. Looking at herself in that mirror, she suddenly remembered something she'd said during the livestream to a girl named Xiaoya: "The more concrete your goal, the more solid your life."
What was her own next "concrete goal"? Beyond continuing to hunt for that faint, elusive thread leading to the mirror-master's descendants, beyond steadily advancing the deep technical integration and commercial partnership with Zhao Yiming's Genesis Engine, beyond the constant iteration and market expansion of the Pretty Big One Emotional AI model... maybe she also owed herself a little time — time to make good on a promise she'd made long ago and kept shelving for one thing or another: the promise of that clear, starry sky over the Gobi.
The elevator reached the silent underground parking garage. Cool air rushed up to meet her, faintly scented with concrete.
She walked toward her reserved space, her steps steady, her heels striking the floor in crisp echoes that rang through the empty garage. The livestream was over, but the night and the life that belonged to Yao Yuan — far more complicated things — were perhaps only just beginning. The city's neon hadn't fully lit yet, but the dusk light already spilled in through the garage exit, dragging a long, silent, resolute shadow out behind her.
On the drive to the Provincial Grand Theater, the city's lamps were just coming on. In Jinshi's summer evening, the day's dry heat was slowly fading, and a lively, languid mood hung in the air. The lights of the restaurants lining both sides of the street were warm, the crowds thick as woven cloth, full of the vivid, fragrant bustle of ordinary life.
Six in the evening, at the main entrance of the Provincial Grand Theater.
Jin Lan was already there, in a sharply tailored navy wide-leg trouser suit of crisp fabric that set off her upright bearing. Her long hair was pulled back into a clean bun, and she carried the steady, capable air unique to a queen of the restaurant world. The moment she saw Yao Yuan, her eyes lit up. She looked her up and down with frank admiration, then shook her head and teased, "Boss Yao, are you here to watch the show, or to crash it? Afraid the dancers up there won't dazzle enough, so you've come to step on stage and outshine them yourself?" Her eyes held nothing but pure, captivated admiration for her friend's presence.
Yao Yuan smiled, walked over, slipped her arm naturally through Jin Lan's, and said lightly, "Can't go embarrassing our Boss Jin and dragging down the average of your social circle, can I?"
The two of them had dinner at "Lanting," a place they'd booked in advance near the theater. The restaurant was elegant and quiet, the dishes refined. Over the meal, Jin Lan was indeed in high spirits. She pulled out her phone, opened the photos and videos saved in her album, and leaned them toward Yao Yuan.
"Here — this troupe, they've been blowing up lately. Look at this lead, here," she said, her fingertip tapping a frozen stage shot on the screen: a figure caught mid-leap in the air, muscle lines carved sharp as knife-work under the harsh light. "Word is he's the soul of the troupe — does all his own choreography, full of ideas. Just look at that body, that control... unreal."
Yao Yuan set down her chopsticks and followed where Jin Lan pointed. The stage lighting in the photos and short clips was strikingly designed, the dancer's figure moving through the frame as lithe as a leopard, full of raw power and a beauty that was precisely calculated. She nodded objectively and gave her professional verdict: "Mm, the foundation looks very solid, the physical expressiveness is strong. He's good."
"I hear their troupe name's interesting too," Jin Lan said, swiping the screen to a section with text. "It's called 'Yanpo' — the 'yan' from flame, the 'po' from soul. The leader-slash-principal dancer's surname is, I think... Zhuge? A compound surname, pretty rare." She read out the name with idle, conversational curiosity.
Zhuge?
Yao Yuan's hand, just reaching for her water glass, paused almost imperceptibly. Her fingertips hung in the air for half a second before they closed steadily around the cup. A name, distant yet unusually vivid, swept across her mind — carrying the damp of a southern plum-rain season, the smell of sweat in a rehearsal studio, and a certain memory both scalding and painful — and left a clear, cool trail behind it.
Zhuge Jinye.
It couldn't be such a coincidence. She denied it instinctively, in her heart. That man who had once crashed into her life like a wildfire out of control — who had loved her fiercely, madly, holding nothing back — and from whom she had finally, brutally, parted because their visions for the trajectory of a life ran in opposite directions. He had talent — could even be called brilliant — but he was proud and sharp-edged, all hard angles. The year they split, his eyes rimmed red, he had said, every word landing like a stone, that one day he would break free of his own road, that he would make her — make the whole world — see, that he would build a Broadway of China. But in the entertainment world, and on the narrow path of pure dance especially, truly breaking through is no easy thing.
"Yanpo"... she repeated the name silently in her heart. She knew it. Once, scrolling her phone late at night, the big-data feed had even pushed a highlight clip of this very troupe's performance in front of her. The man leading the dance in that video — even without a clear view of his whole face, that familiar build, that way of channeling explosive force, and that reckless, all-or-nothing determination in his dancing, as if he meant to burn everything down, himself included — she had recognized him in an instant: that was Zhuge Jinye. She knew he had struggled and floundered in Hangzhou all these years, and that in the end he really seemed to have made a name for himself through that do-or-die ruthlessness and ceaselessly honed craft, his troupe "Yanpo," built around him, gradually heating up, its tour tickets said to be nearly impossible to get. But she had never gone looking, never followed up on the details — like closing a book filled with intense plot and carefully filing it away into some seldom-touched corner of her memory shelf, leaving it to gather dust. It was only a chapter of the past she had long since coolly archived and sealed.
Jin Lan caught, with her sharp instincts, that almost undetectable pause in Yao Yuan, and the faint ripple that flickered through her eyes. She set down her phone, looked at her friend, and a natural note of probing crept into her voice: "What is it, Yuanyuan? From your tone... you both came up as professional dancers. Don't tell me you know him?" She genuinely had no idea about the history between Yao Yuan and Zhuge Jinye. That was a story from the years when Yao Yuan had left Jinshi to make her career in Hangzhou — and Jin Lan's roots, her business, and the vast majority of her social network were all deeply planted in Jinshi. She knew nothing of the specific romantic entanglements of Yao Yuan's southern years.
Yao Yuan had already recovered her composure. She lifted her water glass, took a small sip, her expression its usual calm — even tinged with the indifference of someone mentioning some trivial old matter. "Many years ago, back in Hangzhou, we crossed paths a few times. He's an... old acquaintance, I suppose." She said it lightly, in the tone of someone speaking of a peer she'd met by chance and never grown close to. "I didn't expect he'd actually stick it out and make something of himself. And I certainly didn't expect that the show you so mysteriously dragged me here to see would be his troupe's."
She tucked that thread of stirred, complicated, unfamiliar emotion neatly back down into her heart, gave Jin Lan an easy smile, and tapped the refined dishes on the table with her chopsticks: "Eat up, the food's getting cold. Once we're done we can go see just how 'unreal' our 'old acquaintance' really dances."
Seven in the evening, at the main entrance of the Provincial Grand Theater.
The two of them chatted and laughed as they had their tickets checked and went in. Their seats were dead center in the fifth row — a superb vantage point, close enough to read the dancers' subtlest expressions and muscle control, yet high enough to take in the whole stage's composition and movement. The theater, with a capacity of nearly two thousand, was almost full. The audience was sleekly dressed, mostly fashionable young people, lovers of the arts, and elegant, poised mature women. The air was thick with the lingering notes of various high-end perfumes, the scent of cosmetics, and a suppressed, shared excitement and anticipation.
The lights dimmed gradually, swallowing the last traces of noise like a rising tide. The whole house fell silent, silent enough to hear a pin drop.
The music came crashing in — a magnificent overture mixing ancient Eastern drumbeats, heavy and deep, with the sharp edge of modern electronic effects — seizing every eardrum and heart in an instant. The great curtain drew slowly open. A dim blue overhead light spilled down like moonlight, and on the stage stood several figures, lithe as leopards, arranged in frozen poses full of sculptural weight and the beauty of power — like ancient totems, or like future war machines, holding the force of an explosion within their stillness.
Almost the instant the music began, Yao Yuan sat up a little straighter without meaning to and adjusted her breathing. As a professional trained at the Beijing Dance Academy — a top dance institution — through years of rigorous formal study, even after so many years away from the spotlight, that extreme sensitivity to the language of the body, that exacting eye for the texture of movement, and that instinctive grasp of staging and spatial arrangement, all of it carved into her bones, had never faded. If anything, the sediment of the years had only deepened it. Her gaze turned, in an instant, focused and sharp — like the most demanding examiner, and like a silent observer reunited after a long parting.
The lead dancer broke from the group, slicing apart the stage's stillness in an instant with a string of floor-glides that fused extreme control, explosiveness, and fluidity, linked into jaw-dropping grand jetés, seizing every gaze in the house — it was Zhuge Jinye.
Even across the distance, even with the stage lighting shifting and blurring for effect, Yao Yuan could still clearly make out those distinctive habits of force etched into his body's memory, the path of his shifting axis, and that pursuit of perfect line that ran in his blood. Compared to the youth in her memory — sweating buckets in a Hangzhou rehearsal studio, his brow always knotted with a mix of talent, gloom, and stubborn refusal to lose — the man on stage now had a build more powerful and robust, every muscle straining out clean, forceful lines beneath his tight costume, full of the textured strength that settles only after years of brutal training, countless injuries and healings, and the thousand temperings of the stage. His technique had visibly advanced by leaps and bounds — a qualitative leap, even. His flexibility was astonishing, his jumps light yet full of hang time; the stability, speed, and number of his turns were no longer those of the raw beginner he'd once been, but had reached the top tier of domestic professional dancers, even carrying the marks of internationally minded training.
But what made Yao Yuan hold her breath even more was the intense emotion and narrative force his dancing held, almost spilling over. Every extension seemed to reach for the edge of the sky; every moment of hang time carried a struggling yearning; every halt under a heavy beat struck like a knock against the soul. He was no longer merely executing movements — he was burning with his body, pouring out endless longing, scorching ambition, ripping pain, and the remaking of rebirth. He had woven together modern dance's deep exploration of the body's limits and emotional expression, the raw rhythm and power-aesthetics of street dance, and even, cleverly, the cadence and shape of certain operatic postures — fused seamlessly into a style intensely his own, full of dramatic tension and visual impact. This was no simple piling-up of technique or showing-off of skill. This was expression with a soul, with bone, with flesh and blood.
The other members of the troupe were equally well trained, solidly grounded, their coordination seamless, showing a very high level of professionalism. But Zhuge Jinye was, without question, the absolute core, the soul, the sun. He was like a walking, violent flame, drawing in all the energy and every gaze on the stage, writing across its blank canvas, with sweat and his own body, a savage, magnificent, pain-soaked poem.
Yao Yuan watched in silence, and in her heart gave her objective, cool verdict: he'd done it. And done it far beyond what she'd predicted back then. Not only had he won commercial success on tour, tickets impossible to get — he had also, artistically, found an expressive language all his own, one of tremendous force and resonance, and planted his feet firmly. Back then she'd thought him too obsessive, too heedless of reality, living in a castle in the air. Now it seemed it was perhaps precisely that near-self-destructive obsession and that all-or-nothing gamble that had carried him through the thorns, truly to here — that had built the castle in the air into a solid foundation and a soaring tower.
The performance entered its climax, a male group dance titled "Tempering." Zhuge Jinye's lead section reached a difficulty that beggared belief. Continuous, high-speed fouetté turns flowing into air turns, the landing controlled with precision into a full split, immediately followed by a fierce front tuck-roll into a high-difficulty "exploring the sea" pose... The transitions from one movement to the next were like flowing water and drifting cloud, the shifts between power and suppleness flawless, his control over the core muscles reaching an inhuman level. The knowing members of the audience couldn't help letting out low, stifled gasps. The fingertips Yao Yuan had resting on the armrest tapped lightly, almost imperceptibly — the involuntary, unconscious response of an insider witnessing something exquisite, technique at its absolute peak. His progress and maturity were genuinely striking, enough even to stir respect.
The final dance was Zhuge Jinye's solo, "Embers." The music turned ethereal, plaintive, and vast, like wind over open wilderness. He stripped off the decorative outer robe and wore only the simplest black dance trousers; his bare upper body gleamed with crystalline sweat, like oil-rubbed bronze under the lights, his muscles heaving and trembling with his heavy breathing and his absolute physical control, full of an almost tragic, primal, sacred masculine beauty. He rolled across the stage, curled in on himself, struggled to rise, then seemed crushed down again by an invisible weight, kneeling and prostrate once more... like a soul wandering in the ashes of fate and time, refusing to die, refusing to admit defeat. At last, in a posture of utter exhaustion, he sank to one knee, lifted his head, gazed up at the single beam of light overhead, his throat rolling violently up and down, his chest heaving like a bellows, and in his eyes there seemed to flash a broken glimmer of water — a gaze blank, fragile, scarred all over, yet in its deepest depths still stubbornly flickering a faint, unwilling-to-die spark of starlight.
The music, at its most extreme, cut off all at once.
The lights snapped dark.
The whole house plunged into an absolute, vacuum-like dead silence. As if even breath had been stolen away.
Then came the deafening applause and cheers, nearly enough to lift the roof off the theater, erupting like a tsunami, going on and on without end. Many in the audience leapt to their feet in their excitement, clapping madly, shouting.
The lights came up again, slowly, much softer now. The whole troupe took the stage for their curtain call. Zhuge Jinye stood at the very front, breathing lightly, a few strands of sweat-damp hair clinging to his sharply angled brow. He led the dancers in deep bows toward the audience in every direction, and as he straightened, his face wore the dazzling, sincere smile of a king of the stage — clearly weary, clearly satisfied. His gaze swept across the tide of warm faces below, and in a voice gone a little hoarse but full of infectious feeling, he spoke his thanks.
And then his gaze, as if yanked suddenly by some invisible thread of fate, fell — without warning, with precision, and held there, locked — on dead center of the fifth row, on the figure who, even in the dim of the audience, was impossible to miss, impossible to ignore, for the dazzling peach-pink and the radiant presence she carried.
Yao Yuan.
In the instant their eyes met, time seemed stretched, warped, frozen without limit.
That professional, brilliant smile on Zhuge Jinye's face froze in an instant, like a perfect mask shattered. His pupils contracted sharply, shrinking to a point, and within them roiled an unbelievable storm of towering waves. His fingers, gripping the bouquet, tightened unconsciously, the knuckles paling white with the force. Every sound on the stage — his companions' words of thanks, the audience's cheers, even his own heartbeat — seemed in that moment to recede at high speed, going faint, dissolving into a blurred background noise. His entire world shrank abruptly to nothing but a single figure in the audience: the woman in the peach-pink dress, a cream silk jacket over her shoulders, her makeup flawless beyond reproach, gazing back at him with a pair of tranquil eyes — calmly, even with a trace of professional appraisal.
How many years? Through those days and nights in Hangzhou with no end in sight, sweat soaking every inch of the floor; in the moment of winning an international competition with no one to share the highest joy; behind the successful tour, the flowers and applause he received, and the sense that some part of him still felt hollow... He had, on countless weary or exhilarated nights, imagined all sorts of reunion scenarios. When he'd achieved fame and stood on the highest podium; when he'd returned home in glory, surrounded by flashing cameras; in any moment when she might be down on her luck, or perhaps ordinary. He'd meant to use his own real, solid success to make her see, to make her regret, to make her know what kind of burning energy — destruction and creation marching side by side — she had underestimated back then.
But when this moment truly arrived, in this utterly unprepared way, across a mere dozen-odd meters and a surging sea of people, looking at those tranquil, distant eyes of hers, carrying their clear note of appraisal and even a trace of pure appreciation (the way one appreciates a fine work of art) — all the satisfaction, the mockery, the show of force and release he had rehearsed countless times burst like colored soap bubbles pricked by the lightest touch of a needle, vanishing without a trace in an instant. In their place surged a more violent, more complex, more inexpressible torrent, brutally scouring over the heart's levee he had thought so solid — was it embers not yet fully extinguished, suddenly fanned up by a wild wind? Was it the sharp pain and unwillingness that even the long years had failed to wear smooth? It was enormous pride, the daze of time and space out of joint, the disbelief of sheer coincidence — and a thread of hidden, intense throbbing that even he found deeply repugnant and alarming in himself.
Yao Yuan, too, clearly received the lock of his gaze — that gaze scalding, complex, as if bearing a thousand pounds, meaning to pierce straight through her. She did not flinch, did not panic; even the calm, unruffled expression on her face barely changed. She simply met that gaze, which seemed to hold ten thousand unspoken words, and — very slightly, but in a way she made sure he could clearly see — nodded.
It was not the signal of an old lover at a reunion, not a friend's greeting after a long parting. It was a professional acknowledgment, from a peer and an elder (at least a senior of the same training lineage). It was an objective verdict and a salute to his entire performance tonight, his artistic achievement, his astonishing transformation. Polite. Clear. Cleanly bounded.
Zhuge Jinye's heart felt, at that cool little nod, fiercely clenched, then abruptly released, leaving a sharp ache and a hollowness behind. She had seen. She had acknowledged his dancing, his art, the heights he'd reached. But that was all. That was it. In her eyes, he no longer had anything to do with romance — he was simply a respectable, excellent dancer.
The next second, a companion beside him touched his arm lightly, without a trace, a low reminder. He jolted awake from that dazed trance, and his formidable professionalism snapped back into place in an instant. He lifted the bouquet in his hand once more, offered the audience an even more dazzling, almost flawless smile, his voice carrying through the microphone to every corner of the theater — only, if you listened carefully, you could catch a faint, hard-to-detect hoarseness, the strain of something forcibly suppressed: "Thank you, Jinshi! You're incredible! I love you all!"
His gaze finally moved away from Yao Yuan completely, turning toward the broader, more fervent reaches of the audience, his smile radiant, as if that brief, illusion-like meeting of eyes and that moment of slipped composure had never happened at all.
The performance let out, the crowd thick as woven cloth surging slowly toward the exits.
Jin Lan and Yao Yuan moved out with the flow, unhurried. Jin Lan glanced sideways, studied her friend's calm, unruffled profile, opened her mouth and then thought better of it; a flicker of understanding and concern crossed her eyes. But in the end she asked nothing. She only reached out and patted Yao Yuan's arm gently, passing along wordless support. She was an extremely intelligent woman. The brief, silent storm of towering waves that had passed between stage and seat — perhaps she didn't fully understand its origins, but she had keenly caught the complex tension and history it held, and she knew that silent companionship at this moment was worth far more than any presumptuous questioning.
They stepped out of the theater, and the summer night's warm breeze, carrying the scent of greenery, rushed up to meet them, quickly dispersing the slight stifling closeness left by the theater's overly generous air conditioning. The plaza was bright with lights, the crowd gradually splitting off and dispersing, the clamor subsiding.
"Well, Boss Yao, what score does the professional judge give?" Jin Lan asked with a smile, her tone easy, steering the topic toward the safe ground of technique. "Want to go for a drink, talk over your professional take? Or just head back and rest?"
"Let's head back. I'm a little tired." Yao Yuan drew the thin cream silk jacket closer around herself. That stroke of peach-pink, lit by the night and the plaza lights, was still dazzling, drawing repeated sidelong glances from the scattered passersby. Her expression was perfectly at ease, even with a faint, contented smile at the corner of her lips, the kind that comes after a fine performance — as if that show, charged with invisible tension and the collision of memory, had truly been, for her, nothing more than a high-quality, savored, purely artistic experience and professional viewing.
"All right." Jin Lan said no more, nodding crisply. Her driver had already pulled the car smoothly up to them.
Yao Yuan got into her own car but did not start the engine right away. She took off the diamond hoop earrings that had swayed at her ears all night, dropped them casually into her handbag, and gently rubbed the earlobes left a little sore from the earrings' weight. Her gaze, though, drifted involuntarily out the window, sweeping over Zhuge Jinye's powerful, story-laden silhouette on the giant poster on the theater's outer wall. The heart-guarding jade at her chest, through the fabric, sent up its constant, unchanging warm tenderness, quietly soothing that faint ripple in her heart that had been stirred up.
The phone screen lit up in the dim interior, its cold blue glow falling across her face. It was a message from Zhao Yiming — about some key data summaries and problem analyses from the latest integration-testing phase between the Pretty Big One Emotional AI model and the Genesis Engine. Rational, clear, going straight to the core, full of the exploratory passion and rigorous attitude toward unknown territory, pointing steadily at a future of challenge and limitless hope.
She looked at the message, then lifted her eyes again to the theater plaza outside, slowly returning to quiet, gently wrapped in the night. The faint ripples that had risen in her heart — stirred by that fierce old figure, the burning stage, and the complicated memories — were swiftly settling, sinking, finally returning to a deep, profound calm.
The ashes and shadows of the past might be lifted now and then by the fickle winds of fate, but they could no longer scald her — not the foundation she had built up with her own hands, growing broader by the day. And the blueprint of the future, that chapter drawn together by reason, wisdom, partnership, and limitless possibility, was waiting for her — composed and cool-headed, yet full of power — to add, stroke by stroke, even grander, even more brilliant chapters.
She started the car, the engine giving a low, steady roar. She flicked on the turn signal smoothly and merged into Jinshi's dazzling, never-ceasing summer-night traffic, bright as a river of stars. That stroke of dazzling peach-pink vanished at last into the city's shifting, kaleidoscopic river of light, leaving behind only a faint, almost imperceptible aftertaste in the air — belonging to the height of a summer night, fervent and utterly clear-eyed at once — scattering on the wind.

========================================

Chapter 67 — The Gobi Data Center
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640314.html

The Gobi Data Center

Time slipped away like sand through her fingers, and before she knew it, another month had passed.

The search for the descendants of the mirror-masters was like hunting for one particular grain of sand in the whole Gobi—the progress was painfully, maddeningly slow. Old Chen quietly worked his old connections to dig for clues; Shuai Hongqiang put out feelers through certain back channels; and Yao Yuan herself reached in every direction, using the web of information she had built up through the Pretty Big One. In the end, all of it added up to a single lead, so faint it was almost a wisp of nothing—it seemed to involve a family with the surname "Wang," whose ancestors may have included practitioners of the old occult arts, active long ago in the Shaanxi-Gansu region. The dates were ancient, no one knew whether the line had survived unbroken, and where its descendants might be today was anyone's guess. The lead was thin as a cicada's wing, yet it weighed on her like a thousand pounds: the one faint glimmer in the heavy dark, impossible to abandon, yet with no clear place to begin chasing. On the phone Shuai Hongqiang's voice was full of guilt and urgency, but Yao Yuan, by contrast, stayed calm, answering only: "Having a direction is good. Keep looking. There's no rush."

Maybe it really was the protection of the heart-guarding jade Old Chen had given her, or maybe it was that the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror, locked away in the deep, had lost whatever it leaned on. Either way, this past month Yao Yuan's mind had been unusually steady. Those sudden waves of vertigo, that weightless feeling of being dragged somewhere else, never came back. She slept solid and deep at night, and went through her days bright and full of energy. Even the sharp edge she carried on her livestreams now seemed plated with a warmer, more settled light. The warmth of the old jade against her chest had become a firm barrier between her and that eerie curse, and a quiet, secret reassurance deep in her heart.

In the very hottest stretch of high summer, the third deep model-fusion between the Pretty Big One's emotional core and Zhao Yiming's Genesis Engine reached a critical milestone.

In the fusion lab, on the enormous screen, the intricate branches of the Pretty Big One's emotional decision trees were being docked, infiltrated, and rebuilt against the vast, nebula-like node network at the base of the Genesis Engine—all through some delicate algorithmic protocol. Data streams poured down like a river of stars; indicator lights flashed like mad. Yao Yuan and her core algorithm team, separated by a glass curtain wall from Zhao Yiming's engineers, watched every parameter's flicker without blinking.

After a full forty-eight hours of sleepless joint testing, the last interface protocol passed validation. The two formerly separate systems of light and shadow on the screen finally merged completely, forming a single seamless picture—one that kept evolving in real time, flickering with sparks of intelligence. The lab erupted in a cheer that had been held back far too long.

"Third fusion, core channels all open, baseline efficiency up one hundred seventy percent, emotional-intent recognition accuracy breaking ninety-one percent, complex-relationship inference logic self-consistency hitting eighty-nine point seven percent!" Zhao Yiming's chief engineer—a stern middle-aged man who rarely cracked a smile—announced the preliminary numbers, his voice trembling with excitement.

It meant that the envisioned "Emotional Super-Advisor" AI core—the one that could deeply understand the games people play with one another and offer real, workable strategy—had finally crossed the chasm from zero to one in its hardest, deepest, most foundational construction. What came next was to take this powerful "heart" and refine its "blood vessels" and "nervous system," to fill it with ever more vast and varied "blood" (data), and only then to give it a concrete "body" and "voice," to finally present it to users.

Zhao Yiming walked over to Yao Yuan. His eyes were bloodshot, stubble had pushed up along his jaw, but his gaze was startlingly bright. "It worked," he said, looking at her, his voice rough with exhaustion yet carrying enormous satisfaction. "The hardest bone—we've gnawed it clean."

Yao Yuan was just as exhausted, but her mind sat in a strange calm that comes after a high. She looked at the fused map on the screen, glowing with data, the crystallized labor and wits of countless people, let out a soft breath, and gave Zhao Yiming a smile that was genuine and radiant. "You've worked hard, Boss Zhao. Our 'child' finally has a healthy, strong 'heart.'"

At the celebration banquet the mood ran warm. Zhao Yiming raised his glass to everyone who had taken part, and at last his gaze settled on Yao Yuan. "Next comes optimization and turning this into a product. We'll need to work even more closely together. Boss Yao," he paused, then issued the invitation, "the Genesis Engine's home turf is out in the Gobi. Phase two of the data center just finished its final tuning, with the newest quantum-computing prototype unit hooked in as an auxiliary. Any interest in bringing your people out to see it in person—the 'body' this 'heart' will live in someday, and the 'universe' it runs in? Think of it as a team outing, too. The summer night sky over the Gobi is worth seeing."

The invitation was reasonable on every count: a deepening of the technical partnership, and a chance to draw the teams together. Yao Yuan barely hesitated before agreeing. "All right. I've been wanting to see with my own eyes what kind of 'beast' can swallow that much computing power."

Three days later, at dawn, a convoy of three off-road vehicles pulled out of Jinshi and headed northwest, into the depths of the Gobi. Yao Yuan brought two of her core executives and her head of technology; Zhao Yiming's side came similarly staffed. The journey was four hundred kilometers. The first stretch ran along expressway, oases and farmland still visible on either side, but the farther west they went, the more the green receded like a retreating tide, and the land gradually bared its most primal, coarse, immense face.

The sky was that extremely high, extremely distant blue, scoured clean year after year by wind and sand, without a single cloud. As far as the eye could see lay the endless Gobi flats, paved with black gravel, dotted here and there with stubborn camel-thorn and red-willow thickets, like blood vessels bulging up on the land's parched skin. The far mountains were a dull ochre-red, their lines hard and steep, standing silent on the horizon like guards out of the depths of time. The wind was dry, carrying the scorch of sunlight and the grit of sand; on the face it stung in a way that cleared the head.

By afternoon the convoy reached its destination.

What met the eye first was not the sprawling complex of buildings she had imagined, but a long, low wall of some special material, almost the same color as the Gobi itself. They passed through a checkpoint set with layer upon layer of verification, and the view suddenly opened wide.

It was a... strange presence, hard to describe in ordinary architectural terms.

It looked nothing like any traditional data center—more like a silver metallic ecosystem set into the earth, brimming with a sense of the future. The main buildings were several huge, flattened-oval, silver-gray pods, half-embedded beneath the solid ground at angles that satisfied both mechanics and aesthetics. Their surfaces were sheathed in optical material that adapted to the environment, throwing back a cold, pure metallic sheen under the blazing sun—a starkly jarring contrast against the barren surroundings. Between the pods ran transparent giant pipes, pale blue coolant flowing through them, like the arteries of a living thing.

Farther out stood a matrix of wind turbines on a staggering scale, their huge blades turning slow and steady in the never-ceasing Gobi wind, and great fields of deep-blue solar panels, like a layer of scales laid over the Gobi to drink in light and heat. Every power line was neatly buried underground; almost nothing cluttered the surface.

"All the electricity here is over eighty-five percent self-supplied," Zhao Yiming's voice rose in the wind as he pointed at the power arrays. "Wind and sun are the Gobi's most generous gifts. The geology under our feet is unusually stable, far from any fault line. The average annual temperature is low and dry—a natural, ideal environment for heat dissipation. To make the most of that natural cold source, the main buildings sink below ground, and inside we use our own composite system that combines indirect evaporative cooling with liquid cooling. PUE—power usage effectiveness—can be pushed to a stunning 1.05 or lower."

He led Yao Yuan's group into the main pod. Inside it was utterly clean, ordered, and cold. In the soaring space stood row upon row of neatly aligned racks, like black metal monoliths, their dense indicator lights blinking like breath, giving off a low, far-reaching hum that seemed to rise from deep in the earth. Not a single stray cable was in sight; everything was sealed into uniform modular units. The temperature held steady at around twenty degrees, the humidity extremely low; even in the special anti-static jackets, you could still feel a faint chill. Technicians in matching uniforms quietly inspected and worked on the transparent gangways and platforms, like priests of a future world tending these silent "gods" of vast computing power.

"This—Zone A—is the main compute array already carrying the Genesis Engine 1.0 and part of the 2.0 modules," Zhao Yiming reeled it off like family treasures, his tone carrying a builder's pride and an almost reverent focus. "Next to it, Zone B, is the just-tuned phase two. We've reserved enough space and power capacity specifically to carry our fused 'Emotional Super-Brain' and its future iterations. Every rack group has independent, redundant power, network, and cooling; a single module failing won't affect overall operation. Data travels over a multiply-encrypted, physically isolated 'air-gap' network. Core data is generated, trained, and iterated right here, and never leaves. The backup data center sits inside a mountain thirty kilometers away, synced in real time over dedicated fiber."

Yao Yuan listened and watched in silence. She was no tech fanatic, but standing now inside this "temple of the future" poured out of barrenness with cutting-edge technology, enormous capital, and extraordinary willpower—even though she had already glimpsed this scene back when she'd traveled through time into the days of the thirty-three-year-old Yao Yuan—seeing it again with her own eyes still struck her with a deep awe. This was no laboratory toy. This was hard, foundational bedrock already up and running, ready to support the next wave of artificial intelligence. Zhao Yiming's ambition, his scope, his ability to execute—all of it found its most direct expression here. The executives and engineers she had brought were long since stunned by the sight before them, murmuring to one another, their eyes alight.

The tour lasted several hours. Toward evening, the convoy Zhao Yiming had arranged carried everyone to a "desert town" tourist center more than twenty kilometers away, on the edge of the county seat. The town was clearly a carefully planned creation of recent years—tidy streets, buildings blending modern minimalism with the character of northwestern dwellings, inns, restaurants, specialty shops, even a small desert museum, all of it there. Yet the overall atmosphere still kept the rugged, open feel of the Gobi frontier.

The welcome dinner was not set at a restaurant in town, but deliberately chosen on a flat patch of sand at a campsite outside it, opened up specifically for visitors to experience—its whole selling point being "nostalgia" and "wild fun." A huge bonfire had already been lit, dead Euphrates poplar wood and red-willow roots crackling in the flames, the leaping tongues of fire dyeing the surrounding dunes a warm orange-red. A plump roast lamb turned on a special spit, worked by a local old master; fat dripped into the fire, sparking up brighter flames and a tempting smell of char. The long tables were laid out with great platters of mutton, naan, assorted cold dishes, and locally brewed, none-too-weak liquor served in rough clay bowls—both a local specialty and a quick medium for closing the distance between strangers.

There were no delicate utensils, no fussy etiquette. The Gobi night wind, still carrying the day's warmth, brushed across everyone's face. Both teams quickly broke past their initial reserve, and under the spell of the bonfire, the food, and the wine, they merged into one lively crowd. They laughed and talked loudly, toasted one another, traded stories of what they'd seen that day, and dreamed aloud about the future of the partnership project. The strong liquor went down like a line of fire, burning warmth into their chests, flushing their faces red.

Yao Yuan, too, had changed out of her daytime formal wear into a loose, cream-white linen dress, a thin knit cardigan over it, her long hair braided into a loose plait falling to one side. Holding a rough clay bowl, she sipped the wine the locals pressed on her in small mouthfuls, feeling that coarse heat burn all the way down her throat, drawing up a strange relaxation and high spirits. The firelight played across her face, light and shadow leaping, stripping away her usual shrewdness and distance, revealing a rare beauty—languid and vivid.

Zhao Yiming sat not far off on a wooden stump, also holding a bowl of wine, shirtsleeves rolled to his elbows, the profile of his face clean-cut in the firelight. He didn't say much, mostly listening, answering now and then, his gaze drifting unintentionally again and again over Yao Yuan. Seeing her eyes lit bright by the fire and the relaxed smile at the corner of her mouth, a faint ripple stirred in his own eyes too.

After a few rounds of wine the mood grew freer still. No one knew who started it, but someone put on music—a bright melody thick with the flavor of the western regions. A few local staff and tourists were the first to stand and dance a simple dance around the bonfire. Soon the younger, more uninhibited members of both teams joined in too, laughing and giggling. Their movements were clumsy but full of joy, and the laughter, the music, and the crackle of the flames mixed together, carrying far across the open Gobi night.

At some point Zhao Yiming came over. Seeing the slight haze the wine had brought to Yao Yuan's eyes, he sat down on the empty stump beside her. Half an arm's length apart, the two of them watched the lively, joyful scene together. Above their heads the starry sky unrolled in silence, fading from deep blue into ink-black, the Milky Way as clear as a glowing road of milk, stretching across the vault of heaven. Billions of stars glittered, cold and brilliant, looking quietly down on this small, warm bonfire on the Gobi, and on these briefly joyful lives gathered around it.

========================================

Chapter 68 — Starry Sky Over the Desert (Side Story)
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640315.html

Starry Sky Over the Desert
Starry Sky Over the Desert
Far from the light pollution of the city, the night sky here was vast, deep, and so pure it made the heart ache. A person felt gently wrapped in the boundless universe, and at the same time small as a speck of dust. All the daytime scheming, the worries, the unsolved puzzles — under this sky they all seemed to shrink to nothing.
"Every time I feel boxed in by some specific problem and can't see my way out, I drive out here alone, sit for a while, and look at the stars." Zhao Yiming spoke suddenly, his voice soft, as if half to her and half to himself. "I look at this 'fortress' I helped build with my own hands — one that tries to understand intelligence, even to create it — and then I look up at this universe overhead, billions of years old, still silent and full of mystery. And I realize that humanity's so-called hard problems, technology's so-called frontiers, set against that scale, are nothing more than a child learning to walk. But there's a kind of solemn, tragic beauty in that first walking, and it's worth giving everything to."
Yao Yuan turned her head to look at him. The firelight danced across his face. He gazed up at the stars, his eyes focused and faraway, holding the reason and hunger to explore that she knew so well, and something she rarely saw in him — the loneliness and rapture that belong to an idealist. In this moment he was no longer the rigorous scientist or the shrewd businessman, but more like a child of nature gazing up at the sky from the wilderness.
"You've done things most people wouldn't even dare to imagine, let alone pull off," Yao Yuan said quietly, her tone sincere with genuine admiration.
Zhao Yiming drew his eyes back and looked at her. The firelight flickered deep in his dark eyes. "Yuan." He said her name, dropping her surname in private for the first time, his voice steady but carrying a gravity she had never heard from him before. "Do you remember my 'white paper'? About the system interaction protocol, about the risks, the returns, about… shared governance and trust."
"I remember." Yao Yuan's heart gave a small jump, though her face stayed calm.
"All the rational reasoning, the risk assessments, the protocol clauses — tonight, under this campfire and these stars, they all feel a little pale and clumsy." Zhao Yiming looked at her, his gaze open and direct, the way it was when he dissected a technical problem. "But I still believe in the value of that method, because at the very least it guarantees that the foundation we start from is solid and clear-eyed. Right now, though, I want to skip all the complicated reasoning in between and ask you something straight — something rooted in my own feelings, not in a business deal."
He paused, as if gathering his courage, or making a final check, and then said clearly, "Yao Yuan, I like you. Not admiration, not approval — the way a man likes a woman. I want to build a connection with you that's deeper, more intimate, and more complicated than business partners. When I explore that unknown frontier between technology and human nature, I want you beside me. And in the moments I feel lonely — like now, watching the stars — I want to turn my head and see you there. Will you give me a chance? Let's try and see whether two complex, independent systems, beyond a rational protocol, can build a coordination that's emotional, profound, and exclusive."
His confession still carried that Zhao Yiming flavor — clear, direct, goal-driven, not even forgetting to mention "systems" and "coordination" — but its core was scalding and sincere. No flowery words, no romantic build-up; and there beneath the desert sky, in the crackle of the campfire, it carried a weight all its own.
Yao Yuan didn't answer right away. She lifted the rough clay bowl and tossed back the last small mouthful of strong liquor inside. That burning sensation swept down through her again, making her cheeks hotter while her mind stayed strangely sharp. She looked at Zhao Yiming — this man she respected in his field, who made her feel steady in the way he dealt with people, and who now showed her a rare, tender side.
The old jade against her chest lay warm on her skin, a reminder of those unresolved tangles of fate. But in this moment, with the sky vast and the fire warm, with strong liquor running through her veins and his eyes fixed on her, full of expectation, she suddenly felt that perhaps she didn't have to wait for every last fog to clear before reaching out to touch a possible happiness. The future was always unknowable; the only thing she could hold was a clear-eyed choice in the present.
"Zhao Yiming," she said, using his name too, her voice slightly hoarse from the liquor and the emotion but clear in every word. "I admire you as well, and… I even like the way it feels to be with you, to work with you. I still agree with the principles of your 'white paper,' especially 'shared governance' and 'boundaries.' So my answer is: yes, we can try. But just as the protocol says, public and private must stay separate. Our feelings are a chapter of their own. They shouldn't sway the partnership decisions between the 'Pretty Big One' and the 'Genesis Engine,' and they can't become a stumbling block to each other's careers. If one day the feelings are gone, the basis for working together still has to remain — we have to be able to step back gracefully into the position of partners. Can you accept that?"
It was a classic Yao Yuan answer — even at the very start of accepting a relationship, she clear-headedly drew the boundaries, set the rules in advance, and laid out a way back. But that was exactly the kind of candor Zhao Yiming could understand and admire.
A smile broke across his face — relieved, and brilliantly, expansively bright, as if he'd just solved some major technical problem that had plagued him for ages. "Of course. That was part of the protocol to begin with, and it's what I expect too." He nodded gravely, then added, "And I think a healthy relationship should make both people better, not drag each other down. Our partnership will only grow steadier and more efficient because of this extra trust and understanding."
The deal was struck. No embrace, no kiss — just a smile shared across the dancing light and shadow of the fire. A brand-new relationship, full of exploration and anticipation, quietly took shape beneath that ancient sky.
"Shall we dance?" Zhao Yiming suddenly held out his hand, an invitation, laughter and challenge in his eyes. "Purely a private celebration. Nothing to do with the partnership."
Yao Yuan looked at his outstretched hand, then at the lively, exuberant crowd dancing nearby. Some lock inside her seemed to loosen quietly. She placed her hand in his palm, stood up, and gave a radiant smile. "All right. Let's dance."
Yao Yuan watched the boisterous but utterly chaotic "free-for-all" in front of her and raised an eyebrow with a laugh, the smile carrying a touch of professional teasing in the firelight. "This improvised stuff — I really can't do it. But," she said, turning sharply, a sly glint in her eyes, "if you'd like a taste of the professional version, I could improvise a little something."
Zhao Yiming was clearly drawn in by her words and her manner, and asked with real interest, "Oh? A top graduate of the Beijing Dance Academy improvising — now that I can't miss."
Yao Yuan shook her head with a smile and didn't take up his "professional" thread, only put her hand in his outstretched palm and, riding the liquor and the mood, rose to her feet with easy abandon. "Come on. When in Rome — let's just dance for the fun of it."
Zhao Yiming led her by the hand into the joyful crowd wrapped in firelight and music. The music was an unrestrained folk rhythm with no fixed steps. Yao Yuan hadn't taken a stage in years, but the sense of rhythm and bodily control carved into her bones were still there. She didn't try to show off; she simply moved with the beat, swaying her body freely and easily. Simple steps and gestures, done by her, took on an indescribable fluidity and grace. In the glow of the fire her figure unfurled, carrying a charm that was both effortless and bewitching.
Zhao Yiming was no dancer, but he was clever and well-coordinated, and he quickly caught her rhythm. The two of them faced each other, moving freely with the music, their eyes tangling in the air, the smiles flowing in their gazes. The firelight stretched their shadows long, casting them onto the golden dunes, weaving them together with countless other swaying silhouettes. The kick of the strong liquor, the joyful atmosphere, the new relationship under the stars — all of it let Yao Yuan relax completely. Her smile was bright, light shifting in her eyes, as if she'd returned to a younger, freer time. Zhao Yiming's gaze followed her the whole while, full of undisguised admiration and delight.
The night deepened. The campfire slowly died down, and the crowd drifted off one by one, returning to the comfortable inns of the small town to rest. Zhao Yiming and Yao Yuan lingered at the very back, walking slowly along the edge of the dunes; neither said anything about heading back. The starry sky hung low, as if within reach. The land lay silent on every side, broken only by the faint sound of wind brushing over the sand. The lights of the town glowed gently in the distance, sketching out a peaceful outline.
Zhao Yiming stopped before a few sturdy, comfortable stargazing tents, set up specially for tourists who came to watch the sky. "Want to take a look? The view here is the best, and it's absolutely quiet." He pointed at one of them.
Yao Yuan nodded. The two of them stepped inside. The space within was roomy, the floor laid with a thick, soft faux-fur rug, the furnishings simple but complete. The top was made of a special transparent material, letting you gaze up at the stars with nothing in the way. They lay down side by side, the soft rug beneath them, the flowing Milky Way overhead. There was no lamp — only starlight to pick out the dim shapes inside the tent.
A strange hush and intimacy filled the small space. They could hear each other's faint breathing.
"It's beautiful," Yao Yuan sighed softly, her whole spirit carried off by that pure, vast sky.
"Mm." Zhao Yiming answered, turning onto his side, gazing at her profile in the starlight. His eyes no longer hid anything; they held warmth as they slowly traced the lines of her brows, her nose, her lips.
Yao Yuan felt his gaze and turned her head too. Their eyes met. Under the starlight they could see clearly the reflection of themselves in each other's eyes, and the attraction rising between them that reason could no longer fully hold down.
Zhao Yiming's hand gently covered hers where it rested at her side, fingertips touching, then slowly lacing together. His hand was warm and dry, lightly calloused. He made no further move, only held her like that and watched her, as if waiting for a final permission, as if confirming that this was real.
Yao Yuan didn't draw her hand back. She looked into his eyes, close enough to touch — and beyond the light of reason in them, they were now brimming with tender starlight and a longing and restraint she had never seen in a man before. The old jade at her chest grew slightly warm, but it was no longer a warning, more like some silent resonance. She closed her eyes, then slowly opened them again, and then, ever so slightly, leaned forward.
It was a silent, unmistakable signal.
Zhao Yiming's breath seemed to catch for a moment, and then he hesitated no longer. He drew slowly closer, his warm breath brushing her cheek, and then a kiss landed gently, tentatively, on her forehead, light as a feather. Then her brow, the tip of her nose, and at last, with the greatest reverence, he covered her slightly parted lips.
At first it was a tender brushing, full of careful searching. The cold, bracing aftertaste of the liquor still lingered between their lips, mingling with each other's breath. Starlight spilled through the top of the tent, flowing over their tangled breaths and their slowly warming skin. Yao Yuan's hands climbed unconsciously to his shoulders, feeling the taut, powerful lines of muscle beneath his shirt. Zhao Yiming's kiss deepened, growing hot and certain, his arm circling her waist to pull her tighter against him. The fibers of the rug rubbed at her skin, sending faint shivers through her.
At some point their clothes had grown loose and bothersome. Beneath the silent stars, in the heart of the desert far from all the noise of the world, the levee of reason quietly gave way before primal instinct and surging emotion. There were no more words — only breathing that grew quicker, body heat that twined together, and the indescribable thrill of skin against skin.
In the instant their bodies pressed together, both of them gave an almost imperceptible tremble. That closeness broke through every spoken agreement and every boundary of reason; their souls seemed to embrace tightly in that moment. He moved with the lightest touch, with careful cherishing, as if handling the rarest treasure in the world. His warm breath wrapped around her, his restrained feelings surging without end, all his tenderness and depth poured into every small point of contact.
Starlight like flowing water settled quietly over the two of them lying entwined inside, the air thick with a tenderness that would not dissolve.
As the rhythm gradually slipped out of control, a primal cadence replaced the early tenderness. The air in the tent turned scalding, breaths weaving together, mingled with the faint sounds of bodies meeting. Yao Yuan tilted her head back, her neck drawing a lovely arc, her gaze passing through the transparent top of the tent to fall into that boundless, turning river of stars. In that moment, the joys and sorrows of the individual, the unsolved puzzles, the distant threats — all of it seemed to dissolve into insignificant dust against the backdrop of the universe. Only this — the body's utmost pleasure, the soul's brief merging, and the eternally watching sky overhead — was the only real thing.
After the storm of feeling passed, everything slowly settled into calm. Yao Yuan lowered her eyes and lay quietly in his arms, her fingers resting lightly on his back. Zhao Yiming circled her waist, and a faint sigh escaped his chest.
The night wind drifted over carrying starlight, and the two of them, holding each other, gradually caught their breath. A lazy warmth wrapped around them. No words were needed; simply leaning together like this, they felt utterly at peace, and time itself turned soft and long.
After who knew how long, Zhao Yiming propped himself up slightly, found her lips in the dimness, and pressed a few gentle, desireless kisses to them, then drew her close again, his chin resting against the top of her hair.
"Sleep," he murmured, his voice husky and tender.
Yao Yuan found a more comfortable spot in his arms and closed her eyes. The body's fatigue and the spirit's contentment rolled in like a tide and quickly dragged her down into deep sleep. In her haze she felt him seem to rise, carefully draw a soft thin blanket over them both, then come back and gather her into his arms once more.
Outside the tent, the desert night wind blew on, and the river of stars turned slowly overhead. This ancient, silent land witnessed tonight the most primal and most beautiful union of a man and a woman after the boundaries of reason dissolved away. The future was still full of fog and challenges, but at least in this moment, they had each other, they had this boundless sky, and an attempt just begun — one bound by a clear-eyed promise.

========================================

Chapter 69 — Three Days in the Gobi, a Warmth Growing Tender
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640316.html

They stayed in the Gobi for three full days.
For those three days, stripped of every role and obligation the city had imposed on them, time seemed to be stretched and loosened by the desert wind. By day, they went out with the team, immersing themselves in the vastness and desolation of this land.
In the deepest dark before dawn, wrapped in heavy blankets, they climbed a dune to wait for the desert sunrise. They watched a thin line of crab-shell green seep out of the inky black at the horizon, then deepen into orange-red and gold dust, until at last a round, blazing red sun tore itself free of the horizon and flung its boundless light and heat across the rolling sea of sand. In that moment the world fell solemn and still, and all things woke; the golden grains of sand seemed to catch fire, glittering with hundreds of millions of tiny points of light. Quite naturally, Zhao Yiming took Yao Yuan's hand, and the two of them stood shoulder to shoulder. Neither said a word, only quietly took in this primal, surging burst of life.
At dusk they went again, this time to watch the long river and the setting sun. They drove to a high, open vantage point; below them lay a dry ancient riverbed, winding away like the skeleton of some giant dragon. The clouds in the western sky had burned into a boundless ocean of fire, soaking the sinking sun in a light both tender and tragically grand. "Lone smoke rises straight in the great desert; round sets the sun over the long river," Zhao Yiming murmured the old line of verse. Yao Yuan followed his gaze. In the distance, a single ruler-straight column of smoke—she could not tell whether it was cooking smoke or a pillar of dust—rose silently into the crimson sky, and together with that huge, perfect, blood-red sun slowly slipping behind the ochre mountains on the horizon, it formed a picture that matched the poem exactly, a scene that stilled the very soul. The afterglow of the setting sun warmed the fair skin of her cheeks too, and Zhao Yiming turned his head to look at her, feeling that the woman before him was more captivating than this timeless landscape.
They tasted the local honeydew melons and ginseng-fruit, sweet as honey, brimming with juice and carrying the strong, sun-soaked scent of the place. They rode gentle camels too, following the camel-herders deep into the heart of the sand sea, listening to the camel bells jingle and watching the curves of the dunes shift into soft, graceful ripples under the light. When the wind passed, fine sand slid down the ridges like flowing water, with the faintest hiss, as if the earth itself were breathing.
At night, they still often went to look at the stars. Sometimes right by the camp, sometimes Zhao Yiming would drive her somewhere farther from the light pollution. The starry sky was the same every night, and yet brand new every night. What they talked about, too, drifted from the technology and business of those first conversations into far broader country—funny stories from childhood, the hardships of their student years, vague hopes for the future, even certain fragile moments buried deep in the heart and never spoken of to anyone. Beneath an absolutely silent sky of stars, beside the warmth of a body growing slowly familiar, both speaking and listening became remarkably natural. Catalyzed by the magnificent scenery and the small daily sharings, their feelings warmed quickly and grew sweeter by the day. A single glance, an accidental touch, each carried an unspoken intimacy. Yet they both held to their original "agreement," never letting this private closeness bleed into any work discussion; in front of the team, they remained the professional, reliable "Boss Yao" and "Boss Zhao."
On the morning of the fourth day, the team set out to return to Jinshi.
The convoy drove home along the expressway that cut dead straight across the Gobi. The novelty and anticipation of the journey out had turned, on the way back, into contentment and a faint weariness. Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming rode in the same car; she sat in the front passenger seat while he drove himself. In the back sat one of Yao Yuan's assistants and one of Zhao Yiming's engineers. At first the four of them chatted about all they had seen these past days, but the talk gradually died away, and the assistant and the engineer began to doze with their eyes closed.
Yao Yuan was a little tired too, though still alert enough. She leaned back into the comfortable seat, watching the seemingly endless Gobi landscape rush backward past the window, replaying fragments of these past days in her mind—the cold database, the blazing campfire, the magnificent starry sky, Zhao Yiming's focused profile, the dry warmth of his palm, and those moments under the stars that made her face flush and her heart race… Without realizing it, the corners of her mouth curved into the faintest, gentlest arc.
The car began to enter the mountains, and the road grew winding. Ahead lay the famous Wushaoling tunnel cluster, one long tunnel after another bored through the body of the mountains, slicing the journey into alternating stretches of light and dark.
The air conditioning was on inside the car, and the light dimmed abruptly as they entered a tunnel, leaving only the dashboard and the central screen giving off a faint blue glow. A wave of drowsiness washed over Yao Yuan too; she shifted her position, ready to take a short rest. Just as she closed her eyes and her awareness was about to sink into a haze, the car drove into yet another very long tunnel.
The lights inside the tunnel were a monotonous warm yellow, streaming swiftly backward and forming a regular flow of light and shadow inside the car. For some reason, she had not fallen fully asleep; half-dreaming, half-awake, she instinctively tilted her head slightly, her gaze drifting absently across the rearview mirror on the right.
In the mirror, the image that should have reflected the cars behind and the tunnel walls suddenly twisted and rippled—as if a huge boulder had been thrown into a calm surface of water!
Yao Yuan's breath caught sharply; all her drowsiness vanished.
An instant later, that mirror surface seemed to become a bottomless whirlpool, and a familiar, soul-trembling, overwhelming pull surged in without the slightest warning!
She wanted to grab hold of something, but her fingers were limp and weak. The heart-guarding jade at her breast blazed scorching hot in an instant, as if it would sear her skin, and a gentle yet powerful force tried to drag her back—
The pulling lasted only a brief second or two, yet to Yao Yuan it felt as long as a century.
She felt the whirlpool abruptly swallow her whole! The scorching heat of the heart-guarding jade was instantly cut off, receding far away. Zhao Yiming, the echo of the engine inside the tunnel, the hum of the car's air conditioning… every real sound and sensation was rapidly drawn away and dissolved.
She lost consciousness and sank into a faint.

========================================

Chapter 70 — A Fork in Time, Witnessing a Proposal
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640317.html

Yao Yuan's consciousness slowly came back to itself inside a space of pure white.

Here we go again, she understood at once. The uncontrolled time-travel had pulled her back, after that brief peace and sweetness beneath the desert stars.
