Not far off, in some empty, void-like patch of space, ripples began to spread out clearly, like a still pond struck by a stone. An image went from blurred to sharp, building and taking shape with startling speed—

It was an extravagant, meticulously arranged indoor proposal scene.

From the backdrop, it looked like the ballroom of some top-tier hotel, but completely transformed. From the high domed ceiling hung countless strings of glittering crystal chandeliers and fresh champagne roses; the floor was carpeted in a thick layer of white petals. All around were flickering candle flames in silver candlesticks, and the air drifted with fine perfume and the sweet scent of champagne. There weren't many guests—about twenty or thirty—but every one of them was sharply dressed and refined, clearly a carefully chosen circle of close family and friends.

At this moment, every face wore a smile of blessing, all eyes fixed on the center of the ballroom.

There, thousands upon thousands of Ecuadorian red roses had been arranged into a giant heart shape. At the center of the heart, a young man in a well-tailored navy-blue suit, tall and straight-backed, knelt on one knee.

It was Yu Hao. A few years younger than in Yao Yuan's memory, maybe twenty-seven or twenty-eight, his face still carrying a trace of un-faded boyishness—but the spirited brightness in his brow and his deep, focused devotion in this moment made him look especially radiant. In his hands he held an open navy-blue velvet ring box, and a pear-shaped diamond ring of at least five carats by the look of it refracted dazzling fire under the brilliant lights.

And standing before him, head slightly bowed to look down at him, was a younger Yao Yuan. By her appearance, she was a little past thirty—the age when the rawness of youth has worn away and a woman is at the height of her bloom. She wore an ivory-white satin strapless gown, cut with the utmost elegance, tracing her slender figure. Her long hair was carefully swept up, baring a long, graceful neck, and the diamond tassel earrings at her ears swayed with her faint trembling. One hand covered her mouth, her face full of disbelieving delight, of emotion, and of undisguised, overwhelming happiness, her eyes shimmering with tears that glittered like crushed diamonds in the light.

"Yuanyuan," said Yu Hao from where he knelt, his voice clear, carrying excitement and nervousness as it echoed through the silent ballroom. "From the moment I met you, I knew that my long wait and all my efforts were for the sake of reaching you. You're smart, independent, strong, and you have the softest heart in the world. Every single day with you makes me feel my life is more complete and full of hope than ever before. I won't claim I can give you the whole world, but I'm willing to spend the rest of my life guarding your smile, supporting your dreams, and standing beside you through every storm and every ray of sunshine to come. Yao Yuan, I love you. Will you... marry me?"

His words were sincere and ardent, his gaze blazing, full of a young man's reckless earnestness.

A low, good-natured chorus of cheers and applause rose around them. "Say yes!" "Marry him!" A few of the young women were already moved to cover their mouths.

The younger Yao Yuan's tears finally broke free, sliding down her cheeks. She looked at Yu Hao, then glanced around at all the friends and family smiling their blessings, and at last nodded hard, her voice choked with happiness yet utterly clear and firm: "I do!"

Pure joy burst across Yu Hao's face. Carefully he lifted that brilliant diamond ring from its box and, solemnly, with a slight tremble, slid it onto the ring finger of the younger Yao Yuan's left hand. A perfect fit.

The next second he rose, swept her into his arms, and amid the crowd's cheers and applause, kissed her deeply. A champagne tower was wheeled out by the waiters, bubbles rising merrily. Music came on at just the right moment, soft and romantic. The guests came forward one after another to embrace and congratulate this newly engaged pair.

(In the pure-white space, Yao Yuan hung suspended in midair like a cold bystander, watching this warm, romantic scene. Yet she felt nothing in her heart—only a barren, icy chill, and a sense of dislocation that bordered on the absurd.)

She spotted several familiar faces in the crowd—a few of her old girlfriends from back in Shanghai, still looking a little fresh-faced then; a couple of Yu Hao's buddies, grinning brightly; even... she saw Ding Shitong.

Ding Shitong stood a little toward the outer edge of the crowd, a champagne glass in hand, a proper, social smile on his face as he applauded lightly along with everyone else. But his eyes were deeper and more complicated than anyone else's there. In them was scrutiny, calculation, a faint trace of loss—perhaps one even he hadn't noticed—but more than that, a kind of calm certainty, as if this whole scene had long been within some prediction or observation of his. Now and then he murmured to those beside him, yet his gaze kept drifting, as if by accident, over that embracing pair, and especially over the glaring diamond on the younger Yao Yuan's ring finger; when it did, an unreadable shadow flickered in his eyes. Several young, pretty, fashionably dressed girls clustered near him, their eyes straying every so often to the happy Yao Yuan at the center—a look that held envy, jealousy, and a faint, hard-to-detect, subtle scrutiny of "what makes her so special."

The younger Yao Yuan was clearly fully immersed in the enormous joy of being proposed to and in everyone's blessings, her cheeks flushed, her eyes shining, fingers laced tight with Yu Hao's, accepting wave after wave of teasing and congratulations from her friends.

After a while, she seemed to feel her makeup had been smudged by tears. She murmured something to Yu Hao, then gathered up her skirt and headed toward the restroom off to the side of the ballroom.

The Yao Yuan in the pure-white space found herself drawn along by some invisible force, "floating" after her.

The restroom was decorated just as lavishly, silent and still, a contrast to the liveliness outside. The younger Yao Yuan walked up to the wide mirror, studied her makeup carefully, and took out a compact to touch it up lightly. Looking at her own flushed cheeks, her bright eyes, and that dazzling ring on her finger, she couldn't help breaking into a smile so happy it was almost dazed.

And just then—

The mirror's surface rippled and warped, like still water struck by a stone.

The younger Yao Yuan's hand froze mid-touch-up. She stared, eyes wide with shock, as another face gradually surfaced beside her own reflection—a face exactly like hers, only more mature, more coolly aloof. It was the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan.

In the mirror, the two Yao Yuans from different times faced each other once more across the dressing glass.

The younger Yao Yuan, too, took it almost as a matter of habit—just an instinctive startle at something appearing out of nowhere—but she quickly recognized the figure in the mirror and steadied herself. Sure enough, they were essentially the same person: faced with something they had no power to change, they accepted it fast and adapted fast. Different souls from different times though they were, the inner strength and the core of their thinking hadn't changed.

The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan looked at that young face in the mirror, brimming with unfeigned happiness and full of longing for the future, and her mind was like an overturned palette—tangled and hard to put into words. Only a few months ago, in the real world, she had just made a complete, final break with Yu Hao—the Yu Hao who claimed to love her and depend on her, yet flinched at the most crucial real-world interests and the promise of marriage, wanting only a "child" as a bond and a guarantee. The heartbreak and disappointment hadn't fully faded, and now here she was, traveled back, watching with her own eyes as a younger self in another timeline joyfully accepted a grand proposal from the very same man.

The absurdity of the contrast made her throat tighten.

She opened her mouth, wanting to say something. Wanting to demand answers sharply, to give a piercing warning. Last time, hadn't she already cautioned her younger self to beware of a man surnamed "Yu"? But the words reached her lips and then stuck fast.

That was it. Last time, she'd only managed to get out "If you meet someone surnamed Yu in the venture-capital world, don't get involved with him..." before the most crucial part went unsaid and she vanished abruptly into the mirror. The warning had been only half finished—and might even have backfired, making her younger self instead develop some improper, fate-like curiosity or fixation on the surname "Yu."

A vast helplessness and the mockery of fate swept over her.

In the end, she could only force down those fierce words, look at her young self in the mirror, and ask in a dry voice (she needed to make sense of this timeline's thread): "Last time I saw you... didn't you choose to be with Duan Yanqing of your own will? How... did you end up with Yu Hao instead?"

Hearing her bring this up, the younger Yao Yuan seemed to relax her guard a little, though her eyes dimmed for an instant. Facing the mirror, absently turning the diamond on her finger, she answered in a low voice, her tone carrying the complexity of memory:

"After I last saw you, I looked over and evaluated Zhao Yiming's 'Western World' carefully. The project really was excellent. I went back to Shanghai with the assessment report and some new ideas."

"Not long after, on Duan Yanqing's side... there were some problems with domestic policy and a few of his overseas assets. He decided to pull back all his investments and arrangements in the country, move abroad for good, and start a semi-retired life. Before he left, he formally invited me to go with him."

She raised her head, looking at her future self in the mirror, clear reluctance and ambition in her eyes: "I wasn't willing to settle. I was still young. I felt my life had only just begun—the stage I wanted, the things I wanted to prove, were all still here in the country. I hadn't reached the height I truly wanted. Go with him, to a completely unfamiliar country, and be a 'wife' kept under his protection? That wasn't the future I wanted. So, after weighing it for a long time, I chose to stay."

"About... half a year later," she went on, her tone turning lighter again, carrying the sweetness of someone fallen in love, "at a small closed-door symposium on cutting-edge technology, I met Yu Hao. He was one of the youngest speakers there. His views were razor-sharp, and his read on the future left a deep impression on me. We talked afterward and found... we actually saw eye to eye on so many things, from business trends to attitudes about life—even the books and films we liked lined up uncannily well. He's quick-minded and broadly knowledgeable, and even though he's six years younger than me, I felt no gap in age at all. If anything... he understood me better and respected my ideas and my career more than most of the men I'd met my own age or older."

A blush rose on her face, the natural glow of someone speaking of the one they love: "We started seeing each other very naturally. He courted me with real care, and with a sense of proportion. After half a year together, we... moved in together. Today is the official first anniversary of our relationship. He kept it from me, planned all of this with such care, and proposed." She looked at the ring on her hand, her smile happy and content.

The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan listened quietly. Every word was like a fine needle, pricking gently at her heart. The "self" of this timeline had made different choices, stayed in the country, put down roots in Shanghai by her own efforts, and then, at a more "right" moment, met a "better" version of Yu Hao—a young, passionate, sincere Yu Hao whose goal was marriage. No heavy calculations over real-world interests, no cold detachment of a "companion-of-convenience" arrangement, only pure attraction and a burning promise.

What a... "correct" path. What a "happy" version.

She looked at that face in the mirror, filled to the brim with love and hope, and all her warnings, her cautions, even the resentment carried over from her own wounds, turned pale and powerless—even... a little despicable. Was she really going to shatter with her own hands the happiness this "self" could already touch? On what grounds? Just because she herself, in another timeline, had met the wrong person and given her heart to the wrong man?

In the end, she said nothing. Only, ever so faintly, ever so softly, she breathed a few words at her young self in the mirror, her voice as drifting as a sigh:

"I wish you well."

The younger Yao Yuan clearly let out a breath, her smile blooming again. "Thank you!" She hesitated, then added with a little excitement at sharing the news: "Yu Hao doesn't hold it against me that I had a brief marriage before"—clearly referring to the one with Shuai Hongqiang—"he says it's just life experience. Next week he's taking me to America to meet his parents and family, to discuss the official wedding date." Her eyes shone with boundless longing for the future.

The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's mind stirred: "You're thirty-five this year?"

She got a firm yes from the younger Yao Yuan.

She was about to speak again, to ask the thirty-five-year-old Yao Yuan about the partnership and investment with Zhao Yiming—one of the most central and most hopeful career ties she had in the real world right now.

But then the familiar, icy grip seized her again, without warning—fiercer than before!

"Wait—" She had time only to cry out in her consciousness.

The scene before her—the younger Yao Yuan's happy face, the lavish restroom mirror, even that conspicuous diamond ring—all twisted and stretched in an instant, then were dragged brutally into a dark whirlpool by some immense force.

The mirror's surface returned to calm, reflecting only the thirty-five-year-old Yao Yuan's lovely face, freshly proposed to by the man she loved, still carrying traces of undimmed happiness. Startled again for a moment by the sudden disappearance of her future self, she turned back to the mirror and studied the ring on her hand—that symbol of a promise—the smile at the corner of her lips sweet and real.

...

Cold emptiness, a long sensation of falling.

Then came a heavy suffocation and return, like struggling up from the deep sea to break the surface.

Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open, her pupils contracting sharply, her vision a complete blur, unfocused for several seconds. In her ears was the steady noise of a car in motion; in her nose, the familiar smell of leather and air-conditioning inside the cabin.

She was still in the car. The passenger seat.

Outside the window, harsh sunlight blazed in. They had just emerged from the last long tunnel of Wushaoling, back into the daylight. The "Wushaoling Tunnel" signboard flashed past beyond the window.

She leaned against the seatback. To anyone else, she had perhaps only dozed against the window for a little while, woken by the sunlight as the car came out of the tunnel. But she alone knew that in this seemingly brief span, her soul had been through such an absurd, outrageous journey across time and space—witnessing a "happy" proposal utterly opposite to her own reality, and doing so right beside this man who studied the most advanced frontiers of human brain science and artificial intelligence: Zhao Yiming.

It seemed this time-travel had lasted a shorter time than any of the previous ones. Was it the protective effect of the "heart-guarding" jade that Old Chen had given her? Or because she was far enough from that locked-away bronze mirror that the pull of the "Mirror Pact" was weakened? She couldn't say. That hollowed-out exhaustion and icy aftershock deep in her soul still lingered, but it truly lacked the violent, drained-of-all-energy depletion she'd felt returning from the first five time-travels. The old jade gave off a steady, gentle warmth against her chest, like a warm current slowly mending her damaged spirit.

Just then Zhao Yiming, at the wheel, seemed to sense her slight movement. He turned his head, his gaze settling on her with concern, his voice gentle and low: "Awake? Had a little nap? Did you fall asleep?" His eyes held a natural care, and a faint, hard-to-detect tenderness that belonged to lovers.

Yao Yuan's heart slammed hard against her chest. The confession and intimacy beneath the desert stars, the dance by the bonfire, the scene she'd just witnessed of another timeline's "self" being proposed to, and the real-world break with Yu Hao... all these images collided and tangled wildly in her mind, leaving her dazed for a moment, unable to tell what time it was or where she was.

She quickly lowered her lashes, hiding the complicated emotions churning in her eyes, and when she looked up again she had put on her usual calm—only her face was still a little pale. She raised a hand to rub her temple, her tone carrying a perfectly judged, just-woken languor and slight huskiness: "Mm, I fell asleep. Had a... messy little dream." She tugged at the corner of her mouth, trying for a relaxed smile. "Maybe I relaxed too much these past few days—got drowsy the second I got in the car."

Of course she couldn't tell the top-flight brain scientist and AI pioneer beside her that she hadn't been asleep at all, but had, right next to him, gone through a bizarre "soul time-travel" that no existing science could explain, to watch another timeline's "self" be proposed to. It wouldn't just sound like a fairy tale—it would sound close to the delusions of a deranged mind.

Zhao Yiming studied her face carefully, his brow knitting almost imperceptibly. He seemed about to say something, but in the end didn't press. He only said gently: "Rest a bit more if you're tired. We've still got over two hours to go. If you get carsick or feel unwell, tell me anytime—there's a service area up ahead."

"Okay. I'm fine." Yao Yuan nodded, leaned back against the seat, and closed her eyes. This time she really did need a little while to settle her thoughts, to digest that enormous flood of information and the sense of absurdity.

The rest of the way back passed without incident. The convoy returned smoothly to Jinshi; by then it was evening. Zhao Yiming personally drove Yao Yuan to the foot of her apartment building.

The car came to a stop, and a few seconds of silence fell between them. The new relationship established by the desert trip and the bizarre time-travel she had just lived through formed a subtle, fractured tension in Yao Yuan's heart.

"Want to come up for a bit?" Yao Yuan asked out of politeness, though there wasn't much real invitation in her tone.

Zhao Yiming shook his head. His gaze lingered a moment on her still-tired face, his voice steady and understanding: "No. You've just come back from the desert—you need a proper rest. And besides," he smiled, that smile carrying a practicality they both tacitly understood, "we've been away a few days, and our companies each have a pile of things waiting to be dealt with. Rest up first. Tomorrow... we'll be in touch."

His thoughtfulness and sense of proportion eased Yao Yuan's heart a little, and stirred a flicker of genuine warmth. "Okay. You head back and rest early too—driving all that way was hard work." She unbuckled her seatbelt, ready to get out.

"Yao Yuan." Zhao Yiming suddenly called her back.

She turned.

He looked at her, his eyes especially focused and clear in the dim light of the car: "Whatever you dreamed, it's all in the past. Now, we're back." His words seemed to carry a hidden meaning, and yet seemed merely ordinary comfort.

Yao Yuan's heart stirred faintly. She smiled at him: "Mm, I know. Goodbye."

"Goodbye."

========================================

Chapter 71 — Reviewing Five Trips Through Time
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640318.html

Back in the apartment she hadn't seen for days, the familiar surroundings and scent finally let her taut nerves go slack. She didn't touch her work right away; she walked straight to the bathroom.
She filled the tub with hot water, dropped in a few drops of soothing essential oil, and sank herself completely into the warmth. The hot water wrapped around her weary limbs, and it seemed to wash away too what clung to her soul — the wind and sand of the Gobi, the cold, murky chill that time-travel left behind. Her skin, baked for days in the dry desert air, drank in the moisture now and let out a satisfied sigh.
But her thoughts couldn't relax the way her body did.
Leaning against the rim of the tub, with the steam blurring her vision, the images in her mind only grew sharper and more chaotic — and with them came a stubborn, almost obsessive urge to sort them into order.
Fate really was capricious to the point of absurdity.
Time and again she had traveled back into the past, trying to change things, or unwittingly influencing her younger self. Some of those changes looked like they'd corrected certain mistakes, spared her certain pain.
The first time, for instance, she'd reminded her younger self to see clearly the gulf in social standing between Jiang Hai and his family, sparing her the violent agony of sinking deep into love only to be betrayed, humiliated, her dignity trampled. That kind of pain wasn't just the loss of a relationship; it was a profound doubting and denial of her own worth, one of the roots of her sense of "not being good enough" — something she had instinctively suppressed and forgotten, yet which became a thorn buried deep in her heart, finally awakened by the Mirror Pact. Her younger self had pulled out early; there might still have been pain, but nothing that cut to the bone or shadowed a whole life.
But was the "good" that came from changing things really, entirely, good? Her younger self had broken up early, gone abroad to study, and come back — yet still hadn't managed to build a career in the capital. Because of her mother Zhang Fengxia's sudden, serious illness (an illness that, in the present Yao Yuan's memory, her mother had never had), she'd chosen to return to Jinshi. And she seemed not to have stayed with the dance career she'd once loved, turning instead — like Yao Yuan herself — to the gamesmanship of business.
"If fate grants a gift, in secret it takes something away." The line surfaced in her heart like a cold incantation. One love wound avoided — and yet still she'd missed the chance to stay on a larger stage, given up the field she adored, and even made her mother suffer an illness (one that didn't exist in the original timeline). How was one supposed to weigh that ledger of gains and losses? She didn't know which version of a life was truly "better," and which was "worse."
The second time, she'd warned Shuai Hongqiang before his low self-esteem and traditional notions made him flinch away, and her younger self chose to lay her cards on the table and marry. That spared her the old tragedy of aborting the child and fleeing, heartbroken, to Hangzhou. But the ending? The marriage lasted less than a year before it ended in the loss of a six-month-old child, the trauma of possibly never being able to conceive again, and finally divorce all the same.
Was that change better than before? It spared her the physical and emotional agony of the operating table and the humiliation of fleeing far from home — but it brought instead the immense blow of hope in pregnancy and sudden loss, and a brief, failed marriage. The shame-laden thorn lodged deep in the present Yao Yuan's heart — the one named "chosen again, scrutinized again, and in the end discarded by some invisible rule" — perhaps the pain had only changed its shape; it hadn't gone away.
The third time, faced with that scorching but doomed-to-burn-out relationship with Zhuge Jinye, she had made no intervention at all. Her younger self walked the same path she once had, all over again. It looked like nothing changed — but that reckless love, the one that burned everything to ash and scorched them both, was branded just as deeply into the soul on every timeline, an indelible part of her. Whether she changed it or not seemed to make no difference to the nature, or the ending, of that relationship.
The fourth time, she'd warned her younger self before Ding Shitong could treat her as a bargaining chip. The young Yao Yuan made an active choice, using the subtle tug-of-war between Ding Shitong and Duan Yanqing — and their interest in her — to win herself real, tangible advantages and room to maneuver. That change had no doubt spared her younger self the deepest humiliation and spiritual castration the present Yao Yuan had endured: being appraised, traded, and discarded like merchandise, and the spiritual torment of "do I really not deserve a man's pure, true love?"
That was, without question, a positive, major change. And so far, no "bad fruit" had grown from it.
The fifth time, she'd appeared directly in a scene her own life had never passed through — her slightly younger self (at thirty-three) inspecting Zhao Yiming's Western World Technology, which was itself a chain reaction of the change wrought by the fourth trip. Of this "unknown" territory she had no way to warn her, and only at the last moment, on an instinctive wariness about Yu Hao's surname, had she tried to give a warning — without finishing her words. And that timeline's young Yao Yuan seemed not to have suffered any obvious "ill-luck" backlash from the earlier changes; her life still ran along some kind of "normal" track.
And this time, the sixth trip. What she had witnessed was the far greater divergence produced when the fourth and fifth changes stacked on top of each other — her younger self (at thirty-five) had never gone through that "friends-with-benefits" arrangement with Yu Hao, never endured the cold cruelty and hurt of "I only want a child, I don't want marriage," never tasted the disappointment of a fragile bond built on calculated interest snapping all at once. Instead, she'd met a more "ideal" version of Yu Hao — young, ardent, sincere, holding marriage up as his highest commitment — and, with friends and family as witnesses, had happily accepted his proposal.
Seen as this single "present" slice, the change looked flawless, beautiful as a fairy tale. The young Yao Yuan was spared yet another round of the spiritual torment of being "chosen, weighed in the balance" that she herself had lived through, and was striding straight toward the hall of marriage, toward worldly "fulfillment."
So her trips through time really could change some things. That change sometimes triggered unforeseeable side effects (like her mother's illness), sometimes merely swapped one form of pain for another (like the marriage to Shuai Hongqiang), but sometimes it really did dodge some truly fatal spiritual wounds (like escaping being traded away by Ding Shitong) — and might even, possibly, lead toward a seemingly smoother, "happier" path in life (like the engagement to Yu Hao).
Then what about her own pain, all the struggle and trauma and humiliation and scheming and loneliness she'd lived through on this "unchanged," or "not changed enough," timeline? Was it a necessary price? A wrong turn down a blind alley? Or was it simply that, out of countless parallel versions across countless timelines, she just happened to be living in the comparatively "unlucky" one?
And that "Mirror Pact" — the curse that bound her so eerily to Shuai Hongqiang, draining her very spirit to buy fortune for him — on which "version" of her was it founded? On this "original," trauma-soaked timeline? If her younger self were changed enough, were turned onto a wholly different, happier path, then would this "Yao Yuan's" very "origin" change too? Would the Mirror Pact loosen along with it — even vanish?
The more she thought, the more her thoughts tangled like a knotted skein, no thread to grasp. Logic and feeling, hope and emptiness, warred fiercely in her mind. Not even the warm water could drive off the deep bewilderment and chill spreading out from the bottom of her heart.
Whether from too great a drain on her spirit or from water too comfortably warm, an utter exhaustion swallowed her like a rising tide. Her thoughts slowly stalled, and her consciousness sank into a murky darkness.
She had a dream.
The space in the dream was a boundless, ice-cold expanse of pure white, like the most advanced laboratory, like an unknown void.
She "stood" there, and saw, not far off, the backs of six men, gathered in a circle, all facing some huge surface ahead — a mirror, or a display screen, like a movie screen — watching something intently. Blurred light and streams of data flowed across that "screen"; she couldn't quite make out what they showed.
A strong, inexplicable curiosity and unease rose in her, and without meaning to she crept softly closer.
Closer and closer... she could make out their clothes. They all wore identical, spotless white lab coats, like the most elite researchers.
Then she saw their profiles, and several of them turned their heads —
Jiang Hai. His expression held its usual gentle refinement, but his gaze carried the cool, examining scrutiny of a researcher.
Shuai Hongqiang. A merchant's shrewdness in his brow, with a lingering trace of gloom, his eyes complicated.
Zhuge Jinye. The line of his profile sharp, his gaze fervent yet with an obsessive focus, as if staring at his own work upon a stage.
Duan Yanqing. His bearing steady and composed, a faint, almost imperceptible smile of total control at the corner of his mouth, his eyes deep and unreadable.
Yu Hao. On his young, handsome face, an expression mixing curiosity, appraisal, and a faint, hard-to-detect coldness.
Zhao Yiming. He stood a little off to the side, his expression the most focused and calm of all, the eyes behind his lenses sharp as a scalpel, rapidly analyzing the data flowing across the "screen," his fingers tracing unconsciously through empty air as if modeling something.
Then Yao Yuan's gaze went past their shoulders and landed on that huge "screen."
What it showed was, unmistakably, the scene of this very moment in reality — herself, eyes closed, soaking in the warm water of the tub, steam rising, her face weary. It was an image like a live security feed.
A vast horror, the chill of being spied upon, drowned her dream in an instant like ice water!
"Aah—!"
Yao Yuan jolted awake out of the tub, her heart pounding so hard it nearly burst from her chest. The warm water splashed out with her violent movement and wet the floor. She gasped for breath in great heaving gulps and looked around — it was her familiar bathroom, and she was alone. Curling steam, a silent room.
No pure-white laboratory. No six men in lab coats. No giant surveillance screen.
Just a dream. A grotesque, lurid nightmare born of overthinking and crushing mental strain.
But that terror of being watched, being analyzed, as if she were nothing but a set of data or a test subject, lingered with absolute reality, leaving her cold all over — a chill that even soaking in hot water couldn't drive away.
She wrapped her arms around herself. The hot water slowly went cold.
Beyond the window, the night was thick as ink.
And the fog in her heart was deeper, heavier, than the night.

========================================

Chapter 72 — The Truth About Marriage: Kill the Old Self, and Survive the Game Together
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640319.html

Eight in the evening.
A high-summer night. Beyond the window lay Jinshi's scattered lights against the dark, the faint drone of cicadas. The lighting in the studio had been turned a softer, warmer gold than usual, like the last glow of a setting sun. Lin Wan wore a pale apricot linen short-sleeved shirt, the fabric crisp yet casual, the top two buttons undone to show the clean line of her collarbones. Her short hair caught a healthy sheen under the lights, the ends tucked neatly behind her ears, making her neck look long and slender. Her makeup was light and tasteful; only her lips wore a soft mauve-pink that lifted her color without ever shouting for attention. There was no elaborate tea set in front of her, just a glass of plain hot water sending up thin curls of steam. In the background, that potted Pollux tree—her symbol of protection—stood quietly in one corner, and beside it someone had added a small bundle of dried eucalyptus, its gray-green leaves curling, giving off a cool, calming herbal scent that brought a touch of visual coolness to the summer night.
She looked into the camera, her gaze settled and calm, the kind that comes from a life lived through—like the surface of a windless lake at midnight, clearly mirroring the sky and the stars, yet fathomless beneath.
"Good evening, and welcome to 'A Gentle Crossing.'" Her voice was warm, carrying a power that made people go still. "Tonight, we'll talk about something real, and something less tangible too. We'll talk about marriage—the bindings, the trades, the games of strategy you can see—and the thing you can't see, the thing that may decide everything."
[The scrolling comments drifted by softly, with the slow rhythm that belongs to nighttime:]
[Wanwan looks great tonight—fresh and gentle.]
[The seen and the unseen? Now I'm curious.]
[This feels like it'll be a deep one. Getting my notebook ready.]
"Lately I've gotten a lot of messages on the backend, especially in my private inbox—so many women describing a kind of deep exhaustion inside their marriages." Lin Wan spoke at an unhurried pace, as if talking with a friend late at night. "They say they're working so hard to grow stronger, grinding to make money, never daring to ease up, signing up for course after course to better themselves, terrified that if they stop they'll be called 'a dependent,' afraid of losing their voice in the relationship, afraid of being looked down on. I understand that effort completely, and I respect it absolutely. Of course we should grow. We should have the ability to stand in society and create value. That's our footing as we move through the world, and the bedrock of keeping our dignity and our whole selves intact in any relationship."
She paused for a moment, her eyes sweeping across the screen, as if she could see the figures of all those women quietly digesting their stress and laboring on alone, deep in the night.
"I understand the pain behind that feeling all too well." A barely perceptible note of lament entered her voice—the understanding of someone who has been there. "People who can face the wounds of their family of origin head-on, who can look straight at the uglier scars inside a marriage, tend to live more deeply, more clearly. But the ones willing and able to truly 'look' are always the minority."
"For far too many people, their values were set in stone very early on: find a good man, marry him, and you've solved life. The best ending for a woman who marries seems to be never having to work, being provided for, hiding behind a man for the rest of her days, sheltering from the storms of life." She shook her head lightly—no judgment in the motion, only clear sight. "But only those who've actually walked this road, or watched it up close, know that this puts the cart before the horse, confuses what matters with what doesn't. Any woman who pins her entire hope for survival and happiness on the foundation of 'being provided for by a man' will, more often than not, find no escape from a fate of being manipulated, looked down on, even bullied. I've seen that road. I've tasted what's on it. I see it far too clearly."
[The comments began to fill with recognition and self-reflection:]
[This is the truth. My mom raised me exactly this way, and now my life is anything but good.]
["Being provided for" sounds beautiful, but it's really like walking on thin ice.]
[Has Wanwan been through something? There's more in these words than she's saying.]
"Outsiders looking at a marriage rarely see clearly—they only see the surface." Lin Wan's tone was plain, as if stating an objective fact. "They see that I seem to have a marriage, a man at my side, no worries about material life, and they might slap a 'lucky woman' label on me without a second thought. No one knows—or rather, no one cares—that I may have been trapped inside this besieged fortress of a marriage for many years, that I've long since become a seasoned old prisoner here."
She gave a small laugh, something complicated in it—not bitter, more like the self-mockery of someone who has risen above it: "It's not that I live some fairytale happiness. It's just that I understand the survival rules of this 'fortress' too well—I know how to disguise, to play along, to keep up appearances within its rules, and, in the process, to find the ways that make me relatively comfortable and strong. That's all."
She picked up the glass of water, took a small sip, and went on: "People often ask me in the studio, very bluntly: 'Wanwan, between you and Brother Qiang—are the two of you completely transparent about money?' My answer is just as blunt: of course not. If two people lay their account books fully open, every cent in and out crystal clear, you only fall into endless calculation and tug-of-war—you tallying whether my income's gotten too high, me tallying whether your spending's unreasonable, both of us guarding against each other at every turn, running little schemes in our heads, never able to fight side by side with a clear mind against the bigger world out there. It's precisely because there's a certain gray zone around money—because each of us has a space we can fully control, and the footing that comes with it, because we both understand how hard it is for an adult to live in this world and live with dignity—that we can actually set down a lot of pointless suspicion. We can be like trusted partners, streaming together, facing society together, building a business and an income together. This is a higher kind of trust, grounded in reality and an understanding of human nature."
[The comments split between agreement and dispute:]
[The honest truth! My husband and I split everything down to the line item, and we fight about money all the time.]
[Not transparent? Isn't that just two people each hiding their own agenda?]
[I think she has a point. Total transparency makes it feel like flatmates splitting rent, not a family.]
[The key word is "footing"—you have to be able to earn money yourself before you've earned the right to not be transparent.]
"Some people also say," Lin Wan said, looking at another kind of comment scrolling past, speaking it openly, "that you can tell at a glance: in this relationship, the woman loves the man more, and the man doesn't love the woman as much."
She faced the camera, her gaze clear, not the faintest flinch: "I can openly admit that too. If you measure us by that worldly notion of pure, fantasy-soaked, you-and-no-one-else 'deep romantic love,' then between us—maybe there isn't any. But we're both greedy for money and pleasure, partners in mischief, each taking what we need, binding each other tight. We've twisted reality's interests, the tangled web of human relations, and the most basic logic of survival together like strands of hemp rope—wound into a single body of shared interest, hard to pull apart. There's none of that fairytale, untouched-by-the-mortal-world devotion, only the unspoken symbiosis between two adults; no impractical, naive love, only the tacit understanding and the checks and balances that come after seeing through the complexity of human nature."
"I sit here, in front of the camera, playing out the kind of 'happiness' and 'wisdom' the world's gaze demands, while at the same time seeing through the essence of it all more clearly than anyone. What outsiders see is the traffic, the dignity, the safe harbor; only I know that I'm one of the most clear-eyed players in this game. I've seen through the essence of marriage as a social institution, through the light and dark of human nature, through the illusion and the reality of the online world, and through a certain fate that perhaps no woman of this era can fully escape—and, beneath that fate, the limited but important choices an individual can still make."
Her words were like a scalpel, calmly cutting open the shell of warmth to reveal the complicated, true grain inside. The studio was very quiet; even the comments seemed to slow, everyone digesting the weight of what she'd said.
"And the thing that once tormented me most—and in the end woke me up completely," Lin Wan's tone dropped, drawing on something more personal, "was the way Brother Qiang, deep in his bones, holds an almost religious faith in the power of money. He built everything from nothing, earned a considerable fortune, and he truly believes money is all-powerful, that it can solve the vast majority of problems. In his eyes, marriage is, by its nature, a clear exchange of value and a partnership—nothing low about it, but stripped of any romantic filter, full of reality's cold logic. He never hid this view. At certain moments he'd even tell me, plainly: 'My money can buy you, but it can also buy other women. You aren't the only one. I have enough wealth, which means I can have you and, at the same time, keep other options open.'"
[The comments exploded:]
[That's just too cruel a thing to say!]
[Who could put up with that? Wanwan, how did you bear it?]
[So realistic, so brutal...]
[So do rich men really all think this way?]
"The me of the past, hearing words like that," Lin Wan's voice was very calm, as if telling someone else's story—but beneath the calm lay enormous strength, "would tremble all over with anger, would feel wronged to the core, hurt, certain my feelings had been thoroughly turned into a tool, never cherished. That heart of mine, so full of longing for emotion and connection, was pierced full of holes by words like those. I'd lie awake all night, sinking into deep self-doubt: was it that I wasn't good enough? Had I lost my dignity completely in this relationship, lost my independent self? Back then, even wrapped in luxury goods every day, getting the most expensive cosmetic work done to keep up my looks—glossy on the outside—inside I was always anxious and unsettled, unable to find a single place where I could rest in peace."
She drew a small breath, her eyes turning utterly clear—the clarity left behind when the fog lifts: "I, too, was lost and struggling for a long time. I watched plenty of people around me envy me my comfort, my well-off, stable life; only I knew how much I'd lost in spirit for that 'stability,' into what corner the real 'self' had been pressed. But after the pain reached its limit, what came instead was total clarity. With my own hands I 'killed' that old self—the one so sensitive and fragile, so desperate for emotional value, who hung her happiness on the other person's attitude."
"I began to truly see through it: the way Brother Qiang builds and maintains relationships—intimate ones above all—the way he knows best, and is almost the only thing he's good at, is power control and value exchange. That's how his mind works; it's a major source of his sense of security; and to some degree, it's a baseline of his human nature, a limitation he can't change." Lin Wan analyzed it as coolly as a psychologist. "And me—I stopped fixating on demanding his 'favoritism' and his 'tenderness,' stopped grinding myself raw over some vague 'emotional value' he probably couldn't give in the first place. Instead, I began to deliberately train myself, until I mastered the ability to 'give' emotional value freely, at will. This isn't fawning. It's a clear-eyed skill."
"I pinpointed what I most fundamentally wanted from this relationship—a stable alliance for living, shared interests in growing together, and a 'shell' to house my worldly life and my feelings. Then everything I said and did began to revolve around that core goal, maintaining and running the relationship in the most low-energy, least self-depleting way. I learned to show up in the posture of 'the one being controlled,' deferring to his opinions in all outward things, giving him all the superiority and the sense of control he wanted. When he let that wealth-based arrogance and need for control show again, I no longer got angry, no longer argued. Instead I'd let my eyes curve into a smile, lean in gently, let him kiss my cheek, and then sweetly, half-joking, play along: 'I know, I know, my big boss. I'll work hard today and earn this much to show you—how's that?'"
[The comments split into two poles of interpretation:]
[This... isn't this just groveling? So lowly!]
[Why does this strike me as top-tier wisdom? Conquering hardness with softness.]
["The ability to give emotional value"—I've learned something. The point isn't to "ask," it's to "give."]
[As long as you can stand on your own. Otherwise it really is just lowly.]
"Others may see this as lowly, as caving." Lin Wan's gaze sharpened—the light that belongs to the one in control. "But only I know clearly that this was never dependence, and certainly never flattery. This is the active choice and command of a clear-eyed strong person. I saw through his hunger for the feeling of power, and I saw clearly the essence of marriage as a partnership institution—a dynamic balance of interests and feelings where each takes what they need. He's satisfied with the feeling of power that comes from being looked up to, being relied on; I get to house my life, my feelings, and my growth. At the deepest level, our needs happen to fit. On the surface it looks like he leads everything, but in fact it's me, steadily holding the rhythm and the direction of this relationship—becoming the true controller of emotion and the one who sets the pace in this marriage."
"This little household is like a miniature society. Our days look plain and ordinary, but they're full of invisible contests and adjustments at the level of the mind. Yet I'm long past panicking over it, past fearing it. I've shed the rawness, the sentimentality, and the unnecessary pride of my younger years, and transformed into someone stronger, clearer, who knows how to hold her own in this complicated reality and get what she needs from it."
She paused, then delivered what was perhaps the sharpest line of the night: "Some say that a good marriage, or any deep growth, often comes with 'killing' the self you used to be. I agree completely. I 'killed' that old self who was always anxious about gaining and losing, who kept losing her dignity and her power in the relationship. Inside this imperfect, even somewhat cold marital contract, Brother Qiang and I work each other into shape—and, to a degree, make something of each other. We no longer have those impractical illusions of love; what we have is the clear-eyed understanding that comes after seeing through reality's rules, and the solid, symbiotic relationship built on it. In the end, we've both grown into versions of ourselves that fit the present better, that move through it more at ease—at least, I have."
[The comments fell into long thought and feeling:]
["Killing the self you used to be"—that line has so much power.]
[So it's not whether the marriage is good—it's whether you have the ability to live any marriage into the shape you want.]
[Wanwan is genuinely formidable. This kind of clarity isn't something ordinary people can reach.]
[It sounds so exhausting. Why does marriage have to be this much calculation?]

========================================

Chapter 73 — The Temple of the Spirit in Marriage
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640320.html

The Temple of the Spirit in Marriage
"But here's the thing—" Just as the scrolling comments were buzzing back and forth, Lin Wan's tone shifted abruptly. She leaned slightly forward, those clear eyes seeming to pierce through the screen and look straight into each viewer's heart. "Have we ever stopped and asked ourselves one question: when I push so hard to 'get stronger,' when I work so painfully to maintain some kind of balance—who exactly am I trying to prove it to? Do I truly, deep down, want to be someone who's forever tense, forever locked in a contest, forever in 'combat' mode? Or is it… simply fear? The fear that if I let go, I'll slip in the relationship, lose this so-called 'balance of power,' lose my sense of security?"
【The comments were struck silent for a moment by the question, then a flood of raw confusion poured in:
[…I've never once thought about this.]
[I guess… it's to prove something to my husband and his family?]
[Afraid—of course I'm afraid. Afraid of being looked down on, afraid of being abandoned.]
[But if I'm not strong, doesn't that just make me weak? Doesn't it mean anyone can push me around?]
"Let me use myself as an example again." Lin Wan eased back into her chair, her smile carrying a candid, self-searching honesty. "Old friends who know me well know that Brother Qiang's business is doing pretty well. If I wanted to, I could of course grind harder, go full wolf-pack mode, chase more money, fight for the most central resources, work to become the kind of poised, halo-crowned 'power woman' who moves with ease through the arena of fame and money and all its tangled politics. Objectively speaking, I have the potential and the ability—I'd do a decent job of it, at the very least."
"But the real Lin Wan, deep in her heart, doesn't enjoy and doesn't long to live forever in that state of high-tension 'combat.' I feel that if a life were entirely filled with 'competing,' 'proving,' and 'maneuvering,' it would be utterly draining—and it would make me miss too many of the soft, lovely experiences in life, the ones you can only feel when you quiet down." Her pace slowed, and she spoke with a clear understanding of her own needs. "So you'll notice: whenever Brother Qiang isn't home, what do I spend most of my time on in this livestream? Sharing some fun but useless book I read recently, whether the gardenia on my balcony has bloomed again, a little noodle shop I found tucked away in an alley whose owner has quite a story, or some new thought I've had about a historical detail or a philosophical idea… I'm 'playing.' I'm feeling life itself. And when he comes home and needs me, in my social role as his 'partner,' to take part in certain matters or handle certain occasions together, I can switch channels in an instant—bring out the professionalism, the social energy, and the sharp mind it calls for, to coordinate and to handle things. In plain terms, that's—'earning money for him to see,' or rather, fighting for the shared 'interest entity' that is us."
【The mood in the comments lightened, with more laughter and shared recognition:
[Hahaha, too real! When my husband's out I just lie flat and binge dramas; the second he's back I sit bolt upright reading professional books.]
["Earning money for him to see"—that phrase is both bittersweet and hilarious.]
[So Wanwan, this is a kind of… strategic "playing the pig to eat the tiger"?]
[Isn't this just "acting"? Doesn't switching like that every day get exhausting?]
"This isn't 'acting.'" Lin Wan shook her head, her gaze clear and certain. "It's actively choosing your own state of being and the rhythm of how you allocate your energy. As for 'strength' and 'weakness' in marriage—if our eyes are fixed solely on comparing financial figures, ranking social titles, or even who does more housework, all those quantifiable metrics, then the whole measuring system is too one-dimensional, too flat, and it's especially easy to make a person anxious and lost. Of course we should grow, should possess the ability to demonstrate our social value and to stand on our own—that's the pair of wings we have as independent individuals. But wings are for flying, for taking in the scenery of a wider world, for experiencing a richer, more varied life. They're not just for flapping endlessly in front of your partner, merely to prove that my bird's wings are stiffer than yours, that I flap with more force."
She paused, letting the metaphor sink in, then went on in a steadier voice. "And maybe we don't have to be so fixated on becoming the kind of woman who 'never spends a single cent of a man's money,' who splits every bill down to the second decimal place, drawing a sharp line through everything. In a healthy intimate relationship, measured, mutually willing giving and receiving—including the natural flow of money—is itself an expression of emotional connection and deepening trust. The key is this: you spend his money because you need it or want it, and he happens to have extra and is glad to give—your relationship has simply grown that harmonious. Not because you 'have nothing,' so you 'must hold out your hand and ask.' The former is sharing, nourishment; the latter is begging, depletion. Different mindsets, and the whole flavor and quality of the relationship changes."
【The comments were digesting this finer distinction:
[Sharing versus begging… worlds apart. Mindset decides everything.]
[I used to insist on splitting everything; it felt so distant, and eventually he found it pointless too.]
[Financial independence is the bottom line and the backbone, but it doesn't mean severing every flow of money between two people. That's not a married couple—that's roommates.]
[The point is "natural flow" and "mutual willingness." Anything forced or calculating is meaningless.]
"So then, when we try to lift our gaze, for a moment, off these external, quantifiable, easily-compared metrics of 'strength' and 'weakness,'" Lin Wan's voice dropped lower and softer, yet grew more penetrating, as she posed the night's central question, "what is the truest, most core 'strength' in a marriage—the one that actually determines the lasting quality of the bond, that determines how both people genuinely feel within it?"
She asked and answered herself, her gaze clear as starlight. "I believe it's spiritual stability—the capacity to carry yourself and to nourish. I call it the invisible 'temple of the spirit' inside a person."
"Try to imagine," her voice was like a gentle night breeze, guiding her listeners into a state of inner reflection, "maybe I don't earn the most in the household, maybe I do the housework only so-so, sometimes lazy and unwilling to move. But from the bottom of my heart I like myself this way, I accept this state, and I don't feel anxious or attack myself for it. When I'm not flush with money, I can still feel a real, delicate joy because of a beautiful sunset outside the window today, a cool breeze passing through the leaves, or a cluster of flowers in full bloom I happen to meet by the roadside. I can keep up some habit that keeps me healthy—exercise, an early bedtime—because I cherish my body, the 'vessel' of this temple. I can easily find small things that make me happy and absorbed: reading a few pages, watching a good film, or just having one quality, unhurried chat with a close friend. My emotions aren't easily swayed by the outside world or other people's judgments; my sense of worth isn't built on my partner's feedback and attitude."
Her gaze, under the camera, became utterly firm and bright—a strength sought from within and that cannot be taken away. "A me like this is stable inside, self-sufficient, with an endless wellspring of vitality and joy. So then, in this marriage, my partner—no matter how successful he is out there, no matter how much wealth and social standing he holds—if his inner world is adrift, panicked, hollow, unable to find self-recognition or inner peace, then without me he might be like a 'lost, wandering soul' sitting amid splendor with nowhere for his heart to rest. He'd have so much, yet nowhere to settle his soul. But because I exist, because of this 'field' we've built together—full of stable emotional energy, of deep mutual understanding and acceptance—no matter what storms, pressures, and challenges he meets out there, the moment he returns to this space he finds a deep peace and a sense of being mended, the feeling that he's truly 'come home.' And this 'home' isn't only the house as physical space; it's a harbor where the heart can fully relax and be nourished."
"That," Lin Wan concluded, her tone solemn and calm, "is what I understand the 'temple of the spirit' to be. It isn't swaggering dominance, it isn't a show of control—it's a quiet, unshakable inner strength and order. You don't need to be baring your teeth and brandishing sharp words every moment to prove how impressive or how right you are. Your very existence—your stable, joyful, vital, self-governed state—is the most precious contribution and the deepest nourishment to this family system. Once you've built your inner temple well, made it solid, bright, and warm, you'll naturally draw to you people on the same frequency who need that kind of energy, and you'll be able to give the people you love a real, effective shelter from the storm—the storms of the spirit."
【The comments were deeply moved by this, surging with strong emotional resonance and reflection:
[Crying… I feel like I'm exactly that "lost, wandering soul" inside. On the surface I can do anything; inside I'm hollow.]
["Temple of the spirit"—what a phrase. I'm carving it into my mind as a goal for my life's practice.]
[So true strength is an inner calm, is having the ability to make yourself happy. What Wanwan said tonight is worth a million.]
[Please, Wanwan—how exactly do I start building my own "temple of the spirit"? I have no idea where to begin.]
"How to start building it?" Lin Wan saw that last comment and smiled gently, the smile full of encouragement and conviction. "It really is a long process that takes ongoing patience and self-awareness—there are no shortcuts. But we can begin with very small, concrete things. For example: every day, even just fifteen minutes, given entirely to yourself—handling nothing, doing nothing for any practical gain, only one small thing that is purely for your own happiness and relaxation. Practice noticing your emotions; when one arrives, don't rush to judge it good or bad, don't immediately act on it—just observe and accept its presence gently, like a bystander. Actively develop one or two hobbies or activities that absorb you so fully you forget the passing of time. Treat your true inner feelings and needs with sincerity—don't brush them off, don't run from them, practice being tender with yourself. Slowly, you'll grow more and more familiar with yourself, understand yourself, accept yourself, and then truly come to like and trust yourself. Your 'temple of the spirit' will, in this process, be built up brick by brick, growing steadily firmer."
"When your temple is built solid enough and bright enough, you'll be amazed to find that the petty grievances in marriage that once gnawed at you, the contests of strength and weakness that filled you with fear and unease, the anxieties born of comparison—they all quietly fade, and matter less and less. Because you no longer need to desperately extract attention, approval, and security from the other person to fill the hollow inside you. You are already complete in yourself. And so you can give love and care more freely, more abundantly, and you can receive the other's love and devotion more openly, more easily. Your relationship will gradually move from a tense 'survival cooperation' full of power-jockeying toward something deeper—a seeing of each other's souls, a leaning on each other, a shared dance of life."
The background music in the livestream had shifted, without anyone noticing, to a more ethereal, soothing piano piece, the notes flowing like water through the quiet summer night.
Lin Wan looked into the camera, her gaze tender as water yet holding the clear strength of someone who had passed through the fog. "So, dear friends—while we work to hone our social survival skills and raise our external value, please, truly, don't forget to spend more thought and time turning back to water the garden inside us, to build that 'temple of the spirit' that belongs to you and you alone, that can stand serene no matter how the storms rage outside. That is the most precious, most unassailable estate of your whole life—and the most solid, deepest home and far shore of your existence."
"All right, let's end tonight here. May we all, amid the noise of reality, walk step by step toward inner steadiness, becoming people who own their own temple and are unshaken by the storms. You're welcome to leave a comment with your own views and confusions about marriage—I'll see you to discuss them in the next stream. Good night."
The lights slowly dimmed, and the livestream ended. But the concept of the 'temple of the spirit'—together with Lin Wan's words tonight, a fusion of the keenest clear-eyed insight into reality and a profound inner pursuit—was like a seed brimming with life, planted deep, along with the stillness of the summer night, into the heart of every listener. In many days and nights to come, this seed might quietly sprout and grow, guiding them through the tangled maze of marriage and life to find their own quiet and powerful strength.
The livestream screen went fully dark, and the last bit of light from the running equipment went out too. The room held only the hazy glow seeping in from the distant city lights beyond the window, sketching the furniture into quiet outlines.
Lin Wan was still sitting in the chair, holding her pose toward the camera, only her spine relaxing almost imperceptibly—a hidden trace of exhaustion slipping quietly from the straight line of her shoulders. Two solid hours of high-intensity output, demanding both emotional empathy and rational self-disclosure, had left her temples aching faintly. She raised a hand and rubbed them lightly with her fingertips.
Almost the instant she lowered her hand, the study's half-open door was pushed silently inward. A tall figure came in carrying the cool air from outside, his footsteps light, clearly slowed on purpose. He didn't turn on the main light; by that faint glow alone he walked straight to her side and set a cup of honey water, at just the right temperature, gently on the table before her. The base of the cup met the wooden surface with the lightest "tap."
"Master tactician," the man's voice rose in the dim quiet, slightly hoarse after work, carrying a faint, private note of teasing. "How about a report on tonight's groundbreaking ceremony for the 'temple of the spirit'?"
It was Shuai Hongqiang. He'd just gotten back from a social engagement, still carrying the warmth of the summer night breeze and the very faint smell of smoke and drink, but his eyes were clear—he obviously hadn't had much.
Lin Wan didn't turn around right away; the corner of her mouth curved up first. She reached out and grasped the cup of warm water, the warmth spreading from her fingertips all the way to her heart, driving off some of the drained feeling that followed a stream. Only then did she turn her face and tip her head up to look at him. In the dim light his profile looked softer than usual, but the steady, faintly imposing aura ground out by years in the business arena was still there.
"A sweeping victory," she said, taking a sip to wet her slightly dry throat, her voice returning to its usual clarity, carrying a sly, childlike smugness, as if waiting to be praised. "The enemy forces—oh, no, our dear viewers—have been completely won over by the 'temple' theory my side constructed, hearts yearning toward it. The comments are full of longing, everyone declaring they're going home to start hauling bricks and building their own. Emotional value output held steady. Strategic objective: accomplished in full."
Shuai Hongqiang looked down at her; even in the dark he could clearly feel the lively, faintly show-off light in her eyes. He gave an almost inaudible low laugh, the sound trembling faintly in his chest. He passed no judgment on her "battle report," only reached out, his warm, dry palm with its thin calluses landing naturally on her cropped, neat hair, ruffling it with a touch neither light nor heavy. The gesture shed the habitual distance and need for control of the business world, leaving only a familial, indulgent intimacy.
"One crooked theory after another." His tone was flat, impossible to tell as praise or criticism, but the hand ruffling her hair didn't stop. "'Temple'… aren't you afraid that one day the wind will pick up and blow the roof clean off this little shrine of yours?"
"My temple's foundations run deep," Lin Wan said, narrowing her eyes slightly like a cat being stroked the right way, her tone certain. "No wind or rain can touch it. You, though—great benefactor that you are—might want to consider finding a steady dwelling for your own soul too. So you don't keep wandering around out there as a… mm, 'lost, wandering soul'?" She cleverly turned his own metaphor from the stream back on him, her eyes teasing.
The motion of Shuai Hongqiang's hand in her hair paused, and then he crooked a finger and flicked her forehead, neither lightly nor hard. "No respect for your elders." He withdrew his hand, his tone still even, but deep in his eyes a very faint, complicated, hard-to-read emotion flickered past, too fast to catch. "My dwelling—isn't it right here?" He glanced around this room that doubled as study and livestream space, steeped in her personal presence, his words pointed.
Lin Wan dodged away with a laugh and didn't pursue the topic. She set down the cup and pushed off the table to stand. She'd sat too long, and her legs had gone a little numb; she hissed softly and swayed.
Shuai Hongqiang's hand shot out almost at the same moment, steadying her arm firmly. His palm was large, the force judged just right—offering support without making her feel pinned. "If you're tired, go rest." His voice dropped a few notes, the teasing gone entirely, replaced by an unquestionable practicality. "Aren't you meeting that new business contact, President Wang, tomorrow? Another hard fight. Get enough rest, so you'll have the capital to keep 'conquering' your viewers—and… your 'benefactors.'"
He bit down a little harder on the word "benefactor," carrying a teasing weight only the two of them understood, mixed with some deeper meaning.
Steadying herself against his support, Lin Wan tipped her head up and winked at him. "Got it, Boss Shuai. You'd best turn in early too, sir—tomorrow I'll still be counting on you, this grand 'Buddha,' to sit in command and hold down the room for me." Her tone was light, but the meaning underneath was clear to them both. Tomorrow's meeting concerned a new product-pushing brand she had her eye on, and she needed his network and his judgment to help.
"Mm." Shuai Hongqiang let go, answering faintly, and watched her gather the scattered prompt cards and script pages on the desk. He didn't leave—just stepped back half a pace, leaned against the edge of the desk, and waited quietly. His tall figure in the dim light was like a silent, dependable mountain.
Lin Wan quickly tidied everything and shut down the computer. After one last check, she picked up her phone and the cup and turned around. Shuai Hongqiang straightened up too, falling naturally into step half a pace behind her as they headed toward the bedroom together.
"Oh, right," at the bedroom door Lin Wan suddenly remembered something and looked back at him. "Did you eat tonight? There's soup keeping warm in the kitchen."
The night closed over the quiet house once more. In the study, the Paulownia tree and the dried eucalyptus leaves were nothing but blurred outlines in the dark, as if they too had sunk into sleep. And tonight's deep dissections of "interest-binding" and the "temple of the spirit," the crossing of wits, and the complex feelings and unspoken understanding flowing beneath the surface—all of it, with the stream's end, quietly settled, becoming another layer of the solid, unspoken backdrop that existed between this husband and wife. When tomorrow's sun rose, life and the game would both go on.

========================================

Chapter 74 — A Bond Sealed in Midsummer, Love Plain to See
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640321.html

A Bond Sealed in Midsummer, Love Plain to See
Early July, midsummer blazing like a furnace. At six in the evening the sun still hung arrogantly high in the western sky, pouring down scalding gold without mercy. The air felt set solid, as if baked by fire, and every breath drawn into the lungs carried a searing heat. Summer in Jinshi was dry and violent, its fierce, scorching edge sparing no one.
Yellow River Tower stood on the riverbank, and on its top floor the "Guanlan room" had floor-to-ceiling glass on three sides, taking in without reservation the Yellow River winding away to the north and the vast, hazy mountains on the far shore. Inside the room the air conditioning sent out a soothing coolness without a sound, drawing a clean line between this space and the world outside, where the sun raged unchecked. The setting sun was sinking slowly in a posture both tragic and magnificent, dyeing the whole length of the Yellow River the color of molten gold. The water shimmered, broken flecks of gold dancing on it, so bright it was almost impossible to keep one's eyes open. In the distance, on the iron-boat tea stalls built out over the river's shallows, you could see figures sitting at ease on the boats, leisurely sipping covered-bowl "three-steep" tea against the grand sweep of the river. That ease, and the coolness inside the room, were like two parallel worlds — neither disturbing the other, yet both belonging to this fierce midsummer.
Almost everyone had arrived. Wang Qian sat alone on the sofa by the window, the cup of plain tea before him long gone cold, gazing at the endlessly surging Yellow River outside, his eyes vacant, his thoughts drifting somewhere unknown. Liu Qingzhuo and Chen Ye sat close together; Liu Qingzhuo was holding up her phone to show Chen Ye a new wine-list design, and Chen Ye leaned in, tapping and pointing at the screen, the two of them murmuring to each other. Jiang Tiantian had taken hold of Jin Lan and was rattling off, at top speed, the new in-depth interview series she was planning, her eyes glinting with excitement.
Just then the door was pushed open by a waiter, and Yao Yuan stepped gracefully in. A wave of heat seemed to try to slip in with her, only to be swallowed instantly by the cold air. Today she wore a halter-neck slip dress in true red silk that perfectly showed off the lovely line of her shoulders and neck and her clean-cut collarbone, paired below with a pair of white high-waisted, side-slit suit trousers — crisp and yet sexy. Her thick, long curls had been carefully styled, draped loosely behind her shoulders, swaying gently with each step. Her makeup was bright and retro, lips like fire, skin fairer than snow, and the two gold tassel earrings at her ears swung and shone as she moved, the whole of her dazzlingly, flamboyantly beautiful.
Right behind her came Zhao Yiming, in a light gray-blue cotton short-sleeved shirt with dark gray casual trousers and a plain black watch on his wrist, giving off a clean, fresh air that came straight from the laboratory. The two walked in one after the other, and though there was no show of intimacy between them, Zhao Yiming, very naturally, turned to one side and lightly steadied the doorframe, not letting go until Yao Yuan had fully entered. That small but protective gesture did not escape several sharp pairs of eyes in the room.
"Aiyo! We've been waiting forever for you two!" Jin Lan was the first to spring up from her seat, her gaze sweeping over the pair like a searchlight, the corner of her mouth instantly curling into a mischievous "I knew it" smile. "Boss Yao, you've got the sunset itself wrapped around you, haven't you? Such an eye-catching red! And Boss Zhao, in that outfit… standing next to Boss Yao, you're a tall, upright green pine — steady as anything!"
Yao Yuan shot her a smiling, chiding look but said nothing, greeting everyone with easy poise. Zhao Yiming, too, nodded politely to the company, his expression as composed as ever.
After the round of greetings, everyone took their seats. Exquisite cold dishes and ice-chilled drinks were soon on the table, and the mood in the room livened up at once.
Beside Jin Lan sat a tall, handsome face no one knew — Lu Ang, twenty-nine, once a professional basketball player, with a buzz cut, wheat-colored skin, and a bright, easy laugh. These days he ran several chain fitness brands. Through the meal he looked after Jin Lan with the utmost care, pouring her tea, serving her food, passing her napkins, his movements practiced and natural, his eyes barely leaving her for a moment, a warmth intense enough to melt a person. In front of him, Jin Lan's usual queen-of-the-business-world edge had softened considerably; her eyes curved, and now and then he made her laugh out loud, and she would even give his arm a light, half-petulant tap.
"Sister Lan, this is… aren't you going to introduce him?" Liu Qingzhuo asked with a smile, raising an eyebrow.
Jin Lan took a light sip of chilled champagne, her gaze sliding sideways to Lu Ang, her tone carrying its usual swagger and lazy pride: "Lu Ang, the trainer from my gym. Well? Pretty good physique he's built, don't you think?"
Lu Ang at once grinned wide, showing a mouthful of white teeth, and raised his glass on his own initiative: "Hello, everyone, I'm Lu Ang. Learning a lot from Sister Lan."
Chen Ye smiled and clinked glasses with him: "Trainer Lu looks full of energy. Sister Lan has quite an eye."
"Of course!" Jin Lan said, not a hint of modesty, swirling the glass in her hand. Lu Ang smiled beside her, his eyes glued to her the whole time.
On Liu Qingzhuo and Chen Ye's side, Chen Ye was carefully cutting the tenderest meat off the roast lamb leg and setting it in the small dish in front of Liu Qingzhuo. Liu Qingzhuo, meanwhile, said in a low voice to Yao Yuan, "Yuanyuan, do you have time soon to get afternoon tea together? There's something I'd like to ask you about in private." Her voice was soft, but Yao Yuan caught at once, in the depths of her eyes, that particular caution belonging to a mature woman.
"No problem at all, anytime." Yao Yuan understood immediately, raising her glass to touch it lightly against Liu Qingzhuo's. Liu Qingzhuo's wine business was thriving, Chen Ye came from a well-off family, and the two were steady together. Though Liu Qingzhuo was on her second marriage, both were in their thirties, and the move toward marriage had come naturally. What this so-called "asking for advice" meant, both of them knew perfectly well. Adult love, however ardent, still requires — at the threshold of marriage — a clear-eyed weighing and protecting of one's own interests; that is both maturity and necessity. All the more so since Liu Qingzhuo was stepping into marriage a second time, and would naturally treat it with greater care.
Gao Jieyuan had come uninvited.
This young master, ever since the last New Year's gathering, had launched against Yao Yuan a loud and, in his own mind, romantic "campaign of pursuit." Nine hundred and ninety-nine air-freighted roses piled up at the company front desk, frenzied attention-seeking in her livestream room, asking around everywhere about Yao Yuan's schedule to engineer all sorts of "chance encounters," even an attempt to serenade her in public at a banquet once… an extravagant, dazzling array of tricks. Yet Yao Yuan's only response was polite silence. The roses, once signed for, she had her assistant distribute to the entire staff; in the livestream room she set his tips to be blocked; at the "chance encounters" she would nod in acknowledgment and walk on by; and at the public serenade, she simply left early, citing an important work call.
It was probably the first time he had ever run into such a tough bone to chew on the "field of love," and the colder Yao Yuan was, the more his roused competitiveness and urge to conquer twisted and swelled. Later, when he learned that Yao Yuan had once been together with Yu Hao, it all became clear to him — he finally understood why Yu Hao had sparred with him that time at Crimson Moon Palace. Then he heard the two had already broken up, and secretly rejoiced, thinking his chance had come, getting ready to save up for one big move.
But tonight, the moment he stepped into the room and saw the kind of wordless rapport between Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming — a single glance, a small gesture, and their hearts were understood; saw Zhao Yiming pour Yao Yuan's tea so naturally (she tapped the rim of the cup lightly with her fingertip, and he stopped at exactly the right moment, the amount of tea just right); saw the times their eyes met, and that calm smile that belonged to the two of them alone, as if silently exchanging some complex message… the careless, cynical smile on Gao Jieyuan's face froze in an instant, and the little unrealistic fantasy in his heart shattered, with a "crack," into bits.
He forced himself to sit down, putting on a show of calm as he tossed out jokes and banter, but his eyes, beyond his control, kept stealing glances that way. He saw Zhao Yiming explaining in a low voice the low-temperature slow-cook technique used in one of the innovative dishes, Yao Yuan listening with full attention, her profile especially soft in the light; saw how, when Yao Yuan spoke, Zhao Yiming's gaze stayed fixed on her face — a focus built on intellectual equality, on deep mutual understanding and appreciation, nothing at all like his own kind of pleasure-hunting gaze that saw only the surface.
Gao Jieyuan felt as though a jar of mixed flavors had been knocked over inside him, every taste surging up at once. He, Gao Jieyuan — illustrious family, young and handsome, always smooth-sailing on the field of love — yet facing Zhao Yiming, a man who had built a vast commercial empire from nothing in the hardcore tech field, found himself crushed across the board: in wealth, in the scope of his enterprise, in personal ability, in that steady, reserved aura of having everything under control. Apart from the trifling advantage of being "younger," he could not find a single point on which he could stand against the man. And Yao Yuan, clearly, wanted not "youth" but the "height" and "depth" of someone who could resonate with her soul and look down on the world at her side.
After a few rounds of drinks, the mood grew livelier still. The unholy fire in Gao Jieyuan's heart, mixed with the liquor, came surging straight up, and he couldn't help himself; with a hint of provocation, he spoke up toward Zhao Yiming: "Boss Zhao, I hear your 'Western World' has been tinkering with some kind of emotional AI lately? This thing… can it really be reliable? Cold, lifeless code — can it understand love between men and women, the scheming of the human heart?" His tone was full of a wastrel's contemptuous doubt toward a field he didn't understand.
The laughter and chatter at the table died down at once.
Zhao Yiming set down his chopsticks, picked up a wet wipe and cleaned his hands, then lifted his eyes to Gao Jieyuan, his gaze calm as still water, his tone carrying the steadiness of a researcher laying out the facts: "Mr. Gao, affective computing is a frontier field at the intersection of cognitive science and artificial intelligence. Our work is not about making a program 'possess' feelings.
