"Clear-eyed growth means first acknowledging and accepting the complexity and many sides of human nature and feeling. Is there calculation in love? Yes. Is there no binding of interests in marriage? Impossible. But turn it around—is there really not a shred of true heart in calculation? Can deep dependence and feeling never grow out of a union of interests? Equally possible. True clarity is being able to see and understand all of these layers at once, without being hijacked by any single narrative."

"It means you can believe in the beauty of love while knowing how to protect your own property; you can fight for your career with everything you have while not being ashamed to admit you long for intimacy; you can shrewdly calculate the pros and cons of a partnership while never losing the ability to treat people with sincerity. Clarity is the ability to move freely through multiple realities and make the choices that best serve your overall happiness—not just your economic interests. It lets you see through the rules of the game while never fully becoming a cold pawn within it. You can join in the gamesmanship when you must, and enjoy what's pure when it's safe to."

========================================

Chapter 80 — Facing the Controversy Head-On: Clear-Eyed Awareness Was Never the Commercialization of Human Nature
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640327.html

A clear note of admiration showed in Jiang Tiantian's eyes. She fell silent for a moment, as if digesting the density of what had just been said. Then she sat up straight, folded her hands in her lap, and her expression turned more serious as she put forward a more direct, more challenging question.

"Yao Yuan, this 'cognitive map' and 'methodology' of yours have given you enormous influence—but they've also put you at the center of public debate. I can't avoid a criticism people online have leveled at you very sharply, even abusively. Many accuse you of essentially objectifying women and objectifying feelings—of teaching girls not self-love and independence, but how to 'scheme' and 'extract' more cleverly. Others have dug up some of your early content and clients, attacking the fact that you started out serving so-called 'mistresses' and 'working girls,' arguing that your true colors are teaching people to take shortcuts and latch onto others. Faced with these voices—these very ugly, directly personal attacks—what goes through your mind? How do you face them, and how do you make peace with yourself?"

The question landed like a stone dropped into a still lake, instantly shattering the rational, exploratory mood of before and bringing real pressure, sharp with the smell of gunpowder. The air in the recording booth seemed to congeal.

There was no flash of offended anger on Yao Yuan's face, no flustered rush to defend herself. She even shifted her posture slightly, settling more comfortably against the back of her chair—a posture that was not defense, but acceptance. She was silent for a few seconds, seconds that would feel very long in the recording. Then she lifted her eyes and met Jiang Tiantian's gaze calmly, as if looking through the equipment at everyone who might hold such a view.

"Tiantian, thank you for asking this. It matters, and I never dodge it." Her voice was as steady as ever, but it carried a heavier weight now. "First, I understand where this criticism and anger come from. When we talk about feelings, marriage, self-interest, it's very easy to touch the parts of people they treasure most and feel most vulnerable about—their faith in pure emotion, their hope for fairness and justice, their ingrained sense of what a woman's identity and worth should be. The way I express things—direct, sharp, full of business and game-theory terms—looks to many people like a 'desecration' and 'objectification' of sacred emotion in itself. It's a fierce clash at the level of perception, and I respect that feeling."

Then her tone shifted, becoming clear and firm.

"But understanding is not the same as agreeing. There are a few things I need to clarify very plainly."

"First, on 'objectification.' I believe that pointing out the value exchange, risk assessment, and self-interested games that objectively exist within relationships is not the same as preaching 'objectification.' Quite the opposite—only by daring to face and discuss the real dimensions that romantic narratives deliberately conceal can women avoid being truly 'objectified' and exploited without ever realizing it. I'm teaching girls to see the board and the rules clearly, not teaching them to willingly become pawns to be pushed around on it—or worse, to become the stakes on someone else's board. 'Clarity' is the antidote to 'objectification,' not its accomplice."

"Second, on 'teaching people to extract.' The core premise and ultimate goal of all my advice is to raise a woman's own value, ability, and power to choose. It's teaching them how to build their own careers, protect their own assets, hold their own boundaries, and win equal respect and treatment within a relationship. I encourage 'earning' by your own ability, 'creating,' 'negotiating'—not 'scraping,' 'leeching,' 'begging.' There's a fundamental difference. If someone thinks that learning to protect the interests you're rightly owed, and demanding equal respect when you give, makes you a 'gold-digger,' then perhaps they need to re-examine their assumptions about the role of women."

"Third, on my early audience." Here Yao Yuan paused, and a flicker of complicated emotion crossed her eyes—understanding in it, but also an unwavering candor. "Yes. When I was just starting out, when my reach was still very limited, part of my audience really was women in non-mainstream romantic relationships, or women in especially difficult, even marginalized emotional situations. In mainstream discourse they were ignored, scorned, even cast out. But their pain was real, their predicaments were concrete, and they too longed to be seen, to be understood, to receive practical help. The analytical perspective and practical advice I offered may not fit traditional moral judgment, but it really did help some of them clarify their situations, make choices that served them better, even climb out of the mire. I have never used any group's 'moral standing' to pre-decide who is qualified to receive the help of 'clear-eyed awareness.' The cognitive tool itself is neutral; what matters is the person using it—to save themselves, or to hurt others. My content has always pointed toward the former."

Her tone gradually took on a deep, grounded strength.

"As for the abuse and the attacks—it would be a lie to say they don't affect me at all. I'm human too. But slowly I learned to distinguish. Some criticism comes from misunderstanding and differences in perception, and I'm willing to keep communicating, to keep explaining what I actually mean. But the larger share—the pure malice, the labeling and the insults—I choose not to listen to. Why? Because my time and energy are very limited, and I want to keep them for the people who genuinely need help, who are willing to grow alongside me—for my own work, my team, and the value I want to create. Being liked by everyone has never been my goal. My goal is that the people who trust me and follow me live a little better, a little clearer, a little stronger because of my content. As long as that core goal keeps being met—as long as I see more and more women lift their awareness and walk out of bad relationships, build their own careers, win more equal intimate relationships—then the noise outside is just noise."

She looked at Jiang Tiantian at last, her gaze clear and resolute.

"Tiantian, the 'instruction manual' this era gives women is too vague, even self-contradictory. I'm only trying—based on my extensive 'clinical' observation and my own practice—to write a supplementary guide that comes closer to the real complexity of things: about 'emotional and interpersonal risk control' and 'building your own value.' It isn't perfect; it may offend some people. But it serves others. That's what I do. As for labels—people are free to stick them on, but I know who I am, what I'm doing, and where the value lies. That's enough."

Jiang Tiantian was silent for a long time. She only looked at Yao Yuan deeply, scrutiny in that gaze, inquiry too, and finally it turned into a deep, moved admiration. She took off her glasses, rubbed the bridge of her nose, and put them back on—a tiny gesture that betrayed the stir inside her.

"I understand," she said at last, her voice a little low and rough.

After a pause she pressed forward with another pointed question.

"Yao Yuan, you've turned this extraordinarily profound 'cognitive map' of human nature and feelings into a phenomenon-level business—your livestreams, your courses, your community, and now the AI project you're running with a top technical team. Some marvel that this is wisdom turned into revenue; others question whether it 'vulgarizes' and 'commercializes' emotion. How do you yourself define what you're doing? Is it a business about 'growth,' or a business about 'human nature'? Or are the two simply one and the same?"

The question was sharp enough, and it touched the core of the controversy surrounding her. There was no trace of offense or defensiveness on Yao Yuan's face. She even smiled faintly, a smile with a candid clarity to it.

"Teacher Jiang asks very directly, so I'll answer directly too." Her posture was still elegant, her gaze as keen as ever. "I believe what I do is a business about 'clear-eyed choice.' And the premise of clear-eyed choice is to see the truth as far as possible—the truth about yourself, about others, about how the world actually runs. My content, my products, everything I put out, comes down to one thing: providing cognitive tools, reducing information asymmetry, helping my users—especially my women users—see a little more clearly amid complicated reality and emotional confusion, and thereby raise the odds that they'll make choices that serve them."

"Is emotional confusion a widespread, genuine need? Yes. Is understanding the rules of the games people play with each other an important awareness, one that can spare you a lot of pointless harm? Yes. Is it reasonable commerce to pass that awareness on through livestreams, courses, even smarter means in the future, and charge for it? I think it is. It's like a lawyer providing legal knowledge, a doctor providing medical knowledge. What I provide—based on extensive case study and my own practice—is an 'awareness risk-screening' and 'strategy optimization advice' for feelings and relationships. Of course it has value, and of course it can become a business."

She paused, and her tone grew deeper still.

"As for the charge of 'vulgarizing'—I think there may be a prejudice behind it, the belief that the emotional and spiritual realm should be 'pure,' untainted by commerce. But the reality is that knowledge, wisdom, and experience have value in themselves. Free things are often the most expensive, because they can cost you immeasurable hidden costs—time, emotion, even a lifetime of happiness. Paying for valuable awareness is respect for the person who produced that knowledge, and it's taking responsibility for your own growth. What matters is whether what I provide truly helps people, whether it truly brings positive change. From the feedback of countless users, I think the answer is yes."

"So it's both a business about growth and a business about human nature—because a person's growth is, in essence, the ongoing process of colliding with your own nature, with others' nature, and with the rules of the world, then understanding them and finding a way to coexist. I've simply distilled some of the shared, crucial 'awareness nodes' and 'game models' in that process and turned them into products that can be transmitted and learned." She concluded calmly and with conviction.

Quiet settled over the booth again, only the faint hum of the equipment's current. Jiang Tiantian looked at Yao Yuan for several seconds, then slowly, deeply nodded.

"What I'm hearing isn't just the logic of a successful entrepreneur—it's the life wisdom of someone who has completed a profound integration of self." She said it with feeling, then shifted, her tone taking on a more personal note. "One last question, Yao Yuan. For you personally, having walked all these roads and arrived at the 'clarity' you have now, what do you think matters most? If you could say one sentence to the young women still searching in the fog, what would you say?"

Yao Yuan fell silent. The question reached into a more private territory. She stroked the old jade at her wrist again, and its warmth seemed to flow into every limb. She thought of the unsolved fog of the Mirror Pact, of the starry sky over the Gobi and Zhao Yiming's rational eyes, of the joys and sorrows of different versions of "herself" she had witnessed across her travels through time, and of all the scars and medals she had earned along the way.

"For me personally," her voice grew very soft, yet every word carried the weight of a thousand pounds, "what matters most is perhaps this: that after all the disillusionment, the wounds, the scrutiny and the abandonment, I still didn't give up trusting and shaping myself—and that in the end I learned to make peace with the world's complexity, rather than fight it or flee it. Clarity isn't never being hurt again; it's knowing, after the wound, how to heal and grow tougher. It isn't never believing again; it's learning how to discern and protect yourself. It isn't becoming cold; it's being able, even after you understand the rules deeply, to keep the most precious warmth in your heart and the capacity to choose kindness."

She lifted her eyes, looking straight at Jiang Tiantian, and as if through the equipment, at countless possible listeners.

"If I had to say one sentence to young women, I'd say: cherish and invest in yourself with everything you have—your health, your awareness, your skills, your financial base. That is the lifeboat that will never sink under you, in any relationship, in any storm. Then go bravely—to experience, to love, to be hurt, to succeed, to fail. But whatever you go through, always remember: you are the only, and the most important, author and protagonist of your own life's script. Other people's judgments, society's clock, the comings and goings of love—they're only scenery or interludes in your story; they can't define you. The only thing that can define you is the choices you make for yourself, again and again, after clear-eyed awareness—and the effort you put into those choices, and the responsibility you bear for them."

When her voice fell, the booth stayed silent for a long while.

Jiang Tiantian didn't add any summary or assessment. She only nodded again, gravely and deeply, then made a closing gesture toward the control room.

The interview was over.

As she took off her headphones, the sounds of the world rushed back in. Yao Yuan felt a deep exhaustion, but also a strange ease, as if she'd finished a high-quality session of mental yoga. The thoughts buried deep in her heart—including her response to the sharpest doubts—had all been clearly sorted and expressed through this conversation.

"Thank you, Yao Yuan." Jiang Tiantian rose, came around the little round table, and this time, instead of shaking hands, gave Yao Yuan's shoulders a light embrace—a gesture full of the understanding and support that passes between women. "Today's conversation will mean a great deal to me, and to many of the people who hear it. Thank you for your work."

"Thank you too, Tiantian. It was a wonderful conversation." Yao Yuan returned a sincere smile; she could feel a maturity and generosity in Jiang Tiantian that ran beyond her years.

Leaving the booth, the daylight outside had already deepened to dusk. Her assistant handed over her coat and clutch. Yao Yuan walked to the great floor-to-ceiling window and looked out. The streetlamps in the campus came on one after another, the neon of the distant city beginning to flicker—another ordinary Jinshi evening.

The old jade at her chest quietly gave off its steady warmth. The podcast interview was over, but the clues about the Mirror Pact, the testing of the Pretty Big One, the brand-new relationship just beginning with Zhao Yiming, her own voyage through the currents of the age and her personal fate… all of it still went on.

She drew her coat closer, turned, and walked toward the elevator with a steady stride. Her silhouette, in the thickening dusk and the first-lit lights, looked clear and sure. Whether facing the probing of a deep interview or the unsolved riddles of life, she had already found a rhythm and a way of the heart that were her own—staying clear-eyed amid chaos, guarding her true self amid the games, creating value amid commerce, and, in growth, continually reshaping a stronger, ever more complete self. This was the game Yao Yuan had chosen: the game of the clear-eyed.

========================================

Chapter 81 — The Child She Lost Eight Years Ago: A Truth Arrived Late, a Wound Still Open
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640328.html

The visit to Old Chen came on the first weekend after that podcast interview wrapped. Autumn was settling in. The sky was a high, clear, pale blue, and the wind had shed its sticky summer heat for something crisp and cool. Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang agreed to meet near the Hall of Antiquities and go on together. Even though the Mirror Pact had forcibly bound them back together inside some strange, unreadable knot of fate eight years after their breakup, traveling side by side like this still carried a deliberately maintained calm — an undercurrent moving beneath a still surface. These days they were more like partners thrown together by an unsolved case: a shared goal, but each with their own wounds and their own calculations.

Yao Yuan had prepared carefully for the visit. Besides the pre-Qingming Longjing tea she'd noticed Old Chen favored last time, she had gone out of her way to trade a collector for a fine Qing-dynasty Hetian jade "river-stone" carving — "Abundance Year After Year." The jade was warm and smooth as fat, the lotus leaves and carp carved in fluid, lively lines, the meaning auspicious, well suited for an elder to turn over in his hands. She wrapped the tea and the jade carefully and set them inside a plain rosewood box. The gift was partly a thank-you for the protection the "heart-guarding jade" had given her, and partly in keeping with the old courtesy — "You gave me a peach; I repay you with fine jade." More than that, it was simply how she conducted herself: thorough, complete.

Shuai Hongqiang had brought some tonics too, but next to hers they looked ordinary. His eyes caught for a moment on the box in Yao Yuan's hands, but he said nothing. The Yao Yuan of today was long past being the young woman who once needed him to weigh out his every gesture with care — afraid that giving too much would look desperate, that giving too little would seem insincere. Now her manner of giving was composed and certain, the natural confidence of someone whose money and standing were firmly in place.

Inside the Hall of Antiquities, that air of settled time still hung over everything. Old Chen was hale and sharp-eyed, and was glad to see them — especially glad to see the jade piece Yao Yuan had brought. He turned it over in his hands for a long while, praising it again and again, his eyes holding the pure appreciation a man feels for fine jade, with no trace of greed. That detachment only deepened Yao Yuan's respect for him.

The three of them sat at the tea table, the delicate fragrance of tea curling up. Yao Yuan spoke first, describing the changes of the four months since she'd put on the "heart-guarding jade." Her tone was even, as if reporting on a peculiar medical case. "...there's only been one time-travel, on the road back from the Gobi. It was very short — maybe less than a third of the earlier episodes. And after I came back, that feeling of having my soul scooped out, of being completely drained, was much lighter. There was still some discomfort, but I recovered quickly. Old Chen, this jade really does have a remarkable effect on settling the soul and steadying the mind." Without thinking she pressed a hand to her chest. Even through the fabric she could feel that soothing warmth — a rare, constant comfort in the middle of a turbulent fate.

Old Chen stroked his beard and nodded slowly. "Old jade has a spirit to it, and this kind — a 'heart-guarding' jade that's been worn close and nurtured over years — is best of all at steadying the soul and shutting out outside ills. Its weakening the Mirror Pact's direct pull on you is a good thing. But it's like building a dyke against a flood: the root cause isn't gone, so the danger remains. You can't let your guard down."

The conversation turned naturally to the lead on the descendants of the mirror-makers. Old Chen had called on a few of his most discreet old connections; Shuai Hongqiang had made inquiries through his own channels from many angles; and adding in the scraps of information Yao Yuan had gathered through the "Pretty Big One" network, all three lines, pulled together, pointed faintly toward a local Jinshi family with the surname "Wang." More specifically, the target had narrowed to a man in his seventies named "Wang Baoguo." From the fragments pieced together, the family scholarship seemed somewhat unusual — an ancestor may have been a sort of diviner or occultist — but it was all so long ago that the lineage had vanished into obscurity. Wang Baoguo himself lived in seclusion, scarcely dealing with the outside world. For now there was only a hazy name and an approximate age; his exact address and contact information were still being painstakingly traced, as if some invisible barrier stood in the way.

"Jinshi isn't large, but it isn't small either. To find an old man who's deliberately gone nameless and lives hidden away — it's like fishing a needle out of the sea." Old Chen looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze meant to soothe. "Girl of the Yao family, don't be anxious. Narrowing it down to Jinshi is already major progress. As long as the heart-guarding jade keeps working, you've bought yourself some time. We'll keep searching separately. The truth will come out in the end."

Shuai Hongqiang spoke up too, his tone unusually earnest. "Old Chen is right. Yuan— Yao Yuan, don't fret too much. Everything on my end is going smoothly. A few of the company's projects are moving along even better than I expected, and things at home are peaceful." He paused, his voice dropping, carrying a complicated kind of awe. "Because of the Mirror Pact... you wear yourself out time-traveling, and I... I've been on the receiving end of the good fortune it converts. I've kept Old Chen's instructions in mind the whole time, never daring to slack off, always reminding myself to do more good, to hold a sense of reverence." As he said it, his eyes met Yao Yuan's for an instant. The feeling in them was heavy — there was gratitude, there seemed to be guilt too, and most of all a solemnity, the gravity of a man seized by an unseen force and forced to look his fate full in the face.

Yao Yuan gave a slight nod and said nothing. She couldn't feel any genuine happiness over Shuai Hongqiang's "smooth sailing" — it had been bought with her own mental wear and risk, and the taste of it was complicated. But rationally she understood that there was no use dwelling on it.

A few rounds of tea, and once everything that needed saying had been said, the two of them rose to take their leave. Old Chen saw them to the door of the Hall of Antiquities. The autumn sun was just right, slanting across the bluestone path.

For the short walk to where the cars were parked, the usual silence sat between them. There was only the soft sound of footsteps. Yao Yuan's car was a little ahead, Shuai Hongqiang's behind it. And just as Yao Yuan was about to open her car door — she didn't know which nerve had been touched, didn't know how long the emotion had been building before it found one fine crack — she suddenly turned and looked at Shuai Hongqiang, a few steps away.

The autumn wind passed over her, lifting a stray wisp of hair at her cheek. The expression on her face was faint, faint as that thread of thin cloud in the sky just then, and her voice was eerily calm, as if she were mentioning some old news that had nothing to do with her.

"By the way. There's something I never told you." She paused, her gaze brushing over Shuai Hongqiang's face — momentarily blank — and fixing on the empty distance. "Eight years ago, before I left Jinshi, I was carrying your child."

Time seemed in that instant to freeze, to stretch out, to warp.

Every expression on Shuai Hongqiang's face — that lingering trace of thought about the Mirror Pact lead, the complicated attention he paid Yao Yuan, even the practiced shrewdness and composure of a businessman — all of it shattered and broke apart in a heartbeat. He looked as if he hadn't heard clearly, and also as if a sledgehammer had slammed into his chest. His pupils contracted sharply, his lips moved, but no sound came out. He simply stood frozen, staring blankly at Yao Yuan, as if seeing her for the first time — or rather, seeing for the first time the deeply buried, blood-soaked truth of that relationship eight years ago.

Yao Yuan's tone still held no ripple, carried even a thread of detachment, as if none of it concerned her, but every word fell like an ice-pick driven into Shuai Hongqiang's abruptly stilled heart.

"Eight years ago, the day I made that last call to you, standing in the hospital corridor... You never once gave a clear answer about marrying me, about registering, about getting married. You'd only put me off, go silent, change the subject — say things like 'no rush,' 'let's think it over.' I understood."

She tugged at the corner of her mouth. It wasn't a smile, only an extremely faint, self-mocking pull of muscle.

"So, that first snow of that winter eight years ago, I had the procedure alone. And then I went to Hangzhou."

Having said it, she didn't look again at Shuai Hongqiang's face — the color drained from it in an instant, pale as paper — nor at those eyes flooding all at once with vast, stunned pain, bewilderment, and something close to collapse. She crisply pulled open the car door, slid into the driver's seat, started the engine, and the car glided smoothly out of the space and merged into the street's flow of traffic, without the slightest pause.

It left Shuai Hongqiang standing there like a clay statue suddenly drained of its soul, frozen under the bright but heatless early-autumn sun. The noise of passing cars, the faint sound of distant voices — all of it seemed to come through a thick layer of frosted glass, blurred, warped, unreal. Only Yao Yuan's words, calm to the point of cruelty, detonated in his mind with perfect clarity, word by word, echoing over and over —

"...I was carrying your child..."

"...you never once gave a clear answer about marrying me..."

"...I had the procedure alone..."

...

Eight years ago. He'd made a success of himself, but in his bones that timidity, born of childhood poverty, clung to the bottom of his heart like damp lichen that no sun could dry. Yao Yuan's beauty was breathtaking, and so bright it left him nowhere to hide. She was the fiercest flower blooming in his barren world, dew and thorns and all — he loved to look at her, and was afraid of being pricked. He wavered, unable to decide to marry her, unwilling to let her go. The suppressed catch in her voice on that last call — he'd heard it, but had chosen to read it only as a beautiful, tearful coercion, pushing him to make a decision.

He'd thought the greatest harm could be no more than that: making her sad, watching her turn and walk away wrapped in that dazzling light of hers, like a band of burning sunset clouds at the edge of the sky he could never reach again — once they burned out, the sky went dark. On certain late nights after social obligations, he'd think of those burning clouds, and feel a hollow, scorched-after ache rise in his chest, and then tell himself: this is reality.

He had never once imagined — had never dared to imagine — that behind those silences and that catching breath was hidden a small life just conceived and just as quickly extinguished. Hidden there was her despair as she lay alone on a cold operating table. Hidden there was her resolve, after that double agony of body and heart, to leave for a distant city for good.

If he had known she was pregnant...

The supposition was like a branding iron heated red, searing hard against his nerves.

If he had known... he would certainly have married her!

He would have held this thorned flower, this flower carrying his own blood, close against his chest — firmly, even clumsily! He might still have lost his nerve, but he could never have been so cold-blooded as to let her face alone that abyss of flesh torn from flesh. He would have used the fragile contract of marriage, that single sheet of paper, to build for her at least the most basic social fence; whether the days inside that fence were sweet or bitter would be a matter for later, another flavor a long life had to chew on — anything better than leaving her alone on an operating table, chewing on cold steel and desperate solitude.

But there was no "if."

She had called, and he had not given her the firm answer she needed. So she had used her own way — cruelly, completely — to cut off every possibility, and to bury that unformed child, and the last thread of tenderness between them.

Eight years. For eight whole years she had kept this secret, watching him marry and have a child, watching his "happy" family, watching him rise and fall in business — even, after the matter of the Mirror Pact, watching him live "smoothly" thanks to her possible "sacrifice"...

No wonder. No wonder there was always that inescapable cold and distance in her eyes when she looked at him — not merely the estrangement after a breakup, but something steeped in blood and pain she had borne alone. No wonder she could never truly let it go where he was concerned, holding to a clear line even while they worked together to find the mirror-makers' descendants.

Enormous guilt rose like a surging black tide and drowned him in an instant. Mixed into it was the agony eight years late, the loathing for his own cowardly, muddled younger self, the helpless heartache that Yao Yuan had borne it all alone, and a deep, absurd grief at how fate toys with people. He thought of how he'd just been saying "everything's going smoothly," "do more good," "hold a sense of reverence"... Now those words were the sharpest mockery, cutting him until nothing was left whole. His "smooth sailing" might truly be built, in part, on her pain and on the life of a child he had never met!

He staggered, catching the cold body of the car beside him to barely steady himself. The sun was glaring, yet he felt cold all over, cold enough that the very marrow of his bones trembled. His stomach churned, nausea rising to his throat. He wanted to chase after her, to grab Yao Yuan and ask her clearly, to roar, to apologize, to do something... but what could he do? Eight years. Seas turned to fields. That child had long since turned to dust; Yao Yuan was no longer the Yao Yuan of back then; and he had a family responsibility he could not shrug off. Everything was beyond recall.

So it turned out: some debts, once owed, truly cannot be repaid. Some wrongs, once committed, truly cannot be made right. The mysterious tie of the Mirror Pact might be severed by some mystical means; but this blood-debt, this debt of the heart from eight years ago, had long since been branded deep into the rings of both their lives — a hidden scar that could never truly heal.

Yao Yuan's car had long since vanished around the corner. Shuai Hongqiang still stood rooted in place. The autumn wind blew across a back that seemed in that moment to have stooped a little, lifting a few strands of hair at his brow, baring beneath them a pair of eyes gone vacant and bloodshot, brimming with pain, remorse, and a boundless emptiness. This man, always so shrewd and capable in business, now looked as if every support had been pulled out from under him — nothing left but a shell shattered by the truth.

He knew that, from this moment on, some things were utterly different. Toward Yao Yuan, toward himself, toward this karmic, cursed bond the Mirror Pact had forcibly wound around the two of them. And that key which had unlocked all of it — that blood-colored truth, eight years late — would coil around the rest of his life like the heaviest of shackles, weighed down with enormous guilt and helplessness.

Eight years of silence, like a deep well, dark and lightless, that she'd thought was long since sealed shut. And yet on this ordinary afternoon — autumn sun bright, the tea fragrance about to fade, in the instant she turned to open the car door — that sentence had slipped from her lips, light as air, almost without any emotion at all. So quickly that even she had been startled for a moment.

Having said it, she didn't look at Shuai Hongqiang's face. She got straight into the car. The engine murmured low, the body of the car slid smoothly out, and that frozen figure was quickly thrown into the rearview mirror, smaller and smaller, until at last it vanished around the corner.

Not until she merged into the traffic on the main road did that late, fine tremor finally crawl slowly up her spine. The fingers gripping the steering wheel had gone a little cold. Why say it? Why today?

The cityscape outside the window slid backward at an even pace, like a reel of film that had nothing to do with her. But across her mind, in disordered flashes, came many images.

Yes — perhaps it was because, just now in the Hall of Antiquities, hearing Shuai Hongqiang talk about how "smoothly" things were going, how he "held a sense of reverence," that look on his face — that mix of relief and caution born of being granted fate's extra favor — had stung her. That "smoothness" was like a polished mirror, reflecting the nights of these past four months when, even with the old jade pressed to her chest, she hadn't dared sleep deeply; reflecting the vertigo and palpitations that could drag her into some unknown time and space at any moment. Why should her torment be the background music to his fair winds and gentle rains? That unwillingness pent up in her chest was like a fine, poisonous vine that, without warning, suddenly put out a thorn.

Or perhaps it was that earlier scene, the chance encounter at the mall. Shuai Hongqiang feeding his young son, his gentle wife at his side, the warm picture of an ordinary family of three — like an extremely fine needle that, catching her off guard, pierced an old wound that had long since scabbed but never truly healed. The child's round, lovable, smiling face had stirred awake the darkest corner of her memory — where there was no smiling face, only cold instruments, ghastly white light, and, after a part of her body had been hollowed out, an endless dull pain and emptiness. What she had lost was not merely an embryo, but, in that moment, part of her naive trust in the very thing of "forging a bond of life with another person."

And then there was Yu Hao. A few months back, he had so matter-of-factly proposed that he wanted an "heir" carrying both their bloodlines — yet could offer no promise of a marriage certificate. That was not only a denial of her present worth; it was like a key, flinging open the floodgate of her memory with a clatter. So after all the turns and turns, in some men's eyes, she — Yao Yuan, this person herself — was still something that could be stripped apart, weighed in isolation, "used" selectively. The value of a womb, it seemed, always overrode the value of her soul. Thirty-seven years, and she'd been like a collector's piece picked up, set down, examined, appraised, over and over. Even though she'd worked desperately to set herself in gold and inlay herself with jade, to raise her own valuation, that humiliation of "being chosen" was like a tumor fused to the bone, never truly gone. All her "upward socializing," all her "fighting to grow stronger," that almost ferocious drive to seize control of her own life — what was it if not a wild rebellion against this passive fate? In her livestream room, she dissected human nature and relationships down to the raw flesh, teaching girls to be clear-eyed, independent, even "calculating." Those words, sharp as scalpels, were every one of them survival-fragments she had picked up and polished with her own hands from a road of bloody, fumbling trial and error. How she wished those girls could live forever inside a rose-colored bubble, and never have to understand any of this. But she knew the bubble always bursts. The girls who at first were frightened by her "naked truths," who denounced her for "twisted values," often became her most devoted followers after they'd run head-on into a wall and split their own heads open. Having never tasted another's suffering, don't preach virtue at them; and even having tasted suffering, you may not match their grace.

Life's lessons are always carved deepest into the bone when you receive them firsthand.

The car climbed onto the Yellow River Bridge, and the view opened up all at once. The setting sun was molten gold, plating a tender illusion over the city's sharp-edged buildings.

There was one more thought, fainter still, one she herself didn't want to examine too closely, that perhaps had also driven those words from her mouth just now. Since getting together with Zhao Yiming, a certain biological pendulum, the kind tied to age, seemed to be ticking more clearly inside her. Rational as she was, even she would — on some exhausted late night, before the bathroom mirror — run her fingertip across her flat stomach, across the lines just beginning to show with the years that even expensive skincare couldn't fully smooth, across the warm, smooth edge of the "heart-guarding jade," and feel an extremely complicated wistfulness. In it was a hidden anxiety about aging, a vague imagining of "the possibility of another life," even a fleeting, momentary glimpse toward a "normal" life's path. This murky tangle of thought, the glaring picture of Shuai Hongqiang's complete family she'd faced today, the cold memory of deciding alone eight years ago, the contempt of Yu Hao reducing her to an "excellent gene-carrier" — all of it churned together and fermented into an impulse beyond naming.

Look — you have a virtuous wife and a filial son now, complete and proper. But do you know what your present completeness is built upon?

She hadn't said that sentence out loud, but the revelation of the secret was itself like dropping a depth charge. The force of the blast wasn't in the moment; it was in the silently spreading, ceaselessly eroding shockwave. She wanted him to remember it — wanted him, in every "smooth" moment, to feel faintly that beneath it lay a hole that could never be filled. Maybe that wasn't rational, wasn't "refined," was even a little childishly cruel; but that was her truest reaction in that instant, not coldly calculated, springing purely from the complicated depths of human nature.

What a complicated thing human nature is. She could calmly dissect the emotional labyrinths of millions of people, could plan the most advantageous career path for herself, could refuse to yield an inch in a business negotiation — and yet still couldn't fully untangle all the dark whirlpools in the depths of her own heart. In those whirlpools were unhealed old wounds, the embers of unwillingness, hidden longings, and a malice and grief, sudden and inexplicable, that even she couldn't understand.

She pressed the brake and stopped at a red light. She raised a hand and looked at her own neatly trimmed nails in their exquisite polish. These hands had signed contracts worth millions, had clasped the hands of top-tier collaborators, and on countless late nights had gripped tight the cold edge of a blanket. They looked strong, steady, in control of everything.

But only she knew that, in the instant she spoke that secret, the faint, icy tremor at her fingertips was real.

The light turned green. She stepped on the gas again, and pressed all of it — the surging, complicated weakness and sharpness that belonged to Yao Yuan — back down, steadily, into that fathomless sea at the bottom of her heart. The car accelerated, driving without a backward glance toward the deepening dusk, toward the unknown tomorrow where she had to go on, clear-eyed, moving forward.

========================================

Chapter 82 — Pretty Big One Emotional AI Passes Full-Chain Testing
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640329.html

After telling Shuai Hongqiang that secret, Yao Yuan didn't give herself much time to wallow. That eight-years-late "delivery of the truth" was like a stone dropped into a deep pool: it stirred violent ripples, then sank fast to the bottom, and after a brief churn the surface forced itself back to a cold calm. She didn't know how Shuai Hongqiang was digesting the news — whether in pain and regret, lying awake at night, or whether he'd used a businessman's logic to quickly file it away as an "unchangeable sunk cost." She forced herself not to guess, not to care. The adult world doesn't leave much time to chew over old pain, especially the kind of past that has long since become a museum specimen. She crushed that tangled mix of old wounds, resentment, a secret thrill of revenge, and an even deeper emptiness down into her chest, turned, and focused all her energy like a laser on the one thing that mattered most right now — the final testing and launch of the "Pretty Big One" Emotional AI.
Early September. The dry heat of late summer hadn't fully lifted, but the wind carried its first dry, unmistakable hint of harvest season. Yao Yuan brought several executives and core engineers from the "Pretty Big One" team back to the Western World Technology headquarters in Jinshi's high-tech district. In the afternoon sun, the matte-black smart-glass curtain wall was as severe and self-contained as ever, like a great beast lying in wait.
This time her state of mind was nothing like that first visit. Back then it had been cautious appraisal, a probing test driven by business instinct. Now it was rushing toward a celebration — the birth of something carefully nurtured, about to come into the world.
The elevator went straight to the top floor. They crossed the empty, silent corridor lit only by the cold glow of linear strips, and once again stood before that unmarked metal-grey door. The heavy door slid open without a sound —
That familiar smell hit them: ozone, the heat dissipating off precision instruments, and a kind of metallic, after-rain earthiness. The huge spherical "Mirror Room" came into view again. Walls, ceiling, floor — countless fine hexagonal screens now displayed a deep, flowing starscape. At the center, the floating ring-shaped console gave off a soft glow. Zhao Yiming stood with his back to the door, gazing at the waterfall of blue data streaming through the void ahead. Today he wore a light-grey polo and khakis, a touch more of the lab's austerity and a little less of late-summer ease than last time, though the focused line of his back was as clear as ever.
Hearing footsteps, he didn't turn around. He raised his right hand and made a brief gesture. In an instant the rushing data froze, contracted, and reorganized into an intricate three-dimensional architecture diagram hovering above the console.
"Right on time." Zhao Yiming turned. His eyes swept over Yao Yuan's crisp off-white suit, lingered briefly on the team members behind her, then settled back on her face, pausing for an almost imperceptible instant — there was concern in it, a question, but both were quickly reined in and turned into a partner's focus. "The last full-chain closed test starts in five minutes. If it passes, the 'Pretty Big One' base model and its fusion interface with the Genesis Engine are truly solid."
Yao Yuan nodded and looked toward the intricate visual map, its blue-and-gold light faintly interwoven. It was more seamless than when she'd first seen it months ago, as if it were alive — breathing and growing slowly.
The team got into position fast. Yao Yuan stood a little to the rear, in an observation spot where she could take in the whole picture without interfering with the technical work. Today she'd pinned her long hair up low, with two minimalist pearl studs at her ears, every inch the calm air of a commander. Only the freshly applied haze-blue polish on her fingertips, flecked with fine gold, shimmered with a subtle sheen in the Mirror Room's dim light.
"Begin the test," Zhao Yiming said.
At once a flood of data poured across the ring of screens. A simulated "storm of problems" — drawn from countless extreme emotional cases across every culture in the world — slammed into the just-formed "Pretty Big One" AI core at millions of entries per second. On screen, the fusion map began to heave violently; certain nodes lit warning-yellow, and now and then flickered a glaring red.
The control area was dead silent, nothing but the clatter of keyboards and Zhao Yiming's occasional calm command. Yao Yuan held her breath, feeling the warm comfort of the heart-guarding jade against her chest. This was more than a technical trial. It was the ultimate test of whether she could turn half a lifetime of insight into human nature, her "clinical dissection" of feelings, into a quantifiable, reproducible AI model.
Time seemed to stretch. One by one, the warning points were resolved under the deft scheduling of the algorithms. After violent shaking, the map showed astonishing resilience — like a great tree in a storm, its roots (the Genesis Engine's computing power) gripping the earth, its branches (the "Pretty Big One's" emotional logic) swaying in the gale but not snapping, instead adapting, adjusting, evolving.
"Emotional-intent recognition accuracy, holding steady at 93.1 percent!"
"Self-consistency of complex-relationship reasoning, past 91.5 percent!"
"Humanization deviation rate of strategy recommendations under extreme scenarios, below threshold!"
The metrics kept coming, the voices going from taut to excited. The last one — the "ultimate test question" Yao Yuan had helped design — loaded: an utterly chaotic case mixing betrayal, deceit, financial entanglement, emotional dependence, and self-doubt.
The map heaved again. Countless possible paths radiated, interwove, collided, recombined like beams of light. For a full three minutes, you could have heard a pin drop.
Then the turbulence settled. In the region representing the AI's output, a text report began to generate — clear, calm, rigorously logical, and even carrying a subtle "empathy" and "strategy." The report not only untangled the snarled mess and pinpointed the core demands and weak points; it laid out several highly feasible courses of action, with clear cost-benefit breakdowns, even accounting for the psychological tolerance of whoever would carry them out. Some of the suggestions were so seasoned and precise that even Yao Yuan, the one who'd set the problem, was secretly startled.
"Final scenario test… passed!"
"Full-chain simulation test, passed! All preset metrics met, and some exceeded!"
After a brief, almost suffocating silence —
"Yesss—!"
"It worked!"
Cheers and applause exploded. Months of tension, countless sleepless nights, enormous pressure — all of it turned in that instant into the purest elation. The young engineers high-fived; the women had tears in their eyes. The members of Yao Yuan's own team couldn't hide their excitement either.
Yao Yuan stood there, spine ramrod straight, as if a thousand-pound weight had lifted off her. A scalding rush surged from her heart up to her eyes, and she forced it back down. She drew a slow, deep breath. It had worked. It had truly worked. From a vague idea, to building a team, pouring in a fortune, meeting Zhao Yiming, the sparring, the partnership, the fusion… she had finally reached a milestone on this road.
She turned her head to look at Zhao Yiming.
He was looking at her too. His usual composure was broken by a bright, fulfilled smile, even something boyish in it. Across the cheering crowd he nodded at her, then raised his right hand, made a fist, and tapped it lightly over his heart — the small private gesture between them, born of starry Gobi skies and the tacit understanding beneath their rational pact.
At last, beyond her control, the corners of Yao Yuan's mouth lifted into a smile of pure, dazzling, vivid life.
The celebration was spontaneous and fierce. Champagne someone had stashed got "produced" out of nowhere, the golden liquor merrily fizzing in the glasses. Everyone raised a toast, laughter filling the futuristic space. Yao Yuan held her glass and clinked it with every core member, elegant in her bearing, the absolute center of the room. Zhao Yiming, surrounded by engineers hashing out details, wore the pure satisfaction of a tech obsessive who has hit his target.
After the brief celebration, Zhao Yiming signaled for quiet.
"Everyone, this is only the success of the first-stage model test — the 'zero to one' breakthrough." His voice returned to calm, but it carried an uplift. "Next, we need to dress the 'Pretty Big One' in even prettier 'clothes,' design the most human interaction, and prepare the most solid commercial rollout. So—" he looked at Yao Yuan, their eyes meeting, "Director Yao and I have decided to hold the official launch of 'Pretty Big One Emotional AI 1.0' in Jinshi on October first. We'll invite industry partners, the media, and—"
He paused, and Yao Yuan picked it up right on cue, her voice clear and strong: "And the first hundred new and old members of the 'Sisterhood League' that has supported me all along. They'll be the first batch of core experience officers for the 'Pretty Big One.' On October first, let's witness the launch together!"
Even warmer applause broke out. With the launch date set, the project had entered its true sprint phase.
The crowd gradually dispersed. Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming were the last to linger, both, by unspoken agreement, not leaving the Mirror Room right away.
Inside the spherical space, the starscape slowly began to flow again, gently covering over the traces of the data flood. They stood side by side beside the ring console, looking at the data map before them — calm once more, yet holding infinite possibility.
"How does it feel?" Zhao Yiming tilted his head toward her, his voice soft.
Yao Yuan gazed at those deep "stars," and only after a moment said quietly, "Like finishing a marathon with no finish line in sight and finally spotting the flag of the next aid station. Tired, but… worth it." She turned back to look at him, her eyes clear. "Thank you, Yiming. Without the Genesis Engine, the 'Pretty Big One' would just be a pretty castle in the air."
"Likewise." Zhao Yiming shook his head, his gaze frank. "Without your near-'cruel' precision in dissecting human feelings, and your ability to structure them, no matter how powerful the Genesis Engine, it would still just be a cold supercomputing machine. We created it together." He used the word "created."
Yao Yuan's heart gave a faint tremor. Created. It was almost a kind of… shared "giving birth." The thought made her look away, her ears a little warm — fortunately the Mirror Room's light was dim.
"The next month is going to be busy," she said, changing the subject, her tone back to brisk.
"Mm." Zhao Yiming nodded. "Technical support — absolutely no problem."
A simple exchange, yet the firmest mutual backstop.
As they left Western World Technology, the setting sun was staining the building's black glass curtain wall a warm gold-red. Yao Yuan got into the car and looked back at this structure that lay like a crouching beast.
The heart-guarding jade rested warm against her skin. The shadow of the Mirror Pact still lurked in the distance, but right now her heart was filled with another kind of full, grounded strength.
The "Pretty Big One" was about to break out of its cocoon. This was a new height for her career, the tangible result of refining personal pain into universal value, and a deep interweaving and mutual proof — between her and Zhao Yiming, between reason and feeling, technology and human nature.
The car merged into the evening traffic as the city's neon began to light up one stretch at a time. The headlights cut through the thickening dusk, pointing the way home — and toward the coming dawn, a brand-new one that belonged to the "Pretty Big One."

========================================

Chapter 83 — The Seventh Time-Travel: Echoes of Destiny
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640330.html

The Seventh Time-Travel: Echoes of Destiny
As the car pulled into the underground garage, the last thread of sunset was drawing in along the horizon. The September evening breeze had shed the day's dry heat, carrying the faint chill of early autumn; it brushed the treetops, slipped through the gap in the window, and settled on Yao Yuan's face. She brought the car to a stop, cut the engine, and didn't get out right away. A full day of work, the lingering glow of a successful test, and a deeper feeling she couldn't entirely untangle even for herself all blended together. Out of habit she glanced at the rearview mirror, lifted a hand to smooth a few stray strands of hair, and calmly checked her makeup in the glass. Years of riding the highs and lows of her career, of being tossed about by the warmth and coldness of human dealings, had made composure a habit carved into her bones.
But in the very instant she was about to look away and push open the door, a familiar suffocating sensation suddenly wrapped around her whole body.
The dizziness of her soul being torn loose struck hard; a sharp pain in her skull spread in a heartbeat to every limb. Before she could react at all, her vision went black and she lost all sense of her body, of time, of space.
In the second before the darkness swallowed her consciousness, all that was left in Yao Yuan's heart was a numb understanding—here it was again.
This was the seventh time.
The seventh time she had been dragged into this boundless, pure-white void.
When her consciousness returned, everything around her was still that absolute emptiness: a pure-white space with no edges, no sound, no temperature. Yao Yuan slowly straightened up, long past the panic, the bewilderment, the cautious probing of the first few times. She knew this place too well. Six journeys through time had made this eerie space her most familiar "foreign land."
A cell that held her consciousness captive by force; an uncanny way-station linking the different "selves" across different times and spaces. Its very existence was a mockery of every physical law and logical framework she knew.
Yao Yuan mocked herself with absurd dark humor—she was like a patient forced to come back for regular check-ups, knowing the treatment was painful and the cause unknown, yet not even granted the right to refuse.
Before she could settle her mind, the flat white void ahead suddenly rippled, like still water disturbed by a fingertip, the ripples spreading slowly outward. The scene went from blurred to sharp in moments, building into a setting she knew all too well—
It was the living room of the Jinshi apartment where she lived now.
But the person in that living room was not the her of the present.
A younger Yao Yuan, in wrinkled loungewear, sat curled up with her knees drawn in on the wool rug. An empty wine bottle lay tipped over at her side, and between her fingers she held half a glass of leftover wine, the liquid swaying faintly, reflecting her dejected face and red-rimmed eyes. The whole person looked as if all her backbone and edge had been drained away—broken and helpless.
Thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan watched quietly, an unfamiliar ache welling up in her chest.
She knew she had many faces—radiant, stubborn, shrewd, exhausted, even heartbroken.
All her life she had been good at masking her emotions. When she was sad she never drank alone or sank into private gloom; she would call on friends, wrapping her fragility in lively dinners and noisy crowds. Even if the smile was fake and the merriment was an act, she would never let herself drown alone in grief. When she got drunk she would simply sleep it off, wake up, turn the page, and heal; failing that, she would arrange one gathering after another, embrace fresh faces and new things, and let her small sorrows fade slowly amid the bustle. She never let herself fall this low—this wretched, this defeated, with no one to save her.
But this look of being utterly crushed, as if even her soul had been wrung dry, was something rare in her own memory.
For her—always so proud and so determined to win—to lose her composure like this, she must have suffered a blow that shattered her from the inside.
The worried thought had barely formed when she saw her younger self stagger to her feet and walk on unsteady legs toward the bathroom.
Only then did the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan grow even more certain—this wasn't just her Jinshi apartment; even the decorating details, the placement of the furniture, were exactly the same. A chill crept quietly up her spine. The younger "me"... how could she have come back from Hu City? And moved into this apartment that was like a replica made by fate itself?
The Yao Yuan in the pure-white space found herself drawn along, "watching" toward the bathroom. The younger Yao Yuan turned on the faucet, splashed cold water on her face, then lifted her head and looked into the mirror. The mirror reflected a pale, puffy face with red-rimmed eyes, written all over with helplessness and confusion.
In the very instant those bloodshot eyes met the mirror, the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan felt a force push at her, and her image slowly, clearly, appeared in that vanity mirror belonging to the past.
Two Yao Yuans, separated by the cold surface of the glass, looked at each other once again.
"What happened? How did you let yourself get into such a state?"
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan spoke first, pressing down the alarm and the countless questions in her heart. Her voice had shed its old sharpness, leaving only the worry and calm that the years had settled into. She looked at herself in the mirror—eyes red, putting up a brave front—as if gazing back at an old dream she had lived through but was still powerless to rewrite.
The moment the younger Yao Yuan in the mirror saw the face of her future self, it was as if a dam had finally found its crack: the tears she had held back so long suddenly spilled over. She didn't wail like a little girl; she only bit down hard on her lower lip, her shoulders shaking violently, letting the tears come in a flood. After a long while she managed to steady her breathing, raised her wet, reddened eyes toward the "future" in the mirror, and said in a choked, hoarse voice:
"I... something's gone wrong between me and Yu Hao." She sniffled, trying to keep her words coherent. "Last time... after we met at the place where he proposed, I went to America with him as we'd planned, to visit his parents and family."
She paused, her voice full of bitterness. "Then... his father pulled some strings and looked carefully into my past. He could overlook the fact that I'd had one brief marriage, but... he found out that eight years ago, the massive miscarriage from that car accident damaged me at the root. The doctors said... it would be very hard for me to conceive naturally, and even the success rate of IVF would be extremely low."
Every single word was like a needle dipped in ice, pricking the heart of the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan. So it really was... still this. No matter what shape her earlier "changes" had twisted the road into, the great boulder of fate always seemed to roll back into the same valley—fertility, that measuring stick society, certain classes, and so many men treated as a woman's core value, had once again become the scale on which she was judged.
The younger Yao Yuan's tears fell harder, yet strangely they carried a kind of numb calm. "His father's stance was hard as iron; he flatly opposed us being together. He said the Yu family business needed an heir to carry on the bloodline, and they absolutely could not take a daughter-in-law who... couldn't bear children. Yu Hao... at first he tried to fight for us, but he couldn't hold out against the pressure from his family. In the end, he came to me and proposed a 'solution.'"
She tugged at the corner of her mouth—a self-mocking smile uglier than crying. "He said he was still willing to marry me, that he loved me and wanted to spend his life with me. But he needed an heir of 'his own.' So... he would find another woman, make it a pure money transaction, and have a child. The child would be brought back to us as soon as it was born, and we would raise it. He swore there would be absolutely no emotional entanglement with that woman—it would only be a commercial surrogacy."
She lifted her tear-filled eyes toward the future self in the mirror, her gaze full of the confusion and torment of someone driven to the edge of a cliff. "Tell me... what should I do? Accept this condition, marry him, and keep him... or...?"
"Enough."
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's voice cut her off—calm, even carrying a very faint, absurd trace of amusement. But there was no warmth in that amusement, only endless irony and ice. Here it came, again. In the present timeline, Yu Hao wanted her to bear a child but wouldn't give her marriage. In this past timeline she had "changed," Yu Hao gave her marriage (or at least a promise), but wanted her to raise a child he had with another woman.
A vast, suffocating sense of fate, like a thick black tide, drowned her.
No matter how she struggled, how she "corrected" things, how she tried to pry at the levers of fate, in the end she always seemed to be placed on a scale—"to be examined," "to be chosen," "to be appraised." And the weights on that scale always came back to that ancient system of judging women's worth—marriage, childbearing, the "completeness" of a family. Only the form of the humiliation had changed: from being reduced to a tool in "marriage refused, only a child wanted," to being made a puppet in "marriage granted, but raise another's child."
She had thought that her repeated journeys through time were fixing bugs, applying patches, helping her younger self dodge the risks. But now it looked more like an elaborate, malicious joke designed by fate. You avoided trap A and thought you'd stepped onto a path of flowers, only to find a more hidden cliff B at the path's end. What changed was only the form of the suffering, not its essence. Worse still—because of the hope that had risen from believing the "change" had succeeded, the fall hurt all the more when a more refined betrayal struck, all the more despairing.
"Stop crying." The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan spoke, her voice recovering its usual clarity and composure—the voice of the "Godmother of Feelings." But beneath that composure lay a weariness, like someone who had seen through the cycle of rebirth. "Tears are the most useless thing there is. Besides draining you, they move no one—least of all the people who are already measuring you by their interests and your family genes."
She looked at that face in the mirror, so like her own yet written all over with fragility, as if looking at the self of many years ago who had also once clung to luck, who had also tossed and turned over a relationship. They were essentially the same person; some truths needed no spelling out.
"You're the me of the past; I'm the you of the future. We both know in our hearts that you're just overwhelmed by emotion for the moment. But once you've vented, dry your tears." Her tone was decisive, carrying a hard core forged by weathering storms. "Go and settle accounts with Yu Hao—completely. I don't need to give you any specific steps; I know you won't really swallow your pride and submit. We've gritted our teeth and fought our way here, crawled and clawed, stood up covered in scars—not to be some man's backdrop, not to play along in some performance of being 'gracious and magnanimous,' and certainly not to become a high-end 'nanny' with a marriage certificate, an 'emotional stand-in for a surrogate mother.'"
She leaned slightly forward, her gaze blazing, as if to pierce through the mirror and pour her strength directly into the other's heart. "Listen—so what if you can't have children? If the only way this world has left to measure a woman's worth is her womb, then that's the narrowness and tragedy of the value system, not your flaw! You have financial means, social resources, a clear head. If you love children, there will be plenty of paths in the future. Adoption—giving a child with no blood tie your unreserved love and education—makes you just as great a mother. Child-free—enjoying a pure two-person world with a partner (if there's truly one worth it), pouring your energy into your career, your own growth, and a wider world—is just as fulfilling a life. And even if your thinking changes someday, medicine is advancing too; every possibility exists. The key is—the right to choose must be held in your own hands, built on your will and your happiness, not as a compromise traded for a ticket into marriage!"
The younger Yao Yuan listened, dazed, her tears stopping without her noticing. Her future self's words were like a heavy hammer, smashing the shell that had trapped her in the mire of emotion, exposing the proud core of a soul inside—wounded, humiliated, but never truly dead. She raised a hand and forcefully wiped the tear-tracks from her face, her eyes slowly focusing, recovering a trace of their old brightness and stubbornness.
"You're right." Her voice was still hoarse, but much steadier. "I... I'm just a little reluctant to let go. Yu Hao... apart from this one thing, we're really compatible in every other way—our thinking, our careers, even physically... And I'm already thirty-five." A bitter look crossed her face. "To go and get to know someone all over again, start from scratch, build trust, deal with all the scrutiny and calculation that another person's family might bring... I feel so tired, like I don't have the time or energy for it anymore. And more importantly—" her voice dropped, carrying a deeper wound, "—I can't stand that feeling of being appraised like a product, head to toe, inside and out, and then rejected and abandoned because some 'metric' didn't pass. What gets hurt isn't just my pride..."
"It's your sense of being worth something." The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan picked up her words, hitting the mark, her tone cold. "You start to wonder—besides childbearing, is the person I am simply not worth being loved, not worth being firmly chosen? Are all my efforts, achievements, the charm of my character, utterly worthless in the face of so-called 'carrying on the family line'?"
The younger Yao Yuan nodded hard, her eyes reddening again—but this time with the grievance and anger of finding someone who understood.
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan looked at her and suddenly, half-exasperated, spoke in the tone of a Godmother of Feelings who almost wished she could knock some sense into her: "Look at the state of you! So what if you're thirty-five? Your life isn't even half over! By now you're someone with a bit of success—a career, assets, brains, and a face even I can't help but praise! A three-legged toad may be hard to find, but two-legged men are everywhere—does it have to be Yu Hao and no one else? If you can't find a soulmate who's your equal in career, then find someone you genuinely like, who can look after you fully in daily life, who's emotionally steady and can nourish you—what's wrong with that? Broaden your horizons!"
She swept a disdainful glance over the dejected, lovelorn figure of her younger self in the mirror, her tone cutting: "In the state you're in now, never mind Yu Hao's father looking down on you—even I look down on you! We fought tooth and nail to get to today, and was it so we could pick one of two options a man hands us? It was so we'd have the backbone to tell every disrespectful, unjust term to get lost! It was so we could turn life's multiple-choice questions into essay questions that we set ourselves!"
The younger Yao Yuan flushed red under her words, her self-pity scattered to pieces, while that familiar, unyielding fire she knew so well slowly rose up from within. A little embarrassed, she lowered her head, then raised it again and asked quietly, "Then... what about you now? Are you okay?"
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan fell silent for a moment. Was she okay? There was the unresolved Mirror Pact hanging over her head, the old scar with Shuai Hongqiang that had just been laid bare, the success of her career and the greater responsibilities that came with it, and that brand-new relationship with Zhao Yiming—rational and tender threads woven together, still being felt out. Complicated, exhausting, but a future full of strength and possibility too.
"I'm with Zhao Yiming now." In the end she chose a relatively clear answer, her tone calm. "The 'Pretty Big One' Emotional AI we developed together—the model just passed its final test. It's about to officially launch."
The younger Yao Yuan visibly froze, an astonished look crossing her face; clearly this was completely beyond what she'd expected. "Zhao Yiming? Zhao Yiming of Western World? The one I... the one I've been investing in all along? She seemed to need time to digest it. "A year ago, his core data center had a major accident—the core servers crashed without warning, the whole data-center system collapsed across the board, every link lost connection in an instant. The project nearly fell apart for good, and my investment almost went up in smoke."
"At the time no one had any faith in him; the team's morale fell apart, and the whole industry was writing him off. But he really does have talent, nerve, and backbone—he clawed his way back from a dead end, rebuilt the entire system, and the new architecture was more precise, more solid, more refined than before."
Speaking of those past career upheavals, the thirty-five-year-old Yao Yuan's eyes lit up, her tone touched with anticipation: "I always believed in his ability, and I've always backed him with everything I have. Now we're joining forces to develop an all-purpose AI—not limited to emotional interaction; in the future it'll cover more scenarios, more functions. We're preparing to..."
Before her words had even landed, while the syllables still hung in the air, the light and shadow in the mirror suddenly trembled.
"Wait—" The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan only had time to send half a syllable through her mind.
The scene before her—the younger Yao Yuan's astonished face, the familiar surface of the apartment mirror, even those unfinished words—was instantly twisted, stretched, and then brutally dragged into a dark vortex by an irresistible force.
The mirror surface returned to calm, reflecting only the thirty-five-year-old Yao Yuan's face—tear-tracks still on it, but the light already gathering back within, marked with confusion and deep thought. She gazed at the spot where her future self had vanished, and for a long time did not look away.
...
Cold nothingness; a swift plunge downward.
And then, a heavy suffocation and return, as if struggling up to the surface from the deep sea.
Yao Yuan's eyes snapped open!
Her vision was blurred, her heart pounding wildly in her chest, her eardrums buzzing. She was still in the car, in the driver's seat. The seatbelt pressed against her chest, bringing a touch of real, physical weight. Outside the window, the ghostly white lights of the underground garage shone in, cold.
She was back. Once again.
This journey through time seemed shorter, but the mental shock it brought was more violent than any before. Not only because she'd seen another "self" caught in a similar predicament, but because of the deep powerlessness and absurdity of it—that no matter how she changed things, she seemed unable to escape some core script of fate.
Destiny.
This was a word she hadn't believed in before. She believed that things were made by human effort, that cognition changed choices, and choices changed fate. All her efforts, all her "clarity," were built on the belief that man could conquer heaven (or at least conquer himself).
But these seven journeys in a row, this one most of all, were like resounding slaps across the face of her belief.
The self in another timeline had changed her relationship with Jiang Hai and avoided being humiliated over class and family—yet her mother fell gravely ill, she still went back to Jinshi, and she gave up dance.
The self in another timeline had changed the ending with Shuai Hongqiang, had married him—yet lost the child, and the marriage still fell apart.
The self in another timeline she hadn't interfered with, and Zhuge Jinye—the same blazing fire, the same ashes.
The self in another timeline had changed the fate of being traded by Ding Shitong, had gained profit and the upper hand, and seemed to be walking a better road.
She had warned that self to be careful of anyone surnamed "Yu"—yet the self in another timeline still ran into a "better version" of Yu Hao, and thought she'd found happiness.
And then, just when she thought "that version" might really be happy, she traveled back—only to see that beneath the bubble of "happiness" hid another, more exquisite knife, this one in the name of "childbearing."
While the self in reality, with Yu Hao, was still stuck in the same dead knot of "child versus marriage."
It was as if an invisible pair of hands kept plucking at the strings of fate—no matter which discordant note you dodged, the next one waiting for you was always another stretch of noise, seemingly different but just as grating at its core.
Could it be that all the struggling, all the suffering, all the learning and growth, in the face of some higher-dimensional "setting" or "probability," were nothing but futile, a plot already written out in the draft? Was the clarity she thought she had, the gamesmanship, the calculation, also just the illusion of one actor in this grand drama—an actor a little too immersed in the role, believing she could rewrite the script?
The "heart-guarding" old jade at her chest gave off a warmth noticeably higher than usual, as if striving to soothe her violently fluctuating emotions and over-spent spirit. But in this moment, that warmth made her feel a flicker of irony. The old jade could protect her from being dragged into time-travel too often, could ease the aftereffects—but it couldn't tell her the truth of the journeys, couldn't break this seemingly preordained cycle.
She sat in the car for a long time, until her pounding heart gradually settled and the cold in her fingertips slowly warmed.
No.
A faint but unusually clear voice rose from the deepest, hardest place in her heart.
Even if fate truly has a script, even if the forms of suffering change endlessly, how "I" feel, how I define it, how I respond, how I rebuild from the ruins—that part, no script can take away.
After being rejected by Jiang Hai's family, she had learned to recognize reality's thresholds earlier, and turned her energy toward bettering herself.
After losing her child with Shuai Hongqiang, she had tasted the impermanence of life and the limits of bearing things alone, had gone far away, forced into a rebirth.
After burning out completely with Zhuge Jinye, she had engraved the most extreme emotional experience into herself, and seen clearly the gap between pure passion and the reality of life.
After breaking free of the trade with Ding Shitong, she had mastered a higher level of strategic thinking, turning the exploitation of human nature into a ladder for her own growth.
Faced with Yu Hao's cold-hearted proposal, she had cut off her illusions about "men" entirely, and firmed up the belief that only her own strength was the one way out.
And this time, seeing the "self" in another timeline facing a similar but more hidden exploitation, what she'd given was an unhesitating "settling of accounts" and a "reaffirmation of self-worth."
See—the forms of suffering kept changing, but what "Yao Yuan" as a person forged out of that suffering kept changing too. From wounded and naive, to learning to defend, to taking the offensive in the game, to creating value, to trying to use the lessons and tools she'd summed up to help more people avoid similar pain.
Wasn't this itself a form of resistance? A struggle, within a given framework, to expand the boundaries of free will as far as possible—even to try to change the rules of the game?
Destiny might exist, but it was not a straight slide going downward; it was a sea of surging waves and hidden reefs. Clarity was the seamanship of learning to read the currents, dodge the reefs, even use the wind and waves. You couldn't make the sea calm, but you could choose your heading, reinforce your ship, and through battle after battle with the storms, become a finer, tougher helmsman.
The "Pretty Big One" AI was the sturdier ship she was trying to build—one she could share with more people. It was charted from her countless wrecks on the reefs of this sea of human nature.
Thinking of the "Pretty Big One," of the test that had just succeeded, of that connection with Zhao Yiming where reason and exploration coexisted, of the wealth, career, influence, and right to choose she truly possessed right now... the cold and emptiness that the sense of destiny had brought were gradually dispelled by a more solid strength that belonged to the present.
The Mirror Pact was unresolved; the time-travel went on. But at least in this moment, she was not the girl of eight years ago who could only lie alone on the operating table, not the woman who had to swallow her pride for love. She was Yao Yuan, thirty-seven, with her own business empire, with unfinished career ambitions, with complicated but real emotional ties—and with countless riddles waiting to be solved and people waiting for her to light their way.
She pushed open the car door and stepped out. Her high heels struck the concrete floor, ringing out crisp and firm, carrying across the empty space of the underground garage.
On her way to the elevator, she looked up at the ghostly white lights overhead. Then she took out her phone and sent Zhao Yiming a message—nothing to do with romance, purely work:
[About the visual presentation of the Pretty Big One AI at the launch—I have a new idea. Would it be convenient to discuss it tomorrow morning?]
After sending it, she put her phone away, the corner of her mouth curving into a very faint arc, tired but all the more resilient for it.
Whatever the starry sky overhead, whatever the torrent underfoot, whether fate handed her flowers or thorns—see it clearly, and then, keep walking.
That was the one destiny that belonged to Yao Yuan, the only one—and the strongest.

========================================

Chapter 84 — A Gentle Awakening: The Emotional Ledger of Marriage and the Quiet Art of Saving Yourself
