https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640331.html

A Gentle Awakening: The Emotional Ledger of Marriage and the Quiet Art of Saving Yourself
After Shuai Hongqiang learned that secret, it felt as if a hole had been bored into his heart, hissing as it leaked air. He couldn't name the feeling exactly. Guilt was too thin a word, regret too heavy a one; mostly it was a sense of being suspended in midair. The balance he had spent these last eight years building seemed to rest not on solid ground but on a layer of cracks he had only just discovered—cracks that had been there all along.
Before the year was out, the old-city renovation project passed inspection without a hitch, and the final payment was settled cleanly. The Citizens' Center project, too, moved forward with unusual smoothness, free of the usual obstruction, the cash flow so healthy that even his veteran project managers clicked their tongues in amazement. It was too smooth—smooth enough to make a man uneasy. He used to believe that "your luck turns when the time is right," that "effort always pays off." But now he couldn't help wondering: was all this "smoothness" something Yao Yuan had "traded" for in another timeline, at a cost he couldn't even imagine? The thought drifted up now and then like a ghost, and it sent a chill down his back. Especially after learning that she had once borne such pain alone, this "good fortune" that had come to him through her felt like gilded thorns—gripped in the hand, they bit deep and hurt.
Today was Thursday, the day Lin Wan livestreamed on her fixed schedule. That afternoon she had called, her voice as gentle as ever, asking him to pick up their son Shuai Wenyao after school. He agreed, wrapped up work early, and drove to the school.
With his son in the car, the little boy chattered away about the funny things that had happened at school. Shuai Hongqiang listened, answering now and then, his gaze grazing the side of that young, happy face—and the hole leaking air in his chest grew more pronounced. If, back then... would this child have had an older brother or sister?
Home again, the smell of dinner already drifting out. In her apron, Lin Wan carried the last dish—a soup—out from the kitchen. Three dishes and a soup: steamed prawns, garlic stir-fried lettuce stems, tomato and egg, and a yam-and-pork-rib soup. All home cooking, but plated with care, the colors clean and fresh.
"You're back? Wash up and eat." Lin Wan looked up at him with a small smile, then ruffled their son's hair. "Wenyao, did you pay attention in class today?"
"I did! Mom, I got a perfect score on my math quiz today!" Shuai Wenyao announced, as if showing off a treasure.
"Wonderful! Go wash your hands—as a reward you get a few extra ribs."
At the table was the familiar warmth. Lin Wan picked out food for their son and asked about the details of his day. Shuai Wenyao waved his hands as he talked; Shuai Hongqiang ate in silence, now and then adding something to his son's bowl. The light was a warm yellow, the steam from the food curling in the air—everything fit the most standard template of the word "home." And yet Shuai Hongqiang felt like a man sitting in the audience watching a play. On the stage, "the happy family" was being performed, while he knew exactly what loose threads and unset paint were hidden backstage.
He glanced at Lin Wan. As she bowed her head to sip her soup, the line of her neck curved gently, her profile serene in the light. She seemed wholly absorbed in this daily routine and a mother's attentiveness. But just now Shuai Hongqiang felt with unusual clarity that beneath this calm lay a tension and a calculation he had deliberately overlooked. Like a precision instrument running in silence—its inner gears meshing at high speed while the outer shell never so much as trembled.
When they had finished, he got up of his own accord to clear the bowls and chopsticks. "Go get ready for your stream. I'll wash up."
Lin Wan looked at him, a little surprised, but said nothing—only nodded. "All right."
In the kitchen, the water rushed. Shuai Hongqiang scrubbed the bowls mechanically, his thoughts drifting far. He remembered a great many small things. When his business had hit its low point, Lin Wan had shouldered the household expenses without hesitation—doing livestreams, learning to sell products on air, never once complaining, even managing to spare the energy to preserve his "face." She always knew how to hand him a cup of tea at exactly the right temperature when he was irritable, and to say a few soothing words. She looked after his parents flawlessly and gave him plenty of standing in front of friends and family. She was like an enormous, soft sponge, silently absorbing every bit of the pressure, the frustration, and the occasional out-of-control bad temper he brought home from outside.
He used to think of this as her being "virtuous," as "love," as his "good luck." Now, against his will, he found himself re-examining it all through Yao Yuan's cold lens of "value exchange," "emotional labor," "cost accounting." Was Lin Wan's giving the natural overflow of "love"—or was it the highly professional "emotional management and household operation" that a woman caught inside this marital system had no choice but to perform, in order to keep the system stable and to protect her own and her children's interests?
The thought made him shudder.
The dishes washed, he dried his hands and walked out. Lin Wan had just come out of their son's room, easing the door shut behind her. "Wenyao's done his prep; he'll read a bit on his own and then sleep. I'm going to get ready for the stream."
"Mm." Shuai Hongqiang watched her walk toward that livestreaming corner she had set up so carefully in the study. The lighting there was soft, the backdrop a bookshelf crowded with greenery—warm and intelligent-looking. She sat down, switched on her equipment, adjusted the microphone, her movements practiced. Even though his business had recovered now and no longer needed her bit of streaming income to keep the household running, she had never once mentioned stopping. Shuai Hongqiang used to think she'd "gotten hooked on it," or that she "couldn't bear to part with her fans." Now he had a faint sense that it might be a crucial module of autonomy—a safety buffer—she had built for herself inside the system that was this "home."
He sat down on the living-room sofa, didn't turn on the TV, just listened to the faint sounds drifting from the study: Lin Wan testing her equipment, and the calm, gentle voice with which she ran a sound check into the microphone.
A moment later, the stream began.
A soft, warm halo of light settled gently, wrapping Lin Wan in tenderness. She wore a simple, clean white cotton T-shirt, her makeup so understated it was nearly invisible, just a thin coat of pale rose balm on her lips lending a faint glow to her whole face—quiet and at ease.
Behind her the umbrella tree was lush and full of life, while beside it an aroma diffuser slowly exhaled a fine white mist, and the light, green scent of plants spread through the air, conjuring a room of calm and stillness. The whole frame was clean and gentle, without a trace of aggression—like a cup of honey water at just the right temperature on an autumn afternoon, mild and soothing, easing the heart.
Lin Wan raised her eyes to the camera, the corners of her lips slowly lifting into a faint smile. In that smile lay the clarity that the years deposit, and a trace of weariness she couldn't quite hide. But when she spoke, her voice was still steady and unhurried, gentle yet full of strength.
"Good evening, and welcome to 'A Gentle Awakening.' I'm Lin Wan."
"Tonight we won't talk about how to keep romance sweet, nor the tricks for keeping love fresh. We'll talk about something starker, more real. We'll talk about those emotional bills in a marriage—invisible, untouchable, yet draining a person day after day. We'll talk about an identity we're forced to play until we go numb, until even we ourselves find it strange: the emotional container."
Her tone was even and measured, unfolding gently, as if she were calmly telling someone else's story. Yet the countless listeners on the other side of the screen all keenly caught the surging undercurrent hidden beneath that calm.
"I have a friend who's been married many years. Her husband is in business—under constant high pressure, with enormous mood swings. Over time, she trained herself in an almost instinctive skill: reading faces and catching every emotion before it falls."
"When her husband comes home with a gloomy face, she can tell in an instant whether it's a setback in his career or a knock in his relationships. Then she offers comfort at just the right pitch, or has a table of his favorite dishes ready, never asking a single extra question about what's troubling him, so as not to inflame the conflict a second time. When his career is going well and he's riding high, she offers sincere praise and admiration at the right moment, the worship in her eyes pitched just so, fulfilling his sense of achievement. And if he's been wronged out in the world and comes home trailing a foul temper, his words sharp and impatient, she listens quietly, never argues back, waits until he's vented completely, then softly hands him a glass of warm water and gently soothes him: You've worked so hard. Don't waste your anger on trivial things."
Lin Wan paused slightly, her gaze distant, taking on a deeper restraint.
"She did it too perfectly—so perfectly that, over the years, it became an instinct carved into her bones. But what crushes the balance is never some monumental conflict; it's always some trivial little thing. One day—perhaps it was only that her reply came half a beat too slow, perhaps only that a plain, ordinary answer didn't sound attentive enough—her husband suddenly flew into a rage and kicked over a dining chair on the spot."
"That chair was a style she had picked out and designed herself. The drawings still exist to this day; it was a shape she was especially fond of."
As she spoke, Lin Wan reached for a sheet of paper beside her, smoothed it gently, and held it up to the camera. On it was a hand-drawn sketch of a dining chair, the lines fine and fluid, the corners, dimensions, and wood details all clearly labeled—every stroke made with care.
"Take a look, everyone—this is the design she drew back then." Her fingertips brushed lightly across the paper, her tone light and yet heavy. "That day, what got kicked over was never just a chair. It was also the household balance she had so carefully maintained year after year—the dignity and the security she had always endured, smoothed over, and labored to hold steady."
"Do you know what's the most chilling part when you really think it through?"
Lin Wan set the drawing down and lifted her eyes to the camera. The bright clarity in them broke through the gentle surface, letting through a sharp lucidity.
"It isn't her husband losing control and kicking over furniture. It's that, in the instant the chair hit the floor, her first reaction was an almost reflexive self-rescue and damage control. She immediately launched into an utterly practiced soothing routine: apologizing out of habit even though she had no idea what she'd done wrong; volunteering self-criticism, blaming herself for not being attentive enough in her reply; hurriedly promising in a low voice to be more thoughtful next time. Then, just as smoothly, she cleaned up the wreckage, righted the fallen chair, carefully inspected it for dents, while in the back of her mind she rapidly tallied the repair cost and the lead time for a custom remake—even worrying about whether it would affect the look of her livestream backdrop later."
"In that moment, she was like a well-trained customer-service rep calmly handling a VIP client in emotional meltdown; like a seasoned, professional nurse patiently soothing a patient grown irritable from pain. The whole routine was fluid, precise, carrying an almost terrifying efficiency—no panic, only a competence drilled into the marrow."
Lin Wan's voice slowly dropped lower, shedding every gentle filter, leaving only lucid dissection.
"And it was in that moment that she finally saw her situation clearly. Through the long attrition of marriage, she had long since lived herself into a professional manager of an emotional container. Her invisible KPI was to keep that container called 'home' at a constant temperature—stable, free of violent conflict. Her daily work was to precisely identify, sort, and digest every negative emotion her partner poured out; to purify all that emotional garbage with empathy, compromise, comfort, and concession; to keep outputting a steady, harmonious household atmosphere; to guard, at all costs, the glossy shell of a 'perfect family.'"
The scrolling comments raced up the screen, the whole feed full of teary eyes and resonance, countless people writing "this is so real," "I just broke down for a second." Others asked urgently: if it's this exhausting, why not leave?
Lin Wan caught the comments and shook her head gently, her tone calm yet cutting straight to the heart, without a hint of extremism—only the clear-eyed realism of an adult.
"Many people ask: if it's this stifling, why not pull away and leave?"
"Because within marriage's established system, the cost of leaving is calculated all too clearly by the adult mind. It's not that she never tried to resist. But every faint act of defiance, every expression of her own needs, brought in return a more violent emotional storm, or a long, cold spell of stonewalling as punishment. What followed was a tightening of financial control, cold detachment toward the children, and veiled slander in front of friends and family."
"Over time, the scales in her heart had long since weighed the costs and benefits. To take the initiative to soothe, to compromise and yield, costs only a stretch of emotional labor and a slight erosion of dignity. But to break with him and resist means gambling long-term financial security, the environment in which her child grows up, her reputation in the eyes of the world—and the small piece of hard-won professional sky she relies on to catch her breath."
"This is a ledger any rational adult can read. It isn't that she doesn't want to break free; it's that she doesn't dare, and hasn't the strength to bear the cost of breaking free."
She drew a soft breath, her tone growing ever calmer and more detached, like dissecting an objective case study of a life.
"This marital system of 'emotional container and manager' has its own perfectly self-consistent operating logic. Most women caught inside it trade a steady, continuous supply of emotion, unpaid household labor, and the value of bearing children and offering companionship, in exchange for the financial security marriage brings, a social identity, a stable environment for their children to grow up in, and a life that looks, on the surface, secure."
"When the marital system wavers, when the partner loses control and recklessly pours out his negative energy, the woman serving as 'manager' makes her first priority, always, to repair the rift, steady the situation, and keep the system running—never to question the system's own injustice. Because once the system collapses, the loss and the cost the woman must bear are often far greater than what falls on the other side."
Lin Wan raised a hand and pointed at herself, open and unflinching, hiding nothing.
"And I am that friend. I am also one of countless seasoned managers of marital emotion. I do livestreams, I share the art of running a marriage, I teach everyone efficient communication, putting yourself in the other's shoes, resolving conflict, two-way adjustment. On the surface I'm imparting a methodology for happiness. But dig deeper, and how is it any different from running session after session of 'manager job training'?"
"I teach countless women how to take on emotion and maintain relationships more skillfully, more efficiently, with less internal wear—nothing more than helping everyone win a sliver more comfortable living space, a tiny bit of feeble bargaining power, within marriage's established system."
A faint, bitter smile curved the corner of her lips, wrapped in an adult's helplessness and, more than that, a clear-eyed self-awareness.
"Girls often message me privately: Sister Wanwan, how do you tell whether a man truly cherishes and respects you?"
Lin Wan looked straight into the camera, her gaze earnest and sincere, every word heartfelt.
"My truest answer is this: watch how he handles his own negative emotions. Watch whether he treats you as a bottomless hollow tree to receive his garbage emotions—dumping his temper at will, venting his loss of control however he pleases—or whether he knows emotional self-restraint, understands that joy and anger, sorrow and delight are each a personal cultivation, and digests and regulates them himself. Even when he does occasionally need comfort, he asks for it with measure and gratitude—not as a matter of course, not by raining his displeasure down on you from on high."
"But this is something I rarely said outright on my streams before."
Her tone paused slightly as she frankly laid bare the weighing and the helplessness in her heart.
"Because my livestream, all the backbone of my career, still depends on this vast worldly system. I need traffic, I need sponsors, I need to maintain a public image that's mild, lucid, rational, neutral. I can't be extreme, can't be sharp, can't puncture too many of the comforting illusions that so many people deliberately keep alive. My frankness might prick the nerves of those who benefit from the status quo, make the people around me uncomfortable, send ripples through my own secure life."
"So all I can do is gently counsel everyone: marriage is precious for its mutual adjustment, its tolerance, its understanding of each other."
"But I alone know that the word 'understanding,' in too many marriages, has long since quietly changed its nature. It's no longer a tender, two-way rushing toward each other; it's a one-sided emotional burden, an unlimited acquiescence to a partner's improper behavior—the silent emotional tax you have no choice but to pay on schedule to keep the surface peace."
As her voice fell, Lin Wan reached up and switched off the room's main light. A single fine, gentle spotlight came on alone, falling precisely on that hand-drawn sketch. The white paper grew nearly translucent in the play of light and shadow, yet the lines drawn on it stayed clear and distinct, firm and unwavering.
"That chair that was kicked over—I fixed it afterward."
"Not because it was expensive, but because I need it to stand there, well and whole. With the chair there, the physical structure of 'home' is there, and the sense of psychological belonging won't collapse. With the chair there, my livestream backdrop is still complete and warm, and my life still looks secure and whole. With the chair there, I can keep playing the part well—the gentle, lucid host in the world's eyes, the thoughtful, well-rounded wife—and within this established life-system, win the most security and backbone I can, for myself and for my child."
"But tonight, I want to say a few words to all the women who, like me, quietly play the part of 'emotional manager'—words that aren't all that politically correct, but real enough."
The spotlight pooled across her brow and eyes. Lin Wan's voice was calm and gentle, yet it held the candor and clarity of someone who has burned her boats.
"First: stay clear-eyed and aware. Keep an emotional ledger of your own. You don't have to rush into resistance and rupture, but you must see clearly the true cost of every concession, every act of soothing, every self-compromise. What it drains may be your time and energy, your self-respect and backbone, your emotional health—and even your warmth and hope for life itself. Recording these costs isn't to hoard resentment; it's to stay clear-eyed at every moment. Only by seeing the cost clearly can you weigh the gains against the losses and understand whether what you give is worth it."
"Second: quietly accumulate bargaining capital of your own. Whether it's independent financial ability, reliable social resources, an irreplaceable professional skill, or the connections and influence amassed day by day. This backbone may not directly improve the marriage itself, but it is the solid bedrock on which you stand and weather the storms. When your capital is heavy enough, when your value is prominent enough, the other person will hesitate—will weigh things—before recklessly venting his emotions and draining you at will. This is the most realistic rule of survival for an adult."
"Third: never let yourself be hijacked by sweet talk; don't believe the excuse that 'he's just under too much pressure, he just cares about you too much.' True care and respect come down, at their core, to restraint and cherishing. You are the partner who walks a lifetime beside him—not the dumping ground for his emotions, and certainly not the on-call, unconditional psychiatrist who catches everything. You always have the right to refuse emotional attrition, the standing to step back and stay calm when the other loses control, to wait for him to settle himself. You don't have to charge in every single time to be the one who puts out the fire."
Lin Wan's tone was even, free of grief or sorrow—only a lucid certainty.
"Like many women mired in the same kind of plight, I still choose to stay in the marriage. The reasons are countless—perhaps concern for the child, financial entanglement, the eyes of the world, the inertia of a life settled over many years. But I want to tell everyone: staying is never the same as resigning yourself to fate, and certainly never the same as putting yourself to sleep."
"We can be managers within the marital system for the time being—but we will be managers who keep a clear-eyed ledger, who keep building strength, who never abandon themselves. Always keep, for yourself, the ability and the right to pull out and cut your losses at any moment."
"Marriage may be the survival system we've chosen for now. But how to hold ourselves, how to grow, how to balance role and self, how to nourish our own hearts in a life that weighs us down—this operating program at the core of a life can only be written by our own hands. Never hand it over to anyone else, and least of all to fate."
At the last, she raised her hand and gently stroked that hand-drawn sketch, her eyes clear and resolute.
"This chair—I'll let it stay there always. It's long since ceased to be just a piece of furniture, just a livestream backdrop. It's a gauge, reminding me at every moment of the bottom line of what I give and the cost of what I spend. It's a medal, witness to the resilience of my endurance and the clarity of my resolve. And more than that, it's a silent declaration: I live in the world's smoke and fire, yet I have stayed clear-eyed and self-possessed all along, lived in earnest, and never once compromised."
The soft chime signaling the stream's end rose gently, breaking the room's stillness.
Lin Wan took off her headphones and reached up to rub gently at the space between her brows. The gentle, lucid, calm, resolute outer glow that belonged to the host "Wanwan" slowly faded, and a real, faint weariness crept over her features.
At the study doorway, a figure had been standing silently for who knew how long. Holding a glass of warm water, Shuai Hongqiang stood quietly in the shadow, having heard, from start to finish, this stream that laid her heart bare.
Every sentence tonight, every layer of self-disclosure, was like a fine, gentle, yet forceful needle, sinking layer by layer into his heart. His mind—already in turmoil over Yao Yuan's secret and the riddle of the Mirror Pact—now surged with a still greater storm.
For the first time, he understood with such clarity and depth what silent, deep undercurrents lay beneath his seemingly steady, harmonious marriage. He finally grasped that Lin Wan's years of gentleness and thoughtfulness, of tolerance and understanding, had never been a docile nature, but her clear-eyed, self-possessed wisdom for survival. And he himself was both the maker of this marital undercurrent and the largest disturbance to her system—the one she had spent years draining her energy to manage.
He stepped forward and held out the glass of warm water, his voice carrying a dryness and heaviness he couldn't disguise. "Have some water."
"Thank you."
Lin Wan reached up to take the glass and lifted her eyes to give him a faint smile. It was still the familiar gentleness and warmth—but beneath that tenderness Shuai Hongqiang now clearly glimpsed the core she kept hidden: clear-eyed, self-possessed, keeping a ledger at every step, a resilience that always left itself a way out.
In that instant it dawned on him all at once: the woman who had shared his pillow for years was never a docile, clinging vine. Her world was deeper, heavier, vaster than he had imagined. Her gentleness was a choice, her endurance a weighing, her steadfastness a clarity.
Yao Yuan's secret, the riddle of the Mirror Pact, the suspended feeling of fate, and this brand-new understanding of marriage, of human nature, of living together—all of it wove together, layer upon layer, and poured into Shuai Hongqiang's heart, sinking him into a long meditation.
The night ran deep, every sound slowly going still. Life has never had a standard answer. Each person's life is an exam paper that can only be worked through, step by step, by oneself—saving oneself bit by bit—and amid the ups and downs of the world, slowly settling out one's own way of solving the problem.

========================================

Chapter 85 — Pretty Big One Emotional AI Sets Sail: A Battlefield of Fate With Everyone in Their Seats
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640332.html

Pretty Big One Emotional AI Sets Sail: A Battlefield of Fate With Everyone in Their Seats
October in Jinshi, and the sky was that lofty, crystalline blue that only autumn can scrub clean. The wind carried a dry scent of grass and leaves and a faint sweetness of ripened fruit, brushing every corner of the city — and brushing, too, the enormous standee planted before the Western World Technology headquarters, printed with "Pretty Big One Emotional AI 1.0 — Launch Gala."
Months of preparation, and at last the moment had arrived.
The launch was held in the new-media hall of the city arts center. Inside, the design was full of futurism — silver-gray and deep blue as the dominant tones, the lines crisp and clean. On the vast circular main screen, a pale-gold "Tracing the Light" tree turned and grew in slow rotation, streams of data flowing quietly among its branches like stars. The guests had gathered in force. Besides the familiar faces from tech, investment, and media circles, what drew the eye most were the women — exquisitely dressed, each of a different bearing — the first hundred core members of Yao Yuan's "Sisterhood League." They were the first batch of "experience officers" for "Pretty Big One," and the most solid support network Yao Yuan had drawn together over the past years through clear-eyed wisdom. In one corner of the venue, in a dedicated trial zone, a few were already being guided by staff into early conversations with "Pretty Big One."
Yao Yuan wore a champagne-gold off-the-shoulder satin fishtail gown that traced her graceful, upright figure. Her long hair was swept into an elegant chignon, a few loose strands framing her cheeks. Her makeup was a carefully drawn "goddess look," emphasizing bone structure and poise, the lip color a warm-gold mauve that echoed her dress. A long diamond chain swayed and caught the light at her ear with every movement. She stood at the center of the crowd, exchanging pleasantries and greetings with guests from every quarter, her smile gracious, her eyes bright — unquestionably the focal point, and the queen. Zhao Yiming, in a perfectly tailored dark-gray suit, stood a little apart, talking with several important technical partners, his expression focused. Now and then he glanced toward Yao Yuan, and when their eyes met, there was a steadiness between them that needed no words.
Everything was going to plan — grand, smooth, full of anticipation.
Until the leadership from the Provincial Department of Science and Technology arrived.
When that newly appointed deputy director — famed for a deep background and for being young and accomplished — stepped into the venue with several aides, the once-flowing crowd produced a subtle stall and a ripple of murmurs. Zhao Yiming immediately went to meet him, a genuine smile on his face, reaching out to shake his hand — clearly the two were not strangers.
Yao Yuan adjusted her expression too, wearing the proper, deferential smile of respect for a senior official, and turned her gaze that way.
And then her breath, for a thousandth of a second, hitched almost imperceptibly.
Jiang Hai.
After all these years, time had left little wear on him; if anything, it had added a more composed weight. He wore a well-fitted navy civil-service jacket, his bearing still straight, his face leaner and more refined than in her memory, and deeper. That ease of one inside the system — yet somehow beyond its usual frame — gave him a halo of his own even standing in a crowd. He was shaking hands and talking with Zhao Yiming, the line of his profile the familiar arc from Yao Yuan's memory, and yet strange, as if a lifetime lay between.
Zhao Yiming led Jiang Hai toward the head-table area, gesturing toward Yao Yuan at the same time. Jiang Hai turned his head, and his gaze fell, naturally, on her.
Their eyes met.
For that instant the air seemed drained of all sound.
The moment Jiang Hai's gaze touched Yao Yuan's face, it visibly trembled. Not deliberate — an unguarded astonishment yanked up violently from the depths of memory. He clearly hadn't expected that the "extraordinarily accomplished female partner," the "top insight-leader in emotional AI" Zhao Yiming had spoken of, would be Yao Yuan. There was a flicker of blankness in his eyes, quickly replaced by a complex, swiftly suppressed scrutiny. In that scrutiny was an appraisal of her startling transformation and achievement, a faint stir at the sudden surfacing of the past, and perhaps a thin trace — one even he might not admit — of lament at how the people endure while everything has changed.
After its brief loss of balance, Yao Yuan's heartbeat steadied with even greater force. She was the first to compose herself. She stepped forward, extended her right hand, her smile flawless, her voice clear and steady: "Director Jiang, welcome — we're honored to have your guidance. I've heard so much about you." She used the most standard, safest social formula, locking everything past and personal firmly beneath a professional shell.
Jiang Hai recovered just as quickly. He took her hand — the pressure measured, the duration exact. His palm was dry and warm, his eyes now a deep, steady warmth, carrying a senior's approval for an outstanding entrepreneur. "Boss Yao, a pleasure. Yiming often mentions you. Seeing you today, you are indeed remarkable. Congratulations on the successful launch of 'Pretty Big One' — this is a direction in our province's AI-application field very much worth watching." His words were airtight, but Yao Yuan could feel that, as he held her hand, there was an almost imperceptible, subtle weight laid on the two words "Boss Yao" and "remarkable."
"You flatter me. None of this would be possible without policy support and the backing of all our leaders." Yao Yuan answered with ease, withdrawing her hand, naturally returning the lead to Zhao Yiming. In a handful of seconds, an unexpected reunion that spanned young love, the wounds of class difference, and long years was compressed into a standard, dignified piece of official small talk with no fault to find. Only in each of their hearts did they know what storms had once raged beneath that calm surface, and into what complicated grit they had settled.
This was only the first surprise.
The launch ran smoothly. Yao Yuan's address was clear in logic and broad in vision, positioning "Pretty Big One" as "a professional, deployable emotional solution + full-cycle growth companionship," stressing both its nature as "a problem-solving tool" and its humane care as "emotional companionship," winning applause several times over. Zhao Yiming's technical explanation made the profound simple, and paired with the stunning demonstration of the "Tracing the Light" tree, it married hard-core technology and visions of the future perfectly. The provincial department leader's brief official remarks affirmed the project highly from the policy level and lent it authoritative endorsement, empowering its development.
The whole program came to a satisfying close, and the networking dinner opened in turn.
No one had foreseen that the careless arrangement of seats at the head table would push the "surprise" of this launch toward a climax of dramatic tension that even Yao Yuan had not anticipated.
Whether or not it was coincidence, the head table held the provincial department leaders, the key investors, the core media representatives — Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming naturally among them — and Jiang Hai, seated there as an important guest. Then Shuai Hongqiang and Yu Hao, of all people, appeared at the table one after another. Both claimed they had heard the news and come especially to offer congratulations; neither had received a formal invitation, but given their standing, the staff naturally dared not turn them away, and seats were arranged.
So, when Yao Yuan settled in beside Zhao Yiming and lifted her eyes to the far side and both flanks of the round table, an absurd, almost black-comedy sensation seized her.
Going clockwise: Zhao Yiming (current boyfriend and business partner), Jiang Hai (first love, now deputy director of the Provincial Department of Science and Technology), one empty seat (investor representative), Shuai Hongqiang (ex, tied to the Mirror Pact, local entrepreneur), Yu Hao (former "arrangement partner," would-be father of her child), and back round to herself.
The ex, the ex before that, the ex before that, and the current. All gathered under one roof to celebrate the new chapter of her career. This was no celebratory banquet — this was an offline, live-action retrospective of the "Annals of Yao Yuan's Love Life," staged meticulously by fate, with a high-end social battlefield thrown in as a bonus.
Shuai Hongqiang was the first to come over of his own accord. He was decently dressed today, his color reasonable, but his eyes, when they met Yao Yuan's, still carried a complexity he couldn't fully hide. He first raised his glass to her, a forced openness in his smile: "Yao Yuan, congratulations! An achievement this big — I had to come beg a glass of wine off you." Then his gaze turned to Zhao Yiming beside her, and to Jiang Hai in the seat of honor, his manner respectful and courteous.
Yao Yuan grasped it at once: this was the cue for her to "introduce." Unruffled, smiling, she turned first to Zhao Yiming and Jiang Hai: "Yiming, Director Jiang, let me introduce — this is Boss Shuai, Shuai Hongqiang, an outstanding local entrepreneur from Jinshi, and an old friend of mine of many years." She defined her relationship with Shuai Hongqiang as "old friend" — clear, safe, and drawing a sufficient distance.
"Boss Shuai, this is Boss Zhao — Zhao Yiming, founder of Western World, and now my business partner." She introduced Zhao Yiming first, her tone natural. Though Zhao Yiming was a little surprised by this suddenly appearing "old friend," he at once showed a host's courtesy, rising to shake Shuai Hongqiang's hand: "Boss Shuai, welcome. Thank you for your support."
"Boss Zhao, I've long admired your name — glad to finally meet you." Shuai Hongqiang's grip was firm, his eyes sweeping quickly over Zhao Yiming's face. In that look was a sizing-up of this "current one," a comparison, and perhaps a trace of indescribable dejection — all masked beneath a polished social face.
Then Yao Yuan looked toward Jiang Hai and went on: "This is Deputy Director Jiang Hai, of the Provincial Department of Science and Technology."
"Director Jiang, hello! It's an honor, truly!" Shuai Hongqiang stepped up at once, his posture lowered a touch further, clasping Jiang Hai's hand in both of his. Jiang Hai kept his usual warmth and sense of distance, nodding with a smile: "You're too kind, Boss Shuai. The growth of enterprise depends on the efforts of people like you."
At that very moment, Zhao Yiming's gaze shifted, almost imperceptibly. He had caught the complex look in Shuai Hongqiang's eyes when they fell on Yao Yuan — a look that was anything but that of an ordinary "old friend" — and he had noticed the overly "standard" detachment in Yao Yuan's introduction. The keenness honed in the marketplace let him sense in an instant some invisible thread of the past winding among these three. But he said nothing. He merely lifted his glass and, under cover of drinking, quietly tucked that curiosity away, his face still wearing the proper smile of a host.
Amid the pleasantries, Yu Hao arrived too. He still carried the air of a Shanghai elite. Seeing Yao Yuan, his eyes brightened — and then, noticing Zhao Yiming beside her and Shuai Hongqiang exchanging greetings with Jiang Hai, that brightness quickly dimmed, veiled in a proper but unmistakable shadow of loss. He walked over, aiming squarely for Yao Yuan, his smile a little strained, his tone carrying a hint of intimate reproach and the sourness of one shut out of an important moment: "Yao Yuan, congratulations. A moment this important — I nearly missed it."
Yao Yuan sighed faintly inside, but on the surface her smile was full and bright, drawing a line with her form of address: "Boss Yu, you joke. You're a very busy man — how could I dare disturb you lightly?" She smiled, using the honorific and the courtesies to draw a clear boundary, then turned to Zhao Yiming and Jiang Hai: "Director Jiang, Boss Shuai, this is Boss Yu — Yu Hao, an investor from Shanghai, a friend." She used "friend," a degree more distant than "old friend," and buried their never-public "arrangement" completely.
Seeing Yu Hao, Zhao Yiming did in fact recognize him, and rose to shake hands courteously: "Boss Yu, we meet again. Thank you for your support." His manner was that of a pure business associate reunited — but Yu Hao's overly familiar "Yao Yuan," and the look in his eyes when they fell on her, a look of regret and reminiscence he couldn't quite hide, set the alarm bell in Zhao Yiming's mind ringing faintly once more. Without a flicker, he shook hands and exchanged pleasantries with Yu Hao, while his gaze slid, in passing, from Yu Hao's face over to Shuai Hongqiang's side, then lightly across the unreadable Jiang Hai in the seat of honor, and finally back to Yao Yuan beside him — her bearing impeccable, yet faintly radiating a sense of "command over the battlefield."
Yu Hao shook hands with Zhao Yiming, his smile a little dry: "Boss Zhao, congratulations. This project of yours has tremendous momentum." But his gaze drifted helplessly toward Yao Yuan, and seeing the natural, close warmth between her and Zhao Yiming, he quickly drew it back, his fingertips tightening slightly around his glass.
Jiang Hai merely gave a faint nod. Yu Hao sat down, but his eyes kept drifting toward Yao Yuan, and each time she and Zhao Yiming shared a natural low word or glance, he quickly looked away and bent his head to drink. His mood now was no doubt complex beyond words — loss at how quickly she had moved on (and to someone so outstanding), regret over the absurd suggestion he had once made, and above all a wistfulness at watching her ascend a higher stage while he had already lost the standing to stand beside her.
Zhao Yiming looked at these few men at the table, and the way they looked at Yao Yuan let him taste something out of the ordinary.
But he was Zhao Yiming — a man who had weathered great storms, who could command the situation in both the lab and at the negotiating table. He pressed down the complex tangle that surged up in an instant — curiosity, scrutiny, and a very faint displeasure — and his smile stayed flawless, even more composed than before.
As host, he warmly attended to every one of these "guests." He earnestly discussed with Jiang Hai the details of the province's AI-support policies; courteously sought Shuai Hongqiang's views on the local business environment; even tried to explore some investment trend with the apparently distracted Yu Hao. He introduced Jiang Hai as a "good friend" to Shuai Hongqiang and Yu Hao, and tended attentively to Yao Yuan's "old friend" Shuai Hongqiang and her "friend" Yu Hao. That very attentiveness, instead, added a touch of irresistibly comic absurdity to the subtle awkwardness pervading the table.
Yao Yuan took it all in.
She watched Jiang Hai, beneath the easy bearing of an official as he handled Zhao Yiming's technical-policy questions, glance now and then at her with a thoughtful eye; watched Shuai Hongqiang, as he worked to join the conversation, look at her with a guilt that hovered on the edge of speech; watched Yu Hao, behind his forced cheer, with a loneliness and unwillingness he couldn't hide; and then looked at Zhao Yiming beside her, keeping the mood alive...
The initial tension and absurdity gradually faded, and a strange, almost transcendent calm rose from the bottom of her heart.
Once, every one of these men had written a stroke into some chapter of her life — bold and vivid, or light and sketchy, or piercing to the bone, or never to be reconciled. They stood for the longing, struggle, choice and being-chosen, hurt and growth of her different stages.
And now they sat at the same table, gathered because of the career achievement that "Yao Yuan" the person had created (whatever private thoughts each of them carried). She was no longer the "object" they scrutinized, judged, chose, or abandoned. She was the protagonist of this banquet, in name and in substance — the one who had built the stage, set the rules, and moved among them all with ease.
She smiled, raised her glass, and rose with grace itself. Her gaze swept calmly across each differently-shaded face at the table.
"Today, that 'Pretty Big One' can set sail successfully is owed to everyone present, and to all the friends here, for their unwavering support and trust." Her voice came clear and pleasant over the glass, carrying the sincerity and force of one who owns the room. "This is not merely the launch of a product, but the beginning of a new journey about understanding, growth, and connection. I drink to you all — in gratitude for the past, and in still greater anticipation of the future. May we all be more clear-eyed, and more free."
She drained the glass in one go, her manner free and clean.
Jiang Hai looked at her long and deep, raised his glass in acknowledgment, and drank slowly to the bottom, the last ripple in his eyes settling into a deep-pool calm and the recognition that comes after scrutiny. Shuai Hongqiang hurried to drink along, his eyes complex, but his blessing genuine. Yu Hao drank too, his smile still a little strained, yet some fixation seemed to go down with the wine. Zhao Yiming raised his glass as well, unhurried in his movements, his gaze never leaving Yao Yuan — a gaze of clear admiration, of tacit agreement, and of a wordless support that said, "I understand. I'm here." He drank with composure, as if everything subtle he had just sensed were merely an inconsequential, flavor-adding side dish at this banquet of success.
A "gathering of exes" that should have churned with hidden currents and brimmed with awkwardness was quietly defused — through Yao Yuan's absolute core presence, her clear sense of her own role, Zhao Yiming's wisdom in seeing all yet choosing to play along as guardian, and the unspoken understanding between the two of them. It was even turned inside out into a silent proof of how solid their relationship was, how deeply they trusted each other, and a coronation of Yao Yuan's personal charm and command.
The banquet went on. Lights, music, the sound of conversation. Those past debts of feeling, knots of the heart, unfinished stories — all became the liquid swaying in the glasses of this moment, swallowed, digested, and then made part of the energy that keeps rushing forward.
Yao Yuan sat back down beside Zhao Yiming, and under cover of the tablecloth, slipped her hand lightly into his palm. Zhao Yiming caught it at once, steadily, his fingertips brushing the back of her hand with a warmth of comfort and recognition. He tilted his head and said, low, in a voice only the two of them could hear: "Brilliant." He paused, then added, with a trace of teasing and understanding, "Your 'old friends' all carry a lot of weight."
Yao Yuan glanced at him sidelong, laughter flowing in her eyes, and lowered her voice too: "So now — do you feel your 'business partner' is a little more trouble than you imagined?"
Zhao Yiming looked at her, his eyes deep, and slowly shook his head, his tone earnest and low: "No. I feel my judgment is far better than even I gave it credit for."
The two smiled at each other, and some understanding and connection that needed no words quietly deepened amid the clink of glasses and the lingering undertow. Yao Yuan turned her head back, looking toward the center of the venue. There, the "Tracing the Light" tree still turned its radiance quietly, a symbol of reason, insight, connection, and infinite possibility.
However many entanglements lay in the past, however many complicated gazes surrounded her now, she knew clearly where her true heading lay. Those men were coordinates along the road she had come — bright or dim, marking the detours and shoals she had once crossed. And the future — her course — would, like the "Pretty Big One" she had helped create with her own hands, point toward a vaster, clearer, freer deep sea of human nature.
And so the launch came to its satisfying close, in this strange and eerie atmosphere.

========================================

Chapter 86 — The Mirror-Master's Descendant and the Way to Undo the Mirror Pact
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640333.html

Another month slipped by in a blur of work. Outside the window, the ginkgo leaves turned from brilliant gold to a withered yellow, until one morning a cold wind swept them off the branches and scattered them in a mottled carpet across the ground. The launch of the "Pretty Big One" Emotional AI was like dropping a smart little stone into a calm lake; the ripples spread far beyond anyone's expectations. The strength of user feedback, the paid-conversion rate, the word of mouth spreading on its own across social media — all of it pointed to one clear fact. This was not just a clean technical rollout. It was a beautiful piece of commercial monetization, the kind that comes when insight into human nature locks precisely into market demand. Yao Yuan's team was sunk deep in a high-intensity, high-reward mix of exhilaration and exhaustion. And on the back of the "Pretty Big One" success, Zhao Yiming's Western World was now going all-in on a fully general AI, no longer confined to the vertical emotional lane.
Just as the business was sailing on a full wind, the leads on the Mirror Pact finally produced something real.
Shuai Hongqiang called in every gray-market connection he had built up over the years, and Old Chen reactivated long-dormant old ties. After much back-and-forth and many relayed introductions, they finally pinned down the mirror-master's descendant — an old man named Wang Baoguo — and got hold of his contact details. The conversation went unexpectedly smoothly. The man, well past seventy, spoke in a calm, gentle voice on the phone. He asked almost nothing and simply agreed to Old Chen's invitation to "meet and talk." The place was set: the Guangu Hall.
On the day of the meeting, the season still lingered in late autumn, winter not yet arrived, yet a sky full of fine snow came early. Jinshi saw its first frost-snow of the year, settling over the autumn traces that had not finished falling.
The snow was light — dry, fine grains driven by the north wind, hissing as they struck the windowpanes. The sky was a heavy lead gray, but with the snow-light reflecting up, the room felt clearer and quieter than usual. Inside the Guangu Hall it was warm, the scent of tea curling through the air. On the curio shelves the collected pieces glowed faintly under the lamps, like countless silent eyes watching the strange chain of cause and effect about to be unveiled here.
Yao Yuan arrived early. She wore a smoke-gray cashmere turtleneck and black trousers, her long hair loose, her makeup light — her whole bearing settled into the kind of comfort and calm that made for a long sitting and talking. Only the faint blue shadows under her eyes, dark from constant worry and the intermittent time-travel, betrayed how unquiet she was inside. Shuai Hongqiang came in almost on her heels. He was bundled in a heavy black overcoat, unmelted snow grains still clinging to his shoulders. He shrugged off the coat at the door, a crisp shirt underneath, but there was a tension in his expression he couldn't hide. When he looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze flickered with something complicated. He murmured a greeting, then dropped his eyes and sat down.
Old Chen still had that air of an immortal sage. He sat in the host's seat, unhurriedly scalding and rinsing the tea set. The air filled with the rich, mellow aroma of aged pu'er, that medicinal note unique to it.
About a quarter of an hour later, faint footsteps sounded outside the hall. An assistant led an old man in.
He was perhaps a little past seventy, lean of build, dressed in a half-worn but stiffly starched dark-blue Mao suit, a light-gray sweater beneath it, collar and cuffs immaculate. He wore an old-fashioned pair of black-framed glasses. The eyes behind the lenses were small but bright and clear, carrying the gentle, careful gaze of a man who had spent his life among old papers and documents. His graying hair was combed neatly, his face thin and refined. This was Wang Baoguo. He looked more like a retired scholar from some local-gazetteer office — cultured, quiet, composed.
"Master Wang, you've braved the snow to come — thank you. Please, sit." Old Chen rose to greet him, his manner courteous.
"You're too kind, Old Chen. You called, and of course I came." Wang Baoguo spoke slowly, his voice not loud, with the crisp enunciation of an old-school intellectual. He gave Old Chen a slight bow, then nodded in acknowledgment to Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang. His eyes rested on Yao Yuan's face a moment longer, with a professional kind of caution, as if examining an artifact. Then he took the guest's seat, posture upright, hands resting naturally on his knees.
After a round of tea, Old Chen steered the conversation naturally toward the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern Mirror, and the many phenomena surrounding Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang that defied all ordinary explanation — how Yao Yuan would travel back, beyond her control, to some pivotal node in her past fate, and how Shuai Hongqiang, because of her travels, received the "good fortune" that shifted along with them. Old Chen told it as objectively as he could, stating only the facts, adding no embellishment.
Wang Baoguo listened in silence, his fingers tapping lightly and unconsciously on his knee, as if rapidly searching and cross-referencing information in his mind. It was only when Old Chen mentioned the seven involuntary "spirit-wandering" states — when Yao Yuan seemed to return to some point in the past — that his brows drew together slightly, a flash of understanding and gravity crossing his eyes.
"The mirror — did you bring it?" Wang Baoguo set down his teacup and asked, his voice still even.
Yao Yuan took the bronze mirror from the bag at her side, carefully wrapped in a thick, soft cloth. She unwrapped it and set it gently on the tea table. The archaic Kui-dragon cloud pattern gave off a dull glow under the lamplight, the dragon's eyes like two bottomless black holes.
From a plain canvas bag he carried, Wang Baoguo took out a pair of white cotton gloves and put them on, then produced a small magnifying glass. He did not reach for the mirror at once. Instead he bent forward, keeping a little distance, and studied the patterns on its back through the glass, inch by inch — especially that ring of faint, half-hidden cloud pattern and a few barely perceptible nodes. His movements were professional and absorbed, his very breathing softened, as though he were appraising a crucial excavated relic.
Time ticked by, second by second. The only sounds left in the hall were the hiss of snow grains against the window and the faint murmur of water on the verge of boiling. Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang both held their breath in concentration. Old Chen stroked his beard, his gaze deep.
At last Wang Baoguo straightened slowly, took off the magnifying glass, and carefully peeled away the gloves. He looked at Old Chen, then at Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang, his gaze finally settling back on the bronze mirror, and let out a long, barely audible sigh.
"Yes — this should be it." There was a settled calm in Wang Baoguo's voice, and a deep wistfulness. "My forebears dealt with bronze, stone, and old artifacts for generations, and they passed down certain records about particular objects, things not spoken of to outsiders. This mirror, by the scattered notes my ancestors left, is no ordinary old mirror. Its prototype may trace back to the Tang dynasty, the Tianbao years, to a 'Time-Tracing Mirror' cast by a Daoist adept named Lingxuzi."
"Lingxuzi?" A sharp light flashed in Old Chen's eyes.
"Yes. My ancestors' notes record it in detail. This man was deeply versed in yin-yang and the star signs. By some chance encounter he obtained a thread of the 'Breath of the First Opening of Chaos,' and then, with otherworldly mysterious bronze, he poured his whole heart into casting three mirrors, each hiding its own mechanism." Wang Baoguo spoke slowly, his tone more like an academic report than a tale of the uncanny. "This mirror reflects not form, but… time, and fortune."
"Reflects time? Reflects fortune?" Shuai Hongqiang's throat went dry as he repeated the words.
"Just so." Wang Baoguo pointed to the pattern-nodes on the mirror's back that he had examined so carefully. "Look — this outer cloud pattern is in fact an extremely intricate, ancient 'element-gathering' formation, meant to lock in that one special thread of 'qi' within the mirror. And these few key nodes — the way the patterns run secretly aligns with the constellations, with two complementary ancient talismanic-array systems embedded inside. One is called the 'Lead of Time-Tracing'; the other, the 'Pivot of Fortune-Shifting.'"
He paused, as if weighing a more accessible explanation. "By the notes, this 'Lead of Time-Tracing' is triggered when it meets a person of a certain fate-mark, at a moment of intense emotional upheaval. It draws on that special thread of 'qi' in the mirror and pulls that person's 'spirit-thought' — briefly — back to some pivotal node in their past life. It might be just before a major decision, or at the moment a regrettable thing happens. The time is never long — perhaps a few hours, perhaps a day or two. The returning 'spirit-thought' knows what came after, like an observer from the future, and can warn and guide the past self."
Yao Yuan's heart sank hard. This matched her experience exactly.
"Then… can the past self change fate?" Shuai Hongqiang asked urgently, his voice trembling.
"Hard to say." Wang Baoguo shook his head gently, his gaze behind the lenses rational and careful. "The notes record it: the 'spirit-thought's' guidance is like words spoken in a dream. Whether the past self grasps it, whether it heeds it, and how the track of fate actually shifts afterward… the variables are enormous. Perhaps a few small bumps can be avoided. But the great current of destiny — the brief influence of a consciousness returning — is, I'm afraid, limited. However…"
His tone took a turn, growing more grave. "The crux is this: once the 'Lead of Time-Tracing' is activated, the 'Pivot of Fortune-Shifting' is bound to trigger with it. Every attempt the mirror-holder makes to interfere with their own past fate — whether or not anything is truly changed in the end — that 'disturbance' itself is treated by the 'Pivot of Fortune-Shifting' as a refining and extraction of a fixed 'fortune.' This more concentrated 'fortune-energy,' born of the disturbance, is guided by the formation, fused and refined with that special thread of qi in the mirror, until at last…"
Wang Baoguo turned his gaze to Shuai Hongqiang, with the cool air of dissection. "And you, Mr. Shuai. You are the one who gave the mirror. You formed an invisible link with this special 'fortune-conversion' system in the mirror, which made you the receiving end of this 'fortune-shifting' chain. Each time the person of that certain fate-mark — that is, Ms. Yao — has her 'spirit-thought' drawn back by the 'Lead of Time-Tracing,' creating one 'disturbance' to her past fate, the 'fortune-energy,' refined and purified by the mirror's system, flows along that invisible link and turns toward you instead. Whatever the 'bearer' seeks, that is mostly where the 'fortune' lands. Seek wealth, you get wealth. Seek fame, you get fame."
Shuai Hongqiang's face paled slightly, his fingers unconsciously gripping his knees. Yao Yuan kept her face calm, only the hand resting on her thigh going faintly cold at the fingertips. Old Chen stroked his beard, his expression heavy. What Master Wang said matched the notes handed down through his own master's line.
Wang Baoguo's face was solemn as he went on slowly. "The notes carry a warning: 'Cross the mind too often, and you harm the root; fortune shifts without constancy, so bear its fruit with care.' What it means is this — for a person's primary consciousness to cross the barrier of time and space again and again is an enormous drain on the heart and the soul, like overdrawing one's own source. Do it too many times, and at the least you'll see your spirit wither and your memory grow disordered; at the worst… the consequences don't bear thinking about. And the one who borrows the fortune—" he looked at Shuai Hongqiang, his eyes like a mirror, "—seems to reap without sowing. But the fortune he receives comes from the disturbance and refining of another's fate. It was never stable, and it is tangled deep in cause and effect. What is gained must be repaid; the way of Heaven keeps its balance. What you gain extra in this one area, you may lose in another — in health, in offspring, in family ties, or in something else you treasure — meeting a corresponding loss or trial. This is the conservation of energy, and it is the cycle of cause and effect. When you first gave the mirror, your mind was set on 'wealth,' so the return came mostly as wealth-fortune. But that gift was, in the dark, already marked with a price."
Shuai Hongqiang jerked his head up and looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes flooding with red in an instant, a guilt beyond all measure churning within them. He wanted to say something. His lips moved, but no sound would come.
Yao Yuan avoided his gaze and looked only at Wang Baoguo. "Master Wang," her voice rose, unnaturally calm, though listen closely and you could catch a thread of tension, "now that the root cause is clear — is there a way to undo it?"
Wang Baoguo looked at her, a trace of approval crossing his eyes, as if commending her composure in this moment. He nodded. "There is. The family precept says: if some later generation should truly touch this mirror's mechanism by mistake and seek out our line, we must not shirk it. We must help them sort out the cause and effect, and find a way to dissolve the pact."
He pointed to the bronze mirror on the tea table. "We need one drop each of fingertip blood — from the mirror-master's heir, and from the two who forged this cause and effect — dripped together into the eyes of this Kui-dragon. Then we wrap the bronze mirror tightly, nine layers, in pure-yang red cloth soaked through with a specially prepared cinnabar. Then we choose a spot on the Yellow River where the current runs swift and the water's nature is most yin, most heavy, at a particular hour, and sink it to the riverbed — so that it never sees the light of day again. In this way, the Mirror Pact can be undone, the link severed."
A glimmer of hope lit in Yao Yuan's eyes.
"But," Wang Baoguo's tone turned, growing heavier, "timing is crucial. The rite can only succeed after the 'great fortune-bearer' has undergone her third 'travel,' or some multiple of three. The Daoists say: one gives rise to two, two to three, and three to the ten thousand things. 'Three' is the variable, the small completion of a cycle — and the only node at which the cycle can be cut and life begun anew. Miss a multiple of three, and you can only wait for the next multiple of three."
He looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze like a torch. "Miss Yao, you say you have already crossed seven times. Then you have already missed the 'three' and the 'six.' The next workable moment is after the ninth."
Nine. Yao Yuan's heart sank slowly, and yet, strangely, a fated sense of "so that's how it is" rose in its place. Two more times. That unknown, draining journey.
"And moreover," Wang Baoguo's voice dropped lower and graver, his eyes turning to the ashen-faced Shuai Hongqiang, "Mr. Shuai, you must understand. The fortune the Mirror Pact has 'granted' you is not an effect without cause. Heaven's way keeps its balance; yin and yang wax and wane. The more you have gained from this Mirror Pact, the greater the 'cause and effect' you may have to repay one day when it is undone — or if the pact lasts beyond a certain limit. As the saying goes: 'the gift of wealth must be drawn back out somewhere else in your life.' When you first gave the mirror, your mind held 'wealth,' so the return to you was a flourishing wealth-fortune. But Heaven will not let a man have everything perfect. What you gain extra in this, you may suffer in health, in offspring, in family love, or in some other thing you treasure — an unexpected loss or stripping away. This is the way of 'yin-yang in balance.' And it is the most venomous thing about the Mirror Pact — what it gives, sooner or later it will take back, with interest, in the way you would least want to lose it."
Shuai Hongqiang slumped down into his chair, none of the smug ease of a man whose career was sailing smooth. After a long while, he forced a hoarse sound from his throat. "I… I understand now… Thank you, Master Wang."
Yao Yuan looked at Wang Baoguo, her gaze firm again. "Master Wang — so you're saying I must go through two more travels, and after the ninth, the three of us must gather again, at the bank of the Yellow River, to perform the rite of undoing the pact. Is that right?"
"Yes." Wang Baoguo nodded. "I will bring the cinnabar red cloth that's needed when we next meet. The two of you must take care of yourselves as well. Especially you, Miss Yao — be sure to keep the 'heart-guarding' old jade close against your body at all times. It can protect your spirit and soul, and lessen the drain. Mr. Shuai… conduct yourself well. Before the pact is undone, act only so as to keep your conscience at ease, and build up as much merit and virtue as you can — it may slightly soften the backlash to come."
The hall fell into a long silence. Only the tea's fragrance, the sound of snow, and the heavy breathing of a few people.
The riddle was solved, yet the road ahead was still thick with thorns. But at least there was a direction now, a method, a clear and hopeful endpoint to look toward — after the ninth travel.
Yao Yuan let out a soft breath and carefully wrapped the bronze mirror — the source of so much trouble — back up in its soft cloth. The cold of it came through the fabric, but there was no great fear left in her heart now, only a calm acceptance of a fixed fate, and the planning that went with it.
Shuai Hongqiang sat there as if his soul had fled, as if all his strength had been drained away in an instant. He watched Yao Yuan's calm profile, and the vast guilt and the dread of the coming "backlash" wove themselves into an airtight net that bound him fast.
Old Chen rose and made a solemn cupped-hand salute to Wang Baoguo. "Master Wang, thank you for clearing our doubts, for all your trouble. A debt this great needs no words of thanks."
Wang Baoguo waved a hand and stood as well. His gaze swept once more over Yao Yuan and Shuai Hongqiang, and he left them with these words: "Everything has its appointed time. Forcing it does no good; go with it, that is all. Take care, each of you. After the ninth — I'll see you at the Yellow River." With that, he gave Old Chen a slight nod, and just as he had come, he walked off with a steady stride into the fine snow beyond the door. His figure soon vanished at the far end of the lane.
The snow was still falling. Little by little it laid a thin white over the bluestone path, as if to bury every word and secret of this day.
Yao Yuan rose to take her leave as well. At the doorway she paused, and without turning back, her voice came clearly — meant for Shuai Hongqiang, and as if meant for herself too: "Two more times. Get yourselves ready."
Then she opened her umbrella and walked into the vast curtain of snow. Her figure drew farther and farther away, until at last it merged with the silver-white silence of heaven and earth.
Shuai Hongqiang still sat in the warm room, yet felt a cold that cut to the bone. He gazed toward the direction in which Yao Yuan had disappeared, then dropped his eyes to his own faintly trembling hands. These hands had once cradled that mirror and passed it to her — and had caught the "wealth-fortune" that came of it, stained with her blood and tears. Now, at last, the bill of fate lay clearly before him.
He slowly closed his eyes. The sound of snowflakes striking the window, in that moment, came to him like some distant, heavy countdown.
The ninth time. The Yellow River. Undoing the pact. The backlash.
All of it was heading toward a final verdict. And before then, whether it was Yao Yuan or him, Shuai Hongqiang, both of them had to keep walking the tightrope of their own fates, waiting for that destined moment — to break the shackles, or to bear the price.

========================================

Chapter 87 — Real High-End Love Never Burns Through the One Who Favors You
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640334.html

In the few minutes before the livestream began, Yao Yuan let her mind go blank, the way she always did. Her thoughts drifted like the thin winter clouds outside the window, aimless. The matter of the Mirror Pact had finally taken shape. She still had to wait until after the ninth time-travel, but at last there was a definite method and a definite deadline. The worry that had hung over her for so long actually settled now that it had something solid to rest on — since there was no other way, she would simply wait. And in the waiting days, life kept to its own track; it did not let you stand still.

Her days were full. The content of the stream needed constant refining. After "Pretty Big One Emotional AI" went live, user feedback poured in, and there was no time to lose in iterating and optimizing. Friends' gatherings, industry exchanges — her schedule was packed solid. Her relationship with Zhao Yiming, built on shared creation and a rational understanding between them, had gradually settled into something stable and grounded, like an instrument running in harmony after careful calibration. Now and then she thought this life really wasn't bad. Her career was a clear path climbing upward; at her side was a partner more than capable and able to understand her. A grounded sense of security, built on clear-eyed choices and real strength, wrapped around her. Perhaps this was the most ideal form of life she had clawed and scrapped her way into earning for herself.

She drew her thoughts back. The timing was exactly right.

Wednesday, two in the afternoon, not a second off.

【Livestream Status】

The ring of fill lights flared on without a sound, spilling a sterile, operating-room white that cloaked the central area without a single shadow. Yao Yuan sat poised in the middle of it, like a carefully displayed work of art, and also like an attending surgeon about to take up the scalpel.

Today she had chosen a caramel-brown velvet suit. The heavy, particular texture of the velvet and its restrained sheen fit her upright figure perfectly, lending a note of unmistakable luxury to her composure. Beneath it she wore a silk V-neck blouse in the same shade but a touch lighter; the soft luster of the silk played against the matte texture of the velvet in an exquisite layering. She had swept all her hair back into a sleek, flawless high ponytail, leaving only a few deliberately placed wisps of soft curl at her temples and hairline to soften the clean lines of her face and add a touch of unstudied liveliness. At her ears, a pair of minimalist diamond studs caught and cut the light into cold points of starlight with her every small movement. On her wrist was a platinum watch of clean lines, and on her ring finger a geometrically cut black agate ring shifted with a dark, unreadable gleam under the cold light.

The backdrop was that eternal, vast floor-to-ceiling window. Outside, the winter afternoon light was a cold, indifferent gray-white, and under it the muddy surface of the Yellow River shimmered with a metallic, almost dull gray cold — silent, enduring, pausing for no one's joys or sorrows, only surging slow and stubborn toward the horizon. This carefully constructed space — the precise light, the symbolic backdrop, the eternal chaos beyond the glass, together with the exquisitely dressed, calm-eyed woman within it — formed a single instrument, a precise instrument for dissecting the human heart, weighing emotion, taking desire apart.

The dark "rust red" polish on Yao Yuan's fingertips slid lightly across the smooth surface of the tablet as she prepared to take the first caller. On the screen, the title was clean and sharp, like a honed scalpel pointed straight at some common, aching emotional knot: #Phantom High Ground? Don't "Act Out" Your Love into an Abandoned Construction Site#

Yao Yuan adjusted the tiny mic clipped at her collar, raised her eyes, and gazed calmly into the depths of the lens. In that gaze there was no warming up, no small talk — only the cool, focused attention of someone who saw through everything, and the professional steadiness of someone ready at any moment to begin a "clinical dissection."

"All right," her voice came through top-grade equipment, clear, steady, free of any excess emotion, like the confirmation before a surgery begins. "Let's connect this friend."

【Call Connected】

Caller (a woman's voice, 26, the words edged with a panic she was fighting to hide): "Teacher Yuanyuan… I, I really don't know what to do anymore. My ex-boyfriend… this time it seems he really means it. He doesn't want me anymore." She drew a deep breath and rushed on, as if afraid a single pause would break her. "We were together two years. He was so good to me, that kind of really good. Holiday gifts, anniversary gifts, never half-hearted — always expensive, always thoughtful. If I said in the middle of the night I wanted the congee from the east side of town, he'd drive half an hour to go buy it. When I hit trouble at work, he'd pull strings to smooth things over for me. Sometimes my temper would flare and I'd say awful things, and he'd just take it, then come and coax me out of it…"

"So what was the reason for the breakup?" Yao Yuan asked, her tone calm, offering no comfort, like a doctor taking a history.

"I…" the caller's voice dropped, full of confusion and regret. "Maybe… I 'acted out' too much. I always figured that since he loved me so much, he definitely couldn't do without me. The moment I was unhappy, or felt he'd fallen short somewhere — even just answering a message a little slowly — I'd bring up breaking up. At first he'd get so anxious, all kinds of apologies, trying to win me back, buying me bags and flowers, waiting downstairs for hours. Later… I did it so many times he seemed to get used to it, but every time he'd still come and coax me. Until last month, over something especially tiny again — he'd worked overtime two days straight, he was a little tired, and during our video call that night he yawned. I decided he was impatient, that he didn't value me, and I argued with him on the phone, and said again, 'Let's break up. I'm tired, there's no point.'"

She broke off, her voice thicker with tears, threaded with disbelief: "I thought he'd be like before — that he'd come apologize the next morning after he woke up, beg me not to make a scene. But no… A whole day passed, and all he sent me on WeChat was one line: 'Okay. I respect your decision. Let's break up. Take care.' And then he never reached out to me again! He won't answer calls, he blocked me on WeChat. I went to wait downstairs at his company, and when he saw me, his eyes were like he was looking at a stranger — he just walked right past me… Teacher, I didn't actually want to break up! I just wanted him to care about me a little more, wanted him to fret over me the way he used to! I'm dying of regret now, I can't get anything done, I'll never find anyone better than him… Have I really lost him for good?"

The scrolling comments started racing past:

[Classic "Boy Who Cried Wolf," live on air.]

[The guy gave so much up front — he'd warm up a block of ice — but nobody can take cold water thrown at them every single day.]

[Sis, people's patience and love do run out.]

["Phantom high ground" is too precise a term — when you're acting out you think you're a queen, then you find out the city's been empty for ages.]

Yao Yuan listened quietly, no sympathy or reproach on her face, only a deep concentration. She waited until the girl's stifled sobbing eased a little before she spoke, slowly, not loudly, but every word clear, landing in the silent studio with a force that struck at the heart:

"Young lady, let's first step outside words like 'love or not,' 'care or not.' Let's take it apart using a logic you might understand better — say, project investment and risk management."

On the other end the caller seemed to freeze for a moment.

"Your ex-boyfriend was like an angel investor — huge early commitment, full sincerity, almost no bet-against clauses. What he put in were scarce resources: time, money, top-tier emotional value, and a beautiful set of expectations about the future. And you? You were the promising 'core project' he'd bet on. Early on, the partnership looked smooth, the investor was diligent and conscientious, and the project showed a decent growth curve."

Yao Yuan's tone was as cool as if she were reviewing a business case: "But you, the 'project side,' made a fatal strategic error. You took that unlimited 'credit overdraft' privilege the investor extended to you out of trust and expectation, and treated it as capital you could squander at will. And you turned 'threatening to liquidate and pull out' — bringing up a breakup — into your routine 'management tool' and 'mood regulator.' The moment you felt your expectations weren't met, your risk appetite wobbled — bad mood — or you simply wanted to test the investor's 'loyalty and risk tolerance,' you triggered the 'liquidation threat' program. Once, twice — out of long-term faith in the project and the enormous sunk cost already paid, the investor chose to add investment and raise his risk tolerance: coaxing you, winning you back. But every single 'liquidation threat' violently burned through the investor's already limited reserve of trust and capital of patience, and sharply pushed up the 'risk rating' you held in his mind."

She leaned in slightly, those calm eyes seeming to pierce through the screen: "Until last time, when you once again triggered the Nth 'liquidation threat' over a trivial 'expectation-management error' — he was tired and yawned. This time the investor likely ran a thorough 'post-investment review' and 'risk assessment.' He found that this project's 'core management' — you — lacked basic emotional stability and any awareness of risk control, and was itself the single greatest source of risk. The project's future cash flow — the happiness and sustainability of the relationship — had become wildly uncertain in the internal drain and stalls caused by one 'liquidation alarm' after another, possibly even negative. And so he made a rational final decision — one perhaps also carrying enormous disappointment — to cut his losses in time and execute the liquidation. When he replied 'I respect your decision,' it wasn't sulking. It was a calm, business-final verdict based on a complete assessment."

The comments:

[That analogy is unreal! You get it instantly!]

["Emotion is the single greatest source of risk" — too true!]

["Liquidation verdict" — so cruel but so real. The guy really made up his mind.]

From the caller's end came stifled, rapid breathing.

"So your pain right now doesn't come only from losing him. At a deeper level —" Yao Yuan spoke word by word, her tone cutting, "you've suddenly fallen from the imagined 'phantom high ground' where you thought you could command the wind and rain, down into a brutal reality where you have to face 'project bankruptcy, investor withdrawal' — and you've discovered you hold not a single bargaining chip, and your posture is a mess. That fall destroys a person's self-image far more than you imagine."

"Then can I… can I still win him back? What should I do?" the caller pressed, urgent, like clutching at the last straw.

Yao Yuan was silent for two seconds, then said clearly and firmly: "My advice may be the opposite of what you're hoping for. Not only do I not advise you to 'win him back' now — I'll tell you that in your current state, any so-called 'winning back' is just continuing to make the mistake, pushing yourself into an even lower pit."

"Why?" The caller's voice shook.

"Because we have to clarify a core question right now: what is true 'class' in love, and what is utterly, thoroughly 'low.'" Yao Yuan's gaze was sharp as a knife, as if to cleave through every fog.

"Young lady, listen well. A woman's true class is never shown in the lofty, swaggering pose she strikes while she's being loved and carefully cherished. That isn't control — that's stupidity and a lack of breeding. Taking someone's heart-and-soul devotion as capital you can overdraw without limit and treat as worthless; viewing the other person's tolerance and forbearance as a bottom line you can probe at will and trample on again and again; even 'not treating him as a human being' in the relationship, only as a tool that must obey absolutely and satisfy your every emotional demand on command — this is the lowest sort of behavior. What it exposes isn't your power and charm, but the extreme poverty inside you, your ingratitude and selfishness. You're feeding your forever-bottomless black hole of insecurity by trampling on another person's dignity, and that face you wear is hideous."
