Her tone eased a little, but the core grew harder: "Truly high-end togetherness is cherishing, is reverence. Cherish the person who is good to you with nothing held back; tuck his goodness away carefully and repay it with equal respect, consideration, and shared effort. It's giving clear praise and sweet feedback when the other does well; it's consciously managing yourself when your own emotional storm hits, using clear-headed communication instead of wounding attacks, voicing your needs sincerely instead of threatening a 'breakup.' The confidence of 'not being afraid to lose' shouldn't become a dagger you use to hurt the one who loves you. It should become the backbone that keeps you sincere in the relationship — neither groveling nor arrogant, never swallowing your own grievances to keep the peace."

"So when should you draw the 'breakup' card without a moment's hesitation?" Yao Yuan's eyes turned utterly clear, carrying a flat, absolute resolve. "Only when the other person truly, repeatedly, and without any remorse crosses your principles and your bottom line. Deception, betrayal, infidelity, violence of any kind, or an irreconcilable conflict in core values. A breakup at that point is self-protection, is defending your dignity, is the clear-eyed recognition that this relationship has gone bad and that staying will only keep draining and even destroying you. That kind of breakup is a calm decision, a never-look-back cutting of losses. The posture may be tragic, but it is absolutely beautiful — because you're protecting something more important: your own integrity and bottom line as an independent individual."

"But your 'breakup'?" She looked straight into the lens, as if straight into the girl's eyes, her words hitting the mark in one stroke. "It was a weapon, a test, a chip for extracting emotional value, a threat to force the other person to submit. You took the most precious contract of the relationship and gambled it on the tiniest emotional fluctuation, turning 'dissolving the contract' into an upgraded version of your daily wheedling. And then one day, the way the boy who cried wolf even convinced himself, the other person went from initial alarm and exhaustion, to final numbness — and then to total clarity. The game is over, and only then do you discover you've overplayed it: the table is gone, the house has walked away."

Yao Yuan's tone carried an almost cold-blooded clarity, but beneath that coldness was a far deeper guidance:

"Once you've reached this point, you have to own the loss. Admit, openly and squarely, that you overplayed it — admit that you wrongly and repeatedly deployed 'nuclear deterrence,' and that you finally brought about a 'nuclear winter' in the relationship, irreversible. For you to go apologizing now, trying to win him back, lowering yourself — all of it, at its root, declares to him and to yourself: 'See, all my earlier arrogance, my willfulness, my treating him as less than human — it was all a bluffing paper tiger. I simply can't bear the cost of truly losing you. My earlier 'high ground' was all fake.' That look is even more degrading, even more contemptible, than when you were 'acting out.' Fairies, remember this: when someone is good to you, you should not swagger. Your worth does not need to be proven by pressing on the bottom line of the person who loves you."

"So why do I firmly oppose your trying to win him back now?" Yao Yuan closed the logic into a complete loop. "Because your present 'winning back' is paying an extra shame-tax for all your earlier 'low' behavior, begging for a scrap of pity in an even 'lower' posture. It won't make you look more worthy of love — it will only doubly confirm how correct his decision to leave was: you really are a 'high-risk project' that can't manage its own emotions, can't take responsibility for an intimate relationship, and lacks basic self-respect."

"The only correct road you can walk right now — the only one — is to fully own this loss." Her voice carried an unquestionable force. "Stop all the futile pursuit, the weeping, the self-pity that will only make you more wretched. Take every bit of your effort, your gaze, your energy, and pull it all — completely, cleanly — back off the person you yourself pushed away and who has shut the door on you for good. And then slam it, a hundred percent of it, into yourself!"

"Go and carry out the 'bankruptcy restructuring' from my analogy. First — accept reality, bear the consequences. This finest of investments is the one you acted out of existence. Own it, take responsibility, make no excuses for your mistake. That is an adult's accountability, and also the starting point of a new life. Cut off contact entirely; give each other a final dignity and space. Your not disturbing him is the only — and the last — respect you can offer right now.

"Second — review deeply, scrape the poison from the bone. Take up the scalpel and cut yourself open: why do you need to keep threatening breakups to gain security, to prove you're loved? Behind it is extreme inferiority, a severe uncertainty about your own worth, a profound distrust of relationships — or simply a giant-baby mindset that takes another person's goodness as a tribute owed to you by nature. Go to a professional counselor, read, study, and make up the key lessons missing from your character: emotional management, a sense of boundaries, and empathy. You have to rebuild yourself, with real difficulty, from an 'emotional black hole' and a 'relationship-destroyer' into an emotionally stable, independent individual who carries security and a sense of worth within. This is the rewriting of the core 'competitiveness' and 'underlying code' of your 'life project.'

"Third — rebuild your life, and live out the substance of 'class.' Turn every second you'd spend dwelling on him, missing him, regretting losing him, into action that invests in yourself. Eat every meal properly, sleep every night well, make your work shine, pick up the hobbies you abandoned, tend your friendships with care, raise your understanding and your skills. Your goal must absolutely not be to 'get better so he regrets it' — that still makes him your reference point. Your goal is this: my life was always meant to be lived full, confident, vibrant, magnetic in itself. When you no longer parasite your self-worth on another person's attention and love, when you can become a warm, stable, powerful source of energy on your own, only then do you truly touch the core of 'class.'"

Yao Yuan slowed her voice, but the force in her words built layer upon layer: "So-called 'class' is not playing the imperious queen in a relationship — that is exactly the outer armor of an inwardly weak heart. True class is cherishing every piece of sincerity you meet, respecting every bit of another's giving; it's holding gratitude when you're loved, not growing arrogant on favor; it's putting everything into the relationship while you have it, not lightly giving up; it's leaving decisively, without dragging your feet, when your principles are crossed; it's calmly owning the loss when your own mistake causes it, not blaming heaven or others, and then pouring all your strength into rebuilding a better self. It's being able to run your own life vividly and luminously, self-sufficient — whether or not there's a partner at your side."

"Young lady," she finally called the girl by name, her tone the last bit of warmth after the severity. "Someone being good to you is a gift of fate, a blessing. Your task is to catch it, be grateful for it, and nourish it with the same goodness. Don't take a blessing and 'act' it, with your willfulness and ignorance, into a wretched, unbearable bond in both your lives. Your very existence should make those who come close to you feel warm, grounded, nourished — feel 'it's worth it' — not feel exhausted, afraid, drained. If one day you have to leave a relationship, let it be because the other person isn't worthy, not because you 'don't know how.'"

"Before you learn to 'cherish,' before you possess the capacity for 'high-end' love, please first give it everything you have, and make yourself worthy of that 'goodness' you once held without realizing it — and only mourn now that it's lost. Your journey was never the road back to that man; it's the glorious road of your own rebirth, your character leveling up. First live yourself into someone worthy of being loved, and who knows how to love. As for the rest — heaven keeps its own timetable."

【Call time up; the picture cuts off amid a long, powerful resonance】

For a few seconds the studio was completely silent, and then the comments came surging in like a tide, full of shock, recognition, and self-examination.

Yao Yuan did not immediately look at the churning comments. She leaned back slightly, settling into the chair, picked up the lukewarm water at her hand, and took a light sip. Beyond the window, the Yellow River under the dim winter light still flowed silent, powerful, eastward, pausing for no one.

She set the cup down, and the black agate ring on her finger flashed a faint dark gleam under the lights. Another "clear-eyed operation" complete. She had cut open the surface of "acting out" to expose the core lesions inside — about self-respect, cherishing, character, and growth. Painful, but necessary.

========================================

Chapter 88 — A Sexless, Cold-Violence Sham Marriage: A Smart Woman's Patient Counterstrike
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640335.html

A sexless, cold-violence sham marriage: a smart woman's patient counterstrike.
"All right," she said, lifting her eyes. Her gaze turned clear and focused again, her voice as steady as ever. "Let's take the next call."
The livestream went on. The light of reason kept shining down on one soul after another, each lost in the maze of feelings and searching for a way out.
[Call connected]
Caller (a woman's voice, 31, gentle and soft on the surface but with an exhaustion and a trace of confusion she couldn't quite hide underneath): "Hello, Teacher Yuanyuan. I've followed you for a long time. This is my first time calling in, I'm a little nervous… My situation might be kind of complicated."
"Take your time. We have plenty of it," Yao Yuan said, her tone level, carrying a steadying kind of strength.
The caller drew a deep breath and began. "I'm 31 this year, my husband is 41. We met right after I graduated from college. He'd already started his own business by then and was doing pretty well. We dated for four years. Back then he treated me really well — very gentlemanly, generous with money — it's just that… he was a bit of a chauvinist. He thought a girl shouldn't have to work too hard. I was young, and I took it as being looked after. We married when I was 27. It's been four years now. In three of them, I had a son and a daughter."
(Scrolling comments drifted past: [A son and a daughter — that's the winner's template for life!] [Husband out in the world, wife at home. Very traditional setup.])
"After we got married, he wouldn't let me go out to work. He said the family didn't need my salary, and told me to focus on the home and the kids with my mind at ease. I'd had my own career plans, but I couldn't win against him, and I figured the children needed their mother, so I agreed. We hired three nannies — one for the housework, two just to look after the two children. The truth is… I'm not actually very busy." The caller's voice dropped lower.
"And your daily life, your spending?" Yao Yuan asked.
"He… won't let me buy luxury goods. He says they're vanity, that there's no need. The skincare I use, the clothes I wear — he has his assistant buy them. They're some… decent-quality brands, but with no logo on them at all. He's different, though. His watches, his suits, his car — all top of the line. I've brought it up with him, and he says a man needs a presentable front out in the world, while a woman just needs to be comfortable at home." The caller paused, her voice turning a little strained. "And he often… criticizes me. Says I'm home all day, that my thinking has fallen behind his, that the things I say are childish, that I should read more. But when I do read and share my views with him, he says my ideas are shallow, that I don't understand how the real world works…"
The comments began to turn:
[??? That's some serious double standard!]
[Won't let you work, then sneers that you've fallen behind — isn't that a closed-loop PUA?]
[Three nannies… this doesn't sound like a money problem. It's a respect problem.]
"How does he handle things financially?" Yao Yuan's question cut in like a scalpel, straight to the core.
"Every year he gives me a card with a fixed 400,000 yuan on it. That's the household living expenses and some of the kids' costs. The nannies' wages, the big purchases, his own spending — none of that goes through this card. And every time I spend, he gets a text alert on his end." The caller's voice grew smaller and smaller. "Sometimes I want to buy something a little nicer for my own family, or the bill runs higher when I get together with my girlfriends, and he'll ask about it. He never says anything harsh, but that feeling of being scrutinized… it's very uncomfortable."
The comments blew up:
[400,000 a year? For a family like theirs, isn't that pocket money?]
[Spending monitored? Is he keeping a canary or a prisoner?]
[Three nannies plus two kids — 400,000 isn't necessarily comfortable, especially when she has no freedom.]
Not a ripple crossed Yao Yuan's face. She went on: "How do the two of you get along day to day — communication, intimacy, that sort of thing?"
The caller was silent for an even longer stretch. When she spoke again, her voice carried embarrassment and a deeper bewilderment: "Communication… very little. When he's home he spends most of his time in the study, or on the phone. We barely talk, except about the children. Intimacy… even less." She seemed to make a great decision. "It was never much even before we married — he said he wanted to respect me. After the wedding, especially after the children were born, it got even rarer. Now… maybe two or three times a year. Or… once."
The comments were instantly drowned in exclamation marks:
[??? Once a year???]
[That's a damn roommate!]
[A sexless marriage? You've only been married four years!]
[Something's off. Very off.]
Yao Yuan's gaze narrowed slightly, but her tone stayed even. "You dated for four years, married for four — eight years all told. How much do you know about his social circle, his friends? Is there any woman he's especially close to, beyond the bounds of an ordinary friend? Or… a male friend like that?"
The question seemed to stump the caller. She thought back. "His friends… most are business partners, and there are a few brothers he's known for years. Women… I've never heard of anything unusual. As for male friends… there's a business partner, also his college classmate, surname Zhou, and they're especially close. They travel together for work all the time. Sometimes Mr. Zhou comes to the house and the two of them will talk in the study all night. My husband is… much more gentle and patient with him than with me." Belatedly, she added, "Oh — and at our wedding, Mr. Zhou was the witness, and he gave an especially heavy gift."
The comment-section Sherlocks came online:
[Talking all night? Nicer to him than to his own wife?]
[Wedding witness plus a heavy gift… the more you think about it, the scarier it gets.]
[Combine that with sex once a year… I've got a bold theory…]
When Yao Yuan finished listening, she leaned back slightly into her chair. She didn't speak right away. She folded her hands in front of her chest, fingertips meeting, forming a calm spire. The studio held only the faint hum of electricity and the dim sound of the Yellow River flowing outside the window. An invisible pressure spread out through the screen.
A few seconds later, she spoke slowly. Her voice wasn't loud, but every word landed with the weight of a thousand pounds on every listener's heart:
"Miss, what I'm about to say may be very blunt — even cruel. I'd ask you, and everyone watching, to brace yourselves."
"Based on the fragments you've given me: low sexual demand even during the dating years; a rapid drop after marriage to near-celibacy; strict control over your social life and finances along with psychological suppression; zero interest in — even obstruction of — your personal development and self-worth; yet an urgent need for you to complete the task of childbearing (two children in four years); and a same-□□ partner he trusts and is intimate with to an extreme degree, in whom his emotional investment far exceeds anything he gives you…"
Yao Yuan paused, then stated the cold conclusion clearly:
"There is a very high probability that your husband is in a sham marriage. And it is a sham marriage he himself entered fully aware, with cool calculation."
The comments went completely berserk:
[Holy crap! It really is a sham marriage!]
[No wonder! It all makes sense now!]
[He married her just for childbearing plus a social image!]
[This is too cruel…]
From the caller's end came the sound of a sharp intake of breath, then a longer dead silence, broken only by suppressed, ragged breathing.
Yao Yuan didn't pause. She kept on in a tone so calm it was almost cruel: "This marriage, to him, is not an emotional union. It's a precise, outsourced project for childbearing and the construction of a social image. He needs legitimate heirs to pass on his wealth and to steady his business partnerships and family expectations. He needs a 'wife' to fill the social role, to maintain his public image as a 'successful entrepreneur with a happy family.' And you are the suitable 'project contractor' he selected."
"Not letting you work ensures your social ties stay simple, your dependence high, you easy to control. Not letting you buy luxury goods, monitoring your spending — that controls project costs and keeps you from developing any unnecessary 'awakening of self-awareness' or expanding your social reach because of material abundance. Suppressing you psychologically continually erodes your confidence and independent character, ensuring you settle into the 'contractor' role and never question the absolute authority of the 'client' — him — or the true nature of the project. The near-absence of sex is because he simply has no desire for you. Once the task — childbearing — was complete, this 'partnership' was over as far as he's concerned. His closeness with that Mr. Zhou is most likely where his real emotional belonging lies."
The caller finally let out a choked sob, the sound of a worldview shattering.
"So, Teacher Yuanyuan… what… what should I do? Get a divorce?" Her voice trembled, full of despair.
"Divorce? Of course. But not now." Yao Yuan's tone turned abruptly, intensely practical, with even an edge of cold sharpness. "In your current state — no job, no independent income, no competitive standing in society, two young children, possibly not even aware of the marital assets — if you rush to file for divorce, how much do you think this shrewd, calculating husband of yours will give you? You very likely won't even win custody, because he'll prove you have no capacity to support them, and he holds an overwhelming financial advantage. He might even countersue, claiming you're mentally unstable and unfit to be a mother."
Comments:
[Reality is so brutal! But Teacher Yao is right!]
[Divorce now and she walks out with nothing.]
[What about the kids?]
The reality struck the caller speechless.
"Listen, miss," Yao Yuan said, her voice dropping, carrying the certainty of a commander deploying a strategy. "What you need to do now is not cry, not make a scene. It's to launch, immediately, a two-to-three-year 'Self-Rescue and Counterstrike Preparation' plan. You are going to turn from a passive pawn into the player quietly arranging the board in this game."
"Step one: go completely calm, and play the 'role' well. Stop all the inner grievance and resistance. On the surface, return to gentleness. Let his psychological jabs go in one ear and out the other — never argue back. Play the 'wife' and 'mother' roles flawlessly, so he lowers his guard. Use the 400,000 in living expenses he gives you to quietly build up a private savings — that's one of your future startup funds."
"Step two: gather evidence, cast a wide net. This is the most critical part, and the one that takes the most patience. First, financial evidence: learn as much as you can about the family's real assets, the company's equity, the investments. Keep the spending records on the card he gave you, too — that's evidence of how he controls your spending. Second, evidence of the sham marriage: this isn't about catching them in the act. It's about noting the details of his dealings with Mr. Zhou — coming and going together, intimate gestures, special occasions — any unguarded information that might be on his computer or phone (if you get the chance, and legally), his cold attitude toward your sexless marriage (you can record it, but mind the legal risks). More importantly, gather evidence of his psychological suppression, his control over your social life and work, his economic control — WeChat chats, recordings, and so on. In court, these are key grounds for establishing 'household cold violence' and 'restriction of personal freedom.' Third, evidence for custody: record every detail of you being the primary caregiver — photos, videos, medical records, communications with teachers — proving that you are the core of the children's emotional attachment."
"Step three: better yourself, advance in secret. Use reasons like 'to give the children a better education' or 'to raise my own understanding so I don't embarrass you' to win chances to study. You can sign up for online courses — learn finance, law, psychological counseling, even a professional skill. Slowly rebuild your knowledge base and your social circle, but keep it low-key, without fail. Take care of your body. Your condition is the confidence you'll bring to future negotiations."
Yao Yuan stressed one point in particular: "I heard you mention earlier that he had you act as guarantor on a bank loan?"
The caller answered quickly: "Yes. Before we married, his company had a loan and had me sign for it. After we married there was another one — he said it was a family investment, and had me sign for that too."
Yao Yuan's gaze hardened. "Until every one of these loan debts is fully repaid and your guarantor liability is lifted, do not file for divorce under any circumstances. This is the red line. Otherwise, the moment you divorce, if he plays games with the debts, you could end up shouldering an enormous burden. Your plan must include this milestone — after the loans are paid off."
"So your timeline is this: take two to three years to quietly complete the evidence gathering, the self-improvement, the private savings. At the same time, watch the loan-repayment progress closely. The moment the last loan you guaranteed is paid off, and you already hold evidence of sufficient weight — especially evidence pointing to his sham marriage, his cold violence, his tendency to move assets — you can act."
"At that point, you don't go begging for a divorce. You go in with a lawyer, lay out your leverage, and negotiate. Your goal is not revenge. It's to secure, for you and your children, the most favorable and reasonable division of property, custody and child support, and your own future financial security. You have the evidence; he has the social image and the business. This is a game. The calmer you are, the more thoroughly you prepare, the more leverage you hold — the more secure your future and your children's will be."
The comments turned from anger to exhilaration:
[Now that's a high-level play!]
[Lie low, build up your strength, strike once and hit the mark!]
[Teacher Yao is teaching her to fight a battle to turn it all around!]
[Two to three years — so long, but worth it.]
"Miss, this road will be very hard, very lonely. It will demand enormous endurance and willpower from you. But it is the only way to take back control of your life from being a 'childbearing outsourcing contractor,' a 'controlled person.'" Yao Yuan looked at the camera, her eyes blazing, as if she could pour strength into the person on the other side of the screen. "Don't hate. Hatred will drain you. Stay clear-eyed. Calculate. Act. Treat him as a complex 'project' that requires your careful handling. And your ultimate goal is to complete this 'Self-Rescue Project' beautifully — to walk out of that cold cage with dignity, carrying the spoils you deserve (your children and your assets), and begin your real life."
"Remember: true strength isn't never falling into the trap. It's that after you fall in, you have the patience, the strategy, and the will to climb out step by step on your own — and to make the one who set the trap never able to underestimate you again. These two or three years are the golden time for you to store up strength for your future and your children's. Use them well, and the world opens wide. Use them badly, and there's no coming back. The choice is yours."
From the caller's end came a long, trembling exhale. Then her voice turned strangely calm and resolute: "I understand, Teacher Yuanyuan. Thank you. I know what to do now."
[Call time's up. Feed cut.]
The studio was flooded once more with a surging tide of comments, full of encouragement for the caller and admiration for Yao Yuan's sharp, penetrating analysis.
The afternoon light, filtered through heavy cloud cover, came down a cold leaden gray. Through the vast floor-to-ceiling window, it traced a clear, faintly lonely silhouette around Yao Yuan. The fresh "rust red" matte polish on her fingertips looked, in the cold light, like dried blood. She picked up her water glass and took a slow sip. The water had gone slightly cool. Outside the window, the winter Yellow River glinted with a cold, copper-rust sheen in the light, still silently sweeping its countless silt and secrets onward, rolling forward.
She knew that another soul, after a thorough freezing and dissection, had perhaps found that hard but necessary path of thawing and moving forward. The road was strewn with thorns; it would take calculation, take patience. But compared to slowly suffocating in a cage that looked lavish and warm yet was cold to the bone, this was already a chance to live.
"Next." She set down the glass, her voice as steady as ever, ready to receive the next soul trapped in the maze of feelings and interests, waiting to be lit by the light of reason.

Chapter 89 — The Bloody Coming-of-Age of the Gifted: Making Up the Required Course in People Skills
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640336.html

The Bloody Coming-of-Age of the Gifted: Making Up the Required Course in People Skills
[Call connected.]
The caller (a woman, 28, her voice carrying the crisp logical edge typical of an elite Top-2-university graduate—but beneath that logic was a string pulled taut, about to snap): "Teacher Yuanyuan, I need help. I think I might have a... terminal illness when it comes to workplace relationships." She got straight to the point, almost with the composure of someone presenting a research topic, but the falter at the end of her sentence gave away a trace of trembling.
"Describe the symptoms." Yao Yuan's reply was as concise as a doctor taking down a chief complaint.
"I'm 28. Bachelor's and master's from a Top-2 school, in finance. Graduated first in my class, joined the top-tier investment bank where I work now. It's been a year and three months. My performance speaks in data: of the three deals I've handled, my error rate is the lowest in the department, my reports get used as templates, and my quarterly reviews are all S-grade. My direct supervisor and the MD—the managing director—have both made it clear they're betting on my professional potential." Lin Wei spoke fast, as if fending something off. "But my relationships with my colleagues are a disaster. No one teams up with me for group projects. I always eat lunch alone. Any thread in the work chat goes dead the moment I reply. When I need to collaborate, all I ever get is a robotic 'sure' and the bare minimum of cooperation. Information always reaches me last and least complete. And I even... I overheard them in the break room saying my 'nose is stuck in the air,' that all I know how to do is 'perform for the boss.'"
She paused, and the string drew tighter still: "I don't understand. I genuinely mean no one any harm. Everything I do, I do to push the work to perfection. I don't gossip, I don't waste time on idle chat—is being efficient, focused, and a perfectionist somehow wrong? Why would they rather huddle together with people who are mediocre at the job but who'll grumble together and order milk tea, and yet isolate the one person who can actually get things done? This environment... it suffocates me. Every day I walk into that office, it's like walking into a silent cage full of hostility. I can't make sense of it—is being too good, in itself, a crime?"
The scrolling comments started to split:
[So textbook. Top-student brain slamming into the wall of real society.]
[Even hearing it is suffocating, but I feel like she's offended people without realizing it.]
[The workplace isn't an exam hall, sis. Reading the room is a required course too.]
[I feel a little bad for her, but isolation like this doesn't happen for no reason.]
[Is it just me who thinks she's probably stepped on toes without knowing?]
Yao Yuan listened quietly. No sympathy surfaced on her face, no surprise either. She just leaned back slightly into her chair, the fingertips of both hands meeting to form a steady triangle propped against her chin. The posture didn't look like someone listening to a personal woe; it looked more like an architect surveying a complex system riddled with conflicting code.
Yao Yuan heard her out patiently, then spoke mildly, cutting straight to the heart of it: "Sweetheart, let me give you one very practical line first: no one will dislike you for being excellent. They'll only dislike you for being excellent and convinced of your own superiority."
She spoke again, her voice low but slicing the silence like an ice pick:
"Your problem is not a 'terminal illness in human relationships.' Your problem is that you're running an absolutely rational 'excellence operating system,' one cultivated in a sterile lab, and trying to run it inside a primitive jungle called the 'workplace'—a place teeming with viruses, bacteria, tangled ecosystems, and the law of the dark forest. There are no absolute right answers here. It's overrun with viral, complicated human nature, with bacterial growths of competing interests, and with the dark-forest law of the strong devouring the weak. The system isn't compatible, and it triggers a violent rejection response."
A faint, sharply indrawn breath came through from the caller's end.
"You think the workplace is just an upgraded version of campus—harder questions, richer rewards, but with the same core rule: highest score takes all?" A faint, almost cold curve crossed the corner of Yao Yuan's mouth. "Wrong. Especially in the finance circle you're in, which runs on collaboration and connections, the underlying logic was never about 'scores.' It's about trading resources, contests of power, and alliances for survival. Your professional skill is only one of the resources you hold—and it's a high-risk, high-threat kind of specialized resource. When you merely display that resource and lean on it, when you unconsciously use it to crush everyone around you, then in your colleagues' perception system you are not a 'respectable, excellent partner.' You are a dangerous intruder who has barged into an established tribal territory, brandishing a gleaming blade, eyes full of arrogance, yet utterly ignorant of the tribe's secret codes and taboos. The tribespeople won't worship your sharp weapon. They'll instinctively band together to reject you, to wall you off—until you're forced out, or slowly assimilated. Or until you learn to sheath the blade while mastering the tribe's language and rules, and maybe even become the new chieftain."
The comments froze for a beat, then erupted:
[That metaphor is so precise! Every cut draws blood!]
['High-risk resource'—got it. Being too capable isn't necessarily a good thing.]
['Tribal secret codes'—oh my god, I'm learning those every single day!]
"I... I never wanted to crush anyone." The caller's voice dropped, carrying the awkwardness and frailty of someone who'd just been seen straight through.
"'Intent' and 'impact' are two separate systems." Yao Yuan cut her off, her tone as calm as a scalpel cutting cleanly. "You're immersed in a mental temple you've built out of reason and efficiency, chasing the ultimate in perfection and purity. To you, idle chat is entropy increasing, and accommodating the 'mediocre' drags efficiency down. The overall aura you give off, without meaning to, is this: 'I don't belong to the same tier as you people. My time is worth more. My methodology is superior.' This isn't the content of your words. It's the hidden signal continuously broadcast by every quick turn you make to end a non-work conversation, every look in your eyes as you toss out the 'optimal solution' as if it brooks no question, every report so flawless it makes those beside you feel diminished. There is a vigilance rooted deep in human nature, planted by the survival instinct: we naturally grow wary of, distance ourselves from, even band together to resist anyone who continually makes us feel inferior, who highlights our own incompetence, or who threatens our standing in the group—especially when that person is completely oblivious to it, or simply doesn't care. Your 'brilliance,' lacking the necessary 'lubricant of empathy' and 'buffer of relationship,' is in itself the sharpest offense and the heaviest source of pressure."
Yao Yuan's gaze seemed to drift past the camera, toward some dim corner of her own memory suddenly lit up. Her tone took on a far-off, certain lament:
"I understand you completely, sweetheart. Because many years ago, I too lived inside that bubble of 'excellence' defined by a single standard. I studied dance, and after dancing long enough, my whole world shrank down to the millimeter precision of a movement, the intensity of an emotional release, and the score on the judges' table. I thought the partners I trained with were dead weight. I thought the audience didn't grasp the depth in my choreography. I believed my world was the mirrors of the practice room and the spotlight on the stage—rules clear, rewards and punishments plain. Until my second year of senior high, when reality slapped me awake with a blow that pulled no punches. I discovered that the 'star figure' I thought I was could, in the wider arena of human relationships, be nothing more than a 'misfit who doesn't belong.' The 'excellence' I prided myself on was not only worthless in everyday settings that called for warmth and cooperation—it had become the very root of my isolation and the misunderstandings about me. That early stretch of my life—where I was smug about my good grades and good looks, got disliked by the whole school, then through painful adjustment learned to notice other people's strengths, to praise them sincerely, and finally won people over—taught me a lesson carved to the bone: if you don't understand 'people,' if you can't read the subtle, complicated grain of human nature, if you can't handle those seemingly useless 'relationships,' then 'excellence' on any axis is just a gorgeous, fragile island—unable to connect to real land, let alone form an ecosystem that nourishes a life."
She drew her gaze back, refocusing on the present, and her fingertip—that touch of "rust red"—tapped lightly on the cold, hard desk, giving off a firm, decisive sound:
"This society, especially the society of adults, runs on rules that are bloody and crude. It doesn't care how pure your heart is, how self-consistent your logic, how noble your goals. It only recognizes results—and that 'result' is usually decided by the combined force that emerges after countless complex relationships have played out against each other. You can call it vulgar, call it ugly, call it a place that buries genius. But this is the rule. Either you grow strong enough to ignore the rules, to make the rules—or you must bow your head and learn them, dance within them, even use them."
She paused for a long while, letting that heavy, precious realization settle in the air.
"That experience is the most original, and the most profound, source code behind everything I later came to understand about people, every insight I have into feelings—even behind why I do this livestream." Yao Yuan's voice turned unusually clear and forceful. "It made me understand early, at the price of pain: human nature is subtle and complex, far harder to crack than any dance movement or math formula. The essence of relationships is the flow of energy and a delicate balance of power—not a simple matter of right and wrong, win and lose. A person's worth lies never merely in being 'excellent' on some single axis, but in whether they can build healthy, nourishing, sustainable connections with this world and with other people."
"Sweetheart, the loneliness and rejection you feel in the workplace now—that's the amplified, adult version of what I felt back in my classroom. The rules are more hidden, the price more immediate. But the core hasn't changed. Society won't hand you a 'free pass on the human-relations exam' just because your professional scores are high. On the contrary, when your excellence sails without the escort of emotional intelligence and people-wisdom, it becomes your 'Achilles' heel' all the more easily."
"Change is painful, of course—as painful as scraping poison off the bone. But it's the only road to becoming truly strong. While keeping your core competitiveness, you have to forge yourself a suit of 'social armor' that can adapt to a complex environment, and learn those 'people skills' that look 'useless' but are absolutely vital. This isn't a betrayal of your profession. It's building a 'highway' for your outstanding professional ability—one that lets it reach its goal smoothly, and even amplifies its effect. When you can both solve hard problems and make the people who work with you feel as if they're basking in spring breeze, that's when you become irreplaceable. Otherwise, you're just a useful 'tool' that people keep at arm's length—one that could be swapped out at any moment for a more 'useful' or 'cheaper' tool."
"Teacher Yuanyuan, what exactly should I do?" The caller's voice was dry, but she seemed to have caught hold of something.
"First, run a cognitive reformat. Quarantine that 'ability-is-everything' system in your head for now. Tell yourself that over the next three months, 'building trust' and 'fitting into the environment' are more important KPIs than 'completing tasks flawlessly.' Treat your professional ability as the Swiss Army knife in your toolbox, not the assault rifle you're holding up at all times.
Second, launch a 'micro social investment' plan. Force yourself to make one 'useless' social move every day: bring a colleague a coffee (and remember how they like it), say a sincere 'Nice work, that was fast' when someone finishes a small task, ask 'What does everyone think?' first in a group discussion even when you already know the answer. Shift part of your intelligence from solving math problems to solving the problem of 'people.' Observe who's close to whom, what the department's sensitive topics are, what kind of asking-for-help won't irritate people but instead draws you closer.
Third, learn to expose 'harmless weaknesses.' This isn't about genuinely playing weak; it's about strategically showing a bit of 'imperfection.' For example: 'This Excel formula has actually had me stuck for ages—can you take a look?' or 'Ugh, I stayed up late on the report last night and even coffee isn't working today. Got any secret tricks to stay alert?' Let people feel you're a living human who 'also runs into problems and also needs help,' rather than a flawless AI. This dramatically lowers how threatening and distant you seem.
Fourth, redefine your 'enemies' and your 'allies.' Your enemy isn't those 'mediocre' colleagues; it's the environment that keeps you from playing to your strengths and traps you in internal friction. Those colleagues could be potential allies in changing that environment. Try to discover the value they hold beyond professional skill—who has the best-connected grapevine, who's best at smoothing over conflicts, who has the boss's ear. Replace a competitive mindset with a cooperative one. Even if the cooperation is only on the surface.
Fifth, prepare yourself mentally for a long dormancy—even for the possibility of failure. Turning an impression around takes time, possibly a long time. Along the way you may still feel lonely, awkward, like you're 'not yourself.' Remember, this isn't betraying yourself; it's expanding the dimensions of your personality, learning a new language tied to survival and growth. The process will hurt, like bone growing a second time. But once you get through it, you'll gain a tougher, more three-dimensional, more powerful capacity to survive—the ability to both reach the summit in your professional field and move with ease through complex human relationships. That's closer to real strength than any 'excellence' in the academic sense."
At the end, Yao Yuan looked into the camera, her gaze like the deep water of a cold pool—icy, yet reflecting a light that saw through everything: "Sweetheart, the world won't roll out a red carpet for your excellence. More often, it sets up roadblocks for your excellence. Tearing down a roadblock sometimes takes more than a sharper axe—it takes knowing how to go around it, or finding people willing to help you lift it together. Sweetheart, come down from the lone peak. Walk into the open wilderness of the human world. You'll find that true strength isn't being absolutely correct in a vacuum. It's deeply understanding and mastering both human nature and the rules, and still being able to move forward clear-eyed—until, in the end, the world has no choice but to make way for you. What you have to do now is make up this bloody, necessary lesson. This is the class I was forced to start in my second year of senior high, and I'm still taking the advanced course to this day. You should start too. Good luck."
Yao Yuan's last words rang out like the heavy toll of a bell, echoing through the livestream room.
[Call time is up. The feed cuts off amid an atmosphere heavy with the pressure of reality.]
The comments didn't flood in right away. There was a brief, wide-open blank, as if countless viewers in front of their screens had fallen into a silent storm of thought, digesting this cruel and clear-eyed 'guide to surviving the jungle.' Then, slowly, long comments full of admiration and self-reflection began to scroll like a tide.
Yao Yuan didn't move. She still held that posture, both hands propped against her chin, gazing out the window at the leaden-gray sky and the eternally turbid Yellow River below. The youthful past about dance, about lonely arrogance, about having her bubble torn open by force by reality—the actual blood and tears behind it had long since been dried by time. But the lesson it had hardened into still carried a biting chill in this moment, even as it had been deeply fused into the underlying logic by which she saw the world and answered it.
Excellence is talent; it's effort. But how to make that excellence survive, grow, and even reign in a world that is not always kind—a world often full of envy and calculation—that is a far longer and far crueler campaign. It takes wisdom, it takes resilience, and above all it takes a bone-deep understanding of, and reverence for, the gray zones of human nature. It was precisely that one 'system reinstall' that laid the foundation for her later ability to dismantle emotional tangles and see through the contests of human nature with such a cool, penetrating eye.
She slowly let out a breath, leaving a small, fleeting patch of fog on the cold glass.
"Next." Her voice was steady and professional again, every flicker of emotion reined back in. The operating table was still brightly lit, waiting for the next case to be dissected—a case about growth, and about survival.

Chapter 90 — In the Eyes of the World: What Marriage Gives a Woman
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640337.html

In the Eyes of the World: What Marriage Gives a Woman
On the other side of the city, Shuai Hongqiang had not known a peaceful moment since the day at the Antiquities Hall, when he met Old Chen, Yao Yuan, and that Mr. Wang Baoguo—a man as lean and serene as an old scholar—and heard the whole story laid bare: the origins of the Mirror Pact, its brutal truth, and the slim hope of escape. Ever since, his chest had felt as if a great stone, soaked through with ice water, were lodged there—heavy, cold, never letting him rest. The more his business prospered in a way that bordered on the uncanny—project approvals waved through one green light after another, payments arriving with unheard-of punctuality, even his stray investments seeming to enjoy the special favor of some god of luck—the more that hidden dread spread and grew inside him. He felt like a thief who had blundered into a vault and made off with a staggering fortune, trembling as he enjoyed everything that came so easily—delicacies, attendants at his beck and call, a life that looked dazzling from the outside. But every compliment, every sum that landed in his account, tolled a silent alarm in his ear: this was stolen, bought by overdrawing someone else's peace—maybe even their very life. He waited, every hour, not knowing when the vault door would burst open, when the owner of all he had taken would appear before him, cold-eyed, not only to claim it all back but to cast him—the greedy little thief—into a bottomless pit of karmic ruin, and worse still... to drag down the family he treasured most.
That fear reached its peak once he learned the possible forms the "backlash" might take. Health, children, family ties... the vague yet crushing "price" Wang Baoguo had spoken of hung over his head like the sword of Damocles, so that when he looked at his wife and son, his gaze took on an unspeakable layer of dread and tenderness.
That day, he wrapped up all his business early, begged off every social engagement, and drove home. Passing a hotel he often visited, he pulled over and went to the back kitchen himself to pack up several of the hearty dishes his wife Lin Wan and their son Shuai Wenyao loved, then stopped at a supermarket to pick out fresh fruit. Back home, he did something rare—he tied on an apron and, clumsily but with unusual care, plated the takeout dishes, washed the fruit, even lit a scented candle on the table for atmosphere. Warm amber light, fine porcelain, the air filled with the smell of food and a faint citrus fragrance—he had deliberately staged everything into a near-perfect, cozy family scene. Beneath that perfection lay a panicked urgency: a need to seize something, to make up for something—or, you might say, to perform some futile, premature rite of mourning for a "loss" that might be coming.
The instant he set down the last pair of chopsticks, the lock turned, and Lin Wan came in, leading their son Shuai Wenyao by the hand. The timing was so neat it seemed calculated.
"Daddy!" Shuai Wenyao came charging over like a little cannonball and wrapped his arms around Shuai Hongqiang's leg. He bent down and lifted his son high, pressing his cheek against the boy's soft, warm hair. That living, breathing scent—milk and sweat mixed together—made his eyes sting for no reason he could name, and his arms tightened, as if he wanted to carve this warm weight into his very bones. "How come you're home so early today? And you made dinner?" Lin Wan set down her bag and glanced, a little surprised, at the table laid out with such obvious care, her gaze lingering a moment on Shuai Hongqiang's face. She caught at once the unusual look in her husband's eyes—a tangle of exhaustion, tenderness, and a flicker of alarm she couldn't read—but she didn't press. She only took off her coat and let a gentle smile show. "Wenyao, go wash your hands. Daddy made all this good food."
Dinner went on in an atmosphere of surface harmony. Shuai Hongqiang was almost too attentive, constantly placing food in Lin Wan's and his son's bowls, asking the boy about funny things at school, laughing along when Lin Wan mentioned an amusing comment from one of her livestream fans. He ate very little, but his eyes seemed glued to his wife and son. He watched Lin Wan carefully pick the bones out of the fish for their son, watched the boy's animated little face as he chattered away, watched this scene of "home" under the lamplight—so familiar it could not be more familiar, yet at this moment making his heart tremble. Every bit of warmth was like a sweet needle pricking at his guilt- and fear-filled heart. He drank in this ordinary warmth greedily, and yet, in the deepest part of himself, he dreaded it—would all of this before his eyes, this seemingly solid harbor, one day be wrecked and stripped away by the tide of "backlash," all because of the "cause" he had planted in a single reckless moment years ago? He could almost picture it: the "retribution" landing on his son, or the family torn apart... The thought had barely risen before it chilled him to the bone, and the hand holding his chopsticks gave an almost imperceptible tremble.
Lin Wan took in every bit of her husband's strangeness. His over-forceful thoughtfulness, the flashes of emptiness and panic in his eyes, and that almost suffocating "tenderness" that seemed to want to wrap her and the child up entirely—all of it drew the wary string in her heart a little tighter. But she didn't do what she would once have done: set down her bowl at once and gently, softly press him—"What's wrong? Is something the matter at the company?" The woman she was now—after the long emotional labor of marriage, after struggling together under financial strain, after the baptism of countless women's stories in her livestream room—had long since built a new, clearer-eyed way of relating. She no longer took it upon herself to absorb and process all his emotions, unless he chose to speak openly. The "emotional value" she gave now came with clear boundaries and a clear trigger. She simply went on eating as usual, answering her son, and when her eyes occasionally met Shuai Hongqiang's, she offered a calm, faintly questioning smile—but she would never be the first to tear the thin veil of "everything is fine" that he was trying to keep up.
Of course she knew of Yao Yuan. Working the same emotional-content lane on the internet, how could Lin Wan not know Yao Yuan and that doctrine of "clear-eyed reckoning"—sharp to the point of coldness—and the enormous controversy that trailed it? At first she had been repelled, even disgusted, by that line of thinking that turned feeling and marriage entirely into "tools" to be "appraised"; she found it too refined, too self-serving, dissolving the warm and beautiful parts of human nature. But over these years, inside this marriage that outsiders called "a good match," she had lived through the whole arc—from being full of longing to watching her illusions shatter, from giving her all to learning to calculate, from craving to be loved to craving even more to be "respected" and to "have a choice." When she looked again at many of Yao Yuan's views, what she felt was utterly different. Though she still couldn't fully agree with some of the more extreme claims, she had to admit that Yao Yuan's naked dissections of the power games, value exchange, and emotional costs in relationships between men and women often worked like a keen scalpel, slicing open with precision the truths that ran silently beneath the tender surface of her own marriage. That was "knowledge born of practice"—the part of the rules she had stumbled through and verified for herself, with her own wounds. Only, she had never connected, across time and space, the dazzling, sharp-tongued "Godmother of Feelings," Yao Yuan, on the other side of the screen, with the husband beside her, Shuai Hongqiang, now wholly absorbed in his role as a middle-aged businessman. She knew nothing of that old romance from eight years ago, still less that a strange ancient mirror was binding her husband—the one she saw as enjoying a "second spring" in his career—together with his deeply guarded past and that distant, formidable "ex-girlfriend," lashing them all to a single chain of cause and effect fraught with unknown risk.
To her, Shuai Hongqiang's recent "good fortune" was merely a long-overdue turn of luck, a case of things at their worst finally turning for the better. And their marriage, after they had weathered the harshest financial crisis together, seemed to have entered a new phase: a stable, mutually supportive alliance with a sense of boundaries, reached by two sufficiently mature adults on the basis of a clear understanding of practical interests and a shared love of and responsibility for their child. There was warmth, but not much; there was care, but in measure. It was more like a joint-venture company—equity clearly divided, responsibilities clearly assigned—the two of them jointly running the "family" project, with the goal of steady operation and giving the next generation the best conditions they could. Love might long since have faded, but the bond of "partners" and the tie of a "community of shared interests" kept this ship moving forward across waters that were, on the whole, calm.
After dinner, Shuai Hongqiang practically rushed to clear away the bowls and chopsticks, a near-penitent urgency in his movements. "Go and get ready for your stream. I'll handle this, and keep Wenyao company with a book—I'll give him his bath in a bit," he said to Lin Wan, in a tone of rare thoughtfulness, almost humble.
Lin Wan looked at him and nodded. "All right—thank you, then." She didn't refuse. She turned and walked toward the study, her other world. Shutting the door, she fixed her hair and clothes in front of the mirror and touched up her lipstick. The woman in the mirror had calm eyes—too calm, even. With practiced ease she switched on her equipment, adjusted the lighting, and slipped on, neatly, the mask of "Wanwan"—the gentle host whose every word brimmed with marital wisdom, teaching women how to manage their relationships "clear-eyed." Outside the study, she could faintly hear Shuai Hongqiang's lowered voice telling their son a story, and the sound of running water rinsing the dishes. One door apart, two worlds, two kinds of "clear-eyed wisdom," running in parallel under the same roof—each understood without a word, yet wound by invisible threads of fate around the edge of a larger, stranger riddle, with no one aware whether a storm was brewing somewhere far off.
[Lin Wan's Livestream Room]
Lin Wan didn't sit down right away. She walked to the window and stood still for a moment, gazing out at the deep night, drawing several long, slow breaths. The gentle smile that belonged to a wife and mother slowly settled away, replaced by something quieter and more focused. Then she turned and walked toward that streaming corner bathed in soft light.
She sat down before the camera. The lighting, carefully calibrated, was bright without glare, framing perfectly her exceptionally approachable face—relaxed brows and eyes, a straight nose, a softly shaped mouth—the warm, gentle features that traditional taste called "virtuous and kind," carrying, even unsmiling, a faint reassuring warmth. Tonight she wore a finely textured cream cashmere turtleneck that set off her jade-smooth complexion, her crisp short hair neatly combed, lending her a long, slender neck and an air of cool, capable reason. She wore no jewelry. Her clothes, like the survival strategy she had chosen, were modern minimalism—stripped of anything superfluous, conveying an undeniable weight and sense of order through restraint. Behind her, a lush umbrella tree glowed green, and a diffuser sent up curling white mist with the calm scent of cedar. The whole frame was warm, intelligent, trustworthy—the "safe house" and "platform for her voice" that she had spent years, across countless nights, painstakingly building for "the host Wanwan."
She adjusted the microphone, looked into the camera, her gaze clear and serene, and let a small, relaxing smile spread at the corners of her mouth—a smile with a settling, steadying power.
"Good evening, and welcome to 'A Gentle Crossing to Waking.' I'm Lin Wan."
"Before the stream, I saw one of you—a sister in our fan group—share a conversation: the 'teachings' an elder of hers, whom we'll just call 'Uncle Liu,' gave her. I found it very telling, and with her permission, I'd like to bring it out and talk it over with all of you."
She picked up a tablet, her eyes settling on the screen, her voice steady and objective as she recounted it, like a scholar quoting material from fieldwork:
"Here's what 'Uncle Liu' said to my sister here: 'A woman has to marry. If a woman doesn't marry, she'll be toyed with by lots of men—how pitiful is that. A woman should trust a man's love, shouldn't fight for power or status, shouldn't work herself to the bone. If your husband truly loves you, then just stay home and enjoy the good life. A man's love—that's marrying you and bringing you home. For a man to marry one woman his whole life, that's righteousness and virtue.'"
Lin Wan set down the tablet, raised her eyes, and looked clear-eyed into the camera:
"My sister here asked two questions on the spot, and 'Uncle Liu's' answers are very much worth hearing together."
She leaned forward slightly, as if back at the scene of the conversation, recreating the exchange in a calm tone:
"My sister asked: So how is a man's love shown?"
"'Uncle Liu' answered quickly, with great certainty: 'By marrying you—this woman—by marrying you, by having children with you. The women out there are just for play; only you are the one he marries. Marriage should last a lifetime.'"
The scrolling comments began to react:
[This is so textbook...]
[“The ones out there are just for play”—that's genuinely grating to hear.]
"My sister then asked: So what does a woman get out of marrying?"
Lin Wan paused, imitating the slightly irritated tone of someone stumped by the question:
"'Uncle Liu' was taken aback for a second, then almost ground out through his teeth: 'Marriage! Marriage is the good thing! The lifelong good thing a man gives a woman is marriage! Otherwise, if you don't hurry up and marry and just stay single, how dangerous is that—there'll be lots of men eyeing you, you'll get toyed with.'"
Here her tone took on a note of calm interrogation:
"My sister countered: So you don't get toyed with once you're married?"
"Uncle Liu shot back at once, but his logic developed a strange fold: 'No! Once a woman is married, she only lets one person play with her.'"
At this, Lin Wan shook her head at the camera—very slightly, but very clearly—and there was no anger in her expression, only the understanding of someone who has seen through an absurdity:
"So my sister said: Then you're saying a woman is a 'toy.'"
"As if his tail had been stepped on, Uncle Liu hurriedly hauled out an even bigger signboard: 'For us men to be able to marry one woman is the greatest righteousness and virtue a man can have!'"
The comments surged:
[“Only lets one person play with her”? What kind of thing is that to say!]
[“Righteousness and virtue” has become a fig leaf...]
[This logic is so suffocating.]
Lin Wan didn't look at the comments. She was fully immersed in tracing the logic of the conversation, and threw out the final, key question:
"My sister pressed him: So what good is a man getting out of marrying? Is it so that he himself 'won't be toyed with by a crowd of women,' and only gets played with by one woman?"
At this moment, a flicker of something captured crossed Lin Wan's eyes:
"This 'Uncle Liu' put on a particularly contemptuous look and said, with absolute conviction: 'Of course not—it's for the warmth of a family.'"
[The retold conversation ends; for a few seconds, the livestream room falls into a heavy silence.]
Lin Wan picked up her teacup and took a small sip. She needed this brief pause—to let those naked words settle in the air, and to give the sisters in front of their screens time to digest the unvarnished shock of those ideas, hitting them full in the face.
Then she set the cup down, her gaze turning clear and focused again—the clarity of someone stepping back from a specific case and returning to the role of analyst.
"This conversation is like an unpolished 'specimen of ideas,'" she said slowly, her voice calm and clear. "It wears no warm disguise. It presents, almost plainly, a 'life script' about marriage and gender that is still running in many places to this day. Let's set aside judgments of right and wrong for now, and first look—in terms we can all understand—at how this 'script' is written."
"Act One is called 'Opening with Fear,'" Lin Wan analyzed in a storytelling tone. "It casts the unmarried woman as a 'weakling' stranded in a jungle, danger on every side, whose only road to survival is to find a male 'protector' and obtain 'safety' through marriage. From the very start, this script denies the possibility that a woman, as an independent individual, can possess dignity, safety, and the right to arrange her own life. Your 'safety' must be redeemed through 'being owned.'"
"Act Two is 'The Hollow Promise,'" she went on dissecting. "When pressed on how 'love' is shown and what the 'benefit' is, this script can't offer anything concrete or redeemable—mutual respect, shared growth, sharing the risks—it can only point, over and over, at the ritual of 'getting married' itself. Which means, in this logic, 'love' and 'benefit' are ultimately reduced to a change in 'ownership status'—from 'unowned' to 'owned.' As for the quality of life after being 'owned,' the inner feelings—the script doesn't write that, or rather, it deems it unimportant."
Lin Wan's gaze grew sharp, going straight to the core:
"Act Three is the leak of the essence and its swift repackaging. 'Only lets one person play with her'—that line accidentally shows the script's hand. Here, the woman is reduced to an object with a 'use function.' Marriage is permissions management, going from 'possibly used by many' to 'fixed, used by one.' When this cold underlying logic is exposed, the script immediately wheels out the gorgeous stage set of 'righteousness and virtue.' It pulls off a clever piece of magic: it stages a relationship based on control and possession as the man's noble 'moral achievement' and 'self-sacrifice' (he marries only you)."
"Finally, when asked about the man's own motive," Lin Wan's tone carried a deep insight, "the script can no longer use the lines of 'use' or 'morality.' It can only switch to an unassailable channel—'the warmth of a family.' That phrase is too beautiful to argue against. But we need to think: this blueprint of 'warmth'—who drew it? Who lights the hearth fire? Who adds the firewood? And who senses and defines the temperature? When the very conception of 'warmth' rests, by default, on one party being expected to provide the bulk of the 'service functions'—childbearing, caregiving, emotional comfort—does the individual inside it really always feel 'warmth'?"
The comments had been guided into deeper reflection:
[Once Sister Wanwan breaks it down like this, I understand it all!]
[So underneath that “it's for your own good” lies this exact chain of logic.]
[“A change in ownership status”—that's put so precisely, that's exactly the feeling!]
"I'm not sharing this less-than-comfortable conversation to stir up conflict or manufacture anxiety," Lin Wan's tone eased, taking on a constructive strength again. "Quite the opposite—I believe that clearly seeing the raw source code of certain set 'scripts' is the first step to gaining the ability to write our own life 'screenplay.' When we can tell which words are implanting 'fear,' which promises are actually 'hollow,' and which grand terms are masking a core of 'objectification,' only then can we stay clear-headed inside and keep the initiative in our choices when faced with all kinds of voices."
She thought of her own years of feeling her way through marriage with Shuai Hongqiang, thought of the countless sisters' stories from her livestream room, and a weightier note of understanding entered her voice:
"Seeing clearly some of the cold realities of the structure doesn't mean our only options are rage or escape. On the contrary, it reminds us that, in the present social environment, women all the more need to cultivate a kind of 'clear-eyed wisdom' and 'pragmatic resilience.'"
"This wisdom lies in this: of course we can enter marriage, can pour ourselves deeply into our feelings—but we need to be clear that what we're entering is an 'intimate relationship' that requires building together, not a search for a 'savior' or a 'lifelong employer.'" She used a more accessible analogy: "A relationship is more like jointly running a 'family company' with unlimited liability than signing a one-way, long-term 'labor or gift contract.'"
"The premise of a partnership is that both sides have sustainable 'input' and a clear 'consensus,'" Lin Wan's voice was steady and strong. "Our 'input' is never merely time, childbearing, and housework—it's our emotional intelligence, our management ability, our capacity to learn and adapt, our social resources. And our 'consensus' must never be merely 'gaining a legitimate title,' but building a partnership of mutual respect and shared decision-making—ensuring both parties have room to grow within the relationship, and the right to be informed, to participate, and to fair safeguards over shared resources."
"'Uncle Liu' warned that a woman who fights for power will pay a terrible price. I'll admit it: any attempt to change the established rules of the game, to win more autonomy, will meet resistance," Lin Wan's gaze was gentle and firm. "But there's another price—handing over the controls of your own life, actively or passively, placing the key to your happiness entirely in someone else's hands. From then on, when the road jolts or the direction is lost, you may lose the ability to adjust—you may even forget where you wanted to go in the first place. The first is the challenge of expanding the map of your life; the second is the silent fall of your own territory."
"So, as I see it," she concluded, her voice like still water running deep, calm yet holding power, "true growth is learning to keep cultivating your own inner 'operating principles' even within the established social 'system.' We can be loving partners, and at the same time be clear-eyed observers and strategic builders. Don't treat marriage as life's only answer and its entire wager—treat it as an important, profound journey that takes wisdom to navigate."
"On this journey, we don't only learn how to love and how to give—we also learn how to protect the independence of our own spirit, how to build up the capacity to face change, how to let the roots of our own life sink down deep even in the soil of a relationship, draw nourishment, and grow upward—rather than being merely a clinging vine."
"When we no longer only crave 'being loved' to define our entire worth, but crave to grow, within love, into a more complete and more powerful version of ourselves; when we no longer fear that 'asserting' our rights will destroy the relationship, but understand that a healthy relationship inherently includes the sharing and balancing of rights—then perhaps, amid the complicated realities between men and women, we can find that path where we can pour ourselves in with deep feeling and still not lose ourselves."
[The stream ends; soothing music sets off a warm, strength-filled afterglow.]
Lin Wan took off her headset and rubbed her slightly aching neck. The full, guiding sense of power she felt during the stream slowly receded, and fatigue quietly surfaced. But she felt settled inside. In her own way, she had turned a sharp conversation into a tool of thought—one countless ordinary women could use to examine and reflect on their own situations. Rational yet warm, clear-eyed yet full of hope.
It was quiet outside the study. Shuai Wenyao had probably fallen asleep. She put away her equipment and gently pushed the door open.
In the living room, only a single floor lamp was lit. Shuai Hongqiang sat in the dim halo of light, seemingly lost in thought, turning his head only when he heard the sound. His eyes were complicated, a little blurred in the lamplight—weariness, concern, and what seemed a deeper stirring she couldn't read. He opened his mouth as if to say something, but in the end only pulled out a gentle smile: "Done streaming? You must be tired. I warmed you a cup of milk."
"Thank you." Lin Wan took the warm milk, its soothing heat passing into her fingertips. She looked at Shuai Hongqiang—this man she had shared a bed with for years, who at this moment felt somewhat unfamiliar to her. He didn't know that, just now, behind that door, she had calmly, rationally, all but dissected a certain truth of marriages like theirs. And he sat outside the door, churning inside with another story—of fate, debt, and fear—of which she knew nothing at all.
They stood under the same lamp, bound by the same marriage, yet living inside two utterly different, subtly intertwined narratives. One story was about clear-eyed cooperation within social structures; the other, about the karmic backlash of an ancient pact. The only point where they met was, perhaps, this cup of warm milk right now—and this calm surface called "home" that they ran together and strove so hard to keep up.
The night was still long. The Yellow River flowed on in silence in the distance, carrying away the silt, and covering secrets too. And life went on forward, in this complex balance of each being clear-eyed, and each keeping things from the other.
