Chapter 91 — A Romantic Wedding That Happened to Fall on Christmas
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640338.html

A Romantic Wedding That Happened to Fall on Christmas
Amid the fullness of busy work and the suspense of unresolved waiting, time slid toward the end of the year. Winter in Jinshi was bitterly cold, but the Christmas mood had long since seeped through shop windows, street lights, and the faint anticipation on people's faces, driving off some of the chill and draping the city in a warm, tender filter.
December twenty-fourth, Christmas Eve, was also the day Liu Qingzhuo and Chen Ye were to be married.
Months earlier, Liu Qingzhuo had come to Yao Yuan's company specifically to spend hours in detailed consultation over a prenuptial agreement. Yao Yuan had assigned the sharpest team from the company's legal department. From the real estate, equity, and investment funds in Liu Qingzhuo's name, to the complex asset structures that the Chen family business might involve, all the way down to the final ownership and form of conversion of the bride-price and dowry — every detail, however small, was weighed line by line. The agreement's core goal was clear and cool-headed: within the bounds of the legal framework and social convention, to maximize the security of Liu Qingzhuo's personal property and her future economic autonomy within the marriage. Chen Ye proved unusually generous and at ease about all of it, accepting almost the entire thing wholesale. Behind that readiness lay the confidence his family's strength afforded him, and a clear-eyed recognition of the true nature of this marriage of two powerful houses. In the end, driven jointly by both families, the wedding date was set for this festive Christmas Eve.
The wedding was held in the banquet hall of Jinshi's most exclusive hotel. Crystal chandeliers poured down like a river of stars, and tens of thousands of air-freighted Ecuadorian roses interwove with white-and-green Christmas decorations to create a setting both lavish and dreamily romantic. Guests gathered in a glittering crowd of silk and perfume; more than half of the prominent figures from Jinshi's political and business circles had come, proof enough of the roots and connections of the Liu and Chen families. It was a celebration blessed by all sides — and one that tacitly acknowledged its essence as an alliance of equals.
Yao Yuan attended together with Zhao Yiming. She had chosen a pearl-white velvet strapless gown, minimally cut yet perfectly tracing the lines of her figure, over which she wore a heavy black cashmere coat. Her long hair was loosely pinned up, and at her throat she wore only a slender diamond necklace, with star-blue sapphire studs to match. She was understated, refined wealth from head to toe — complementing the bride's dazzling radiance, yet carrying a presence impossible to ignore. Zhao Yiming wore a classic black tuxedo. He stood tall, the cold technical edge about him softened by the close-fitting cut, lending him a rare touch of formality and reserve. When the two of them appeared hand in hand, they made a strikingly matched pair and drew many eyes.
Her old friend Jin Lan had come too, with that gym-owner boyfriend, Lu Ang, at her side. Lu Ang was young and handsome, his physique impeccably maintained, and standing beside the brilliant, fully commanding Jin Lan, he looked like a pretty vase carefully kept on display. Jin Lan wore a wine-red velvet gown with a deep V, her hair in curls and her lips red, exuding charm in every gesture, completely unguarded in her intimacy with Lu Ang. Yao Yuan also spotted Gao Jieyuan; as Chen Ye's childhood friend, he was naturally on the guest list, accompanied by a girl who looked no more than twenty-one or twenty-two — exquisitely made up and clinging sweetly to him — his new girlfriend. When Gao Jieyuan caught sight of Yao Yuan, his gaze flickered, and then, even more deliberately, he wrapped an arm around his little girlfriend's waist and bent to whisper in her ear, his posture intimate, as if silently announcing something. Yao Yuan simply pretended not to notice and nodded at him with a smile.
Wang Qian, too, was among the invited. As a banker, he was smooth and well-versed in every social grace; his bearing was impeccable, and his smile when he greeted Yao Yuan was the same as ever — the familiar courtesy between old friends. At this point, Yao Yuan still knew him only as a banker friend who had given her no small measure of support and convenience in both work and life. She had no idea at all that he was also the son of old Mr. Wang Baoguo — the very man who had revealed to her the truth of the Mirror Pact and set the appointment by the Yellow River. Wang Qian kept that secret sealed tight. And so, beneath his customary friendliness, the look he gave Yao Yuan hid a complicated scrutiny and quiet worry known only to himself — he knew what had happened to her, yet he said nothing, only exchanging pleasantries as usual.
The wedding ceremony was warm and grand. As Liu Qingzhuo, wearing a crown set with the family's heirloom jadeite and diamonds and a haute-couture gown worth a fortune, walked toward Chen Ye on her father's arm, Yao Yuan watched quietly. She saw the longing for the life ahead in Liu Qingzhuo's eyes, and she saw the practiced tenderness and certainty on Chen Ye's face. This was a union blessed by all, thoroughly calculated, well-matched in standing — love perhaps present, but by no means the only or whole foundation. There was no ripple in Yao Yuan's heart, only the calm of witnessing a "project successfully delivered."
When it came time to throw the bouquet, a crowd of unmarried women stepped forward, laughing. Yao Yuan had been standing somewhat to the back, but Jin Lan laughingly pushed her into the fray. Whether by coincidence or by the bride's design, that bouquet of lily of the valley — symbol of "happiness passed on" — traced a graceful arc through the air and fell, neither too far nor too near, straight into Yao Yuan's arms.
The soft lily petals carried a crisp, cool fragrance. Almost caught off guard, with a flicker of dazed surprise, Yao Yuan instinctively clutched the bouquet. Then her gaze cut across the silk, perfume, and glittering lights and found, instantly and precisely, Zhao Yiming.
He stood there. Unlike the other male guests, he neither cheered nor reached for a camera; he only watched her quietly, his eyes deep behind his lenses, flowing under the banquet hall's brilliant lights with an indescribable, gentle, intent glow. There was no teasing in that gaze, no urging — only a still, patient waiting, and a barely perceptible, restrained anticipation that belonged to him.
Rational as he was, Zhao Yiming naturally understood the social symbolism of the "bouquet." The concept of "marriage" that the symbol pointed to had, because of Yao Yuan's existence, gone from a vague sociological model to a concrete, even worthwhile prospect that could be assessed. Through his mind flashed the precisely running "Light-Traced" tree they had created together in the "Mirror Room," her tightly reasoned yet deeply empathetic speech at the launch, and the undying spark of curiosity about the world and human nature in her eyes after countless late-night discussions. Building a family with her was, to him, the optimal life-partnership plan that reason had deduced — and a clear choice of his own inclination as well.
He needed her, admired her, trusted her, and longed to make this state of standing side by side lasting and formalized. Seeing the bouquet that symbolized "passing on" fall into her hands, what rose in his heart was a certainty mixed with "this is only right," and a trace of anticipation, waiting for his partner's final confirmation. His reaction was not excitement, but a deeper consideration and preparation.
Yao Yuan's heart, in that moment, gave a very light flutter.
It was not pure joy or shyness, but something complicated, a current stirring beneath the ice. Reason told her that marriage was only a piece of paper, a way of binding a social relationship, perhaps even a high-risk investment. She had seen too much scheming, betrayal, and attrition; she herself had once rebuilt a fortress of reason on the ruins of feeling. One of the underlying logics of the "Pretty Big One" AI model she had built for herself and for countless women was precisely to help people assess relationships clearly and avoid blind investment. Marriage, emotionally speaking, had come to seem to her an option she could take or leave.
But in the instant the bouquet landed in her arms, in the split second her eyes met Zhao Yiming's, some corner of her heart that reason had sealed away tight seemed to crack open a faint, fine fissure. A weak but real longing — one that belonged to "feeling" — quietly poked its head out.
That longing was not for the wedding ceremony itself, but a hidden yearning for a kind of "certainty" and of being "firmly chosen." It was knowing full well that the world was chaos, human nature complex, the road ahead unknowable — and still longing for someone who could see, through all the carefully built armor of reason and survival strategy, the soul that might still feel uneasy, still be fragile, still hope to be properly cherished, and who would be willing to give a clear, solemn, calculation-free promise. To be chosen not because she was "useful" or "suitable," but simply because she was "her" — chosen without hesitation, and with a willingness to face all the uncertainty of the future together.
That longing surprised even her, and a touch of self-mockery rose right after. Was she really regressing the longer she lived? She quickly composed herself and, from across the room, openly lifted the bouquet in her hand toward Zhao Yiming, her lips curving into a flawless smile poised somewhere between a joke and an easy candor. Zhao Yiming smiled faintly too and gave her a light nod. Everything understood without a word, and yet as if nothing had been said at all.
The wedding banquet began. Yao Yuan, Zhao Yiming, Jin Lan and Lu Ang, Wang Qian, Gao Jieyuan and his little girlfriend, along with several close friends, were seated at the same table.
The table was lively in the extreme. Lu Ang was young, and a few drinks in, he couldn't help draping an arm over Jin Lan's shoulder and asking, eyes bright: "Sis Lan, look how grand this wedding is! When are we going to do one too? When the time comes I'll throw you the most spectacular one!" A doting smile on her face, Jin Lan tapped his forehead with a finger and spoke as if coaxing a child: "All right, all right — when you're a little more mature and you've grown the gym into a nationwide chain, we'll do it, okay?" The words humored him, but in her eyes lay a clear-eyed indifference. Ten years apart in age, with different experiences and life stages, she enjoyed the passion and companionship of this relationship, but had never truly factored "marriage" into any future plan with Lu Ang. To her, this was an adults' game — each taking what they needed, enjoying the moment. Talk of marriage would only weigh it down.
On the other side, Gao Jieyuan all but wrote the word "deliberate" across his face. He attentively peeled shrimp for his little girlfriend, served her dishes, asked in low tones whether the food was to her taste, and gazed at her tenderly now and then, as if lost in fresh love. But his eyes would always drift, with seeming casualness, toward Yao Yuan, carrying a faint, hard-to-spot edge of rivalry and proof — see, I'm doing just fine without you, loved and loving in return. Yao Yuan only found it amusing, while her own manner stayed perfectly polite and at just the right distance. She talked quietly with Zhao Yiming, occasionally placing a dish he liked into his bowl, her posture naturally intimate — a silent answer to all of Gao Jieyuan's childish theatrics.
Wang Qian was unusually quiet today, simply drinking in silence. Only, the number of times he glanced toward Yao Yuan seemed slightly greater than usual, and his expression more complicated. There was the heaviness of one who knew a secret, worry over a friend's situation, and perhaps a trace of awe at an unknown fate. Several times he raised his glass as if he wanted to say something to Yao Yuan alone, but in the end it dissolved into a deeper silence, and he drained the cup. Yao Yuan sensed that he seemed weighed down tonight, but took it for trouble at work or at home and thought nothing more of it, only returning a caring smile when he looked her way.
The bride and groom came around to toast. By the time they reached this table, the mood grew warmer still. Liu Qingzhuo had changed into her toasting dress, glowing with radiance, with Chen Ye attentively at her side. When she toasted Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming, Liu Qingzhuo teased with a smile: "Boss Yao, Boss Zhao, you've caught the bouquet — so isn't it about time we drank at your wedding? When are you two holding yours? We're all waiting!"
The rest of the table immediately joined the chorus, Jin Lan most of all, laughing the most brilliantly. Gao Jieyuan's smile turned a little stiff. Lu Ang chimed in noisily too: "Boss Zhao, Boss Yao, get on with it!"
Holding his glass, amid the cheering, Zhao Yiming turned to look at Yao Yuan. His gaze seemed especially serious under the lights, with hardly any teasing in it, and he said, clear and steady: "I'm ready anytime. As long as Yao Yuan agrees, we could go register the marriage tomorrow."
The moment the words were out, the cheering swelled louder. Yao Yuan hadn't expected him to put it so directly. However much of a joke it might be, the earnestness in his tone was unmistakable. Her heart skipped a beat, but a perfectly judged blush flew up on her face in an instant (perhaps a touch of it real). She gave Zhao Yiming's arm a light, reproachful pat and laughed to the newlyweds: "Don't listen to his nonsense! In his dreams — where do you find a bargain that cheap? He hasn't even proposed yet, and already he wants to trick someone into registering?" With a charming pout and a joke, she deftly defused this subtle, probing moment, giving Zhao Yiming his face while holding to her own pace. Hearing this, Zhao Yiming only smiled indulgently and let it go, tipping his head back to finish his glass. In that smile lay a knowing tacit understanding, and a hidden mood in no hurry to rush things.
Today was a good day — a close friend's wedding, and Christmas Eve besides. The mood was warm, and naturally the drinks flowed more freely than usual. Yao Yuan was in good spirits and, rarely for her, relaxed, downing a few extra glasses of champagne and red wine. By the time she left the table for the private restroom of the VIP suite, her cheeks already felt hot and her steps a little unsteady, though her mind was still fairly clear.
In the corridor she ran into Wang Qian, who had stepped out for some air.
"President Wang, out here for some peace and quiet too?" Yao Yuan teased with a smile.
Wang Qian looked at her, his gaze lingering a moment on her wine-flushed face, on the verge of speaking but holding back. In the end he only said, in the tone of an old friend with a touch of worry: "You've had quite a lot to drink. Lately… is everything all right?"
Something stirred in Yao Yuan's heart; thinking he meant work, she laughed: "I'm fine — thanks for the concern, old friend. It's just busy at year's end. I've drunk a bit too hard, so I'm off to the restroom to clear my head."
As she turned to go, Wang Qian took two steps after her and called out low: "Yao Yuan…" She turned back, looking at him in puzzlement. Wang Qian gazed at her, his eyes flickering bright and dim under the lights, holding worry and hesitation, until it all dissolved into a barely audible sigh. He shook his head and forced a smile: "It's nothing… drink a little less." Yao Yuan nodded: "Thank you for caring, old friend." She turned and walked away, able to feel that gaze behind her not moving for a long time. Before long, Wang Qian, unable to hold his liquor, was collected early by his wife.
Yao Yuan splashed cold water on her face and looked at herself in the mirror — cheeks flushed, eyes bright and flowing. The woman in the mirror was beautiful and successful, with an enviable career and a seemingly perfect partner. Yet only she herself knew the heavy waiting in the depths of her heart over the Mirror Pact, and the complicated state of mind, woven of reason and feeling, about the ultimate destination of her emotions.
She let out a soft breath of wine-warmed air and prepared to touch up her lipstick.
Just as her fingertips were about to reach the lipstick tube —
The surface of the mirror, without the slightest warning, rippled outward in a ring.
The familiar sensation of her soul peeling away washed over her. Her clear reflection in the mirror began to twist and blur.
"Ah…" She let out an instinctive cry, her hand bracing weakly against the cold edge of the washstand. Everything before her — the restroom's gorgeous lights, the marble countertop, the clamor of the wedding in her ears — rapidly drained of color, stretched out, and was dragged into an endless whirlpool. At last her body slumped soft into the cushioned chair before the vanity mirror.
The eighth time.
Before the last shred of clarity vanished, a thought, cold to the point of near cruelty, flashed through her mind:
"This time, is it going to make me see clearly at which step 'I' once came within a hair of damnation? Or is it going to make me witness whether another choice can truly lead to happiness?"
On the night of a Christmas wedding that symbolized union, promise, and a new beginning — at the very moment reason and feeling had just held a brief, profound dialogue — she was once again flung out of the track of time by that ancient mirror entangled with her fate, plunging toward another time and space, forced to watch one scene after another of past "possibilities."
The thought had no answer. The darkness closed in, gentle and inexorable.

========================================

Chapter 92 — The Eighth Time-Travel: Witnessing the Human Game of Self-Interest
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640339.html

The Eighth Time-Travel: Witnessing the Human Game of Self-Interest
Coming back to consciousness felt like floating slowly up from the bottom of deep water.
Her vision sharpened before her thoughts did. All around her stretched the same boundless, silent, pure white. Yao Yuan blinked quietly, then calmly braced herself and rose to her feet. There was none of the panic of the first times, no urge to investigate—just a quiet standing, like a spectator waiting for the next scene to begin, her gaze steady on some point in the void.
And sure enough, somewhere in the blankness, a ripple spread. Like a drop of water falling into a still lake, the rings widened outward, and at the center an image grew clear and solid, becoming a vivid, living scene with sound—
A small meeting room. Clean, modern decor, sunlight slicing through the blinds into bands of light and shadow across the floor. But the mood was at odds with all that brightness. The air held an invisible string pulled taut, as if the lightest touch would snap it.
On one side of the conference table sat a younger Yao Yuan, in a sharply tailored dark-gray suit, her long hair pulled back not a strand out of place, her makeup immaculate, her face betraying nothing. Only the "rust red" polish at her fingertips tapped, once, lightly and unconsciously, against the document on the table.
Across from her sat Zhuge Jinye. Thirty-three or thirty-four, the rawness gone, replaced by the shrewdness and composure that years of riding the business tides had ground into him—yet a dancer's particular command of his own body still lay hidden in his seemingly relaxed posture.
"Yao Yuan, that's the situation." Zhuge Jinye slid a document across to her, his tone a carefully measured sincerity. "'Flame Soul' has grown big now. Capital has come in, the team has to be fed, suppliers and partners up and down the chain have to be managed. Keeping to the old split just isn't realistic anymore—it isn't good for the company's long-term growth either. We've already had professional legal and financial people evaluate this new equity and dividend plan. The return on your early investment has been given full and proper consideration, I promise you."
The younger Yao Yuan didn't reach for the document right away. Her eyes rested on Zhuge Jinye's face for two seconds, then lowered to the cover. Her gaze was as calm as if she were looking at an ordinary project report.
"Skip the preamble, Zhuge Jinye." She spoke, her voice low but level and clear. "Tell me straight—under the new plan, how much is my equity diluted, and the projected dividends over the next three years, calculated on this new model, how many percent lower than the original agreement."
Zhuge Jinye paused, as if he hadn't expected her to cut so directly to the bone. "The exact figures are all detailed in the document—"
"I can read figures." The younger Yao Yuan cut him off, finally reaching out to pick up the document, though she didn't open it—she only weighed it in her fingertips. "But I need to hear you state the core terms out loud, yourself. That's the basic good faith of a negotiation."
There was no anger in her tone, only a brisk, by-the-book coolness that left no room to maneuver.
Zhuge Jinye pressed his lips together and leaned forward slightly. "Fine. Under the new plan, your equity goes from the original 40% to 10% of founding shares plus a stake in an option pool. The profit dividends over the next three years will be adjusted proportionally as well—but we'll guarantee that the absolute dividend you take home each year won't fall below the average of the past three years. This already fully accounts for your historical contribution—"
"Historical contribution." Yao Yuan repeated the words softly, the corner of her mouth seeming to curve the faintest fraction, a smile that never reached her eyes. "So my investment seven years ago, plus all these years of connecting resources and giving advice—in your current valuation system, it's worth the same as a... wealth-management product with a guaranteed annual yield? And what's more, a product whose yield can be 'adjusted' whenever you like?"
"That's not what I mean—" Zhuge Jinye frowned.
"Then what do you mean?" Yao Yuan lifted her eyes, her gaze like two searchlights turned down low, calmly fixed on him. "Do you mean that back then I bet you'd make it, and I bet right. Now you've made it, and you think the stake I put down when I bet on you takes too big a share of your net worth, and you want to renegotiate the split on the bet?"
Her words were a scalpel, cutting cleanly through all the gentle, sentimental packaging. The air in the meeting room seemed to congeal.
"Yao Yuan, do we really have to put it so... ugly?" Zhuge Jinye's voice dropped, taking on an edge of awkward, stiff embarrassment at having his real thoughts laid bare. "Business has its own rules, and a company goes through different stages. You can't take the 'shared hardship' bond of the startup days and demand a 'shared prosperity' split in the mature stage. It isn't reasonable, and it isn't professional."
"Reasonable. Professional." Yao Yuan nodded, as if she fully endorsed the two words. She finally opened the document in her hands, her gaze sweeping quickly over the dense terms, and began to lay things out at an even pace, as if conducting a project post-mortem:
"First, about the 'bond.' From the moment three years ago when you brought in your first round of institutional funding and signed a valuation-adjustment agreement behind my back, there's been no 'bond' between us anymore—only black-and-white contractual obligation. You and I both know that perfectly well."
"Second, about the 'rules.' The rule is that my forty percent of equity corresponds to my bearing the entire risk of taking the project from zero to one. That risk included losing every cent of my capital, and it included the chance that you, personally, might fail. Now the risk period is over and the return period has arrived, and you're using 'the company's growth needs' to rewrite the rules. In essence, that's a unilateral revision of the correspondence between risk and return. That isn't a business rule. That's a breach of contract."
"Third—" She snapped the document shut with a light crack and locked her gaze back onto Zhuge Jinye. "About this new plan of yours, drawn up after 'full consideration.' I've read it. My answer is: I do not accept any form of equity dilution. The dividends for the next three years must be carried out strictly according to the original agreement. As for three years from now—if the company really can reach the scale you've laid out in your PowerPoint, we can sit down, and based on the fair valuation at that time, work out an exit or adjustment plan we can both accept."
With that she leaned back slightly, her gaze settling into stillness as she waited. It was not sulking, but a clear, unshakable declaration of where the line lay.
Zhuge Jinye's face went fully cold, the earlier sincerity gone, leaving only the hard, businessman's calculus. "And if I told you this is the board's final resolution—no room for discussion?"
Even that faint trace of a curve vanished from Yao Yuan's face. She looked at him for a few seconds, then gave a nod so slight it was almost imperceptible, like the confirmation of an answer she'd already expected finally landing.
"I understand what you mean." She stood, her movements crisp, and began gathering the notebook and pen she'd spread out in front of her, sliding the document gently back to the center of the table. "My position and its basis rest on our original investment agreement, the various capital-increase agreements over the years, and the supplementary memorandum to that valuation-adjustment clause you signed three years ago and never disclosed to me. My lawyer will compile all of it into a formal letter and send it to you and the 'Flame Soul' board."
She picked up her clutch, her gaze passing one last calm time over Zhuge Jinye's face, now full of shock and fury.
"Oh, and—" At the door, as if suddenly remembering something, she half-turned. "A reminder: that valuation-adjustment bet you signed three years ago has a personal joint-liability clause. If, before the next funding round, 'Flame Soul' becomes embroiled in litigation involving a core founder in an equity dispute, the clause is triggered, and the amount you'd have to make up personally is, as I recall... a rather considerable figure. Good luck, President Zhuge."
The door was pulled softly shut. In the corridor, the younger Yao Yuan walked on her heels at a steady pace, her rhythm clear, heading toward the restroom. Her back was held very straight, and there was no anger on her face—only the calm that comes after exhaustion, and deep in her eyes, an ice-cold understanding.
In the empty meeting room, Zhuge Jinye stood where he was, facing the bright sunlight outside the window, saying nothing for a long while, his shoulders sagging slightly. There was guilt in that figure, and struggle—but more than that, a hard resolve, the kind a businessman settles on after weighing the costs and gains.
The younger Yao Yuan walked into the empty restroom and locked the door behind her. Leaning back against the cold panel of the door, she finally let out the breath she'd been holding. A crack appeared in the calm mask on her face, and beneath her eyes churned the anger she'd forced down. She went to the sink, turned on the tap, cupped the cold water, and splashed it hard against her face.
Droplets dripped from her jaw. She lifted her head and looked at herself in the mirror. The makeup was still flawless, but her eyes were rimmed faintly red—not the red of having cried, but the mark left by blood rushing under fierce emotion. The woman in the mirror had a sharp gaze, yet it hid a trace of something almost undetectable: bewilderment—the bewilderment of confirming, once again, the bottom line of human nature.
And in that moment, the smooth surface of the mirror rippled silently into a familiar pattern.
Another version of her own face slowly surfaced at the center of the ripples. Only the eyes were stiller—still as a deep pool, as if every raging wave had long since sunk to the very bottom. It was her own future self, who had appeared so many times before.
Two "Yao Yuans," separated by a single pane of mirror, gazed quietly at each other.
No gasp, no fear. This was the eighth time. Even a surreal meeting like this carried an absurd sense of the everyday. The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror even thought, with a touch of self-mockery: look at that—humanity's capacity to adapt really is terrifyingly strong. Even meeting your "future self" can become something you simply get used to.
This time it was the Yao Yuan before the mirror who spoke first. Her voice had already calmed, carrying a weariness and a clarity that went down to the bone. "You saw all of it, didn't you?"
She used a statement, not a question.
"Seven years ago, after I left Hangzhou and broke up with Zhuge Jinye, I listened to your 'advice.' I took most of my savings and invested in the dance troupe he'd just put together." She twitched the corner of her mouth into a bitter smile. "Look how ironic it is. Seven years, and his 'Flame Soul' really did make it. A national sensation, tickets impossible to get. And the first thing he did after his success was try to kick me out—me, the earliest investor, the one who staked everything."
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror nodded gently. There was a complicated emotion in her gaze, like understanding, like pity, and yet also like a pure, distant watching.
"The human heart," the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan said, twitching her mouth into a smile uglier than tears, "in the face of large enough interests, is it always this fragile? Seven years ago I bet on him as a person, on his talent and his character. I won—the troupe took off. And what my winning won me was sitting here, counting out every fraction of equity with him, tearing off all pretense and haggling over money." Her voice sank, heavy with self-mockery and disappointment. "All those beautiful things we went through together, the grand words we said... in the face of self-interest, they weigh as little as a joke."
In the mirror, the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan listened quietly. Only when the other had finished did she speak, slowly, her voice coming through the mirror, steady, yet carrying a power that smoothed down the rough edges: "It isn't that the human heart is fragile. It's that human nature has always been this way. To chase gain and dodge harm, to calculate loss and profit—it's written into the genes. Emotion, morality, promises—those are the coat civilized society drapes over it. When the seas are calm, the coat looks fine; when the storm comes, the coat is usually the first thing to be torn off."
She paused, her gaze clear as she looked at the version of herself outside the mirror, eyes rimmed red with anger.
"What you need to do now isn't to grieve that the coat has torn. It's to accept that it's already torn, and then use something sturdier to protect what's rightfully yours. You've been in the investment world all these years and seen no small number of cases—you shouldn't be fully grasping this only now."
The words were like a basin of ice water, dousing the last flicker of false heat in the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan's heart. She drew a deep breath, then let it out slowly, and the bewilderment and pain in her eyes were replaced by a burn-the-boats kind of clarity. "I understand. The original agreement may not have had a formal contract, but there's a trail to follow. What's mine, not a cent less. I'll get back what I'm owed in the most professional way there is."
She looked at the "future self" in the mirror, and suddenly asked, "Are you... all right now? How far have things gone with Zhao Yiming?"
She paused, her tone taking on a small, "past" sort of pride and the urge to share: "The all-purpose foundational AI model I developed together with my Zhao Yiming here has already gone live, and the market response is even better than we expected. The next step is deep optimization and iteration of the core features—we'll be rolling out paid services soon. This path, we chose right."
In the mirror, a faint, genuine smile surfaced on the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan's face, a smile that held admiration, and relief, and a distant resonance. "You've done very well," she affirmed, her voice softening. "This path is the right one. As for Zhao Yiming and me, our relationship is very stable—————"
She didn't get to finish.
Just as before, with no warning, the image in the mirror flickered and warped violently, like a faulty electric signal, and then, with a crack, it dispersed into the air, taking the unfinished answer with it. The restroom fell back into dead silence. Only the thirty-six-year-old Yao Yuan was left, facing alone that version of herself in the mirror—eyes rimmed faintly red, gaze ice-cold.
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan opened her eyes very, very slowly.
Her vision went from blurred to clear, and the first thing to meet her eyes was the ornate diamond-patterned ceiling of the VIP room's restroom, warm yellow light spilling softly down. She was still slumped in the soft chair before the mirror, her posture no different from when she'd "left," as if she'd only closed her eyes to rest for a moment.
She lifted her wrist and glanced at the hands of her watch. From leaving the table to come to the restroom, to coming back to consciousness now, only about ten minutes had passed. Time, in the gap where she was dragged forcibly into the "past" and then sent back, seemed to have been compressed, stolen, and then returned with precision. She had a vague sense that each "stay" in the past was growing shorter—whether because the rising number of crossings had bred some tolerance between her body and that uncanny mechanism, or because the "heart-guarding" jade she wore against her skin was truly, quietly working some effect she couldn't comprehend.
She didn't get up right away, holding instead that slightly reclined posture, sitting before the mirror. The clamor of the banquet outside the restroom was shut out by the heavy door, leaving only a faint, muffled swell of sound, like the background hum of another world.
In her mind, everything she'd just "witnessed" replayed clearly. Zhuge Jinye's calm, ruthless face; her own thirty-six-year-old self with her angry, wounded eyes; all of it about investment, returns, human nature, and betrayal...
Self-interest.
The word fell like a cold stone into the lake of her heart—stirring not a raging wave, but ring after ring of a heavy, spreading ripple, a ripple about the word "destined."
She had thought that by crossing through time and warning her past self to steer clear of the "Godmother of Feelings" path—glamorous on the surface but standing forever in the crosshairs of public opinion—she could give "Yao Yuan" a life with fewer hardships and more smooth sailing. But what was the result?
At thirty-six, she had not dissected human hearts in a livestream studio for traffic and controversy—instead she had walked into an even more brutal, more real arena of business rivalry. She had run into the abandonment of a business partner and the naked scramble over interests.
Every wall life had to be hit, she'd hit—not one less. It was only that the wall she struck was not the same wall.
But the roads led to the same end.
It was as if an invisible hand were plucking the strings of fate from behind. No matter which fork she chose, which slope she climbed, the chill she was meant to feel, the lesson she was meant to swallow, the dark side of human nature she was meant to see clearly—it would always wait, quietly, in another form, with another face, at some turning of her life.
It only confirmed further how small an individual is before the vast laws of fate; how unavoidable the price of "growth" is; how "clear-sightedness" itself is a credit of blood and tears, earned through hitting one wall after another and "seeing." You cannot go around the pain—you can only choose the posture in which you bear it, and, having borne it, what shape of awareness and armor you forge it into.
Just as these thoughts surged and ebbed like a tide, closing in around her, there came a soft knock at the restroom door.
"Yao Yuan?" It was Zhao Yiming's voice, coming through the panel with a barely perceptible concern. "Are you all right? You've been in there a long time."
Yao Yuan came back to herself with a start. She closed her eyes, pressing down all the dread surging beneath them—about fate, about unreality—and when she opened them again she had recovered her usual lucidity. She stood, smoothed the slightly creased hem of her dress, went to the sink, dipped her fingertips in a little cold water, and patted it gently around her eyes once more. Then, facing the mirror, she put on a flawless, calm smile.
"I'm fine. Coming out now." She answered aloud, her voice steady.
She turned the lock, and there was Zhao Yiming, standing in the warm yellow glow of the corridor outside. He'd shifted into a more casual stance, but his eyes lingered on her face for an instant—a scrutiny that wasn't suspicion, but a sharp observation born of familiarity.
"Really all right?" he asked, his tone mild, even as he reached out and took, perfectly naturally, the small clutch holding her lipstick and compact from her hand. "You were gone so long, Wang Qian left ahead of us. I was afraid you'd had too much to drink and weren't feeling well."
"I'm not unwell," Yao Yuan shook her head and, just as naturally, slipped her arm through the one he offered, drawing on the steady, real warmth and strength coming from his arm to pull herself fully back to solid ground from that ethereal sense of fate. "Just a little tired. The drink hit me, so I sat and let it pass. It was too stuffy in there."
Side by side, they walked along the thickly carpeted corridor toward the faint swell of sound from the banquet hall. Yao Yuan felt the line of the muscle in Zhao Yiming's arm, caught the faint, clean scent of aftershave on him, heard his steady breathing and the regular fall of his steps.
All of it was so real, so concrete, so consistent with the logic of the physical world.
And yet only minutes ago, her consciousness had still been talking with her past self, witnessing a quarrel about equity and betrayal. Her life held, on one side, the rigorous, verifiable, deducible numerical science and business logic that Zhao Yiming represented, and on the other, the bronze mirror's wholly inexplicable, metaphor-and-pain-laden journeys back through time and space that no existing science could account for.
The mystical and the scientific, the absurd and the everyday, false memory and real touch... so contradictory, so split, and yet so strangely, so seamlessly interwoven in her life.
For one instant, an utterly absurd thought struck her: this world she lived in—was it a true and solid existence, or, as depicted in the American sci-fi series "Westworld," merely a vast, peerless, painstakingly designed virtual theater? And she, and everyone she met, only programmed characters (Hosts) set up by some higher existence—every love and hate, every struggle to grow, every business contest, even the uncanny time-travel, only a long, lifelike "situational experience" being observed?
The thought sent a chill down her spine, but soon a deeper rationality welled up.
Whatever the truth, whether the world was real or illusory, the warmth coming through his arm in this moment was real; the unfinished Mirror Pact was real; the countdown to the ninth time-travel was real; the human scramble over interests she'd just witnessed in the "past" was real; and this man beside her, willing to leave a noisy banquet to come looking for her—his concern, his presence, in the feeling of this moment, was real too.
Perhaps, before the ultimate answer was revealed (if such an answer even existed), the only thing that mattered was to keep walking clear-eyed through this "situation," at once so absurd and so utterly real—to shoulder her choices, to feel the warmth, to solve the problems—whether by using the weapon of the law to fight for her equity, or using what courage remained to wait for and face the final pact by the Yellow River, or trying to believe in and accept a warmth that, even after a rational assessment, another real soul was still willing to hold out to her.
She tightened the hand looped through Zhao Yiming's arm, pressing those nihilistic tremors about the nature of existence deep down into her heart. Ahead, the light and the voices of the banquet hall grew clearer—the world of the "now," the one that still needed her to face it clear-eyed, was waiting there.

========================================

Chapter 93 — The Mirror Pact's Backlash Comes True: Shuai Hongqiang's Daughter Is Hurt in an Accident
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640340.html

Christmas sunlight poured through the dining room's floor-to-ceiling windows, cutting bright patches across the solid wood table. Shuai Hongqiang was bent over, peeling shrimp for Shuai Wenyao, while the boy chattered away about wanting to visit the newly opened science park that afternoon. Lin Wan ladled out a bowl of soup and handed it over, emerald scallions floating on the surface, steam curling up.

That was when the phone rang.

His ex-wife, Qiu Ying. After all these years divorced, they barely spoke beyond what their child required. The instant his fingertip slid across the answer key, something in Shuai Hongqiang's chest gave an inexplicable lurch.

"Hello?" His voice sounded normal.

Qiu Ying's voice came through, stripped of its usual crisp efficiency, carrying a tremor and panic she was fighting hard to hold down. "Hongqiang... something's happened to Wenrui."

The chopsticks in Shuai Hongqiang's hand clattered onto the porcelain plate with a sharp clink. Both Lin Wan and Wenyao looked up at him.

"Don't panic, take it slow. What happened?" He heard his own voice come out steady enough, but a layer of clammy cold sweat instantly seeped from the palm gripping the phone.

"She went skiing with classmates... she fell coming down an intermediate slope. Her left leg—her left tibia and fibula are fractured, and she has a mild concussion. She's at the Provincial People's Hospital, just out of surgery..." Qiu Ying's account came in broken fragments, threaded with suppressed sobs. "The doctor says the surgery went well, but afterward... it's hard to say how the recovery will go. It depends on her..."

Skiing. Fracture. Surgery.

Each word dropped like a cold stone into Shuai Hongqiang's abruptly frozen mind. But the first thing that surged up in him was not heart-wrenching pain over his daughter's injury, nor a father's instinctive urgency—it was a chill, bone-deep terror that shot up from his spine.

Like a bolt of lightning splitting open the chaos in his head, it lit up the words he had never dared to dwell on, the ones that had hung over him day and night: the Mirror Pact's backlash, cause and effect coming due.

Old Mr. Wang Baoguo's low voice seemed to echo in his ears again: "...The force of cause and effect is mysterious beyond words. The backlash need not fall on you yourself—it may also strike your closest blood kin..."

So that was it. It had come. Retribution isn't absent, it just bides its time. The smooth fortune he had stolen, the luck he had overdrawn—someone would have to pay the interest in the end. He just never imagined the interest would be charged to his daughter.

Wenrui had only just started her first year of university. Her life had barely begun to unfold. She loved to dance so much; as a little girl she'd clung to him begging to learn ballet, and he'd refused on the grounds that it would "interfere with her studies." If her leg... Shuai Hongqiang couldn't let himself think further, his stomach churning with nausea.

"Daddy?" Shuai Wenyao tugged at his sleeve, his small face full of worry. "Is Jiejie sick?"

The child's tender voice pulled Shuai Hongqiang briefly back from the ice pit. Only then did he notice that Lin Wan had risen at some point and was standing beside him, her hand resting lightly on his tensed shoulder, her gaze calm and questioning.

"...I understand." Shuai Hongqiang said into the phone, his voice dry in a way that didn't sound like his own. "Orthopedics at the Provincial People's Hospital, right? I'll come right over."

He hung up. The dining room fell silent. Shuai Wenyao looked at his father, then at his mother, not daring to make any more noise.

"Wenrui fell skiing. Broke her leg. She's in the hospital." Shuai Hongqiang told Lin Wan plainly. He tried to force a smile to reassure his son, only to find the corners of his mouth too heavy to lift. The terror and the guilt pressed down on him like two mountains. In all these years, what had he ever given Wenrui beyond the generous living allowance deposited into her account on schedule and the occasional perfunctory holiday greeting? He didn't even know what major she'd actually enrolled in, which celebrity she was crazy about lately, or whether she had a boyfriend. He had provided her abundant material comfort while being absent for her entire upbringing. And now this "retribution" had landed with precision on his daughter—which cut him deeper than if it had fallen directly on himself, and frightened him all the more at the cold, exact cruelty of that invisible cause and effect.

"Don't panic." Lin Wan's voice was steady and gentle. Her hand pressed his shoulder with a little more force. "Eat first. When we're done, I'll drive you to the hospital. Wenyao, eat quickly—when you finish, Mommy will take you and Daddy to see Jiejie."

Her composure was like a piece of driftwood, letting Shuai Hongqiang—almost drowning in guilt and fear—barely grab hold of some sense of reality. He nodded, picked up his chopsticks again, but everything tasted like wax. The rest of that lunch ended quickly, in a stifled silence.

On the way to the hospital, Shuai Hongqiang kept staring at the streetscape rushing past the window. Christmas decorations were everywhere, red and green intertwined, brimming with cheap cheer that only made his heart feel colder and grayer. Lin Wan drove with focus, glancing at him now and then in the rearview mirror, saying little. Shuai Wenyao seemed to sense the heavy mood too, sitting obediently in his car seat, clutching the plush teddy bear he'd brought for his sister.

The orthopedics ward corridor at the Provincial People's Hospital was filled with the crisp, distinctive smell of disinfectant. Shuai Hongqiang's steps felt unsteady. At the ward door he actually had to pause, draw a deep breath, before he could push it open and go in.

In the single room, Qiu Ying stood by the window speaking quietly on the phone, her back looking somewhat thin and frail. Hearing the door, she turned. After several years apart, she was still well kept, only the fine lines at the corners of her eyes had deepened, and right now her brow was full of exhaustion and worry. Seeing Shuai Hongqiang, she hung up and walked over quickly.

"Hongqiang." She called out, her gaze passing over Lin Wan and Wenyao behind him, pausing slightly, then nodding politely to Lin Wan. "Lin Wan came too."

"Sister Qiu." Lin Wan stepped forward, her voice gentle, setting a fruit basket on the bedside cabinet. "How is Wenrui? What exactly did the doctor say?" Her form of address was pitched just right—neither too distant nor too close.

Just then, a man rose from a chair toward the back of the room, behind Qiu Ying. He was about fifty, wore glasses, with a refined, scholarly air. He was Qiu Ying's current husband, surnamed Chen, a university professor. Shuai Hongqiang had only glimpsed him from a distance once, years ago, when dropping off Wenrui.

"Professor Chen." Shuai Hongqiang nodded to him and greeted him first. His tone was as calm and courteous as he could manage, betraying little emotion.

"Hongqiang's here." Professor Chen nodded back at once, his face carrying the composure of an intellectual along with a measure of the appropriate gravity such an occasion called for. He stepped half a pace forward but did not extend his hand, expressing only in words a familiar, shared-concern stance: "Long trip. Wenrui just fell asleep. The doctor came to check on her and said the surgery went very well, told us not to worry too much."

Their exchange was brief and restrained, like two friends drawn into contact only by a matter of mutual concern, swiftly trading the most essential information and then having nothing more to say. There was a faint subtlety in the air, the kind grown-ups silently understand. No hostility, no comparison—just a clear, tacit acknowledgment of their respective positions and relationships. Having said his piece, Professor Chen naturally stepped back half a pace, leaving more room for Qiu Ying and Shuai Hongqiang, and turned his gaze toward Lin Wan, who was quietly arranging the fruit basket, nodding to her with the same courtesy.

This process of "greeting each other like friends" was quick and bland, yet like an extremely fine needle, it drew across Shuai Hongqiang's already tangled state of mind another mark—nearly invisible, but real—reminding him that in his daughter's life he had long since retreated to a peripheral position, one maintained by the identities of "ex-husband" and "father," while the daily companionship and the vigil at this very moment were another man's duty and right. This realization, mingled with the towering terror and guilt in his heart—the suspicion that he himself had brought down this disaster—left a taste beyond words.

"The surgery's done. They put in a plate. The anesthesia hasn't fully worn off, so she's sleeping." Qiu Ying rubbed her brow and told Shuai Hongqiang the details of what had happened. "They said she rolled down the slope, twisted her left leg, and couldn't move it on the spot. The classmate she went with was terrified, called an ambulance and brought her straight here. Thank goodness her head and spine weren't hurt—it's just this leg..." Looking at her daughter's pale face on the hospital bed, her voice choked again.

"Who's the attending physician? Is there a post-op rehab plan yet? Did they say whether it'll affect her in the future..." Shuai Hongqiang threw out a string of questions, each one hiding a fear he didn't dare voice—would it affect her walking down the line? Could she still run and jump? Could she still... dance?

"The doctor will come around for rounds in a bit. Ask him the details." Qiu Ying said, her gaze turning again to Lin Wan, forcing something that barely passed for a smile. "Thank you both for making the trip. Wenyao's gotten so big." She crouched down, wanting to pat Wenyao's head, but the child instinctively leaned toward Lin Wan's leg. Qiu Ying's hand paused slightly in midair, then withdrew naturally, her smile fading a little.

"It's the least we could do." Lin Wan gently nudged Wenyao forward. "Wenyao, say hello to Auntie Qiu."

"Hello, Auntie Qiu." Shuai Wenyao said in a small voice, his big eyes darting toward the bed the whole time.

There was a subtle barrier in the air—not openly confrontational, yet undeniably present. Two women, who at different stages of life had formed the most intimate bond with the same man, now stood together in one hospital room. No hostility, even a courteous concern, but the invisible line between "ex-wife" and "current wife" was as clear as the curtain dividing the room into sections. The past marriage hung like a faint shadow across the simple pleasantries of the present. Lin Wan was considerate but would not overstep; Qiu Ying was courteous, yet couldn't quite hide a trace of complicated feeling—after all, the woman before her was occupying the place she had once held, keeping her ex-husband company.

"I'll go see Wenrui." Lin Wan broke the subtle stillness at just the right moment and walked to the bedside.

Shuai Wenrui was still in a deep sleep, no color in her young face, her lashes casting a small shadow beneath her eyelids. Her left leg was in a heavy cast, suspended by a brace—a startling sight. Lin Wan looked at her quietly for a moment, then reached out and ever so lightly tucked in the corner of the blanket. That was all she could do. As a stepmother, her contact with this stepdaughter was genuinely scant; their few meetings had mostly been polite holiday meals together. Too much concern now would only seem false. She straightened up and said softly to Shuai Hongqiang and Qiu Ying: "You two talk. I'll take Wenyao out for a bit—I'm afraid it's stifling for him in here."

Qiu Ying nodded. "Thank you."

Lin Wan led Wenyao out—the boy looking back every few steps—and gently closed the door behind them.

With the door shut, the air in the ward seemed to loosen a little, only to fill again with another kind of heavy worry. Shuai Hongqiang walked to the bed and gazed at his daughter. When was the last time he had looked at her this closely? It seemed to have been her birthday last year. He'd booked a restaurant; the gift was the newest phone, picked out by Lin Wan. She'd said thank you then, her smile sweet and well-mannered, but with no real delight in her eyes. Somewhere along the way, all that was left between them was this fixed routine—flowing with money, yet drained of intimacy.

Now, watching his daughter lie there so fragile on the hospital bed, the guilt mixed with fear in Shuai Hongqiang's heart surged forward like a tide, nearly drowning him. Was it him? Was it truly because of that moment of greed, that pact he'd made with that uncanny mirror, the fortune he'd stolen that was never his to take—was that why the retribution was now falling on his daughter? If Wenrui's leg really did carry some lasting damage, casting a shadow over her whole life... Shuai Hongqiang squeezed his eyes shut, not daring to think further. That self-loathing and panic left him feeling more powerless than any business disaster ever had.

"The doctor said she's young, with strong recovery, and as long as she does her rehab properly there won't be any major problems." Qiu Ying's voice came from beside him—whether to comfort him or herself, who could say.

Shuai Hongqiang opened his eyes, pulled his wallet from his pocket, drew out a card, and pressed it into Qiu Ying's hand. "Use the best medicine, hire the best rehab therapist. Don't worry about money. The PIN is Wenrui's birthday."

Qiu Ying looked at the card in her hand, silent for a few seconds, then took it without refusing. "Mm."

Just then, Shuai Wenrui on the bed let out a faint moan. Her eyelids fluttered and slowly opened. Her gaze first focused blankly on the ceiling, then turned slowly toward the bedside.

"Wenrui?" Shuai Hongqiang immediately bent over her, his voice careful in a way even he didn't notice. "You're awake? How do you feel? Does your leg hurt?"

Shuai Wenrui's eyes lingered on his face for a moment, as if only now fully waking, and she called softly, "Dad." Then she looked toward Qiu Ying and Professor Chen. "Mom. Uncle Chen." Her voice was hoarse and weak.

"You're awake, that's good, that's good." Qiu Ying hurried over and pressed the call button. "Is anywhere especially uncomfortable? Do you feel nauseous, like you might throw up?"

A nurse came in quickly, checked all the readings, and said her condition was stable. As the anesthesia wore off, the pain grew clearer, and Shuai Wenrui's brow knit slightly—but she was very quiet, not crying out.

The ward door was nudged quietly open a crack, and a small head peeked in. It was Shuai Wenyao. He was hugging that plush bear tightly. Seeing that his sister was awake, his eyes lit up, and only after a nod from Lin Wan did he tiptoe in and sidle up to the bed.

"Jiejie," he said in a tiny voice, full of a child's worry, "does your leg hurt?"

Seeing her little brother, a faint, genuine smile crossed Shuai Wenrui's face. "A little," she said honestly.

Shuai Wenyao immediately held out the bear in his arms. "Here, you hug my teddy. If you hug it, it won't hurt. Last time I fell down, Mommy had me hug the teddy."

The childish words eased the heavy atmosphere in the ward a little. Shuai Wenrui didn't take the bear; she only reached out and gently stroked her brother's hair. "Thank you, Wenyao. Jiejie's a grown-up now, I'm not afraid of pain. You keep the teddy yourself."

"Oh." Shuai Wenyao looked a little disappointed, but soon tilted his face up again. "Then Jiejie, get better quick. When you're better, Daddy can take us to the amusement park together. You said next time we'd ride the big roller coaster."

The child's offhand words pricked at Shuai Hongqiang's heart like a fine needle. He had promised "next time" so many times, only to break his word, mostly because he was "busy." Watching the simple exchange between brother and sister—Wenyao's complete closeness and reliance on his sister, Wenrui's natural tenderness toward her brother—he suddenly realized with clarity that in the days of his absence, his daughter had quietly grown into a gentle adult, one who knew how to take care of others and bear pain. And he—beyond providing the ever-growing number on a bank card, what else had he given her?

"All right. When Jiejie's better, we'll definitely go." Shuai Hongqiang heard himself promise, his voice slightly hoarse.

He stayed a while longer, carefully asking the nurse and the attending physician who came in afterward a whole string of questions, confirming the treatment and rehab plan over and over. Finally, he smoothed back the loose strands of hair on his daughter's forehead, the gesture a little clumsy.

"Wenrui, Dad has some things to handle at the company, so I'll head back first. Listen carefully to your mom and the doctors, cooperate with the treatment, don't think about anything—Dad's got it all handled." He paused. The words "It's Dad who let you down" rolled and rolled on his tongue, but in the end they were choked back by the enormous fear and shame, and never made it out. They turned instead into something heavier: "Dad will come see you again tomorrow."

"Mm, Dad, go do what you need to. I'm fine." Shuai Wenrui nodded, ever the sensible one.

Stepping out of the ward, closing the door, shutting that thick disinfectant smell and his daughter's pale face behind him, Shuai Hongqiang leaned against the cold wall and only then let the shoulders he'd been holding up collapse just a sliver. The light from the corridor's overhead lamps was a glaring white, yet his vision kept blacking out in waves, his ears ringing—filled with the words "cause-and-effect backlash" and "closest blood kin" screaming wildly.

Lin Wan waited quietly off to the side, holding the hand of an already yawning Wenyao. She didn't rush him, didn't press him with questions—only, as he straightened up again, placed Wenyao's small hand into his.

"Let's go home," she said, her voice low but carrying a steady strength that could pierce through frozen ice.

The soft, warm touch of the child's hand reached his palm. He lowered his head, meeting his son's innocent, clear eyes, and his other hand was gently taken by Lin Wan. Her hand wasn't cold and fragile like his daughter's was right now—it was warm and strong. Shuai Hongqiang's thoughts, muddled and stinging, gnawed at by fear, were tugged by these two patches of real warmth, dragged bit by bit back to the cold, concrete reality.

The family of three—their figures stretched long under the lamps of the empty hospital corridor—walked silently toward the elevator. On Christmas night the hospital was still ablaze with light, faint voices drifting through. Shuai Hongqiang walked in the middle, one hand leading the son bound to him by the blood of his present family, the other gripped firmly by the wife tied to him by present law and life, who at this moment was his only support. And behind that door, the daughter who might be suffering an undeserved disaster because of his moment of greed and its backlash was enduring her pain, watched over by another family—one he felt grateful to, and yet one that left him with nowhere to hide his shame.

All of it formed a picture full of fated irony and silent torment, nailing him firmly to the dock of cause and effect's judgment. The future stretched ahead like a dark, endless tunnel, the ninth time-travel hanging at its far end like a faint glimmer—he couldn't tell whether it meant release or final judgment—while the warmth pressing into his palm from those beside him, the warmth of ordinary human life, was the only fragile, real piece of driftwood he could grasp as he struggled, suffocating, in the dark. He gripped that hand tightly, like a dying man clutching his last hope of survival.
