========================================

Chapter 94 — A Marriage in a Blended Family Needs an Upgraded Partnership Agreement
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640341.html

After getting home from the hospital, it still wasn't too late, so Lin Wan went ahead with her original plan and started tonight's livestream.

Eight in the evening. Warm yellow light spread evenly across Lin Wan's study. The pothos plant stretched its thick green leaves in one corner of the frame; beside her hand, a cup of red-date and goji tea sent up a gentle, mellow steam, and the faint scent of cedar incense drifted through the air, calming the nerves thread by thread. She sat in her usual spot, in a soft, light smoke-gray cashmere sweater, her short hair neat — only there was a trace of quiet, almost imperceptible fatigue between her brows tonight, like a piece of fine porcelain veiled in the lightest cool mist you could sense only by touch.

She adjusted the microphone, met the camera with a steady gaze, and her lips curved into that warm, certain arc her countless fans knew so well.

"Good evening, everyone, and welcome to 'The Gentle Sobriety.' I'm your host, Lin Wan."

The scrolling comments came in right on cue:

[Evening, Wanwan!]

[Here on time as always!]

[Tonight's mood feels especially calm?]

"Thank you all for coming on time." Lin Wan's voice was clear and even, like a stream gliding over pebbles. She paused slightly, looking frankly into the camera as if she could see through the screen and meet the eyes of every listener. "Tonight's topic was decided at the last minute. The subject I'd originally prepared, we'll set aside for later."

The comments:

[Huh? A last-minute change?]

[Did something happen, Wanwan?]

"I changed the topic because two things collided today." Her tone was calm, as if recounting an ordinary thing worth pondering. "The first was that earlier today I went to the hospital to visit a young girl who'd been admitted. She's the daughter of Brother Qiang and his ex-wife — the child broke her leg skiing. I stood there, in my role as her stepmother." She paused for a moment here, looking honestly into the camera.

"What I saw in that hospital room was very concrete. The child's birth mother, and that birth mother's current husband — the child's stepfather — kept watch at the bedside, fussing over her meals and care, asking the doctors and nurses their questions. That was real, day-and-night, wrapped-around worry and responsibility. Brother Qiang and I — the child's biological father and her later stepmother — we rushed over, anxious inside, but we knew there was a lot we couldn't reach. That was the birth mother's home turf. When you stand in that position, a lot of things come into sharp focus: when the child hurts, who's the first person she wants; through the night at the bedside, who should be the one keeping watch; the medical bills — who pays as a matter of course, and who pays out of goodwill but is still, in the end, one layer removed; even a word of comfort — who should say it, and how, the weight of it is never the same."

Her tone stayed level, with no complaint, simply stating a complicated "position" and "sense of measure" she had felt firsthand.

"It's these very concrete things that make you suddenly understand — in a newly blended household, the difference between 'blood' and 'step,' between 'kin' and 'family in the eyes of the law'; the weight of that responsibility, that worry; and the subtle measure and distance in how people deal with one another; and each person's position and the hardships they carry in this relationship."

Her words came unhurried, with no dramatic flourish, yet that calm, firsthand account made the people on the other end of the screen unconsciously go still.

The comments fell quiet for a moment, then slowly drifted by:

[Sigh, the honest truth. It makes my heart ache to hear it.]

[It's so hard for Wanwan. That position, that sense of measure, is the hardest to get right.]

[This is the reality of second marriages. She put it so clearly.]

"The second thing was a letter I read in the back-end inbox right before going live, from a fan we'll call Sister S for now. She's in a blended-family marriage, with an eight-year-old son of her own, while her husband has a ten-year-old daughter. The letter was long, filled with how a wife trying to be a good stepmother had, over the course of two full years, been worn down to nothing — by her stepdaughter's invisible hostility, by her husband's helpless avoidance — to the point of an exhausted, heart-cold consideration of divorce."

The comments began to scroll quickly:

[Blended families really are so hard!]

[Is being a stepmother an original sin?]

[My aunt went through exactly this. She nearly fell into depression.]

"What I saw at the hospital was a sharp, focused instant; Sister S's letter is a long, day-after-day erosion. From different scales of time, they both point to the same, more complicated model." Lin Wan's fingertip tapped absently at the desk — a little habit of hers when she was thinking deeply. "So tonight, if you're all willing, I thought we could talk about this: when a marriage upgrades from 'two people in a startup together' to 'a merger and restructuring where each side brings their own core asset — a child' — how exactly should this new, more complicated 'Partnership Agreement' be signed, so as to avoid systemic exhaustion and collapse?"

The comments:

[Partnership Agreement! Great metaphor!]

[So it's not about who's right or wrong — it's that the rules were never set?]

[Wanwan's drawing on her own life tonight. It hits even harder.]

"Let's start with Sister S's predicament." Lin Wan steered the discussion to the concrete case, her voice steady, patient as she teased the threads apart. "She tries hard to be good to her ten-year-old stepdaughter, but the child thinks she 'stole Dad away' and opposes her at every turn — one face to her, another behind her back. She talks to her husband, and he says 'the child's still young,' 'be more tolerant,' or even asks, 'Is it that you can't make room for her?' It sounds like the child is being difficult and the husband is refusing to muddle things over, doesn't it?"

She gave a small shake of her head, the gesture of denial gentle but firm.

"If we stop at moral judgment — is the child an ingrate, is the husband playing favorites — then the situation is dead. We have to see this new family's system bug. The bug is that everyone assumes, or unconsciously treats, the stepmother — the newly joined partner, Sister S — as the only variable in the system that can be debugged, the one expected to tolerate without limit. Meanwhile the states of the original shareholder who actually needs adjusting — the husband — and the core asset — the child — are allowed to stay exactly as they are. Their emotions and problems even become the fixed environment that the new variable has to adapt to and absorb."

The comments:

[System bug! Right to the point!]

[The new partner debugs herself while the old shareholder sits back and enjoys it?]

[So true. I feel like my eyes have been opened.]

"Is that fair? Of course not. But it usually forms unconsciously." Lin Wan picked up her tea and took a sip, then her well-moistened voice went on, clear and full of empathy. "The child's hostility very likely comes from a loss of security after the family upheaval. She uses confrontation to test how firm the new boundaries are — even, subconsciously, hoping to trade 'driving off the stepmother' for the family picture she used to have. And the husband's avoidance, beyond whatever guilt he may feel, is also an escape from responsibility. He can't handle his daughter's complicated emotions, and he can't firmly establish new rules between his daughter and his new wife. So the simplest method is to ask the adult who seems more 'sensible,' more 'in love with him' — namely his wife — to endure, to absorb it all."

"And so Sister S is stuck fast. Forward — she can't break through the child's defenses, can't get the closeness she longs for. To the left — she can't get her husband's allied support, and is questioned instead. Stay put — and it's day after day of erosion. All her 'kindness' turns into shoveling sand into a bottomless emotional black hole."

The comments:

[Tearing up. That's me!]

[Men really do love to avoid conflict!]

[So there's no solution? How hopeless.]

"It's not unsolvable. The rules of the game simply have to change — or rather, this 'Upgraded Partnership Agreement' has to be redrafted." Lin Wan's gaze turned sharp and clear-eyed, the calm strength of someone who has seen through the structure. "This new agreement needs at least three core clauses, and they have to be talked out in black and white by the two core partners — the husband and wife — and firmly carried out in daily life."

"The first is called the 'Original Shareholder Responsibility Wake-Up' clause." Her voice was iron-firm. "Make it explicit: each person is the first party responsible for the emotional management and behavioral upbringing of their own child. When a child develops emotions or behaviors directed at the stepparent, the biological parent must step forward and take primary responsibility for communicating, guiding, and setting boundaries. This isn't 'not making room for the child' — this is putting authority and responsibility where they belong. The spouse can offer support, but isn't a stand-in to charge into battle, much less a dedicated recycling bin for negative emotions. The agreement has to spell it out: when the child gives you dirty looks, sabotages things, shows one face to your face and another behind your back, what your husband needs to do is not turn to you and say 'the child's still young.' He must face his own child and tell him or her clearly: this is our new family member, respect is the bottom line. We can talk about your feelings, but treating family this way is not allowed."

The comments:

[The husband needs to step up! So right!]

[A lot of men lack exactly this kind of backbone.]

[Wanwan said that with such firmness!]

"The second is called the 'Partners United Outward' clause." Lin Wan continued, her tone leaving no room for doubt. "Inside the family, especially in front of the children, the husband and wife must be a solid management alliance. That means the basic family rules — respect, honesty, responsibility — have to be upheld together. When one of them is carrying out their duty as the first responsible party, disciplining their own child, the other needs to give clear, visible support: an affirming look, a word echoing the rule, or, afterward and in private, recognition of what the spouse has put in. Absolutely no undermining, no questioning, no muddling things over in front of the children. That line from the husband — 'Is it that you can't make room for her?' — is blowing up the very foundation of the alliance with his own hands. The agreement has to state clearly: behind closed doors we can argue about anything, but in front of the children, on upholding the family's basic rules and each other's dignity, we must be on the same side."

"The third is called the 'Emotional Expectation Management' clause." At this point her tone softened, taking on more understanding and pragmatism. "Allow that building affection takes time — even accept a degree of 'friendly but slightly distant.' Don't demand that the stepparent instantly love the child as their own, and don't demand that the child instantly feel a child's longing for the stepparent. Lower the fixation on 'instantly close as one family,' and put the focus on building a clear, stable, mutually respectful pattern of behavioral interaction. Be good neighbors first, then talk about being good family. At the same time, the husband and wife have to deliberately tend each other's emotional account — that's the new company's most important cash flow. Communicate regularly, affirm each other's contributions and difficulties, make sure the connection between you is solid. Only when the two of you are steady do you have the strength to steady this more complicated system."

The comments:

[Lower your expectations! Be neighbors first!]

[The marriage itself is the anchor that holds everything steady.]

[So well said. I feel like there's a way forward.]

"Sister S's predicament is, to a large degree, the complete absence of this new agreement. Everyone's still running a multilateral system on the fuzzy expectations of the old two-person world." Lin Wan summed up, her gaze clear and forceful, as if it could drive away the fog. "She's been single-handedly debugging herself this whole time, trying to be a 'perfect stepmother' to fit a system riddled with holes — and the result is the system swallowed her."

"So if there's a sister in a similar situation right now," she leaned forward slightly, her voice full of gentle firmness, "perhaps you could try shifting your gaze away from the self-interrogation of 'Am I not good enough?' and 'Why doesn't the child like me?' — back to the most fundamental question. Have a clear-eyed, and possibly difficult, conversation with your partner:"

"Our new company is founded — have we made clear who's responsible for whose child? When one of us has friction with a child, or a child has friction with a new family member, what's our process for supporting each other? Do we have realistic, aligned expectations about the emotional intensity and interaction pattern of this new family?"

"Marriage has never been easy. So, coming back to that first metaphor of ours," Lin Wan's voice grew ever more quiet and strong as the broadcast neared its end, as if all the analysis had to settle at last into a graspable core, "when a marriage upgrades from 'a two-person partnership' to 'a joint venture after asset restructuring,' the measure of its health is no longer whether it looks 'close as one family' on the surface. It's whether that invisible 'Partnership Agreement' is clear, fair, and jointly upheld."

"That agreement doesn't need to be filled with vague words like 'love' or 'sacrifice.' What it needs to write down are specific, fine-grained clauses like 'allocation of responsibility,' 'allied stance,' and 'emotional expectations.' Its purpose isn't to create estrangement — quite the opposite. It's to build, on the premise of acknowledging differences and histories, an order in which everyone — including ourselves — won't be inexplicably drained, and can exist safely."

She looked into the camera with clear eyes, as if she could see the weary figures behind the screen.

"When we work hard to run a blended family, the ultimate goal may not be to prove that 'we can be just as good as a first-marriage family.' It's to prove that, even carrying the scars and marks of the past, we can still use reason and sincerity to create together a new, resilient way of getting along. That way may not be perfect, but it's real enough; it may not be passionate, but it's stable enough. It respects the natural bond of blood, and it also defends the new covenant forged out of love and commitment."

"It asks us — especially the husband and wife, the core 'partners' — to stand up out of the quagmire of emotion and take our place as builders of rules. To clearly divide responsibility, firmly form an alliance, and pragmatically manage expectations. That's much harder than simply demanding of yourself that you 'do more' and 'love deeper' — but it's also much more effective."

"Finally, I want to say to Sister S, who wrote to me today, and to everyone in a similar predicament:" Lin Wan's tone was full of a gentle, firm strength. "When you feel drained, wronged, isolated with no one to turn to, it's very likely not because you aren't good enough, but because something is wrong with the system's 'default settings.' Your energy shouldn't all go into patching the system's holes. What you need to do, perhaps, is take some of that energy back, light up yourself — and then use it to light up that negotiating table between you and your partner, the one about rules and responsibilities."

"Go talk, divide, build. If the negotiation fails, then at least you'll know where the limits of the system are. And if it succeeds, then together you'll have driven the first — and firmest — stake of rules into this complicated, precious home."

"That's all for tonight. May we all have the courage, in our complicated relationships, to be the clear-eyed builders — not merely the silent ones who endure. Good night."

The soft music marking the end of the broadcast began to play. Lin Wan took off her headphones but didn't leave right away. Only the faint hum of the incense diffuser remained in the study. The screen went dark, and the black surface reflected her face — calm, yet unable to fully hide a trace of fatigue.

She thought of the hospital that afternoon — of the wordless, understanding support in the way Teacher Chen had quietly handed over warm water; of the "mother" aura around Qiu Ying, mixed with anxiety and a refusal to be questioned. That was a "partnership" already fully run in, one whose solid structure showed itself in a crisis.

And her own partnership? She thought of Shuai Hongqiang gripping her hand, of the uncontrollable faint tremor and the cold sweat in his palm. That bottomless fear of his — where on earth did it come from? Their tacit, "limited-liability" partnership agreement — in the face of the coming, perhaps even greater storm, was it solid enough?

She didn't know. But she did know, just as she'd said tonight, that clear rules and shared responsibility are the bedrock for getting through any complicated relationship. She picked up the now-cold tea and took a small sip; the faintly bitter aftertaste melted on the tip of her tongue, and let her tangled thoughts settle.

No matter what, first guard the order and calm within her own heart. That was the most important thing she could do for herself — and for this partnership.

After a while, she packed up her equipment, turned off the main light, and then gently pushed open the study door.

In the living room, only a single floor lamp in the corner was lit, its light dim and yellow, carving the spacious room into great swaths of shadow. Shuai Hongqiang sat on the sofa at the edge of the shadow, staring blankly out at the thick night beyond the window. Hearing the door open, he snapped back as if startled and turned his head.

The look of him made Lin Wan's steps falter slightly.

In the depths of Shuai Hongqiang's eyes was a desolation, like a place swept through by a storm. His lips moved, but he couldn't pull up his usual smile.

"Done broadcasting?" he said, his voice badly hoarse, as if he hadn't spoken in a long time. He tried to stand, but his movements were a little slow.

"Mm. Is Wenyao asleep?" Lin Wan sat down, a seat's width away.

"Just fell asleep." His eyes clung to her face, then seemed to pass through her, gazing at some void. "Today… thank you, it was hard on you."

"I'm fine. Wenrui will be okay. Don't blame yourself too much."

The words "blame yourself" made him give a sharp shudder; he abruptly turned his face away, his jawline pulled taut. The living room sank into a silence that swallowed all sound.

He suddenly sprang up, his movements a little flustered. "Milk — I warmed some milk for you." He left the living room almost as if fleeing, as though one more second there and the heavy secret would spill out of his eyes on its own.

Lin Wan sat where she was, watching his back disappear at the kitchen doorway. The cloud of doubt in her heart grew thicker. His state today was far too abnormal — well beyond the range of worry over a daughter hurt by accident. It was a kind of… fear, as if something invisible were chasing and gnawing at him, about to drag him down. But he wouldn't say, and she couldn't press him. This was the tacit understanding — or rather, the boundary — that had formed between them over the years.

Shuai Hongqiang came back carrying the milk, and when he handed it to her, his fingertips were icy, even with a faint, barely perceptible tremor. The warmth of the milk came through the glass, just the right kind of warm.

"Thank you." Lin Wan took it, cupping it in both hands, the warm sensation spreading out from her palms. She watched Shuai Hongqiang sit back down, still sunk in that heavy silence. An arm's length apart, yet it was like a misty abyss between them. She knew he was lingering alone at the edge of that abyss, but she couldn't see what lay at the bottom, and didn't know how to reach out — or whether he would even let her.

The night deepened, and the city lights outside the window thinned out. They sat there quietly in a room of dim yellow light and silence, one cupping warm milk, the other steeped in an unspoken, mutual chill. All those rational discussions in the livestream — about responsibility in blended families, about system rules, about partnership agreements — were now refracted back, concrete and small, onto themselves: forming a partnership that looked solid, yet in which each carried an utterly different, unspeakable secret.

The Yellow River flowed soundlessly under the night, and the countdown to the ninth time-travel ticked away in someone's heart. And life, in this strange balance of keeping each other company while keeping each other shut out, went on with its unresolved narrative.

========================================

Chapter 95 — Refuse to Marry Down: Making Money Is a Woman's Highest Form of Confidence
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640342.html

Refuse to Marry Down: Making Money Is a Woman's Highest Form of Confidence
On the last afternoon of the year, at exactly two o'clock, the livestream went live, right on the dot.
Yao Yuan had not reached her current heights by accident. In private she had her own diversions, her own casual fun, but once a scheduled livestream was on the calendar, no reason, no emergency, ever made her miss so much as a minute. That dedication, carved into her very bones, was the foundation she stood on—and it had carried her steadily upward, into success and wealth.
This was the year's final broadcast.
Behind the host's chair, an enormous painting of the Yellow River stretched out in silence, its muddy waves churning, carrying a thousand years of silt as they washed over the weathered, folded banks. The sound of wind mingled with the low sob of running water and drifted past the windowsill—beyond the bright, bustling lights of the studio lay the ancient, vast stillness of mountains and rivers.
To one side of the camera, the gold-framed diagram of the Energy Pyramid stood out with sharp, clean edges, its matte surface glowing in the warm white light—understated, yet hiding a blade beneath. The tiered structure, rising level by level, framed a clear order, and quietly mirrored the desires and trade-offs buried deep in the human heart. On the screen was an endless flow of viewers and scrolling comments; on the wall hung the cold, fixed rules of hierarchy. One in motion, one still; one boiling, one frozen—a subtle hedge against each other.
On this special hinge between the old year and the new, Yao Yuan's look today was especially grand and lavish, her presence even more commanding than usual. She wore a heavily designed paneled shirt-dress, a style that balanced hardness and softness, full of tension. The upper half was crisp, clean white poplin that kept the dignified lines of a formal shirt, the collar unbuttoned two buttons down and drawn slightly back to bare the elegant line of her collarbones; at her throat, the heart-guarding jade that had been with her so long gave off a warm, gentle glow that softened the sharpness all around her. At her ears hung a pair of intricate, ornate vintage metal earrings, finely engraved, exuding an effortless air of luxury as light and shadow shifted across them—every lift of her hand, every lowering of her gaze, was pure allure.
The skirt portion was paneled with an asymmetric black leather wrap skirt, the stiff material and sharp cut tracing the taut, flowing curve of her waist and hips just so, while a small slit at the knee added a touch of liveliness and dash. On her feet were calf-high heeled leather boots in the same color, lengthening her legs in a straight line; she stood tall and erect, her presence fully open.
She had swept all her long hair back and coiled it into a sleek, tight bun at the nape, not a single loose strand showing, baring her whole stunning, radiant face without reserve. Her features were exquisite and three-dimensional, edged with their own sharpness, beautiful in a way that was almost aggressive.
Her makeup was the bold, opulent "Wealth Blossom" style. Her brows were carefully drawn, the arches lifted, crisp and flamboyant; her eye makeup was layered in golds and deep browns, the colors rich and saturated in their transitions, the upper liner drawn out cleanly at the corner to a fine, sharp point, the lower lid lightly brushed with the same shade to deepen the contour. Lift her eyes, and the gaze was deep and still, carrying a touch of aloof arrogance and scrutiny. Her lips were a velvet true red, thickly applied, the lip line clean as if cut by a blade, dazzling and vivid. Her freshly done manicure was a black-and-gold checkerboard pattern, matte black as the base, metallic lines tracing neat squares that threw off cold, fine glints under the lights.
The whole look fused elegance, beauty, hardness, and detachment; beneath that elegant shell, every inch betrayed careful calculation and layers upon layers of guarded defense.
The real-time data screen by her phone kept flickering. The moment the broadcast began, the live audience count had already burst past two million. The comments had started scrolling early, fans eagerly guessing at the theme of the year-end finale.
Yao Yuan lifted a hand and gently adjusted the tiny microphone at her collar, her gaze steady on the camera, calm and full of power. Her clear, composed voice came through the equipment crisply, ringing through the studio: "Welcome, my darling fans, to the Clear-Eyed Operating Table. Now let's have our little assistant connect today's first call-in."
[Call connected.]
(A woman's voice, weary and repressed, carrying years of accumulated grievance and unwillingness, every word soaked in a hurt that wouldn't dry.) "Teacher Yuanyuan, I've been following you for a long time. Today I really can't hold it in anymore. My husband and I got together in our sophomore year of college. My family is urban middle class; he came from a mountain village, dirt poor. When we were dating, the gap between us was total—horizons, the way we spoke, our understanding of the world, even basic taste in living."
She drew a deep breath; that breath was full of grit.
"Back then, everyone thought I'd lost my mind. They begged me not to marry down. But I was soft-hearted. I was greedy for that almost humble attentiveness of his, that dependence in his eyes that saw only me—I thought it was true love, priceless emotional value. I always believed the human heart is made of flesh, that if I suffered alongside him, lowered myself to meet him, gave him everything to pull him ashore, he would surely be grateful, would cherish me twice as much, would feel that marrying me was a blessing he'd earned by burning incense for eight lifetimes."
Her words began to come faster, the suppressed grievance and the sheer absurdity of it all gushing out:
"All these years, I lifted him up the whole way, made him who he is. I gave up a chance to study abroad because he said long-distance made him insecure. I chose a stable job with limited room to grow, so I'd have more time to take care of him, to help him adjust to the city. My connections, my family's resources—I fed them to him bit by bit, watching him go from an awkward boy to the 'Boss Lin' he is now, holding court at the dinner table, making a name for himself in the industry. Now he's thriving, his horizons broad, all the connections and resources his. I thought this was our shared victory, the sweet that comes after the bitter."
She paused, and when she spoke again, it was with a deep, total chill:
"But he changed. No—maybe he didn't change. He just finally climbed high enough that he no longer had to pretend. He started to despise my thinking as 'unable to keep up with his pace,' mocking me for being content with the status quo, for having 'no fighting spirit.' Everything he's mastered, he never offers to teach me; instead, when I try to learn, a barely perceptible contempt slips out. He needs my family's background to keep up appearances, yet behind closed doors he complains that my 'family superiority' suffocates him. And the most ironic thing? He never once thinks it was me who helped him turn the tables, me who made him. He believes he got to where he is entirely on his own ability and luck. That he could marry someone with better conditions—me—is just more proof of his own talent. He enjoys the dignity and security I bring while secretly chasing other women, taking it all for granted, cherishing nothing."
Her voice finally cracked into sobbing:
"I poured out all my energy, my resources, my possibilities to lift him up. I thought I was building a ship for us to sail far together. Instead, he stepped on my spine to climb ashore, then turned around and complained my spine wasn't straight enough, wasn't worthy of the view he was seeing. He kept crushing my confidence, denying my worth, manipulating my emotions. I got more and more drained, repressed for so long that I'm already seeing a therapist."
"I really hate myself for being such a poor judge of people! I could have matched with someone far better, but I chose him—worse than me in every way—drained myself dry to make an ungrateful man. This marriage—was I just blind, or is human nature simply this ugly to begin with? Right now, I honestly don't know what to do."
After a brief lag, the comments exploded:
[So real! Marrying down is a top-tier dimensional collapse into a trap!]
[Marrying down is the cruelest thing a woman can do to herself!]
[Craving emotional value and lifting a poor man out of poverty is a girl's biggest mistake!]
[What he loves isn't you—it's the resources behind you and that ladder!]
[You thought he'd be grateful; he thinks his charm is off the charts!]
[Every grievance of marrying down was bought by lowering your own bottom line in the first place!]
[Emotional value is the cheapest thing; money and ability are the real confidence!]
[It's true! Men don't cherish the woman who suffered with them—only the one they had to work hard to win!]
Yao Yuan leaned slightly forward, her eyes sharp and clear-eyed. No comforting platitudes—every word pierced straight to the essence, her tone calm to the point of cruelty:
"Let me tell you the most stabbing truth first," she began, her voice like a scalpel sliding across sterile cloth, cold and clear. "Every relationship built on collapsing down a dimension, on lifting a poorer partner from a lower position—the ending is always the same: it consumes you, it turns on you."
"Carve this sentence into your mind forever: human nature has no gratitude, only weighing of interests. When your conditions are better than the other person's, when you marry down by choice, lower your own worth, lift a weaker person up—in his eyes it is never 'how lucky I am.' It is 'I'm capable enough to match better resources.'"
"The first thing a weak person does after striking it rich isn't to be grateful for the companionship. It's to make up for his inferiority complex, to finally hold his head high, to push back and crush you in return. Because the inferiority buried deep in his bones needs to balance itself by trampling you, denying you, manipulating you."
"You craved his emotional value and ignored his underlying ability, his original framework, his level of cognition. The deep devotion you imagined you were giving—in the model of how the sexes spar, that's a high-value party offering downward compatibility, transfusing blood for free, devaluing herself."
"Emotional value is the least valuable thing of all. Sweet talk, gentleness, tolerance—anyone can perform those. But class, cognition, ability, wealth, vision—those are the sediment of decades. They can't be faked, can't be performed."
"Your biggest mistake was trading your own high value for someone else's cheap emotions; betting your own good life on one person's conscience. The most loss-making deal in love and marriage is a woman offering downward compatibility, lifting a poor man out of poverty."
She paused, then weighted her words, the logic crisp, dismantling it layer by layer, every line empowering:
"Second, here's an iron law of mate selection, fixed for every woman: when cognition differs, never settle; when the tier differs, never play poverty-relief. Better high-quality single than low-quality marrying down."
"The core of choosing a partner is value matching, upward empowerment, mutual achievement. Not you sacrificing yourself to fulfill the other person, not you draining your energy to haul someone ashore."
"If you can't find a perfectly aligned soulmate, you can absolutely choose pragmatically. Find someone whose tier, ability, and resources surpass yours—even without the ultimate emotional value, at least he can share the real-world pressure, the survival pressure, the child-rearing pressure."
"You free up all your time, energy, and emotion to focus on building your career, your assets, your growth. Marriage can be pragmatic, childbearing can be planned, but shining as yourself and continually appreciating in value is always your top priority."
"You have to understand one top-tier logic: a man's cherishing was never bought by you suffering alongside him. It's bought by your high value, your irreplaceability, and the confidence that you can walk away at any moment."
"As long as you keep shining, keep growing richer, keep moving up, your life always has a way out, a trump card, a choice. Drain yourself and offer downward compatibility, and all your life will have left is internal exhaustion, grievance, and being looked down on."
Her voice was calm, yet it held the power to cut through the fog:
"Remember everything I'm saying today:"
"Don't be a poverty-relief lover, don't be a low-position marriage;"
"Don't live off a man's conscience, only stand on your own assets;"
"Emotional value can exist, but it can never be the main course;"
"Gentleness can exist, but you must never throw away your edge and your confidence."
"A woman's highest clarity: stop consuming yourself, throw everything into making money, throw everything into growing. When you bloom, every good match, every bit of respect and favor will come rushing toward you."
The comments boiled over completely, screen after screen of resolve scrolling past:
[Stop marrying down! Go all in on making money! Move up for life!]
[Relying on others is worse than relying on yourself; savings are the top-tier sense of security!]
[Emotional value is worth nothing; real strength is the hard truth!]
[Clear and lucid! This is the cognition a woman should have!]
[No internal exhaustion, no poverty-relief, only upward! Holding this as a life principle!]
Yao Yuan spoke again, the tone shifting, her voice like a cold scalpel scraping across skin. "So, girl, the way I see it, your marriage story reads like a completely failed due diligence report on a personal-value investment. In this report, you misjudged the target (your husband), used the wrong valuation model (marriage), and most fatally of all—you staked your entire net worth without setting up any exit mechanism for yourself."
"So, how do you maximize the recovery of your losses from this 'failed investment'?" Yao Yuan's tone turned into a clear, cold operating manual. "Step one: immediately initiate 'bankruptcy liquidation.' Halt every form of 'additional investment' in him or in this relationship—including emotional attention, emotional rumination, and the self-interrogation of 'why did it turn out this way.' Accept that this investment has completely failed, the principal gone, nothing recoverable. Your pain doesn't come from the loss; it comes from your unwillingness to acknowledge the sunk cost you've already put in. A decisive cut is the only way to stop the bleeding."
"Step two: carry out a thorough 'restructuring of your personal balance sheet.' Your core assets have merely been badly undervalued and misappropriated, but your educational background, your work experience, even the deepest insight into human nature this marriage gave you—those are all hidden assets. Now, put every one of them on the books. Use your existing hidden assets to exchange for things that generate new cash flow: a solid skill, a side business that brings income, a healthy, strong body. Rebuilding your cash flow is the first cornerstone of rebuilding the say you have over your own life."
"Step three—and this is the ultimate rule for all future decisions: build and fiercely defend your 'value-investment moat' and your 'risk-control bottom line.' From now on, examine any relationship—family, friendship, love—through this model: 1. Value matching: can the other person provide hard value roughly equal to yours, and sustainable—resources, cognition, growth support? 2. Risk controllable: does what you give have clear boundaries, and can you independently bear the worst outcome? 3. Exit unobstructed: do you always keep the ability to survive and the psychological capacity to turn and leave at any moment?"
At the end, Yao Yuan's voice turned deep and full of power—not comfort, but the handing-over of a weapon:
"Girl, you're not blind. You simply once believed the lovely fairy tale of human nature, the one about 'gratitude' and 'growing together.' The reality is that marriage and love are often a more naked, less regulated arena of human gaming than the financial markets. Here, emotion is the lubricant, but interest is the steering wheel, value is the passport, and the confidence to leave is the cash flow that never goes bankrupt."
"What is a woman's highest form of confidence? It's not marrying the right person. It's having the capital and ability to hit reset with one tap after marrying wrong—even to live better. That capital comes from the ever-growing number in your bank account, from the irreplaceable cognition in your brain, from your unshakable faith in your own value. Making money, building skills, guarding your heart—it isn't to crush anyone. It's so you never have to entrust your fate to another person's 'repaying kindness' or 'pang of conscience.'"
"Now, hang up the phone. Go do three things: 1. Find the best lawyer and maximize the protection of your residual assets in this 'failed investment.' 2. From today on, convert every minute you spend obsessing over him and draining yourself into an equivalent amount of money, then force yourself to earn it back. 3. Stand in front of the mirror and tell yourself: I am not a loser. I'm just an investor who paid an expensive tuition, finally graduated for good, and from now on makes only clear-eyed, sovereign investments for her own life."
[Call ended.]
Yao Yuan slowly took a sip of the black coffee at her side, her gaze sweeping over the still-boiling comments on the screen, then turning to the Yellow River beyond the window, the water that never looks back. Muddy, deep, carrying all things—and burying all things too.
She turned back to face the camera, lowered her eyes slightly, and closed with an even, unhurried tone:
"Never blame yourself for poor judgment. You were only too kind, too starved of being favored. From this day on, put away the soft heart, quit settling, give up poverty-relief. Making money is the most noble thing; independence is the most dignified; shining is the most free. Your life is for pleasing only yourself, for achieving only yourself."

Chapter 96 — Once You've Seen Top-Tier Devotion, Your Standards Outrun Your Means
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640343.html

"The first operation on the table is complete." She faced the camera, her voice neither glad nor mournful, drawing a calm rest-stop at the end of a violent emotional dissection. "The year-end special of the 'Clear-Eyed Operating Table' continues. Little assistant, put the next caller through."
[Call connected]
The caller—a woman's voice, twenty-six, anxious and tangled, full of contradiction, reluctance, and self-confusion: "Teacher Yuanyuan, I'm honestly so lost. I want to ask you how on earth I'm supposed to choose a partner. My own circumstances are very ordinary—family, job, looks—nothing about me stands out. But my fatal problem is exactly that ordinariness, because I've experienced what 'not ordinary' is like."
She drew a deep breath, as if gathering the courage to touch something forbidden.
"A few years ago, by sheer chance, I dated a man worth billions, a real heavyweight in his circle. He was genuinely, incredibly generous with me—so lavish that I still can't believe it when I look back. While we were together he bought me a car, a house, made huge transfers, covered my every need—food, clothing, housing, travel, all of it. He doted on me beyond measure, tolerated everything, and he was serious—he openly said he wanted to marry me, that he was in it to build a life together. In that relationship I fully tasted what top-tier giving, top-tier favoritism, top-tier sincerity from a man feels like."
Her voice began to tremble, whether from longing or from pain:
"Later, because of certain practical and family reasons, we split. But for the past few years I've kept going on blind dates, trying to settle down. And now I'm completely warped—I can't take to anyone."
"The men my relatives and friends introduce me to honestly aren't bad: steady jobs, dependable temperaments, sincerely willing to marry and build a life. Everyone, my parents included, says I should be content, that I should treasure it."
"But I can't. The moment one of them quibbles over the price of a meal, I'm turned off. The moment they fail to read my moods precisely, I find them dull. The moment they mention mortgage pressure while planning the future, I feel suffocated. Every reaction I have, the measuring stick in my head, is unconsciously, wildly benchmarking against that heavyweight—against how cleanly he solved problems by just throwing money at them, against his forever-steady, forgiving calm, against that 'if the sky falls, I'll hold it up' sense of total cover, even against that unhesitating sincerity of 'I simply want to marry you.'"
"I know perfectly well what I'm worth. I know I can't reach that tier, that I have not the slightest ability to match that kind of life. But my eyes, my instincts, my threshold—they've been raised too spoiled to ever go back. Now I earn a few thousand a month myself, yet I think men on a million-a-year salary are shabby. Teacher Yuanyuan, I know my standards outrun my means, I know this is wrong, but I can't control that enormous psychological gap and disappointment. What on earth do I do? Have I ruined my whole life like this?"
[After a brief stunned silence, the scrolling comments surged like a burst dam:]
[The exact same illness! Once you've seen the top spec, ordinary people just don't register.]
[So real! Once you've eaten a royal banquet, how do you go back to chaff and pickles?]
[The cruelest thing isn't never having had it—it's having had the best, then losing it.]
[Her own rank didn't rise, but a heavyweight dragged her taste and threshold sky-high.]
[A heavyweight gives "no worries about survival" love; ordinary people give "above-survival" love. Different dimensions.]
[Wake up! The treatment a heavyweight gives is the heavyweight's ability, not her standard package!]
[One jackpot win, and she mistakes herself for someone born to live a billionaire's life.]
Yao Yuan listened to the whole thing coolly, with no sympathy, then gave a light laugh, the smile cold, clear, and see-through:
"Let me first puncture all your self-delusions—plenty of women crash at this exact step in the emotional market. Your biggest problem right now is that you've confused 'the other person's ability' with 'your own standard-package value.'"
"Let me run a brutal cognitive disinfection on you first." Her fingertips met out of habit, bracing into a steady triangle, her gaze like a surgical shadowless lamp lighting up every corner of self-deceit.
"That heavyweight you describe—the 'generosity, wealth, total cover, firm intent to marry' he displayed—is his baseline cultivation, a show of his ability, his way of operating. It is not a deserved, sustainable reward that you earned by reaching some peak of personal value."
"It's like this: an ordinary person wanders by chance into a private museum and is allowed to handle a priceless antique with their own hands. That experience is real; the touch is stunning. But the mistake is this—after leaving the museum, you start to find your family's heirloom porcelain bowl crude and unbearable, and you demand to know why every piece of porcelain on the market is unworthy of your touch."
"You've confused 'experience' with 'entitlement,' 'a chance encounter' with 'the norm.' Everything he gave is the basic operating rule of his world. And you were only a guest, invited in by chance for a brief tour. An admission ticket is not a deed of ownership."
[Dense waves of "got it" and "right in the heart" drifted across the comments.]
[An admission ticket is not a deed of ownership! That line is worth a million!]
[His world's baseline rules, my world's ceiling experience.]
[So the pain comes from mistaking someone else's starting line for your own passing grade.]
"Everything you 'can't take to,' everything that 'turns you off,' all your 'pickiness,'" Yao Yuan went on at an even pace, yet bearing down word by word, like a hammer driving nails into wood, "stems from this: you are using an extreme set of standards about 'love and giving'—standards drawn from a top-tier survival system—to harshly judge one healthy, normal sample after another, each running inside an ordinary survival system."
"The love of ordinary people is, at its core, crossing over together. It's two limited pools of resources added together to fend off risk; it's two imperfect temperaments grinding into each other to find a comfort zone; it's the slow-flowing companionship and the carefully budgeted future. Its keyword is 'we, together.'"
"The love of top-tier people is, at its core, giving. It's using his surplus resources to cover your shortfalls; it's using his powerful system to absorb your fluctuations; it's one-sided lifting and bestowing. Its keyword is 'I give you.'"
"You've experienced the latter's 'giving mode,' and now you can no longer adapt to the former's 'crossing-over mode.' But here's the fatal cognitive trap: you think your inability to adapt is because 'crossing-over mode' isn't good enough. In reality, it's because you yourself do not hold the long-term entry pass or the playing ability for the 'giving mode' game. That one time, the system issued you a trial card. The card expired, and the game was supposed to end. Yet you stubbornly stand in the arcade doorway, convinced that every other amusement in the world is unbearably boring."
[The clever metaphor detonated the comments again.]
[Crossing-over mode vs giving mode! Instantly crystal clear!]
[A trial-card player mistaking herself for a lifetime VIP.]
[So it's not that ordinary people give too little—it's that what she wants is no longer the kind ordinary people can give.]
"The essence of your 'standards outrunning your means' is that your own value coordinate is still parked on the ordinary plain, while the threshold of your emotional experience was hauled, in one irreversible stroke, up to the clouds." Yao Yuan's analysis cut deeper, cold and precise. "The reality you won't face is this: that top-spec relationship was an extremely fluky moment in your life—a brief compatibility, a downward projection from a high-dimensional system onto a low-dimensional individual. It's like the sun occasionally focusing its light on a single grain of sand. The grain blazes in an instant, but that brilliance belongs to the sun's power, not the grain's essence. The sun moves away, and the grain is a grain again—yet it can no longer tolerate the brightness of an ordinary candle flame."
"Your contradiction is this: retreat to the plain, and you can no longer stand the ordinary scenery; fly to the clouds, and you have no wings. You're stuck in between, using the standard of the clouds to despise everything on the plain, while forgetting that your own two feet have never truly left the ground."
[The comments filled with painful resonance and awakening.]
[A downward projection from a high-dimensional system onto a low-dimensional individual... so precise, it hurts to the bone.]
[The sun and grain of sand metaphor—incredible, I am that dazed grain.]
[So the problem isn't high standards; it's that her own "dimension" didn't rise, yet she wants "high-dimensional" treatment.]
"So, how do we treat this?" Yao Yuan's tone shifted into a clear, cold, hard solution mode, as if writing a prescription.
"Plan A: total transformation, match upward. Stop all self-pity and fantasy. Recognize that the one truly useful thing that encounter left you is not consumer habits, not aesthetic pickiness, but a fleeting glimpse of 'how the top tier of the world operates,' and a powerful inner drive of 'I will at least move toward that direction.' Convert all your unwillingness into terrifying self-discipline: work yourself to the bone improving your professional skills until you're a leader in your field; ferociously accumulate social capital and cognitive depth; hone your character and your scope. Make your own value truly worthy of the spoiled eye you've been given. This road is brutally hard, but it's the only cure that gets to the root—if you still want to touch that world."
"Plan B: system reset, put down roots. If you assess yourself and conclude you can't endure Plan A's suffering, that you lack the fate and the grit for it, then you must perform a complete formatting reset on your 'spoiled' emotional system. Actively, consciously, set out to understand, learn, even 'downgrade-adapt' to ordinary people's 'crossing-over mode.' Learn to appreciate the solidity of two people saving up to buy a house together; learn to feel the intimacy of solving one small headache as a pair; learn to discover the tiny romances in ordinary days. This requires you to fully set down the obsession with 'being given' and learn the joy of 'creating together.' This road is a compromise, but for you it may be the more pragmatic choice, the one more likely to bring peace."
"The stupidest, most painful path, doomed to end with nothing, is to stay in your current state: guarding the body of the plain while dreaming the dreams of the clouds, using the standard of the clouds to curse everything in front of you. That is self-imposed punishment."
Yao Yuan's voice settled at the end, carrying an almost cruel calm—the calm of one who has lifted the veil on the truth:
"Stop using that heavyweight's cultivation and ability as a ruler to measure every ordinary man who sincerely wants to build a life with you. It isn't fair, and it's utterly meaningless."
"You've seen the light. That's good. That beam of light should become the fuel that drives you to run forward, to try to shine on your own—not the reason you can never again appreciate, never again belong to, ordinary lamplight."
"Remember: top-tier experiences are meant to broaden your vision, not raise your threshold; meant to make you reflect on yourself, not despise the masses. When you yourself become a steady, glowing light source, you'll naturally attract—and deserve—another beam of light, one that is real and lasting in its favor."
[Call ended]
Cold white light filled the studio. Yao Yuan sat ramrod straight, her face calm and clear, her voice steady and sharp: "The biggest mistake many women make in this life isn't never meeting a good man. It's seeing the top spec once, never raising their own rank, yet letting their eye be spoiled. That's exactly how so many people end up with standards that outrun their means."
The broadcast went on. Outside, the northern winter dusk had long since been swallowed by thick night; the bitter wind was sealed off by heavy glass, leaving only a muffled moan. The city's neon ran riot with color, blurring into a cold, flowing brilliance on the window behind her, like an unreal backdrop cut off from the rational world inside. She sat upright before the camera. Hours of continuous output across the livestream had left not a trace of fatigue on her exquisitely made-up face—instead, it had refined her into a stillness so complete it became detachment.

========================================

Chapter 97 — Make a Living First, Then Make Love: Three Lessons for a Life
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640344.html

"Alright. Let's take tonight's final call-in." She spoke calmly, without a ripple, no emotion to be read in her voice—as if reciting a fixed procedure.
[Caller connected]
Caller (a woman's voice, 28, hoarse, edged with obvious agitation and despair): "Teacher Yuanyuan… save me. I broke up almost three months ago, and I feel like I'm rotting from the inside out."
Her voice began to shake as she tried to describe the state she was in—incoherent, numbed by a kind of self-loathing:
"For those three months, I just couldn't work. By day I'd stare at the computer screen, my head full of the last thing he said to me; at night I couldn't sleep, scrolling through every one of his social-media posts. My project reports slipped later and later, and when I had to present in front of everyone I babbled. My boss called me in three times. The last time he said, 'You need to take a break.' I didn't. I couldn't. Then a key data point I was responsible for had a fatal error, and it nearly cost the company a million-yuan deal."
She paused, and when she spoke again her voice was utterly hollow:
"I quit. I wasn't laid off—I just couldn't face staying. I thought quitting would let me focus on 'processing' my feelings, but it got worse. I maxed out two credit cards buying dresses he'd once said looked good on me, going to places he might go to 'run into' him, even… believing the nonsense those relationship outfits sell, paying over thirty thousand yuan to learn some 'how to win him back' technique. Now I have no job, no savings, I'm in debt, and every day I lie in bed scrolling through his posts from the small hours until dawn. I watch my old coworkers get promoted, travel, show off their lives. I don't dare answer my parents' calls, don't dare see my friends. I feel like I'm rotten through and through, stinking from the inside out. Teacher, am I beyond saving? I know I should pull myself together, but the moment I think of him, think of how I've lost him forever, the grief crushes me all over again…"
[The scrolling comments are instantly flooded with "my heart breaks for you" and "I've been there"]
[ Oh my god, I think I just saw last year's me…]
[ Losing your job is brutal—once your financial base collapses, it's even harder to get your spirit back on its feet.]
[ The worst part of a breakup is losing the person, the money, and your soul all at once.]
[ Don't cry, sis—Teacher Yuanyuan will have a plan!]
Yao Yuan did not rush to comfort her. Only after the last trembling note of the caller's voice had struggled into silence over the line did she lean forward slightly, her gaze a calm, steady searchlight cutting through the screen. "Cried enough? Good. Tears are the blood of emotion—better out than pooled inside you, festering into a tumor. But once this minute of crying is over, I want you to wipe your face and listen carefully to every word I'm about to say."
(She paused, waiting until the caller's sobbing faded.)
"All your pain, your collapse, your self-loathing right now—the root of it isn't 'losing him.' It's that the moment you lost him, you severed the main lifeline of 'making a living.' You made love the entire structure of your life, and 'making a living'—your job, your savings, your social ties, your personal order—you tore down the load-bearing walls of that building yourself, brick by brick, until it was rubble. Now the main structure—the love you imagined—has collapsed, and you're lying in the wreckage, feeling cold, feeling hopeless. This isn't the aftermath of heartbreak. This is the most direct physical reaction to having your survival foundation hollowed out. You're not heartbroken. Your whole life has stalled out, lost power, and is about to be forcibly evicted for unpaid bills."
[An uproar in the comments—some find it harsh, more find it spot-on]
[ Tearing them down yourself… the load-bearing walls…]
[ So the deepest damage wasn't dealt by him—she finished it with her own hands.]
[ Survival foundation hollowed out—god, that line chilled me to the bone.]
"Let's do a clear attribution," Yao Yuan said, her pace even but every word a hammer striking against the walls of false belief. "A relationship ended. That's normal. The way a healthy adult handles a breakup is this: allow yourself to grieve, then repair while you keep running along a stable life track. Like a car with a flat tire—you pull over, put on the spare, and keep driving toward your destination."
"And you—what did you do? You decided the flat tire meant the car wasn't good enough. So you ripped out the engine, smashed the steering wheel, drained the fuel tank, then sat down beside a heap of scrap metal and wept about how hard the road is."
"You set making a living—your job, your income, your social value—completely against making love—your emotional needs—and without a moment's hesitation you sacrificed the former on the altar of the latter. That isn't devotion. That's stupidity, and it's an extreme failure of responsibility to yourself."
She let the cruel verdict settle, then turned to clear, cold marching orders:
"Now, stop every form of self-pity and rumination. You don't need comfort. What you need is an emergency plan for rebuilding after the disaster."
"Step one: execute 'financial and information isolation' immediately. 1. List all your debts, savings, and remaining assets on a single sheet of paper. See exactly how far you are from your survival line giving way. 2. Permanently delete every channel that lets you track any news of him. That isn't a love memory—it's a malicious program blocking your brain's recovery. 3. Cut off all spending that isn't essential to survival. You've lost the right to splurge."
"Step two: launch the 'minimum survival guarantee' program. Your one and only top priority right now is to restore cash flow. Forget your former title, your face, your career plan. Your goal is to find any legal work that brings in income before your next rent is due. Delivering food, working as a shop assistant, picking up odd jobs—there's no shame in that. Going hungry, getting thrown out, going bankrupt on your credit—that's the shame, that's the real dead end. Keep yourself alive first. That's the baseline duty you owe yourself as the sole person responsible for your own life."
"Step three: on top of survival, launch 'value-system repair.' Once you're no longer in danger of starving or freezing, set aside a fixed time each day to answer one question: What is the core skill I make my living with? How much has it depreciated? How do I make it appreciate again at the lowest possible cost? Go learn—even the cheapest online course. Go practice—even if it means starting over from the most basic cases. This process will be long and slow, but every bit of progress lays new foundation on the rubble of your life."
[Many people in the comments quietly start taking down the steps]
[ This isn't feel-good fluff—these are surgical steps to save your life.]
[ Financial isolation, survival guarantee, value repair—got it.]
[ I suddenly went calm. I know what to do now.]
"I know you'll ask: why is this so hard? Why can other people get over a breakup so fast, while I destroyed everything?" Yao Yuan's gaze deepened, moving into a more fundamental explanation. "Because your underlying operating system has the wrong survival priorities preset into it. And tonight, I want to use three mirrors to help you see that error clearly—and reinstall your system."
"The first mirror: Qiong Yao—the ultimate risk sample of the 'love above all' system." Her tone held no praise or blame, only the calm of a scalpel.
"Her whole life she wrote, lived by, and deeply believed that 'love transcends everything.' Her story with Mr. Ping Xintao was the real-life version of the romance-defies-convention tales she penned. But you should go read the last few pages of that story. When the ledger of his life was about to close, at the final reckoning, the partner she had poured all her feeling into, fought the whole world to 'win,' made the choice most aligned with traditional social rules and real-world interest in dividing his estate: he left the substantive wealth to his own blood children."
She paused, letting the fact echo in the silence.
"What does that mean? It means the 'world-shaking love' she spent a lifetime burning for, singing of, and building her career upon may never have truly shaken his core hierarchy of values or his ultimate framework of interests—a framework whose underlying code was always blood, duty, and real-world security. She got the title of love, the copyright to the story, even the prosperity of the publishing empire they built together. But in the end, in the most symbolically meaningful transfer of substantive wealth, she was drawn outside the category of 'one's own people.' This cruelly reveals: in the face of cold, real-world property rights and blood-based inheritance law, the protective power of even the most extreme romantic narrative can be as fragile as paper. Her case is a warning bell: when you stake your entire life on the narrative of 'being loved' and treat that as your only proof of value, you may well find, at the final real-world settlement, that all you're left holding is a stack of deeply moving but unredeemable old scripts—while the real hard currency completed its preordained course long ago, in a dimension you couldn't see."
"Qiong Yao's dream tells you love is great. But when the dream ends, cold reality will tell you this: whoever holds the means of production and the property rights holds the final say over the story—and the final security."
"The second mirror: Yi Shu—the demonstration of the rational architecture of 'make a living first, then make love.'" Yao Yuan's tone took on a cold, almost admiring rationality.
"People say she's cold-hearted, but her coldness is of a higher order. Her core algorithm is crystal clear: make a living first, then make love. Making a living is building the hardware of your life—your financial independence, your professional skills, your coordinates in society. Making love is the lovely application running on top of that stable hardware. Only when the hardware is powerful and stable can the application run smoothly—stable enough, even, that you can run several at once and choose at your leisure."
"That line from her character Xi Bao—'I want lots and lots of love. If there's no love, then lots and lots of money. If there's neither, well, health is good too.'—is treated by many as a classic quote."
Yao Yuan picked up the book at her elbow. "What's the bedrock of a Yi Shu heroine? That 'the most important thing in life is to carry yourself well.' And what does carrying yourself well rely on? Not a man's pity. It relies on having money in your own pocket, a plan in your own head, a road under your own feet. A woman is like a flower; and a flower that wants to bloom—vivid, dignified, free—has to be nourished by money. Tenderness, devotion, companionship with no material foundation beneath them are all castles in the air. Broke up? You can shut your door and cry for one night. But the next day the sun rises as usual, and you have to put on your makeup, go to work, answer your emails, and keep the number in your account climbing steadily. Yi Shu tells you: love is only the icing on the cake."
"Let me correct a fatal misconception every woman should hear: many people think making money is vulgar, that being money-minded is wrong. Dead wrong. Stop letting the poisonous fluff of 'talking about money is crass' brainwash you. Making money and having ideals are never in conflict—they reinforce each other. The more you can earn, the more choices you have. The more assets you hold, the less you depend on anyone. The wealthier you are, the less you'll wrong yourself for a scrap of emotional value."
"Dou Wentao said it on Round Table: 'When we were young we all fancied ourselves the type to see money as dirt. But after going through some things, we understood—money was never dirt; we are. Earning money legally isn't crass in the slightest, and it isn't the least bit shameful. Being able to make money on your own ability is, in fact, an adult's dignity.' So understand this: money is the security firewall of your personal system, the passport to your freedom of choice, the line of calm, effective 'access denied' code you can run when you face any injustice."
"Yi Shu's own road in love was hardly smooth, but through a single pen—clear-headed, diligent, fiercely disciplined—she earned herself 'lots and lots of money' and absolute autonomy over her life. Her 'cold-heartedness' is a strategic protection of her own life energy and attention. The result: she may never have tasted the searing, peak love of a Qiong Yao romance, but she was thereby exempt from the risk of being swallowed by an emotional quagmire, spared the humiliation of becoming a sacrifice when interests came to a head, and granted a lifelong, stable dignity, high output, and autonomy of the soul. She proved that when the lamp of 'love' may go out, gripping tight the bundle of firewood that is 'money'—your own capacity to create value—is the best and most reliable strategy for keeping the fire of your life burning."
[The comments erupt in fierce agreement]
[Money isn't dirt! Only the incompetent see money as dirt!]
[A woman is a flower and really does need to be nourished by money! So true!]
[Emotional value can lie to you—savings and ability never will!]
[Making money is the lifelong career; a man is just the icing!]
[Women who won't chase money or admit they value it end up swallowing their pride in marriage and love!]
"The third mirror: Eileen Chang—the lonely, ultimate form of the 'insight into human nature' system, and a specimen of love's disillusionment." Yao Yuan's voice dropped low, carrying the cold precision of a museum docent.
"She saw the calculation, the desolation, the illusion in human nature and love down to the marrow. In her writing there was never any wishful thinking: love is the fleas crawling beneath a magnificent qipao, the eternal ruin left after a city falls for one love affair. But her tragedy is this: she saw through the barrenness beneath all tenderness, yet failed—or found it nearly impossible, in that era—to build for herself a strong system of 'making a living' that depended on no one and could carry that clarity. Her own relationship with Hu Lancheng is the most painful and most truthful footnote to her cold philosophy—even one as brilliant and clear-eyed as she could sink low into the dust in love; and his promiscuity and betrayal merely confirmed, in the cruelest way, her pessimistic expectations of human nature."
"In her later years she chose to live apart from the world, nearly in total isolation, carrying that detachment to the very end of her life. Her strategy was this: since she had long seen through the calculations of interest and the perishability of feeling beneath every veil of tenderness, she would no longer hold unrealistic illusions about any person or relationship. She actively shut off the emotional interfaces of deep dependence and expectation in exchange for absolute independence of the inner world and purity of perception. Toward love she was, her whole life, a thorough deconstructionist and a pessimistic prophet. She gained a depth and clarity no one could match, and avoided the possibility of being deceived by illusion. But this stance of extreme defense—even self-exile—came at the price of eternal detachment and an inerasable desolation in the base color of her life. She is a proudly lonely lighthouse: logically self-consistent, never falling. But beneath the lighthouse there is only the vast sea and the endless echo of solitude."
"Eileen Chang is an awe-inspiring cautionary mirror: merely 'seeing through' the illusions of love is not enough. Without the solid footing that 'making a living' provides, seeing through only deepens the pain instead of leading to release."
"Eileen Chang's famous line: 'Life is a magnificent qipao, and love is the fleas crawling beneath it.' But I want to tell you, girl: you have to be able to earn that magnificent robe yourself first. As for the fleas—the troubles of love, the setbacks of life—once you have a solid robe, you'll naturally have the mood and the means to clean them off, little by little. When you're standing there half-dressed and shivering, the thing you should be thinking about isn't whether the embroidery on the robe is pretty. It's how to find the cloth first, so you can stay alive and stay warm."
Yao Yuan drew back her gaze and faced the camera head-on, as if to see through the screen straight into that soul curled up in the rubble:
"Three women, three survival algorithms. Qiong Yao is a romantic single process—high energy cost, fragile permissions. Yi Shu is steady multithreading—heavy defense, sovereignty intact. Eileen Chang is an absolute inner loop—zero dependencies, at the cost of utter solitude."
"The road you took before was Qiong Yao's. Now you lie at the end of it, having tasted the bitter fruit in full."
"Eileen Chang's road is a possible destination once you see everything clearly—but it's too cold there, too desolate."
"The only road you should choose, and the only one you can choose now, is Yi Shu's—first become a fanatical builder of your own infrastructure, then think about a poetic dwelling for the spirit."
Her voice gathered, at the last, into a calm and boundless force:
"So, to answer your first question: are you still beyond saving?"
"You're not. But what saves you won't be any love, any feel-good fluff, any outside force. What saves you can only be your own two hands—hands that can work, can earn, can clear away the rubble bit by bit—and that brain of yours that has finally agreed to set 'survive, and live well' as the highest priority of your life."
"From tonight, from this moment, treat yourself as the sole CEO, chief engineer, and fully responsible party of the project that is your life. Your company—your life—is currently insolvent, on the brink of bankruptcy. Your job is not to sit in the failed office and weep about how heartless your competitor—your ex—is. It's to walk out the door right now, land the first piece of business that brings money in, pay next month's rent, and keep the company—you—alive first."
"Love is a 'strategic partnership' or 'joint venture' between two healthy, thriving 'life limited companies,' based on appreciation and joy. It is never—and should never be—a 'bankruptcy-restructuring plan' or 'rescue financing' that one near-bankrupt 'company' begs from another."
"Get your own company running well first—a healthy balance sheet, stable cash flow. Only then will you have the right, and the confidence, to consider whether to enter a new 'partnership,' and with whom."
The sobbing on the other end of the line had long since stopped. There was only heavy breathing, and one quiet line, soft but weighted with force: "…I understand. Thank you, Teacher."
[Call ended]
Yao Yuan gave a slight nod, and spoke tonight's closing words to the camera:
"Remember: on the balance sheet of your life, your capacity to make a living is the permanent core asset—it's the '1.' Love, respect, happiness, freedom—those are all the '0's that follow. Without that '1' up front, no matter how many '0's come after, your life still adds up to zero."
"May we both forge that solid '1' with our own hands. Everything after it only has meaning then."
"My dears, that's where we'll end tonight's broadcast. I wish you all a smooth and happy year in the new year to come."
With that, Yao Yuan reached out a finger and pressed the red "End Broadcast" button, clean and decisive.
The screen went black in an instant, swallowing all the scrolling text and the virtual clamor.
The ring of fill lights went dark one by one, giving off a faint electrical hum like a sigh. In the end, only the antique-style desk lamp in the corner remained, spreading a small, warm, quiet pool of light.
Only now did the immense exhaustion seem to break free of every restraint of reason and well up, gentle and heavy. The expression she'd worn for hours—sharp as a drawn blade—slowly melted and faded. In its place came a deep calm, and beneath that calm, a vast emptiness no one would ever see.
The room was very quiet. Quiet enough to hear the gurgle of the humidifier's circulating water, the bitter wind of the northern winter night blurred behind the glass. And beneath the wind, the never-ceasing flow of the Yellow River—thick, all-embracing, free of any joy or anger, simply existing, simply moving forward, sweeping along endless time and silt, as if silently digesting and carrying all the despair, the sudden understanding, and the resolve to be reborn that had poured out of this small broadcast room tonight.
She sat quietly in the dim glow, watching her own rich, still silhouette in the black glass of the window across from her.
A long while.
Ever so faintly she tugged at the corner of her lips—like the almost cold clarity of confirming, after a precise surgery that drained the last of one's strength, that the diseased tissue had been removed and the system could reboot.
At last she rose slowly and walked to the window.
Outside was the all-devouring winter night, thick as ink, and that great river—unseen yet forever present, flowing on through the ages.
She knew there were still countless souls swept along by the silt of feeling, longing to receive from her a "filter"—perhaps sharp, perhaps painful.
And she would still sit here. Behind her, that silent Yellow River, carrying down silt and all, yet surging magnificently forward.
With the most clear-eyed and most cruel language, she would forge such "filters."
And then hand them to every person who, in the dark, finally decided to reach out a hand.
As for whether each of them could use that filter to sift out their own "true gold," and in this ceaseless river of life build a boat that would not sink, would not drift—a boat of their own—
that answer lay in every act of restraint that said "deal with work first," in every income earned "for myself," in every late night that said "I can stand firm on my own."

========================================

Chapter 98 — A New Year's Hotpot and Family Talk: Mother and Daughter Open Their Hearts and Recall the Road They Came By
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640345.html

A New Year's Hotpot and Family Talk: Mother and Daughter Open Their Hearts and Recall the Road They Came By
The last livestream of the year ended on a perfect, settled note.
The blazing studio lights dimmed one bank at a time, and the buzz of voices slowly ebbed away. The team, worn out from a full day's work, shrugged off their fatigue, wished one another a happy and healthy New Year, packed up the equipment, double-checked the closing numbers, and set off with light steps toward their own New Year's celebrations. In an instant the vast studio hall fell silent, leaving only the faint warmth of lights gone dark, a quiet that settled over all the heat and bustle of the day.
Yao Yuan glanced at her phone. On the screen, invitation after invitation to New Year's gatherings popped up, lively and bright, wrapped in all the grand ceremony of a worldly holiday. She swiped past them with a smile, not lingering.
Yao Yuan glanced at her phone. A few invitations from friends' gatherings jumped up; she swiped past them with a smile, not lingering. She opened the pinned conversation. Zhao Yiming's message from noon was still sitting there: "The Pretty Big One finished its third round of model runs. The numbers are beautiful. Come by the company tomorrow morning? We should sit down and really talk through the pricing model."
She wrote back a simple "Sounds good," and the corners of her mouth curved up without her meaning them to. They hadn't made plans for tonight, but her heart felt steady. There seemed to be an understanding between them already: toward those holidays that get hyped up until you feel you must "do something," they both kept an ordinary, unbothered attitude. Save your energy for what actually matters — like the model they'd nail down tomorrow, the one that decided the future of the "Pretty Big One." Building out a business map side by side, walking toward a future you could actually see land — that was the steadiest, most lasting thing to run toward.
The other message was from Zhang Fengxia, timestamped half an hour ago: "Come over when you're done streaming. Your Uncle Zhou saved the best cucumber-cut and lamb shoulder for you. We're waiting."
No extra words, and yet it warmed her heart more than any lively invitation. She replied "On my way," grabbed her coat, and headed out.
Driving through the deepening dusk, she passed shops on both sides of the street all decked out in festive cheer. Stopped at a red light, she saw a father by the roadside carrying a little girl on his shoulders; the girl was waving a glittering balloon, her laughter ringing out. Yao Yuan watched and found herself smiling softly too. These simple, lively little fragments of life didn't feel noisy to her just then — they had a vivid, lovable quality instead.
The three-story building of "Zhou's Lucky-Year Hotpot" blazed with light along the street, a warm magnet drawing in everyone who came looking for a good meal. Before she'd even parked, she could see through the fogged-up first-floor windows the shifting silhouettes of people inside, the place packed and bustling. She pushed open the door and a wave of warmth rolled out to meet her — rich sesame paste, fresh lamb, the sweet sharpness of sugar garlic, carried on a tide of clamoring voices — and the cold lifted off her in an instant.
"Aiyo, Yuanyuan, you made it!" Zhou Lufei was standing by the counter chatting with a regular, and the moment he spotted her his whole face broke into a grin. He had on a brand-new dark-red sweater today that brought out his color and made him look especially spry. "Was the traffic bad? Come on up, your seat's been saved a while now — your mom's asked after you twice already."
He led Yao Yuan toward the second floor; even the stairs had been hung with festive little lanterns. As they climbed he couldn't help sharing his joy, his voice low but every word brimming with happiness. "Guess what? Just like you taught me — no reciting a script on the stream, just chatting like family. These past few months the set menus online have been selling like crazy! Revenue's up by this much over last month!" He spread out five fingers and gave them a shake, the crow's-feet at his eyes crinkling up in a smile. "Lots of young customers order online and then bring their friends down to eat in. So lively!
