The restaurant business is tough these days, but this old place of ours, plus your new idea — we've steadied things, and we're doing even better than before!"
Yao Yuan listened, her heart brightening too. "It's because you've got a solid foundation here, Uncle Zhou — honest meat, a hard-earned reputation. All I did was help 'pass' your good word along."
"It's not as simple as passing a word along," Zhou Lufei said earnestly. "You taught me how to 'open up' to friends I've never even met. Honest talk — it works better than any fancy advertisement!"
The little window booth on the second floor was quiet, with a good view. Zhang Fengxia was already sitting there, a small copper pot in front of her bubbling with clear broth, a few red dates and goji berries bobbing up and down. When she saw them come in, she lifted her eyes. "You're here? Pour yourself some pear soup, it's still warm." Her tone was as flat as ever, but her hands had already picked up the serving chopsticks, fishing a heaping spoonful of tender bean sprouts and golden fried tofu puffs out of the big simmering pot beside her and setting them in the dipping bowl in front of Yao Yuan.
"Your mom timed it just right to stew that. She knows streaming wears out your throat, says this is the most soothing thing." Zhou Lufei sat down cheerfully. A server quickly and deftly brought over glossy, fragrant sesame paste, freshly fried chili oil, and a few plates of crisp cold dishes.
Yao Yuan sat and took a sip of the warm pear soup; its clean sweetness slid all the way from her throat to her stomach, and the faint dryness from an afternoon of talking was smoothed away at once. She picked up a chopstickful of bean sprouts coated in sesame paste and put it in her mouth — the flavor was wonderful.
"Everything still going smoothly at the company?" Zhang Fengxia asked, even as she slipped hand-cut fresh lamb into the rolling clear broth. The lamb was sliced paper-thin, its red-and-white grain like plum blossoms fallen on snow; it cooked the instant it touched the broth, and she scooped it up with practiced ease, sliding most of it onto Yao Yuan's plate.
"Smooth. It's going well." Yao Yuan ate the meat her mother had served her. The lamb was so tender it hardly needed chewing, and the rich aroma of sesame paste melting in her mouth was a solid, genuine satisfaction.
"As long as it's smooth." Zhang Fengxia nodded and asked nothing more, then used a strainer to swish a couple of slices of tripe in the spicy pot beside her and dropped them in Zhou Lufei's bowl. "You eat too, don't just keep talking."
Zhou Lufei chuckled and picked up a chopstickful of his proudest sugar garlic for Yao Yuan. "Try this. It's your mom's new recipe this year — sweet-and-sour, crisp, and unbeatable for cutting the grease."
A few plates of meat and vegetables down, warmth spread through them, and the conversation opened up more. Zhou Lufei talked eagerly about his plans for spring. "I'm thinking I'll fix up that little rooftop terrace on the third floor, put up a grape trellis, and in summer do some cold dishes and beer. The young folks are sure to love it."
"You and your ideas." Zhang Fengxia shot him a glance, but for all she said it, she didn't argue, and she fished out another piece of frozen tofu soaked full of broth for Yao Yuan.
"Business is like living your life — you've got to keep pushing forward, keep something to look toward." Zhou Lufei took a satisfied sip of barley tea and looked at Yao Yuan, his eyes gentle. "Oh, and your mom's sugar garlic — lots of customers ask if they can buy some to take home after they've tasted it. After the New Year, I want to find a reliable factory to talk to, see if we can make it into small packages, so the folks who love that flavor can have it at home too."
Yao Yuan listened, watching the hope for the future in Uncle Zhou's eyes and the quiet, capable care her mother put into everything, and the thoughts that had been circling in her mind — over the stream, over the Mirror Pact, over all the unresolved things — were gradually softened and smoothed flat by this honest, steaming warmth. This was the most solid foundation of life.
When they'd nearly finished eating, Zhang Fengxia wiped her hands with a napkin and spoke as if in passing. "You and Yu Hao — you've split for good?"
"And now — you're with Zhao Yiming?" Zhang Fengxia asked it directly, no roundabout way. Hearing this, Zhou Lufei lowered his head and concentrated on his hotpot, as though he'd suddenly developed a great interest in the doneness of that slice of lamb.
Yao Yuan finished chewing slowly, lifted her eyes, and met her mother's gaze. There was probing in Zhang Fengxia's eyes, and concern, but more than that a kind of calm appraisal — not gossip, but a mother's assessment of an important choice in her daughter's life.
"Yes, we're together." Yao Yuan's voice was steady. "But Mom, don't read too much into it. We get along well, and the rest will take its course. The most important thing for you and Uncle Zhou now is to take care of your health, not to worry about my affairs."
"Take its course?" Zhang Fengxia repeated the phrase and gave a soft snort through her nose. "Back when you were with Yu Hao, you said it would take its course too. And what came of it? A few years together and still just 'buddies.'" She used the trendy internet word, her tone subtle.
"That's different." Yao Yuan set down her chopsticks, her tone still even but carrying more weight now. "Yu Hao is Yu Hao, and Zhao Yiming is Zhao Yiming. I know what I'm doing."
"As long as you know." Zhang Fengxia didn't press further; instead she served her a chopstickful of greens, her voice softening a little. "After today you'll be thirty-eight. I just hope there's someone steady by your side, someone who knows when you're warm and when you're cold." It was only then that Zhou Lufei cut in, smoothing things over. "Fengxia, let the kids handle their own affairs. People in tech are steady and reliable. Yuanyuan has good judgment and a mind of her own — you can rest easy. Here, tea instead of wine, let's clink glasses. May next year be good for all of us!"
Three teacups touched lightly together with a clear, pleasant ring. In that simple little ritual was packed the plainest of wishes for the year to come.
The mood at the table eased again. But Zhang Fengxia's words were like a small stone tossed into the lake of Yao Yuan's heart, rippling out faintly. She thought of her appointment with Zhao Yiming tomorrow — Western World, the pricing model for the Pretty Big One AI... Between the two of them, beyond the tangled, unresolved riddles of the Mirror Pact and time and space, it really had reached the stage where some practical matters needed to be "made real."
The conversation drifted, somehow, to longer ago. Perhaps it was this warm reunion that made one think of the road already traveled.
"Remember little Xiaoping next door, back when we lived in the Liu Lane alley?" Zhang Fengxia asked suddenly.
Yao Yuan's memory stirred. Blurry images surfaced: a cluttered courtyard always hung with the smell of cheap cigarettes and leftover food. The woman of that household wore heavy makeup and often came home late at night; the man loafed about doing nothing. Their little girl was called Xiaoping — eight or nine years old, yet never seen at school, running wild in the alley all day with a pack of half-grown kids, her grubby little face lit by startlingly bright eyes.
"I remember." Yao Yuan nodded. "You never let me play with her back then."
"That's right, I didn't." Zhang Fengxia's tone was firm and absolute, even carrying a trace of the old hardness. "Your dad passed early, and the two of us were crammed into a ten-square-meter rental. Times were hard. But however hard it got, I had to tell you — there are some roads you don't take, some people you don't get close to. I understood the lesson of Mencius's mother moving three times. I didn't have the means to move us out of that place right away, but I could keep you from running around with that child."
She looked at Yao Yuan, her gaze the same as always. "You were small then. You thought I was unkind, you thought Xiaoping was pitiful — no school, no one looking after her. But I saw it clearly: the way that whole family lived was rootless duckweed, getting through one day at a time and calling it good enough. Her parents drifted through their days, and the world the child saw was a frightened one. What that kid took in with her eyes, heard with her ears, turned over in her heart from the time she was little — how could it ever be the same as yours? You play one day in the mud with her, and what she learns is how to snatch other kids' marbles, how to swear without repeating herself; you come home and read one more page of a book, write one more character, and that gain goes right into your own bones."
Yao Yuan listened quietly. The small grievances and confusion she'd felt as a child over being forbidden to play were, in this moment, re-decoded by her grown-up mind. Now, at thirty-eight, sitting in the settled life she'd fought to build, listening to her mother speak of those long-ago choices in that calm tone, what she felt was not accusation but a late-arriving, deep understanding.
The white steam rising off the hotpot blurred her mother's face, yet let her see clearly how that mother — once just as young, stripped of any support, struggling at the edge of survival — had, in her own lonely, brave, clumsy way, fought to draw the first strokes about "the bottom line" and "direction" onto the canvas of her daughter's life.
She suddenly thought of those desolate women in Eileen Chang's writing, swallowed by their circumstances. She thought of the heroines in Yi Shu's novels, the ones who no matter what would struggle to keep their dignity, to haul themselves out of a wretched situation. And her mother, Zhang Fengxia — a woman who'd come up out of a mountain gully, with little schooling — had, by a kind of instinctive, plain, and tenacious survival wisdom, lived out the same sort of principles.
She didn't understand any "theory of family of origin" or "social class stratification," but by a mother's instinct and a beast-like vigilance, she knew she had to "draw a line" for her child. She might not have been able to give Yao Yuan a privileged start, but she did everything she could to keep her daughter's character from being eroded too soon by a bad environment. She used the most direct method — forbidding — to mark out a safety line. Behind that line was a faint but stubborn conviction: "however hard it is, we climb upward."
This kind of upbringing shaped certain hard cores in Yao Yuan's character: a wariness of one's surroundings, a deliberate distance from forces that "drag you down," an obsession with self-building, and that clear-eyed knowledge that "in the end you can only rely on yourself." It wasn't warm — it even hurt — but it worked. Like cutting a path through a thicket of thorns: the process is bound to leave you scratched, but in the end it lets you cross the mire and reach more open ground.
"Thinking about it now," Yao Yuan said, her voice very soft, "you were right back then."
"Mom," Yao Yuan said, her voice very soft but carrying a smile and a kind of release, "I understand now. Back then... you were right."
The hand holding Zhang Fengxia's chopsticks paused slightly. She lowered her eyes and, as if to cover it, poked at the long-cold leaves of vegetable in her bowl. Several seconds passed before she said, in a voice even softer than before, "...As long as you understand. Hurry and eat, the meat's getting cold."
But Yao Yuan saw the corners of her mother's eyes flush red for a quick instant. Then she stood, saying she'd go get more greens, and as she turned she raised a hand and wiped her cheek, very fast.
Zhou Lufei took it all in and said nothing, only smiled and served Yao Yuan another piece of sesame flatbread. "The chive-flower paste your mom pickled goes perfect with this. Unbeatable."
The meal lasted until nearly nine at night, host and guests all content. Going downstairs, the first floor was still packed full, laughter and chatter everywhere. Zhou Lufei walked Yao Yuan all the way to the door and pressed a heavy eco-bag into her hands. "Take it! There's freshly mixed sesame paste inside, your mom's new sugar garlic and chive-flower paste, and two pieces of braised beef shank — slice it up any day you don't feel like cooking."
Zhang Fengxia carefully tucked her scarf in for her. The night wind was cold; she gripped her daughter's hand a little tighter and murmured, "Drive slow. Send a message when you get home. With Zhao Yiming... be good to each other."
"I know, Mom. Uncle Zhou, you two turn in early too, you've worked hard all day." Yao Yuan opened her arms and hugged her mother gently. This time Zhang Fengxia didn't go stiff; she raised her arms and hugged her back, then quickly let go and urged her on. "Off you go now, the roads are busy."
Settled into the car and driving for a good stretch, she could still see in the rearview mirror those two figures standing side by side at the shop door, looking after her, especially warm against the cold night. She cracked the window a little and the crisp air poured in, carrying the faint smell of fireworks, while her heart felt full to the brim.
On the drive home to her apartment, the city glittered with light. Many shops along the way were still open, getting ready to greet the turning of the old year into the new. But Yao Yuan's heart was quiet, all that clamor as if behind a pane of glass. Her mother's words, her childhood memories, and those sharp dissections of human nature, love, and survival from the livestream wove together, collided, and settled in her mind without a sound.
Back at her riverside apartment, the rooms were silent and serene. She didn't switch on the main light, only lit a single floor lamp beside the sofa — a gentle halo of light circling out a room's worth of solitary peace.
Beyond the floor-to-ceiling windows, the city's neon spread out like a sea, the traffic threading through like broken streams of light, sketching out the splendor of the metropolis. In the darkness the Yellow River ran on as it had since ancient times, flowing silently through year after year, witness to every rise and fall, every parting and reunion in the human world.
She suddenly remembered this day last year.
The same New Year's Eve, by Yellow River Tower, fireworks filling the sky, old friends at her side. In the span of just one short year, people and circumstances had shifted, and her state of mind had transformed. The people once around her had each gone their own way, each carrying the sweetness and the weight of their own lives, while she had long since shed her youthful restlessness and empty obsessions, and had grown into someone self-sufficient, settled, with her own quiet ballast.
Now, standing alone at the window, she felt not the slightest chill of "ringing in the New Year by herself." Instead she was filled with a full, ripe calm and a faint, quiet anticipation. It was the steadiness of knowing where you draw your strength from, and where you are running toward.
Up in the night sky, a soft crack — a single firework shot up, burst open with a hiss, brilliant in gold, and in a blink sank back into the silent dark.
She stood here, the silent river before her, but the secret of the Mirror Pact, the countdown to the ninth crossing through time, the daily push and pull of a present life tangled up with Zhao Yiming, the road ahead for commercializing the Pretty Big One AI, her mother's "make it real" circling in her mind...
"Bang—!"
Another point of golden light rose not far off, leaping hard, bursting open with a snap at its highest point, shattering into a handful of glittering stars that came drifting down like a tiny golden rain.
The beauty of fireworks lies in the instant of extinction after the most extreme burning. In knowing it must turn to ash, and holding your breath for that moment of splendor all the same. How much it resembles the human longing for love, for glory, for all things brief and beautiful — the infatuation and the mourning of it.
Eileen Chang saw through to the desolation and emptiness behind such beauty. Yi Shu chose to admire the bustle outside the window only now and then, after she'd secured her own steady "study." And the multitude of ordinary souls mostly struggle back and forth between Qiong Yao's dreamlike romance and hard reality, trying to grasp at a little light to warm themselves.
And what about her, then?
A thunderous boom — another giant firework soared up and burst at the very top of the night sky, a thousand streams of gold pouring down, scattering into a sky-wide rain of stars, drifting down. The dazzling light and shadow fell into her jet-black pupils, bright one instant, extinguished the next.
The utmost splendor, in the end, hurries to a close.
What moves us most about fireworks was never their lasting forever, but the way they pour out everything in a moment's burning, ardent and unguarded. Just like the love and glory of the human world, every kind of beauty — splendor wrapped around emptiness, ardor hiding desolation.
The fireworks spent, the night sky went quiet again, and the faint scent of gunpowder drifted in through the evening wind to the window.
The old year had drawn to a close; the new year was opening its first page.
Outside the window the Yellow River ran on as it had for ten thousand ages, silent witness to the turning of time, holding within it all the splendor and loneliness of the world, all the clear-eyed waking and the sinking under, all the beginnings and the goodbyes.
Yao Yuan slowly turned and walked toward the study.
On the desk the plain old bronze mirror lay quietly on its side, its surface deep and still, reflecting neither the sky full of fireworks outside nor the look in her eyes, the feeling in her heart, just now.
But the countdown to the ninth crossing through time, deep in her mind, silently ticked off its numbers.
The new year drawing near, the fireworks ordinary, the road ahead vast, the fate not yet at rest.

========================================

Chapter 99 — A New Year's Date, a Surprise Proposal: In the Mirror Room Beneath the Stars, a Promise of the Rest of Their Lives
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640346.html

The New Year's morning light filtered through the gauze curtains and gently woke Yao Yuan.
She had slept beautifully, a whole night without dreams, and woke clear-headed, her very body light and at ease. She rose, spread a yoga mat in the open space of the living room, and stretched slowly to soft music. Her breaths ran long and deep, her body finding its balance between suppleness and strength. It was a quiet ritual — a farewell to what was past, a welcome to a new beginning.
After bathing, she stood before the mirror in her dressing room. It was the first day of the New Year, and also an important day: she was going to Western World Technology to discuss the pricing model for the Pretty Big One AI with Zhao Yiming. She had no wish to meet the day half-heartedly. She wanted to greet the New Year, her beloved, and the road ahead in her very best form.
Her gaze swept the rows of crisply cut, finely tailored dresses, and at last settled on a mellow, warm wine-red in the corner.
It was a long cashmere-blend knit dress, its color not a loud, glaring true red but the deep red of an aged wine settled by the years — rich and restrained, glowing with a soft warmth of its own, understated yet full of texture. The cut was exquisite. The high collar fit snugly against her slender neck, lending its line a fine elegance; the long sleeves tapered cleanly to her wrists, dignified and refined, every proportion just right. From the shoulder line down, the fabric followed the contours of her body, cinching precisely at her slim waist, while the hem flared gently to soften any hint of severity.
The exquisitely fitted cut performed the most moving kind of "restrained sensuality." Wrapped close all over, not an inch of skin bared, it nonetheless traced every full, graceful curve of her figure — hidden yet suggested, noble allure and mature charm fused into one, growing only more flavorful the longer one looked.
The moment she slipped into the dress, the woman in the mirror was lit up by that warm red glow. Her black hair cascaded like a waterfall over her shoulders, setting off skin white as snow, brows and eyes bright and radiant, her whole presence suddenly unfolding — the composure of years settled within her, and the vivid freshness of one who has weathered a thousand sails.
Her makeup was chosen to match the dress, every measure pitched just right. Her brows were softened into a gentle, flowing arc, shorn of the sharp edge of her workdays; her eye makeup was blended layer on layer, the outer corners lifting in fine, delicate lines, every lash distinct, curled and lively, making her eyes look clear and deep; her lips wore a stroke of rich true red, full and lovely, like petals flushed by a winter sun's gentle kiss — vivid, yet never showy.
A pair of diamond tassel earrings hung at her ears, and a fine pearl sweater chain layered at her throat. The scattered sparkle of diamonds and the soft luster of pearls swayed lightly with her smallest movement, refracting a sky full of tiny starlight in the morning sun, mirroring the mellow wine-red of the dress and lending it just the right note of refined luxury — elegant, never gaudy.
Over it she wore a hip-length black fur coat, its pile fine and soft, its sheen warm as water. The classic clash of red and black blended seduction and steady strength to the utmost — at once a woman's tenderness and that nerve and presence not to be underestimated that belonged to Yao Yuan alone.
She let her long hair fall loose and easy, then lifted her eyes one last time to the bright, composed woman in the mirror. A faint smile rippled at the bottom of her eyes. She picked up a simple clutch, and pushed open the door to keep her date.
Yao Yuan brought her car to a stop and looked up. Through the clear glass curtain wall she saw a familiar figure inside the door, smiling and waving. It was Xiao Lin, the project assistant who handled her company's business with them on an ongoing basis.
A flicker of surprise crossed her mind. Western World had always run on humane management — it never ate into its staff's holidays — and Zhao Yiming, ever considerate of his subordinates, would never schedule pointless work on a statutory holiday like New Year's Day and put his people through the wringer. For Xiao Lin to be specially on duty waiting today was no ordinary business matter.
"Director Yao, Happy New Year!" Xiao Lin hurried forward, eyes crinkling, her smile bright, a barely contained excitement and delight she couldn't hide in her gaze. "Director Zhao arranged for me to wait here for you specially."
"Happy New Year. Thank you — coming in specially on New Year's Day." Yao Yuan pushed down the doubt in her heart and answered warmly. She filed it under special circumstances for now — perhaps some pricing-model data needed urgent verification — and followed Xiao Lin into the building.
The office tower on a holiday should have been empty and cold, utterly silent, yet today a warm, living current hung in the air. It wasn't the even warmth of central air conditioning, but the warm atmosphere woven from many people gathered together, hearts full of anticipation, brimming silently across the whole floor.
From the elevator hall all the way down the corridor into the company's interior, a delicate silver flower stand stood every few steps, each holding great armfuls of full, vivid red roses, their petals strung with crystal dew. A rich, sweet fragrance drifted slowly out, weaving a tender, lingering net of sweet warmth that shut out every trace of winter's chill.
Yao Yuan's steps paused slightly. The hazy guess in her heart spread, like a stone dropped into a still lake, in clear ripple after ripple. Her face betrayed nothing as she followed Xiao Lin on with the same composure. They passed through an office area likewise dotted with roses, the rows of workstations all empty, yet holding the lingering warmth of people who had only just left — clearly everyone had withdrawn early on purpose, all to prepare for this surprise.
Xiao Lin did not take her to the meeting room where they usually discussed work, but went straight toward the dedicated mirror room at the end of the corridor.
This was where she and Zhao Yiming had begun their work side by side, digging in together. Through countless days and nights they had tuned models here, broken through technical bottlenecks, polished their algorithms, and out of cold, intricate code and data had together nurtured the artificial intelligence that understood human emotion — the Pretty Big One Emotional AI. Here lay the founding heart and the milestones of their work, and here too was the hidden corner where their feelings for each other had quietly sprouted and slowly taken root.
The heavy sensor door slid open without a sound.
The instant Yao Yuan stepped inside —
"Pop! Pop!" A few soft, crisp bursts went off in turn, and a riot of confetti and fine glittering foam drifted down from the dome, dazzling across the air, settling gently all around her.
This spherical mirror room, built for absolute secrecy and holographic-projection research, had been decked out tender and romantic now, stripped of its old cold, technological feel. The eight-meter-high dome, seamlessly assembled from countless hidden hexagonal screens, flowed at this moment with a vast, magnificent sea of stars — the galaxy slowly wheeling, nebulae drifting tender and dim, gathering all the romance and tenderness of the boundless universe into this one small space.
Around the walls of the room hung weightless silver balloons and warm white string lights, their fine glow flickering bright and dim, tender and lingering. On the ring-shaped console floating at the center, the familiar blue data streams poured down like a waterfall, and through the usually cold, orderly sequences of code there now wove pulsing pink heart-shaped lights, while a line of faint glowing text quietly surfaced: Love Algorithm Running…
And at the center of all this romance stood a crowd of old friends, smiling brightly, the room full of tender blessing, all of it rushing toward her.
Zhao Yiming stood at the very front of the crowd, the irreplaceable focus. Today he wore an ink-blue bespoke suit, crisply and sharply cut, fitting his tall, upright frame to perfection, the fabric giving off an understated, fine sheen in the warm light. A matching waistcoat was buttoned neatly, and a light gray-blue tie in a Windsor knot sat trim and proper — shorn of his everyday lab casualness and workplace edge, with a few measures of the utmost solemnity added.
He had purposely combed back the loose hair from his forehead, baring a clean, full brow. The smart glasses he usually wore were swapped for a warm gold wire-rimmed pair, setting off his deep, gentle features, his bearing steady and noble. In his hands he held a great bouquet of full-bloomed red velvet roses. He stood tall and straight, his expression more solemn than ever before, even his brows carrying a barely perceptible tension, hiding a heart full of nerves and hope.
Around him were gathered those closest to them: Jin Lan held Lu Ang's arm, eyes crinkled, smiling bright and sincere; Liu Qingzhuo and Chen Ye stood side by side, the one elegant, the other steady, both with tender blessing in their eyes; Jiang Tiantian held her arms lightly crossed, the gaze behind her lenses shorn of its usual sharpness, rippling with a gentle smile; Wang Qian held up a phone, steadily recording this precious moment that belonged to the two of them. Beyond them stood the core colleagues from both companies, close in business and deep in private friendship, all standing quietly, their gazes tender, their hearts full of hope.
Yao Yuan stood frozen in the doorway, her body going rigid in an instant. The hazy guess in her heart had settled completely, yet the scene before her — so grand, so sincere and tender — struck her with a force far beyond anything she had imagined.
Zhao Yiming drew a deep breath, pressing down the nerves and gravity surging within him, and, holding the roses, came slowly toward her step by step. His stride was steady and sure; only the bob of his throat betrayed the agitation he was holding in check.
He stopped one step away from her, his eyes brilliant as a sea of stars, every measure of his tenderness focused on her alone. Under the hushed, breath-held gaze of the whole room, with no warning at all, he sank to one knee in a posture of the utmost solemnity.
"Yuanyuan."
His voice was low and clear, ringing steadily through the quiet, vast mirror room — gentle, yet weighted like a thousand pounds.
"From the time we created the Pretty Big One together, breaking through technical barriers side by side and digging deep into the emotional-AI field, right up to now, we've signed countless business contracts and technical agreements, and worked through countless data models and partnership plans."
He tilted his head up slightly, his gaze clear and sincere, looking straight into her eyes, every word earnest, every line from the heart: "But to me, every formal document and commercial contract in this world is worth less than one love white paper that belongs only to us. From start to finish it holds just a single core clause, never once amended — Zhao Yiming, for the whole of this life, will have no one but Yao Yuan."
He raised his hand and gently lifted the bouquet. At the center of the layered red velvet roses a diamond ring sat quietly, blazing out a brilliant, ardent fire in the light of the starlit sky — dazzling, breathtaking.
"This invisible white paper — I've written, revised, and polished it over and over in my heart, and in the days and nights you couldn't see, I've silently submitted it and held to it countless times."
"Today, at the dawn of the New Year, with all things renewed, I formally apply to you to launch the life project of spending the rest of our lives together, growing old side by side — to make real a lifetime of companionship and commitment."
His eyes were full of devotion and certainty, his voice growing firmer and deeper: "Yao Yuan, will you marry me?"
The instant his words fell, the hush of the whole room shattered, and a fierce roar of cheers and applause exploded out, filling the entire mirror room with tenderness.
"Say yes! Yuanyuan!"
"Marry him! It's too sweet!"
"Director Yao, Director Zhao spent so long preparing for this day!"
Jin Lan was the first to laugh and chime in, her eyes full of heartfelt blessing; Liu Qingzhuo clapped softly, a smile in her eyes; Chen Ye gave a slight nod, his face full of approval; Jiang Tiantian pushed up her glasses, showing a rare tender smile; Wang Qian held up the lens, steadily freezing each warm moment. Everyone was moved by this rational romance, this utter sincerity that belonged to Zhao Yiming alone — the whole room warm, ardent, glowing.
Yet Yao Yuan, standing at the eye of the storm, was suddenly seized by an extreme, dazed weightlessness.
The cheers and teasing, the laughter and clamor at her ears seemed to come through a hazy mist — distant and blurred. The man kneeling before her, the roses in full bloom, the dazzling diamond ring, and the unreserved hope and cherishing in his eyes — all of it was clear and real, yet beautiful as a fragile dream.
This year, she was thirty-eight.
At twenty-two she had once dreamed, heart full, of a wedding gown and vows. But in all the years that followed, reality had shattered her every tender fantasy with cold truths, time and again.
At twenty-four, her first love could not withstand the shackles of family, and she had let go in helplessness — for the first time tasting in full the bitterness of being weighed and discarded by reality, of giving up against her will. At twenty-eight, a wealthy suitor doted on her, lifted her up, yet always felt she shone too sharply, was too hard to handle, willing only to keep her in ambiguous companionship and never to grant her the name of marriage — making her see clearly her own pricing as "fit to be doted on, not to be honored." She had the child taken away and left without hesitation. At thirty, she rose into the top circles of fame and fortune, became the capable right arm of a Shanghai magnate, yet came to understand in the end that she was only a chip at the negotiating table, to be swapped out and sacrificed, never truly cherished. At thirty-six, she met a compatible "buddy," seemingly steady and well-matched, yet he treated her only as a quality "mother for his children" and "life partner" — and merely because her public IP image carried too high a risk and would affect their family business, he flatly refused any legal binding. Once again she was reduced to a functional option to be screened and weighed, rather than the one chosen with certainty.
Year after year, again and again, she had been compared, picked apart, abandoned, pushed away.
She had long since clawed her way through the thorns and mire of love and forged a hard suit of armor, polishing and freezing over her soft true heart again and again. She no longer longed for marriage as a safety net, no longer craved another's shelter, no longer needed anyone to define the worth of her life. She dug deep into her work, built up her own ground to stand on, and with her own hands raised a stretch of steady ground for herself — living as a deep-rooted, leaf-rich great tree that feared no wind or rain, strong enough to hold up alone against all the frosts of the years.
But now, when someone had pierced through her layers of armor, ignored all her past history, her worldly labels, her weak points — not weighing, not hesitating, not making do, but simply, firmly affirming her as this very person, and telling her without reserve, "It has to be you" —
What surged up in her heart was not the expected wild joy and emotion, but a sudden, defenseless panic, and an instinctive shrinking back.
A gift of beauty too grand, too pure, too utterly unreserved, threw her by habit into dread and unease. She feared this was once more a fleeting splendor of fireworks — that after the ardent bloom there would be only desolation as far as the eye could see, deeper loneliness, wounds cut deeper. All those late nights of being failed and forsaken, all those fragments of self-doubt and inner attrition, turned now into a cold tide, surging fierce upon her, trying to drag her hard away from the tenderness and warmth before her.
"Yuanyuan?"
Zhao Yiming's faint voice, wrapped in a barely perceptible tension, gently broke open her tangled, sinking thoughts and pulled her back from the shadows of the past into the present. He held his kneeling posture all the while — no urging, no impatience, only waiting quietly, the tenderness in his eyes unchanged, embracing all her hesitation and panic.
"Yuanyuan, look at you — stunned silly with emotion?" Jin Lan stepped up and gently nudged her arm, teasing with a smile, her tone warm and reassuring. "So even our Teacher Yao — decisive in battle, composed in any crisis — has a day when she's at a loss like this."
Moved.
Yao Yuan blinked slowly, her long lashes trembling, and in an instant a layer of warm tears welled up in her eyes.
It was far more than being moved. It was the release of having come full circle through a thousand sails; the comfort of having every toughness and fragility read and understood; the bittersweetness of years of grievance soothed in a single moment; a flood of disbelief and brimming joy all woven together.
She gazed quietly at the man before her. He was a top-tier helmsman digging deep into cutting-edge technology — rational, restrained, rigorous and meticulous, used to taking apart every logic, calculating every risk, controlling every variable. Yet to her, of all people, he gave all his tenderness, sincerity, and patience.
He was her most compatible ally in work, her most reliable partner, the lover who could catch all her sharp edges, embrace all her weariness, and read all her clear-sightedness and softness. He was no flawless fairy-tale prince, but the most real, the most solid, the most fitting Zhao Yiming for her. He had given the few measures of tenderness and romance he had, all of it, to her alone.
None of the past hurts and hardships had been gone through in vain. That mire and frost had honed the independent, strong, clear-eyed and transparent woman she was today; and the splendid, certain woman she was now deserved exactly this kind of love before her — equally pure, ardent, firm, and singular.
A warm sob rose in her throat, bittersweetness and warmth woven together, crashing against her heart. The Yao Yuan who calmly dissected the riddles of love in her livestream, revered by millions as the Godmother of Feelings, at last laid down all her armor before the sincere love that belonged to her, and her eyes reddened.
Scalding tears slid from her eyes, washing away the last trace of hesitation and dread, rinsing clean every shadow of unease from the past.
She drew a deep breath, pressed down the surging tide of feeling, and beneath the breath-held, hopeful gaze of the whole room, she slowly, firmly nodded. Her voice carried a faint sob, yet every word rang clear: "I will."
"Wow——!!!"
Earth-shaking cheers swept the whole room again, confetti flying everywhere, romance spilling out unrestrained.
A brilliant sea of stars lit up in Zhao Yiming's eyes in an instant, brimming with the utmost joy and tenderness. His fingertips trembled faintly — a rare lapse of composure under such joy — as he carefully took out the diamond ring and, with the clumsiness and earnestness peculiar to a science man, slid it steadily onto the ring finger of her slender left hand.
The size was just right, neither loose nor tight, locking fast the bond they were certain of for this life.
He rose and strode forward to gather her into his arms, his strength gentle but firm, holding her steadily in his embrace. Yao Yuan lifted her arms in turn to hold him back, burying her cheek against his shoulder, the clean, cool scent of his aftershave lingering at the tip of her nose — steadying and healing.
Amid the ardent teasing and blessings of the whole room, Zhao Yiming bowed his head slightly and gently kissed her lips. The kiss was restrained yet deeply tender — nothing of desire, nothing of vanity, full of cherishing, certainty, and the solemn promise to stay together for the rest of their lives, tenderly soothing the heart that had drifted half a lifetime.
The grand celebration party was set in the meeting room next to the mirror room. The originally orderly long conference table had long since been carefully arranged into an exquisite buffet station.
A crystal-clear three-tier champagne tower rose layer on layer, bubbles rustling up through the golden liquor, fine and lively. Beside it, a multi-tiered themed cake was exquisite and splendid, its sugar-sculpted code patterns and starry motifs the romantic understanding that belonged to the two of them alone — tender and distinctive. Fresh fruit, desserts, and small bites dazzled the eye, exquisitely plated, perfect in color, aroma, and taste.
At the center of the venue, a three-Michelin-star chef and his team cooked on the spot, their snow-white uniforms lending them a professional elegance. Thick-cut Angus steaks landed on the grill, sizzling, their meaty aroma mingling with the sharp scent of rosemary and black pepper, drifting freely; hand-kneaded pizza bases swelled slowly in the oven, the cheese melting and stretching, rich fragrance wrapped in ardent, lively warmth; specially mixed cocktails were poured with practiced skill, their gradient colors stunning, each glass a piece of dedicated craft.
Everyone raised their glasses in congratulation, the crisp clink of cups never ceasing, laughter and cheer filling the whole room.
"Congratulations, congratulations! May the rest of your lives go smoothly, and may you stay together year after year!"
"The two of you joining strength to strength — career and love both complete!"
"We'll be waiting for your grand wedding — you two must be truly happy!"
Yao Yuan answered each with a smile, the diamond ring on her finger heavy, real and substantial. Zhao Yiming stayed close at her side the whole time, his arm naturally around her waist, silently guarding her there. His usually cold, distant brows and eyes were now stained with a tenderness that would not melt away, the doting in his gaze impossible to hide.
The champagne left her pleasantly tipsy, warmth spreading through her, yet a happiness this ardent and grand felt too vast, leaving her always with a touch of unreality. The joy in her heart was full and scalding, yet it stayed suspended in the clouds, unable to fully land and settle. She needed a moment alone to dissolve this sudden, utter tenderness and untangle the complicated feelings churning inside her.
While everyone was gathered watching the chef show off his skills, in all the lively clamor, Yao Yuan quietly slipped away, withdrawing alone from the noisy meeting room and walking back into the mirror room where the proposal had just taken place.
The sensor door closed softly, shutting out all the clamor and laughter outside, leaving only a room of quiet and sanctity.
The sea of stars on the dome still wheeled slowly, vast and tender; the blue data streams trickled down; fine starlight flickered point by point; the sweet scent of roses had not yet faded, tender and lingering as before. Stripped of the press of the crowd, here remained only the cold of technology and the lingering warmth of the utmost romance — quiet enough to hear her own clear heartbeat.
Yao Yuan walked slowly to the ring-shaped console and brushed her fingertips lightly over the cool, smooth surface, her heart a tangle of a thousand feelings.
At twenty-two she had vainly hoped to cross class lines and seek security through marriage, believing that marrying was life's only destination. Back then she had never imagined that her road toward marriage would be so winding and thorny, marked by countless betrayals and wounds.
Later she slowly woke, slowly healed, slowly raised high walls. She shed all her obsession with marriage, no longer treating it as a life necessity, but only as a luxury that might be met but never sought. She poured all her heart into her work, building her own kingdom brick by brick, living independent, clear-eyed, and certain — needing to lean on no one, yet able to be steady year after year, blooming with each step.
But fate had always been a mysterious thing, forever bestowing surprises when least expected. Just as she had let go completely, grown indifferent to love and marriage and asked only for steady years, this most compatible, most complete, most pure marriage and love rushed toward her without warning.
No weighing of gains and losses, no added conditions, no worldly coercion — only a certainty of running toward each other from both sides, an unreserved choice, and a vow to stay.
She quietly asked herself: was this sudden completeness fate's belated compensation, or a new round of unknown trial? No one answered. The diamond ring on her finger was cold and hard, yet it refracted a warm, brilliant light beneath the starlit glow — cold and warmth woven together, just like the tumultuous first half of her life.
She lifted her head and gazed at that brilliant sea of stars on the dome, lit for her, her heart stirred beyond words. The galaxy was vast, and a person but a speck of dust; to find a kindred soul to walk alongside was already great fortune.
Just as her thoughts drifted far, her gaze unconsciously following the wheeling track of one especially bright, whirlpool-shaped nebula in the sea of stars —
An extremely familiar pull, rising from the very depths of her soul, seized her without any warning.
In her sight, the center of that whirlpool-shaped nebula suddenly rippled outward in violent ring after ring, like the surface of a lake struck by a dropped stone! Then the whole starscape in her vision, the data streams, even the outline of the mirror room itself, all began to twist, stretch, and spin, like an oil painting crumpled by an invisible giant hand.
Vertigo crashed over her like a mountain-toppling wave, draining in an instant all the strength from her limbs and bones.
Her body crumpled down beyond her control, falling onto the cool floor — itself assembled from the same hidden hexagonal screens.
In the last instant before her consciousness was wholly swallowed by darkness, a thought, clear and calm to the utmost, surfaced above her tangled feelings:
The ninth time-travel had come.
In the mirror room, the starry sky still wheeled, the data still poured down like a waterfall, the roses still gave off their fragrance. Only the figure in the red dress lay quietly against the console, as if utterly exhausted, sunk into a deep sleep that no one would disturb.
Outside the window, the New Year's sunlight was just right.

========================================

Chapter 100 — The Ninth Time-Travel Glimpse: However Different the Roads, the Destination Is the Same
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640347.html

The Ninth Time-Travel Glimpse: However Different the Roads, the Destination Is the Same
Yao Yuan's consciousness settled once more into that familiar pure-white void.
Before she could fully adjust to the emptiness and quiet, the void beside her rippled gently, layer upon layer. Fine flickers of light pulsed like ink dropped into clear water, spreading from faint to deep, slowly resolving into a concrete picture that finally unfurled before her in full.
Another proposal. Once again, that mirror room that blended the feel of technology with a romantic mood.
Overhead, the domed ceiling turned with its steady starry sky; all around stood the neatly arranged ring-shaped consoles, crowded with beaming friends and family. For a dizzy instant, Yao Yuan felt an overwhelming sense of déjà vu — she even half wondered whether the real proposal she had just lived through, that soul-shaking confirmation, had been nothing more than an illusion spun by her overloaded emotions.
But in the next instant, the subtle differences surfaced clearly and shattered that illusion of overlap.
The guests' clothing, the cut and the overall color scheme in this picture, were utterly unlike the scene she had just lived. What Zhao Yiming cradled in his arms was not a fierce, vivid bouquet of red velvet roses, but a soft, lingering bunch of champagne-colored flowers. Most telling of all, the slightly younger version of herself in the picture had eyes that were clear and unrippled — not a trace of the soul-shaking daze, the weightless disorientation. Calmly, brightly, decisively, she took the ring, and accepted that solemn declaration.
In the pure-white space, Yao Yuan stood quietly, her heart still as water.
After eight crossings through time and space, she had long shed the fear, the shock, the confusion of those early days. By now she had grown used to being the one watcher in this chess game of fate — forced to halt, to stand silent on the sidelines. The confusion had faded; all that remained in her heart was a settled calm. Since none of her struggle or resistance could shift the fixed track, she would steady herself instead and look closely, and see just what answer fate meant to reveal to her this time.
The picture flowed on slowly: the congratulations in the room, the warmth of embraces, the crisp pop of champagne corks — all replaying a similar liveliness.
Yao Yuan studied the crowd around her. Jin Lan, Wang Qian, Liu Qingzhuo, Chen Ye were all present — only Jiang Tiantian was missing. Of the other colleagues, only two or three faces overlapped with the people she knew; the rest were strangers, all-new faces.
She understood at once, and instantly traced every thread of cause and effect.
The root of it all lay in the life-track she had rewritten, crossing after crossing. After her third crossing changed the course of her life, the "Yao Yuan of that past timeline" had not become the Godmother of Feelings caught in the eye of the storm. Instead she had turned, far earlier, to technology and business. She had met Zhao Yiming sooner, bound herself to deep collaboration with him sooner, and developed a more fully featured AI — one not confined to the narrow vertical of feelings.
Her life's center of gravity had shifted; the direction she pushed forward had shifted; and so the people around her, naturally, were reshuffled too. Some who had walked beside her had quietly parted ways at fate's forking roads; some all-new faces had come rushing in along the new track. The branch of her life still stretched toward the sun — but through countless small adjustments, it had already grown wholly different limbs and veins.
The shot turned, switching to the temporary banquet hall converted from the room next door.
Yao Yuan's gaze flickered. The arrangement of the long tables, the neatly stacked champagne tower, the chef team standing by on site, even the exquisite styling of the desserts — all of it overlapped almost exactly with the engagement celebration she had just lived through herself.
A tangle of feelings, absurd and yet clear-eyed, washed through her whole body.
In this moment, she saw through to the most paradoxical truth of fate.
A person can, perhaps, rewrite the rough places of the road ahead, swap out the scenery along the way, choose which companions to keep, fine-tune every detail of life through choice after choice. But the base color rooted deep in the soul, the character, the longing — like a riverbed set deep into the earth — have long decided the general flow of a life. However outside forces stir it, in the end it can hardly be wholly overturned, hardly made to change course.
That "Yao Yuan of the past timeline," because of her intervention, had sidestepped the public backlash, the business traps, and the more tangled emotional snarls that came with becoming the "Godmother of Feelings." She had walked a road that looked more "solid," more focused on business and technology. The change was enormous — enough to make the two "Yao Yuans'" lives look strikingly different on the surface. And yet, when each of their lives reached a certain crucial node, the people they were meant to meet, the things they were meant to live through, always came to pass.
This was not entirely "inescapable." It was more like a deep gravity grounded in "character" and "need." Whichever version of herself it was, the same base color lay buried in the bone. Worn down by the world, tempered by trauma, forced to build a hard armor of reason — she looked clear-headed, independent, unbreakable, yet at the bottom of her heart she always longed for one pure, steadfast, solid favoring, one steadfast companionship.
She and Zhao Yiming were, from the start, the optimal solution to each other's logic, cognition, ambition, and feeling.
So however the road ahead twisted and doubled back, once the two of them — their minds, their experience, their life stages — arrived at the point where they fit, once forging a deep alliance became each one's best choice, then turning toward each other, settling on each other, was the inevitability written into fate.
So, was the change meaningful? Of course it was. It changed the concentration of suffering in the other timeline's Yao Yuan's life. It reduced her self-tearing and inner drain. It dodged the erosion from the public eye and the grinding of the heart, the kind that could wreck a mind. In exchange came a smoother career, a lighter state of mind, a past with fewer regrets. What changed was the experience of the process, the specific shape of the hardships and the harvests along the way. But in the end it could not rewrite the essence of the soul. A person's character, cognition, core longings had long decided the choices made at life's crucial nodes. So two equally mature minds, facing similar life problems, after that double calculation of reason and feeling, would most likely point toward the same "optimal solution."
This was the inner logic of human nature and human need. What people call "fate," perhaps, is nothing but countless such "inner-logic choices" stacked one upon another.
In the picture, the warmth of celebration still went on.
Yao Yuan watched quietly from the pure-white space — watched another self ringed by the congratulations of friends and family, watched Zhao Yiming stay close at her side throughout, his arm draped naturally around her waist, his posture loose and certain, holding a wordless possessiveness and a careful, tender guarding. The familiar scene replayed, so familiar it gave her a sudden illusion — as if she herself had lived through one more grand, gentle proposal.
A moment later, the Yao Yuan in the picture slipped away from the noisy crowd and turned back alone to the quiet mirror room.
The Yao Yuan watching from above felt her heart stir. She knew clearly: the most crucial moment of this whole crossing was about to arrive.
And so it was.
When the slightly younger Yao Yuan in the mirror room raised her eyes toward the slowly turning vortex of star-clouds above the dome, a hexagonal screen on one wall — until now dim and silent — abruptly lit up. Light flowed like spilled mercury, like ink staining plain paper, and the figure of the thirty-eight-year-old Yao Yuan slowly condensed out of the void, her outline growing ever clearer.
Two timelines, two ages of the same self, gazed quietly at each other across the cold screen and the unbreachable barrier of time and space.
There was no shock or dread of a first meeting, no tense scrutiny of a repeated encounter. After nine crossings through time and space, this gaze held only the calm and release of dust settling. They shared the same thread of soul, had walked utterly different lives that mirrored each other, and now met here — like a reunion across the years, gentle and at peace.
The thirty-eight-year-old Yao Yuan on the screen spoke first, her voice clear and bright, carrying a knowing little laugh and, beneath it, a flicker of haste that came and went in an instant — she had long grasped the rules, and knew the time for this gaze across time was extremely limited.
"When I confirmed things with Zhao Yiming, it wasn't long after Yu Hao had proposed to you. I never expected that on my side, Zhao Yiming has only just proposed to me, and over here you've come to the very same ending."
Her gaze was clear, piercing through layer upon layer of time to land on the self across from her, her tone certain and full of feeling. "It seems some things really can't be gotten around. No matter how many turns you take in between, no matter which road you switch to, the meeting-point where you're meant to converge always waits in the same place."
The thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan outside the mirror heard this, and a tangled resonance surged in her heart. Almost without thinking, she turned the brand-new ring on the ring finger of her left hand, the diamond's edges scraping the pad of her finger with a real, solid feel. "Yes. I didn't see it coming myself, either." She spoke as if confiding to an old friend who knew every cause and consequence, far more relaxed now. "After I last saw you, I threw everything into handling the equity dispute with Zhuge Jinye. Zhao Yiming was a huge help — the lawyer he introduced is top-tier in the field, someone an ordinary person could never even reach. Once the storm settled, our paths crossed more and more, naturally."
She tilted her head slightly, looking back over the days of being together, her eyes full of clear-eyed certainty. "The longer we were together, the more fitting it felt. There was no earth-shaking flutter, but there was a full-spectrum sameness of frequency. The rhythm of our thinking, the scale on which we handle things, our ambition for our careers, our hopes for marriage and the future — all of it on the same channel."
"I'm thirty-seven, he's forty. We're both long past the age of chasing illusory romance."
"For us, the best feeling has never been unconditional favor and pampering. It's deep understanding, recognition, and fit. It's a lifelong alliance, standing shoulder to shoulder, holding each other up. For me, he is simply the most suitable, the steadiest and most fitting, partner in life."
She said "suitable" with an easy candor, no romanticized gloss — and yet it caught exactly the ultimate emotional pursuit of women like them: not illusory doting, but a solid alliance grounded in deep understanding and recognition.
The Yao Yuan in the mirror nodded, not at all surprised. Of course she understood. However the fine-tuning had gone, they were the same soul at the source, with the same reason and clarity, the same pride and fragility. Facing life's great choices, their judgment and their trade-offs naturally arrived at the same destination by different roads.
Just as she was about to press for more details — how Zhuge Jinye fared afterward in the other timeline, or whether there had been new progress in business — that familiar feeling of the soul being pulled loose returned without warning.
This time the tug was unusually gentle, and yet unusually swift.
Unlike the crude dragging of before, it was more like consciousness settling briefly into stillness, then being gently carried away from the void. Beneath her wine-red cashmere sweater, the heart-guarding jade worn against her skin suddenly warmed — a clear, mellow heat, like a wordless farewell, and like a final protection.
The thirty-eight-year-old Yao Yuan in the mirror faded and grew transparent at speed, until she dissolved completely into the faint light, as if she had never appeared at all.
Only the thirty-seven-year-old Yao Yuan remained, standing alone in the mirror room of her own timeline, facing a screen that had just returned to ordinary and reflected back her solitary figure, lost for a moment in thought.
...
Yao Yuan's consciousness returned again, like slowly waking from a long, deep meditation.
The first thing she caught clearly was a solid, warm embrace, and beneath the chest the steady, regular thud of a heartbeat — boom, boom — settled and strong. Then came the cool, crisp scent of aftershave lingering at the tip of her nose, mixed with the faint, cold smell of metal and ozone unique to the mirror room — familiar and reassuring.
Her long lashes trembled lightly, and she slowly opened her eyes.
"Yuanyuan?"
A low, taut voice fell from above, pressing down on an anxiety it couldn't quite hide. The arms around her tightened all at once, holding a careful preciousness and a lingering dread.
Her vision focused out of the blur, and Zhao Yiming's features, close at hand, came clear. Behind the gold-rimmed glasses his eyes were heavy with worry, his brow knotted tight, his thin lips pressed into a clean, taut line.
He was holding her, his steps halted, as if he had been about to carry her out of the mirror room. Behind him the sensor door was sliding slowly open, and from the corridor outside soft, bright light spilled in, throwing the panic in his eyes into even sharper relief.
"I..." Yao Yuan managed a single syllable, her voice a little hoarse.
"You're awake!" Zhao Yiming stopped at once, adjusting his hold with the gentlest of motions so she could rest more comfortably in the crook of his arm, then lowered his head to study her face closely. "How do you feel? Where does it hurt? You fainted just now."
Fainted... right, the blackout caused by the crossing. Yao Yuan quickly pieced the situation together. It seemed Zhao Yiming had noticed she was gone, come out to look for her, and found her collapsed on the floor. She took quick stock of herself: apart from a faint weariness and that mild floating sensation that always came after switching consciousness, nothing felt wrong. The lingering warmth of the heart-guarding jade still threaded through her flesh, gently mending the wear on her spirit — perhaps that was why she had come through the crossing in such steady shape.
"I'm fine." Yao Yuan cleared her throat softly and kept her tone as steady as she could, raising her eyes to the man brimming with worry. A gentle, soothing smile spread in her eyes as she lifted a hand to pat his tense arm. "Really, I'm fine, don't be nervous. Put me down — don't worry the family outside. Today is our good day."
Zhao Yiming didn't let go right away. He still held her firmly, his sharp gaze examining her face, her pupils, her expression in fine detail, confirming again and again that her eyes were clear, her breathing steady, her color slowly warming back — only then did the tense line of his shoulders ease a fraction.
He was silent for a moment, then at last did as she asked, carefully and steadily setting her down, his arm staying firmly around hers the whole time, not daring to slacken in the slightest, guiding her at a slow pace to sit on the corridor's rest bench.
"Really no discomfort?" He turned to look at her, his tone carrying the practical, brook-no-argument concern unique to a science man. "If there's even the slightest thing wrong, we go to the hospital right now for a full checkup."
Warmth stirred in Yao Yuan's heart. She leaned in slightly and rested gently against his chest. It was a rare thing for her — a fully reliant, willing closeness, soft and candid.
"Don't make a mountain out of a molehill." She raised her eyes to him, real tenderness pooling there, her tone holding just the right measure of languor and softness. "It's probably that last night's year-end livestream drained me too much, and then today I got hit out of nowhere by your surprise proposal. The swings of emotion were too big — for a moment my blood and breath went unsteady and I went weak and lost myself."
"My fiancé's proposal packs too much firepower — I couldn't take it for a second there." Half-joking, she dissolved his anxiety, attributing every strangeness to being moved and being tired — reason and feeling fused, beyond reproach.
Zhao Yiming looked at the true tenderness and warmth in her eyes, the fully trusting ease of her, confirmed she wasn't merely forcing herself through, and only then did the boulder in his heart slowly come to rest. He knew her: she wasn't someone fragile enough to faint easily, but she also never joked about her own body. Her composure now meant her own judgment was that there was nothing seriously wrong.
In the end he didn't insist on the hospital. He only lifted a hand and lightly touched the back of his fingers to her slightly cool cheek, then firmly wrapped up her slightly cool fingertips, the warmth of his palm flowing into her without pause, steadily soothing every ripple in her heart.
"Rest here a while longer. The moment anything feels off, tell me at once."
"Mm." Yao Yuan answered obediently, leaning quietly into his arms, drawing in the steadiness and warmth that were his alone.
And in her heart, she had already calmly and clearly reviewed the whole sequence from beginning to end.
This crossing — from fainting to waking — had taken, in real time, probably less than ten minutes.
From the first to the ninth, all the crossings through time and space, spanning several months, came in this moment to a complete close.
Nine returns, nine times watching from the sidelines, nine conversations and confirmations with the self of another timeline. At last she had seen, in full, every thread of fate, every gain and loss, everything fixed and everything that could be changed.
By her agreement with the old gentleman Wang Baoguo, after nine crossings would come the time for her to keep the appointment at the Yellow River and dissolve the Mirror Pact.
She raised her eyes to the man at her side — tenderly guarding her, his heart full of concern, the man who had just become her fiancé — and a tangle of feelings welled up in her heart. The bronze mirror, the Mirror Pact, the time-travel, the borrowing of fortune: this greatest secret and variable, the one that had run through her whole past year and hung over her fate, she had never breathed to anyone. For one, there was no way to begin; for another, no one would believe so bizarre an experience, so far beyond the bounds of science; and for a third, she didn't want to drag others into it for no reason.
But now everything had changed.
Zhao Yiming was already her fiancé, the one she had assented to with her own lips and settled on, the lifelong alliance who would walk shoulder to shoulder with her for the rest of her days. They were about to bind the most intimate emotional and legal ties, sharing fortune and misfortune, honor and disgrace alike.
The trip to the Yellow River held an uncertain fate. Whether the shackles of the ancient mirror could be wholly dissolved, whether the Mirror Pact would backlash, how many unknown risks lay hidden on the road ahead — she could foresee none of it.
She could no longer keep him on the outside, could no longer let him face the unknown crisis at her side while knowing nothing at all. Still less could she go on bearing every secret and danger alone, separated from the person closest to her by a barrier that could never be pierced.
It was time to confess everything.
Once today's festivities ended, she would tell Zhao Yiming everything — every hidden thing, every experience, every torment and fear — exactly as it had been.
Sitting quietly to steady her breath for a while, Yao Yuan shed the last of her lingering weariness, her color fully back to normal. She straightened up, smoothed the hem of her dress, and raised her eyes to Zhao Yiming, brows curving, her smile bright and open. "There, I've completely come round. Let's head back — don't keep everyone waiting too long."
Zhao Yiming studied her a while longer, confirmed she was full of life and entirely well, and only then nodded, reassured, reaching out to lace his fingers through hers again.
On their ring fingers, the two diamond rings occasionally brushed together with a small, crisp chime, gentle and solemn. He quietly tucked away all his worry and returned to his usual steady, composed self, walking shoulder to shoulder with her back into the room full of liveliness and sincere congratulations.
The time that followed was warm and peaceful, the hours calm and serene.
Yao Yuan reined in every feeling, shed the turmoil and weight at the bottom of her heart, and met everyone's congratulations with composed gentleness. She traded easy laughter and chat with Jin Lan and Liu Qingzhuo, accepted her friends' heartfelt good wishes, and conversed lightly with the core staff of Zhao Yiming's company — measured in every step, gracious and warm.
The room was sweet with champagne, fragrant with fine food, bright with the laughter of family and friends, warm with a homely glow. She settled serenely into it all, savoring the completeness and tenderness of the moment, yet always keeping one corner of clarity in her heart, quietly planning everything that came next.
At two in the afternoon, the banquet drew toward its end, and the guests began saying their goodbyes and leaving one after another.
Jin Lan stepped up and hugged her tight, her eyes full of genuine joy. "It's finally settled! You and Zhao Yiming are a true perfect match — you'd better be happy. We're all waiting for the wedding wine!"
Lu Ang stood to one side, nodding along with a smile, all sincere congratulations.
Liu Qingzhuo took her hand, her eyes gentle. "I'm truly happy for you today. If there's anything at all I can help with as you prepare for the wedding, just say the word, don't be shy."
Chen Ye stepped over to shake Zhao Yiming's hand. Between men, congratulations needed no words — all of it tucked into a knowing look and the strength of a grip.
Wang Qian was among the last batch of guests to leave.
He walked at an unhurried pace up to the two of them, his smile warm and considerate, decorous and composed as ever. "Yao Yuan, Yiming, congratulations to you both — a true union of pearl and jade, a match made by heaven."
His gaze rested on Yao Yuan's face for the briefest instant, and through his eyes flashed something very faint and hard to fathom — like insight, like understanding — gone in a blink, too quick to catch. Before Yao Yuan could savor it, he had already withdrawn his gaze, clapped Zhao Yiming on the shoulder with a smile, and taken his composed leave.
The clamor finally dispersed; every guest had gone.
The vast banquet hall fell quiet in an instant, leaving only a scattering of staff tidying the remains in soft voices. The liveliness that had boiled through the room moments before receded like a tide, leaving behind only the full, settled stillness of dust come to rest.
"Tired?" Zhao Yiming turned to look at her, lifting a hand to brush aside a stray wisp of hair by her ear, the motion tender to the utmost.
"I'm all right." Yao Yuan shook her head gently and raised her eyes to him. Her gaze was calm yet unusually grave, carrying a seriousness that couldn't be ignored. "Yiming, let's go to my apartment. There's something important I have to tell you."
Zhao Yiming raised an eyebrow slightly.
He knew Yao Yuan too well — steady and unruffled by nature, never letting anything show on her face, rarely speaking of anything in so grave and serious a tone. Something stirred in his heart, but he asked nothing extra, trusting her completely, and nodded gently. "All right."
The car drove smoothly toward Yao Yuan's apartment, threading through the bright, clear light of a New Year's Day afternoon. The streets still carried the rich flavor of the holiday, full of festive life.
Yao Yuan gazed quietly out the window at the streetscape rushing backward, her fingertips unconsciously stroking the diamond ring on her ring finger over and over. Hard, cold, and then, warmed by her body heat, gradually warm. It stood for the rest of her life now fixed, and it weighed on her with the secret she had hidden for a year.
What she was taking him back to was not just an apartment.
It was that ancient bronze mirror — the one through which she had tried, in another timeline, to change the course of her own life, to go back over the torments and insights of the past.
What she was going to confess to him was every time-and-space experience and pact of fate that lay beyond the bounds of science, absurd and bizarre, that no one could understand.
She couldn't foresee his reaction — whether he would be shocked, doubtful, baffled, or wholly accepting, standing by her side.
But she had made up her mind.
Not only to face the unknown by the Yellow River, but more because, from the moment she had nodded yes to his proposal, the rest of her life — bright and clear or treacherous and rough, steady and smooth or perilous and uncertain — was to be lived in honesty with him.
The car came to a steady stop below the apartment building.
Yao Yuan drew a deep breath, pressed down the ripple in her heart, pushed open the door, and stepped out.
Nine returns through fate had now come to a close.
And the true chapter of the future was only about to be drawn slowly open, with her confession.

========================================

Chapter 101 — Coffee in the Warm Sun: Telling Zhao Yiming the Secret of the Mirror Pact
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640348.html

Back at the riverside apartment, the afternoon sun was at its fullest.

The two of them stepped inside and changed their shoes. The soft click of the latch in the entryway shut out the last of the lingering bustle from the world outside. Yao Yuan told Zhao Yiming to make himself comfortable, then went straight to the kitchen, where she brewed two cups of espresso in a moka pot. Carrying the dark-brown coffee with its rich, oily aroma, she invited him to come into the study with her.

To Yao Yuan, the study felt like the right place to lay out every absurd secret and confess everything that had come before.

The north side of the study was one whole wall of floor-to-ceiling glass. The warm northern winter sun poured in without restraint, splitting the room into two interwoven blocks of light and shadow. Beyond the window the Yellow River surged on without pause; under the clear sky its rippling waters were crushed by the sunlight into a sky full of leaping golden scales, rising and falling slowly with the current.

They sat facing each other across the desk, bathed in the cascade of light, each cradling a warm porcelain cup, the long, bitter fragrance of coffee drifting quietly through the still air. For a while neither of them spoke. There was only the faint trickle of the water heater, which made the room feel all the more peaceful—and quietly set the stage for a confession that would upend everything he thought he knew.

After a long moment, Yao Yuan gently set down her cup, drew her fingers in, and pressed down the last trace of unease in her heart. She leaned forward and pulled open the bottommost drawer of the desk. Inside there were no cluttered files or odds and ends—only a dark-brown box of old sandalwood, lying quietly. The box had been handled over and over through the years; the patina on its surface was warm and thick, glowing with a calm, deep light.

She set the box down between them and slowly lifted the lid. There, cushioned in soft dark velvet, a plain, palm-sized bronze mirror lay quietly dormant, steeped in years and in unknown mystery.

Yao Yuan pinched the wrapping velvet between her fingers, lifted the bronze mirror out steadily, and held it across to Zhao Yiming. "Take a look at this."

Zhao Yiming raised his hand and took it. A bone-deep chill spread across his palm in an instant, as though the old mirror carried its own cold, sealed off from all the warmth around it. The mirror face was only seven or eight centimeters across, its edges polished smooth and round, fine to the touch as jade—nothing like ordinary metal. The body of the mirror was a deep, brooding gray-green, its mottled verdigris scattered across the surface—not messy or grimy, but layered, holding a dark, subtle texture all its own.

At the center of the mirror's back was a small, delicate bridge-shaped knob, ringed by an intricate, finely cast relief of Kui-dragons and cloud-and-thunder patterns, interlacing and coiling in layer upon layer. The slanting sunlight swept across the body of the mirror; the grooves of the pattern sank into shadow, while the raised ridges refracted fine threads of gold. At the dragon's eyes especially, the lines ran in a strange, secretive direction—stare at them long enough and a dizzying illusion would rise, the sense of being watched from somewhere hidden.

He turned the mirror over. Its face was a dim, dull yellow; the reflection it gave back was blurred and warped, sketching out only the faint outline of his features, like looking at himself through a thick fog. As an object alone, it was a finely crafted, well-preserved old bronze mirror of considerable collector's value—and beyond that, nothing unusual at all.

Zhao Yiming lifted his eyes to Yao Yuan, a clear question in his gaze, waiting quietly for her to go on.

Yao Yuan met his clear, rational gaze, drew in a soft breath, and for a moment had no idea where to begin. The man across from her had spent his whole life believing in mathematical logic, biological law, AI modeling, and objective principles—a lifetime spent deconstructing everything in the world through science. To explain to him an old mirror, time-reversal, the borrowing of fortune, a fated pact—absurd things that fell entirely outside the modern scientific framework—was far harder than dissecting human nature or playing out a negotiation.

She needed a real, concrete time-anchor that they had both witnessed, something to give all these bizarre accounts a place to stand.

"Do you remember that winter two years ago, the night we first met for dinner?" she said softly, her voice unusually clear in the quiet study.

Zhao Yiming paused, and the memory rose up sharp and clear at once; a faint, gentle warmth flickered in his eyes. "I remember it well. You wore a red dress under a white sable coat, and when you pushed the door open and walked in, you were like a fierce rose blooming straight out of the flames—dazzling and bold."

He paused, his tone still even but carrying a hint of amused recollection. "And the very first thing you said was loaded with provocation. You showed no mercy at all, peeling open my scars right away—bringing up my first-generation 'Genesis Engine' model, how it burned through several hundred million and finally blew up my own server room."

At that, the corners of Yao Yuan's lips curved up into a faint smile.

Back then she had come in probing step by step, with the goal of sizing up a partnership, her bearing aggressive and her words sharp, set on seizing the upper hand and taking control of the negotiation. And he had given no ground either—rational, incisive, his whole presence on full display. Neither of them could have foreseen that that needle-sharp, swords-drawn first meeting would ultimately lead to the deep bond of souls and shared fate they had now.

That smile quietly diluted the heaviness of the room, which was about to face its secret head-on; the taut atmosphere loosened a little.

"It was that day." Yao Yuan reined in her smile and returned to the point, her words clear and steady, landing solidly. "Before that dinner, Shuai Hongqiang came to my company to discuss a project. This bronze mirror was the gift he gave me that day—our first meeting in eight years."

She instinctively studied Zhao Yiming's expression, then went on candidly: "You should remember—at the Pretty Big One AI launch, he came to offer his congratulations. Shuai Hongqiang is an old friend I've known for years in Jinshi, and also... my ex-boyfriend."

Not a ripple crossed Zhao Yiming's face. No surprise, no probing—he simply listened quietly and waited for the rest. That utterly steady acceptance gave Yao Yuan the courage to go on.

"After that dinner, once I got home, the strange things started happening." Yao Yuan slowed her pace, recounting this most absurd and inexplicable experience in the calmest, most objective tone she could manage. "Without any warning, my consciousness was forcibly pulled out and sent straight back to the point in my life when I was twenty-two. It was like being an immersed bystander to a hologram of the past—watching old events with my own eyes, even able to talk to the version of myself in that other time and space."

Zhao Yiming's brow shifted almost imperceptibly, but he didn't interrupt; the fingers gripping his cup only tightened slightly.

"At first I thought it was a hallucination, a fantasy born of sheer exhaustion." Yao Yuan owned her initial struggle frankly. "But after it happened again and again, I had to admit this was a real anomaly. Later, out of guilt over the 'borrowing of fortune,' Shuai Hongqiang confessed the secret of the bronze mirror and took me to see Old Chen, a highly respected antiques appraiser here in the city."

"That was when I learned this is no ordinary relic. It's said to have come from the hands of a Tang-dynasty alchemist, Lingxuzi, with secret arts hidden inside it. And my fate is unusual—I bear the Great Fortune of the Nine-Purple Departing Fire, and under specific emotions and states of mind, I accidentally activated the mirror's time-tracing mechanism."
