Yao Yuan chose her words carefully, trying to avoid anything too mystical, but the core facts couldn't be sidestepped. "Put simply, it can briefly draw my consciousness back to certain key moments in my past life. It isn't the body traveling through time—it's pure consciousness tracing back, watching the past, connecting with the version of myself in another time and space."

"Briefly drawn back? Sent back?" Zhao Yiming seized on the key words with precision, his questions cutting straight to the heart of it, carrying a researcher's particular caution. "Frequency? Duration? Trigger conditions?"

"There's no fixed pattern to the triggers. Most of them are tied to swings in my emotions, major life decisions, shifts at the turning points of fate." Yao Yuan carefully reviewed every experience and answered precisely. "The perceived duration drifts—sometimes it feels as long as several hours, sometimes it's gone in a flash. But measured against real time, the longest is under an hour, the shortest only a few minutes. Take that ninth time-tracing just now in the mirror room: it felt like I spent at least half an hour in that other time and space, but in reality less than ten minutes had passed."

Zhao Yiming was silent for a moment, his gaze falling back to the bronze mirror in his palm, his fingertips tracing the cool Kui-dragon pattern. In an instant he strung all the clues together. "So when you suddenly fainted in the mirror room just now, it wasn't weakness or a daze—it was the normal drain after your ninth tracing-back ended, your spirit briefly spent?"

"Yes." Yao Yuan nodded gently, and felt something in her chest ease.

The greatest fortune of confiding in someone supremely clever and transparently rational is that there's no need to belabor the causes, no need to lay groundwork or explain—they always piece the fragments together in an instant, smooth out the logic, and strike straight to the core.

Now that the most crucial opening had been made, everything that followed flowed effortlessly. Yao Yuan went along the timeline, slowly recounting every secret: from her self-doubt and search for proof after the first time-travel, to the confirmation and fear of the second and third; to how Shuai Hongqiang, jittery day and night, unable to bear the karmic pressure of being the bearer of the Pact, came forward to confess the bronze mirror's origins and the Mirror Pact's karmic bond between the two of them; from how the two of them tracked down the descendants of the mirror-maker, to finally finding the old Mr. Wang Baoguo, the mirror-maker's descendant, and the method for breaking this uncanny pact.

Throughout, her tone stayed even, with no subjective coloring, no emotional filter—like reviewing a complex, precise project, clear in its order and complete in its logic. The bronze mirror, the Mirror Pact, the nine tracings-back, the backlash from borrowing fortune, the conditions for breaking the pact—she laid them all out, one by one, in full.

When the last word landed, the study sank into a long stillness.

Beyond the window the Yellow River rolled on without end, its ancient, ceaseless sound faintly reaching the ear, making the silence inside feel all the deeper.

Yao Yuan picked up her now-cooled coffee and took a sip. The bitterness washed over the tip of her tongue, yet her heart felt lighter and looser than ever before. For an entire year this absurd, heavy secret had pressed on her heart like solid ice—no one to tell, no one who could understand, not even a word of it shared with her own mother. Now that it lay fully bared before the person she trusted most, more than half the thousand-pound weight she had borne alone had quietly lifted.

Zhao Yiming stayed silent for a long while.

He raised the mirror to the light again, studying every patch of verdigris, every line of pattern, every inch of texture. On the physical level—the material structure, the marks of craftsmanship—this was simply a beautiful, ancient mirror from long ago. But the supernatural qualities of "time and space," "pact," "tracing back," "borrowing fortune"—none of them could be captured by any instrument or corroborated by any law.

And yet he chose to believe completely.

He didn't believe in empty mysticism or fated destiny, but he believed absolutely in Yao Yuan's mind and character. She had always been rational and clear-eyed, steady and restrained, rigorous in her logic; she never indulged in baseless fantasy, and she certainly wouldn't fabricate an absurd story at the expense of her own fate and safety. What's more, every separation, every tracing-back, every detail she described was real and finely textured, echoing layer upon layer—nothing that could be conjured up out of thin air.

That science couldn't explain it didn't mean it didn't exist. The beauty of mathematics lay in accepting that certain axioms need no proof; the complexity of biology lay in the countless unsolved mysteries life itself still held. He studied AI, exploring the boundaries of consciousness and intelligence; he understood better than most that humanity's grasp of the world, of itself, of time and existence, might not have touched even the tip of the iceberg.

After a long while, he set the bronze mirror down, raised his eyes to Yao Yuan with a clear, sharp gaze, and cut straight into the most practical risk analysis. "Let me lay it out. To break this 'Mirror Pact,' you need fingertip blood from three people—you, Shuai Hongqiang, and that old Mr. Wang. It has to be done after a number of time-travels that is three, or a multiple of three. You wrap the mirror in nine layers of specially made cinnabar-red cloth, and sink it to the riverbed at a specific stretch of the Yellow River, at a specific hour. Is that right?"

"That's right," Yao Yuan answered.

"Before you found old Mr. Wang, you'd already completed seven tracings-back, missing the third and sixth marks, so you could only wait for the ninth." Zhao Yiming's mind raced, swiftly locking onto the core risk. "Today you've just completed the ninth—it's the safest window right now, the one with the fewest variables. If you delay, there's a very high chance of triggering a tenth or eleventh tracing-back, missing the mark again, and then you'd have to wait until the twelfth. In the meantime, all the unknown backlash, all the twists of fate—we'd have no way to predict any of it."

"So we have to break the pact as soon as possible."

"Right." Watching him so calm and composed, with everything grounded and accounted for, the last trace of unease in Yao Yuan's heart dissolved completely, replaced by a deep sense of recognition and warmth.

He hadn't questioned whether the story was true, hadn't pressed her on the mystical details, hadn't shown any morbid curiosity or fear, and certainly hadn't fixated on the fact that she'd kept it from him. Instead, he'd swiftly grasped the heart of the problem: risk control and a solution. That utterly "Zhao Yiming"–style response was exactly the security she most needed.

She let herself relax a little and deliberately blinked, taking on a teasing tone. "I've just told you all these bizarre, eerie things—aren't you going to think I'm making up a story? Or... blame me for keeping it from you all this time?"

Looking at the rare, faintly innocent contrast in her expression, a very faint, gentle smile crossed Zhao Yiming's eyes, though he kept his face deliberately serious. "I do blame you for one thing."

Yao Yuan arched a brow slightly, waiting for the rest.

"For being too stubborn, for carrying this alone for so long." His tone softened in an instant, his gaze sincere and grave. "A secret this absurd and inexplicable, with no one to share it—anyone in your place wouldn't dare show it lightly to another soul. That you were willing to tell me means you trust me completely. I'm grateful for that, and I cherish it."

He paused, then added a line of his usual cool humor: "At the very least, now I don't have to worry that my fiancée might suddenly be swept off by some unknown consciousness one day, or be invited by some special agency to 'assist with research.'"

At that, Yao Yuan finally let go entirely, her eyes curving as she laughed.

None of the heaviness, doubt, confusion, estrangement, or argument she'd braced for appeared. In their place was total acceptance, transparent understanding, and the tacit alliance of two people standing side by side to face the storm. The moment the secret was laid bare, every barrier melted away, and the bond between them grew deeper and firmer than before.

"The urgent thing now is to coordinate with Shuai Hongqiang and old Mr. Wang—nail down the time, the place, the list of materials." Zhao Yiming reined in his smile and returned to his practical mode, arranging things crisply. "The sooner it's done, the better. Cut out every variable. What do you need me to help with?"

"I'll contact Shuai Hongqiang first." Yao Yuan picked up her phone. "He's more anxious than I am. The Mirror Pact, the bearing of fortune, the karmic backlash—it could fall on him. He's very frightened."

When the call connected, Shuai Hongqiang was in a hospital ward. The cast on his daughter Shuai Wenrui's leg was a glaring white. This sudden injury had long since driven his fear of the Mirror Pact's karmic backlash to its peak, leaving him uneasy day and night. Thankfully his daughter was in much better spirits now, propped up at the head of the bed watching a show on a tablet. Seeing that the call was from Yao Yuan, Shuai Hongqiang's heart gave an inexplicable jump. He signaled to his daughter and stepped out into the corridor before answering.

"Shuai Hongqiang, it's me." Yao Yuan's voice came through the receiver, calm and steady, carrying the certainty of dust finally settling. "The ninth time-travel... just ended. The mark for breaking the Mirror Pact is set."

Shuai Hongqiang's knuckles tightened abruptly around the phone, the veins standing out faintly, his throat dry and tight, both thrilled and afraid. "Good! Good! We've finally made it!" he said, the words tumbling out.

Out of the corner of his eye he glanced through the gap in the ward door at his recovering daughter inside, and the fear in his heart grew thicker still—he both longed to break the pact as soon as possible and dreaded what karmic backlash he, as the bearer of the Mirror Pact, might still suffer before it was fully broken, after yet another time-travel of hers. "Let's set it for tomorrow! The sooner the better! Delay breeds trouble. I'll contact old Mr. Wang right now, nail down the hour, the stretch of river, and the full list of everything we need, and I'll tell you the moment it's settled!"

"Then you contact old Mr. Wang, settle the time and place, and let me know." Yao Yuan understood his urgency. "And go over the list of things we need to prepare with him once more."

"Understood. I'll call right away. Stay in touch." Shuai Hongqiang hung up in a rush and immediately pulled up Wang Baoguo's number from his contacts. His hands were shaking a little; he took two deep breaths before pressing the dial key.

Listening to the busy tone on the line, Yao Yuan put down her phone and looked at Zhao Yiming. "Shuai Hongqiang's gone to make the call. We wait for word."

Zhao Yiming nodded gently, rose, and walked over to stand before her. He reached out naturally, pulled her up from the chair, and drew her steadily into his arms.

It was a purely comforting, supporting embrace—not a trace of desire in it, only complete acceptance, protection, and the certainty of standing side by side.

"It'll go smoothly," he said by her ear, his voice steady and strong.

Yao Yuan buried her face against his shoulder, breathing in the reassuring scent of him, and gave a soft "Mm."

Outside the window the daylight slanted west, the sky full of warm sun gradually shedding its fierce glare, dyeing the rolling Yellow River a gorgeous orange-red before it slowly settled into a calm purple-gray. In the changeover between day and night, all things lay silent and still.

The secret was now shared. The road ahead was still uncertain; the breaking of the pact at the Yellow River held fortunes good or ill no one could foretell, and the karmic backlash was not yet over. But from now on, she was no longer alone in fighting an absurd fate.

Their embracing silhouettes formed a quiet, solid outline in the lengthening light and shadow of the study.

The countdown to breaking the pact had begun. And all the tangled fate, the shackles of the Mirror Pact, the old fears of the past would, before long, be handed over to that great river that had flowed in silence for millions of years.

Chapter 102 — New Year's Night Fireworks, Disaster in an Instant
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640349.html

New Year's Night Fireworks, Disaster in an Instant
Shuai Hongqiang reached out at once to Wang Baoguo, descendant of the mirror-masters.
On the phone, the old man's voice was as low and unhurried as ever, betraying no emotion. Shuai Hongqiang fought down the anxiety churning in his chest, speaking fast, even with a faint, barely perceptible tremor, as he explained that Yao Yuan had just completed her ninth time-travel.
A long silence settled over the line, broken only by the faint sounds of pages turning and fingertips counting off calculations. A full five minutes later, Wang Baoguo's steady voice came back, every word landing with weight: "Tomorrow — the Yi-Si Year of the Snake, the third day of the twelfth lunar month, the hour of You."
"A yin year, a yin month, a yin day, a yin hour — four layers of yin at their peak. As the sun sets and the air sinks, when the Yellow River's vapors reach their most yin and heavy, that is the best time to dissolve the Mirror Pact and seal away the cursed thing."
He then fixed the final location: "The place will be the White Horse Rapids shoal below Jincheng Pass."
"The White Horse Rapids…" Shuai Hongqiang repeated under his breath, his heart sinking abruptly, an inexplicable chill creeping up his spine.
He knew well how treacherous that place was. The White Horse Rapids of the Yellow River — the perilous shoal below Jincheng Pass. The channel was narrow and choked with jagged rocks, the current roaring with towering waves; beneath the surface, hidden reefs tangled together and undertows and whirlpools ran thick. Mountains on both sides locked the valley shut, sunlight rarely reached it, and the gorge was cold and damp, the atmosphere thick and heavy with a sinking weight.
Wang Baoguo seemed to read his misgivings, and explained mildly: "From ancient times this place has been both a ferry crossing and a strategic pass fought over by armies. The killing energy is heavy, the current sinks deep, the field is sealed shut, yin and yang are clearly drawn. It can lock down evil, seal the malevolence, and steady the flow of time — it is the ideal place to dissolve the Mirror Pact and bring this karma to an end. There's no need to worry. Tomorrow at the hour of You, as the sun sinks behind the western mountains and the yin energy first rises, the timing will be exactly right. You and Yao Yuan need only bring the bronze mirror to the appointed place. Cinnabar, red cloth, the offering implements — I will have all of it ready. When the time comes, each of you will draw blood from a fingertip, and that will start the pact-dissolving rite."
"Understood. I'll tell her right away." Shuai Hongqiang answered, then paused and asked cautiously, "Master Wang, what about Old Chen… should we let him know? Old Chen was the first to see through the bronze mirror's secret; he gave Yao Yuan the soul-quieting old jade to protect her, and helped us track you down every step of the way. In all fairness, he deserves to know the outcome."
Wang Baoguo considered for a moment. "Old Chen and I go back a long way. He's a sensible man, and he can keep a secret. There's no harm in telling him. If he wants to come and witness the result, he may. Only bear in mind: this matter must not be made known to anyone unrelated. When dissolving the Mirror Pact, nothing is worse than a crowd of people and a tangle of energies leaking the workings of Heaven."
"You can rest easy on that." Shuai Hongqiang agreed.
Hanging up, Shuai Hongqiang immediately wrote out a message laying out everything Wang Baoguo had worked out and settled — the hour, the place, the reasons, and all the requirements for dissolving the pact — set down clearly point by point, and sent it to Yao Yuan so she could prepare in advance and gather her strength.
Afraid he might have left something out, he sent Yao Yuan another short, definitive message: "Tomorrow at the hour of You (5 p.m.), gather at the White Horse Rapids shoal. Master Wang will prepare the cinnabar, the red cloth, and the rest. We arrive on time."
With that done, he stood under the cold lights of the hospital corridor, drew a deep breath, and slowly let it out. Then he found Old Chen's number and dialed. The call connected, and Old Chen's voice was as gentle as ever. Shuai Hongqiang skipped the pleasantries and told him directly that the time and place for dissolving the pact were now set — tomorrow at dusk.
Old Chen was silent for a few seconds on the other end, then said slowly, "…So it's come to this, after all. Boss Shuai, I understand. I'll be there tomorrow. Some karma only feels complete when you see it ended with your own eyes. Take every care, all of you." There was a complicated emotion in his tone, like the sigh of dust finally settling, and also like a heavy gravity in the face of some unknown ending.
The words drifted down lightly, yet for no clear reason they pressed an aching weight onto Shuai Hongqiang's chest. The gloom that had clung to him for days hadn't lifted now that the date was set; if anything, it had grown thicker.
He stood in the cold hospital corridor, the smell of disinfectant seeping in everywhere. His daughter, Shuai Wenrui, had been bedridden with a leg injury for nearly a week, the limb fixed in a cast; she wouldn't be discharged to recover until next week. Qiu Ying had gone home briefly to wash up and change before returning to the room, her face freshly tidied, but with the exhaustion of days of bedside care in her eyes. She was sitting beside the bed now, looking at her phone. Shuai Hongqiang spoke with her quietly about their daughter's condition, looked at the girl's peacefully sleeping face, and let his heart ease a little, then turned and left the hospital.
Today was the first day of the new year, and he and Lin Wan had arranged earlier to have dinner that evening with two families of close friends.
These two families were deeply tied to him. One was Old Xu, a mid-level leader in a government department with real power, who oversaw approvals for one of his key projects; the other was Boss Li, who had worked under him for years and was now the contractor on his largest project. The three families were close, their interests entangled and their bonds warm, and the children were near in age, raised side by side, close to one another and frequently in and out of each other's homes.
On the drive to the mall where they were to meet, Shuai Hongqiang forced down the unease in his heart, sealing away for the moment all the gloom of the Mirror Pact, the dissolving of it, and the karmic backlash. He needed a brief stretch of ease, and he needed to maintain this solid foundation of connections.
He parked, walked into the Korean barbecue restaurant on the mall's top floor, and was met by a wave of noisy chatter and the smell of food. Old Xu's and Boss Li's families had already arrived, seated around a large table, the children chasing and roughhousing in the open space nearby. Lin Wan rose to meet him and asked quietly, "How's Wenrui?"
"Recovering well. She can be discharged next week. Qiu Ying's watching over her," Shuai Hongqiang answered simply, raising a hand to pat the back of hers. He shed the heaviness clinging to him, put on the mild smile of social occasions, and slipped into the gathering.
"Hongqiang's here! We were just waiting on you!" Old Xu called out with a laugh, while Boss Li was already on his feet pouring him a drink. The women smiled and exchanged pleasantries too. On the table the charcoal burned bright, pork belly sizzling on the grill, fat dripping and flaring into tiny flames that lit up the relaxed smiles on every face.
The three men were soon trading toasts. Old Xu lifted his cup and remarked with a meaning behind his words, "Hongqiang, that project of yours is really riding high lately, the prospects are excellent! Come, a toast to you — here's to a fresh start in the new year, and to climbing even higher!" Shuai Hongqiang thanked him over and over and drained his cup, knowing full well how much of that toast was friendship and how much was an expectation that the project would go smoothly. Boss Li raised his cup too, his words more down-to-earth: "Brother Qiang, next year on the construction side, don't you worry — I, Old Li, will do it up beautifully for you and never drop the ball! After all these years working together, it's the straight dealing that's carried us through!" Shuai Hongqiang clinked cups with him, a smile on his face, both their eyes holding the trust and unspoken understanding ground out over many years.
The adults traded toasts, talking business, current affairs, and their children's education, the mood lively and genuine — interests intertwined, but human warmth in it too. This was the social arena of grown-ups: complicated and solid.
On the other side, the three little boys — Old Xu's nine-year-old son Xu Hao, Boss Li's ten-year-old son Li Haoran, and Shuai Wenyao — had long since eaten their fill and, with the adults' tacit permission, run off to the lounge area by the window to play a card game they'd brought along.
"It's my turn to draw a card!" Shuai Wenyao reached out eagerly.
"Wait," Xu Hao said, pressing down on the deck, sounding like a little grown-up. "Wenyao, that card you just played broke the rules. It's supposed to be my turn." Though only a year older than Shuai Wenyao, he spoke with an unconscious certainty modeled on his father's.
Li Haoran looked at Xu Hao, then at Shuai Wenyao, and said nothing — he just quietly nudged a good card from his hand over toward Xu Hao. He was the oldest and the tallest, yet in their play he subtly deferred to Xu Hao as the leader. His father was the contractor, Xu Hao's father the representative of the "client side," and perhaps this delicate hierarchy, already picked up by the children from the way the adults spoke and behaved at the dinner table, had been internalized and was unconsciously playing out again in their own small world.
Shuai Wenyao blinked, looked at Xu Hao, then at the silent Li Haoran, pursed his lips — but pulled his hand back all the same. "Fine, you go first."
A card game among children, where winning or losing hardly mattered, and yet that brief moment of who got to "interpret the rules" and the small "concession" was like a single drop of water, reflecting a blurred, inverted image of the adult world's tangled web of relationships. Luckily, this little episode was soon swallowed up by the excitement of a cool new card someone drew, and the children were laughing and tumbling together again, that invisible barrier as if it had never existed. They really were good friends who got along, and that childhood friendship was just as real.
The dinner ended amid the noise and a pleasant buzz of wine. The three little boys had long been thinking about the main event still to come — setting off fireworks. The year before, the adults had promised to take them this New Year's night to the designated launch area by the Yellow River to enjoy themselves.
The three mothers drove out, the trunks stuffed full of every kind of firework they'd prepared, to the city-government-designated central launch zone by the Yellow River. It was a wide concrete embankment facing the rushing Yellow River, with an excellent view. By now it was packed with people: young couples leaning together with sparklers in hand, whole families of all ages looking up to watch, and most of all families like theirs, bringing half-grown children to experience the thrill of lighting fireworks themselves. Huge combination fireworks burst open with a roar in the night sky, scattering into dazzling rains of blossom; handheld sparklers traced lines of silver; the children's excited shrieks and the adults' laughter wove together with the distinctive smell of gunpowder smoke into a clamorous festival night scene.
The moment they arrived, Shuai Wenyao, Xu Hao, and Li Haoran were like colts let out of a pen. Amid their mothers' repeated calls of "Be careful!" "Don't point it at people!" "Hold it farther away!" they couldn't wait to start setting off their own, relatively safe fireworks. "Little Bees" buzzed and spun up into the sky, "electric flowers" hissed and burned in their hands, lighting up small faces flushed with excitement and joy. Lin Wan stood with Old Xu's and Boss Li's wives, chatting and smiling as they watched over the children.
Shuai Hongqiang, Old Xu, and Boss Li stood a little farther off by the embankment railing, a few "color-ball launchers" in their hands, firing them off casually at the black river surface, watching the colored balls of light shriek through the night and burst into brief flashes above the water before being swallowed by the darkness and the roar of the current. They chatted idly, savoring this rare, relaxed moment, their gazes drifting tenderly now and then to the children romping not far away.
No one noticed that in another knot of people not far off, a small boy about the same age as Shuai Wenyao and the others, in a blue down jacket, was staring longingly at the "handheld waterfall" Shuai Wenyao had just lit — it spewed a fountain of silver flame, quite a sight. Still less did anyone notice that this little boy had just been turned away by Shuai Wenyao and the others when he tried to cut in line to play with a large fountain firework at a public launch point, on the grounds that "you have to wait your turn." The boy had stomped off in a huff, muttering, "What's the big deal."
And right now, the blue-jacketed boy's father had his back to him, smoking and chatting with a few friends, a big plastic bag stuffed with fireworks at his feet. The boy's gaze flicked between his father's back and that plastic bag. Then he quietly crouched down and quickly fished out from the bag an adult-grade Gatling firework — a thick tube with multiple launch holes, shaped like a miniature rocket launcher. This kind of firework was powerful, with long range and a fierce, rapid discharge: a dangerous category expressly forbidden for children to set off alone.
The boy seemed to have no idea how dangerous the thing was — perhaps he'd only seen adults use them and thought it looked cool. Imitating the grown-ups, he glanced left and right, then pulled out a lighter and clumsily lit the fuse at the tail of the "Gatling."
"Hsss—"
The sharp hiss of the fuse burning fast was drowned out by the deafening firework blasts and the crowd's laughter all around.
The next second—
"Bang! Bang! Bang! Bang! Bang! Bang!"
A burst of dense, violent, machine-gun-sharp explosions cracked out all at once! Not toward the sky, but in a fan, firing level and flat!
Blinding white balls of light, searing to the eyes, wrapped in acrid gunpowder smoke and a visible wave of scorching heat, tore at astonishing speed straight into the backs of Shuai Wenyao, Xu Hao, and Li Haoran — who had their backs turned this way, crouched on the ground trying to light a new firework!
"AHHH—!!!"
The first to ring out was Boss Li's wife's short, piercing scream.
Time stretched and warped without limit in Shuai Hongqiang's eyes. He saw a ball of blinding white light explode where his son was crouched, accompanied by a dull thud of impact. Xu Hao and Li Haoran were yanked into a tackle by the quick-reacting Old Xu and Boss Li, rolling away to either side; the children's terrified cries and the adults' pained grunts rang out at once.
And the one at the very center of that fan of firepower — Shuai Wenyao—
That small figure in the red down jacket, like a little ball of fire, was shoved forward by some enormous unseen force, then crumpled limply, soundlessly, face-down to the ground. Across the back of the red down jacket, several charred black holes appeared in an instant, their edges still wisping faint blue smoke.
"Wenyao—!!!"
Lin Wan's scream cracked and broke. She rushed over like a madwoman, stumbling on the way, almost falling.
Shuai Hongqiang's mind went blank. Every sound in the world — the bursting fireworks, the crowd's cries, his wife's wails — all of it fell away in an instant, leaving only a piercing ringing in his ears and the wild pounding drum of his own heart. Almost on pure instinct, he barreled through every obstacle in his path and threw himself down beside that tiny red figure.
The overpowering, nauseating stench of burnt flesh, mixed with the smell of gunpowder, drove straight up his nose.
"Wenyao… Wenyao!" Lin Wan knelt beside him, both hands hovering in the air, shaking violently, wanting to touch her son but not knowing where she dared, tears bursting out like a breached dam, blurring her vision in an instant. "Baby, look at Mama… look at Mama, please!"
Shuai Hongqiang forced his scattered gaze to focus and looked at his son.
Shuai Wenyao lay face-down, his left cheek and ear pressed against the cold, rough cement. The right cheek that showed… Shuai Hongqiang's stomach convulsed violently.
It was no longer the smooth, rosy little face of his son that he knew. The right half of his face, from cheekbone to jaw, was a mess of blood and torn flesh. The skin was a horrifying mottle of charred black tangled with raw red, the flesh curled back in places to expose deeper tissue beneath, blood welling out from those ghastly wounds and rapidly staining the ground under his cheek. His right ear… the rim of it was scorched black and curled, the ear canal caked with dark red scabs and unidentifiable debris. Most shattering of all was his right eye, clamped shut, the lid swollen and black, a tear at the corner from which tear fluid laced with blood kept seeping, cutting a harrowing streak down the little face caked with black ash and gore.
And atop his head, through the thick black hair, there were clear marks of burning and bloodstains too.
The boy didn't move, gave no response to Lin Wan's heart-rending cries — only the faint, almost imperceptible rise and fall of his chest proved this little life hadn't been entirely snuffed out.
Shuai Hongqiang forced his crumbling composure steady and, with extreme care, used a trembling hand to first check his son's breath — faint as a thread. Then he felt for the carotid artery — a pulse, weak and fast.
Still alive! The realization split his fogged mind like a bolt of lightning.
"Hospital! Get to a hospital!!!" he screamed hoarsely, not knowing to whom. He tore off his own heavy down jacket, heedless of the cold, and with the utmost gentleness and care wrapped the inside of it as completely as he could around his son's bloodied head, neck, and upper body, avoiding the wounds and keeping him as warm as possible. Then, in a posture of near-reverence, summoning every ounce of his self-control, he steadily and firmly lifted that small body wrapped in the jacket — terrifyingly light and yet heavy as a mountain.
"The car! Our car's over there!" His eyes were bloodshot, veins bulging at his temples. He looked toward Lin Wan, who had all but collapsed and was holding herself up on sheer will alone, and his voice forced out through clenched teeth carried the taste of blood: "Lin Wan! Get up! Drive! To the Provincial People's Hospital! Hurry! Hurry, now!!!"
Lin Wan, on the brink of breaking down, was jolted awake by that scream into the most tenacious instinct of motherhood. Her scattered gaze snapped back into focus, landing on the small bundle her husband cradled so carefully — that little mound wrapped in the down jacket, still seeping its stark, vivid red. She forced down the surging despair, wiped away her tears, her eyes turning abruptly cold and sharp, staggered to her feet, and sprinted full-speed toward the parking area.
"Old Xu! Call the police! Hold that family! Don't let a single one of them get away!" Shuai Hongqiang shouted over his shoulder without turning, his son in his arms, as he sprinted toward the car.
"Don't worry! Leave it to me!" Old Xu already had his phone out, dialing 110, rapidly giving the location and the situation, while he and Boss Li planted themselves like iron towers in front of the blue-jacketed father and son — who were dumbstruck with terror and trying to slip away with the boy who'd caused the disaster. Boss Li barked at his own wife, white-faced with fright and clutching the crying Xu Hao and Li Haoran, "Get the kids in the car! Go home right now! Call me when you're there!" Then he glanced at Shuai Hongqiang and Lin Wan racing off and said urgently to Old Xu, "You handle things here! Keep an eye on them! I'm going with them to the hospital — they need the help right now!"
Two cars, against the backdrop of New Year's night's brilliant yet indifferent sea of lights and the fireworks still going off, shot out like two arrows of despair, ripping through the festive clamor, racing wildly and running several red lights, hurtling toward the Provincial People's Hospital as if for their very lives.
Shuai Hongqiang sat in the back seat holding his son while Lin Wan drove so fast the car all but flew, her knuckles on the wheel ghost-white from the force of her grip. With one hand Shuai Hongqiang pressed down hard on the jacket wrapped around his son; with the other, trembling, he fumbled out his phone and, in a hoarse, broken voice, called every leader and specialist at the Provincial People's Hospital he could think of who might be able to help.
"President Zhang! It's me, Shuai Hongqiang! Something's happened to my son! Blast injuries! Severe head trauma! We're on our way to your hospital! Please, get your best neurosurgeons, burn specialists, and eye doctors ready! Operate right away!"
"Director Liu! My son, Shuai Wenyao! Blast injuries! Open cranial trauma! His eye, his ear, both injured! Please, get to the hospital right now! I'll be there any minute!"
"President Wang…"
One call after another, his words incoherent, his voice choking, repeating "blast injuries," "severe head trauma," "he's slipping away." He set aside all his pride and dignity for the sake of buying back even one more second of life for his son. He could feel the small body in his arms — so light, so soft, the breath of life so faint, as if it might slip through his fingers at any moment. A terror unlike any he had ever known flooded him from head to toe like an icy tide, all but suffocating him.

========================================

Chapter 103 — Outside the ICU, Shuai Hongqiang Is Consumed by Guilt and Regret
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640350.html

The car braked hard and stopped at the entrance of the emergency department of the Provincial People's Hospital. Shuai Hongqiang shoved the door open and rushed out with his son in his arms. At the entrance, the head of the emergency department was waiting in person, leading several doctors and nurses with a crash cart at the ready—his phone calls had paid off.
"Give me the boy!" The director's tone was calm and decisive. With practiced efficiency, the medical staff helped Shuai Hongqiang transfer Shuai Wenyao onto the crash cart with the utmost care. In that instant, as the boy was moved, the down jacket wrapped around him slipped open at one corner, and his son's horribly mangled right cheek and swollen, tightly shut eye came back into view. Shuai Hongqiang's vision went black and he nearly toppled over, caught at the last second by Boss Li, who was right behind him.
"Preliminary assessment: severe open traumatic brain injury, with deep burns to the head and face and a ruptured eyeball! Take him straight to the hybrid operating room! Notify the heads of neurosurgery, burns and plastics, ophthalmology, and anesthesiology for an emergency consult! Prep for surgery!" The director gave orders even as he examined the boy, and the crash cart raced down the green-light corridor, vanishing in an instant behind the glow of the "Surgery in Progress" sign.
Shuai Hongqiang tried to follow inside but was stopped by a nurse. "Family members, please wait outside the operating room!"
He felt as if every bone had been pulled from his body; only by leaning against the cold wall did he keep from sliding to the floor. Lin Wan parked the car and ran in. The moment she saw him, then turned toward that shut door with its glaring red light, her steps stopped dead and the last trace of color drained from her face. She didn't cry again—she just stared straight at that door, her body trembling faintly like a leaf in the wind.
Boss Li helped her to a bench at the side and sat her down, then handed Shuai Hongqiang a cigarette. Shuai Hongqiang's hands were shaking so badly it took several tries to light it. He took a fierce drag, and the acrid smoke filling his lungs set off a violent fit of coughing—coughing so hard it brought tears to his eyes.
The minutes and seconds crawled by, each one stretching as long as a century. The corridor outside the operating room was terrifyingly silent, broken only by the uneven, heavy breathing of the three of them and the faint distant clamor of the hospital. The red light above that door was like a cold, merciless eye, fixed on them, passing judgment.
Before long, Old Xu hurried over and reported the situation, his expression grave. "The father and son who caused this are already in police custody, and statements are being taken. The man works at the emergency management bureau—the negligence is confirmed, and he's clearly willing to take full responsibility and cooperate with compensation."
The outcome was clean and clear-cut, but at this moment Shuai Hongqiang and Lin Wan had no mind to spare for blame or compensation. Money and justice meant nothing, shrank to nothing, in the face of their child's life or death.
At last, after a wait that felt like a whole lifetime, the operating room door opened. A doctor in blue scrubs, mask, and cap stepped out, his eyes full of exhaustion and a kind of gravity that comes from having seen life and death too many times.
Shuai Hongqiang and Lin Wan sprang from their chairs like coiled springs and rushed up to him.
"Doctor, how is my son?" Shuai Hongqiang's voice was so hoarse it was barely audible.
The doctor pulled down his mask, revealing the serious face of a middle-aged man. He didn't answer right away. His gaze swept over the ashen, despairing faces of Shuai Hongqiang and Lin Wan, and he hesitated a few seconds before speaking in a voice as steady as he could manage, though every word landed with crushing weight. "Parents, you need to prepare yourselves. The boy's condition… is not optimistic."
Lin Wan's body lurched violently. Shuai Hongqiang held her up with all his strength, his own heart seized as if by an icy hand.
"A high-velocity firework fragment entered the skull from behind the right eye socket, causing combined fractures of the orbital and temporal bones. The fragment is lodged right against a branch of the middle cerebral artery and the cavernous sinus—extremely likely to trigger fatal intracranial hemorrhage. At the same time, the blast wave caused acute diffuse brain swelling. Intracranial pressure is off the charts, and early-stage brain herniation has already set in, compressing the brainstem. He could lose his life at any moment."
"The right eyeball has ruptured completely, with massive loss of intraocular contents—the chance of saving any vision is nearly zero. The right ear has third-degree burns and a perforated eardrum, which will most likely result in permanent sensorineural deafness. There are extensive third-degree burns to the head and temporal region, with gunpowder, mud, and fabric fragments embedded deep into the tissue, and severe contamination of the wounds."
"We've already performed an emergency craniotomy to evacuate the hematoma and a decompressive craniectomy, which has temporarily stabilized the intracranial pressure and slowed the worsening of the herniation. But the deep, high-risk fragment is in a treacherous position—removing it rashly would mean certain death, so all we can do is observe conservatively. For the eye and the face, we've only done preliminary debridement and suturing."
The doctor paused, looking at the two parents before him who seemed to have aged ten years in an instant, then went on. "Right now the boy is not out of danger. He's being transferred to the ICU on ventilator support. After this he'll have to make it through three life-or-death barriers in succession: delayed hemorrhage, severe infection, and multiple organ failure. Even if he survives by some miracle, he'll most likely be left with lifelong aftereffects—coma, cognitive damage, paralysis of the limbs, secondary epilepsy, blindness, deafness, all are possible. And repairing the burn damage to his face will be a long, grueling ordeal stretching over years."
Every word weighed a ton; every sentence cut to the bone.
When Lin Wan had heard it all, her body swayed, and finally she reached her limit. Her vision went black and she slid slowly down the wall to sit on the floor. From the moment of the accident until now, the reason, calm, and resolve she had forced herself to hold onto all the way through snapped completely at last when she heard that her son's life was in danger. She sat on the floor, not crying out loud, only her shoulders shaking violently as the tears poured out in silence. It was as if every word the doctor said had turned into a red-hot knife, stabbing deep into her soul. After a long while, she very slowly, bit by bit, turned her head toward that shut operating room door, then slowly turned back to look at the doctor.
Then she spoke, her voice drifting as if it came from another world. "Doctor… use the best medicine… the best equipment… bring in the best specialists… money is no object… we'll spend everything we have, it doesn't matter… I'm begging you… please, save my son… as long as he can live… as long as he can live…" By the end her voice had sunk almost beyond hearing, yet it carried a heartbreaking, animal-like plea and despair, the kind only a mother knows.
Shuai Hongqiang wanted to go help her up, but his legs felt like they'd been filled with lead and wouldn't move. Only by leaning against the cold wall did he keep from falling. His ears were ringing, and the doctor's words seemed to come from somewhere far away, every one of them like a heavy hammer striking his temple, leaving him dizzy and disoriented, his ears buzzing. Delayed hemorrhage… severe infection… multiple organ failure… ruptured eyeball… blindness… deafness… secondary epilepsy… paralysis of the limbs…
These cold medical terms, linked to the son who had once been warm and alive in his arms, the boy who laughed and made a fuss, formed a picture of horror he could not bear and dared not imagine.
Why? Why had it come to this?
On New Year's Eve, a single firework, a stranger child's ignorant prank—how had it turned his healthy, lively son into this thing lying in the operating room, hovering between life and death, with maybe nothing left ahead but a broken shell of a body?
It wasn't fair! It was so goddamn unfair!
Rage, despair, and a heart-rending agony crashed against the inside of his chest, almost tearing him apart.
Old Xu and Boss Li, eyes red, helped Lin Wan up and supported her over to the bench at the side, sitting her down and murmuring words of comfort—though language was pale and powerless at a moment like this.
A long time passed, and a nurse came out to say that the boy had been transferred to intensive care for observation, and the parents could look at him for a moment through the glass. The two of them rushed to the observation window of the ICU. They saw their son's tiny body lying on a bed surrounded by instruments, his head wrapped in thick bandages, half his face grotesquely swollen, the exposed skin covered in bruises and the marks of medicine, tubes inserted into his nose and arm. So still, so fragile—like a completely different person from the bouncing, lively boy of just a few hours before.
Lin Wan's tears burst the dam again. She clamped her hand hard over her mouth to keep from crying out and disturbing the son inside.
Through the glass, Shuai Hongqiang looked at his son, and from the place where his heart sat came wave after wave of a suffocating, wrenching pain. He spun around abruptly, turning his back to the glass, and pressed his forehead against the cold wall.
And it was at that moment that the deeper, darker, more spine-chilling thought—the one held down by sheer terror and chaos—rose silently like a demon from deep water and seized his heart in a tight grip—
The Mirror Pact.
The karmic backlash.
When his daughter had broken her leg skiing, this thought had drifted across his mind like a dark cloud. And now, with his son blown to a bloody pulp before his eyes, hovering between life and death… that dark cloud had turned into a downpour of blood, drenching him through and freezing him solid.
That was it… that was it! It had to be it! Old Master Wang Baoguo had said that the backlash doesn't necessarily fall on you yourself—it can strike down those of your own flesh and blood! Wenrui's leg, Wenyao's disfigurement and grave injuries… this was the price! It was because he, Shuai Hongqiang, in a moment of greed, had made a pact with that demonic bronze mirror and stolen a smooth career and fortune that were never his to begin with. Now the wheel of heaven had turned, the karma had come due, and the entire price was to be paid by his two children! He even thought: was it because, back then, he had never clearly promised Yao Yuan marriage, leaving her so heartbroken that she had aborted their child—and the "good fortune" he had taken on had been transformed out of Yao Yuan's journey through time, so that as karmic backlash, he had made Yao Yuan lose her child, and now heaven was punishing him by making him lose his children too?
A towering guilt and a boundless terror engulfed him in an instant, even greater than when he'd first learned of his son's injuries. It was a shudder and a self-loathing that came from the depths of his soul. Cold sweat soaked through the clothes beneath, clinging icily to his skin. He couldn't stop himself from trembling—from his fingertips to his teeth, everything was chattering. He didn't even dare turn to look at his son behind the glass, didn't dare look at his wife beside him, broken with grief.
He was no lucky beneficiary. He was the executioner who had pushed his own children into the abyss with his own hands, the harbinger of disaster who had dragged down the whole family.
"Hongqiang? Hongqiang, what's wrong? Are you cold?" Old Xu, sensing something off about him, came over to steady him.
Shuai Hongqiang violently flung his hand away, the movement so sharp it left Old Xu stunned. He staggered back two steps, and only by leaning against the wall did he steady himself. He lifted his eyes toward Lin Wan, who was held up by Boss Li and still gazing at the ICU window, weeping silently.
Her profile, in the dim corridor light, looked so frail, so despairing, and yet it carried a stubborn, hard-clenched strength that belonged to a mother. Only moments ago, in front of the doctor, she had been able to force down her collapse and say, "Use the best medicine, we'll cooperate with everything." A mother grows iron-strong for her child's sake.
But what about him? He was the one who had buried such a catastrophe under this family.
Tomorrow was the day the pact was to be dissolved.
But the bitter fruit had already appeared; the backlash had already come.
He couldn't hide it any longer.
His son was still lying in there, his fate uncertain. If… if tomorrow's dissolution didn't go smoothly, if the backlash was still continuing… he couldn't let Lin Wan bear the blows that might come one after another while she knew nothing. Still less could he, after his son had paid such a brutal price, keep guarding this filthy secret alone, pretending it had all been just an accident.
He owed her the truth. He owed this family an explanation.
Even though that confession might destroy the last of her trust in him, might pile snow on the frost of a family already battered by the storm. But this was a consequence he had to bear. He had planted the cause; it was for him to pay it back.
With all the strength in his body, Shuai Hongqiang stopped his trembling. He stood up straight, his face wearing a calm that was almost the gray of ashes. He walked over to Lin Wan and said in a low voice to Boss Li, who was holding her up, "Boss Li, Old Xu, today… thank you. Go home and rest now—the boy and the household both need someone. Here… the two of us are enough."
His voice was terribly hoarse, but his tone carried a strange finality. Old Xu and Boss Li exchanged a glance, knowing in their hearts that staying any longer truly wouldn't help. They comforted the couple a few more times, urged them again and again to call at any moment if anything came up, and only then left together.
In the long, cold ICU corridor, in the end there were only the two of them, and the young child in the ward whose life still hung in the balance.
Shuai Hongqiang walked up to Lin Wan, slowly bent his knees, and half-knelt, taking her cold, trembling hands in his.
Lin Wan, dazed, looked down at him through tear-blurred eyes.
The dim light fell over the two of them, casting a floor full of brooding despair.
"Lin Wan," Shuai Hongqiang said, gazing at his haggard, broken wife. His throat worked, and every word came out hoarse, as if ground through flesh and blood—heavy and sincere.
"There's something I've kept from you… for a long time. I know now isn't the time, but I have to tell you now."

========================================

Chapter 104 — Lin Wan Learns the Truth Behind Shuai Hongqiang's "Good Fortune"
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640351.html

The corridor outside the ICU was steeped, as it always was, in the cold, sharp sting of disinfectant. The dead silence of the small hours was pierced by the faint, steady beep of the monitors, a stillness so heavy it was hard to breathe.

When Lin Wan heard what Shuai Hongqiang said, she only stared at him blankly, her eyes hollow, as if she couldn't understand how, at a moment when their son hung between life and death, he had the heart to talk about anything else. Every fiber of her attention was still fixed on that heavy door of the monitoring room, on those cold machines, on her son's swollen face.

Shuai Hongqiang shut his eyes, and when he opened them again there was a bottomless pain and remorse in them. He gripped her hand tightly. That hand had once been warm and soft; now it felt like holding a block of melting ice.

He knew there was no longer any room to hide it. This disaster, the one tearing through their whole family, had its roots in his own desperation two years ago and the greed that had driven him to break the deadlock at any cost.

Shuai Hongqiang's voice was hoarse and dry. He spoke slowly, every word scraped out of his throat with effort, trembling in a way he couldn't disguise. Slowly he traced back over everything, laying that absurd, eerie bargain out before Lin Wan word by word.

The story began two years ago, in the dead end of a near-total financial collapse. By then the company's cash flow had snapped, the bank loans were closing in step by step, and a mountain of debt left him unable to breathe. His business had hit a total impasse, and the whole household lay under the shadow of bankruptcy and debt. At the lowest point, with nowhere left to turn, he went to the bank to ask Wang Qian — who had by then been promoted to bank president — to help him maneuver. Over dinner after work, Wang Qian had murmured in his ear, half in jest and half in earnest, that he should give Yao Yuan the bronze mirror he'd bought at the "Yaji Studio" winter auction eight years earlier — the one said to be able to lend a man fortune — as a last desperate gamble. Every word struck at the soft spot of a man frantic to claw his way out.

Drunk and dizzy, cornered with no way out, treating any cure for a dying horse as worth a try, Shuai Hongqiang finally lost his judgment. Greed got the better of him for a moment, and the very next day he gave that ancient bronze mirror — treasured for eight years — to his ex-girlfriend, Yao Yuan.

He slowly laid out the strange truth of the mirror: this was no ordinary antique. A person with a special fate could trigger a hidden "Time-Reversal Talisman" within it. From the moment the mirror reached Yao Yuan's hands, and she — carrying the great fortune of the Nine-Purple Departing Fire — triggered the "Time-Reversal Talisman," the fates of the two of them were forcibly bound together, sealing the Mirror Pact with the bronze mirror. Each time Yao Yuan traveled back through time, the "good fortune" churned up from the altered course of fixed destiny fell, in full, onto him — the bearer of the Pact.

From then on, every dead end met a turnaround beyond all reason. He spoke of the morning after he'd given away the mirror: over breakfast, they received the five hundred thousand that Ji Wenshan owed — ten years of principal and interest at once. Then a municipal debt stalled for three years was suddenly settled; the looming eight-million bank loan crisis quietly dissolved; the business that had teetered on bankruptcy came back from the dead; and one prime project after another, things he'd once chased in vain, came falling into his lap.

Every one of these earth-shaking turns of fortune, Lin Wan remembered clearly. She had stayed up with him through the exhaustion of checking contracts deep into the night, sat through dinner after business dinner at his side, shared the joy of pulling back from the brink, and watched his career warm and recover. Back then the two of them had weathered the harshest winter hand in hand, and she had simply taken it as bitterness giving way to sweetness, the worst passing into better — a steady spring earned by their mutual loyalty, life's reward for their resilience. Never once had she imagined that behind this smooth run lay some force this absurd, this far beyond ordinary understanding.

Shuai Hongqiang went on. In those days of good luck pouring in, he had not been entirely at peace. He said that the moment the antiques appraiser, Old Chen, told him the Mirror Pact might carry a karmic backlash, a bone-deep dread had latched onto him.

To defuse the danger and dodge the disaster, he had formed a secret alliance with Yao Yuan. Two exes who had long since cut all ties were bound together again by an eerie Mirror Pact, and they searched everywhere in secret, behind everyone's backs, desperately seeking a descendant of the mirror-makers who could break the contract.

And that hidden bond with another woman was a secret his lawful wife had never known.

After many twists and turns, they finally found a mirror-maker's descendant, Wang Baoguo — only to learn the cruel truth: the Mirror Pact's karmic backlash did not necessarily fall on the bearer himself. It could just as easily strike his own flesh and blood.

In the instant that truth landed, every small doubt of the past linked up into a single line, turning into a cold blade that pierced Lin Wan's heart.

She had never known that her husband's smooth-sailing success these two years had hidden such a sinister bargain and such a heavy price.

Shuai Hongqiang spoke again of the moment Shuai Wenrui broke his leg, and the cold, bone-deep suspicion that had crept up his spine. He had lived for days on end in suspicion and panic, restless day and night, yet he had always clung to a sliver of false hope, never daring to face the fact that he was the chief culprit dragging his own children down.

Lin Wan thought of Shuai Wenrui's broken legs not long ago. At the time she'd taken it for an accident; now, looking back, that was the first warning of the Pact's backlash.

Shuai Hongqiang's account came at last to the present. Just that evening, before the three families gathered for dinner, he had finalized the only way out with Yao Yuan, Old Wang, and Old Chen — the four of them: tomorrow at dusk, in the hour of the Rooster, they would go to White Horse Surf and hold the un-binding ritual, severing the Mirror Pact for good and ending the karmic backlash.

But never had he dreamed that, before the ritual could even be held, the backlash had struck again. This time the disaster had fallen on their young son, Wenyao, sending the child straight into the ICU, his life hanging in the balance.

As he spoke out all of it — the buried secrets, the false hopes, the fear and the regret — Shuai Hongqiang's composure shattered completely.

His voice began to choke and break, yet he forced himself to lay out every crucial detail clearly. He hated his own foolish greed back then, his frantic grab at any cure; hated his false hope, the way he'd covered his ears and stolen the bell. When he spoke of his suspicion that his children's misfortunes were all the backlash, his whole body trembled uncontrollably; when he spoke of tomorrow's un-binding and how it might go either way, his eyes were full of a bottomless dread.

"I thought… I was only borrowing a little luck for a while, just to get through that crisis…" He gripped Lin Wan's hand so hard his knuckles went white, his voice shaking to pieces, every word weeping blood. "I really never thought — this wasn't borrowing luck at all. It was signing my life away in a deed of sale! Paying off a debt to the King of Hell with the lives and health of my own dearest children!"

A vast guilt and despair swept over him. He could hold on no longer; his breathing turned ragged, tears spilling freely, every shred of dignity and restraint crumbling away until nothing was left but the rawest, most absolute repentance.

Lin Wan listened in silence the whole time, never once cutting in.

At first her eyes were blank and hollow, as if she couldn't take in any of this absurd, impossible thing. But the moment the name "Yao Yuan" left his mouth, her dead, still pupils suddenly contracted, and the fog of her thoughts was pierced through.

Only after Shuai Hongqiang had finished recounting the whole affair — fantastical as a book from heaven, yet undeniably real — did she softly repeat the name "Yao Yuan," her voice faint and far-off, like talking to herself, or like seeking confirmation.

Hearing Lin Wan speak at last, Shuai Hongqiang reined in his breakdown for a moment, even as his heart clenched. The karmic truth of the Pact's backlash could not be told without Yao Yuan; he knew there was no way left to cover it, and he confessed in a rasping voice: "It's her. She's my ex-girlfriend. We cut off all contact completely before I married — I never imagined I'd be tangled up with her again."

Lin Wan slowly raised her eyes, their depths still and waveless, and asked softly, her voice drifting as if from somewhere far away: "The Yao Yuan who's so controversial online — the one people call the 'Godmother of Feelings'?"

Shuai Hongqiang lifted his gaze to her, unable to read the emotion beneath that calm face, and could only nod heavily. "Mm."

Another long, suffocating silence.

The corridor's lights were dim and cold, falling on Lin Wan's face, and in the play of light and shadow the gentleness of her former self had drained away. Her gaze fell first back on the ICU's sealed door, then moved slowly to the face of the wretched, regretful man before her. The chaos of her thoughts settled bit by bit, and after the extremity of grief came a piercing clarity.

She was pulling herself, inch by inch, out of the haze of her son's critical condition, parting the surging emotions to reveal the coldest, sharpest core at her center.

"So she's your ex-girlfriend." Lin Wan's tone was flat, as if recounting some past affair that had nothing to do with her. "She and I are both relationship streamers. I work deep in how men and women get along inside a marriage; she dabbles in every form of feeling between men and women. Once, like everyone else online, I stood on the moral high ground and judged her as I pleased, dismissed her style. Later, after I'd been through more, I understood that my naivety back then, my old aloofness, was just the privilege of someone who'd never tasted real suffering. Now, instead, I can understand a great many of her views."

She paused briefly, the image of an old household object flashing through her mind, then asked with precision: "So that ancient bronze mirror you used to keep in the wooden box in your study drawer — that's the one you said you gave to her?"

Shuai Hongqiang gave a slight start. He hadn't expected Lin Wan to still remember after all these years. From the day he'd bought it, that mirror had sat in his study for eight years, like a silent witness — to the rise and fall of his career, the shifts in his family, sealing away the deepest secret and cowardice in his heart. Then again, he understood at once: Lin Wan was always meticulous and sharp; every object in the house, every small change, she knew inside out.

He had nothing to argue, and only answered: "Yes."

Then Shuai Hongqiang explained to Lin Wan, in detail, why he'd given Yao Yuan that mirror in the first place — not only Wang Qian's egging-on, but the desperation deep inside him to break out of his financial bind. That urge to "turn his luck around fast," catalyzed by alcohol and a dead end, had twisted into this whole absurd, devilish story.

He asked Lin Wan whether she still remembered the five hundred thousand from Ji Wenshan — ten years of principal and interest — that had suddenly come in over breakfast two years ago. That was the first stroke of "good fortune" he'd received after Yao Yuan first traveled back through time.

Lin Wan did remember it. At the time she'd only assumed Ji Wenshan had finally become able to repay, or had suddenly found his conscience. She'd never dreamed it was because of some force this absurd, this far beyond ordinary understanding.

But now, looking back, every key "stroke of luck" she had counted herself so fortunate for — Shuai Hongqiang's career climbing higher and higher, sailing smoothly on — was now overlaid with an eerie, sinister shadow.

Shock, horror, disbelief — layer after layer of emotion swept through her, and at last settled into an ice-locked understanding.

She finally understood it completely.

Her husband's smooth success these two years had never been heaven rewarding the diligent. Behind all that easy sailing lay a price this eerie and terrible. The string of disasters that had struck Wenrui and then her son were not pure accidents, not fate's unfairness at all — they might well stem from a muddled, greedy bargain their father had made two years ago, mortgaging his soul and the lives and health of his own kin.

Lin Wan looked at this man half-kneeling before her, weeping bitterly, drowning in remorse — her lawful husband, the father of her son. She did not fly into a rage, did not scream her questions, did not even pull her hand back from his grip.

She only felt cold — a cold seeping out from between her bones. And a deep, all-consuming exhaustion.

So the "partnership" marriage she had worked so hard to run, the one she'd thought rational and solid enough, had a dark whirlpool churning beneath it that she'd known nothing about. So the accidents and storms she'd taken for ordinary life had, in part, been summoned by her own most intimate "partner's" own hands.

The long silence stretched on in that cold corridor thick with the smell of disinfectant. Only the faint beep of the monitors carried through the heavy glass door, weak and far-off.

She didn't know how long it had been when Lin Wan, ever so slowly, inch by inch, drew her own cold hand out of Shuai Hongqiang's sweaty, frantic grip, with a distance and finality that left no room for argument.

She straightened her stiff spine, rose, and walked to the observation window of the monitoring room, her gaze returning to that small, fragile figure inside. This time her body no longer trembled; the panic in her eyes had drained entirely away, leaving only the calm and resolve of someone who had settled.

A moment later she finally spoke, her voice so level there wasn't a single ripple in it — and yet it dropped Shuai Hongqiang, standing behind her, straight into an ice pit, cold all over.

"Shuai Hongqiang, every single word you just said, I heard clearly."

She slowly turned to face him, meeting his frantic, guilt-ridden eyes. The dim light of the corridor cut shifting shadows across her face, but her own eyes were unusually clear and sharp. It was the particular clarity — lucid to the point of being almost cruel — that belonged to Lin Wan the streamer, dissecting the truth of a marriage on camera.

"Now, tell me," she said slowly, every word weighed and measured to the extreme, heavy and forceful, "tomorrow, when you go to White Horse Surf — how good are your odds of breaking this evil… contract?"

Shuai Hongqiang opened his mouth, only to find his throat too dry for sound. He swallowed hard and rasped: "Old Wang said… it's the only way. But no one has ever truly un-bound a Mirror Pact like this, so… there's no guarantee."

"No guarantee." Lin Wan repeated it, as if stating an objective fact that had nothing to do with her, no joy or anger in it — which only made it more unnerving.

She pressed on, every word heavy, every line cutting to the heart: "And if, by luck, it is broken — will all this suffering my son is going through stop completely? Will he come back to me, whole and unharmed?"

The question went into Shuai Hongqiang's heart like a blunt knife, cutting slowly. He shook violently, unable to answer — because he truly did not know. The karmic backlash had already happened, the harm already done, the water already spilled. Even if the source were cut off and the backlash stopped, could the damage already done be taken back?

"I don't know…" he admitted in despair. "But if it isn't broken, the backlash might… might still go on. Wenrui's legs, Wenyao's injuries… I'm afraid… I'm afraid there'll be a next time…"

Lin Wan gazed at him quietly, silent for a very, very long while.

Then she nodded gently, as if she had finally traced out every thread and understood it all.

"I see."

Three light, weightless words — and yet they shook Shuai Hongqiang to the core. He would rather she wept bitterly, cursed and questioned him, broke down in hysterics — anything would be better than this dead, still calm.

She only stood there, dissecting this absurd, terrible truth that had dragged her family into the abyss with the cold steadiness of a scalpel.

Before he could collect himself, Lin Wan had already turned and walked back to the bench, picked up her bag, taken out her phone, and unlocked the screen. Her fingers slid swiftly across it, the motions smooth and steady, perfectly ordered — nothing like a mother whose young child lay dying, who had just learned of a catastrophe shattering her family.

In the face of the ultimate disaster, her emotions had already been forced to close their curtain, replaced by the instinct and resilience to confront the problem and resolve the crisis.

"Give me Yao Yuan's contact details." Lin Wan didn't even look up, her tone certain and calm.

Shuai Hongqiang was at a loss. "What are you going to do?"

"Contact her." Lin Wan raised her eyes, her gaze clear and resolute. "Since tomorrow's un-binding decides whether my son lives or dies, decides the fate of our whole family, I need to know every detail. The time, the place, the procedure, the taboos, every unknown risk — I want all of it clear."

Shuai Hongqiang didn't dare defy her in the slightest. Obediently he took out his phone and pulled up Yao Yuan's contact.

Her fingers tapped quickly across the screen, the motions fluid, her thinking clear — nothing like a mother whose son had just gone into the ICU, who had just learned a staggering secret. The rational, professional poise she'd once shown on camera now stood against the storm of disaster with an almost cruel composure.

"What are you going to tell her?" Shuai Hongqiang asked instinctively.

"Tell her Wenyao had an accident, that he's in critical condition in the ICU, and that I now know the whole truth of the Mirror Pact." She spoke evenly as she sent the message. "Ask her whether tomorrow's plan needs to change, and confirm every detail."

Once it was sent, she put the phone away and looked Shuai Hongqiang in the eye: "This disaster started with you and her, and it's dragged in my child and my family. There's no way I can stand outside of it. I have to have an answer — an answer for why my son is lying in that ICU."

Shuai Hongqiang stared at her blankly, realizing for the first time, truly, that he had never really understood the woman beside him.

He had seen her as a gentle, considerate wife, as a careful, patient mother, as a composed, clear-eyed streamer — but never like this. In the face of an apocalyptic dead end and a truth that brought the sky crashing down, she forced down the bone-eating grief, cast off all the inner churn of emotion, and switched at once into problem-solving mode.

This was never coldness. Quite the opposite — it was the deepest love and responsibility. On the brink of an emotional collapse, she had built a dam by force out of reason. She didn't dare break down, couldn't break down — the child in the ICU was still waiting for her, the tottering family still needed her to hold it up. She raised a high wall of reason to hold back the surging despair, all to guard the last shred of hope.

The phone buzzed faintly, breaking the dead silence of the corridor.

Lin Wan lowered her head to read Yao Yuan's reply, her gaze resting briefly on the screen, her face still as waveless as ever. She slowly put the phone away and repeated it calmly: "She says the plan stays the same. Tomorrow, the hour of the Rooster, at White Horse Surf, on time. Once the Mirror Pact is un-bound, the karmic backlash may stop completely — so tomorrow's ritual has to go ahead."

Shuai Hongqiang nodded numbly, drained to the soul.

Lin Wan slowly sat back down on the bench, her two hands clasped tightly on her knees, knuckles pressed white. Still she gazed at the ICU's sealed door, and after a long while she suddenly spoke again, softly — and this time her voice let slip a barely perceptible tremor, the emotion she'd just forced down quietly coming loose.

"Shuai Hongqiang, do you know what I hate most?"

Shuai Hongqiang's heart strings drew taut, his breath catching, and he looked at her in dread.

"What I hate most isn't that you got tangled up with your ex behind my back, nor that you hid the secret." Lin Wan's voice was very soft, almost talking to herself, yet every word cut to the bone. "Those are enough to turn my heart cold, but they aren't what's most fatal."

She turned her head to look at the man beside her, a pain suppressed to its limit and a cold disillusionment surging in her eyes.

"What I hate most is that you stripped away my right to choose — alone, without me."

"What is a marriage? It's the two of us swearing before the altar, contracting under the law, becoming each other's most intimate partners. We share our property, bear the risks together, raise our children, plan our future. And what is the heart of a partnership? It's being informed. It's consent. It's shared decision-making."

"But you?" Her voice trembled slightly, yet every word stayed clear. "You signed, all on your own, a contract that mortgaged our children's well-being — and you never once asked whether I was willing. You made me, with no knowledge of any of it, a co-liable party to that contract. You made me and the children bear the full risk of your decision, completely in the dark."

She drew a deep breath, the cool air filling her chest, the cold of it tightening around her heart.

"Day after day in my livestream room, I tell every woman trapped in a hard marriage: a good marriage is never held together by love and sacrifice. It's held together by clear rules, equal responsibility, transparent honesty. The bedrock of a marriage is being informed, living as one, having each other's back."

"I teach other people to sort out who's responsible for what in a marriage, where the alliance stands, where the emotional bottom line lies — but what about my own marriage?"

A single rhetorical question, holding all the absurdity and desolation within it. Her eyes finally welled, but she forced it down, refusing to let the tears fall.

"My own partnership agreement was built on lies and concealment from the very start. I thought we were running a home together — and it turned out you'd long since secretly mortgaged our future."

Shuai Hongqiang shut his eyes in pain. Every sentence from Lin Wan was like a sharp whip, lashing hard at his conscience. She was right about all of it. He wasn't only greedy and foolish — he was base and selfish. With his own hands he had destroyed the honesty of their marriage, trampled his wife's trust, stripped her of the most basic right, as a partner and as a mother, to know and to choose.

"I'm sorry…" was all he could do, repeating those three pale, powerless words.

"Is sorry any use?" Lin Wan quickly pressed down the ache in her heart, her voice returning to calm — but beneath that calm was a bottomless ice. "Can sorry restore Wenyao's face to what it was? Can sorry keep him out of the ICU, out of this life-and-death uncertainty?"

She shook her head, looked at him no longer, and turned her gaze back to the door of the monitoring room.

"Shuai Hongqiang, I don't want to hear sorry right now. Right now I only want to do two things."

Her tone was firm, every word ringing, carrying the finality of someone who had burned her boats.

"First, do everything possible to save my son. The best medicine, the best doctors — even if it costs every cent we have, I will keep him alive, and as well as he can possibly be."

"Second—" she paused slightly, a flash of icy resolve crossing her eyes, "tomorrow, White Horse Surf, I'm going with you."

Shuai Hongqiang's head jerked up. "What?"

"I said, tomorrow I'll go with you." Lin Wan said it word by word. "Since this absurd farce concerns my son, then as your wife and Wenyao's mother, I have to watch it end with my own eyes. I will watch that damned mirror be dealt with, watch you and Yao Yuan break that contract — with my own eyes."

"But it's dangerous there. Old Wang said—" Shuai Hongqiang hurried to dissuade her.

"Dangerous?" Lin Wan cut him off, her lips curving into an arc bitter to the extreme. "Is there any situation more dangerous than this? My son is lying in there, between life and death. My husband has just told me all of it may be because of one moment of his greed two years ago."

She turned her head and looked straight into Shuai Hongqiang's eyes. In those eyes there was despair, anger, pain — but not the slightest retreat.

Shuai Hongqiang gazed dumbly at the resolve in her brow and eyes, momentarily lost for words. He suddenly recalled how she'd looked when they first met — gentle and intelligent, yet resilient and bold at the core, never bowing to fate or hardship.

After a long while, he set aside every word of protest and nodded heavily, his voice hoarse and weak: "All right. Tomorrow, we'll go together."

Lin Wan nodded and said no more. Her hands stayed clasped tightly on her knees, her spine straight, keeping her quiet vigil outside the ICU door.

Beyond the windows the sky broke fully into dawn. The faint light of morning spilled the length of the corridor, driving off the darkness of the deep night — yet it could not drive off the heavy oppression and sorrow hanging in the air. The second day of the new year held no joy of new beginnings, only the torment of a long wait, and a ritual about to come, its outcome unknown.

Shuai Hongqiang looked at his wife's stiff, upright profile, at the sealed door of the monitoring room ahead, and felt a barren waste inside. It struck him with sudden clarity: tomorrow's un-binding ritual at White Horse Surf, win or lose, had already changed something for good — there was no going back to before.

The layer of marriage between them — that partnership agreement of reason, restraint, and mutual space — had long since been torn utterly to shreds by this bloody, brutal truth.

What they were about to face was no longer how to keep a comfortable distance in their marriage, but how to learn trust again on a field of ruins, how to redefine responsibility, how to rethink what it meant to share "good fortune and ill alike."

If, that is, there was still such a chance.

The beep of the monitors was especially clear in the bright morning light, echoing again and again down the corridor, like a countdown — or the tolling bell of a judgment.

Chapter 105 — Breaking the Mirror Pact at White Horse Rapids; Sinking the Mirror in the Yellow River to Sever Cause and Effect
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640352.html

Breaking the Mirror Pact at White Horse Rapids; Sinking the Mirror in the Yellow River to Sever Cause and Effect
The Year of the Snake, the third day of the twelfth lunar month, the hour of the Rooster — five o'clock in the afternoon.
Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming set out early, driving to the Guangu Hall to pick up Old Chen. The three of them headed west along the riverside road. One by one the city lights fell away behind them, the houses thinned out, and the cold, desolate winter countryside spread open all around. The sky hung low and leaden, and the mighty Yellow River ran quietly below their eyes — like a faded old length of silk, wrapped in the harsh, dull chill unique to a northern twelfth month, running silently across heaven and earth.
They were the first to reach White Horse Rapids.
The parking spot was still a hundred-odd meters of dirt track from the riverbank. The moment they pushed the doors open and climbed out, a bitter north wind poured into their collars. The wind by the Yellow River was unlike the wind in the city. It came sweeping down all the way from the upstream gorges, carrying fine shards of ice and spray off the river's surface, finding every gap, piercing through layer after layer of clothing like countless needles of ice driven into the skin. Yao Yuan tugged the hood of her fur coat down further, until her whole face was nearly buried in the thick, plush collar, leaving only her eyes. In the dim daylight those eyes looked unusually clear, carrying the stillness of someone keeping an appointment.
Zhao Yiming wore a long black down coat, his hood likewise drawn up. He first carefully tightened Old Chen's scarf against the wind, then turned to Yao Yuan and reached out to take her gloved hand in a steady grip. The warmth of his palm came through the leather, calm and certain.
Old Chen stood by the car in a navy, dark-patterned Chinese-style padded gown with a buttoned front, a black camel-down vest over it, a fleece-lined cotton cap pressed down to his brow, his scarf half-covering his jaw. This seventy-year-old appraiser of antiquities looked like a man who had walked straight out of an older age — heavily wrapped, yet standing perfectly straight.
