His gaze went toward the not-distant Yellow River, and in his eyes lay the settled depth of one long used to watching the years roll by.
"Let's go," Old Chen said.
The three of them set off, treading across the frozen, rock-hard earth toward the riverbank. The ground beneath their feet was cracked and uneven, and across the whole stretch the withered grass had been frozen by the deep cold into brittle, charred black stalks. With every step came a small, crisp crunch. White Horse Rapids in the dead of the twelfth month was all bleakness and lifeless silence. Here the hills on both banks drew suddenly inward, brute-forcing the wide channel into a narrow, treacherous gorge. The hills were a gray-brown drained of all life — bare rock, not a single blade of grass, desolation taken to its extreme. The whole riverbank was a sloping jumble of stone, blue-gray boulders of every size strewn from the bank down to the waterline, their surfaces glazed with thin ice that gave off a hard, cold gleam in the dim dusk.
Here the Yellow River had shed every trace of its gentle, placid temper and bared its full violence. Where the channel narrowed the current turned suddenly fierce; the muddy yellow water carried ice floes that had not yet frozen solid and slammed them against the reefs packed thick along the riverbed. The roar of it never let up, setting the whole valley humming. The waves struck the rocks hard, bursting into spray ten feet high, sending mist flying across the sky that fell again as fine flecks of ice, soaking the entire bank in a bone-deep wet cold.
The three of them stood alone on the frozen riverside, like three stumps of dead wood lost in the open wilderness, keeping watch over a vast emptiness amid all the cold.
Before long, from the dirt track behind them, footsteps came again.
It was Shuai Hongqiang and his wife Lin Wan.
The two of them had kept vigil outside the ICU for a full day and night, worn out in body and mind. Only around noon that day, after the hospital director had taken the lead and specialists from several departments had held a joint consultation, did they barely manage to stabilize Shuai Wenyao's vital signs and pull him temporarily out of the most dangerous stage — only stabilized, with recovery still nowhere in sight. Lin Wan had asked her parents to rush to the hospital to keep watch in the meantime, then hurried off with Shuai Hongqiang to make the appointment.
Neither of them had had a chance to change clothes; they were still in what they'd worn when the accident happened the day before, the fabric badly creased, smeared with dried blood and dust. Shuai Hongqiang's hair was a wreck, heavy shadows hung beneath his eyes, ragged stubble had sprouted along his jaw, and the whole spirit of the man had collapsed — he radiated utter exhaustion and ruin.
Lin Wan's face was just as pale and haggard, yet she had forced her hair into some kind of order, revealing a thin, cool face. Her once gentle, clear eyes were deeply sunken and red-rimmed, brimming with the weariness of a sleepless night and a grief cut to the bone — and yet, deep in those eyes, there was a clarity unlike anyone else's. It was the instinctive self-possession wrung out of her after extreme pain had ground over her, the lucid sanity a mother holds onto by the sheer conviction that she cannot fall, cannot break.
Yao Yuan was the first to step forward to meet them. Her gaze rested briefly on Shuai Hongqiang's haggard, disheveled face, then turned to Lin Wan. Her tone was gentle and low, carrying over the howling wind: "How is the child?"
Shuai Hongqiang's throat trembled. He opened his mouth, but not a single sound came out. Lin Wan answered, her voice hoarse and dry but unusually steady: "Stable for now. Still under observation in the ICU."
A single short sentence, with nothing more added. The brutal injuries, the night-long ordeal, the gnawing dread — all of it had long passed beyond what language could describe. Extreme pain has never needed dressing up. Yao Yuan nodded gently and did not pile on superfluous words of comfort. She knew well that, in the depths of despair, all pity and pleasantries were pale and useless.
This was the first time Yao Yuan and Lin Wan had formally met. Two years earlier, she had once happened to glimpse Shuai Hongqiang and his family of three at a shopping mall: the Shuai Hongqiang of that time was gentle and considerate, the woman at his side soft and serene, the young child lively and bright. In that moment a small ripple had stirred in her heart — so the home he had finally chosen was the world's standard picture of a virtuous wife and good mother, gentle and steady, the kind suited to making a home, the complete opposite of her own flashy, sharp-edged, never-back-down self.
Now, face to face up close, Yao Yuan finally saw Lin Wan clearly. She was slight and small, and even wrapped in a heavy down coat she still gave off an air of fragility. Her face was full of exhaustion, pain hidden in her eyes, yet none of it could mask the refined, scholarly grace in her bones. Even in such a wretched, desperate state, even after being struck so hard, the decorum and sense of order she had drilled into herself over many years was still carved into her bones, and had not collapsed.
A complicated, hard-to-name tide of feeling churned in Yao Yuan's heart. Half of it was the subtle comparison between women. In their younger years, the verdict Shuai Hongqiang had implied — "you're too beautiful, too bright, too headstrong; bring you home and there'd be no holding you down" — echoed softly now, after all these years. And the woman before her was gentle, soft, like a quiet plant. That old wound of being weighed, picked over, and finally cast aside as "not the kind you marry" was lightly touched at this most inopportune of moments. Very lightly — but it was real.
The other half was a deeper empathy, woman for woman, a sense of kinship. Watching the tense line of Lin Wan's shoulders, the fingertips she clenched and released over and over, the way she choked down her grief, Yao Yuan understood this gritted-teeth strength all too well. A truly strong woman has only ever been able to plant her own feet by force of will, to steady her spirit atop the ruins and face the storm head-on.
At the same time, Lin Wan was quietly sizing up Yao Yuan too.
Until now, Yao Yuan had been, to her, merely a hugely controversial symbol in the online world of relationship content — a wildly divisive, sharply distinctive emotional livestreamer adored by countless people and reviled by just as many. She had watched clips of her broadcasts: calm, sharp, every word drawing blood, with a cold beauty to the way she dissected love and laid it out for display. She admired her clarity, and had privately studied her techniques of expression. Between peers in the same field, there had always been this complicated kind of attention.
But now, seeing her in the flesh, this woman who lived inside the lens of the internet was real, vivid, wrapped in costly fur, beautiful in a way that was almost aggressive. The exquisite face, the noble dress — even set down in the coarse, barren surroundings of a Yellow River bank, her presence was still extraordinary. And she was, of all things, Shuai Hongqiang's ex-girlfriend. The other party to the pact made over that uncanny bronze mirror. The woman who, these past two years, had been bound tight to her own husband's fate, sharing the most secret of alliances.
In Lin Wan's heart there was none of the jealousy, hostility, or resentment she might have expected. After a night of extreme torment she was already drained, body and soul, with no strength left to summon up those "wife" emotions. All that remained was an endless sense of absurdity — fate was like a net woven at random, dragging utterly unrelated people into the same whirlpool. And this woman, in a certain sense, was one of the net's victims too.
"This is Zhao Yiming, my fiancé." Yao Yuan spoke up at the right moment, breaking the silence, turning slightly to introduce the man at her side, her tone natural and open.
Zhao Yiming gave a slight nod. His gaze was steady and restrained; he did not let himself study the two of them freely. His voice was gentle and measured: "Take care of yourselves. The child will be all right."
Shuai Hongqiang nodded numbly, his mind in turmoil. But Lin Wan started slightly — fiancé.
Learning that Yao Yuan had long since found a home of her own, that thin, inexplicable comparison and grudge in her heart quietly dissolved. So it was that everyone drifts and sinks through this mortal world, each enduring their own storms, each chasing their own peace; no one is spared, no one has it easy.
Yao Yuan's gaze fell back on Lin Wan's face, and a flicker of recognition crossed her eyes. She suddenly asked, "Haven't I seen you somewhere? You wouldn't happen to be Lin Wan, the host of the 'Gentle Awakening' livestream room?"
Lin Wan was a little surprised. She hadn't expected Yao Yuan to recognize her, even if they were both in relationship content. But on second thought, it made sense. People who are genuinely good at this always come onto their peers' radar. She nodded, though the weariness on her face and the grief in her eyes were still heavy: "I see you online often too — I watch your content. You're very good."
That remark held no pleasantries, no rivalry; it was pure recognition and appreciation between peers.
Hearing it, the corner of Yao Yuan's mouth curved up faintly. "So are you."
Three simple words — recognition, understanding, the confirmation that comes in the instant of glimpsing one's own kind amid this vast human world, this rolling muddy current. There on the Yellow River bank, with the cold wind howling, two women who had never met yet whose fates had been woven together by the same man and the same absurd catastrophe held no hostility, no contest — only the subtle kinship of like souls who, standing in dire straits and knowing well how hard life is, read each other clearly. Very faint — but real.
Just then, from the dirt track in the distance, came slow footsteps again.
Everyone turned toward the sound, and saw old Mr. Wang Baoguo, descendant of the mirror masters, being carefully supported as he walked slowly forward by a middle-aged man. As the two drew near, Yao Yuan, Zhao Yiming, Shuai Hongqiang, and Lin Wan all froze — the one supporting Old Wang was, of all people, that banker friend they all knew, Wang Qian.
Wang Qian wore a heavy mountaineering down jacket and a knit cap, his face flushed red by the cold wind. Facing the four pairs of eyes turned in unison upon him, his expression was a little awkward, but he still spoke up of his own accord, his voice tight in the wind: "Wang Baoguo is my father. I... didn't mean to hide it from you."
The riverbank fell suddenly silent.
Only the roar of the surging Yellow River filled the emptiness of the whole valley.
Yao Yuan looked at Wang Qian and remembered how strange his manner had been at their last few gatherings — words half-spoken, then swallowed. So there had been signs of it all along. He had recognized the heart-guarding jade that Old Chen had given her, knew she was searching so painstakingly for a descendant of the mirror masters, and yet had said nothing, only watching coldly from the side.
Behind his glasses, Zhao Yiming's gaze was calm; he was swiftly reconstructing every detail of his past dealings with Wang Qian.
Shuai Hongqiang, for his part, went stiff all over and stared at Wang Qian in chilled astonishment. So his desperate turnaround two years ago, that single greedy, obsessive impulse, the calamity that had wrecked his home — from beginning to end, this friend he had known for years had known and stood by watching, had even egged it on, pushed it forward.
And Lin Wan watched the scene in silence, a deeper, heavier chill welling up inside her — so everything her family had suffered had been seeded years ago, and they, like chess pieces, had walked all unknowing to this day.
No one spoke up to demand answers; no one raised a voice to accuse. Yet, intangibly, the old friendship, trust, and unspoken understanding had already shattered with a crash. Some concealments, once exposed, can never be repaired; from then on, whenever they meet, a gulf will forever lie in their hearts — "you knew all along, and stood by watching."
Wang Qian read the distance and disappointment in everyone's silence. His lips moved; he wanted to explain, but in the end swallowed it all. The crack had already formed; words were pale, and no amount of argument could mend anything.
Old Chen stepped forward, and he and Wang Baoguo nodded to each other in greeting. One was an appraiser who knew old objects through and through and glimpsed their hidden workings; the other was a descendant of the mirror masters who carried on a secret art and guarded its taboos. The two old men, reunited on the bleak Yellow River bank, met each other's eyes, and in both lay the heavy fatedness of "so it has come to this after all."
"Old Wang, can the pact-breaking rite begin now?" Shuai Hongqiang could hold back no longer; an anxious tremor showed in his voice. He didn't want to wait a single second more, as if a moment's delay would let that unseen backlash snatch away the last spark of life from his son in the ICU.
Wang Baoguo raised his eyes to him. His murky but piercing gaze swept lightly over the man's worn-out, panicked face, and his brow knit slightly: "Something happened at home?"
The question was like a needle, piercing the composure Shuai Hongqiang had forced himself to hold. His shoulders dropped abruptly; he buried his face in his hands, and from between his fingers spilled a broken, sob-choked voice: "My son... Wenyao... last night he was setting off fireworks, and got hit point-blank by a Gatling firework... severe head and face injuries, ruptured eyeball, intracranial bleeding... he's still in the ICU, still not out of danger... and my daughter, a while back she broke her leg skiing..."
He was incoherent, but his meaning was clear. Old Chen and Wang Baoguo, who hadn't known, exchanged a glance, and a heavy "just as we thought" showed in their eyes. The backlash of the Mirror Pact never misses.
Wang Qian drew a sharp breath, his face going white in an instant, his heart full of guilt. He opened his mouth, wanting to offer a few words of comfort, only to find that any language was pale and powerless at this moment. He could only give Shuai Hongqiang's shoulder a heavy pat.
Yao Yuan and Zhao Yiming had already learned of it the night before, but hearing Shuai Hongqiang recount it again in such a broken state still sent a jolt of unease through them. It was not only sympathy for what a child had suffered, but a stark dread before the word "cause-and-effect" made flesh. It was formless and without substance, yet precise and cold, always striking hardest where you are most fragile.
Through it all, only Lin Wan stood in silence. She watched her broken-down husband, watched the others with their varied expressions, like a bystander calm to the point of cruelty. But Yao Yuan understood: that was not indifference. It was a mother, after the most extreme pain, drawing all her life-force inward into an energy-saving state, spending it only on "holding onto her clarity" and "solving the problem." Crying out, complaining, collapsing — all of these cost energy, and right now she could not waste a shred of it. It all had to be saved for the son lying in the hospital, for the pact-breaking rite before her that bore on the whole future.
A surge of respect for Lin Wan rose in Yao Yuan's heart. True strength has never been about never breaking down — it is about staying clear-headed atop the ruins and still doing what must be done.
Wang Baoguo drew back his gaze, asked nothing more, and lifted his eyes to the horizon.
The hour of the Rooster had arrived in full. The last ray of the setting sun sank behind the western hills, leaving on the horizon only a thick, murky layer of orange-gray, like congealed old blood. The river valley darkened swiftly; lead-colored clouds pressed low over the peaks; the cold wind grew ever more biting, rolling ice flecks off the river's surface as it raged across the wilderness.
"The hour has come. Set the array and break the pact." The old man pronounced it in a deep voice, motioning for Yao Yuan to bring out the bronze mirror.
Yao Yuan did as told, taking from her handbag a dark cloth pouch. She untied the knot and lifted out the Kui-Dragon Cloud-Pattern bronze mirror. In the dim dusk its face glowed a deep, dull yellow; on its back, the Kui-dragon relief coiled and wound, still clear and sharp after a hundred years of erosion, faintly exuding an eerie solemnity. The bronze mirror was icy and heavy in her hands, like holding a slab of time and fate frozen for a century.
Wang Baoguo took from his own cloth pouch a slender silver needle wrapped in red cloth, and produced a small porcelain dish. He looked first at Yao Yuan: "Left index finger."
Yao Yuan pulled off her glove and held out her left hand. Wang Baoguo gripped her fingertip, and the silver needle pricked it in a flash. A bead of crimson blood welled up and dripped into the porcelain dish. Then it was Shuai Hongqiang's turn; his fingers were shaking badly, so Wang Baoguo held them firmly and pricked his finger the same way. Last, the old man pricked his own fingertip.
Three drops of blood, belonging to the three parties of the Mirror Pact: Shuai Hongqiang, who had taken on the "shifted fortune"; Yao Yuan, the one who "traveled back through time"; and Wang Baoguo, descendant of the pact's maker and executor of its breaking. Three threads of crimson blended faintly in the white porcelain dish, mirroring the dim sky, glaring and stark.
Wang Baoguo dipped the silver needle into the merged drop of blood and dabbed it carefully into the sunken hollows of the Kui-dragon's eyes on the mirror's back. The warm blood slowly soaked along the ancient patterns, spreading, seeping into the fine pores of the object. The blood-stained patterns gave off a moist, dark red glow in the gloom, like a wound just scabbed over, silently sealing away two years of entangled cause and effect.
Once the blood had congealed slightly, Wang Baoguo brought out a square of coarse crimson cloth. The color was deep and blazing; on a riverbank all gray and yellow and bleak, it was like a leaping tongue of bright flame. The cloth was thick and rough, steeped through with cinnabar by ancient method and aired in the sun again and again, giving off on the wind a faint, bitter medicinal scent.
The old man laid the bronze mirror in the center of the red cloth and began, layer by layer, to wrap it carefully. His movements were slow and steady; with each layer he smoothed the cloth flat, pressed the corners tight, left not the slightest gap. Layer after layer of red folded over it, and the outline of the bronze mirror gradually vanished, until at last it became a thick, heavy, perfectly square red bundle — as if this alone could shut out all the uncanny aura and the entanglements of fate.
Nine layers. Nine is the number of the utmost, signifying sealing and severing. It was an ending, a cutting-off of every connection.
Wang Baoguo cradled that heavy mass of red and walked toward the river. The others followed in silence. The spot the old man had chosen was where White Horse Rapids ran swiftest, where the whirlpools were most pronounced. The stones underfoot were jagged, glazed with slick thin ice. The Yellow River churned and roared before them, its yellow waves smashing against the reefs, shattering into a sky full of spray laced with ice flecks that struck their faces, cold to the bone.
At the water's edge Wang Baoguo set out a few simple offerings of dried fruit and pastries and lit three slender sticks of incense. The pale smoke was no sooner rising than the violent wind tore it apart and scattered it. The old man bowed three times toward the Yellow River, his mouth chanting an ancient incantation of strange syllables and desolate cadence — a language no one could understand, like a secret tongue that had conversed for a thousand years with this land, this river, these jagged stones.
The chant finished, he carefully bound the red bundle to a fist-sized blue-gray stone and tied a dead knot. Then the old man stepped back two paces and motioned to Shuai Hongqiang: "You do it. It began with you; it ends with you."
A great shock ran through Shuai Hongqiang's heart, and he understood at once. This absurd disaster had begun with one greedy, obsessive impulse of his, and it was only right that he bring it to a close with his own hands, sever it with his own hands.
He stepped forward two paces and took the heavy red bundle steadily in both hands. The bitterness of cinnabar mingled with the raw cold of the river water rushed into his face, weighing down on his chest. He moved onto the great boulder at the very edge of the water; beneath his feet the waves struck the rock, trembling faintly, the roar of the water flooding both his ears.
He looked back once. Lin Wan stood a few steps away, watching him in silence, no expression on her face, only a bottomless weariness and calm. That gaze sent a sharp pain through his chest, yet strangely it also gave him a last shred of strength. He turned back, faced the surging Yellow River, drew a deep breath of the cold, fishy-smelling air, and with all his might hurled the red bundle in his hands toward the center of the river, to where the whirlpool ran fastest.
Shuai Hongqiang drew his gaze back, turned to face the endlessly surging Yellow River, breathed in a lungful of the icy, bone-piercing spray, gathered his strength and raised his arm, and flung the red bundle in his hands fiercely into the swiftest whirlpool at the center of the channel.
The bright red bundle flashed once in the muddy yellow waves, was swiftly swallowed by the churning water, and sank into the dark depths, drawn into the unseen undercurrents. Only the bound stone dragged it down, plunging toward the deeps of the Yellow River, toward those secrets buried under silt for a thousand years.
The wind paused for an instant; the howl of the waves went on.
Shuai Hongqiang stood frozen at the bank, staring blankly at the surging water, his mind in a daze. As if he could not believe that the nightmare that had entangled him for two years, the uncanny pact for which he had finally paid such a brutal price, had just like that, so simply and so completely, been swallowed whole by the rolling Yellow River.
Just like that? Just like that... it was over?
And then, as everyone's thoughts rose and sank in private reflection, the change struck without warning.
Yao Yuan, who had been leaning against Zhao Yiming's side, watching that streak of red vanish into the Yellow River's whirlpool, went suddenly rigid. She felt a pair of huge hands seize her soul and clench down hard, and a familiar wrenching-away followed with no warning at all — that sensation more violent, more complete than all nine times before, as if something were being torn up by the roots from the very bottom of her life as that bronze mirror sank.
The Yellow River, the jumbled stones, the figures of the people in her sight all began to twist, stretch, and spin, like a scroll wantonly crumpled by a giant hand. Zhao Yiming's frantic cry receded layer by layer in her ears, growing faint and unreal: "Yuanyuan?!"
The last shred of consciousness she had left was of falling into a solid, warm embrace, with that familiar, reassuring scent lingering at the tip of her nose. And then darkness swallowed all awareness completely.
The Yellow River still surged on without end, its waves roaring, the valley vast and dim, as if nothing at all had happened.
Only the few people standing at the bank, and that woman gone limp in her fiancé's arms, as if she had sunk into sleep.
The old pact has sunk; is cause and effect severed? No one knows.
The mighty river says nothing, only carries a thousand years of silt and flows silently east, hiding away every unknown and every answer.

========================================

Chapter 106 — A Whole Life of Striving, and It Turns Out It Was All Someone Else's Script
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640353.html

A whole life of striving, and it turns out it was all someone else's script.

Yao Yuan's consciousness clawed its way upward, inch by inch, as if dragging itself out of the bottom of a deep, viscous mire of chaos.

No sound, no light — only a boundless weightlessness wrapped around her. She felt herself sinking endlessly in the deep sea, then floating in a vacuum — until, at some critical point, gravity caught hold of her again.

In absolute silence, she slowly opened her eyes.

Beneath her was a cool, hard, smooth floor; before her stretched an endless pure white. White ceiling, white walls, white floor — clean, without a single impurity. It was the pure-white space she had come to know across nine journeys through time, familiar and strange all at once.

"Back here again." Yao Yuan let out a soundless sigh in her heart. She braced a hand against the floor and rose slowly, her mind full of doubt. "The pact-breaking ritual was clearly complete. Why have I fallen into this way-station between times once more? Did the ritual fail?"

This white void was a way-station she had privately named — the "window" through which, across nine journeys, she had looked back on her past, watched it, and spoken to it.

This time the feeling was strange. Her body was lighter than after any of the previous nine crossings, as if some invisible burden had been lifted from her. But the crossing itself had been more painful than any before — that searing agony of a soul torn forcibly from its body still echoed faintly through her nerve endings.

She looked around. She had been to this pure-white space nine times. Every time, it had been utter emptiness — no objects, no sound — and every time she had stood as a silent observer, watching another version of herself in another timeline struggle, choose, get hurt, and grow. This place was the way-station of her fate, the window through which she spoke to her past.

But this time, something seemed different.

Yao Yuan's gaze drifted slowly across the empty white, then suddenly fixed.

On the wall some ten meters ahead, a door was set silently into the surface.

Its color blended almost seamlessly into the pure-white wall, perfectly hidden; were it not for the faint, cold glint off the metal handle, it would have been impossible to know the door was there at all.

"This door… was it here before?" she murmured to herself, memory rewinding fast. In all nine previous stays, she had never once seen any solid object in this space. It had always been an empty white nothing — until the images of her past surfaced like a projection.

It was no illusion. This door was new.

Yao Yuan was not the type to sit and wait for death. Thirty-eight years of life, nine journeys through time, countless instances of forcing herself back onto her feet in hopeless straits — all of it had long since worn away any passivity or timidity. What was carved into her bones was the nerve to take the initiative and break the deadlock, and she would not flinch from the unknown.

She drew a deep breath and stepped toward the hidden door.

Her footsteps were jarringly loud in the absolute silence, yet eerily without echo — every sound, the instant it was born, was swallowed whole by the white space, not a trace left behind.

She reached out and gripped the handle. The cold, hard metal was utterly real, nothing like a dream. A light twist of her fingertips —

Click.

The soft sound landed, and the door slid soundlessly inward.

The scene beyond made Yao Yuan freeze for a moment.

It was still pure white, but of a different texture. The floor underfoot had become silver metal that gleamed with cold light, smooth as a mirror, reflecting her blurred form. In the air hung an extremely faint, high-frequency hum, like the background noise of some precision instrument at work.

This was a laboratory.

A vast, empty, futuristic laboratory.

Yao Yuan stood in the doorway, her gaze running forward. About thirty meters off, six men in white lab coats stood with their backs to her, gathered in a ring, intent on a huge display screen before them. The screen nearly filled the whole wall, complex streams of data and rapidly switching images flowing across it, the shifting light casting changing blocks of color over their white-clad backs.

For no reason, her heartbeat skipped.

An inexplicable pull drew her forward, step by step, toward the six men. The silver floor was bright as a mirror, and her reflection warped, stretched, shattered, and reformed with each stride. The high-frequency hum in the air seemed to intensify slightly as she drew near.

When she was still ten paces from them —

The six men turned as one.

The motion was almost mechanically precise, yet carried a strange coordination.

In that instant, time froze.

Yao Yuan's breath stopped completely. The blood drained from her limbs back to her heart, then froze to ice the next second. Her eyes went wide, pupils contracting sharply, her whole body rigid where she stood, unable to so much as tremble a fingertip.

Six faces.

Six faces carved into the course of her life, fused into the marrow of her memory, faces that had made her taste every shade of love and hate, joy and grief.

From left to right they stood, in order — as if every important node of her thirty-eight years had been rewound and laid out before her eyes.

Jiang Hai. The earnest first love of her youth. At twenty-four they had spoken of marriage, but in the end the gulf between their families was too wide, and under his clan's crushing pressure he had resolutely given her up. Now he stood as the youngest, most handsome version her memory held, the white coat setting off his clean, upright frame — but those eyes, always brimming with gentle laughter, now held only a researcher's cool scrutiny. He looked at her as if observing an interesting colony of bacteria in a petri dish, worth studying.

Shuai Hongqiang. Once her lover of three passionate years, who had poured out tenderness and indulgence and given her extreme favor — yet who, when she fell unexpectedly pregnant at twenty-eight and most needed the security of a marriage certificate, had pulled back on the excuse that she was "too dazzling, too hard to handle." Now he was stripped of the smooth, weary glaze of a middle-aged businessman; his brow and eyes held the sharp, calculating keenness of their first meeting, and deep in them lay a cold appraisal she had never glimpsed before, his gaze sweeping over her like the verification of a precise data point.

Zhuge Jinye. Their brief half-year together had been the most ardent, purest time in her barren life. No debts owed, no scheming — only the white heat of two matched souls and a love that ran both ways. The sweat of the dance studio, the hormones, the passionate tenderness — all of it was vivid still. Now he was as dazzling and sharp as ever, the control bred into a dancer's very bones holding him still as a drawn bow — yet those eyes, which had once burned with sincere love for her, now held only obsessive focus, as if fixed on an experimental work nearing completion, waiting to be accepted.

Duan Yanqing. Their acquaintance had begun in calculation and deals, yet he had genuinely built her a staircase to grow on, gifting her rare connections, resources, and scope. That line beside the window of his private jet — "You are my most successful investment" — she remembered to this day. Now he carried the steady, weighty bearing of middle age, that familiar knowing smile still at the corner of his lips, but stripped of all desire and the urge to conquer, leaving only a detached, connoisseur's interest, looking down on her, head tilted slightly, quietly studying her.

Yu Hao. The companion who had begun in compatibility and ended in interests. Matched in thought, aligned in outlook, drawn by hormones, the feeling had deepened over time — yet in the end it could not outweigh his calculus of profit and loss. He had wanted an heir to his family who would carry her excellent genes, but had refused her a shred of legal protection. Now he was as handsome as ever, the look behind his gold-rimmed glasses bright and sharp, holding a faint curiosity and appraisal — but underneath lay a cool, outsider's detachment she had never once read before.

Last, her gaze fell on the man at the far right.

Zhao Yiming.

She breathed the name softly, a surge of warm memory roiling in her eyes. He was the one lover in her life who had never brought her pain, who had given her nothing but tenderness and security. The cold wind off Baima Beach, the warmth lingering in his arms, the anxious calls at her ear, the cold touch of the ring on her fingertip — all of it came rushing back, overlapping perfectly with the man before her.

But this Zhao Yiming wore the same white coat as the others, his frame tall and upright. Behind the gold-rimmed glasses, his eyes held the calm reason she knew so well — yet without a trace of love, without worry, without the thrill of having lost and found her again, and least of all the dread of nearly losing her at the beach just now. There was only pure, professional, absolute calm, with a touch of the relief and certainty of one who has just completed a major experiment.

In the instant their eyes met, his softened for the briefest moment, as if some deep-rooted program had triggered a conditioned reflex — so fast one might mistake it for an illusion. The next instant, he returned at once to a detached clarity and ice.

Yao Yuan's throat tightened, so dry it could make no sound. Countless thoughts smashed, exploded, and collapsed in her mind.

She had been through nine journeys through time, had tampered again and again with the trajectory of fate — could it be that the six people tied to those altered turning points had all been swept into this timeline together?

Just as her mind reeled and her thoughts scattered, the corner of her eye caught, without intent, a decorative mirror beside the giant screen.

The silver mirror was clear and bright, reflecting her present self in perfect detail, sharp down to the finest hair.

In the mirror was a face young enough to be a stranger.

Not the thirty-eight-year-old Yao Yuan, weathered by storms, the vicissitudes of years settled in her eyes.

It was her at twenty-three — still naive and sharp, not yet ground down by the world.

Her skin was full and clear, her eyes bright and limpid, holding the newborn edge and stubbornness of youth. Her long hair fell loose over her shoulders like a waterfall; a simple white dress made her clean and pure. She could even see the small, pale-brown "tear mole" below her left eye — the one that had been removed the year she turned twenty-eight.

In disbelief she raised a hand and touched her own cheek. The skin was smooth and fine beneath her fingertips, without the faint roughness of long-worn makeup, without the fine lines the years had left. She lowered her head to look at her hands — ten slender fingers, nails trimmed clean and neat, none of the exaggerated manicures she had later worn for the livestream's sake. These hands, this face, belonged completely to the twenty-three-year-old Yao Yuan.

Could it be she had truly shaken the foundations of fate, reset all cause and effect, and returned with her memory intact to twenty-three, when nothing had yet begun?

The thought had barely risen when a dizzying wild joy swept through her — but in the next instant a deeper unease welled up.

No, the logic was wrong.

Everything was wrong.

Their eyes were wrong, their bearing was wrong, the air about them was wrong; the cold, withdrawn, observing atmosphere that filled this space — all of it was wrong.

Yao Yuan stepped forward, her gaze sweeping urgently across the six familiar faces, trying to find on them a single trace of shock, bewilderment, the alarm of being caught in this place together, or the tremor of reunion.

But she found nothing.

Not one of the six showed any flicker of emotion.

The six stood quietly in the silver laboratory — uniform white coats, uniform detached posture, uniform scrutinizing gaze — looking down on her from above, as if through her young twenty-three-year-old body they were examining the entire core of her thirty-eight years of life. More like six researchers in a sterile lab, observing a sample in a petri dish that had just finished a long life-cycle experiment.

No thrill of long-parted reunion, no bewilderment at being in another world, no surprise at her sudden youth.

Only an extreme cold, professional, detached deathly stillness.

Yao Yuan fought to steady herself and looked at them, her voice hoarse, dry, and shot through with dread. "You… you don't know me?"

The question was absurd in the extreme. These six faces, carved with half a lifetime of her love and hate, were familiar to the bone. Yet the strangeness and detachment in their eyes was cold enough to break her heart.

After a long, dead silence, Zhao Yiming, at the far right, finally spoke.

His tone was steady, rational, restrained — the voice she knew so intimately — yet every word was tempered with ice, precise, sharp, cruel, driving one by one into her eardrums, shattering all her understanding and belief.

"Of course we know you, Sample 001."

Sample 001.

The five words burst like a thunderclap. Yao Yuan's mind went instantly blank, buzzing. She opened her mouth but could make no sound at all, every drop of blood in her body frozen solid.

Zhao Yiming kept the flat tone of one stating an experimental conclusion and went on, slowly, every word a blow to the heart:

"The small sandbox world you have just left is an immersive observation field we built exclusively for you."

"The thirty-eight years of life you have lived — from the moment of your birth to the end of the pact-breaking at Baima Beach on the Yellow River — is a complete life-observation sample we tailored and set for you."

"The birth, family, schooling, career, the tangles of love and hate, the rises and falls you believed were real — even your power of journeying through time nine times and the mystery of the Mirror Pact —"

He paused slightly, the eyes behind his lenses reflecting her suddenly pale face with perfect clarity. "All of it was the simulated environment and the stimuli we laid down for you, to observe how certain variables would respond."

Boom — !

In Yao Yuan's ears it was as if hundreds of millions of stars collapsed and exploded at once, then abruptly fell into dead silence.

Every sound, every ray of light, every sensation receded in an instant. She felt herself standing in an absolute void, a bottomless chasm splitting open beneath her feet.

An illusion?

An observation field?

Her whole life — only an experimental sample?

Was the sweat of practicing dance before dawn fake? Were the wrinkles at the corners of her mother Zhang Fengxia's eyes, as she watched her off to school at the mouth of the alley, fake? Was the drum-loud thrill of first love fake? Was the agony of losing her child, her body hollowed out, fake? Was the dryness in her throat as she dissected herself before tens of thousands of strangers' IDs in the livestream room fake? Was the tremor of Zhao Yiming's fingertips as he slid the ring onto her in the mirror room on New Year's Day fake? Was the pact-breaking ritual on the bank of the Yellow River just now, which had spent every last drop of her hope, also fake?

Her whole body went stiff, her limbs cold and numb, even her thoughts frozen solid to ice. Only Zhao Yiming's voice flowed on — steady, clear, cruel — each word grinding over every inch of the thirty-eight years of land she had believed was real:

"You must always have wondered why you alone possessed the power to journey back through time."

"Why, returning to the past nine times, persuading, mending, struggling, seeming to change the details of the process again and again, you could never shake the final direction of the key fated nodes."

Yao Yuan's eyes snapped up, already shot through with red. This was the fixation and confusion rooted in the deepest part of her soul, the source of her despair countless times over. She had thought it was destiny, the will of heaven, the karma and lesson she was bound to carry, a heavenly decree no mortal could resist.

Zhao Yiming watched the pain and accusation roiling in her eyes, tilted his head slightly, as if reading off a set of fixed data, then said lightly the ultimate, absurd, despairing truth:

"Because every 'past Yao Yuan' you met on each journey back was never truly you."

"Those were nine sets of secondary clones we cultivated from your original genetic sample, numbered 001-1 through 001-9, corresponding precisely to the nine key fate-correction points of your life."

Yao Yuan's body lurched violently, as if struck full in the chest by an unseen sledgehammer, swaying on the verge of collapse.

"Every 'young Yao Yuan' you spoke with, tried to warn, to save, to pour your hope into — all of them were scripted roles we arranged, carrying preset response programs."

"As for every key fated node, its direction was set from the very start of the observation experiment as an unalterable observational constant."

"You thought you were journeying through time."

"You thought you were making peace with your past."

"You thought you were redeeming yourself."

"You thought you were defying heaven and rewriting your fate."

"You thought that through one trauma after another you were forging a tougher, more resilient soul, and would at last break free of destiny to win clarity, independence, and love."

Zhao Yiming's gaze settled on her face. In it there was no mockery, no pity — only the absolute calm of a scientist stating an experimental conclusion. And that calm, more cruelly than any blade, brought an end to all the faith and meaning she had built over thirty-eight years of blood and tears:

"You were only performing, within the closed experimental environment we set up, following the observation script — day after day, flawlessly enacting the behavioral model and emotional curve of 'an intelligent, resilient woman gradually awakening through repeated trauma.'"

"Every pain, every breakdown, every struggle, every so-called breakthrough of yours fell within our expected range of data fluctuation."

"The more earnestly you lived, the harder you fought, the more genuinely you threw yourself into those 'lives,' the more precious the observation data you produced — and the better it verified our string of hypotheses about 'post-traumatic growth mechanisms,' 'the entrenchment and rupture of emotional patterns,' 'the internalization of and resistance to social gender roles,' and the rest."

Dead silence.

In the laboratory only that high-frequency hum remained. Now, in her ears, it was like hundreds of millions of bees beating their wings at once, laughing at her earnest, obsessive life.

Yao Yuan stood blankly where she was, looking at the six faces before her — familiar to the bone, yet strange to the point of horror — and everything in her heart collapsed, shattered, ground to dust.

Thirty-eight years of life's rise and fall, nine journeys through time, countless climbs out of the abyss, battles against fate, vigils kept over a flickering light in despair. Those tears no one knew of in the dead of night, those hopeless straits gritted out through clenched teeth, that relief at surviving disaster, that certainty of having at last grasped tenderness… it turned out that from beginning to end, it had all been a meticulously choreographed play.

Her suffering was a precisely designed experimental variable.

Her time-travel was a program-preset observation mechanism.

Her hope was bait to keep her producing data.

All her heart-rending struggles, all her cries she had thought came from the depths of her soul, her phoenix-like rebirth — all of it had only ever been code, charts, data, and raw material under the pens of six observers.

A long time passed.

So long it felt as though another lifetime had gone by.

Yao Yuan raised her head with excruciating slowness. The blood-red had drained entirely from her eyes, leaving only the desolation of land after a storm of wind and snow — empty, cold, barren of a single blade of grass. Her voice was light as something drifting outside the world, every word carrying the dust of a soul broken apart:

"Then who… am I?"

The six exchanged a glance. There was no communication in it — more like a set of coordinated programs completing some confirmation step.

In the end it was again Zhao Yiming who answered, his voice clear and ringing, echoing through the empty silver laboratory, pronouncing sentence on the entire meaning of her existence.

"You are a sandbox-observation clone we cultivated privately, numbered 001."

"You have no birth family, no real past. The mother who raised you, Zhang Fengxia, was a high-precision personality simulation program; your stepfather, Uncle Zhou, was a directed environmental-interaction model; Jin Lan, Liu Qingzhuo, and all your relatives and social ties were group-interaction variables implanted for you."

"Your childhood, your studies, your career, your social life, your loves and hates — your entire life history — were all projections of an immersive narrative backdrop."

"You had no self from the moment you came into being. The sole meaning of your existence was, within the holographic sandbox we built — one that approached real rules as closely as possible — to live out, with a complete system of biological perception and emotion, a full life on our behalf: one no one could live for you, that could not be escaped, struggled through with your own hands, experienced through from start to finish."

"The entire meaning of your existence was, within this holographic sandbox we built — approaching real physical and social rules as closely as possible — with a complete body's perception and emotional-feedback system, to live out a full life on our behalf: one no one could take over, that could not be escaped, that from beginning to end you had to grope through, struggle through, and fight your way up entirely on your own, until you finally broke free of your fetters."

In Yao Yuan's chest, a violent, real, physical wringing pain suddenly came.

The pain was so sharp, so concrete, that it bent her double. Yet the pain itself had now become an absurd joke — she could no longer tell whether it was a real reaction of her body, or a preset neural feedback of the clone vessel.

So that was it.

She was not, at all, one of the living.

She was only an observation tool, cultivated from who-knew-where.

And the whirlpool of destiny she had spent her whole life bleeding and weeping to escape — that she had thought she was steering a lone boat through towering waves to break free of — was nothing but a water-circulation track drawn by an experimenter on the outside of a glass tank.

She had swum with all her might for a lifetime.

She had gritted her teeth and fought for a lifetime.

She had thought she was fighting the whole world, when in fact she had only been hurling herself, again and again, against a transparent wall, at boundaries set in advance.

What she had taken for "breaking the deadlock" — the clarity, the independence, the resilience she had been so proud of — had only ever been part of the experiment's design.

It was the performance she, as "Sample 001," was required to complete in the small sandbox world.

Her whole earnest, striving, love-and-hate-tangled, hard-seeking life had, from beginning to end, been nothing but a play under someone else's pen.

Yao Yuan stood in the center of the silver laboratory, before the six "creators" who had wrought all the tragedy and joy of her life, and suddenly she wanted to laugh.

But the corner of her mouth had barely moved when scalding liquid, without any warning, came surging out of her eyes.

These tears — were they real?

She no longer knew.

========================================

Chapter 107 — (Side Story) All Beings Suffer, All Beings Are Imprisoned
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640354.html

All Beings Suffer, All Beings Are Imprisoned
In the pure-white laboratory, time seemed to have frozen solid.
Yao Yuan stood rooted to the spot, every ounce of strength drained out of her by the truth packed into those few short sentences. It was not her body that had given out. It was the spiritual bedrock that had carried thirty-eight years of belief, collapsing all at once.
Everything from the sandbox world was still churning vividly through her mind — the bone-deep pain, the joys — and in the space of one weightless sentence it was all negated: you are clone 001, your life is observational data, your struggle was a performance watched from start to finish.
Yao Yuan slowly lifted her eyes.
There was no loss of control, no wild fury, none of the hysterical breakdown one might have expected.
Thirty-eight years of rising and falling, nine trips through time — they had long since tempered a cold, quiet clarity into her very bones. Right now those eyes held nothing but a barren calm, like a field swept by a blizzard, where not a single blade of grass remained.
She looked at the six faces before her, familiar and yet strange, her voice dry:
"So I'm an experiment you built."
"Then who are you?"
Zhao Yiming took half a step forward.
He was the only one in the sandbox world who had never caused her pain, and he was the fiancé who, just moments ago at the edge of the Yellow River, had held her tight, his voice full of panic and worry. But now, in a white lab coat, his features cold and distant, he stood before her like a stone carving without warmth, every trace of the old tenderness gone.
"We are clone laborers of the Ruins Domain."
His tone was flat, as if he were stating an objective fact that had nothing to do with him. Yet what he stated was a truth about the layers of reality that Yao Yuan had never once touched.
"The small sandbox world you just left sits at the third layer of reality — what we call the 'Sandbox Illusion.'"
"The six of us dwell in the second layer, the Ruins Domain."
"And the topmost first layer, which you have never seen, is the Source Domain — the home of the native natural-born humans."
Yao Yuan's pupils contracted slightly.
For the first time, a whole new picture of the world, sharply tiered, was laid bare before her. Not parallel timelines, not a multiverse, but… a cage of layers stacked one atop another.
Unhurried, Zhao Yiming kept tearing away every disguise, each word a scalpel that cut precisely into the flesh of this world:
"You struggled half your life inside the sandbox, thinking you'd reached the limit of human suffering. But you didn't know that the thing you possess is a luxury we will never have in our whole lives."
"You had a complete life — joy and anger, sorrow and joy, love and hate and obsession. You had room to make mistakes, the chance to start over, ties of flesh and blood, the freedom to choose whom to love and whom to hate."
"We, from the moment of our birth, had our fate nailed shut by our genes."
As his words landed, a faint shadow passed across the faces of the other five in unison. That desolation was no act. It was the weariness and suppression carved deep into their genes by years of shackles and the grinding of fate, the marks left by carrying a heavy load year after year.
Yao Yuan's heart sank suddenly. She realized in a flash that these six people before her — gentle or cutting, the ones who had arranged her entire life — were just as deeply mired in the swamp of fate.
"Our genes, all six of us, are exact copies of the gene sequences of six top-tier native humans in the Source Domain." The next to speak was Shuai Hongqiang, his tone wrapped in heavy fatigue. "Our one and only purpose in life is to live for them, to labor for them, to bear all the worldly suffering, pressure, wear, and loss in their place."
Yao Yuan's breath caught.
"The native humans of the Source Domain have the most lavish resources, the most secure lives, the highest level of access to the world."
"They loathe toil, loathe hardship, loathe being worn smooth by life, loathe every 'dirty, exhausting job' that has to be done with their own hands."
"So they used the world's foundational laws to legally cultivate us — six cloned stand-ins."
Shuai Hongqiang raised his eyes and looked at Yao Yuan, his gaze too complex to read — self-mockery, numbness, and a very faint, almost kindred pity:
"They enjoy, we labor. They rest easy, we carry the load. They ponder philosophy and art, we handle data and crises. We are their shadows, their consumables, the tools they'll never feel a pang of pity for — used up and thrown away."
"Our lifespans are artificially compressed to one-third of the originals'. Our genes are permanently locked by the 'labor contract.' We can never defect, never break the contract, never possess a true 'self.' Even our emotional responses and thought patterns are tuned to the most 'efficient and pragmatic' specifications, so we can shoulder the maximum possible burden for them."
Each word fell, cold and piercing to the bone.
Yao Yuan stared at them blankly, and the spark of rage in her heart — at being deceived, at being toyed with — strangely guttered out, most of it gone.
So… they were not creators holding the power of life and death either.
They were prisoners too.
Prisoners trapped in a larger cage — a cage with genes for locks and class for walls, far more unsolvable than the sandbox world.
"We refuse to accept it."
This time it was Jiang Hai who spoke. This man, forever gentle and refined in Yao Yuan's memory, now had a defiance welling in his eyes, the residue of countless years.
"But we can't rise up against the native humans of the Source Domain. The pressure of the hierarchy, the shackles of our genes, the world's foundational rules — layer upon layer locks off every way out." Jiang Hai's voice was very soft, yet heavy as a mountain. "Upward, there is only a sheer cliff with no path."
"So we could only search downward for a way to live."
Zhuge Jinye picked up the thread. This man who had once burned his life away on the stage, who had given her a pure and blazing love, still had that focused gaze — but its object had shifted from art to a far crueler reality:
"We spent countless Ruins-Domain years secretly hoarding scraps of technology discarded by the upper layers, piecing together the data garbage that leaked between realities, and in the blind spots of the Ruins Domain's surveillance, we built this underground laboratory."
"And then we made you — clone 001."
At last, the original purpose of the whole experiment was fully laid bare.
Yao Yuan held her breath, listening to the true meaning of her own birth.
"We made you not to spectate suffering, nor to verify a theory." Duan Yanqing began slowly. This man, who had once been the guide on her 'life's ladder,' now spoke with a deep weariness and obsession. "It was to save ourselves."
"We wanted to observe a complete sample of a 'natural-born human' life — one with no stand-in, no one to lean on, no one to do the work, where everything had to be done with one's own hands."
"We wanted to know — when an intelligent being is thrown into a survival game it is entirely responsible for, facing real pain, temptation, betrayal, loss, hardship, and love, what kind of life force would burst out of it? Does true growth, the true breaking of a deadlock, the true tearing-free, come from outside opportunity, or from inner awakening?"
"We wanted to find, in your one lifetime, a sliver of possibility — a faint glimmer that could crack the shackles of our genes, dissolve the contracts bound to our originals, and free us from our eternal fate as 'consumables.'"
Yao Yuan's throat tightened, as though something invisible had it by the neck.
So her suffering, her struggle, the rise and fall of most of her life — none of it had ever been idle mockery or a cruel game.
It was the single straw that six people in a desperate corner, with nowhere left to turn, had clutched with all their strength. It was the one source of light they had gazed toward in the dark.
"In the first stage of observation, we watched how you turned your life around by yourself in a sandbox with no outside help at all." Yu Hao pushed up his glasses, his rational, analytical tone hiding a tension hard to detect. "The data confirmed one of our basic conjectures: under absolutely rigid class rules, the effort, endurance, even the local comebacks of a single individual cannot shake the rule structure at the top. The power of the individual has its limits."
"But the greatest obsession of the world has never been 'not trying hard enough.'" Zhao Yiming took over, his eyes settling on Yao Yuan's face with the focus of a researcher studying crucial data. "The deepest delusion people hold is, 'If only I could start over.'"
"If I went back to the past, corrected that wrong choice, avoided the person destined to hurt me, seized the chance I missed… would my life be different?"
The gazes of the six now fell on Yao Yuan all at once.
Calm, scrutinizing, yet carrying a trace of… an almost pitying understanding.
"So we launched the second stage of the experiment." Zhao Yiming's voice echoed clearly through the empty laboratory. "We implanted in you a fake 'time-reversal' access. Using your genes, we cultivated nine secondary clones — numbered 001-1 to 001-9 — each an exact copy of one of the nine pivotal moments of your youth. At the underlying logic level we locked the final outcome of every core fate node, opening up only the room to adjust the process variables."
Yao Yuan's fingertips began to tremble, faintly, beyond her control.
Those nine returns, nine all-out attempts at persuasion, nine times watching her 'younger self' nod in agreement and then walk toward the same ending in despair… all the scenes, emotions, and confusions now strung themselves together in an instant, forming a complete and cruel closed loop of logic.
At last she understood, completely.
Why, even when she had clearly persuaded her past 'self,' even when she had clearly steered around the disaster right in front of her, she still couldn't escape a similar ending.
It was not that heaven was merciless.
It was not that fate was unbreakable.
It was not that she hadn't tried hard enough or wasn't clear-eyed enough.
It was that from the very start, all her 'do-overs,' all the 'salvation' into which she had poured her entire hope and feeling, had been a carefully designed controlled experiment. The variables were the process; the constant was the ending. The proposition she was meant to verify had been written long ago.
"We wanted to verify a second answer." Zhao Yiming looked at her, word by word, sealing the verdict on her thirty-eight years. "To verify whether a human being can break free of a predetermined track of fate by making up for regrets, correcting the past, and achieving reconciliation with the self."
The answer had long been obvious.
Nine returns, nine changes, nine attempts at salvation.
Still… no solution.
"Your thirty-eight years of life, your countless struggles, pains, hopes, and despairs, proved one thing for us beyond all doubt." Shuai Hongqiang's voice dropped low, carrying a profound weariness, even a trace of… disillusionment. "Regret is useless. Starting over is useless. The self-salvation of an individual has limited effect in the face of systemic class shackles."
"An individual's obsession, awakening, even local breakthroughs, can never break through the established rules of the upper reality and the pressure of its structure. True freedom does not come from correcting the past; it comes from a structural understanding of the present and a response to it. But understanding itself, with no power to back it, cannot break through the systemic shackles of class — it remains only the suffering of being clear-eyed."
The laboratory sank into a deep silence.
Only that pervasive high-frequency hum remained, telling of the cold, absolute laws by which this world ran.
Yao Yuan closed her eyes.
Countless emotions churned in her heart — fury, desolation, absurdity, release, a weariness that reached the marrow, and… a strange thread of understanding.
Did she hate them?
She did.
She hated that her whole life had been manipulated, hated that her deep feeling and pain had been harvested as data, hated that all her effort and growth had been only a performance under observation.
And yet she couldn't bring herself to hate them entirely.
Because these six people who had deceived her, designed her, observed her whole life, were themselves pitiful creatures crushed by fate, struggling inside an even larger cage.
The native humans of the topmost Source Domain hid in their absolutely safe nest of comfort, reaping what others sowed.
The clone laborers of the middle layer were trapped inside an eternal 'contract,' carrying their load forward with nowhere to turn.
And she, at the bottom, was trapped in the Sandbox Illusion, struggling in vain, thinking she was fighting fate when in truth she had never once touched the real rules.
One layer pressing on the next, one ring locked into another.
All beings suffer; all beings are imprisoned.
No one was a truly, purely 'evil' person. Everyone was only a fish trapped in a fish tank of a different size, struggling with all its might to swim upward, to breathe, to break free.
The only difference was that some fish had seen the boundary of the tank, while others thought what lay before them was the whole world; some fish were ramming the boundary, while others were preying on smaller fish.
A long while passed.
So long it felt as if she had lived another whole life.
Yao Yuan slowly opened her eyes. The barren ice and snow in their depths gradually melted — not into warmth, but congealing into something colder, harder, and clearer: a clarity that cut through all lies and disguises straight to the essence.
"So what now?"
She raised her eyes and swept her gaze calmly over the six, her voice betraying no emotion.
"Is the experiment over?"
The six were silent for a moment.
Zhao Yiming slowly nodded, the motion carrying the deep weariness of someone who has just completed a great task.
"Of all our experimental samples, you are the only one who has perfectly awakened, broken free of the sandbox logic by your own consciousness, and possesses a complete, independent personality with reflection on your own existence — a successful sample."
"From the standpoint of the experiment, there is no longer any value in keeping you in the Sandbox Illusion."
Yao Yuan let out a soft "Oh," the tail of it lifting, carrying an almost innocent puzzlement — yet it made all six of their hearts clench at once.
"So," she asked, her tone as flat as if she were asking about the weather, "what is my ending?"
The six exchanged a glance.
In that glance there was struggle, there was guilt, but in the end it was all covered over by a deeper despair and survival instinct that belonged to 'clone laborers.' It was Duan Yanqing — the man who had once taught her the rules of 'value exchange' — who spoke the coldest reality:
"The original contingency for ending the experiment was this — to extract all of your consciousness data, your cross-reality gene-expression data, and the complete model of your growth trajectory. That data will be used to attempt to reverse-engineer the 'labor contract' in our genes, to look for a possible loophole or an override."
He paused, his voice dropping lower:
"The data extraction process will completely break down the carrier of your consciousness. For you, it will be a painless, eternal… dissolution of consciousness."
Boom.
The last layer of the illusion of tenderness shattered completely.
Even if the original intent was self-salvation. Even if every one of them was a prisoner of fate. Even if they too were covered in wounds.
When the moment of choice came, when the interests of the lower-layer 'consumables' clashed with the even-lower-layer 'experiment,' they still chose to sacrifice her.
To trade her total annihilation for their slim, uncertain hope of release.
This was the law of survival inside the cage. There was no right or wrong, only position.
After hearing it all, Yao Yuan did not break down, curse, or beg as they had expected.
She only stood up straight, slowly and with perfect steadiness. Inside that thin, twenty-three-year-old frame there suddenly rose an unbending, razor-edged pride, forged through thirty-eight years of storms.
She had known the deepest love and betrayal, walked through the most hopeless swamps, endured countless nights of breaking herself apart and rebuilding. She could accept that she was an experiment, accept that her life was a false sandbox, accept that all her struggles, seen from a higher dimension, were only data.
But she would never accept this — born to be a tool, ending as a consumable, her life and death decided by others, her worth defined by others.
She lifted her eyes, her gaze clear and sharp, like a drawn blade slicing through the silent air.
"I don't agree."
Three short words, clear and calm, yet carrying a force that brooked no question, echoing through the empty silver laboratory.
All six knit their brows at once.
"You have no right to choose." Jiang Hai's voice turned cold, the tone of a 'manager.' "Your very existence is a violation. Your life and death have never been yours to decide."
Yao Yuan smiled faintly.
That smile was very light, without warmth, yet strangely dazzling — like a poisonous flower blooming from the rubble of a hopeless place.
"I'm a clone you created by illegal means."
"My life is a sandbox script you set."
"My pain and my awakening are the data you needed to observe."
"My value is the experimental result you defined."
She took a step forward. Though she stood below them, her gaze carried a piercing power that met them eye to eye — even looked down on them:
"But, from beginning to end — my will, my feelings, every genuine sorrow and joy of my thirty-eight years, every moment I gritted my teeth and stood back up, every choice to seize a glimmer of light in despair… those belong only to 'Yao Yuan.'"
"You were in a desperate corner, and you used me to find a way out. I don't blame you for that."
"But now you want to trade my total annihilation for your possible release."
Her voice turned cold all at once, each word like an ice bead striking a silver plate:
"Impossible."
In this moment.
The fish that had climbed up from the bottommost sandbox, through one bloody, battered collision after another, one stubborn unextinguished thought after another, one rebuilding of meaning after another in the void, until at last it smashed through the glass wall…
no longer settled for merely 'seeing a bigger fish tank.'
It had grown teeth.
It had grown the ambition to tear apart every visible net — and even to ram the boundary of the tank itself, the boundary that had seemed impossible to shake.
The six clone laborers looked at the suddenly transformed Yao Yuan, and for the first time their faces turned truly grave.
They realized in a flash that this 'perfect sample' they had cultivated with their own hands, observed so carefully day and night… really did seem to have leapt out of all their preset models.
She was no longer the variable being observed. She was the new variable about to reshape the whole board.

========================================

Chapter 108 — Everything Is a Cage; To Follow One's Own Heart Is Freedom
https://www.xbanxia.cc/books/417648/72640355.html

Everything is a cage; to follow one's own heart is freedom.

But the standoff did not last—

Hmmmm!!!

A low, sweeping mechanical roar, carrying the pressure of absolute authority, came without warning from above the laboratory's pure-white dome.

The sound was nothing like the room's background hum. It was older, more majestic, more… exalted. As if it were the heartbeat of the world itself.

The faces of the six laborer clones went white as paper in an instant.

It was not fear. It was something deeper—an instinct written into their genetic code: absolute obedience.

Their bodies bent slightly, beyond their control; their heads bowed. It was the posture countless generations of clones had been preset to assume before their originals. Even with their minds full of resentment and rebellion, the chains in their genes tightened at once, until they could barely breathe.

Yao Yuan jerked her head up and looked toward the dome.

The once seamless white ceiling had split open into a web of fine golden lines. The lines spread and interlaced with astonishing speed, until they formed a complex, dazzling array of light.

The glow flared.

Six figures, accompanied by a heart-stopping ripple in space, slowly descended from the array and solidified onto the floor.

The light faded.

Six people stood quietly on the raised platform at the center of the laboratory.

Their features, their bone structure, the contours of their faces…

Identical to the six trembling laborer clones in front of them.

Identical, too, to the six men in Yao Yuan's memory—the ones she had been tangled with in love and hate inside the sandbox world.

But their bearing was a world apart.

It was the looseness, the languor, of those who have lived at the top for so long, fed by endless resources, never knowing real scarcity or struggle. Their eyes were clear, yet carried a careless detachment, as if everything before them—including the six clones who wore their faces—was no more than irrelevant background scenery.

Their very existence radiated an invisible, suffocating air pressure of "class."

The Source Realm. Native-born naturals. The originals of the six laborer clones.

The true "masters," seated at the top of the first fish pond, looking down on all living things.

The air in the laboratory froze solid.

"No wonder. For the last few Ruins Realm cycles, the 'labor attrition rate' has been fluctuating strangely, and the performance data they reported had subtle logical flaws."

The man standing in the middle—the one who wore Zhuge Jinye's face—spoke mildly. His voice was not loud, yet it carried a natural authority; every syllable pressed clearly onto every heart in the room.

His gaze swept idly over the six deeply bowed clones who dared not lift their heads, as if scanning a few malfunctioning tools. At last it settled on the only one standing upright, the only one looking back at him: Yao Yuan.

Something flickered through that gaze—a clear surprise that quickly thickened into a more probing, faintly amused scrutiny.

"So it was you six pieces of stock—quietly diverting the Source Realm's gene-bank permissions to run an illegal private experiment."

The word "stock," tossed off so lightly.

It crushed the last shred of the six laborer clones' dignity, and made their absolute place in this world utterly clear: tools. And tools about to be discarded.

The six clones went rigid all over, fingernails biting deep into their palms, yet not a single thought of resistance or protest could rise. Before the true original, the shackles of their genes were an absolute, unbridgeable chasm.

The original "Zhuge Jinye" stepped slowly down from the platform. Ignoring the six "copies," he walked straight up to Yao Yuan. He tilted his head slightly, like a man admiring an interesting work of art he had unexpectedly acquired.

"Native-natural gene sequence, copied to a high degree of fidelity… purity even exceeding those six rejects," he murmured to himself, fingertips twitching unconsciously, as if invisible streams of data were sliding past before his eyes. "No stand-in-contract contamination, a fully natural growth trajectory, extremely high consciousness integrity—and she's even broken through the lower-plane barrier…"

He lifted his eyes to Yao Yuan, the corners of his mouth curving into a smile that held no warmth, yet was striking enough:

"Interesting. You pieces of stock, blundering along, actually produced a 'sample' far more interesting than you worn-out 'tools' yourselves."

A single sentence shattered every scheme and every hope the six laborer clones had held.

And in that instant it carved out the three cruel, crystal-clear tiers of the laboratory:

The Source Realm's top-tier native naturals—they make the rules, enjoy the spoils, born to be masters.

The Ruins Realm's middle-tier laborer clones—they carry out the rules, bear the burden, born to be servants, and about to be phased out.

The sandbox-realm bottom-tier breakthrough, Yao Yuan—an accidental product, an observation sample, a new and more promising "tool" candidate.

Three tiers, sharply separated, yet all bound fast by invisible chains.

The original "Zhuge Jinye" raised his eyes, his gaze drifting lazily over the six ashen-faced laborer clones. His tone was casual, yet carried the absolute indifference of one deciding the life and death of others:

"You diverted gene sequences without authorization and ran an illegal cross-plane experiment, in grave violation of the Source Realm's Basic Law on Clone Control. By that law, the clones involved should be forcibly scrapped at once, the laboratory permanently sealed, all data erased."

The six clones shuddered violently; despair drowned them like a flooding tide.

"However," the original's tone shifted, his gaze returning to Yao Yuan, full of undisguised appraisal and interest, "in view of this 'accidental result,' I can give you a chance to atone."

He extended a finger and pointed lightly at Yao Yuan:

"Tame her. Control her. Strip away her autonomous consciousness, but preserve her growth potential and her breakthrough traits. Then deliver her to me, whole."

His voice was calm, yet it issued the cruelest of orders:

"I want her to become my new, more perfect, more malleable… ultimate stand-in sample."

"As for you—" his gaze swept coldly over the six clones, "if you do well, perhaps you'll be spared scrapping and demoted to secondary maintenance units. If you do poorly…"

The unfinished words were more terrifying than any threat.

Boom!

The six laborer clones jolted as if struck by lightning, their whole bodies convulsing. Their heads snapped up, and for the first time their eyes blazed with uncontrollable, extreme panic and the deepest despair.

For untold ages they had endured, gambling everything, scheming down to the last detail, building Yao Yuan—and all they had sought was a sliver of a chance to break free of their shackles.

And what was the ending they got in the end?

The "hope" they had raised with their own hands would replace them, becoming a tool more convenient to the master's grip.

While they themselves would lose even the right to be "tools." Their best fate was to sink into lower-grade "stock"; the more likely one, to be utterly "scrapped."

All their struggling, all their scheming, before the true rule-makers at the top, was nothing but a futile joke. They had not won. Yao Yuan had not won. The winners were always those seated calmly on the fishing platform, harvesting every fruit at their leisure.

Above the fish pond, there is another fish pond.

Above the harvesters, there are still more harvesters.

This game, it seemed, would never end.

The laboratory fell into a deadlocked three-way struggle, the air so taut it seemed it might burst at any moment:

The Source Realm original wanted Yao Yuan, the superior "new tool," and through her meant to clear away the disobedient "old tools."

The six laborer clones did not want to be replaced and scrapped, but the genetic shackles made it impossible to resist the original, and their only road to survival was to destroy with their own hands the Yao Yuan they had created—the one who might also be the only being who understood their pain.

Yao Yuan—unwilling to be tamed, dissected, or defined as a tool by anyone, whether clone or original.

Everyone was struggling.

Everyone was a pawn.

Everyone was trapped in their own tier of the "fish pond," straining to swim out, yet perhaps only swimming into another, larger net.

And there, in that suffocating dead silence.

There, as the Source Realm original frowned slightly, seemingly displeased by the "tools'" sluggishness.

There, as the six laborer clones' eyes wavered violently, swinging between genetic obedience and self-destruction.

Yao Yuan suddenly, softly, laughed out loud.

The laugh was faint, yet it pierced clearly through the congealed air and drew every gaze.

She lifted her head, her gaze passing calmly over the Source Realm original high above, over the agonized, struggling laborer clones, and finally up to this pure-white laboratory dome—the symbol of absolute control and reason.

Her eyes were astonishingly clear. There was no anger in them, no fear, no despair—only something that had pierced through all the fog and seen some essential truth…

Understanding, and compassion.

She spoke quietly. Her voice was not loud, yet strangely it sounded in the depths of everyone's heart:

"So this is the whole of the truth."

"The bottom struggles, believing it is fighting against fate."

"The middle bears its burden, believing it is searching for a way out."

"The top looks down, believing it is in control of the game."

Her gaze drifted slowly over the twelve faces before her—identical, yet utterly distinct (six originals, six clones)—and the smile at the corner of her mouth deepened, and grew colder:

"You all keep asking—where is the way out? Where is freedom? Where is the key to breaking out of the cage?"

"You look up, and there are sheer cliffs on every side. You look down, and there are weaker strugglers. So you tear at one another, scheme against one another, using the blood of those below to try to grease your own rusted-shut shackles."

She took a step forward. Though she stood in the lowest position of all, her presence seemed to rise without limit.

"But have any of you ever considered—"

Her gaze came at last to rest on the face of the Source Realm original, "Zhuge Jinye." It was so calm that even this top-tier natural, who had weathered every storm, felt a flicker of nameless unease.

"What if the 'upper tier' you imagine yourselves to be is also nothing more than 'fish' in another, larger pond?"

"What if your so-called 'original,' 'Source Realm,' 'top-tier authority' is also nothing more than an 'observation sample' or 'management tool' designed by some higher being?"

"What if these layer upon layer of fish ponds, this endless harvesting, this seemingly impregnable hierarchy and these rules—"

Her voice suddenly dropped, yet it carried an earth-shattering force:

"What if all of it is, in itself, an endless, meaningless… infinite loop?"

In the laboratory, the silence was deathly.

The pupils of the Source Realm original contracted, almost imperceptibly.

The six laborer clones lifted their heads, dazed.

Yao Yuan looked at them, looked at all this absurd cruelty, and the last trace of anger and resentment in her heart suddenly dissolved like smoke.

In its place came a calm she had never known, and… freedom.

It was freedom on the level of awareness. A freedom that rose from the depths of the soul, undeniable, once one had seen through the essence of all rules, all lies, all oppression.

I am I.

My experience is what is real.

My pain and my joy are the proof that I existed.

Whether that existence is "natural" or "manufactured," "real" or "virtual," "original" or "clone."
